articles.headline: Chloroquine and pyronaridine-artesunate both cure P. vivax malaria in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Efficacy of chloroquine plus low-dose primaquine and pyronaridine-artesunate plus low-dose primaquine against Plasmodium vivax malaria in adults and transmission to mosquitoes in Ethiopia: a phase 2/3, observer-masked, randomised, parallel-group trial.
articles.authors: Bezabih MK, Sisay S, Behaksera SW, Ramjith J, Teka H, Girma S, Shimelash A, Ejigu LA, Kassa FA, Hailegiorgis B, Tadesse M, Misganaw T, Gizat A, Gashaw A, Habtamu G, Legesse NS, Hundera NL, Milki DW, Abdo M, Chali W, Tongren JE, Hwang J, Bousema T, Tadesse FG
articles.details: A phase 2/3, observer-masked, randomised parallel-group trial at two Ethiopian health centres enrolled 176 adults (88 per group) with uncomplicated P. vivax malaria, comparing chloroquine plus 14-day low-dose primaquine versus pyronaridine-artesunate plus low-dose primaquine. Day-28 adequate clinical and parasitological response (ACPR) was 98.7% (78/79) for chloroquine and 98.8% (82/83) for pyronaridine-artesunate, with day-42 ACPR of 96.4% (80/83) for pyronaridine-artesunate. Adverse events were mild (headache, weakness, body pain, chills) with no serious events. In the transmission substudy, 55 (92%) of 60 gametocyte carriers were infectious to mosquitoes. Both regimens were efficacious and well tolerated.

articles.headline: Wealth, ITN use, and rural residence drive under-5 malaria risk
articles.title_original: Determinants of malaria prevalence among children under 5 years of age in 17 sub-Saharan African countries: insights from Malaria Indicator Surveys.
articles.authors: Kassie AT, Bazezew AM, Tamir TT, Tekeba B, Zeleke GA, Mekonen EG, Endalew HL, Wassie M, Zegeye AF
articles.details: A secondary analysis of Malaria Indicator Surveys (2016-2022) including 51,457 children under five across 17 sub-Saharan African countries used multilevel mixed-effects logistic regression to identify malaria determinants. More than one-third of children were infected. Individual-level risk factors included age 1-2 years (AOR=1.46) and low household wealth (AOR=1.94), while maternal primary education (AOR=0.84) and insecticide-treated bed net use (AOR=0.80) were protective. At the community level, rural residence (AOR=2.69) and high community illiteracy (AOR=1.76) raised risk. Authors urge expanding bed-net access and targeted health education for vulnerable groups.

articles.headline: Caretakers' experiences of children with post-severe-malaria neurologic deficits in Uganda
articles.title_original: Perceptions and experiences of caretakers of children with neurologic deficits after severe malaria in Uganda.
articles.authors: Okullo AE, Kisakye S, Idro R, Boele van Hensbroek M, John CC, Kaawa-Mafigiri D
articles.journal: Int J Qual Stud Health Well-being
articles.details: A qualitative thematic analysis (phenomenological approach) explored perceptions and experiences of caretakers of children with neurologic deficits after severe malaria in Uganda, using 23 in-depth interviews and four focus group discussions. Deficits included motor, movement, cognitive, behavioural, and sensory impairments and epilepsy. Eight themes emerged, including mixed perceptions, difficulty educating impaired children, community mistreatment, mental and emotional distress, financial strain, misconceptions, limited access to specialised care, and adaptation. Caretakers held misconceptions about the cause of deficits and reported negative experiences but learned to cope. Authors recommend community health messaging and education campaigns targeting caretakers.

articles.headline: AL and pyronaridine-artesunate cure P. falciparum despite Kelch13 mutations in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Evaluation of the effectiveness of artemether-lumefantrine and artesunate-pyronaridine each combined with single low-dose primaquine in adults with Plasmodium falciparum infection in Ethiopia: a phase 2/3, observer-masked, randomised, parallel-group trial.
articles.authors: Bezabih MK, Misganaw T, Ejigu LA, Rosillo S, Teka H, Alemayehu DH, Adnew B, Hailegiorgis B, Girma S, Tadesse M, Kassa FA, Gebremichael M, Tefera DA, Chali W, Behaksera SW, Alemayehu L, Deressa JD, Abdo M, Habtamu G, Gizat A, Yalew WG, Jember TH, Bitew M, Mumba P, Plucinski MM, Souza S, Patel DS, Talundzic E, Taffese HS, Geleta KE, Mehari D, Bayissa GA, Bousema T, Tongren JE, Hwang J, Tadesse FG
articles.details: A phase 2/3, observer-masked, randomised parallel-group trial at two Ethiopian health centres, in an area with high pfkelch13 mutation and pfhrp2/3 deletion prevalence, enrolled 199 individuals (median age 25 years) with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria, comparing artemether-lumefantrine plus primaquine (n=101) versus pyronaridine-artesunate plus primaquine (n=98). Day-28 PCR-corrected ACPR was 96.8% (95% CI 90.9-99.3) for artemether-lumefantrine and 98.9% (93.9-99.9) for pyronaridine-artesunate; day-42 ACPR for pyronaridine-artesunate was 96.5%. No early treatment failures or serious adverse events occurred; common mild events were headache, weakness, and body ache. Findings support continued use of artemether-lumefantrine plus primaquine.

articles.headline: Meta-analysis identifies mortality predictors in malaria with acute kidney injury
articles.title_original: Prognostic factors in malaria patients with acute kidney injury: a systematic review and meta analysis.
articles.authors: Tear AA, Fauziah N, Riswari SF, Purba AR, Akhlaq MM, Susanto NH
articles.details: A systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated predictors of mortality in malaria patients with acute kidney injury (AKI), including 16 observational studies with 1,104 patients, with study quality assessed by the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale. Factors significantly associated with mortality included ventilator requirement (OR 30.35, p<0.001), respiratory distress syndrome (OR 18.50, p<0.001), hypotension (OR 18.23, p<0.001), neurological involvement (OR 12.83, p<0.001), disseminated intravascular coagulation (OR 12.00, p<0.001), jaundice (OR 5.03), acidosis (OR 4.88), leukocytosis (OR 4.87), hyperkalemia (OR 3.99), hyponatremia (OR 3.67), and oliguria (OR 3.41). Authors stress vigilance in managing patients with these conditions.

articles.headline: Higher malaria positivity in family-replacement blood donors in Saudi Arabia
articles.title_original: Transfusion-transmitted infections among blood donors in Saudi Arabia: demographic and malaria risk differences by donation purpose.
articles.authors: Alshalani A, Alzamil L, Alrezaihi A, Alsughayyir J, Bin Muhannaa NI, Alshehri FS, Almuqrin AM
articles.details: This retrospective cross-sectional study analyzed records of 49,590 blood donors (10.6% family replacement, 89.4% voluntary; 92.6% male) at King Fahad Medical City, Riyadh, screening for HBV, HCV, HIV, syphilis, and malaria. Rates of the major viral infections and syphilis did not differ significantly between donor types, but malaria prevalence was significantly higher among family replacement donors (5.3%) than voluntary donors (2.9%), an effect especially pronounced among non-Saudi donors, with nationality modifying the association. The authors stress continued malaria screening and donor selection to keep transfusion safe.

articles.headline: Review finds Matrix-M-adjuvanted vaccines, including R21 malaria, well tolerated
articles.title_original: Safety of Matrix-M-adjuvanted COVID-19, seasonal influenza, combination influenza-COVID-19, and malaria vaccines: a review of the evidence.
articles.authors: Fix J, Lee S, Nachbar J, Sadadia P, Lövgren Bengtsson K, Stertman L, Palm AE, Walker R, Draghia Akli R, Sellers S
articles.details: This review evaluated reactogenicity and safety data from 66 clinical trials and post-marketing studies of Matrix-M-adjuvanted COVID-19, seasonal influenza, combination influenza-COVID-19, and malaria (R21/Matrix-M for P. falciparum) vaccines, searched through 1 November 2025. Across 64,101 participants receiving ≥1 dose (143,170 doses total: 55,939 COVID-19, 2,477 influenza, 1,422 combination, 4,263 malaria), all authorized and candidate vaccines were well tolerated, including in immunocompromised people and children. Active-comparator data showed favorable reactogenicity versus licensed vaccines, notably lower reactogenicity after NUVAXOVID than mRNA COVID-19 vaccines.

articles.headline: Chlorfenapyr-pyrethroid nets outperform pyrethroid-only nets against resistant mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Chlorfenapyr-pyrethroid nets for pyrethroid-resistant malaria vectors: efficacy, resistance risks, and policy implications.
articles.authors: John Mwang'onde B
articles.details: This systematic review of 31 eligible studies (from 113 records, literature 2010-2024) assessed chlorfenapyr-pyrethroid combination nets against pyrethroid-resistant Anopheles. The nets consistently outperformed pyrethroid-only nets, showing a 1.8-fold increase in mosquito mortality (95% CI 1.5-2.1), with community trials reporting 40-60% reductions in malaria infection incidence and entomological inoculation rates. However, early chlorfenapyr resistance emerged in Anopheles gambiae in Central Africa (RR 2.4, p=0.01) linked to CYP6P4 overexpression, and agricultural pesticide use correlated with resistance (r=0.62, p<0.05). Authors urge integrated resistance management to sustain effectiveness.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic carriers harbor artemisinin-resistant P. falciparum in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Asymptomatic malaria: a hidden reservoir for the transmission and circulation of artemisinin partial-resistant Plasmodium falciparum in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Mazigo E, Lee WJ, Jun H, Fitriana F, Syahada JH, Louis JM, Muh F, Lu F, Lee KJ, Nam JW, Han JH, Kwon TH, Lee SJ, Na S, Chun W, Park WS, Han ET, Todd J, Manjurano A, Kidima W, Han JH
articles.details: This community-based cross-sectional study in Tanzania enrolled 3,489 participants from high-transmission Geita and Kigoma and low-transmission Arusha, testing blood by RDT, microscopy, and qPCR (positivity 24.4%, 15.8%, and 26.2%, indicating many submicroscopic infections). Of 802 isolates sequenced for pfk13 and pfmdr1, 24 (3.0%), all from high-transmission areas, carried validated pfk13 partial-resistance markers Y493H (0.2%), R561H (2.0%), and A675V (0.7%); low-transmission isolates were wild-type. All retained pfmdr1 N86, and the lumefantrine-linked NFD haplotype appeared in 48.1% and 48.4% of high- and low-transmission isolates. Mutations were commoner in under-fives and females. Authors conclude asymptomatic carriers are a hidden reservoir of resistant parasites needing molecular surveillance.

articles.headline: Cold stress curbs malaria parasite growth by blocking red-cell invasion
articles.title_original: Cold stress restricts malaria parasite growth by affecting erythrocyte invasion.
articles.authors: Odagawa T, Saiki E, Kanuka H
articles.details: This laboratory study examined how cold stress affects the intraerythrocytic development of P. falciparum. In vitro culture at 27-33°C suppressed parasite growth and differentiation in a temperature-dependent manner, and exposing mice to 4°C reduced parasitemia in a rodent malaria model. Cold stress enhanced growth suppression when combined with low-dose artesunate both in vitro and in vivo. Cold-exposed parasites showed decreased erythrocyte invasion efficiency with broad downregulation of invasion-related genes, including reduced ama1 expression at schizont and ring stages, identifying temperature as a key environmental regulator of parasite development.

articles.headline: Stage-specific RNA editing mapped across P. falciparum blood stages
articles.title_original: Genome-wide detection of conserved stage-specific RNA editing events for nuclear genes in the Plasmodium falciparum 3D7 malaria parasite.
articles.authors: Azad MTA, Sugi T, Qulsum U, Kato K
articles.details: This genome-wide computational study detected developmental stage-specific RNA editing in tightly synchronized Plasmodium falciparum 3D7 at 16, 24, 32, and 40 h using REDItools1-De novo and REDItools2. REDItools1 revealed extensive A-to-G, G-to-A, and T-to-C variations, while G-to-A and C-to-T events occurred at much higher levels. Editing appeared at the 16 h ring stage, peaked at the 32 h trophozoite stage, and declined at the 40 h schizont stage. Adenosine deaminase expression did not correlate with editing over time, whereas cytidine deaminase was higher at 16 h and decreased with maturation; RNA-binding and related pathways were downregulated at 40 h, suggesting robust editing machinery.

articles.headline: Avian malaria parasite P. relictum shows cryptic species and introgression
articles.title_original: Phylogenomic analyses reveal introgression and cryptic speciation in the globally distributed, vector-transmitted pathogen Plasmodium relictum.
articles.authors: Weyer E, Theodosopoulos AN, Palinauskas V, Bensch S, Westerdahl H, Duc M, Ciloglu A, Huang X, Kim KS, Tsuda Y, Atkinson CT, Zehtindjiev P, Fleischer RC, VanTassel NM, Taylor SA, Hellgren O, Ellis VA
articles.details: Researchers used sequence capture data from 52 Plasmodium relictum infections from multiple continents to build a genomic dataset of this mosquito-vectored avian malaria pathogen. Maximum likelihood and Bayesian phylogenomic trees with multi-species coalescent models, tested by varying missing data, established species limits. Results suggest two cryptic species with evidence of genetic introgression between them. One cryptic species, GRW4, devastated Hawaii's naïve avifauna after introduction roughly 100 years ago; despite hypothesized release from host selective pressure, no positive selection was found in the Hawaiian GRW4 clade. The authors emphasize genomic analyses for resolving pathogen species limits and evolution.

articles.headline: Review of Indonesian medicinal plants with antimalarial activity
articles.title_original: Harnessing Indonesian natural products against neglected tropical diseases: Malaria.
articles.authors: Nugraha AS, Nawiyanto, Muhyidin A, Qonitah ZN, Anggraeni BA, Triatmoko B, Pratama ANW, Keller PA, Siregar JE, Khasanah U, Wangchuk P
articles.details: This ethnopharmacological review compiled Indonesian medicinal plants with antimalarial potential from four databases, yielding 126 relevant publications. Among 208 plants with reported antimalarial activity, 99 crude extracts and fractions from 61 species were evaluated against various Plasmodium falciparum strains, and 17 species underwent chromatographic isolation yielding 146 compounds. Several compounds showed nanomolar antiplasmodial activity, including samaradine X, norcaesalpinins E, methyl 15-acetoxylansiolate, and dehatrine, with IC50 values of 14, 90, 170, and 170 nM respectively (chloroquine IC50 = 290 nM). The authors conclude Indonesian flora are promising sources of new antimalarial agents warranting further investigation.

articles.headline: Modern housing linked to lower child malaria risk across Africa
articles.title_original: Housing and malaria risk: Evidence across ecological and climatic gradients in sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: AlAbbas M
articles.details: This pooled analysis used DHS and MIS data from 17 countries (2006-2022; n=206,723) with logistic regression to predict positive malaria RDT, adjusting for child, household, ecological (biomes), and climatic factors. Modern housing was associated with lower odds of RDT positivity (β=-0.543, p<0.001), net of socioeconomic status, WASH conditions, net usage, and ecological-climatic controls. Predicted RDT positivity varied substantially by ecological context but remained lower in modern dwellings, with the housing advantage consistent across climatic gradients. The authors support framing housing quality as a durable, non-insecticidal correlate of child malaria vulnerability.

articles.headline: Reversible white matter lesion reported in pediatric cerebral malaria
articles.details: No abstract was provided. Based on the title only, this report describes a reversible white matter diffusion restriction lesion in pediatric cerebral malaria. No further details, imaging specifics, or outcomes are available.
articles.title_original: Reversible White Matter Diffusion Restriction Lesion in Pediatric Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Lai S, Taskin D, Schaefer Abu Hana L, Kim JA

articles.headline: Simplified mathematical model preserves malaria control dynamics
articles.title_original: Model reduction and analysis: A case study of a malaria control model.
articles.authors: Korsah MA, Johnston ST, Tiedje K, Day K, Walker CR, Flegg JA
articles.details: This modeling study applied a reduction technique to a comprehensive malaria control model to derive a simplified system retaining essential dynamics. The reduced model was validated against the full model using Bayesian MCMC, alongside a simulation study, parameter identifiability analysis, and sensitivity analysis. Results showed the reduced system exhibits similar transmission characteristics to the full model while offering greater flexibility and computational efficiency. The authors conclude the reduced model is a valuable tool for evaluating and implementing malaria control strategies.

articles.headline: Comment on Brazil malaria and Chagas disease surveillance analysis
articles.title_original: Comment on "Malaria and Chagas disease in non-endemic settings in Brazil: a comparative analysis of national surveillance data (2007-2024)".
articles.authors: V VKK, Kshirsagar P, Mishra M, Panwar S
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: P. vivax vaccine-candidate PvMSP-1 42 kDa polymorphic in Vietnamese isolates
articles.title_original: Genetic polymorphism and natural selection of merozoite surface protein 1 C-terminal 42 kDa in Vietnamese Plasmodium vivax isolates.
articles.authors: Nguyễn ĐTD, Nguyễn TH, Võ TC, Lê HG, Van Khanh C, Trinh NTM, Hanh NTL, Cho M, Goo YK, Quang HH, Na BK
articles.details: This study analyzed genetic polymorphism and natural selection of the carboxy-terminal 42 kDa fragment of Plasmodium vivax merozoite surface protein-1 (PvMSP-142), a vaccine candidate, in 88 P. vivax isolates from four malaria-endemic provinces in Vietnam. PvMSP-142 showed polymorphism arising from nucleotide substitutions and meiotic recombination, with balancing selection apparently preserving diversity; most polymorphic sites occurred in the PvMSP-133 subfragment while PvMSP-119 was relatively conserved. No geographical clustering was found, and PvMSP-119 was well-conserved across global populations. The authors conclude these findings inform development of a PvMSP-1-based vaccine.

articles.headline: Chloroquine-resistance markers declining in Gabon P. falciparum after drug withdrawal
articles.title_original: Status of pfcrt and pfmdr1 mutations in Plasmodium falciparum isolates in Gabon in 2019 after chloroquine withdrawal.
articles.authors: Dinzouna-Boutamba SD, Iroungou BA, Akombi FL, Yacka-Mouele L, Moon Z, Lee S, Na BK, Hong Y, Goo YK
articles.details: Using archived 2019 dried blood spots from Libreville, this study genotyped pfcrt and pfmdr1 drug-resistance markers in 70 successfully sequenced Plasmodium falciparum clinical isolates by Sanger sequencing, nearly two decades after chloroquine withdrawal. Resistance-associated SNPs were found in 25.71% of pfcrt and 20% of pfmdr1; common pfcrt mutations were N75(E/K) 24.3%, M74I 22.86%, and K76T 17.14%, with the wild-type CVMNK haplotype in 74.29% and mutant CVIET in 17.13%. For pfmdr1, Y184F (18.57%) predominated and wild-type NEYND was seen in 80%. The authors conclude resistant genotypes persist but are declining, suggesting progressive restoration of chloroquine susceptibility.

articles.headline: Review of critical care for HIV, TB, and malaria in low-resource settings
articles.title_original: The Global Intensive Care Unit: Human Immunodeficiency Virus, Tuberculosis, and Malaria.
articles.authors: Riunga F, Wambua GM, Lee BW
articles.details: This review addresses critical care for HIV, tuberculosis, and malaria, which remain leading causes of intensive care admissions and high mortality in low- and middle-income countries where access to critical care is limited. It summarizes the disease burden, clinical presentation, diagnostic strategies, and management principles for these infections in critically ill patients, emphasizing early recognition and timely therapy to improve outcomes. No quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: Anacardic acid curbs inflammation, may protect against severe malaria
articles.title_original: Saturated anacardic acid shows promise as adjuvant therapy for preventing severe malaria via attenuation of inflammatory response by modulation of the NF-κB pathway.
articles.authors: Gontijo ACGC, Corazza D, Albuquerque LFF, Ciarlini ACL, de Oliveira AS, Monteiro NC, de Oliveira MS, Couto SCP, Romeiro LAS, Borges TKDS, Muniz-Junqueira MI
articles.details: This study evaluated saturated anacardic acid (SAA), an NF-κB inhibitor, as a potential adjunct therapy against severe malaria using cerebral-malaria-susceptible CBA mice infected with Plasmodium berghei ANKA and in vitro THP-1 monocytes and BV-2 microglia co-incubated with P. falciparum or P. berghei ANKA. SAA increased survival in infected mice. In cells, co-incubation with Plasmodium and SAA decreased RelB but increased RelA in BV-2 microglia (RelA also rose in THP-1), and lowered TNF-α, IL-8, MCP-1, ROS, NO, and COX-2 while increasing IL-6. The authors conclude SAA modulates key components of severe-malaria immunopathogenesis and may be a promising adjunct therapy to prevent progression to severe malaria.

articles.headline: Corrigendum to 'Interactions between urbanization, malaria infection and avian cloacal microbiome' [Environ. Res. 297 (2026) 124073]
articles.title_original: Corrigendum to 'Interactions between urbanization, malaria infection and avian cloacal microbiome' [Environ. Res. 297 (2026) 124073].
articles.authors: Garrigós M, Jiménez-Peñuela J, Saavedra I, Veiga J, García-López MJ, Garrido M, Ruiz-López MJ, Figuerola J, Moreno-Indias I, Martínez-de la Puente J
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Immunoinformatic design of multi-stage subunit malaria vaccine
articles.title_original: Designing a next-generation multi-stage vaccine for malaria prevention.
articles.authors: Arya H, Tripathi H, Bhatt TK
articles.details: This in silico study used an immunoinformatic approach to design a multi-epitope subunit vaccine targeting five key Plasmodium proteins. Three constructs were built from adjuvants, cytotoxic and helper T-lymphocyte epitopes, B-cell epitopes, and linkers; all were predicted to be antigenic, non-allergenic, and non-toxic. Molecular docking with TLR-4 showed the most stable binding for construct 1, and molecular dynamics simulations indicated strong, stable interactions, while in silico cloning suggested efficient expression in E. coli. The authors note that in vitro and in vivo studies are still needed to validate efficacy.

articles.headline: P. vivax MSP-3α and β genetic diversity in Pakistani isolates
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity of plasmodium vivax merozoite surface protein 3α and β field isolates.
articles.authors: Shaheen N, Ashraf A, Malik N, Naeem M, Zeb I, Nabi G, Alwethaynani MS, Mohamed RAEH, Elkhadragy MF, Al-Hoshani N
articles.details: This molecular study analyzed genetic diversity of the Plasmodium vivax merozoite surface protein 3α and 3β (Pvmsp-3α/β) genes in field isolates from Azad Jammu and Kashmir, Pakistan, using nested PCR and RFLP with AluI and PstI enzymes. Of 207 blood samples from malaria-suspected patients, PCR detected P. vivax in 188/207 (90.82%) and P. falciparum in 19/207 (9.18%). Among P. vivax samples, 98/188 (52.13%) amplified for Pvmsp-3α and 90/188 (47.87%) for Pvmsp-3β, showing three and two major allelic variants. For Pvmsp-3α: type A 65/98 (66.33%), type B 11/98 (11.22%), type C 13/98 (13.27%); for Pvmsp-3β: type A 69/90 (76.67%), type B 21/90 (23.33%). The authors conclude this informs molecular epidemiology and control strategies.

articles.headline: Black cumin acha cookies tested in malaria-infected diabetic mice
articles.title_original: Functional-Diet Potential of Black Cumin-Enriched Acha-Based Cookies on Plasmodium berghei-Infected Diabetic Mice.
articles.authors: Nwanna EE, Olanrewaju BT, Oyeleye IS, Ojueromi OO, Oboh G
articles.details: This animal study assessed black cumin-enriched, acha-based cookies in a comorbid type 2 diabetes and malaria model. High-fat-diet mice (20–25 g, n=8 per group, eight groups) received a single intraperitoneal dose of streptozotocin (35 mg/kg) to induce diabetes, then were infected with the NK65 strain of Plasmodium berghei and fed the cookies for 14 days, with parasitemia suppression and blood glucose measured at 3-day intervals. Effects on pancreatic α-amylase, α-glucosidase, antioxidant enzymes (superoxide dismutase, catalase, glutathione peroxidase), reduced glutathione, and inflammatory markers (NF-κB, interleukin-10) were evaluated. The authors report the fortified cookie diet reversed damage compared with the negative control and suggest it is a promising nutraceutical therapy; no specific quantitative results were given in the abstract.

articles.headline: Multi-antigen transmission-blocking vaccine cuts mosquito oocyst load
articles.title_original: Multi-antigen transmission-blocking malaria vaccine elicits Th1-biased antibody responses and SMFA-confirmed oocyst reduction in Anopheles stephensi.
articles.authors: Pourhashem Z, Siasi S, Nourani L, Yousefi H, Sani JJ, Vand-Rajabpour H, Raz A, Pirahmadi S, Zargar M, Djadid ND, Zakeri S, Mehrizi AA
articles.details: This study developed a multi-component transmission-blocking vaccine combining the antigens APN-1, CelTOS, and cd-HAP with adjuvants CPG, MPL, and QS-21. Recombinant antigens were expressed in E. coli, formulated as single- or triple-antigen vaccines (with or without adjuvants), and used to immunize BALB/c mice, with functional activity tested by standard membrane feeding assay (SMFA) using Plasmodium falciparum NF54 gametocytes and Anopheles stephensi. The vaccine elicited robust high-avidity IgG against all three antigens without antigenic interference, induced Th1-type responses with increased IFN-γ and TNF, and the antibodies substantially reduced oocyst load. The authors conclude the multi-component approach supports further development of combination transmission-blocking strategies.

articles.headline: Malaria persists in older Ugandans, shaped by vegetation and water proximity
articles.details: This community-based cohort (SchistoTrack) analyzed 4308 participants aged 5–90 across 52 villages in three lakeside Ugandan districts (Mayuge, Buliisa, Pakwach), enrolled January 2022–February 2024, using a generalized additive mixed model with NDVI-derived vegetation exposure from Sentinel-2 imagery. RDT prevalence was 41.2% (1776/4308) and microscopy 32.3% (1363/4219), predominantly P. falciparum (83.1%), mostly low-density (71.6%). Prevalence peaked non-linearly at ages 10–11; among RDT-positives, fever fell from 30.8% (ages 5–10) to 11.2% (≥20). Dense vegetation (OR 3.25, 95% CI 1.33–7.96) and distance from health centers (OR 1.87, 1.34–2.59) raised infection odds. Authors support age-specific interventions targeting school-aged children with continued adult surveillance.
articles.title_original: Non-linear age dynamics of malaria infection and fine-scale environmental exposure in rural Uganda.
articles.authors: Lang MM, Tuhaise V, Kafuko P, Nakato A, Mohamed A, Nabatte B, Kabatereine NB, Donnelly CA, Chami GF

articles.headline: Uncomplicated Malaria in Infancy and Neurodevelopment at 1 and 6 Years: Evidence From the MiPPAD Birth Cohort Study of Benin
articles.details: Uncomplicated Malaria in Infancy and Neurodevelopment at 1 and 6 Years: Evidence From the MiPPAD Birth Cohort Study of Benin
articles.title_original: Uncomplicated Malaria in Infancy and Neurodevelopment at 1 and 6 Years: Evidence From the MiPPAD Birth Cohort Study of Benin.
articles.authors: Khongpetch J, Boivin MJ, Cot M, Milet J, Zoumenou R, Fermanian C, Massougbodji A, Bodeau-Livinec F

articles.headline: Facility readiness and determinants of malaria test-before-treat implementation in public health facilities in Kaduna and Kano States, Nigeria
articles.details: Facility readiness and determinants of malaria test-before-treat implementation in public health facilities in Kaduna and Kano States, Nigeria
articles.title_original: Facility readiness and determinants of malaria test-before-treat implementation in public health facilities in Kaduna and Kano States, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Igbokwe U, Babandi Z, Uahomo P, Manu YA, Musa B, Danga S, Muhammad H, Musa FM, Muhammad-Idris ZK, Akanmu I, Yusuff-Lawal S, Ehimen O, Yahaya M, Ichegbo N, Ojosipe O, Natsah J, Kaha R, Gugong T, Popoola S, Fadeyi O, Aigbogun E Jr

articles.headline: Molecular surveillance of antimalarial drug resistance markers in the pfcrt, pfmdr1 and pfmrp1 genes of Plasmodium falciparum isolates from southeastern Iran
articles.details: Molecular surveillance of antimalarial drug resistance markers in the pfcrt, pfmdr1 and pfmrp1 genes of Plasmodium falciparum isolates from southeastern Iran
articles.title_original: Molecular surveillance of antimalarial drug resistance markers in the pfcrt, pfmdr1 and pfmrp1 genes of Plasmodium falciparum isolates from southeastern Iran.
articles.authors: Jafari M, Rezapour R, Nemati F, Mehrizi AA, Pirahmadi S

articles.headline: Association of ABO Blood Group and Severe Plasmodium falciparum Malaria: An Updated Meta-Analysis and Trial Sequential Analysis
articles.details: A meta-analysis and trial sequential analysis of 23 case-control studies (3,553 severe and 6,112 uncomplicated malaria cases) found that non-O blood groups carry a higher risk of severe Plasmodium falciparum malaria than group O, with elevated odds for groups A (OR 1.98), B (OR 1.87), AB (OR 1.91), and non-O overall (OR 1.88). These associations held in African and South Asian populations, while Melanesians showed different patterns. Group A was linked to severe malarial anaemia (OR 1.75) whereas group B appeared protective (OR 0.75), and severe-malaria patients had lower haemoglobin. The authors conclude non-O blood groups are a susceptibility factor across ethnicities and ages but do not support population-wide screening solely for malaria risk prediction.
articles.title_original: Association of ABO Blood Group and Severe Plasmodium falciparum Malaria: An Updated Meta-Analysis and Trial Sequential Analysis.
articles.authors: Ranjan S, Dubey M, Sharma S, Behera SR, Meher S, Panda AK

articles.headline: Archetype-based stratification to tailor malaria interventions across Zambia
articles.details: To refine Zambia's health-facility-catchment-area (HFCA) malaria stratification, researchers fitted a zero-inflated negative binomial mixed-effects model to 2017–2024 data from the Zambia Health Information Management System and public datasets, then clustered HFCAs using the Clustering Large Applications algorithm. Significant variables included walking travel time to facilities, malaria shares among pregnant women and under-fives, under-five bednet coverage and use rate, elevation, enhanced vegetation index, precipitation, and housing quality. Four archetypes emerged, with elevation, bednet use rate, and housing quality as top contributors. The archetype-based approach showed greater spatiotemporal stability than incidence-based stratification, helping tailor and prioritize interventions toward elimination.
articles.title_original: Archetype-based stratification to inform sub-national tailoring of malaria interventions in Zambia.
articles.authors: Chiziba C, Chiwaula J, Hamainza B, Chitambala-Otiono S, Silal S

articles.headline: Acalculous cholecystitis with blackwater fever and kidney injury in children with malaria
articles.details: This case series describes four Ugandan children with malaria-associated acute acalculous cholecystitis (AAC), gallbladder inflammation without gallstones, who also presented blackwater fever and severe acute kidney injury. All were referred at advanced disease stages; AAC was managed conservatively, two children required dialysis, and all recovered both gallbladder and renal function. An accompanying literature review identified 28 previously reported malaria-associated AAC cases (only 7 pediatric); including this series, 32 cases (11 pediatric) are now reported. Authors suggest AAC is likely underrecognized in malaria-endemic regions and urge heightened clinical awareness and early assessment.
articles.title_original: Acute acalculous cholecystitis and blackwater fever with acute kidney injury in children with malaria: case series and literature review.
articles.authors: Cugliari M, Piacentini F, Montorfani SMMA, Fiddy O, Testa S

articles.headline: Marker-free fluorescent P. falciparum line for whole-life-cycle imaging
articles.details: Researchers used Plasmodium-optimized CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing to integrate an mCherry expression cassette into a selected intergenic locus of P. falciparum without disrupting genes, producing a marker-free line, NF54-mCh. The line showed intense fluorescence across all developmental stages—asexual and sexual blood stages, mosquito ookinete, oocyst and sporozoite stages, and liver stages—with normal proliferation, gametocytogenesis, and efficient transmission to mosquitoes. Ultra-high brightness in salivary-gland sporozoites enabled non-invasive identification of infected mosquitoes, and sporozoites remained highly infectious to humanized mouse livers, completing the full life cycle. Because the editing method introduces no drug-resistance markers, NF54-mCh serves as a parental line for further genetic modification.
articles.title_original: Versatile, marker-free platform for life cycle-wide imaging of Plasmodium falciparum by integrating an exogenous gene cassette into a conserved intergenic locus.
articles.authors: Sekine T, Shinzawa N, Kubota R, Kobayashi D, Okubo Y, Itokawa K, Isawa H, Amino H, Ishino T

articles.headline: Ancient genomes reveal novel P. falciparum haplotype in Medici remains
articles.details: Researchers (Ochoa et al.) identified a novel Plasmodium falciparum haplotype in ancient osteological remains of two notable members of the Medici family. The authors report that the findings expand current knowledge of historical P. falciparum genetic diversity and confirm its expansion in Europe.
articles.title_original: Unveiling malaria history using ancient genomes.
articles.authors: Kojom Foko LP

articles.headline: Proposed immune tolerance link: malaria exposure, waning antibodies, Burkitt lymphoma
articles.details: No abstract was provided. Based on the title only, this article proposes selective immune tolerance as a unifying mechanism linking heavy malaria exposure, waning vaccine antibodies, and Burkitt lymphoma. No study design, results, or quantitative findings are available.
articles.title_original: Selective Immune Tolerance: A Unifying Mechanism Linking Heavy Malaria Exposure, Waning Vaccine Antibodies, and Burkitt Lymphoma.
articles.authors: Bedsaul-Fryer JR, Mbulaiteye SM

articles.headline: P. vivax RhopH3 protein binds human red blood cells
articles.details: This study characterised the Plasmodium vivax RhopH3 protein, evaluating its genetic diversity, natural selection, erythrocyte-binding ability, and antigenicity. Three regions (R1, R2, R3) were identified from sequence conservation and selection signals, then cloned and expressed in two expression systems. Immunoprecipitation and rosette-formation assays showed R1 and R3 bind human erythrocytes, with R1 having the highest binding rates, while sera from naturally infected patients showed low reactivity against R1 and R3. The authors conclude PvRhopH3 is a conserved, erythrocyte-binding protein with low natural antigenicity that could be exploited for P. vivax control strategies.
articles.title_original: The Plasmodium vivax high molecular weight (HMW) rhoptry 3 protein (RhopH3) is involved in human red blood cell binding.
articles.authors: Gutiérrez-Ortegón D, Barreto-Santamaría A, Arévalo-Pinzón G, Patarroyo MA

articles.headline: Malaria exposure linked to faster waning of pertussis antibodies in Kenyan children
articles.details: Chronic early-life malaria exposure may be associated with accelerated waning of infection-induced pertussis antibodies, based on a longitudinal study of 92 children from two coastal Kenyan communities with contrasting malaria transmission—Junju (high, n=44) and Ngerenya (low, n=48)—followed for up to 14 years with pertussis toxin (PT)-specific IgG measured by an in-house protein microarray. Vaccinated children showed gradual post-vaccination antibody decay, stabilising at low levels by about 10 years of age. Among vaccinated children who later experienced natural infection, anti-PT titres rose sharply but then diverged by setting: levels remained elevated for several years in low-transmission Ngerenya, whereas in high-transmission Junju they returned to pre-boost levels within a few years.
articles.title_original: Longitudinal evidence of differential pertussis antibody durability following natural infection among vaccinated children living in contrasting malaria transmission settings in coastal Kenya.
articles.authors: Safari MS, Gicheru ET, Mburu MW, Makori TO, Nyawa O, Odoyo SO, Shee F, Ndungu F, Foley DJ, Nyagwange J, Chege T, Sande CJ

articles.headline: AI system diagnoses malaria from digitized blood smear images
articles.details: This study evaluated an automated cognitive-analysis system (the online "Aidos" intelligent system) for diagnosing Plasmodium malaria from digitized blood smear images, which were captured across 12 light spectra. The database comprised 191 blood smears from malaria-infected patients and 227 images from healthy patients, with spectral analysis then performed on 18 new patients (10 seconds duration). The average similarity value for Plasmodium malaria recognition reached 66.965%, and no false positive decisions occurred for digitized smears from healthy patients. The authors conclude the system offers instant diagnostic support usable by workers with limited microscopy or AI expertise.
articles.title_original: Intelligent automation cognitive system for accurate malaria diagnosis using digital blood smears.
articles.authors: Malaekah E, Saied H, Alfahad O, Utkina T, Saleh MA, Aljaffer AA

articles.headline: Twice-daily artesunate dosing improves treatment of experimental cerebral malaria in mice
articles.details: This study optimized artesunate treatment protocols in Plasmodium berghei ANKA (PbA)-infected mice with experimental cerebral malaria (ECM), testing four protocols started on day 5 post-infection using two daily doses or slow continuous delivery. Protocol G4 (20 mg/kg IP loading dose plus 3-day continuous osmotic-pump delivery) gave the greatest parasitemia reduction at 97.2% ± 0.86 over 24 h but caused recrudescence in 50% of treated animals, whereas protocol G1 (40 mg/kg loading dose then 20 mg/kg twice daily for 5 days) reduced parasitemia 90.5% ± 5.39 without recrudescence. A G1 variant (two 40 mg/kg doses on day 1) yielded a 42% survival rate with ~30% drops in hematocrit, hemoglobin, and red blood cell count; severe thrombocytopenia (196 ± 118.3 vs 1,291 ± 256.4 platelets/μL in uninfected controls) was largely unaffected by treatment. The authors conclude two daily doses or slow continuous delivery improve artesunate efficacy over single-daily dosing and are suitable for ECM adjunctive-therapy studies.
articles.title_original: Improved protocols using sodium artesunate for the treatment of experimental cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Merlone MP, Gomes F, Sanches GS, Daniel-Ribeiro CT, Carvalho LJdM

articles.headline: Gametocyte density predicts mosquito infectivity in controlled human malaria infection
articles.details: Researchers analysed longitudinal parasitemia, gametocytemia, and transmission data from 91 participants across 9 controlled human malaria infection studies of P. falciparum (Brisbane, Australia, n=8; Nijmegen, Netherlands, n=1), with transmission to Anopheles stephensi assessed by direct and membrane feeding assays across 5 trials and 30 participants. Maximum gametocyte density correlated positively with asexual parasitemia at first treatment (ρ 0.44-0.89); median maximum densities at 19-21 days post-inoculation were 1087 gametocytes/mL (female) and 219 gametocytes/mL (male). Higher gametocyte densities, growth rates, and parasite densities occurred in those who infected mosquitoes. Infectivity ranged 53-54% (direct skin), 29-33% (direct membrane), 54-100% (membrane after serum replacement), and 89-100% (enriched membrane). Transmission potential was correctly predicted for 71-74% of direct and 50-78% of serum-replacement assays.
articles.title_original: Characterising the circulation of gametocytes and mosquito infectivity in induced blood stage malaria.
articles.authors: Marquart-Wilson L, Alkema M, Collins K, Lanke K, O'Rourke P, Llewellyn S, Webb L, Barber B, Bousema T, McCarthy J

articles.headline: Modifiable factors drive trimester gaps in pregnancy asymptomatic malaria, Ghana
articles.details: A facility-based cross-sectional study screened 462 pregnant women in Kintampo South District, Ghana for asymptomatic malaria in pregnancy (AMiP) using microscopy, applying Oaxaca-Blinder decomposition to partition trimester disparities. Overall AMiP prevalence was 13.4%, significantly higher in the second trimester (χ2=10.96, p<0.001), with Plasmodium falciparum accounting for most infections. Observed characteristics explained 73.7% of the trimester disparity, driven largely by suboptimal IPTp-SP uptake (71.3%), frequent evening outdoor activity (58.6%), and infrequent mosquito repellent use (50.6%); LLIN ownership reduced the unexplained disparity by 41.9%. Authors conclude disparities stem from modifiable socioeconomic, programmatic, and environmental factors rather than biological gestational differences.
articles.title_original: Drivers of trimester disparities in asymptomatic malaria during pregnancy in Kintampo South District: evidence from an Oaxaca-Blinder decomposition Analysis.
articles.authors: Bardoe D, Hayford D, Bio RB, Ahiaka G, Abubakar LA, Nyarko S

articles.headline: Plasmodium vivax surging in Mizoram, Northeast India, threatening malaria elimination
articles.details: Spatiotemporal analysis of national and district-level malaria surveillance data (Mamit district, 2021–2025) documenting an epidemiological shift from P. falciparum to P. vivax in Mizoram. P. vivax rose from 23% of cases in 2021 to 47% in 2024 and 42% in 2025; in Mamit district it reached 68% of cases in 2025, and 86% among children under 5 years. P. falciparum transmission coincided with the monsoon while P. vivax was perennial. The authors urge therapeutic efficacy studies given chloroquine-resistant P. vivax reported in neighboring Myanmar.
articles.title_original: Rising burden of Plasmodium vivax in Mizoram: an emerging threat to malaria elimination in Northeast India.
articles.authors: Vanlalhriata C, Telugu Prakash P, Balasubramani K, Pautu L, Zawmi L, Hlunpuii L, Pilankatta R, Senthil Kumar N, Bhowmick IP, Balabaskaran Nina P

articles.headline: Review: microbiological barriers to Plasmodium vivax control in India
articles.details: This review synthesizes current evidence on the epidemiology, biology, and control of Plasmodium vivax in India, which accounts for nearly half of the global P. vivax burden. It highlights how dormant liver-stage hypnozoites, low parasitemia, early gametocytogenesis, and immune evasion complicate diagnosis and treatment, while microscopy and rapid diagnostic tests poorly detect asymptomatic infections and molecular surveillance cannot map relapse without hypnozoite biomarkers. Standard radical cure with chloroquine plus 14-day primaquine is limited by poor adherence and restricted G6PD testing, while single-dose tafenoquine awaits wider implementation. Urban malaria, largely driven by P. vivax and Anopheles stephensi, is increasing.
articles.title_original: Plasmodium vivax malaria in India: microbiological barriers to diagnosis, treatment, and elimination.
articles.authors: Kumar C, Bharti PK, Gupta H

articles.headline: Bed net coverage and malaria prevalence among Ethiopian health center patients
articles.details: This facility-based cross-sectional study assessed 403 randomly selected suspected malaria patients at Bahir Dar Health Center, Amhara Regional State, Northwest Ethiopia (February–March 2021), using a structured questionnaire and blood-smear microscopy. 70.2% (283/403) owned at least one LLIN and 66.5% (268/403) used them regularly; overall malaria prevalence was 3.7% (15/403), predominantly P. falciparum (60.0%), then P. vivax (26.7%) and mixed infections (13.3%). Prevalence was significantly higher in rural vs urban residents (14.3% vs 2.9%; p=0.002) and in non-owners vs LLIN owners (8.0% vs 1.8%; p=0.002). Authors urge stronger universal coverage and rural-targeted interventions.
articles.title_original: Assessment of Long-Lasting Insecticide-Treated Bed Net Possession, Utilization, and Prevalence of Malaria Among Patients Visiting Bahir Dar Health Center.
articles.authors: Gudeta EA, Mengistu DM, Mekonnen SM

articles.headline: Community views on mass malaria vaccine and drug administration in Bangladesh
articles.details: This qualitative study explored community perspectives on the Mass Vaccine and Drug Administration (MVDA) trial in the Chittagong Hill Tracts, Bangladesh, using focus group discussions, in-depth interviews, and key informant interviews with 105 participants in Lama and Alikadam sub-districts of Bandarban between September and October 2024, analysed by inductive thematic analysis. Participants recognised malaria as a recurrent rainy-season illness but showed pluralistic health-seeking, often relying on traditional healers and informal drug sellers first. COVID-19 experiences shaped vaccine mistrust, and vaccine knowledge was limited, though participants voiced conditional acceptance if interventions were safe, effective, and transparently communicated. Mass drug administration was viewed more positively than mass vaccination. Authors conclude MVDA success depends on trust, legitimacy, and context-specific community engagement.
articles.title_original: Community perspectives on mass malaria vaccine and drug administration in the Chittagong Hill Tracts, Bangladesh: a qualitative study.
articles.authors: Rabby MF, Tripura R, Khalil I, Ahmed DI, Peto TJ, Cheah PY, Hossain MA, Rahman AKMF, Samad R, Rashid R, Sayeed AA, White NJ, Day NPJ, Dondorp AM, von Seidlein LP, Adhikari B, Faiz MA

articles.headline: Community acceptability of new malaria vector control tools in Papua New Guinea
articles.details: This exploratory qualitative study examined community and health-system acceptability, readiness, and perceptions of current and supplementary vector control tools for malaria in Papua New Guinea, under the NATNAT project assessing residual spraying, spatial emanators, and larval source management alongside insecticide-treated nets. Data came from in-depth interviews and focus group discussions in four villages on the north coast of Madang Province (Megiar, Bulal, Mirap, and Wasab), analysed thematically. Community members showed strong understanding of malaria risk, seasonal transmission, and breeding sites, and expressed prospective acceptability of residual spraying driven by leadership, personal values, and desire to close protection gaps. Insecticide safety concerns informed by historical DDT campaigns, affordability, and program integration were identified as critical. Authors stress sustained, context-specific community engagement for implementation.
articles.title_original: Understanding community and health system acceptability, readiness and perspectives on the introduction of new vector control approaches for malaria control in Papua New Guinea.
articles.authors: Kerry Z, Farquhar R, Manuv K, Auwun A, Johnson P, Daly P, Macdonald M, Bell S, Ome-Kaius M, Karl S, Laman M, Robinson LJ

articles.headline: Dual-antigen mRNA-lipid vaccine gives 100% sterile malaria protection in mice
articles.details: This preclinical study developed a lipid-polyplex (LPP)-formulated mRNA vaccine targeting both pre-erythrocytic and blood stages of P. falciparum, with constructs encoding circumsporozoite protein (PfCSP) and reticulocyte-binding protein homolog 5 (PfRH5), alone or as a dual-antigen tandem. The optimized RH5-T2A-CSP construct (Mal05) elicited high-titer, durable antibodies that inhibited sporozoite invasion and erythrocytic growth in vitro and induced Th1-biased CD4+ and CD8+ T-cell responses. In mice, Mal05 conferred 100% sterile protection against challenge with transgenic P. berghei expressing PfCSP. The authors conclude it is a promising multistage next-generation candidate.
articles.title_original: A dual-antigen mRNA-lipid polyplex vaccine elicits durable multistage immunity and sterile protection against malaria in mice.
articles.authors: Wang X, Wang A, Huang W, Tian P, Cao L, Xiao B, Han J, Huang Z, Jiang L

articles.headline: Geospatial machine learning maps malaria risk in flood-prone districts
articles.details: This geospatial modeling study built a malaria risk-zonation framework combining the Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP) with a Random Forest (RF) model, using eleven predictors including elevation, land surface temperature, rainfall, flood inundation, and land use/land cover. The RF model was trained on 250 union council-level malaria Test Positivity Rate records from 2014 to 2024 and achieved 93.3% accuracy, 0.95 precision, 0.93 recall, and a Kappa of 0.86. AHP classified 69.2% of the area as moderate risk and 13.8% as high risk, with flood proximity, LST, rainfall, and LULC as dominant predictors; validation against 2022 outbreak data showed strong spatial agreement. The authors conclude the dual-model framework is useful for climate-resilient malaria control.
articles.title_original: Geospatial and machine learning approaches for malaria risk mapping in flood-prone districts: Implications for public health decision-making.
articles.authors: Khan Y, Nisar UB, Mughal MR, Hussain A, Daud S, Ehsan SA

articles.headline: Point-of-care hemoglobin tests adequate for malaria radical cure decisions
articles.details: This clinical perspective discusses hemoglobin (Hb) test performance requirements for clinical decisions versus population surveys, using malaria case management and radical cure as the example, where G6PD deficiency testing (the STANDARD G6PD Test) informs treatment. Reviewing clinical evaluations comparing venous and capillary Hb measured by the HemoCue 201+ and the STANDARD G6PD Test against an automated hematology analyzer, overall agreement with the gold standard for identifying severe anemia varied from 98.4% to 99.5%. The authors argue that without Hb measurement, radical cure could be incorrectly prescribed or withheld in more people with severe anemia, and that POC tests should be judged by clinical risk/benefit in their intended setting.
articles.title_original: Clinical perspectives on hemoglobin measurement: The example of malaria radical cure.
articles.authors: Chu CS, Brindle E, Degaga TS, Duparc S, Gerth-Guyette E, Hsiang M, Lacerda M, Thriemer K, Noviyanti R, Pereira D, Price R, Robinson LJ, Vidal-Cardenas E, Yilma D, Domingo GJ, Zobrist S

articles.headline: Proteogenomic reanalysis of CDPK1 mutant reveals new P. falciparum genes
articles.details: Researchers applied a proteogenomic pipeline integrating proteomic and phosphoproteomic data with six-frame genome translation to reanalyze a publicly available P. falciparum CDPK1 (calcium-dependent protein kinase 1) mutant dataset (PRIDE: PXD005207). The analysis discovered 24 new protein-coding genes (17 exonic and 7 intronic peptides), with peptides such as NILLTFDK, THNNNPQPNPQQK, and EVTSNFGNIR mapping to previously unannotated regions showing orthologous evidence in other Plasmodium species. Phosphoproteomic reanalysis identified 37 novel peptides implying altered phosphorylation signaling upon CDPK1 knockdown, including conserved peptides linked to metacaspase and HSP70. The authors conclude proteogenomics can improve genome annotation and reveal candidate molecular targets.
articles.title_original: Proteogenomic Analysis of CDPK1 Mutant in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Choudhary N, Rai AB, Prasad TSK, Dagamajalu S

articles.headline: Reticulocyte redox niche shields P. falciparum from artemisinin
articles.details: Using highly purified human CD71+ reticulocytes and mature erythrocytes, this study assessed P. falciparum invasion, intraerythrocytic development, and drug susceptibility alongside integrated metabolomic and proteomic profiling. P. falciparum preferentially invaded reticulocytes and developed faster within them; multi-omics showed reticulocytes provide a metabolically enriched, antioxidant-modulated environment with increased nucleotide precursors, enhanced glutathione metabolism, and elevated ROS-detoxifying enzymes. Parasites in reticulocytes showed significantly reduced susceptibility to artemisinin, dihydroartemisinin, and other redox-active antimalarials while remaining sensitive to non-oxidative drugs, plus enhanced survival and recrudescence after artemisinin exposure. The authors conclude erythroid maturation state is a key host determinant of antimalarial efficacy, forming a redox-protected tolerance niche.
articles.title_original: Host Reticulocyte Redox Attenuation Creates a Protective Niche for Artemisinin Tolerance in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Davis C, Lakshmi VS, Meloth S, Naidu R, Chu TTT, Sreejith R, Vijayan K, Kumar M, Shanmugam D, Chandramohanadas R

articles.headline: Malaria knowledge high but prevention practices lacking among Ethiopian students
articles.details: Institution-based cross-sectional study of 366 summer bachelor's degree students at Debre Markos University, Ethiopia (July–September 2025), assessing malaria knowledge, attitudes, prevention practices, and self-reported prevalence via structured questionnaire with bivariate and multivariate logistic regression. 16.7% tested positive for malaria between April and June 2025; 75.4% showed satisfactory knowledge and 82.8% correctly identified the causative agent, but only 71.58% reported consistent ITN use, 39.07% sought facility treatment, and 15.57% completed a full antimalarial course. Rural residence, inconsistent ITN use, and failure to eliminate mosquito breeding sites were significantly associated with malaria prevalence (p<0.05).
articles.title_original: Assessment of Knowledge, Attitudes, Prevention Practices, and Self-Reported Malaria Prevalence Among Summer Degree Students at Debre Markos University, Ethiopia, 2025.
articles.authors: Habtamu A, Fenta L

articles.headline: New copper(II)-imidazole complex shows anti-malarial activity in vitro
articles.title_original: Synthesis, Characterization, and Anti-Plasmodium falciparum Activity of a New Copper(II) Complex Containing 2-(Tert-Butoxy)-6-(1H-imidazol-1-yl)pyridine.
articles.authors: Patiño-Cubides A, Velasco-Pareja MC, Macías MA, Yasnot-Acosta MF, Rodriguez A, Valderrama-Aguirre A, Hurtado JJ
articles.details: A laboratory chemistry study synthesized and characterized a new copper(II) coordination complex derived from an imidazole-based ligand, with its structure confirmed by single-crystal X-ray diffraction showing the copper atom in an octahedral environment. In vitro testing against Plasmodium falciparum (3D7 strain, chloroquine-sensitive) showed the complex had significantly higher antiparasitic activity than the free ligand, and it displayed low hemocytotoxicity (≤20% at concentrations above 500 µg/mL). The authors conclude that coordination to the metal center substantially enhances activity, providing a foundation for further mechanistic study.

articles.headline: Systematic review of antimicrobial peptides against malaria and tuberculosis
articles.title_original: Antimicrobial peptides in malaria and tuberculosis management: a systematic review of emerging evidence.
articles.authors: Walter NS, Gorki V, Medhi B, Avti PK
articles.details: This systematic review summarized the origin, sequence, efficacy, and structure-activity mechanisms of natural and synthetic antimicrobial peptides (AMPs) against Plasmodium spp. and Mycobacterium tuberculosis, searching the APD3 database (through June 2025) and PubMed (January 1965 to June 2025). It reports that anti-malarial AMPs are grouped as defensins and cecropins, and describes how hydrophobicity, charge, and amphipathicity influence activity. The authors highlight challenges including toxicity, and emerging approaches such as nanocarriers and targeted erythrocyte delivery for malaria, concluding AMPs are promising alternatives against these multidrug-resistant pathogens.

articles.headline: Retraction notice for seaweed-derived TiO2 nanoparticle larvicide study
articles.title_original: Retraction notice to 'Seaweed Sargassum wightii mediated preparation of TiO(2) nanoparticles, larvicidal activity against malaria and filariasis vectors, and its effect on non-target organisms' [Environ. Res. 225 (2023) 115569].
articles.authors: Mathivanan D, Kamaraj C, Suseem SR, Gandhi PR, Malafaia G
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Urban-rural drivers of Anopheles sinensis ecology in China
articles.details: A six-year longitudinal One Health surveillance study (2016–2021) in rural and urban eastern China monitored Anopheles sinensis biting rates and adult densities using human-baited double net traps and UV-light traps, analyzed with GLMM-DLNM, regression, and random forests. Across 49,970 specimens, temperature was the dominant predictor, with rural biting and population peaking at 27.45–30.13 °C after a 1-month lag, while precipitation showed rural threshold effects peaking at 65.57–99.66 mm. In rural settings, a male-biased sex ratio increased biting rates 12.2–14.0% (IRR 1.122–1.140), crop cultivation raised vector risk (IRR 1.034–1.043), and cattle or buffalo presence provided strong zooprophylaxis (IRR 0.667–0.780), while urbanization rate modestly increased vector density (IRR 1.053–1.077). A protective urban density paradox emerged, with higher human population density suppressing vectors (IRR 0.633–0.682), and models captured 37.8–90.8% of spatiotemporal variation.
articles.title_original: Divergent urban-rural drivers of malaria vector ecology: a 6-year One Health longitudinal study in China.
articles.authors: Xu E, Zhu G, Wang Y, Chong Z, Cheng T, Liang J, Liang W, Cao J, Müller O, Zhao J, Li R, Lu G

articles.headline: Barriers and enablers to seasonal malaria chemoprevention in South Sudan
articles.details: This qualitative study examined barriers and enablers to delivering and accessing Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention (SMC) during the 2024 round in Aweil South and Aweil West Counties, South Sudan, where coverage remained at 79%, below targets. Data came from 60 stakeholders via 42 key informant interviews and 18 in-depth interviews with caregivers of eligible children who received all or no doses and of ineligible children who received SMC, analyzed using deductive thematic analysis. Enablers included government support, community trust, efficient logistics, integrated activities, information access, and Boma health worker involvement, while challenges included healthcare worker and drug shortages, geographic and transportation difficulties, caregiver relocation, delayed referrals, and poor communication. The authors call for targeted interventions to strengthen workforce, communication, and logistics for sustainable SMC in conflict-affected settings.
articles.title_original: Implementing seasonal malaria chemoprevention in conflict-affected settings: experiences from Northern Bahr el Ghazal, South Sudan.
articles.authors: Ayuiel ARD, Okot F, Khan J, Mubiru D, Dau AD, Jacob A, W Apary E, Katongole SP, Norman A

articles.headline: RTS,S vaccine reduced under-5 clinical malaria in western Kenya
articles.details: This impact evaluation used predictive models of routine surveillance data from 23 implementing and 23 comparison subcounties in western Kenya to assess the RTS,S malaria vaccine under the Malaria Vaccine Implementation Programme. Monthly confirmed uncomplicated malaria cases (under 5 years and 5 years and older) from 132 implementing and 139 comparison facilities were extracted from the Kenya Health Management Information System; prevaccination data (January 2015–December 2019) predicted counterfactual counts for the evaluation period (January 2020–December 2022). The proportion of vaccine-eligible children reached an estimated 60% by December 2022. Under-5 malaria cases were 2.3% lower than predicted in non-implementing facilities and 13.8% lower in implementing facilities, a net reduction of 11.6%, rising from 5.6% in the first year to 8.2% after two years; the authors conclude high-quality routine data can monitor vaccine impact on clinical malaria.
articles.title_original: The Malaria Vaccine Implementation Programme reduced clinical malaria in Kenya, 2020 to 2022 : Impact evaluation with predictive time-series models of routine surveillance data.
articles.authors: Painter JA, Wallender E, Hill AN, Hamel M, Okine R, Furrer E, Jalang'o R, Keitany KK, Kariuki S, Nyang'au I, Seda B, Samuels AM, Westercamp N

articles.headline: Pfcrt genetic diversity and evolution in Indian and global P. falciparum
articles.details: This molecular study analyzed 979 Indian (exon 2) and 719 global full-length Pfcrt chloroquine-resistance gene sequences to assess genetic diversity, selection pressure, and population structure. Three major haplotypes were identified—CVIET, SVMNT, and CVMNK—with CVIET predominating, especially in the North-East. Greatest diversity occurred between 2010-2019 despite falling incidence, and Mizoram showed the highest Tajima's D (D=3.310, p<0.01). Globally, 65 haplotypes were detected with highest diversity in Cambodia, plus positive selection at 6 codons, indicating drug-driven functional adaptation and the need for continued surveillance.
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity and evolutionary perspectives of Pfcrt in Plasmodium falciparum: insights from Indian and global isolates.
articles.authors: Narang G, Hawadak J, Jakhan J, Singh V

articles.headline: Scoping review of traditional and AI methods for malaria diagnosis
articles.details: This review examines current malaria diagnostic methods and the translational potential of artificial intelligence, particularly deep learning for automatic parasite detection in blood smears. The authors note microscopy is labor-intensive and operator-dependent while rapid diagnostic tests lack sensitivity and give only qualitative results. They report that numerous AI systems achieve accuracy comparable to human experts while increasing throughput and reducing costs, with potential to expand testing access in endemic regions and reduce misdiagnosis where malaria is rarely encountered. Key remaining challenges include inconsistent dataset annotation standards and limited representation of diverse endemic regions.
articles.title_original: A scoping review of traditional and artificial intelligence methods in malaria diagnostics.
articles.authors: Xing F, Lazarev S, Woo J

articles.headline: Review of transfusion-transmitted malaria risk in deployed military settings
articles.details: This review examines the risk of transfusion-transmitted malaria from emergency blood donor panels (walking blood banks) in deployed military environments, where whole blood transfusion supports trauma haemorrhage care under constrained logistics. It argues that activating emergency donor systems in malaria-endemic settings poses unique infectious risks, and that conventional civilian donor deferral criteria may be impractical or overly restrictive. The authors call for context-specific risk-management approaches and evaluate mitigation strategies intended to preserve transfusion safety while maintaining operational capability. No quantitative results are reported.
articles.title_original: Understanding the problem of transfusion transmitted malaria in the deployed military environment.
articles.authors: Makin S, Shanks GD, McNamara JF

articles.headline: Review of recent advances and future directions in malaria vaccines
articles.details: This review summarizes progress in malaria vaccine development across multiple parasite life-cycle stages, including pre-erythrocytic, blood-stage, and transmission-blocking candidates in various trial phases. It highlights persistent challenges in identifying pre-erythrocytic antigens due to difficulties obtaining infectious sporozoites and reliable human hepatocyte models, while noting that mass spectrometry, bioinformatics, and structural biology may help. The authors describe ongoing implementation of the RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M vaccines through national Expanded Program on Immunization schedules in Africa and emphasize the need for next-generation vaccines with greater durability and breadth. No new quantitative results are reported.
articles.title_original: Advances in the development of malaria vaccines.
articles.authors: Sorgho H, Rouamba T, Natama HM, Tinto H

articles.headline: Meta-analysis: shared risk factors for malaria-schistosomiasis co-infection
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis searched five databases through February 19, 2025 and included 30 studies from 12 African countries to identify shared risk factors for malaria-Schistosoma co-infection. Pooling 23 studies, schistosomiasis was associated with 1.27 times higher odds of malaria (OR 1.27; 95% CI 1.17-1.39). Risk peaked in older children and adolescents (typically 8-17 years); males had higher co-infection odds in community-based studies (OR 2.08; 95% CI 1.64-2.63) but not school-based studies (OR 0.87; 95% CI 0.64-1.19), and direct water contact was strongly associated (OR 2.53; 95% CI 1.60-4.00). Heterogeneity was high (I²>80%). The authors conclude co-infection reflects overlapping environmental and behavioral exposures, especially water contact.
articles.title_original: Shared risk factors for malaria and schistosomiasis co-infection: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Lang MM, Lyne B, Donnelly CA, Chami GF

articles.headline: Cellulose nanobead lateral flow test boosts P. vivax antigen detection
articles.details: This study developed a lateral flow immunoassay using blue-colored cellulose nanobeads (CNBs) as colorimetric reporters to detect P. vivax lactate dehydrogenase (PvLDH). Six novel monoclonal antibodies with sub-nanomolar dissociation constants were generated, and an optimal pair (clone #6 for detection, clone #4 for capture) was selected. The assay achieved limits of detection of 5 ng/mL in buffer and 10 ng/mL in blood, a 50- to 100-fold sensitivity improvement over conventional gold nanoparticle-based commercial RDTs, with no cross-reactivity to P. falciparum LDH or human LDH. The authors conclude it is a promising next-generation point-of-care diagnostic for P. vivax.
articles.title_original: Highly sensitive detection of species-specific malaria antigens using a cellulose nanobead-based lateral flow immunoassay.
articles.authors: Kim YJ, Kim SY, Yu SE, Ha SO, Choi JW

articles.headline: Disrupting P. falciparum LRR5 reshapes development and primes gametocytes
articles.details: Using single-cell RNA sequencing of unsynchronized cultures, researchers profiled transcription across the intraerythrocytic cycle in wild-type NF54 P. falciparum and an isogenic LRR5 (PF3D7_1432400) piggyBac mutant. Prior work showed LRR5 disruption increases sensitivity to artemisinin and heat-shock and enhances gametocyte production. The mutant showed marked downregulation of protein folding, protein export, and host-cell remodeling pathways (Maurer's cleft components, PTEX machinery, HSP70-x), with upregulation of cytoplasmic heat-shock and translation genes. LRR5pB parasites showed premature gametocyte programs, including elevated early marker Pfs16 confirmed at protein level, and accelerated, mistimed transcriptional progression. The authors propose LRR5 links proteostasis, host-cell remodeling, and developmental timing.
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum leucine-rich repeat 5 disruption alters the transcription progression during asexual and sexual stage development.
articles.authors: Pires CV, Wang CCQ, Zhang M, Micchelli C, Prakashi P, Oberstabler J, Rayner JC, Otto TD, Adams JH

articles.headline: Walnut leaf extract: anthelmintic activity and in silico antiplasmodial docking
articles.details: This study tested 70% methanolic Juglans regia (walnut) leaf extract for anthelmintic activity against adult Eisenia fetida worms at 50, 100, and 200 mg/ml, with mebendazole (10 mg/ml) as reference and distilled water as control, and used Schrodinger Glide XP molecular docking of identified phytochemicals against P. berghei target proteins (Profilin, L-lactate dehydrogenase, Cytochrome c oxidase, Cytochrome b, and Pyridoxal 5'-phosphate synthase subunit). The extract showed dose-dependent activity, with 200 mg/ml causing paralysis and death faster than mebendazole, plus cuticular erosion and epidermal vacuolization on histology. Compounds including γ-sitosterol, stigmasterol, 3-O-methyl-D-glucose, and 4H-pyran-4-one derivatives showed docking scores from about -4.494 to -9.799 kcal/mol. Authors say in vitro and in vivo validation against Plasmodium is still needed.
articles.title_original: Biological activity of Juglans regia leaf extract and in silico evaluation of its phytochemicals against Plasmodium berghei target proteins.
articles.authors: Alharbi HM, Dkhil M, Santourlidis S, Albeshr M, Al-Shaebi EM, Abdel-Gaber R

articles.headline: Commentary on whether malaria's impact on immune memory is clinically concerning
articles.details: This article, presented by its title only with no abstract available, addresses whether malaria infection's impact on immune memory is of clinical concern. No study design, results, or conclusions can be summarized from the information provided.
articles.title_original: Is the impact of malaria infection on immune memory of a clinical concern?
articles.authors: Yui K, Macalinao ML, Hafalla J

articles.headline: Meta-analysis: insecticide-treated nets cut malaria incidence and deaths
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized and cluster-randomized trials assessed insecticide-treated nets (ITNs) for reducing malaria incidence and mortality, pooling 25 studies (19 on incidence, 6 on mortality). ITNs reduced malaria incidence by 29% in Africa (RR = 0.71; 95% CI 0.54-0.93; p = 0.0133) and 68% in Asia (RR = 0.32; 95% CI 0.17-0.62; p = 0.0007), with a pooled odds ratio of 0.60 in African studies (40% reduction). Malaria-related mortality in Asia showed an 18% reduction (RR = 0.82; 95% CI 0.77-0.89; p < 0.0001) from 5 studies. Meta-regression confirmed a significant protective effect (β = -0.84; p = 0.05). Authors conclude effectiveness varies by region.
articles.title_original: Impact of insecticide-treated nets on malaria morbidity and mortality: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials.
articles.authors: Otolorin GR, Castellanos ME, Adegboye OA, Ratnayake HE, McBryde ES

articles.headline: Host, not parasite, drives reduced Treg expansion in helminth-malaria coinfection
articles.details: Using a murine model coinfected with the nematode Heligmosomoides polygyrus (Hp) and the protozoan Plasmodium yoelii (Py), researchers studied how helminths adjust immunomodulation during coinfection. Coinfected mice showed reduced regulatory T cell (Treg) expansion in vivo, yet Hp excretory-secretory products (HES) from single-infected and coinfected worms induced similar Treg expansion in vitro, and the TGF-β mimic gene Hp-TGM-1 was upregulated in worms from coinfected hosts. Experimental evolution over eight generations revealed no consistent differences between parasite lines. Authors conclude the reduced Treg expansion is host-driven rather than parasite-driven.
articles.title_original: In vivo reduction of Treg expansion in rodent helminth-malaria coinfection.
articles.authors: Bourbon L, Dusuel A, Pernet N, Rialland M, Roche B, Faivre B, Sorci G

articles.headline: Host blood type and haemoglobin shape malaria progression in Ghana
articles.details: In a prospective cohort nested within a cross-sectional survey of 353 participants of all ages in Fanteakwa South District, Ghana, PCR detected more P. falciparum infections (52.1%) than mRDT (42.8%), indicating a substantial burden of submicroscopic asymptomatic malaria. Among those with asymptomatic infection followed weekly for 28 days, 131 participants (37.1%) converted to mRDT and microscopy positivity, with 43.5% converting in the first week. Early conversion was most frequent among children (54.5%, p<0.001), and individuals with blood groups A and AB progressed earlier, while all conversions occurred among HbAA individuals and none among HbAS carriers. The authors conclude that progression from asymptomatic to symptomatic infection is strongly shaped by age and host erythrocyte phenomic traits.
articles.title_original: From silent infection to symptomatic malaria: host phenomic determinants of Plasmodium falciparum outcomes in Fanteakwa South District, Ghana.
articles.authors: Aninagyei E, Abbew GA, Boatey CA, Menu RY, Nyarkotey ET, Acheampong DO

articles.headline: Parasite genotype diversity, not density, tied to symptomatic malaria in children
articles.details: A comparative study in Kindu genotyped P. falciparum from dried blood spots of children aged 6 months to 12 years using nested PCR for msp2 allelic families (FC27, 3D7, multiplicity of infection) and qPCR for copy number, comparing symptomatic and asymptomatic infections. Of 522 children, symptomatic ones were younger (mean 5.77 vs 7.25 years; p<0.001) and more often male (70%), with higher P. falciparum prevalence (69.8%). The FC27 family and polyclonal infections were strongly associated with symptomatic malaria (aOR=6.50 and 24.58; p<0.01), while copy number was not independently associated after adjustment (aOR=0.98; p=0.134). Authors conclude symptomatic malaria relates more to genotype diversity than density.
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity of the Plasmodium falciparum msp2 gene in children from the Eastern Democratic Republic of Congo: a comparative study of clinical and subclinical infections.
articles.authors: Nasibu MM, Mulakwa LM, Chirimwami SM, Amisi CB, Ahadi JB, Zakitoka RK, Bulabula ANH, Bulabula A

articles.headline: Climate variability linked to malaria morbidity in Tamale, Ghana
articles.details: A time series analysis modelled population-adjusted, laboratory-confirmed malaria morbidity (rates per 1,000) against temperature, rainfall, and relative humidity in the Tamale Metropolitan Area using monthly data from January 2014 to December 2020 (84 observations) and an Autoregressive Distributed Lag (ARDL) bounds testing approach. The test confirmed a long-run equilibrium relationship between each climatic variable and malaria morbidity. In the short run, higher relative humidity, temperature, and rainfall were significantly associated with higher morbidity (p<0.05 to p<0.001), whereas in the long run a 1 °C temperature increase was associated with a 0.37 case per 1,000 decrease (p=0.038). Authors support developing climate-informed early warning systems for northern Ghana.
articles.title_original: Modelling the impact of climate variability on malaria morbidity in the Tamale Metropolitan Area: a time series analysis.
articles.authors: Zakaria AG, Ziblim SD, Amadu Y

articles.headline: Author correction on HLA and infection outcomes in HbE/β-thalassemia cohort
articles.details: This is an author correction to a study reporting that HLA polymorphisms shape divergent outcomes of Toxoplasma and Plasmodium infection in an Eastern Indian HbE/β-thalassemia cohort. No abstract was provided, so further study details, methods, and results are not available here.
articles.title_original: Author Correction: HLA polymorphisms shape divergent outcomes of Toxoplasma and Plasmodium infection in Eastern Indian HbE/β-thalassemia cohort.
articles.authors: Bhattacharya S, Rahaman M, Suman S, Rout DK, Mondal S, Purwar S, Dhingra B, Shukla PC, Mukherjee G, Gupta M, Guha D, Bhattacharyya MK, Dolai TK, Chakravorty N, Mukherjee B

articles.headline: Engineered iPSC endothelial model reproduces malaria blood-vessel pathology
articles.details: This in vitro methods study generated an induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC) line with inducible, simultaneous expression of ETS transcription factors (ETV2, FLI1, ERG) to produce endothelial cells with improved endothelial identity and strong barrier function (ETS-iBMEC) for studying P. falciparum sequestration in the microvasculature. Parasite-infected red blood cells and neutrophils bound highly to ETS-iBMEC, and exposure to parasite products altered metabolic, splicing, endothelial barrier, and angiogenic gene expression. The study confirms a role for the angiopoietin-Tie2 axis in parasite-mediated barrier disruption and highlights VEGF-Notch signalling, presenting the platform as a new in vitro approach to study the pathogenesis of human vascular infections.
articles.title_original: ETS-guided iPSC-endothelial models recapitulate malaria pathogenesis.
articles.authors: Korbmacher F, Long RKM, Fleckenstein H, Poliński P, Crusius D, Piatti L, López-Gutiérrez B, Batzilla A, Trivedi V, Ebisuya M, Bernabeu M

articles.headline: Human Chk1 inhibitor CHIR-124 kills P. falciparum via dual targets
articles.details: This in vitro drug-repurposing study tested the human checkpoint kinase 1 (Chk1) inhibitor CHIR-124 against Plasmodium falciparum. The compound was active against both drug-sensitive and drug-resistant asexual blood-stage parasites, competitively bound several Plasmodium kinases, and showed moderate activity against liver and gametocyte stages. Conditional knockdown experiments confirmed that P. falciparum Aurora-related kinase 1 (PfArk1) contributes to its parasiticidal effect, and CHIR-124 also inhibited β-hematin (synthetic hemozoin) formation, causing a dose-dependent rise in free heme that correlated with growth inhibition. The authors conclude CHIR-124 acts via polypharmacology—dual inhibition of PfArk1 and hemozoin formation—offering activity against resistant strains with lower resistance risk.
articles.title_original: The Human Chk1 Inhibitor CHIR-124 Shows Multistage Activity against the Human Malaria Parasite Plasmodium falciparum via Polypharmacological Inhibition of PfArk1 and Hemozoin Formation.
