articles.authors: Wicht KJ, Woodland JG, Garnie LF, Langeveld H, Taylor D, Godoy LC, Pasaje CFA, Laureano de Souza M, Siqueira-Neto JL, Ghidelli-Disse S, Lafuente-Monasterio MJ, Gamo FJ, Coertzen D, Reader J, van der Watt M, Bridgford JL, Girling G, Coyle R, Scheurer C, Wittlin S, Alder A, Gilberger TW, Lee MCS, Voss TS, Winzeler EA, Fidock DA, Niles JC, Birkholtz LM, Coulson LB, Chibale K

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria prevalence 5% in Al-Zuhra District, Yemen
articles.title_original: Prevalence of asymptomatic malaria and its associated factors in the Al Zuhrah district, Al-Hodeidah Government, Yemen, 2022.
articles.authors: Hajjam MA, Al Amad MA, Nasher SS
articles.details: A cross-sectional community-based study used multistage sampling to enroll 422 asymptomatic participants (mean age 35.7 ± 11 years; 47% male) in Al-Zuhra District, Al-Hodeidah Governorate, Yemen, testing blood by Rapid Diagnostic Test. Overall asymptomatic malaria prevalence was 5%, with Plasmodium falciparum accounting for 95% of infections. Being educated was protective (APOR = 0.35; 95% CI 0.13–0.97; p = 0.043), while absence of window screens (APOR = 9.56; 95% CI 2.15–42.51; p = 0.003) and living near stagnant water (APOR = 4.93; 95% CI 1.50–16.23; p = 0.009) were significantly associated with infection. The authors emphasize strengthening household vector control.

articles.headline: Pf12 and Pf41 antigens conserved in asymptomatic carriers, Tanzania
articles.title_original: Conserved genetic and antigenic profiles of Pf12 and Pf41 in asymptomatic Plasmodium falciparum carriers in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Fitriana F, Lee WJ, Sim HY, Jun H, Syahada JH, Muh F, Lee SJ, Na S, Lee KJ, Nam JW, Han ET, Todd J, Manjurano A, Kidima W, Mazigo E, Han JH
articles.details: Researchers analyzed genetic variation and antigenicity of the blood-stage vaccine candidate 6-cysteine proteins Pf12 and Pf41 in 172 Plasmodium falciparum isolates from asymptomatic individuals in Tanzania, sequencing PCR-amplified DNA from dried blood spots and measuring IgG by protein microarray. Both genes showed low nucleotide diversity (pf12 π = 0.00042 ± 0.00007; pf41 π = 0.00061 ± 0.00004) and negative Tajima's D, indicating selective constraint and strong conservation. Pf12 and Pf41 elicited high IgG seropositivity (97.7% and 98.3%) with strongly correlated antibody levels (ρ = 0.721, p < 0.001); a predominant Pf41 S232R substitution occurred in 47.8%. The authors conclude these conserved antigens warrant further vaccine investigation.

articles.headline: Review: how Plasmodium invades, is neutralized, and evades immunity
articles.title_original: Host-Pathogen Interactions in Malaria: Invasion, Neutralization, and Evasion.
articles.authors: Dickey TH, Tolia NH
articles.details: Narrative review of receptor-ligand interactions between the Plasmodium parasite and the human host across the parasite life cycle, focusing on clinically relevant antigens. It covers parasite proteins that enable functions such as motility, cell traversal, and invasion; well-characterized antibody-antigen interactions mounted by the human immune response; and several mechanisms the parasite uses to evade immunity. The authors discuss how this evolutionary back-and-forth has inspired vaccines and therapeutics, including ongoing efforts toward next-generation versions. No quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: Meta-analysis: insecticide-treated net use lags ownership in Southeast Asia
articles.title_original: Insecticide-treated nets utilization and determinants in Southeast Asia toward malaria elimination: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Soe BK, Huertas-López A, Nugraheni YR, Adisakwattana P
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis (PRISMA) of insecticide-treated net (ITN) utilization in Southeast Asia; of 450 records screened, 28 studies published between 2003 and 2023 met inclusion criteria, with 20 in quantitative analyses comprising 14,556 households and 122,258 individuals. A consistent gap between ITN ownership/access and actual use was found, with substantial heterogeneity across settings. Geographic region was the strongest determinant of non-use, with higher odds in coastal than delta areas (pooled OR=4.73, 95% CI 1.59-14.06), followed by socioeconomic status (poorest vs richest pooled OR=1.70, 95% CI 1.01-2.86). Authors call for area-specific risk assessment.

articles.headline: Antibodies to RIPR synergize to block P. falciparum invasion
articles.title_original: Analysis of monoclonal antibodies against the malaria invasion complex protein RIPR reveals the structural basis for synergistic antibody protection.
articles.authors: Williams BG, Barrett JR, Scott JB, Rigby CA, Cagiada M, Quinkert D, McHugh K, Huhn A, Burnap SA, Gourjault C, Byrne F, Raghavan SSR, Rodrigues A, Bergamaschi L, Balzarotti B, Watson S, Miller N, King LDW, Donnellan FR, Gladstone CA, Paterson J, Scalabrino S, Silk SE, Salkeld J, Minassian AM, Skinner K, Struwe WB, Deane CM, Reece ST, Ward AB, Draper SJ
articles.details: The study characterized 83 human IgG monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) against Plasmodium falciparum RH5-interacting protein (RIPR), a component of the essential PTRAMP-CSS-RIPR-CyRPA-RH5 (PCRCR) complex and a leading target of blood-stage malaria vaccines, from RIPR-vaccinated Kymouse-platform mice. Single mAbs had minimal neutralizing activity, but pools targeting the RIPR-tail region showed high-level synergistic inhibition. Structural characterization and molecular dynamics showed that mAbs against EGF-like domains 6-8 (but not 9-10 or the C-terminal domain) constrained the RIPR-tail conformation and dissociated PTRAMP-CSS from RIPR, enabling additional antibodies to bind and potentiate inhibition. Adding these mAbs to IgG from humans immunized with the R78C candidate vaccine enhanced malaria growth inhibition, providing a framework for next-generation blood-stage vaccine design.

articles.headline: Cambodia's digital malaria surveillance app enables timely case reporting
articles.title_original: Digital Health Center App for Community and National Malaria Surveillance in Cambodia: Implementation Case Study.
articles.authors: Ngor P, Rekol H, Thyda E, Rith R, Kimleng S, Rattana Y, Vunsokserey O, Vanna H, Pumeoung C, Ringwald P, Maude RJ, White L, Sovannaroth S
articles.details: Implementation case study evaluating Cambodia's Android-based health center app within its elimination-oriented digital Malaria Information System, assessed via system metadata, national surveillance data, and user surveys covering 761 health centers across 21 provinces, with in-depth surveys at 9 centers in 3 provinces. Data completeness reached 99% (89/90 fields in 2024). Between January 1 and July 31, 2025, 69 malaria cases were reported (23 locally acquired, 7 domestically imported, 39 internationally imported), with 95.7% (66/69) notified and classified within 1 day and reactive case detection completed within 3 days for all 21 eligible cases. User satisfaction was 96.3% (733/761); authors conclude the system supported reduced incidence and strong operational responsiveness.

articles.headline: Ginsenoside Rg1 targets parasite glucose transporter to inhibit P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Selective targeting of Plasmodium falciparum hexose transporter by phytochemical Ginsenoside Rg1 disrupts glucose metabolism and blocks development of parasite.
articles.authors: Shafi S, Maurya P, Bansal R, Sharma J, Mishra SK, Najmi AK, Singh S
articles.details: Using molecular docking and structure-based drug design, researchers identified the natural compound Ginsenoside Rg1 (G-Rg1) as an inhibitor of the Plasmodium falciparum hexose transporter (PfHT), with specificity validated in a yeast complementation model. PfHT expression was elevated in artemisinin (ART)-resistant versus ART-sensitive parasites. In vitro, G-Rg1 suppressed growth of both ART-sensitive and resistant P. falciparum and potentiated dihydroartemisinin in combination and ring survival assays. In Plasmodium berghei ANKA-infected mice, G-Rg1 alone and combined with ART reduced parasite multiplication and increased mean survival time. The authors propose G-Rg1 as a candidate for drug development against drug-resistant malaria.

articles.headline: DHA-piperaquine malaria prevention does not alter HIV drug levels in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Effects of malaria intermittent preventive treatment with dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine on antiretroviral drug concentrations in African pregnant women living with HIV.
articles.authors: Stöger L, Mischlinger J, Nhampossa T, Mombo-Ngoma G, Esen M, Tchouatieu A-M, Brunet M, Mendes A, Dimessa Mbadinga LB, Gamberini S, Ndzebe Ndoumba W, Figueroa-Romero A, Lell B, Lagler H, Zoleko-Manego R, El Gaaloul M, Méndez S, Botella N, Sanz S, Piqueras M, Sevene E, Saúte F, Wicha SG, Soy D, Menéndez C, Ramharter M, González R
articles.details: This study, nested in a clinical trial (NCT03671109), measured antiretroviral serum concentrations in 105 pregnant women living with HIV in Mozambique and Gabon between January and November 2021, comparing monthly intermittent preventive treatment with dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DHA-PPQ) against placebo. Maternal and cord blood were analyzed by UHPLC-tandem mass spectrometry. At end of pregnancy, median dolutegravir was 835.1 ng/mL (maternal) and 1,204.2 ng/mL (cord); lamivudine was 108.3 ng/mL (maternal) and 186.5 ng/mL (cord). No significant differences in ARV concentrations were found between arms, suggesting DHA-PPQ does not affect antiretroviral levels.

articles.headline: Targeting mosquito iron storage protein blocks Plasmodium transmission
articles.title_original: Targeting Iron Homeostasis Curtails Mosquito Fertility and Plasmodium Transmission Potential.
articles.authors: Tupe C, Pasupareddy R, Bhatt S, Goyal B, Kumar A, Gupta A, Lalwani A, Das RN, Sunil S, Singh S, Pandey KC, Chakraborti S
articles.details: Using computational, phylogenetic, and functional approaches, researchers characterized ferritin, the major iron-storage protein, in mosquitoes for the first time, finding a chimeric architecture combining iron-binding motifs with heme-coordinating residues and exceptionally high heme-binding affinity exceeding bacterioferritin. Knockdown of the ferritin heavy-chain subunit in adult Anopheles stephensi females caused profound reproductive tissue defects. Exploiting host-parasite competition for iron, the team screened chelators and identified β-thujaplicin as a transmission-blocking agent that significantly suppressed Plasmodium oocyst development, positioning iron homeostasis as a dual-target axis for transmission-blocking interventions.

articles.headline: IL-33 gene variants linked to P. falciparum malaria susceptibility
articles.title_original: Association of interleukin-33 gene polymorphisms with susceptibility to Plasmodium falciparum infection.
articles.authors: Aljarba NH, Alghnnam F, Al-Qahtani AA, Al-Hazani TM, Al Marzan A, Bohol MFF, Alhamlan F, Shafeai MI, Rudiny FH, Motaen AM, Alkahtani S, Al-Qahtani AA
articles.details: This case-control study genotyped 13 IL-33 SNPs in 250 individuals with active P. falciparum malaria and 200 healthy controls from the Jazan region of Southwestern Saudi Arabia. rs10118795 CT was associated with increased susceptibility (OR=1.584, 95% CI 1.068-2.350; p=0.022), while rs55726619 CT was protective (OR=0.042, 95% CI 0.002-0.725; p<0.001). In children aged 1-5 years, rs10815393 CC and rs55726619 CC were strongly enriched versus those ≥6 years (both 100.0%; adjusted ORs 650.46 and 172.69). Haplotype set 4 was associated with susceptibility (p=2.76×10⁻⁶). Authors conclude IL-33 variants may influence susceptibility, parasite burden, and age-dependent effects.

articles.headline: Rainfall predicts malaria cases at 8-week lag in Mozambique
articles.title_original: Exploratory analysis of the correlation between precipitation and storms and malaria cases from national surveillance in Mozambique.
articles.authors: Zhu H, Zhang L, Khan E, Srivastava J, Croal M, Candrinho B, da Silva M, Searle KM
articles.details: This study analyzed district-level monthly malaria case counts from Mozambique's National Malaria Control Program alongside NASA precipitation data, using Pearson correlations and Poisson generalized linear mixed models to estimate relative risk while accounting for spatio-temporal autocorrelation. The analysis found strong spatial variation in malaria cases, hospitalizations, and deaths. Nationally, increased malaria relative risk occurred at an 8-week temporal lag after high precipitation, but this varied by region: 6-8 weeks in the North, 10-12 weeks in the Central region, and 10-13 weeks in the South. The authors highlight important spatial heterogeneity in temporal lag times between precipitation events and malaria cases.

articles.headline: Degrading host p38-MAPK reduces Leishmania and malaria parasite load
articles.title_original: Chemical degradation of Human p38-MAPK reveals it as a potential host target to combat parasitic infections by Leishmania donovani and Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Singhal J, Rawat N, Bansal R, Gupta A, Srivastava P, Madan E, Jha N, Ghouse Peer GD, Pandey RP, Ranganathan A, Singh S
articles.details: Researchers used NR-7h, a PROTAC targeting human p38-MAPK, to assess the kinase's role in Leishmania donovani and Plasmodium falciparum infection of host cells. NR-7h degraded host p38-MAPK in a time- and dose-dependent manner and reduced parasite load dose-dependently. In Leishmania infection, degradation modulated cytokine profiles and oxidative burst correlating with parasite death, and combining NR-7h with Amphotericin B enhanced efficacy. For Plasmodium, growth inhibition and invasion assays showed impaired growth and merozoite invasion, implicating host p38-MAPK in invasion and intraerythrocytic development. Authors conclude NR-7h has potential as antiparasitic therapy targeting this host pathway.

articles.headline: Extraction-free CRISPR test detects malaria from whole blood in 40 minutes
articles.title_original: Extract-free one-pot ambient RPA-CRISPR detection of Plasmodium in whole blood.
articles.authors: Liao J, Su Y, Jiang F
articles.details: Researchers developed an extraction-free, one-pot recombinase polymerase amplification (RPA)-CRISPR assay with lateral flow readout to detect Plasmodium directly from crude whole-blood lysates at room temperature. It was evaluated on 116 malaria-positive specimens and 109 malaria-negative controls for P. falciparum and P. vivax. The detection limit was 100 copies/μL (~12-20 parasites/μL), with 93.1% sensitivity and 100% specificity versus reference qPCR; sensitivity was 97.8% for high-density, 95.1% for moderate-density, and 82.8% for low-density infections. The workflow finished within 40 min without specialized instrumentation and targets conserved 18S rRNA regions.

articles.headline: Novel lysyl-tRNA synthetase inhibitors show multistage activity against malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Structure-Guided Optimization of Novel Inhibitors of Plasmodium Lysyl-tRNA Synthetase with Multistage Activity against Malaria Parasites.
articles.authors: Forte B, Bellany F, Campbell PS, Chemi G, Dawson A, Anderson M, Aniweh Y, Burkhard AY, Aguiar ACC, Churchyard A, Cooper CA, Dos Santos Ferreira A, Famodimu MT, Fang FG, Hu X, Huijs T, Baud D, Jansen C, Jiménez Díaz MB, Bonnert R, Boyd S, Crespo-Fernández B, Mitasev B, Montagna S, Mok S, Murugesan D, Narwal SK, Norcross NR, Okombo J, Park H, Peet C, Pereira DB, Post JM, Reader J, Riley J, Robinson DA, Shinkyo R, Simeons FRC, Simpson L, Smith A, Smith D, Striepen J, Teles CBG, van der Laak R, Uhlemann AC, Vantaux A, Wilson C, Witkowski B, Wood G, Yeo T, Zuccotto F, Angulo-Barturen I, Baum J, Bolscher JM, Guido RVC, Birkholtz LM, Delves MJ, Dembele L, Fidock DA, Gamo FJ, Kyle DE, Maher SP, Popovici J, Walpole C, Gusovsky F, Willis PA, Read KD, Gilbert IH, Baragaña B
articles.details: Structure-guided lead optimization of a fused dihydropyrrolidino-pyrimidine hit from the Global Health Chemical Diversity Library, targeting Plasmodium lysyl-tRNA synthetase (KRS), produced analogues with potent parasite growth inhibition and >1000-fold biochemical and cellular selectivity. The optimized basic KRS inhibitor, compound 30, had an extended half-life enabling single-dose cure in the SCID mouse model, favorable safety, and activity across Plasmodium species and drug-resistant and sensitive P. falciparum strains and field isolates. However, compound 30 lacked oral bioavailability, which a prodrug approach could not fix; authors say the series informs future KRS drug programs.

articles.headline: High malaria prevalence in urban and suburban Bamako, Mali
articles.details: This cross-sectional study described malaria epidemiology among patients with febrile illness at urban (Dravéla, all ages) and suburban (Nafadji, children 0-59 months) community health centers in Bamako, Mali. P. falciparum prevalence was 68% in Dravéla and 61.16% in Nafadji. Severe malaria cases reported in Nafadji included cerebral malaria (15.15%) and severe malaria anemia (1.64%). Self-medication and use of impregnated mosquito nets were 45.5% and 69.5% in Dravéla, versus 32.8% and 39.8% in Nafadji. The authors conclude malaria is the main cause of fever and morbidity, with lower net use in the suburban area, and that control efforts should be maintained during the dry season.
articles.title_original: Malaria Epidemiological Characteristics in Urban and Suburban Areas of Bamako, Mali.
articles.authors: Kone AK, Maiga B, Dolo A, Camara B, Traoré A, Mounkoro JD, Kamissoko S, Kayentao K, Diallo M

articles.headline: Nearly half of suspected patients at Ethiopian hospital test malaria-positive
articles.title_original: Assessment of malaria prevalence and determinants among clinically suspected individuals attending Dejen Primary Hospital, East Gojjam Zone, northwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Emnetu A, Habtamu A, Lemlemu A, Yeshanew S
articles.details: A facility-based cross-sectional study from April to November 2024 enrolled 385 clinically suspected individuals at Dejen Primary Hospital, northwest Ethiopia, with blood examined by microscopy. Overall, 46.2% tested positive, and P. falciparum was predominant, accounting for 56.1% of cases. In multivariable analysis, lack of formal education (AOR 2.16; p=0.001), living near mosquito breeding sites (AOR 2.15; p=0.020), not using insecticide-treated nets (AOR 1.72; p=0.001), and not receiving indoor residual spraying (AOR 1.02; p=0.035) were associated with infection. Authors stress community education, environmental management, and consistent prevention use.

articles.headline: New digital PCR assay measures P. vivax drug-resistance gene copy numbers
articles.title_original: Droplet digital PCR assays for Pvmdr1 and Pvcrt-o gene copy number variation determination in Plasmodium vivax.
articles.authors: Srisutham S, Boonpeng K, Pitchayatanakorn P, Kijprasong K, Nosten FH, Day NPJ, Dondorp AM, Imwong M
articles.details: Researchers developed and validated a duplex digital PCR (ddPCR) assay to simultaneously quantify pvmdr1 and pvcrt-o copy number variation, using pvtubulin as a single-copy internal reference. Quantification limits were 22.4 ± 3.5 and 21.5 ± 2.6 copies/µL for the pvmdr1/pvtubulin and pvcrt-o/pvtubulin assays, respectively. In field samples from Tak (N=25) and Kanchanaburi Provinces (N=97), Thailand, 16% of Tak isolates in 2008 carried multiple pvmdr1 copies while all had a single pvcrt-o copy, whereas Kanchanaburi samples from 2023–2025 showed no multiple copies of either gene. The authors conclude the assay is a robust, reproducible tool for monitoring P. vivax drug-resistance markers in endemic regions.

articles.headline: Comparing genotyping methods to distinguish malaria recrudescence from new infection
articles.title_original: Comparison of techniques and markers to distinguish Plasmodium falciparum recrudescence from new infection in Rwanda.
articles.authors: Cantoreggi SL, Golumbeanu M, Zwyer M, Uwimana A, Niyonzima JD, Mbituyumuremyi A, Lucchi NW, Plucinski MM, Nsanzabana C
articles.details: Using samples from a Therapeutic Efficacy Study conducted in Rwanda in 2018, researchers compared two genotyping techniques and three decision algorithms for PCR correction of recurrent P. falciparum infections: capillary electrophoresis of length-polymorphic markers (msp1, msp2, glurp, microsatellites) and amplicon deep sequencing of SNP-rich markers (cpmp, ama1, cpp, csp). Among microsatellites, TA40 and PfPK2 best detected minority clones. The WHO algorithm identified the fewest recrudescences and the 2/3 algorithm the most, with the Bayesian algorithm intermediate. SNP-rich amplicon sequencing gave the most consistent results regardless of algorithm, showing strong potential for reliable PCR correction.

articles.headline: Floods raise malaria risk; ~12 million P. falciparum cases exposed in Africa
articles.title_original: Geospatial mapping of malaria risk in flood-prone zones of Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Eudaric J, de Ruiter MC, Sairam N, Camero A, Shahi KR, Smith MW, Zhu XX, Kreibich H
articles.details: This geospatial modeling study integrated high-resolution flood maps (2000-2018) from the Global Flood Database, malaria incidence from the Malaria Atlas Project, and population data across 492 flood-prone zones in 38 countries to estimate malaria relative risk and its drivers. In East and West Africa, malaria relative risk was elevated in flood-prone regions versus national baselines. The authors estimate ~12 million people diagnosed with P. falciparum were exposed to flooding, about one-third of the flood-affected population, and identify flood exposure as one of the main drivers of malaria burden in these zones.

articles.headline: New antimalarial MMV367 rapidly clears P. falciparum in volunteer infection study
articles.title_original: The pyrrolidinamide antimalarial drug MMV367 rapidly clears blood-stage Plasmodium falciparum in healthy adults with experimental malaria.
articles.authors: Barber BE, Denhardt-Eriksson R, Guirou EA, Flynn J, Sahai N, Leelasena I, Mathison S, Moller C, Lim I, Evangelista C, Webster R, Potter AJ, Llewellyn S, Amante FH, Gower J, Peters J, Khoury DS, Thathy V, Narwal SK, Yeo T, Fidock DA, Marx MW, Gamo FJ, Sharma R, Gibson RA, Sanz L, Cahn A, Bestgen B, Chalon S
articles.details: In a volunteer infection study, the pyrrolidinamide drug MMV367 (GSK3772701), which targets P. falciparum acyl-CoA synthetases 10 and 11, was tested against blood-stage P. falciparum in 12 healthy malaria-naïve adults given single oral doses of 3, 5, 10, 20, 90, or 1500 mg. Peak plasma concentrations occurred 2-4 hours postdose with an elimination half-life of 12.9-18 hours. For doses ≥20 mg, parasite clearance half-life was 2.2-4.3 hours. The model-derived minimum inhibitory concentration was 6.3 ng/ml (95% CI 5.2-7.8). No resistance emerged and no serious adverse events occurred, supporting further development.

articles.headline: Mesenchymal stem cells ease experimental cerebral malaria by rebalancing macrophages
articles.title_original: Macrophage markers and gene signature profiling reveals mesenchymal stem cells mediated immune modulation in Plasmodium berghei ANKA infection.
articles.authors: Gupta A, Sharan Thakur R, Ojha RK, Khan T, Kalkal M, Das J
articles.details: In a mouse model, C57BL/6 mice infected with P. berghei ANKA received intravenous mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) on the day of infection to test prophylactic immunomodulation in experimental cerebral malaria. MSC administration reduced parasitemia, improved survival, preserved blood-brain barrier integrity (Evans blue assay), and attenuated brain pathology. Flow cytometry of splenic F4/80+CD11b+ cells showed reduced M1 markers with maintained or enhanced M2 markers, while qPCR showed downregulated iNOS-2 and upregulated Arginase-1, EGR-2, GPR-18, FPR-1, and PTGES-2. The authors conclude MSCs reprogram macrophages toward a balanced M1/M2 response, mitigating disease severity.

articles.headline: Parasite protein IMC55 is dispensable for P. falciparum blood-stage growth
articles.title_original: The inner membrane complex protein, IMC55, is dispensable for intraerythrocytic development of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Vick GW, Fierro MA, Castiglione A, Park L, Brooks C, Muralidharan V
articles.details: Investigating how the egress regulator PfERC functions in red blood cell invasion, researchers used proteomics to identify PfERC-interacting proteins, including IMC55, which localizes to the inner membrane complex. Although whole-genome transposon mutagenesis screens predicted IMC55 to be essential for the asexual cycle, conditional mutants generated with two different systems showed no defect in replicative fitness during the asexual blood stage. The authors conclude IMC55 is either dispensable or functionally redundant for P. falciparum asexual blood stages, possibly with a role in mosquito-stage development.

articles.headline: New quinolone HLQ-102 targets P. falciparum cytochrome bc1 Qi site
articles.title_original: Revisiting 2-Substituted-4(1H)-Quinolones for Targeting the Plasmodium falciparum Cytochrome bc(1) Complex.
articles.authors: Pou S, Liebman KM, Winter RW, Nilsen A, Li Y, Lyche IN, Riscoe TM, Montgomery J, Gramelspacher MJ, Dodean RA, Zakharov LN, Nepal B, Kelly JX, Smilkstein MJ, Kortagere S, Vaidya AB, Alday PH, Doggett JS, Kudyba K, Roncal N, Ashraf K, Leed S, Lee PJ, Madejczyk MS, Roth A, Riscoe MK
articles.details: This medicinal chemistry study synthesized HLQ-102, the 2-position isomer of the preclinical antimalarial ELQ-300 (a 3-position diphenyl ether), using procedures that avoid expensive catalysts and retain the critical 3-position methyl group. HLQ analogs were profiled for antimalarial activity, with pharmacokinetics assessed for the lead molecule. Cross-resistance patterns indicate that, like ELQ-300, HLQ-102 targets the Qi site of the parasite cytochrome bc1 complex. The authors suggest the target protein has two separate troughs able to accommodate large substituents at either the 2- or 3-position of the quinolone ring.

articles.headline: Rural Ghana community sees mass drug administration as acceptable, beneficial
articles.title_original: Community perspective on feasibility of malaria mass drug administration: a qualitative evidence from the Eastern Region of Ghana.
articles.authors: Ndong IC, Chu CE, Ndang NH, Baah CA, Adu GA, Amponsah E, Peprah NY, Malm K, Ogundahunsi O, Ahorlu CS, Amambua-Ngwa A
articles.details: A cross-sectional qualitative study in the Pokrom subdistrict explored community and provider perceptions following pilot malaria mass drug administration (MDA), using 11 focus group discussions (9 community, 2 provider; 97 participants) and 8 in-depth interviews, analyzed via reflexive thematic analysis. Participants described notable reductions in malaria episodes and household treatment costs after MDA, with adverse events (dizziness, abdominal pain, weakness) perceived as mild and transient. Early challenges included inadequate sensitization and negative health-worker messaging. Willingness to join future MDA rounds remained high, with perceived effectiveness outweighing adverse-event burden.

articles.headline: Mathematical analysis of stock-and-flow models for insecticide-treated net distribution
articles.title_original: Dynamical and time-series approach to understanding compartmental stock and flow models: a case study in malaria intervention models.
articles.authors: Tan E, van den Berg M, Vargas C, Gething PW, Symons TL
articles.details: This methodological paper analyzes compartmental stock-and-flow (SNF) models used to describe insecticide-treated net (ITN) ownership, distribution, and attrition, using both time-series and dynamical-systems approaches. The authors show a reduced SNF model is equivalent to a linear time-invariant system, emphasize the role of attrition functions, and propose an iterative adapted expectation-maximization (EM) algorithm to handle calibration across disparate sampling frequencies and data sparsity. The EM method was applied to delivery, distribution, and household survey data across 44 countries spanning 24 years to estimate net distribution and volumes, with numerical convergence and uniqueness results provided.

articles.headline: pfmdr1 and Kelch13 resistance mutations common in northern Uganda
articles.title_original: Polymorphism of pfmdr1 Gene Mutation Conferring Resistance to Artemisinin-Based Combination Therapy in Plasmodium falciparum in Patients at Gulu Regional Referral Hospital in Northern Uganda.
articles.authors: Amito FP, Angwech H, Ojok L, Wokorach G, Ochaya S, Ogwang Q, Opiro R, Echodu R
articles.details: A cross-sectional study at Gulu Regional Referral Hospital in northern Uganda (April 2022–January 2024) enrolled 353 febrile patients (adults and children ≥6 months) with malaria confirmed by mRDT, microscopy, and PCR, then sequenced multidrug-resistance genes. P. falciparum prevalence was 60.6% (n=214). Among 214 isolates, the pfmdr1 SNP at codons 1034/1042 was most prevalent (29.6%, n=94), followed by codons 86/184 (28%, n=89) and codon 1246 (25.8%, n=82); the Kelch13 propeller gene (artemisinin resistance) was found in 16.7% (n=53). Codon 1246 differed significantly by location (p=0.017); parasite load differed significantly by mutation status. Authors urge continued monitoring of K13 mutations.

articles.headline: Culture, gender, and poverty shape malaria treatment-seeking in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: The social experience of malaria treatment-seeking: pathways, gender, and inequality in Jimma Zone, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Gerbi T, Teshome D, Sudhakar M
articles.details: An ethnographic study in Jimma City, southwest Ethiopia examined how cultural beliefs, gender roles, and economic barriers shape malaria treatment choices, using 32 in-depth interviews, 8 key informant interviews, two focus group discussions, participant observation, and document review. Malaria was understood through overlapping cultural and biomedical models (local terms buda, busa, sekera), with treatment following a pluralistic pathway from home remedies, herbs, and prayer to biomedical care. Women often recognized illness first but lacked decision-making authority and financial control, delaying care. Poverty, drug stockouts, transport, and weak infrastructure further constrained timely treatment.

articles.headline: Fatal lupus endocarditis with stroke and mixed Plasmodium infection
articles.title_original: Libman sacks endocarditis with multivalvular involvement, hemorrhagic stroke, and concurrent mixed plasmodium malaria in systemic lupus erythematosus: a case report.
articles.authors: Khan A, Tahir O, Batool A, Farman J, Javaid MH, Shehzad E, Soroush AR, Hassan F, Wazir HU
articles.details: A case report describes a 29-year-old female with a four-year history of systemic lupus erythematosus, non-adherent to hydroxychloroquine, prednisolone, and mycophenolate mofetil, presenting with fever, polyarthritis after pregnancy loss, and rapidly progressive encephalopathy (GCS 7/15) with right hemiplegia. Labs showed hemolytic anemia (Hb 6.0 g/dL), thrombocytopenia (platelet nadir 39×10³/µL), high anti-dsDNA (>250 IU/mL), low complement, and high antiphospholipid antibody titers. Peripheral blood smear confirmed mixed P. vivax and P. falciparum infection. Echocardiography showed multivalvular Libman-Sacks endocarditis; CT revealed a 7×5.2 cm left frontoparietal hemorrhagic infarct with 15 mm midline shift. Despite methylprednisolone pulses, artesunate, and supportive care, the patient could not be managed, and an extremely poor prognosis was conveyed to the family.

articles.headline: Malaria training boosts Nigerian drug vendors' knowledge but not guideline compliance
articles.title_original: Effect of malaria training on community healthcare providers' compliance with malaria treatment guidelines in Nigeria using propensity score matching.
articles.authors: Baruwa S, Akomolafe TO, Okafor EE, Ubuane O, Diallo R, Alagbile M, Ladokun J, Ekele J, Yusuf D
articles.details: A quasi-experimental evaluation with a comparison group (data February–December 2022) used propensity score matching to assess a malaria training module for Community Pharmacists and Patent and Proprietary Medicine Vendors under the IntegratE Project 2.0 across 11 Nigerian states. Training significantly improved knowledge of malaria diagnosis and treatment (β=0.052; 95% CI 0.006–0.097; p=0.03) and acceptability of the WHO test, treat, and refer guidelines (β=0.432; 95% CI 0.041–0.822; p=0.03), but had no effect on compliance with treatment guidelines or identification of quality drugs. Authors recommend scaling up training and further research on compliance.

articles.headline: Neurological signs predict ICU transfer in Gabonese children with severe malaria
articles.title_original: Clinical profiles and predictors of intensive care unit transfer among children hospitalized with severe Plasmodium falciparum malaria in a tertiary care hospital in Libreville, Gabon: a retrospective study.
articles.authors: Moutombi-Ditombi BC, Lembet Mikolo AM, Ndong Ngomo JM, M'bondoukwé NP, Mihindou CJ, Moussavou Mabika DA, Mawili-Mboumba DP, Ategbo SJ, Bouyou-Akotet MK
articles.details: A retrospective study at a university referral hospital (CHUMEFJE) in Libreville, Gabon reviewed children under 17 (January 2021–July 2022) with microscopically confirmed P. falciparum malaria, applying WHO 2014 severe malaria criteria. Of 3,009 hospitalised children, 480 (15.9%) had severe malaria; median age was 6 years, overall mortality was 0.7%, and 8.5% (n=41) required ICU transfer. Neurological manifestations predominated (64.2%), and in multivariable analysis impaired consciousness (aOR 14.86; 95% CI 5.58–42.40) and coma (aOR 53.3; 95% CI 10.9–178.1) were the strongest independent predictors, while severe anaemia and hyperparasitaemia were not. The risk of ICU transfer rose sharply beyond three concurrent severe malaria criteria (aOR 10.59).

articles.headline: Sysmex XN-31 analyzer shows excellent malaria detection in febrile children
articles.title_original: Field diagnostic performance of the Sysmex XN31 automated hematology analyser for malaria screening among febrile children in a sentinel site for malaria surveillance site in Libreville, Gabon.
articles.authors: Chesly MDB, Patrick MN, Bedrich PN, Adissa AA, Joanito O, Tatiana N, Patricia MD, Karine BAM
articles.details: A prospective diagnostic study among febrile children under 15 at a malaria surveillance sentinel site in Libreville, Gabon compared the Sysmex XN-31 haematology analyser with microscopy. Among 1,034 participants, malaria prevalence was 23.9% by microscopy and 24.3% by XN-31. Overall sensitivity was 98.4% (95% CI 96.9–99.9) and specificity 99.9% (95% CI 98.8–99.9), with lower sensitivity in children under five (96.9%) and at low parasitaemia (90.7%); sensitivity dropped significantly below 100 parasites/µL (p=0.030). ROC analysis of the MI-RBC count gave an AUC of 0.991, with an optimal threshold of 20 parasites/µL. Authors conclude it is a robust complementary surveillance tool.

articles.headline: IL-9 drives MDSC expansion and worsens experimental cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: IL-9 Orchestrates MDSC Expansion and Inflammatory Programming to Amplify Immunopathology During Experimental Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Ghosh P, Ghosh S, Khamaru P, Gangopadhyay A, Choudhury A, Hossain Daptary A, Halder S, Brahma S, Thakur P, Singha P, Karmakar D, Mukherjee S, Bhattacharyya A
articles.details: Using the P. berghei ANKA (PbA) rodent model of experimental cerebral malaria, researchers investigated how IL-9 regulates myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs) and Th-9 (CD4+ IL-9+) cell differentiation. IL-9 promoted MDSC expansion (particularly PMN-MDSC proliferation) and enhanced pro-inflammatory phenotypes in MDSCs, macrophages, and dendritic cells, increasing host morbidity. In vivo IL-9 neutralization reduced immunopathology and increased survival, while MDSC depletion decreased the Th-9 population, indicating a positive feedback loop; MDSC-secreted IL-1β was a pivotal driver of Th-9 differentiation. The authors suggest blocking IL-9 as a possible adjunct therapeutic approach.

articles.headline: Cryptic severe falciparum malaria in non-traveler in Tuscany, Italy
articles.title_original: Cryptic severe Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Maremma (Tuscany, Italy), 2024: a case report from a historically receptive area.
articles.authors: Cassol C, Rossetti B, Cosma C, Radi A, Gobbi F, Zacchini F, Stella G, Angeli G, Lanari A, Croci L, Chigiotti S, Carli T, Briganti G, Romani D, Ceriale E, Bonaccorsi G, Lorini C, Del Riccio M, Bonanni P, Nencioni C
articles.details: A case report describes a severe, non-imported (cryptic) Plasmodium falciparum malaria case diagnosed in August 2024 in the Grosseto area (Maremma coast, Tuscany, Italy) in an Italian patient with no travel to endemic countries and no alternative risk factors, who had been staying in a historically malarious, still-receptive area harboring Anopheles labranchiae. Marked thrombocytopenia prompted blood smear microscopy showing 10% parasitaemia, confirmed by LAMP and species identification. The patient was treated with intravenous artesunate with clinical improvement. Investigation found no index case, secondary cases, or transmission chain. The case underscores the need for clinical suspicion even without travel history.

articles.headline: Computational screening identifies new PfDHODH inhibitor candidates against malaria
articles.title_original: Virtual screening and molecular dynamics analysis of Plasmodium falciparum dihydroorotate dehydrogenase (DHODH) inhibitors targeting pyrimidine biosynthesis pathway.
articles.authors: Alzahrani AR, Alhuthali HM, Alzahrani S, Hawsawi NM, Alzahrani AYA, Rehman ZU, Almohammed KH, Khan A
articles.details: This in silico study used structure-based virtual screening, DFT analysis, redocking, 500 ns molecular dynamics simulations, and MM/GBSA calculations to find novel inhibitors of Plasmodium falciparum dihydroorotate dehydrogenase (PfDHODH), an enzyme in pyrimidine biosynthesis and a validated antimalarial target. From 1,500 initial hits, three compounds (17507474, 24348860, 17433008) were shortlisted on binding affinity. All complexes showed persistent binding stability; MM/GBSA identified compounds 17433008 (−86.46 kcal/mol) and 17507474 (−85.91 kcal/mol) as most thermodynamically favorable. The authors propose these two as promising leads for experimental validation.

articles.headline: Malaria antibody Fc-fucosylation depends on antigen surface exposure
articles.title_original: Fc-fucosylation of Plasmodium falciparum-specific IgG varies with antigen and immunization.
articles.authors: Partey FD, Dos Santos MI, Seidu Z, Tiono AB, Thomson-Luque R, Wuhrer M, Larsen MD, Vidarsson G, Kurtis J, Blackman MJ, Lanzer M, Theisen M, Ofori MF, Hviid L, Lopez-Perez M
articles.details: Researchers measured levels and Fc-fucosylation of IgG specific for several P. falciparum antigens in plasma from children naturally exposed to P. falciparum and from vaccinated adults. IgG against PfEMP1 (a known infected-erythrocyte surface antigen) and PfGARP (a putative infected-erythrocyte surface antigen) was markedly Fc-afucosylated, whereas IgG against merozoite, gametocyte/gamete, and sporozoite membrane antigens was fully Fc-fucosylated in most children; immunization with recombinant MSP1, Pfs230, and CSP also induced fully fucosylated IgG. Children with Fc-afucosylated PfGARP-specific IgG had reduced risk of carrying parasites, independent of antibody level. The authors conclude Fc-afucosylation requires antigen exposure on a host membrane.

articles.headline: WHO announces first malaria drug for infants and new rapid diagnostic tests
articles.title_original: WHO Announces First Malaria Drug for Infants and New Rapid Diagnostic Tests.
articles.authors: Anderer S
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Hemoglobin digestion genes conserved in lizard-infective Plasmodium species
articles.title_original: Hemoglobin Digestion Genes Are Conserved in Lizard-Infective Plasmodium Species With Different Host Cellular Niches.
articles.authors: Pangburn SJ, Borner J, Misra S, Zavalunova J, Perkins SL
articles.details: This study assembled parasite transcriptomes from infected Saban anole (Anolis) blood to examine hemoglobin digestion genes in three lizard-infective Plasmodium species: P. floridense (infects red blood cells, produces hemozoin), P. azurophilum (red blood cells, no hemozoin), and P. leucocytica (infects white blood cells, no hemozoin). All three transcribe the canonical hemoglobin-digestion pathway genes found in mammalian Plasmodium, the first evidence of conservation across the Plasmodium tree. However, there is evidence of shifts in selective pressure on some proteins in all three species, suggesting these genes may be less functionally important, though they are not yet pseudogenes.

articles.headline: Copper-driven cuproptosis worsens malarial acute liver injury in mice
articles.title_original: Copper overload drives hepatic stellate cell activation via cuproptosis to exacerbate acute liver injury in murine malaria.
articles.authors: Mo X, Chen Q, Zhong R, Lin Y, Liu Z, Zhang X, Hou X, Lin H, Zhang M, Liu W, He J, Chen Z, Ye M, Deng Z, Jin X, Liu Z, Song J, Yin H, Huang B
articles.details: Using a P. berghei ANKA-infected C57BL/6 mouse model, researchers tested whether malaria-induced hepatic copper overload triggers cuproptosis that activates hepatic stellate cells (HSCs) and worsens acute liver injury, modulating copper with the ionophore disulfiram (DSF) and chelator tetrathiomolybdate (TTM). Infection caused progressive hepatic copper accumulation, upregulated cuproptosis markers (FDX1, DLAT, DLST), enhanced HSC activation (α-SMA/Collagen I) and fibrosis. DSF amplified copper overload, cuproptosis markers, parasitemia, HSC activation, fibrosis, and shifted cytokines toward profibrosis (TGF-β/PDGF up; IFN-γ/HGF down) and inflammation (TNF-α/IFN-γ up, IL-10 down), while TTM reversed these effects, supporting copper-dependent cuproptosis as a driver of HSC activation.

articles.headline: Review: organoids and organ-on-chip models for malaria research
articles.title_original: Beyond the mouse: organoids, spheroids, and organs-on-chips as the (inevitable) future of malaria research?
articles.authors: Dormoi J, Amalvict R, Pradines B
articles.details: This narrative review examines experimental models for malaria research, contrasting murine models with emerging human-relevant microphysiological systems. It argues that mouse models remain central for studying immunity, pathophysiology, transmission, and drug efficacy, but fail to reproduce key features of human P. falciparum and P. vivax disease such as cytoadherence, placental structure, blood-brain barrier interactions, and relapse. The authors propose that spheroids, organoids (liver, brain, placenta, vasculature), and organ-on-chip systems complement rather than replace murine models, helping bridge in-vitro assays and human disease while supporting replacement, reduction, and refinement principles.

articles.headline: New TaqMan PCR assays detect three simian malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Accurate and sensitive TaqMan real-time PCR assays to detect and quantify simian malaria parasites Plasmodium cynomolgi, Plasmodium inui and Plasmodium coatneyi.
articles.authors: Ariffin MM, Syaripuddin K, Kadir KA, Lubim D, Yaman K, Divis PCS
articles.details: Researchers developed and optimized species-specific TaqMan real-time PCR assays targeting variable 18S rRNA gene regions to detect and quantify the simian parasites Plasmodium inui, P. cynomolgi, and P. coatneyi, which can infect humans in Southeast Asia. Assays were validated against plasmid DNA and genomic DNA from macaques and Anopheles mosquitoes. Each assay was highly specific, detecting as few as 10 template copies per reaction (about five parasites) with amplification efficiencies above 95% and linear standard curves over 10–10^6 copies. The authors conclude the assays suit routine zoonotic malaria surveillance, including submicroscopic and low-density mosquito samples.

articles.headline: AI-evaluated SPR biosensor design proposed for malaria diagnosis
articles.title_original: Artificial intelligence enabled performance evaluation of an enhanced SPR biosensor for malaria diagnosis.
articles.authors: Utshob MAAI, Juwel MB, Ishtiak KM, Rahman A, Rahman MMA, Imam SA, Huq SMI, Khosru QDM
articles.details: This modeling study proposes a surface plasmon resonance (SPR) biosensor with a multilayer N-FK51a+SiO2+Cu+HfO2+black phosphorus structure for malaria diagnosis, analyzed using transfer matrix, finite element, and finite-difference time-domain methods. The design achieved a maximum angular sensitivity of 541.42 deg/RIU, an FWHM of 1.98 deg, detection accuracy of 0.502 deg^-1, and a quality factor of 272.21 RIU^-1, reported as suitable for ring-stage detection. ANN, ANFIS, and RBFNN models were used to predict sensor responses and parasite stages. The authors report enhanced sensitivity and accuracy versus previously published SPR sensors.

articles.headline: China builds malaria microscopy workforce after elimination
articles.title_original: Development of human resources in malaria microscopy during and after the malaria elimination phase in China.
articles.authors: Li M, Wang Y, Zhou R, Liu H, Xia Z, Yan G, Deng Y, Deng Y, Wang Z, Wang S, Dai Y, Zhou X, Li S
articles.details: This report describes development of malaria microscopy human resources in China through WHO External (ECAMM) and National (NCAMM) Competency Assessment courses conducted from 2017–2024 and 2019–2024 respectively. Across six WHO-ECAMM courses, 64 microscopists participated (average age 41.0 years in 2024), with 29 certified at Level 1 and 17 at Level 2. Among 72 NCAMM participants, 10 reached Level A and 33 Level B. Age, workplace, education, and gender did not significantly affect competence (n=72; ρ=0.2, P>0.05), whereas years of experience showed moderate positive correlation (ρ=0.4, P<0.01), and species-identification scores improved significantly (Z=-7.3, P<0.01). The authors propose a three-tier national framework.

articles.headline: First report of Plasmodium bubalis Type I in Indian water buffaloes
articles.title_original: Clinical, hematobiochemical and phylogenetic studies of bubaline malaria in Indian water buffaloes (Bubalus bubalis).
articles.authors: Chavhan SG, Awandkar SP, Jadhav RK, Kulkarni MB, Kondre BM
articles.details: This study investigated bubaline malaria in domestic Indian water buffaloes, using PCR, sequencing, blood smears, and a pLDH-antigen lateral flow assay. Of 56 suspected cases, 5 (8.93%) were PCR-positive for Plasmodium bubalis, while smear examination detected only 3 (5.36%); the human pLDH rapid diagnostic test was negative for both P. bubalis and human Plasmodium species. Infected buffaloes showed fever, tachycardia, tachypnoea, and icteric or pale mucous membranes, with marked anaemia, thrombocytopenia, hyperbilirubinemia, and hypoalbuminemia. Phylogenetic analysis revealed, for the first time, Plasmodium bubalis Type I infection in this host.

articles.headline: Extracellular vesicles support P. falciparum proteostasis and heat shock survival
articles.title_original: Extracellular vesicles participate in proteostasis and heat shock adaptation in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Avalos-Padilla Y, Román-Álamo L, Bouzón-Arnáiz I, Arce EM, Muñoz-Torrero D, Fernàndez-Busquets X
articles.details: This laboratory study examined how extracellular vesicles (EVs) help Plasmodium falciparum survive the proteotoxic stress of febrile heat shock, focusing on the ESCRT protein PfVps60 using a PfVps60 knockout line. Proteomic profiling found 44.8% of EV-associated proteins from the 3D7 strain overlapped with aggregation-prone proteins. Loss of PfVps60 impaired EV-mediated export of chaperones PfHsp70-x and PfHsp110, altered aggregation near the parasitophorous vacuole, reduced PfHsp70-1 induction, and caused early loss of viability after heat shock. Supplementing knockout parasites with EVs from heat-stressed 3D7 partially rescued heat tolerance dose-dependently, suggesting EVs are a potential malaria intervention target.

articles.headline: Optimizing a novel spatial repellent's impact on malaria in Western Kenya
articles.title_original: Optimizing the impact of a novel spatial repellent on malaria incidence in Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Huwe T, Koepfli C, Ochomo EO, Gimnig JE, Achee NL, Grieco JP, Abong'o B, Moshi V, Perkins A, Moore SM
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: IgG to PfHRP2, not gene copy number, tracks malaria severity
articles.title_original: Differential IgG responses to PfHRP2 across the malaria severity spectrum.
articles.authors: Bhandari S, Krishna S, Verma AK, Ranjha R, Gupta H, Bharti PK
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: This study analyzed 240 samples to compare Pfhrp2 gene copy number variation and IgG responses against the PfHRP2 peptide across mild (MM), severe (SM), and cerebral malaria (CM) cases in Plasmodium falciparum infection, using real-time qPCR and ELISA. Parasitaemia was significantly higher in MM than SM (P=.0009) and increased progressively from CM to SNCM to MM (P<.05). Pfhrp2 copy numbers were similar across all groups, but IgG responses against PfHRP2 were notably higher in SM, CM, and SNCM than MM (P<.0001), correlating positively with age and var P5 peptide (P<.01). The authors conclude Pfhrp2 copy number is not a reliable severity biomarker, whereas the IgG response appears more critical.

articles.headline: Household wealth, not net use, predicts under-five malaria in Mozambique
articles.title_original: Risk factors for malaria among children under five living in net-owning households in Mozambique from the 2022-3 Demographic and Health Survey.
articles.authors: Mooney HA, Muhammad MA, Owusu DN, Adediji PO, Cudjoe NI, Brooks B
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: This cross-sectional study used multivariable logistic regression on 2022-3 Mozambique national survey data (n=1924 households with at least one child under 5) to test household mosquito net use as a predictor of household-level under-five malaria positivity (at least one RDT-positive child). After controlling for wealth, household net use showed no significant association with malaria. Wealth showed a dose-response: middle-resource households were 43% less likely (aOR=0.57; 95% CI 0.38–0.84; P<.0048) and highest-resource households 84% less likely (aOR=0.16; 95% CI 0.09–0.27; P<.0001) than the poorest. Seven regions had higher odds than Cidade de Maputo, notably Nampula (aOR=18.97) and Cabo Delgado (aOR=14.70). The authors recommend prioritizing lower-wealth households and higher-risk regions.

articles.headline: Microscopy misses low-density and mixed malaria infections versus qPCR in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Diagnostic gaps in light microscopy for malaria case management and control in Dilla Zuria Health Facility, southern Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Bogale A, Tefera S, Mitiku A, Solomon Z, Misganaw T
articles.details: This single-gate design study of 474 cases at Dilla Zuria Health Facility in Ethiopia compared onsite light microscopy against qPCR (detecting Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax) as the reference standard. Variation between microscopy and qPCR was 24.7% (95/384) for positive cases and 18.8% (17/90) for negative cases. By species, discrepancy was 21% (48/228) for P. falciparum, 28.3% (32/113) for P. vivax, and 34.8% (15/43) for mixed infections, with 18.8% of microscopy-negative cases qPCR-positive. The authors conclude low-density and mixed infections are often missed, and that competency-based training plus quality assurance is needed to improve diagnostic accuracy.

articles.headline: Corrigendum to transmission-blocking multi-antigen malaria vaccine study
articles.title_original: Corrigendum to "Multi-antigen transmission-blocking malaria vaccine elicits Th1-biased antibody responses and SMFA-confirmed oocyst reduction in Anopheles stephensi" [Vaccine 84 (2026) 128683].
articles.authors: Pourhashem Z, Siasi S, Nourani L, Yousefi H, Sani JJ, Vand-Rajabpour H, Raz A, Pirahmadi S, Zargar M, Djadid ND, Zakeri S, Mehrizi AA
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Mosquito midgut peritrophins Per-44 and Per-48 aid Plasmodium transmission
articles.title_original: Molecular characterization of midgut Peritrophin-44 and Peritrophin-48 and their role in Plasmodium development.
articles.authors: Dubey A, Chauhan VK, Chaitanya RK
articles.details: This laboratory study provides the first functional characterization of two peritrophic matrix proteins, Peritrophin-44 (Per-44) and Peritrophin-48 (Per-48), in the malaria vector Anopheles stephensi. Both contain a peritrophin A-domain with conserved six-cysteine motifs (Class III chitin-binding proteins), are predominantly expressed in the midgut across larvae, pupae, and adults, and are significantly upregulated during ookinete invasion after an infectious blood meal. RNA interference silencing of both genes increased mortality and reduced fecundity in infected mosquitoes; Per-48 knockdown caused the most severe phenotype, significantly reducing oocyst burden. The authors conclude both proteins are essential midgut components and viable targets for transmission-blocking strategies.

articles.headline: Review finds insufficient evidence on delayed tafenoquine for vivax cure
articles.title_original: Efficacy of Delayed Tafenoquine Administration for Radical Cure of Plasmodium vivax Malaria: A Systematic Review.
articles.authors: DeVita TN, Hwang J, Gutman JR, Ridpath AD, Williams S, Chang MA
articles.details: This systematic review assessed the efficacy of a single delayed dose of tafenoquine (≥300 mg) given three or more days after initiation of chloroquine/hydroxychloroquine for blood stage malaria in patients with confirmed infection, relevant when G6PD testing is delayed or unavailable. Searching five databases and one register through January 2026 yielded 394 unique articles, of which two randomized open-label trials (conducted 1996-1999) met inclusion criteria; both used single-dose tafenoquine (500 or 600 mg) on Day 4 or Day 6 after a 3-day chloroquine course. The authors conclude there is insufficient evidence to definitively recommend for or against delayed tafenoquine, though it likely outperforms chloroquine alone and could be preferable to the 14-day primaquine regimen when adherence is unlikely.

articles.headline: Simulated SPR biosensor detects glucose and falciparum HRP-II together
articles.title_original: Machine Learning Surface Plasmon Resonance Biosensor for Simultaneous Detection of Glucose and Plasmodium falciparum Biomarkers.
articles.authors: Wekalao J, Almawgani AHM, Alhawari ARH, Alammar MM, Abdullah M, Rajakannu A
articles.details: This theoretical/simulation study designed a multilayer surface plasmon resonance (SPR) biosensor for simultaneous, label-free detection of blood glucose and Plasmodium falciparum histidine-rich protein II (HRP-II) from one sample, addressing co-presentation of falciparum infection and hypoglycemia in low-resource settings. The sensor stack (Ag/LiB3O5/BiB3O6/Cu/FASnI3/black phosphorus on BK7 prism, 633 nm) gave a 1.82-fold sensitivity gain over a single-layer silver reference. A polynomial regression model trained on 18,000 simulated points achieved R²=0.997 (glucose) and R²=0.994 (HRP-II). Angular sensitivities were 221.3 deg/RIU (glucose) and 212.7 deg/RIU (HRP-II), with detection limits of 0.15 mg/dL and 0.8 ng/mL; estimated per-chip cost USD 1.50-4.00.

articles.headline: High-dose primaquine better prevents vivax relapse in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Analysis of the effect of primaquine dose on efficacy, safety, and tolerability in patients with Plasmodium vivax malaria in Ethiopia: a systematic review and individual patient data meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Degaga TS, Thriemer K, Rajasekhar M, Abreha T, Assefa A, Douglas NM, Gadisa E, Hailu A, Hwang J, Koh GCKW, Lacerda MVG, Llanos-Cuentas A, Mekonnen DA, Simpson JA, Tadesse FG, Taylor WRJ, von Seidlein L, Woyessa A, Yeshiwondim AK, Yilma D, Price RN, Bayissa GA, Commons RJ
articles.details: This systematic review and individual patient data meta-analysis pooled data from 1,378 patients across 5 eligible Ethiopian studies (of 297 identified) of uncomplicated P. vivax mono-infection to compare primaquine total dose regimens. Cumulative incidence of P. vivax recurrence by day 180 was 61.0% (95%CI 55.3-66.8) with no primaquine, 16.6% (12.0-22.7) with low total dose (3.5 mg/kg), and 8.9% (6.4-12.4) with high total dose (7 mg/kg); high versus low dose gave an adjusted hazard ratio of 0.40 (95%CI 0.24-0.68, p=0.001). Gastrointestinal disturbance on days 5-7 occurred in 19.1% (no primaquine), 16.0% (0.5 mg/kg daily), and 24.1% (1 mg/kg daily). The authors conclude both doses substantially reduced recurrence with high dose conferring greater protection, and clinically significant haemolysis was uncommon in patients with ≥30% G6PD activity.

articles.headline: Review: zoonotic malaria vector complexity and control in Southeast Asia
articles.title_original: Zoonotic malaria in Southeast Asia's changing landscapes: vector complexity, challenges, and future strategies.
articles.authors: Jeyaprakasam NK, Zulhaimi AH, Lau KW, Pramasivan S, Phang WK, Ponlawat A, Samung Y, Zhong D, Idris ZM, Sattabongkot J
articles.details: This narrative review synthesizes ecological, behavioural, and taxonomic challenges of zoonotic malaria vectors in Southeast Asia, where Plasmodium knowlesi and other zoonotic Plasmodium species are increasingly reported. It argues that gaps in entomological knowledge, morphologically similar vector species complexes prone to misidentification, and a narrow range of poorly suited vector control tools hinder progress. The authors note that deforestation, agricultural expansion, and habitat fragmentation may promote outdoor and forest-associated biting and shift risk toward human settlements, and call for integrated, context-specific, One Health-oriented strategies.

articles.headline: Scoping review: serological surveillance for malaria elimination in Southeast Asia
articles.title_original: Serological surveillance for malaria elimination in Southeast Asia: a scoping review.
articles.authors: Sangaran K, Hassan NW, Goh SJ, Izwan AAMF, Idris ZM
articles.details: This PRISMA-ScR scoping review searched PubMed, Web of Science, and Scopus for English-language primary studies on malaria serology in Southeast Asian countries published between January 2000 and May 2025. Of 6,849 records identified, 33 studies across seven countries met inclusion criteria: 26 cross-sectional, five longitudinal, one prospective cohort, and one control trial. Plasmodium falciparum and P. vivax markers predominated, with recent studies adding P. knowlesi and vector exposure antigens, and increasing use of multiplex assays, protein microarrays, seroconversion estimation, and machine learning. The authors conclude serological surveillance supplements diagnostic gaps in low-transmission settings.

articles.headline: Next-generation bednet durability over 24 months in Mozambique
articles.title_original: Durability of the insecticidal activity of next-generation insecticide treated nets distributed for malaria control in Mozambique: Findings from the New Nets Project (2020-2022).
articles.authors: Fagbohoun J, Abílio AP, N'Dombidje B, Todjinou D, Baes M, Pigeon O, Padonou GG, Fornadel C, Candrinho B, Koenker H, Robertson M, Wagman J, Ngufor C
articles.details: This study evaluated the 24-month insecticidal durability of next-generation ITNs distributed in Mozambique (New Nets Project, 2020-2022), collected from four districts at 6, 12, and 24 months and tested with WHO cone and tunnel bioassays plus experimental hut trials in Covè, Benin. All net types retained 100% bioefficacy against susceptible mosquitoes. Against resistant mosquitoes, Olyset Plus efficacy fell from 73% to 40%, Royal Guard varied (43% to 87%), and Interceptor G2 stayed high (≥92% meeting WHO criteria); hut mortality for Interceptor G2 declined from 55% to 39%. Only 8% of PBO and 32% of chlorfenapyr remained at 24 months. The authors support prioritising pyrethroid-chlorfenapyr nets where pyrethroid resistance exists.

articles.headline: Repurposed amide drugs inhibit P. falciparum Plasmepsin V enzyme
articles.title_original: Repurposing amide-based drugs unveils inhibition mechanisms of plasmodium falciparum Plasmepsin V enzymatic activity for antimalarial therapy.
articles.authors: Prajapati AK, Singh G, Lopus M, Choudhary S
articles.details: This biochemical study tested whether three approved amide-based drugs—favipiravir, 5-fluorouracil (5-FU), and saquinavir—inhibit Plasmodium falciparum Plasmepsin V (PfPlmV), an aspartic protease essential for parasite protein export. All three bound PfPlmV with affinities of ~10^4 M^-1 via hydrogen bonds and hydrophobic interactions (isothermal titration calorimetry). Saquinavir engaged the catalytic dyad Asp118 and Asp365 as a competitive inhibitor with IC50 0.35 ± 0.01 μM and Ki 0.14 ± 0.02 μM; 5-FU bound only near Asp118 (mixed-type inhibition, higher IC50/Ki); favipiravir was a weak, non-inhibitory peripheral binder. The findings may guide repurposing strategies for selective PfPlmV inhibitors.

articles.headline: Malaria incidence and mortality inequalities in young Somali children, 2010-2019
articles.title_original: Spatial and temporal inequalities in malaria incidence and mortality among children aged 0-4 years in Somalia: a subnational analysis, 2010-2019.
articles.authors: Hassan YSA, Omar MA, Ali AS, Adebayo UO, Okesanya OJ, Sani J, Ahmed MM
articles.details: This retrospective subnational analysis examined inequalities in malaria incidence and mortality among children aged 0-4 across Somalia's 18 regions (2010-2019), using modeled IHME estimates via the WHO Health Equity Assessment Toolkit and five inequality measures. Plasmodium falciparum incidence rose sharply between 2010 and 2015, peaking at 51.6% in Awdal and remaining elevated in central/southern regions (Bay 32.0%; Juba Dhexe 31.1%), while P. vivax stayed low and homogeneous. P. falciparum between-group variance peaked at 64.3 (2015), falling to 20.2 by 2019; mortality inequalities widened (difference 33.9 in 2010 to 118.5 in 2014; ratio 4.24 in 2019). The authors conclude children in central and southern Somalia were over four times more likely to die, reflecting structural healthcare inequalities.

articles.headline: Prenatal malaria linked to poorer child growth in Ghanaian cohort
articles.title_original: Prenatal and child malaria and child growth: evidence from a Ghanaian pregnancy cohort.
articles.authors: Sowole O, Kaali S, Li M, Mujtaba M, Wylie BJ, Gyaase S, Chillrud S, Agyei O, Jack D, Lee A, Asante KP
articles.details: Prospective Ghanaian pregnancy cohort (Ghana Randomized Air Pollution and Health Study) enrolling 1414 pregnant women in Kintampo, with 1144 mother-child dyads analyzed; prenatal malaria was defined by placental histopathology and early child malaria by active surveillance with rapid diagnostic testing over the first year. 250 (21.9%) had prenatal malaria and 525 (45.9%) had early child malaria. Using latent class growth analysis, prenatal malaria was associated with poorer length growth (adjusted OR 1.46, 95% CI 1.05-2.02), poorer length-for-age z-scores (aOR 1.53, 1.14-2.05), and smaller head circumference (aOR 1.43, 1.04-1.98); early child malaria was associated with poorer length-for-age z-score (aOR 1.12, 1.01-1.24). Authors conclude malaria beginning in pregnancy was associated with poorer growth outcomes.

articles.headline: P. vivax dominates malaria surveillance in Sorong City, Papua, Indonesia
articles.title_original: Epidemiological patterns and healthcare utilization among malaria patients in Sorong City, Papua, Indonesia: A cross-sectional analysis of national surveillance data.
articles.authors: Susilawati TN, Susanto H, Pham OKN, Kondororik F, Dewi VNL, Ahmar H, Putri NR, Lee R, Duong MC
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of national surveillance data on 3,953 laboratory-confirmed malaria cases in Sorong City, Papua. Most patients were ≥15 years (65.9%) and male (59.4%); community health centers reported 61.7% of cases and microscopy was the primary diagnostic tool (70.1%). Plasmodium vivax predominated (65.5%) and 99.9% of infections were uncomplicated, but nearly 21% of patients received non-standard antimalarial regimens. Predictors of hospital attendance included older age (aOR 1.02), non-Papuan ethnicity (aOR 1.77), and living in low-endemicity areas (aOR 4.57); children <15 were more likely to be hospitalized once diagnosed (aOR 1.9, 95% CI 1.18-3.06). Authors call for strengthened community services and adherence to treatment guidelines.

articles.headline: P. vivax infects Duffy-negative Cameroonians; RDTs miss most cases
articles.title_original: Epidemiological insights and genetic diversity of the Duffy binding protein of Plasmodium vivax in Duffy-negative Cameroonians.
articles.authors: Dieng CC, Ning RT, Kipayko C, Schroeder RE, Mdluli-Berndt N, Ayukenchengamba B, Nematchoua Weyou Z, Sona TD, Reward AE, Zhou G, Sumbele Ngole Ule I, Kimbi HK, Lo E
articles.details: Study screening 1,373 samples by microscopy, RDT, and qPCR across three ecological zones in Cameroon, with Duffy genotyping and PvDBP1 region II sequenced from 75 P. vivax isolates. P. vivax prevalence was 10.8% in hospital patients (86/793) and 5.5% in community participants (32/580), and all confirmed infections occurred in Duffy-negative individuals; hospital infections had higher parasitemia than asymptomatic cases. PvLDH-based RDTs failed to detect over 85% of qPCR-confirmed infections. PvDBP1 carried eight nonsynonymous mutations, most commonly I379L (74.1%) and E225K (61.3%), and isolates clustered phylogenetically with Botswana lineages. Authors highlight widespread subclinical infection, poor RDT performance, and the need to prioritize P. vivax and investigate alternative invasion pathways.

articles.headline: Tradition and cost sustain herbal malaria, typhoid remedies in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Family traditions, accessibility and cost shape the continued reliance on Cajanus cajan and Azadirachta indica for malaria and typhoid despite prescription medicines in urban and rural Nsukka, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Osayi EE, Urama DC, Amujiri AN, Agyo RA, Afyare AAA, Andong FA
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 400 respondents (200 urban, 200 rural) in Nsukka, Nigeria, using structured questionnaires analyzed with generalized linear models and multinomial logistic regression, examining indigenous use of Cajanus cajan (pigeon pea) and Azadirachta indica (neem) for malaria and typhoid. Rural residents relied more on herbal remedies due to accessibility, affordability, and family traditions, while urban respondents often alternated them with prescription drugs. Residence, occupation, and age were significant predictors of knowledge and use (P<0.05), whereas education was not, and perceived efficacy and high prescription costs further sustained use. Authors call for public health education and integration of evidence-based traditional practices into primary healthcare, noting the cross-sectional design limits causal inference.

articles.headline: CNBP drives IL-12β/IFN-γ immunity against blood-stage Plasmodium
articles.title_original: Cellular nucleic acid-binding protein (CNBP) dependent cytokine programming shapes host defense against Plasmodium infection.
articles.authors: Rashid R, de Souza Silva L, Banday S, Caffrey DR, Kurt-Jones EA, Fitzgerald KA, Golenbock DT
articles.details: Mechanistic study in mice and human cells investigating cellular nucleic acid-binding protein (CNBP), a zinc-finger transcriptional regulator, in immunity to Plasmodium (P. falciparum plus rodent P. chabaudi and P. berghei ANKA). On exposure to infected erythrocytes, CNBP rapidly translocated to the nucleus, bound the IL12β promoter, and was required for optimal IL12β induction; genetic ablation in mice and siRNA knockdown in human monocyte-derived dendritic cells markedly reduced IL12β and downstream IFN-γ while largely sparing TNF-α. Hematopoietic-specific CNBP deletion caused elevated peak parasitemia, impaired clearance, and relapse, yet paradoxically delayed mortality in a lethal infection model with reduced inflammatory remodeling. Authors position CNBP as a context-dependent modulator of the IL12β-IFN-γ axis and a potential therapeutic target.

articles.headline: Severe falciparum malaria first mistaken for gastrointestinal infection
articles.title_original: Severe Plasmodium falciparum Malaria Presenting with Gastrointestinal Symptoms in South Sudan: A Case Report.
articles.authors: Tran AD, Nguyen HT, Pham HK
articles.details: This case report describes a 29-year-old Ghanaian man working in South Sudan who presented with nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea and had taken no malaria chemoprophylaxis. Initially treated for acute gastrointestinal infection, after 24 hours he developed high fever, tachycardia (120 bpm), and hypotension (85-90/55 mmHg); a rapid diagnostic test was positive and smear showed P. falciparum parasitemia graded (++). Severe malaria was confirmed by elevated bilirubin (39.6 µmol/L), creatinine (120 µmol/L), prolonged prothrombin time (22.4s, INR 1.84), and thrombocytopenia (50 ×10^9/L). He was treated with intravenous artesunate and supportive care, became afebrile and stable after 3 days. The authors stress that gastrointestinal-onset can delay diagnosis.

articles.headline: New Aotus monkey model developed for P. vivax blood-stage vaccines
articles.title_original: Development of novel Aotus nancymaae non-human primate model for the evaluation of Plasmodium vivax blood stage vaccines and immunoprophylactics.
articles.authors: Ventocilla JA, Tapia LL, Villena FE, Cedamanos D, Buchta J, Pannebaker D, Valdivia HO, Redinger KR, Bosch J, Carias L, Londono-Renteria B, King C, Wilder BK
articles.details: Researchers characterized an Aotus nancymaae non-human primate model of Plasmodium vivax blood-stage infection to test vaccines and immunoprophylactics. Using direct blood-stage infection, they selected the Vietnam-IV strain and tested doses of 0.3, 1.0, and 2.5 ×10^6 infected red blood cells; the 2.5 ×10^6 dose gave the most consistent infection kinetics (coefficient of variation 15-23%). Acute infection raised lymphocytes and lowered neutrophils and platelets (to 23-68 ×10^9/L). In a pilot test of monoclonal antibody mAb 099100 against Duffy Binding Protein-II, 1 of 3 monkeys was partially protected versus 0 of 3 controls. The authors conclude the stringent challenge needs high-titer/high-potency mAb and provides a foundation for evaluating P. vivax interventions.

articles.headline: Plasmodium malariae often found in asymptomatic migrants in Barcelona
articles.title_original: Ten years of Plasmodium malariae infections in an International Health Reference Center in Barcelona: temporal trends and insights.
articles.authors: Pérez-Ugarte A, Sulleiro E, Rovira-Plujà J, Bayram M, Zarzuela F, Maturana CR, Serre-Delcor N, Aznar ML, Pou D, Goterris L, Salvador F, Mediavilla A, Bosch P, Martinez-Campreciós J, Treviño B, Martínez-Vallejo P, Silgado A, Molina I, Oliveira-Souto I
articles.details: This retrospective observational study at a Barcelona international health referral center (June 2015 to May 2025) examined Plasmodium malariae diagnosed by microscopy or qPCR. Among 6,293 individuals screened, 404 (6.4%; 95% CI 5.8-7.1) were positive for Plasmodium spp., of whom 55 (13.6%; 95% CI 10.6-17.3) had P. malariae. Most infections (52/55; 94.5%) were in asymptomatic migrants from sub-Saharan Africa with very low parasite densities (≤80 parasites/µL), and 13/55 had lived in Spain over 12 months before diagnosis, supporting long-term persistence; one symptomatic traveler relapsed after artemisinin-based combination therapy. The authors conclude asymptomatic chronic carriers form a silent reservoir that may hinder elimination.

articles.headline: Intravenous artesunate for artemisinin-resistant severe malaria in Uganda
articles.title_original: Intravenous Artesunate in Artemisinin-Resistant Severe Malaria in Uganda.
articles.authors: Maitland K, Okao M, Conrad MD, Etwop T, Akite M, Oguda E, Alaroker F, Aromut D, Muhindo R, Uyoga S, Olupot-Olupot P, Mogaka C, Connon R, Katairo T, Okitwi M, Briggs J, Gibb DM, Day NPJ, White NJ, Williams TN, Dondorp AM, Walker AS, George EC, Rosenthal PJ
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Structures reveal how antibody lineage binds malaria CSP protein
articles.title_original: Structural basis for conserved and distinct antigen recognition by a lineage of malaria-protective antibodies.
articles.authors: Jain M, Cannac F, Agrawal S, Lee WH, Loeffler JR, Fernández-Quintero ML, González-Páez GE, Moskovitz R, Ward AB, Wilson IA
articles.details: This structural study used X-ray crystallography and cryo-EM to characterize two monoclonal antibody Fabs (7160 and 7118) from the VH3-49/VK2D-29 germline lineage that target the NANP repeat region of the Plasmodium falciparum circumsporozoite protein (PfCSP). Both Fabs bound NANP6 repeats with high affinity (KD 6-10 nM). Fab 7160 formed germline-encoded head-to-head inter-Fab homotypic interactions resembling mAb 399, while Fab 7118 lacked such contacts and adopted a distinct binding mode. The authors conclude that CDR loop variations modulate antibody conformation and homotypic interactions, offering mechanistic insights with implications for germline-targeting malaria vaccine design.

articles.headline: P. falciparum ATAD3A is essential mitochondrial protein and drug target
articles.title_original: ATAD3 megadalton complex in Plasmodium falciparum is essential for mitochondrial and cellular viability.
articles.authors: Okoye IC, Lamb IM, Cheung YW, Morrisey JM, Sharma M, Kumar R, Dass S, Shukla A, Rell RS, Mather MW, Llinás M, Chang YW, Vaidya AB
articles.details: This laboratory study characterized PF3D7_0707400, a recently identified mitochondrial protein in Plasmodium falciparum, identifying it as an ATAD3A homolog. The protein was shown to be essential for parasite survival and to exist within a megadalton complex critical for multiple mitochondrial processes, including mitochondrial RNA stability, membrane potential, ultrastructure, and protein import. The authors report this as the first characterization of ATAD3A in a unicellular organism and conclude it expands knowledge of apicomplexan mitochondrial biology and represents a potential new antimalarial drug target.

articles.headline: Burkina Faso malaria program cut cases but may rebound by 2030
articles.title_original: Evolution of the Performance of the Malaria Control Program in Burkina Faso: Analysis from 2020 to 2024.
articles.authors: Ouedraogo WD, Kabore A, Kompaore S, Somda S, Sie A, Sawadogo H, Meda ZC, Drabo M, Meda N, Hien H
articles.details: This ecological time-series study used secondary National Malaria Control Program data from Burkina Faso (2020-2024) to assess intervention coverage and malaria incidence, with ETS forecasting to 2030. In 2024, LLIN use was 81.02% among pregnant women and 68.2% among children under 5, seasonal malaria chemoprevention coverage was 97.8%, and RDT and ACT adoption were 98.5% and 97.96%. Malaria incidence fell from 537.5 to 446.4 cases per 1,000 annually, but supply chain disruptions persisted, and forecasts suggest incidence could rise to 473.06 per 1,000 (CI 242.81-703.28) by 2030. The authors conclude sustained resources and governance are needed.

articles.headline: French ANOFEL/LABAC recommendations for standardized malaria diagnosis
articles.title_original: [Malaria diagnosis: French recommendations ANOFEL/LABAC].
articles.authors: Touafek F, Berry A, Giannoli JM, Ait-Ammar N, Coron N, Haim-Boukobza S, Larréché S, Pernot-Marino E, Robert-Gangneux F, Roques C, Thellier M, Houzé S
articles.details: This article presents the ANOFEL/LABAC recommendations aimed at standardizing biological diagnosis of malaria in France under the NF EN ISO 15189 standard. Any fever after return from an endemic area should prompt urgent Plasmodium testing using a sensitive technique such as rapid molecular biology or thick smear, with a thin smear to identify species, stages, and parasitemia if positive; rapid diagnostic tests are supplementary and should not be used alone. The recommendations emphasize biologists' roles in rapid result communication, identifying severe cases, mandatory reporting of indigenous malaria, and consulting reference laboratories for complex cases.

articles.headline: CYP2D6 variants affect primaquine but not tafenoquine in P. vivax relapse
articles.title_original: Differential impact of Tafenoquine and Primaquine on Plasmodium vivax recurrence: the role of CYP2D6 variants.
articles.authors: Godinho VKC, Mwangi VI, Morais MC, Lopes APB, Alves JRdS, Brito MAM, Lacerda MVGd, Trindade Maranhão Costa F, Almeida ACGd, Melo GCd
articles.details: This nested case-control cohort study in Manaus, Brazil evaluated whether CYP2D6 activity influences the efficacy of tafenoquine (TQ) and primaquine (PQ) in preventing Plasmodium vivax relapses. Patients were classified as cases (recurrence within 180 days) or controls and genotyped for CYP2D6 by real-time PCR. CYP2D6 polymorphisms did not affect TQ efficacy (P=0.172), whereas PQ-treated patients had higher recurrence risk over six months (P=0.016), and predicted intermediate or poor metabolizers were more likely to recur than normal metabolizers (P=0.036). The authors conclude CYP2D6 variability influences PQ treatment response but not TQ.

articles.headline: Review: Babesia, Leishmania, Trypanosoma and sickle cell disease
articles.details: This narrative review synthesizes current evidence on how sickle cell disease (SCD), a hereditary hemoglobinopathy marked by abnormal erythrocytes, chronic hemolysis, and vaso-occlusion, may alter host susceptibility to vector-borne parasites beyond Plasmodium—namely Babesia spp. (infect erythrocytes), Leishmania spp. (reside in macrophages), Trypanosoma cruzi (invades muscle cells), and Trypanosoma brucei (extracellular in blood and lymphatics). It examines how intracellular versus extracellular localization influences immune evasion, disease progression, and treatment response, focusing on pathophysiology, immune responses, clinical manifestations, and management. The authors highlight implications for targeted screening, therapy, and transfusion safety, and note critical gaps for future research.
articles.title_original: More than malaria: Exploring Babesia, Leishmania and Trypanosoma in sickle cell disease.
