articles.authors: Bhosale CR, Morcos ZL, Escudero A, Neupane R, Kahook Z, Miranda G, Deehan E, Gregg RK, Potter AK
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis

articles.headline: Artemisinin-resistance K13 R561H spreading eastward in Tanzania's Kagera region
articles.details: Molecular surveillance genotyped 2,866 Plasmodium falciparum isolates from seven districts of Tanzania's Kagera region (2021–2023) using 121 molecular inversion probes targeting resistance loci. The WHO-validated kelch13 artemisinin partial-resistance mutation R561H persisted in border districts Karagwe and Kyerwa (14–26%) and appeared for the first time in Muleba (10.0%, 2022) and Bukoba rural (0.7%, 2023), indicating eastward spread toward Lake Victoria; regional R561H prevalence rose from 5.5% (2021) to 6.9% (2023). Validated A675V and candidate V568G and P441L occurred at low frequencies, while DHFR/DHPS mutations approached fixation. Authors stress sustained high-resolution monitoring to preserve ACT efficacy.
articles.title_original: Temporal trends in artemisinin partial resistance and other antimalarial drug mutations in Plasmodium falciparum from Kagera region, Northwestern Tanzania, 2021-2023.
articles.authors: Simkin A, Mandai SS, Fola AA, Young NW, Marglous J, Pereus D, Bakari C, Madebe RA, Seth MD, Mrengela RB, Kisambale AJ, Chacha GA, Mandara CI, Francis F, Mbwambo D, Garimo I, Chacky F, Aaron S, Lusasi A, Molteni F, Njau RJA, Kajange S, Nhiga SL, Mohamed A, Juliano JJ, Ishengoma DS, Bailey JA

articles.headline: PCR finds two-thirds of pregnancy malaria missed by routine tests in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Submicroscopic malaria infections and inadequate IPTp-SP coverage among pregnant women in rural Burkina Faso: An observational study.
articles.authors: Tibiri YNG, Soulama I, Zongo D, Ouedraogo O, Sombie S, Millogo ON, Platon L, Nikiema S, Diarra WFS, Yanogo JN, Zoure OADZ, Ily RP, Kabore FCA, Sawadogo S, Saliou YEKI, Wade A, Chala B, Thiebaut L, Caspar E, Strubel PÉ, Muller A, Piola P, Zongo C, Menard D
articles.details: This observational study (June 2023-June 2024) at a health centre in Koubri, central Burkina Faso, combined a cross-sectional first-antenatal-visit assessment with retrospective record abstraction to evaluate submicroscopic malaria and IPTp-SP coverage in pregnant women. PCR detected Plasmodium in 30.1% (155/515); among 183 women tested by all three methods, PCR prevalence was 23.5% versus 8.2% by microscopy and 8.7% by RDT. Primigravidae had twice the infection risk (aPR 2.14, 95% CI 1.21-3.79), and each additional IPTp-SP dose lowered prevalence (aPR 0.89, 0.80-0.99); only 18.9% reached ≥5 doses. Among 225 deliveries, low birthweight was 14.7%, and PCR-confirmed infection was associated with increased LBW risk (aPR 1.52, 1.01-2.28).

articles.headline: Historical account of Francesco Torti and quinine for malaria
articles.title_original: Francesco Torti (1658-1741) and his description on the therapeutic use of quinine: the first effective method of treating malaria based on an extract of the bark of the Peruvian cinchona tree.
articles.authors: Artico M, Costi R, Gazzaniga V, Iorio S, Pastore FS, Cofone L
articles.details: This historical paper examines the work of Francesco Torti (1658-1741), who developed the use of cinchona bark—the source of quinine—to treat malaria during an era still influenced by humoral theory. It describes his 1712 book introducing the "fever tree" and detailing forms of intermittent fevers and their response to therapy. The authors note his meticulous clinical observation and argue his efforts spread cinchona across Europe and reflected a broader shift toward patient-centred, experience-based medicine. No quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: Review proposes studying VEGF signaling in placental malaria
articles.title_original: Placental malaria and VEGF modulation by P. falciparum: Bridging the immunopathological gap using evidence from human cerebral and severe, non-cerebral malaria studies.
articles.authors: Jain V, Shanmugam R, Singh PP, Singh VP
articles.details: This review bridges immunopathological evidence to argue that Plasmodium falciparum dysregulates angiogenic factors—VEGF, sVEGFR1, angiopoietins, sEndoglin, and PLGF—in placental malaria, where infected erythrocytes sequester in the intervillous space. The authors note evidence that late-stage parasites internalize host VEGF and express receptors (mainly activated VEGFR2) on parasitized red blood cells, findings drawn largely from immunohistochemistry/immunocytochemistry of fatal cerebral and severe non-cerebral malaria and in-vitro 3D7 cultures. They conclude that disrupted VEGF signaling may contribute to placental insufficiency, fetal growth restriction, and preeclampsia, and call for quantitative plasma and tissue studies in placental malaria across endemic areas.

articles.headline: Dynamic distribution models link vector suitability to dengue, malaria
articles.title_original: Predicting short-life-cycle species suitability via dynamic species distribution models: Applications in dengue and malaria epidemiology in Brazil.
articles.authors: Oliveira D, da Silva FF, Teixeira IBDF, Trindade-Vilanova DM, Rodrigues MS, Moura TA, Martinez PA
articles.details: Researchers built Dynamic Species Distribution Models (DSDMs) for Aedes aegypti and Anopheles darlingi using monthly occurrence and climatic data from 2016 to 2022 with the MaxEnt algorithm, then estimated Incidence Rate Ratios for dengue and malaria across Brazilian regions and states. Models performed well (Ae. aegypti AUC=0.8, TSS=0.6; An. darlingi AUC=0.9, TSS=0.8), with vapor pressure the most influential predictor for both vectors. Ae. aegypti suitability was widespread and seasonal (peaking spring to early summer) with dengue concentrated in the Southeast and South, while An. darlingi suitability centered on the Amazon Basin; suitability, case counts, and IRR were significantly associated (p<0.01).

articles.headline: Fatal cerebral malaria autopsy in untreated returning traveller
articles.title_original: Malignant malaria: autopsy findings and morphological features.
articles.authors: Colombo D, Del Nonno F, D'Abramo A, Corpolongo A, Ascoli Bartoli T, De Marco P, Garotto G, Bocci MG, Nicastri E
articles.details: This case report describes autopsy and morphological findings in fatal Plasmodium falciparum (malignant) malaria, in which infected red blood cells aggregate and adhere to small-vessel endothelium, causing microvascular sequestration, ischemia, and perivascular hemorrhages. The patient was a 54-year-old woman with untreated multiple myeloma who had travelled to Central Africa for business without antimalarial chemoprophylaxis; she presented with fever, headache, and asthenia and progressed rapidly to multiorgan failure, dying on day four of hospitalization. The authors emphasize the role of autopsy in determining cause of death and the need to consider P. falciparum in febrile travellers from endemic regions.

articles.headline: Under a third of pregnant women in SSA use both ITN, IPTp
articles.title_original: Use of insecticide-treated nets (ITN) and intermittent preventive treatment (IPTp) for malaria prevention and its associated factors among pregnant women in Sub-Sahara Africa.
articles.authors: Alemu EA, Tesfa MA, Nigatu SG, Birhan TY, Yitageasu G, Demoze L, Tadesse AA
articles.details: This study analyzed Malaria Indicator Survey/Demographic and Health Survey data from 18 sub-Saharan African countries (2018-2024), with a weighted sample of 63,561 women who had given birth in the preceding 3 years, using multilevel multinomial regression to assess use of insecticide-treated nets (ITNs) and intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp). Only 28.9% (95% CI 28.4-29.3%) used both an ITN (slept under one the previous night) and IPTp (≥3 doses), while 17.6% (95% CI 17.2-18.0%) used neither. Age, education, sex of household head, wealth index, mass media exposure, residence, and SSA sub-region were significant factors; the authors call for community- and individual-level strategies.

articles.headline: Malaria infected one-third of febrile patients at a Northwest Ethiopia health center
articles.title_original: Prevalence of malaria and associated factors among febrile patients visiting Gissa Health Center, Awi Zone, Northwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Amare B, Hailegebriel T
articles.details: This study examined 411 febrile patients (97% response rate) attending Gissa Health Center, Awi Zone, Northwest Ethiopia, using microscopy of thin and thick blood films. Overall, 140 (34.1%) were positive for malaria parasites, with Plasmodium falciparum at 20.9%, Plasmodium vivax at 9.7%, and mixed infections at 2.4%. Independent predictors of infection on multivariable logistic regression were age below 5 years (AOR 2.77, 95% CI 1.09-7.00), age 6-15 years (AOR 3.85, 95% CI 1.49-9.92), being illiterate (AOR 2.83), lack of regular ITN use (AOR 2.08), and absence of indoor residual spraying (AOR 1.54). The authors call for strengthened ITN use and regular IRS.

articles.headline: Scoping review of malaria elimination progress and barriers in Cambodia
articles.title_original: Advancing malaria elimination in Cambodia: a scoping review of progress, challenges, and strategic priorities.
articles.authors: Srun M, Langkulsen U
articles.details: This review searched PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science for open-access publications from 2014 to 2024 on malaria elimination interventions and programs in Cambodia, with 25 articles meeting inclusion criteria. The review found that malaria programs have achieved reductions in transmission and case fatalities, but persistent barriers include gaps in services and programs, inadequate infrastructure, low coverage, and poor access, especially in high-risk areas. The authors conclude that strategies to strengthen surveillance and case management and to address inequities in access to malaria care need to be prioritized.

articles.headline: pfhrp2 deletions not causing false-negative malaria RDTs in Kenya
articles.title_original: Absence of false negative RDT results due to pfhrp2 deletion among symptomatic malaria infections in Kenya: findings from the 2022 Kenya national longitudinal surveillance of pfhrp2/3 deletions.
articles.authors: Akala HM, Kandie R, Githuka GN, Kiplagat R, Ahmeddin O, Keitany K, Kiarie J, Wamari A, Machini B, Oyugi E, Mahugu S, Otieno JD, Egbo TE, Juma DW, Sang LP, Haynes RC, Osoti V, Vesely BA, Smith D, Travis J, Cunningham J, Cheng Q, Oyier I, Ogutu B
articles.details: This cross-sectional survey collected 5,394 samples from individuals with malaria symptoms at health facilities in ten Kenyan counties, each tested with both PfHRP2-based and PfLDH-based RDTs, with discordant (PfLDH+/PfHRP2-) samples and a random positive subset sequenced for HRP2/3 deletions and pfk13. The two RDTs showed similar positivity (44% vs 45%) with 1.26% discordance, and none of the discordant samples carried pfhrp2 deletions. Among 598 sequenced samples, single pfhrp2 and pfhrp3 deletion prevalences were 4.5% and 12.4%, with no double-deletions detected. pfk13 sequencing revealed 0.7% mutation prevalence, comprising A578S (n=2) and the WHO-validated artemisinin partial-resistance marker A675V (n=2), and the authors conclude HRP2-based RDTs remain reliable.

articles.headline: Pf41 antibody serology detects past malaria exposure in low-transmission Indonesia
articles.title_original: Pf41 serological profiling reveals potential malaria exposure in low transmission setting in the Menoreh Hills, Central Java, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Ilyas M, Yuwanita MR, Natanegara S, Daud MM, Rahayu AA, Hitipeuw D, Wusono AD, Nuranindita R, Fitriana F, Syahada JH, Jeon BY, Jun H, Martini M, Nuridzin DZ, Lu F, Han JH, Muh F
articles.details: This cross-sectional seroepidemiological study (January–June 2025) analyzed anti-Pf41 IgG antibodies by protein microarray in serum from 120 individuals across four malaria-endemic villages in the Menoreh Hills, Central Java, plus 24 non-exposed controls. Overall Pf41 seropositivity was 71% (85/120), with marked heterogeneity across villages (Kembaran 93%, Sedayu 83%, Wadas 60%, Kemejing 47%), and mean standardized MFI was significantly higher in all exposed groups than controls (p<0.001). Antibody responses were significantly associated with site-specific behavioral factors, including frequent nighttime outdoor mosquito bites, nighttime livestock feeding, and repellent use. The authors conclude Pf41 serology could complement surveillance by identifying populations with serological evidence of prior exposure in low-transmission settings.

articles.headline: Ventilation and hypertonic saline fail to improve cerebral malaria outcomes
articles.title_original: Adjunctive Mechanical Ventilation or Intravenous Hypertonic Saline in Malawian Children with Cerebral Malaria and Highly Increased Brain Volume: A Randomized, Phase 3 Clinical Trial.
articles.authors: O'Brien NF, Postels DG, Chimalizeni Y, Fonseca Y, Wooldridge G, Parker E, Galeano X, Urbina E, Lintner-Rivera M, Wynkoop HJ, Chetcuti K, Goyal MS, Biondo-Savin E, Juvvadi S, Nicholas J, Vidal L, Raees M, Doctor A, Remy KE, Bhutta A, Holloway A, Moxon CA, Seydel KB, Liomba AM, Chilingulo C, Ibrahim K, Gardiner J, Taylor TE
articles.details: This randomized, open-label phase 3 trial in Blantyre, Malawi (2018-2023) enrolled children aged 6 months to 12 years with cerebral malaria (Blantyre Coma Score ≤2, P. falciparum parasitemia) and severe brain swelling on MRI (brain volume score 6-8); of 206 screened, 57 were randomized to early mechanical ventilation (23), 3% hypertonic saline (17), or usual care (17). Neither intervention reduced 7-day mortality (risk difference -0.075, p=0.59 for ventilation; 0.002, p=1.0 for HTS), and neurologic morbidity did not differ. HTS produced sustained increases in cerebral blood flow velocities, and among children with baseline cerebral hyperemia, HTS was associated with worse combined morbidity or death (relative risk 3.0, 95% CI 1.02-10.9, p=0.04). The authors conclude non-phenotype-guided neuroprotective strategies are ineffective and may cause harm.

articles.headline: Climate and illiteracy predict malaria incidence in Tete, Mozambique
articles.title_original: Climatic and social health determinants of malaria incidence in Tete, Mozambique: Bayesian spatio-temporal modeling of routine data (2016-2022).
articles.authors: Mussafo AJA, da Cruz RF, de Arruda Silveira LV, Govone JS
articles.details: This study used a hierarchical Bayesian spatio-temporal model (INLA approach, with a reparameterized Besag spatial structure, an AR1 temporal process, and Fourier seasonality terms) on malaria case data from Tete Province, Mozambique (2016–2022). Precipitation and illiteracy rate were significant predictors, associated with estimated increases of 6.7% and 1.6% in malaria incidence, respectively. Incidence tended to rise shortly after the onset of the rainy season and decline during winter, and the districts of Macanga, Chiuta, and Moatize showed higher risk while Tete City had lower incidence. The authors conclude climatic and social factors, together with spatio-temporal dynamics, play a decisive role in shaping transmission.

articles.headline: Humidity shifts thermal performance of malaria vector Anopheles stephensi
articles.title_original: Beyond Temperature: Relative Humidity Systematically Shifts Juvenile Thermal Performance and Projected Population Growth in a Malaria Vector.
articles.authors: Huxley PJ, Brown JJ, St Laurent B, Johnson B, Cheung OY, Asamoah A, Hollingsworth BD, Bump ER, Wimberly MC, Pascual M, Johnson LR, Murdock CC
articles.details: Using laboratory experiments, this study showed that relative humidity alters juvenile (immature stage) life-history trait responses in the malaria vector Anopheles stephensi. The authors integrated these humidity-dependent juvenile traits into an analytic maximal population growth rate (rm) model to examine how humidity shifts the temperature dependence of projected population growth. Heuristic climate-suitability comparisons illustrated that temperature-humidity interactions acting through juvenile traits alone can change qualitative conclusions about where temperature-only models over- or underestimate environmental suitability. The authors highlight the importance of incorporating humidity alongside temperature when assessing vector responses to climate.

articles.headline: GIS mapping links 19th-century malaria deaths to settlement expansion
articles.title_original: Ecology of Malaria Mortality: A Spatiotemporal Mapping Approach.
articles.authors: Cooke A, Brickley MB
articles.details: This study geocoded 2,683 probable malaria-attributed deaths from Ontario death certificates (1869–1900) and analyzed them with Geographic Information Systems to assess how settlement, landscape, and infrastructure structured transmission risk in a temperate setting, using spatial clustering (Average Nearest Neighbor, kernel density), negative binomial regression, and generalized additive models. Mortality was significantly clustered across all decades but became progressively less spatially concentrated over time, with locations shifting farther from mapped wetlands as drainage intensified while their proximity to settlements consistently decreased. Settlement proximity was the strongest and only significant predictor of mortality density, whereas distances to wetlands were not independently associated once settlement effects were accounted for.

articles.headline: Photonic crystal biosensor designed for early malaria detection
articles.title_original: Highly Sensitive 1D Photonic Crystal Biosensor for Early Malaria Detection via Refractive Index.
articles.authors: Kaspar K, Karuppiah K, Karuppiah P, Ramanujam NR
articles.details: This theoretical study used the Transfer Matrix Method to design a one-dimensional photonic crystal biosensor (Air/(PbS/SiO2)4/defect/(PbS/SiO2)4/substrate) for early malaria detection, optimizing defect layer thickness and incidence angle around a 1550 nm resonance. Operating over a refractive index range of 1.371-1.402 RIU (corresponding to different infection stages), it achieved a maximum sensitivity of 977.74 nm/RIU and a quality factor of 93.8×10^3 at a defect thickness of 1.2da; increasing to 1.4da raised sensitivity to 1163.22 nm/RIU with a quality factor of 82.57×10^3. The design showed a detection limit of ~10^-6 RIU, indicating potential for rapid, cost-effective diagnosis.

articles.headline: Low-density P. vivax and G6PD deficiency challenge Myanmar elimination
articles.title_original: Sub-RDT Plasmodium vivax infections and G6PD deficiency in Kayin State, Myanmar.
articles.authors: Thu AM, Phyo AP, Pateekham C, Blu SH, Thwin MM, Kajeechiwa L, Watthanaworawit W, Okkararungrot Y, Bancone G, Gornsawun G, Moo PK, Klain K, Aung PL, Nguitragool W, Pan-Ngum W, Nosten F
articles.details: This study assessed low-density, sub-RDT Plasmodium vivax infections and G6PD deficiency across 23 villages in Kayin State, Myanmar (2020-2021), screening participants with mRDT, reverse-transcriptase real-time PCR (rRT-PCR), ELISA, and a quantitative point-of-care G6PD test. Among 5,509 individuals the mRDT detection rate was 0.5%; rRT-PCR positivity was 14.3% (317/2219) for P. vivax and 8.3% (185/2219) for P. falciparum, with sub-RDT P. vivax prevalence of 14.1% (311/2211). G6PD deficiency was 21.7% in males and 10.6% in females (P<0.001), with no association between G6PD status and sub-RDT P. vivax. The authors conclude these coexisting reservoirs pose a major barrier to elimination, requiring high-sensitivity diagnostics and reliable G6PD testing.

articles.headline: Poverty is the dominant driver of childhood malaria clustering in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Geographic Patterns and Drivers of Paediatric Malaria in Nigeria: Walking in the shadow of Poverty.
articles.authors: Osayomi T, Adedun OA
articles.details: This spatial epidemiology study used published data and analytical techniques (Global Moran's I and Getis-Ord Gi*) to map the geographic pattern and drivers of paediatric malaria across Nigeria. Paediatric malaria showed significant spatial clustering, with hotspots in varying areas across study periods and coldspots generally in the southwest corner. Stepwise linear regression identified poverty as the dominant predictor (R2 = 0.435). The authors conclude that free anti-malaria treatment for children and poverty reduction may be required to reduce the malaria burden.

articles.headline: Machine learning models accurately predict malaria prevalence in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Application of machine learning models in predicting malaria prevalence in Nigeria: an analysis of the 2015-2020 demographic and health surveys.
articles.authors: Ayoka TO, Nnadi CO
articles.details: This study developed machine learning regression models to diagnose malaria in rural communities in Nigeria, using patients' symptoms and clinical information from the 2020 Nigerian Demographic and Health Surveys Program Geospatial Covariate dataset. The models were validated with cross-validation and holdout testing. On the test set, coefficient of determination (R2) scores were 0.9937 for the random forest regressor, 0.9916 for multiple linear regression, and 0.9924 for the ridge model. The random forest learning curve showed performance consistently improving as the volume of data increased, and the authors conclude the approach supports a shift from reactive diagnostics to proactive risk prediction and resource allocation.

articles.headline: Imported vivax malaria sustains autochthonous transmission in Hormozgan, Iran
articles.title_original: Malaria dynamics in Iran: insights from autochthonous and asymptomatic.
articles.authors: Sadeghi F, Nodez SMM, Khosravani M
articles.details: This surveillance study analyzed primary CDC data on autochthonous and imported malaria from Hormozgan province, Iran, collected between April 2018 and May 2023, using SPSS. Among 289 cases, 87.5% were male and 12.5% female, mostly aged 11-29 years. Vivax malaria accounted for 78.9% of cases. Afghans (imported) represented 56.7%, Pakistanis (imported) 30.8%, and Iranians (autochthonous) 12.5%; cases peaked in 2023, especially in Jask County, and asymptomatic cases accounted for 45.25%. The authors conclude that imported malaria significantly contributes to maintaining transmission among native populations and call for active surveillance, rapid diagnosis, treatment, and follow-up.

articles.headline: Pfs230 gene highly conserved across East Africa, supporting transmission-blocking vaccine
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity of the Plasmodium falciparum Pfs230 gene in four East African countries supports its potential as a malaria transmission‑blocking vaccine candidate.
articles.authors: Kisambale AJ, Moshi R, Pereus D, Mbwambo RB, Mandai SS, Chacha GA, Lyimo B, Madebe RA, Budodo R, Bakari C, Semboja HJ, Petro DA, Challe DP, Aaron S, Mbwambo D, Lusasi A, Kajange S, Lazaro S, Mandara CI, Seth MD, Kulohoma BW, Juma G, Ishengoma DS
articles.details: This study assessed the genetic diversity and evolutionary dynamics of the Plasmodium falciparum Pfs230 transmission-blocking vaccine candidate gene using MalariaGEN Pf7 genomic data from Ethiopia, Kenya, Tanzania, and Uganda. Of 1471 sequences retrieved, 1312 passed quality filtering. Nucleotide diversity was low (π = 5.9 × 10-4); haplotype diversity was 0.999 with 496 haplotypes among 718 monoclonal sequences, 8.1% (n = 40) shared between populations. The non-synonymous to synonymous ratio was 0.43, and Tajima's D was negative in all countries, significantly so in Kenya (-2.131; P < 0.01) and Tanzania (-2.056; P < 0.05); PCA and FST showed no population differentiation. The authors conclude Pfs230 is highly conserved, supporting its potential as a transmission-blocking vaccine candidate.

articles.headline: District HMIS managers' attitudes and practices in Uganda malaria reporting
articles.title_original: Attitude and practices of district-level HMIS managers towards malaria routine reporting in Uganda: a 2024 cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Kigozi SP, Okiring J, Kigozi LS, Emuron P, Talimusa EA, Kigozi RN, Alegana V, Sartorius B, Giorgi E, Drakeley C, Yeka A
articles.details: This 2024 cross-sectional study assessed attitudes and practices of district-level Health Management Information System (HMIS) managers toward malaria routine reporting across all 15 malaria endemicity regions of Uganda between January and June 2024, using semi-structured interviews and descriptive statistics. The 34 participants from 30 districts and cities included biostatisticians (70.6%) and HMIS focal persons (23.5%). All reviewed received reports, with 75.8% documenting mistakes but only 31.6% able to show documented queries; 81.6% of expected reports had been received (25.4% after active follow-up), and 93.9% received queries (mean 4.2 per implicated facility). The authors conclude that capable district teams administer reporting, but gaps remain in SOP adherence, scheduled support supervision, and advanced analytical skills for data use.

articles.headline: Parasite genome variants linked to sickle haemoglobin in Ghana malaria
articles.title_original: Associations between the Plasmodium falciparum genome and sickle haemoglobin identified in mild malaria cases from Ghana.
articles.authors: Hamilton WL, Forster AJ, Aniweh Y, Asoala V, Drury E, Rockett KA, Awandare GA, Kwiatkowski DP, Band G, Amenga-Etego LN
articles.details: A genome-wide association study tested the Plasmodium falciparum genome against human β-globin genotypes in 1,368 people with mild malaria in northern Ghana. It replicated previously reported sickle-associated parasite loci Pfsa1 and Pfsa3 with HbS, while Pfsa2 was absent from this population. A candidate new locus in the serine/threonine kinase FIKK4.2 (termed Pfsa4) was also associated with HbS and replicated in a published Mali sample. No strong associations were found with haemoglobin C. The authors conclude these findings add complexity to human–parasite co-evolution, with the fourth locus requiring further validation.

articles.headline: Raman spectroscopy plus machine learning stages malaria in red blood cells
articles.title_original: Population-Level Raman Biochemical Staging of Malaria in Human Red Blood Cells Using Interpretable Machine Learning.
articles.authors: Wu L, Gupta A, Tripathi AK, Barman I
articles.details: This study presents a population-level Raman spectroscopy framework coupled with interpretable machine learning to define biochemical signatures of Plasmodium falciparum-infected red blood cells, targeting circulating ring stages and transmissible gametocyte stages. Using confocal and spatially offset Raman spectroscopy on synchronized cultures, the authors captured biochemical alterations across parasite development, with SHAP analysis identifying discriminative spectral regions, notably hemoglobin-associated vibrational modes. They also demonstrated sensitivity to infection signatures beneath a skin-mimetic phantom, confirming subsurface detectability under tissue-like scattering. The authors conclude this provides a basis for noninvasive, stage-aware diagnostics capable of identifying infection and transmission potential.

articles.headline: Malaria shifts neutrophil subsets, possible link to Burkitt lymphoma
articles.title_original: The impact of malaria-induced neutrophil subset shift and a link to Burkitt lymphoma.
articles.authors: Okoth SA, Tonui RK, Maina TK, Agwati E, Oduor CI, Racenet Z, M'Bana V, Njuguna FM, Keitany KK, Chepsiror D, Ayieko C, Moormann AM, Kinyua AW, Forconi CS
articles.details: A prospective study used multiparameter flow cytometry to profile neutrophil subsets (by CD15, CD16, CD10, CD11b, CD182, CD184, CD62L) in children with differing Plasmodium falciparum exposure histories, children diagnosed with Burkitt lymphoma (BL) from Western Kenya, and healthy malaria low-exposed Kenyan adults. Malaria-exposed children showed increased frequencies of aged neutrophil subsets and reduced mature active subsets compared with low-exposed children. A positive correlation in immature neutrophils was observed between malaria-exposed healthy and BL children (rs = 0.7; p < 0.0001). The authors conclude malaria exposure may expand immature neutrophils, potentially creating a permissive environment for BL.

articles.headline: ICOS gene variants not linked to P. vivax antibody response in Venezuela
articles.title_original: No Evidence for Association Between ICOS (a T-Cell Co-Stimulatory Molecule) Gene Polymorphisms and Anti-MSP-1(19) IgG Response in Venezuelan Endemic Plasmodium vivax Malaria.
articles.authors: Sevilla RD, Regalado-Gutiérrez OA, Wide A, Puche-Quiñonez R, Patarroyo MA, Noya-González O, Fernández-Mestre M
articles.details: This study analysed 91 individuals with uncomplicated Plasmodium vivax infection in Venezuela, measuring total IgG against recombinant PvMSP19 by ELISA and genotyping three ICOS polymorphisms (rs4404254, rs4675379, rs10183087). PvMSP19-specific IgG was detected in 70.3% of participants, and a three-cluster solution showed significantly different median reactivity indices across low (0.408), medium (0.862), and high (1.267) responders (p < 0.001). ICOS genotypes and haplotypes showed no significant associations with antibody levels under any inheritance model, though strong linkage disequilibrium was observed. Antibody responses positively correlated with prior malarial episodes (r = 0.803, p < 0.0001). The authors conclude other costimulatory molecules may more strongly influence humoral immunity.

articles.headline: Whole-genome study reframes Neotropical malaria mosquito evolution
articles.title_original: Reassessing malaria-transmitting mosquito evolution with a neotropical lens.
articles.authors: Carter TE, Gunarathna I
articles.details: This commentary discusses Tennessen et al.'s whole-genome analysis of Anopheles darlingi, a major Neotropical malaria vector, addressing gaps in understanding its evolutionary drivers. The work revealed geographically structured An. darlingi populations with no evidence of sympatric species and strong signals of widespread insecticide resistance arising through convergent evolution. No quantitative results are reported in the abstract.

articles.headline: Artemether-lumefantrine stays 96% effective in Laos, but lumefantrine resistance signals emerge
articles.title_original: Artemether-lumefantrine for the treatment of Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Laos: a therapeutic efficacy study coupled with genomic and in vitro phenotypic analyses.
articles.authors: Feufack-Donfack LB, Khim N, Tat B, Banouvong V, Khamlome B, Chindavongsal K, Chanthavisouk C, Reyburn R, Shortus M, Kloeung N, Ke S, Khean C, Seng C, Kul C, Eam R, Orban A, Salvador J, Vezain M, Dorgebray PE, Coppée R, Ariey F, Witkowski B, Ringwald P, Popovici J
articles.details: A therapeutic efficacy study enrolled 198 patients aged 1–60 years with microscopically confirmed P. falciparum malaria across four southern provinces of Laos, treating 195 with 3-day oral artemether-lumefantrine and following them to day 28. PCR-adjusted adequate clinical and parasitological response was 96% (95% CI 92–98), with 2% treatment failure and 2% reinfection. Of 190 sequenced isolates, 9 (5%) carried the pfkelch13 R539T mutation and 43 (23%) the C580Y mutation, both linked to day-3 parasitaemia (p=0·044); one recrudescent isolate showed reduced lumefantrine susceptibility (IC50 59·9 nM) and high in vitro artemisinin resistance (ring-stage survival 35·8%). Authors warn of potential emerging lumefantrine resistance and urge close monitoring.

articles.headline: P. vivax antigen genetic diversity rarely undermines serological exposure markers
articles.title_original: Assessing the effect of parasite antigen genetic diversity on performance of Plasmodium vivax serological exposure markers for malaria: a multicentre observational diagnostic accuracy study.
articles.authors: Bareng APN, Wu KW, Takashima E, Argyropoulos DC, Smith L, Naung MT, Mazhari R, Schoffer K, Kiernan-Walker N, Abraham A, Lamont M, Mehra S, Lim PS, Sattabongkot J, Monteiro W, Lacerda M, Healer J, Chitnis CE, Tham WH, Tsuboi T, Mueller I, Barry AE, Longley RJ
articles.details: This observational diagnostic accuracy study analysed global P. vivax genetic data for 14 leading serological markers and expressed new haplotypes for eight antigens reflecting global sequence diversity, compared against the Sal-1 reference. Antibody responses were tested in 774 participants from Thailand and 923 from Brazil, relating the magnitude of response to how recently each participant had a qPCR-detectable blood-stage infection. Extensive diversity was found in MSP5 (π=14·8×10⁻³) and DBPII (π=7·7×10⁻³), and RBP2a and DBPII showed strong differences in immunogenicity by region and construct. However, for five of eight proteins (MSP5, RiPR, PTEX150, Pv-fam-a, and RBP2b) this diversity had no significant effect on identifying exposure within the previous 9 months, and affected markers were overcome by combining multiple antigens, leading the authors to conclude that even highly diverse antigens can be effective serological markers.

articles.headline: Imported pediatric malaria at 9 US hospitals: delayed diagnosis raises severe-disease risk
articles.title_original: Malaria in Children at 9 US Hospitals: 2016-2023.
articles.authors: Sundararaman SA, Roper B, Davis AKF, Hanze Villavicencio KL, Mayhew JA, Wang ML, Tang NL, Soma VL, Wang Z, Feeney ME, Trehan I, Weatherhead JE, John CC, Gerber JS, Odom John AR
articles.details: A retrospective descriptive study examined 171 children treated for malaria at 9 US hospitals from 2016 to 2023, analysing demographics, outcomes, and risk factors for severe disease. Most had traveled to West Africa to visit friends and relatives. Fever was the most common symptom (90%) and most had at least one abdominal symptom (66%); 32% were diagnosed with severe malaria and no deaths occurred. Delayed diagnoses were common (26%), occurred at similar rates across hospitals, and were a risk factor for severe malaria. Authors stress chemoprophylaxis and prompt diagnosis of travel-acquired malaria to improve outcomes.

articles.headline: Nine imported malaria cases reported in malaria-free northwest Iran
articles.title_original: Challenges and strategies in malaria control: A case series from East Azerbaijan Province, 2023-2024.
articles.authors: Golshaniniya P, Porkar Khatibi M, Pourhassan A, Mirzaaghazadeh E, Babapour A, Rezaei H, Koosha A, Khayatzadeh S, Deihim M, Bagheri R, Abbasi M
articles.details: This retrospective case series describes nine malaria cases identified in the malaria-free northwest region of Iran (East Azerbaijan Province) to inform surveillance in elimination settings, using treatment follow-up and hospital records for eight hospitalized patients. All patients had traveled to malaria-endemic areas, including southern Iran or countries such as Pakistan, Kenya, and Sierra Leone. Three cases were P. falciparum and six were P. vivax, confirmed by rapid diagnostic tests and microscopy. Authors note that influx of infected individuals during peak mosquito season near potential vector breeding habitats could enable localized transmission, underscoring the need for stronger personnel training and surveillance of imported cases during Iran's elimination phase.

articles.headline: Survey of febrile-illness care and LLIN use across 10 malaria-endemic Indian states
articles.title_original: Comprehensive Analysis of Health Service Utilization for Febrile Illness and Malaria in Malaria-Endemic States in India: LLINs as a Pillar of Control Strategies.
articles.authors: Chalageri VH, Marinaik SB, Singhal R, Gupta SK, Nathan SN, Eapen A, Yadav CP, Singh PK, Tripathi PK, Malla WA, Pradhan SC, Sinha DP, Singh SP, Singh K, Bhuyan PJ, Rahi M, Jain T, Sharma R, Anvikar AR
articles.details: A subnational cross-sectional study surveyed 37,849 households covering 177,644 individuals in ten malaria-endemic Indian states to estimate health service and long-lasting insecticidal net (LLIN) utilization for febrile illness and malaria. Government healthcare facilities were accessible within 2.5 km (IQR 1–5 km), and 57.4% of febrile individuals visited or contacted a government provider within 24–48 hours. Adequate prevention knowledge and behavior was observed in 89.2% of households, while 73.6% reported regular LLIN use. Lack of formal education and tribal population status were high risk factors for malaria. Authors highlight gaps between facility accessibility and utilization, and between LLIN ownership and regular use.

articles.headline: Crystal structure solved for Plasmodium vivax immune-mimic protein MIF
articles.title_original: Crystal structure of Plasmodium vivax macrophage migration inhibitory factor.
articles.authors: Nair A, Lin M, Srivastava A, Liu L, Cooper A, Battaile K, Harmon E, Myler PJ, Staker BL, Lovell S, Chakafana G, Asojo OA
articles.journal: Acta Crystallogr F Struct Biol Commun
articles.details: This structural biology study reports the production, crystallization, and 1.8 Å resolution crystal structure of Plasmodium vivax macrophage migration inhibitory factor (PvMIF), deposited as PDB entry 9b0m. PvMIF crystallized in space group P63 with a single molecule in the asymmetric unit, and its biological unit is the prototypical MIF trimer. PvMIF is a homolog of host MIF that binds host CD74 and can suppress host MIF-CD74 signaling; its plasma concentrations correlate with P. vivax malaria severity, and Plasmodium MIFs are candidate vaccine antigens. No clinical outcomes were assessed.

articles.headline: Opinion: genomic polygenic scores to track mosquito insecticide resistance
articles.title_original: Polygenic scores for genomic surveillance of insecticide resistance in malaria control.
articles.authors: Donnelly MJ, Kamau A, McDermott D, Nagi SC, Summers S
articles.details: This opinion article argues that genomic approaches can more accurately characterise patterns of insecticide resistance in malaria vectors than current phenotyping methods, which are too insensitive to quantify the impact of resistance before vector-control tools fail. The authors note that recent increases in the global malaria burden are thought to be due in part to rising insecticide resistance, which reduces the efficacy of long-lasting insecticide nets and indoor residual spraying. They contend that estimating the impact of resistance is a prerequisite for evidence-based vector control.

articles.headline: Computational protein redesign stabilizes malaria drug targets for screening
articles.title_original: Stabilizing Plasmodium falciparum proteins for small molecule drug discovery.
articles.authors: Amann M, Sträßer T, Einsle O, Günther S
articles.details: This drug-discovery methods study tested whether ProteinMPNN-guided sequence design could generate stabilized surrogate constructs of difficult-to-produce Plasmodium falciparum proteins while retaining wild-type structure and binding. Designs were made for three targets—PfBDP1-BRD, PfBDP4-BRD, and PfK13-KREP—with constraints preserving conserved and binding-site residues, and showed markedly increased thermal stability. Isothermal titration calorimetry confirmed PfBDP1-BRD variants retained wild-type-like binding thermodynamics with a known ligand, the PfK13-KREP construct yielded an apo structure with a near-wild-type pocket, and a stabilized PfBDP4-BRD surrogate enabled validation of virtual-screening hits and a 1.25 Å co-crystal structure with a new inhibitor. The authors conclude stabilized surrogates are practical tools for structure-enabled malaria drug discovery.

articles.headline: Recurrent P. vivax infections in Ethiopia frequently infect mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Parasite and gametocyte dynamics and infectivity to mosquitoes during recurrent Plasmodium vivax infections: A longitudinal study from Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Tadesse FG, Chali W, Ejigu LA, Abdo M, Kassa FA, Bulto MG, Bezabih MK, Alemayehu L, Hundera NL, Bayu SL, Nibret D, Ashine T, Assefa M, Solomon Z, Demisse M, Gashaw A, Shimelash A, Tebeje SK, Hailemeskel E, Gadisa E, Rosado J, Obadia T, Taylor AR, White M, Massebo F, Ramjith J, Drakeley C, Bousema T
articles.details: This one-year longitudinal study in Arba Minch Zuria district, Ethiopia, enrolled patients with clinical Plasmodium vivax infection at Shele health center; all received chloroquine plus 14-day primaquine and were followed monthly with molecular parasite/gametocyte quantification and mosquito feeding assays. Over follow-up, 54.6% (96/176) had at least one recurrent parasitemia, and 167 (70.2%) of 238 feedings infected at least one mosquito, with similar rates at baseline (117/166, 70.5%) and recurrent symptomatic episodes (50/72, 69.4%). Mosquito infection correlated with asexual (ρ=0.29, p<0.0001) and gametocyte (ρ=0.32, p<0.0001) density; genotyping suggested 45.7% (42/92) of recurrences were relapses, and relapses were less infectious than new infections after adjusting for gametocyte density (OR 0.48, 95% CI 0.26-0.87, p=0.016). The authors conclude radical cure should be strengthened to curb relapse-driven transmission.

articles.headline: pvmdr1 mutations common in North Indian P. vivax, resistance role unclear
articles.title_original: Molecular detection of chloroquine resistance in Plasmodium vivax isolates from North India: Analysis of single nucleotide polymorphisms in pvmdr1 gene.
articles.authors: Nityadarshini N, Bir R, Biswas J, Jadon RS, Mirdha BR, Verma N
articles.details: This molecular surveillance study screened 7530 patients with acute undifferentiated febrile illness in North India using microscopy, rapid diagnostic tests, and PCR, then sequenced the pvmdr1 gene of confirmed Plasmodium vivax mono-infections to assess chloroquine resistance markers. P. vivax was confirmed in 75 (0.99%) patients (mean age 29.2 years, male-to-female ratio 2:1), and 36 of 75 isolates were successfully sequenced. The M908L and F1076L mutations were present in 100% of isolates and T898A in 5.6%, with Indian isolates clustering phylogenetically with Madagascar and Afghanistan haplotypes. The authors conclude that although M908L and F1076L were fixed, their role in clinical chloroquine resistance remains uncertain given the absence of Y976F, underscoring the need for continued molecular surveillance.

articles.headline: BISSAU molecular form confirmed as distinct Anopheles lineage in The Gambia
articles.title_original: Genomic insights into the population structure of malaria vectors of the Anopheles gambiae complex in The Gambia reveals distinctiveness and spatio-temporal stability of BISSAU molecular form.
articles.authors: De Marco CM, Caputo B, Pichler V, Hamid-Adiamoh M, Gibba B, Clarkson C, Assogba BS, D'Alessandro U, Amambua-Ngwa A, Della Torre A
articles.details: Population genomic analyses (PCA, ADMIXTURE, TreeMix, neighbour-joining, TESS3R) using over 200,000 chromosome-3 SNPs were applied to 2283 specimens of An. coluzzii, An. gambiae, An. arabiensis, An. melas, and the BISSAU molecular form collected between 2005 and 2021 at 35 locations across The Gambia. Results consistently identified BISSAU as a genetically discrete, temporally stable unit in a sister clade with An. gambiae and An. coluzzii, showing no admixture with sympatric members. The authors conclude BISSAU is an independently evolved, reproductively isolated lineage, most likely occurring on the north bank of the Gambia river roughly 10–120 km from the coast in brackish/freshwater swamp habitats.

articles.headline: Microsporidia MB symbiont alters mosquito mating chemistry, not human host-seeking
articles.title_original: Behaviour of the malaria vector Anopheles arabiensis infected with the endosymbiont Microsporidia MB: host seeking and cuticular hydrocarbons associated with mating.
articles.authors: Chepkemoi L, Cheseto X, Nyanjom SG, Herren JK, Tchouassi DP
articles.details: This study tested whether the transmission-blocking endosymbiont Microsporidia MB affects Anopheles arabiensis attraction to human odours, blood-meal source selection, and male cuticular hydrocarbon (CHC) profiles linked to mating. Blood-fed mosquitoes from CDC light traps were screened by PCR, and isolines were used for olfactometer assays and CHC extraction. In olfactometer tests, infected mosquitoes showed no preferential attraction to human over cattle odours, and logistic regression found no association between infection and selective human feeding over livestock, bird, or monkey hosts. CHC profiles differed between infected and uninfected males, though random forest models showed no single hydrocarbon drove this. Authors conclude the symbiont imposes no cost on human host-seeking but may alter mating-related chemical signalling.

articles.headline: Meta-analysis compares RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccine efficacy
articles.title_original: Efficacy and immunogenicity of RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccines: - Systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Asjad H, Sadiq MW, Siddiqui S, Sherali S, Yousafzai MT, Taseen S, Qamar FN
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis searched PubMed, Scopus, and Cochrane for studies published 2000–2024; 832 records were screened and 8 included. RTS,S/AS01 provided significant protection in the 6–12-week age group, with a 23.0% malaria risk reduction (risk ratio 0.77). In the 5–17-month group, the risk ratio for malaria reduction was 0.61 for both vaccines, with efficacy declining over time, underscoring the value of a booster dose; RTS,S/AS01 was reported safe with a strong antibody response across age groups. R21 showed high efficacy (event rate 0.22) and a strong antibody response but more frequent adverse events. Authors conclude both vaccines advance malaria elimination efforts.

articles.headline: Perspective argues innate immune memory shapes repeated Plasmodium infections
articles.title_original: The case for exploring innate memory in Plasmodium infection.
articles.authors: Ho NN, Tongogara T, Tran TM, Kaushansky A, Glennon EK
articles.details: The authors argue that innate immune memory—epigenetic reprogramming that regulates immune responses to sequential infections independently of adaptive immunity—may help explain the dynamics of recurrent Plasmodium infection in highly endemic areas, where individuals can be bitten by an infected mosquito daily. They note that most laboratory studies assess only a single infection, leaving gaps in understanding how recurrent field infections affect pathogenesis and immunity upon re-infection. They summarize the basic concepts and supporting findings, and postulate that innate memory in non-immune cells, only beginning to be described in other disease systems, may have significant consequences in the complex Plasmodium lifecycle.

articles.headline: Review of DNA markers for metabolic insecticide resistance in malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Metabolic insecticide resistance DNA markers in malaria vectors.
articles.authors: Tchouakui M, Djoko Tagne CS, Tekoh TA, Tatchou-Nebangwa NMT, Kouamo MM, Wondji CS
articles.details: This review synthesizes the discovery of DNA markers underlying metabolic insecticide resistance in malaria vectors, contrasting it with simpler target-site resistance. Multiomics approaches have identified markers in detoxification genes (cytochrome P450s, glutathione S-transferases), including CYP6P4, CYP6P3, and GSTe2 in Anopheles gambiae, and CYP6P9a/b, CYP6P4a/b, CYP9K1, GSTe2, and ASL in Anopheles funestus. The authors describe how these markers support resistance management by tracking spatiotemporal resistance evolution, cross-resistance, fitness costs, and impacts on transmission, review available DNA-based diagnostics for genomic surveillance, and outline priorities for extending the framework to new insecticides and vector species.

articles.headline: Review: how malaria parasites develop inside Anopheles mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Demystifying Plasmodium development within the Anopheles mosquito.
articles.authors: Shaw WR, Catteruccia F
articles.details: This review summarizes Plasmodium development within Anopheles mosquitoes (sporogony), the series of lifecycle transitions parasites undergo before transmission to the next host. The authors note these stages are hard to study in vivo because of a major bottleneck in parasite numbers and an increase in parasite ploidy that can rescue mutant phenotypes. They describe how recent advances in microscopy, single-cell RNA sequencing, and genetic screening have advanced understanding of these mosquito-stage processes, which could inform novel transmission-control tools.

articles.headline: Correspondence raising concerns about spatial emanator malaria control study
articles.title_original: Concerns regarding study of spatial emanators for malaria control.
articles.authors: Bradley J, Hayes R, Smith PG, Kleinschmidt I
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Authors' reply to concerns about spatial emanator malaria study
articles.title_original: Concerns regarding study of spatial emanators for malaria control - Authors' reply.
articles.authors: Allan RJ, Cross CL, Messenger LA
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: New pan-Plasmodium PCR assay confirms malaria in blood donor screening
articles.title_original: Development and validation of a pan-Plasmodium molecular assay as a confirmatory method for blood donor screening.
articles.authors: Milani P, Wen L, Montalvo L, Coco SB, Di Germanio C, Bres V, Yadav M, Livezey K, Levi JE, Linnen J, Busch MP
articles.details: Researchers developed and validated a pan-Plasmodium reverse transcription quantitative PCR (RT-qPCR) assay targeting a conserved 18S rRNA region shared by all five human-infecting Plasmodium species, as a supplemental confirmatory tool for nucleic acid testing-based blood donor screening; it is compatible with whole blood lysed in Grifols' parasite transport medium and identifies species via amplicon sequencing. The assay showed 97.2% amplification efficiency, linearity R2=0.99, and a 95% limit of detection of 5.3 infected RBCs/mL (95% CI 3.2–8.6). Clinical sensitivity was 100% across all five species and specificity was 100% (95% CI 99–100%), with no cross-reactivity with Babesia microti. The authors conclude it is a sensitive confirmatory tool supporting regulatory use and research into asymptomatic parasitemia.

articles.headline: Review: mitochondrial metabolic shift in monocytes during P. vivax malaria
articles.title_original: The mitochondrial metabolic shift in inflammatory monocytes during Plasmodium vivax malaria.
articles.authors: Antonelli LR, Gazzinelli RT
articles.details: This review discusses immunometabolism in Plasmodium vivax infection, focusing on a metabolic-mitochondrial shift in highly activated monocytes during human P. vivax malaria. The authors describe how these cells show enhanced phagocytic and microbicidal activity while producing high levels of proinflammatory cytokines and reactive oxygen species that may contribute to disease signs during acute episodes. A dedicated section on experimental malaria is included to provide complementary mechanistic insight into the immunometabolic pathways involved.

articles.headline: Malaria and Chagas trends in non-endemic Brazil, 2007–2024
articles.title_original: Malaria and Chagas disease in non-endemic settings in Brazil: a comparative analysis of national surveillance data (2007-2024).
articles.authors: Tudella GCN, Sasazaki FY, Kelnhar I, de Conto T, de Oliveira JVB, Franco FW, Oliveira DH, Cocco OD, Boufleuer IS, Martins FC, Soares TP, da Silva ACM, Wendt JDR
articles.details: Ecological study of Brazilian SINAN surveillance data on confirmed malaria and acute Chagas disease (ACD) cases reported in non-endemic areas between 2007 and 2024, using Prais-Winsten regression, ARIMA forecasting, and Getis-Ord spatial statistics. Of 10,146 cases (9,755 malaria; 391 ACD), malaria predominated in males (78%) and adults aged 20–39 (47.8%), mostly non-autochthonous (89.7%); ACD was slightly more frequent in females (50.1%) with oral transmission in 53.2%. Malaria showed a significant decreasing trend (β=−0.0174; p=0.0397) and ACD an increasing trend (β=0.0342; p=0.0267), with five malaria hotspots and one ACD hotspot in Piauí. Authors conclude both remain epidemiologically relevant and call for strengthened surveillance.

articles.headline: Micro-level malaria hotspots mapped in a remote Indian district
articles.title_original: Identification of micro-level malaria hotspots and its implications for achieving malaria elimination target in a remote district of India.
articles.authors: Misra BR, Mishra A, Tiwari SK, Srivastava DN, Joshi HS, Mishra N, Dwivedi GR
articles.details: Analysis of 2017–2024 malaria surveillance data from Sonbhadra district, India, using Kulldorff spatial scanning with a discrete Poisson model and Mean/Maximum Annual Rate of Change to identify high-risk clusters and project elimination. Among 18,217 reported cases, test positivity fell with a 95% reduction in cases; annual parasite incidence dropped from 2.7 (2017) to 0.1 (2024), while annual falciparum incidence fluctuated late, rising to 0.09 in 2024 after near-total suppression in 2022–2023. The main hotspot was at Chopan block, with sub-district high-risk clusters of both P. falciparum and P. vivax. Authors recommend micro-level targeting of resources to accelerate elimination.

articles.headline: Dengue–malaria coinfection common among febrile patients in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Molecular detection and epidemiological characterization of dengue and malaria coinfection in the Guiriko region, Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Belem LRW, Yao RK, Amara MF, Taita AVW, Kaboré BP, Kouadio KWU, Ibemgbo SA, Sangaré I, Gomgnimbou MK
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 408 patients with suspected dengue or malaria at two medical centres (Do, Bobo-Dioulasso, and Houndé) between June and September 2023, using dengue rapid tests and RT-PCR, malaria microscopy and PCR, and CRP measurement. Coinfection of recent dengue (NS1 and/or IgM positive) with malaria was 17.64% (72/408), and acute DENV (RT-PCR positive) plus malaria was 7.10% (29/408). All RT-PCR-positive dengue cases were PCR-positive for Plasmodium (29/29), while 68.96% (20/29) were positive by microscopy. Elevated axillary temperature and high CRP (mean 83.79 mg/l) were associated with acute coinfection; multiplex RT-PCR improved detection.

articles.headline: Avian Plasmodium and Haemoproteus detected in Colombian dry-forest birds
articles.title_original: Molecular detection of avian hemoparasites (Plasmodium and Haemoproteus) in resident and migratory birds from an urban fragment of tropical dry forest in northern Colombia.
articles.authors: Rodríguez MM, Flores FS, Castro LR
articles.details: Molecular survey of avian haemosporidians (Plasmodium and Haemoproteus) in birds from an urban tropical dry forest fragment in northern Colombia, sampling 217 individuals across 27 resident and 7 boreal migratory species, with PCR targeting cytochrome b and 18S rRNA genes and maximum likelihood/Bayesian phylogenetics. Infections were detected in eleven individuals representing six species, including resident and migratory birds, for an overall prevalence of 5.1%. Several host-parasite associations were new records for Colombia. Authors emphasize the value of dry-forest fragments for revealing novel interactions and using multiple molecular markers.

articles.headline: Peripheral blood erythrophagocytosis in P. falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Peripheral Blood Erythrophagocytosis in Plasmodium falciparum Malaria.
articles.authors: Almeida T, Ferreira CA
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Review: why malaria vaccines work poorly in young infants
articles.title_original: Malaria immunity in the infant: progress and pitfalls.
articles.authors: Feeney ME
articles.details: This review examines malaria immunity in fetuses and infants and the performance of current preventive tools. It notes that the RTS,S/AS01E and R21/Matrix-M vaccines, plus monoclonal antibodies targeting the circumsporozoite protein (CSP), have shown success, but elicit suboptimal immunity in young infants and are recommended only from five months of age. The authors discuss how the infant immune system differs fundamentally from older children and adults, and how in-utero exposure to plasmodial antigens and transplacentally acquired maternal antibodies may modulate responses to P. falciparum infection and vaccination. No quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: Perspective on rethinking radical cure for Plasmodium vivax
articles.title_original: Reimagining radical cure for Plasmodium vivax.
articles.authors: Aung PL, Cui L
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Shorter primaquine and single-dose tafenoquine for vivax relapse prevention
articles.title_original: Effectiveness and safety of 7-day high-dose primaquine and single-dose tafenoquine versus 14-day low-dose primaquine in patients with Plasmodium vivax malaria (EFFORT): a multicentre, open-label, randomised, controlled, superiority trial.
articles.authors: Degaga TS, Pasaribu AP, Tripura R, Ghanchi N, Rajasekhar M, Adhikari B, Ley B, Bamboro SA, Jabbar FA, Hasibuan N, Tego TT, Zehra S, Qurashi B, Mnjala H, Lee G, Li P, Kazi AM, Safitri W, Yulita Y, Siagian DSP, Syahputra D, Hadi H, Muhammad T, Ibrahim A, Syed N, Dost K, Puspitrasari AM, Kariodimedjo PP, Rai A, Rumaseb A, Sutanto E, Ean M, Khan A, Sokha M, Commons RJ, Weston S, Noviyanti R, Peto T, Callery JJ, Ali U, Mehmood T, Dondorp A, Devine A, Setiawan E, Mwaura M, Cassidy-Seyoum S, Temesgen R, Abate DT, Erjabo EB, Gessa GG, Kiros FG, Usmani MI, Raza A, Woyessa A, Hailu A, Simpson JA, Karahalios A, Beg MA, von Seidlein L, Dysoley L, Auburn S, Price RN, Thriemer K
articles.details: In a multicentre, open-label, randomised superiority trial across Ethiopia, Pakistan, Indonesia, and Cambodia, 960 adults with uncomplicated P. vivax and G6PD activity ≥70% were assigned 1:1:1 to unsupervised 7-day high-dose primaquine (7 mg/kg), single-dose tafenoquine (300 mg), or 14-day low-dose primaquine (3.5 mg/kg), alongside blood schizonticidal drugs. Cumulative 6-month P. vivax recurrence was 13.0% with 7-day high-dose primaquine versus 18.5% with 14-day low-dose primaquine (HR 0.66, 97.55% CI 0.40-1.09, p=0.063), and 12.6% with tafenoquine (HR 0.64, 0.39-1.05, p=0.041 vs 14-day). Both new regimens were well tolerated.

articles.headline: WBC count channel discrepancy as a possible clue to malaria
articles.title_original: Discrepancy Between White Blood Cell Count Channels as a Clue to Malaria Infection.
articles.authors: Li C, Wang YH, Meng SI, Chen SC, Lee JA
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Lab conditions may mislead gene targets for malaria mosquito control
articles.title_original: Beyond the static lab: environmental variability in genetically modified mosquito target gene identification for malaria control.
articles.authors: Dael L, Simões ML
articles.details: This perspective argues that research identifying target genes for genetically modified mosquitoes (GMMs) to reduce malaria transmission is typically conducted under static insectary conditions of constant temperature and humidity, which do not reflect natural environmental and climatic variability. The authors contend this 'lab-to-field' gap is a significant knowledge gap, since environmental factors influence Anopheles and Plasmodium biology, mosquito innate immunity, and vector competence, potentially compromising the intended function of genetic modifications. They call for incorporating realistic environmental variability into upstream GMM development amid climate change. No quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: Historical review questions malaria therapy's effectiveness for neurosyphilis
articles.title_original: Malaria therapy for neurosyphilis at Mont Park Hospital for the insane in Australia, 1927-1928.
articles.authors: Clayton A
articles.details: A historical analysis of malaria therapy for general paralysis of the insane (neurosyphilis) finds that the treatment's apparent effectiveness was likely overestimated. Using published accounts and medical record archives, the author examines the therapy as conducted by psychiatrist Reginald Ellery at Victoria's Mont Park Hospital for the Insane in 1927-1928, identifying factors—diagnostic drift, adjunct interventions, and healthy-patient selection—that likely inflated results, and finding patient outcomes in the records notably less positive than Ellery's published reports. The findings do not support claims that malaria was an effective treatment for neurosyphilis.

articles.headline: P. vivax CSP gene shows high diversity during Thailand malaria resurgence
articles.title_original: Genetic signatures of Plasmodium vivax Circumsporozoite Surface Protein during malaria resurgence in Thailand.
articles.authors: Tapaopong P, Holzschuh A, da Silva G, Chintanawiwat P, Bantuchai S, Rungsarityotin W, Cui L, Koepfli C, Sattabongkot J, Nguitragool W
articles.details: Amplicon sequencing of 69 P. vivax isolates from the 2022-2023 malaria resurgence in northwestern Thailand characterized diversity of the circumsporozoite surface protein gene (pvcsp). Both canonical variants appeared: VK210 (n=66) and VK247 (n=3). VK210 showed high central-repeat polymorphism with 21 haplotypes (H1-H21) of 13-20 repeat motifs; haplotype H2 was most common (half of VK210 sequences) and, in membrane feeding assays with Anopheles dirus, appeared to produce more salivary-gland sporozoites per oocyst than other haplotypes, suggesting repeat-region variation modulates vector competence. The authors highlight three globally prevalent motifs (GDRADGQPA, GDRAAGQPA, ANGAGNQPG) as targets for future PvCSP vaccines.

articles.headline: Red blood cell gene variants linked to higher malaria risk in Thailand
articles.title_original: Red blood cell membrane protein polymorphisms related to risk of malaria infection in the population living in malaria-endemic areas of Thailand: An analytic cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Muhamad P, Panrit L, Srikhamdokkhae O, Laohapensaeng K, Tarasuk M, Sa-I S, Na-Bangchang K
articles.details: This study examined red blood cell protein polymorphisms (MN antigen, Miltenberger, Duffy antigen and promoter, and complement receptor-1 at position 981) using PCR-based techniques among populations on the Thai-Myanmar and Thai-Malaysian borders. Of 616 blood samples, 270 were from malaria-infected and 346 from non-infected individuals. The NN antigen was more frequent in infected versus non-infected samples (22.2% vs 11.1%; OR 3.7, 2.0-6.7, p<0.0001), as were FYA/FYA or FYA/FYB genotypes with wild-type Duffy promoter (76.1% vs 46.9%; OR 4.7, 3.0-7.3, p<0.0001). The authors conclude most of the population is at increased malaria infection risk.

articles.headline: Computational screen finds natural compounds as PfDHFR-TS antimalarial candidates
articles.title_original: Discovery of novel Plasmodium falciparum PfDHFR-TS inhibitors from ConMedNP natural compounds: a multi-computational approach.
articles.authors: Haiwang Djefoulna VH, Atiya Atiya M, Fifen JJ, Conradie J
articles.details: Using the ConMedNP database of Central and West African medicinal-plant natural compounds, this in silico study targeted P. falciparum dihydrofolate reductase-thymidylate synthase (PfDHFR-TS). Molecular docking of 2,754 compounds gave a mean binding affinity of -8.80 kcal/mol (SD 1.4; median -8.9), with 75% outperforming artemether's -8.0 kcal/mol reference. A Random Forest QSAR model reached test R²=0.8321 (RMSE 0.5294). Top performers were RDC0118 (-13.5), RDC0119 (-13.4), and CA0001 (-13.0 kcal/mol). ADMET profiling flagged RDC0118 and RDC0119 for potential mutagenicity/hepatotoxicity, while CA0001 and artemether were safer; MD and DFT analyses positioned CA0001 as a promising PfDHFR-TS inhibitor warranting experimental validation.

articles.headline: Review: adding nutrient supplements to malaria prevention in pregnancy inconclusive
articles.title_original: Effects of Combined Lipid-Based Nutrient Supplements and Malaria Preventive Treatment during Pregnancy on Malaria, Maternal Nutrition, and Birth Outcomes: A Scoping Review.
articles.authors: Parikh P, Zeilani M, Pieret A, Abbeddou S
articles.details: This PRISMA-ScR scoping review searched MEDLINE, EMBASE, Scopus, CENTRAL, and gray literature (January 2024, updated July 2024) to summarize effects of lipid-based nutrient supplements (LNS) combined with intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy (IPTp) on malaria incidence, maternal nutrition, and birth outcomes. It identified 17 studies focused on 5 main trials (2 SQ-LNS, 2 balanced energy-protein, 1 LQ-LNS). LNS supplementation did not significantly affect malaria incidence, anemia, or nutritional status, though some studies reported improved birth outcomes, particularly in subgroups with baseline undernutrition. The authors conclude evidence for effects on malaria incidence or maternal anemia remains inconclusive and call for further research.

articles.headline: Malaria Scoring System validated in non-endemic emergency department
articles.title_original: External validation of the Malaria Scoring System in a non-endemic emergency department.
articles.authors: Paglietta G, Giamello JD, D'Agnano S, Melchio R, Carralero GL, Gianelli A, Giubbini G, Manassero S, Artana N, Barutta L, Basile E, Fenoglio L, Lupia E, Lauria G
articles.details: This retrospective single-center study at an emergency department in Cuneo, Italy externally validated the Malaria Scoring System (MSS), which combines recent travel to Sub-Saharan Africa, thrombocytopenia, anemia, lymphopenia, neutropenia, and high fever (score ≥3 flags higher risk). Among 365 patients tested for malaria, 114 (31.2%) were diagnosed. Malaria cases had a higher median MSS (4 [3;5] vs 1 [1;2], p<0.001) and more thrombocytopenia (78.1% vs 8.4%), lymphopenia (71.9% vs 32.7%), and anemia (53.5% vs 35.1%). The MSS showed excellent discrimination (AUC 0.902, 95% CI 0.869-0.935) and good calibration (Hosmer-Lemeshow p=0.195), supporting clinical utility.

articles.headline: Imported malaria in NE Italy: procalcitonin flags severe cases
articles.details: Retrospective study (2016-2025) of 124 symptomatic imported malaria cases in Friuli Occidentale, Italy (88.71% P. falciparum), of whom 90.32% were visiting friends and relatives, mostly from West Africa, and only 12% had adequate prophylaxis. Severe malaria occurred in 19.35% (24/124), including two cerebral malaria cases, with no fatalities; severity peaked at 60% (6/10) in 2025. On multivariable analysis, parasitaemia ≥2% (aOR 8.44; 95% CI 2.41-29.58; p=0.001) and procalcitonin (aOR 1.23; 95% CI 1.06-1.42; p=0.007) were independent severity predictors. PCT outperformed CRP (AUC 0.84 vs 0.73), with a 95.9% negative predictive value at a 5.10 ng/mL cut-off. Authors conclude PCT plus parasitaemia should guide triage and parenteral therapy.
articles.title_original: Epidemiology of imported malaria in an area of North-Eastern Italy (2016-2025): clinical focus on cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Corich L, Bortolin MT, Tedeschi R, Casarotto M, Pancino A, Molaro A, Anzanello V, Basaglia G, Corich MA

articles.headline: Ferroptosis in splenic macrophages worsens malaria in mice
articles.title_original: HIF-1α/HMOX1-Mediated Ferroptosis in Macrophages Exacerbates Malaria Progression.
articles.authors: Liang G, Li J, Xie X, Yuan H, Xiao W, Chen M, Lu K, Xu L, Pan L, Wei H, Li L, Zhao S, Xie H, Pan X, Huang J
articles.details: This mechanistic study found that Plasmodium infection triggers ferroptosis in splenic macrophages, marked by HMOX1 upregulation alongside HIF-1α downregulation, leading to Fe2+ overload. The ferroptosis inhibitor Fer-1 blocked ferroptosis in infected-red-blood-cell-exposed macrophages in vitro and reversed elevated ferroptosis biomarkers in P. yoelii-infected mice. Conditional GPX4 knockout mice (GPX4flox/flox Lyz2Cre+) showed worsened health and more severe parasitemia than controls, and the same ferroptosis signature appeared in monocytes from malaria patients' PBMCs. The authors conclude that targeting the HIF-1α/HMOX1 axis may alleviate malaria progression as a host-directed intervention.

articles.headline: Deleting parasite MSP8 protects mice via splenic CCL19-CCR7 signaling
articles.title_original: Deletion of Plasmodium yoelii Merozoite Surface Protein 8 Protects Mice From Lethal Blood-Stage Malaria and Is Associated With Enhanced Splenic CCL19-CCR7 Signaling.
articles.authors: Sun Y, Fan Z, Ge J, Cui W, Zuo S, Tan L, Han S, Yan Y, Wang B, Cao J, Lv Z, Zhu G, Cheng Y
articles.details: Using MSP8 immunization, CRISPR/Cas9 deletion of Plasmodium yoelii msp8, and chemokine-receptor perturbation in the lethal P. yoelii 17XL murine model, researchers examined MSP8's role in immunity. Antisera against P. falciparum MSP8 partially inhibited merozoite invasion of human erythrocytes in vitro, and PyMSP8 immunization gave partial protection against lethal challenge. The ΔMSP8 knockout line produced reduced peak parasitemia, attenuated splenomegaly, and a self-resolving course, with upregulated splenic CCL19, preserved CCR7, and increased IFN-γ. AAV-shRNA knockdown of CCR7 partially abrogated this survival advantage and raised mortality. The authors propose targeting MSP8 or augmenting CCL19-CCR7 signaling as malaria vaccine and host-directed strategies.

articles.headline: COVID-19 disrupted then rebounded child malaria reporting in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on reported malaria incidence in children under five years of age in Cameroon: an interrupted time series analysis with regional heterogeneity (2017-2022).
articles.authors: Mekontchou LM, Nguefack-Tsague G, Sonegueng AVK, Deffo PY, Sepde E, Samadoulougou S
articles.details: This interrupted time series study used monthly DHIS2 surveillance data (2017-2022) to estimate the COVID-19 pandemic's impact on reported malaria incidence in children under five in Cameroon, modeled with negative binomial regression adjusting for LLIN and SMC activity, pediatric consultation rate, and lagged rainfall. In March 2020, reported incidence fell immediately by 36.9% (IRR=0.631; 95% CI 0.539-0.738; p<0.001), followed by a +3.1% per-month slope change (IRR=1.031); the January 2022 interruption showed no significant change. Counterfactual analysis estimated a deficit of 69,708 notifications in 2020-2021 and an excess of 74,831 in 2022, with severe initial declines in Centre (IRR=0.467) and East (IRR=0.544) regions. Authors urge maintaining service continuity and surveillance during crises.

articles.headline: Tropical ocean patterns drive malaria variability in Malawi via soil moisture
articles.title_original: Tropical oceans drive variability in soil moisture and malaria in Malawi.
articles.authors: Elling MT, Karnauskas KB, Kowalcyk M, Mategula D, Chirombo J, Livneh B, McCann RS, Buchwald AG
articles.details: This study combined malaria incidence with observational, reanalysis, and modeled climate data to identify mechanistic links between climate and malaria variability in Malawi. Two ocean-based climate patterns drove interannual variability: a warm tropical Atlantic produced wet conditions and increased malaria cases, while a warm Indian Ocean drove hot, dry conditions and reduced cases. Soil moisture was the crucial link between these remote drivers and local disease dynamics. The authors note future climate change is expected to reduce Malawi's soil moisture by 2100 (magnitude uncertain), potentially reshaping transmission, and argue these findings support building physically grounded early warning systems.

articles.headline: Two RNA-binding proteins keep P. vivax hypnozoites dormant in liver
articles.title_original: Two Plasmodium vivax hypnozoite-expressed RNA-binding proteins inhibit liver stage replication.
articles.authors: Vo KC, van Biljon R, Zanghi G, Zavala B, Betz W, Jennison C, Vaughan AM, Painter HJ, Kappe SHI
articles.details: Differential transcriptome analysis of Plasmodium vivax identified two hypnozoite-expressed transcripts encoding putative RNA-binding proteins. The authors expressed each protein individually in liver stages of the rodent parasite Plasmodium yoelii, which does not naturally form hypnozoites. Each protein blocked liver-stage schizogony, leaving parasites small and uninucleate. A screen showed the proteins' RNA-binding domains bind distinct, highly specific RNA motifs, suggesting they suppress replication by binding these RNAs. The authors propose this as a molecular mechanism of hypnozoite quiescence and named the proteins IESI-1 and IESI-2.

articles.headline: High-resolution map of protein locations in P. falciparum schizont
articles.title_original: The spatial proteome of the Plasmodium falciparum schizont illuminates the composition and evolutionary trajectories of its organelles.
articles.authors: Chisholm SA, Flores V, Kemp A, Breckels LM, Koreny L, Dos Santos Pacheco N, Barylyuk K, Kuroshchenkova A, Lilley KS, Rayner JC, Waller RF
articles.details: Using the spatial proteomics technique hyperLOPIT, the authors generated subcellular localisation maps for the invasive schizont stage of Plasmodium falciparum. They obtained data for 3000 proteins expressed in late schizonts and resolved 24 distinct cellular niches. Supervised machine learning classified 1646 proteins into these compartments, including exported sites within the host erythrocyte. Through comparative genomic analyses, the data resolve spatial patterns of cell evolution that have shaped the development of Plasmodium species, helping to assign function to the large fraction of the parasite proteome with previously unknown location.

articles.headline: Simulation guides age-based sampling for malaria seroconversion estimates
articles.title_original: Optimizing age-structured sampling for estimating the seroconversion rate in malaria seroepidemiology: a simulation study.
articles.authors: Ko YK, Li S, Britton T, Kagaya W, Kaneko A
articles.details: This Monte Carlo simulation study assessed how age-based sampling affects precision of the seroconversion rate (SCR) in malaria seroepidemiology, using reverse catalytic models and serological responses to P. falciparum MSP1. They varied SCR, seroreversion rate (SRR), and whether SRR was known. In stable low-transmission settings, sampling older individuals improved precision, while younger groups were more informative in high-transmission settings when SRR was known; when SRR was unknown, balanced age sampling performed best. Applying the framework to published Sri Lanka data, the adult-skewed original design reduced precision and power to detect transmission change.

articles.headline: Single-cell RNA-seq reveals malaria parasite drug responses and transmission shift
articles.title_original: Single-cell analysis of Plasmodium falciparum transcripts after drug perturbation identifies feedback regulation as well as increased transmission potential.
articles.authors: Godinez-Macias KP, Calla J, Jepsen K, Winzeler EA
articles.details: The authors applied single-cell RNA sequencing to hundreds of thousands of asynchronous P. falciparum-infected red blood cells treated with three antimalarial chemotypes: artemisinin, atovaquone, and ganaplacide. Each treatment produced distinct cell populations with different transcriptional profiles. Compared with synchronized-cell reference patterns, artemisinin treatment enriched cells expressing gametocyte-associated genes, while atovaquone and ganaplacide caused fewer lifecycle perturbations; bulk analysis showed enrichment of pyrimidine biosynthesis transcripts for atovaquone. The authors conclude scRNA-seq can profile drug responses across lifecycle stages and help classify drug mechanisms of action.

articles.headline: In-silico screen finds Blighia sapida compounds targeting PfDHODH
articles.title_original: In-silico study of the bioactive compounds of Blighia sapida koenig revealed a novel Plasmodium falciparum dihydroorotate dehydrogenase inhibitor for malaria.
articles.authors: Akinyemi DO, Odunitan TT, Ojeniyi FD, Akinyemi AO, Rahman N, Zafar H, Ehigie LO, Afolabi OK, Ojurongbe O
articles.details: This in silico study evaluated the antimalarial potential of Blighia sapida ethanolic leaf extract against the target protein P. falciparum dihydroorotate dehydrogenase (PfDHODH). GC-MS identified 21 compounds, the most abundant being D-Fructose 3-O-methyl (21.02%), phytol (20.77%), and hexadecanoic acid ethyl ester (13.11%). Molecular docking showed three compounds with strong binding affinities (-8.1 to -10.0 kcal/mol) versus the positive control (-8.9 kcal/mol). In molecular dynamics, 2-methyl-6-(4-methylenecyclohex-2-en-1-yl)hept-2-en-4-one was most stable (RMSD 0.12), followed by octadecanoic acid ethyl ester (RMSD 0.13). The authors highlight B. sapida's promising antimalarial constituents.

articles.headline: New geospatial model maps malaria vulnerability across Nigeria
articles.title_original: Topological Fuzzy Analytic Hierarchy Process (ToF-AHP) for geospatial assessment of malaria vulnerability in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Atijosan A, Atijosan A
articles.details: This methodological study developed a Topological Fuzzy Analytic Hierarchy Process (ToF-AHP) to build a composite malaria vulnerability index for Nigeria from five indicators: ITN usage, maternal education, socioeconomic status, antenatal care non-attendance, and distance to health facilities. Results showed pronounced north-south disparities, with the Northeast and Northwest exhibiting high to very high vulnerability and the south generally low to moderate. Maternal and gender-linked socioeconomic factors accounted for 34.63% of overall vulnerability, and approximately 62.52% of very high malaria incidence zones coincided with high and very high vulnerability areas. ToF-AHP produced more stable priority weights than conventional fuzzy AHP.

articles.headline: New 7-chloroquinoline hybrids block β-hematin and clear murine malaria
articles.details: Researchers synthesized a series of 7-chloroquinoline hybrids (derived from albendazole-, thioacetazone-, and primaquine-based drugs) incorporating a ureido group designed to inhibit β-hematin formation (βHF). In vitro screening flagged compounds 13, 14, 16-18, and 27-29 as βHF inhibitors; compound 16 was most active at 90.68% ± 0.043% inhibition versus 92.55% ± 0.019% for chloroquine. In vivo against the CQ-susceptible Plasmodium berghei ANKA murine strain, compound 16 reduced parasitemia to 1.2% ± 0.12% and extended survival to 27.33 ± 0.15 days (chloroquine: 0.88% parasitemia, 29.6 days). At 10 mM it caused 17.23 ± 0.13 hemolysis versus 29.65% for CQ, and the authors conclude compound 16 is a promising antimalarial candidate.
