articles.title_original: Design and synthesis of 7-chloroquinoline-derivative-bearing urea, carbamate, or thiourea moieties and their antimalarial activity against Plasmodium berghei via the inhibition of β-hematin formation.
articles.authors: Ramírez H, Mijoba AS, Espinosa S, Barazarte AR, Acosta ME, Charris JE, Fernandez-Moreira E

articles.headline: MAHRP2 protein needed for tethers and cytoadherence in infected red cells
articles.details: This molecular study found that deleting the Membrane Associated Histidine Rich Protein 2 (MAHRP2) in Plasmodium falciparum-infected red blood cells caused a loss of the tube-like tether structures that connect Maurer's clefts to the RBC membrane. MAHRP2 deletion also reduced immobilization of Maurer's clefts at the RBC membrane and increased cleft size. Deletion further impacted PTP2-related vesicle formation and reduced the parasite's ability to cytoadhere to the endothelial ligand CD36. Electron tomography of 3D7-infected RBCs had revealed these tethers, at which MAHRP2 is the only protein known to localize, and their formation and function were previously unclear.
articles.title_original: MAHRP2 is required for tether formation and cytoadherence in Plasmodium falciparum infected red blood cells.
articles.authors: Shibu MA, Shami GJ, Hanssen E, Carmo OMS, Vaibhav V, Yousef J, Dagley LF, McCarthy J, Dixon MWA

articles.headline: Zinc-finger protein PfMD3 drives male gamete fertility in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: The CCCH-type zinc-finger Pf MD3 promotes translation for malaria parasite transmission.
articles.authors: van Biljon R, Bhatnagar S, Kumar R, Gliozzi M, Xu G, Tripathi A, Ashrafi A, Swearingen K, Sinnis P, Llinás M, Painter HJ
articles.details: This molecular study characterized the CCCH-type zinc-finger protein Plasmodium falciparum Male Development 3 (PfMD3) in gamete formation. Genetic disruption of PfMD3 caused a male fertility defect that decreased infectivity to mosquitoes. PfMD3 bound mRNA transcripts linked to male gamete development through a specific RNA sequence, and proteins encoded by these transcripts decreased significantly in PfMD3 knockout parasites, indicating an effect on translation. PfMD3 also interacts with proteins involved in mRNA processing and nuclear export. The authors conclude PfMD3 is an RNA-binding protein that impacts lineage-specific translation and male gamete fertility.

articles.headline: Climate modeling maps suitable habitat for Argentine malaria vector
articles.title_original: MODELING THE POTENTIAL CLIMATIC SUITABILITY OF ANOPHELES PSEUDOPUNCTIPENNIS, THE HISTORICAL VECTOR OF MALARIA IN ARGENTINA.
articles.authors: Duque PL, León R, Coello Peralta RD, Villarroel Martínez EI, Liria-Salazar J, Dantur Juri MJ
articles.details: Using ecological niche models (MaxEnt) with occurrence records and WorldClim 2.1 bioclimatic variables, researchers mapped climatically suitable areas for Anopheles pseudopunctipennis, a historical malaria vector, in Argentina. After filtering and 5 km spatial thinning, 312 records from 287 localities were retained. The best-supported model used LQHP features with regularization multiplier 1.0, showing a 10% omission rate of 0.11, validation AUC of 0.83, and lowest AICc. Suitable conditions concentrated in northwestern Argentina (Jujuy, Salta, Tucumán, Catamarca), with additional areas elsewhere. The findings support vector surveillance in a country certified malaria-free in 2019 but at risk of reintroduction.

articles.headline: Human mobility linked to malaria incidence in low-endemic Indonesia
articles.title_original: Mobility and malaria incidence in Indonesia's low-endemic areas during the COVID-19 period.
articles.authors: Arisanti RR, Maula AW, Ahmad RA, Utarini A
articles.details: This study examined the association between population mobility and malaria incidence in four low-endemic Indonesian districts during the COVID-19 period, using Facebook mobility data, COVID-19 reports, and district malaria records analyzed with multivariable Poisson regression. Among 480,575 mobility events, malaria examination rates dropped early in the pandemic, and incidence outside Java was 64% lower than in Java. In pooled analysis, mobility, malaria screening, and COVID-19 positivity were each associated with malaria incidence (IRR 1.25, 95% CI 1.21-1.30; IRR 2.33, 95% CI 2.22-2.45; IRR 1.12, 95% CI 1.08-1.17). The authors conclude the relationship is complex and context-dependent and these associations are not causal.

articles.headline: Cross-border control project cut malaria transmission in South Korea
articles.title_original: Evaluating the impact of South-North Joint Malaria Control Project on spatiotemporal transmission dynamics in South Korea using dynamic mode decomposition.
articles.authors: Seo H, Kim BN, Abdulali A, Lee S
articles.details: Researchers applied a compatible-window dynamic mode decomposition (CwDMD) framework to malaria incidence data from 2001 to 2024 across 17 provinces and 37 subregions to evaluate control programs near the demilitarized zone. The South-North Joint Malaria Control Project (2008-2011) achieved a 63% reduction in national incidence between 2007 and 2011 and disrupted synchronization among border and metropolitan regions. The 5-Year Re-Elimination Plan (2019-2023) was associated with an approximately 49% decrease from 2018 to a 2021 low, but incidence then rose about 30% by 2023, with partial re-emergence of regional synchrony. The authors conclude sustained control requires weakening spatiotemporal coupling among high-risk regions.

articles.headline: Extending seasonal malaria chemoprevention to older children cuts cases in Niger
articles.title_original: Extension of seasonal malaria chemoprevention to older children: a pragmatic comparative study in two health districts in the Zinder region, Niger.
articles.authors: Djedanem M, Ibrahim A, Nazibou MY, Noura MS, Yacoudima EYM, Zakari A, Mbega HO, Mamane Z, Issaka M, Adehossi E, Testa J, Jambou R
articles.details: Retrospective comparative observational study using routine DHIS2 data from two health districts in the Zinder region, Niger (Damagaram Takaya and Matameye) over a 36-month period (2021–2023), comparing one district that extended SMC (sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine plus amodiaquine) to children aged 5–10 years with one that restricted it to under-5s. Children aged ≥5 had higher uncomplicated malaria incidence than younger children, reaching 33.34 per 1,000 versus 12.45 per 1,000 in under-5s in Damagaram Takaya in 2021. Following the SMC expansion, uncomplicated and severe cases declined significantly in 2023 in the intervention district but not the control district, though the transmission peak was not delayed. The authors conclude that extending SMC to older children had a positive impact and is an effective, low-cost prevention strategy.

articles.headline: Oxford Nanopore whole-genome sequencing method developed for P. falciparum surveillance
articles.title_original: Development of an Oxford nanopore sequencing technology-based whole genome sequencing method for Plasmodium falciparum to support malaria molecular surveillance.
articles.authors: Bakari C, Holzschuh A, Seth MD, Madebe RA, Pereus D, Mandara CI, Schneeberger P, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Ishengoma DS, Nsanzabana C
articles.details: Methods study optimizing an Oxford Nanopore Technologies (ONT) long-read whole-genome sequencing protocol for Plasmodium falciparum from whole blood and dried blood spots (DBS), using laboratory strains 3D7, HB3 and Dd2 in mock infections plus clinical samples from a therapeutic efficacy study. DNA was extracted with Qiagen or Tween-Chelex 100 and enriched via McrBC/MspJI digestion, NEBNext microbiome enrichment (NMDEK) and selective whole genome amplification. Tween-Chelex gave higher yield (2–4 Ct difference) while Qiagen gave longer reads (median 2791 vs 2252 bp); NMDEK raised the human β-tubulin Ct by ~10 cycles, and combined enrichment yielded >75% parasite DNA at 100 p/µl with >90% genome coverage, median read lengths >2 kb, >30× depth and 99.8% accuracy. The protocol showed high concordance with Sanger sequencing and detected additional mutations and pfmdr1 and pfgch1 copy number variations, supporting its use in malaria molecular surveillance.

articles.headline: Decision-to-seek-care delays drive late malaria treatment for Malawi children
articles.title_original: Three delays of malaria care-seeking in Malawi: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Olapeju B, Bride M, Volkmann T, Cash S, Dembo E, Kayange M, Gumbo A, Mafuleka T, Boyle J, Mang'ando A, Mbeye NM, Mukwakwa C
articles.details: Cross-sectional study applying the Three Delays Model to identify barriers to prompt malaria care-seeking, using nationally representative data from 913 female caregivers who reported a child with fever in the past two weeks. Prompt care-seeking (same or next day from a qualified provider) was primarily associated with delay 1 (deciding to seek care; adjusted OR 0.58, 95% CI 0.43–0.78) and psychosocial factors (aOR 0.59, 95% CI 0.44–0.79). Significant factors included incorrect knowledge of symptoms/cause/diagnosis (aOR 0.71, 95% CI 0.53–0.97), negative attitudes toward care-seeking (aOR 0.58, 95% CI 0.41–0.82), incorrect knowledge of when and where to seek care (aOR 0.19, 95% CI 0.07–0.50), and far distance from a facility (aOR 0.67, 95% CI 0.49–0.93). The authors conclude that despite free services, the initial decision-making phase remains a key bottleneck warranting targeted social and behavior change interventions.

articles.headline: Red blood cell adhesion gene variants affect sickle cell severity and malaria risk
articles.title_original: Genetic variants in red blood cell adhesion-related genes influence the severity of sickle cell anemia in a malaria-endemic region : Short title: Genetic variants in red blood cell adhesion-related genes in sickle cell anemia.
articles.authors: Matos I, Santos B, Gonçalves E, Lopes P, Brito M, Arez AP, Faustino P
articles.details: Study of 65 pediatric sickle cell anemia (SCA) patients living in Luanda or Caxito, Angola, examining whether variants in red blood cell adhesion genes CD36 and ICAM-1 influence SCA manifestations alongside malaria, with 15 polymorphic sites genotyped by PCR, Sanger sequencing and capillary electrophoresis and Plasmodium detected by PCR on blood spots. The CD36 variant rs3211891_C was identified for the first time as a potential modulator of anemia severity, while CD36 rs3211938_G and ICAM-1 rs5491_T and rs5496_A significantly affected the hematological phenotype severity. Patients carrying the ICAM-1 rs5494_T variant had a 5.63-fold increased risk of malaria infection versus wild-type. The authors conclude these findings improve understanding of genetic modifiers of RBC adhesion and their influence on pediatric SCA severity amid frequent concomitant malaria.

articles.headline: PfGBP-130 identified as LFA-1 ligand triggering NK cell killing of infected cells
articles.title_original: LFA-1 interaction with GBP-130 on Plasmodium falciparum-infected red blood cells mediates NK cell activation and parasite control.
articles.authors: Mukhtar O, Dutt R, Panda A, Kumari P, Singh SS, Paul G, Prakash N, Abbas M, Islam MM, Arora P, Tammour A, Mohmmed A, Kumar D, Malhotra P
articles.details: Laboratory study identifying glycophorin binding protein-130 (PfGBP-130) as the surface-expressed ligand on Plasmodium falciparum-infected red blood cells (iRBCs) that binds the I-domain of the integrin LFA-1, mediating natural killer (NK) cell recognition. Using an LFA-1 αI-Fc fusion protein, the authors showed stage-specific binding to iRBCs, and LC-MS/MS of immunoprecipitates identified PfGBP-130 as a high-confidence interactor; recombinant PfGBP-130 bound NK and THP-1 cells in an LFA-1-dependent manner. Co-culture assays showed PfGBP-130 promotes NK cell activation, degranulation and contact-dependent killing of iRBCs, while neutralizing antibodies against PfGBP-130 significantly impaired these responses. The authors conclude PfGBP-130 is the LFA-1 ligand on iRBCs, offering insight into NK cell-mediated immunity and a potential target for host-directed interventions.

articles.headline: Placental malaria common in first-time mothers, linked to poor birth outcomes
articles.title_original: High burden of histology-confirmed placental malaria and associated adverse birth outcomes among primigravidae in Northwestern Tanzania: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Buhaga G, Mujuni F, Matiku S, Kiritta R, Matovelo D, Ndaboine E, Mazigo HD
articles.details: A hospital-based cross-sectional study enrolled 422 primigravidae women delivering at Sengerema Designated District Hospital, Mwanza, Tanzania, screening maternal, cord, and placental blood by RDT, microscopy, and placental histology. Histology-confirmed placental malaria prevalence was 39.8% (95% CI 35.1–44.5), versus 23.7% by microscopy; maternal peripheral malaria was 19.7% by microscopy and 6.9% by mRDT, and congenital malaria 6.9%. Fewer than four ANC visits (AOR 1.75, p=0.04) and moderate anemia (AOR 1.84, p=0.04) predicted placental malaria, which was significantly associated with low birth weight (p=0.01), preterm birth (p=0.001), and small-for-gestational-age neonates (p=0.048).

articles.headline: RNA virus of P. vivax boosts transmission and shifts host immunity
articles.title_original: MaRNAV-1, an intracellular virus of Plasmodium vivax , is associated with increased parasite transmission and altered host immune response.
articles.authors: Seng D, Ko K, Orban A, Heng S, Feufack-Donfack LB, Grünebast J, Ya N, Dumetz F, Tebben K, Vignolini T, Dore G, Khim N, Salvador J, Ouaid Z, Lefèvre T, Cohuet A, Sommen C, Flamand C, Ruberto AA, Abebe A, Tsigie M, Tasew G, Tollera G, Malleret B, Shenkutie TT, Lo E, Anstey NM, Petrone ME, Cantaert T, Baumgarten S, Serre D, Popovici J
articles.details: This study shows MaRNAV-1 is a genuine intracellular RNA virus present in Plasmodium vivax across blood, sporozoite, and liver stages, with prevalence differing between Cambodian and Ethiopian parasites. Higher viral presence and load were positively associated with transmission potential, reflected in greater gametocyte abundance and higher oocyst prevalence and intensity in membrane feeding assays. Viral loads were higher in symptomatic than asymptomatic infections and linked to elevated body temperature, independent of parasitemia. MaRNAV-1 elicited antibodies and dendritic cell activation, a Th2-to-Th1 shift, more double-negative B cells, and higher circulating IFN-γ, CXCL10, IL-1RA, and IL-6.

articles.headline: Editorial: filling research gaps in malaria pathobiology
articles.details: This is an editorial on filling research gaps in malaria pathobiology. No abstract was provided, so no study details or results are available.
articles.title_original: Editorial: Filling the research gaps of malaria pathobiology.
articles.authors: Lee WC, Hailu T, Albrecht L

articles.headline: Fungal metabolites kill malaria mosquito larvae and adults
articles.title_original: Larvicidal and adulticidal activities of Purpureocillium lilacinum Secondary Metabolites against the Malaria Vector Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Chen D, Niu G, Lai L, Zerpa CV, Li J
articles.details: Researchers tested secondary metabolites of the entomopathogenic fungus Purpureocillium lilacinum against malaria vector mosquitoes. Fourth-instar larvae were more susceptible than second-instar larvae (about four-fold lower LC90), and extended fermentation lowered the crude extract LC90 from 129 µg/mL to 61 µg/mL. Purified pulixin and leucinostatin A had LC90 values of 68 µg/mL and 9 µg/mL against fourth-instar larvae, respectively, and leucinostatin A had an LD90 of about 1 µg per adult female mosquito. The authors conclude these metabolites are promising, environmentally friendly larvicides, with leucinostatin A offering a potential dual-action transmission-blocking strategy.

articles.headline: Parasite gene pfgdh shows promise as P. falciparum diagnostic target
articles.title_original: Comparative evaluation of Plasmodium falciparum glutamate dehydrogenase with known Plasmodium falciparum diagnostic targets using quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction.
articles.authors: Ahmad A, Nema S, Krishna S, Gupta H, Singh A, Verma AK, Singh MP, Anvikar AR, Bharti PK
articles.details: A retrospective qPCR analysis of 150 P. falciparum-positive and 50 malaria-negative stored DNA samples evaluated P. falciparum glutamate dehydrogenase (pfgdh) against established targets pfhrp2, pfhrp3, pfaldolase, and pfldh, using a threshold cycle ≤37. Sensitivities were 72% (pfhrp2), 71% (pfhrp3), 69% (pfgdh), 29% (pfaldolase), and 19% (pfldh), with specificities of 100%, 96%, 98%, 100%, and 100%, respectively; AUC values were 86%, 84%, 83%, 64%, and 60%. The authors conclude pfgdh has notable diagnostic potential and could help overcome challenges from pfhrp2 gene deletions.

articles.headline: Adding DHA-piperaquine to cotrimoxazole cuts maternal side effects in HIV+ pregnancy
articles.title_original: Maternal, Fetal, and Neonatal Outcomes of Additional Dihydroartemisinin-Piperaquine to Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole in Intermittent Preventive Treatment for Malaria in HIV-Positive Pregnancies: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
articles.authors: Yunivita V, Maharani AJ, Bernadus CA, Sinaga EIJ, Simanjuntak ER
articles.details: This PRISMA-guided systematic review and random-effects meta-analysis of three studies (1353 participants) assessed adding dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DHA-PPQ, 40/320 mg given three times) to trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (800/160 mg) for intermittent preventive treatment in HIV-positive pregnancies. Adding DHA-PPQ showed non-significant trends toward lower maternal parasitaemia (OR 0.83; 95% CI 0.52–1.33; p=0.44) and placental malaria (OR 0.66; 95% CI 0.40–1.08; p=0.10), and significantly reduced maternal adverse events (OR 0.78; 95% CI 0.67–0.91; p=0.003). No significant differences appeared in fetal adverse events (OR 1.05; p=0.69) or birth outcomes (OR 1.13; p=0.81).

articles.headline: First lab culture of Plasmodium coatneyi reveals barrier to human infection
articles.title_original: First continuous in vitro culture of Plasmodium coatneyi reveals insights into barriers to human infection.
articles.authors: Suwanarusk R, Chua ACY, Ezenwanne CS, Snounou G, Malleret B, Kosaisavee V, Lombardini ED, Imerbsin R, Sunyakumthorn P, Katakai Y, Sakaguchi M, Kyle DE, Kawai S, Annoura T, Rénia L, Bifani P, Kaneko O, Russell B
articles.details: Researchers report the first continuous in vitro culture of Plasmodium coatneyi, a model for severe malaria due to its P. falciparum-like sequestration, and assess its zoonotic potential with a dual-colour flow cytometry assay. Molecular evidence of human infection was found in 3.5% (3/85) of identified simian malarias in Malaysian archival samples. Robust culture was established in Macaca fascicularis erythrocytes for over 40 days, reaching 3% parasitaemia after cryopreservation recovery. P. coatneyi invaded human erythrocytes but asexual development arrested post-invasion, falling below detection by day 9; microscopy confirmed failure to complete the 48-h cycle, identifying a biological barrier to sustained zoonotic transmission.

articles.headline: Indonesia meeting weighs tafenoquine and high-dose primaquine for vivax radical cure
articles.title_original: Dissemination of EFFORT trial results in Indonesia: a stakeholder meeting report on tafenoquine and primaquine for radical cure of Plasmodium vivax.
articles.authors: Pasaribu AP, Jimanto V, Ainur F, Saragih NR, Hadi H, Siagian DSP, Sinaga BJ, Satyagraha AW, Laihad FJ, Diptyanusa A, Ubra RR, Tekege O, Darmayanti L, Herdiana H, Adella J, Mannion K, Curry E, Poespoprodjo JR, Kenangalem E, Burdam FH, Sutanto I, Nelwan EJ, Ganadhi GJ, Shrestha B, Mnjala H, Hawley WA, Prameswari HD, Theodora M, Silalahi FSM, Susanto H, Immanuella F, Puspadewi RT, Fajariyani SB, Samad I, Supriyanto D, Muhafilah N, Isa R, Price RN, Thriemer K
articles.details: This paper reports a national stakeholder meeting held in May 2025 to disseminate Indonesia-specific findings from the EFFORT trial, conducted in Cambodia, Ethiopia, Indonesia, and Pakistan, which evaluated high-dose primaquine (7 mg/kg over 7 days) and single-dose tafenoquine (300 mg) versus standard 14-day low-dose primaquine for radical cure of Plasmodium vivax. In Indonesia both regimens were co-administered with dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine. Discussions centred on evidence gaps for tafenoquine combined with ACTs, the feasibility of high-dose primaquine as a near-term option pending strengthened G6PD testing, and the need for subnational strategies. No new trial efficacy figures are reported.

articles.headline: Hospital wards in Abeokuta, Nigeria show nosocomial malaria transmission risk
articles.title_original: Nosocomial malaria transmission risk in hospital wards in Abeokuta Nigeria: entomological and parasitological evidence from secondary healthcare facilities.
articles.authors: Fawole JK, Lawal NF, Babalola AS, Ojo DA, Sam-Wobo SO, Idowu OA
articles.details: This cross-sectional study in two secondary hospitals in Abeokuta, Ogun State, Nigeria, examined P. falciparum prevalence among inpatients admitted for non-malaria conditions and caregivers staying at least three nights, plus indoor-resting mosquitoes. Among 134 participants (80.6% patients, 19.4% caregivers), prevalence was 74.6% (75.0% of patients, 73.1% of caregivers), mostly low-density, with prevalence not differing by sex, ward, or admission duration (p > 0.05); only 3.7% used nets. Of 714 mosquitoes, 80 were Anopheles females (all An. gambiae s.l.), 81.3% fed on human blood, 38.8% parous, and 2 (2.5%) tested positive for P. falciparum sporozoites. Authors conclude a plausible nosocomial transmission risk.

articles.headline: Meta-analysis: R21 shows higher efficacy than RTS,S in young African children
articles.title_original: Efficacy and safety of RTS, S and R21 malaria vaccines in children under five in Africa: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials.
articles.authors: Nurani KM, Kadernani N, Korir E, Estreed MA, Dhali A, Clinton R, Kadernani K, Kipkorir V, Omullo P, Masika M
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis pooled 11 randomized controlled trials enrolling 27,178 African children under five (RTS,S: 21,589; R21: 5,589). For RTS,S, pooled efficacy was 32% against first clinical malaria episode (HR 0.68, 95% CI 0.61-0.76), 29% against multiple episodes (IRR 0.71, 0.55-0.91), and 17-22% against severe malaria (RR 0.78-0.83). R21 showed 68% efficacy against first episodes (HR 0.32, 0.26-0.39) and 67% against multiple episodes (IRR 0.33, 0.27-0.41) in a single phase 3 trial. Pooled serious adverse event incidence was 11.2% for RTS,S and 3% for R21, with no vaccine-attributable deaths. Authors conclude both vaccines are protective with acceptable safety, R21 showing higher efficacy in initial trials.

articles.headline: High malaria awareness but prevention gaps in Jozini, South Africa
articles.title_original: Community and healthcare worker contributions to malaria prevention: a culturally tailored behaviour change approach in Jozini, South Africa.
articles.authors: Baloyi RE, Ngcobo S, Kruger T, de Jager C
articles.details: This cross-sectional mixed-method study in six communities of Jozini local municipality, KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa, during 2025 surveyed 384 community members (median age 36 years; 72% female) and interviewed 15 healthcare workers to inform a Social Behavioural Change Communication strategy. Malaria awareness was high (96%), with 92% correctly identifying mosquito bites as the main transmission route; 97% had high knowledge, 79% positive attitudes, and 64% good practices. Knowledge was significantly associated with age (χ2 = 9.61, p = 0.01) but not with gender, education, employment, or residence duration, and not with attitudes or practices (p > 0.05). Qualitative findings highlighted irregular training, staffing gaps, and shortages of diagnostic tools.

articles.headline: Cryo-EM reveals how candidate ACT-451840 inhibits malaria resistance protein PfMDR1
articles.title_original: Structural and mechanistic insights into the inhibition of Plasmodium falciparum MDR1.
articles.authors: Zhao Z, Li J, Wang X, Liu X, Wang N, Xu H, Quan C, Gao Y, Zhang J, Wang X, Guo L, Kato N, Deng D, Jiang X
articles.details: This structural and biochemical study determined the cryo-electron microscopy structure of P. falciparum Multidrug Resistance Protein 1 (PfMDR1) bound to the clinical phase I antimalarial candidate ACT-451840 at 3.42 Å resolution. ACT-451840 binds within the central cavity, locking PfMDR1 in an inward-open conformation and inhibiting its basal ATPase activity. Comparison with the mefloquine-bound state offers a molecular explanation for how ACT-451840 resistance mutations can sensitize the parasite to mefloquine, and comparison with human ABCB1 reveals the basis for ACT-451840's selectivity against PfMDR1.

articles.headline: Three pyrimidine biosynthesis enzymes shown essential in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Genetic analysis of pyrimidine biosynthetic enzymes in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Rajaram K, Sievert ML, Elahi R, Blauwkamp J, Dillard LB, Absalon S, Prigge ST
articles.details: This genetic study examined three enzymes in the de novo pyrimidine biosynthesis pathway of P. falciparum: aspartate transcarbamoylase (PfATC), carbamoyl phosphate synthetase II (PfCPSII), and dihydroorotase (PfDHO). Despite a predicted apicoplast trafficking peptide, PfATC showed no apicoplast localization; its knockdown was lethal and could not be rescued by an apicoplast metabolic bypass, and truncating its N-terminal domain impaired growth. PfCPSII was likewise essential for proliferation. Deleting PfDHO in a uracil-salvaging parasite line caused uracil auxotrophy, confirming its essential role. The authors conclude these enzymes represent multiple vulnerable drug-target nodes.

articles.headline: Cameroon village shows silent malaria transmission despite satisfactory awareness
articles.title_original: Knowledge, attitudes and practices towards malaria in a peri-urban Cameroonian village: Persistent silent transmission despite satisfactory awareness.
articles.authors: Joko S, Poumachu Y, Ouko R, Kala-Chouakeu NA, Bamou R, Elanga-Ndillé E, Awono-Ambene P, Antonio-Nkondjio C, Tchuinkam T, Ndo C
articles.details: This cross-sectional knowledge, attitudes and practices (KAP) study in Emana village, Cameroon, screened 249 participants by microscopy, with a subgroup of 104 having complete sociodemographic data, and analyzed associations with parasitological prevalence. Knowledge and attitudes were generally satisfactory with no significant sociodemographic associations (all p > 0.05), while practices were poor but significantly better among women than men (p = 0.014). Overall malaria prevalence was 24.9%, significantly higher in the 5–18 years age group (13.25%, p = 0.027). The authors conclude a persistent knowledge-to-practice gap drives silent transmission, especially among 5–18-year-olds.

articles.headline: Distinct PfEMP1 antibody gaps in cerebral malaria versus severe malarial anemia
articles.title_original: Distinct Plasmodium falciparum Erythrocyte Membrane Protein 1 Domain Cassette-Associated Antibody Gaps Specific to Cerebral Malaria and Severe Malarial Anemia.
articles.authors: Travassos MA, Han P, Coulibaly D, Stucke EM, Zhou AE, Shrestha B, Nakajima R, Jain A, Taghavian O, Jasinskas A, Lawton JG, Laurens MB, Niangaly A, Pike A, Ouattara A, Berry AA, Adams M, Takala-Harrison S, Kouriba B, Kone AK, Rowe JA, Doumbo OK, Lyke KE, Felgner PL, Plowe CV, Thera MA
articles.details: Using a custom protein microarray of 79 PfEMP1 fragments (including domain cassettes 1, 4, 5, 8, 13, and 15) from reference and sequenced Malian field isolates, researchers measured serological responses in Malian children with cerebral malaria (CM), severe malarial anemia (SMA), concurrent CM with SMA, and age-matched uncomplicated malaria controls. Children with severe malaria had distinct antibody gaps: DCs 1, 8, and 13 for SMA and DC13 for CM. Few DCs were differentially seroreactive, but reactivity to downstream domain couplets differed. The authors suggest antibodies to downstream PfEMP1 domains may reflect exposure or protective immunity.

articles.headline: RNA-binding protein PfSR-X2 is essential for malaria blood-stage growth
articles.title_original: The serine-arginine-rich protein PfSR-X2 modulates human malaria parasite gene expression during the intraerythrocytic developmental cycle.
articles.authors: Hu Y, Zhuang C, Li M, Wu J, Zhou L, Li M, Li X, Du X, Shang X, Bai Y, Zhao D, Chen S, Fan Y
articles.details: This study characterized the serine/arginine-rich protein PfSR-X2 (PF3D7_0319500) during P. falciparum asexual blood-stage development using sequence/structural analysis, localization assays, and a glucosamine-inducible glmS conditional knockdown generated by CRISPR-Cas9. PfSR-X2 was expressed throughout the intraerythrocytic cycle and localized to nuclear and cytoplasmic compartments; gene disruption failed, indicating it is essential. Depletion reduced proliferation and impaired merozoite production, and RNA sequencing showed stage-specific gene expression changes affecting RNA metabolism, antigenic variation, and ApiAP2 pathways, strongly perturbing the rif family while leaving overall var patterns largely unchanged. Alternative splicing changes were limited, mainly at schizont stage. The authors conclude PfSR-X2 is an essential stage-specific post-transcriptional regulator.

articles.headline: Cardiolipin synthase enzyme is essential for P. falciparum blood-stage survival
articles.title_original: A prokaryotic-like Cardiolipin Synthase is essential to maintain mitochondrial function, lipid homeostasis and survival of blood stage malaria parasite Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Islam MM, Anwar MO, Yamaryo-Botté Y, Rath PP, Abbas M, Thakur V, Rathore S, Botté CY, Mohmmed A
articles.details: Using inducible knock-down in Plasmodium falciparum asexual blood stages, researchers identified and characterized a prokaryotic-like cardiolipin synthase (PfCLS) functioning in the parasite mitochondrion. Knock-down disrupted parasite development and killed parasites, impaired assembly of mitochondrial respiratory super-complexes, and caused hypersensitivity to mitochondrial electron transport chain drugs such as atovaquone. Lipidomic analysis showed loss of cardiolipin and broad phospholipid disturbances, while metabolomics revealed disruption of the TCA cycle and amino acid levels. The authors conclude PfCLS is essential, linking mitochondrial membrane biogenesis, function, and phospholipid homeostasis.

articles.headline: Subclinical malaria common in sickle cell disease, microscopy linked to lower hemoglobin
articles.title_original: Subclinical malaria in sickle cell disease in Northern Uganda: prevalence, predictors, and hematologic impact.
articles.authors: Matejak NJ, Ngolobe H, O'Connell CT, Otika D, McGowan K, Ogwang Q, Nakyeyune ML, Azrin S, Dieckhaus KD, Bongomin F
articles.details: This cross-sectional study of 274 sickle cell disease patients (median age 12 years; 54.4% male) at a Northern Uganda SCD clinic assessed subclinical malaria prevalence, predictors, and hematologic impact using five hierarchical diagnostic criteria. Overall subclinical malaria prevalence was 23.4% by blood smear or RDT (RDT 22.3%, smear 11.7%). Rural residence was the only independent predictor. Smear-positive participants had lower mean hemoglobin than smear-negative (6.87 vs 7.82 g/dL, p=0.0027), while RDT-based definitions showed no hemoglobin association. Hydroxyurea use raised median Hb non-significantly (p=0.107). Authors conclude only microscopy-confirmed parasitemia was clinically significant; RDTs had limited utility.

articles.headline: LEF1-LAG3 axis regulates CD4+ T cells in mouse malaria infection
articles.title_original: The LEF1-LAG3 axis regulates CD4(+) T cell function during Plasmodium yoelii NSM infection.
articles.authors: Peng W, Liang G, Lu K, Mo F, Xie X, Chen M, Yuan H, Luo L, Wang X, Xu L, Wei H, Li L, Zhao S, Xie H, Pan X, Huang J
articles.details: Using a Plasmodium yoelii NSM murine model with single-cell RNA sequencing, flow cytometry, dual-luciferase reporter assays, and the Wnt agonist CHIR99021, researchers examined how LEF1 regulates LAG3 expression on CD4+ T cells. Infection caused splenomegaly, increased CD4+ T cell numbers, upregulated activation markers (ICOS, CD69), downregulated CD62L, and enhanced IL-10 and IFN-γ. LAG3+CD4+ T cells showed an activated phenotype (Ki67+, CD44hiCD62Llo) with co-inhibitory receptors PD-1, TIM-3, and TIGIT. LEF1 was downregulated and directly bound the Lag3 promoter as a transcriptional repressor; CHIR99021 dose-dependently reduced LAG3+CD4+ T cell frequency. Authors propose the LEF1-LAG3 axis fine-tunes anti-malarial immunity.

articles.headline: Prime-and-trap vaccine defines minimal needs for malaria liver-stage protection in mice
articles.title_original: A prime-and-trap vaccination strategy to evaluate minimal requirements for malaria liver stage protection in mice.
articles.authors: Watson FN, Reynolds RA, Kalata AC, Shears MJ, Murphy SC
articles.details: This mouse study used a prime-and-trap vaccination strategy that traps Plasmodium yoelii circumsporozoite protein (CSP)-specific CD8+ T cells in the liver via DNA-encoded CSP delivered by hydrodynamic transfection (HDT), progressively de-escalating trapping doses to define minimal requirements for liver-stage protection. Prime-and-HDT trap induced sterile protection against sporozoite challenge with CSP-HDT doses as low as 5 ng DNA, but protection was lost at an ultra-low 50 pg dose, which corresponded to low liver CSP-specific CD8+ T cell levels. The authors conclude a threshold of liver CSP-specific CD8+ T cells is required for sterile protection, offering a tractable, inexpensive model.

articles.headline: Review: decentralization and diversification of malaria parasite genomics
articles.title_original: Malaria Parasite Genomics: Decentralization, Diversification, and Development Goals.
articles.authors: Amambua-Ngwa A, Neafsey DE, Volkman SK, Diallo MA
articles.details: This review examines how malaria parasite genomics has evolved from a descriptive, discovery-focused field into a global system for routine surveillance informing public health decisions, driven by changing technology, expanded capacity, shifting funding, and broader applications. It highlights wider access to sequencing and cost-effective targeted sequencing methods, while noting emerging needs to standardize data formats, analysis pipelines, sharing practices, and approaches for translating genomic insights into action. The authors outline the progressive decentralization and diversification of genomic data generation, emerging applications, and key development needs and growth opportunities for the maturing field.

articles.headline: Climate change projected to raise P. vivax malaria risk across Brazil
articles.title_original: Climate change and Plasmodium vivax Malaria Risk in Brazil: Developing adaptive tool for Brazilian Municipalities.
articles.authors: C M Sousa T, Hacon SS, Pedra GU, Lemos CMG, Sallum MAM, Ladeia-Andrade S, Reis FB, Arcoverde G, Alves L, Ometto J
articles.details: This modeling study assessed Plasmodium vivax malaria risk under climate change scenarios for 2030 and 2050 across all Brazilian municipalities, using the AdaptaBrasil MCTI approach, multilevel analysis, and a binary logistic model under two emission scenarios (RCP 4.5 and RCP 8.5). Maximum temperature increase was the most influential factor (β = 0.35), followed by the Simple Daily Intensity Index (SDII, β = 0.19) and relative humidity (β = 0.12). The authors found growing malaria risk under both scenarios, greatest in the Amazon and expanding to the Southeast and Northeast by 2050, underscoring the need for targeted public health and climate adaptation measures.

articles.headline: Study examines genetic diversity of RIPR invasion protein in P. vivax
articles.title_original: Unraveling the genetic diversity of RIPR, a key invasion protein in Plasmodium vivax malaria.
articles.authors: Cebrian-Carmona J, Gonzalez-Ceron L, Valero-Galvez V, Garrido-Cárdenas JA, Mesa-Valle CM
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Review of hydroxamic acid HDAC inhibitors as new antimalarial drug leads
articles.title_original: Hydroxamic Acids as HDAC Inhibitor Drug Leads for Malaria.
articles.authors: Dawood WA, Macdonald JR, Anzenhofer C, Skinner-Adams TS, Reid RC, Kurz T, Fairlie DP, Andrews KT
articles.details: This review evaluates histone/lysine deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitors as antimalarial drug leads to address Plasmodium falciparum drug resistance, noting an estimated 282 million malaria cases and 610,000 deaths reported in 2024, with most mortality from P. falciparum and the highest burden in Sub-Saharan Africa. It assesses antiplasmodial structure-activity relationships for more than 750 hydroxamic acids with published in vitro activity against P. falciparum. The authors conclude these comparative data offer insights into potency, selectivity, and physicochemical properties to guide development of this chemotype against malaria and other parasitic diseases.

articles.headline: Multi-species non-falciparum transmission and insecticide resistance in Cameroon highlands
articles.title_original: Complex non-falciparum transmission and widespread insecticide resistance in Penka-Michel, a hyperendemic malaria focus of the Cameroon highlands.
articles.authors: Kiam BC, Ibrahima I, Mbida JAM, Bouopda-Tuedom AG, Abate L, White SJ, V CTN, Tumamo BF, Morlais I, Ayong L, Lin JT, Ali IM, Juliano JJ, Dinglasan RR, Nsango SE
articles.details: A 12-month longitudinal entomological study (September 2023-August 2024) in Penka-Michel, Western Highlands of Cameroon, characterized malaria transmission and insecticide resistance markers. Transmission depended on three vectors—Anopheles funestus (43.7%), An. gambiae (42.9%), and An. ziemanni (13.5%)—with a hyperendemic annual EIR of 839.5 infective bites/human/night; Plasmodium falciparum predominated (70.2%) alongside a substantial non-falciparum burden (P. malariae 27.5%; P. ovale 22.1%). Resistant homozygotes were detected in 70% of infected mosquito samples, and the authors found associations of L119F-GSTe2 with P. ovale curtisi transmission (IRR = 1.15, p = 0.03) and L1014F-kdr with P. malariae (IRR = 2.2, p = 0.04), highlighting a complex multi-species system sustaining often-overlooked non-falciparum malaria.

articles.headline: P. falciparum invasion ligand transcript levels differ between Malaysian and Gambian parasites
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum invasion ligand gene transcript profiles in different populations.
articles.authors: Lantero-Escolar E, Stewart LB, Singh B, Claessens A, Amambua-Ngwa A, Conway DJ
articles.details: This study compared relative transcript levels of three eba genes and five Rh invasion-ligand genes in Plasmodium falciparum schizonts from Malaysian isolates (Sabah State, Borneo, sampled before endemic elimination) and Gambian isolates cultured under identical conditions, using RT-qPCR. Significant between-population differences were seen for three ligand genes: eba175 was higher in Malaysia, while eba181 and Rh2b were lower, with variation not associated with single versus multiple genotypes per isolate; eba181 levels correlated positively with parasite multiplication rate among Malaysian but not Gambian isolates. The authors conclude that expression of these three merozoite ligands may be regionally differentiated, with implications for vaccine candidates based on them.

articles.headline: Imported malaria persists in Trinidad with multiple Plasmodium species
articles.title_original: Imported malaria in Trinidad, West Indies: Detection of multiple non-endemic Plasmodium species.
articles.authors: Sant C, Mohammed N, Oura C, Sanford B, Kirk S, Ibeleme D, Nicholls S, Georges K
articles.details: This study analyzed 153 blood samples from suspected malaria cases in Trinidad collected from December 2018 to December 2023, using microscopy and PCR targeting the Plasmodium cytochrome oxidase 1 gene with sequencing. Fifty-six (36.60%) were positive by both methods, with 100% concordance between methods and 98.21% agreement on species identification. Imported P. vivax, P. falciparum, and P. ovale were confirmed; P. malariae was found in three samples, two without travel history, suggesting local transmission. Logistic regression identified headache and nausea as statistically significant risk factors for PCR positivity.

articles.headline: Malaria and dengue vectors surveyed across central and northern Benin
articles.title_original: Species diversity and human biting rates of malaria and dengue vectors in central and northern Benin.
articles.authors: Hoyochi I, Zoungbédji DM, Konkon AK, Salako AS, Konkon AC, Adjovi RV, Adoha CJ, Fongnikin A, Sominahouin A, Baba-Moussa L, Akogbéto MC, Padonou GG
articles.details: Using a multimethod entomological approach (larval surveys, human landing catches, oviposition traps, pyrethrum spray collection) across five communes in central and northern Benin, researchers collected 2,337 adult mosquitoes dominated by Aedes and Anopheles, with statistically significant intercommunal differences (p < 0.001). Of 993 inspected breeding sites, 51.0% were positive, with household containers most productive for Aedes. After emergence, 1,988 adults represented seven species including Aedes aegypti, Ae. albopictus, Anopheles gambiae, and Culex quinquefasciatus. Aedes aegypti bit predominantly by day and An. gambiae at night, signaling potential combined malaria and dengue transmission risk.

articles.headline: HLA alleles differentially protect against Toxoplasma and malaria in thalassemia patients
articles.title_original: HLA polymorphisms shape divergent outcomes of Toxoplasma and Plasmodium infection in Eastern Indian HbE/β-thalassemia cohort.
articles.authors: Bhattacharya S, Rahaman M, Suman S, Rout DK, Mondal S, Purwar S, Dhingra B, Shukla PC, Mukherjee G, Gupta M, Guha D, Bhattacharya MK, Dolai TK, Chakravorty N, Mukherjee B
articles.details: Integrating NGS and Sanger sequencing from 61 HbE/β-thalassemia patients and 50 healthy controls in an Eastern Indian cohort with ex-vivo infection assays, researchers found individuals carrying HLA-A*33 showed significant protection against Toxoplasma gondii, independent of transfusion frequency, via restricted intracellular replication and enhanced CD8⁺ IFN-γ⁺ responses linked to high A*33 binding affinity for the SAG2C peptide. A*33 did not protect against Plasmodium falciparum; instead, HLA-C*07 showed a potential negative association with Plasmodium infection, with strong binding to the MSP3 peptide. The authors conclude host HLA diversity drives pathogen-specific apicomplexan outcomes.

articles.headline: Thrombocytopenia strongly linked to P. falciparum malaria in Ghana
articles.title_original: Association between thrombocytopaenia and Plasmodium falciparum infection in Ghana.
articles.authors: Duneeh RV, Amoada ST, Yankey E, Asante JA, Allotey EA, Akorsu EE, Klugah MA, Ablordey K
articles.details: This comparative cross-sectional study across three Ghanaian sites (Ashaiman, Kwesimintsim, Kyebi) from June to September 2025 enrolled 388 participants (P. falciparum-positive and malaria-negative), diagnosing malaria by microscopy and measuring haematological parameters. Thrombocytopenia was the most consistent finding, with median platelet counts of 152.0 versus 234.0 × 10⁹/L in controls (p < 0.001), and it showed the strongest association with infection (adjusted OR = 11.8; 95% CI 6.18–24.7; p < 0.001). A model with haematological indices outperformed sociodemographic factors (AUC 77.6% vs 57.9%). The authors recommend integrating platelet count into diagnostic algorithms in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Scoping review maps malaria diagnostic practices in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Current practices and innovative diagnostics for Malaria in Ethiopia: a scoping review.
articles.authors: Bantie GM, Teshome DF, Asres GD, Moges NA, Tadege M, Awoke AK, Berneh AA, Ferede AG, Abebe DL, Tiruneh EK, Melese AA, Sewagegn N, Wubetu GY
articles.details: This scoping review, following Arksey and O'Malley's methodology and PRISMA-ScR guidelines, examined current and innovative malaria diagnostics in Ethiopia. Thirteen studies were eligible, with 38.5% published between 2008 and 2024; most (84.6%) used cross-sectional designs and one (7.7%) was an RCT, with studies concentrated in the Amhara (43.75%) and Oromia (31.25%) regions. Identified diagnostics were light microscopy, rapid diagnostic tests (RDT), PCR, and loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP), each with distinct advantages and limitations. The authors conclude that tailored, accurate, user-friendly diagnostics are needed to support malaria elimination efforts.

articles.headline: RDT outperforms microscopy for malaria diagnosis in pregnant women
articles.title_original: Evaluation of microscopy and first response™ malaria Ag PAN/Pf RDT compared to qPCR and hemoglobin levels in pregnant women at delivery in Busia County, Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Kipchirchir D, Wakoli DM, Imbusi G, Makandi R, Chimwani E, Chumbe SA, Ghebremichael M, Ong'echa JM, Bargul JL, Idris AH, Ondigo BN
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 236 women delivering at Sio Port and Port Victoria Sub-County Hospitals, Busia County, Kenya, comparing microscopy, the First Response Malaria Ag PAN/Pf RDT (mRDT), and qPCR. Peripheral malaria prevalence was 7.2% (16/223) by microscopy, 8.1% (18/223) by mRDT, and 9.0% (20/223) by qPCR; placental prevalence was 7.2% (17/236) by microscopy versus 15.7% (37/236) by qPCR. Against peripheral qPCR, mRDT had higher sensitivity (90%) but lower specificity (80%) than microscopy (80% sensitivity, 89% specificity). P. falciparum predominated and anaemia prevalence was 69.9%; no significant associations were found between malaria in pregnancy and ABO blood groups or anaemia (P>0.05).

articles.headline: Plasmodium vivax-associated virus MaRNAV-1 mapped across Asia, South America, Oceania
articles.details: Using primary and publicly available metatranscriptomic data, researchers mapped the geographic distribution and host associations of Matryoshka RNA virus 1 (MaRNAV-1), a bi-segmented single-stranded RNA virus associated with the human malaria parasite Plasmodium vivax. The virus was detected throughout Southeast Asia, in parts of South America, and for the first time in Oceania, but was found exclusively in metatranscriptomes also containing P. vivax, suggesting a specific virus–host relationship. The emergence date could not be estimated, though phylogeographic analysis suggested MaRNAV-1 is widely dispersed across Southeast Asia. Findings carry evolutionary and public health implications.
articles.title_original: Mapping the global distribution and spread of the Plasmodium vivax-associated virus MaRNAV-1.
articles.authors: Petrone ME, Charon J, Parry RH, Grigg MJ, Piera KA, Westaway JAF, Shioda K, Russell B, Price RN, William T, Kenangalem E, McCarthy JS, Barber BE, Holmes EC, Anstey NM

articles.headline: Madagascar national survey maps anemia, micronutrient gaps, and malaria
articles.title_original: Burden and Co-Occurrence of Anemia, Micronutrient Deficiencies, Inflammation, and Malaria Among Women, Adolescents, and Children in Madagascar: A National Cross-Sectional Survey.
articles.authors: Rohner F, Rakotovao JM, El-Mallah C, Andrianarison R, Randrenarizo N, Wegmüller R, Rakotovao R, Ravaoarisoa L, Joyeux M, Wirth JP, Annan Z, Petry N, Wieringa FT, Bastaraud A, Randrianirina F, Labonté JM, Abdelnour A, Jones KS, Rezzi S, Rajoelina MT, Ralaimiadana AR, Vololontiana HM, Yasmine LL, Galetti V
articles.details: The 2024 National Micronutrient Survey used a 2-stage probabilistic design across 23 regions of Madagascar, sampling preschool children (PSC), adolescents, and non-pregnant women for malaria, anemia, inflammation, and multiple micronutrient biomarkers. Malaria was detected in 4%–14% and inflammation in 21%–46% of participants; anemia affected 42% of PSC and about one-third of older groups. Iron deficiency was most common in PSC (26%), zinc deficiency affected about one-third of all groups, and riboflavin deficiency was very high (75% of PSC, nearly 90% of older groups); vitamin A deficiency was 12% in PSC and folate deficiency reached 21% in non-pregnant women. Co-occurrence of conditions was frequent but did not exceed chance, and authors call for integrated national strategies.

articles.headline: Community engagement shaped Nepal's HIV, TB, and malaria priorities
articles.title_original: Community and stakeholder engagement in national priority setting and participatory research for HIV, Tuberculosis, and Malaria programs in Nepal.
articles.authors: Neupane S, Shiwakoti R, Aryal L, Nzima M, Lohar I, Sitaula A, Karmacharya U, Paudel K, Bhattachan A, Devkota S, Bhattarai RK, Adhikari B
articles.details: Qualitative study documenting the community and stakeholder engagement process used by Nepal's Country Coordinating Mechanism to set national HIV, TB, and malaria priorities. The process involved community group discussions across all seven provinces and 72 municipalities, with over 2,000 participants in 700 sessions plus 100 key informant interviews, analyzed using deductive and inductive methods. Participants reported that recommendations (e.g., HIV testing points for migrants at border areas) and identified system gaps such as drug stockouts and stigma were reflected in national strategic plans, and the government pledged 20% domestic co-financing. Authors conclude the participatory approach grounded priorities in community realities and may inform other low- and middle-income countries.

articles.headline: Drug stockouts and cost delay malaria treatment for Ugandan children
articles.title_original: Determinants of time to malaria treatment-seeking behaviour among caregivers of children under five in Uganda: a case study of Apac district.
articles.authors: Odur B, Opio B, Nabulya O
articles.details: Cross-sectional analytical study of 240 households in Apac District, Uganda, using structured questionnaires and Cox proportional hazards regression to examine factors influencing how quickly caregivers sought malaria treatment for children under five. All caregivers sought treatment (70% were female). Secondary education significantly increased the likelihood of timely treatment-seeking (HR=1.67, p=0.044), while significant delays were associated with no medicine at the hospital (HR=0.61, p=0.001) and financial constraints (HR=0.49, p<0.001). Authors conclude that improving drug supply and educating caregivers on early treatment are key to ensuring timely care.

articles.headline: Artemether-lumefantrine stays over 90% effective in Mozambique meta-analysis
articles.title_original: Efficacy of artemether-lumefantrine for uncomplicated plasmodium falciparum malaria treatment in mozambique: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Nhama A, Aide P, Torres-Fernandez D, Varo R, Nhacolo A, Bassat Q
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of artemether-lumefantrine (AL) efficacy for uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria in Mozambique, including six studies comprising 20 single-arm trials and 1,862 children and adults evaluated by the WHO standard protocol. Pooled uncorrected efficacy was 91.0% (95% CI 84.2–94.6) and PCR-corrected efficacy was 98.3% (95% CI 96.9–99.1). Over 28-day follow-up, early treatment failure was 0.05%, late clinical failure 2.73%, and late parasitological failure 5.3%; recrudescence was 1.8%, new infections 8.6%, and the day-3 positivity rate about 0.78%. Authors conclude AL remains efficacious (above 90%) and can continue as first-line treatment for all vulnerable groups in Mozambique.

articles.headline: History of South Korea's national malaria eradication service, 1945-1969
articles.title_original: Mobilizing Public Health in South Korea: NMES (National Malaria Eradication Service) and its Legacy, 1945-1969.
articles.authors: DiMoia JP
articles.details: This historical paper examines the public health strategies, language, and pedagogy used by the Republic of Korea government to engage rural villages following the Korean War. It focuses on the formation of the National Malaria Eradication Service (NMES) in the early 1960s, created to address the wartime rise in malaria cases. The authors argue that South Korean public health transformed itself from an outside force into a familiar partner, depending heavily on citizen and villager participation to function.

articles.headline: Adjuvant supply chains for new TB and malaria vaccines
articles.title_original: Understanding adjuvant supply chains for new TB and malaria vaccines.
articles.authors: Gotham D, Frick M, Harrington M
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Malaria and helminth prevalence in pregnant women, southwest Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Malaria and Soil-Transmitted Helminths and Associated Factors Among Pregnant Women Visiting Mizan-Tepi University Teaching Hospital, Southwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Duguma T, Fikadu S, Kochito S, Mohammed H, Demango F
articles.details: A cross-sectional study of 330 pregnant women attending an antenatal clinic at Mizan-Tepi University Teaching Hospital, Southwest Ethiopia, assessed the prevalence and associated factors of malaria and soil-transmitted helminth (STH) infections. Malaria prevalence was 25.2% (83/330) and STH prevalence was 29.4% (97/330), with Ascaris lumbricoides most common. Rural residence (AOR=2.011; p=0.024), farming occupation (AOR=4.801; p=0.048), stagnant water (AOR=2.150; p=0.008), and insecticidal residual spray (AOR=1.756; p=0.047) were associated with malaria. The authors call for integrated antenatal interventions including health education, sanitation, bed nets, and deworming.

articles.headline: New multiplexed microhaplotype panel for P. vivax genomic surveillance
articles.title_original: PvGAP: Development of a globally-applicable, highly-multiplexed microhaplotype amplicon panel for Plasmodium vivax.
articles.authors: Hubbard A, Solares E, Bradley L, Jeang B, Yewhalaw D, Janies D, Lo E, Yan G, Hemming-Schroeder E
articles.details: Researchers developed PvGAP, a Globally-applicable Amplicon Panel for Plasmodium vivax, selecting targets based on genetic diversity and genetic distance to maximize discrimination between geographic regions. The panel contains 80 high-diversity targets for population genomics plus eight targets of epidemiological interest, such as putative drug-resistance markers. It was evaluated with field samples from Ethiopia and in silico using whole genomes from the MalariaGEN Pv4 database, achieving robust amplification and competitive accuracy for relatedness inference across three geographic regions. The authors conclude PvGAP is a cost-effective tool to support drug-resistance surveillance and distinguish local from imported cases.

articles.headline: Atriplex halimus extracts repel malaria vector Anopheles pharoensis
articles.title_original: Phytochemical profiling, metabolomics, and molecular docking studies of Atriplex halimus aerial parts revealing potential insecticidal activity against the malaria vector Anopheles pharoensis.
articles.authors: Elhawary EA, Waheeb HO, Abdelhafiz AHA, El-Waseif AA, Shehata AZI
articles.details: Aerial parts of Atriplex halimus were extracted with 70% methanol and fractionated into dichloromethane (DCM), ethyl acetate (EtOAc), n-butanol, and aqueous fractions. UPLC/MS profiling identified seventy-eight secondary metabolites, predominantly flavonoids. Antimicrobial testing against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli showed the methanol extract and EtOAc fraction had the strongest inhibition. In repellent bioassays against starved female Anopheles pharoensis, the DCM fraction at 6.67 mg/cm² achieved the highest repellency (81.92%). Molecular docking explored the interactions involved. The authors highlight A. halimus as a source of bioactive metabolites with dual antimicrobial and mosquito-repellent potential for natural vector control.

articles.headline: Call for open access to malaria intervention data worldwide
articles.title_original: Public Availability of Malaria Intervention Data Will Be Vital for the Acceleration of Its Control and Elimination.
articles.authors: Kojom Foko LP, Syed Shah AH, Yadav CP, Rahi M, Sharma A
articles.details: This policy/advocacy piece argues that critical malaria intervention data (e.g., rapid diagnostics used, antimalarial drugs used, insecticide-treated net distribution, and other vector control tools) remain restricted and should be mandated as openly accessible public health goods to support surveillance, research, and equitable resource allocation. The authors note malaria claimed an estimated 610,000 lives in 2024, predominantly in sub-Saharan Africa. Using India as a case study, they highlight gaps in data availability at the district level and propose strategies including standardized data platforms to accelerate control and elimination.

articles.headline: Malaria cases rising in five high-burden African countries
articles.title_original: Turning the tide against malaria in high-burden African countries: Trends, threats, and solutions.
articles.authors: Kojom Foko LP, Sharma A
articles.details: Using WHO data from 2015-2024 and the Mann-Kendall trend test, the authors analyzed epidemiological trajectories in High Burden to High Impact (HBHI) countries. They found sustained increases in malaria cases in Nigeria (MK=3.94, p=0.002), the Democratic Republic of the Congo (MK=3.76, p=0.002), Cameroon (MK=3.40, p=0.005), Uganda (MK=2.86, p=0.02), and Mali (MK=2.50, p=0.04), while mortality trends were heterogeneous and mostly non-significant. They cite emerging WHO biological threats (antimalarial drug resistance, insecticide resistance, pfhrp2 gene deletions, Anopheles stephensi invasion) plus climate change, conflict, and displacement, and note Cabo Verde's recent malaria-free certification. They conclude the WHO ≥90% burden-reduction target by 2030 is ambitious but feasible with locally driven, multisectoral action.

articles.headline: Geospatial risk mapping identifies high-malaria zones in Malawi district
articles.title_original: Mapping malaria risk using geospatial and AHP approaches in Nsanje District, Malawi.
articles.authors: Banda YM, Nyengere J, Saka K, Tholo H, Chisenga C, Njalammano J, Kamanga BN, Gondwe S, Mtewa AG, Abraham A, Kachedwa L, Chirombo J, Nguvulu A, Chinkaka E, Kadewa W, Mvula RLS
articles.details: This study mapped malaria risk in Nsanje District, southern Malawi, using environmental variables (temperature, rainfall, elevation, slope, river proximity) for hazard and socio-economic factors (proximity to health facilities and roads, population density) for vulnerability, with Sentinel-2 land cover classified via random trees in ArcGIS Pro. Analytical hierarchy process and weighted overlay analysis generated hazard, vulnerability, and risk maps. Temperature and rainfall contributed most to hazard, with 20.4% of the area classified high-hazard and 35.1% very high-hazard; 43.1% of the district was categorized as high risk and 40.5% as moderate risk. The authors recommend improving healthcare accessibility and developing early warning systems and risk maps for targeted control.

articles.headline: CDC guidance on investigating locally acquired US malaria cases
articles.title_original: CDC Operational Guidance for Investigating Locally Acquired Mosquito-Transmitted Malaria - United States, 2026.
articles.authors: Ranadive N, Barratt JLN, Dotson EM, Gimnig JE, Lenhart AE, Levine RS, Mace KE, McElroy PD, Raphael BH, Sayre D, Steinhardt LC, Sutcliffe AC, Williams SG, Ridpath AD
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Mathematical model: insecticides plus treatment best control malaria
articles.title_original: Effects of insecticides and awareness on the dynamics of a delayed malaria model: A real-data calibration.
articles.authors: Khatun MS, Paul P, Ghosh D, Das P
articles.details: The authors introduce a time-delayed human-malaria interaction model incorporating two delays (malaria incubation period and time to implement awareness programs) to evaluate awareness-based interventions. The delayed and non-delayed models were calibrated using observed malaria case data from India, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, and Uganda, with global sensitivity analyses (MeFAST and LHS-PRCC) identifying key parameters. They establish supercritical Hopf bifurcations producing periodic oscillations and show multiple delays destabilize the system earlier than a single delay, with chaotic dynamics at high reporting delays. Delayed optimal control results indicate that combining insecticide measures and treatment is most effective at controlling infections.

articles.headline: Low birthweight, not malaria chemoprevention, lowers infant pneumococcal vaccine response
articles.title_original: Low birthweight and prematurity, but not malaria chemoprevention, are associated with reduced pneumococcal vaccine immunogenicity in Ugandan infants.
articles.authors: Musinguzi K, Sbarra AN, Bach F, Nankya F, Achom KB, Mwubaha C, Nayebare P, Nansubuga E, Kakuru A, Kizza J, Maato Z, Arinaitwe E, Press KD, Bagaya BS, Tukwasibwe S, Ssewanyana I, Nankabirwa JI, Kamya MR, Dorsey G, Takahashi S, Jagannathan P
articles.details: Nested in the MIC-DroP trial in eastern Uganda, this study measured serotype-specific IgG to the 10-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV-10) at 8 and 24 weeks in 202 selected infants (195 completed the three-dose series), comparing perennial malaria chemoprevention (PMC) and malaria exposure against birth outcomes. Neither PMC nor malaria exposure was associated with immunogenicity or seroconversion. In contrast, low birthweight (<2500g) was linked to 82% lower antibody fold-change (p=0.003) and reduced seroconversion (OR=0.19, p=0.003), and preterm birth (<37 weeks) showed similar effects (79% lower fold-change, p=0.018; OR=0.181, p=0.009).

articles.headline: IFN-γ and IL-10 levels track malaria severity in Sudanese children
articles.title_original: Interferon-γ and Interleukin-10 Profiles Correlate with Disease Severity in Sudanese Children with Plasmodium falciparum Malaria.
articles.authors: Hajali TA, Mekki MIE, Alaagib NA, Omer IIA, Yahia RM, Hamdan HZ
articles.details: This hospital-based case-control study in Omdurman, Sudan enrolled 105 children aged 3 months to 16 years in three age- and sex-matched groups (n=35 each): severe malaria (SM), uncomplicated malaria (UM), and healthy controls, with plasma IFN-γ and IL-10 measured by ELISA. SM was associated with lower hemoglobin and markedly reduced platelets, and both IFN-γ and IL-10 were significantly elevated in SM versus UM and controls (p<0.001). IFN-γ and IL-10 correlated strongly (rho=0.688, p<0.001), and IL-10 correlated with platelet count (rho=-0.338, p=0.009). Both showed moderate accuracy distinguishing SM from UM (IL-10 AUC=0.720; IFN-γ AUC=0.687).

articles.headline: Pfmdr1 copy number variation found but DHA-piperaquine still fully effective in Indonesia
articles.title_original: The Impact of pfmdr1 Gene Copy Number on the Efficacy of Dihydroartemisinin-Piperaquine in Treating Malaria in West Sumba and Kupang Districts, East Nusa Tenggara, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Irdayanti I, Aswad H, Asih PBS, Rozi IE, Herdiana H, Diptyanusa A, Prameswari HD, Yusuf Y, Bahrani B, Hidayah N, Artika IM, Syafruddin D
articles.details: This descriptive molecular surveillance study analyzed 41 dried blood spot samples from patients with P. falciparum malaria treated with dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DHA-PPQ) in East Nusa Tenggara, Indonesia, assessing Pfmdr1 gene copy number variation by real-time PCR. All 41 patients showed 100% adequate clinical and parasitological response (ACPR) on Day 42. Pfmdr1 analysis found 36 isolates (87.81%) with a single copy and 5 (12.19%) with multiple copies. The authors conclude DHA-PPQ remains clinically effective but the genetic markers warrant proactive surveillance for shifts in drug sensitivity.

articles.headline: Systematic review maps P. falciparum drug-resistance markers across Angola
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum antimalarial resistance in Angola: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Matias D, Chaves C, Cândido AM, Morais J, Antunes ML, Guler J, Nogueira F, Salamandane A
articles.details: Following PRISMA 2020 guidelines, this systematic review searched PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science (2000-2024) for molecular markers of P. falciparum drug resistance in Angola (pfcrt, pfdhfr, pfdhps, pfmdr1, pfk13, and pfmdr1/pfpm2/3 copy number variation), with 20 studies meeting inclusion criteria. The chloroquine-resistant pfcrt CVIET haplotype persisted in northern and coastal provinces, and the SP-resistant pfdhfr/pfdhps IRNGE haplotype was detected in Cabinda and Zaire, while no validated pfk13 artemisinin-resistance mutations were reported. Pfmdr1 86Y and 1246Y frequencies declined with wild-type NYD/NFD haplotypes predominating, and CNV prevalence was low though emerging amplification appeared in southern Angola. The authors call for expanded surveillance in underrepresented provinces and pregnant women.

articles.headline: Proactive community malaria visits no better than fixed-point care in Mali trial
articles.title_original: Impact of proactive community case management of malaria on malaria prevalence in Bankass, Mali: a cluster-randomised controlled trial.
articles.authors: Thwing J, Doumbia S, Traore A, Sylla M, Sogoba M, Kamara I, Samaké Y, Guindo L, Rapp S, Treleaven E, Chiu C, Whidden C, Kone A, Djimde AA, Johnson AD, Kayentao K
articles.details: This 3-year cluster-randomized controlled trial in Bankass, Mali (population over 100,000) compared proactive community case management (ProCCM)—community health workers visiting every household twice monthly with maternal/child services and malaria testing and treatment—against control CHWs offering the same services from a fixed community point. At endline, there was no difference between arms in parasite prevalence across all ages, or in fever, care seeking, or access to diagnostic testing among children under 5. CHWs in the intervention arm reached the goal of two visits per month in less than half of households. The authors conclude both approaches offered comparable benefits in extending case management access.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria affects about a third of African schoolchildren
articles.title_original: Prevalence and determinants of asymptomatic malaria infection among schoolchildren in Africa: insights from a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Mohamed K, Mussema A, Simieneh A, Woldesenbet D
articles.details: This PRISMA systematic review and meta-analysis pooled studies of asymptomatic malaria infection in schoolchildren across 33 African countries. The summary prevalence was 34% (95% CI: 28-40%) with very high heterogeneity (I²=98.9%), ranging from 5% in Ethiopia to 53% in Cameroon; P. falciparum was most common at 29.3%, followed by mixed infections (2%), P. vivax (1%), P. malariae (1%), and P. ovale (0%). Infection was associated with male gender (OR 1.26), anemia (OR 5.51), not sleeping under a bednet (OR 1.99), and previous malaria treatment (OR 3.82). Authors call for targeted, context-specific school-based screening and prevention.

articles.headline: Review of community barriers to indoor residual spraying in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Community-level barriers and facilitators of indoor residual spraying for malaria control: a systematic review of qualitative evidence from sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Pitua I, Okorobe PM, Apili L, Nandera KV, Acoo PG, Nantalaga KC, Lwembawo KD, Otto KA
articles.details: This qualitative evidence synthesis (following ENTREQ guidelines) reviewed peer-reviewed studies on community perceptions of indoor residual spraying for malaria control in Sub-Saharan Africa. From 321 records, 10 studies from seven countries met inclusion. Eight analytical themes emerged, including operational and household burdens (home preparation labor), perceived efficacy and concerns about bedbugs, trust in authorities, sprayer conduct, gender dynamics in household decisions, seasonal incompatibility, and local health infrastructure. Authors conclude that uptake depends on social, gendered, and political factors and recommend human-centered approaches that reduce household labor, address nuisance-insect concerns, and build community trust.

articles.headline: Malaria in U.S. service members rose in 2025, P. vivax from Korea
articles.details: Surveillance summary of malaria among U.S. service members in 2025, with trends over 2016–2025. In 2025, 36 cases were diagnosed, a 12.5% increase from 32 in 2024; most were male (94.4%), in the active component (80.6%), and serving in the Army (63.9%). Africa was the leading acquisition region (n=14), mainly P. falciparum, but P. vivax became the predominant species at 41.7% (n=15), up from 10% in 2024, with 80.0% acquired in Korea. 72.1% of cases were diagnosed May through October.
articles.title_original: Malaria among members of the U.S. Armed Forces, 2025.
articles.authors: Armed Forces Health Surveillance Division

articles.headline: Putative metal transporter identified in malaria parasite apicoplast
articles.title_original: Identification of a Putative Metal Transporter in the Apicoplast of Malaria Parasites.
articles.authors: Nalder SA, Mayank AK, Liou W, Wohlschlegel JA, Sigala PA
articles.details: Using tetracycline-affinity proteomics in Plasmodium falciparum, researchers identified a doxycycline-interacting, uncharacterized transmembrane protein (UCT) localized to the apicoplast periphery but not proteolytically processed. Though lacking sequence similarity to known proteins, UCT's predicted structure resembles pentameric CorA-family metal transporters. UCT was dispensable for blood-stage asexual parasites, suggesting redundant metal-uptake mechanisms, but knockdown in gametocytes impaired development of sexual parasites critical for mosquito transmission. The authors propose UCT functions in metal transport into the apicoplast and note its heightened expression in mosquito-stage parasites.

articles.headline: D-dimer moderately predicts severe imported P. falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: D-dimer as a marker of severe disease in imported malaria: a retrospective observational study.
articles.authors: Poloni A, Rizzo A, Giacomelli A, Colaneri M, Arrigo C, Caronni S, Pieruzzi ME, Behring AH, Grosso S, Dolci A, Antinori S
articles.details: This retrospective single-center study assessed D-dimer (measured within 24h of diagnosis) as a marker of severe disease in adults with imported Plasmodium falciparum malaria. Among 85 patients (63.5% male, median age 39), 21 (24.7%) had severe malaria. Median D-dimer was higher in severe versus non-severe cases (10,943 vs 2,245 μg/L FEU; p=0.0005), and on multivariable logistic regression each additional 1,000 μg/L FEU raised the odds of severe disease (aOR 1.14, 95% CI 1.06-1.23). At an optimal cutoff of 4,864 μg/L FEU, sensitivity was 0.76 and specificity 0.73 (accuracy 0.74). Authors conclude D-dimer's accuracy for severity is moderate.

articles.headline: Microscopy-confirmed malaria prevalence pooled at 7% in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Prevalence and associated factors of microscopy-confirmed malaria among the general population in Ethiopia: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Faltamo TT, Assele DD, Meskele M, Tadesse T
articles.details: This PRISMA 2020 systematic review and meta-analysis estimated microscopy-confirmed malaria prevalence and determinants in Ethiopia's general population. Fourteen studies with 8,762 participants gave a pooled prevalence of 7.1% (95% CI: 4.9-10.2%; I²=95.7%), with regional variation highest in Amhara (10.7%) and the southwest (7.3%); trim-and-fill adjustment raised the estimate to 8.2%. Significant determinants included stagnant water (OR 6.71), mosquito breeding sites (OR 6.13), absence of indoor residual spraying (OR 5.90), travel to endemic areas (OR 5.86), ITN non-use (OR 4.8), houses with eaves (OR 4.5), and prior malaria (OR 3.54). Authors recommend strengthening ITN use and environmental management.

articles.headline: Trial protocol: PBO-treated ceiling nets vs LLINs for malaria in Kenya
articles.title_original: Evaluating the impacts of piperonyl butoxide-treated ceiling nets on malaria risk among high-risk populations with limited access to conventional LLINs in Homa Bay Country, Kenya: study protocol for a household randomized control trial.
articles.authors: Ko YK, Kagaya W, Kanoi BN, Kongere J, Gitaka J, Minakawa N
articles.details: This is a household randomized controlled trial protocol (superiority and non-inferiority design) evaluating piperonyl butoxide-pyrethroid (PBO)-ceiling nets as a house-screening tool among high-risk households with poor LLIN use in Homa Bay County, Kenya. 500 households will be randomized into three arms: PBO-ceiling nets alone, PBO-LLINs alone (one net per two members), or both combined. The primary outcome is prevalence of Plasmodium infection by PCR across all ages at 12 months post-intervention; secondary outcomes include entomological indicators such as vector density from CDC light traps. No results are reported as this is a protocol.

articles.headline: Meta-analysis: malaria-HIV coinfection burden in Ethiopia is 9%
articles.title_original: Burden of malaria-HIV coinfection and associated adverse outcomes in Ethiopia: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Yeshanew TM, Gidey B, Abebe B, Waktole G, Getie B, Getu T, Birhane N
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis estimated the burden of malaria-HIV coinfection and associated outcomes in Ethiopia, including ten studies selected from 429 reviewed, using a random-effects model. The pooled prevalence of malaria-HIV coinfection was 9% (95% CI 3-15). Pooled prevalence of associated clinical conditions was 31% (95% CI 12-50) for severe anemia, 41% (95% CI -8-90) for hyperparasitemia, and 35% (95% CI 14-56) for low CD4 counts (≤200 cells/µL). The authors conclude the overall coinfection burden in Ethiopia is 9%.

articles.headline: Landscape review of malaria rapid diagnostic tests
articles.title_original: Advances in Rapid Diagnostic Tests for Malaria: A Landscape Review and Analysis.
articles.authors: Zhang S, Lauff A, Morton JF, Kalanga N, Drain PK
articles.details: This landscape review and analysis identified 67 malaria rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) for blood-based Plasmodium biomarkers, from 27 different international manufacturers, that are commercially available or in late-stage development. Most are simple to use on a whole blood specimen, provide results within 30 minutes, and report high diagnostic sensitivity and specificity (≥98%). The WHO has endorsed malaria RDTs for first-line testing, and next-generation assays are being developed to detect multiple Plasmodium antigens; the authors note that future RDTs to detect drug resistance would be beneficial if drug resistance continues to expand. Clinical and implementation science studies will be needed to determine optimal delivery and care strategies in malaria-endemic settings.

articles.headline: Genomic surveillance maps antimalarial resistance clusters in Eswatini
articles.title_original: Genomic Surveillance Reveals Clusters of Plasmodium falciparum Antimalarial Resistance Markers in Eswatini, A Low-Transmission Setting.
articles.authors: Nhlengethwa N, Aranda-Díaz A, Vilakati S, Hubbard A, Routledge I, Lauterbach SB, Makhanthisa TI, De Amaral F, Chisenga M, Mangena B, Sikaala C, Chimumbwa J, Dlamini Q, Raman J, Smith JL, Dlamini SV
articles.details: This genomic surveillance study used multiplexed amplicon deep sequencing to genotype 12 antimalarial-resistance genes, detect hrp2/3 deletions, and assess relatedness in 437 samples from RDT-positive and symptomatic RDT-negative individuals in Eswatini, a low-transmission setting. The artemisinin partial-resistance marker kelch13 P553L appeared in a cluster of four infections (0.9% of samples), likely from importation; the DHPS/DHFR sextuple mutant (high-level sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance) was found in 7.8%, and the mdr1 N86 genotype (reduced lumefantrine susceptibility) was nearly fixed. Over 60% of infections were polyclonal, no hrp2/3 deletions were detected, and non-falciparum coinfections were rare (<1%).

articles.headline: Modelling shows larviciding plus ITNs can interrupt malaria transmission in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Modelling the impact of larviciding as a supplementary malaria vector control intervention in rural south-eastern Tanzania: A district-level simulation study.
articles.authors: Shirima G, Fairbanks EL, Gavana T, Kiwelu G, Nambunga I, Mlacha YP, Mirau S, Chaki P, Chitnis N, Kiware S
articles.details: This district-level simulation study adapted and extended the Vector Control Optimization Model (VCOM) to incorporate local seasonality and assess larviciding combined with insecticide-treated nets (ITNs) against Anopheles gambiae s.l. in Tanzania, parameterized with district-level field data on mosquito mortality and Bayesian inference. The immature mosquito mortality rate due to larviciding was 61% based on field data. At 80% ITN coverage, larviciding substantially reduced mosquito density and entomological inoculation rate (EIR); combining 80% ITNs with larviciding coverage ≥60% lowered EIR below 1 infectious bite per person per year, the threshold required to interrupt transmission. The authors conclude that larviciding is an effective complement to ITNs for interrupting malaria transmission.

articles.headline: PH-domain protein PyCryPH2 shapes Plasmodium crystalloids and sporozoite maturation
articles.title_original: A pleckstrin homology domain protein is involved in crystalloid formation in Plasmodium ookinetes and affects the maturation of infective sporozoites.
articles.authors: Tachibana M, Shinzawa N, Baba M, Torii M, Culleton R, Ishino T
articles.details: This molecular/genetic study in the malaria parasite Plasmodium yoelii characterized PyCryPH2, a novel crystalloid protein containing a pleckstrin homology (PH) domain and a paralog of the previously identified CryPH. Targeted disruption of the pycryph2 gene produced an irregular microstructure of particles within the crystalloid organelle. Although pycryph2-deficient parasites formed morphologically normal sporozoites, they had a significantly reduced ability to invade mosquito salivary glands and to infect mouse liver. The authors conclude that PyCryPH2 helps form well-organized crystalloids and contributes to the maturation of functional sporozoites.
