articles.headline: Presumptive malaria treatment common despite testing in Gabonese children
articles.title_original: Diagnostic and therapeutic practices for paediatric malaria: Gap between guidelines and clinical reality in south-east Gabon.
articles.authors: Biteghe-Bi-Essone JC, Imboumy-Limoukou RK, Noronha ID, Moukodoum DN, Ontoua SS, Atiga N, Kouna LC, Okouga AP, Lekana-Douki JB, Oyegue-Liabagui LS
articles.details: This prospective observational study assessed adherence to WHO recommendations for paediatric malaria management in Franceville, south-east Gabon, from January 2021 to July 2023, including 3,195 children aged 0–16 with fever or fever history. Overall 41.6% tested positive by thick blood smear, and 21.4% of those had severe malaria. Despite negative smears, 86.7% received a presumptive diagnosis and 98.8% of those were treated with antimalarials. Presumptive diagnosis was significantly associated with asthenia (aOR=1.41, 95% CI 1.01–2.00), headache (aOR=1.71, 95% CI 1.02–3.03), and nausea-vomiting (aOR=1.95, 95% CI 1.20–3.31). Authors warn of overprescription and resistance risk.

articles.headline: Meta-analysis finds no clear ITN link to falciparum infection
articles.title_original: Impact of Insecticide-Treated Nets on Plasmodium falciparum Infection Rates: A Meta-Analysis.
articles.authors: Karaca Biçakçi N, Çalmaz A, Ayyildiz Akin M, Tan CS, Barasarathi J, Oyinloye BE, Umidjon A, Egamberdiyev K, Avazmetova I
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis synthesized 2021–2025 epidemiological evidence on insecticide-treated nets (ITNs/LLINs) and Plasmodium falciparum infection prevalence, with 10 of 3,151 records meeting inclusion criteria. The crude-effects analysis (six studies, 7,466 participants) gave a pooled odds ratio of 0.67 (95% CI 0.42–1.07; p=0.078), with high heterogeneity (I²=79.8%); the adjusted analysis (nine studies) gave aOR=0.88 (95% CI 0.42–1.86; p=0.71; I²=88.7%). Subgroups showed ITNs OR=0.72 and LLINs OR=0.59, with significant effect modification for children under five (p=0.0098) and Uganda studies (p=0.0378). Authors found no conclusive significant association and stress context-dependence.

articles.headline: IPTp dose compliance very low in Sierra Leone antenatal study
articles.title_original: Compliance with intermittent preventive malaria treatment in pregnancy at a tertiary hospital in Sierra Leone.
articles.authors: Lahai WK, Vandy AO, Prajitha KC, Fofanah BD, Kamau EM, Owusu H, Squire JS, Barrie HA, Kamara IF, Falama AM, Sesay N, Kamara ARY, Kanu F, Kanu MS, Kamara MM, Sesay MA, Koroma JA, Moses F, Lakoh S, Kenneh S, Grant LF
articles.details: This non-concurrent cohort study used routinely collected 2024 data from Princess Christian Maternity Hospital, a tertiary teaching hospital in Freetown, Sierra Leone, to assess intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp). Among 733 antenatal attendees, 682 (93%) registered after the first trimester and only 30 (4%) had ≥4 antenatal contacts. Only 406 (55%) received a first dose and just 62 (8%) received the recommended three doses (maximum six in one woman). In adjusted analysis, women with fewer than four antenatal contacts had three times higher risk of non-compliance (adjusted relative risk 2.7, 95% CI 1.6–4.5). Authors call for increasing antenatal contacts.

articles.headline: Suboptimal artesunate dosing linked to worse outcomes, Sierra Leone
articles.title_original: Parenteral artesunate compliance and hospital outcomes in children under five with suspected severe malaria in Sierra Leone.
articles.authors: Kamara ARY, Kamara IF, Thekkur P, Falama AM, Sillah-Kanu M, Lahai WK, Fiona OO, George A, Fofanah BD, Sesay N, Tengbe SM, Farma-Grant L, Bah A, Thomas F, Tejan YS, Kanu JS, Lakoh S, Mustapha M, Zachariah R, Chandrasekharan Prathija K, Kenneh SM
articles.details: This cohort study used routinely collected data from Ola During Children's Hospital, a tertiary paediatric hospital in Freetown, Sierra Leone, from January to December 2024, evaluating guideline compliance and outcomes in children under five admitted with suspected severe malaria. Of 735 admitted children, 657 (89%) had rapid diagnostic tests but none had microscopic confirmation; 650 (88%) received all three parenteral artesunate doses at 0, 12, and 24 h, though correct weight-based dosing and scheduling occurred in only 420 (compliance 57%). Overall 642 (87%) were discharged and 73 (10%) had unfavourable outcomes, including 59 deaths. Underweight status, multiple convulsions, and incorrect artesunate dosing were associated with unfavourable outcomes.

articles.headline: Malaria hotspot mapping reveals persistent clusters in Sierra Leone
articles.title_original: Mapping malaria hotspots through spatial and spatio-temporal analysis in Sierra Leone, 2021-2024.
articles.authors: Falama AM, Omoniwa O, Kanu MS, Lahai WK, Kamara ARY, Ameh G, Kamara IF, Fofanah BD, Kenneh S, Kamara MN, Sesay MA, Sesay N, Kanu F, Alwani AA, Thekkur P, Zachariah R, Kanu MS, Lakoh S
articles.details: Cross-sectional geospatial study using DHIS2 data analyzed the spatial and spatio-temporal distribution of malaria incidence and its relationship with rainfall in surrounding areas in Sierra Leone, 2021–2024, applying Moran's I, space-time Poisson models, and bivariate Moran's I. 7.4 million malaria cases were reported, with chiefdom-level incidence ranging from 21.2 to >750 per 1,000 population. Significant spatial clustering was found (Moran's I > 0; P < 0.01), with persistent high-high and low-low clusters; space-time analysis identified high-risk districts (RR 1.2–1.9) and low-risk districts (RR 0.5–0.9; P < 0.01). No significant national association between incidence and surrounding rainfall was observed.

articles.headline: Correction to P. falciparum genomic and resistance study, Sierra Leone
articles.title_original: Correction: Comprehensive genomic study of Plasmodium falciparum in Sierra Leone: genetic variation and resistance patterns.
articles.authors: Zhan XJ, Chen SB, Wang TY, Duan YW, Shen HM, Chen JH, Xie JJ
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Mosquito ecology and antibody evidence of malaria exposure, Central Java
articles.title_original: Anopheles ecology and serological evidence of malaria exposure in clinically malaria-free areas of Menoreh Hills Central Java, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Muh F, Qanita NG, Hitipeuw D, Saputra EY, Nuranindita R, Wusono AD, Natanegara S, Daud MM, Rahayu AA, Pratama DA, Putri FA, Syahada JH, Fitriana F, Jun H, Jeon BY, Cahyani NKD, Pandupuspitasari NS, Lee MR, Ju JW, Han JH
articles.details: This field study examined the ecology, spatial distribution, and host-feeding behavior of Anopheles mosquitoes, along with larval habitat characteristics and human serological evidence of exposure, across four villages in a low-transmission region approaching elimination in the Menoreh Hills of Central Java. Dominant vector species were consistently present throughout the night and closely associated with livestock environments, with opportunistic feeding on both humans and livestock. Serological findings revealed marked differences in exposure between villages, with higher antibody responses in some locations despite the absence of clinical cases. The authors conclude that localized ecological conditions, vector behavior, and human practices jointly shape malaria risk, highlighting the importance of context-specific approaches.

articles.headline: CRISPR-ready hepatocytes enable study of malaria liver-stage host factors
articles.title_original: Cas9-expressing HC-04 hepatocytes facilitate CRISPR-based analysis of Plasmodium falciparum sporozoite-host interactions.
articles.authors: Verzier LH, Hesping E, Doerflinger M, Herold MJ, Boddey JA
articles.details: Researchers engineered human HC-04 hepatocytes, the only known cell line supporting P. falciparum liver-stage development, to express Cas9-mCherry for CRISPR-based functional genomics of sporozoite traversal, invasion, and intracellular development. They validated Cas9 activity in HC-04.2B3, demonstrated guide-RNA-directed gene disruption via non-homologous end joining, and optimized traversal and invasion assays with a cytometric readout. Disruption of 10 human genes previously implicated in bacterial and viral infection confirmed the platform's utility, providing a basis for genome-wide CRISPR screens of hepatocyte host determinants of infection.

articles.headline: Comment on whole-genome sequencing of Plasmodium in UK travellers
articles.title_original: Comment on Rapid Whole genome sequencing of Plasmodium DNA from cryptic malaria cases in UK travellers provides insights into infection origins, transmission, and antimalarial resistance.
articles.authors: Chen Q
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Post-acute symptoms persist longer after arboviruses than malaria in travellers
articles.title_original: Frequency and persistence of post-acute symptoms after chikungunya, dengue, Zika, and malaria in travellers: a prospective multi-centre study.
articles.authors: Hamer DH, Loreau JM, Huits R, Díaz-Menéndez M, Grobusch MP, Barkati S, Yansouni CP, Gobbi F, Bottieau E, Chen LH, Duvignaud A, Malvy D, Castelli F, Odolini S, De Pijper CA, Asgeirsson H, Moro L, Yamamoto K, Sánchez-Montalvá A, Zeana C, Kelly P, Piyaphanee W, Bourque DL, McCarthy A, Simon F, Gautret P, Mardon JB, Texier G, Javelle E
articles.details: This prospective multi-centre observational study at 15 GeoSentinel sites enrolled 273 travellers with confirmed acute chikungunya (35), dengue (110), Zika (19), or falciparum malaria (109), following symptoms and SF-12 quality of life at 1, 3, 6, 12, and 18 months. At month 3, persistent symptoms affected 24/35 (69%) chikungunya, 27/110 (25%) dengue, 8/19 (42%) Zika, but only 12/109 (11%) malaria patients; impaired quality of life showed a similar pattern (4% in malaria). Female sex, Zika, chikungunya, and acute musculoskeletal symptoms predicted month-3 persistence. The authors conclude post-arboviral symptoms persisted beyond six months, whereas post-malaria fatigue resolved within three months.

articles.headline: Review of how subclinical Plasmodium infections silently sustain malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Mechanistic insights into subclinical Plasmodium infections: Unveiling the silent drivers of malaria transmission.
articles.authors: Ranjha R, Gupta A, Singh K, Sikka R, Anvikar AR, Gupta H, Bharti PK
articles.details: This article examines subclinical and asymptomatic Plasmodium infections, which often evade conventional diagnostic tools due to low parasite densities yet serve as hidden reservoirs that sustain transmission. It discusses parasite factors (density thresholds, antigenic variation, cytoadhesion, multiplicity of infection, and emerging drug resistance) and host factors (naturally acquired immunity, age, pregnancy, genetic polymorphisms, and epigenetic influences), and links chronic infection to anemia, impaired cognitive development in children, and adverse pregnancy outcomes. The authors examine the epidemiological burden, biological mechanisms, diagnostic challenges, and health consequences, underscoring the urgent need for field-deployable, sensitive diagnostic tools and integrative approaches to support malaria elimination.

articles.headline: Household micro-mosaic indoor spraying matches conventional deployment in rural Zambia
articles.title_original: Indoor residual spraying of distinct insecticide formulations in a household-scale micro-mosaic format: Feasibility and effectiveness for malaria vector control in rural Zambia.
articles.authors: Chinula D, Mumba K, Zulu R, Ndhlovu K, Kavishe DR, Hobbs NP, Kiware S, Reed T, Hamainza B, Killeen GF
articles.details: This programmatic pilot in Luangwa district, Zambia tested micro-mosaic indoor residual spraying (IRS), varying insecticide formulations between households, nested within a village-scale macro-mosaic for comparison. Nine health facility catchment areas were assigned one of three regimes: DDT across all houses, a clothianidin–deltamethrin (CD) mixture across all houses, or a micro-mosaic alternating DDT and CD by house, against a background of deltamethrin-PBO LLINs. Over 1620 trap nights using CDC light traps in 15 houses per area, vector catches were very low. Anopheles densities were comparable across regimes (p ≥ 0.1420), with no differences in An. funestus group (p ≥ 0.072) or An. gambiae complex (p ≥ 0.1410) composition. Micro-mosaic IRS may be as effective short-term as macro-mosaic deployment.

articles.headline: Imported P. vivax malaria surges in New York City with migration
articles.title_original: An Increase in Imported Plasmodium vivax Malaria in New York City: Clinical and Demographic Trends Following Recent Migration.
articles.authors: Berto CG, Policarpio MA, Vargas-Pena C, Antwi M, Slavinski S, Abdool A, King RN, Kojaoghlanian T, Daily JP, Prasad N, Velasquez MR, Ridpath AD, Coyle CM
articles.details: This retrospective review analyzed Plasmodium vivax cases reported to the NYC Department of Health (2018–2024) and clinical data from NYC Health + Hospitals admissions, with diagnosis by blood smear, rapid diagnostic test, or PCR. P. vivax rose from 6 of 206 malaria cases (2.9%) in 2018 to a record 63 of 346 (18.2%) in 2023, with 43 of 250 (17.2%) in 2024. Of 41 admissions, 40 (97.6%) were recent migrants, mainly from South America and Asia; severe malaria occurred in 10 (24.4%) and 5 (12.2%) needed intensive care. Primaquine was prescribed to 32 (78%) after G6PD testing, and relapses occurred in 7 (17%), mostly without liver-stage therapy. The authors stress timely diagnosis and liver-stage treatment to reduce morbidity and relapse.

articles.headline: Case report: severe falciparum malaria in late pregnancy after Ghana travel
articles.title_original: Severe malaria in pregnancy in the United States: A case report and management considerations.
articles.authors: Reyes Lizaola S, Kumari S, Di Francesco L, Chedid J, Cay Martinez C
articles.details: This single case report describes a nulliparous patient at 36 weeks 4 days of gestation presenting to a U.S. community hospital with fever, headache, and abdominal discomfort after recent travel to Ghana, with maternal fever, fetal tachycardia, thrombocytopenia, and an equivocal smear. Artemether-lumefantrine was started and escalated to artesunate when repeat testing confirmed Plasmodium falciparum at 5.4% parasitemia, meeting severe malaria criteria. The patient had spontaneous preterm prelabor rupture of membranes, underwent induced vaginal delivery, developed preeclampsia with severe features, and recovered; congenital malaria was ruled out. The authors highlight diagnostic challenges, rapid progression, and gaps in pregnancy-specific guidance, suggesting algorithms for trimester-specific dosing, fetal surveillance, and neonatal screening.

articles.headline: New tRNA-synthetase-hijacking compounds kill P. falciparum, resist resistance
articles.title_original: Potent reaction hijacking inhibitors of Plasmodium falciparum asparagine tRNA synthetase.
articles.authors: Ye X, Esherick LY, Ketprasit N, Narwal SK, Godoy LC, Boonyalai N, Dogovski C, Morton CJ, Rabie T, Famodimu MT, Tai CW, Yeo T, Le LHM, Leeming MG, Laureano De Souza M, Chenu E, Creek DJ, Delves MJ, Birkholtz LM, Duffy J, Lobb K, Durst G, Lee MCS, Fidock DA, Niles JC, Siegel MG, Tilley L, Xie SC
articles.details: This drug-discovery study characterized a series of 4-amino-quinazoline-based sulfonamides against Plasmodium falciparum. Exemplar compounds showed nanomolar potency against blood-stage cultures with up to 300-fold selectivity over a mammalian cell line, were active against transmissible stages, and were refractory to resistance development. Mass spectrometry showed the compounds act as reaction-hijacking inhibitors of aminoacyl tRNA synthetases; subtle structural changes switched the main target from cytoplasmic threonine synthetase (PfThrRS) to asparagine synthetase (PfAsnRS), increasing potency and selectivity. Human AsnRS was less susceptible. Targets were confirmed by selective knock-down and tolerance selection.

articles.headline: Review finds low zoonotic malaria infection in Thailand's Anopheles mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Systematic review of Anopheles abundance and meta-analysis of nonhuman primate malaria infection in mosquitoes in Thailand.
articles.authors: Weerakoon SN, Hii J, Nararak J, Sukkanon C, Ngoen-Klan R, Arunyawat U, Maneeon P, Thongsahuan S, Vythilingam I, Manguin S, Chareonviriyaphap T
articles.details: This systematic review and PRISMA-guided meta-analysis assessed Anopheles distribution, bionomics, and nonhuman primate (NHP) malaria infectivity in mosquitoes in Thailand, including 92 of 811 accessed papers from a 2013–2025 literature search. The pooled mosquito infection prevalence was 0.01 (95% CI 0.00–0.03), indicating low NHP parasite infectivity, with a pooled infection prevalence of 0.2% by nested PCR. Anopheles sawadwongporni, An. minimus, and An. dirus were incriminated as zoonotic vectors. The authors call for targeted, context-specific vector control addressing local species' feeding and resting behaviors.

articles.headline: Hybrid CNN diagnoses malaria from blood smears with high accuracy
articles.title_original: A hybrid machine learning approach for automated malaria diagnosis from thin blood smear images.
articles.authors: Siddiqui GA, Bera A, Lhila A, Rajput AS, Nitika N, Bharti PK, Das A, Mahapatra T
articles.details: This study developed a hybrid Inception-v3 convolutional neural network for automated malaria diagnosis from thin blood smear images, using 15,938 microscopic images from the National Institute of Malaria Research (ICMR-NIMR). Images were preprocessed and segmented with Otsu thresholding before training. The model achieved 98.9% training accuracy, 97.3% sensitivity, and 99.9% specificity, with high F1 and AUC values, outperforming other machine learning classifiers and comparing favorably to conventional microscopy. The authors conclude the framework could reduce reliance on expert microscopists and strengthen malaria control in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: P. falciparum MSP-2 diversity and mixed infections in N'Djamena, Chad
articles.title_original: Genetic polymorphism of Plasmodium falciparum MSP-2 (3D7/IC) and multiplicity of infection in N'Djamena, Chad.
articles.authors: Cedric Y, Diomède N, Hervé MM, Pacome TK, Abel D, Ngaryedji T, Saleh B, Hu H, Christelle Nadia NA
articles.details: This cross-sectional study assessed P. falciparum merozoite surface protein 2 (msp2) 3D7/IC allelic diversity and multiplicity of infection among 217 patients at a hospital in N'Djamena, Chad. P. falciparum prevalence was 23.5%, and the msp2 3D7/IC allelic family was found in 94.11% of samples, with one predominant genotype (18.46%). Single-clone infections were most frequent (64.65%), followed by double (33.33%) and triple (2.08%) infections, with no quadruple infections. Phylogenetic analysis revealed eight clades. The authors conclude there is substantial genetic diversity reflecting ongoing transmission, underscoring the need for expanded molecular surveillance.

articles.headline: New avian malaria species described in stranded Cory's Shearwater
articles.title_original: A new Plasmodium Marchiafava & Celli, 1885 (Apicomplexa: Haemosporida) species in Cory's Shearwater (Calonectris borealis) [Cory]) (Aves: Procellariiformes) stranded in a coastal area in Brazil.
articles.authors: Dos Anjos CC, Damato BA, Ferioli RB, Sánchez-Sarmiento AM, da Mata AR, Barbosa CB, Neto HG, Chagas CRF, Valkiūnas G, Kirchgatter K
articles.details: This study identified and characterized a new Plasmodium species in an adult male Cory's Shearwater (Calonectris borealis) found live-stranded on the northern coast of São Paulo, Brazil. Blood smears showed parasitemia ranging from 0.04% to 2.09%, and PCR of a cytochrome b fragment yielded a sequence 100% similar to lineage pLK06, placing the parasite in the Novyella clade with morphology close to P. vaughani. Necropsy and histopathology showed hepato- and splenomegaly, hepatitis, and pulmonary hemorrhage, with clinical relapse despite anti-malaria treatment. The authors describe Plasmodium borealis sp. nov., the first haemosporidian reported in this seabird.

articles.headline: Most fevers in southern Angola are not malaria
articles.title_original: Age-Related and Seasonal Variation in Malaria and Other Causes of Fever and Their Association With Clinical Outcomes in Southern Angola: A Hospital-Based Study.
articles.authors: Gonçalves HEM, Antunes ML, Seixas J, Ferreira MU
articles.details: Hospital-based study of 849 febrile patients (age 2 months to 94 years) at two tertiary hospitals in Lubango city, southern Angola, enrolled September–October 2023 and May–June 2024, using clinical assessment, local diagnostics, and logistic regression. 67.6% tested negative for malaria by microscopy; leading nonmalarial causes were lower respiratory (19.5%), gastrointestinal (16.7%), and upper respiratory (13.0%) infections, with malaria predominating early in the dry season. Malaria and lower respiratory infections raised hospitalization risk (OR 3.20 and 5.17, p<0.0001); malaria and CNS infections raised mortality (OR 1.90 and 6.30, p<0.0001). Authors conclude better diagnostics are needed to guide treatment and curb unnecessary antibiotic use.

articles.headline: Wolbachia wAnD strain linked to fewer Plasmodium sporozoites in mosquitoes
articles.title_original: The presence of the wAnD strain of Wolbachia is correlated with lower levels of Plasmodium sporozoites and a less diverse microbiome in wild Anopheles demeilloni mosquito cephalothoraxes.
articles.authors: Sougoufara S, Bandibabone J, Chatterley L, Hughes I, Taberes CM, Ball J, Palliaser T, Dhokiya V, Heinz E, Hughes G, Walker T
articles.details: Study of wild-caught Anopheles demeilloni female body parts from Sud Kivu, Democratic Republic of Congo (collected 2021 and 2024), using qPCR, 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing, and Sanger sequencing of the wsp gene to test correlations between the wAnD Wolbachia strain, P. falciparum sporozoites, and the microbiome. Wolbachia prevalence and density were significantly higher in abdomens than cephalothoraxes (P<0.0001). Overall sporozoite prevalence was 1.3% (9/704), not significantly different between Wolbachia-positive and -negative cephalothoraxes (P=0.3630), though P. falciparum was only found in Wolbachia-negative cephalothoraxes; Wolbachia-positive abdomens had a lower sporozoite rate (P=0.0329). Cephalothorax microbiome composition differed by Wolbachia status (P<0.05). Authors suggest wAnD could be explored for malaria biocontrol.

articles.headline: Systematic review of malaria and leishmaniasis epidemiology in Thailand
articles.title_original: Epidemiology of malaria and leishmaniasis in Thailand (2004-2025): A systematic review.
articles.authors: Apajamjarut W, Siriwongsanon N, Tonpon P, Boonlertworakul L, Joosiriwong R, Soe BK, Kaewmee S, Saingamsook J, Walton C, Lee SJ, Lin C, Dimopoulos G, Jariyapan N
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: PRISMA systematic review synthesizing the epidemiology of malaria and leishmaniasis in Thailand from 2004 to 2025, searching PubMed, Scopus, and ScienceDirect plus grey literature. From 1397 records, 57 studies were included (36 malaria, 21 leishmaniasis). Nine Plasmodium species were documented, including the zoonotic P. knowlesi and P. cynomolgi; P. vivax and P. falciparum predominated nationwide, with P. falciparum declining since 2012, and transmission persisted in border and forest-associated areas. Six Leishmania species were identified, with L. martiniquensis and L. orientalis predominant among people living with HIV. Seven provinces reported both diseases. Authors recommend integrated surveillance within a One Health framework.

articles.headline: IL-6 outperforms HO-1 as a host biomarker for malaria
articles.title_original: Plasma haem oxygenase-1 and interleukin-6 as adjunct host biomarkers associated with malaria.
articles.authors: Wakai T, Kintung I, Ogundimu T, Chinedu S, Afolabi I
articles.details: Cross-sectional hospital-based study evaluating plasma haem oxygenase-1 (HO-1) and interleukin-6 (IL-6) as host-response biomarkers in a malaria-endemic population; 647 individuals were screened and 304 with complete microscopy and haematological data retained (152 microscopy-positive, 152 microscopy-negative), with a 40-participant subset used for ELISA and ROC analyses. Microscopy-positive participants had lower haemoglobin, haematocrit, red-cell and platelet counts and higher temperature and white-cell counts (p<0.001). IL-6 was higher in malaria-positive participants (median 141.28 vs 106.58 pg/mL, p=0.027), while HO-1 showed a non-significant rise (230.98 vs 193.73 ng/mL, p=0.131). IL-6 correlated with HO-1 (rho=0.702), parasitaemia (rho=0.387), and negatively with haemoglobin (rho=-0.417). IL-6 AUC was 0.709, HO-1 0.641, combined 0.687. Authors conclude IL-6 is the more informative marker, with HO-1 needing further validation.

articles.headline: Citronella and pyrethroids tested against coastal Thai malaria vector
articles.title_original: Do natural or synthetic excito-repellents work better? A study on coastal malaria vector Anopheles epiroticus in Ko Chang, Thailand.
articles.authors: Sukkanon C, Jabjai S, Ritthison W, Chareonviriyaphap T, Nararak J
articles.details: Excito-repellency assay study comparing behavioral escape responses of laboratory and field strains of the coastal malaria vector Anopheles epiroticus from Ko Chang, Trat Province, eastern Thailand, to four synthetic and two natural repellents under contact and non-contact conditions. In the laboratory strain, deltamethrin (42%) and permethrin (35%) at LC50 gave the highest contact escape, alpha-cypermethrin produced limited effect (4–14%), and citronella oil at 5.0% gave the strongest non-contact repellency (>50%), outperforming DEET (18–26%) and vetiver oil (26–40%). The field strain differed: DEET (64%) and deltamethrin (60%) were most effective in contact trials, while citronella and vetiver oils had minimal non-contact effect (~14%). Authors stress accounting for behavioral variation between strains when designing vector control.

articles.headline: Review: insecticide resistance and Anopheles ability to transmit malaria
articles.title_original: Insecticide resistance and malaria transmission: divergent effects on Anopheles vector competence.
articles.authors: Adeboye SO, Nwinyi OC, Ayanda OI
articles.details: This review examines how insecticide resistance in Anopheles mosquitoes affects vector competence—their ability to acquire, harbour, and transmit Plasmodium. It describes resistance mechanisms including target-site mutation, metabolic resistance, cuticular thickening, and behavioural resistance, and notes contradictory evidence: some studies report impaired parasite development following insecticide exposure while others report enhanced vector competence. The authors conclude the relationship remains poorly understood and underexplored, and argue that integrating omics-based approaches grounded in ecological reality is crucial to resolve current contradictions and accurately predict the epidemiological implications for malaria transmission.

articles.headline: Pyrethroid resistance escalates in Anopheles funestus across Kenya's Rift Valley
articles.title_original: Signature of resistance gene evolution and pyrethroid resistance escalation in the major malaria vector Anopheles funestus across Kenyan malaria-endemic regions separated by the Rift Valley.
articles.authors: Tchouassi DP, Mustapha AM, Rotich G, Milugo TK, Musimbi B, Oruni A, Tagne CSD, Kamau L, Gadji M, Tchouakui M, Torto B, Wondji CS
articles.details: Researchers genotyped Anopheles funestus s.s. for resistance markers across western, coastal, and Kerio Valley (KV) regions of Kenya separated by the Rift Valley, using PCR, TaqMan plus nested PCR for Plasmodium sporozoites, and WHO phenotypic assays on F1 progeny. The 4.3kb-SV (n=336) and G454A-Cyp9k1 (n=445) alleles were nearly fixed in western Kenya but declined toward the coast, while L119F-GSTe2 (n=392) increased west-to-coast. Infection odds were lower with L119F-GSTe2-RR versus RS (OR 0.2, P=0.046) and higher with the 4.3kb R allele (OR 5.7, P=0.049). Coastal mosquitoes showed extreme permethrin resistance (<10% mortality at 10× dose); all populations stayed susceptible to bendiocarb, pirimiphos-methyl, clothianidin, and chlorfenapyr.

articles.headline: Plasmodium falciparum myosin PfMyoF carries unique Rab-like trafficking domain
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum PfMyoF contains a Rab-like tail domain and associates with perinuclear membrane trafficking proteins.
articles.authors: Holmes AJ, Ilani P, Batters C, Kemp A, Aniweh Y, Amenga-Etego LN, Awandare GA, Rayner JC, Buss F
articles.details: Researchers characterized PfMyoF, a class XXII myosin of Plasmodium falciparum, using structural prediction, biochemical assays, and imaging. PfMyoF is a plus-end-directed, processive motor with a long neck region able to bind up to six copies of the calmodulin homologue PfCaM, and its tail contains predicted WD40 and Rab-like domains not identified in any other eukaryotic myosin class. Pull-down experiments revealed interactions with trafficking proteins including the vesicle marker PfRab18, and the protein localises to a perinuclear membrane compartment. Transient glmS-ribozyme knockdown impaired growth, suggesting an important role in membrane trafficking during asexual blood-stage replication.

articles.headline: Microhaplotype genotyping tracks cross-border P. vivax spread in South Asia
articles.title_original: Genetic evidence of cross-border Plasmodium vivax spread in a malaria pre-elimination region of South Asia.
articles.authors: Rai A, Sutanto E, Ghimire P, Wangchuk S, Drukpa T, Alam MS, Ghanchi N, Rahim AG, Rumaseb A, Trimarsanto H, Hoon KS, Adhikari N, Adhikari S, Banjara MR, Rijal KR, Nepal R, Regmi RS, Qurashi B, Zaidi SE, Beg MA, Ley B, Price RN, Thriemer K, Auburn S
articles.details: Using the 93-microhaplotype vivaxGEN panel, researchers genotyped Plasmodium vivax from clinical-trial patients in Bangladesh (n=35), Bhutan (n=27), and Nepal (n=19) collected 2013–2023, comparing with open-access data from Afghanistan (n=159), India (n=24), and Pakistan (n=213) from 2014–2024. High-complexity infections were observed in 27.04% (43/159) of Afghan, 16.43% (35/213) of Pakistani, and 28.57% (10/35) of Bangladeshi isolates, but were low in Bhutan (3.70%; 1/27) and Nepal (5.26%; 1/19). Identity-by-descent analyses revealed three genetic clusters and two Bhutan sub-populations separating local from cross-border imported cases. The authors conclude high-resolution genetic data enhance monitoring of transmission intensity and cross-border importation.

articles.headline: Active EBV infection linked to severe malaria in infants under two
articles.title_original: Infants under 24 months with severe malaria have a serological profile of active infection with Epstein Barr Virus.
articles.authors: Cheng WT, Fogang B, Jain A, Davies DH, Felgner PL, Eboumbou C, Koki PN, Speck SH, Joyner CJ, Ayong LS, Lamb TJ
articles.details: This cross-sectional study enrolled 195 parasitaemic infants aged 6–24 months in Cameroon, classified malaria severity by WHO criteria, determined EBV status by standard serology, and quantified the magnitude, breadth, and invasion-blocking capacity of anti-Plasmodium falciparum antibodies. 24% of children were serologically positive for active EBV infection, and the highest proportion of severe malaria cases occurred in children with active EBV. These children showed elevated antibody magnitude and breadth with increased in vitro invasion-blocking capacity, though in vivo circulating parasitaemia was similar. The authors conclude primary EBV infection may be a risk factor for developing severe falciparum malaria in young children.

articles.headline: Fecal DNA detects Plasmodium knowlesi in wild macaques across Asia
articles.title_original: Tracking Plasmodium knowlesi Through Fecal DNA for Monitoring Zoonotic Transmission in Wild Macaques Across Southeast and South Asia.
articles.authors: Wannigama DL, Amarasiri M, Phattharapornjaroen P, Hurst C, Modchang C, Cynthia B, Miyanaga K, Cui L, Fernandez S, Melhem NM, Luk-In S, Singer AC, Ragupathi NKD, Htun TS, Sei K, Ngamwongsatit N, Hui NS, Shimotai Y, Ounjai P, Kanthawee P, Tacharoenmuang R, Kauba A, Eang C, Romone L, Dharne M, de Araujo JC, Ndatuwong LG, Werawatte WKCP, Chanthasiri T, Zhao J, Mori H, Besa JJV, Kurt Ö, Kanjanabuch T, Zahraei-Ramazani AR, Higgins PG, Aoyagi T, Kicic A, Trowsdale S, Hongsing P, Yang X, Wang Y, Khatib A, Sano D, Shibuya K, Abe S, Hamamoto H
articles.details: A noninvasive surveillance study screened 4752 fecal samples from wild macaques across 9 endemic countries for Plasmodium knowlesi DNA. Parasite DNA was detected in 390 samples (8.2%), with positivity rates ranging from 1.4% to 18.4%. The authors report this as the first field-based evidence that P. knowlesi DNA can be detected in naturally shed macaque feces, and conclude that fecal sampling is a practical, scalable tool for tracking zoonotic malaria and could be integrated into forest-runoff and rural wastewater surveillance for early pathogen detection at the human-wildlife interface.

articles.headline: Phase 1 trial finds DON well tolerated in adults with malaria
articles.title_original: Safety, Tolerability, and Pharmacokinetics of 6-Diazo-5-Oxo-L-Norleucine in Malawian Adults With and Without Malaria: A Phase 1 Dose-Escalation Clinical Trial.
articles.authors: Riggle BA, Chamzas A, Gopalakrishnan M, Nyirenda OM, Nampota-Nkomba N, Mallewa JE, Masonga R, Manyozo C, Zuze K, Liomba AM, Alt J, Rais R, Slusher B, Moodley N, Miller LH, Pierce SK, O'Brien NF, Laurens MB, Postels DG
articles.details: An open-label, prospective, dose-escalation phase 1 trial in Blantyre, Malawi tested single-dose intravenous 6-diazo-5-oxo-L-norleucine (DON), a glutamine antagonist proposed as adjuvant therapy for cerebral malaria. Forty healthy adults and 38 adults with uncomplicated malaria received DON in dose-cohorts of 10 at 0.1, 1.0, 5.0, or 10 mg/kg. Transient, asymptomatic creatinine elevation occurred at 12 hours in 18% of participants, and nausea and vomiting were more frequent at 5.0 and 10 mg/kg; all DON-related adverse events resolved without sequelae. Pharmacokinetics were minimally influenced by malaria (terminal half-life 1.7–4.1 hours), and the authors conclude DON was well tolerated, informing dosing for children with cerebral malaria (NCT05478720).

articles.headline: Urban house sparrows show lower avian malaria prevalence
articles.title_original: Interactions between urbanization, malaria infection and avian cloacal microbiome.
articles.authors: Garrigós M, Jiménez-Peñuela J, Saavedra I, Veiga J, García-López MJ, Garrido M, Ruiz-López MJ, Figuerola J, Moreno-Indias I, Martínez-de la Puente J
articles.details: This study examined associations between habitat urbanization, avian malaria infection (Plasmodium and Haemoproteus grouped together), and cloacal microbiome composition in wild house sparrows (Passer domesticus). Urban birds had lower parasite prevalence than those from natural and rural habitats, and the link between infection and cloacal bacterial-microbiome composition depended on habitat type. In natural habitats, infected birds showed a nearly significant increase in bacterial richness and significant differences in the relative abundance of various taxa versus uninfected birds, whereas infection status was not associated with any microbiome parameter in rural or urban birds. The authors conclude habitat type is associated with malaria prevalence and may modulate the parasite–microbiome relationship.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic carriers drive malaria transmission; cryptic P. ovale found
articles.title_original: Uneven Plasmodium falciparum Transmission and Cryptic ovale Transmission From the Asymptomatic Reservoir in Bagamoyo, Tanzania.
articles.authors: Lin JT, Mathias DK, Yang G, Loya M, Muller MS, Basham C, Nyasembe V, Amagai K, Rutha I, Gaither C, Nyange M, Said H, Chhetri SB, Swinehart B, Lin FC, Dinglasan RR, Juliano JJ, Tarimo B, Ngasala B
articles.details: In Bagamoyo, Tanzania, direct skin feeding (DSF) assays with colony-reared Anopheles gambiae measured the infectiousness of asymptomatic carriers over 5 years of age positive for P. falciparum by RDT or PCR. Among 491 participants (median density 5.1 parasites/μL), 22% were infectious to mosquitoes, and 9.1% (342/3741) of mosquitoes fed on infectious carriers were parasite-positive; RDT-positive carriers infected roughly twice as many mosquitoes as RDT-negative/PCR-positive persons. The top 16 transmitters (3% of those undergoing DSF) infected 57% of infected mosquitoes, and RDT-positive children aged 6–15 (27% of the cohort) contributed 58%. Unexpectedly, midguts from 39 DSFs (44% of oocyst-positive feeds analyzed) tested positive for P. ovale, indicating frequent co-transmission.

articles.headline: Pfs230 D7 DNA vaccine blocks P. falciparum transmission in mice
articles.title_original: Pfs230 Domains 7-10 Encoded by DNA Vaccines Induce Potent Complement-Independent Transmission-Blocking Antibody Against Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Cao Y, Hayashi CTH, Tripathi AK, Takashima E, Tsuboi T, Harju TB, Kumar N
articles.details: DNA vaccines encoding Pfs230 domains D7–D10 (D7D10) were developed and tested in BALB/c mice as a P. falciparum transmission-blocking vaccine candidate. Immunization elicited potent D7D10-specific antibodies, and purified anti-D7D10 IgG showed strong dose-dependent transmission-reducing activity in standard membrane feeding assays regardless of active complement. Western blot analysis indicated the antibodies predominantly target D7, and DNA vaccines encoding the smaller D7 and D7D8 subdomains similarly induced antibodies with strong transmission-reducing activity. The authors conclude Pfs230-D7D10, particularly D7, is a novel and promising complement-independent transmission-blocking vaccine candidate.

articles.headline: Fever boosts ring-stage P. falciparum binding to brain blood vessels
articles.title_original: Febrile Temperature Augments Ring-stage Plasmodium falciparum Adhesion to Brain Endothelial Cells.
articles.authors: Joof F, Hu R, Seydel KB, Cohee LM, Zheng Y, Smith JD
articles.details: This laboratory study examined how febrile temperature affects sequestration of ring-stage Plasmodium falciparum-infected erythrocytes. Febrile exposure increased transcript levels of several exported parasite genes involved in trafficking the PfEMP1 adhesion ligand and accelerated PfEMP1 surface display on ring-stage infected erythrocytes, producing a 2-fold increase in their binding within a perfusable 3D human brain microvessel model. Parasite exposure also enhanced binding of uninfected erythrocytes. The authors conclude that fever and parasite biomass interact in the pathogenesis of cerebral malaria.

articles.headline: Case report: dengue and falciparum malaria co-infection with ARDS
articles.details: This case report describes a 25-year-old female admitted to a critical care unit at a tertiary care center in Jharkhand, India, with co-infection of Plasmodium falciparum malaria and dengue virus. She had multiorgan failure including severe respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation, hepatic dysfunction, and hematological abnormalities; dengue was confirmed by positive NS1 antigen and IgM by ELISA, and malaria by peripheral blood smear. She developed acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) requiring intubation, lung-protective ventilation, and prone positioning. With multidisciplinary supportive care, fluid management guided by IVC distensibility index and serial lactate, and antimalarial pharmacotherapy, the patient recovered.
articles.title_original: Co-infection of Dengue and Malaria Complicated With Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS) in a Critically Ill Patient: A Case Report From a Tertiary Care Center in Jharkhand, India.
articles.authors: Chakravorty M, Bhushan K, Singh S, Ahmed A

articles.headline: Bivalent SPECT1/CSP virus-like particle vaccine protects mice from malaria
articles.title_original: Bivalent virus-like particles expressing SPECT1 and CSP trigger pre-erythrocytic malaria immunity and protect against transgenic Plasmodium falciparum sporozoite challenge in mice.
articles.authors: Turan G, Mukhopadhyay E, Truby A, Fedorova K, Peralta Alvarez MP, Alharbi NK, Hill AVS, Salman AM
articles.details: Researchers engineered recombinant Hepatitis B surface antigen virus-like particles (VLPs) displaying Plasmodium falciparum SPECT-1 alone or combined with NANP repeats and the C-terminal region of CSP, then tested them against R21 in a prime-boost regimen in BALB/c mice followed by challenge with transgenic P. berghei expressing the P. falciparum antigens. Bivalent VLPs induced IgG against both NANP and PfSPECT-1 epitopes; one candidate (N4) gave efficacy similar to R21 with Matrix-M adjuvant, while adding PfSPECT-1 reduced efficacy of another candidate (N2). NANP-specific IgG negatively correlated with blood-stage parasite load; LMQ or SMNP adjuvants did not improve responses.

articles.headline: Apical polar ring protein PfAPR3 is essential for P. falciparum invasion
articles.title_original: The apical polar ring is essential during the blood stage of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Gurung P, Back PS, Ali I, Hernandez GM, Dvorin JD
articles.details: This molecular study identified and characterized PfAPR3 as an apical polar ring (APR)-resident protein in Plasmodium falciparum. Conditional knockout of PfAPR3 blocked asexual replication: knockout parasites contacted red blood cells but failed to form a tight junction, completely blocking invasion. Using PfAPR3 as bait, the authors identified three additional APR proteins (PfAPR4, PfCHAKRA, PfAPR5) and used ultrastructure expansion microscopy to map APR biogenesis, finding PfCHAKRA critical for cytoskeletal network organization. The findings define the molecular architecture and function of the APR in P. falciparum.

articles.headline: Geospatial mapping of environmental drivers of malaria in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Environmental factors influencing mosquito breeding and malaria transmission using geospatial technologies in the Wabi Shebele sub-basin, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Merga BB, Tabor KW, Geletu MH, Zerihun E, Gemeda EA, Moisa MB, Gemeda DO
articles.details: This geospatial study assessed seven environmental factors influencing mosquito breeding and malaria transmission in the Wabi Shebele sub-basin, Ethiopia, including land use/land cover, land surface temperature (LST), vegetation, water and moisture indices, elevation, and rainfall. High LST on bare land (33.66 °C), grassland (31.76 °C), and agricultural land (31.42 °C, comprising 24.78% of land) favored vector breeding, while forested areas with lower LST (26.98 °C) and high-NDVI/low-NDWI highland zones showed low risk. Agricultural land, high LST, low elevation, high soil moisture, and moderate-to-high rainfall were the primary drivers, supporting spatially targeted control.

articles.headline: Avian malaria lineages found in declining cerulean warbler population
articles.title_original: EFFECTS OF AVIAN MALARIA IN A POPULATION OF CERULEAN WARBLER (SETOPHAGA CERULEA) WITH FIRST DOCUMENTATION OF HAEMOSPORIDIAN LINEAGES INFECTING THE SPECIES.
articles.authors: Grudens JB, Islam K
articles.details: Researchers studied avian haemosporidian infections and cytochrome-b lineages in a southern Indiana breeding population of cerulean warbler (Setophaga cerulea; n=51) over 2 years using microscopy and molecular methods. Parasitemia was low (<1% of cells infected) for most birds, indicating chronic infection, with no negative effect on male body condition (n=49); birds with Plasmodium sp. infections had higher mean body condition, possibly reflecting survivorship bias. Prevalence was 31.4% across both years, and 11 unique Haemoproteus and Plasmodium lineages were documented, 2 of them novel, providing the first haemosporidian records for this species.

articles.headline: New amplicon sequencing panels for Plasmodium vivax surveillance
articles.title_original: PvGTSeq and PvCRiSP: Two amplicon-based targeted sequencing panels for Plasmodium vivax.
articles.authors: Manrique-Valverde PC, Hasund CM, Kelley KA, Amaya-Romero JE, Arévalo-Herrera M, Brosula R, Calzada JE, Chenet SM, Corredor V, Early AM, Fontecha G, Forero-Peña DA, Herrera S, Lana JT, Laws M, Niles-Robin R, Obaldia N, Santamaria AM, Schwabl P, Auburn S, Neafsey DE
articles.details: This methods paper introduces two multiplexed amplicon sequencing panels for P. vivax genomic surveillance. PvGTSeq requires selective whole-genome amplification and contains 249 amplicons (36 for antimalarial resistance, 213 for population structure), while PvCRiSP uses four highly polymorphic amplicons without sWGA to estimate complexity of infection, detect clonal transmission, and characterize recurrences. Both achieved ≥75% amplicon recovery at parasitemias as low as five parasites/μL; PvGTSeq sequencing error rate was 3.85e-4% to 2.87e-3%. The panels were validated with samples from Colombia, Guyana, Honduras, Panama, and Venezuela, with in-silico assessment across 16 countries.

articles.headline: RTS,S antibody fine specificity correlates with malaria protection in children
articles.title_original: Antibody fine specificity correlates with protection from malaria for the RTS,S vaccine in young African children: A post hoc analysis of a phase IIb randomised controlled trial.
articles.authors: Hysa A, Opi DH, Waterhouse J, Chishimba S, Horton JL, Kingston N, Netter HJ, Wetzel D, Piontek M, Feng G, Sacarlal J, Dobaño C, Kurtovic L, Beeson JG
articles.details: This post hoc analysis of a phase IIb clinical trial assessed IgG responses to six P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein (CSP) peptides in 735 children aged 1-4 years vaccinated with RTS,S/AS01. Children with high IgG responses to a short NANP-repeat epitope (NANP2) and an N-terminal epitope (J1) had significantly reduced risk of clinical malaria (NANP2 aHR 0.838, 95% CI 0.716-0.981, p=0.028; J1 aHR 0.718, 95% CI 0.611-0.844, p<0.001), and these responses were associated with higher Fc-mediated functional activity. The authors propose NANP2 and J1 as promising correlates of protection to inform next-generation CSP-based vaccines.

articles.headline: Malaria's economic burden in Brazilian Amazon estimated near $182 million
articles.title_original: Economic burden of malaria in the Brazilian Amazon from a societal perspective.
articles.authors: Andrade MV, Noronha KVMS, Guedes GR, Carvalho LR, Bracarense H, Julião NA, Souza A, Diniz BC, Silva VA, Motta-Santos AS, Peterka C, Castro MC
articles.details: This study estimated the societal economic burden of malaria in the Brazilian Amazon, combining public health expenditures with a household survey of 1,131 individuals who had a malaria episode between January 2019 and May 2022 across nine municipalities in five states. The total economic burden in 2019 was approximately US$181.9 million (2024 PPP-USD), with the Unified Health System (SUS) bearing the largest share at 72.4%, primarily for control and prevention. Amazonas, Pará, and Roraima carried the highest total burden, while household burden was driven mainly by indirect and mortality costs and health-related quality-of-life losses.

articles.headline: Role of activated platelets in cerebral malaria pathogenesis
articles.title_original: The role of activated platelets in cerebral malaria pathogenesis.
articles.authors: Schlesinger M
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Deep learning infers demographic history of Anopheles malaria vectors
articles.title_original: On the use of generative models for demographic inference in malaria vectors from genomic data.
articles.authors: Eneli AA, Siu PC, Perez MF, Burt A, Fumagalli M, Mathieson S
articles.details: This study adapts and applies a generative deep learning algorithm to infer the joint evolutionary history of Anopheles gambiae mosquito populations sampled in Guinea and Burkina Faso. Using a model selection approach, the authors found that an evolutionary model including post-split migration fit the pair of populations better than a model without migration, and the method accurately captured population genetic differentiation. The authors conclude that machine learning and generative models are a valuable direction for jointly inferring demography and natural selection in malaria vectors to support control interventions.

articles.headline: Deformable P. falciparum gametocytes are immediately infectious to mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Defining the signature of deformable and infectious Plasmodium falciparum gametocytes.
articles.authors: Ravel D, Rijpma S, Beraldi D, Graumans W, Meerstein-Kessel L, Crouch K, Passecker A, van Gemert GJ, Blanken SL, Arinaitwe E, Rianne S, Ngotho P, Neafsey D, Voss T, Huijnen M, Bousema T, Marti M
articles.details: Using phenotypic assays (microsphere filtration for deformability; exflagellation and mosquito feeding assays for infectivity) combined with transcriptional analysis, researchers characterized the transition of Plasmodium falciparum gametocytes from immature, rigid, bone-marrow-sequestered stages to mature, deformable, circulating stages. They found major transcriptional differences between input and deformable (filtered) gametocytes, but high similarity between deformable and infectious gametocytes. The authors conclude that deformable gametocytes are immediately infectious upon release from the bone marrow, and they identified a set of infectivity markers usable as diagnostic tools to map the human infectious reservoir.

articles.headline: Ruthenium-atovaquone complexes hit both asexual and sexual Plasmodium stages
articles.title_original: Ruthenium Complexes of Atovaquone Acting on Multiple Stages of the Plasmodium Life Cycle.
articles.authors: Fabbri C, Marcon PHS, Santiago AS, Sousa CC, Quadros HC, de Sena Lamar Nunes L, de Menezes DDM, Santana RAG, Justiniano SCB, D'Alessandro S, Basilico N, Moreira DRM, de Araujo-Neto JH, Lopes SCP
articles.details: This study synthesized, characterized, and evaluated ruthenium complexes of the broad-spectrum drug atovaquone (ATV) for antiplasmodial activity. Structure-activity analysis showed that oxidation state [Ru(III) vs Ru(II)] and hydrophilic/lipophilic coligands were key determinants of potency, and the complexes were active against both asexual and sexual parasite stages, acting faster than ATV, with ruthenium selectively accumulating in the parasite milieu. The complexes blocked transmission from humans to insects at both gametocyte and oocyst stages, whereas ATV blocked only oocysts. The authors report this as the first metal complex inhibiting both asexual and sexual parasites with similar potency.

articles.headline: Platelet mTOR drives heme-induced cerebral malaria pathology in mice
articles.title_original: Heme-induced ITAM signaling exacerbates malaria-associated neuropathogenesis through activation of platelet mTOR.
articles.authors: Portier I, Denorme F, Tolley ND, Kosaka Y, Andrianova I, Sigala PA, Lamb TJ, Zimmerman GA, Castro-Faria-Neto HC, Morrell CN, Rondina MT, Campbell RA
articles.details: Investigating cerebral malaria mechanisms, researchers showed the mTOR pathway is activated in platelets ex vivo after coincubation with P. falciparum-infected red blood cells and in vivo in Plasmodium berghei ANKA (PbA)-infected mice. Platelet-specific mTOR deletion (mTORplt-/-) significantly increased survival in experimental cerebral malaria, independent of parasitemia and thrombocytopenia, with reduced brain platelet deposition, improved cerebral blood flow, and reduced vascular permeability. Plasma heme correlated with intracerebral platelet accumulation in mTORplt+/+ but not mTORplt-/- mice; heme activated platelet mTOR via ITAM signaling (predominantly CLEC-2), and blocking heme activation with cobalt protoporphyrin reduced mTOR activation and mortality.

articles.headline: Repeated P. falciparum infection not linked to telomere shortening in children
articles.title_original: Cumulative Plasmodium falciparum infections do not drive long-term telomere shortening in Kenyan children.
articles.authors: Miglar A, Amadi D, Grannas D, Widman L, Mwacharo J, Musyoki J, Kai O, Bejon P, Maccharia A, Williams TN, Färnert A, Asghar M, Ndungu FM
articles.details: This study measured correlations between telomere length (TL, quantified by qPCR in cross-sectional 2007, 2010, and 2013 blood samples) and cumulative malaria/P. falciparum episodes in children under weekly febrile-malaria surveillance: 218 children from Junju (moderate transmission) and 90 age-matched controls from Ngerenya (no longer endemic). TL declined with age independently of cumulative malaria exposure (p>0.05), with steeper decline in those with longer baseline TL. Asymptomatic P. falciparum parasitaemia was associated with a positive TL change of 0.22 kb (95% CI 0.02-0.42, p=0.03). The authors conclude repeated symptomatic and asymptomatic infections were not associated with TL shortening, the apparent increase being of marginal significance.

articles.headline: Review of merozoite physiology underlying red blood cell invasion
articles.title_original: Physiology of invasive Plasmodium merozoites.
articles.authors: Siddiqui MA
articles.details: This review summarizes current understanding of the physiology of invasive Plasmodium merozoites, the specialized stage that invades red blood cells to multiply in the bloodstream. Beyond molecular interactions at the merozoite-host cell surface, it emphasizes that invasion requires active parasite movement, environmental sensing, and responses to signals such as ion changes or red blood cell contact, which trigger secretion of specialized organelles and activation of gliding motility. The authors highlight how motility, signal sensing, and secretory organelle function are coordinated to drive invasion, and discuss implications for potential malaria control and intervention strategies.

articles.headline: Short-term ex vivo culture filters P. vivax clones, preserves gene expression
articles.title_original: Stage-aware transcriptomics reveals selective haplotype persistence in short-term ex vivo cultured Plasmodium vivax.
articles.authors: Abagero B, Dumetz F, Ford CT, Tolosa T, Tesefay D, Lukas B, Shenkutie T, Popovici J, Yewhalaw D, Serre D, Lo E
articles.details: Using paired in vivo and short-term ex vivo transcriptomes from Ethiopian P. vivax clinical isolates, researchers applied stage deconvolution and PvMSP1 haplotyping. Ex vivo maturation modestly increased inferred schizont representation while preserving trophozoite and gametocyte proportions; after adjusting for stage composition, in vivo and ex vivo transcriptomes remained globally similar with no significantly differentially expressed genes. However, culture reduced multiplicity of infection, contracted within-host haplotype diversity, and non-randomly depleted specific haplotypes, consistent with a clonal bottleneck. The authors conclude ex vivo culture enriches late asexual stages and selectively filters clones rather than inducing a common transcriptional program, so studies should model developmental composition and infection complexity.

articles.headline: FHR-1 deficiency linked to malarial anemia, not overall severe malaria
articles.title_original: Factor H-related protein 1 deficiency and risk of severe falciparum malaria in Ghanaian children: a cohort analysis.
articles.authors: Byrd AU, Wahler L, Thye T, Reiss T, Bachmann A, Pradel G
articles.details: Researchers analyzed plasma from 500 Ghanaian children presenting with different clinical manifestations of P. falciparum malaria to test whether Factor H-related protein 1 (FHR-1) deficiency—common because one-third of Africans carry a CFHR3/1 deletion—affects disease severity. One-third of samples lacked detectable FHR-1, but logistic regression showed no association between FHR-1 deficiency and risk of severe malaria. FHR-1 deficiency was, however, overrepresented among children presenting with anemia and multiple syndromes, and affected children had markedly lower hemoglobin levels. The authors conclude FHR-1 deficiency does not affect overall progression of severe falciparum malaria but correlates with severe malarial anemia.

articles.headline: Paper-based lysis device enables malaria diagnostics from dried blood
articles.title_original: Creating an improved workflow for paper-based malaria diagnostics by integrating total lysis of whole blood.
articles.authors: Prat-Trunas J, Code A, Avalos-Padilla Y, Fernández-Busquets X, Mace CR, Baldrich E
articles.details: Researchers developed a three-dimensional paper-based lysis device, fabricated alternatively by laser cutting and by wax-printing, to add whole-blood red cell lysis to dried blood spot sampling for low-resource settings. The device achieved 100% cellular lysis independent of sample composition, and successful lysis enabled detection of a malaria biomarker comparable to the standard liquid lysis procedure of Plasmodium-infected samples. Device-dried samples showed antigen stability for over one week at room temperature, facilitating delayed laboratory testing. The authors conclude these lysis devices have the potential to expand the types of environments from which samples tested for malaria can be collected.

articles.headline: Why iron chelators kill malaria parasites less effectively than artemisinin
articles.title_original: Why are iron chelators not as effective as artemisinin in killing malaria parasites?
articles.authors: Sun J, Fang C, Si W, Qin X, Wang F, Li Y, Sun J
articles.details: To explain why iron chelators are weaker antimalarials than artemisinin, researchers compared artemisinin derivatives (dihydroartemisinin, DHA; artemether, ATM) and the iron chelator desferrioxamine (DFO) on P. falciparum 3D7 in vitro and P. yoelii in vivo, using single-cell RNA sequencing at 3, 9, and 24 h post-treatment and transmission electron microscopy. All developmental stages were sensitive to 24-h DHA, but parasite counts at 12–30 hpi fell faster with 9-h DFO than 9-h DHA; DHA upregulated iron-utilization genes at 3 h post-treatment whereas DFO did not. DFO reduced infection in vitro but failed to inhibit P. yoelii in vivo, and ATM plus DFO was less potent than ATM alone. TEM showed ATM localized to digestive vacuoles and disrupted heme aggregation. The authors conclude artemisinins are more potent because they accumulate in the digestive vacuole and disrupt heme/iron homeostasis.

articles.headline: Febrile heat boosts P. falciparum cytoadhesion via PfEMP1 trafficking
articles.title_original: Physiological febrile heat stress increases cytoadhesion through increased protein trafficking of Plasmodium falciparum surface proteins into the red blood cell.
articles.authors: Jones D, Belda H, Broncel M, Fuchs G, Anaguano D, Nofal SD, Treeck M
articles.details: Exposing P. falciparum-infected red blood cells to physiologically relevant febrile heat stress (39 °C), derived from patient data, enhanced cytoadhesion through increased trafficking of the major virulence factor PfEMP1 to the infected-cell surface. The effect extended beyond PfEMP1 and common laboratory strains to the nutrient channel PSAC in four clinical isolates of diverse geographic origin, occurring without changes in overall protein expression or parasite developmental progression. Using phosphoproteomics and proximity labelling, elevated temperature also increased trafficking and phosphorylation of exported proteins, with enhanced export likely reliant on a transmembrane domain as shown by NanoLuc reporter assays. The authors conclude that febrile temperatures can accelerate protein export, potentially influencing disease severity through earlier infected-cell sequestration.

articles.headline: Soluble pyrophosphatases essential for P. falciparum blood-stage survival
articles.title_original: Pyrophosphate homeostasis in multiple subcellular compartments is essential in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Nwankwo I, Ke H
articles.details: This lab study used reverse genetics and biochemistry to characterize two soluble pyrophosphatase isoforms (PfsPPase1, PF3D7_0316300.1; PfsPPase2, PF3D7_0316300.2) encoded from a single locus in Plasmodium falciparum. PfsPPase2 carries a 51-amino-acid leader sequence absent in PfsPPase1. PfsPPase1 localized solely to the cytoplasm, while PfsPPase2 targeted the mitochondrion, apicoplast, and cytoplasm. Both isoforms were shown to be essential for asexual blood-stage growth. The authors conclude this dual-targeting mechanism is novel and that soluble pyrophosphatases are promising antimalarial drug targets.

articles.headline: Hemozoin-bound proteins disrupt brain endothelial barrier in cerebral malaria model
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum hemozoin-associated biomolecules induce brain endothelial cell barrier disruption in an in vitro model of cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Crotty KA, Clotea I, Ueberheide B, Cammer M, Sall J, Liang A, Rodriguez A
articles.details: Using an in vitro model of cerebral malaria with human brain microvascular endothelial cells, researchers found that the barrier-disrupting activity of P. falciparum-infected red blood cells resided entirely in the hemozoin fraction. The hemozoin crystal itself did not disrupt the barrier; rather, biomolecules associated with it did. Protease treatment of purified hemozoin inhibited barrier disruption, while nuclease treatment did not, indicating proteins are responsible. P. falciparum hemozoin also disrupted a pulmonary endothelial barrier. The authors identify hemozoin-bound proteins as a key mechanism in cerebral malaria pathogenesis and a potential treatment target.

articles.headline: Global malaria and NTD burden trends and 15-year projections
articles.title_original: Global epidemiological trends, distribution of NTDs and malaria, and disease burden projections for the next 15 years.
articles.authors: Huang H, Gao Y, Zhu H, Xu Y, Li M, Meng F, Chen X, Du G, Ma W, Qian J, Li Y, Tan W
articles.details: This modeling study used Global Burden of Disease 2021 data to analyze incidence, prevalence, mortality, DALYs, and age-standardized rates of malaria and neglected tropical diseases from 1990 to 2021, applying Joinpoint analysis and a vector autoregression (VAR) model to identify risk factors and project the next 15 years. The global malaria burden decreased, especially in Sub-Saharan Africa, while NTD incidence rose until 2016 then declined. The Socio-Demographic Index influenced both burdens, particularly in low-SDI regions, with environmental sanitation and behavioral factors significantly affecting malaria. The authors recommend strengthening sanitation and international cooperation.

articles.headline: Used RDT kits as alternative DNA source for malaria surveillance
articles.title_original: Rapid diagnostic test kit as a source of DNA in comparison with filter paper for malaria molecular surveillance and drug resistance monitoring: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Woldegiorgis BT, Wondale B, Tamiru G, Lindtjørn B, Massebo F
articles.details: Researchers compared DNA recovery from used rapid diagnostic test (RDT) kits with the conventional dried blood spot (DBS) filter-paper method for malaria molecular surveillance, collecting 417 paired samples from malaria-positive cases at six health centres in the Gamo Zone of southern Ethiopia; DNA was extracted with Chelex-100 and analyzed by nested PCR targeting the 18S rRNA gene, with 65 paired sub-samples also amplified for the pfmdr1 drug-resistance gene. Nested PCR was positive in 391/417 (93.8%) of DBS samples versus 349/417 (83.7%) of RDT samples. Positive Percent Agreement for RDT was 89.2% for P. falciparum and 81.9% for P. vivax, with an overall Kappa of 0.64 (0.75 at parasite loads >100,000/μL), and pfmdr1 amplification succeeded in 63/65 (96.7%) of RDT versus 65/65 (100%) of DBS samples. The authors conclude used RDT kits can serve as an alternative DNA source for molecular surveillance and drug-resistance monitoring when DBS is unavailable.

articles.headline: Plasmodium kinase NEK4 drives meiotic entry and zygote maturation
articles.title_original: A divergent Plasmodium NEK4 acts as a key regulator driving the early events of meiosis.
articles.authors: Yanase R, Hair M, Zeeshan M, Ferguson DJP, Brady D, Pasquarello C, Bottrill A, Bhanvadia S, Neal A, Tromer EC, Le Roch KG, Hainard A, Holder AA, Vaughan S, Guttery DS, Tewari R
articles.details: This study identified the Plasmodium berghei NIMA-related kinase NEK4 as a regulator coupling meiotic initiation with zygote morphogenesis, which in Plasmodium occurs post-zygotically. Using ultrastructure expansion microscopy, NEK4 was shown to accumulate at the microtubule-organising centre and apical polar complex after fertilisation, and zygotes underwent MTOC-associated nuclear migration. Deletion of Pbnek4 caused complete developmental arrest: blocked MTOC duplication and microtubule formation, uncondensed chromatin, and failed nuclear migration and cell polarity. Transcriptomic and phosphoproteomic analyses showed collapsed meiotic and cytoskeletal regulatory networks, with reduced expression and phosphorylation of HOP1, REC8, and AP2-O. The authors establish NEK4 as a key driver of meiotic entry.

articles.headline: Commentary on mouse versus human models of cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Comment on: Cerebral malaria: of mice and men.
articles.authors: Carvalho LJM, Sanches GS
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: P. falciparum MSP2 diversity and multiplicity of infection in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity of the IC/3D7 allelic family of the merozoite surface protein 2 (MSP2) of Plasmodium falciparum and multiplicity of infection in four health facilities in the Mbouda Health District, Cameroon.
articles.authors: Cedric Y, Nadia NAC, Merveille TT, Pacome TK, Mathieu KT, Guy-Armand GN, Abel D, Edna GDM, Sandra WNI, Yval KT, Hu H
articles.details: Researchers assessed the genetic diversity of the P. falciparum merozoite surface protein 2 (msp2) gene among 481 symptomatic patients tested by rapid diagnostic test and thick blood smear at four health centers in the Mbouda Health District, Cameroon, with msp2 amplified by nested PCR. Of 481 samples, 137 (28.48%) were P. falciparum positive (mean parasite density 2196.77 ± 1344.36 parasites/µL); msp2 amplified in 64% of positives, revealing 15 distinct alleles and an overall genetic diversity of 14.15%. The mean multiplicity of infection was 1.20, with mono-, double-, and triple-genotype infections at 81.68%, 18.18%, and 1.33%, respectively, and phylogenetic analysis identified 13 distinct clades. The authors conclude the considerable diversity and multiple infections suggest high transmission intensity.

articles.headline: Severe childhood malaria linked to long-term cognitive and academic deficits
articles.title_original: Long-Term Cognitive Ability and Academic Achievement After Childhood Severe Malaria.
articles.authors: Bangirana P, Mellencamp KA, Ren J, Nakitende JA, Conroy AL, Datta D, Kautzman C, Goings MJ, Opoka RO, Namazzi R, Robberstad B, Idro R, John CC
articles.details: This follow-up of two Ugandan cohorts analyzed 889 children under 18 (mean age 11.1 years), tested 4 to 15 years (mean 8.4) after a severe malaria episode: cerebral malaria (n=184), severe malarial anemia (n=249), other severe malaria (n=239), and unaffected community children (n=217). Compared with community children, those with prior cerebral malaria or severe malarial anemia had lower overall cognition (adjusted mean differences −0.41 and −0.31) and math scores (−0.46 and −0.32), while attention and reading did not differ; other forms of severe malaria showed no significant deficits. Acute kidney injury, hyperuricemia, and elevated plasma angiopoietin-2 during the acute episode were associated with worse cognitive scores. The authors conclude cerebral malaria and severe malarial anemia are associated with lasting cognitive and academic impairment.

articles.headline: Editorial on long-term neurocognitive outcomes after severe malaria
articles.title_original: Long-Term Neurocognitive Outcomes After Severe Malaria Infection: No Peace of MIND.
articles.authors: Sundararaman SA, Odom John AR
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: P. falciparum PX1 haplotype reduces artemisinin and lumefantrine susceptibility
articles.title_original: A Plasmodium falciparum PX1 haplotype is associated with reduced susceptibility to artemisinin and lumefantrine.
articles.authors: Bower-Lepts C, Jangra A, Kanatani S, Tripathi A, Mlambo G, McCotter-González A, Romano L, Orena S, Okitwi M, Niaré K, Rosenthal PJ, Conrad M, Sinnis P, Mok S
articles.details: Using a P. falciparum genetic cross between a drug-sensitive line and a Ugandan strain with reduced susceptibility to dihydroartemisinin and lumefantrine, researchers performed targeted deep sequencing of progeny pools and 460 recombinant progeny clones under drug pressure. Polymorphisms in px1 (encoding a phosphoinositide-binding protein) were the strongest correlates of reduced susceptibility, and the PX1 PIN haplotype (L1222P, M1701I, D1705N) was enriched after dihydroartemisinin or lumefantrine treatment and conferred reduced susceptibility versus wild-type in culture-adapted Ugandan lines. Competitive fitness assays showed PX1 mutations give asexual blood-stage parasites a growth advantage. The authors conclude the PX1 PIN haplotype is a robust marker of reduced artemether-lumefantrine susceptibility, useful for resistance surveillance.

articles.headline: Outdoor spraying most cost-effective against zoonotic P. knowlesi malaria
articles.title_original: Cost-effectiveness of outdoor residual spraying, insecticide-treated nets, and their combination for zoonotic malaria control in Sabah, Malaysia: a comparative analysis.
articles.authors: Firdaus MH, Wan Puteh SE, Md Idris Z, Zhueng TJ, Abdul Hamid MH, Suli Z, Abdul Manap M, Abdul Manaf MR
articles.details: A cost-effectiveness analysis from the healthcare provider perspective over a one-year horizon compared Outdoor Residual Spraying (ORS), Long-Lasting Insecticidal Nets (LLINs), and their combination for P. knowlesi malaria across three high-endemic districts in Sabah, Malaysia (Keningau, Ranau, Kota Marudu), versus a no-intervention scenario. Annual provider costs ranged from MYR 3912.92 (no intervention) to MYR 39,764.14 (ORS+LLINs). ORS averted the most cases (n=13) and had the lowest incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (MYR 2046 per case averted, ~USD 470), remaining most favourable across sensitivity analyses. The authors conclude a stratified approach prioritising ORS is economically efficient.

articles.headline: Global genomic analysis maps region-specific P. falciparum drug-resistance adaptation
articles.title_original: Global-scale population genetic analysis of Plasmodium falciparum identifies region-specific patterns of malaria parasite adaptation.
articles.authors: Billows N, Dombrowski JG, Thorpe J, Vanheer L, Moss S, Gitaka J, Sutherland CJ, Marinho CRF, Thi Hong Ngoc N, Thi Huong Binh N, Thieu NQ, Campino S, Clark TG
articles.details: This population genetic study analysed 17,565 P. falciparum isolates (17,489 previously published, 76 newly sequenced) from 39 countries using identity-by-descent (IBD) networks, population structure inference, and selection scans. Analyses identified region-specific adaptive processes, especially in Southeast Asia. New Brazilian sequences showed increased IBD around pfcrt consistent with local drug selection, while Vietnamese isolates showed high relatedness and elevated IBD around pfKIC3, all carrying the pfkelch13 C580Y artemisinin-resistance mutation. Elevated IBD in pfKIC7 and pfKIC9 in South America and the Horn of Africa suggests alternative pathways for future artemisinin resistance. The authors conclude this improves resolution for genomic surveillance.

articles.headline: Rapid nanopore sequencing enables African malaria genomic surveillance under $25
articles.title_original: Continental-scale genomic surveillance of Plasmodium falciparum malaria across sub-Saharan Africa with rapid nanopore sequencing.
articles.authors: Mwenda M, Mosler K, Bohmeier B, Chomba M, van Loon W, Mambwe B, Gaye A, Olorunfemi A, Suliman S, Yanogo NJ, Sow D, Ngom B, Zoure OAZ, Tibiri YNG, Ojeniyi F, Zongo A, Anoh EA, Achi V, Chiyesu C, Otieno S, Ogola B, Niangaly M, Carrasquilla M, Goboto DK, Feldt T, Tufa TB, Oliveira R, Schallenberg E, Sogoba Y, Ntalla C, Ouedraogo O, Otieno K, Opaleye O, Olowe A, Gibbons M, Drakeley C, Schubert G, Mockenhaupt FP, Portugal S, Deme AB, Soulama I, Ndiaye D, Ojurongbe O, Kariuki S, Shi YP, Schultz JS, Hawela M, Bridges DJ, Hendry JA
articles.details: Researchers initiated decentralised, continental-scale P. falciparum genomic surveillance by locally sequencing and analysing 1065 samples across six African countries in one year. A novel nanopore protocol was rapid (~5 hr) and cost-effective (<$25 USD/sample), surveilling antimalarial drug-resistance genes, hrp2/3 deletions, the vaccine target csp, and the polymorphic gene ama1, coupled to an offline laptop-based dashboard showing real-time mapping and variant calls. They demonstrated robust coverage across parasitemia levels and laboratories, accurate identification of resistance markers and hrp2/3 deletions, and sensitive detection of minor-clone mutations via a novel variant caller.

articles.headline: Dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine matches chloroquine for P. vivax malaria in Brazil
articles.title_original: Dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine versus chloroquine for the treatment of uncomplicated Plasmodium vivax malaria with concurrent or delayed high-dose primaquine in Brazil (Curavivax): an open-label, single-centre, randomised clinical trial.
articles.authors: Baía-da-Silva DC, Machado KVA, Rabelo ALR, da Silva Balieiro AA, Barbosa ASL, Costa MRF, Sampaio VS, Melo GC, Alexandre MAA, Alencar ACC, Nascimento CT, Jerônimo CMP, Marcovski MS, Barbosa LRA, Valle CS, Carrilho TAA, Vieira JLF, de Melo MM, Dinelly KMO, Omena AG, Brito-Sousa JD, Barreiros GA, Rocha YV, Filho ACA, Bassat Q, Alecrim MDGC, Siqueira AM, Lacerda MVG
articles.details: Curavivax was an open-label, single-centre, randomised trial in Manaus, Brazilian Amazon, enrolling 419 patients older than 6 months with microscopically confirmed P. vivax, randomly assigned 1:1:1:1 to dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine or chloroquine, each plus primaquine started on day 0 or delayed to day 42. Day 42 per-protocol recurrence was similar for chloroquine plus primaquine (2.0%) and dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine plus primaquine (1.0%; HR 0.48, p=0.44), while dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine alone with delayed primaquine (2.4%) was superior to chloroquine alone with delayed primaquine (27.3%; HR 0.08, p<0.0001). All treatments were well tolerated, supporting dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine for P. vivax in Latin America.

articles.headline: Cardiolipin synthase enzyme vital for Plasmodium liver-stage maturation
articles.title_original: A bacterial-type cardiolipin synthase in Plasmodium spp. supports mitochondrial respiration and is important for liver stage maturation.
articles.authors: Adhur KA, Nirdosh N, Chandramouli A, Basu S, Lahiri A, Kamat SS, Mishra S, Habib S
articles.details: This study characterized a Plasmodium bacterial-type, phospholipase D-type cardiolipin synthase (Cls), probing the enzyme activity of recombinant P. falciparum Cls and localizing PfCls to the mitochondrion in asexual blood stages, with additional cytosolic-periphery PfCls signal and plasma-membrane cardiolipin staining in late gametocytes. A P. berghei knockout had significantly impaired asexual blood-stage proliferation, lower cardiolipin abundance, reduced mitochondrial membrane potential, and reduced basal oxygen consumption. Although knockout parasites completed mosquito development and produced sporozoites capable of hepatocyte invasion, they had a severe liver-stage maturation defect. The authors conclude Plasmodium Cls is vital for parasite development and mitochondrial function.

articles.headline: How P. falciparum triggers vesicle release to escape red blood cells
articles.title_original: Spatiotemporal dynamics of signal dependent exocytosis and parasitophorous vacuolar membrane rupture during Plasmodium falciparum egress.
articles.authors: Dedkhad W, Tran T, Fierro MA, Brooks C, Muralidharan V
articles.details: Using live imaging of P. falciparum-infected red blood cells, researchers targeted the exocytosis reporter superecliptic pHluorin (SEP) to egress-specific vesicles and an EXP2 fluorescent reporter to track the parasitophorous vacuolar membrane (PVM). Signal-dependent exocytosis was triggered as early as 3 hours before merozoite egress, and the PVM ruptured at a single site that rapidly expanded, releasing merozoites into the RBC before RBC membrane rupture. Using conditional mutants, the authors show that loss of the endoplasmic reticulum calcium-binding protein PfERC inhibits exocytosis of egress-specific vesicles, demonstrating PfERC is required for this step.

articles.headline: Planned review of cost-effectiveness of ACTs for first-line malaria treatment
articles.title_original: Cost-effectiveness of artemisinin-based combination therapies for first-line treatment of malaria among pediatric and adult populations: a systematic review protocol.
articles.authors: Ma B, Kr S, Jith A, Gr S, Janakiram C, John D
articles.details: This is a protocol for a systematic review (not yet results) evaluating the cost-effectiveness and cost-utility of artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) versus non-ACT or alternative ACT dose regimens as first-line treatment for malaria in pediatric and adult populations. Following JBI methodology, two reviewers will screen records, assess methodological quality, and extract data, using measures such as the incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER), ICER per disability-adjusted life year averted, and quality-adjusted life years gained, with certainty appraised via GRADE. The aim is to guide policymakers in resource-limited settings (PROSPERO CRD420251058688).

articles.headline: Scoping review of Plasmodium genetic diversity in Southeast Asia
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity and multiplicity of Plasmodium spp. infections in Southeast Asia: a scoping review.
articles.authors: Helen AP, Martini M, Jeon BY, Han JH, Muh F
