articles.details: This scoping review synthesized molecular evidence on Plasmodium genetic diversity and multiplicity of infection (MOI) in Southeast Asia from 16 included observational studies, comprising 1,609 genotyped P. falciparum, 1,526 P. vivax, 37 P. malariae, and 188 human P. knowlesi infections. P. falciparum showed polyclonal infection rates of 3.9%–73.9% and mean MOI of 1.05–4.9; P. vivax showed polyclonal rates of 16.9%–71.4%, mean MOI of 1.1–1.91, and expected heterozygosity of 0.66–0.87; P. malariae had low MOI, and P. knowlesi had polyclonal rates of 7.3%–21% with mean MOI of 1.04–1.06. The authors conclude P. falciparum and P. vivax remain genetically diverse with heterogeneous transmission, and call for standardized molecular surveillance to support elimination.

articles.headline: Severe malaria
articles.title_original: Severe Malaria.
articles.authors: Samandari T, Gillespie E
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Data on the cultural history of malaria in Hungary
articles.title_original: [Data of cultural history of malaria in Hungary].
articles.authors: Kiss L
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: RTS,S malaria vaccine cuts young-child mortality in three countries
articles.title_original: Impact of introducing RTS,S/AS01(E) malaria vaccine on mortality in young children in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi: an observational evaluation of a cluster-randomised implementation programme.
articles.authors: Mwapasa V, Asante KP, Milligan P, Akech S, Oduro A, Mathanga DP, Fogelson A, Kwambai TK, Hamel MJ, Kapito-Tembo A, Gyan T, Westercamp N, Okine RNA, Moore KA, Pellaux-Furrer E, Stanley CC, Ansong D, Kariuki S, Njuguna P, Mvalo T, Welaga P, Otieno L, Snell P, Schellenberg D, Chimala A, Afari EA, Bejon P, Maleta K, Agbenyega T, Snow RW, Zulu M, Odei-Lartey E, Chinkhumba J, Samuels AM
articles.details: This observational evaluation of a cluster-randomised implementation programme assessed the impact of introducing the RTS,S/AS01E malaria vaccine on all-cause (excluding injury) mortality in young children in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi, delivered in a four-dose schedule. 158 clusters (66 Ghana, 46 Kenya, 46 Malawi) were randomly assigned 1:1 to implementation or comparison areas, with community surveillance and verbal autopsy over 46 months. Coverage assessed in 2022 was 82.8% (95% CI 80.7–84.9) for dose one, 71.1% (68.8–73.5) for dose three, and 39.9% (36.9–42.9) for dose four. Among children eligible for the third dose, there were 5576 deaths in implementation versus 6152 in comparison areas, giving a mortality rate ratio of 0.87 (95% CI 0.77–0.97; p=0.016). The authors conclude RTS,S introduction was associated with a significant mortality reduction, averting about one in eight deaths even with moderate three-dose and low fourth-dose coverage.

articles.headline: First fatal avian malaria case in critically endangered Fatu Hiva monarch
articles.title_original: First record of fatal Plasmodium relictum (GRW4) infection in a highly endangered Fatu Hiva monarch (Pomarea whitneyi) fledgling: a case report.
articles.authors: Igual I Beltrán M, Duc M, Blanvillain C, Bruslund S, Valkiūnas G, Pendl H
articles.details: This case report describes the first confirmed fatal avian malaria infection in a recently fledged juvenile Fatu Hiva monarch (Pomarea whitneyi), a Critically Endangered passerine of which fewer than 20 individuals remain. The bird was found dead and necropsy revealed severe emaciation, an empty gastrointestinal tract, diffuse pallor, and hepatosplenomegaly. Blood-smear and tissue imprint assessment showed high parasitaemia with intraerythrocytic stages and abundant birefringent malarial pigment; histopathology confirmed a hemozoin-producing haemosporidian infection. Nested cytochrome b PCR and sequencing of dried blood on household filter paper confirmed single infection with the GRW4 lineage of Plasmodium relictum. Authors conclude acute severe P. relictum infection was the proximate cause of death and urge integrating disease surveillance and vector management into conservation.

articles.headline: Artemisinin and lumefantrine resistance markers found in northeastern DRC border region
articles.title_original: Cross-border molecular epidemiology of Plasmodium falciparum drug susceptibility: insights from the Uganda-Democratic Republic of the Congo border.
articles.authors: Dilufwasayo PM, Balikagala B, Uchopa MA, Amaa JA, Ngoyi DM, Mita T
articles.details: This molecular epidemiology study conducted active and passive P. falciparum case detection across 20 villages and a central hospital, analyzing resistance markers including pfkelch13, pfmdr1, pfcrt genotyping and copy number variation. Among 536 individuals sampled, 49.8% were PCR-positive for P. falciparum. The pfmdr1 N86 allele was fixed and 50.3% carried the NFD haplotype linked to reduced lumefantrine susceptibility; pfmdr1 CNV was 4.4% (≥1.5 threshold) rising to 11.7% at a 1.4 threshold, and pfpm2/3 CNVs were 7.0%. Validated pfkelch13 mutations A675V and C469Y were detected in 4.3% and 1.3% of village samples. Whole-genome sequencing showed DRC parasites carrying these mutations clustered with Ugandan strains, supporting cross-border spread. Authors call for integrated genomic surveillance and suggest alternative ACTs such as artesunate-amodiaquine merit evaluation.

articles.headline: Molecular dynamics reveals how PfPLP1 forms membrane pores
articles.title_original: Deciphering Membrane Pore Formation Mechanisms of Plasmodium falciparum Perforin-Like Protein 1 (PfPLP1).
articles.authors: Patil SB, Dasgupta S, Bhaumik P
articles.details: This computational study investigated the membrane binding and pore-formation mechanism of Plasmodium falciparum Perforin-Like Protein 1 (PfPLP1), a MACPF-domain β-pore-forming protein expressed during the human life cycle and proposed as an underexplored therapeutic target. The authors modeled the PfPLP1 structure and ran all-atom and coarse-grained molecular dynamics simulations in soluble and membrane-associated states. PfPLP1 comprises a canonical MACPF domain and an apicomplexan perforin β-domain (APCβ); initial membrane binding is mediated by cationic residues at the base of the APCβ domain interacting with lipid headgroups. In the membrane-inserted tetramer, water penetrated between the tetramer and bilayer to initiate pore opening, lipids reorganized into a toroidal edge, and larger oligomers showed a tendency to form pore-like structures. Findings are intended to inform understanding of pore formation in other PLPs and similar toxins.

articles.headline: Higher malaria transmission linked to faster tetanus antibody waning in children
articles.title_original: Assessing the effect of malaria exposure history on tetanus antibody waning rates among children in Jinja and Tororo Districts, Uganda.
articles.authors: Sbarra AN, Stone W, Tetteh K, Ssewanyana I, Musinguzi K, Rodríguez-Barraquer I, Jagannathan P, Bach FA, Drakeley C, Takahashi S
articles.details: This longitudinal study assessed how malaria exposure history affects tetanus antitoxin IgG antibody kinetics, analyzing 3,064 plasma samples from 597 children aged 6 months to 10 years residing in Tororo (high transmission) and Jinja (low transmission). Mixed-effects models estimated antibody half-lives and hazard models estimated sero-reversion risk. Observed tetanus seroprevalence was 87.5% among 6-to-11-month-olds, and antibody concentrations were estimated to fall below protective levels by age 8.1 years (95% CI 6.4–10.2). Antibody half-lives were significantly longer in Jinja (15.8 years, 95% CI 8.8–28.5) than in Tororo (2.8 years, 95% CI 2.6–3.1), and hazard of sero-reversion was 2.7-fold higher (95% CI 1.7–4.1) in Tororo. Authors conclude that children in the high-transmission district had faster waning and suggest research on whether malaria endemicity should inform tetanus booster timing.

articles.headline: Drug-resistance gene surveillance of vivax and falciparum malaria in Korea
articles.title_original: Molecular Surveillance of Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium falciparum Drug Resistance Genes in the Republic of Korea: 2022-2025.
articles.authors: Jung H, Shin HI, Ku B, Park E, Lee MR, Ju JW, Lee HI
articles.details: Molecular surveillance monitored drug-resistance genes in blood from Plasmodium vivax patients (soldiers, relapsed, and imported cases) and P. falciparum patients (soldiers and civilians) in the Republic of Korea between 2022 and 2025. In P. vivax, Pvmdr-1 mutations S515R, G698S, L845F, M908L, T958M, and F1076L were present in nearly all isolates, with T529 and E1233 at moderate prevalence; S513R, Y541C, A829V, and S1358 appeared occasionally, all in imported cases or soldiers on prophylaxis. In P. falciparum, Pfmdr-1 Y184F was highly prevalent and PfK13 A675V was detected only in soldiers. Mutation prevalence differed significantly among groups (p<0.05), but no temporal trends were seen.

articles.headline: T-cell responses and epitopes for malaria vaccine candidate GMZ2.6c
articles.title_original: Cellular Immune Response and T Cell Epitope Mapping of Plasmodium falciparum Chimeric Vaccine Candidate GMZ2.6c and Its Components (MSP-3, GLURP and Pfs48/45) in Individuals Naturally Exposed to Malaria in Brazilian Amazon.
articles.authors: Baptista BO, Soares IF, de Souza HADS, Barros JP, Riccio EKP, Martorano RM, Rodrigues-da-Silva RN, Amoah LE, Singh SK, Theisen M, Lima-Junior JDC, Totino PRR, Daniel-Ribeiro CT, Pratt-Riccio LR
articles.details: This study assessed cellular immune responses to the P. falciparum chimeric vaccine candidate GMZ2.6c and its components (MSP-3, GLURP, Pfs48/45) using peripheral blood mononuclear cells from individuals with active P. falciparum infection and non-infected malaria-exposed individuals from Cruzeiro do Sul, Mâncio Lima (Acre State) and Guajará (Amazonas State), evaluated by flow cytometry and ELISpot. Infected individuals showed greater CD8+ T-cell activation to MSP-3 and Pfs48/45, increased CD4+ TCM and reduced CD4+ TEM cells; both groups produced elevated IL-6 and TNF, but only non-infected individuals had high IL-10. Predicted promiscuous T-cell epitopes in MSP-3, GLURP, and Pfs48/45 elicited IFN-γ responses in both groups. The authors conclude P. falciparum infection may modulate responses toward pro-inflammatory, supporting further study of GMZ2.6c.

articles.headline: Influenza coinfection protects mice from malaria lung injury
articles.title_original: Protective coinfection: influenza reprograms myeloid cells to limit CD8 T cell-mediated malaria pathology.
articles.authors: Reed JS, Nayan R, Deckers M, Cornwall DH, Ostrand-Rosenberg S, Evavold BD, Lamb TJ
articles.details: In a mouse model, co-infection with non-lethal influenza A/HKx31 (X31) protected against malaria-associated acute lung injury, preventing pulmonary vascular leak and death. X31 co-infection expanded Ly6C+ monocyte-derived alveolar macrophages that inhibited pathogenic CD8 T cells in a contact-dependent manner; in vivo blockade of monocytic myeloid cells with gemcitabine abolished protection. Protection was independent of parasite burden and type I interferon signaling, and co-infected pulmonary CD8 T cells showed broad transcriptional reprogramming and impaired inflammatory cytokine production. The authors suggest therapeutics that expand suppressive myeloid cells could serve as adjunctive treatment for malaria-associated acute lung injury.

articles.headline: Genetic basis of avian malaria resistance in Hawaiian honeycreeper
articles.title_original: Evolution of Resistance to Avian Malaria Infection in an Endemic Hawaiian Honeycreeper.
articles.authors: Neddermeyer JH, Cassin-Sackett L, Paxton EH, Campana MG, Fleischer RC, Foster JT
articles.details: Using whole-genome sequences from 35 O'ahu 'amakihi (Chlorodrepanis flava), researchers conducted a genome-wide association study to find SNPs linked to avian malaria (Plasmodium relictum) infection, with infection status determined by real-time PCR on blood samples. They identified 582 SNPs associated with infection, annotated to long ncRNAs and genes involved in transcription regulation, apoptosis, T-cell and complement activation, autophagosome assembly/transport, and intracellular calcium flux. The authors conclude that host limitation of parasite replication may involve restricting intracellular components such as calcium needed for the parasite life cycle, not only adaptive immunity, broadening known mechanisms of disease resistance.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccine reduces child deaths by 13% in Africa, WHO reports
articles.title_original: Malaria vaccine reduces child deaths by 13% in Africa, WHO study reports.
articles.authors: Iacobucci G
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Modelling RTS,S malaria vaccine impact in young children in Benin
articles.title_original: Estimating the impact of the RTS,S/AS01 malaria vaccine in Benin: A mathematical modelling study.
articles.authors: Alohoutade SV, Hounsell R, Dandonougbo C, Aikpon R, Degila J, Silal S
articles.details: A compartmental mathematical modelling study estimated clinical and severe Plasmodium falciparum malaria cases averted by the primary series of the RTS,S/AS01 vaccine in children under five in Benin over a 10-year period (2025-2034), comparing scenarios at different coverage levels combined with long-lasting insecticidal nets. Vaccinating at coverage similar to the third dose of DTP (DTP3) alongside current net use is projected to reduce clinical malaria cases and deaths by 40% compared with nets alone. However, a 1.5-fold decrease in net use is projected to increase malaria burden, potentially offsetting these gains. The authors conclude childhood vaccination is a useful additional malaria control tool, but reduced net usage may diminish benefits.

articles.headline: NGO lessons on maintaining malaria services during COVID-19
articles.title_original: Delivering malaria services during the COVID-19 pandemic: challenges and lessons from an international non-governmental organisation.
articles.authors: Chestnutt EG, Marasciulo M, Magumba G, Maxwell K, Rassi C, Parkes A, Nelson C, Tibenderana JK
articles.details: This reflective article describes how a team maintained malaria services during the COVID-19 pandemic through a coordinated, evidence-driven response. Key actions included establishing a dedicated taskforce, developing an outbreak management plan with four risk phases, and adapting protocols to local contexts to enable safer delivery methods. The authors present reflections from staff in decision-making roles, offering practical insights for other organisations preparing for future operational challenges. No quantitative outcomes are reported.

articles.headline: Political economy barriers to malaria elimination in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Accelerating Nigeria towards malaria elimination requires moving away from business as usual: insights from a political economy analysis.
articles.authors: Chestnutt EG, Meredith S, Ipaye B, Getachew D, Tibenderana JK, Anyachukwu E, Obot T, Maxwell K
articles.details: This political economy analysis examined contextual factors hindering malaria elimination progress in Nigeria, using a desk review, stakeholder mapping, and semi-structured interviews with key influencers and high-level managers, analysed thematically. Factors affecting resource allocation included low government prioritisation of malaria, weak citizen advocacy (so elimination is not an electable issue), and no direct communication channels between the malaria program and decision-makers; poor coordination among partners affected use of allocated resources. The authors conclude that progress requires predictable financing from sustained political will, citizen demand, better partner coordination, high-level advocacy groups, and integrating malaria priorities into the national development plan.

articles.headline: High sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance mutations in pregnant women, Mozambique
articles.title_original: Prevalence of pfdhfr-pfdhps Sextuple and Gametocyte-Associated Quintuple Sulfadoxine-Pyrimethamine Resistance Mutations in Plasmodium falciparum Isolates from Pregnant Women in Mozambique.
articles.authors: Drissi-El Boukili Y, Rovira-Vallbona E, Guetens P, Chiheb D, Kattenberg JH, Kestens L, Enosse SMM, Rosanas-Urgell A, Arnaldo P
articles.details: Using molecular methods, researchers examined 100 Plasmodium falciparum isolates collected from Mozambican women at delivery in 2014-15 to determine sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance polymorphisms in the pfdhfr and pfdhps genes and gametocyte presence by RT-qPCR. Overall, 54% and 7% of parasites harboured quintuple and sextuple pfdhfr/pfdhps mutant haplotypes, respectively, and gametocytes were detected in 34% of isolates. Gametocyte carriage was significantly associated with quintuple mutant infections (AOR = 7.5, p = 0.001), which accounted for 80% of gametocyte-positive infections. The authors highlight the relevance of ongoing SP resistance surveillance to guide future IPTp approaches and anticipate implications for parasite transmission and maternal-fetal health.

articles.headline: Low childhood immunization coverage in high-malaria communities, Bioko Island
articles.title_original: Immunization Coverage and Associated Factors Among Children Aged 18 to 72 Months in Communities With High Malaria Transmission, Bioko Island.
articles.authors: Eburi Losoha E, Lopez Mikue MSA, García GA, Athuman T, Ross A, Daubenberger C, Merten S
articles.details: This cross-sectional survey, nested in a larger study and conducted between September 2019 and March 2020, assessed routine immunization coverage among children aged 18 months to 6 years in 13 malaria-endemic communities in Malabo District, Bioko Island, Equatorial Guinea, ascertaining status from vaccination cards or caregiver recall. Of 297 children with verified status, only 86 (29%) were fully immunized, with similar estimates by card (28%, 34/121) and recall (30%, 52/176). Children aged 18 months to 2 years had higher completion than those aged 2-6 years (45%, 39/86 versus 22%, 47/211; aOR = 0.42, 95% CI 0.20-0.87); sex, ethnicity, and community area were not significantly associated. The authors conclude coverage was low and call for further community-based studies.

articles.headline: Artemisinin resistance clearance thresholds may miss African cases
articles.title_original: Artemisinin partial resistance at a crossroads: evidence for continental variation in Plasmodium falciparumclearance phenotypes.
articles.authors: Hosangadi V, Fidock DA
articles.details: A systematic review of 96 studies from southeast Asia and Africa (35 reporting genotype-linked parasite clearance half-life and/or ring-stage survival assay data) assessed continental differences in P. falciparum clearance dynamics under artemisinin partial resistance. In southeast Asia, canonical kelch13 (k13) mutations consistently produced prolonged clearance half-lives concordant with elevated RSA survival. In east Africa and the Horn of Africa, independently emergent k13 mutations generally showed faster clearance kinetics despite elevated RSA survival, suggesting clearance half-life thresholds derived from Asia may under-detect emerging resistance in Africa. The authors call for region-specific surveillance integrating clearance half-life, RSA, molecular markers, and partner-drug susceptibility.

articles.headline: Trial protocol comparing low- vs standard-dose primaquine for gametocyte killing
articles.title_original: Protocol for a multicentric open-label randomized controlled trial comparing low-dose (0.25 mg/kg) and standard-dose (0.75 mg/kg) primaquine for gametocytocidal efficacy and safety in Plasmodium falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Gulati P, Singhal R, Gupta A, Anjali, Tripathi PK, Kumar D, Sinha DP, Singh SP, Singh K, Baharia RK, Bhatt KN, Vasava AH, Gamit A, Patki MB, Malla WA, Kori L, Savargaonkar D, Chakma N, Parmar D, Alagesan L, Kashyap R, Singh K, Anvikar AR, Bharti PK, Nitika N
articles.details: This is a protocol for a hospital-based, open-label, multicentric randomized controlled trial at four sites in India (target 496 participants, 124 per site) comparing single low-dose primaquine (0.25 mg/kg on day 1) against standard high-dose primaquine (0.75 mg/kg on day 2) for gametocytocidal efficacy and safety in adults (>18 years, >40 kg, Hb >8 g/dL) with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria and normal G6PD activity (>4.0 IU/g Hb), all treated with ACT. Efficacy is measured by gametocyte density and point prevalence (blood smears plus QT-NASBA for Pfs25 mRNA on days 1, 2/3, 7, 14); safety is based on haemoglobin drops and adverse events. No results are reported; enrollment started August 2025 and is ongoing.

articles.headline: Bed net ownership and use below target in rural Tanzania
articles.title_original: Socio-demographic predictors of insecticide-treated bed net ownership and utilization for protection against malaria by rural community members across five regions of Mainland Tanzania.
articles.authors: Chacha GA, Seth MD, Mandai SS, Petro DA, Challe DP, Kisambale AJ, Budodo R, Madebe RA, Mbwambo RB, Bakari C, Pereus D, Aaron S, Mbwambo D, Lusasi A, Lazaro S, Mandara CI, Ishengoma DS
articles.details: A community-based cross-sectional survey of 10,228 rural participants (aged ≥6 months) across 15 villages in five districts of five Mainland Tanzania regions evaluated socio-demographic predictors of insecticide-treated net (ITN) ownership and use via logistic regression. Overall, 7,939 (77.6%) reported owning and 7,899 (77.2%) using ITNs, with significant variation across districts (p<0.001). Females had higher odds of ownership and use (aOR=1.27, 95% CI 1.12–1.45, p<0.001) than males, and under-fives were more likely to own (aOR=1.83, 95% CI 1.56–2.15) and use (aOR=2.26, 95% CI 1.62–3.15) nets than adults. Higher education and household socio-economic status were also independently associated with greater ownership and use. Coverage remained below the 80% national target, and the authors urge addressing persistent disparities.

articles.headline: Actinomycin derivatives show selective antimalarial activity in vitro
articles.title_original: Discovery of actinomycin derivatives with improved selectivity against malaria parasites from a Streptomyces culture library.
articles.authors: Teshima A, Teklemichael AA, Hirata A, Akimoto M, Taniguchi M, Muslimin R, Ogaki S, Ali A, Suzuki T, Mizukami S, Arakawa K
articles.details: Researchers screening a Streptomyces culture library isolated antimalarial actinomycins from strain Streptomyces antibioticus HUT6035 (NBRC3117) via large-scale fermentation and chromatography. The strain accumulated actinomycins X2 (4-oxo) and X0β (4-hydroxy proline) derivatives of actinomycin D. Actinomycin X2 showed IC50 = 0.241 nM with cytotoxicity CC50 = 6.71 nM (selectivity index 27.9), while X0β showed IC50 = 7.69 nM and CC50 = 818 nM (SI 106), compared with actinomycin D (IC50 = 0.469 nM, CC50 = 6.71 nM, SI 14.3). Both derivatives had two- to seven-fold higher selectivity than actinomycin D, suggesting they are potential antimalarial agents.

articles.headline: How PfCoronin mutations drive artemisinin resistance in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Cellular and molecular basis of PfCoronin function in artemisinin resistance in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Ullah I, Farringer MA, Khushu M, Burkhard AY, Hathaway E, Willett BC, Shin SH, Sharma AI, Mallick R, Istvan ES, Martin MC, Harrigan O, Shao KL, Dvorin JD, Hartl DL, Volkman SK, Bopp S, Absalon S, Wirth DF
articles.details: This mechanistic study defines the cellular basis of PfCoronin-mediated artemisinin resistance in Plasmodium falciparum, complementing the established role of Pfkelch13 mutations. PfCoronin interacts with PfActin and localizes to the parasite plasma membrane, digestive vacuole membrane, and small vesicles containing host cell material; mutations disrupt this localization, perturb PfActin homeostasis, and reduce the proportion of ring-stage parasites adopting the cup-shaped architecture, lowering uptake of host cell contents. Like Pfkelch13 mutants, Pfcoronin mutants show reduced hemoglobin uptake, though the two proteins occupy distinct compartments and show no direct interaction. The authors propose that reduced ring-stage hemoglobin uptake limits heme-dependent activation of artemisinin, decreasing its cytocidal activity.

articles.headline: Soil-transmitted helminth exposure not linked to asymptomatic malaria in children
articles.title_original: Asymptomatic Plasmodium falciparum Infection Was Not Associated with Exposure to Soil-Transmitted Helminths in Children in Esse, Cameroon.
articles.authors: Lajos L, Fogang B, Jensen A, Atchombat D, Cornwall DH, Donkeu C, Nana-Mbianda CM, Slam C, Djeunga HCN, Zhan B, Koki Ndombo PO, Ayong LS, Lamb TJ
articles.details: A 1-month longitudinal study of 134 primary school children across three school-based sites in Esse, Centre Region, Cameroon, tested whether soil-transmitted helminth (STH) infection/exposure is associated with asymptomatic Plasmodium falciparum carriage. At screening, 94.8% were microscopy-positive for P. falciparum and 85.8% had asymptomatic infection, while 87.4% were serologically positive for at least one STH antigen. STH exposure did not differ between asymptomatic and uncomplicated symptomatic children (85.7% vs 90.9%, P>0.05), and no correlation was found between anti-STH antibody level and parasite load (P>0.05). The authors conclude STH exposure is not a major contributor to asymptomatic P. falciparum carriage.

articles.headline: Correction notice for Thai-Myanmar border malaria elimination article
articles.title_original: Correction: Challenges and responses of malaria elimination in a high-endemic area along the Thai-Myanmar border: A health systems perspective.
articles.authors: Saita S, Parker DM, Suk-Uam K, Phuanukoonnon S, Pooseesod K
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Review proposes cleavage-free dCas9-SSAP gene editing for malaria parasites
articles.title_original: The dCas9-SSAP as a promising genome editing tool in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Quansah E, Yang S, Jia Y, Yu L, Zhang C
articles.details: This review discusses the dCas9-microbial single-stranded annealing protein (dCas9-SSAP) system as a next-generation, 'cleavage-free' genome editing tool for Plasmodium parasites, contrasting it with classical CRISPR-Cas9 nuclease approaches that rely on double-strand breaks and damage-repair and leave inheritable genetic scars. The authors outline the genotoxicity pitfalls of nuclease-based editing, propose a plausible mechanistic framework for dCas9-SSAP-mediated editing, and discuss how it aligns with Plasmodium biology. No quantitative experimental results are reported.

articles.headline: Artemisinin-resistant parasites win under drug but vary without it
articles.details: This in vitro study used pairwise competitive growth assays to assess relative fitness of genetically distinct artemisinin-resistant (ART-R) and ART-sensitive Plasmodium falciparum clones isolated from the Thailand–Myanmar border, mixing clones and growing them alone or together with and without dihydroartemisinin while measuring relative densities via fluorescent microsatellite markers. ART-R strains showed a competitive advantage under high drug doses, but this advantage was variably lost in the absence of drug, producing a range of fitness phenotypes. The authors conclude that whether resistant parasites ultimately prevail when drug is removed depends on additional, possibly interacting genetic factors, complicating predictions of resistance spread.
articles.title_original: Competitive suppression and release in artemisinin-resistant Plasmodium falciparum field isolates.
articles.authors: Brenneman KV, Qahash T, Beckman KA, Nosten FH, Ferdig MT

articles.headline: Phage display identifies novel antigens on P. falciparum-infected red cells
articles.title_original: Identification of Novel Malaria Antigens Expressed on the Surface of RBCs Infected with Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Shakri AR, Mohapatra AD, Badhai J, Anand A, Varghese A, Raj DK
articles.details: Researchers sought new blood-stage malaria vaccine candidates by immunizing BALB/cJ mice with live P. falciparum-infected red blood cells (Pf-iRBCs), their membrane fraction, and live uninfected human RBCs in adjuvants, then using the polyclonal sera in phage-display biopanning of a P. falciparum cDNA library. The anti-Pf-iRBC sera specifically interacted with parasite-origin surface antigens and showed significant parasite-killing activity in an in vitro growth inhibition assay. Screening identified both known Pf-iRBC membrane antigens and several previously unknown antigens predicted (PlasmoDB) to associate with the Pf-iRBC membrane, which the authors plan to evaluate as vaccine candidates.

articles.headline: Case report links ACE polymorphism to severe falciparum malaria with thrombosis
articles.title_original: Potential Role of ACE Polymorphisms in Severe Malaria: A Case Report.
articles.authors: Pisapia R, Pomicino A, Minei G, Iadevito I, Perrella A
articles.details: This case report describes a 40-year-old woman from Seychelles, residing in Ivory Coast, who developed severe P. falciparum malaria complicated by thrombosis, with hyperparasitemia, acute kidney injury, and metabolic acidosis. Real-time PCR genetic testing identified the angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) I/D polymorphism along with thrombophilic variants MTHFR C677T and A1298C, which the authors suggest may have influenced the clinical course. They call for comprehensive studies of the genetic landscape in malaria-endemic regions to inform personalized treatment.

articles.headline: Long-acting injectable ivermectin kills malaria mosquitoes for months in cattle
articles.title_original: Selection of the optimal long-acting injectable formulation of ivermectin for use in humans to target malaria vectors in Western Africa: evaluation of pharmacokinetics and mosquitocidal efficacy in cattle under laboratory conditions.
articles.authors: Zela L, Pooda SH, Porciani A, Beneteau S, Sagna AB, Le Lamer-Déchamps S, Moiroux N, Ouédraogo COW, Somé AF, Pennetier C, Roberge C, Belem AMG, Dabiré KR, Dayo GK, Mouline K
articles.details: Laboratory study in cattle comparing three long-acting injectable ivermectin formulations (BEPO depot technology) given at 0.6 mg/kg (n=5 calves per formulation, plus 5 untreated controls) to identify the optimal candidate for human use against Anopheles vectors. mdc-STM-001 had the best pharmacokinetics (Cmax 34.5±12.7 ng/ml, 12% inter-individual variability). Its 10-day hazard ratios exceeded 4 with cumulative mortalities >50% for at least 60 days in both susceptible (Kisumu) and resistant (VK5) strains, and the 10-day LC50 for resistant mosquitoes (3.66 ng/ml) was maintained ≥126 days. Authors conclude mdc-STM-001 meets WHO Preferred Product Characteristics and supports progression to phase 1 trials.

articles.headline: Climate factors and vector control shape malaria trends in Southeast Iran
articles.title_original: The influence of climatic factors and vector control on malaria incidence in Southeast Iran: a 23-year longitudinal analysis.
articles.authors: Dehghan A, Rahimi Foroushani A, Rahimi M, Nejati J, Youssefi F, Nikpour F, Raeisi A, Hanafi-Bojd AA
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Half of adults had malaria, most had arbovirus exposure in DRC
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Malaria and Arbovirus Co-Exposure in the Boende Health Zone, Northwestern Democratic Republic of the Congo.
articles.authors: Tshilumba SM, Larivière Y, Zola Matuvanga T, Mutwadi A, Engbu D, Kapour G, Lemey G, Salloum M, Champagne M, Mangungulu D, Van Damme P, Muhindo-Mavoko H, Tevuzula VM, Mariën J, Peeters M, Geertruyden JV, Mitashi-Mulopo P
articles.details: In a December 2023 cross-sectional door-to-door survey of 379 adults in Inkanamongo village (Lokolia Health Area, Boende Health Zone, Tshuapa Province, DRC), malaria prevalence was 51.7% (95%CI 46.7-56.7) and overall arboviral seroprevalence was 78.4%, dominated by O'nyong-nyong virus (42.8%), Rift Valley fever virus (32.0%), and chikungunya virus (23.4%). Malaria was assessed by HRP2-based rapid diagnostic tests and arboviral IgG antibodies by Luminex multiplex immunoassay on dried blood spots. Concurrent malaria infection and arboviral exposure occurred in 40.4% of participants, with no sociodemographic factors significantly associated in multivariable analysis. The authors call for integrated surveillance and improved diagnostics in this remote Congo Basin setting.

articles.headline: Bed nets in Cameroon refugee camp failed against mosquitoes after 18 months
articles.title_original: Physical integrity and bioefficacy of long-lasting insecticidal nets 18 months after use among Central African Republic refugees in Gado-Badzere, a high malaria burden camp in Cameroon.
articles.authors: Nematchoua Weyou Z, Djemna Djieyep F, Lontsi-Demano M, Ngangue-Siewe IN, Tchigossou G, Ning Teh R, Adanzounon D, Kuokuo Kimbi H, Ule Ngole Sumbele I
articles.details: Cross-sectional study assessing physical integrity and bioefficacy of long-lasting insecticidal nets (LLINs) 18 months after distribution in the Gado-Badzere refugee camp, Eastern Cameroon. Of 440 LLINs examined, 60.7% were in good condition, 14.5% acceptable, and 24.8% deteriorated, with deterioration linked to washing practices, household size, and sex. All 30 nets tested in WHO cone assays were ineffective against field Anopheles gambiae s.l., with knock-down of 1.5% and mortality of 3.1%; vectors identified were An. gambiae (86.9%) and An. coluzzii (13.1%). Authors urge introducing next-generation LLINs and stronger monitoring.

articles.headline: No artemisinin resistance markers found in Madagascar malaria survey
articles.title_original: Updated mapping of Plasmodium falciparum kelch13 gene polymorphisms in Madagascar, 2024.
articles.authors: Roesch C, Ratovoson R, Delaunay C, Savary S, Duteurtre L, Saiman J, Villier V, Bourke C, Elison HE, Maminjanahary ET, Rabetokotany RL, Rakotondramanana HN, Mangahasimbola RT, Razafimaharo LY, Raobela O, Steinhardt L, Bowen A, Ariey F, Randremanana R, Coppée R, Witkowski B
articles.details: Nationally representative cross-sectional survey collected dried blood samples from 4850 febrile patients at 65 health facilities to map Plasmodium falciparum pfkelch13 polymorphisms, using targeted amplicon deep sequencing. Of 1944 P. falciparum-positive samples, 963 (49.5%) sequences were obtained and 885 (91.9%) were 3D7 wild-type; non-synonymous mutations occurred in 1.8% (17/963) and synonymous in 6.2% (60/963), with the most frequent being synonymous C469C (3.0%) and P417P (2.8%). None of the WHO-validated artemisinin partial resistance markers were detected. Authors conclude resistant parasites have not yet become established and provide a baseline for genomic surveillance.

articles.headline: Non-lethal Plasmodium protects mice against lethal Babesia via macrophages
articles.title_original: Immunity beyond borders: non-lethal Plasmodium confers cross-protection against lethal Babesia via macrophage activation.
articles.authors: Zafar I, Li Y, Kondoh D, El-Sayed SAE, Hasan T, Taniguchi T, Li H, Inoue N, Kato K, Xuan X
articles.details: In a rodent co-infection study, Plasmodium berghei XAT conferred robust heterologous protection against Babesia rodhaini, assessed by survival, parasitemia, hematology, splenic histopathology/qPCR, cytokine and antibody measurement, and flow cytometry, with mechanistic dissection using SCID mice and selective depletion of NK cells and macrophages. P. berghei XAT significantly reduced parasitemia and splenic damage, linked to pro-inflammatory IFN-γ, IL-12p70, and TNF-α balanced by increased IL-10, plus increased ROS/NO and expanded splenic macrophages. Protection persisted in SCID mice and after NK-cell depletion but was abolished by macrophage depletion. The authors conclude cross-protection is macrophage-driven, supporting macrophage-targeted immunomodulation for multivalent vaccines against apicomplexan parasites.

articles.headline: Computer-designed Plasmodium peptides show antibiofilm and anti-inflammatory activity
articles.title_original: Computer-Aided Design of Plasmodium chabaudi-Derived Peptides with Dual Antibiofilm and Anti-inflammatory Activities.
articles.authors: Quigua Orozco RM, Santos ADO, Rossetto JP, Madalozzo ES, Luchi LV, Rezende SB, Chan LY, Buccini DF, Macedo MLR, Mehta A, Craik DJ, Franco OL, Cardoso MH
articles.details: Researchers used the Joker computational design algorithm to generate three Plasmodium chabaudi-derived peptides (PcDBS1R1, PcDBS1R5, PcDBS1R9), then synthesized and characterized their structure and function. Biophysical analyses showed PcDBS1R5 and PcDBS1R9 adopted predominantly α-helical, highly amphipathic structures with antibacterial activity against an Acinetobacter baumannii clinical isolate, while PcDBS1R1 had greater structural plasticity and pronounced antibiofilm effects. None were cytotoxic to murine macrophages, and all significantly reduced nitric oxide production in LPS-stimulated macrophages, suggesting anti-inflammatory activity. The authors conclude these peptides are promising scaffolds for treating biofilm-associated bacterial infections.

articles.headline: SP in pregnancy reshapes gut microbiome to boost birthweight
articles.title_original: Beyond malaria prevention: sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine treatment in pregnancy selectively remodels the maternal gut microbiome to increase gestational weight gain and improve birthweight.
articles.authors: Waltmann A, Puerto-Meredith SM, Chinkhumba J, Mzembe E, Kayange M, Carroll I, Roach J, Mathanga DP, Gutman JR, Juliano JJ
articles.details: This study examined how intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP), an antifolate with antibiotic activity, improves birthweight compared with dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP), focusing on the maternal gut microbiome and gestational weight gain (GWG); prior work (NCT03009526) showed GWG mediates two-thirds of SP's birthweight benefit. In this first longitudinal characterization, about 45% of SP's GWG advantage over DP was explained by gut microbial changes consistent with its pharmacology. Microbiome-mediated GWG coincided with 126g higher birthweight in SP but not DP recipients (95%CI 22.6–229.3g; p=0.019). Relative to DP, SP suppressed gastrointestinal pathobionts and enriched anaerobic commensals, a microbiome-sparing pattern distinct from antibiotic-associated dysbiosis.

articles.headline: Quantized AI malaria microscopy models collapse under adversarial attacks
articles.title_original: Evaluating the Adversarial Robustness and Clinical Safety of Quantized Hierarchical Transformers for Edge-Based Malaria Microscopy.
articles.authors: Hasan U, Alghamdi TG, Nayeem MA
articles.details: This adversarial audit evaluated post-training INT8 quantization of Vision Transformers for edge-based malaria microscopy, testing a Swin-Tiny transformer against ViT-Tiny and MobileNetV3 baselines on a 27,558-image malaria dataset plus an out-of-distribution White Blood Cell dataset. Quantization compressed the Swin model 3.9× (to 27.89 MB) with a 0.11% accuracy drop while maintaining reliability on OOD tests. Both MobileNetV3 and INT8 Swin-Tiny collapsed to 0.00% accuracy under iterative white-box Projected Gradient Descent attacks, though the quantized Swin-Tiny resisted black-box transfer attacks, holding 81.00% accuracy. The authors conclude integer quantization provides no innate defense against targeted iterative perturbations, a critical vulnerability for diagnostic IoT networks.

articles.headline: In silico screen finds erucamide as antimalarial DXR inhibitor candidate
articles.title_original: Antimalarial Candidate Study from Three Different Fractions of Streptomyces hygroscopicus subsp. Hygroscopicus targeting Plasmodium DXR: An In Silico Approach.
articles.authors: Faratisha IFD, Fitri LE, Winaris N, Widodo N
articles.details: This in silico study identified compounds from fractions 13, 50, and 51 of Streptomyces hygroscopicus subsp. hygroscopicus using LC/HRMS, then evaluated antimalarial potential against the Plasmodium apicoplast MEP-pathway enzyme DXR (PDB 3AU9) via ADME/toxicity prediction, molecular docking (AutoDock Vina), and 100 ns molecular dynamics. From 18, 24, and 44 compounds identified in the three fractions, 9 were selected as potential antimalarials; all met drug-likeness criteria but only two had acceptable toxicity profiles. Dibenzylamine (fractions 13 and 50) and erucamide (fraction 51) showed higher DXR binding affinity than the native ligand, and the erucamide-DXR complex was more stable than dibenzylamine or the native ligand. The authors conclude erucamide is a promising candidate warranting in vitro and in vivo validation.

articles.headline: Media exposure boosts Ugandan women's malaria prevention knowledge, inequities persist
articles.title_original: Effect of cumulative exposure to media channels for malaria messages on knowledge of malaria prevention among women (15-49 years) in Uganda.
articles.authors: Natuhamya C, Obiora RU, Nwankwo GI, Agbi DM, Isiko I, Mwebesa E
articles.details: A secondary analysis of the 2018-2019 Uganda Malaria Indicator Survey assessed 7,124 women aged 15-49 for knowledge of four WHO-recommended prevention methods, using t-tests, margins analysis, and mixed-effects negative binomial regression. Awareness was 76.5% for bed nets but only 9.1% for ITNs, 6.2% for preventive medicine, and 4.3% for IRS. Cumulative media-channel exposure was significantly associated with knowledge of ITNs (p<0.001), preventive medicine (p=0.002), and IRS (p<0.001), but not bed nets. Women with secondary or higher education were more likely to report multiple-channel exposure (IRR=1.86; 95% CI 1.59-2.17), while rural and refugee women had lower exposure (IRR=0.77 and 0.28). Authors call for equity-focused communication strategies.

articles.headline: Methylene blue still kills artemisinin-resistant ring-stage P. falciparum in vitro
articles.title_original: Methylene blue retains in vitro activity against early ring-stage artemisinin-resistant Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Verdier O, Christensen P, Kaewkanya C, Kobphan P, Beau C, Phyo AP, Imwong M, Chotivanich K, White NJ, Nosten F, Chaumeau V
articles.details: This in vitro study tested whether methylene blue retains activity against early (0-3 h post-invasion) ring-stage P. falciparum carrying artemisinin-resistance kelch13 genotypes, using a modified ring survival assay on clinical isolates with wild type (n=3), R561H (n=9), or P441L (n=10), analyzed with a Bayesian mixed-effects Emax model. Methylene blue suppressed ring survival at low nanomolar concentrations with no kelch13-mediated cross-resistance: mean IC50 was 23 nM (95% CrI 15-37) for wild type, 27 nM (95% CrI 21-36) for R561H, and 14 nM (95% CrI 10-20) for P441L. Authors conclude methylene blue remains active against ring-stage artemisinin-resistant parasites.

articles.headline: P. vivax infects Duffy-negative people in Sudan, meta-analysis confirms
articles.title_original: Plasmodium vivax infection in Duffy-negative populations in Sudan: a systematic review and meta-analysis of host-parasite genetic adaptation.
articles.authors: Elfaki M, Ahmed S, Albsheer MMA, Hamid MMA
articles.details: A PRISMA-guided systematic review and meta-analysis (PROSPERO CRD42025127714) synthesized evidence on P. vivax infection, Duffy antigen polymorphisms, and parasite genetic adaptation in Sudan from studies spanning 2005-2025. Sixteen studies with 5,753 participants were included; P. vivax occurred in both Duffy-positive and Duffy-negative individuals. A random-effects meta-analysis of five studies reporting Duffy status found a pooled P. vivax prevalence of 11.7% (95% CI 7.2-17.3%) among Duffy-negative individuals, with moderate heterogeneity (I²=56%). Substantial PvDBP genetic diversity was observed, including multiple haplotypes and Malagasy-type and Cambodian-type gene duplications. Authors conclude Duffy-negativity does not confer complete protection and urge strengthened molecular surveillance.

articles.headline: Environment shapes avian Plasmodium communities in two Culex mosquito species
articles.title_original: Environmental factors affect the communities of avian Plasmodium in two species of Culex mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Martín-López AI, Ruiz-López MJ, Martínez-de la Puente J, Magallanes S, Taheri S, Ruiz S, Figuerola J
articles.details: This study examined how environmental factors influence avian Plasmodium communities in Culex pipiens and Culex perexiguus mosquitoes collected from 16 sites over two years, using nested PCR for detection, Sanger sequencing for lineage identification, linear mixed models, correspondence analysis, and Mantel tests. In Cx. pipiens, Plasmodium prevalence increased with NDVI while lineage richness declined with land surface temperature. In Cx. perexiguus, prevalence and lineage richness increased with minimum temperature, and diversity decreased with land surface temperature. Plasmodium communities differed between mosquito species, with community similarity correlating with geographic distance only in Cx. pipiens. Authors conclude environmental context and vector identity shape avian malaria lineage structure and distribution.

articles.headline: Merozoite antigen combinations linked to clinical malaria immunity in adults
articles.title_original: Controlled human malaria infection in adults identify combinations of merozoite antigens associated with clinical immunity.
articles.authors: Ogwang R, Nkumama IN, Mwai K, Odera D, Nyamako L, Fürle K, Rosenkranz M, Kimathi R, Njuguna P, Sim BKL, Hamaluba M, Frank R, Kapulu MC, Bejon P, Tuju J, Osier FHA
articles.details: Using samples from a controlled human malaria infection study of Kenyan adults (CHMI, n=86/142; NCT02739763) and a custom KILchip protein microarray, researchers measured IgG reactivity against seventy recombinant P. falciparum merozoite antigens to identify targets of naturally acquired clinical immunity. Data were analyzed with five statistical approaches: Wilcoxon rank sum test, Cox proportional hazards, modified Poisson regression, LASSO, and random forest. Antibodies against MSP1, MSP11, RAMA, MSP7, PF3D7_1401600 (PHISTB), and PTEX150 were consistently associated with clinical immunity, and in combination predicted complete protection. Authors suggest these findings may help prioritize next-generation blood-stage vaccine candidates.

articles.headline: Nearly half of Yemeni IBS patients had malaria co-infection
articles.title_original: Systemic inflammation and malaria co-infection in irritable bowel syndrome: a cross-sectional study of 142 Yemeni patients.
articles.authors: Ali NT, Mehdi MAH, Abdullah RS, Gubran ANM, Al-Abd NM, Ali GS, Mohsen Ali H
articles.details: This multicentre cross-sectional study assessed 142 adult patients (aged 18-70) diagnosed with irritable bowel syndrome by Rome IV criteria, testing for malaria by rapid diagnostic test. Malaria co-infection prevalence was 45.1% (64/142). Malaria-positive patients had significantly higher neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR; mean difference 0.56, p<0.001) and platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio (PLR; mean difference 22.6, p<0.001) and lower mean platelet volume (-0.60 fL, p<0.001). In adjusted logistic regression, higher NLR (aOR 3.21, 95% CI 1.89-5.45) and PLR (aOR 4.02 per 10-unit increase) independently predicted co-infection. Authors suggest these biomarkers could aid targeted screening in endemic settings.

articles.headline: CXCR6+CD127- Tr1 cells balance immunity and parasite persistence
articles.title_original: CXCR6+CD127- Tr1 cells balance immunity and persistence in Plasmodium falciparum infection.
articles.authors: Nideffer J, Bach F, Strubbe S, Lopez L, Zedi M, Nankya F, Briggs J, van der Ploeg K, Musinguzi K, Kim S, Garcia Romero A, Keya A, Camanag K, Lewis S, Abdelbasset M, Wang B, Boss A, Nansubuga E, Nankabirwa JI, Arinaitwe E, Takahashi S, Dorsey G, Greenhouse B, Rodriguez-Barraquer I, Kamya MR, Bacchetta R, Ssewanyana I, Haque A, Roncarolo MG, Jagannathan P
articles.details: Using scRNA-seq and in vitro/in vivo studies in malaria-endemic children in Uganda, researchers validated CXCR6+CD127- as markers enriching for bona-fide type 1 regulatory T (Tr1) cells, which comprise nearly 90% of the P. falciparum blood-stage antigen-specific CD4+ T cell pool. These Tr1 cells suppressed other CD4+ T cell proliferation via IL-10, were the dominant subset responding to infected red blood cells, and expanded after malaria while expressing IL-10 and IFNγ. Higher Tr1 frequencies correlated with a lower probability of symptoms given parasitemia but also with delayed parasite clearance in untreated asymptomatic children, suggesting they mediate clinical immunity while facilitating parasite persistence.

articles.headline: Genomics of African P. malariae shows no geographic structure
articles.title_original: Population genomics of Plasmodium malariae from four African countries.
articles.authors: Popkin-Hall ZR, Carey-Ewend K, Aghakhanian F, Oriero EC, Seth MD, Kashamuka MM, Ngasala B, Ali IM, Mukomena Sompwe E, Mandara CI, Kharabora O, Sendor R, Simkin A, Amambua-Ngwa A, Tshefu A, Fola AA, Ishengoma DS, Bailey JA, Parr JB, Lin JT, Juliano JJ
articles.details: Researchers performed hybrid capture and whole-genome sequencing of 77 Plasmodium malariae isolates from Cameroon (n=7), DRC (n=16), Nigeria (n=4), and Tanzania (n=50) collected 2015-2021 to characterize population structure and demography. They found no evidence of geographic population structure, with significantly lower nucleotide diversity and higher linkage disequilibrium than co-localized P. falciparum. Genome-wide selection scans flagged no invasion ligands or resistance orthologs as top hits, but targeted analyses revealed selective sweeps around four erythrocyte invasion ligands and six antimalarial resistance orthologs. Demographic modeling suggests African P. malariae is recovering from a bottleneck.

articles.headline: Pirimiphos-methyl IRS plus nets sharply cut malaria in Uganda
articles.title_original: Impact of indoor residual spraying and insecticide-treated nets on malaria burden in 8 districts in West Nile and Acholi regions, Uganda: a quasi-experimental study.
articles.authors: Namuganga JF, McDermott DP, Epstein A, Nankabirwa JI, Gonahasa S, Opigo J, Nabende I, Maiteki-Sebuguzi C, Kamya MR, Donnelly MJ, Dorsey G, Staedke SG
articles.details: This quasi-experimental study compared monthly malaria incidence across three reference centres receiving indoor residual spraying plus ITNs (West Nile, intervention) and five receiving ITNs only (Acholi, control) over baseline and two IRS periods, using negative binomial difference-in-difference models adjusted for lagged rainfall and seasonality. During IRS-1 with clothianidin-deltamethrin, the 15% incidence difference was non-significant (aIRR 0.85, 95% CI 0.69-1.04, p=0.11). During IRS-2 with pirimiphos-methyl, intervention incidence fell 79.3% from baseline (720.9 to 149.1 per 1000 person-years), a 73% reduction versus control (aIRR 0.27, 95% CI 0.22-0.34, p<0.001). Authors stress selecting context-specific insecticides amid pyrethroid resistance.

articles.headline: New pyrazole PfDHODH inhibitors designed as antimalarial leads
articles.title_original: Structure-based design of new pyrazole inhibitors targeting Plasmodium falciparum dihydroorotate dehydrogenase (PfDHODH).
articles.authors: Pippione AC, Villella N, Fornaseri F, Giorgis M, Sharma M, Rolando B, Sainas S, Salamanca E, Gimenez A, Cojean S, Vyas VK, Buccinnà B, Ramondetti C, Piccinini M, Poli G, Tuccinardi T, Alberti M, Miggiano R, Lolli ML, Boschi D
articles.details: Using structure-based optimization over three iterative rounds, researchers designed, synthesized, and evaluated new hydroxypyrazole derivatives as selective inhibitors of P. falciparum dihydroorotate dehydrogenase (PfDHODH), a validated pyrimidine-biosynthesis drug target. Several analogs showed PfDHODH activity with selectivity over the human isoform (SI>30), and X-ray crystallography confirmed predicted binding modes. The best compounds (derivatives 6, 7, 12, 13, 22) had low micromolar enzymatic inhibition (IC50 1.3-5.9 μM), favourable physicochemical properties, and antiparasitic activity in P. falciparum 3D7 cultures (EC50 15-22 μM) with negligible cytotoxicity (CC50>150 μM except compound 22). Despite poor antiplasmodial potency, the work provides SAR data for next-generation PfDHODH inhibitors.

articles.headline: Mosquito-parasite protein interface is potent malaria transmission-blocking target
articles.title_original: Disruption of the AgMOBP1-PfSyn5 interface abolishes malaria transmission.
articles.authors: Ramelow J, Niu G, Kostallas L, Lai L, Wang X, Li J
articles.details: Laboratory study characterizing AgMOBP1, an Anopheles gambiae midgut protein induced by blood feeding, and identifying its P. falciparum binding partner, the syntaxin-5-like protein PfSyn5. Recombinant AgMOBP1 enhanced Plasmodium infectivity by increasing midgut oocyst burden, while anti-AgMOBP1 antibodies significantly reduced infection, establishing it as a host factor promoting transmission. Anti-PfSyn5 polyclonal antibodies blocked transmission completely at 1 μg/mL, with an estimated IC50 of ~3 ng/mL (~20 pM). Authors conclude the AgMOBP1-PfSyn5 axis is a promising target for transmission-blocking vaccines and monoclonal antibodies.

articles.headline: Calcifer protein stabilizes Plasmodium basal body during male gamete formation
articles.title_original: Organelle mechanics: A brace for the Plasmodium basal body.
articles.authors: Engstler M
articles.details: Commentary on a study identifying Calcifer as a late-recruited stabilizer protein that protects the structurally simplified Plasmodium basal body during male gamete formation. Axoneme release places extraordinary mechanical stress on this vulnerable organelle, and Calcifer is described as guarding it against force-induced failure. No quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: Twelve-year blood film review shows persistent malaria in Bishoftu Town
articles.title_original: The persistent threat of malaria in Bishoftu Town: twelve-year trends and their implications for Addis Ababa.
articles.authors: Dufera BC, Mamo H
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of 252,610 blood films from the Bishoftu Town Health Office (January 2011–December 2022) identified 5,696 malaria cases (2.3%), with Plasmodium vivax accounting for 63.8% and P. falciparum 31.0%. The highest incidence (804 cases) occurred in 2017, and individuals older than 14 made up 66.3% of cases. Authors describe persistent malaria with a fluctuating trend near largely malaria-free Addis Ababa and emphasize the need for aggressive surveillance and targeted control, noting concern about potential spread of the invasive vector Anopheles stephensi.

articles.headline: Invasive vector Anopheles stephensi detected in Kisumu, first south of Equator
articles.title_original: Serendipitous detection of invasive malaria vector Anopheles stephensi in Kisumu, Kenya in June 2022.
articles.authors: Ndenga BA, Owuor KO, Wambua S, Bartilol B, Maia M, Mwangangi J, Omukuti R, Chemutai S, Arabu D, Miringu I, Bosire C, Mwendwa K, Winter CA, Rono MK, Mutuku FM, Taylor R, Bisanzio D, LaBeaud AD, Gerken KN
articles.details: Mosquitoes collected from an urban slaughterhouse in Kisumu City, western Kenya, in mid-2022 underwent bloodmeal amplicon analysis. 19.6% (11/56) of amplicon sequence variants matched mosquito species, including An. stephensi, from one pool of five mosquitoes morphologically classified as An. gambiae s.l.; mitochondrial cox1 sequencing confirmed the species across two well-supported clades, some grouping with northern Kenya and others with Ethiopian and Sudanese haplotypes. This is the first report of the invasive vector south of the Equator, and the authors recommend continued entomological and case surveillance.

articles.headline: Anticancer drug berzosertib predicted effective for malaria at low doses
articles.title_original: Repurposing Drugs for Malaria through a Human Dose Prediction: A Case Study with Berzosertib.
articles.authors: Redhi D, Mulubwa M, Mmonwa MM, Njoroge M, Krugmann L, Chibale K
articles.details: Drug-repurposing study evaluating the anticancer kinase inhibitor berzosertib for malaria. An oral dose-fractionation study in a P. falciparum-infected humanized mouse model showed antimalarial activity depended strongly on the time concentrations remained above the minimum parasiticidal concentration. Using a human physiologically based pharmacokinetic model, the authors predicted six daily intravenous 75 mg doses would treat uncomplicated blood-stage infection—lower than the cancer maximum tolerated dose of 440 mg IV twice weekly over a 21-day cycle for up to 18 weeks. They conclude further preclinical development is needed.

articles.headline: Repurposed antibiotics target P. falciparum RAD5 and WD11 proteins
articles.title_original: Repositioning Antibiotics Against Plasmodium falciparum RAD5 and WD11 as Novel Antimalarial Targets.
articles.authors: Albaqami AS, Oyewole OA, Adam KM
articles.details: This computational and in vitro study evaluated PfRAD5 and PfWD11 as novel antimalarial targets by screening a library of 216 antibiotics through molecular docking and molecular dynamics simulations, then testing top compounds against P. falciparum 3D7 cultures. Puromycin showed the highest single-agent inhibition (85% at 100 μg/mL), the triple combination of puromycin + raltegravir + dicloxacillin achieved complete inhibition, and puromycin + chloroquine was synergistic in a concentration-dependent manner. qPCR showed puromycin-based treatments downregulated PfRAD5 and PfWD11, while chloroquine alone upregulated PfRAD5. The authors conclude repurposed antibiotics offer a cost-effective strategy against emerging resistance.

articles.headline: Calcifer protein stabilizes Plasmodium basal body during exflagellation
articles.title_original: Mechanoregulation of basal body integrity during Plasmodium exflagellation.
articles.authors: Guan J, Gong Y, Liang W, Cui H, Yuan J
articles.details: This laboratory study identified Calcifer, a conserved Plasmodium protein essential for male gamete formation and mosquito transmission. Calcifer translocates to the basal body after axoneme assembly and persists during exflagellation; parasites lacking it form intact axonemes but show rapid axoneme disintegration during exflagellation, a defect rescued by inhibiting axoneme beating. In vivo and in vitro assays showed Calcifer forms a wrapping structure around the basal body at the axoneme minus end, directly stabilizing basal body integrity against mechanical forces from axoneme beating. The findings reveal a mechanical stabilization mechanism safeguarding basal body integrity.

articles.headline: Malaria peaks in autumn, declines over years in Malakand, Pakistan
articles.title_original: Spatial Distribution, seasonal dynamics and molecular confirmation of malaria in District Malakand, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan.
articles.authors: Rabia R, Ullah W
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: This retrospective study analyzed clinical data from the District Headquarters Hospital in District Malakand, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan from 2011 to 2017, with a random sample of 120 patients from 2017 used for microscopic and molecular (PCR) species confirmation. Of 41,863 tests, 6,246 were positive (overall test positivity rate 14.92%); males (58.89%) were more affected than females, and the 1–20-year age group had the highest case proportion. Annual test positivity declined from 32.97% in 2011 to 10.77% in 2017. Autumn had the highest seasonal case proportion (46.73%) and winter the lowest (7.09%), with Gharhi, Dargai and Haryankot identified as hotspots.

articles.headline: Commentary urges action on artemisinin partial resistance in Africa
articles.title_original: Africa cannot afford to wait for malaria treatment failure.
articles.authors: Pindolia D, Martinez-Vega R, Uzakbayeva G, Ishengoma D, Ngou O, Gosling R
articles.details: This commentary warns that artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs), the backbone of malaria treatment across Africa for more than two decades, are now under threat from artemisinin partial resistance (ART-R). Molecular surveillance has detected ART-R across eastern Africa, the Horn of Africa, and southern Africa. It has already been confirmed in four countries—Rwanda, Uganda, Eritrea and Tanzania—with signals reported in several neighbouring countries.

articles.headline: Stable malaria transmission hotspots identified in Mali district
articles.title_original: Analysis of clinical malaria hotspots according to transmission levels in two health areas of the Kolondieba health district, Mali.
articles.authors: Berthé I, Cissoko M, Diarra M, Mbaga DS, Koné M, Boly A, Togola OB, Haidara B, Théra I, Ongoiba A, Togola T, Mathias Dolo AD, Amaguiré Sy EHI, Konaté A, Coulibaly Y, Sissoko L, Bougoudogo I, Niangado B, Togola A, Traore D, Rabarijaona LP, Keita K, Traoré B, Tidiane Traoré CA, Sagara I
articles.details: This retrospective cross-sectional study analyzed 35,934 confirmed malaria cases from consultation registers between 2019 and 2021 in two health areas of the Kolondieba district, Mali—Kadiana (high risk) and Kolondieba Central (moderate risk)—using Kulldorff's method to identify high-risk clusters by transmission period. In Kadiana, most clusters were stable, with persistent hotspots across all eight periods (Kadiana and Tienkourani; RR 1.8–6.3). In Kolondieba Central, hotspots evolved from a localised configuration toward spatial expansion in later periods (RR 1.7–2.2), with Kolondieba town remaining a persistent hotspot. The authors emphasize fine-scale transmission heterogeneity and the importance of human mobility in guiding control.

articles.headline: Culex restuans key carrier of avian malaria parasites in Illinois
articles.title_original: Heterogeneous distribution of avian malaria parasites across mosquito species: disproportionate involvement of Culex restuans in the Midwestern United States.
articles.authors: Ciloglu A, Mackay AJ, Cara C, Kim CH, Marguccio A, Yan J, Stone CM
articles.details: Surveillance study screening 233 pools of 6170 unfed female mosquitoes from seven species collected at 38 protected natural areas in Illinois, using nested PCR for avian haemosporidian cytochrome b DNA, plus phylogenetics and pooled-binomial regression. 44 of 233 pools (18.9%) were positive, yielding 11 lineages (nine Plasmodium, two Haemoproteus), one Plasmodium lineage novel. pTUMIG03 (P. unalis) was most frequent and pSYAT05 (P. vaughani) most widespread; Culex restuans had the highest lineage diversity and infection rate and was most strongly associated with avian Plasmodium detection. Authors conclude Cx. restuans likely drives local enzootic circulation.

articles.headline: In silico method maps gene-control elements in malaria mosquito germline
articles.title_original: Decoding cis-regulatory elements in the germline of the human malaria vector Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Chesters E, Ravenscroft L, Thompson TA, Sylla L, Tembely B, Kerr J, Hasenauer J, Page N, Winny T, Nolan T, Sharakhov I, Tonge D, Galizi R
articles.details: Methods study presenting an in silico approach to discover cis-regulatory elements (CREs) in the germline of the malaria vector Anopheles gambiae by correlating DNA-motif searches with gene expression and analyzing motif frequency and distribution in putative promoters. The approach identified hundreds of putative CREs correlated with germline expression in one or both sexes, often linked to sperm developmental stages and chromosomal locations. A top-ranked testis-enriched CRE was validated with an in vivo dual-reporter assay, where mutating conserved nucleotides drastically altered male germline expression. Authors describe this as the first nucleotide-resolution regulatory annotation of the A. gambiae germline, aiding promoter design for genetic control strategies.

articles.headline: Sudan's 2023 conflict disrupted malaria surveillance reporting
articles.title_original: Impact of the 2023-armed conflict on routine malaria imapact indicators in Sudan (January 2020-March 2025): a mixed-methods analysis of surveillance data.
articles.authors: Alnaeem KF, Saha UR, Ahmed HF, Mergenthaler C
articles.details: Mixed-methods analysis of quarterly DHIS2 surveillance data (January 2020–March 2025) from 17 Sudanese states combined with interrupted time series analysis (11 states with complete post-conflict data) and three key-informant interviews, assessing the April 2023 armed conflict's effect on malaria impact indicators. Missing DHIS2 reporting rose substantially after April 2023, with persistent gaps in western and southern states but continuity in northern and eastern states. ITSA showed a statistically significant decline in quarterly reported malaria case rates per 100,000 population at the conflict quarter (p=0.02), with no significant trend change afterward. Informants cited facility destruction, workforce shortages, unpaid salaries, and communication breakdowns. Authors call for strengthening surveillance in conflict-affected settings.

articles.headline: Alpha-mangostin nanoparticles plus chloroquine act synergistically in malaria mice
articles.title_original: Synergistic Antimalarial Activity of Alpha-Mangostin Chitosan Alginate Nanoparticles with Chloroquine in Mice Infected with Plasmodium berghei: The Potential of a New Antimalarial Drug Combination.
articles.authors: Tjahjani S, Hermanto F, Muchtaridi M, Aulifa DL, Haq FA
articles.details: In vivo 4-day suppressive (Peter's) test in P. berghei-infected Swiss Webster mice evaluating combinations of soluble alpha-mangostin chitosan alginate nanoparticles (ACAN) with chloroquine at three weight ratios (189:1, 63:1, 567:1) across six concentrations in triplicate, with interaction assessed via the sum of fractional effective dose 50 (∑FED50; <1 indicates synergy). Combination ED50 values were 9.196, 7.626, and 82.13 mg/kg BW, all below 100 mg/kg. ∑FED50 values were 0.069 (ratio-1), 0.113 (ratio-2), and 0.414 (ratio-3), all far below 1. Authors conclude the ACAN–chloroquine combination shows marked synergistic antimalarial activity, strongest at ratio-1.

articles.headline: Low-altitude areas show higher malaria transmission in Pakistan province
articles.title_original: A Topographic Analysis of Malaria Transmission - Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan, 2019-2022.
articles.authors: Haq IU
articles.details: Topographic analysis of malaria transmission across different regions of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Province, Pakistan. High-altitude regions experienced low transmission, whereas low-altitude regions showed high transmission rates. The authors suggest that incorporating topography into malaria control strategies may help policymakers design more targeted and effective prevention measures to reduce the recurrent malaria burden in the region.

articles.headline: World Malaria Day 2026 commentary: driven to end malaria
articles.title_original: World Malaria Day 2026: Driven to End Malaria.
articles.authors: Zhou X, Chen J, Xia Z, Li S
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Cerebral malaria overview: suspect with impaired consciousness, treat with artesunate
articles.title_original: [Cerebral Malaria].
articles.authors: Ohnishi K
articles.details: This overview describes cerebral malaria, which should be considered when malaria patients show impaired consciousness. It explains that although malaria parasites infect red blood cells rather than neurons, cerebral malaria is thought to arise when parasite-infected red blood cells adhere to brain microvascular endothelial cells and obstruct the vascular lumen, though the detailed pathophysiology remains unclear. The authors advise initiating intravenous artesunate as soon as possible when cerebral malaria is suspected. No quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccine antigen PfARMA antibodies mark exposure, not protection
articles.title_original: The N-terminal region of malaria vaccine candidate asparagine-rich merozoite antigen is immunodominant and targeted by polyreactive antibodies.
articles.authors: Garza R, Marchioni JM, Honeycutt JD, Hurlburt NK, Torres C, Garcia A, Neog S, Loranc E, Yemington E, Towers D, Ssewanyana I, Pancera M, Lavinder JJ, Jagannathan P, Greenhouse B, Bol S, Bunnik EM
articles.details: Using samples from P. falciparum-exposed individuals, researchers studied naturally acquired B cell and antibody responses to the blood-stage vaccine candidate asparagine-rich merozoite antigen (PfARMA). Responses were dominated by IgM+ B cells recognizing the N-terminal intrinsically disordered region 1 (IDR1); a human monoclonal antibody to IDR1 was non-neutralizing while one binding the folded domain showed weak neutralization. Plasma IgM and IgG predominantly targeted IDR1 and rose with exposure, but did not correlate with protection in age- and exposure-matched children. About 30% of unexposed individuals had polyreactive IgG targeting IDR1. PfARMA localized in or near micronemes containing PfEBA-175. The authors conclude these responses may reflect recent exposure rather than functional immunity.

articles.headline: Meta-analysis: bird genetic diversity not linked to avian malaria
articles.title_original: Avian Malaria Infections Are Not Associated With Host Genetic Diversity: A Meta-Analysis.
articles.authors: Podmokła E, Pluciński B, Dubiec A
articles.details: This meta-analysis synthesized 35 studies covering 27 bird species and 85 effect sizes to test whether individual host genetic diversity relates to avian malaria infections caused by Plasmodium, Haemoproteus, and Leucocytozoon. It examined moderators including genetic marker type (functional vs. neutral), parasite genus, infection assessment method, host migratory behaviour, and latitude. Contrary to expectations, no significant association was found between genetic diversity and either infection prevalence or intensity, regardless of marker type, and no tested moderator (including MHC class or genotyping method) significantly influenced the relationship. The authors conclude broad diversity measures may not reliably predict infection outcomes and recommend integrating allele-specific analyses and antibody-based diagnostics.

articles.headline: How digital molecular diagnostic tools could help tackle malaria
articles.title_original: How can the use of digital molecular diagnostic tools help to tackle malaria?
articles.authors: Chamai M, Jebreel WMA, Kabuya JB, Rakotomalala Robinson D, Cunnington AJ
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: High sulfadoxine-resistance Pfdhps mutations in Cameroon malaria patients
articles.title_original: Polymorphisms of the plasmodium falciparum dihydropteroate synthase gene among patients attending LEPI and ADLUCEM hospitals in the west region of Cameroon.
articles.authors: Edna GDM, Nadia NAC, Cedric Y, Raoul SNS, Christian NO, Pacome TK, Sidiki NNA, Sandra TNJ, Merveille TT, Line GAP, Yval KT, Hu H, Choukem SP
articles.details: This molecular epidemiology study tested blood from 286 patients (198 women, 88 men) at two health facilities for Plasmodium falciparum dihydropteroate synthase (Pfdhps) mutations using semi-nested PCR and RFLP for the A437G and A581G alleles. P. falciparum was detected in 87 samples (30.41%), and positivity was significantly associated with education level (p=0.001; highest at 41.66% among those with primary education only). The A581G mutant allele frequency was 67.60% and A437G was 45.07%, with mixed alleles only for A437G (1.40%). Authors conclude the high mutation frequency indicates substantial sulfadoxine resistance pressure, though pyrimethamine-related dhfr mutations were not assessed so overall SP efficacy cannot be determined.

articles.headline: Shared Plasmodium lineages between humans and wild primates in Amazon
articles.title_original: Zoonotic and Anthroponotic Plasmodium spp. Circulation between Wild Primates and Indigenous Community, Peruvian Amazon, 2007-2020.
articles.authors: Ulloa GM, Greenwood AD, Cornejo OE, Alonso H, Santolalla Robles ML, Montero S, Lescano AG, Mayor P
articles.details: A molecular survey screened an Indigenous community (n=141) and sympatric nonhuman primates (n=341; 10 species) for Plasmodium using nested/quantitative PCR (cytb, cox3, 18S rRNA) and sequencing. Plasmodium was detected in 43.3% of humans and 51.9% of nonhuman primates; P. vivax/simium predominated in humans (42.1%) while P. brasilianum/malariae predominated in primates (24.6%), and P. falciparum was rare in both. Bayesian phylogenies showed >99.9% identity among human and primate lineages, supporting shared lineages, and one third of human infections were asymptomatic. Authors call for integrating wildlife surveillance into Amazon malaria elimination.

articles.headline: Modeling weather drivers and climate projections of South African malaria
articles.title_original: Understanding and modeling meteorological drivers of the incidence and spread of malaria in South Africa.
articles.authors: Wright CY, Kapwata T, Naidoo N, Maharaj R, Aleksandrov N, Tošić M, Blesić S
articles.details: This study examined how malaria depends on meteorological drivers across five district municipalities in Limpopo province, South Africa, using weekly hospital-admission data linked to regional temperature, rainfall, and evapotranspiration. Wavelet transform spectral analysis identified characteristic time lags tied to mosquito and pathogen development and human incubation, which informed a regression model for admission cases and critical threshold values of temperature, rainfall, and evapotranspiration that initiate disease spread. Disease projections were generated for 2021-2050 and 2051-2080 under Representative Concentration Pathways RCP2.6 and RCP8.5. No specific quantitative effect estimates are reported in the abstract.

articles.headline: Long-acting injectable ivermectin modeled to cut malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Modelling the impact of ivermectin-based optimal strategies on malaria control: the role of formulation, coverage, and mosquitocidal efficacy timing.
articles.authors: Dongmo FJ, Porciani A, Moiroux N, Pooda HS, Dabiré RK, Beneteau S, Le Lamer-Déchamps S, Wondji CS, Mouline K, Djidjou-Demasse R
articles.details: A mathematical transmission model incorporating ivermectin's pharmacodynamic persistence, stratifying humans by age and sex, compared three regimens: monthly oral 3×300 μg/kg for 4 months, monthly oral 1×400 μg/kg for 3 months, and a long-acting injectable BEPO formulation at 0.6 or 1.0 mg/kg, with the target population excluding women of childbearing age and children under 15 kg. Under perennial transmission and 50-90% coverage, oral regimens reduced total cases over one year by 7-11%, 9-14%, and 24-38%, while a single long-acting injectable campaign achieved 16-29%, rising to 33-57%, 48-74%, and 59-78% with two, three, and four campaigns. Authors conclude long-acting injectable ivermectin offers sustained efficacy against residual transmission and that Phase 1 trials are warranted.

articles.headline: Review of state-of-the-art pharmacotherapy for malaria in infants
articles.title_original: The impact of state-of-the-art pharmacotherapy for malaria in infants: a breakthrough?
articles.authors: Grobusch MP, Adegbite BR, Hanscheid T, Gupta N
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Avian malaria parasites found in Malaysian jungle fowl and poultry
articles.title_original: Molecular detection and genetic diversity of avian haemosporidia (Plasmodium and Haemoproteus) infecting red jungle fowls (Gallus gallus spadiceus) and domestic poultry in peninsular, Malaysia.
articles.authors: Fufa Ido G, Sharma RSK, Zakaria A, Lin LC, Ola-Fadunsin SD, Abdullah DA, Babjee SMA, Sani RA
articles.details: This survey screened 713 blood samples from red jungle fowls (241), village chickens (322), and broilers (150) of the Gallus gallus complex in peninsular Malaysia using both microscopy and PCR targeting a 500 bp (478 bp without primers) segment of the parasite cytochrome b gene. Microscopy detected Plasmodium in 4.6% and Haemoproteus in 1.1%, while PCR raised prevalence to 30.6% for Plasmodium and 10.7% for Haemoproteus. Sequencing and phylogenetic analysis confirmed Plasmodium gallinaceum, P. parasitese, and Haemoproteus sp., the first such molecular characterization in this host complex in the region.

articles.headline: Plasmodium huffi confirmed as generalist infecting Brazilian vultures and falcons
articles.title_original: No host too tough: New records of Plasmodium huffi in competent avian hosts, including Brazilian vultures and falcons.
articles.authors: Vieira LMC, Braga ÉM
articles.details: Using molecular and morphological methods, researchers report Plasmodium huffi—originally described in toucans—in previously unrecorded avian hosts in Brazil: one black vulture (Coragyps atratus, n=29) and one yellow-headed caracara (Daptrius chimachima, n=6), plus two red-legged seriemas (Cariama cristata, n=14). This is the first report of haemosporidian infection in black vultures in South America and the first record of P. huffi in a Falconiformes species. The presence of gametocytes in peripheral blood and high morphometric plasticity across hosts support the conclusion that P. huffi is a broadly adapted generalist capable of sustaining and potentially transmitting infection.

articles.headline: Review explores repurposing agricultural insecticides against resistant malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Malaria vector control: Can repurposed agricultural insecticides offer solutions to resistance issues?
articles.authors: Malone D, Zahouli JZB, Ekra AK, Lucas JR, Van Leeuwen T, Koudou BG
articles.details: This review evaluates the potential of insecticides originally developed for agriculture as new tools against insecticide-resistant Anopheles malaria vectors, as well as Aedes and Culex. It synthesizes evidence on neonicotinoids, pyrroles, broflanilide, meta-diamides, growth regulators, ivermectin, methoxyfenozide, and halofenozide, noting activity against both adult and larval stages and, for some compounds, dual action on vector survival and Plasmodium parasite development. The authors note several are already in WHO-recommended tools (nets, indoor residual sprays, larvicides) while others feature in non-endorsed interventions such as endectocides and attractive targeted sugar baits, and call for novel chemistries bridging agricultural and public health science.
