articles.headline: Profile of Eizo Takashima developing a new class of malaria vaccines
articles.title_original: Eizo Takashima-developing a new class of malaria vaccines.
articles.authors: Kirby T
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Uncomplicated malaria co-infection does not worsen COVID-19 outcomes
articles.title_original: Uncomplicated malaria as a risk factor for COVID-19 duration and severity in western Kenya and Burkina Faso (MALCOV): a prospective cohort study.
articles.authors: Barsosio HC, Tangara B, Marlais T, Kaboré JMT, Tiono AB, Otieno K, Wanjiku M, Achieng M, Onyango ED, Ondieki ED, Aura H, Odawo T, Allen DJ, Hannan L, Tetteh KKA, Soulama I, Ouedraogo A, Serme SS, Soulama BI, Barry A, Badoum ES, Matthewman J, Brazal-Monzó H, Canizales J, Drabko A, Wu W, Kariuki S, Lesosky M, Sirima SB, Drakeley C, Ter Kuile FO
articles.details: In a cohort study in western Kenya and Burkina Faso, 742 ambulatory patients with laboratory-confirmed SARS-CoV-2 and valid malaria tests were enrolled, of whom 151 (20%) had acute uncomplicated P. falciparum co-infection (treated with artemether-lumefantrine or pyronaridine-artesunate). Time to COVID-19 symptom resolution was similar with versus without malaria (median 9 vs 10 days; adjusted HR 1.14, 95% CI 0.91–1.42; p=0.26), and hospitalizations and deaths were comparable. SARS-CoV-2 clearance was slower in the malaria group by day 7 (aHR 0.69, 95% CI 0.51–0.94; p=0.017) but similar by day 28, and previous malaria exposure was associated with faster symptom clearance (pinteraction=0.042). The authors conclude that appropriately treated uncomplicated malaria co-infection does not adversely affect COVID-19 progression.

articles.headline: Review explores the relationship between malaria and COVID-19
articles.title_original: The fascinating malaria-COVID-19 relationship.
articles.authors: Awandare GA, Morang'a CM
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Intensive control reshapes malaria parasite genomics near elimination in Myanmar
articles.title_original: Impact of intensive control on malaria population genomics under elimination settings in Southeast Asia.
articles.authors: Li X, Arya GA, Thu AM, Landier J, Parker DM, Delmas G, Reyes A, Lwin KM, Sriprawat K, Nosten F, Anderson TJC
articles.details: Population genomic study of 2,270 genome-sequenced P. falciparum infections from 283 malaria posts in Kayin State, Myanmar, sampled over 58 months (2015–2020) during an elimination programme using malaria posts and mass drug administration that reduced transmission by 97%. Parasite effective population size declined, but artemisinin-resistance allele frequency changed minimally until 2020, when a single predominant kelch13-R561H genotype remained. The authors found sustained localized transmission of unique genotypes with positive relatedness correlations up to ~20 km, and MDA-driven founder effects, providing genomic evidence of control efficacy and rapid allele-frequency shifts near elimination.

articles.headline: Mapping gametocyte membrane protein complexes for transmission-blocking targets
articles.title_original: Deciphering Membrane Protein Complexes in Plasmodium falciparum Gametocytes via Integrative Structural Systems Biology.
articles.authors: Mostafavi S, Maurer V, Ruwolt M, Schwartz J, Stein F, Niedermüller K, Gilberger TW, Liu F, Seuring C, Witt S, Kosinski J, Löw C, Filarsky M
articles.details: Integrative structural systems biology study of the Plasmodium gametocyte membrane proteome, the transmissible sexual stages that remodel host erythrocytes and are not susceptible to most antimalarials. The authors used cross-linking mass spectrometry to detect residue-level protein proximities, size-exclusion cofractionation mass spectrometry for orthogonal support, and AlphaFold/AF3x modeling with cross-links as restraints. They prioritized known and previously undescribed host-pathogen and pathogen-pathogen membrane-associated complexes across parasite and erythrocyte compartments, advancing understanding of gametocyte biology to inform future transmission-blocking interventions.

articles.headline: Perspective argues proteomics should be core to malaria systems biology
articles.title_original: Integrative proteomics to illuminate host-parasite interactions in malaria.
articles.authors: Attallah N, Srivastava H, Carlton JM, Idaghdour Y
articles.details: This perspective argues that proteomics should be a central component of malaria systems biology, complementing genomics by directly quantifying protein abundance, post-translational modifications, interaction networks, localization, and turnover in both host and parasite. The authors contend these approaches can illuminate mechanisms of cytoadhesion, endothelial dysfunction, immune modulation, tissue-specific pathology, and variability in therapeutic response. They highlight high-throughput, single-cell, and spatial proteomics combined with AI-driven pipelines as enabling comprehensive, quantitative profiling of host-parasite crosstalk. No empirical quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: Survey finds knowledge-practice gap in malaria prevention among Congolese miners
articles.title_original: Knowledge, attitudes and practices of malaria control and prevention in a high-exposure occupational group: A cross-sectional survey of mining workers in Haut-Katanga Province in the Democratic Republic of Congo.
articles.authors: Liang C, Du Y, Frumence G, Ke X, Kasombo PM, Mukolo JM, Gu Y, Zhou H, Zhu G, Huang J, Cao J
articles.details: Cross-sectional KAP survey of 606 mining workers (485 valid questionnaires) in Haut-Katanga Province, Democratic Republic of Congo, assessing malaria control and prevention knowledge, attitudes, and practices via structured questionnaire and multivariate logistic regression. Overall 365 workers (75.3%) had adequate knowledge; middle school education (adjusted OR=0.37; 95% CI 0.22–0.64; p<0.01) and early-morning/evening shifts (adjusted OR=0.46; 95% CI 0.24–0.90; p=0.02) were barriers to adequate knowledge. Willingness to use insecticide-treated nets was 99.3% and reported net use 91.1%. The authors conclude a persistent knowledge-practice gap requires tailored training, subsidized tools, and malaria-specific paid sick leave.

articles.headline: Why Sudanese migrants in Egypt delay seeking malaria care
articles.title_original: Sudanese migrants' pathways to malaria care in Egypt: An in-depth qualitative study.
articles.authors: Elafef E, Edris H, Abdelrahman S, Elnahry W, Hadjiabduli S, Abdelkhalek A, Adam A, El-Ghitany E, Hilali M, Hamed Arisha A, Bani I, Seedat F, Hargreaves S, Evangelidou S, Requena-Méndez A
articles.details: Qualitative study using semi-structured interviews with 21 adult migrants recruited in Cairo (all Sudanese, 90.5% female) who had prior malaria experience, exploring care-seeking behaviour in newly malaria-free Egypt. Five participants (24%) developed symptoms immediately on arrival; 19 (90.5%) initially relied on traditional remedies or self-medication without malaria testing. Barriers included distrust of healthcare workers, stigma, fear of quarantine, concerns about drug side effects, and misinformation, plus system factors of affordability, accessibility, and perceived quality. The authors conclude that improving access requires targeted health education to address misconceptions and build trust.

articles.headline: Malaria deaths from child growth failure rose globally after 2019
articles.title_original: The unequal malaria burden attributable to child growth failure: a global analysis among children under five from 1990 to 2021.
articles.authors: Deng JJ, Cheng XE, Li S, Chang YW, Wang P, Tao SS, Pan HF
articles.details: Systematic analysis of Global Burden of Disease 2021 data estimating the under-5 malaria burden attributable to child growth failure (CGF) from 1990 to 2021, using age-standardized mortality (ASMR) and DALY rates with annual percentage change metrics. The CGF-attributable malaria burden declined overall (AAPC -1.26; 95% CI -1.36 to -1.17) but rose after 2019 (APC 3.66; 95% CI 2.38 to 4.80). Burden was highest in males (ASMR 17.59 per 100,000), infants aged 6-11 months (31.63 per 100,000), West and Central Africa (88.09 per 100,000), and Niger (149.94 per 100,000), with socio-demographic index inversely correlated with burden.

articles.headline: Dual-antigen bioconjugate vaccine protects mice and reduces malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Robust Bioconjugated Antigens Induce Immune Responses Preventing Malaria Infection and its Transmission.
articles.authors: Sivakumaran N, Flores-Garcia Y, Naar CM, Chen S, Mehta M, van Leeuwen FWB, Tripathi A, Murugan R, Roestenberg M, Zavala F, Rehm BHA
articles.details: Researchers used engineered Escherichia coli to bioconjugate the pre-erythrocytic RTS,S antigen and the sexual-stage Pfs47 subdomain into biopolymer particles, creating a dual-antigen Pfs47-RTS,S-BP vaccine. The fused formulation showed synergistic immunogenicity versus single-antigen particles, retained antigenicity after storage at 37°C, and induced durable antibody, T cell, and liver-resident memory T cell responses. It generated RTS,S-specific antibodies 5.3-fold above the RTS,S/AS01 protective threshold, achieved 80.4% protection against mosquito-bite challenge, and 68.15% transmission-reducing activity. The authors note functional in vivo assessment of the thermostable formulations has not yet been performed.

articles.headline: New hydroxamate inhibitors selectively block P. falciparum aminopeptidase P
articles.title_original: Hydroxamate-based inhibitors reveal structural determinants of selectivity for Plasmodium falciparum aminopeptidase P.
articles.authors: Mills BJ, Gregory KS, Cozier GE, Taweechai S, Loughlan E, McConkey GA, Isaac RE, Foster R, Acharya KR
articles.details: Using structure-activity relationships of the existing aminopeptidase P inhibitor apstatin, researchers designed more potent inhibitors of Plasmodium falciparum aminopeptidase P (PfAPP), a target that limits amino acid supply during erythrocytic-stage hemoglobin breakdown. Adding a hydroxamic acid metal-binding group and an aromatic P4' phenylalanine (compound 6d) improved potency from apstatin's Ki of 16 μM to 685 nM, and replacing the P3' proline with a 2-substituted piperidine (6e) further lowered Ki to 24 nM. Crystal structures showed active-site binding with the hydroxamic acid coordinating the di-metal center; the P4' phenylalanine drives selectivity by interacting with Tyr617 of the adjacent monomer.

articles.headline: Over half of febrile returning travelers tested for malaria stay undiagnosed
articles.title_original: High rate of undiagnosed cases of fever among returning travelers from tropical areas tested for malaria: a single-center retrospective study.
articles.authors: Caronni S, Pieruzzi ME, Salari F, Behring AH, Rizzo A, Poloni A, Grosso S, Mancon A, Galimberti L, Oreni L, Casalini G, Mileto D, Gori A, Dolci A, Giacomelli A, Antinori S
articles.details: Observational, retrospective single-center study in Italy (January 2018-December 2024) of 778 febrile travelers tested for malaria with symptom onset within 30 days of returning from tropical areas. 446/778 (57%) remained undiagnosed, though definite diagnoses rose from 29.4% in 2018 to 57.8% in 2024. Arboviral infections were found in 107 (13.6%), with dengue most frequent (95, 12.2%), followed by malaria (72, 9.3%) and pneumonia (42, 5.4%). Compared with travelers from Africa, those from the Americas (aOR 0.45, 95% CI 0.28-0.73, p=0.001) and Western Pacific (aOR 0.49, 95% CI 0.26-0.95, p=0.034) had lower odds of remaining undiagnosed.

articles.headline: Biosensor beats rapid test for G6PD screening in malaria patients
articles.title_original: Diagnostic Accuracy of Point-of-Care Devices for Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase Deficiency Detection Among Malaria Patients: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
articles.authors: Unnikrishnan G, Singh A
articles.details: A systematic review and meta-analysis of 13 cross-sectional studies evaluated point-of-care devices for detecting glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency in malaria patients, comparing the CareStart G6PD rapid test and the STANDARD G6PD Biosensor against spectrophotometry at activity thresholds of 30% and 70%. CareStart's pooled sensitivity was 0.82 (95% CI 0.55–0.95) at 30% but fell to 0.54 (0.31–0.76) at 70%, with specificity 0.96 at both. The STANDARD Biosensor performed better, with sensitivity 0.99 (0.96–1.00) at 30% and 0.96 (0.90–0.98) at 70%, and specificity 0.98 and 0.96. Authors conclude the quantitative Biosensor is preferable for identifying intermediate G6PD activity to guide safe radical cure with 8-aminoquinolines.

articles.headline: Plant compound brazilein kills P. falciparum by disrupting iron homeostasis
articles.title_original: Brazilin and its oxidized form "brazilein", the natural fluorescently traceable agents, toxify Plasmodium falciparum to pyknotic death via disrupting its iron homeostasis.
articles.authors: Kulaphisit M, Jaikamlue W, Jaitan N, Sirirak J, Tamdee P, Pomlok K, Chumchanchira C, Changkhong S, Wipasa J, Saenjum C, Lithanatudom P
articles.details: Using a Multispectral Imaging Flow Cytometry algorithm to measure parasitemia, stage and survival, researchers screened a decoction of Caesalpinia sappan heartwood for anti-plasmodial activity against Plasmodium falciparum. Brazilin was the major active compound, while its oxidized, water-stable form brazilein binds labile Fe2+ pools in the parasite and red blood cells, infiltrates hemoglobin, and binds the active site of apical membrane antigen 1 (AMA1), inhibiting hemoglobin digestion and erythrocyte invasion. Both compounds are reversibly fluorescent, allowing real-time tracking of their transformation. These disruptions of iron homeostasis induced pyknotic parasite death, identifying brazilein as a traceable natural anti-plasmodial agent with a novel mechanism. No quantitative potency values were reported in the abstract.

articles.headline: Dengue and malaria co-circulate in febrile children in Libreville, Gabon
articles.title_original: Molecular evidence of co-circulation of different dengue virus serotypes associated with malaria co-infection among febrile children in Libreville, Gabon.
articles.authors: Moutombi Ditombi BC, Fritz M, Kombila Koumavor CA, Ndong Ngomo JM, Moussavou Mabika DA, Ndong Akomezoghe L, Agbanrin AA, Mawili-Mboumba DP, Leroy EM, Bouyou-Akotet MK
articles.details: A retrospective molecular study analyzed plasma from 151 febrile children at a malaria sentinel surveillance site in Libreville, Gabon, between September and October 2022, screening for arboviruses (DENV, CHIKV, ZIKV) by RT-qPCR and malaria by microscopy. DENV RNA was detected in 18.5% (serotypes DENV-1 and DENV-3, possibly DENV-4), with no CHIKV or ZIKV; malaria prevalence was 56.9% and dengue-malaria co-infection 9.3%. Dengue predominated in children under 5, while those aged 5–10 had higher odds of malaria (OR 2.61, 95% CI 1.07–7.02; p=0.03) and co-infection (OR 16.28, 95% CI 1.85–79.40; p=0.002). Severe anemia was most common in malaria (p=0.02) and thrombocytopenia most marked in co-infected patients (p<0.01). Authors urge stronger differential diagnosis and surveillance for febrile illness.

articles.headline: Simulations propose tetrandrine as inhibitor of P. falciparum heme detoxification
articles.title_original: Atomistic simulations identify the Tetrandrine as potent anti-malarial drug candidate against Plasmodium falciparum targeting Heme Detoxification Protein (HDP).
articles.authors: Bux K, Abbasi U, Hasan KA, Hussain A, Malik F, Herwig R
articles.details: This computational study screened natural compounds against the Heme Detoxification Protein (HDP) of Plasmodium falciparum, which converts toxic heme into inert hemozoin, as a novel anti-malarial target. With no crystal structure available, HDP was modeled using AlphaFold and SWISS-MODEL, and the AlphaFold model passed stereochemical and Ramachandran assessments. Molecular docking gave tetrandrine, a bis-benzyl-isoquinoline alkaloid, the highest binding affinity (-13.0 kJ/mol), exceeding Bisnordihydrotoxiferine (-10.2), Neferine (-9.01), and Oxytetracycline (-10.9), with stabilizing interactions at His122, His172, His175, and His197. A 100 ns molecular dynamics simulation (RMSD, RMSF, Rg, SASA) confirmed complex stability, and MM-PBSA free energy of -30 kcal/mol supported thermodynamically favorable binding.

articles.headline: Mass spectrometry finds P. vivax proteins in patient urine for diagnosis
articles.title_original: Discovery of parasite proteins in the urine of vivax malaria patients through mass spectrometry.
articles.authors: Tripathi H, Kar PP, Babu AP, Srivastava A, Pachori G, Bhatt TK
articles.details: Aiming for non-invasive malaria diagnostics, researchers used mass spectrometry to analyze urine samples from Plasmodium vivax-infected patients compared with controls, since urine is an accessible source for biomarker discovery. The comparison revealed proteins uniquely present or absent between groups, including 252 human proteins detected only in infected samples, which gene ontology analysis linked to key host and parasite biological pathways. Twenty-eight P. vivax proteins were identified in infected urine, including a rifin-like protein, single-stranded DNA-binding protein, and profilin. Authors propose these parasite proteins as candidate biomarkers for non-invasive detection of Plasmodium infection.

articles.headline: Rethinking the biological identity and clinical meaning of malaria-derived particles
articles.title_original: "What are we measuring?" reconsidering the biological identity and clinical interpretation of malaria-derived particles.
articles.authors: Lei Z
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Spatial repellent emanators cut malaria infections in Nigerian displacement camps
articles.title_original: The effectiveness of long-lasting spatial repellent emanators against malaria in humanitarian crisis settings in northern Nigeria: a two-arm pragmatic, open-label, controlled trial.
articles.authors: Allan RJ, Scherrer R, Estecha-Querol S, Weetman D, Paris L, Ba'abba Goni U, Idris Abdulhamid F, Nfornuh Alenwi B, Ahmad S, Cross CL, Ashir GM, Waziri M, Ntadom G, Messenger LA
articles.details: A 6-month, two-arm pragmatic, open-label, controlled trial in 24 camps for internally displaced people in Maiduguri, Nigeria, enrolling a monthly cohort of children aged 6–10 years (1655 participants; 842 intervention, 813 control across 12 vs 12 camps). After adjusting for baseline prevalence, spatial repellent emanators significantly reduced malaria infection incidence (incidence rate ratio 0·713, 95% CI 0·584–0·869; p=0·001), with 22·5% protective efficacy against first infection (hazard ratio 0·775, 95% CI 0·637–0·943; p=0·011). They also reduced pyrethroid-resistant female Anopheles gambiae s.l. density (IRR 0·0231; p<0·0001) and blood feeding (0·0195; p<0·0001), with high acceptability. Authors conclude emanators add an efficacious vector-control tool for humanitarian emergency settings.

articles.headline: Cyclic peptides block Plasmodium oocyst-rupture protein dimerization to halt transmission
articles.title_original: Targeting the histone-fold dimerization interface of oocyst rupture proteins from Plasmodium berghei for antimalarial inhibitor discovery.
articles.authors: Ballabio F, Bertaso C, Villa M, Livero O, Del Cont Bernard A, Russo R, Gessmann R, Siden-Kiamos I, Preira CMF, Kumawat A, Gabrieli P, Camilloni C, Currà C, Masiero S, Nardini M, Gourlay LJ
articles.details: A structural and drug-discovery study targeting the histone-fold domain (HFD) heterodimerization of Plasmodium Oocyst Rupture Proteins ORP1 and ORP2, which is essential for oocyst rupture in the Anopheles vector. The authors solved the first HFD ORP heterodimer structure by X-ray crystallography at 3.1 Å resolution and used a GAL4-based yeast two-hybrid combinatorial library of cyclic peptides to identify six candidates that inhibit dimerization in vitro. Molecular docking confirmed all six bind at the dimer interface, allowing ranking for in vivo testing of oocyst-rupture blockade. This targets the under-exploited mosquito stage of the parasite lifecycle.

articles.headline: Flow cytometry method quantifies malaria-derived particles in blood
articles.title_original: A novel flow cytometry approach to quantify malaria-derived particles.
articles.authors: Palasuwan A, Sophondilok K, Ampawong S, Srisutham S, Noulsri E, Palasuwan D
articles.details: The study optimized a flow cytometry approach to enumerate malaria-derived particles (MDPs) per microliter of blood, using propidium iodide, red blood cell lysis solution, a CytoFlex cytometer, size-standard beads, and counting beads, with electron microscopy of parasite ultrastructure. MDP levels were significantly increased in P. falciparum and P. vivax infections versus fewer than 1 particle/μL in controls, and MDP counts correlated with the percentage of infected red blood cells by manual counting (R2 = 0.94). Parasite density correlated with MDPs in both P. falciparum (R2 = 0.94) and P. vivax (R2 = 0.91). The authors conclude the approach is reliable and straightforward for routine diagnosis and treatment monitoring.

articles.headline: Multistage malaria vaccine shows first efficacy against infection and transmission
articles.title_original: First efficacy evidence for a multistage malaria vaccine targeting infection and transmission.
articles.authors: Moncunill G, Dobaño C
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Multistage malaria vaccine ProC6C shows 76% efficacy in human challenge trial
articles.title_original: Efficacy of ProC6C-AlOH/Matrix-M against Plasmodium falciparum infection and mosquito transmission: a phase 2, randomised, controlled human malaria infection study.
articles.authors: Kone M, Plieskatt J, Thienta M, Kamate B, Ofori EA, Gmeiner M, Haidara B, Kanoute MB, Traore B, Poudiouguo OA, Bayogo HS, Diarra I, Bengaly K, Zeguime A, Sylla D, Sacko A, Hoffman SL, McCall MBB, Theisen M, Sagara I
articles.details: In this randomised, double-blind controlled human malaria infection (CHMI) study in malaria-experienced Malian adults aged 18-50, the multistage P. falciparum vaccine ProC6C-AlOH/Matrix-M (targeting PfCSP, Pfs230, and Pfs48/45) achieved a per-protocol vaccine efficacy of 76% (95% CI 36-91, p=0.0022), or 54% by proportional analysis (95% CI 9-77, p=0.029). 34 participants received three injections of vaccine (n=17) or rabies control (n=17), and 32 underwent CHMI with 22,500 P. falciparum sporozoites 94 days after the last dose. Six of 16 vaccine recipients versus 13 of 16 controls developed parasitaemia, with median time to positivity delayed by 2 days (14 vs 12 days, p=0.049). Vaccination was well tolerated.

articles.headline: P. falciparum LSA3 protein aids final stage of red blood cell invasion
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Liver Stage Antigen 3 plays a role in completing the final stages of merozoite invasion of red blood cells.
articles.authors: Morita M, Jennison C, O'Neill MT, Kanoi BN, Ishino T, Tsuboi T, Boddey JA, Takashima E
articles.details: This in vitro study investigated the role of Liver Stage Antigen 3 (LSA3), a merozoite dense granule protein and blood-stage vaccine candidate, in P. falciparum red blood cell invasion. LSA3 localised to the posterior end of invading merozoites and was accessible to anti-LSA3 antibodies. lsa3 knockout (ΔLSA3) parasites grew more slowly, and time-lapse imaging showed a 65% reduction in normal invasion completion relative to wild-type. ΔLSA3 parasites frequently displayed aberrant accolé-form morphology after internalisation. The authors conclude LSA3 plays an important role in the late-invasion phase of the erythrocytic cycle, the first such evidence for a dense granule protein.

articles.headline: Computational study suggests antimalarials may allosterically target Plasmodium food-vacuole protein
articles.title_original: Computational characterization of a HotDog domain-containing hypothetical protein from the food vacuole of Plasmodium falciparum reveals potential allosteric regulation by antimalarials.
articles.authors: Gupta P, Kaushik NK, Sachdeva C, Mathur K, Pai SS, Saha B, Dhir S, Sharma A
articles.details: This bioinformatics study characterized PF11_0364 (PfHDDCP), an uncharacterized P. falciparum food vacuole-associated hypothetical protein. Conserved domain analysis identified a HotDog fold typical of acyl-CoA thioesterases, suggesting a role in lipid metabolism. A 3D model built with I-TASSER had over 90% of residues in favored Ramachandran regions and an acceptable ProSA Z-score. Docking with HDOCK and AutoDock predicted Acetyl-CoA and Acyl Carrier Protein bind at the catalytic Binding-Site 1, while antimalarial compounds bind at a distinct secondary pocket (Binding-Site 2), suggesting a possible allosteric site. Molecular dynamics indicated stable complexes. The authors stress these predictions require experimental validation.

articles.headline: Sustained malaria transmission found in high-altitude Ethiopian highlands, not from travel
articles.title_original: High-Altitude Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax Reservoirs in Ethiopia Are Not Linked to Recent Travel.
articles.authors: Ewnetu Y, Kayode TA, Oduma CO, Adeyemi-Kayode TM, Zemene A, Lemma W, Berhane N, Koepfli C
articles.details: Subclinical prevalence surveys were conducted at 8 sites spanning altitudes below 1900 m to 2900 m in the Ethiopian highlands, screening by qPCR plus microscopy or RDT, with recent-travel data collected. Among 1241 subclinical individuals, P. falciparum prevalence by qPCR decreased from 67% at 1850 m to 16% at 2850 m, while P. vivax ranged 10-34% with no altitude trend; 20.3% reported recent travel, which did not affect infection risk. Among 1775 microscopy-positive clinical patients, recent travel ranged from 2-16% below 2500 m up to 100% at 2830 m. LDH-based RDT detected nearly twice as many subclinical infections as HRP2-based. The authors conclude sustained local transmission likely occurs up to 2800 m and sites up to 2500 m should be considered endemic.

articles.headline: National genomic profiling of antimalarial resistance in Zambian children
articles.title_original: National genomic profiling of Plasmodium falciparum antimalarial resistance in Zambian children participating in the 2018 malaria indicator survey.
articles.authors: Fola AA, Ciubotariu II, Dorman J, Mwenda MC, Mambwe B, Mulube C, Kasaro R, Hawela MB, Hamainza B, Miller JM, Bailey JA, Moss WJ, Bridges DJ, Carpi G
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Molecular study of malaria vectors in rural Osun State, Nigeria
articles.title_original: Molecular Characterization and transmission indices of malaria vector in a rural community in Osun State south-west Nigeria.
articles.authors: Oyewusi RA, Surakat OA, Abdulsalam MM, Yusuff YO, Igenoza AJ, Iwalewa OZ, Ganiu OD, Busari LO, Adeleke MA
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Moderate adherence to Ethiopia's malaria test-and-treat guideline during resurgence
articles.title_original: Implementation fidelity of Ethiopia's Malaria test-and-treat guideline amid a resurgence in Amhara Region: A mixed-methods study.
articles.authors: Lake MW, Gelaye KA, Asemahagn MA, Muchie KF, Engedaw HA, Yenesew MA
articles.details: A convergent parallel mixed-methods study assessed implementation fidelity of Ethiopia's malaria test-and-treat guideline in 53 health facilities (38 public, 15 private) in Amhara Region, using Carroll's framework (content, coverage, frequency) standardized to a 0-100 composite score, plus 32 in-depth interviews. Overall mean fidelity was 64.3% (SD 12.1); 40% of facilities reached high fidelity (≥75%) and 13% scored <50%. Public facilities outperformed private ones (median 67% vs 63%; Wilcoxon p=0.041). Higher fidelity was associated with greater participant responsiveness (β=3.4, p<0.001), stronger facilitation (β=2.8, p<0.001), and lower perceived complexity (β=2.1, p<0.001). Authors recommend strengthening supervision, including private facilities.

articles.headline: Anopheles coluzzii dominates malaria vectors near Burkina Faso dam
articles.title_original: Characteristics and dynamics of malaria vectors around the Soum dam in Nanoro Health District, Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Ilboudo H, Sanon EDG, Hien DFS, Traoré F, Hansson H, Kaboré B, Tahita MC, Derra K, Rouamba E, Bouda I, Rouamba T, Alifrangis M, Sorgho H, Magnussen P, Tinto H
articles.details: This entomological study characterized malaria vector dynamics in five villages at varying distances from the Soum dam in Nanoro Health District, Burkina Faso, collecting indoor resting mosquitoes monthly by pyrethrum spray catches and using PCR for species, blood meal source, and P. falciparum infection. Of 11,378 Anopheles collected (69.9% female), An. gambiae s.l. was most abundant (86.5%), followed by An. funestus (10.6%); within the gambiae complex, 91.5% were An. coluzzii. Vector density was highest in Soum (49.7%) and fell with distance from the dam. The overall sporozoite rate was 6.2%, with higher rates in distant villages (Seguedin 9.5%, Soala 8.7%). Among tested mosquitoes, 34.7% fed on humans. Authors highlight complex dam-associated vector dynamics and the need for localized control.

articles.headline: Commentary: Plasmodium gets inside through the back door
articles.title_original: Plasmodium gets inside through the back door.
articles.authors: Jajosky RP, Stowell SR
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Six long-latency P. vivax relapses after Korean Peninsula deployment
articles.title_original: To Break a Fever: Relapse of Long-Latency Plasmodium vivax Malaria After Service in the Korean Peninsula.
articles.authors: Robben PM, Woo NW, Merk AR, Jansen NK, Mody RM
articles.details: A case series reports 6 patients from a cluster of Plasmodium vivax malaria diagnosed at Fort Bliss in 2022 among active duty personnel who had redeployed from the Korean Peninsula, with exposure in the Dagmar North training area, Gyeonggi province, in 2021. All were males (mean age 23 years, range 20-27) with uncomplicated malaria and an average time from symptom onset to diagnosis of 57 days (range 8-121). Laboratory findings included mean hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL, platelets 123 × 10³/µL, and parasitemia <1% (0.1%-0.9%); none received chemoprophylaxis. Patients were treated with artemether/lumefantrine and primaquine, with parasitemia clearance after an average of 2.8 days. Authors emphasize clinician vigilance given long latency and the value of pre-deployment prevention.

articles.headline: P. knowlesi adapts in vitro to invade Duffy-negative red cells
articles.title_original: Plasmodium knowlesi can adapt to infect Duffy-negative erythrocytes.
articles.authors: Zinga M, Ibrahim A, Mohring F, Chainarin S, Jonsdottir TK, Ngernna S, Amabilino-Perez B, Pholcharee T, Turkiewicz A, Campino S, Clark TG, Miao J, Cui L, Roobsoong W, Sattabongkot J, Moon RW, Nguitragool W
articles.details: Laboratory study showing the zoonotic malaria parasite Plasmodium knowlesi, which normally relies on the Duffy antigen receptor (DARC) for erythrocyte invasion, can adapt in vitro to invade and replicate within Duffy-negative (Fy-) erythrocytes. The adaptation was stable, independent of DARC binding, and resistant to inhibition by α-DARC antibodies. Genomic analysis identified a recombination event between the parasite's dbpα and dbpγ genes producing a chimeric gene, dbpαγ, and CRISPR-Cas9 targeted reversion showed dbpαγ is essential for invasion of Fy- erythrocytes. The findings indicate P. knowlesi has greater invasion plasticity than previously recognized, as only Duffy-positive individuals had been thought susceptible to infection.

articles.headline: Review of nested heme metabolism as a malaria drug target
articles.title_original: Russian Dolls of Heme Metabolism in Malaria-Infected Red Blood Cells: Nested Vulnerabilities and Therapeutic Opportunities.
articles.authors: Adapa SR, Siddiqui FA, Jiang RHY
articles.details: Review examining the compartmentalized 'Russian doll' architecture of heme metabolism in malaria-infected red blood cells, comprising the host erythrocyte's truncated heme synthesis, the parasite's own heme pathway, and host heme released during hemoglobin digestion in the food vacuole. These distinct heme pools influence redox balance and activation of artemisinin-based drugs. The authors note that exogenous 5-aminolevulinic acid (5-ALA) can perturb host heme biosynthesis, potentially raising protoporphyrin IX and sensitizing parasites to oxidative stress, though effects on artemisinin susceptibility depend on parasite stage and exposure duration. The review discusses exploiting these vulnerabilities therapeutically.

articles.headline: P. falciparum genetic metrics track transmission intensity in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Genetic metrics decode Plasmodium falciparum diversity: complexity of infection, parasite connectivity, and transmission intensity in Mainland Tanzania's diverse regions.
articles.authors: Pereus D, Fola AA, Bakari C, Seth MD, DeFeo R, Lyimo BM, Mandara CI, Madebe RA, Mbata D, Popkin-Hall ZR, Moshi R, Mbwambo RB, Mbwambo D, Aaron S, Lusasi A, Lazaro S, Giesbrecht DJ, Kulohoma B, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Juma G, Mobegi VA, Ishengoma DS
articles.details: A cross-sectional survey of symptomatic patients at 100 health facilities across Mainland Tanzania (February–July 2021), stratified into four transmission-intensity groups, found that the mean complexity of infection (COI) was significantly higher in the high and moderate transmission strata than in the low and very low strata (p<0.001), while the odds of polyclonal infection were significantly lower in the moderate, low, and very low strata compared with the high stratum (p<0.001). Of 12,875 dried blood spot samples, 7,199 rapid-test-positive samples were sequenced with molecular inversion probes targeting 1,832 SNPs; the countrywide mean COI was 1.5, with 59.6% of samples monoclonal. Genetic differentiation among regions was very low (FST 0–0.006) with weak relatedness (IBD<0.1). The authors conclude that COI and polyclonality could be integrated into malaria genomic surveillance.

articles.headline: Automated analyzer misclassifies P. vivax as artefactual cells
articles.title_original: Classification of Plasmodium vivax as artefactual cells by the CellaVision DM9600: a system limitation in a non-endemic area.
articles.authors: Chien LC, Wu FT, Hsu SF, Su YD, Lai HY, Hsu HC, Hsiao CT, Cheng MY, Hsueh PR
articles.details: A 46-year-old Taiwanese man returning from Indonesia presented with fever and thrombocytopenia and was diagnosed with Plasmodium vivax, confirmed by peripheral blood smear and PCR. An automated cellular morphology analyzer flagged artefactual cells without providing a definitive diagnosis. He was treated with artemether-lumefantrine and primaquine, with rapid recovery and no relapse.

articles.headline: Switzerland's harmonized maps guide traveler malaria prevention
articles.title_original: [Travel and malaria prevention : what lies behind the maps].
articles.authors: Veit O, Eperon G, Staehelin C, Chappuis F
articles.details: Article describing a transparent methodology developed in Switzerland in collaboration with other European countries to classify malaria risk areas based on current epidemiological data and present harmonized preventive measures in detailed maps. The maps are intended to support travel medicine experts and primary care physicians in giving consistent, evidence-based advice to travelers visiting endemic areas, where rigorous preventive measures are emphasized given malaria's high incidence in sub-Saharan Africa. The resources are available on the Swiss travel medicine reference website, www.healthytravel.ch.

articles.headline: One-third of Myanmar migrants in Thailand show recent P. vivax exposure
articles.title_original: Sociodemographic and behavioral determinants of Plasmodium vivax-specific antibody responses among short-term Myanmar migrants in Thailand.
articles.authors: Sripoorote P, Inthitanon N, Wattanagoon Y, Lim PS, Cui L, Sattabongkot J, Parker DM, Nguitragool W, Chotirat S, Longley RJ, Aung PL
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey in March–April 2025 of 300 short-term Myanmar migrants across six high-burden villages of Tha Song Yang District, Tak Province, measuring IgG to eight Plasmodium vivax antigens by multiplexed Luminex assay on dried blood spots, with recent exposure classified by a validated random forest algorithm at 95% specificity. Among 294 PCR-negative participants, 104 (35.4%) were predicted recently exposed. Higher odds were linked to age 30–39 (aOR 3.11), secondary education or above (aOR 3.28), and Karen ethnicity (aOR 3.68); seasonal/temporary work and income >5,000 THB were protective. Authors support sero-surveillance for border elimination.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria strongly linked to anaemia in Ghanaian high schoolers
articles.title_original: Asymptomatic malaria parasite carriage and anaemia among senior high school students in Tempane District of the Upper East Region of Ghana: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Awindago JA, Captain-Esoah M, Motey GA, Angyiereyiri ED, Azameti MK, Adjei MR, Donkor MN, Frempong KK, Dassah SD
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 290 senior high school students aged 15–22 at Tempane Senior High School, Upper East Region, Ghana, using microscopy for parasite detection and haemoglobin measurement. Malaria parasite carriage prevalence was 23.1% and anaemia 49.0%. Day students had higher malaria prevalence (59.2%) than boarders (15.8%), and non-ITN users higher (39.7%) than users (5.0%); in multivariate analysis, not using ITNs (aOR 15.97) and being a day student (aOR 5.65) predicted infection. Infected students had lower mean haemoglobin (11.50 vs 12.56 g/dL), and infection raised anaemia odds (aOR 9.36). Authors urge school-based malaria programmes.

articles.headline: Schistosoma mansoni tied to gut inflammation independent of malaria, HIV, HBV
articles.title_original: Schistosoma mansoni Infections Are Associated With Fecal Calprotectin Markers of Gut Inflammation After Accounting for HIV, Hepatitis B, and Malaria.
articles.authors: Wilburn L, Bui HL, Akampurira P, Ddamba R, Oguttu DW, Nabatte B, Kabatereine NB, Chami GF
articles.details: Cross-sectional study nested in the SchistoTrack cohort in Pakwach district, Uganda, examining 640 participants aged 6–85 in 2024 for Schistosoma mansoni by Kato-Katz and measuring fecal calprotectin (fCal) by ELISA. Detectable fCal occurred in 74.4%, ≥100 µg/g in 22.3%, and >250 µg/g in 7%; S. mansoni prevalence was 49.1%. Infection intensity was positively associated with all fCal outcomes (ORs 1.11–1.26; continuous β 0.06). HIV was associated with fCal ≥100 µg/g (OR 2.52), while malaria and hepatitis B were uninformative. Authors suggest integrating fCal thresholds into schistosomiasis management.

articles.headline: Outcrossing gives fitness advantage in P. falciparum mosquito-stage crosses
articles.title_original: Genetic crosses using fluorescently labelled malaria parasites indicate advantages of outcrossing in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Li X, Jutzeler K, Abatiyow B, Rezakhani N, Haile M, Leeb A, Patel H, Kappe S, Nosten F, Cheeseman IH, Ferdig M, Anderson TJC, Vaughan A, Kumar S
articles.details: Experimental genetic crosses of fluorescently labelled malaria parasite clones, NF54 (mCherry; African) and NHP4026 (GFP; Thai), found that outcrossing confers a substantial fitness advantage, possibly by removing deleterious mutations accumulated during asexual replication. Genotyping 8,540 oocysts from 435 mosquitoes collected 7–14 days post-infection showed that across two independent crosses the proportion of outcrossed oocysts decreased and selfing increased over time, consistent with faster maturation of outcrossed oocysts. Selfed NF54 oocysts were larger than outcrossed or selfed NHP4026 oocysts. The authors conclude that faster maturation of outcrossed parasites may maximize recombination in transmitted parasite populations.

articles.headline: CD4 T-cell responses largely similar in P. falciparum and P. vivax malaria
articles.title_original: Comparison of CD4 T-Cell Response in Plasmodium falciparum and vivax Malaria.
articles.authors: Nalubega M, Soon MSF, Andrew D, Ortega-Pajares A, Canning J, Dooley N, Loughland JR, Engwerda C, Kenangalem E, Price RN, Minigo G, Anstey NM, Oyong DA, Boyle MJ
articles.details: Comparative immunology study assessed CD4 T cells in adults infected with either Plasmodium falciparum or Plasmodium vivax, measuring activation and proliferation ex vivo and functional capacity by intracellular cytokine staining via flow cytometry. The phenotype, activation, and proliferation of CD4 T cell subsets were largely comparable between species. Within the peripheral T follicular helper (pTfh) compartment, there was some evidence of species-dependent activation, with relatively increased pTfh1 cells in P. falciparum, alongside increased IL-10 production including within IL-21-producing CD4 T cells. Authors conclude these pTfh differences may affect antibody development.

articles.headline: FcγRIIa drives antibody-dependent neutrophil phagocytosis of malaria-infected red cells
articles.title_original: Antibody-Dependent Neutrophil Phagocytosis of Plasmodium falciparum-Infected Erythrocytes Is Mediated by FcγRIIa.
articles.authors: Saeed M, Aitken EH, Wines BD, Rogerson SJ
articles.details: This in vitro study used purified neutrophils from healthy donors to determine whether FcγRIIIb or FcγRIIa mediates antibody-dependent neutrophil phagocytosis (ADNP) of Plasmodium falciparum-infected erythrocytes. Treating neutrophils with TNF plus PIPLC cut detectable FcγRIII by 79% and raised FcγRIIa detection by 82%; ADNP by these FcγRIIIb-depleted neutrophils rose to 585% ± 108% versus untreated cells (P = .042). Blocking FcγRIIa alone reduced ADNP more than 5-fold to about 17% ± 1.5% (P < .05), and combined blockade gave about 24% (P < .05). The authors conclude FcγRIIa is the main phagocytic receptor while FcγRIIIb acts as a decoy.

articles.headline: Travel importation drives malaria in low-transmission Ugandan villages
articles.title_original: Quantifying the Role of Importation on Sustained Malaria Transmission in a Low-to-Moderate Burden Region of Southwest Uganda.
articles.authors: Epstein A, Aramanzan O, Nabende I, Kayondo TM, Obbo M, Tumwesigye R, Wiringilimaana I, Mbabazi M, Kagurusi BA, Tukwasibwe S, Ssewanyana I, Routledge I, Briggs J, Wesolowski A, Greenhouse B, Dorsey G, Arinaitwe E, Rodriguez-Barraquer I
articles.details: A 14-month longitudinal cohort study in Southwest Uganda followed 1918 individuals from 400 households bimonthly, capturing symptomatic Plasmodium falciparum episodes through surveillance and household reports and asymptomatic infections by qPCR, and used multilevel logistic regression and population attributable fractions to assess travel's role. Of 283 infections recorded (244 symptomatic, 39 asymptomatic), overnight travel was positively associated with malaria in lower-transmission villages (OR = 4.38, 95% CI 1.80–10.64), strongest for short trips to nearby higher-incidence areas. Travel accounted for 14% of cases in low-transmission villages overall, rising to 30% during low-transmission periods. The authors highlight the need to address both local transmission and importation.

articles.headline: Dobinin K kills chloroquine-resistant malaria parasites by disrupting redox system
articles.title_original: Antimalarial Effects of Dobinin K against Chloroquine-resistant Plasmodium falciparum Through Disruption of the Parasite's Redox System.
articles.authors: He LF, Sun H, Cang LQ, Wang JD, Xiao CJ, Jiang B, Shen L
articles.details: This laboratory study evaluated the antiplasmodial activity and mechanism of Dobinin K against the chloroquine-resistant Plasmodium falciparum Dd2 strain using SYBR Green I and Giemsa assays, transcriptomics, redox substrate/enzyme measurements, electron microscopy, JC-1 mitochondrial membrane potential, qRT-PCR, and TUNEL. Dobinin K showed concentration- and time-dependent activity affecting all developmental stages. It increased oxidative products, elevated the GSSG/GSH ratio, inhibited antioxidant enzymes PfGR, PfTrxR, and PfSOD, decreased the NADP+/NADPH ratio, disrupted mitochondrial membrane potential, upregulated apicoplast metabolic enzyme mRNAs, and induced apoptosis. The authors conclude Dobinin K acts by disrupting parasite redox homeostasis, a mechanism distinct from artemisinin.

articles.headline: Automated LAMP tested for detecting Plasmodium in Anopheles mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Assessment of automated loop-mediated isothermal amplification-(LAMP-)based xenomonitoring for Plasmodium spp. in Anopheles mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Eisenbarth A, Adegnika AA, Boussougou-Sambe ST, Glyschewski L, Jacobs T, Kann S, Krüger A, Hagen RM, Ramharter M, Mehmel E, Ndalembouly AGD, Frickmann H
articles.details: This study assessed an automated commercial loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) assay, originally designed for malaria parasite DNA in human blood, for xenomonitoring Plasmodium in Anopheles mosquitoes, comparing it with generic and species-specific real-time PCR. For mosquitoes artificially exposed to Plasmodium berghei, LAMP detected 15/43 (34.9%, 95% CI 20.1%–50.9%) versus 22/43 (51.2%) by PCR. For field Anopheles spp. imported to Germany, results were closer: 12/172 pools (7.0%) by LAMP and 13/172 (7.6%) by PCR, with an overall infection rate of 1.2% (20/1711) involving P. falciparum, P. malariae, and P. ovale. The authors conclude LAMP shows comparable reliability for human-malaria species and is suitable for tropical field use.

articles.headline: Correction notice for study on immunity and malaria virulence gene expression
articles.title_original: Correction: Controlled human malaria infection with Plasmodium falciparum demonstrates impact of naturally acquired immunity on virulence gene expression.
articles.authors: Bachmann A, Bruske E, Krumkamp R, Turner L, Wichers JS, Petter M, Held J, Duffy MF, Sim BKL, Hoffman SL, Kremsner PG, Lell B, Lavstsen T, Frank M, Mordmüller B, Tannich E
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Modeling shows malaria wastewater surveillance feasible only in endemic settings
articles.title_original: Modeling and Laboratory Experiments to Assess Detection of Plasmodium falciparum in Wastewater: Insights for Wastewater-Based Surveillance of Malaria.
articles.authors: Chen W, Ochomo E, Muok E, Bibby K
articles.details: Researchers combined a modeling framework with laboratory spike-in experiments to assess wastewater-based detection of Plasmodium falciparum. Assuming shedding of 5.31 log10 genome copies/day per infection, models predicted that in nonendemic countries case rates would need to rise more than 1000-fold for a 50% detection probability (even with eight PCR replicates at a process limit of detection of 3.0 log10 GC/L), whereas about 84% of endemic African countries could reach this. Experiments showed first-order decay (0.035-0.11 h-1 at 4-35°C) with minimal modeling impact, but freeze-thawing caused >70% DNA loss and HA membrane filtration recovery was <2% in most cases. The authors conclude the results help guide future malaria wastewater epidemiology.

articles.headline: New marker-free CRISPR/Cas9 method advances P. falciparum gene editing
articles.title_original: A heterologous marker-free selection approach for CRISPR/Cas9-based gene editing in the malaria parasite Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Carrington E, Ballmer D, Niederwieser I, Thommen BT, Brancucci NMB, Voss TS
articles.details: This methods study developed a heterologous marker-free CRISPR/Cas9 strategy (CRISPR/Cas9pyrR) for Plasmodium falciparum that simultaneously edits a target gene and introduces pyrimethamine resistance mutations into the pfdhfr-ts gene, with the donor and Cas9 cassette on separate plasmids so only parasites acquiring both survive pyrimethamine pressure. As proof of principle, the authors generated GFP-tagged PfGEX1 and PfNUP116 lines, finding PfGEX1-GFP marks the nuclear envelope specifically in gametocytes but not asexual blood stages, while PfNUP116-GFP is undetectable in asexual parasites and localizes to a perinuclear region in early gametocytes, suggesting dynamic changes at the nuclear periphery during sexual differentiation. They further demonstrated sequential engineering using the human dhfr marker with WR99210 selection to additionally tag PfAP2-G and PfNUP313. The method complements the existing genetic toolkit for malaria research.

articles.headline: Iron micronutrient powders cut malaria-induced anemia but not growth in Ghana
articles.title_original: Effect of Iron-Containing Micronutrient Powders on Growth and Malaria-Induced Anaemia Among Preschool Children in Ghana: A Cluster-Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial.
articles.authors: Tchum EKY, Koto JE, Kanyoke F, Opoku O, Ussher F, Dassah SD, Amoani B, Tchum SK, Nyarko E
articles.details: This secondary analysis of a cluster-randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in the Bono Region, Ghana, gave children aged 6-35 months (n=1,958) a daily micronutrient powder containing 12.5 mg elemental iron or placebo for five months; 1,806 were analyzed. Iron-containing MNP had no significant effect on height-for-age (β=0.026, p=0.609), weight-for-age (β=-0.015, p=0.719), or weight-for-height (β=-0.035, p=0.463) z-scores, but reduced the risk of malaria-induced anemia (risk difference 0.050, 95% CI 0.004-0.096; p=0.032). Female sex was associated with higher HAZ (β=0.149, p=0.005). The authors conclude iron fortification appears safe in malaria-endemic settings but does not improve short-term growth.

articles.headline: Compound screen finds invasion inhibitors; PfNCR1 identified as target
articles.title_original: Unpacking boxes: identification of novel inhibitors of malaria parasite invasion.
articles.authors: Barnes CB, Schneider MP, Ventura OD, Razook Z, McCann K, Boulet C, Literski AH, Barry AE, Sleebs BE, Crabb BS, Gilson PR, Bullen HE
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: To find new antimalarials targeting erythrocyte invasion, researchers screened the Medicines for Malaria Venture COVID Box (160 compounds) and Global Health Priority Box (240 compounds) against Plasmodium falciparum at 1 μM during the egress and invasion period, identifying 20 invasion inhibitors and two partial egress inhibitors. Comparing growth and invasion IC50 values indicated invasion inhibition was central to the parasiticidal action of six compounds; two (MMV006931 and MMV024850) directly blocked invasion of mechanically purified merozoites. Robust resistance was selected against MMV006931 but not MMV024850, and whole-genome sequencing of resistant clones identified mutations in the sterol exporter PfNCR1. Sensitization to a sterol-dependent detergent confirmed PfNCR1 as the likely target of MMV006931.

articles.headline: Malaria strongly linked to anemia in HIV-positive children on ART in Uganda
articles.title_original: Anemia in HIV-Infected Children on ART in Uganda: High Risk Associations with Malaria, Opportunistic Infections, and Poor Adherence.
articles.authors: Anyelwisye CL, Ogwal JK, Kadogosa SE, Timuzigu R, Anyetaba C, Wagubi R, Olong C, John EA, Muwanguzi E, Okongo B
articles.details: This cross-sectional study assessed anemia prevalence and associated factors among 293 HIV-positive children under 18 receiving antiretroviral therapy at two urban ART clinics in Mbarara City, Southwestern Uganda. Overall anemia prevalence was 6.14% (95% CI: 3.4%-8.9%), with normocytic normochromic anemia predominant (72.2%). In multivariate logistic regression, a recent episode of malaria (AOR=9.61, 95% CI 3.26-28.34), presence of an opportunistic infection (AOR=6.78, 95% CI 1.28-35.83), and poor ART adherence (AOR=14.75, 95% CI 1.31-165.65) were independently associated with anemia. The authors conclude anemia, though lower than global averages, remains a concern and call for integrated malaria control, OI management, and adherence support.

articles.headline: Humidity drives seasonal malaria peaks in Rohingya refugee camps
articles.title_original: Seasonal Climate Drivers and Immunohematological Profiles of Malaria in Rohingya Refugee Camps, Bangladesh: A 4-Year Repeated Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Islam A, Akhi MM, Hossain A, Palit PK, Rahman N, Baidya S, Prome TM, Rahman M, Sultana A, Chowdhury MMH, Alam MS
articles.details: A 4-year repeated cross-sectional study (2021-2024) enrolled 582 participants—486 from a malaria-endemic Rohingya refugee camp and 96 healthy controls from a nonendemic region—screened for P. falciparum and P. vivax by rapid diagnostic test with microscopy confirmation. Of 582 individuals, 345 malaria cases were confirmed, with peak transmission during the monsoon (June-September), especially August, and P. falciparum showing earlier, sharper peaks than P. vivax. Relative humidity correlated most strongly with incidence (r = 0.724-0.77), followed by rainfall and temperature. Infected participants showed anemia, thrombocytopenia, and elevated ESR, more pronounced in P. falciparum cases, and higher antibody titers; authors advocate climate-sensitive, species-specific control.

articles.headline: Spatial clustering of malaria in pregnancy mapped in Amhara, Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Mapping inequity: spatial clustering of malaria in pregnancy in the Amhara region, Northwest Ethiopia (2018-2024).
articles.authors: Bogale KA, Asemahagn MA, Alemu K, Muchie KF, Engedaw HA, Yenesew MA
articles.details: A retrospective spatial analysis used routine surveillance data on confirmed malaria among pregnant women in the Amhara region, Ethiopia (January 2018-December 2024), applying SaTScan Poisson clustering and a spatial error model. Among 5.8 million pregnant women under surveillance, 53,478 confirmed cases gave an annual incidence of 9.2 per 1,000, and seven significant high-risk clusters were detected. Malaria incidence was negatively associated with ANC4 utilization (IRR = 0.92; 95% CI 0.91-0.94), soil moisture (IRR = 0.85), API (IRR = 0.98), and temperature (IRR = 0.65), but positively associated with ANC1 utilization (IRR = 1.07). Authors call for strengthened antenatal care and geographically targeted, climate-informed interventions.

articles.headline: One in ten febrile Ghanaian children have malaria-bacterial coinfection
articles.title_original: Malaria and bacteremia in febrile children presenting at a teaching hospital in Kumasi, Ghana.
articles.authors: Gyasi WA, Boampong K, Sylverken J, Badu K
articles.details: This cross-sectional study enrolled 270 febrile children at Komfo Anokye Teaching Hospital, Ghana (April 2022-July 2023), with malaria diagnosed by rapid diagnostic test and microscopy and bacteremia assessed by blood culture and antibiotic susceptibility testing. Malaria was present in 33% and bacteremia in 31%, with coinfection in 10.4% (95% CI ±3.6%). Seizures were independently associated with malaria-bacterial coinfection (adjusted OR = 3.57; 95% CI 1.28-9.95; p = 0.015). Staphylococcus aureus (43.8%) and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (6.3%) were the most common isolates, and multi-drug resistance was found in 34.5% of isolates. Authors stress antibiotic stewardship and resistance monitoring.

articles.headline: Review of malaria prevention tools: drugs, vaccines, and vector control
articles.title_original: Malaria Prevention: Progress to Date.
articles.authors: Grobusch MP, Schnyder JL, Schlagenhauf P, de Jong HK, Hanscheid T
articles.details: Malaria prevention relies on combining drug-based prevention, vaccines, and vector control, distinguishing chemoprophylaxis in non-immune travellers from intermittent preventive treatment in vulnerable groups and mass drug administration in endemic communities, alongside vector repelling for travellers and more comprehensive vector control in endemic areas. The WHO-endorsed RTS,S/AS01 (Mosquirix) and R21/Matrix-M vaccines, rolled out in many highly malaria-endemic sub-Saharan African countries, provide overall moderate protection in young children but significantly reduce severe disease and death and are considered safe; no malaria vaccine is yet available for travellers. Monoclonal antibodies are being developed for pre-exposure protection of immunocompromised or unvaccinated high-risk individuals and for disrupting seasonal transmission with a single application. The authors envisage further harmonisation alongside more personalised travel medicine recommendations.

articles.headline: Trimeric NF-Y-like complex enables Plasmodium sporozoite egress from oocysts
articles.title_original: Sporozoite egress from Plasmodium oocysts requires a trimeric NF-Y-like complex.
articles.authors: Preira CMF, Deligianni E, Andreadaki M, Tzagkaraki S, Kokkas E, Kołakowska A, Gessmann R, Bertaso C, Masiero S, Russo R, Nardini M, Siden-Kiamos I, Gourlay LJ, Currà C
articles.details: This laboratory study investigated how malaria parasite sporozoites escape the oocyst capsule on the mosquito gut, building on previously identified Oocyst Rupture Proteins ORP1 and ORP2, which carry Histone-Fold Domains resembling NF-YB/C subunits. The authors identify ORP3 as a third subunit that, despite limited similarity to NF-YA, retains the conserved helix A1 needed for trimerization with the ORP1/2 dimer. An orp3(-) mutant showed defective oocyst opening like orp1(-) and orp2(-) mutants, ORP3 localized to the oocyst periphery, and deleting helix A1 abolished excystation; trimer formation was confirmed in vitro by yeast-3-hybrid. Mutant sporozoites remained motile but failed to develop into exoerythrocytic forms, blocking transmission.

articles.headline: Antibody Fc region largely dispensable for blocking malaria sporozoite infection
articles.title_original: Relevance of the antibody Fc fragment and epitope valency in protection against malaria sporozoites.
articles.authors: Flores-Garcia Y, Aguirre-Botero MC, Tripathi P, Locke E, Park M, Mathis-Torres S, Salgado-Jimenez B, Herrera-Arevalo S, Gladston A, Asady B, Coppens I, Amino R, Zavala F
articles.details: Using Fab fragments from protective human monoclonal antibodies and antibodies carrying LALA and KA mutations that reduce Fc functions, this experimental study tested how the antibody Fc region and epitope valency contribute to protection against Plasmodium falciparum circumsporozoite protein (CSP) (NPNA)3 repeats. Protection was assessed with transgenic P. berghei strains expressing CSP with varying numbers of (NPNA)3 epitopes. Fab fragments and Fc-mutated antibodies showed protective efficacy comparable to intact antibodies, indicating a limited role for Fc functions. Binding to a single epitope strongly reduced liver burden, while two separate epitopes were needed for sterile protection.

articles.headline: Maculatus Group mosquitoes confirmed as P. falciparum malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Anopheles maculatus s.l as potential malaria vectors with opportunistic feeding in Meghalaya, Northeast India.
articles.authors: Sihag AK, Rajkonwar J, Kharbisnop B, Sangma DM, Budnah C, Acharya P, Chellappan S, Hazarika G, Talukder P, Pebam R, Baruah K, Bhattacharyya DR, Hajong SR, Kaur H, Narain K, Bhowmick IP
articles.details: This molecular survey examined species composition and vector potential of anophelines resting in the Khasi Hills and Garo Hills regions of Meghalaya, Northeast India, with adults collected between 2019 and 2023 (excluding 2021). Of 215 specimens, 177 (82.32%) were An. maculatus sensu stricto, 33 (15.34%) An. pseudowillmori, and 5 (2.32%) An. willmori. Natural Plasmodium falciparum infection was detected in 49 (27.68%) of 177 An. maculatus s.s. and 16 (48.48%) of 33 An. pseudowillmori. Blood meal analysis showed 15% fed on humans, 16% on cattle, and 17% on both, indicating opportunistic feeding. Authors highlight the need for targeted vector control.

articles.headline: Nanoparticle CIS43 junctional vaccine broadens anti-malaria antibody response
articles.title_original: Protective immunity against malaria by a nanoparticle CIS43-based junctional vaccine alone or in combination with R21.
articles.authors: Tripathi P, Koo JH, Chen X, Silva Pereira LD, Dillon M, Zhang B, Lofgren M, Nguyen KT, Teng IT, Bonilla B, Kerscher S, Kong WP, Henry AR, Stephens T, Tsybovsky Y, Weldon SR, Douek DC, Pierson TC, Batista FD, Idris AH, Seder RA, Kwong PD, Zhou T
articles.details: This preclinical study developed a self-assembling nanoparticle vaccine displaying tandem repeats of the junctional epitope of P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein (targeted by the CIS43 antibody), tested alone or combined with the R21 vaccine using B cell analysis and monoclonal antibody isolation. At a 1 μg dose, the best junctional vaccine and R21 produced comparable reductions in liver burden, while at 0.25 μg the junctional vaccine gave only a modest reduction compared to R21. Combining the two did not yield substantial improvement. R21 elicited antibodies mainly against the major repeats, whereas the junctional vaccine elicited antibodies against both junctional and major repeat regions.

articles.headline: Low chikungunya seroprevalence and malaria co-infection in Nigerian febrile patients
articles.title_original: Seroprevalence of chikungunya virus infection and co-occurrence with malaria among patients with febrile illness in Oguma, the middle belt region of Nigeria.
articles.authors: Daudu MJ, Omatola CA
articles.details: This cross-sectional study screened 200 febrile patients in Oguma, Bassa Local Government Area, serologically for chikungunya virus antibodies and microscopically for malaria parasites, detecting anti-chikungunya antibodies in 9 (4.50%) — IgM in 4 (2.0%) and IgG in 5 (2.5%). Three patients (1.5%) with active chikungunya infection were co-infected with malaria and 1 (1.0%) had chikungunya mono-infection, while 60 (30%) had malaria alone. Active chikungunya cases occurred only in males, and rash and back pain were frequently observed alongside active infection (p < 0.05). The authors confirm low-level circulation of chikungunya virus in the previously unexplored Oguma area.

articles.headline: Review of mass drug administration strategies for malaria elimination
articles.title_original: Application Strategy and Research Progress of Large-Scale Population Drug Intervention in Malaria Control.
articles.authors: Cao Z, Gu Y, Li G, Deng C
articles.details: This review examines the evidence base, controversies, and application strategies of mass drug administration (MDA) for malaria control and elimination, noting an estimated 282 million malaria cases worldwide in 2024 and stalled or reversed progress in some regions. The authors describe MDA's important role alongside core limitations including long-term drug resistance risk, insufficient implementation sustainability, high regional adaptation failure, and challenges from multiple malaria species and the newly recognized Plasmodium knowlesi. They propose an "MDA+" collaborative strategy integrating vaccines, digital monitoring, and cross-border cooperation to optimize resources and achieve full coverage across parasite species.

articles.headline: How malaria prevention drugs in pregnancy affect infant growth
articles.title_original: Effect mechanisms of different malaria chemoprevention regimens in pregnancy on infant growth outcomes: causal mediation analysis of a randomized controlled trial.
articles.authors: Nguyen AT, Nankabirwa JI, Kakuru A, Roh ME, Aguti M, Adrama H, Kizza J, Olwoch P, Kamya MR, Dorsey G, Jagannathan P, Benjamin-Chung J
articles.details: In a causal mediation analysis of infant follow-up data (N=761) from a randomized trial (NCT04336189) of monthly IPTp with dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP), sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP), or DP+SP, IPTp-DP+SP lowered mean weight-for-length z-score (WLZ) versus IPTp-SP by 0.28 (95% CI 0.07-0.49) at 4-6 months, with the largest differences among primigravidae. Lower risk of active placental malaria partially offset this (+0.06, 95% CI 0.02-0.10). IPTp-DP+SP also lowered mean length-for-age z-score by up to 0.28 at 7-13 months versus IPTp-DP, with low birthweight a persistent mediator of linear growth. The authors conclude that adverse birth outcomes contributed to early growth faltering, partially counteracted by prevention of placental malaria.

articles.headline: PBO-treated bed nets show reduced efficacy against Kenyan malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Reduced Efficacy of Piperonyl Butoxide Based Long-Lasting Insecticide-Treated Nets Against Malaria Vectors in Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Kahindi S, Abel L, Kirwa E, Amunga M, Kimachas E, Omondi E, Cherono M, Mangeni J, Obala A, Markwalter C, O'Meara WP
articles.details: In a field evaluation in Bungoma County, Kenya, mortality of wild-collected Anopheles exposed to new PBO long-lasting insecticide-treated nets (LLINs) was only 68% in WHO cone bioassays, falling as low as 34% with used, 24-month-old nets. A total of 2,020 female Anopheles were tested (1,480 wild-collected and 540 of a Kilifi-susceptible strain), and results were confirmed with the WHO susceptibility assay. Anopheles arabiensis was the main vector that survived exposure, and susceptibility tests showed very low mortality after permethrin with or without PBO. The authors attribute the reduced sensitivity partly to net aging and partly to very high levels of resistance in the local vector population.

articles.headline: Monoclonal antibody L9LS cuts malaria infection 43% in Kenyan children
articles.title_original: Safety and efficacy of the monoclonal antibody L9LS for malaria prevention in children exposed to perennial malaria transmission in Kenya: a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled, phase 2 trial.
articles.authors: Steinhardt LC, Kwambai TK, Oneko M, Ouma E, Njoroge R, Callier V, Hu Z, Gutman JR, Yego R, Otieno K, Onoka K, Otieno L, Oduol K, Serebryannyy L, Lin BC, Adams W, Hickman S, Preston AC, Carlton K, Holdsworth M, Xiao Y, O Ter Kuile F, Odongo W, Murphy SC, Tran TM, Kariuki S, Crompton PD, Seder RA
articles.details: Double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled phase 2 trial of the long-acting monoclonal antibody L9LS in infants and children in Siaya county, western Kenya, a perennial P. falciparum transmission setting. Parts 1a/1b tested safety in 96 children across doses of 5-40 mg/kg subcutaneously; part 2 randomized 324 children aged 5-59 months to one-dose L9LS, two-dose L9LS, or placebo. In the two-dose group, 70 (66%) of 106 children had at least one P. falciparum infection over 12 months versus 91 (83%) of 110 on placebo (protective efficacy 42.7%, 95% CI 22.5-57.7; p=0.0003). Grade 3+ related adverse events were rare (1%), with no related serious adverse events. Authors conclude L9LS was protective without safety concerns but a higher dose may be needed for high-level efficacy.

articles.headline: Antibody-based malaria prevention in intense perennial transmission
articles.title_original: Antibody-based malaria prevention in an intense perennial transmission setting.
articles.authors: Engwerda CR, Haque A
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Comparing safety monitoring methods in the RTS,S malaria vaccine rollout
articles.title_original: Assessing safety in the RTS,S/AS01 malaria vaccine implementation programme: an overview and comparison of methods.
articles.authors: Schellenberg D, Hamel M, Njuguna P, Furrer E, Praet N, Balakrishnan MR, Jain S, Maiga D, Schuerman L, Whitney CG
articles.details: This overview describes and compares the safety monitoring approaches used in the RTS,S/AS01 malaria vaccine implementation programme, which evaluated safety signals from the phase 3 trial. Methods included sentinel hospital and community-based mortality surveillance, strengthened pharmacovigilance to detect rare unanticipated vaccination-related signals, and cohort event monitoring to identify rare conditions of special interest. The authors summarize the strengths and weaknesses of these complementary methods. No quantitative results are reported in the abstract.

articles.headline: Perspective urges reprioritising malaria research amid funding crisis
articles.title_original: Prioritising in a crisis: what next for malaria research?
articles.authors: Chestnutt EG, Afolabi MO, D'Alessandro U, Delves M, Gosling R, Jagoe G, Lasry E, Maiteki C, Moon RW, Oriero E, Pitt C, Tadesse F, Tibenderana JK, Rao VB
articles.details: This Perspective, drawing on a diverse group of stakeholders, argues that malaria research must adopt a dual mandate of protecting lives now while preparing for future biological and systemic threats, as gains stall or reverse amid deepening funding shortfalls, parasite and vector resistance, climate- and urbanisation-driven transmission changes, and conflict and displacement. It highlights three priorities: pragmatic, equity-focused implementation and health-systems research; sustained investment in innovation (next-generation chemoprevention, vaccines, monoclonal antibodies, novel vector control, and digital/AI-enabled surveillance) with operational research; and a research stream on sustainable, diversified financing and economic and political-economy evidence to inform decision-making.

articles.headline: Nigerian caregivers willing but largely unaware of R21 malaria vaccine
articles.title_original: Awareness, acceptability and determinants of the new malaria vaccine among caregivers of under-five children in Asaba, Nigeria: a multi-centre cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Ilikannu CO, Uwadia OM, Ilikannu SO, Agenu P, Mbachu CNP, Okoye NP, Uwaezuoke AC, Arinze B, Akinkunmi I, Jideije JA, Onwuasoeze C, Dan-Nwankwo C, Aladegbehingbe OE, Igboejesi PC, Onomuighokpo HO, Chukwu N, Igwe E
articles.details: A multi-centre cross-sectional study surveyed 358 consenting caregivers of under-five children at outpatient and immunization clinics in three health facilities in Asaba, Delta State, Nigeria, using pretested self-administered questionnaires to assess awareness and acceptability of the R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccine. Only 36.3% of respondents were aware of the vaccine, though 85.8% were willing to vaccinate their children; reasons for reluctance included fear of adverse reactions (45.1%), lack of interest (19.6%), and spousal disagreement (13.7%). Type of health facility (p=0.038), occupation (p=0.018), and caregiver gender (p=0.006) were significantly associated with vaccine knowledge or willingness. Authors conclude targeted awareness campaigns addressing safety concerns and spousal engagement are needed.

articles.headline: Three regional experts reflect on global malaria control progress
articles.title_original: Unite against Malaria: challenges and achievements in malaria control around the globe.
articles.details: To mark World Malaria Day 2026, this article features three researchers from three global regions sharing their experiences working toward the WHO Global Technical Strategy 2016-2030 goal of reducing global malaria incidence and mortality by 90% between 2015 and 2030. The experts are Dionicia Gamboa (molecular surveillance and vector genomics in the Peruvian Amazon), Fitsum Girma Tadesse (malaria molecular epidemiology in Ethiopia and the Horn of Africa), and Leanne Robinson (malaria epidemiology and implementation research in the Asian-Pacific region, with a focus on Papua New Guinea). They discuss the unique challenges and opportunities that remain.

articles.headline: Review of molecular mechanisms driving artemisinin resistance in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Insights into the factors contributing to artemisinin resistance in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Sharma P, Verma K, Tyagi S, Singh K, Bharti PK
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: This narrative review examines factors contributing to artemisinin resistance in Plasmodium falciparum, which threatens malaria elimination despite the central role of artemisinin-based combination therapies. While mutations in the P. falciparum Kelch13 gene, particularly the propeller domain, are linked to resistance, the authors note that in vitro and field studies indicate additional genetic determinants and pathways also contribute, including reduced haemoglobin endocytosis, increased phosphatidylinositol-3-kinase levels, activation of the unfolded protein response, and polymorphisms in various other genes. The review provides an overview of these molecular mechanisms.

articles.headline: Structural biology and cryoEM guiding blood-stage malaria vaccine design
articles.title_original: Atomic clarity: how structural biology is shaping blood-stage malaria vaccines.
articles.authors: Nema S, Rathore S, Banerjee M, Zhou ZH, Mohmmed A, Malhotra P
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: This review discusses how structural biology is shaping development of blood-stage malaria vaccines, which is challenging because Plasmodium parasites use complex invasion strategies and rely on large multiprotein surface complexes that are difficult to target. It describes how cryogenic electron microscopy (cryoEM) enables atomic-level analysis of dynamic, membrane-associated complexes without crystallization, revealing processes such as protein trafficking, nutrient uptake, and host cell remodeling. The authors note that advances in artificial intelligence and deep learning have improved cryoEM data analysis and structural modeling, and that integrating these with genomic data is accelerating progress toward broadly protective vaccines.

articles.headline: Adaptive vector control trial in Kenya favors PBO nets first-line
articles.title_original: Adaptive intervention strategies for malaria vector control in western Kenya: a cluster, sequential, multiple-assignment, randomised, open-label trial.
articles.authors: Zhou G, Wang X, Lee MC, Machani MG, Kidwell KM, Atieli HE, Kazura JW, Githure JI, Zhong D, Yan G
articles.details: This two-stage cluster, sequential multiple-assignment randomized (SMART) open-label trial in Muhoroni and Nyakach, Kisumu County, western Kenya, enrolled 47,614 participants from 14,246 households across 84 clusters. Stage 1 compared standard pyrethroid LLINs, piperonyl butoxide (PBO) nets, and standard LLINs plus annual indoor residual spraying (IRS); non-responding clusters were re-randomized in stage 2. PBO nets cut clinical malaria incidence versus standard LLINs at 1-6 months (aIRR 0.75, 95% CI 0.69-0.81; p<0.0001) and 7-12 months (aIRR 0.87; p=0.0012). In stage 2, adding annual IRS to PBO nets beat larviciding at 13-18 months (aIRR 0.89; p=0.0081), and enhanced IRS beat biannual IRS (aIRR 0.67; p<0.0001). Authors support replacing pyrethroid-only LLINs with PBO nets.

articles.headline: Tinospora crispa extract plus artesunate shows synergy in malaria mice
articles.title_original: Antimalarial Potency of Tinospora crispa Stem Extract and Artesunate Against Plasmodium berghei: A Study on Combination Therapy.
articles.authors: Ounjaijean S, Somsak V
articles.details: This preclinical study tested Tinospora crispa extract (TCE) and artesunate (ART), alone and combined, against Plasmodium berghei ANKA infection in ICR mice. Both showed dose-dependent antimalarial activity, with effective doses (ED50) of 200.31 mg/kg for ART and 1.96 mg/kg for TCE. The ART-TCE combination was synergistic, with combination index values <1 at ED50, ED50/2, and ED50/4, achieving significantly higher parasitemia inhibition than monotherapy. The combination also better preserved packed cell volume and body weight and prolonged mean survival time, and the authors conclude it is a promising antimalarial strategy warranting further mechanistic and clinical study.

articles.headline: Review highlights gaps in treating malaria in infants under 6 months
articles.title_original: Malaria in children aged <6 months: a narrative review of current evidence, recommendations and practice gaps.
articles.authors: Dalsuco J, Bassat Q, Ahmed H, Pfeffer S, Winnips C, Boulton C, Otsyula N, D'Alessandro U
articles.details: This narrative review synthesizes evidence, treatment recommendations, and practice gaps for malaria in children aged under 6 months (or weighing under 5 kg), drawing on PubMed, EMBASE, and Google Scholar plus WHO and region-specific guidelines. It notes that diagnosis is challenging because symptoms overlap with other infant conditions, risking misdiagnosis or treatment delay. Prevalence data are highly variable and heterogeneous, and evidence on antimalarial safety and efficacy in this group is limited; until recently no agents were licensed for neonates or infants under 4.5 kg. Authors call for harmonized policy and practice changes to address this vulnerable population.

articles.headline: Mapping and forecasting malaria hotspots across Uganda, 2014-2023
articles.title_original: Hotspot Mapping, Spatiotemporal Clustering and Forecasting of Malaria Incidence in Uganda, 2014-2023: Strategies to Inform Targeted Public Health Interventions.
articles.authors: Paasi G, Alunyo JP, Nakiyemba A, Ocen GG, Pande S, Alaroker F, Waako PJ, Olupot-Olupot P
articles.details: This ecological study used district-level malaria surveillance data from Uganda's DHIS2 (2014-2023) to map spatial heterogeneity, identify spatiotemporal clustering via Emerging Hot Spot Analysis, and forecast national incidence with an ARIMA(2,1,1) model. Burden was highest in West Nile, Acholi, and Lango, with sporadic hotspots in West Nile, oscillating hotspots in Karamoja, and consecutive hotspots through parts of Lango, Bunyoro, Busoga, and southern Buganda; cold spots and downward trends concentrated in Kigezi. National forecasts project monthly cases remaining approximately 1.0 to 1.2 million through 2030, with seasonal peaks from April to June. Authors recommend geographically targeted control and routine forecasting.

articles.headline: Short review questions malaria RDT reliability in emergency settings
articles.title_original: Are rapid diagnostic tests reliable for the detection of malaria in the emergency department?
articles.authors: Moran C, Coyne S
articles.details: This short systematic review assessed the diagnostic accuracy of malaria rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) in febrile adults from malaria-endemic regions. Searches of MEDLINE, EMBASE, Cochrane, and Google Scholar returned 138 papers, of which 6 met inclusion criteria and were analyzed. The abstract does not report pooled accuracy estimates or a quantitative conclusion.
