articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria linked to gut microbiome shifts in Ghanaian children
articles.title_original: Gut microbiome alterations among Ghanaian children with asymptomatic malaria infections.
articles.authors: Larbi AA, Etsey M, Brew O, Koduah B, Mawuenyega RE, Amewu EKA, Essilfie NK, Wireko S, Kwarteng A, Gyan BA
articles.details: This study profiled the gut microbiome (16S rRNA V4 sequencing, DADA2 pipeline) in Ghanaian children with asymptomatic malaria, predominantly mixed P. falciparum and P. malariae infections, versus healthy controls. In infected individuals, Firmicutes abundance fell from 52.5% to 44.0% while Bacteroidetes rose from 34.7% to 45.6% and Proteobacteria from 3.0% to 4.8%. Escherichia-Shigella was significantly elevated only in mixed infections, and Faecalibacterium significantly declined in infected individuals. Malaria status significantly affected microbial composition (PERMANOVA, p<0.05), explaining ~19.1% of variation, suggesting a link between infection and gut microbiota alterations.

articles.headline: cTfh2 cells help B cells produce anti-P. vivax PvDBPII antibodies
articles.title_original: A functional crosstalk between circulating follicular helper 2 T cells and memory B cells drives anti-Plasmodium vivax antibodies.
articles.authors: Salsabila ZZ, Kochayoo P, Khunsri T, Tianpothong P, Leepiyasakulchai C, Thawornpan P, Hafalla JCR, Adams JH, Chootong P
articles.details: This study examined how circulating T follicular helper 2 (cTfh2) cells drive antibody responses in P. vivax malaria. Of 40 subjects with acute P. vivax infection, 23 were seropositive for anti-PvDBPII antibodies (High and Low responders), and 3 High Responders produced antibodies inhibiting PvDBPII-erythrocyte binding. cTfh2 cells expanded in seropositive subjects; frequency did not differ between responders, but ICOS expression was higher in High Responders. PvDBPII antigen specifically activated cTfh2 cells, which in co-cultures promoted memory B cell differentiation into antibody-secreting cells producing anti-PvDBPII IgG; adding IFN-γ decreased cTfh2 activation.

articles.headline: Low malaria risk in PNG infants; postpartum maternal treatment no growth effect
articles.title_original: Malaria in Papua New Guinean infants: a prespecified analysis of a randomised postpartum antimalarial treatment trial.
articles.authors: Tesine P, Woon SA, Laman M, Yadi G, Kasian B, Lorry L, Salman S, Pomat W, Manning L, Chih H, Moore BR, Davis TME
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: In an open-label randomised controlled trial in Madang Province, Papua New Guinea, 183 mothers were assigned to no treatment (NT) or artemisinin combination therapy (artemether-lumefantrine or dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine) postpartum, and their infants were assessed monthly for 6 months. Of 151 infants completing the study, 2 developed slide-positive malaria (1 P. falciparum, 1 P. vivax; 1.3%, 95% CI 0.2–5.2) and 3 had PCR-detected infections, giving a 6-month incidence of 3.3% (95% CI 1.2–8.0). Four of five cases occurred in NT-group infants. No significant between-group differences in growth or developmental milestones were seen.

articles.headline: One Health proposal combining treated livestock, zooprophylaxis, and bed nets
articles.title_original: Integrating zooprophylaxis, insecticide-treated livestock and mosquito nets for malaria control: a One Health proposal.
articles.authors: Otolorin GR, Adegboye OA, Dishon CH, Dunka HI, Adanu AW, Ihieri CO, McBryde ES
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: This short communication proposes an integrated One Health malaria vector control strategy combining insecticide-treated livestock (ITL), zooprophylaxis, and insecticide-treated nets (ITNs). The authors note malaria transmission persists despite ITNs due to outdoor and early-evening biting and insecticide resistance, and that while zooprophylaxis has shown mixed epidemiological effects, treating livestock with topical or systemic insecticides significantly reduces mosquito survival. They argue combining ITL and zooprophylaxis with ITNs could improve control of vectors that feed outdoors and prefer animal hosts, and review this approach within integrated vector management.

articles.headline: Malaria shaped human habitat choice in Africa over 74,000 years
articles.title_original: Malaria shaped human spatial organization for the past 74 thousand years.
articles.authors: Colucci M, Leonardi M, Blinkhorn J, Irish SR, Padilla-Iglesias C, Kaboth-Bar S, Gosling WD, Snow RW, Manica A, Scerri EML
articles.details: This study explored whether P. falciparum-induced malaria influenced human spatial organization in sub-Saharan Africa from 74,000 to 5,000 years ago. Combining species distribution models of mosquito complexes with palaeoclimatic and epidemiological data, the authors estimated an index of malaria transmission risk over time and correlated it with an independent reconstruction of the human niche. They found that humans avoided, or were unsuccessful in, potential malaria hotspots, concluding that disease distribution—beyond climate—shaped past population structure, habitat choice, and dispersal.

articles.headline: China interprets new standard for malaria rapid diagnostic tests
articles.title_original: [Interpretation of a health industry standard Detection of Plasmodium spp. Immune-chromatographic test (WS/T 10029-2025)].
articles.authors: Xu S, Zhu G, Cao J
articles.journal: Zhongguo Xue Xi Chong Bing Fang Zhi Za Zhi
articles.details: This article interprets China's recommended health industry standard 'Detection of Plasmodium spp. Immune-chromatographic test (WS/T 10029-2025).' It explains the immunochromatographic (RDT) testing principles, sample collection and detection procedures, and result interpretation for detecting Plasmodium antigens. The authors note that thousands of imported malaria cases are still reported annually after China achieved malaria elimination, and that misunderstandings about RDT use persist among medical and disease-control workers. The piece aims to standardize technical guidelines and promote the standard's dissemination and implementation. No quantitative diagnostic performance results are reported.

articles.headline: Placental malaria vaccine cost-effective for first-time mothers in Africa
articles.title_original: Modelling the Cost-Effectiveness of a Placental Malaria Vaccine in Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Chinkhumba J, Manda-Taylor L, D'Alessio F, Madanitsa M
articles.details: A decision-analytic modelling study estimated the cost-effectiveness of a pre-pregnancy placental malaria (PM) vaccine in sub-Saharan Africa, comparing vaccinating women of childbearing age versus no vaccination from the provider's perspective. Assuming 40% vaccine efficacy, 30% uptake, and a $5 dose price, the incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) was $321 per perinatal DALY averted for primigravidae versus $4444 for multigravidae; best-case assumptions gave $225 vs $3148. Cost-effectiveness was defined as an ICER below $1556 (half of 2025 GDP per capita). Results were robust for primigravidae across plausible ranges, supporting prioritized deployment targeting first-time mothers.

articles.headline: Review: cerebral malaria diagnosis and treatment in resource-limited Africa
articles.title_original: Cerebral malaria in resource-limited African regions: Diagnostic and therapeutic challenges.
articles.authors: Akintunde JK, Oluwajembola AM, Oladosu MA, Akintunde DG, Akintunde OG, Ojo AE, Golagha R
articles.details: This review discusses diagnostic and therapeutic challenges of cerebral malaria (CM), a severe manifestation of Plasmodium falciparum infection predominantly affecting children and pregnant women in sub-Saharan Africa. It reports that CM remains associated with high mortality (15%–30%) and long-term neurological sequelae among survivors (10%–30%), and that up to 23% of clinically diagnosed CM cases may not meet strict diagnostic criteria. The authors highlight barriers including misdiagnosis, delayed presentation, lack of fundoscopy, neuroimaging and reliable biomarkers, poor referral systems, and absent rehabilitation services, and offer context-appropriate recommendations to reduce CM morbidity and mortality.

articles.headline: Ethiopia bed-net ownership high but usage below protective threshold
articles.title_original: Assessment of Long-Lasting Insecticidal Net (LLIN) Ownership, Utilization, and Associated Barriers in Malaria-Endemic Communities of Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Waldetensai A, Tasew G, Yewhalaw D, Takie H, Gidey B, Kinde S, Gemechu F, Yirga S, Kinfe E, Hailemariam A, Tadesse H, Solomon H, Assefa G, Dilu D, Bashaye S, Wuletaw Y, Abdulatif B, Kebede T, Tadiwos S, Gebrewold G, Hailu S, Tesfaye F, Tollera G, Hailu M, Guiyun Y, Eukubay A, Gebresillassie A
articles.details: A community-based cross-sectional survey collected data from 9,222 households (34,427 individuals) using two-stage stratified cluster sampling to assess long-lasting insecticidal net (LLIN) ownership, utilization, and barriers in malaria-endemic communities of Ethiopia. Household LLIN ownership was 71.5%, with sufficient nets for every two people in 58.3%; overall utilization among owners was 59.9%, higher in rural (72.7%) than urban areas, and higher among pregnant women (78.5%) and children under five (67.2%). Age and pregnancy status were strongest predictors of use (ORs 0.258 and 0.662, both p<0.001). Barriers included lack of confidence in hanging nets (60.5%) and the misconception that malaria risk is seasonal (64.1%). Utilization remained below the 80% threshold for community protection.

articles.headline: Guillain-Barré syndrome after malaria in a 7-year-old child
articles.title_original: Guillain-Barré Syndrome After Malaria: A Case Report of a 7-Year-Old Child With Asymmetric Onset of Acute Motor Axonal Neuropathy.
articles.authors: Abusaada MIM, Ali MMI, Adam MMA, Ali MHO, Baraka MAM, Bakheit AAM, Mohamed Ahmed KAH, Mohmed IYK
articles.details: This case highlights that acute motor axonal neuropathy can present with asymmetric, relapsing weakness in children. The authors emphasize that in malaria-endemic and resource-limited settings, clinicians should consider atypical Guillain-Barré variants early, since timely recognition and treatment can significantly improve outcomes despite diagnostic and systemic constraints. No quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: Severe childhood malaria linked to later cognitive deficits
articles.title_original: Malaria may cause long-term brain deficits, study shows.
articles.authors: Vogel G
articles.details: Ugandan children who survived a severe infection performed worse on cognitive tests many years later. The abstract provides no further details, such as the type of infection, sample size, or specific cognitive measures.

articles.headline: Lifetime malaria exposure not linked to SARS-CoV-2 infection in adolescents
articles.title_original: Life Course Malaria Exposure and SARS-CoV-2 Seroepidemiology in Ugandan Adolescents: A Longitudinal Study Nested in a Birth Cohort.
articles.authors: Zirimenya L, Nayebare D, Baine C, Nassuuna J, Namutebi M, Zziwa C, Nsubuga D, Kiwudhu F, Akello F, Tumusiime J, Oduru G, Kabami G, Akurut H, Nakyesige R, Natukunda A, Wajja A, Lubyayi L, Kimuda S, Nkurunungi G, Serwanga J, Doores KJ, Webb EL, Elliott AM
articles.details: A longitudinal study nested in the Entebbe Mother and Baby Study birth cohort in Uganda enrolled 476 adolescents (mean age 16.4 years; 43.1% female) with baseline and 6- and 12-month follow-up, testing whether prospectively measured life-course malaria exposure (clinical malaria, asymptomatic parasitaemia, and anti-PfMSP-2/PfAMA-1 antibodies) was associated with SARS-CoV-2 seropositivity (spike and nucleocapsid IgG). Overall, 12.0% had ever had asymptomatic parasitaemia and 61.3% had clinical malaria; SARS-CoV-2 seropositivity among unvaccinated participants rose from 63% to 89.3% to 94.9%. Multivariable analyses showed no consistent association between malaria exposure and seropositivity or antibody levels.

articles.headline: Malaria RDT P.f line cross-reacts with P. malariae and P. ovale
articles.title_original: Strong Cross-Reactivity of Anti-Plasmodium falciparum pLDH Monoclonal Antibodies With Plasmodium malariae pLDH in the Abbott Bioline Malaria Ag P.f/P.f/P.v Rapid Diagnostic Test.
articles.authors: Montoia G, Sanchez MCA, Lima-Cooper G, Farinas MLRN, Inoue J, Di Santi SM
articles.details: This diagnostic accuracy study analysed blood from 40 smear-positive patients (10 P. falciparum, 10 P. vivax, 15 P. malariae, 5 P. ovale), with species confirmed by nested PCR, to assess cross-reactivity of the Abbott Bioline Malaria Ag P.f/P.f/P.v rapid diagnostic test based on parasite lactate dehydrogenase detection. The test correctly identified all P. falciparum and P. vivax samples, but 12 of 14 P. malariae samples and 4 P. ovale isolates gave positive results on the T2 line intended to be Pf-pLDH-specific; specificity of the anti-Pf-pLDH antibodies against P. malariae was only 14.29% (p < 0.05). The authors report the first evidence of this cross-reactivity and call for validation of alternative biomarkers.

articles.headline: New synthesis yields selective P. falciparum proteasome inhibitors
articles.title_original: An Optimized Route to the Syringolin Natural Products Enables Combinatorial Synthesis of Selective, Bioactive Inhibitors of the Plasmodium falciparum 20S Proteasome.
articles.authors: Gu X, Fajtova P, Yan NL, Saxena A, Fei F, Zhu J, Tse E, Melo A, Yoo E, Buchwald NH, Southworth DR, Bogyo M, Derisi JL, Gestwicki JE, O'Donoghue AJ, Sello JK
articles.details: Researchers developed an improved synthetic route to the syringolin natural products, covalent 20S proteasome inhibitors, that is amenable to solution and solid-phase synthesis and overcomes a problematic macrocyclization. Using this approach, they generated a collection of hypothetically selective inhibitors of the Plasmodium falciparum proteasome, an emerging antimalarial drug target. They identified compounds with high second-order rate constants for Plasmodium proteasome inhibition, nanomolar antiparasitic activity, and selectivity for the Plasmodium over the human proteasome, and solved cryo-EM structures of an inhibitor bound to both 20S proteasomes revealing key contacts favoring species-selective inhibition.

articles.headline: Mefloquine-metal complexes show activity against malaria and schistosomes
articles.title_original: Examination of Coligands in Mefloquine-Metal Complexes Reveals the Structural Determinants of Activity against Plasmodium falciparum and Schistosoma mansoni.
articles.authors: Villarreal W, Quadros HC, Colina-Vegas L, Aboagye SY, Dziwornu GA, Ribeiro GH, Barros AI, Kucharski DJ, Rahbari M, Brandstädter C, D'Alessandro S, Basilico N, Masike K, Mehlala N, Nobrega JA, Deflon VM, Navarro M, Boratyński PJ, Chibale K, Williams DL, Batista AA, Moreira DRM
articles.details: This study synthesized and evaluated mefloquine (MQ)-metal complexes of formula [M(II)(L)(MQ)]PF6, varying the metal center (platinum or palladium) and coligand (phosphine or bipyridine), against Plasmodium falciparum and Schistosoma mansoni, with MQ coordinating as an N,O-bidentate ligand. The metal center augmented antiparasitic activity by acting as a thioredoxin/glutathione reductase-targeting moiety while the coligand modulated reactivity; MQ-Pt complexes showed high in vivo efficacy, and despite being isostructural, Pd complexes differed from Pt in ligand dissociation behavior. The work establishes a structural framework for metal-based antiparasitic agents that selectively target parasite biochemistry while sparing mammalian cells.

articles.headline: Protocol for review of malaria surveillance in African pharmacies
articles.title_original: Facilitators and barriers to malaria surveillance in community pharmacies and over-the-counter medicine sellers in sub-Saharan Africa's health sector: a scoping review protocol.
articles.authors: Bonful HA, Owiredu D, Annor RB, Opoku-Mireku M, Abokyi LN, Vroom BD, Kenu E, Kretchy IA, Koduah A
articles.details: This is a scoping review protocol that will systematically collate published and unpublished evidence on the facilitators and barriers to malaria surveillance in community pharmacies and over-the-counter medicine sellers in sub-Saharan Africa, where many private-sector malaria cases go uncaptured by routine surveillance. The authors will search PubMed, CINAHL, Scopus, Web of Science and Hinari from inception to 15 June 2026, with two reviewers independently screening studies and a narrative synthesis approach. No results are reported, as this describes planned methods only.

articles.headline: Review of archaeological and historical malaria evidence in Asia
articles.title_original: Historical texts and archaeological evidence for malaria and its interactions with humans from East to South Asia.
articles.authors: Li G, Yuan X, Wang T
articles.details: This interdisciplinary review synthesizes archaeological, biological and ecological evidence to explore human–malaria interactions across East, Southeast and South Asia, drawing on published genetic analyses of Plasmodium species, ancient Chinese historical and medical documents, and osteoarchaeological skeletal markers. It examines how the evolution of both Plasmodium and humans shaped human demographic shifts and the biological and societal resilience of people, and highlights how human activities such as watering fields for rice cultivation inadvertently created environments favorable to malaria transmission. No quantitative results are presented.

articles.headline: Review of the malaria parasite's understudied oocyst stage
articles.title_original: Oocyst: knowns and unknowns about the lengthiest life stage of the malaria parasite.
articles.authors: Bailey AJ, Vlachou D, Christophides GK
articles.details: This review synthesizes current knowledge of the Plasmodium oocyst, the longest life stage of the parasite, which forms on the mosquito midgut basal lamina after sexual reproduction and ultimately yields thousands of sporozoites. The authors note the oocyst represents a severe population bottleneck with the lowest parasite numbers of the life cycle, yet remains poorly understood. They highlight major gaps in transcriptional and epigenetic regulation, nutrient acquisition, metabolic remodelling, cell cycle control, and mosquito immune interactions, and discuss how deeper insight could inform next-generation transmission-blocking strategies.

articles.headline: Human blood-stage challenge model developed for Plasmodium knowlesi
articles.title_original: Development of an experimental human blood-stage model for studying Plasmodium knowlesi.
articles.authors: Gower JSE, Webster R, Westaway JAF, Tickner JA, Wang CYT, Peters JM, Andrew D, Tan AF, Webb L, Potter AJ, Woo R, Mathison S, Sahai N, Chen N, Cheng Q, Boyle MJ, Anstey NM, Grigg MJ, Woodford J, Jennings HE, Amante FH, Barber BE
articles.details: Researchers manufactured a GMP-grade P. knowlesi YH1 parasite bank adapted to human serum and tested it in an induced blood-stage malaria (IBSM) model in four adults intravenously inoculated using a dose-escalation strategy (10-fold increases from ~2,800 parasites), with parasitaemia monitored by qPCR and all participants treated with artemether-lumefantrine. Two of four developed detectable parasitaemia: participant two (~28,000 parasites) reached ~6 parasites/mL by day 21, and participant three (~280,000 parasites) peaked at 202 parasites/mL by day 24 with a 32-hour parasite multiplication rate of 2.33 (95% CI 1.66–3.25); participant four (~280,000) did not become infected. Adverse events were mostly mild, and the authors conclude the bank establishes patent human infection and can probe inter-individual susceptibility.

articles.headline: Perspective on China-Africa cooperation for malaria control
articles.title_original: [Practice and prospect of China's participation in international cooperation for malaria control from the perspective of the "China-Africa Year of People-to-People Exchanges"].
articles.authors: Liang C, Zhu G, Cao J
articles.journal: Zhongguo Xue Xi Chong Bing Fang Zhi Za Zhi
articles.details: This perspective article discusses China's participation in international cooperation for malaria control in the context of the 2026 "China-Africa Year of People-to-People Exchanges." It outlines the strategic significance and practical paths for such cooperation and offers policy recommendations for optimizing the China-Africa cooperation model, aiming to support progress toward global malaria elimination. No study data or quantitative results are presented.

articles.headline: Magneto-optical microscopy quantifies hemozoin in blood for malaria diagnosis
articles.title_original: Magneto-optical microscopy platform for quantitative imaging of hemozoin in blood for malaria diagnosis.
articles.authors: Kinyua DM, Kotar J, Cicuta P
articles.details: This methods/instrumentation study presents a magneto-optical (MO) imaging approach that integrates directly into a conventional polarizing microscope to achieve label-free, quantitative detection of malaria. An external magnetic field induces torque on paramagnetic hemozoin crystals, aligning them and modulating optical anisotropy to produce a field-dependent change in transmitted polarization contrast. Using ratiometric intensity analysis and threshold-based segmentation, the authors report a quantitative correlation between MO signal strength and hemozoin concentration, and thus parasite developmental stage. They conclude the stable, objective metrics are well-suited for automated diagnostics and can be embedded into existing microscopy workflows in endemic regions.

articles.headline: MKT1 domain protein dispensable for P. falciparum blood and sexual stages
articles.title_original: An MKT1 domain protein is dispensable for erythrocytic stages of plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Chakraborty B, McGee SM, Kumar S
articles.details: This laboratory study investigated the role of the MKT1 domain protein PfMKT1, a posttranscriptional gene-regulation molecule, in Plasmodium falciparum stage differentiation. PfMKT1 was expressed in both asexual and sexual stages, but parasites with the MKT1 gene deleted (Pfmkt1¯) proliferated asexually similarly to wildtype NF54 parasites and differentiated into mature male and female gametocytes. The knockout gametocytes underwent gametogenesis to form male and female gametes with no apparent defect in flagellar gamete formation. The authors conclude that the MKT1-like protein is dispensable during both asexual and sexual stage development.

articles.headline: Antibody profiles flag malaria surveillance blind spots in low-transmission Gambia
articles.title_original: Malaria antibody responses augment surveillance in low-transmission settings in the Upper River Region, the Gambia.
articles.authors: Aydin RA, Keller T, Dabira ED, Mohammed N, Erhart A, Drakeley C, D'Alessandro U, Stresman G
articles.details: Antibody responses to Plasmodium falciparum antigens could classify populations by P(Detect)—the proportion of infections captured by surveillance—in low-transmission settings. Data from 5,300 seropositive individuals across 32 villages in The Gambia, following mass drug administration, were analyzed with random forest models predicting low vs. high P(Detect) from responses to 19 antigens, stratified by transmission intensity. Using a reduced 8-antigen panel, models achieved precision-recall AUCs of 0.92–0.93 (<5% PCR prevalence) and 0.89–0.90 (<10%), with PfSEA, PfMSP1-19, gSG6, and Etramp4.Ag2 consistently important predictors. The authors conclude serological data can help identify hidden reservoirs and support targeted elimination.

articles.headline: In silico Rac1 promoter analysis links antihypertensive resistance and malaria susceptibility
articles.title_original: The Rac1 Promoter as a Molecular Nexus for Antihypertensive Therapy Resistance and Enhanced Malaria Susceptibility.
articles.authors: Ameke SL, Fosu K, Amenga-Etego L, Sarpong KAN, Kwofie SK
articles.details: This in silico study analyzed the human Rac1 promoter region (-2000 to +500 bp relative to the transcription start site) using biomaRt, BSgenome, and the JASPAR2022 database anchored to the GRCh38/hg38 genome, considering African ancestry variants from gnomAD, to explore links between antihypertensive therapy resistance and malaria susceptibility. The 2501 bp promoter was GC-rich (57.34%) with 164 CpG sites and an observed/expected ratio of 0.75, defining a canonical CpG island. Analysis revealed binding motifs for the mineralocorticoid receptor, hypoxia-inducible factors, inflammatory mediators, and erythroid-specific GATA-1, with nonrandom clustering. The authors conclude this architecture suggests a molecular basis for MR-mediated ACEI/ARB resistance and enhanced malaria susceptibility via erythrocyte remodeling.

articles.headline: Framework cuts costs of malaria routine data quality audits in Zambia
articles.title_original: A framework for identifying cost efficiencies in malaria routine data quality audits: methodology and application in Zambia.
articles.authors: Nguyen A, Malama P, Lungu C, Mwanza M, Phiri D, Silumbe K, Chiwaula J, Hainsworth M, Das S, Roca-Feltrer A
articles.details: This study developed a systematic four-stage framework—costing analysis, cost-driver identification, resource-optimized scenarios, and cost-efficiency evaluation—to identify cost efficiencies in malaria routine data quality audits (RDQA), applied to previously collected data from Zambia. Nine RDQA interventions by four projects (2022–2024) covering 10 provinces, 91 districts, and 1,189 health facility catchment areas (HFCAs) were evaluated. Average economic costs were $24,938 per intervention ($205 per HFCA), with personnel (64%) and transportation (25%) the primary cost drivers. Optimized scenarios using virtual orientation, streamlined audit teams, and harmonized allowances reduced total costs by 41%, with digital tools projecting up to 52% additional savings. The authors conclude the framework offers a practical methodology for substantial savings while maintaining coverage and quality.

articles.headline: Baseline survey before RTS,S malaria vaccine rollout in western Kenya
articles.title_original: The Malaria Vaccine Programme Evaluation in Kenya: results of a baseline household survey prior to the introduction of the RTS,S/AS01 vaccine.
articles.authors: Westercamp N, Nyangau I, Kwambai TK, Seda B, Akach D, Okanda I, Marube E, Siambe P, Radiro E, Wafula F, Ondiegi B, Shah MP, Seffren V, Moore KA, Fogelson A, Milligan P, Kariuki S, Samuels AM
articles.details: In a population-representative baseline household survey of 4065 households and 4948 children aged 5–48 months across 46 sub-counties of western Kenya before RTS,S/AS01E vaccine introduction, P. falciparum prevalence by rapid diagnostic test was 22% (95% CI 19–26), varying widely by sub-county (1–71%) and higher in areas randomized to the vaccine, indicating baseline imbalance. ITN ownership and use were high (93% and 87%); 38% of children had fever in the prior two weeks, of whom 70% sought care, and 39% of those care-seekers received antimalarials (nearly all artemisinin-combination therapies). Only 36% of caregivers had heard of the vaccine, yet over 98% were willing to vaccinate. The authors conclude the context favored RTS,S introduction, though gaps in second-year-of-life services may challenge delivery of the 4th dose.

articles.headline: Model estimates artemether-lumefantrine's economic benefit in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Socioeconomic Impact of Artemether-Lumefantrine in Malaria Treatment across Sub-Saharan Africa: A Model-Based Historical Analysis with 2022 as Reference Year.
articles.authors: Atitallah A, Peristeris P, Lovera D, Branner J, Shah R, Gandhi P, Gruenberger JB
articles.details: A de novo Excel-based decision-tree model took a societal perspective to estimate the socioeconomic burden of malaria and the benefit of artemether-lumefantrine (Coartem) for uncomplicated malaria across sub-Saharan Africa, using 2022 as the reference year and three age groups (<5, 5-14, ≥15 years). Burden was measured as lost paid-work productivity from DALYs. For 2022, the model reported 30.8 million malaria-related DALYs among those aged ≥15, equating to roughly US $82.8 billion in socioeconomic burden. The estimated total socioeconomic benefit of AL versus no treatment was US $24.2 billion through avoided deaths, years of life lost, and caregiving days. Authors conclude AL provides substantial economic benefit.

articles.headline: NK cell killing transiently spikes during acute P. falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: NK cell cytotoxicity is transiently enhanced during acute malaria and modulated by the host microenvironment.
articles.authors: Xi P, Sandoz PA, Lautenbach MJ, Bilev E, Önfelt B, Färnert A, Hammer Q, Sundling C
articles.details: In a longitudinal study of malaria patients in a nonendemic setting, the authors found a transient but potent activation of NK cell cytotoxicity during acute P. falciparum infection, marked by rapid granzyme B-mediated killing and elevated cytotoxicity gene expression (PRF1, GZMB, GZMA). Increased plasma granzymes and proinflammatory cytokines enhanced NK cell function in vitro, while inhibitory mediators such as soluble cytokine receptors dampened responses. The authors conclude the host microenvironment tightly regulates NK cell activity, boosting cytotoxicity during acute infection and downmodulating it after treatment, which may inform strategies to harness NK cells for malaria control.

articles.headline: Engineered dimeric aptamer improves point-of-care malaria antigen detection
articles.title_original: Engineering High-Performance Aptamers via Optimized SELEX and Structure-Guided Dimer Assembly for Clinical Malaria Diagnostics.
articles.authors: Xiong D, Ren Y, Gu J, Wu B, Dong X, Salena BJ, Zhang Z, Li Y
articles.details: Researchers combined an optimized SELEX workflow with structure-guided dimer engineering to create aptamers targeting Plasmodium falciparum lactate dehydrogenase (PfLDH). A symmetric inverted dimeric aptamer with a 3'-3' linkage, matched to the PfLDH homotetramer, achieved picomolar affinity (>800-fold higher than existing aptamers) and enhanced nuclease resistance. Incorporated into a cascading colorimetric amplification system, it detected PfLDH in human serum with a limit of detection of 3.3 ng/mL. Clinical evaluation of 52 human blood samples showed 81.8% sensitivity and 100% specificity, which the authors say compares favorably with commercial rapid diagnostic tests.

articles.headline: Blocking palmitoylation stalls P. falciparum growth and gametocyte formation
articles.title_original: [Role of protein palmitoylation modification in the proliferation and gametogenesis of Plasmodium falciparum].
articles.authors: Zhang M, Zhang M, Yang T, Zhu G, Tang J
articles.journal: Zhongguo Xue Xi Chong Bing Fang Zhi Za Zhi
articles.details: This study measured palmitoyltransferase (PAT) activity across P. falciparum stages using click chemistry and tested the inhibitor 2-bromopalmitate (2-BP) at 10 μmol/L against control and DMSO groups. PAT activity varied by stage, higher in trophozoites, merozoites, and gametocytes and lower in rings (F=38.120, P<0.001). 2-BP significantly inhibited PAT activity and reduced parasitemia area-under-curve (8.370 vs 25.700 control; F=83.440, P<0.001), lowered merozoites per schizont, and cut gametocyte production area-under-curve (7.507 vs 18.900; P<0.001), with delayed asexual development, abnormal morphology, and arrested gametocyte maturation. Transcriptomics showed downregulation of cell cycle, mitosis, and DNA damage/response genes, supporting PATs as a potential antimalarial target.

articles.headline: Imported malaria surges in Aseer, Saudi Arabia; P. vivax now dominant
articles.title_original: Clinical Characteristics and Outcomes of Malaria Patients in the Aseer Region, Saudi Arabia: A Retrospective Study (2022-2025).
articles.authors: Alshehri FI, Alkhosafi DA, Al Asmari EA, Saeed AB, Zarbah AM, Algarni SA, Ahmed MG, Mohamed MA, Mady FA, Albakri SMZ, Ghazy RM
articles.details: Retrospective study at Khamis Mushait General Hospital, Aseer Region, Saudi Arabia (Jan 2022–Dec 2025) of 311 malaria patients, 90.0% male, mean age 28.8±11.3 years; nearly half were Ethiopian nationals (48.2%). P. vivax was most common (51.1%) over P. falciparum (40.2%), and fever was the leading symptom (89.4%). Severe malaria (≥1 WHO criterion) occurred in 43.7%, most often severe anemia (26.0%) and jaundice (23.2%); 84% of cases fell in 2024–2025 with 43 cases peaking in October 2024. Independent predictors of severity were higher parasitemia (aOR 1.70 per 1% increase, 95% CI 1.40–2.11, p<0.001) and non-Saudi nationality (aOR 2.40, 95% CI 1.10–5.62, p=0.027). Authors conclude malaria is largely imported and recommend enhanced screening and surveillance.

articles.headline: Bed net ownership and use low among Fulani pregnant women, Ghana
articles.title_original: Factors Associated With Insecticide-Treated Bed Net Possession and Utilization in Malaria Prevention Among Fulani Pregnant Women in the Savannah Hinterlands of Ghana: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Salifu Y, Lasong J, Wobi EB, Salifu T, Nantomah B, Dandeebo G
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey (April–June 2022) of 159 Fulani pregnant women in West Gonja Municipality, northern Ghana, using interviews and multivariable logistic regression. Only 54.7% (n=87) owned an insecticide-treated net, and among owners just 52.9% (46/87) used it. ITN ownership was associated with awareness that ITNs prevent malaria (AOR 3.45, 95% CI 1.33–8.94), prior counseling on ITN use (AOR 4.52, 95% CI 1.53–13.34), and lower wealth (AOR 0.21, 95% CI 0.09–0.49). Utilization was linked to knowledge of malaria symptoms (AOR 0.18, 95% CI 0.05–0.64), having more than three children (AOR 0.02, 95% CI 0.002–0.12), and owning multiple ITNs (AOR 0.07, 95% CI 0.02–0.28). Authors call for strengthened health education and mobile-responsive distribution.

articles.headline: Herbal capsules from neem and Khaya bark show antiplasmodial activity
articles.title_original: Formulation and Evaluation of Capsules From Azadirachta indica and Khaya senegalensis for Malaria Treatment.
articles.authors: Adoley WN, Johnson R, Boakye-Gyasi ME, Owusu FWA, Obeng LM, Osei-Asibey A, Asamoa Mensa KA, Zoiku FK
articles.details: Formulation study converting traditional decoctions of Azadirachta indica (AI) leaves and Khaya senegalensis (KS) stem bark into capsules using absorbents (bentonite, magnesium carbonate, microcrystalline cellulose, kaolin) at varying concentrations. AI with 35 mg/dose kaolin (release 97.31±0.74%) and KS with 50 mg/dose bentonite (release 97.54±0.88%) were encapsulated, passing all quality control tests with disintegration times of 10.01±0.34 and 4.48±0.23 minutes and cumulative drug release of 78.13±0.37% and 95.63±1.00%, respectively. By SYBR green assay, AI showed good antiplasmodial activity (IC50 2.74±0.70 μg/mL) and KS moderate activity (IC50 24.37±4.19 μg/mL), both selective for the Plasmodium parasite. Authors conclude the capsules could replace the bitter decoctions.

articles.headline: Convulsions, delayed care raise under-five malaria death risk in Uganda
articles.title_original: Risk factors for malaria-related mortality among children under five at Mbale Regional Referral Hospital, Uganda, 2020-2024: A case-control study.
articles.authors: Kwizera P, Migisha R, Mutesi C, Rukundo G, Kabwama SN, Kwesiga B, Bulage L, Ario AR
articles.details: Unmatched 1:2 case-control study using retrospective 2020–2024 records at Mbale Regional Referral Hospital, Eastern Uganda, where pediatric malaria case fatality was 2.7%. Cases were 100 children ≤59 months with WHO-defined severe P. falciparum malaria who died; 200 recovered controls were sampled from about 32,400 admissions. Among cases, 73% were under 24 months and 61% male. Multivariable logistic regression linked mortality to convulsions (aOR 17, 95% CI 4.2–71), loss of consciousness (aOR 14, 95% CI 1.4–113), severe anemia (aOR 3.4, 95% CI 1.4–8.2), vomiting (aOR 3.1, 95% CI 1.4–6.9), and care-seeking delay >24 hours (aOR 8.8, 95% CI 2.3–34). Authors conclude early recognition of danger signs and prompt care-seeking could reduce deaths.

articles.headline: Commentary on malaria in pregnancy and differing maternal-fetal interests
articles.title_original: Malaria in pregnancy: what's good for the goose may not be good for the goslings.
articles.authors: Rogerson SJ, Unger HW
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: IPTp drug regimens show no lasting effect on infant malaria or growth
articles.title_original: Impact of intermittent preventive treatment for malaria in pregnancy with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine, dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine, and their combination on infant outcomes: A randomized controlled trial.
articles.authors: Nankabirwa JI, Kakuru A, Nguyen AT, Roh ME, Aguti M, Adrama H, Kizza J, Olwoch P, Camanag K, Benjamin-Chung J, Rosenthal PJ, Kamya MR, Dorsey G, Jagannathan P
articles.details: This double-blind randomized controlled trial compared monthly intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (IPTp-SP), dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (IPTp-DP), or IPTp-DP+SP, following infants to 12 months by passive and active surveillance. Among 871 infants there were 667 episodes of clinical malaria; incidence was similar versus IPTp-SP for IPTp-DP (IRR 1.04, 95% CI 0.78-1.38) and IPTp-DP+SP (IRR 0.93, 95% CI 0.68-1.26), and overall parasite prevalence was 21.8%. Wasting at birth was more common with IPTp-DP+SP (PR 2.30, 95% CI 1.07-4.97) and IPTp-DP (RR 2.25, 95% CI 1.03-4.94) but resolved by 3-6 months. Authors conclude infant outcomes did not differ between arms apart from transient wasting with DP-containing regimens.

articles.headline: Review of how malaria may shape cancer risk in Africa
articles.title_original: Malaria driven mechanisms shaping cancer risk and aggressiveness in African populations.
articles.authors: Udobi ME, Bella-Omunagbe M, Effiong PE, Afolabi IS, Chinedu SN
articles.details: This narrative review synthesizes data from PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science on mechanistic links between chronic or recurrent malaria infection and carcinogenesis, progression, and therapeutic response in sub-Saharan African populations. It highlights five axes: malaria-induced oxidative stress and chronic inflammation driving genomic instability; gut microbiome dysbiosis altering immunity and the tumour microenvironment; shared molecular targets such as endothelial protein C receptor (EPCR) and oncofetal chondroitin sulfate; cooperation between malaria and oncogenic viruses like Epstein-Barr virus in lymphomagenesis; and malaria-associated vitamin D deficiency. It also notes antimalarials (artemisinin derivatives, chloroquine, quinacrine) with anticancer properties, and calls for integrative research to identify biomarkers and therapeutic targets.

articles.headline: Commentary: P. vivax as Guillain-Barré trigger only after excluding others
articles.title_original: Plasmodium Vivax Can Only Be Responsible for GBS if All Other Triggers Have Been Ruled Out.
articles.authors: Finsterer J
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Historical data on the cultural history of malaria in Hungary
articles.title_original: [Data for cultural history of malaria in Hungary].
articles.authors: Kiss L
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Workshop reviews vaccine strategies to prevent malaria in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Immunization strategies to prevent malaria in pregnancy - a multistakeholder workshop.
articles.authors: D'Alessio F, Honkpehedji J, Sirima S, Gamain B, Morten N, Ndam N, Mordmüller B, Dicko A, Adechina R, Dossou A, Chinkhumba J, Kaur M, Houngnihin R, LaMontagne DS, Menendez C, Amani A, Mayani T, Hamel MJ, Carrió E, Sayeh A, Adegnika AA
articles.details: This report summarizes a multistakeholder workshop, part of the ADVANCE-VAC4PM project, on strategies for using malaria vaccines to prevent malaria in pregnancy (MiP), convening researchers, regulators, civil society, global health experts, and policymakers. It notes that pregnant women are highly vulnerable, with an estimated 12.4 million affected in 2023, and that placental malaria risk is greatest in primigravidae and secundigravidae. Vaccine candidates targeting the VAR2CSA antigen (PRIMVAC and PAMVAC) are in development, and existing infection-preventing malaria vaccines are being repurposed for MiP. Recommendations included strengthening community engagement, defining efficacy endpoints, reinforcing pharmacovigilance, ensuring early regulatory alignment, and considering reduced-dose schedules.

articles.headline: New sequencing assay classifies P. vivax relapse vs reinfection
articles.title_original: Understanding Plasmodium vivax recurrent infections using an amplicon deep sequencing assay, identity-by-descent and model-based classification.
articles.authors: Rosado J, Han J, Obadia T, Munro J, Traore Z, Schoffer K, Brewster J, Bourke C, Vinetz JM, Taylor AR, White M, Bahlo M, Gamboa D, Mueller I, Ruybal-Pesántez S
articles.details: Researchers developed PvAmpSeq, an amplicon deep sequencing assay targeting 11 SNP-rich genomic regions, to distinguish Plasmodium vivax relapse, reinfection, and recrudescence using identity-by-descent and model-based classification. It was applied to field isolates from a clinical trial in the Solomon Islands and a longitudinal cohort in Peru. In the Solomon Islands trial, half of recurrent infections showed >50% IBD relatedness to baseline parasites, allowing classification as probable relapses/recrudescences but with wide uncertainty, whereas in Peru 68% of recurrences showed <25% relatedness. The authors conclude PvAmpSeq enables high-resolution genotyping of recurrences while noting limitations of current statistical methods.

articles.headline: Long non-coding RNAs mapped across P. falciparum blood stages
articles.title_original: Dynamic regulation of long non-coding RNAs across asexual and gametocyte development in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Grünebast J, Singhal R, Olson S, Bromley R, Kanatani S, Ko K, Dunning Hotopp J, Sinnis P, Llinás M, Serre D
articles.details: This study integrated Oxford Nanopore direct RNA sequencing, ribosome profiling, and single-cell transcriptomics to characterize long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) in Plasmodium falciparum asexual and sexual blood stages. The authors generated stage-specific lncRNA annotations and confirmed their non-coding nature by ribosome profiling. Most lncRNAs showed pronounced stage-specific expression and were particularly abundant in mature gametocytes, with many differentially expressed between female and male gametocytes. Many lncRNAs were antisense to protein-coding genes and co-expressed with sense mRNA, while others overlapped coding sequences or 3' UTRs with negatively correlated expression. The authors suggest roles in gene regulation, gametocytogenesis, and transmission.

articles.headline: What drives mosquito density and species mix in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Variance partitioning reveals contrasting random effect contributions to the density and species composition of malaria-transmitting mosquitoes in western Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Hui TJ, Epopa PS, Millogo AA, Yao FA, Koulmaga D, Noulin F, Diabate A, Burt A
articles.details: Using pyrethroid spray catch sampling conducted over a 60-month period between 2012 and 2019 in four villages in the Sudano-Sahelian region of Burkina Faso, researchers partitioned variance in Anopheles vector density and species composition into between-house, between-village, and between-year components. For female An. gambiae s.l. density, between-house variation was the largest variance component, followed by between-year then between-village; density rose with household inhabitant number but was uncorrelated with bed net presence. For species composition of An. coluzzii and An. gambiae, between-village variance contributed up to 76% of total random variation and between-house variation was statistically insignificant. The authors note these components help parameterise future vector-control field trials.

articles.headline: P. falciparum dominates; P. vivax found in hypo-endemic Niger
articles.title_original: Prevalence and spatial distribution of malaria-causing Plasmodium infections in the Niger Republic.
articles.authors: Sanoussi MK, Issa I, Mahaman Moustapha L, Idrissa Y, Hachimou I, Wilfried H, Seini HI, Abdoulaye D, Abou Y, Mounkaila B, Ibrahim ML
articles.details: P. falciparum strongly predominated among malaria infections in the Niger Republic, while other species were also detected. In this cross-sectional study during the high-transmission period (September–November 2022), febrile patients across hypo-, meso-, and hyperendemic zones were tested by RDT, thick smear, and PCR; of 5096 patients analyzed and 304 PCR samples, 55% (166/304) were Plasmodium-positive. P. falciparum accounted for 90% (149/166), P. ovale 5% (9/166), P. malariae 3% (5/166), and P. vivax 2% (3/166), with P. vivax identified in a hypo-endemic area. The authors conclude P. falciparum predominates while P. vivax circulates in a hypo-endemic area, informing national diagnosis and management strategy.

articles.headline: Review: targeting protein-protein interactions as antimalarial drug targets
articles.title_original: Targeting protein-protein interactions in Plasmodium: from asexual replication to sexual development.
articles.authors: Tang J, Tang W, Xie Y, Yang W, Luo X, Yu Y, He B, Liu C, Li Z
articles.details: This review synthesizes structural and functional advances in multi-subunit protein complexes across the Plasmodium life cycle, focusing on asexual stages of P. falciparum and sexual development of P. berghei and P. yoelii, drawing on cryo-electron microscopy, proximity-dependent biotinylation, and genetic manipulation. The authors argue that conserved protein-protein interactions (PPIs) governing erythrocyte invasion, intracellular survival, egress, and transmission have lower mutational potential than enzyme catalytic sites and so offer resistance-resilient drug targets. They cite the 2025 World Malaria Report's 282 million cases and 610,000 deaths in 2024, and review PPI-targeted neutralizing antibodies, vaccine candidates, and small-molecule inhibitors as next-generation, transmission-blocking strategies.

articles.headline: Transfluthrin emanator cuts pyrethroid-resistant mosquito landing and feeding in Benin huts
articles.title_original: Experimental hut evaluation of mosquito shield, a transfluthrin spatial emanator, for control of wild pyrethroid resistant malaria vectors in Benin.
articles.authors: N'dombidjé B, Syme T, Ahoga J, Pearce B, Yadouleton A, Yamadjako M, Ngufor C
articles.details: An experimental hut trial in Benin evaluated Mosquito Shield, a passive transfluthrin-based spatial emanator, against wild pyrethroid-resistant Anopheles gambiae s.l. over two 32-day product cycles using 16 huts assigned to product or placebo. Local mosquitoes were confirmed highly resistant to pyrethroids including transfluthrin. The product gave 43.0% protective efficacy (95% CI 24.0–57.0; p<0.001) against An. gambiae and 38.0% against Mansonia africana, with 64% personal protection, 48.5% deterrence, and 29.9% blood-feeding inhibition. It induced An. gambiae mortality up to 49.0% and >96% in M. africana. Authors say results support WHO prequalification.

articles.headline: Most patients misdiagnosed by malaria RDTs over time in DRC cohort
articles.title_original: Challenges in malaria diagnosis and treatment in Kinshasa Province, Democratic Republic of the Congo.
articles.authors: François-Zafka R, Westreich D, Banek K, Kashamuka MM, White SJ, Bala JA, Nkalani M, Kihuma G, Atibu J, Mahilu GE, Thwai KL, Kokolomami J, Emch M, Edwards J, Juliano JJ, Tshefu A, Parr JB
articles.details: Using samples from a cohort of children and adults across seven sites in Kinshasa Province, DRC, researchers quantified RDT misdiagnosis against PCR as the gold standard among 2,269 participants. One-year cumulative incidences of false-positive and false-negative RDTs were 23.1% and 17.8%, rising to 77.7% and 83.4% by seven years. Misdiagnosis was associated with age, recent treatment, transmission intensity, and parasite density. About 58% of RDT-negative participants received antimalarial treatment, while 4% of RDT-positive participants did not; fever reduced adherence to test-and-treat guidelines. Authors highlight RDT limitations and the need for better clinical decision-making.

articles.headline: Dual-ingredient Interceptor G2 nets reduced malaria cases up to 40% in Ghana
articles.title_original: Reductions in malaria cases after deployment of dual-active ingredient insecticide-treated nets in Ghana-a Bayesian interrupted time series analysis.
articles.authors: Oppong SK, Owusu-Akrofi O, Atta-Obeng C, Coleman S, Amratia P, Symons TL, Alene KA, Peprah NY, Gething PW, Malm KL
articles.details: Distribution of dual-active-ingredient Interceptor G2 ITNs in 2021 was associated with a 30% reduction in malaria cases in Ghana's Western North Region immediately afterward (OR 0.696; CrI 0.623–0.778). Using Bayesian Poisson regression time series models on monthly confirmed malaria cases from health facilities (2018–2023), adjusted for vector habitat and temperature suitability, reductions were sustained at 26% at 6 months (OR 0.739; CrI 0.653–0.837) and 40% at 30 months (OR 0.594; CrI 0.492–0.718). The intervention was also associated with reductions in seven of the nine districts. The authors conclude these next-generation nets are effective in this pyrethroid-resistant area and support scale-up.

articles.headline: DRC healthcare workers broadly accept Artemisia annua tea for malaria
articles.title_original: Public Health Perspectives on Integrating Artemisia annua Tea for Uncomplicated Malaria Treatment: A Cross-Sectional Study of Perceptions and Acceptability Among Healthcare Workers in Kalima District, Maniema, DRC.
articles.authors: Munyangi Wa Nkola J, Akilimali Zalagile P, Lukuke Mbutshu H, Kabala Munyemo S, Ramazani Bin Eradi I, Camara A
articles.details: A cross-sectional survey of 337 healthcare professionals in the Kalima health district of Maniema province, DRC, assessed clinical acceptability and perceptions of integrating Artemisia annua tea into formal malaria control, despite international guidelines opposing such infusions over risks of sub-therapeutic dosing and resistance selection. Overall acceptability was 81.0%, with 82.8% perceiving the preparation as effective, and rural residency was the strongest predictor of adherence (AOR=6.847; p=0.003), reflecting frequent ACT stockouts and high treatment costs. However, 49.0% cited a lack of clinical evidence as a major barrier and 91.4% demanded formal training on standardized dosing. Authors describe a policy-practice gap and note a complementary PfKelch13 genomic investigation underway in the same area.

articles.headline: Culex mosquito research neglected in malaria and dengue-endemic countries
articles.title_original: Forgotten in the tropics: research on Culex mosquitoes is overshadowed in malaria and dengue-endemic regions.
articles.authors: Abdi AA, Almeida R, Harris T, Magalhaes T, Juarez JG, Hamer GL
articles.details: A systematic literature review of mosquito studies published in 2010 and 2020 tested whether Culex spp. publications are proportionally fewer in malaria- and dengue-endemic countries, using PubMed and Web of Science searches and beta-binomial generalized linear mixed models adjusted for year and GDP per capita. After screening 10,834 publications, 1,389 met inclusion criteria. Non-endemic countries reported more mosquito genera per study, and Culex data appeared in 64.5% of non-endemic-country publications versus 30.2% for malaria-endemic and 34.2% for dengue-endemic countries. Aedes reporting rose and Anopheles fell between years. Authors urge including broader mosquito community data.

articles.headline: Socioeconomic and geographic factors shape household malaria risk perception in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Household malaria risk perception in Ethiopia: multilevel and spatial analysis using the 2022-2023 National Health Equity Survey.
articles.authors: Leul Y, Ararso D, Girmay AM, Zeru A, Taye G, Bahre W, Berhanu K, Lemlem A, Getachew T, Achamyeleh H, Shibiru Y, Alemu A, Tadele A, Wasie A, Molla G, Getachew F, Tadele W, Beshah SA, Tesfaye H, Zelalem D, Sori SA, Ayele S, Dagne T, Kabeta T, Belay YA, Terefe B, Fekadu L, Tiru G, Tollera G
articles.details: This study analyzed household malaria risk perception using data from 9,157 households in the 2022–2023 Ethiopian National Health Equity Survey, applying multilevel mixed-effects logistic regression and spatial analysis (Global Moran's I, Getis-Ord Gi*). Overall, 76.4% of households reported feeling at risk of malaria (95% CI 74.9–77.9). Rural households had 54% higher odds of perceiving risk than urban (AOR 1.54, 95% CI 1.22–1.95), and households with higher education nearly twice the odds (AOR 1.89, 95% CI 1.36–2.63), while older household heads (AOR 0.49) and those without a mobile phone (AOR 0.59) or bank account (AOR 0.59) were less likely. About 38% of variability occurred between woredas (Median Odds Ratio 2.62), with significant spatial autocorrelation (Global Moran's I = 0.42, p < 0.001) and 11% of areas as High–High clusters. Authors conclude perception is shaped by socioeconomic disparities and geographic clustering and recommend integrating perception indicators into surveillance.

articles.headline: Malaria species and G6PD variants in conflict-hit Sudan zones
articles.title_original: Characterisation of malaria and glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency in conflict-affected zones of southern and eastern Sudan.
articles.authors: Jebreel WMA, Abdel Hamid MM, Mustafa SA, Awad F, Chamai M, Malpartida-Cardenas K, Boadu EA, Amoah LE, Rodriguez-Manzano J, Cunnington AJ, Mohamed AO
articles.details: This cross-sectional study (2023–2024) enrolled 717 patients with suspected malaria in Kosti (n=252) and Kassala (n=465), using microscopy and qPCR plus PCR-RFLP for G6PD genotyping. By PCR, malaria prevalence was 62.6% (291/465) in Kassala and 52% (133/252) in Kosti. Kassala infections were 54% P. vivax, 34% P. falciparum, and 12% mixed, whereas Kosti was 97.7% P. falciparum. Submicroscopic infections totaled 8.7% (37). The G6PD B variant predominated; the African A− variant appeared in 9 (3.5%) Kosti individuals. No significant association was found between G6PD genotype and parasite density, and prevalence did not differ between displaced persons and residents.

articles.headline: Acute kidney injury affects one-third of admitted malaria patients
articles.title_original: Prevalence of acute kidney injury and its associated factors among malaria patients admitted to public hospitals in the Ilu Aba Bora and Bedelle Zones of Oromia region, Southwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Befkadu Z, Teferi S, Endalu T, Mitiku A, Barkesa B, Darge T
articles.details: This facility-based cross-sectional study (February 1–April 30, 2024) enrolled 307 adult malaria patients across four public hospitals in Ethiopia's Ilu Aba Bora and Buno Bedelle Zones, defining AKI as a serum creatinine rise of ≥0.3 mg/dL within 48 hours. AKI prevalence was 28.3% (87/307). Factors significantly associated with AKI included male sex (AOR 1.98), age 40–59 years (AOR 2.52), age ≥60 years (AOR 4.46), dehydration (AOR 1.74), P. falciparum infection (AOR 2.73), mixed infection (AOR 3.30), and high parasitaemia (AOR 2.71). The authors stress the need for renal assessment in malaria patients.

articles.headline: Switch to neonicotinoid IRS linked to higher malaria in Ghana
articles.title_original: Malaria incidence rose following the introduction of neonicotinoid-based IRS in selected districts in northern Ghana: An observational analysis.
articles.authors: Coleman S, Atta-Obeng C, Nkolokosa C, Mohammed AG, Akrofi OO, Isaka I, Mohammed W, Peprah NY, Asiedu S, Dadzie SK, Dery DB, Tangena JA, Epstein A, Malm K
articles.details: This observational analysis used monthly district-level confirmed malaria incidence (2015–2022) across 22 IRS districts in four northern Ghana regions, fitting an interrupted time series model with generalized estimating equations to 2015–2019 organophosphate-era data to generate counterfactuals, adjusting for rainfall, temperature, vegetation, seasonality, and chemoprevention. After the 2018–2019 transition to neonicotinoid-based IRS, observed incidence exceeded expected levels, with malaria 26% higher than under continued organophosphate use (IRR 1.26, 95% CI 1.07–2.08). Significant increases occurred in Northern (IRR 1.76), North East (IRR 1.83), and Upper East (IRR 4.26) regions, but not Upper West (IRR 1.25). The authors suggest the switch reduced IRS effectiveness.

articles.headline: Computer modeling of red cell mechanics in merozoite invasion
articles.title_original: In silico analysis of the invasion mechanics and invasiveness of the Plasmodium falciparum merozoite.
articles.authors: Msosa C, Abdalrahman T, Franz T
articles.details: This in silico study used finite element models of a human erythrocyte and a spherocyte, with smoothed particle hydrodynamics for the cytoplasm and a constitutive model for merozoite-induced membrane damage, to examine how erythrocyte deformability affects Plasmodium falciparum merozoite invasion and whether mechanical markers could screen invasion-blocking drugs. Merozoite invasiveness decreased with greater erythrocyte sphericity (as in hereditary spherocytosis) and was higher when membrane damage occurred early in invasion. The minimum force predicted for invasion was 11 pN. The authors conclude nanoindentation could help assess membrane damage for screening novel invasion-blocking antimalarials.

articles.headline: Modeling free care and better diagnosis to cut Guinea malaria
articles.title_original: Model-Based Assessment of the Effects of Selective Free Healthcare and Differentiated Diagnosis on Malaria Burden in the Republic of Guinea.
articles.authors: Diallo EM, Loum MA, Diallo OO, Gning LD, Ndiaye K, Ly AY, Bakhoum LN, Diallo A, Gerbaud L, Camara A
articles.details: A modeling study using EMOD software assessed how optimizing case management (selective free healthcare, differentiated diagnosis, and the combined strategy) would affect malaria burden across nine ecological archetypes in Guinea over 2025-2030, compared with a baseline of current interventions. Free healthcare for children under five plus differentiated diagnosis raised the early ACT access index from 0.60 (Tougue) to 0.81 (Nzerekore), with effects strongest in low-incidence areas where incidence dropped below 50 cases per 1000 by 2028. The combined strategy could prevent 13,428 complicated paediatric malaria cases between 2025 and 2030 (95% CI: 7768-19,088). Authors conclude targeted age-focused interventions improve ACT access and reduce burden.

articles.headline: Cytokines and metabolism shape γδ T cells in placental malaria
articles.title_original: Ex vivo cytokine responses and metabolic reprogramming are associated with γδ T cell differentiation in Plasmodium falciparum placental malaria.
articles.authors: Nana Mbianda CM, Kwanou Tchakounte BD, Bitye Zambo BM, Ntankheuh Tchinkou J, Atchombat Nyasse D, Fogang B, Argüello RJ, Ayong L, Megnekou R
articles.details: A case-control study of 50 women at delivery (21 with placental malaria, 29 without) examined ex vivo associations between cytokines, metabolic profiles, and γδ T-cell differentiation, using multiparametric flow cytometry on peripheral, placental intervillous, and cord blood mononuclear cells, with IL-8, IL-33, and IL-35 measured by Luminex/ELISA and immunometabolism assessed by SCENITH in a subset of 15 uninfected samples. Naïve and central memory γδ T cells predominated in cord blood, while effector memory and TEMRA phenotypes dominated peripheral and intervillous blood. IL-8 was associated with down-regulation of TIM-3 and PD-1 in a compartment-dependent manner, IL-8 plus IL-33 with increased peripheral TEMRA frequency, and terminally differentiated cells showed enhanced mitochondrial metabolism. Authors conclude placental malaria is associated with compartment-specific γδ T-cell differentiation, identifying potential targets for therapeutic intervention.

articles.headline: Chloroquine sensitivity restored in P. falciparum in Gabon
articles.title_original: Reversal of Chloroquine Resistance in Plasmodium falciparum in Gabon: A Phenotype-Genotype Relationship over the Last 20 Years.
articles.authors: Inoue J, Rodi M, Bohissou FET, de Carvalho LP, Lohse K, Koehne E, Agnandji ST, Mombo-Ngoma G, Kremsner PG, Kreidenweiss A, Held J
articles.details: Researchers compiled ex vivo chloroquine susceptibility (IC50) data from four time points (2004-2024) and assessed pfcrt and pfmdr1 polymorphisms by real-time PCR and Sanger sequencing at six time points (2009-2024) in Plasmodium falciparum from Lambaréné, Gabon. Median chloroquine IC50 fell stepwise from 113.8 nM in 2004 (n=42) to 46.7 nM in 2009 (n=26), 15.6 nM in 2017-2018 (n=46), and 2.1 nM in 2024 (n=39). The chloroquine-sensitive pfcrt haplotype CVMNK rose from 3.6% (1/28) in 2009 to 98.2% (56/57) in 2024, and wild-type pfmdr1 N86 from 23.1% (6/16) to 100% (19/19); IC50 values were associated with both markers. Authors conclude chloroquine-sensitive P. falciparum has re-established in Lambaréné.

articles.headline: Malaria control targets only partly met in Kinshasa, DRC
articles.title_original: Evaluation of Performance Indicators for Malaria Control in Kinshasa from 2020 to 2023, the Democratic Republic of the Congo.
articles.authors: Lutumbu BB, Mbanzulu KM, Kapour GK, Ndona MM, Zanga J, Mukendi JPK, Kayembe H, Mbangama A, Wumba R
articles.details: A descriptive cross-sectional study used National Malaria Control Program data from 23 health zones in Kinshasa (2020-2023) to evaluate diagnostic, therapeutic, and preventive indicators (LLINs and sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine-based IPT) against strategic-plan thresholds. Malaria was present in all health zones with heterogeneous distribution; incidence was 30%, with rising suspected and confirmed cases peaking in 2022. LLINs distributed to pregnant women at antenatal visits were 62%, 61%, 45%, and 88% across 2020-2023. RDT was used to diagnose 83.1% of suspected cases, and confirmed cases received antimalarial treatment. Authors conclude the 2020-2023 plan objectives were only partially achieved, with no health zone reaching 100% RDT diagnosis.

articles.headline: New compound kills P. falciparum by blocking plasmepsin X
articles.title_original: A novel 2-piperazino-pyrimidine compound exhibits asexual antimalarial activity by targeting Plasmodium falciparum plasmepsin X.
articles.authors: Hong J, Sakura T, Asada N, Fuchino K, Kuwana D, Kaki R, Ishii R, Kato T, Kita K, Matsumura K, Inaoka DK
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: Researchers identified a 2-piperazino-pyrimidine compound (FPSA) from Shionogi's chemical library with antimalarial activity against Plasmodium falciparum, acting by blocking merozoite release from infected erythrocytes; treated parasites halted as schizonts with an egress defect. Selection produced FPSA-resistant clones with modest EC50 shifts: two clones had single-nucleotide variants in the plasmepsin X gene predicting I252V or E55G substitutions, and three had duplications spanning that gene; genome-edited parasites with these substitutions showed similar EC50 fold changes. FPSA inhibited recombinant PMX proteolytic activity and substrate cleavage, and produced transcriptomic patterns resembling the PMIX/PMX dual inhibitor WM382. Authors conclude FPSA is a novel antimalarial class targeting plasmepsin X.

articles.headline: Computational screen: natural compounds bind Plasmodium NDH2 but also human off-target
articles.title_original: Comparative Structural and Functional Analysis of Plasmodium Type II Nicotinamide Adenine Dinucleotide: Quinone Oxidoreductase and its Human Homologue, Apoptosis-Inducing Factor, Mitochondrion-associated 1 (AIF-M1).
articles.authors: Baye BC, Moses V, Ugbaja SC, Lobb KA
articles.details: Comparative computational study across five human-infecting Plasmodium species (P. falciparum, P. knowlesi, P. malariae, P. ovale, P. vivax) screening the South African Natural Compounds Database (SANCDB) against the antimalarial target type II NADH:quinone oxidoreductase (NDH2), using homology modeling and molecular dynamics simulations. Compounds SANC00101, SANC00344, and SANC00418 showed strong binding to PfNDH2 (-8.6, -8.8, and -10.0 kcal/mol), but MD simulations showed they also stabilized the human homologue AIF-M1, with Phe482 identified as a critical off-target anchor; PkNDH2 was destabilized despite favorable docking scores. The authors conclude that effective pan-Plasmodium inhibition requires simultaneous optimization against human AIF-M1 and consideration of variable ortholog dynamics.

articles.headline: Gut microbiota modulation protects mice from cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Gut microbiota bidirectionally influences protection and severity in cerebral malaria in mice.
articles.authors: Taniguchi T, Miyauchi E, Kawabata-Iwakawa R, Nishiyama M, Umemiya-Shirafuji R, Toma H, Sasaki N, Hisaeda H, Tomita H, Ohno H, Kishimoto H, Suzuki H
articles.details: Experimental study in male C57BL/6N mice given a four-antibiotic cocktail (ampicillin, neomycin, metronidazole, vancomycin) in drinking water for 2 weeks before P. berghei ANKA infection, to examine effects of microbiota alteration on experimental cerebral malaria (ECM). Approximately 80% of mice with modified gut microbiota avoided ECM and showed reduced blood-brain barrier disruption and lymphocyte infiltration into the brain. 16S rRNA gene sequencing identified specific bacterial species contributing to protection or pathogenesis, and mono-colonization of germ-free mice showed distinct species could attenuate or exacerbate ECM independent of antibiotic effects. The authors conclude that distinct gut microbial populations modulate host susceptibility or resistance to cerebral malaria.

articles.headline: Routine microscopy misdiagnosed 30% of malaria cases versus LAMP in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Unveiling the overlooked burden of malaria misdiagnosis using lamp-based re-evaluation of routine malaria diagnosis in health centers: implications for public health and clinical practice in Northwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Alula MT, Tegegne B, Setegn A, Yimer M, Tefera MW, Gedfie S, Ashagre A, Abere A, Tamir M, Mezgebu B, Abebe AD, Almaw A, Pillai DR, Lemma W, Eshetu T
articles.details: Health facility-based cross-sectional study at four health centers in Northwest Ethiopia (July 1–August 30, 2024) re-evaluating routine microscopy results for 384 malaria-suspected individuals (116 microscopy-positive, 268 microscopy-negative) using a LAMP molecular assay on venous blood samples. 30.0% of routine microscopy results were discordant with LAMP, comprising false-positive results (7.6%), false-negative results (18.8%), and species misidentification (3.6%), with McNemar's test showing a significant difference (p<0.001). The authors conclude that malaria misdiagnosis is high, but microscopy remains the accessible WHO-endorsed operational gold standard and requires strengthened training, quality assurance, and supervision.

articles.headline: Triple Plasmodium infections raise mortality in children with severe falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium ovale and Plasmodium malariae Mixed Infections and Associated Mortality in Children with Severe Falciparum Malaria.
articles.authors: Lima-Cooper G, Datta D, Bond C, Mellencamp KA, Namazzi R, Gupta M, Conroy AL, Opoka RO, John CC
articles.details: Cohort of 440 Ugandan children hospitalized with severe malaria, diagnosed by microscopy and re-tested by nested PCR for P. falciparum, P. ovale, P. malariae, and P. vivax. P. falciparum mono-infection occurred in 329 (74.8%), P. falciparum/P. ovale mixed infection in 100 (22.8%), and P. falciparum/P. ovale/P. malariae triple infection in 9 (2.1%). Triple-infected children had higher mortality than mono-infected children (3/9 [33%] versus 23/329 [7%]; odds ratio = 7.0; 95% CI 1.8–28; P=0.005) and more respiratory distress (6/9 [67%] versus 115/329 [35%]; P=0.08). The authors conclude that improved detection and broader surveillance of non-falciparum malaria may help identify patterns associated with adverse outcomes.

articles.headline: Machine-learning tool accurately classifies P. falciparum var gene upstream groups
articles.title_original: upsML: A high-accuracy machine learning classifier for predicting Plasmodium falciparum var gene upstream groups.
articles.authors: Pangilinan EA, Quenu M, Claessens A, Otto TD
articles.details: Researchers developed upsML, a machine-learning classifier trained on 2,530 curated var genes to assign PfEMP1 upstream groups (upsA, upsB, upsC, upsE) from partial gene regions such as DBLα tags or RNA-Seq assemblies. Comparing seven methods including support vector machines, random forests, XGBoost, and HMMER models, several upsML models achieved 83% accuracy for DBLα-tag sequences and 92% for full-length PfEMP1 sequences, outperforming existing tools. A separate model distinguishing internal from subtelomeric var genes, applied to a global collection of P. falciparum genomes, revealed a higher frequency of internal var genes in Asia. The tool can classify var genes from 20 genomes in under one second.

articles.headline: Rapid whole-genome sequencing resolves cryptic UK malaria cases
articles.title_original: Rapid whole genome sequencing of Plasmodium DNA from cryptic malaria cases in UK travellers provides insights into infection origins, transmission, and antimalarial resistance.
articles.authors: Tan MKI, Billows N, Nolder D, Moss S, Phelan JE, Thorpe J, Edgeworth JL, Sutherland CJ, Chiodini PL, Campino S, Clark TG
articles.details: This study applied near-real-time whole-genome sequencing (WGS) to Plasmodium DNA from 9 clinical isolates using Illumina or Oxford Nanopore platforms, analysed with Malaria-Profiler for variant calling, drug-resistance prediction, and AI-based geographic origin inference against a database of over 15,000 isolate genomes. Researchers profiled parasites from four cryptic P. falciparum cases, identifying drug-resistance markers and predicting origins, and analysed family-related clusters of P. ovale spp. and P. falciparum using identity-by-descent and multiplicity-of-infection methods to confirm (sub)species and resolve transmission clusters. The authors conclude that real-time WGS and AI-enhanced tools enable high-resolution genomic surveillance supporting timely public health responses and sustaining malaria elimination in non-endemic settings.

articles.headline: Reply on cluster-randomized trial of ivermectin for malaria control
articles.title_original: Ivermectin to Control Malaria - A Cluster-Randomized Trial. Reply.
articles.authors: Chaccour C, Rudd M
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Cluster-randomized trial of ivermectin to control malaria
articles.title_original: Ivermectin to Control Malaria - A Cluster-Randomized Trial.
articles.authors: Last A, Hayes R, Bradley J
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Reply on permethrin-treated baby wraps preventing malaria in children
articles.title_original: Permethrin-Treated Baby Wraps for the Prevention of Malaria in Children. Reply.
articles.authors: Boyce RM, Shook-Sa BE, Mulogo EM
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Permethrin-treated baby wraps to prevent malaria in children
articles.title_original: Permethrin-Treated Baby Wraps for the Prevention of Malaria in Children.
articles.authors: Javelle E, de Santi VP, Briolant S
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: CRISPR study probes CLAG3 helix role in malaria nutrient channel
articles.title_original: A CRISPR-Based Mutagenesis Strategy for Examining CLAG3 Helix 44 Contribution to Malaria Parasite Nutrient Uptake Channels.
articles.authors: Gonzalez-Chavez Z, Siddiqui MA, Ganesan S, Desai SA
articles.details: Laboratory study using a two-step CRISPR transfection strategy in malaria parasites to test whether the CLAG3 amphipathic α-helix 44 (α-H44) lines the plasmodial surface anion channel (PSAC), the CLAG3/RhopH2/RhopH3 ternary complex that imports nutrients into infected erythrocytes. A CLAG3 truncation lacking α-H44 phenocopied a CLAG3 knockout, while restoration with a recodonized α-H44 fully restored PSAC activity. A saturation mutagenesis library split the helix into four segments; two engineered mutants showed distinct PSAC phenotypes and failed to expand in medium approximating in vivo nutrient availability. Authors conclude α-H44 contributes to channel permeation and strain-specific inhibitor block, supporting PSAC as an antimalarial drug target.

articles.headline: Deep-learning ensemble detects malaria in blood smears at 96% accuracy
articles.title_original: CT-Malaria Detection via Adaptive-Weighted Deep Learning Models.
articles.authors: Gasmi K, Krichen M, Alanazi A, Almenwer S, Almaghrabi S, Yahyaoui S
articles.details: Methods paper proposing a two-track image-analysis pipeline for malaria diagnosis from thin blood smears, combining classical machine learning (real-time augmentation, convolution-based feature extraction, calibrated classifiers) with end-to-end convolutional networks, then forming paired ensembles using a hybrid, data-driven method to select convex combining weights that maximize validation accuracy. Optimized weighted ensembling reduced false negatives for subtle parasites and false positives from staining artefacts. The approach achieved 96.35% accuracy on held-out data with reduced cross-fold variance. Authors conclude that integrating augmentation, multiple modeling tracks, and optimal pairwise ensembling yields the highest accuracy and supports future multi-class extensions and operating-point calibration.

articles.headline: P. vivax clinical forms drive distinct CD4+ T cell responses
articles.title_original: Different clinical forms of Plasmodium vivax infection result in unique T cell epitope-specific immune responses.
articles.authors: Costa CM, Almeida GG, Lindestam Arlehamn CS, Queiroz-Glauss CP, Gunderman VHG, Pereira MMM, Amaral LRD, Pereira DB, Tada MS, Martins-Filho OA, Gazzinelli RT, Antonelli LRDV
articles.details: Immunology study developing a 310-peptide Plasmodium vivax library (MPv310) to assess antigen-specific CD4+ T cell responses and memory subsets across symptomatic-before-treatment (SY-I), symptomatic-after-treatment (SY-T), asymptomatic (ASY), and uninfected individuals, measuring IFN-γ, TNF, CD69, and CD154 by flow cytometry and ELISpot. MPv310 stimulation increased CD154+ and CD69+CD154+ cells in symptomatic patients and IFN-γ+TNF+ cells in SY-T patients; ASY status was associated with higher frequencies of IFN-γ+TNF+ CD4+ cells and IFN-γ+ effector memory cells. Dividing the pool into antigen minipools revealed distinct activation patterns between symptomatic and ASY individuals. Authors conclude MPv310 is a potential tool to study immune mechanisms across clinical presentations and protection.

articles.headline: Narrative review of congenital and neonatal malaria in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Congenital and Neonatal Malaria in Ethiopia: A Narrative Review.
articles.authors: Leake Y, Hagos HH, Reta BK
articles.details: Narrative review (PRISMA-guided screening of PubMed, Google Scholar, Medline, and grey literature) synthesizing evidence on congenital and neonatal malaria in Ethiopia, covering epidemiology, clinical presentation, diagnosis, management, and prevention. Ten Ethiopian studies reported a total of 46 cases; Plasmodium falciparum predominated (35 cases), followed by Plasmodium vivax (8 cases) and mixed infections (3 cases). Forty cases were classified as congenital and six as neonatal, with most reports being case reports or case series. Authors conclude these conditions are under-recognized despite Ethiopia's high burden and call for greater clinical awareness, improved diagnostic vigilance, and larger epidemiological studies.

articles.headline: G6PD deficiency variants linked to lower P. knowlesi infection risk
articles.title_original: G6PD variants but not Filipino beta-thalassemia are associated with reduced risk of Plasmodium knowlesi infection in Sabah, Malaysia.
articles.authors: Nuin NA, Rajahram GS, Barber BE, Anstey NM, Grigg MJ, Chua TH
articles.details: Retrospective case-control study comparing 106 PCR-confirmed Plasmodium knowlesi cases with 89 controls from Sabah, Malaysia, genotyping eight major Southeast Asian G6PD variants (multiplex PCR) and Filipino β-thalassemia deletions (gap-PCR), with logistic regression adjusting for age, sex, ethnicity, and district. G6PD deficiency variants were present in 4/106 (3.8%, 95%CI 1.0-9.4%) cases versus 11/89 (12.4%, 95%CI 6.3-21.0%) controls (OR=0.28, 95%CI 0.09-0.91; p=0.034), with the Viangchan variant (871G>A) predominant (71%). No association was found with severe malaria or sex, and Filipino β-thalassemia showed no association. Authors conclude G6PD deficiency variants confer substantial protection against P. knowlesi susceptibility while β-thalassemia appears neutral.

articles.headline: E3 ubiquitin ligase complex controls P. falciparum sexual development
articles.title_original: GID/CTLH E3 ligase complex control cell fate programs for sexual development of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Marapana DS, Lopaticki S, Balan B, Reaksudsan K, Cobbold SA, Geoghegan ND, Khurana S, Scally SW, Hickey P, Jayakrishnan N, Vaibhav V, Spall S, Yousef JM, Dagley LF, Goodman CD, Cozijnsen A, McFadden GI, Rogers KL, Jex A, Komander D, McCarthy JS, Tonkin CJ, Dixon MWA, Cowman AF
articles.details: This molecular study shows the PfGID E3 ubiquitin ligase complex regulates critical gametocyte cell-fate programs in Plasmodium falciparum through targeted ubiquitination of key proteins. Deleting PfGID complex components arrested gametocyte development and abolished transmission to mosquitoes. PfGID fine-tunes the protein levels of two substrates: the ZFP36-family RNA-binding protein GD1, which holds developmental transcripts in translational repression, and the cryptochrome-like protein PfDPL, which regulates male-specific proteins essential for gametogenesis early in development.
