The authors conclude these findings illuminate the molecular control of Plasmodium sexual development and transmission.

articles.headline: Anopheles funestus drives persistent malaria transmission across Tanzanian districts
articles.title_original: Molecular surveillance of malaria in Tanzania: Baseline surveillance of vectors that drive persistent malaria transmission in regions with varying transmission intensities.
articles.authors: Derua YA, Batengana BM, Francis F, Mandara CI, Madebe RA, Seth MD, Challe DP, Ishengoma DS
articles.details: A cross-sectional entomological survey collected mosquitoes indoors (CDC light traps) and outdoors (Furvela tent traps) over three nights in selected villages across five districts (Buhigwe, Kyerwa, Ludewa, Muheza, Nyasa) from August to October 2023. Of 19,898 mosquitoes, the An. funestus group made up 44.3% and culicines 52.4%; An. funestus accounted for 95.3% of malaria vectors, with An. funestus s.s. predominant (96.3%) and An. arabiensis the main An. gambiae complex species (75.0%). Of 1,799 vectors tested by PCR, 9 (0.5%) were P. falciparum-positive. Estimated annual entomological inoculation rates for An. funestus were 242.6, 66.5, and 13.0 infectious bites/person/year in Kyerwa, Nyasa, and Muheza. The authors conclude An. funestus-targeted interventions are urgently needed.

articles.headline: Integrated malaria services during antenatal care remain very low in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Integrated malaria service delivery and its determinants among pregnant women in Ethiopia: Multi level analysis of 2021/22 National Service Provision Assessment Survey.
articles.authors: Bogale KA, Alemu K, Muchie KF, Asemahagn MA, Engedaw HA, Yenesew MA
articles.details: This secondary analysis of the nationally representative Ethiopia Service Provision Assessment Survey 2021/22 used multilevel logistic regression on 4,273 pregnant women nested across 662 health facilities to assess delivery of integrated malaria services during ANC. Only 7.9% attended ANC visits where all integrated malaria service components were delivered concurrently, with substantial regional disparities. Predictors of integrated service uptake included two previous pregnancies (AOR=1.67, 95% CI 1.06–2.62), three or more ANC visits (AOR=1.58, 1.04–2.40), receiving an insecticide-treated net during ANC (AOR=2.81, 1.29–6.12), malaria-trained providers (AOR=4.24, 1.80–10.00), and rural facility attendance (AOR=2.73, 1.04–7.19). The authors conclude delivery is unacceptably low and call for strengthened provider capacity and ITN policy.

articles.headline: Chromatin and epigenetic control of var2csa and placental binding in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Epigenetics and chromatin structure regulate var2csa expression and the placental-binding phenotype in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Lenz T, Sirel M, Hoppe H, Shafeeq S, Le Roch KG, Ribacke U
articles.details: Using phenotypic and systems-biology assays, this study examined how Plasmodium falciparum activates var2csa, the gene encoding the placental-binding adhesin VAR2CSA responsible for cytoadherence in pregnancy malaria. The authors established phenotypically homogeneous VAR2CSA-expressing, placenta-binding parasites that exclusively transcribed var2csa while other var genes stayed silenced. Transcriptional activation was strongly associated with depletion of repressive H3K9me3 marks and, via chromatin conformation capture, with a small but significant repositioning of var2csa relative to heterochromatic telomeric clusters. 5-methylcytosine occupancy was present in all var genes but independent of transcriptional repression and switching. The findings clarify epigenetic and chromatin-structure mechanisms regulating antigenic variation.

articles.headline: Plasmodium vivax malaria
articles.title_original: Plasmodium vivax Malaria.
articles.authors: Menkin-Smith L, Koval K, Samandari T
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Historical account: first description of placental malaria pigment in 19th-century Scotland
articles.title_original: Placental malaria in nineteenth-century Scotland.
articles.authors: Brabin BJ
articles.details: This historical review credits Scottish obstetrician James Young Simpson (1811-1870) with very probably the first description of malaria pigmentation, observed in an archived placental sample taken at 4 months gestation from an abortive pregnancy or maternal death. Black tissue pigmentation had been described before but not in the placenta. Simpson presented these observations in 1835 at his inaugural lecture as President of the Royal Edinburgh Medical Society, in what is described as the first academic review of placental pathology. The paper sets this in the context of malaria in early 19th-century Scotland and Simpson's pioneering insight into placental inflammation and infection.

articles.headline: Legacy chloroquine-resistance backgrounds shape drug susceptibility in Gambian P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Chloroquine resistance transporter drives divergent multi-locus drug resistance genetic backgrounds and susceptibility in Gambian Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Drammeh NF, Bojang F, Nganyewo NN, Jawara AS, Manneh B, Correa S, Amambua-Ngwa BN, Mbye H, Oriero EC, Sutherland CJ, D'Alessandro U, Amambua-Ngwa A
articles.details: This study genotyped pfcrt, pfmdr1, pfdhfr, pfdhps, and pfkelch13 by amplicon sequencing of 364 Plasmodium falciparum isolates from The Gambia and measured in vitro IC50s for 68 isolates against chloroquine, dihydroartemisinin, lumefantrine, mefloquine, piperaquine, and amodiaquine. Two pfcrt-driven backgrounds dominated: CVIET (62%) was chloroquine-resistant, while CVMNK was less sensitive to mefloquine and dihydroartemisinin. The pfmdr1 NFD haplotype was 60% prevalent (184F at 67%), and CVIET-NFD isolates (31.7%) had the highest chloroquine IC50s; pfdhfr S108N (98%) and pfdhps A437G (85%) were highly prevalent, with no validated pfkelch13 mutations. The authors conclude ACTs remain effective, but reversion to chloroquine sensitivity tends to result in reduced sensitivity to ACT drugs, warranting continued monitoring.

articles.headline: Acridone candidate T111 active against blood, liver, and mosquito malaria stages
articles.title_original: Potent acridone antimalarial against all three life stages of Plasmodium.
articles.authors: Kancharla P, Dodean RA, Li Y, Zhang X, Kelly S, Charlton J, Binauhan AJ, Navarrete K, Garcia L, Mu J, Lu J, Caridha D, Madejczyk MS, Jin X, Dennis WE, Kudyba K, McEnearney S, Lee PJ, Blount C, DeLuca J, Vuong C, Pannone K, Dinh HT, Mdaki K, Leed S, Martin ML, Pybus BS, Chin GC, Chim-Ong A, Cui L, Qahash T, Shoue DA, Ferdig MT, Mockenhaupt FP, van Loon W, Ndoli JM, Kudyba H, Vega-Rodriguez J, Okitwi M, Kreutzfeld O, Docan C, Green C, Liu Y, Agulay A, Mirsalis J, Farrell SN, Probst AS, Nilsen A, Alday PH, Stone Doggett J, McFadden GI, Goodman CD, Catteruccia F, Rosenthal PJ, Riscoe MK, Cooper RA, Roth A, Kelly JX
articles.details: This preclinical study presents T111, an acridone antimalarial chemotype potent against blood, liver, and mosquito stages of Plasmodium. The lead candidate showed potent in vitro activity against cultured parasites, ex vivo activity against clinical isolates, oral single-dose cure in an asexual blood-stage rodent model, inhibition of sexual blood-stage parasites, activity against relapsing parasites in non-human primate liver cells, prevention of parasite development in mosquitoes, and synergy with tafenoquine against blood- and liver-stage parasites. Resistance selection suggests inhibition of the mitochondrial electron transport chain via a mechanism distinct from existing antimalarials. Toxicology in rats showed no hemolytic toxicity and low genotoxicity and cardiotoxicity, indicating a favorable therapeutic index. The authors conclude T111 is a promising candidate for treatment, radical cure, and transmission blocking.

articles.headline: Review of biomarkers and point-of-care biosensors for malaria diagnosis
articles.title_original: Potential biomarkers and their recent applications ofr point-of-care biosensors for the diagnosis of malaria.
articles.authors: Karadurmus L, Cetinkaya A, Kocabay P, Jaballah MB, Baldrich E, Ozkan SA
articles.details: This review surveys malaria biomarkers and recent (2022–2025) advances in biosensor engineering, nanomaterials, and microfluidic automation for point-of-care diagnostics. It provides a comparative, mechanistic evaluation of electrochemical, optical, and piezoelectric biosensors, emphasizing primary research and focusing on signal transduction mechanisms, analytical trade-offs, microfluidic integration, and real-world deployability. The authors highlight challenges including clinically atypical malaria, asymptomatic carriers, climate change, limited healthcare infrastructure, new vector species, and rising drug and insecticide resistance, and argue that converting complex biosensor techniques into affordable, infrastructure-free POC tests, ultimately enabling sensitive quantification of biomarker panels, is key to future malaria detection and control. No quantitative diagnostic performance figures are reported.

articles.headline: Multiplex isothermal urine assay detects P. falciparum and T. vaginalis
articles.title_original: Multiplexed Isothermal Amplification Assay for the Detection of Plasmodium falciparum and Trichomonas vaginalis in Urine.
articles.authors: Dupaty J, Shiluli C, Schaus B, Lolabattu SR, Gitaka J, Klapperich C
articles.details: This study presents a multiplexed isothermal nucleic acid amplification assay (iso-IMRS) for simultaneous detection of Plasmodium falciparum and Trichomonas vaginalis, building on prior iso-IMRS (P. falciparum) and PCR-IMRS (T. vaginalis) work with a workflow adapted for near point-of-care use. Motivated by diagnostic challenges of malaria in pregnancy due to placental sequestration and by adverse outcomes from co-infection, the assay was tested in simulated and human urine. It detected both P. falciparum and T. vaginalis at 100 copies/μL in processed human urine, indicating clinically relevant sensitivity for noninvasive multiplexed detection. The authors note that neat urine requires a processing step that may limit POC use, but at their clinics in Kenya samples are processed nearby, and a fast thermocycler-free test fitting within a prenatal visit would benefit patients.

articles.headline: Integrated serosurvey tracks NTDs, malaria, and vaccine immunity in urban Guyana
articles.title_original: Hotspot-based integrated serosurveillance of neglected tropical diseases, malaria, and vaccine-preventable diseases in urban Guyana.
articles.authors: Niles-Robin RA, Sampson A, Valz O, Scott O, Dickson KE, Escalada R, Nicholson D, Woolford J, Ade MP, Carvalho Scholte R, Luciañez A, Bravo P, Rey-Benito G, Ibarra-Ozcariz S, Morice A, Saboyá-Díaz MI
articles.details: This hotspot-based integrated serosurveillance study used a multiplex bead assay on dried blood spots from 300 conveniently sampled individuals aged ≥6 years in each of four coastal regions (III, IV, V, X), testing antigens from 12 pathogens including lymphatic filariasis, malaria, and vaccine-preventable diseases. Reactivity to Wb123 and Bm14 matched prior elevated LF transmission. Seropositivity to P. falciparum (pfMSP1-19) and P. vivax (pvMSP1-19) was higher among adults. Measles and rubella seroprotection was highest in children and older adults with gaps in young adults; tetanus and diphtheria protection was minimal. Authors conclude the approach efficiently monitors multiple disease groups.

articles.headline: Protocol to develop a core outcome set for severe malaria trials
articles.title_original: Synthesis and Standardization of Outcomes in Severe Malaria Treatment Trials: Protocol for the Development of a Core Outcome Set (the COSSMaT Study).
articles.authors: Asamoah GD, Love SB, Gibb DM, Maitland K, George EC
articles.details: This is a protocol to develop the first core outcome set (COS) for severe malaria treatment trials, following COMET and COS-STAP guidance. It combines an updated systematic review of RCT outcomes (CENTRAL, MEDLINE, LILACS, and trial registries), qualitative interviews with parents and caregivers, a two-round multistakeholder Delphi survey (target 60–80 participants), and a consensus meeting. Preliminary results identified 326 records, of which 5 eligible RCTs conducted between 2020 and 2024 in Africa and Asia were included, and 26 parents and caregivers were interviewed. Remaining steps are expected to be completed by September 2026.

articles.headline: IRS and seasonal chemoprevention reduce minor Plasmodium species in northern Ghana
articles.title_original: The dynamics of asymptomatic Plasmodium spp. infections following 10 years of malaria control interventions in Northern Sahelian Ghana.
articles.authors: Rios-Teran CA, Tiedje KE, Bangre O, Deed SL, Argyropoulos DC, Koram KA, Oduro AR, Ansah PO, Day KP
articles.details: This observational, interrupted time-series study assessed how sequential indoor residual spraying (IRS) and seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) combined with bed nets affected non-falciparum species in Bongo District, Ghana, across five age-stratified surveys (~2,000 participants each) from 2012 to 2022, using species-specific 18S rRNA PCR. P. vivax was never detected. After IRS, prevalence fell for P. malariae (13.7% to 1.4%) and P. ovale spp. (5.7% to 0.4%). By 2017, 32 months after IRS stopped and SMC began, both rebounded (P. malariae 2.9%, P. ovale 4.0%) except in under-5s targeted by SMC. By 2020–2022, P. malariae rose further (7.4%, 5.8%) while P. ovale declined (2.6%, 1.3%). Both interventions reduced minor species.

articles.headline: PfRESA3 anchors to erythrocyte cytoskeleton and acts as co-chaperone
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum ring-infected erythrocyte surface antigen 3 (PfRESA3) is a cytoskeleton-interacting protein and potential co-chaperone.
articles.authors: Mallick NR, Sahu W, Ojha DK, Mund S, Das A, Pati AK, Elangovan S, Reddy KS
articles.details: Using recombinant protein constructs and antibodies, this laboratory study characterized Plasmodium falciparum ring-infected erythrocyte surface antigen-3 (PfRESA3) as a dense granule merozoite protein exported early to the inner erythrocyte membrane, where it associates with the cytoskeleton. Constructs lacking the J domain bound inside-out vesicles, implicating PRESAN and DnaJ-X domains in cytoskeletal interactions. Recombinant PfRESA3 stimulated the ATPase activity of human HsHSPA8 and enhanced malate dehydrogenase refolding, whereas truncated constructs did not. The authors conclude PfRESA3 modulates host HsHSPA8 to support cytoskeletal remodeling and parasite protein folding early in infection, exposing a potential vulnerability.

articles.headline: Review of peripheral immune responses to blood-stage malaria infection
articles.title_original: Cellular and molecular interactions underlying the peripheral immune responses to blood-stage malaria infections.
articles.authors: Tebben K, Asawa R, Serre D, Lyke KE
articles.details: This is an up-to-date review of human immune responses to blood-stage Plasmodium (malaria) infection and the parasite's immune evasion mechanisms during this part of the life cycle. It notes that Plasmodium parasites have coevolved in primates and developed numerous mechanisms to escape human immune defenses. No quantitative study results are reported.

articles.headline: Royal Thai Army a key malaria reservoir in Thai border province
articles.title_original: Combatting malaria together: key findings from a rapid formative assessment on Royal Thai Army malaria practices and military-civilian collaboration in Sisaket Province, Thailand.
articles.authors: Dantzer E, Tabprasit S, Suwannarong K, Jongsakul K, Kana K, Dharmarak P, Feldman M, Sudathip P, Chaiwan J, Yokani W, Kramyoo M, Thammasutti K, Cotter C
articles.details: A mixed-methods rapid formative assessment in Sisaket Province, Thailand combined a 2016-2020 retrospective analysis of malaria case data with 16 focus group discussions and 17 key informant interviews (116 respondents: 72 Royal Thai Army, 44 Ministry of Public Health). 432 military cases occurred over the period, 18% of the province's 2425 cases (ranging 14-30% annually); all were male with median age 28, 96% were diagnosed and treated at MoPH facilities, 41% classified indigenous and 40% imported. Authors conclude formalizing joint procedures and strengthening military-civilian coordination is crucial for malaria elimination.

articles.headline: Rice-grown Beauveria bassiana fungus kills Anopheles stephensi in bioassays
articles.title_original: Effect of solid substrates on the production and germination of Beauveria bassiana (Balsamo) Vuillemin, and their infectivity against the malaria vector, Anopheles stephensi (Diptera: Culicidae).
articles.authors: Siddaramegowda R, Chalageri VH, Eapen A
articles.details: This laboratory study evaluated eleven solid substrates for producing conidia of the entomopathogenic fungus Beauveria bassiana strain PDBC-Bb5a and tested infectivity against Anopheles stephensi using WHO cone bioassays (1×10^7 conidia/mL, 30-min exposure, four replicates of 20 mosquitoes). White rice yielded the most conidia (6.8×10^5 CFU/g), followed by sorghum and broken wheat (p<0.05). Conidia from white rice caused 90% and 86.25% mortality on cement and mud panels (median survival 5 and 7 days), versus 51.25% and 47.50% for barley. Authors conclude white rice is a superior substrate, pending multicentric field testing.

articles.headline: Suboptimal IPTp-SP coverage for malaria in pregnancy, southern Madagascar
articles.title_original: Prevalence of malaria during pregnancy and coverage of intermittent preventive treatment with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine in urban Amboasary-Sud, Southern Madagascar.
articles.authors: Zafindraibe NJ, Razafindrakoto AC, Zemey CB, Rakotozandrindrainy R, Rasamindrakotroka A, Razanakolona LRS
articles.details: A retrospective descriptive study used routine antenatal care data from two public health facilities in Amboasary-Sud, southern Madagascar in 2021, including 1262 pregnant women, to assess IPTp-SP coverage and malaria prevalence. 64.9% (819/1262) received at least one dose of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine but only 49.9% (409/819) completed three or more doses; RDT positivity among 289 symptomatic women was 21.1% (61/289), giving 4.8% confirmed symptomatic malaria among all attendees. All maternal (7) and fetal (3) complications occurred among untreated women. Living within 5 km of a facility (aOR 1.54) and attending ≥4 ANC visits (aOR 2.38) predicted adequate coverage.

articles.headline: Seasonal shifts in Anopheles funestus larval habitats inform source management
articles.title_original: Seasonal variation in aquatic habitat availability and use by the malaria vector, Anopheles funestus.
articles.authors: Kahamba NF, Kifungo K, Jumanne M, Trujillano F, Finda MF, Msugupakulya B, Baldini F, Nelli L, Okumu FO, Ferguson HM
articles.details: Cross-sectional surveys across five villages in south-eastern Tanzania during the dry season (September-November 2021) and rainy season (February-May 2022) mapped and characterized aquatic habitats of Anopheles funestus. Of 2824 habitats identified, 27% were positive for An. funestus. Larval occurrence and density were significantly influenced by habitat type, village, season and their interactions, plus physicochemical factors including water depth, vegetation, algae, clarity, and water source. The vector occupied river streams, ground pools and ditches year-round but expanded into spring-fed wells, rice fields and dug pits during the wet season. Authors suggest targeting persistent habitats in the dry season for more efficient larval source management.

articles.headline: Cabamiquine active against P. ovale and P. malariae field isolates
articles.title_original: Antimalarial cabamiquine inhibits blood-stage of different field isolates of Plasmodium species.
articles.authors: Dembele L, Aniweh Y, Soulama A, Sogore F, Diakité O, Diallo F, Dao F, Amenga-Etego LN, Demarta-Gatsi C, Spangenberg T, Djimde AA
articles.details: An ex-vivo study in Ghana and Mali assessed susceptibility of non-falciparum field isolates of P. ovale and P. malariae to the protein-synthesis inhibitor cabamiquine, compared with P. falciparum, and explored cross-resistance with existing antimalarials. P. ovale showed decreased dihydroartemisinin susceptibility (median IC50 3.962 nM) versus P. falciparum (p=0.0371) but no significant difference for cabamiquine (median IC50 0.9441 nM). P. malariae was less susceptible to cabamiquine (median IC50 3.915 nM) than P. falciparum (p=0.001). Cabamiquine-resistant P. falciparum strains showed limited variation in susceptibility to a panel of existing antimalarials. Authors conclude both species are susceptible to cabamiquine.

articles.headline: Malaria incidence and risk factors with a fractional mathematical model
articles.title_original: Incidence and risk factors of malaria with hematological and immunological evaluation and integrable analysis of a fractional mathematical model.
articles.authors: Murad MA, Murad SA, Abdeljawad T, Alqudah MA
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Sex-distorter gene drive biases Anopheles gambiae offspring toward males
articles.title_original: Sex distorter male drive for resistance-resilient population control of the human malaria vector Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Grilli S, Vertsimakha O, Marston L, Gonzalez IA, Burt A, Crisanti A, Bernardini F
articles.details: Laboratory study in the malaria vector Anopheles gambiae characterizing two germline-specific promoters, spo11 and vasa1, with distinct temporal and spatial expression suited to CRISPR-based gene drives. The authors built a Sex Distorter Male Drive (SDMD) that produces highly male-biased progeny and spreads via super-Mendelian inheritance, simplifying earlier construct designs. They conclude SDMD is a promising, potentially resistance-resilient tool for suppressing Anopheles populations in malaria-endemic regions; no efficiency percentages are reported in the abstract.

articles.headline: Severe malaria referral patterns in adults at an Ethiopian hospital
articles.title_original: "Referral patterns and associated factors among adult patients admitted with Severe Malaria at Shedeho Meket Primary Hospital, Northeast Ethiopia".
articles.authors: Addis GT, Addis MT, Mihret Y, Addis ET, Tadesse TY
articles.details: Among 165 adults admitted with severe malaria at Shedeho Meket Primary Hospital, Northeast Ethiopia (April 2023–April 2025), 151 (91.5%) recovered with no deaths, while 23 (13.94%) were referred to other facilities. The cohort was 94 (57.0%) male and 71 (43.0%) female, with a mean age of 34.44 years. Referral was significantly associated with hyperparasitemia (AOR=2.083), severe malarial anemia (AOR=3.052), and significant bleeding (AOR=8.012). The authors stress early detection and intervention even in lower-prevalence areas.

articles.headline: Editorial on malaria protective immunity from infection and vaccination
articles.title_original: Editorial: Dissecting malaria protective immunity: acquired by natural infection and/or vaccination.
articles.authors: Kanoi BN, Nguitragool W, Palacpac NMQ, Tsuboi T
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Plasmodium sporozoites are retained and survive in the spleen
articles.title_original: Retention and survival of Plasmodium sporozoites in the spleen.
articles.authors: Celli S, Thiberge JM, Formaglio P, Martins RM, Snounou G, Amino R
articles.details: Experimental study showing that intravenously inoculated P. yoelii and P. berghei sporozoites are retained in the spleen on their journey toward the liver. In situ imaging of GFP-expressing parasites revealed live sporozoites persisting in the splenic parenchyma for at least three hours after inoculation. The authors conclude the spleen may play a role beyond simply trapping and destroying sporozoites en route from skin to liver.

articles.headline: Plasmodium infection drives lung injury via dendritic cell autophagy pathway
articles.title_original: Plasmodium yoelii infection induces lung injury by modulating type 2 conventional dendritic cells autophagy via the STAT3-IRF4 signaling.
articles.authors: Hong C, Deng G, Jiang Z, Lu Y, Wu Q, Huang C, Tang J, Zhou H, Liu Q, Luo X, Zhao Y, Qi Y, Li Q, Ma X, Yang Q
articles.details: This mouse study examined how lung type 2 conventional dendritic cells (cDC2) contribute to lung injury during Plasmodium infection. Infected mice showed significant cDC2 accumulation and activation in the lungs, with reduced phagocytosis but enhanced promotion of CD4+ T cell differentiation toward Th1 cells, worsening lung injury. Mechanistically, the effect of Plasmodium on cDC2 was mediated by the JAK/STAT3 signaling pathway, and the JAK inhibitor JSI-124 (blocking STAT3 phosphorylation) almost completely abolished it; the role of cDC2 was further substantiated in IRF4-deficient mice. The authors conclude this clarifies the pathogenic mechanisms of Plasmodium infection and offers a basis for malaria prevention and control.

articles.headline: Malaria expands cytotoxic Vδ2+ T cells in human lymphoid organoids
articles.title_original: Cytotoxic Vδ2+ T cell subsets expand in response to malaria in human tonsil and spleen organoids.
articles.authors: Press KD, Bach F, Sola E, Camanag K, Dooley NL, Ivanawati A, Oyong D, Nalubega M, Kakuru A, Matsiko S, Nankya F, Musinguzi K, Nalwoga A, Nansubuga E, Ategeka J, Ebusu C, Odongo B, Kashiwagi C, Ji X, Miranda M, Tachiwa-Appiah J, Sandhu K, Kamalyan L, van der Ploeg K, Boyle M, Wagar LE, Davis MM, Jagannathan P
articles.details: Using newly developed human tonsil and spleen organoids from malaria-naïve donors, researchers exposed tissues to Plasmodium falciparum-infected red blood cells (iRBC) to study lymphoid immune responses. iRBC exposure did not disrupt organoid formation and significantly increased Vδ2+ γδ T cell frequencies in both tonsil and spleen organoids; single-cell RNA/TCR sequencing showed these cells were clonally expanded with activated, cytotoxic phenotypes (upregulated granzymes, interferon-stimulated genes, antigen presentation machinery). Findings were validated in tonsils from Ugandan children with asymptomatic parasitemia, showing expanded cytotoxic Vδ2+ T cells. Malaria pre-exposure or γδ T cell depletion did not significantly alter influenza vaccine recall responses in most donors.

articles.headline: Arginine methyltransferase inhibitor kills P. falciparum across multiple stages
articles.title_original: Protein Arginine Methyltransferase Inhibitors Target Multiple Stages of Plasmodium falciparum Parasites In Vitro.
articles.authors: Opperman D, Rabie T, Maré M, Naude M, van der Watt M, Thibaud JL, Shannon M, Sanders N, Bolscher JM, van der Laak R, Willemsen R, Bronkhorst A, Boonyalai N, Lee MCS, Birkholtz LM
articles.details: This study used a chemical repositioning strategy to test known protein arginine methyltransferase (PRMT) inhibitors against Plasmodium falciparum, which possesses three PRMTs maintaining a unique histone arginine methylation landscape. The authors identified a potent compound, TC-E 5003, active across multiple stages of parasite development, and proposed PfPRMT1 as its most likely target, with a clear structure-activity relationship demonstrated by analogs from a hit expansion campaign. They conclude this opens a new pathway for identifying multistage-active antiplasmodial candidates targeting a novel protein family in P. falciparum.

articles.headline: P. malariae and P. ovale misdiagnosed as P. vivax in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: The hidden malaria: Misidentification of Plasmodium malariae and Plasmodium ovale in low-transmission setting targeted for elimination in Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Getachew H, Wang C, Habtamu K, Demissew A, Degefa T, Birhanie SK, Kazura JW, King CL, Yan G, Yewhalaw D
articles.details: This cross-sectional study assessed Plasmodium malariae (Pm) and P. ovale (Po) infections misdiagnosed as P. vivax (Pv) at health facilities in southwestern Ethiopia. Of 3590 febrile patients, 323 were Pv-positive by microscopy or RDT; 245 were re-evaluated by qPCR with species-specific primers. Among the 245, PCR confirmed 215 (87.7%) Pv, 9 (3.7%) P. falciparum, 8 (3.3%) Pm, 6 (2.4%) Po, and 7 (2.8%) mixed Pf/Pv infections; no Pm or Po were detected by RDT or microscopy, and 8.6% (14/162) of subsamples were RDT-negative. The authors call for integrating Pm and Po into Ethiopia's control program and strengthening diagnostics.

articles.headline: AL and ASAQ remain effective for child malaria in Sierra Leone
articles.title_original: Efficacy and safety of artemether-lumefantrine (AL) and artesunate-amodiaquine (ASAQ) for the treatment of uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria among children 6-59 months in three sentinel sites of Sierra Leone, 2021-2022.
articles.authors: Cavros I, Abanyie F, Kamara ARY, Chalobah F, Fofana N, Ahmed J, Larbi K, Kennedy C, Horton B, Bajic M, Richardson M, Matlock B, McCaffery J, Kanu S, Schnabel D, Labor TSA, Cash S, Plucinski M, Komeh JP, Sillah-Kanu M
articles.details: A 28-day WHO-protocol in vivo efficacy study found that artemether-lumefantrine (AL) and artesunate-amodiaquine (ASAQ) remain efficacious against uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria in Sierra Leonean children aged 6 months to 5 years. AL was evaluated in three sentinel sites (Bo, Bombali, and Kenema) and ASAQ in two (Bo and Bombali); of 1777 children screened, 496 reached a study endpoint. PCR-uncorrected efficacy ranged from 89.5% to 96.1% for AL and from 92.0% to 94.5% for ASAQ, while PCR-corrected efficacy was 100% in all AL arms and ASAQ Bo, and 99.1% for ASAQ Bombali. New infections accounted for 72.7% (16/22) of AL and 63.6% (7/11) of ASAQ recurrences, and no validated Pfk13 resistance mutations were detected.

articles.headline: Malaria microscopy quality varies widely across rural Kenyan clinics
articles.title_original: Malaria microscopy evaluation and quality assurance in rural clinics of Rarieda and Alego Usonga sub-counties of Siaya County, western Kenya.
articles.authors: Schultz JS, Towett O, Obiet K, Seda B, Otieno K, Omondi D, Odongo W, Seffren V, Wafula P, Oreri E, McDermott DP, Staedke SG, Kwambai TK, Kariuki S, Gutman JR
articles.details: A quality-assurance evaluation of malaria blood-smear microscopy in 29 health facilities in Siaya County, western Kenya (January–July 2024) compared routine on-site readings against expert microscopy at KEMRI as the gold standard. Of 1,494 smears examined, 501 (34%) were positive. Concordance between routine and expert re-reading was 91% (1,289/1,414), ranging 55%–100% by facility; agreement between the routine slide and the gold-standard slide was 86% (1,276/1,485). Routine results had 76% sensitivity and 92% specificity, yielding 24% undertreatment and 8% overtreatment. Accurate diagnosis was less likely at 1–100 p/μL (OR=0.12; 95% CI 0.05–0.29; p<0.001) and more likely above 10,000 p/μL (OR=6.08; 95% CI 2.22–25.1; p=0.002). Six facilities (21%) produced poor-quality smears, underscoring the need for ongoing quality assurance.

articles.headline: Wealth, education and residence drive delayed child malaria treatment
articles.title_original: Determinants of late malaria treatment among under-five children in West Africa.
articles.authors: Shittu SB, Shittu RO, Adedini SA
articles.details: A cross-sectional analysis of Malaria Indicator Survey data examined the prevalence and determinants of delayed malaria treatment for children under five in Mali, Niger, Nigeria, and Senegal, using descriptive statistics, chi-square tests, and binary logistic regression. Treatment delay was highest in Mali. In Senegal, child sex (AOR=0.41; CI 0.25–0.66), maternal wealth index (AOR=0.037; CI 0.3–0.96), and place of residence (AOR=1.82; CI 1.04–3.16) were associated with delay; in Nigeria, maternal wealth (AOR=0.54; CI 0.3–0.96) and education (AOR=0.49; CI 0.29–0.83); in Niger, place of residence (AOR=1.24; CI 0.54–2.87); and in Mali, mother's age (AOR=0.6; CI 0.38–0.95). The authors conclude targeted interventions could reduce malaria burden in these countries.

articles.headline: ESCRT ATPase PfVPS4 essential for P. falciparum growth and transmission
articles.title_original: PfVPS4, an ESCRT AAA-ATPase, is essential for asexual proliferation and gametocyte sexual conversion in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Yang W, Wu J, Zhou Y, Yu Z, Shang X, Huang J
articles.details: Using CRISPR-Cas9 conditional knockdown in Plasmodium falciparum 3D7 and NF54 strains, researchers found that PfVPS4, the core ATPase of the ESCRT membrane-remodeling complex, is essential for asexual blood-stage replication and sexual conversion. Knockdown caused an 84% reduction in asexual progeny, fewer merozoites, and a 46% reduction in gametocyte conversion rate without affecting subsequent gametocyte maturation. Biochemical assays showed ATPase activity optimal at pH 7.5 and 37°C and Mg²⁺-dependent (Vmax 2.23±0.053 U/mg; Km 0.086 mM), and site-directed mutagenesis confirmed catalytic residues D213/E214 and species-specific residues T161/I288 are essential while cofactor PfVta1 enhances activity. The authors conclude PfVPS4 and its complex are attractive antimalarial drug targets.

articles.headline: WWII visual media campaigns promoted soldier anti-malaria compliance
articles.title_original: Attention for Compliance: Visual Media in Second World War Anti-Malaria Efforts.
articles.authors: Marble WS, Shanks GD
articles.details: This historical account describes how malaria casualties harmed the US war effort during the Second World War, with tens of thousands of soldiers falling ill due to inadequate mosquito control, imperfect chemoprophylaxis, and noncompliance. It examines an extensive parallel media campaign—posters and films using humorous education, command authority, links between antimalarial efforts and winning the war, and the promise of faster return home—to persuade soldiers to cooperate. Artists who later became famous, such as Dr. Seuss, contributed mosquito cartoons. The author concludes that medicines, engineering, and media together made important contributions to malaria control in the US military.

articles.headline: Health-system gaps, not awareness, sustain Thai-Myanmar border malaria
articles.title_original: Challenges and responses of malaria elimination in a high-endemic area along the Thai-Myanmar border: A health systems perspective.
articles.authors: Saita S, Parker DM, Suk-Uam K, Phuanukoonnon S, Pooseesod K
articles.details: This mixed-methods study applied the WHO Six Building Blocks framework to assess malaria elimination challenges in Tha Song Yang, a border district in Tak Province, Thailand, using 24 key-informant interviews, a household knowledge/attitudes/practices survey (n=388), and surveillance data on the 1-3-7 strategy from 2018 to 2022. Adherence to Day 1 and Day 3 activities improved to 96% and 100%, but Day 7 adherence fell sharply to 69% in 2022. Community malaria knowledge (75.77%) and positive prevention attitudes (94.59%) were high, while qualitative findings identified short-term migrant surges, workforce shortages, reduced domestic funding, and logistical barriers as key constraints. The authors conclude that persistence is driven by systemic and operational challenges rather than awareness gaps, requiring resilient, sustainably financed health systems.

articles.headline: New mini chamber bioassay tests chlorfenapyr bednets for malaria control
articles.title_original: The mini chamber test: a novel bioassay for bioefficacy testing of chlorfenapyr treated nets used in malaria control.
articles.authors: Lewis A, Jones-Brown R, Harvey G, Praulins G, Reid E, Lugenge AG, Mechan F, Gleave K, Lees RS
articles.details: This laboratory method-development study optimised a novel animal-free, higher-throughput "mini chamber test" for evaluating pyrethroid-chlorfenapyr dual-active-ingredient ITNs and evaluated it against the gold-standard tunnel test using pyrethroid-susceptible and -resistant mosquito strains. Mosquito number per chamber (10/20/30) had no effect on 72-hour mortality (98% vs 99% vs 99%, p=0.46), nor did chamber orientation (horizontal vs vertical: 70% vs 74%, p=0.35), while mortality fell with shorter exposure (99% overnight, 74% at 4h, 35% at 2h). In direct comparison, the mini chamber gave 95% 72-hour mortality versus the tunnel test's 70% with overnight exposure, and a trend between HPLC-measured chemical content and mortality at 6-hour exposure suggests use in durability monitoring. The authors note that internal and multi-site validation is still needed but conclude the method shows promise as a bioassay where tunnel tests are unavailable.

articles.headline: Underdiagnosed P. vivax and rising pvmdr1 amplification in Brazilian Amazon
articles.title_original: Hidden Infections, Emerging Resistance: Underdiagnosed Plasmodium vivax and multidrug resistance-1 (pvmdr1) gene Amplification in the Guiana Shield.
articles.authors: Puça MCSB, Pereira Mascarenhas ME, Huguet QB, Salazar YEAR, Nogueira IR, Louzada J, de Pina-Costa A, de Siqueira AM, de Silva MO, Pereira DB, Ladeia-Andrade S, Ferreira MU, de Oliveira Ferreira J, Gil JP, de Sousa TN
articles.details: This molecular epidemiology study enrolled 727 confirmed malaria cases from three Brazilian states, primarily Roraima in the Guiana Shield, and assessed Plasmodium vivax multidrug resistance-1 (pvmdr1) copy number variation to examine indirect drug exposure in a P. vivax/P. falciparum co-endemic area. Molecular diagnosis revealed frequent misclassification of mixed infections, with P. vivax often missed by microscopy due to higher P. falciparum densities. While nearly all western Amazon isolates carried a single copy, pvmdr1 amplification was detected in 60% of Roraima isolates, rising to over 70% after 2020. The authors conclude that accurate diagnosis and rational antimalarial use are critical, since inadequate or unintended drug exposure may drive selection of reduced-susceptibility parasites.

articles.headline: Nigeria malaria: 38% prevalence; wealth and urban living lower risk
articles.title_original: Risk factors and geospatial associations with Plasmodium falciparum malaria infection and IgG seroprevalence: Nigeria, 2018.
articles.authors: Jaramillo-Underwood A, Ogunniyi A, Zhou H, Uhomoibhi P, Mba N, Ndodo N, Dawurung AB, Abubakar AG, Okoye MI, Iriemenam NC, Maire M, Steinhardt L, Rogier E, Ihekweazu C
articles.details: This study analysed blood samples from a nationwide 2018 HIV household survey in Nigeria, testing for active/recent Plasmodium falciparum infection via HRP2 and LDH antigens and for exposure via IgG against a panel of antigens, with risk factors assessed by logistic regression and geospatial analysis. Current/recent Pf prevalence was 38.3% (95% CI 37.2-39.5%), and seroprevalence across five antigens ranged from 48% to 87%. Children aged 5-14 had the highest odds of active infection versus those under 5; the wealthiest category showed a 5-fold reduction in infection odds and urban living a 30% reduction, while serology showed a 10-fold wealth reduction and 60% urban reduction in exposure. Authors conclude malaria is hyperendemic but heterogeneous, and serology augments risk-factor and spatial analyses.

articles.headline: Imported malaria in Germany: 58 cases over 11 years, mostly P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Malaria: 11-Year Experience With Imported Cases at a German University Hospital and Epidemiological Trends Amid the COVID-19 Pandemic.
articles.authors: Mester P, Kunst C, Schmid S, Zimmermann K, Bülow S, Rennert J, Lampl BMJ, Schilling T, Müller M, Pavel V
articles.details: This retrospective single-center study reviewed 58 patients treated for malaria at University Hospital Regensburg, Bavaria, Germany, between 2012 and 2023, analysed alongside the COVID-19 pandemic context. Median age at diagnosis was 37.5 years and 65% were male; 31.6% required intensive care, with a 98% survival rate. Only 17% had taken malaria prophylaxis, Plasmodium falciparum was the causative agent in 96% of cases, and the primary laboratory abnormality was thrombocytopenia. The authors conclude that imported malaria remains rare in Europe but severe cases still occur, often requiring intensive care, and that delayed diagnosis in nonendemic settings causes preventable deaths.

articles.headline: Climatic extremes and migration reshape malaria risk in South Africa
articles.title_original: Malaria at the margins: climatic extremes, migration, and urban informal settlement risk in South Africa.
articles.authors: Matamanda SH, Musakwa W, Dzomba A
articles.details: This analysis combined climate data (minimum/maximum temperature, rainfall, humidity; 2000-2024) for Limpopo, Mpumalanga and KwaZulu-Natal, laboratory-confirmed malaria cases from provincial surveillance, and in-migration data (2017-2024) into Thembelihle and parts of Soweto, using OLS regressions, anomaly analyses of shock years, and Pearson correlations (p<0.05). Same-year humidity was positively associated with malaria incidence in KwaZulu-Natal and Mpumalanga, whereas lag-1 rainfall showed a significant relationship in Limpopo, reflecting hydrological carry-over. Shock-year analyses (KwaZulu-Natal 2022 floods; Limpopo 2017 Cyclone Dineo) showed compound anomalies extending environmental suitability, and after the April-May 2022 floods migration into Thembelihle rose with temporal alignment between malaria tail years, rainfall, higher minimum temperatures and migration. Authors conclude tail-aware, lag-sensitive, mobility-integrated surveillance is essential for elimination planning.

articles.headline: Avian malaria parasites persist via blood stages; first RNA virus link
articles.title_original: New experimental insights into the biology of avian malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Valkiūnas G, Iezhova TA, Chagas CRF, Tchoumbou M, Himmel T, Petrone ME, Duc M
articles.details: This experimental study transferred three widespread avian Plasmodium species (P. circumflexum pTURDUS1, P. vaughani pSYAT05, P. relictum pCOLL7) from naturally infected wild birds into non-infected Eurasian siskins by inoculation of infected blood, monitoring infections up to 185 days using microscopy, histology, CISH, PCR, and RNA sequencing. All recipient birds were susceptible; no tissue stages were detected, suggesting long-lasting persistence is mainly due to erythrocytic merogony, and pCOLL7 parasitaemia consisted almost exclusively of gametocytes. The authors also report the first association of a Matryoshka RNA virus (MaRNAV-9) with an avian Plasmodium parasite (P. circumflexum).

articles.headline: HIV, TB, malaria investments averted billions of primary-care visits and costs
articles.title_original: The benefits of investments to combat HIV, tuberculosis, and malaria for primary healthcare from 2000 to 2023: An economic modeling analysis.
articles.authors: Su JS, Stover J, Pretorius C, Winskill P, Sweeney S, Hallett TB, Menzies NA
articles.details: This economic modeling analysis applied established mathematical models across 108 low- and middle-income countries to estimate reductions in primary healthcare (PHC) utilization from scaling up HIV, tuberculosis, and malaria (HTM) services over 2000–2023, comparing actual scale-up against a counterfactual holding coverage at 2000 levels. Scale-up averted an estimated 6.9 billion outpatient PHC visits (95% UI 4.4–10.5) and 3.9 billion inpatient bed-days (95% UI 2.5–5.9), representing US$135 billion (95% UI 71–250) in averted costs, greatest in sub-Saharan Africa and East Asia/Pacific. These represented a median 4.4% of hospital bed capacity and 1.6% of government health spending in 2023 (22.9% and 5.1% in low-income countries). The authors conclude these benefits should be considered when assessing investment impact.

articles.headline: AI model classifies mosquito species at 99.42% accuracy for risk mapping
articles.title_original: MosQNet-SA: Explainable convolutional-attention network for mosquito classification with application as a RESTful API for dengue and malaria risk mapping.
articles.authors: Masud MA, Akter S, Sultana N, Islam MS, Abu Yousuf M, Noori FM, Uddin MZ
articles.details: MosQNet-SA, a convolutional-attention network for mosquito classification, reached 99.42% accuracy on a dataset of 1,000 images across three mosquito species (Aedes, Anopheles, and Culex) while using 10-fold fewer parameters than existing approaches. The model combines a spatial attention mechanism and depthwise separable convolutions, with explainability provided via Saliency, GradCAM, LIME, and Kernel SHAP. The authors also implemented a RESTful API for real-time classification and disease risk mapping to support public health surveillance.

articles.headline: Kelch13 mutations confer high-level artemisinin resistance in Bangladeshi P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Functional assessment of prevalent kelch13 mutations reveals high-level artemisinin resistance potential in Bangladeshi Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Nima MK, Bhattacharyya N, Shoue D, Park J, Phru CS, Sazed SA, Padmanabhan PK, Kumar S, Alam MS, Ferdig M, Mukherjee A
articles.details: Using CRISPR-Cas9 to introduce the three most prevalent kelch13 substitutions (C580Y, F446I, R561H) into two recent Chittagong Hill Tracts (CHT) P. falciparum isolates, researchers found that C580Y and R561H markedly increased artemisinin partial resistance in both an artemisinin-sensitive strain and a moderately resistant, K13-independent strain (CHT-R), while F446I had only a modest effect in CHT-R. All three substitutions incurred minimal asexual fitness costs except F446I in CHT-R, and R561H conferred extreme resistance in CHT-R (RSA 30.09% ± 1.9%) with a 5.1%-per-generation growth advantage. Edited lines were phenotyped using ring-stage survival assays (RSAs), post-treatment recovery, and competitive fitness assays. The authors conclude CHT parasites are genetically primed to sustain high-level resistance, warranting enhanced surveillance during Bangladesh's malaria elimination phase.

articles.headline: IFN-γ signaling in T cells drives malaria-associated lung pathology in mice
articles.title_original: Comprehensive immune profiling reveals IFN-γ signaling in T cells mediates parasite phagocytosis in a rodent malaria model.
articles.authors: Chen S-s, Yang Q, Zhong Y, Liu D, Zhou L, Wei H-c, Li C-l, Song J, Wang Y-h, Hu X, Chen L, Lin J-w
articles.details: Using a rodent malaria model with flow cytometry, time-series bulk transcriptomics, and spatial transcriptomics, this study profiled the immune landscape of malaria-associated lung pathology and identified IFN-γ signaling in T cells as a key driver. Disrupting IFN-γ signaling in T cells reduced lung parasite load and attenuated pathology by enhancing T cell–monocyte interaction, which increased a proinflammatory monocyte subset characterized by CD8 and Ly6C expression that showed elevated phagocytic capacity compared with its CD8- counterpart. The authors conclude IFN-γ signaling in T cell–monocyte interaction is important in malaria-associated lung pathology.

articles.headline: New Pfk13 and Pfubp1 variants linked to artemisinin-lumefantrine resistance in Africa
articles.title_original: Novel Pfk13 and Pfubp1 genotypes in African Plasmodium falciparum isolates exhibiting reduced susceptibility to the antimalarials artemisinin and lumefantrine.
articles.authors: Pratt S, van Schalkwyk DA, Stewart L, Hocke EF, Mannan S, Berry C, Muwanguzi-Karugaba J, Nolder D, Rogers C, Chiodini PL, Henrici RC, Moss S, Billows N, Phelan J, Clark TG, Campino S, Sutherland CJ
articles.details: This in vitro phenotype-genotype study examined an extended panel of 50 Plasmodium falciparum isolates collected from UK travelers since 2012 to identify markers of reduced susceptibility to artemisinin and lumefantrine. Parasites with reduced ring-stage artemisinin susceptibility carried new or established pfk13 propeller and non-propeller variants and came from travelers to Kenya, Uganda, Zambia, and Namibia; variant pfap2μ, pfcoronin, or pfubp1 alleles were also found. Among 39 evaluable isolates from 15 countries, six of the ten least susceptible to lumefantrine were from travelers to Uganda. The authors conclude that both pfk13-dependent and -independent partial artemisinin resistance has arisen in multiple African settings, potentially enabling reduced lumefantrine susceptibility.

articles.headline: Baseline P. falciparum csp vaccine-antigen diversity mapped before RTS,S rollout
articles.title_original: Haplotype diversity of Plasmodium falciparum circumsporozoite protein prior to malaria vaccine implementation in Kaelé Health District, Cameroon, 2022-2023.
articles.authors: Ali IM, Vasquez Martinez B, Tchuenkam VPK, Sadler JM, Gorman CC, Nsango SE, Junior VV, Ateba JM, Wanda GY, Bailey JA, Dinglasan RR, Lin JT, Juliano JJ
articles.details: This molecular surveillance study analyzed 100 P. falciparum-positive dried blood spots from Mapoussere, Kaele Health District, Cameroon (2022-2023) using the 4CAST amplicon sequencing assay targeting csp, ama1, sera2, and trap, to establish baseline antigen diversity before RTS,S/AS01 vaccine implementation. csp was genotyped in 35% of samples, yielding 22 haplotypes (He=0.908; π=0.021), with the vaccine-matched haplotype present in 20% of genotyped infections. The TH2 and TH3 T-cell epitopes of csp showed signs consistent with balancing selection, and mean within-participant haplotype counts were highest for ama1 (2.8), then sera2 (2.1), csp (1.3), and trap (1.2). The authors conclude high epitope diversity may influence vaccine efficacy and continued surveillance is needed.

articles.headline: Antibody avidity not a strong correlate of malaria vaccine function or durability
articles.title_original: Relationship between antibody avidity, Fc-mediated functional activity and longevity of malaria vaccine responses in clinical trials.
articles.authors: Horton JL, Chan JA, Kurtovic L, Reiling L, Feng G, Persson KEM, Sacarlal J, Anders RF, McCarthy JS, Dobaño C, Boyle MJ, Beeson JG
articles.details: This analysis investigated whether antibody avidity predicts Fc-mediated function and antibody longevity using samples from two P. falciparum vaccine trials: a phase 1 trial of merozoite surface protein 2 (MSP2) in malaria-naïve adults and a phase 2b RTS,S trial in African children. Vaccine-induced IgG magnitude correlated strongly with avidity, but avidity was not a major determinant of Fc functional activities (complement fixation, Fcγ-receptor binding, opsonic phagocytosis); IgG magnitude was a stronger determinant than avidity, and avidity showed only a weak, antigen-specific association with longevity. The authors conclude that vaccine approaches focused on increasing avidity alone may be insufficient for more efficacious or longer-lasting vaccines.

articles.headline: PvDBP gene amplification helps P. vivax evade host antibodies in vivo
articles.title_original: PvDBP gene amplification is associated with functional immune evasion by Plasmodium vivax in vivo.
articles.authors: Baldor L, Seng D, Heng S, Khim N, Hor S, Thin S, Salinas N, Tolia N, Chitnis C, Mueller I, Foroughi S, King C, Lo E, Witkowski B, Flamand C, Feufack-Donfack L, Popovici J
articles.details: This longitudinal cohort study (2019-2020) across nine villages in Eastern Cambodia with P. vivax prevalence from ~5% to ~30% tested whether pvdbp gene amplification protects parasites in vivo, using a PCR assay for the pvdbp duplication and a flow cytometry assay for binding-inhibitory anti-PvDBP antibodies (BIAbs) in 657 participants' plasma. The proportion of multi-copy parasites rose with prevalence, from 35% in low- to 47% in high-prevalence villages (p=0.0246), and was higher in individuals with high BIAbs (87%, 40/46) than those without (38%, 193/514; p<0.0001). Multi-copy parasites were also more frequent in asymptomatic carriers than symptomatic patients. The authors conclude pvdbp duplication enables evasion of host anti-PvDBP immunity.

articles.headline: Mapping US hospitals treating severe malaria to optimize IV artesunate supply
articles.title_original: Mapping Hospitals That Treat Severe Malaria Cases in the United States: Evidence to Inform Health Systems Strategies for Intravenous Artesunate Supply.
articles.authors: Ravishankar K, Mace KE, Koiso S, Ridpath AD, Gulbas E, Thwing J, LaRocque RC, Williams S, Ryan ET, Brown TS, Hyle EP
articles.details: This study mapped locations of hospitalized severe malaria cases reported to the US CDC during 2012-2018 and used spatial optimization to identify level 1 trauma centers (L1TCs) as candidate intravenous artesunate distribution points. Locations were adjudicated for 1466 of 1791 reported cases; cases came from 292 of 3142 US counties (9.3%) across 46 states, concentrated in New York, Maryland, Texas, the District of Columbia, and Pennsylvania with minimal year-to-year variation. Of 1458 cases in the contiguous US, 95.8% were hospitalized within 2 hours' driving time of an L1TC. The authors conclude the distribution was heterogeneous but consistent, helping prioritize sites for on-site IVAS supply.

articles.headline: Review of artesunate use for severe malaria in nonendemic countries
articles.title_original: Artesunate Treatment of Severe Malaria in Nonendemic Countries: When, Where, How, and What Now?
articles.authors: White AC Jr
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Kenyan pharmacies show high adherence to pediatric malaria testing and treatment
articles.title_original: Guideline-concordant care and outcomes for pediatric malaria cases: descriptive evidence from pharmacy-based fever management in Kenya.
articles.authors: Dieci M, Nekesa C, Kayaro NI, Vernon J, Graetz I, Winskell K, Cummings JR
articles.details: A longitudinal cohort study at 39 private stand-alone pharmacies in malaria-endemic regions of Kenya surveyed caregivers of children under five presenting with fever, with phone follow-up two weeks later, to assess guideline-concordant malaria care. Among 239 febrile children, 69% received a malaria diagnostic test; of those tested, 59% were malaria-positive, and 78% of positives received appropriate antimalarial treatment, while only 3% of negatives received antimalarials. Among treated malaria-positive children, 88% had fully recovered at follow-up. Predictors of testing included caregiver education, younger child age, and provider knowledge. Authors conclude pharmacies showed high guideline adherence but with heterogeneity warranting equity improvements.

articles.headline: Malaria cases near zero by 2021 in Vietnam's Dak Lak province
articles.title_original: Malaria situation in a highly endemic province in the Central Highlands of Vietnam: a retrospective analysis.
articles.authors: Nguyen-Tien T, Tran V, Nguyen TM, Hoang PH, Le DM, Maude RJ
articles.details: A retrospective analysis of monthly district-level passive surveillance data from Dak Lak province in Vietnam's Central Highlands used logistic regression, time series decomposition, negative binomial models, and spatial analyses to characterize malaria cases and risk factors for P. falciparum and P. vivax. Over the six-year study period, 2147 cases were reported, 58.2% indigenous and 39.1% imported, with P. falciparum dominant; cases declined markedly and approached zero since 2021, peaking in October-November during the late rainy season. Temperature, rainfall, and prior-month case counts were strongly associated with cases, and gender, ethnicity, and high-endemicity residence were linked to infection. Authors recommend maintaining preventive interventions for high-risk groups ahead of elimination.

articles.headline: Kidney and endothelial biomarkers may predict malaria complications and mortality
articles.title_original: Kidney and endothelial injury biomarkers as predictors of complications and mortality in patients with malaria: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Nasserala JCL, Leitão FNC, Dos Santos AVR, Martins AMC, Meneses GC, de Souza RN, da Silva Junior GB, De Francesco Daher E, de Deus Morais MJ
articles.details: A systematic review (PRISMA 2020; prospectively registered in PROSPERO as CRD42025642952; searching PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science) found that combining renal and endothelial injury biomarkers with clinical and laboratory parameters improves characterization of malaria-associated acute kidney injury (AKI) and supports risk stratification. Elevated biomarker levels were consistently associated with increased risk of renal complications and adverse outcomes. Included studies were evaluated using the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale and MINORS. The authors conclude these renal and endothelial biomarkers are promising tools for early detection and monitoring of malaria-associated AKI.

articles.headline: Mozambique mass drug administration campaign well-accepted but missed 80% coverage target
articles.title_original: Operational performance of a programmatic mass drug administration campaign for malaria in southern Mozambique: a comprehensive mixed-methods evaluation of implementation outcomes.
articles.authors: Fuente-Soro L, Tusell M, Vecchio J, Nhangave A, Bapu K, Riley C, Materrula F, Dimene M, Sibindy S, Candrinho B, Aide P, Guinovart C
articles.details: A mixed-methods evaluation assessed operational performance of two rounds of programmatic mass drug administration (pMDA) using door-to-door dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine in Chidenguele, Gaza province, Mozambique, targeting roughly 59,271 individuals, via a household survey (n=770), health staff survey (n=28), and field observations (n=149). Acceptance was high: 96.2% (607/631) of eligible round-2 participants accepted, and 91.3% (703/770) found preventive asymptomatic antimalarials acceptable. Programmatic/contact coverage reached 73.9% (569/770) while operational coverage was 90.2% (569/631); fidelity was lower, with teams correctly explaining the visit purpose in 74.5% (111/149) and emphasizing treatment completion in 60.4% (90/149) of interactions. Authors conclude delivery was well-accepted with good operational performance but reaching the 80% coverage target remains challenging.

articles.headline: Dengue confirmed as major non-malarial fever cause in Kenyan children
articles.title_original: Unmasking non-malarial mosquito-borne infections among febrile children in malaria-endemic regions of western Kenya.
articles.authors: Ogony J, Karanja S, Ayodo G, Akinyi I, Onguru D, Angira C, Menya D, Mangeni J
articles.details: This facility-based screening study tested febrile children under 5 at outpatient departments in Busia and Kisumu Counties for both malaria and dengue using rapid test kits. Of 1004 children screened, 380 met recruitment criteria: 215 (21.4%) were positive for P. falciparum alone, 90 (8.9%) for dengue alone, and 75 (7.5%) had co-infections. Busia had the highest P. falciparum-only rate (23.4%) and Kisumu the highest dengue-only rate (12.6%). The authors conclude dengue is a major contributor to non-malarial febrile illness and call for revised screening algorithms to improve arboviral surveillance.

articles.headline: African genetic loci shape iron status and severe malaria risk
articles.title_original: African-specific genetic loci determine iron status and risk of severe malaria and bacteremia in African children.
articles.authors: Muriuki JM, Mentzer AJ, Band G, Chong AY, Macharia AW, Mogire RM, Abuga KM, Mitchell R, Gilchrist JJ, Webb EL, Ndungu FM, Raffield LM, Ekunwe L, Bentley AR, Sirima SB, Madhi SA, Hill AVS, Prentice AM, Bejon P, Hemani G, Smith GD, Sandhu MS, Elliott AM, Williams TN, Adeyemo A, Atkinson SH
articles.details: Genome-wide association studies of six iron-related biomarkers in 3928 children across five African sites, with replication in 2868 African American adults, examined links to severe malaria and bacteremia. The authors identified novel genome-wide significant loci for transferrin (GTF3C5) and hepcidin (CHCHD7/SDR16C5); DUP4 haplotype variants (Dantu blood group, rs552439837) associated with soluble transferrin receptor levels. Variants at GTF3C5 (rs2905094) and DUP4 conferred protection against severe malaria and bacteremia, while the CHCHD7/SDR16C5 variant (rs73596248) raised hepcidin and reduced risk of Klebsiella pneumoniae and Staphylococcus aureus bacteremia. European-derived polygenic risk scores transferred poorly to African populations.

articles.headline: Public awareness seen as key to Sudan's malaria vaccine rollout
articles.title_original: Public awareness and education: key to the success of the malaria vaccine rollout in Sudan.
articles.authors: Mohammed AAEA, Elagib HKE, Ibrahim MAA, Rafai MAA, Ali ODMH, Yaseen OYM
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Review of biological control approaches against malaria mosquito vectors
articles.title_original: Exploring the Scope and Status of Biological Interventions in Malaria Control.
articles.authors: Kumari S, Siddaramegowda R, Beeranthbail A, Shankar G, Kumar M, Sinha S, Anvikar A, Chalageri VH, Tripathi PK, Marinaik SB
articles.details: This narrative review surveys biological interventions for malaria control as alternatives to conventional measures facing insecticide resistance, environmental concerns, and climate change. It covers natural organisms and agents targeting Anopheles vectors—including viruses, fungi, microbial pathogens, natural predators, alkaloids, essential oils, and larvicidal plants—plus genetic techniques such as the sterile insect technique and genetically modified mosquitoes, highlighting entomopathogenic fungi. The authors cite an estimated 282 million infections and 610,000 deaths in 2024 and present biological control as a viable route toward sustainable malaria control and eventual eradication.

articles.headline: Malaria, diarrhea, respiratory infections linked to slower child growth
articles.title_original: Associations of Malaria, Diarrhea, and Respiratory Infections with Growth Velocity of Children in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Hiruy HN, Liu E, Worku A, Manji KP, Duggan CP, Berhane Y, Fawzi WW
articles.details: Using data from 2,397 Tanzanian children enrolled in a randomized controlled trial and followed for 18 months (30,079 monthly visits), this study used mixed-effects models to assess associations between infections and monthly growth velocity. Average velocities were 1.58 cm/month (height) and 358 g/month (weight). Malaria was associated with a 0.14 cm/month reduction in height velocity (-0.14 [-0.23 to -0.05]) but not weight; respiratory infections reduced height by 0.10 cm/month and weight by 37 g/month; diarrhea reduced height by 0.26 cm/month and weight by 120 g/month. The authors emphasize integrated health care to support healthier child growth.

articles.headline: Chloroquine resistance transporter PfCRT regulated by redox-dependent disulfide bonds
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Chloroquine Resistance Transporter (PfCRT) Is a Redox-Dependent Drug Transporter.
articles.authors: Chernitsky-Hamd D, Roy R, Schemm J, Schall A, Petros M, Willems A, Roepe PD
articles.details: Mechanistic study of cysteine residues in loop 7 (L7) of the Dd2 isoform of Plasmodium falciparum chloroquine resistance transporter (PfCRT), using site-specific mutagenesis, maleimide labeling, redox buffering, chloroquine transport measurements, and molecular dynamics calculations. Chloroquine transport by PfCRT was regulated by redox potential, with disulfide bonds at the C289/C312 and C301/C309 pairs required for full CQ transport activity; C101, C139, C171, and C328 modulate transport. Divalent metal likely does not coordinate to the L7 CXXC motif. MD trajectories (10 ns and 1 μs) suggested large L7 conformational changes upon SH-to-S-S interconversion, supporting a model in which redox state regulates PfCRT access to CQ in the digestive vacuole.

articles.headline: Liver-stage LS2 viral-vector malaria vaccine fails to protect in challenge trial
articles.title_original: Phase I/IIa study to assess the safety, immunogenicity and efficacy of ChAdOx1-MVA vectored vaccines expressing a novel liver-stage malaria dual antigen LS2 by sporozoite challenge in malaria-naïve adults.
articles.authors: Silman D, Flaxman A, Datoo M, Edwards NJ, Ramos-Lopez F, Mitton C, Mair C, Bellamy D, Bowyer G, Morter R, Halbroth B, Venkatraman N, Folegatti PM, Marshall J, Poulton I, Bajer A, Salman AM, Berrie E, Baum J, Blagborough AM, Crocker W, Roberts R, Lawrie AM, Spencer AJ, Gilbert SC, Ewer KJ, Hill AVS
articles.details: In a non-randomised, open-label, dose-escalation phase I/IIa trial in UK adults, ChAdOx1-MVA viral-vectored vaccines expressing a novel dual liver-stage antigen LS2 (combining LSA1 and LSAP2) failed to protect against malaria: all vaccinated volunteers became infected during controlled human malaria infection (CHMI) by mosquito bite, with a median time to diagnosis of 13 days versus 13.25 days in 6 unvaccinated controls. Group 1 (n=3) received ChAdOx1 LS2 5x10^9 vp, while group 2 (n=10) received a heterologous prime-boost (ChAdOx1 LS2 2.5x10^10 vp plus MVA LS2 2x10^8 pfu). The vaccines were well tolerated with no serious adverse events, and IFN-γ ELISpot showed a high median response of 4473 SFC/10^6 PBMC. The authors conclude that despite being a safe, highly effective CD8+ T-cell platform, LSA1 and LSAP2 did not translate to protection from infection in humans.

articles.headline: Scoping review maps insecticide resistance in Odisha malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Mapping the Insecticide Resistance Landscape of Malaria Vectors in Odisha, India (1993-2024): A Scoping Review.
articles.authors: Barik TK, Velamuri PS, Bhuyan KB, Sabar S, Mohapatra P, Sahu B, Das MK, Pati S, Dash AP, Raghavendra K
articles.details: This scoping review compiled insecticide resistance data from 28 published sources (1993–2024) on Anopheles malaria vectors in Odisha, identifying An. culicifacies as the most resistant species — showing resistance to DDT in 26 districts, malathion in 17, and deltamethrin in 10. Applying WHO adult susceptibility criteria (susceptible >98% mortality; possible resistance 90–97%; confirmed resistance <90%), the review reported An. culicifacies in all 30 districts, An. fluviatilis in 24, An. annularis in 22, and An. minimus in two, with intensity bioassay data presented from 10 southern districts. The authors conclude that emerging multiple insecticide resistance in An. culicifacies underscores the urgency for insecticides with a novel mode of action and integrated vector management.

articles.headline: Review of malaria–HIV co-infection burden in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Insights into Malaria and HIV Co-infection: Trends and Impact in Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Ayoola AO, Okanlawon BM, Idowu EM, Adedokun TT, Ige AA, Olorunfemi AB, Ojurongbe OD
articles.details: This review examines the epidemiology of malaria and HIV co-infection in sub-Saharan Africa, focusing on key risk factors, the health outcomes of dual infection, and clinical management challenges including diagnostic limitations, treatment interactions, and health system constraints. The authors note evidence that closer coordination of malaria and HIV services may improve patient outcomes and the efficiency of health delivery, and identify persistent gaps in surveillance systems, clinical management, and integrated control strategies. No quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: Climate gradients shape 2La inversion in Nigerian malaria vector
articles.title_original: Spatial eco-climatic gradients drive adaptive 2La chromosomal inversion polymorphism in Anopheles coluzzii, a major malaria vector in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Adeogun AO, Adetunji O, Babalola AS, Izekor RT, Oyeniyi TA, Adesoye O, Wahedi J, Busari LO, Aniekeme G, Ajayi S, Chabi J, Awolola ST
articles.details: Study characterizing 2La chromosomal inversion polymorphism in Anopheles coluzzii larvae sampled across 12 states spanning Nigeria's southern, central, and northern ecological zones, with species confirmed by PCR and 2La karyotypes determined by molecular assays. The 2La/2La homokaryotype predominated in arid northern Sahelian and Sudan savanna ecozones, while the standard 2La+/2La+ arrangement predominated in humid southern forest and mangrove regions; heterokaryotypes occurred at intermediate frequencies in the central transitional belt, producing a latitudinal cline with heterozygosity peaking in central Nigeria. Authors conclude that eco-climatic gradients (aridity and humidity) drive adaptive inversion distribution, with implications for resting behaviour, insecticide response, and vector control.

articles.headline: Housing and community measures to prevent malaria and cholera
articles.title_original: Household and community-based approaches to malaria and cholera prevention: A narrative review with case studies from Uganda and Mozambique.
articles.authors: Guido G, Totaro V, Lochoro P, Ictho J, Occa E, Chhaganlal K, Segala FV, Dall'Oglio G, Mechendura BF, Gomonda E, Putoto G, Penco F, Asaduzzaman M, Nicastri E, Farkas FB, Iatta R, Lakatos B, Ali RW, Saracino A, Di Gennaro F
articles.details: This review combines published literature with case studies from Uganda and Mozambique on housing-based interventions for infectious disease prevention. It examines structural home modifications—screened windows and doors, sealed eaves, elevated flooring, and insecticide-treated barriers—for reducing mosquito entry and malaria risk, alongside improved latrines, proper waste disposal, and handwashing for waterborne diseases such as cholera. No quantitative effect estimates are reported. The authors conclude that combining structural modifications with domestic behavior change offers a low-cost, community-acceptable, and sustainable complementary strategy, though success depends on affordability, cultural adaptation, and integration with existing control programs.

articles.headline: Avian malaria found only in peri-urban Culex mosquitoes, not urban
articles.title_original: Urbanization and Vector Species Shape Avian Malaria Prevalence in Mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Parra P, Veiga J, Garrigós M, Panisse G, Garrido M, Martínez-de la Puente J
articles.details: This study assessed avian malaria prevalence in two mosquito species across urban and peri-urban habitats. No infections were detected in either species in urban areas, whereas a 2.53% prevalence was found in the native Culex pipiens from peri-urban sites. The authors highlight that urbanization influences parasite transmission dynamics.

articles.headline: Loa loa encephalopathy after ivermectin in malaria-co-infected patient
articles.title_original: Probable Loa loa-associated encephalopathy following ivermectin administration in a patient with malaria infection: A case report from the Democratic Republic of the Congo.
articles.authors: Posite CM, Findama JK, Vwiravwahali MK, Molo IM, Bunduki GK
articles.details: This case report describes a 37-year-old woman from Ituri Province, eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo, who developed altered mental status after receiving ivermectin for loiasis. She had been diagnosed with intestinal parasitosis and malaria, and blood smear revealed Plasmodium trophozoites and Loa loa microfilariae. Within 24 hours of ivermectin, she developed meningeal signs, and cerebrospinal fluid contained Loa loa microfilariae without bacterial or fungal infection. After intensified antimicrobial therapy, albendazole, and supportive care, she recovered fully and was discharged after 19 days. The authors stress microfilarial burden assessment and pharmacovigilance before ivermectin use in Loa loa-endemic settings.

articles.headline: Review: eliminating malaria in pregnancy in conflict-affected eastern DRC
articles.title_original: Time to Act; Let Us Unite to Eliminate Malaria Among Pregnant Women in War Zone From Eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo: A Review.
articles.authors: Akilimali A, Mehmood L, Njazi PI, Ali B, Maqbool S, Younas M, Sagide M, Rizvi SVZ, Munsab R, Rizvi SUA, Fatima S, Alhassan A, Wei CR, Langat AK
articles.details: This narrative, non-systematic review synthesized peer-reviewed articles, policy documents, and guidelines to assess epidemiological, immunological, and conflict-related factors driving malaria severity among pregnant women in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, which accounts for 12% of global malaria cases. Findings indicate malaria-in-pregnancy prevalence between 17% and 40%, with a 26% asymptomatic pooled estimate creating silent transmission reservoirs, and early parasitemia linked to reduced umbilical artery resistance and fetal growth restriction. Armed conflict has caused shortages of insecticide-treated nets and intermittent preventive treatment, while emerging artemisinin resistance and funding gaps hinder progress. The authors call for strengthening all six health-system pillars.

articles.headline: Agent-based model simulates spatial malaria interventions in Vietnam
articles.title_original: Spatio-temporal agent-based modelling of malaria.
articles.authors: Walker CR, Anwar MN, Bräuninger L, Richards J, Ataide R, Thang ND, Thang NX, Canavati S, Flegg JA
articles.details: Researchers developed a stochastic spatiotemporal agent-based model of Plasmodium falciparum malaria that captures how spatially distributed interventions affect transmission. The model uses spatiotemporal estimates of mosquito climatic suitability and household location data to simulate interactions between human and mosquito agents, applied to investigate intervention-distribution strategies for households in Vietnam. The study suggests that providing some level of protection to a wide range of households could reduce malaria prevalence more than providing strong protection to a limited number of households.

articles.headline: Malaria-schistosome coinfection affects 10-13% of sub-Saharan children
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum coinfection with Schistosoma haematobium and Schistosoma mansoni among children in sub-Saharan Africa: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Hassan U, Siddiqa A, Zhu Y, Zhao J
articles.details: This PRISMA systematic review and meta-analysis of 21 observational studies estimated pooled prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum coinfection with Schistosoma haematobium and/or S. mansoni in children aged 0-17 across sub-Saharan Africa, using a random-effects model. P. falciparum-S. haematobium coinfection prevalence was 10.0% (95% CI 6.0-14.0%; I2=98%), highest in West Africa (13.0%). P. falciparum-S. mansoni coinfection was 13.0% (95% CI 6.0-24.0%; I2=99.28%), highest in East Africa (25.0%). Estimates varied significantly by diagnostic method. Authors conclude coinfection remains a major pediatric health challenge with diagnostic limitations likely underestimating true prevalence.

articles.headline: Lung ultrasound reframes respiratory distress in cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Malarial pneumonopathy in Malawian children with cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Wynkoop HJ, Lintner-Rivera M, Taylor T, Seydel KB, Pensulo P, Ayamie M, Chetcuti K, Vidal LLM, O'Brien NF
articles.details: In a prospective observational study of 97 Malawian children aged 6 months to 12 years meeting WHO criteria for cerebral malaria, investigators characterized respiratory distress using vitals, lactate, blood gas analysis, and 12-zone point-of-care lung ultrasound. Twenty-seven children (28%) met WHO respiratory distress criteria; despite elevated lactate (median 7.0 mmol/L), median pH was 7.41, and metabolic acidosis was the sole disturbance in only 12 (12%). Primary respiratory alkalosis occurred in 33%, and subcentimeter consolidations appeared in 88%. Respiratory distress was not associated with increased mortality. Authors propose the term 'malarial pneumonopathy,' challenging the metabolic-acidosis-only explanation.

articles.headline: RTS,S malaria vaccine pharmacovigilance found no new safety concerns
articles.title_original: Safety monitoring of the RTS,S/AS01(E) malaria vaccine: experiences and lessons from routine pharmacovigilance in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi.
articles.authors: Ashie A, Mandale M, Ndalama A, Darko D, Seaneke S, Boateng EK, Sabblah G, Asamoa-Amoakohene A, Amponsa-Achiano K, Mohammed NT, Jalang'o R, Nkansah E, Adu-Boahen Y, Siyoi F, Toroitich A, Khaemba C, Nambwa P, Jain S, Okine RNA, Mssusa AK
articles.details: This report describes routine pharmacovigilance experiences monitoring the RTS,S/AS01E malaria vaccine during pilot implementation in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi, using strengthened existing national reporting systems to track adverse events following immunization. Causality assessment of serious AEFIs by national expert advisory committees revealed no new safety concerns and did not confirm safety signals observed in Phase 3 trials. Challenges included underreporting of AEFIs, delays in data submission and investigation, and poor report quality. Authors emphasize sustained investment, training, and innovations to improve health worker reporting for future rollouts in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Joint spatial modelling maps malaria hotspots in southwestern Benin
articles.title_original: Identification of malaria hotspots in southwestern Benin through spatial joint modelling of malaria incidence and vector abundance.
articles.authors: Monteiro GM, Aïkpon RY, Dandonougbo C, Sedda L, Djogbenou LS
articles.details: Researchers applied a Bayesian framework for joint Gaussian spatial processes to log-transformed Anopheles gambiae s.l. and Anopheles funestus counts and human malaria incidence across eight communes of southwest Benin, combining mosquito surveillance with District Health Information System 2 incidence data. Joint risk modelling identified contiguous hotspots mainly on the western shores of Lake Ahémé and in Atchannou, Sè, Avloh, and Grand-Popo districts. Wind speed, mid-infrared reflectance, leaf area index, and land surface temperature were consistent drivers. An. funestus showed stronger spatial correlation with malaria incidence (119.95 km2) than An. gambiae s.l. (89.90 km2). Authors call for species-agnostic, spatially targeted interventions.

articles.headline: Pediatric malaria severity independent of presumed immunity in Italy
articles.title_original: Pediatric malaria in a non-endemic European setting: clinical patterns, laboratory markers, and the ambiguous role of immunity.
articles.authors: Caiazzo R, Panero B, Carello M, Coppola C, Di Tonno R, Markowich AH, Stracuzzi M, Valentino MS, Grosso S, Gigantiello A, Giacomet V
articles.details: This retrospective study reviewed 74 children diagnosed with malaria at a tertiary center in Milan, Italy (2004-2024), stratified into Non-Immune (Italian-born, 47.3%) and Presumed-Immune (foreign-born or frequent travelers, 52.7%) groups.
