P. falciparum (62.2%) and P. vivax (27%) predominated; severe malaria (WHO 2015) occurred in 23%, with no significant difference between groups (p=0.783). Serum sodium was inversely associated with hospital stay length (ρ=-0.284, p=0.020), CRP correlated positively with fever duration (ρ=0.256, p=0.033) and strongly negatively with platelet count (ρ=-0.585, p<0.001). Intravenous artesunate replaced quinine post-2015. Authors conclude severity was largely independent of presumed immune status.

articles.headline: Uric acid not a reliable severity marker in pediatric complicated malaria
articles.title_original: Serum Uric Acid as a Biomarker of Disease Severity in Children With Complicated Malaria: A Cross-Sectional Study From Eastern India.
articles.authors: Kumar M, Jha S, Kumar H, Sarangam B, Munda PK, Garg V, Mudgal SK
articles.details: This hospital-based cross-sectional study enrolled 102 children aged 1–18 years with laboratory-confirmed complicated P. falciparum malaria at a tertiary care hospital in eastern India, measuring serum uric acid (SUA), SGOT, SGPT, total bilirubin, and CRP. Hyperuricemia was found in 80.4% (82), with elevated SGOT in 72.5%, SGPT in 83.3%, and bilirubin in 65.7%. Adverse outcomes occurred in 29.4% (30) and were significantly associated with abnormal SGOT (χ²=6.499, p=0.011), SGPT (χ²=8.500, p=0.004), and bilirubin (p=0.004), but not SUA (p=0.115). The authors conclude hyperuricemia is common but SUA alone may not reliably predict outcomes.

articles.headline: Review of global malaria burden, epidemiology, and control strategies
articles.title_original: Malaria in the 21st Century: Global Disease Burden, Epidemiological Insights, and Strategic Control Approaches.
articles.authors: Alharbi BF, Ahmed MA
articles.details: This review summarizes the current global epidemiology of malaria, a disease caused by Plasmodium parasites transmitted mainly through bites of infected female Anopheles mosquitoes (and also via blood transfusions, organ transplants, or congenitally from mother to child), drawing mainly on the WHO World Malaria Report 2024 and Global Burden of Disease estimates. It brings together evidence on regional trends, determinants, and control strategies, with particular focus on socio-economic factors, intervention methods, and emerging challenges including drug resistance, climate change, and limited funding. The authors note that prevention and management require global, multifactorial approaches tailored to the local environment and strengthened health education. The review concludes with a discussion of policy priorities needed to meet the WHO Global Technical Strategy goals for malaria elimination by 2030.

articles.headline: Ultra-sensitive PCR shows falciparum prevalence fell 53% in Rwanda
articles.title_original: Molecular surveillance of Falciparum malaria in Rwanda: Shifts in parasite prevalence and risk factors between the 2014-15 and 2019-20 Rwanda Demographics and Health Surveys.
articles.authors: Zuromski J, Young NW, Gashema P, Iradukunda V, Bosco NJ, Sadler JM, Gaither C, Munyaneza T, Connelly SV, Lee L, Goel V, Muvunyi CM, Dieu Butera J, Mazarati JB, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA
articles.details: Using ultra-sensitive quantitative real-time PCR on 7,127 adult dried blood spots collected for HIV testing in the 2019-20 Rwanda Demographic and Health Survey, researchers measured P. falciparum infection prevalence. National prevalence was 7.7% (95% CI 6.8-8.7%), with predominantly low-density infections (median parasitemia 7.3 parasites/uL). Male sex, lower household wealth, lower educational achievement, and residence at lower elevation were significant predictors. National prevalence decreased 53% relative to the parallel 2014-15 survey, despite reports of rising artemisinin partial resistance-associated mutations, suggesting national control efforts have substantially reduced burden even amid increasing antimalarial resistance.

articles.headline: Double Kelch13 mutations drive resistance to next-generation ozonide antimalarials
articles.title_original: Double Mutations in Plasmodium falciparum Kelch13 drive resistance to next-generation artemisinin derivatives in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Bower-Lepts C, Ward KE, Wittlin S, Stokes BH, Yeo T, Qahash T, Small-Saunders JL, Park H, Uhlemann AC, Llinás M, Fidock DA, Mok S
articles.details: Cambodian Kelch13 (K13) mutant parasites pressured with the ozonide artefenomel (OZ439)—a next-generation artemisinin with a prolonged in vivo half-life—developed resistance through a novel non-propeller K13 A212T mutation identified by whole-genome sequencing. Gene editing confirmed that the K13 double mutation R539T+A212T is a determinant of OZ439 resistance, with accelerated parasite recrudescence after OZ439 or OZ277 exposure, a phenotype also seen in C580Y+A212T double mutants. Metabolomic profiling found no change in hemoglobin-derived peptides but increased glutathione, nucleotide, and aspartate-glutamate metabolites, indicating resistance arises from enhanced redox regulation rather than altered drug activation.

articles.headline: Imported malaria makes up ~89% of cases in Asia Pacific eliminating countries
articles.title_original: Estimating the burden of imported malaria in the Asia Pacific Region: A Systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Penjor K, Thapa B, Tsheten T, Mazumdar S, Bagheri N, Kotepui M, Wangdi K
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis (PROSPERO CRD420251008817) searched PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science for studies on imported malaria in 22 Asia Pacific malaria-eliminating countries. Based on 42 studies from nine countries totaling 435,454 malaria cases, the pooled prevalence of imported malaria was 89.0% (95% CI 75.9-95.5, I2=100%). Imported cases were 94% male (95% CI 92.0-96.0), 57.0% P. falciparum (95% CI 47.2-65.5), and 64.3% of African origin (95% CI 43.1-81.0). Prevalence ranged from 95.0% in Sri Lanka and China to 3.2% in South Korea. The authors stress the need for strengthened cross-border surveillance and tailored interventions.

articles.headline: Malaria burden in reproductive-age women falls but stays high in East Africa
articles.title_original: Fatal and non-fatal health outcomes of malaria among reproductive age women in Eastern Sub-Saharan Africa, from 1990 to 2023.
articles.authors: Tadesse S, Korma W, Sherfa A, Bedewi J, Alemseged EA, Angaw Y, Assefa L, Fekadu Y, Ashenafi G, Tsegaye D, Gemechu T
articles.details: This Global Burden of Disease 2023 modeling study estimated fatal and non-fatal malaria outcomes among women of reproductive age across Eastern Sub-Saharan Africa from 1990 to 2023, using CODEm for mortality/YLLs and Bayesian meta-regression for prevalence, incidence, and YLDs. In 2023, Mozambique had the highest prevalence (19,627 cases per 100,000 women; 95% UI 12,347–27,601) and incidence (16,076 new cases per 100,000), plus the highest mortality, YLLs, and DALYs; Eritrea (842) and Rwanda (1,603) had the lowest prevalence and Ethiopia the lowest fatal burden. Rates fell substantially region-wide versus 1990 except in Djibouti and Ethiopia, but remain high and heterogeneous.

articles.headline: High malaria, anaemia, and pyrethroid resistance found in Muheza, Tanzania baseline
articles.title_original: Baseline malaria burden and pyrethroid resistance in Muheza, Tanzania informing a cluster randomized trial of the 3D window screens.
articles.authors: Kathet S, Bwana VM, Fereji MA, Mhando JB, Magogo FS, Mwingira V, Kisinza W, Meri S, Khattab A
articles.details: This baseline cluster survey across 20 hamlets in 17 villages of Muheza District, Tanzania preceded a cluster-randomized trial of a 3D window screen. Among 778 children aged 6 months to 14 years tested by mRDT, malaria prevalence was 40.2% and anaemia 55.2%. An. funestus s.l. (70.2%) and An. gambiae s.l. (29.8%) dominated; combined annual EIR was ~27.7 infectious bites per person. Pyrethroid resistance was high (24-hour mortality 56.5% to permethrin, 52.8% to deltamethrin; kdr-East L1014S frequency 0.45). Children ≥5 years had ~threefold higher infection odds (aOR 3.03, 95% CI 2.09–4.40), while bed net use was protective (aOR 0.36, 95% CI 0.18–0.70).

articles.headline: Targeting forest contacts boosts malaria case detection yield in Indonesia
articles.title_original: Adapting reactive case detection strategies for high-risk populations to increase malaria infection detection in Aceh Province, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Bennett A, Cotter C, Zarlinda I, Silaen MG, Lina RN, Cueto C, Elyazar I, Jacobson JO, Ekawati L, Hsiang MS, Noviyanti R, Smith JL, Coutrier FN
articles.details: This study in Aceh Province, Indonesia compared risk-based reactive case detection (testing non-household contacts sharing forest worksites or travel) against standard household-based RACD (testing members within 500 m), using microscopy, LAMP, and PCR. Among 47 enrolled index cases, 28 were P. knowlesi infections and most reported forest exposure. Standard RACD tested 847 household members from 34 cases and found just 1 infection (0.1%; 95% CI 0.0–0.7%). Risk-based RACD tested 179 contacts from 32 cases and found 8 LAMP-positive infections (4.5%; 95% CI 1.9–8.6%), 3 PCR-confirmed (1.7%). Exposure-driven targeting substantially raised yield where transmission occurs outside the home.

articles.headline: Correction notice for study on nighttime malaria mosquito biting in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Correction: A matter of timing: Biting by malaria-infected Anopheles mosquitoes and the use of interventions during the night in rural south-eastern Tanzania.
articles.authors: Namango IH, Moore SJ, Marshall C, Saddler A, Kaftan D, Tenywa FC, Makungwa N, Limwagu AJ, Mapua S, Odufuwa OG, Ligema G, Ngonyani H, Matanila I, Bharmal J, Moore J, Finda M, Okumu F, Hetzel MW, Ross A
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Rainfall raises and bed nets cut child malaria deaths in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Assessing the role of interventions and climate on malaria mortality among children under five years of age: insights from two decades of data from the Health Demographic Surveillance System of Nouna, Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Traoré N, Zabré P, Millogo O, Sié A, Vounatsou P
articles.details: This study used two decades of data from the Nouna Health Demographic Surveillance System in northwest Burkina Faso to assess effects of interventions and climate on malaria mortality in children under five, applying wavelet analysis (2002–2021) and Bayesian negative binomial models (2013–2021). Malaria mortality, rainfall, and land surface temperature followed a 12-month seasonal cycle, with rainfall lagging 1.5–2 months. Rainfall was positively associated with mortality (MRR 1.59; 95% BCI 1.18–1.95), higher land surface temperature was associated with lower mortality (MRR 0.68), and insecticide-treated nets reduced mortality (MRR 0.59; 95% BCI 0.42–0.79), while ACTs showed no statistically important effect. Authors urge climate-informed planning and sustained ITN coverage.

articles.headline: Inflammation doesn't bias vitamin A biomarkers in heavier women and children
articles.title_original: Inflammation Does Not Mediate Relationships between BMI/BMI-for-Age Z-Score and Retinol or Retinol-Binding Protein in Adult Women or Young Children without Underweight, Wasting, or Malaria: An Analysis of 24 Surveys.
articles.authors: Davis JN, Williams A, Arnold CD, Addo Y, Oaks BM, Wirth JP, Suchdev PS, Young MF, Engle-Stone R
articles.details: This cross-sectional BRINDA analysis used data from 24 surveys (16,771 women aged 15-49 y in 13 surveys; 24,707 children aged 6-59 mo in 22 surveys), excluding underweight, wasting, pregnancy, or malaria, to test whether inflammation from overweight/obesity distorts retinol or retinol-binding protein (RBP). Using linear regression and structural equation modeling with CRP and/or AGP, BMI was positively associated with retinol/RBP in 5 of 13 women's surveys and BAZ in 3 of 22 children's surveys; inflammation partially mediated the BMI/BAZ-retinol/RBP relationship in only 3 of 13 women's and 1 of 22 children's surveys, with varying direction. The authors conclude inflammation from excess weight does not appreciably impact vitamin A assessment in these populations.

articles.headline: Social protection weakly integrated into African TB, HIV, malaria policies
articles.title_original: Assessing the integration of social protection into infectious disease policies: a multi-country analysis of TB, HIV, and malaria strategies in Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Munyangaju I, Osório D, Nacarapa E
articles.details: A structured policy-document review assessed how national TB, HIV, and malaria strategic plans in Kenya, Malawi, Mozambique, Nigeria, and Zambia operationalize social protection, reviewing 111 documents published between 2015 and 2025 against a five-domain matrix scored 0-3. No country had institutionalized catastrophic-cost monitoring. Overall readiness ranged from 2.6 to 2.9 (Malawi 2.9 and Zambia 2.8 highest; Mozambique 2.6 lowest), with implementation and coordination strongest (3.0) and financial protection weakest (2.1). The authors conclude significant gaps persist in financial-risk monitoring, budgeting, and accountability.

articles.headline: Broad IgG to PfEMP1 domains linked to malaria protection in young children
articles.title_original: IgG and IgM responses to PfEMP1 domains associated with semi-immunity to clinical malaria in Burkinabe children under five.
articles.authors: Dankyi BO, Yuguchi T, Rojrung R, Nagaoka H, Kanoi BN, Tiono AB, Nebie I, Ouedraogo A, Tanaka K, Miyake Y, Miura K, Sattabongkot J, Sirima SB, Tsuboi T, Takashima E
articles.details: Using AlphaScreen, researchers measured IgG and IgM responses to 271 P. falciparum PfEMP1 (3D7) domains in plasma from 80 Burkinabe children aged 0-60 months, sampled before and during peak transmission. Pre-season, 96% of domains were IgG-reactive (breadth increasing with age) and 71% IgM-reactive (no age trend). IgG responses to 132 domains (49%), including many non-adhesion domains, remained significantly associated with reduced clinical malaria risk after FDR correction, while 5 nominally protective IgM responses did not survive correction. Opsonic phagocytosis correlated with protection for only one antigen, suggesting protection via mechanisms beyond phagocytosis.

articles.headline: Frequent, genetically diverse P. vivax relapses sustain transmission in Cambodia
articles.title_original: Frequent and genetically diverse Plasmodium vivax relapses contribute to malaria transmission in Cambodia.
articles.authors: Seng D, Ko K, Orban A, Heng S, Ea M, Tat B, Feufack Donfack LB, Eng V, Tebben K, Khim N, Lefèvre T, Cohuet A, Sommen C, Flamand C, Serre D, Popovici J
articles.details: In a cohort of 59 Cambodian P. vivax patients treated with artesunate and relocated to a malaria-free area to prevent reinfection, participants were followed every two days for 90 days by qPCR and microscopy, with parasite genome sequencing and Anopheles dirus membrane-feeding assays. Relapses were common: 81% of patients had at least one relapse within 90 days (up to four consecutive), with a median time to first relapse of 20 days. Relapsing parasites in monoclonal infections could be identical, sibling, or unrelated to enrollment parasites, and polyclonal infections relapsed significantly more. Even low-density, asymptomatic relapses were frequently infectious to mosquitoes, underscoring the need for radical cure.

articles.headline: Haemoglobin AA linked to higher asymptomatic malaria odds in Ghana
articles.title_original: The Role of Haemoglobin Variants and Red Blood Cell Polymorphisms in Asymptomatic Malaria: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Aninagyei E, Boatey CA, Deku JG, Boakye AA, Nyarkotey E, Larbi G, Acheampong DO
articles.details: This study recruited community members without clinical signs of malaria from four endemic Ghanaian communities, screening whole blood for P. falciparum HRP2 and typing ABO/RhD groups and haemoglobin phenotypes (HbAA, HbAS) by alkaline electrophoresis (pH 8.6), with multivariate logistic regression. Haemoglobin variants (p=0.021) and red cell polymorphisms (p<0.001) were significantly associated with asymptomatic malaria, whereas RhD alone was not (p=0.877). Individuals with HbAA had significantly higher odds of asymptomatic infection (AOR=2.1, p=0.02); higher odds for O RhD-positive (AOR=2.39), O/HbAA (AOR=1.15), and B/HbAS (AOR=1.22) were not statistically significant. The authors highlight the complex role of host genetic factors and call for larger studies.

articles.headline: Computational screening of hydroxamate inhibitors against P. falciparum PfA-M1 enzyme
articles.title_original: In silico targeting of plasmodium falciparum metallo-aminopeptidases using hydroxamate inhibitors.
articles.authors: Sodum N, Rao V, Chhabra V, Kumar N, Rao CM
articles.details: This in-silico study evaluated eight cinnamoyl sulfonamide hydroxamate derivatives (NMJ 1-8) against Plasmodium falciparum metallo-aminopeptidase PfA-M1 (PDB ID 4X2U), a zinc-dependent enzyme that degrades host hemoglobin, using the orally available inhibitor tosedostat as a reference. Extra-precision docking, MMGBSA, and induced-fit docking showed favorable interactions with IFD scores from -1984.16 to -1989.93 kcal/mol; NMJ-2 and NMJ-3 had the strongest binding and formed stable complexes in molecular dynamics simulations, with persistent contacts at residues HIS500, GLU463, GLU519, and HIS496. Both satisfied ADME parameters and overlapped tosedostat's binding pattern, suggesting they are promising antimalarial scaffolds requiring preclinical validation.

articles.headline: Duffy genotype variation in vivax malaria patients; Duffy-negatives still infected
articles.title_original: Genetic variation in the Duffy blood group among vivax malaria patients and its impact on disease susceptibility.
articles.authors: Tsigie M, Baldor L, Feufack-Donfack LB, Shenkutie TT, Khim N, Melese D, Abera A, Girmachew F, Feleke SM, Hailemariam A, Tasew G, Tollera G, Hailu M, Popovici J, Abebe A, Lo E
articles.details: A facility-based cross-sectional study genotyped the Duffy blood group in 485 microscopy-confirmed P. vivax-infected patients using SYBR Green qPCR screening and a TaqMan qPCR Duffy assay. Of the samples, 93.8% were mono-P. vivax infections and 6.2% were mixed P. vivax/P. falciparum. Almost all participants were Duffy positive—75.1% (364/485) heterozygous and 24.5% (119/485) homozygous—while only 2 individuals (0.4%) were Duffy-negative, both presenting with mixed infections and low parasitemia. The authors conclude Duffy negativity is not an absolute barrier to P. vivax infection, suggesting an alternative invasion pathway warranting further study.

articles.headline: Malaria vector composition and outdoor early-morning biting in two Cameroon settings
articles.title_original: Mosquito vector composition and biting behavior of malaria vectors in forest versus humid savanna eco-epidemiological settings of Cameroon.
articles.authors: Bouopda-Tuedom AG, Kiam CB, Aabate L, Bayibeki AN, Ibrahima I, Nanssong-Vomo CT, Tumamo BF, Onguene C, Awono-Ambéné PH, Ayong LS, Moukoko CEE, Morlais I, Nsango SE
articles.details: This entomological study compared malaria vectors in a forest region (Mfou) and a savanna region (Tibati) in Cameroon using Human Landing Catches indoors and outdoors, with morphological and molecular identification and qPCR-based Plasmodium detection. Of 3,651 Anopheles collected across 9 species, An. gambiae s.l. predominated in Mfou (68.96%, 1253/1817) while An. funestus s.l. dominated in Tibati (49.95%, 916/1834). Biting peaked outdoors between 4-5 a.m. (2.68 bites/person/hour in Mfou, 2.31 in Tibati), and the entomological inoculation rate was higher in Mfou (1.47 indoor, 1.00 outdoor infectious bites/person/night) than Tibati (~0.25/0.23). The authors stress that substantial outdoor and early-morning biting limits bed nets and necessitates complementary control.

articles.headline: Heavy malaria burden in Ugandan infants' first year despite partial protection
articles.title_original: Natural History and the Burden of Malaria During the First Year of Life in the High-Transmission Setting of Uganda.
articles.authors: Aguti M, Nankabirwa JI, Kizza J, Kakuru A, Ssemukuye T, Adrama H, Olwoch P, Opira B, Odongo B, Camanag K, Nakalembe M, Clark T, Rosenthal PJ, Dorsey G, Jaganathan P, Kamya MR
articles.details: A cohort of 855 infants in Busia District, Uganda, born to HIV-uninfected women and enrolled at 4-8 weeks, was followed by active and passive case detection to age 1 (2021-2025, pre-vaccine rollout) with parasitemia assessed every 4 weeks by microscopy and qPCR. Over 706.7 person-years, 662 malaria episodes occurred; microscopic parasitemia prevalence was 7.9% and combined microscopic/submicroscopic prevalence was 21.8%. Sickle cell trait (HbAS) conferred 39% protection against symptomatic malaria but not against parasitemia, while modern housing and higher maternal education reduced risk. District-wide distribution of alpha-cypermethrin plus chlorfenapyr nets in October 2023 was followed by an 80% reduction in malaria incidence. Authors urge age-appropriate interventions for young infants.

articles.headline: Wavelet and SARIMA time-series forecasting of malaria in three Nigerian states
articles.title_original: Exploring the past and forecasting the future of malaria in selected Nigerian states: A time series modelling approach using wavelet and SARIMA.
articles.authors: Bakare EA, Mogbojuri OA, Bakare DA, Odewusi OJ
articles.details: This study applied wavelet analysis and SARIMA modeling to 10 years (January 2015-December 2024) of confirmed uncomplicated malaria case data from Nasarawa, Kwara, and Zamfara States, Nigeria, to characterize seasonality, synchrony, and forecast cases for 2025-2026. Nasarawa showed a strong annual cycle peaking in September 70% of the time; Kwara showed mixed semi-annual/annual/multi-annual cycles peaking mostly in August; Zamfara showed a moderate yearly cycle peaking in September. Nasarawa and Zamfara were almost perfectly synchronized. SARIMA forecasted average monthly cases of 51,482 (95% CI 23,899-116,073) in Nasarawa, 20,850 (3,381-202,511) in Kwara, and 67,463 (27,317-171,418) in Zamfara, supporting state-specific and regionally coordinated, optimally timed interventions.

articles.headline: Duffy-null allele selection from African admixture may explain low P. vivax in Oman
articles.title_original: Adaptive admixture at ACKR1, the Duffy blood group locus, may have shaped Plasmodium vivax prevalence in Oman.
articles.authors: Haffener PE, Al-Riyami AZ, Al-Zadjali S, Busby GBJ, Al-Mahdhuri S, Al-Rawahi M, Al-Hosni S, Al-Marhoobi A, Al-Sheriyani A, Leffler EM
articles.details: Using whole-genome sequence data from 100 Omani individuals, researchers estimated that 15.6% of their genetic ancestry is shared with African populations (95% CI 10.8%–20.3%), with African ancestry rising to 76% at the Duffy locus, where the P. vivax-protective Duffy null allele (FyES) was at high frequency. The estimated selection coefficient at FyES was 0.038 (95% CI 0.027–0.051), with likely introduction at least 59 generations ago—older than estimates in other African-admixed populations. Some P. falciparum-protective alleles were also more frequent in Oman than in other Arabian Peninsula populations, but African ancestry was not enriched, indicating no evidence of adaptive admixture driven by P. falciparum pressure. The authors conclude that Oman's long history with East African populations led to early introduction and selection of Duffy null alleles and may have influenced regional P. vivax prevalence.

articles.headline: Computational tool prioritizes conserved P. falciparum T cell epitopes for vaccines
articles.title_original: TEpiNom: A computational framework integrating population data to prioritize Plasmodium falciparum T cell epitopes.
articles.authors: Laurenson AJ, Pierce BG, Takala-Harrison S, Laurens MB
articles.details: Researchers built TEpiNom, a decision-support framework integrating P. falciparum sequence diversity, predicted T cell epitope–HLA binding (NetMHCpan-4.1 and NetMHCIIpan-4.1), and HLA allele frequencies to prioritize conserved epitopes. They analyzed 42 candidate proteins, generated consensus sequences from 18 African countries, and incorporated HLA frequencies from 24 sub-Saharan populations, using integer linear programming with thresholds of >95% conservation and median binding rank <10%. The tool identified 2265 conserved MHC I and 1992 MHC II epitopes across parasite life stages; prioritized MHC I epitopes from pre-erythrocytic antigens FabZ, FabG, p36, and PKG achieved 100% predicted inter-locus MHC I coverage. The framework aims to streamline preclinical vaccine development.

articles.headline: Greater perceived control of malaria linked to higher vaccine hesitancy in Ghana
articles.title_original: Impact of caregivers' perceptions of malaria on their hesitancy toward childhood malaria vaccination.
articles.authors: Kretchy IA, Bonful HA, Opoku-Mireku M, Koduah A, Opare-Addo M, Marfo AFA, Atobrah D, Klobodu HK, Alhassan Y, Kretchy JP, Opuni KFM
articles.details: This cross-sectional household survey of 2590 adults across six regions of Ghana measured malaria vaccine hesitancy using an adapted seven-item Oxford COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy scale and malaria perceptions via an adapted Brief Illness Perception Questionnaire. The prevalence of malaria vaccine hesitancy was 44.8%. Greater perceived control and understanding of malaria was associated with increased hesitancy (adjusted prevalence ratio 1.15; 95% CI 1.09–1.21; p < 0.001), along with region, marital status, ethnicity, housing, employment, existing health conditions, and alcohol intake. The authors conclude that familiarity with malaria weakens preventive behavior and recommend locally resonant, language-appropriate communication.

articles.headline: Iron-oxide nanoparticles boost antibody response to blood-stage malaria vaccine in mice
articles.title_original: Iron-oxide nanoparticles (SPIONs) enhance malaria vaccine antibody response.
articles.authors: Al-Abboodi A, Falih IQ, Al-Asadi M, Hussein BA, Al-Saady MAAJ, Abdullah TA, Al-Saady M, Nhat T, Le ND, Le PC
articles.details: In BALB/c mice (n = 8/group), carboxylated superparamagnetic iron-oxide nanoparticles (Fe3O4-COOH SPIONs) were tested as a carrier for the recombinant 19-kDa C-terminal fragment of P. falciparum merozoite surface protein-1 (PfMSP-1). Mice received subcutaneous SPIONs alone, soluble PfMSP-1 (25 μg, days 0/21/42), a single SPION-PfMSP-1 dose (0.25 mg SPIONs + 25 μg antigen), or three-dose SPION-PfMSP-1, with serum IgG assessed by ELISA. SPIONs alone induced no detectable antigen-specific IgG and soluble antigen produced only moderate titers, while SPION-bound antigen significantly enhanced responses; the three-dose SPION-PfMSP-1 schedule gave the highest, most durable titers. The authors support SPIONs as a delivery platform for blood-stage malaria antigens.

articles.headline: Avian malaria parasite P. juxtanucleare highly prevalent in Thai chickens
articles.title_original: Molecular prevalence and phylogenetic characterization of Plasmodium juxtanucleare in Thai native chickens and fighting cocks across Kalasin Province, Northeast Thailand.
articles.authors: Insee J, Thanasuwan S
articles.details: This cross-sectional survey collected 181 blood samples from Thai native chickens (n = 112) and fighting cocks (n = 69) across 18 districts of Kalasin Province, Thailand, between January and April 2025, detecting Plasmodium by PCR targeting the mitochondrial COXIII gene. Overall molecular prevalence was 62.98% (114/181), higher in native chickens (67.85%) than fighting cocks (55.07%) but not statistically significant (p > 0.05); prevalence varied significantly among districts (20%–100%, p < 0.05). Sequencing revealed two haplotypes clustering in Clade A with 99%–100% identity to Plasmodium juxtanucleare. The authors confirm hyper-endemic circulation and local genetic diversity, highlighting the need for molecular surveillance and vector control.

articles.headline: Sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance markers widespread in Indian P. falciparum over three decades
articles.title_original: Genetic profiling of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance markers in Indian Plasmodium falciparum field isolates.
articles.authors: Narang G, Jakhan J, Gupta D, Singh V
articles.details: This molecular surveillance study analyzed 307 P. falciparum field isolates collected across 14 Indian states, successfully sequencing 243 for Pfdhfr and 241 for Pfdhps mutations by Sanger sequencing, with phylogenetic and selection analyses. The most frequent mutations were Pfdhfr S108N (77.8%) and C59R (61.7%), and Pfdhps A437G (31.9%) and K540E/N (4.98%); 83% of isolates carried resistant alleles, with double and triple mutants predominating and weak positive selection at Pfdhfr 51 and Pfdhps 436/540. The authors conclude SP resistance is widespread with strong regional differences, and the rise of higher-order mutants, especially from the North-East, threatens India's 2030 malaria elimination target.

articles.headline: Review urges stronger quality assurance for malaria rapid diagnostic tests
articles.title_original: Quality assurance in rapid diagnostic tests for accurate diagnosis of malaria.
articles.authors: Ahmed N, Yadav A, Ranjha R, Anvikar AR, Bharti PK
articles.details: This review discusses the role of quality assurance in ensuring the reliability of rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) for malaria, noting that microscopy has long been the reference method while RDTs have transformed point-of-care diagnosis with billions of units distributed. The authors argue that inaccurate test performance can cause misdiagnosis, inappropriate treatment, increased mortality, and potential emergence of drug-resistant strains, and that strengthening quality assurance frameworks is essential to safeguard diagnostic accuracy and support malaria elimination. No quantitative study results are reported.

articles.headline: Parasite protein PyTRAg7 drives splenic adhesion via vimentin in mice
articles.title_original: Plasmodium yoelii tryptophan-rich antigen 7 mediates infected erythrocyte adhesion to splenic fibroblasts via binding to vimentin.
articles.authors: Zhu Y, Zhang H, Sun Y, Han S, Cheng Y
articles.details: Using protein binding assays and gene-edited parasites in a murine Plasmodium yoelii model, this study investigated tryptophan-rich antigen 7 (PyTRAg7) in spleen parasite-host interactions. PyTRAg7 was shown to bind vimentin on mouse splenic fibroblasts, activating NF-κB p65 signaling and increasing ICAM-1 and integrin β1 expression. Deletion of PyTRAg7 reduced infected red blood cell adhesion to splenic fibroblasts, lowered splenic parasite burden, delayed parasitemia onset, prolonged host survival, and preserved splenic architecture with reduced hemozoin deposition. The authors identify PyTRAg7 as a mediator of cytoadherence and splenic remodeling and a potential intervention target relevant to human malaria parasites.

articles.headline: Comparison of four P. falciparum culture synchronization methods evaluated quantitatively
articles.title_original: Comparative evaluation of synchronization methods for Plasmodium falciparum: Efficiency, practicality and experimental applicability.
articles.authors: Deng Y, Yin Y, Li Y, Xu J, Han JH, Liu Y, Lu F
articles.details: This in vitro study quantitatively compared four synchronization methods for P. falciparum 3D7 cultures—5% D-sorbitol, isosmotic Percoll-Sorbitol (60% PS), discontinuous Percoll-Sorbitol gradients (70%-40% PS), and the Plasmion method—using microscopy and flow cytometry, with 20 nm polystyrene fluorescent microspheres (PFMs20) as a novel tool to validate mature-stage enrichment. D-sorbitol achieved 88.37% ring-stage purity; 60% PS reached a PFMs20 binding rate of 87.47%; 70%-40% PS showed 88.12% binding in its schizont-enriched fraction and enabled hemozoin-layer separation; Plasmion yielded 73.37%. The authors propose a decision matrix: sorbitol for ring-stage studies, Percoll gradients for stage-specific work, and Plasmion for resource-limited settings, pending further validation.

articles.headline: Opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome after falciparum malaria in HIV-positive patient
articles.title_original: Opsoclonus myoclonus syndrome in an HIV patient, following plasmodium falciparum infection.
articles.authors: Dominique ETH, Caroline K, Daniel GM, Tchokote SE, Mélanie MA, Laura N, Gishlen I, Sandrine MD, Sandra DGN, Mahaman SAT, Raissa MTJ, Yacouba MN
articles.details: This case report describes a 47-year-old HIV-positive woman on Tenofovir-Lamivudine-Atazanavir with undetectable viral load who developed opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome (OMS) two weeks after treatment for severe Plasmodium falciparum malaria. She presented with multidirectional ocular saccades, trunk myoclonus, aggressive behavior, sleep disturbances, and severe ataxia; post-infectious OMS was diagnosed after excluding HIV-related CNS infection and a paraneoplastic cause. High-dose IV methylprednisolone gave partial improvement, and adding rituximab led to complete symptom resolution and restored functional autonomy. The authors suggest a possible link between malaria and OMS in HIV and highlight IV corticosteroid plus rituximab as an alternative to IV immunoglobulins in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Commentary: malaria deaths rising despite vaccines
articles.title_original: Vaccines mean malaria deaths should be falling - not rising.
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: New method isolates live P. vivax merozoites, shows NK cell activation
articles.title_original: Accessing invasion-competent Plasmodium vivax merozoites reveals antibody-dependent NK cell activation.
articles.authors: Salvador J, Guselli L, Yeat R, Lay S, Thol T, Ya N, Feufack-Donfack L, Heng S, Bohaud C, Orban A, Baldor L, Tat B, Chitnis C, Cantaert T, Popovici J
articles.details: Researchers developed a laboratory platform to obtain viable, invasion-competent Plasmodium vivax merozoites directly from ex vivo clinical isolates, overcoming the lack of continuous in vitro culture. They used the cGMP-dependent protein kinase inhibitor ML10 to reversibly halt egress and synchronize parasites at the segmented schizont stage, then mechanically released merozoites via liposome-based extrusion, achieving recovery rates exceeding 75% while preserving structural integrity and invasive capacity. Using this platform, they showed that antibody-opsonized P. vivax merozoites activate human natural killer cells consistent with antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity, enabling direct study of merozoite invasion biology and immune responses.

articles.headline: Burundi: no artemisinin-resistance markers but delayed clearance, multidrug resistance widespread
articles.title_original: Border Region Surveillance of Malaria Drug Resistance, Northern Burundi, 2023-2024.
articles.authors: Niyomwungere D, Sinarinzi P, Caspar E, Thiebaut L, Strubel PE, Tibiri YNG, Ma L, Rurihafi J, Ndizeyimana P, Hakizimana P, Niyizompa N, Witonze A, Ndayiragije V, Ndayisenga F, Ndikumukiza M, Niyonkuru O, Niyonsaba JC, Bangirinama C, Bigirimana JL, Ntahondi N, Mugisha L, Nibakire M, Nzoyikorera N, Sinzinkayo D, Menard D, Nyandwi J
articles.details: In a drug-resistance surveillance study of 423 children under 5 with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria across 8 health facilities in northern Burundi (December 2023–June 2024), children received artemether/lumefantrine with only the first dose directly observed. On day 3, 4.5% remained parasitemic, but no pfkelch13 mutations (validated or candidate ART-R markers) were detected. Antifolate and 4-aminoquinoline resistance markers were widespread: the dhfr triple mutant N51I/C59R/S108N was nearly fixed (92%), dhps double and triple mutants were common (41% and 47%), and pfcrt CVIET predominated (84%). The authors warn that delayed clearance and near-fixed multidrug-resistant haplotypes call for strengthened efficacy and molecular surveillance.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence 24% across 19 sub-Saharan countries; lowest with treated nets
articles.title_original: Spatial epidemiology of malaria by bed net utilization in 19 sub-Saharan African countries: a DHS-based study (2013-2023).
articles.authors: Yitageasu G, Demoze L
articles.details: This community-based cross-sectional study used the most recent Demographic and Health Survey data from 19 sub-Saharan African countries, analyzing 126,424 individuals who underwent malaria rapid diagnostic testing across 11,594 clusters. Pooled malaria prevalence was 24.0% (95% CI 18–30%), highest in Central Africa (26%), then West Africa (25%) and East Africa (20%). Prevalence was highest among those with no bed net (32.75%), followed by untreated net users (28.91%), and lowest among insecticide-treated net users (13.23%). Spatial analysis showed significant clustering (Global Moran's I = 0.159, p ≤ 0.001), with hotspots in Benin, Burkina Faso, Togo, Uganda, Rwanda, and parts of the Republic of the Congo and Mozambique. The authors call for targeted control in identified hotspots.

articles.headline: AMA1 c1L haplotypes not linked to malaria symptoms in Malian children
articles.title_original: Distribution of Plasmodium falciparum apical membrane antigen-1 cluster one loop haplotypes and their association with malaria symptoms in Bandiagara, Mali.
articles.authors: Ouattara A, Adams M, Niangaly A, Coulibaly D, Kone AK, Traore K, Cissoko Y, Kouriba B, Diallo DA, Plowe CV, Doumbo OK, Djimdé A, Laurens MB, Takala-Harrison S, Thera MA
articles.details: Using ama1 gene sequences from samples of 100 children in a three-year malaria incidence study in Bandiagara, Mali, researchers tested whether variants of the P. falciparum apical membrane antigen-1 cluster one loop (c1L), a blood-stage vaccine candidate target of invasion-inhibiting antibodies, were associated with clinical malaria phenotypes. After adjusting for participant location, specific c1L haplotypes were not associated with malaria symptoms, and none of the most frequent haplotypes were linked to clinical malaria (defined as temperature >37.5 °C and parasitemia ≥2,500 parasites/µL). The authors suggest malaria symptoms may be driven by infection with new alleles rather than alleles already encountered by an individual.

articles.headline: Climate change reshaping malaria control across six African countries
articles.title_original: Climate change and malaria control in Africa: country experiences and strategic responses.
articles.authors: Yahouedo AG, Merle CS, Diallo I, Ndiop M, Muhire A, Mukashema C, Mutabazi A, Hakizimana E, Muteba C, Kaseba AN, Tanoh MA, Aimain AS, Coulibaly KA, Razanakotomalala V, Diar MSI, Djaskano MI, Kodindo I, Ayala D, Caminade C, Diouf I, de Chevigny T, Ngou O, Pothin E, Girond F, Tchouatieu AM, Fleutelot E, Noseda V, Deuve JL
articles.details: This manuscript synthesizes testimonies from six African countries (Chad, Côte d'Ivoire, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Madagascar, Rwanda, and Senegal) on how climate variability has recently influenced malaria epidemiology and how national malaria control programmes are adapting. It reports correlations between changing rainfall patterns, flooding, expansion of mosquito vector habitats, and malaria transmission. Adaptive strategies include integrating climate and health data, strengthening surveillance and early warning systems, community engagement, multisectoral collaboration, and leveraging technology. The authors note systemic barriers (limited data integration, infrastructural deficits, behavioural factors, financial constraints) and call for sustained political commitment, technical capacity, and long-term investment in climate-resilient health systems.

articles.headline: ZY19489 resistance restores piperaquine and chloroquine activity in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Collateral hypersensitivity between ZY19489 and piperaquine neutralizes PfCRT-mediated drug efflux and Plasmodium falciparum resistance.
articles.authors: Okombo J, Ndiaye T, Qahash T, Moura IMR, Gil-Iturbe E, Hagenah LM, Bridgford JL, Bonatto V, Ward KE, Yeo T, Narwal SK, Orta LV, Anderson I, Dhingra SK, Pasaje CFA, Park H, Kim J, Guido RVC, Jiménez-Diaz MB, Angulo-Barturen I, Niles JC, Mancia F, Uhlemann AC, Mok S, Quick M, Winzeler EA, Leroy D, Llinás M, Thathy V, Fidock DA
articles.details: Researchers characterized ZY19489, a triaminopyrimidine antimalarial in a Phase Ib trial, using asexual blood-stage Plasmodium falciparum. Parasites pressured with ZY19489 acquired low-grade resistance via a novel mutation in the P. falciparum chloroquine resistance transporter (PfCRT) that caused slow growth and a substantial fitness cost; these parasites lost chloroquine resistance and became hypersusceptible to piperaquine. Proteoliposome uptake studies showed ZY19489 blocks mutant PfCRT-mediated piperaquine and chloroquine transport. Metabolomics showed reduced short hemoglobin-derived peptides and accumulation of pyrimidine deoxynucleotides. The authors propose pairing ZY19489 with piperaquine as a resistance-refractory combination and a marker for tracking resistance.

articles.headline: Protocol for scoping review of African views on gene-drive mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Knowledge, perspectives, and risk perceptions on gene drive and genetically modified mosquitoes for malaria control among African stakeholders: a scoping review protocol.
articles.authors: Sogodogo A, Diarra S, Keita S, Diallo I, Hartley S, Telly N, Dolo H, Sanogo D, Kané F, Tangara CO, Diakité M, Sangho O, Kayentao K, Fritz H, Winch PJ, Doumbia S
articles.details: This is a scoping review protocol aiming to map African stakeholders' knowledge, perspectives, and risk perceptions regarding gene drive and genetically modified mosquitoes for malaria control. The planned search covers PubMed, Embase, Science Direct, Cochrane, and Google Scholar, with two independent reviewers screening titles/abstracts and full texts in Rayyan and references managed in Zotero, followed by thematic analysis of extracted data. No results are reported because the review has not yet been conducted. The protocol is registered on the Open Science Framework, and the authors intend the findings to improve community engagement.

articles.headline: Antimalarial compound library screen yields new nematode-killing chemotypes
articles.title_original: High-throughput phenotypic screening of Medicines for Malaria Venture's Hit Generation Library 1 identifies new nematocidal chemotypes.
articles.authors: Taki AC, Byrne JJ, Chang BCH, Laleu B, Wells TNC, Jabbar A, Gasser RB
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: A large-scale phenotypic screen tested 139,916 compounds (98.7% of the 141,786-compound Medicines for Malaria Venture Hit Generation Library 1) against exsheathed third-stage larvae of Haemonchus contortus, with selective cross-species evaluation in Caenorhabditis elegans, using infrared-based motility and developmental assays in 384-well format (mean Z' = 0.799 ± 0.012). They identified 272 primary hits (0.194%) and confirmed 110 active compounds; 39 had IC50 < 10 μM and 33 caused complete developmental arrest at ≤12.5 μM. Four of the 39 were non-toxic to HepG2 cells, and 16 met criteria for medicinal-chemistry progression. The authors conclude antimalarial libraries can yield selective nematocidal scaffolds.

articles.headline: Plasmodium vivax gametocytes in bone marrow with pancytopenia and splenomegaly
articles.title_original: A mysterious foe in a case of pancytopenia with splenomegaly: Plasmodium vivax gametocytes in the bone marrow.
articles.authors: Patel GR, Singh V
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Gut microbiome composition predicts malaria parasite burden
articles.title_original: Gut check: converging evidence links microbiome to malaria risk.
articles.authors: Berry AA
articles.details: This perspective highlights work by Gustin et al. showing that pre-infection gut microbiome composition predicts the level of Plasmodium parasitemia in both rhesus macaques and human volunteers. Convergent evidence across the two systems pointed to the genus Bifidobacterium as potentially protective, suggesting the gut microbiome may be an underappreciated determinant of malaria parasite burden beyond host genetics and immunity.

articles.headline: How malaria parasites coordinate var gene switching for immune evasion
articles.title_original: Mechanistic insights into coordinated var transcriptional switching in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Visone JE, Florini F, Hadjimichael E, Patel V, Deitsch KW
articles.details: This mechanistic study examines how Plasmodium falciparum switches expression of the multi-copy var gene family, which encodes the surface antigen PfEMP1 underlying antigenic variation and chronic infection. The authors show that transient activation of the var2csa locus mediates var switching, while non-coding RNAs from the locus repress var2csa transcription and modulate switching frequency. An upstream open reading frame in the var2csa 5'-UTR destabilizes the mRNA via the nonsense-mediated RNA decay pathway, promoting activation of an alternative var gene.

articles.headline: Review: single antimalarial drugs that hit multiple parasite targets
articles.title_original: Polypharmacology in malaria treatment: single drugs, multiple mechanisms, greater impact.
articles.authors: Milić M, Obi P, Vanderwal CD, Ben Mamoun C, Le Roch KG
articles.details: This review explores polypharmacology in malaria treatment, the strategy of designing single drugs with multiple targets to achieve combination-therapy-like efficacy in one compound. It discusses how natural-product-derived drugs such as quinine and artemisinin attack Plasmodium on multiple fronts simultaneously, and how multitarget mechanisms reduce the likelihood of resistance. The authors argue that harnessing polypharmacology in antimalarial drug design could prolong therapeutic durability against drug-resistant parasites.

articles.headline: ACT partner-drug resistance markers found in P. falciparum in northern Brazil
articles.title_original: Emergence of artemisinin-based combination therapy resistance markers in Plasmodium falciparum from the Brazilian tri-border region of the Guiana Shield.
articles.authors: Salazar YEAR, Rezende AM, Puça MCSB, Louzada J, Mascarenhas MEP, Lagström S, Fletcher D, Oliveira-Ferreira J, Gil JP, de Sousa TN
articles.details: Using targeted deep sequencing, researchers analyzed 99 Plasmodium falciparum isolates collected in Roraima State, northern Brazil (2016-2020), assessing variants in pfcrt, pfmdr1, and pfk13 and copy number variation in pfpm2 and pfpm3. The pfmdr1-NFD haplotype was found in 6% (6/99), pfcrt-C350R in 7% (7/99), pfpm2 and pfpm3 amplifications in 5% (5/94) and 6% (6/97), and the pfpm2/3 hybrid in 4% (4/99); no validated or candidate pfk13 artemisinin partial-resistance mutations were detected. This is the first report of the pfpm2/3 hybrid and pfmdr1-NFD haplotype in Brazil, and the variants are linked to reduced susceptibility to lumefantrine and piperaquine, underscoring the need for continued genomic surveillance.

articles.headline: Review: manipulating Anopheles gut microbiome as a malaria control strategy
articles.title_original: Exploring the gut micriobiome in Anopheles mosquitoes: A promising new approach to malaria control.
articles.authors: Minwuyelet A, Atenafu G
articles.details: This critical review evaluates manipulation of the Anopheles mosquito gut microbiome as a complementary malaria control approach, given that insecticide resistance is eroding conventional tools such as insecticide-treated bed nets and indoor residual spraying. It examines bacterial, fungal, and viral microbiome components and mechanisms including immune modulation, nutrient competition, direct antiparasitic activity, and interference with mosquito physiology to block Plasmodium transmission. The authors emphasize key caveats—laboratory bias, oversimplified microbial consortia, context-dependent outcomes, and ecological uncertainties—that challenge field deployment.

articles.headline: Two divergent VAR2CSA lineages found in Thai P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Two divergent VAR2CSA lineages with distinct evolutionary signatures circulate in Plasmodium falciparum from Thailand.
articles.authors: Chaikitgosiyakul S, Kuamsab N, Tia T, Putaporntip C, Jongwutiwes S
articles.details: Researchers sequenced complete var2csa coding sequences (9.1–9.9 kb) from 31 symptomatic Plasmodium falciparum isolates collected in five endemic provinces in Thailand using long-range PCR and Sanger sequencing. They found 24 distinct haplotypes with high diversity (h = 0.97 ± 0.02) and two co-circulating lineages: a dominant 3D7-type and a divergent Dd2-type (7 isolates) with an extended ID4 region (645 bp). Nucleotide diversity was significantly higher in the 3' region than the 5' region (π = 0.4253 vs. 0.1033, p < 0.0001). The authors conclude this bipartite variation in the placental malaria vaccine target VAR2CSA must be understood to design broadly protective vaccines.

articles.headline: New bisquinoline compounds kill drug-resistant malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Designing novel bisquinoline antimalarials from historical 4-aminoquinolines to combat drug-resistant malaria.
articles.authors: Handford MJ, Li Y, Riscoe T, Zhang X, Kelly JX, Riscoe MK
articles.details: This structure-activity relationship study optimized bisquinoline antimalarial analogs based on the 4-aminoquinoline ADC-028, featuring a 4-anilinoquinoline core and varied bridging linkers. Several analogs showed potent in vitro activity (IC50 < 20 nM) against both drug-sensitive and multidrug-resistant Plasmodium falciparum strains with favorable cytotoxicity. Compound 25 had improved metabolic stability (t1/2 = 121 min) and potent in vivo efficacy (ED50 = 0.32 mg/kg/day), achieving complete curative protection at a reduced dose versus ADC-028, despite lower oral bioavailability (F = 43%) and a shorter half-life (T1/2 = 27.2 h). The authors highlight this as a promising route to antimalarials that overcome resistance.

articles.headline: 5-ALA fails to boost artemisinin activity against malaria parasites
articles.title_original: 5-ALA does not potentiate dihydroartemisinin against Plasmodium falciparum malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Behrens HM, Henshall IG, Spielmann T
articles.details: In this Correspondence, Behrens and colleagues report that the small molecule 5-aminolaevulinic acid (5-ALA), which potentiates artemisinin activity in cancer, does not produce the same effect in malaria parasites. They conclude that co-administering artemisinin and 5-ALA does not appear to be a promising strategy for improving malaria treatment. No quantitative results are reported in the abstract.

articles.headline: Digestion gene catalog mapped in coastal malaria vector mosquito
articles.title_original: Genomic profiling of digestion related enzymes in Anopheles aquasalis a major coastal neotropical malaria vector.
articles.authors: Alencar RM, Sepulveda CCP, D'Elia GMA, de Souza IB, Duarte APM, Santana RA, Nacif-Pimenta R, Godoy RSM, de Melo GC, Monteiro WM, Secundino NFC, Koerich LB, Pimenta PFP
articles.details: Using reciprocal BLAST against annotated Anopheles gambiae digestion proteins, plus orthology and phylogenetic analyses in MEGA, researchers identified digestion-related genes in the genome of the neotropical malaria vector Anopheles aquasalis. They found 241 digestion-related genes: 56 for carbohydrate digestion, 51 for lipid digestion, and 134 for protein digestion, closely related to those of Anopheles darlingi and Anopheles albimanus. The authors highlight genes of interest for vector control and transmission-blocking vaccines, including aminopeptidase N1, carboxypeptidase B, and midgut serine proteases (trypsins and chymotrypsins) that can affect Plasmodium development.

articles.headline: pfhrp2/3 gene deletions affecting rapid tests found in travellers
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum histidine rich protein 2/3 gene deletions affecting rapid diagnostic test performance in travellers.
articles.authors: Detzlhofer O, Lötsch F, Kirchner J, Schneider R, Weiss-Tessbach M, Winkler S, Walochnik J, Schubert L
articles.details: In this traveller cohort, Plasmodium falciparum pfhrp2/3 gene deletions were detected in 4 of 115 patients (3.5%), with no double deletions observed. Notably, 51.3% of patients had visited countries anticipated to surpass the WHO 5% pfhrp2/3 threshold by 2028. The authors conclude that continued monitoring of pfhrp2/3 deletions in non-endemic settings is needed to guide testing strategies.

articles.headline: Few refugees need screening to find one falciparum infection
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum infections and number needed to screen among refugees arriving from sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Bergstrand T, Wyss K, Eliasson I, Kaldhusdal V, Requena-Méndez A, Färnert A
articles.details: This modelling study estimated subclinical Plasmodium falciparum infections among sub-Saharan African refugees arriving in Sweden by applying Malaria Atlas Project country-specific, age-standardized prevalences to demographic data on 56,248 asylum applicants and quota refugees. An estimated 3930 (7%) were infected, including 1789 children, 2141 adults, and 851 women of reproductive age, with about 491 asymptomatic infections per year (range 356-546) and 80% from high/moderate-transmission countries. The number needed to screen was 2-14 for arrivals from high- and moderate-transmission countries. The authors conclude few migrants need screening to detect one case, which can guide screening strategies.

articles.headline: Transformer-based deep learning model for malaria cell image classification
articles.title_original: ISTNet: a multi-scale transformer-based architecture for malaria cell classification.
articles.authors: Wu P, Gao X, Yu H
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Correction notice: endothelial receptors in fatal pediatric cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Correction to: Mapping the expression of endothelial adhesion receptors for Plasmodium falciparum-infected erythrocytes in fatal cerebral malaria in Malawian children.
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Piperaquine-resistance markers shifted across Southeast Asia over 25 years
articles.title_original: Changing genetic profiles of Plasmodium falciparum piperaquine resistance in Southeast Asia over 25 years.
articles.authors: Srisutham S, Suwannasin K, Saejeng A, Khantikul N, Sugaram R, Phyo AP, Proux S, Pongvongsa T, Nguon C, Tripura R, Chau NH, Lek D, Dang Trung NH, Peto TJ, Callery JJ, van der Pluijm RW, Amaratunga C, Dhorda M, von Seidlein L, Mayxay M, Thuy-Nhien NT, Newton PN, Day NPJ, Ashley EA, Nosten FH, Smithuis FM, White NJ, Dondorp AM, Imwong M
articles.details: This molecular surveillance study analyzed 898 Plasmodium falciparum isolates from Thailand, Myanmar, Cambodia, Laos, and Vietnam (1995-2023) for pfcrt mutations, pfplasmepsin2 copy number, and microsatellite variation to track piperaquine resistance. In Cambodia, H97Y reached 15% and T93S 8%; in Srisaket/Ubon, Thailand, I218F was 24.44%, G353V 20%, T93S 17%, F145I 13%, H97Y 4%; Vietnam showed T93S at 21%, while Yala and Kayin remained wild-type. Over 65% of pfcrt-mutant parasites also carried pfplasmepsin2 amplification, which in Cambodia peaked at 88% in 2015 after DHA-piperaquine introduction. Reduced diversity around mutant alleles indicated selective sweeps; authors stress ongoing genetic monitoring to preserve ACT efficacy.

articles.headline: Review: malaria in pregnancy pathogenesis, immunity, and prevention
articles.title_original: Malaria in pregnancy at the frontline: a delicate balance.
articles.authors: Kokuhennadige V, Aitken E, Rogerson S
articles.details: This concise review summarizes recent findings on malaria in pregnancy, covering pathogenesis, immunity, offspring outcomes, and interventions, noting the burden has increased in Africa while decreasing elsewhere. Placental Plasmodium infection and inflammation drive maternal morbidity and adverse birth outcomes, with new evidence that antenatal infection affects infant immune and neurocognitive development, and protection accruing over successive pregnancies. Despite parasite resistance to sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine, it still improves birth weight as intermittent preventive treatment, whereas dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine, though more effective against parasitemia, does not improve birth outcomes alone or combined with SP. The authors highlight needs for first-trimester, blood-stage, and primigravidae-targeted vaccine interventions.

articles.headline: Blood group and α-globin variants linked to malaria susceptibility in India
articles.title_original: Genetic Determinants for Vivax Malaria Susceptibility in Chiplun (Ratnagiri), Maharashtra, India: A Case-Control Study.
articles.authors: Roshan S, Kanjaksha G, Arti S, Kanchan M, Jyoti J, Shivam S, Ajit G
articles.details: Case-control study enrolling 228 microscopically confirmed malaria cases and 2149 healthy controls in Chiplun taluka, Ratnagiri district, Maharashtra, India, testing ABO/Rh blood groups, α-globin gene deletions, CR1 polymorphisms, and Gerbich variants by agglutination, multiplex PCR, and PCR-RFLP. Blood group B+ was associated with increased risk (OR=1.36, 95% CI 1.02–1.80, p<0.05) and A+ appeared protective (OR=0.72, 95% CI 0.52–0.99, p<0.05). The homozygous-α3·7 deletion genotype showed markedly higher susceptibility (OR=3.33, 95% CI 2.00–5.53, p<0.001), contradicting the protective effect reported elsewhere. No associations were seen for Rh, CR1, or Gerbich variants.

articles.headline: Review of CRISPR-Cas9 for studying antimalarial drug resistance in Africa
articles.title_original: Innovative approach in malaria research: Harnessing CRISPR-Cas9 for antimalarial drug-resistance studies in Africa.
articles.authors: Ajibaye O, Dada IS, Mbye H, Nwankwo IC, Oriero E, Amambua-Ngwa A
articles.details: This review and systematic scoping review surveys the CRISPR-Cas9 genome-editing system—its origin, evolution, and application—to study Plasmodium falciparum antimalarial drug resistance, with emphasis on African studies. The authors examine CRISPR's role in elucidating resistance pathways, validating molecular markers, and enhancing diagnostics, and map the geographic scope of CRISPR-related malaria research across Africa. They report significantly low application and adoption to date. Despite technological and regulatory barriers, the authors conclude CRISPR holds strong potential to accelerate antimalarial drug discovery and precision medicine, supporting control and elimination efforts.

articles.headline: Blood-count ratios as supportive markers for malaria in pregnant women
articles.title_original: The clinical significance of monocyte-to-lymphocyte and neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratios in malaria infection and parasitaemia among pregnant women in Nanoro, Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Traore O, Bihoun B, Koné FT, Lengane AL, Tahita MC, Kere AR, Bonko MDA, Compaore ZMJ, Dabone KCS, Yaro APK, Zango SH, Kaboré B, Kiemde F, Bouda I, Rouamba T, Ilboudo H, Natama HM, Sorgho H, Valéa I, Traoré-Coulibaly M, Belem AMG, Tinto H
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: Study of 270 third-trimester pregnant women attending antenatal care at two health centres in Nanoro District, Burkina Faso, assessing the neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR) and monocyte-to-lymphocyte ratio (MLR) from routine blood counts against malaria diagnosed by microscopy and rapid diagnostic tests. MLR was significantly higher in infected women (0.48 vs 0.40, p=0.021) and NLR was elevated with high parasitaemia (3.06 vs 2.55, p=0.029). MLR gave ROC values of 0.60 for infection and 0.61 for parasitaemia, and independently predicted malaria (OR=4.19) and high parasitaemia (OR=7.16). The authors conclude MLR and NLR may serve as supportive markers, though diagnostic performance was modest and further validation is needed.

articles.headline: Ferrostatin-1 reduces ferroptosis and eases experimental cerebral malaria in mice
articles.title_original: Ferrostatin-1 alleviates experimental cerebral malaria by regulating immune cell functions and brain endothelial ferroptosis.
articles.authors: Yao S, Zhou X, Liao T, Yao C, Sun M, Gou H, Hu X, Liu J, Zheng L, Zhao Y, Cao Y
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: Using a murine cerebral malaria model with Plasmodium berghei ANKA (PbA) infection, the authors found iron metabolic disorders and ferroptosis in spleen and brain, marked by iron, ROS, and lipid peroxide accumulation, upregulated TFRC and ACSL4, and downregulated SLC7A11 and GPX4. The ferroptosis inhibitor Ferrostatin-1 (Fer-1) ameliorated iron dysregulation, reduced lipid peroxidation, decreased parasitemia, extended survival, eased neurological symptoms, and improved blood-brain barrier integrity. Mechanistically, Fer-1 enhanced dendritic cell antigen presentation (MHC II, CD80/86), promoted M1 macrophage polarization, increased IFN-γ+ Th1 and Treg cells, and inhibited endothelial ferroptosis—downregulating CXCL9/CXCL10 and ICAM-1/VCAM-1 and reducing cerebral CD8+ T-cell infiltration. The authors conclude Fer-1 offers a novel ferroptosis-targeted strategy for cerebral malaria.

articles.headline: Computational screening identifies indole alkaloids as mosquito 3-HKT inhibitors
articles.title_original: Computational evaluation of indole alkaloids as inhibitors of 3-hydroxykynurenine transaminase in malaria vector control.
articles.authors: Makaye AI, Kwiyukwa LP, Paul L, Banyikwa A, Vuai SAH
articles.details: Computational study evaluating indole alkaloids as inhibitors of 3-hydroxykynurenine transaminase (3-HKT), an enzyme producing xanthurenic acid required for Plasmodium falciparum gametogenesis in Anopheles mosquitoes, using virtual screening, molecular dynamics (RMSD, RMSF, ROG), and MM/PBSA analyses. Spiroindolone (EP4), asperpenazine (SP4), and strychnine (ST6) showed the strongest binding free energies (−7.38, −7.21, −7.12 kcal/mol for PDB 2CH1; −7.47, −7.13, −7.08 for 2CH2). MM/PBSA gave EP4 −69.658/−77.025, SP4 −68.658/−75.947, and ST6 −49.294/−39.859 kJ/mol, with average RMSF ~0.3 Å. The authors conclude EP4 and SP4 are stable candidates for anti-mosquito insecticide design, while ST6 needs optimization, pending in vitro and in vivo validation.

articles.headline: Perspective: salivary gland invasion as a target for genetic mosquito control
articles.title_original: Genetic control of malaria transmission: a salivary gland-centric perspective.
articles.authors: Eze S, Nguyen VT, Morvay A, Lee Y, Burini BC
articles.details: This article examines genetic control of malaria transmission with a focus on the mosquito salivary gland. The authors note that although many stages of Plasmodium development in the mosquito have been characterized, the sporozoite's ability to recognize and invade the salivary glands remains poorly understood, involving a complex interplay of proteins and other molecules. They argue that better understanding this critical step will help design more effective transgenes for genetic control strategies, particularly mosquito population replacement. No quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic P. falciparum common in wet season, peaks in June, Lagos
articles.title_original: Longitudinal Assessment of Asymptomatic Malaria Infections in a Suburban Community in Lagos, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Owoloye A, Ligali FC, Musa AZ, Aina O, Idowu ET, Oyebola KM
articles.details: A study of asymptomatic malaria screened 1,860 asymptomatic individuals for Plasmodium falciparum using rapid diagnostic test (RDT), microscopy, and PCR. Detection rates were 2.7% (50/1860) by microscopy, 8.8% (163/1860) by RDT, and 11.7% (218/1860) by PCR; using PCR as ground truth, RDT sensitivity was 34.4% versus 22.0% for microscopy, while microscopy had higher positive predictive value (96.0% vs 46%). Asymptomatic infection was more frequent in the wet season (14.9%, peaking at 36.0% [54/150] in June) than the dry season (7.2%), in school-age children 5–14 years (14.1%), and in males (12.9%) versus females (10.7%). The authors call for increased nationwide surveillance.

articles.headline: Third parasite esterase PARE aids fatty acid scavenging in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Defining the minimal enzymatic requirements for fatty acid scavenging from lysophosphatidylcholine by erythrocytic Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Liu J, Fike KR, Dalal S, Dapper C, Klemba M
articles.details: This in vitro study of intraerythrocytic Plasmodium falciparum investigated catabolism of host-derived lysophosphatidylcholine (LPC), a source of choline and fatty acids. Beyond the two known lysophospholipases XL2 and XLH4, the authors identified a third enzyme, 'prodrug activation and resistance esterase' (PARE), mediating low-level LPC hydrolysis. Loss of PARE alone had no effect, but combined with loss of XL2 and XLH4 it impaired scavenging of both choline and fatty acids; PARE/XL2/XLH4-deficient parasites could not complete a replication cycle in defined media with LPC as the sole exogenous fatty acid source. PARE is membrane-associated at the parasite periphery, implicating parasite-encoded enzymes rather than host erythrocyte enzymes in LPC catabolism.

articles.headline: Computational screen finds FDA drugs that could target PfEMP1 in cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Computational Drug Repositioning for Targeting PfEMP1: Potential Therapeutics for Cerebral Malaria in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Verma K, Patel K, Yadav A, Gopikrishnan M, Sharma R, Mohan M, Mani A, Bharti PK
articles.details: This computational drug-repositioning study screened FDA-approved drugs to target Plasmodium falciparum erythrocyte membrane protein 1 (PfEMP1), which facilitates sequestration of infected red blood cells in the microvasculature in cerebral malaria. Using receptor-based screening and ADME analysis, the top candidates were Lumacaftor, Vilazodone, Tucatinib, Lenvatinib, and Hydrocortisone Cypionate, which showed favorable docking energies of −9.1 to −8.3 kcal/mol and potential oral bioavailability. Molecular dynamics simulations, protein-ligand interaction analysis, and Gibbs free energy landscape analysis supported stable drug–PfEMP1 complexes. The authors propose these FDA-approved drugs as candidates for repurposing against cerebral malaria.

articles.headline: Malawi women show high acceptance of future maternal malaria vaccine
articles.title_original: Assessing Readiness for Future Maternal Malaria Vaccines: Knowledge, Practices, and Vaccine Attitudes Among Women of Reproductive Age in Malawi.
articles.authors: Kaur M, D'Alessio F, Kajombo MC, Nkolokosa M, Olesen OF
articles.details: A facility-based cross-sectional survey of women assessed knowledge, practices, and attitudes toward a future maternal malaria vaccine addressing placental malaria. General malaria knowledge and preventive practices were high, and 53.4% reported fever during pregnancy. High knowledge (aOR 0.30, 95% CI 0.16–0.57) and perceived risk awareness (aOR 0.35, 95% CI 0.18–0.68) were lower in Mpemba than Thyolo, and biomedical healthcare service use was lower in Madziabango than Thyolo (aOR 0.47, 95% CI 0.23–0.96). Although 92% acknowledged possible harm, 97% reported willingness to accept a future maternal malaria vaccine, leading the authors to conclude that acceptability is high but context-specific delivery strategies are recommended.

articles.headline: Humidity reshapes temperature limits of invasive malaria mosquito Anopheles stephensi
articles.title_original: Humidity shapes the thermal niche of Anopheles stephensi, an invasive malaria vector.
articles.authors: Johnson B, Huxley PJ, Brown JJ, Hollingsworth BD, Bump ER, Laurent BS, Skrotzki J, Johnson LR, Pascual M, Wimberly MC, Mohanty A, Murdock CC
articles.details: A factorial life-table experiment crossed five relative humidities (30-90%) with seven temperatures (16-38°C) to test how humidity modifies the thermal performance of key adult fitness traits in Anopheles stephensi, an invasive urban malaria vector. When fitness traits were integrated into a demographic model, humidity markedly reshaped projected population fitness relative to temperature-only models, suppressing growth and contracting year-round suitability in hot, arid regions while enhancing fitness in more humid or high-elevation climates. The authors conclude that temperature-only approaches may mischaracterize both the magnitude and spatial structure of vector population fitness, and that multiple environmental drivers must be integrated into climatic suitability projections.

articles.headline: Acyl carrier protein is essential for malaria parasite apicoplast independent of fatty acid synthesis
articles.title_original: Acyl Carrier Protein is Essential for Apicoplast Biogenesis in Malaria Parasites Independent of Fatty Acid Synthesis.
articles.authors: Geher SWR, Falekun S, Pita-Aquino JN, Swift RP, Okada M, Jami-Alahmadi Y, Wohlschlegel JA, Prigge ST, Sigala PA
articles.details: Using gene knockout and ligand-dependent knockdown in blood-stage Plasmodium falciparum, the authors show that apicoplast acyl carrier protein (ACP) is essential, in contrast to other type-II fatty acid synthesis (FASII) enzymes that can be disrupted without a fitness defect. Loss of ACP impaired biosynthesis of essential isoprenoid precursors and blocked apicoplast biogenesis. Proximity biotinylation and biochemical studies showed ACP binds and stabilizes apicoplast pyruvate kinase II (PKII), the organelle's only known source of nucleoside triphosphates required for isoprenoid synthesis; ACP knockdown destabilized and lost PKII. The authors conclude ACP has an essential FASII-independent role at a central hub of apicoplast metabolism.

articles.headline: Adaptive entomological surveillance outperforms routine mosquito monitoring in Ghana and Mozambique
articles.title_original: Evaluation of the Entomological Adaptive Surveillance Framework for malaria vector monitoring: a comparative field trial with routine surveillance in Ghana and Mozambique.
articles.authors: Sedda L, Jalilian A, Opiyo M, Gowelo S, Marrenjo DJ, Atta-Obeng C, Boampong E, Oppong SK, Owusu-Akrofi O, Thomsen E, Tatarsky A, Malm KL, Candrinho B, Lobo NF
articles.details: This study compared a newly developed entomological adaptive surveillance framework (EASF) against routine National Malaria Control Programme surveillance in Ghana and Mozambique, evaluating both on Anopheles catch rates, model predictive performance, and the proportion of obsolete sampling locations. EASF used a Bayesian exceedance-probability design and a Bayesian spatiotemporal model with nugget effects on log-transformed counts to select adaptive sites. EASF outperformed routine surveillance on most metrics, yielding higher standardized mosquito catches, more stable predictions (lower coefficients of variation), and consistently fewer obsolete locations, though performance varied by country. The authors conclude EASF offers an effective, evidence-based way to improve surveillance precision while maintaining operational feasibility.

articles.headline: Malaria and helminth coinfection causes early red-cell changes in Nigerian under-fives
articles.title_original: Haematological responses in children under 5 years with malaria and soil-transmitted helminth coinfections in Delta State, Nigeria: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Odibo EO, Nmorsi OPG, Egwunyenga OA, Mgbere O
articles.details: A hospital-based cross-sectional study recruited 269 children under 5 years across the Delta South Senatorial District, Nigeria, diagnosing malaria by microscopy and soil-transmitted helminths (STH) by stool analysis, with haematological parameters measured by automated techniques. Co-infection was most common in toddlers aged 1-2 years (40.9%) and more frequent in males; Hb AA predominated (57.3%), followed by Hb AS (36.3%) and Hb SS (6.3%). Children with malaria, STH, or co-infection showed a macrocytic hypochromic pattern with increased mean corpuscular volume and reduced mean corpuscular haemoglobin concentration (p<0.001) despite only mild, non-significant reductions in haemoglobin and RBC counts; parasitaemia varied with age (p=0.033) but correlated weakly with red-cell indices. The authors conclude anaemia was influenced more by host factors than parasite density, with early red-cell alterations preceding overt anaemia.

articles.headline: Most Nigerian women with adequate antenatal visits miss recommended malaria preventive doses
articles.title_original: Prevalence and inequalities of missed opportunities for intermittent preventive therapy (IPTp) of malaria among pregnant women: an analysis of 2018 Nigeria DHS.
articles.authors: Ladan SM, Lima NP, Orimadegun AE, Wado YD, Akinyemi JO, Jatau AA, Blumenberg C
articles.details: A secondary analysis of the 2018 Nigeria Demographic and Health Survey assessed missed opportunities for intermittent preventive treatment with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (IPTp-SP), defined as women attending four or more antenatal visits but receiving fewer than three IPTp-SP doses, among 12,498 women aged 15-49 with a recent live birth. Missed opportunities were high (75%), representing 42.5% of the sample, with a pro-rich/pro-educated inequality. The worst-case scenario (<4 ANC visits and <3 doses) was highest among ages 40-49 (44.7%), rural residents (50.4%), women with no education (61.6%), and the poorest quintile (64.0%; all p<0.001); probability of the worst case was 75% among poorest uneducated women versus 5% among wealthiest educated women. The authors conclude IPTp-SP delivery during antenatal care is deficient and inequitable, warranting targeted policy action.

articles.headline: Helminth coinfection linked to milder malaria in young children
articles.title_original: Helminth coinfections mitigate clinical, parasitological, and immune outcomes in Mozambican children with malaria.
articles.authors: Cuamba I, Santano R, Grau-Pujol B, Vidal M, Aguilar R, Cossa A, Jairoce C, Mejia R, Nhabomba A, Muñoz J, Moncunill G, Dobaño C
articles.details: In a study of 441 malaria-symptomatic children aged 2-10 years in Manhiça District, Mozambique, infection was assessed by rapid diagnostic test, microscopy, and qPCR, with 30 cytokines and antibody responses to 12 P. falciparum antigens measured by Luminex. Among 74 children with malaria, 22% (16) were coinfected with soil-transmitted helminths (STH) and 78% (58) had P. falciparum only. STH coinfection was associated with lower parasite density (P=0.005), less fever (P=0.047), reduced pro- and anti-inflammatory cytokines, and elevated antibodies (e.g., SSP2, MSP3, RH5) and total IgE. Authors conclude coinfection may produce milder episodes and enhanced protective immunity.

articles.headline: Community health volunteers expand malaria testing and treatment access in Kenya
articles.title_original: Community health volunteers improve access to malaria case management in Siaya County, Kenya.
articles.authors: Odongo W, Seffren V, Obiet K, Seda B, Towett O, Zhou H, McDermott DP, Kariuki S, Staedke SG, Ter Kuile F, Kwambai TK, Samuels AM, Gutman JR
articles.details: This analysis evaluated community health volunteers (CHVs) for malaria case management in Siaya County, Kenya, using 1.4 million encounters from 936 CHVs and 86 health facilities across 713 villages (2021-2023). CHV coverage was adequate (one per ≤500 people) in 82.5% of villages versus 30% for facilities. CHVs managed 28.1% of all encounters, 12.1% of suspected malaria cases, and 15.6% of RDT-positive cases, and recorded higher test positivity than facilities (82.0% vs 64.3%). Incorporating CHV data raised malaria incidence estimates by 18% (467 vs 549 per 1,000). Authors conclude CHVs extend access and align with national guidelines.

articles.headline: One-third of under-five children malaria-positive in Zambian district
articles.title_original: Prevalence and factors associated with malaria amongst under-five children in Senga Hill District, Northern Province, Zambia, a community-based cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Shaba-Santu A, Moonga L, Ogata S, Hayashida K, Simuunza MC
articles.details: A community-based cross-sectional study assessed malaria among 216 under-five children in Senga Hill District, Zambia (November 2022 to August 2023), using a Care Start RDT detecting P. falciparum HRP2 and P. vivax pLDH, with risk factors collected by questionnaire and analyzed by logistic regression. Overall prevalence was 31% (95% CI 26-38%). Correct ITN hanging reduced odds of infection by 88% (OR 0.012), having two ITNs by 86% (OR 0.14), and indoor residual spraying by 58% (OR 0.42), while being male increased odds (OR 3.04, 95% CI 1.52-6.07). Authors urge strengthened ITN use and IRS acceptance.

articles.headline: Meta-analysis finds ivermectin does not consistently cut malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Efficacy of ivermectin for malaria vector control: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials.
articles.authors: Ramadan S, Talkhan A, Mansour A, Kanaan D, Abbas AN, Awad A, Sahel OB, Hamzeh LH, Alfaki A, Hany M, Elnouty M, Amin MHJ
articles.details: This PRISMA-guided systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated ivermectin for malaria vector control, including 10 randomized and cluster-randomized trials in adults totaling 63,192 participants, comparing ivermectin with placebo, standard care, or non-ivermectin controls. Community-level analyses showed no significant reduction in malaria prevalence, with very wide confidence intervals. Entomological trials showed increased mosquito mortality (RR 1.89; 95% CI 1.26-2.83), though heterogeneity was substantial (I2 >97%). Ivermectin was generally well tolerated with no significant increase in adverse events. Authors conclude current evidence does not demonstrate consistent reductions in malaria incidence or prevalence, and call for further large cluster-randomized trials.

articles.headline: Qualitative study reveals provider barriers to malaria control in Guinea
articles.title_original: Healthcare providers' knowledge, perceptions and practices of malaria control strategies in the Republic of Guinea: insights from a qualitative study.
articles.authors: Diallo EM, Langlet A, Onyango LA, Kourouma M, Kambou BM, Diallo FB, Bufferne A, Petrova A, Deyra M, Gay C, Camara A, Gerbaud L
