articles.details: A qualitative study (December 2022 to March 2023) explored healthcare providers' knowledge, perceptions, and practices of malaria control across nine health districts in Guinea, using 63 individual interviews and 18 focus groups analyzed via the Clark and Braun thematic approach and COREQ reporting. Analysis identified 264 modalities grouped into 45 subthemes and 9 themes. Providers showed high malaria knowledge but reported gaps in vector control (ITN distribution, use, larval destruction), delayed seasonal malaria chemoprevention, and non-compliance with case-management guidelines including overcharging and over-reliance on clinical diagnosis. Authors conclude persistent operational barriers—weak free case management, SMC implementation, and ITN design—require sustained improvement.

articles.headline: 2La inversion in Anopheles gambiae follows south-north cline in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Spatial distribution of the 2La chromosomal inversion in Anopheles gambiae populations across Nigeria: ecological associations and implications for malaria control.
articles.authors: Adeogun AO, Izekor RT, Babalola AS, Oyeniyi TA, Adetunji O, Adesoye OA, Wahedi J, Busari LO, George A, Ajayi S, Chabi J, Awolola ST
articles.details: This vector-genetics study karyotyped the 2La chromosomal inversion in Anopheles gambiae across 12 Nigerian states. Of 1,200 mosquitoes collected, 999 were An. gambiae s.l. (An. gambiae s.s. 39.7%, An. coluzzii 51.5%, An. arabiensis 8.7%), and 399 An. gambiae s.s. were karyotyped by PCR. A distinct south-north cline emerged: the 2La/2La homokaryotype predominated in arid northern savannas, while 2La+/2La+ dominated humid southern forests and mangroves. Inversion frequency correlated significantly with latitude (R2=0.68–0.73, P<0.001), with Hardy-Weinberg deviations only in Bayelsa and Kebbi. Authors conclude the inversion aids ecological adaptation, with operational relevance for vector control.

articles.headline: Microfluidic device shows blood flow affects malaria parasite invasion
articles.title_original: Microfluidic platform for automatic quantification of malaria parasite invasion under physiological flow conditions.
articles.authors: Kals E, Kals M, Introini V, Vodenicharski B, Kotar J, Rayner JC, Cicuta P
articles.details: Researchers developed a four-channel microfluidic device, each channel with dimensions similar to those of a post-capillary venule but with different flow velocities, to quantify Plasmodium falciparum egress and red blood cell invasion under flow, using newly developed custom video analysis that automates cell type identification and trajectory tracking. Both wild-type parasite lines and lines with deletions of invasion-related genes were tested. Deletion of erythrocyte binding antigen 175 (PfEBA175) significantly impaired invasion under flow but not in static culture. The authors conclude that flow conditions can critically affect parasite invasion in a genotype-dependent manner, establishing a method applicable to other processes affected by fluid motion such as cell adhesion, migration, and mechanotransduction.

articles.headline: Dual TLR adjuvant boosts antibodies to liver-stage malaria antigens in mice
articles.title_original: Poly(I:C) and R848 adjuvants elicit sizeable humoral immunity to liver stage malaria antigens.
articles.authors: Tandel N, Thakkar M, Ahuja M, Shah J, Trivedi DD, Pied S, Singh AP, Prajapati C, Tyagi RK, Dalai SK
articles.details: This mouse immunization study tested two liver-stage malaria antigens—P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein (PfCSP) and P. berghei liver stage tryptophan-rich protein (PbSLTRiP)—alone or combined, formulated with a dual TLR adjuvant combination of Poly(I:C) and R848. BALB/c and C57BL/6 mice received a multidose regimen, with humoral responses measured 30–80 days post-immunization via total IgG, IgG isotypes, and avidity. Co-administration of Poly(I:C) and R848 elicited greater antibody responses than antigen alone; PfCSP-specific IgG persisted to 80 days then declined gradually, while PbSLTRiP produced progressive increases in total IgG along with detectable IgG2c responses and modest changes in avidity. Differences between adjuvant regimens were limited and non-significant, indicating preliminary trends warranting further study.

articles.headline: Review: malaria-associated acute kidney injury drives mortality in endemic areas
articles.title_original: Malaria-Associated Acute Kidney Injury: A Key Driver of Mortality in Endemic Regions.
articles.authors: Olwit G, Gutanamuka TB, Chris-Uchendu HC, Mugisha AI
articles.details: This review, based on a focused PubMed/MEDLINE search (2015–2025) across age groups and Plasmodium species, synthesizes evidence on malaria-associated acute kidney injury (MAKI). It describes MAKI as a multifactorial complication of severe malaria involving intravascular hemolysis, microvascular obstruction, inflammation, endothelial dysfunction, and volume depletion. P. falciparum is most commonly implicated, with P. vivax and P. knowlesi increasingly recognized. MAKI is associated with prolonged hospitalization, greater need for renal replacement therapy, higher mortality, and risk of incomplete recovery progressing to chronic kidney disease; the authors stress early recognition, prompt management, and post-recovery renal monitoring, especially in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: School-aged children are major asymptomatic P. falciparum reservoir in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: School-aged children as a silent reservoir of Plasmodium falciparum: findings from a cross-sectional survey conducted in Burkina Faso in 2022.
articles.authors: Tarama CW, Soré H, Yade MS, Niyomwungere D, Débé S, Kinda R, Ganou A, Ménard D, Gansané A
articles.details: This cross-sectional survey enrolled 1,127 children aged 6 months to 10 years across Banfora, Orodara, and Gaoua districts of Burkina Faso in July 2022, using HRP2-based RDT, expert microscopy, and qPCR, excluding febrile children from asymptomatic analysis. Asymptomatic P. falciparum prevalence was 28.0% by RDT and 31.9% by microscopy. Among school-aged children (5–10 years), 66.5% were microscopy-positive versus 21.6% in under-fives. Gametocyte carriage was 3.9% overall and twice as high in school-aged children (67 vs 32 gametocytes/μL). Microscopy outperformed RDT in sensitivity (76.7% vs 67.7%), specificity, and PPV; P. falciparum caused 98.2% of infections. Authors conclude school-aged children are a key reservoir warranting inclusion in surveillance.

articles.headline: P. knowlesi malaria mostly affects adult male forest workers in Aceh
articles.title_original: Socio-demographic characteristics and parasite density patterns of Plasmodium knowlesi Malaria in Aceh, Indonesia: insights from hospital-based data.
articles.authors: Maulana T, Marthoenis M, Almira CS, Moe S, Abas AL, Htay MNN
articles.details: Retrospective hospital-based study of 40 patients with P. knowlesi malaria confirmed by microscopy and PCR at a hospital in Aceh Besar, Indonesia. Median age was 31 years (IQR 25–43); farmers and plantation workers made up 67.5% and orangutan caretakers 10%. All patients presented with fever and headache, were treated with dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine plus primaquine, and recovered completely. Among 35 patients with density data, parasite density was high in 42.9%, moderate in 45.7%, and low in 11.4%; median hospital stay was 3 days, longer in lower-density patients. Authors call for targeted surveillance of high-risk occupational groups.

articles.headline: Antibodies to PfVFT antigen block P. falciparum blood stages multiple ways
articles.title_original: Vaccine-induced mouse antibodies targeting Plasmodium falciparum PfVFT antigen inhibit blood stages through multiple mechanisms.
articles.authors: Goh YS, Mao H, Hor PX, Loh CY, Chang ZW, Hermsen CC, Nosten F, Sauerwein RW, Rénia L
articles.details: Screening sera from protected and non-protected volunteers against a P. falciparum antigen library identified PfVFT1 as associated with protection, with anti-PfVFT1 antibodies detected in all protected individuals. Vaccine-induced mouse anti-PfVFT sera inhibited parasite reinvasion into red blood cells, promoted complement deposition to induce parasite lysis, and supported phagocytosis and antibody-dependent cellular inhibition. The authors conclude that PfVFT1-specific antibodies engage multiple effector mechanisms relevant to antimalarial immunity, marking the antigen as a potential vaccine target.

articles.headline: Correction notice: Plasmodium kinases organize male gametogenesis structures
articles.title_original: Correction: Divergent Plasmodium kinases drive MTOC, kinetochore, and axoneme organisation in male gametogenesis.
articles.authors: Yanase R, Zeeshan M, Ferguson DJ, Markus R, Brady D, Bottrill AR, Holder AA, Guttery DS, Tewari R
articles.details: This study reports how Plasmodium kinases regulate MTOC, axoneme, and kinetochore organisation during male gametogenesis, providing insights into potential targets for malaria transmission control. The abstract reports no quantitative results.

articles.headline: Malaria is leading cause of acute kidney injury in Nigerian children
articles.title_original: A Study Using Point-of-Care Creatinine Testing in a Nigerian Primary Health Care Centre: Malaria as the Leading Cause of Acute Kidney Injury Particularly in Children.
articles.authors: Ugwem-Ikuru Y, Laurent-Ordu V, Emem-Chioma P, Erekosima I, Lewis D, Poulikakos D
articles.details: Study introducing point-of-care creatinine (POC Cr) testing at a Nigerian primary health care centre, testing 424 patients (301 adults, 123 children) with acute kidney injury (AKI) staged by KDIGO criteria. Malaria was the most frequent diagnosis (293 cases, 61.1%). Among adults, AKI occurred in 2/301 (0.6%), both with malaria. Among children (median age 5), 70/123 (56.9%) had AKI—stage 1 in 20.3%, stage 2 in 21.2%, stage 3 in 15.4%—and 49/70 (70%) of pediatric AKI cases had malaria, highest in children under 5. Authors highlight AKI as a common malaria complication in young children.

articles.headline: STAT1 drives malarial anemia in mouse blood-stage infection
articles.title_original: Role of STAT1 in modulating the host immune response to Plasmodium yoelii 17XL-infected murine blood-stage malaria.
articles.authors: Sun Y, Du C, Xu Q, Chen Z, Feng X, Han S, Yan Y, Wang B, Lv Z, Zhu G, Cao J, Cheng Y
articles.details: Using proteomic analysis of spleens from Plasmodium yoelii 17XL-infected mice, the authors identified STAT1, a core interferon-pathway molecule, as markedly upregulated during infection. Genetic STAT1 ablation enhanced survival and reduced peripheral parasitemia and splenic parasite burden, and lowered proportions of IFN-γ-producing CD8+ T cells and IL-10-producing CD4+ T cells at day 7 post-infection. STAT1-knockout mice had higher hemoglobin concentrations and reticulocyte proportions, indicating STAT1 deficiency may alleviate malarial anemia. The authors conclude STAT1 is pathogenic in the spleen and a potential therapeutic target.

articles.headline: India survey: donor malaria screening common, transfusion-transmitted cases underreported
articles.title_original: Transfusion-transmitted malaria in India: A national survey of epidemiology and testing practices.
articles.authors: Jacobs JW, Choudhury N, Arora S, Jindal A, Bhatnagar N, Basavarajegowda A, Shastry S, Chandra T, Daniel D, Drews SJ, Bloch EM
articles.details: A cross-sectional survey of blood collection facilities in India assessed malaria screening and transfusion-transmitted malaria (TTM). Of 262 facilities, 256 (97.7%) reported routine screening of all donations, most using antigen RDTs (228/256, 89.1%). Facilities tested 9,275,688 donations with 1231 confirmed positive (13.27 per 100,000 units), and annual positivity rose from 3.4 to 22.0 per 100,000. Only 8 facilities (3.1%) reported TTM cases, 45.3% participated in external quality assessment, and 33.6% performed lookback investigations. The authors conclude donor positivity may have risen while TTM is under-recognized or unreported.

articles.headline: Transcription factor PfAP2-V governs P. falciparum invasion and virulence
articles.title_original: The DNA-binding protein PfAP2-V regulates erythrocyte invasion and pathogenesis of the human malaria parasite Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Zhang Y, Wang C, Li Q, Li A, Zhang Y, Su Z, Yu L, Zheng K, Liu T, Jiang N, Zhang Y, Sang X, Feng Y, Chen R, Wei G, Zhang Q, Chen Q
articles.details: This laboratory study characterized the ApiAP2 transcription factor PfAP2-V (PF3D7_1239200) in blood-stage Plasmodium falciparum. Its expression was temporally coordinated with activation of virulence genes, including merozoite surface antigens, inner membrane complex families, and var genes encoding PfEMP1 cytoadherence proteins. Conditional knockdown significantly decreased intraerythrocytic proliferation, caused structural abnormalities in the infected erythrocyte surface, and markedly reduced cytoadhesion to human endothelial receptors CD36, ICAM-1, and PECAM-1/CD31. PfAP2-V directly associated with specific chromosomal regions and facilitated transcriptional activation. The authors establish PfAP2-V as a master regulator of parasite proliferation and virulence factor expression.

articles.headline: Tanzania framework tracks mosquito and human behaviors driving malaria exposure
articles.title_original: Nationally representative programmatic surveillance of mosquito and human behaviours that influence human exposure to malaria transmission and the impact of vector control across Tanzania.
articles.authors: Baravuga ME, Michael P, Kihwele F, Masanja B, Mmbaga S, Mlalwe AT, Namango IH, McCallum AJ, Mirzai N, Mwalimu CD, Lazaro S, Killeen GF, Kiware S, Ferguson HM, Govella NJ
articles.details: This study introduced and field-tested a nationally representative surveillance framework in Tanzania, with one randomly selected village in each of 25 sentinel districts surveyed about annually over three years across wet and dry seasons using mosquito electrocuting traps and human behaviour questionnaires. Anopheles arabiensis was widespread and fed predominantly on cattle, while An. funestus s.s. and An. gambiae s.s. were more localized and predominantly human-fed. Outdoor host-seeking was higher for An. arabiensis (64.9%) than An. gambiae s.s. (58.4%) or An. funestus s.s. (42.4%). People were most often outdoors during 18:00–21:00, especially adolescents and adults; adolescent males used bednets least, and higher temperatures reduced reported dry-season bednet use.

articles.headline: Benin health workers record malaria RDT results with high accuracy
articles.title_original: The accuracy of recording malaria rapid diagnostic test (RDT) results in public health facilities in Benin; results from the MaCRA project.
articles.authors: Ahogni I, Avanon H, Hueha C, Kpemasse A, Aissan J, Affoukou C, Accrombessi M, Aponte JJ, Hilton E, Cooper S, Griffith K, Humes M, Lindblade KA, Ngufor C
articles.details: A six-month mixed-methods prospective observational study in 16 public health facilities across two departments in Benin assessed healthcare workers' (HCWs) fidelity in recording malaria RDT results, using a digital RDT reader (HealthPulse, Audere) with images independently interpreted by an external panel. Of 35,720 RDTs, agreement between HCWs and the reference panel was 94.3% (Cohen's kappa = 0.88); results misrecorded as positive (5.0%) exceeded those misrecorded as negative (0.7%). Accuracy was highest for children under 5 (96.7%) and lowest for patients over 15 (91.6%). Among 226 HCWs surveyed, 89.4% believed a malaria patient could have a negative RDT but only 19.5% supported treating such cases; only 40.5% waited the recommended reading time and glove use was 15.6%.

articles.headline: Microscopy misses submicroscopic malaria in Ethiopia's southern Rift Valley
articles.title_original: Microscopy underestimates submicroscopic malaria infections in Ethiopia's southern rift valley: a community-based cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Jima B, Wondale B, Eligo N, Wudineh F, Wegayehu T, Lindtjørn B, Massebo F
articles.details: A community-based cross-sectional study in malaria-endemic districts of the Gamo Zone, southern Rift Valley of Ethiopia, compared microscopy and nested PCR for malaria detection as part of a baseline assessment. Among 4745 participants screened by microscopy, prevalence was 2.7% (126/4745; 95% CI 2.2–3.2%). PCR on 1183 samples confirmed 67% (65/97) of microscopy-positive cases (PCR-corrected prevalence 1.4%) and detected submicroscopic infections in 6.1% (66/1086) of microscopy-negative samples. Microscopy frequently misclassified species: 19.5% of P. falciparum read as P. vivax, 14.0% of P. vivax as P. falciparum, and 33.0% of microscopy-positives were PCR-negative. The authors conclude microscopy underestimates community malaria prevalence.

articles.headline: Lipid transfer protein PfVPS13L1 essential for malaria parasite invasive stages
articles.title_original: A bridge-like lipid transfer protein is critical for generation of invasive stages in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Guillén-Samander A, Perepelkina N, Horáčková V, Behrens HM, Rodriguez Cruz HO, Mesén-Ramírez JP, Ribeiro-Holbein A, Haberkant P, Stein F, Spielmann T
articles.details: Laboratory study in Plasmodium falciparum using the ER adapter VAP to identify lipid transfer mechanisms at ER membrane contact sites. The bridge-like lipid transfer protein PfVPS13L1, which interacts with PfVAP, bridges the ER with the nascent inner membrane complex (IMC), an organelle required for schizogony (daughter-cell formation). Loss-of-function of PfVPS13L1 reduced IMC growth and produced smaller anucleated progeny, impairing schizogony. The authors conclude VPS13L1 mediates bulk transfer of lipids from the ER to the growing IMC and is critical for forming apicomplexan invasive stages.

articles.headline: Azole fungicide tebuconazole linked to chronic malaria reactivation in birds
articles.title_original: Chronic exposure to a common azole fungicide is associated with reactivation of chronic Plasmodium infections in farmland birds.
articles.authors: Pigeault R, Bichet C, Bellot P, Brischoux F, Fritsch C, Angelier F
articles.details: Semi-natural experiment in wild-caught adult house sparrows (Passer domesticus) testing whether an eleven-week dietary exposure to the triazole fungicide tebuconazole alters chronic avian malaria infection dynamics, monitored before and after exposure from late autumn to mid-winter. At the start of the experiment, 70% of birds were chronically infected. While prevalence declined in controls over winter, it increased by 20% in tebuconazole-exposed birds, with no significant difference in parasite load observed between groups. The authors suggest tebuconazole may have promoted the reappearance of parasites from exo-erythrocytic stages, and conclude that exposure can influence chronic malaria dynamics.

articles.headline: Naturally acquired antibodies recognize P. vivax liver-stage antigen Pv_LISP-2
articles.title_original: Naturally acquired antibodies to Plasmodium vivax Pv_LISP-2, a potential liver stage vaccine antigen.
articles.authors: Donassolo RA, Soares da Veiga GT, Nisimura LM, Bittencourt NC, Salazar YEAR, Barros DKADS, Lopes SCP, de Sousa TN, Costa FTM, Albrecht L
articles.details: Study evaluating naturally acquired humoral responses against two structural regions (Pv_L.seq1 and Pv_L.seq2) of P. vivax Liver-Specific Protein 2 (Pv_LISP-2) in P. vivax-infected individuals from the Brazilian Amazon (Manaus and Boa Vista), using ELISA for IgM, IgG, and IgG subclasses, with longitudinal follow-up up to 180 days. Both fragments were recognized; acute-phase responses showed high IgM followed by IgG, especially against Pv_L.seq1, with cytophilic IgG1 and IgG3 predominant. IgM correlated with parasitemia and symptom duration but negatively with hemoglobin, hematocrit, and erythrocytes. The authors conclude Pv_LISP-2 is naturally antigenic and merits investigation as a liver-stage vaccine candidate.

articles.headline: Ethiopia bed net ownership high but usage below protective threshold
articles.title_original: Assessment of Long-Lasting Insecticidal Net (LLIN) Ownership, Utilization, and Associated Barriers in Malaria-Endemic Communities of Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Waldetensai A, Tasew G, Yewhalaw D, Takie H, Gidey B, Kinde S, Gemechu F, Yirga S, Kinfe E, Hailemariam A, Tadesse H, Solomon H, Assefa G, Dilu D, Bashaye S, Wuletaw Y, Abdulatif B, Kebede T, Tadiwos S, Gebrewold G, Hailu S, Tesfaye F, Tollera G, Hailu M, Guiyun Y, Eukubay A, Gebresillassie A
articles.details: Community-based cross-sectional survey of 9,222 households (34,427 individuals) across 11 administrative regions in Ethiopia from October 2024 to January 2025, assessing LLIN ownership, utilization, and barriers using two-stage stratified cluster sampling and hierarchical multivariable logistic regression. Household LLIN ownership was 71.5%, with sufficient nets for every two people at 58.3%; utilization among owners was 59.9% (rural 72.7% exceeding urban), and higher among pregnant women (78.5%) and children under five (67.2%). Age and pregnancy were the strongest predictors (ORs 0.258 and 0.662, both p<0.001). Major barriers included lack of confidence hanging nets (60.5%) and the misconception that malaria risk is restricted to the rainy season (64.1%). Overall utilization remained below the 80% threshold.

articles.headline: pfdhps A581G resistance mutation emerging in southern Senegal under chemoprevention
articles.title_original: Emergence of Plasmodium falciparum pfdhps A581G mutation in Southern Senegal under Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention pressure, 2020-2023.
articles.authors: Diallo MA, Tine A, Sow D, Sene A, Gaye A, Ndiaye YD, Gomis J, Dieye B, Deme AB, Yade MS, Ngom B, Garba MN, Ndiaye IM, Diongue K, Badiane AS, Seck MC, Ndiaye M, Ndiaye D
articles.details: Analysis of dried-blood-spot samples from therapeutic-efficacy studies in two seasonal malaria chemoprevention districts (Kolda and Kédougou) in southern Senegal, 2020–2023, using targeted amplicon deep sequencing to track P. falciparum pfdhfr and pfdhps resistance markers under sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine plus amodiaquine pressure. The pfdhfr triple mutant (N51I+C59R+S108N) was near-fixed (>90%) and pfdhps A437G was common (47–100%). K540E was absent in 2020 and only sporadic afterward, while the A581G mutation emerged in 2022 (8.1% in Kolda), rising to 14.9% in Kolda and 6.5% in Kédougou by 2023, with similar frequencies in children ≤5 and 6–10 years. The authors conclude the regimen remains clinically effective but the rising pfdhps A581G mutation warrants close monitoring.

articles.headline: Imported malaria in western Nepal tied to male labor migrants to India
articles.title_original: Understanding imported malaria in western Nepal: Implications for malaria control and elimination.
articles.authors: Kandel S, Koirala M, Dahal G, Sah RK, Upadhayaya P, Jongcherdchootrakul K, Thitichai P
articles.details: A community-based cross-sectional study analyzed 113 imported malaria cases reported in Narainapur Rural Municipality, Banke District, using structured interviews and medical record reviews. All 113 cases were male, mostly aged 20-29 years (46.02%) and of Muslim ethnicity (84.07%); 99.11% had traveled to India for work, predominantly Maharashtra (96.47%) and South Mumbai (95.60%), with catering the main occupation (71.43%). Notably, 85.84% lacked fixed housing, only 56.64% received pre-travel malaria information, and 59.30% were unwilling to use long-lasting insecticidal nets. The authors conclude imported malaria concentrates in well-defined urban hotspots among mobile migrants, urging targeted, migrant-sensitive prevention and cross-border collaboration.

articles.headline: Wildlife blood hosts may sustain malaria vectors inside African conservation areas
articles.title_original: Blood host preferences and competitive inter-species dynamics within an African malaria vector species complex inferred from signs of animal activity around aquatic larval habitats.
articles.authors: Walsh KA, Kavishe DR, Duggan LM, Tarimo LJ, Msoffe RV, Elisa M, Govella NJ, Eichhorn MP, Kaindoa EW, Butler F, Killeen GF
articles.details: This field study surveyed aquatic habitats for Anopheles gambiae complex larvae across a gradient of ecosystem integrity in southern Tanzania, from human settlements to a Wildlife Management Area and Nyerere National Park, recording animal tracks and signs as indirect indicators of blood-host availability. Only the vector An. arabiensis occurred in domesticated areas, while non-vector An. quadriannulatus dominated intact ecosystems; An. arabiensis larvae proportions were positively associated with human/cattle activity and An. quadriannulatus with impala, warthog and bushpig. An. arabiensis persisted over 40 km inside the park, implying self-sustaining refuge populations on unidentified wild hosts that may confound elimination but could aid insecticide-resistance management.

articles.headline: Low CYP2D6 activity doubles P. vivax relapse risk after primaquine
articles.title_original: Reduced cytochrome P-450 (CYP) 2D6 activity and Plasmodium vivax malaria risk in Amazonians: A retrospective, population-based cohort study.
articles.authors: Corder RM, Puça MCSB, Prete CA, Ladeia WA, Rodrigues PT, Johansen IC, de Sousa TN, Ferreira MU
articles.details: A retrospective, population-based cohort study in Brazil's main malaria hotspot used Cox models to assess how genotype-determined CYP2D6 activity scores affect Plasmodium vivax recurrence within 6 months after chloroquine plus primaquine (total dose 3.5 mg/kg). Among 466 P. vivax-infected participants, those with null/low CYP2D6 activity (activity score ≤0.25), but not those with intermediate activity (0.5-1.0), had twice the recurrence risk versus AS >1.0 (hazard ratio = 2.22, P = 0.004). However, community-wide vivax incidence across 997 Amazonians over 5 years (mean 26.6 cases/100 person-years) did not differ by CYP2D6 level. The authors conclude that severely reduced metabolizers need alternative relapse-suppression regimens for radical cure.

articles.headline: Privacy-preserving federated vision transformers detect malaria from blood smears
articles.title_original: FedMal-XAI: an explainable federated vision transformer leveraging knowledge distillation for privacy-preserving malaria detection.
articles.authors: Bhuiyan TA, Rahman A, Khan FI, Noori FM, Masum AKM
articles.details: This study proposes a privacy-preserving federated learning system using Vision Transformers (ViT-B/16, DeiT-Tiny, Swin-T, DINOv2) to automatically identify malaria from segmented red blood cell images without sharing data across institutions. The federated transformer variants matched centralized CNNs and outperformed a ResNet50+VGG16 ensemble, reaching 98.15% accuracy, with FedDistill-DeiT at 97.79%, FedAvg-Swin-T at 97.75%, and FedDistill-Swin-T achieving a ROC-AUC of 0.9977. The authors conclude that federated Vision Transformers offer a reliable, scalable, interpretable malaria screening solution that maintains high accuracy and privacy even under heterogeneous data distributions.

articles.headline: Review of heat shock protein Gp96 at the malaria host-parasite interface
articles.title_original: Heat shock protein Gp96 (Grp94) in malaria: functional insights at the host-parasite interface and therapeutic perspectives.
articles.authors: Djoumoi D, Diouara AAM, Diop M, Nguer CM, Mbengue B, Thiam F
articles.details: This review synthesizes current knowledge on the endoplasmic reticulum heat shock protein Gp96 (Grp94/endoplasmin), an Hsp90 paralog that regulates ER proteostasis and the folding and trafficking of immune clients such as Toll-like receptors, integrins, and immunoglobulins. In malaria, host-derived Gp96 released under cellular stress acts as a danger-associated molecular pattern, and elevated circulating levels have been associated with severe malaria phenotypes, supporting its potential as a biomarker of host stress and immune dysregulation. Plasmodium falciparum expresses its own ER-resident homolog, PfGp96, with parasite-specific adaptations, though its functions, client repertoire, essentiality, and drug-target validation remain poorly defined. The authors highlight opportunities and challenges for biomarker and therapeutic development and outline priorities for future research.

articles.headline: Amphipathic helix domain anchors PfNCR1 for parasite cholesterol transport
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Niemann-Pick Type C1-Related protein relies on its physicochemical properties for membrane contact site localization required for cholesterol homeostasis.
articles.authors: Ray A, Istvan ES, Goldberg DE, Garten M
articles.details: This molecular study investigated PfNCR1, a Niemann-Pick Type C1-Related protein and candidate antimalarial drug target that maintains cholesterol homeostasis at the P. falciparum-red blood cell interface, where it localizes to exceptionally narrow membrane contact sites (~3-4 nm aqueous space). The authors identified a 141-amino-acid helix-linker-helix (HLH) domain unique to Plasmodium that is necessary for efficient localization to these narrow sites via its physicochemical properties. GPI-anchoring the isolated HLH domain, or a version with helices replaced by human ATG3's amphipathic helix, was sufficient to target a fluorescent protein to endogenous PfNCR1 regions. The degree of localization qualitatively correlated with cholesterol homeostasis, linking membrane contact to transport function.

articles.headline: P. vivax recurrences in low-transmission Ethiopia are mostly relapses
articles.title_original: High genetic diversity, clonal activation of hypnozoites and relapse of Plasmodium vivax isolates in low-transmission setting of Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Getachew H, Zhong D, Habtamu K, Abossie A, Demissew A, Tsegaye A, Degefa T, Wang C, Zhou G, Lee MC, Kazura JW, King CL, Yewhalaw D, Yan G
articles.details: Using amplicon deep sequencing of the pvmsp1 gene, this study assessed P. vivax genetic diversity and multiplicity of infection (MOI) in 370 blood samples from 215 individuals at the Arjo-Didessa sugarcane plantations and surroundings in Oromia, southwestern Ethiopia. High population-level diversity produced 67 unique haplotypes (haplotype diversity 0.799, nucleotide diversity 0.044, expected heterozygosity 0.826), but MOI was low at 1.4 with 34.6% polyclonal infections. Among 215 participants, 82 had one to five recurrent infections; paired primary/recurrent episodes showed 81.3% genetic homology, with 55.6% of homologous pairs sharing identical alleles. The authors conclude most recurrences were relapses and urge strengthened surveillance for mobile populations.

articles.headline: qPCR reveals hidden asymptomatic, sub-microscopic malaria reservoirs in southern Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Uncovering silent malaria reservoirs in Southern Ethiopia: community-based molecular detection of asymptomatic and sub-microscopic infections and their implications for diagnosis and control.
articles.authors: Bogale A, Tefera S, Taye Gebremeskele B, Kassie A, Dube SJ, Tekleselassie T, Gemechu T, Abebe AA
articles.details: This cross-sectional, community-based study enrolled 525 participants across ten kebeles in five woredas of the Gedeo Zone (September–December 2024), screening blood and dried blood spots by microscopy, rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs), and qPCR. qPCR detected substantially higher asymptomatic prevalence (164/525, 31.2%) and sub-microscopic infection (72/433, 16.6%) than microscopy (92/525, 17.5%) or RDT (69/525, 13.1%). Among sub-microscopic infections, 34/433 (7.9%) were P. vivax, 31/433 (7.2%) P. falciparum, and 7/433 (1.6%) mixed, with higher rates among those aged 15–24 and females. The authors conclude symptom-based strategies underestimate true prevalence and sensitive diagnostics are needed.

articles.headline: Pfmdr1 Y184F resistance marker rising sharply in Ethiopian P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Incidence of kelch13 and Pfmdr1 gene mutations associated with antimalarial drug resistance in Plasmodium falciparum isolates from Ethiopia: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Yeshanew TM, Brhane BG, Begashew BA, Bayisa GW, Birhane N
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis pooled 15 studies (11,269 samples, 5,521 positive P. falciparum cases) to estimate the prevalence of kelch13 (R622I, P441L, A675V) and Pfmdr1 (Y184F, D1246Y) mutations in Ethiopian isolates, using a random-effects model. Pooled kelch13 prevalence was 11% for R622I, 1% for A675V, and 0% for P441L; Pfmdr1 was 6% for D1246Y and 77% for Y184F. Subgroup analysis showed Y184F rose significantly over time, from 66% (2007–2014) to 91% (2015–2025). The authors conclude most resistance markers remain rare, but the rising Pfmdr1 Y184F mutation is a growing concern.

articles.headline: Cameroon's first routine malaria vaccine rollout shows high dropout rates
articles.title_original: Evaluation of the first malaria vaccine introduction: ongoing learning opportunities during the introduction into routine immunisation in Cameroon.
articles.authors: Josue Kedakse TSN, Kaba M, Baonga SF, Mboussou F, Njoh AA, Amani A, Efouba G, Kouontchou Mimbe JC, Njinkeu A, Yohou KS, Nembot Mekoueng R, Takamiya M, Wirngo M, Hedje J, Jalloh MF, Walldorf JA, Habimana P, Impouma B
articles.details: This mini-postintroduction evaluation (mini-PIE) reviewed Cameroon's introduction of the malaria vaccine into routine immunisation across 42 health districts in all 10 regions, using desk review, standardised interviews of immunisation staff and caregivers, and a WHO intra-action review discussion. Monthly report completeness exceeded 95% in all regions except North-West and Adamawa (87%). Only 56% of eligible children received a first dose, with second- and third-dose coverage of 31% and 28% and high dropout across all regions. Among caregivers, 88% (42/48) were aware of the introduction and 60% (29/48) knew the importance of continued net use. The authors deem the introduction largely successful and recommend the mini-PIE methodology for other countries.

articles.headline: Household costs of malaria and febrile illness in Indonesia
articles.title_original: The household economic burden of human-only and zoonotic malaria, compared to other causes of acute febrile illness in Indonesia.
articles.authors: Abraham P, Lubis IND, Novivanti R, Trianty L, Nisa FA, Kariodimedjo P, Amalia R, Cahyadi RAD, Mossadeq Al Qorny T, Perlambang YA, Permatasari R, Surendra H, Anstey NM, Grigg MJ, Devine A
articles.details: A health facility-based cross-sectional cost-of-illness survey at eight clinics in North Sumatra and North Kalimantan, Indonesia (January 2022–October 2023) recruited 2244 patients, of whom 153 (6.8%) were malaria-confirmed by PCR (P. vivax n=97, P. knowlesi n=35, P. malariae n=12, P. falciparum n=3, P. ovale n=1, plus five mixed infections). Mean total household cost was US$33 (SD 57) per malaria episode versus US$17 (SD 38) per non-malarial fever episode, dominated by indirect productivity losses (70% and 61% respectively). Catastrophic health expenditure affected 16% of malaria patients and 11% of other febrile patients. The authors conclude these out-of-pocket costs matter for malaria control policy in near-elimination settings despite subsidised care.

articles.headline: Outdoor occupation and dawn/dusk activity vs malaria risk, Mozambique
articles.title_original: The Effect of Head of Household Occupation and Activity at Sunrise and Sunset on Malaria Prevalence in Sussundenga, Mozambique.
articles.authors: Jorgenson B, Earland DE, Bibe AF, Novela A, Muhiro V, Ferrão JL, Searle KM
articles.details: A cross-sectional study in Sussundenga, Mozambique analyzed 297 individuals across 84 households using generalized linear mixed models to assess whether head-of-household occupation and being active at sunrise and/or sunset were associated with malaria infection. After adjustment, the difference in predicted malaria prevalence for outdoor versus indoor head-of-household occupation was 0.015 (95% CI -0.12 to 0.15), and for activity at both sunrise and sunset was 0.10 (95% CI -0.063 to 0.26). An interaction model found the occupation-related prevalence difference was 0.33 (95% CI 0.037–0.62) greater among those active at both times, though the additive interaction test was not statistically significant. The authors suggest combined exposures may indicate differing malaria risk.

articles.headline: Whole-genome population study of South American malaria vector mosquito
articles.title_original: Population genomics of Anopheles darlingi, the principal South American malaria vector mosquito.
articles.authors: Tennessen JA, Brosula R, Chabanol E, Bickersmith S, Early AM, Laws M, Kelley KA, Grillet ME, Gamboa D, Lucas ER, Duchemin JB, Quiñones ML, Sallum MAM, Bergo ES, Moreno JE, Nagi S, Arisco NJ, Sooklall M, Niles-Robin R, Castro MC, Cox H, Gendrin M, Conn JE, Neafsey DE
articles.details: Researchers sequenced and analyzed whole genomes of 1094 Anopheles darlingi mosquitoes (median depth 18x) from six South American countries, the principal malaria vector across tropical Latin America. They found deep geographic population structure, high genetic diversity including 13 putative segregating inversions, and no evidence of sympatric cryptic taxa despite high interpopulation divergence. Strong selection signals, plausibly insecticide-driven, were notable on cytochrome P450 genes. The authors conclude the findings will support mosquito surveillance and control while highlighting challenges a genetically diverse vector poses for malaria elimination in the Western hemisphere.

articles.headline: Malaria parasite protein TRAg7 drives macrophage inflammation via CD71
articles.title_original: Interaction of Plasmodium yoelii tryptophan-rich antigen 7 with CD71 on macrophage membrane regulates host inflammatory response.
articles.authors: Sun Y, Chen Z, Du C, Lei Y, Li J, Zhang H, Huang X, Wang B, Zuo S, Lv Z, Cao J, Han S, Cheng Y
articles.details: Using a murine malaria model, this study shows that Plasmodium yoelii tryptophan-rich antigen 7 (PyTRAg7), an exported protein, interacts with the macrophage receptor CD71 and activates NF-κB p65 signaling, promoting production of proinflammatory factors. Genetic deletion of TRAg7 led to decreased parasitemia, reduced inflammation, and improved host survival. The authors conclude that parasite exported proteins can orchestrate host inflammatory responses beyond their canonical role in erythrocyte invasion, offering a framework for understanding malaria pathogenesis and potential therapeutic strategies.

articles.headline: Host SNARE proteins help Plasmodium liver-stage parasites get nutrients
articles.title_original: Host SNARE Proteins Mediate Lysosome and PVM Fusion to Support Plasmodium Liver Infection.
articles.authors: Atchou K, Kramer N, Bindschedler A, Schmuckli-Maurer J, Caldelari R, Heussler VT
articles.details: Using CRISPR/Cas9 knockouts in HeLa cells and microscopy of Plasmodium berghei-infected cells, researchers identified endolysosomal SNARE proteins VAMP7, VAMP8, Vti1B, and Stx7 recruited to the parasitophorous vacuole membrane (PVM) with distinct timing—VAMP7 and Vti1B within 30 minutes post-infection and VAMP8 and Stx7 around 24 hours. Single knockouts had minimal impact, but VAMP7-VAMP8 and VAMP7-Vti1B double knockouts significantly reduced parasite infection and growth, with Vti1B dominant; triple knockouts mirrored VAMP7-Vti1B disruption. SNARE depletion impaired lysosome-PVM association and LAMP1 vesicle recruitment. The authors conclude Plasmodium hijacks a host SNARE network to fuse lysosomes with the PVM for nutrient uptake, and that targeting Vti1B offers a host-directed antimalarial approach without host toxicity.

articles.headline: G6PD deficiency mapping in Indonesia supports primaquine screening for vivax
articles.title_original: G6PD deficiency in Indonesia: a systematic review and update of prevalence and variant maps in the context of malaria elimination.
articles.authors: Sadhewa A, Panggalo LV, Nanine I, Fadilah I, Hutagalung J, Kenangalem E, Limardi PC, Malik SG, Noviyanti R, Pasaribu AP, Poespoprodjo JR, Prameswari HD, Price RN, Puspadewi RT, Setiadi W, Trianty L, Sudoyo H, Sutanto I, Syafruddin D, Kevin Baird J, Ley B, Elyazar IRF, Satyagraha AW
articles.details: This systematic review (PROSPERO CRD42022368319) updated G6PD deficiency (G6PDd) prevalence and variant maps in Indonesia to inform primaquine (PQ) use against P. vivax hypnozoites, where high-daily-dose PQ (1 mg/kg/day over 7 days) will require G6PD screening. Across 45 studies published 1964–2024, G6PDd (<30% activity) prevalence was 0.0–19.9% across 87 sites (n=23,166), females with deficient/intermediate activity were 0.8–44.6% across 35 sites (n=6729), and male G6PDd allele frequencies were 0.0–25.9% across 82 sites (n=10,680). Fifteen class B variants causing acute haemolytic anaemia were reported. The authors support routine G6PDd screening but note heterogeneous, incomplete data, especially in high P. vivax areas.

articles.headline: Urban Anopheles surveys in Accra show irrigation, peri-urban hotspots
articles.title_original: Urban malaria vector dynamics in Accra, Ghana.
articles.authors: Mohammed Sabtiu AR, Akuamoah-Boateng Y, Owusu-Asenso CM, Abdulai A, Sraku IK, Halou DK, Doe RT, Boadu EN, Mensah SKE, Azumah JD, Adu-Barima S, Awotwe LE, Walker A, Yanney SA, Ebuako AA, Eyeson NAS, Appiah AA, Obeng BC, Amoateng G, Danquah GA, Abusah NE, Agbotah EE, Kwarteng RO, Boateng J, Hinne IA, Appiah-Kwarteng C, Forson AO, Aboagye-Antwi F, Afrane YA
articles.details: A vector surveillance study in Accra, Ghana assessed Anopheles gambiae s.l. abundance, species composition, and behavior across fifteen sites of differing socioeconomic settings during dry and rainy seasons of 2022 to 2024. Researchers collected 20,945 host-seeking and 1,613 resting Anopheles mosquitoes. Abundance was highest in irrigation and peri-urban sites and lowest in low socioeconomic areas. An. gambiae s.s. dominated host-seeking populations while An. coluzzii dominated resting ones. The authors conclude irrigation and peri-urban areas are hotspots requiring targeted surveillance and control.

articles.headline: IL-10 loss improves early malaria control in mice independent of IFN-γ
articles.title_original: Early enhanced control of Plasmodium yoelii infection in IL-10-deficient mice is independent of IFN-γ, IL-12, and the humoral response.
articles.authors: Jones MD, O'Neal KA, Zeltner SL, Gouch AR, Stumhofer JS
articles.details: This mouse study examined how IL-10 deficiency enhances control of Plasmodium yoelii infection, since Il10-/- mice carry lower parasite loads. Abolishing IL-10 increased TH1 polarization and IL-12 and IFN-γ production, but these cytokines did not explain the improved parasite control. Loss of IL-10 signaling reduced germinal center B cells and plasmablasts in the spleen; although B cells were essential for survival, they were not critical for early parasite control. Il10-/- mice lacking both IFN-γ and B cells still limited early parasite expansion, suggesting IL-10 suppresses host-protective pathways beyond B cells and IFN-γ.

articles.headline: Modelling R21/Matrix-M catch-up campaigns and extra booster doses
articles.title_original: Public health impact of catch-up vaccination or additional booster doses with pre-erythrocytic malaria vaccine R21/Matrix-M: a modelling study.
articles.authors: McCain K, Topazian HM, Challenger JD, Okell L, Winskill P, Ghani AC
articles.details: Using a stochastic individual-based P. falciparum transmission model, researchers estimated the impact of one-off R21/Matrix-M catch-up campaigns (ages 6 months–14 years) and extra boosters at 2, 5, and/or 10 years post-primary series across transmission settings. Catch-up in moderate-to-high transmission targeting young children averted the most uncomplicated cases per 1000 doses (358, 95% CrI 113–570, ages 6 months–2 years at 45% PfPR2-10). An extra booster 5 years post-primary averted 835 (605–1274) clinical cases per 1000 doses at 45% PfPR2-10 versus 247 (177–345) for routine vaccination at 5%. The authors conclude catch-up or extra boosters can add benefit, varying by transmission and seasonality.

articles.headline: Analysis of malaria outbreak years across India over a decade
articles.title_original: Epidemiology of malaria outbreak years in India provides key insights into malaria elimination.
articles.authors: Yadav CP, Hussain SSA, Tarique ST, Maheshwari V, Rahi M, Sharma A
articles.details: This epidemiological analysis defined a malaria outbreak year when annual cases exceeded the sum of the last three years' moving average plus 2.5 times the moving standard deviation, using the past decade of data. India experienced 402 district-level outbreak years over the decade, with 2011, 2014, and 2015 marking peak periods and 2020 recording the fewest outbreaks. Among 28 states, seven—Mizoram, Tripura, Jharkhand, Odisha, Madhya Pradesh, Uttar Pradesh, and Meghalaya—were identified as more outbreak-prone, with over 70% of their districts experiencing at least one outbreak year between 2011 and 2020. The authors conclude outbreak frequency has declined but susceptible districts need heightened surveillance.

articles.headline: Host macrophages aid malaria transmission by suppressing mosquito gut bacteria
articles.title_original: Host macrophages/monocytes promote malaria transmission by modulating mosquito microbiota via SR-A-mediated phagocytosis.
articles.authors: He B, Li M, Guo S, Fang J, Fan Y, Tan N, Liu T, Li J, Zhu F, Zhang J, Xu W
articles.details: This mechanistic study, in rodent models and human samples, found that macrophages/monocytes—not neutrophils or NK/NKT cells—enhance malaria transmission to mosquitoes. These cells migrate into the mosquito midgut and suppress transmission-blocking microbiota, including E. anophelis, S. sonnei, and K. oxytoca, mainly via non-opsonic phagocytosis mediated by scavenger receptor A (SR-A). Depleting macrophages/monocytes or using anti-SR-A neutralizing antibodies increased these bacteria and boosted the transmission-blocking efficacy of anti-Pfs25 monoclonal antibodies, achieving complete blockade of Pfs25-transgenic P. berghei transmission. The authors suggest anti-SR-A antibodies as a strategy to inhibit transmission and improve anti-Pfs25 antibody efficacy.

articles.headline: Malaria–dengue co-infections found in Papua, Indonesia patients
articles.title_original: Co-infections of malaria and dengue in Timika, a highly endemic malaria area in Central Papua, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Rana B, Trianty L, Santoso MS, Puspitasari AM, Nisa FA, Amalia R, Carlier L, Tetteh K, Noviyanti R, Sasmono RT
articles.details: Sixty-nine microscopy-confirmed malaria patient samples from Timika, Central Papua, Indonesia, collected in 2022, were screened for dengue virus (DENV) by qRT-PCR, with malaria speciation also by qRT-PCR and viral whole-genome sequencing via MinION Oxford Nanopore. Three patients (4.3%) were co-infected with DENV-2 (1) and DENV-4 (2); infecting parasites were P. vivax, P. falciparum, and P. malariae. Sequenced DENV-4 was Genotype II, closely related to strains from Makassar, South Sulawesi. The authors conclude co-infections occur and pose clinical and diagnostic challenges, supporting combined clinical and molecular surveillance.

articles.headline: Modeling extends MDA malaria-reduction estimates across Senegal communes
articles.title_original: Mapping the local effectiveness of mass drug administration for malaria using transportability methods.
articles.authors: Roh ME, Tong Y, Heitmann GB, Jia J, Ba EKC, Ndiaye JL, Fogelson A, Milligan P, Seck A, Diallo A, Lo AC, Baiocchi M, Gosling R, Bennett A, Hsiang MS, Benjamin-Chung J
articles.details: Using transportability models with machine learning, the authors generalized a Senegal cluster randomized trial—in which mass drug administration (MDA) reduced malaria incidence by 55% in areas routinely using seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC)—to 116 non-trial communes where SMC is standard of care. Accounting for weather, vegetation, and population density, they estimated incidence reductions of 36% to 65% in 74 non-trial communes, with larger decreases where precipitation and vegetation were higher and temperatures lower. MDA was not effective in the postintervention year, supporting that its effects are short-lived.

articles.headline: Iranian plant extract kills and repels malaria mosquito larvae
articles.title_original: Eco-friendly mosquito control: Assessing the larvicidal and repellent effects of Dorema aucheri extract on Anopheles stephensi, the malaria vector.
articles.authors: Hosseinpour A, Vahedi M, Hamedi A, Soltanian N, Kazemi F, Rezaee E, Soltani A
articles.details: This study tested hydroalcoholic extracts of green and white aerial sections of Dorema aucheri, a medicinal plant native to Iran, against Anopheles stephensi using WHO larvicidal bioassay guidelines and the Klun & Debboun repellency method, with Probit analysis for LC₅₀/LC₉₀ and ED₅₀/ED₉₀. Extracts showed dose-dependent larvicidal activity reaching up to 98% mortality at 400 mg/L, with LC₅₀ of 98.54 mg/L (green) and 104.72 mg/L (white). Repellency reached 90% (green) and 85% (white) at 0.5 mg/cm²; complete protection times for 5% doses were 64 and 52 minutes. The authors suggest it is a promising eco-friendly control candidate.

articles.headline: Review: human behavior shapes malaria intervention effectiveness
articles.title_original: Investigating the impact of human behavioural factors on the effectiveness of malaria interventions in malaria-endemic regions: A systematic review.
articles.authors: Agbo LN, Orish VN, Husaini DC, Madaki PD, Orisakwe OE
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: This systematic review synthesized evidence on how behavioural factors influence uptake, use, and impact of malaria interventions in endemic regions, searching PubMed, Google Scholar, Dimensions, and HINARI for studies from 2000–2025, using the PEO framework, CASP quality assessment, and narrative synthesis. Of 59 included studies, 83% were from sub-Saharan Africa, 63% used cross-sectional designs, and 85% had low risk of bias. Key themes were the ITN ownership–use gap (discomfort, misconceptions), delayed healthcare-seeking (cost, access, trust), and distrust diverting care to informal providers. The authors conclude intervention effectiveness is contingent on behaviour and requires locally tailored, trust-building strategies.

articles.headline: Anti-GPI antibody fragment protects mice from cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Anti-GPI scFv as a Promising Tool for Intervention Against Cerebral Malaria in Mice.
articles.authors: Klein SG, Mota KCP, Borges BC, Vieira MS, Neves MM, Mendes LS, Ferreira FB, de Lima IL, Santos FAA, Bastos LM, Lima WR, Goulart Filho LR, Vieira da Silva M
articles.details: In a preclinical study, C57BL/6J mice infected with Plasmodium berghei ANKA were treated with a single-chain variable fragment (scFv) targeting glycosylphosphatidylinositol (GPI) or with PBS on days 0, 3, and 6 post-infection, then assessed for morbidity, mortality, parasitemia, blood counts, cytokines, and histopathology. Anti-GPI scFv prevented lethality in 71.4% of infected animals, promoted recovery from weight loss, reduced parasitemia, inhibited neurological and systemic signs, and improved hematological and histopathological parameters in brain, lungs, and kidneys. The authors conclude anti-GPI scFv is a promising tool against severe/cerebral malaria.

articles.headline: Review: education frameworks for Plasmodium knowlesi zoonotic malaria prevention
articles.title_original: Educational frameworks for zoonotic malaria prevention: a narrative review integrating behavioural theory, participatory design, and instructional models for Plasmodium knowlesi in Southeast Asia.
articles.authors: Hijazi MHA, Jeffree MS, Woon FC, Jelip J, Avoi R, Ahmed K
articles.details: This narrative review synthesizes evidence from the past decade on educational and behaviour-change interventions for malaria prevention, drawing on PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science, and Google Scholar plus WHO and Malaysian Ministry of Health policy documents, to inform prevention of zoonotic Plasmodium knowlesi in Southeast Asia. The authors report that educational interventions consistently improved malaria knowledge and often preventive practices, with stronger effects when grounded in behavioural theory, but most lacked explicit instructional design and rarely addressed ecological, occupational, and cultural dimensions. They propose a hybrid framework combining behavioural models, participatory research, and instructional design, and a concept of "zoonotic literacy.

articles.headline: Systematic review of malaria transmission models in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: A systematic review of epidemiological models for malaria transmission in Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Adegoke TM, Nji AM, Silal SP, Ali IM, Wondeu ALD, Madeleine PF, Sylvanie MT, Tah CF, Antonio-Nkondjio C, Bigoga JD, Leke RFG, Stresman G, Cui L, Oladoja OM, Adegoke GK, Obisesan KO, Mbacham WF
articles.details: Following PRISMA 2020 guidelines and a PROSPERO-registered protocol, this systematic review searched five databases for studies published up to December 31, 2024 on epidemiological models of malaria transmission in sub-Saharan Africa, appraising methodology with the ISPOR-SMDM framework. Of 102 included studies, transmission-focused models were most common (52.9%, 54 articles), followed by intervention models (21.6%, 22), combined optimal control–cost-effectiveness models (15.7%, 16), and optimal control models (9.8%, 10). Key gaps included limited incorporation of drug and insecticide resistance, migration dynamics, and climate variability; the authors call for models better tailored to local transmission and programmatic needs.

articles.headline: Case report: acute liver failure from falciparum malaria, non-endemic country
articles.title_original: Acute Liver Failure Due to Plasmodium falciparum: A Rare Case from a Non-Endemic Country.
articles.authors: Batıbay E, Yüksekyayla O, Polat M
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: CLIP protein is essential for Plasmodium berghei host cell invasion
articles.title_original: The CLAMP-Linked Invasion Protein (CLIP) plays an essential role in Plasmodium berghei zoites.
articles.authors: Unhale T, Das S, Marinach C, Briquet S, Franetich JF, Boeykens L, Sterckx YG, Silvie O
articles.details: Using CRISPR-assisted conditional genome editing with dimerisable Cre recombinase in the rodent malaria parasite Plasmodium berghei, researchers studied the function of the CLAMP-Linked Invasion Protein (CLIP), part of the CLAMP-CLIP-SPATR microneme complex. Deleting clip in blood stages impaired parasite growth and prevented erythrocyte invasion by merozoites; deletion in transmission stages did not affect sporozoite development in mosquitoes but dramatically reduced salivary gland invasion. CLIP-deficient sporozoites were also impaired in cell traversal, productive invasion of mammalian hepatocytes, and gliding motility, recapitulating the CLAMP-deficient phenotype. The authors conclude CLIP plays an essential, conserved role in host cell invasion.

articles.headline: Parasite gene programs drive severe Plasmodium knowlesi malaria
articles.title_original: Integrative genomics of Plasmodium knowlesi reveals parasite-intrinsic regulators of severe human malaria.
articles.authors: Westaway JAF, Benavente ED, Kucharski M, Nayak S, Huy DTQ, Auburn S, William T, Rajahram GS, Piera KA, Trimarsanto H, Hoon KS, Bourke C, Jantim A, Manah AM, Gotulis W, Moon RW, Amato R, Barber BE, Drakeley C, Anstey NM, Field MA, Bozdech Z, Grigg MJ
articles.details: This integrative genomic study performed the first genome-wide association (GWAS), transcriptomic, and expression quantitative trait locus (eQTL) analyses on clinical P. knowlesi malaria isolates. WHO-defined severe disease was associated with transcriptional activation of stress-response and host-interaction pathways, while invasion-linked programs tracked more strongly with parasite burden and immune-evasion pathways linked to both. GWAS/eQTL analyses revealed genetic regulation of transcriptional states, including immune-evasion variant antigen families (SICAvar and kir) and chromatin-associated regulators. Mature gametocyte transcripts were detectable across infections, including low-parasitaemia cases. Authors conclude severe disease reflects genetically regulated parasite programs not fully explained by parasite burden.

articles.headline: Historical analysis of early Zionist rural malaria control in Palestine
articles.title_original: The Zionist decolonisation of rural malaria control in Palestine from 1922 onwards - what it teaches.
articles.authors: Alexander A
articles.details: This historical narrative examines rural malaria control in Palestine. It recounts that in 1918 the British Army temporarily controlled malaria for six months using thousands of mainly Egyptian labourers to destroy mosquito breeding sites, but the disease returned after control ceased on 19 September 1918, leaving many rural areas desolate. The author argues that, lacking the British Army's finances and manpower, the Zionists devised a creative, sustainable, country-wide malaria control programme conducted by all inhabitants for all inhabitants, which the author frames as an early example of the decolonisation of malaria control. No quantitative outcomes are reported.

articles.headline: Odorant-binding protein AsOBP21f mediates human-odor detection in Anopheles sinensis
articles.title_original: Functional characterization of AsOBP21f, an odorant-binding protein involved in human odor detection in the malaria vector Anopheles sinensis.
articles.authors: Li F, Xu O, Zhou J, He S, Chen B, He Z
articles.details: This laboratory study functionally characterized the odorant-binding protein AsOBP21f in the Asian malaria vector Anopheles sinensis using cloning, RT-qPCR, phylogenetics, recombinant protein fluorescence competitive binding assays with 35 human odorants, molecular docking, electroantennogram (EAG) recordings, behavioral assays, and RNAi knockdown. AsOBP21f was predominantly expressed in female olfactory tissues (antennae and proboscis) and selectively bound hydrophobic C10–C15 odorants, with strong affinity for methyl tridecanoate, dodecanal, decanal, and pentadecanoic acid. Methyl tridecanoate showed significant attraction and dodecanal clear repellency. RNAi silencing significantly reduced antennal responses to host odors and markedly decreased blood-feeding success, suggesting AsOBP21f is a potential malaria-control target.

articles.headline: China-Africa institutional network promotes malaria elimination cooperation
articles.title_original: The Institution-based Network on China-Africa Cooperation for Malaria Elimination: fostering a transcontinental hub for strengthening China-Africa cooperation on malaria control and elimination efforts.
articles.authors: Lu S, Ding W, Huang L, Liu L, Xiao N, Abdulla S, Okpeku M, Thiongane A, Gibba B, Kompaore SCB, Lv S, Li S, Weldedawit MA, Zhou XN, Wang D
articles.details: This descriptive policy article presents the Institution-based Network on China-Africa Cooperation for Malaria Elimination (INCAM), established in 2018 to foster transcontinental knowledge exchange, product innovation, and policy advocacy following WHO certification of China as malaria-free in 2021. Between 2019 and 2025, INCAM convened six fora to promote adaptation of China's anti-malaria practices to African contexts, including demand-driven research, innovative anti-malarial products, and evidence-based policies. The authors argue INCAM exemplifies the potential of China-Africa collaboration to accelerate malaria control and elimination in Africa, and call for stronger Public-Private-Academic Partnerships and enhanced capacity-building. No quantitative outcomes are reported.

articles.headline: Malaria cases rose over six years in Central Ethiopia
articles.title_original: A six years trend analysis of malaria prevalence in Central Ethiopia Region.
articles.authors: Teshome Z, Animut A, Negash Y, Giday M, Aklilu E
articles.details: This retrospective study analyzed aggregated district-level malaria surveillance data from 2019 to 2024 across 12 districts and two towns in the Hadia, Gurage, and Yem zones of the Central Ethiopia Region. Among 368,490 suspected cases, 86,604 (23.5%) were Plasmodium-positive, of which 61% were P. falciparum and 39% P. vivax. Cases showed an increasing trend since 2019 with fluctuations, peaking in May (13,811; 15.9%), the autumn season (27,266; 31.5%), and the spring season (25,555; 29.5%); distributions of P. falciparum and P. vivax differed significantly across districts, months, seasons, and years (Pearson chi-square). The authors conclude both species are endemic and recommend sustaining and scaling up malaria interventions.

articles.headline: Angiotensin II for refractory septic shock in falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Angiotensin II use in refractory septic shock due to Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Meconi T, Pazzi M, Papa R, Ruggiano P, Conti D
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Overview of malaria prophylaxis
articles.title_original: Malaria Prophylaxis.
articles.authors: Stachler L, DeVos E, Samandari T
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: HP1 phase separation silences P. falciparum var virulence genes
articles.title_original: Phase separation of heterochromatin protein 1 regulates virulence gene expression in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Mamatharani DV, Purkayastha D, Niederwieser I, Rai SK, Gaur P, Ganji M, Mukhopadhyay S, Voss TS, Karmodiya K
articles.details: A molecular study showing that Plasmodium falciparum heterochromatin protein 1 (PfHP1) undergoes liquid-liquid phase separation in vitro and compartmentalizes DNA, a process tunable by RNA and PfH2A.Z. Single-molecule assays showed PfHP1 preferentially compacts AT-rich DNA, and FRAP of GFP-PfHP1 parasites indicated fluid condensate behavior in vivo. Point mutations in the intrinsically disordered region that disrupt phase separation caused dispersed nuclear localization, altered chromatin binding, and derepression of up to 54 of 60 var genes, with Hi-C revealing loss of heterochromatic interactions. The authors conclude PfHP1-mediated phase separation is a key mechanism for heterochromatin organization and var gene silencing.

articles.headline: Anopheles pseudopunctipennis lays eggs mainly at dusk
articles.title_original: Daily pattern of oviposition and fecundity in Anopheles pseudopunctipennis Theobald, the main vector of malaria in Mexico.
articles.authors: Villarreal-Treviño C, González-Cerón L, Ríos-Delgado JC, Penilla-Navarro RP
articles.details: A laboratory and semi-field study in Tapachula, Chiapas, of oviposition and fecundity in nulliparous and parous females of Anopheles pseudopunctipennis, the primary malaria vector in Mexico, tested individually and in groups under insectary and field conditions. Females showed a primary oviposition peak between 18:00 and 20:00 with gradual decline until midnight; nulliparous insectary females stopped after 24:00, while a small percentage of parous females continued laying until shortly before dawn (between 24:00 and 06:00), and parous females laid more eggs than nulliparous ones. The authors propose pyrethroid chemical control by ultra-low volume or thermal fogging of breeding sites from dusk until midnight near areas with active malaria transmission.

articles.headline: Review finds weak entomological design in malaria vector-control trials
articles.title_original: A systematic review of entomological outcomes and sampling approaches used in the evaluation of cluster randomised controlled trials for malaria vector control products.
articles.authors: Githu V, Michael P, Biggs J, Cook J, Kiware S, McConnachie A, Johnson P, Ferguson HM
articles.details: A systematic review of entomological outcomes, sampling methods, and design features in cluster randomised controlled trials (cRCTs) of malaria vector control interventions, searching four databases and two trial registries and using an Entomological Study Design Risk (ESDR) metric and a linear mixed-effects model. Sixty-two cRCTs were identified between 1992 and 2021, with 74% conducted in Africa, using 13 different entomological outcomes and 12 sampling methods with considerable variation; 70% were categorized as having high potential entomological design risk due to a lack of power analyses and inadequate or unclear randomisation of sampling points. The only significant predictor of outcome precision was the number of entomological clusters in which data were collected, with precision increasing as cluster number increased. The authors call for standardization and clearer reporting guidelines to improve quality, comparability, and policy relevance.

articles.headline: Historical sources on the cultural history of malaria in Hungary
articles.title_original: [Data for cultural history of malaria in Hungary].
articles.authors: Kiss L
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Indole peptidomimetics kill drug-resistant P. falciparum in lab tests
articles.title_original: Indole Peptidomimetics Show Potent and Selective Activity against Drug-Resistant Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Januário MAP, Valdes TA, Maluf SEC, Mendes GR, Bonatto V, Moura IMR, Mourão PS, Corrêa AG, Guido RVC
articles.details: This drug-discovery study designed, synthesized, and tested indole-based peptidomimetics against sensitive and multidrug-resistant Plasmodium falciparum strains. Structure-activity analysis showed aromatic/halogen substituents and indole-nitrogen and amide-linkage modifications drove potency and selectivity. Lead compounds LSPN954 (4i) and LSPN959 (4k) showed low micromolar potency (IC50 against 3D7 = 1.7 and 1.0 μM), low cytotoxicity on HepG2 and HEK293 cells (CC50 ≥ 100 μM), and high selectivity indices (59 and 95). They were slow-acting, additive with artesunate, and retained efficacy against resistant strains (Dd2, K1, Dd2R_DSM265, 3D7R_MMV848) with no cross-resistance. Authors conclude these are promising antimalarial scaffolds for further optimization.

articles.headline: Review of progress and obstacles in P. falciparum malaria vaccines
articles.title_original: Recent Advances, Bottlenecks, and Future Directions in Plasmodium falciparum Vaccine Development.
articles.authors: Turan G, Boggio MJ, Othman AS, Nnaemeka V, Hill AVS, Salman AM
articles.details: This review summarizes current malaria vaccine strategies and their key limitations. It reports an estimated 282 million malaria cases and 610,000 deaths in 2024, disproportionately affecting children under five and pregnant women in sub-Saharan Africa. Although antimalarial drugs effectively clear infections, the authors present vaccines as a critical tool for reducing malaria-associated morbidity and mortality and interrupting transmission, yet the complex lifecycle of Plasmodium parasites complicates identifying protective antigens and optimal formulations. They also highlight emerging opportunities for future research and development.

articles.headline: Modelling RTS,S vaccine impact on child malaria in Benin
articles.title_original: Assessing the potential impact of the RTS,S/ASO1 vaccine on malaria burden in Benin: a mathematical modelling approach.
articles.authors: Gbaguidi GJ, Loum MA, Gning LD, Tahiho AK, Diallo EM, Koutou O, Dembélé A, Topanou N, Ketoh GK
articles.details: This mathematical modelling study used an Epidemiological Multi-Diseases Model (EMOD), parameterised with entomological, epidemiological, and climatic data from Benin covering 2010-2023, to assess the RTS,S/AS01 vaccine's potential impact on malaria in children under five in Couffo province. Nine scenarios combining coverage levels and dose schedules were compared against a no-vaccination baseline using one-way ANOVA and Tukey's HSD. Higher coverage and booster doses gave greater reductions; Scenario 9 (90% coverage, two boosters) averted 9.8% of PfPR, 11.06% of cases, and 21.83% of severe cases over 2030. The authors support integrating RTS,S/AS01 into Benin's Expanded Programme on Immunisation alongside continued surveillance.

articles.headline: Placental malaria common among pregnant women in eastern Uganda
articles.title_original: Prevalence of placental Plasmodium falciparum malaria and associated factors among pregnant women in health facilities in Eastern Uganda.
articles.authors: Rosset AA, Gerald B, Hussein M, Doreen N, Wahabu WA, Rudolf B, Hope M, Bagenda CN, Edgar K, Moses O
articles.details: This cross-sectional study assessed placental Plasmodium falciparum malaria prevalence and associated factors among 180 pregnant women delivering at health facilities in Mayuge district, eastern Uganda (mean age 23.2 ± 5.8 years). Maternal-side placental blood was screened by Giemsa microscopy and qPCR for sub-microscopic infection. Placental malaria prevalence was 11.67% (95% CI 7.71-17.28) by microscopy and 23.89% (95% CI 18.19-30.71) by qPCR. Being aged 15-19 years (aOR 5.87, p=0.023) and not attending antenatal care (aOR 4.27, p=0.011) were significantly associated with placental malaria. The authors conclude placental malaria is common and linked to fewer than three ANC visits, urging efforts to increase ANC attendance.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic Plasmodium ovale parasitemia frequently recurs in coastal Tanzania
articles.title_original: Frequent Plasmodium ovale recurrence in coastal Tanzania.
articles.authors: Carey-Ewend K, Shepard H, Yang G, Odas M, Peter EE, Dominick MC, Rutha I, Amagai K, Vasquez-Martinez B, Mulei LA, Chhetri S, Kharabora O, Adams JH, Lin FC, Bailey J, Edwards JK, Gower EW, Juliano JJ, Ngasala BE, Lin JT
articles.details: Prospective cohort of 74 asymptomatic adults and children >5 years in Bagamoyo, Tanzania who were PCR-positive for P. ovale, followed up to 24 weeks with biweekly PCR for P. ovale and P. falciparum; persistent or recurrent infections were offered artemether-lumefantrine. At baseline 31 had P. falciparum co-infection; 55 were followed to 24 weeks and 32 treated. Most initial parasitemias were short-lived, with only 19/74 (26%) persisting to enrollment (median 4 days after screening), but 41/74 (55%) developed recurrent P. ovale a median of 10 weeks later (IQR 4-12w) and nine had a second recurrence. Recurrence risk did not differ by sex or age but appeared higher in the dry season; microsatellite genotyping showed most recurrences were heterologous. The authors conclude asymptomatic P. ovale frequently recurs, though the proportion due to relapse remains unclear.

articles.headline: Genomics reveals recent host jumps between P. vivax and P. simium
articles.title_original: Genomic Insights Into Host Shifts Between Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium simium in Latin America.
articles.authors: Lefebvre MJM, Degrugillier F, Arnathau C, González C, Rondón S, Link A, Chaves A, Benavides JA, Campos AAS, Dos Santos E, Huff R, da Silva CMD, Vanderhoeven E, De Thoisy B, Fontaine MC, Prugnolle F, Rougeron V
articles.details: Population-genomic study screening 719 monkey samples from five Latin American countries, which identified 23 Plasmodium-positives but only four with sufficient mitochondrial DNA for reliable species identification; these were analyzed alongside whole-genome variation from 19 P. simium and 405 P. vivax isolates. P. vivax, typically human-restricted, was found in three Colombian and one Brazilian monkeys, suggesting host niche expansion, and analyses indicated recent genetic exchange between the species with P. simium arising from a host jump roughly one to two centuries ago. Genome-wide scans showed positive selection in P. simium genes for primate-host interaction (PvRBP2a, PvRBP1b) and mosquito-vector interaction (PvCMRP1, PvPAT, Pvs47). The authors emphasize zoonotic risk and the need to include monkeys in malaria control.

articles.headline: DHA fish oil protects gut but not lungs in malaria-infected mice
articles.title_original: Differential Effects of DHA-Rich Fish Oil Supplementation on Intestinal and Pulmonary Alterations in Experimental Malaria Caused by Plasmodium berghei NK65.
articles.authors: Pereira LPMC, Silva-Rodrigues JH, Pinheiro FM, Machado LF, da Silva ACF, Marques CB, Gomes MT, Gonçalves FMM, Bezerra-Bellei JC, Brum G, Dias VC, Hipólito JR, Bastos AN, Nascimento HLDS, E Silva FMC, D'Avila H, Neto AF, Reis Junior JL, Rocha VN, Ribeiro-Gomes FL, Castro JMA, Scopel KKG
articles.details: Experimental study in C57BL/6 mice infected with Plasmodium berghei NK65 testing whether DHA-rich fish oil (omega-3) supplementation modulates lung injury and intestinal changes, given the gut-lung axis and prior benefit seen with the P. berghei ANKA strain. Supplementation was associated with partial preservation of intestinal morphology, including reduced intestinal shortening, but these effects did not translate into improved pulmonary outcomes. The authors conclude that omega-3 effects are strain- and pathogenesis-dependent, underscoring the importance of considering diverse Plasmodium pathogenic profiles when evaluating nutritional or immunomodulatory interventions.

articles.headline: Hospitalized malaria in rural Madagascar: disease severity drives longer stays
articles.title_original: Clinical Characteristics and Outcomes of Hospitalized Malaria Patients in Rural Madagascar.
articles.authors: Kasprowicz D, Korzeniewski K, Wilczyńska W
articles.details: Three-year retrospective study (2023-2025) of 134 patients of all ages hospitalized with malaria confirmed by rapid diagnostic test and microscopy at a rural district hospital in Ambatoboeny, Madagascar; median age was 15 years (IQR 7-25) and 52.2% were female. P. falciparum predominated (94.0%) with 6.0% mixed-species infections; 20.1% had severe malaria including 10.4% cerebral malaria, and co-infections were frequent (52.2%), most commonly Schistosoma haematobium (14.2%) and typhoid fever (12.7%). Intravenous artesunate was given to 97.8% of patients; median length of stay was 2 days (IQR 2-3) and 12.7% had prolonged stay (≥5 days). Prolonged stay was significantly associated with cerebral malaria, high parasitemia (≥5%), blood transfusion, and age <15 years (all p≤0.034). The authors stress early severity recognition and resource planning.

articles.headline: Land use and climate shape malaria vector biting in Amazon
articles.title_original: Reframing vector control: land use and climate shape malaria risk in Amazonian communities.
articles.authors: Acosta AL, Bergo ES, Figueira EAG, Conn JE, Sallum MAM
articles.details: Field study of the human biting rate (HBR) of the malaria vector Nyssorhynchus darlingi measured by human landing catches at 70 sites across four Brazilian Amazonian ecoregions, with simultaneous temperature and humidity recordings and landscape composition quantified within 100-, 250-, and 500-m buffers, analyzed using linear regressions, mixed-effects models, and principal component analysis. Researchers recorded 14,689 bites across 384 events; HBR was highest in areas of intermediate forest degradation, positively associated with temperature and negatively with humidity, with floodable vegetation the strongest land-cover predictor, especially at the 500-m scale, and intrazonal variation exceeding interzonal differences. The authors conclude vector control in the Amazon should be climate-sensitive and spatially targeted using land-cover and microclimate data.

articles.headline: Combining malaria control measures protects against severe malaria, not progression
articles.title_original: Progression from uncomplicated to severe malaria among children in settings receiving different combinations of malaria control interventions in sub-Saharan Africa: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Okek EJ, Jehopio J, Sande OJ, Musinguzi B, Awor S, Asio J, Le DK, Lutwama J, Alison AK, Bongomin F, Ocan M, Kayondo J
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis (PRISMA; 12 studies, 9,283,160 individuals) assessed whether different combinations of vector and parasite control interventions in sub-Saharan Africa affect progression from uncomplicated to severe malaria and the prevalence of parasitaemia and gametocytaemia in children. There was no significant difference in progression across intervention settings (OR 0.56; 95%CI 0.31–1.02; P=0.06). Deploying multiple combined interventions was associated with lower odds of severe malaria (pooled OR 0.41; 0.30–0.56; p<0.00), parasitaemia (OR 0.52; 0.38–0.70), uncomplicated malaria (OR 0.64; 0.43–0.95; p=0.003), and gametocytes (OR 0.41; 0.28–0.60). Authors conclude combined interventions protect against disease but add no extra protection against progression.

articles.headline: Discarded malaria RDTs reveal DENV-3 dengue circulating in Haiti
articles.title_original: Characterization of arbovirus infections in patients within Haiti by screening discarded malaria rapid diagnostic test cassettes.
articles.authors: Matulis GA, Smith HP, Hall G, Katich RS, Delp KL, Douglas CE, Williams J, Hirsch S, Kumar R, Pshea-Smith I, Lilak AA, Okech BA, Ricks K, Stefan CP, Existe A, Kugelman JR, Sutherland I, Dunford J, Boncy J, von Fricken ME, Koehler JW
articles.details: Researchers screened nucleic acids from discarded malaria rapid diagnostic test (RDT) cassettes collected 2021–2023 from febrile patients in the Sud and Ouest Departments of Haiti to characterize circulating arboviruses. After RNase P integrity checks and a flavivirus real-time PCR assay, 52 RDTs were flavivirus-positive and 21 indeterminate; a DENV quadraplex assay identified 68 samples positive for DENV-3, all collected in 2023, with sequences phylogenetically similar to Caribbean sequences from 2022–2023. No samples were positive for alphaviruses (0/517) or Oropouche virus (0/293). The authors conclude discarded malaria RDTs are a useful tool for remote arbovirus surveillance.

articles.headline: Malaria infection alters liver enzymes and lipid profile in adults
articles.title_original: Malaria parasitemia and its association with liver function parameters, and lipid profile among malaria-infected adult patients in western Ethiopia: A comparative cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Checkole N, Cheneke W, Ibrahim T, Bekele S, Teklay B, Tefera M, Gebretsadik A, Fikadu B, Kassahun GB
articles.details: This institution-based comparative cross-sectional study in Assosa town, Ethiopia (December 23, 2021 to March 4, 2022) enrolled 302 participants (151 malaria-infected, 151 comparison) selected by consecutive sampling, with parameters measured on a Cobas 311 analyzer.
