Among participants, 212 (70.2%) were male and 70.86% of malaria patients were infected with P. falciparum, with low, moderate, and high parasitaemia in 30.46%, 46.36%, and 23.18%. Mean aspartate aminotransferase, alanine aminotransferase, alkaline phosphatase, and serum bilirubin were significantly higher in malaria patients than controls (p<0.001), while total cholesterol, HDL, and LDL cholesterol were significantly lower (p<0.05). Authors recommend assessing liver function and lipids in malaria-infected individuals.

articles.headline: In silico bispecific antibody designed to block Plasmodium erythrocyte invasion
articles.title_original: Computational epitope discovery and in silico design of a bispecific antibody model (COMED hook) to block Plasmodium invasion into erythrocytes.
articles.authors: Köseoğlu AE, Gül N, Duran T, Öztürk C, Hekim N
articles.details: This computational study aimed to identify conserved, immunogenic B-cell epitopes in P. falciparum and human erythrocytes to design a bispecific antibody model (COMED hook) that blocks erythrocyte invasion, using epitope prediction, conservancy assessment via BLAST, structural modeling, docking, and antibody design with DiffAb. A total of 123 antigenic P. falciparum B-cell epitopes were identified, of which 58 were conserved across species, and 92 non-conserved human erythrocyte epitopes were found, with 12 selected as host-targeting domains. Two highly conserved Plasmodium epitopes (KHVETWTQRVQNM, LHSNFYIKRF) showed favorable Fab binding. The authors present this as an in silico framework requiring experimental validation.

articles.headline: Flow cytometry method quantifies liver-stage infection by non-fluorescent Plasmodium
articles.title_original: Flow cytometry-based evaluation of hepatic infection by non-fluorescent Plasmodium parasites.
articles.authors: Teixeira B, Nunes-Cabaço H, Mota MM, Fontinha D, Prudêncio M
articles.details: This methods paper presents a novel flow cytometry-based approach to quantify hepatic infection by Plasmodium parasites lacking fluorescent reporter genes, addressing limited tools for studying the asymptomatic liver stage. The method detects parasites using two proteins: heat shock protein 70 (HSP70) in the parasite cytoplasm and upregulated in infectious sporozoites 4 (UIS4) on the parasitophorous vacuole membrane. The authors demonstrate rapid, cost-effective quantification of hepatic infection and intracellular development, proposing it as a tool for evaluating reporter-free whole-sporozoite vaccine candidates and broader malaria research.

articles.headline: Meta-analysis: thalassemia shows non-significant protective trend against malaria
articles.title_original: Exploring the protective effects of thalassemia against malaria in Africa: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Donkor AB, Nyarko SB, Asare KK, Asa-Atiemo CKM, Kusi KA, Afrane YA, Amoah LE
articles.details: This PRISMA-registered systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated whether thalassemia protects against malaria, drawing on studies conducted in Africa. Of 4,957 screened articles, 15 met inclusion criteria and 8 provided sufficient data for meta-analysis using a random-effects model. Thalassemic individuals had lower odds of developing malaria (pooled OR 0.856), but the association was not statistically significant and showed substantial heterogeneity. Mechanistically, alpha thalassemia reduced cytoadhesion and rosette formation and enhanced immune clearance, with favorable interactions with the haptoglobin Hp2-1 phenotype. The authors call for further molecular research.

articles.headline: Pharmacokinetics support higher clindamycin dose for young children
articles.title_original: Population pharmacokinetics of fosmidomycin and clindamycin in combination with artesunate for uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Gabonese children and adults.
articles.authors: Pfaffendorf C, Dejon-Agobé JC, Edoa JR, Maïga-Ascofaré O, Ahenkan E, Adegnika AA, Ramharter M, Wicha SG, Mischlinger J
articles.details: Within an open-label randomized trial, population pharmacokinetic models were developed for fosmidomycin and clindamycin given with artesunate for uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria. Forty patients aged 3-57 years received oral artesunate (2 mg/kg), fosmidomycin (30 mg/kg), and clindamycin (10 mg/kg) every 12 hours for three days, with plasma analyzed by LC-MS/MS. One-compartment models with first-order absorption best fit both drugs; body weight (allometric scaling) drove clearance and volume, and body temperature affected fosmidomycin clearance. Weight-based fosmidomycin dosing gave consistent exposure across ages, but clindamycin exposure was substantially lower in children under 12. Simulations suggest increasing clindamycin to 12 mg/kg in children under 35 kg.

articles.headline: MERISCREEN HRP-II rapid test reliable in rural Ghana children
articles.title_original: Evaluation of malaria rapid diagnostic test kit performance in a high-transmission setting: a facility-based cross-sectional study in rural Ghana.
articles.authors: Boadu S, Axame WK, Abanga WA, Saeed FU, Adjei MR, Kubio C
articles.details: This facility-based cross-sectional study evaluated the MERISCREEN Malaria Pf HRP-II Ag RDT against microscopy in six public health facilities in Nandom Municipality, Ghana. A total of 404 children under five were recruited by systematic sampling and tested by both microscopy and RDT. The RDT showed sensitivity of 98.1% (95% CI 92.6-99.7) and specificity of 92.6% (95% CI 88.9-95.2), with PPV 82.4%, NPV 99.1%, and overall accuracy 94.8%. Real-world performance was marginally lower than manufacturer values, but the authors conclude prequalified RDTs are reliable for guiding treatment where microscopy is unavailable, given proper storage, training, and quality assessment.

articles.headline: Biocredit rapid tests outperform conventional RDTs in southern Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Evaluation of NxTek™ Eliminate Malaria-PF test and biocredit rapid diagnostic tests for detecting Plasmodium species in Southern Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Bogale A, Tefera S, Taye Gebremeskele B, Mitiku A, Degefa T
articles.details: This health facility-based cross-sectional study assessed the NxTek Eliminate Malaria-Pf, Biocredit Pf (pLDH/HRP2), and Biocredit Pf/Pv (pLDH/pLDH) RDTs in 384 malaria-suspected febrile subjects at Dilla Zuria health facility, using microscopy and qPCR as references. Against microscopy, NxTek had 87.6% sensitivity and 91.3% specificity for P. falciparum, while Biocredit Pf/Pv showed 97.9% sensitivity/97.4% specificity for P. falciparum and 94.5%/97.5% for P. vivax; the conventional SD Bioline reached only 51.3% sensitivity for P. falciparum. Against qPCR, Biocredit Pf/Pv showed 95.5%/96.4% for P. falciparum and 90.8%/99.1% for P. vivax. The authors conclude Biocredit RDTs met WHO criteria and suit resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Urban malaria in Libreville highly heterogeneous with shifting hotspots
articles.title_original: Spatio-temporal heterogeneity of urban malaria in Libreville, Gabon (2012-2023): neighborhood-level hotspot analysis for micro-stratified control.
articles.authors: Mohamed-Djawad MH, Moutombi Ditombi BC, Ndong Ngomo JM, Mbang Nguema OA, Agbanrin AA, Pongui Ngondza B, Kono HN, Nymane T, Ombago J, Migueba V, James LAJ, Moussavou Mabicka DA, Mihindou CJ, Ndong Akomezoghe L, Ndong Mouity JT, Mawili-Mboumba DP, Bouyou-Akotet MK, M'Bondoukwé NP
articles.details: This study characterized neighborhood-level malaria dynamics among 9,507 febrile children at the Melen sentinel site in Libreville, Gabon (Jan 2012-Dec 2023), using microscopy. P. falciparum prevalence was mapped across 44 neighborhoods and four periods, with Moran's I and Getis-Ord Gi* hotspot analysis. In 2023, PfPR was highest in semi-rural (50.9%, 95% CI 47.9-54.0) and semi-urban areas (46.5%), followed by shanty-towns (44.6%) and urban areas (38.0%), with marked within-category heterogeneity. WHO classification shifted from mostly mesoendemic in 2015 to 41.5% hyperendemic neighborhoods in 2023, and hotspots moved over time. The authors support micro-stratified control prioritizing recurrent clusters.

articles.headline: Review: malaria drug resistance, new treatments, and formulation advances
articles.title_original: Advancing global malaria control: therapeutic strategies, drug discovery, and formulation innovations.
articles.authors: Sarma K, Sarma H, Deka J
articles.details: This review examines challenges and recent progress in malaria control, focusing on drug resistance, treatment strategies, drug discovery, and formulation development. It notes that artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) remain the primary treatment but that new regimens are urgently needed due to partially rising resistance linked to Pfkelch13 mutations, and it highlights Medicines for Malaria Venture (MMV) pipeline candidates including Z439, KAF156, MMV371, MMV055/167, INE963, and GSK701. It also describes improved diagnostics from microscopy to PCR and formulation innovations that enhance drug stability, bioavailability, and patient compliance. The authors conclude that integrated, multidisciplinary strategies are needed to advance malaria eradication.

articles.headline: Review proposes microbiota–barrier–immunity strategy for African malaria control
articles.title_original: Reinventing malaria control: hierarchical intervention strategies for African settings based on the microbiota-barrier-innate immunity axis.
articles.authors: Zhu Y, Wu S, Qi Y, Wang J
articles.details: Review proposing a unified "microbiota-barrier-innate immunity" axis hypothesis for malaria control. It argues that gut microbiota normally modulates host innate immunity via metabolites and maintains barrier integrity, while Plasmodium infection disrupts this axis, causing microbiota imbalance, metabolic derangement, and intestinal barrier leakage that worsen disease pathology. Based on this, the authors construct a hierarchical three-tier (foundational–targeted–precision) intervention pyramid tailored to resource-limited African settings, intended to address parasite drug resistance and inadequate vaccine protection.

articles.headline: Artemisinin-resistance K13 mutation found in recurrent Ethiopia malaria cases
articles.title_original: Recurrent parasitemias with artemisinin partial resistance mutations during the 2024 Ethiopia malaria resurgence: a case series.
articles.authors: Geleta D, Brhane BG, Abera A, Belachew M, Sime H, Gebreegziaxher A, Getu M, Ali A, Tesfaye N, Habtetsion M, Kokobe B, Kebede M, Worku Z, Tasew G, Tadesse G, Tollera G, Fola AA, Bailey JA, Juliano JJ, Parr JB, Abte M, Assefa A
articles.details: Case series using Ethiopia's Public Health Emergency Management surveillance system to investigate antimalarial drug-resistance markers among recurrent clinical P. falciparum malaria at three resurgence sites in central Ethiopia. Isolates from 15 patients with confirmed recurrence were genotyped for k13, pfcrt, pfmdr1, pfdhfr, and pfdhps using PfSMARRTer multiplex amplicon sequencing. Three of 15 patients (20%) were infected with parasites carrying the WHO-candidate artemisinin partial-resistance marker K13 P441L, while other resistance markers were largely fixed. The authors conclude that ACT resistance-associated markers are present and call for strengthened molecular surveillance integrated with case monitoring.

articles.headline: High-density peptide array maps immunogenic Plasmodium vivax proteins
articles.title_original: Comprehensive characterization of Plasmodium vivax antigens using a high-density peptide array.
articles.authors: Asawa RR, Hazzard B, Tebben K, Tan J, Cantaert T, Berry A, Tolia N, Popovici J, Serre D
articles.details: Study using a high-density peptide array of 4.2 million peptides covering the entire protein sequence of all P. vivax genes to characterize antibody responses in infected and malaria-naïve individuals. The analysis identified 283 proteins commonly immunogenic in symptomatic individuals, including most proteins known to be involved in erythrocyte invasion, a putative new invasion protein, several nucleoporins, and many uncharacterized proteins. A distinct antibody response pattern against PIR proteins in asymptomatic individuals could be associated with protection against clinical vivax malaria. The authors present this as a resource for developing diagnostics and vaccine candidates.

articles.headline: Case report: cerebral malaria from Plasmodium vivax in Colombian adult
articles.title_original: Cerebral malaria caused by Plasmodium vivax in an afrodescendant adult in Colombia: case report.
articles.authors: Sastre-Martínez AD, Gutiérrez MAG, Vallejo-Serna RA, Muñoz-Lombo JP
articles.details: Case report of a 43-year-old woman from the southwestern Pacific coast, Colombia, who developed severe neurological symptoms with an initially unclear diagnosis. A thick blood smear confirmed P. vivax infection. She was treated with intravenous artesunate and subsequently with chloroquine and primaquine, and achieved a full recovery. The authors emphasize that P. vivax, not only P. falciparum, can cause cerebral malaria through mechanical and inflammatory mechanisms, and that early treatment with parenteral artesunate plus intensive supportive care is key to improving outcomes.

articles.headline: Abbott-Bioline malaria RDT performs well in high-transmission Madagascar despite border failures
articles.title_original: Field and laboratory evaluation of Abbott-Bioline™ Malaria Ag Pf/Pv RDT performance in a high-transmission setting: contrasting results with a low-endemic area.
articles.authors: Fenomanana J, Ravoavy A, Rakotoarisoa M, Platon L, Wade A, Chala B, Tibiri YNG, Muller A, Thiebaut L, Caspar E, Strubel PE, Ménard D, Ratsimbasoa A
articles.details: A laboratory and field evaluation tested Abbott-Bioline™ Malaria Ag Pf/Pv RDTs (using serial dilutions of cultured P. falciparum) and enrolled 218 consecutive febrile patients at Ranomafana health centre, southeastern Madagascar, comparing both Abbott-Bioline and Parascreen® RDTs against microscopy and real-time PCR. Field prevalence was 70.2% by PCR and 48.6% by microscopy; against microscopy Abbott-Bioline reached 99.1% sensitivity and 93.7% specificity, while against PCR sensitivity fell to 73.2% (specificity 98.5%). Lab testing showed inter-lot variability with detection failures at 97–373 parasites/µL. The authors conclude RDT performance is highly context-dependent, likely reflecting higher parasite densities in high-transmission settings, and both tests missed about 25% of PCR-positive infections.

articles.headline: How plants biosynthesize a crucial malaria drug
articles.title_original: Botanical mystery solved: how plants make a crucial malaria drug.
articles.authors: Thompson B
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Malaria coinfection boosts intestinal worm egg output in mice
articles.title_original: Coinfection with malaria alters the fecundity and within-host persistence of an intestinal nematode.
articles.authors: Bourbon L, Dusuel A, Groetz E, Rialland M, Roche B, Faivre B, Sorci G
articles.details: Using a mouse model, researchers tested whether coinfection with Plasmodium yoelii alters the fecundity and within-host persistence of the nematode Heligmosomoides polygyrus. Coinfected mice excreted more worm eggs than singly infected mice while intestinal worm biomass was unchanged, indicating a plastic increase in worm fecundity; coinfection also extended the patent period, with higher cumulative egg output up to 99 days post-infection. The effect was driven by suppressed host Th2 immunity—coinfection lowered IL-4/IL-13 gene expression, plasma IL-5 and IL-13, and GATA-3+ CD4+ T cells—and neutralizing IL-13 reproduced the increased egg excretion while recombinant IL-13 reduced it. The authors conclude malaria coinfection could increase the environmental reservoir of soil-transmitted helminths.

articles.headline: Review: targeting Plasmodium purine salvage for antimalarial drug design
articles.title_original: Targeting Plasmodium falciparum with purine antimetabolites as a therapeutic strategy.
articles.authors: Tashie W, de Koning HP, Duah-Quashie NO, Quashie NB
articles.details: This review discusses targeting the purine salvage pathway as an antimalarial strategy, noting that P. falciparum lacks de novo purine biosynthesis and depends entirely on salvaging host purines. The authors focus on using P. falciparum Equilibrative Nucleoside Transporters (Pf ENTs) as conduits to deliver purine analogs that are selectively activated within the parasite to disrupt purine metabolism and nucleic acid synthesis. They note no purine-based antimalarial has yet reached clinical use and propose Pf ENTs as a framework for target-based design of purine-analog therapies.

articles.headline: CRISPR editing confirms K13 C580Y drives artemisinin tolerance in Ethiopian parasites
articles.title_original: Functional validation of the Plasmodium falciparum K13 C580Y mutation in recently collected Ethiopian isolates.
articles.authors: Mukherjee A, Assefa AB, Turlo CV, Needham LC, Shoue D, Qahash T, Belachew M, Tadesse D, Kassie E, Berihun M, Brhane BG, Parr JB, Ferdig MT, Adane S, Tesfaye A, Zuromski J, Fola AA, Tasew G, Tollera G, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA
articles.details: Following the first detection of the P. falciparum kelch13 C580Y substitution in the Horn of Africa, researchers used CRISPR-Cas9 to introduce C580Y into two recently collected Ethiopian clinical isolates. Ring-stage survival assays showed significantly elevated in vitro dihydroartemisinin (DHA) survival in edited parasites compared with isogenic controls. The authors conclude that C580Y confers artemisinin tolerance in contemporary Ethiopian genetic backgrounds, providing one of the first causal assessments of this mutation in recent African isolates.

articles.headline: Serological markers poorly predict P. vivax relapse risk in falciparum patients
articles.title_original: Predicting risk of Plasmodium vivax microscopy-detected episodes using serological markers in patients with Plasmodium falciparum malaria: a multi-country diagnostic performance evaluation.
articles.authors: Hafidzah M, Degaga TS, Christian M, Alam MS, Hossain MS, Baird JK, Sutano I, Smith L, Argyropoulos D, Amelia AR, Noviyanti R, Robinson LJ, Price RN, Mueller I, Thriemer K, Longley RJ
articles.details: This multi-country diagnostic evaluation tested whether Plasmodium vivax serological exposure markers (SEMs) could identify P. falciparum patients at risk of a microscopy-detected P. vivax episode within 63 days, to guide universal radical cure. Using PRIMA study samples from Ethiopia, Indonesia, and Bangladesh, IgG antibodies were measured in 244 P. falciparum participants, of whom 22 developed a vivax episode. An existing random forest classifier showed 82% sensitivity but only 27% specificity; a re-trained study-specific model raised specificity to 68% while sensitivity dropped to 68%. The authors conclude SEMs have limited suitability for guiding radical cure here.

articles.headline: High Wolbachia prevalence found in wild Tanzanian Anopheles funestus mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Detection of supergroup B Wolbachia strains and their co-infection with Plasmodium falciparum in wild Anopheles funestus in southeastern Tanzania: implications for malaria control.
articles.authors: Mmweteni RE, Philbert AB, Mkandawile G, Abilahi F, Abbasi S, Msemo J, Milonge S, Tumbo FA, Mwalugelo Y, Muyaga LL, Msaky D, Kipekepeke AR, Okumu FO, Kaindoa EW, Baldini F
articles.details: This field study assessed natural Wolbachia infection and co-infection with Plasmodium falciparum in wild Anopheles funestus sensu lato collected from 52 households across five villages in southeastern Tanzania, using CDC light traps and Prokopack aspirators, with detection by conventional and nested PCR plus ELISA for sporozoites. Among 400 mosquitoes, Wolbachia prevalence was 46.5% by primary PCR and 70.8% by nested PCR. P. falciparum sporozoites were found in only 3 mosquitoes (0.8%), with one Wolbachia co-infection (0.3%). Sequencing of 16S rDNA, coxA, and wsp genes placed strains in supergroup B; authors call for larger studies on the Wolbachia–P. falciparum association.

articles.headline: Review: delayed malaria care-seeking harms African children under five
articles.title_original: Delay in malaria care-seeking for children under five in Africa: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Amoadu M, Alhassan A, Odoom E
articles.details: This systematic review, following Joanna Briggs Institute and PRISMA guidance, synthesized evidence on the prevalence, determinants, and outcomes of delayed malaria care-seeking among children under five in sub-Saharan Africa. The reported prevalence of delayed care-seeking ranged widely from 2% to 95.8%. Contributing factors included financial constraints, long travel distances, reliance on traditional treatments, perception of malaria as mild, poor health-worker attitudes, and shortages of facility logistics. Delays were associated with increased mortality, progression to severe malaria, and neurological and haematological complications. The authors recommend interventions addressing financial barriers, access, and health-system readiness.

articles.headline: Stochastic model links climate and interventions to malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Assessing the impact of climate and control interventions on spatio-temporal malaria dynamics using a stochastic metapopulation model.
articles.authors: Angelakis A, Beloconi A, Nyawanda BO, Khagayi S, Kariuki S, Munga S, Munywoki PK, Bigogo G, Vounatsou P
articles.details: This modeling study developed a process-based stochastic metapopulation transmission model to capture malaria dynamics in the persistently high-transmission lowlands of western Kenya, incorporating immunity, infectivity, host and vector mobility, migration-driven parasite importation, control interventions, and local climate variability within a single mechanistic framework. The authors report that, for 2008 to 2019, the model accurately captured the effects of small-scale heterogeneity at the subpopulation level. They argue the framework is generalisable to other endemic regions and can support localized forecasting and intervention planning by integrating future climatic conditions with intervention scenarios.

articles.headline: Human peptide LL-37 kills malaria parasites by targeting infected cells
articles.title_original: LL-37 selectively targets Plasmodium-infected erythrocytes and exhibits antimalarial activity.
articles.authors: He X, Zhang Y, Lou J, Wu J, Xu S, Zhu G, Tang J, Fang Y, Cao J
articles.details: This laboratory and animal study shows the human antimicrobial peptide LL-37 has antiplasmodial activity against both drug-sensitive and drug-resistant parasites in vitro, selectively targeting infected red blood cells through membrane disruption mediated by phosphatidylserine externalization and cholesterol depletion. Elevated plasma LL-37/CRAMP was seen in malaria patients and infected mice; exogenous LL-37/CRAMP reduced parasitemia, improved survival, and lowered pro-inflammatory cytokines, while CRAMP-deficient mice were more susceptible. However, therapeutic administration after infection showed limited efficacy, likely from rapid peptide degradation, and effective killing concentrations far exceeded endogenous levels. The authors call for stabilized analogs, better delivery, and combination approaches.

articles.headline: Neurologic deficits common after childhood cerebral malaria, meta-analysis finds
articles.title_original: Prevalence and risk factors of gross neurologic deficits in children after severe malaria: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Okullo AE, Ouma S, John CC, van Hensbroek MB, Ojiambo KO, Otike C, Kinengyere AA, Conroy AL, Ocan M, Obuku EA, Idro R
articles.details: This PRISMA-guided systematic review and meta-analysis pooled 41 studies from 16 countries in Sub-Saharan Africa and Asia, comprising 11,635 children, on gross neurologic deficits (motor, movement, sensory, speech) after severe malaria, searching MEDLINE, EMBASE, Web of Science, and Global Index Medicus. Prevalence of deficits at hospital discharge was 15.2% (95%CI 11.5–18.8) in cerebral malaria (CM) versus 2.4% (95%CI 2.0–2.8) in broader severe malaria, declining to 5.7% by 12 months. Regionally, Sub-Saharan Africa showed 14.2%, East Asia & Pacific 2.3%, South Asia 6.1%. Risk factors included profound coma, coma ≥48 hours, multiple convulsions, hypoglycaemia, and acute kidney injury.

articles.headline: Chronic asymptomatic malaria linked to traumatic splenic rupture needing splenectomy
articles.title_original: Chronic Malaria Is Associated With Trauma-related Splenic Rupture Requiring Splenectomy.
articles.authors: Shanti PAI, Alexander K, Candrawati F, Andries B, Kambuaya NN, Rini H, Amelia AR, Puspitasari AM, Amalia R, Rahmayenti DA, Leonardo L, Prayoga P, Trianty L, Pava Z, Kenangalem E, Auburn S, Price RN, Laksanawati IS, Buffet PA, Noviyanti R, Anstey NM, Poespoprodjo JR, Kho S
articles.details: In Timika, Papua, Indonesia, researchers measured PCR-detectable asymptomatic peripheral parasitemia in patients undergoing trauma-related splenectomy (2015-21) and compared it with a 2013 household survey of the general population. Asymptomatic parasitemia was more than twice as common in splenectomy patients: 87.9% (29/33) versus 38.6% (697/1807); P < .0001. The authors conclude that asymptomatic infection with either P. falciparum or P. vivax is associated with splenic rupture following trauma, indicating an additional consequence of chronic infection in endemic areas.

articles.headline: False-positive malaria rapid tests common in Ugandan under-5s
articles.title_original: False-positive Malaria Rapid Diagnostic Tests are Prevalent Among Children Under 5 Years of Age in Uganda.
articles.authors: Cassidy CA, Shook-Sa BE, Boyce RM, Ciccone EJ, Gower EW, Young AM, Edwards JK
articles.details: Using the 2018-2019 Uganda Malaria Indicator Survey, investigators estimated the prevalence of false-positive mRDTs (mRDT-positive/microscopy-negative) among children under 5 with paired results, applying survey weights and lasso regression models. False-positive prevalence was 10.7% (849/6786) and correlated with region-level transmission intensity. Prevalence was higher with recent fever (PD 17.2%; 95% CI 13.7%-20.6%), recent antimalarial use (14.7%; 7.1%-22.3%), and anemia (8.1%; 5.9%-10.3%), and lower with recent antibiotic use (-17.6%; -22.5% to -12.7%). A combined model predicted false positives best (weighted AUC 0.79). The authors conclude these results inflate community prevalence estimates and may drive misdiagnosis and unnecessary antimalarial use.

articles.headline: Duffy blood group and age shape vivax malaria risk in Amazonians
articles.title_original: How Duffy Blood Group (FY) Polymorphism and Age Modulate Vivax Malaria Risk at the Community Level: A Population-based Retrospective Cohort Study in the Amazon.
articles.authors: Prete CA Jr, de Sousa TN, Naziazeno IM, Puça MCSB, Ladeia WA, Rodrigues PT, Johansen IC, Paula GA, Ferreira MU, Corder RM
articles.details: This study of a household-based random sample of 1737 Amazonians with a balanced distribution of FY alleles, who were exposed to P. vivax and contributed 7878.9 person-years over 5 years of follow-up, used a zero-inflated negative binomial model to assess infection risk. Infections were heterogeneously distributed, with 0 to 11 cases per person and an average incidence of 25.8 cases/100 person-years at risk. Fy-negativity remained a major malaria resistance trait contributing to structural zeroes, while Fya/Fyb-associated differences in risk among young participants attenuated with age, likely because more susceptible Fy(b+) individuals develop clinical immunity faster than Fy(a+) individuals. The authors conclude that FY polymorphism modulates the rate at which immunity to P. vivax develops.

articles.headline: Praziquantel shows modest antimalarial activity in asymptomatic Gabonese adults
articles.title_original: Efficacy of Praziquantel for Treatment of Plasmodium falciparum Infection in Asymptomatic Gabonese Adults: A Double-Blind Placebo-Controlled Randomized Trial.
articles.authors: Mischlinger J, Pechmann K, Werner J, Zinsou AH, Vogler M, Ndoumba WN, Okwu DG, von Wedel C, Kurth F, Kreidenweiss A, Bachmann A, Adegnika AA, Zoleko-Manego R, Kremsner PG, Sarpong F, Badjina L, Ramharter M, Mombo-Ngoma G
articles.details: The CORMA-MAL study was a double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled phase 2b trial in Lambaréné, Gabon, enrolling semi-immune adults with asymptomatic P. falciparum parasitemia, randomized 1:1 to placebo or 40 mg/kg praziquantel once daily for 3 days with 7 days follow-up. Among 44 participants, praziquantel produced a highly significant hourly reduction in parasitemia (slope β=-0.006, P<.001) versus a nonsignificant trend in placebo (β=-0.0013, P=.3), but only 9/22 (41%) in the praziquantel arm versus 6/22 (27%) in placebo achieved parasite clearance by day 7. The authors conclude praziquantel has statistically significant but moderate antimalarial activity and suggest investigating combinations with antimalarials.

articles.headline: Several P. vivax vaccine antigens show high diversity and immune selection
articles.title_original: Extreme Genetic Diversity and Signatures of Balancing Selection in Plasmodium vivax Blood-Stage Antigens.
articles.authors: Bareng APN, Naung MT, Martin E, Mehra S, Munro JE, Takala Harrison S, Barry AE
articles.details: This population-genomics study analyzed 289 of 353 publicly available Plasmodium vivax whole genome sequences from Asia, the Americas, and Oceania to assess the diversity and balancing selection of 14 P. vivax vaccine candidate antigens across both linear sequences and 3D protein structures. The blood-stage antigens AMA1, CyRPA, RBP1a, and P41 showed high genetic diversity and signatures of balancing selection that were consistent across all endemic regions, indicating immune selection, whereas CSP, TRAP, MSP4, MSP9, MSP10, GAMA, S12, ARP, S25, and S28 showed low to intermediate diversity. Sequences from the Sal-1 vaccine strain had extremely low global frequencies, suggesting that current Sal-1-based formulations may have limited efficacy.

articles.headline: Budget analysis of tafenoquine and G6PD testing for P. vivax in Brazil
articles.title_original: [Expansion of the use of technologies to reduce cases of malaria due to Plasmodium vivax in the Brazilian Amazon: a budget impact analysis].
articles.authors: Costa MGSD, Zimmermann I, Costa ACCD, Moraes AC, Pinto M
articles.details: This budget impact analysis modeled, from the perspective of Brazil's Unified National Health System (SUS) over a five-year horizon, the incremental cost of expanding tafenoquine and quantitative point-of-care G6PD deficiency testing to P. vivax patients under 16 years and by weight, plus G6PD testing for all primaquine-eligible patients, in the Brazilian Amazon. Tafenoquine and primaquine can cause hemolysis in G6PD-deficient patients, so testing is recommended before administration. The incremental budget impact was BRL 12.5 million; incorporating tafenoquine was predicted to reduce hospitalization and recurrence costs by about 9% and 11%, respectively, with test-strip cost and waste influencing results.

articles.headline: Drug-resistance markers in P. falciparum from rural Angola
articles.title_original: Molecular markers of antimalarial drug resistance in pfk13, pfmdr1, pfdhfr, and pfdhps genes of Plasmodium falciparum in a rural municipality in Cubal, Benguela Province, Angola (2022-2023).
articles.authors: Mediavilla A, García X, Martínez-Vallejo P, Molina-de la Fuente I, Febrer-Sendra B, Silgado A, Francisco K, Maturana CR, Martins JF, Nindia A, Martínez-Campreciós J, Aznar ML, Oliveira-Souto I, Molina I, Sulleiro E, Berzosa P
articles.details: This cross-sectional study sequenced pfk13, pfmdr1, pfdhfr, and pfdhps in 139 real-time PCR-confirmed P. falciparum infections from Cubal, Benguela Province, Angola (2022-2023), using dried blood spots to monitor selective pressure from artemether-lumefantrine (AL) and sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP). Among 120 pfk13 sequences, 12 (10%) carried mutations (five nonsynonymous) but none were validated or candidate artemisinin-resistance markers. In pfmdr1, Y184F was most prevalent (43%), N86Y rare (1%), D1246Y absent. The triple pfdhfr N51I/C59R/S108N mutation occurred in 77.2%, pfdhps A437G was nearly fixed (98%) and K540E 23.8%; 58.4% were partially resistant (IRNG) and 17.8% fully resistant (IRNGE). Authors note pfmdr1 haplotypes raise concerns about AL efficacy.

articles.headline: Cytoadherence phenotypes of pfhrp2-negative P. falciparum from Peruvian Amazon
articles.title_original: Phenotypic and genotypic characterization of Plasmodium falciparum field isolates lacking pfhrp2 gene.
articles.authors: Bendezu J, Larrauri-Aguilar P, Bautista R, Villasis E, Vinetz JM, Gamboa D
articles.details: This in vitro study characterized the phenotype and genotype of 5 P. falciparum field isolates from the Peruvian Amazon with differing pfhrp2 patterns (three positive, two negative by HRP2 rapid diagnostic test, confirmed by PCR), testing cytoadherence to chondroitin sulfate A (CSA) and HUVEC endothelial cells, rosetting, and pfhrp2/pfhrp3 expression. Two of three pfhrp2-positive isolates showed rosetting or CSA cytoadherence, while both pfhrp2-negative isolates adhered to HUVEC cells; pfhrp2 expression was higher in pfhrp2-positive isolates. The pfhrp2 gene and flanking regions were genomically stable over a year of culture, though one isolate showed two pfhrp3 patterns reflecting mixed genotypes. Authors describe findings as preliminary.

articles.headline: Protozoan co-infections alter cytokine profiles in French Guiana patients
articles.title_original: Unravelling complex interactions during Toxoplasma, Plasmodium, and Leishmania co-infections in French Guiana.
articles.authors: Néron K, Fesel C, Demar M, Pied S
articles.details: This seroprevalence study analyzed plasma and clinical data from 253 patients and healthy controls at Cayenne Hospital Centre (2012-2022) in French Guiana to assess multi-infection with Toxoplasma gondii, Plasmodium spp., and Leishmania spp. and its effect on host immune responses versus single infections. Seroprevalence was determined by chemiluminescence and ELISA, with cytokines/chemokines measured by multiplex assay and multivariate analysis. About 60% of patients were seropositive for at least two pathogens, and 2.4% had concomitant infections. Cytokine profiles differed significantly between single and co-infected patients, particularly IL-10, IL-17, IL-33, and IFN-α, highlighting complex co-infection dynamics relevant to public health strategies and biomarkers.

articles.headline: Climate models project shrinking habitat for invasive malaria vector in Iran
articles.title_original: Suitable ecological niches of invasive malaria vector under present and projected climatic conditions in South of Iran.
articles.authors: Bozorg-Omid F, Abbasi M, Yaghoobi-Ershadi MR, Hanafi-Bojd AA
articles.details: This study used MaxEnt ecological niche modeling to map current and projected distribution of the invasive urban malaria vector Anopheles stephensi in Hormozgan Province, Iran, using 72 quality-filtered occurrence points (from 96) and WorldClim v2.1 bioclimatic data under various Shared Socioeconomic Pathways. The model showed moderate accuracy (average AUC 0.765, TSS 0.519), with altitude the most influential predictor followed by precipitation. It predicts a significant decline in environmental suitability under future climate scenarios, especially in western, central, and coastal areas, with Bashagard County identified as a stable refuge. Authors stress adaptive vector control and continuous monitoring.

articles.headline: Narrative review of Bangladesh's malaria progress and last-mile elimination hurdles
articles.title_original: Malaria in Bangladesh, 2000-2025: Navigating the Headwinds From Control to Elimination.
articles.authors: Mamun AA, Kayenat MSA, Islam KAMI
articles.details: This narrative review synthesized PubMed/Medline literature (January 2000–June 2025) on malaria epidemiology, diagnostics, treatment, and vector biology in Bangladesh ahead of its 2030 elimination goal. It documents a >90% reduction in cases (2008–2020) driven by targeted vector control, RDTs, and ACTs, with residual burden concentrated in the Chittagong Hill Tracts and P. falciparum remaining the dominant species. Emerging challenges include non-kelch13 artemisinin partial resistance in P. falciparum, P. vivax radical cure complicated by G6PD deficiency, outdoor-biting vectors, high cross-border mobility, and a large asymptomatic reservoir. Authors conclude elimination is attainable but requires genomic surveillance and routine G6PD screening.

articles.headline: Survey of healthcare workers' beliefs and acceptance of malaria vaccine in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Healthcare workers' beliefs, readiness, and acceptance of malaria vaccine in Southwest Nigeria: addressing misconceptions and uptake barriers.
articles.authors: Esan DT, Adeleke TO, Rasaq NO, Ajisafe ZA, Ramos CG
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccine rollout would consume large share of Uganda's health budget
articles.title_original: The Cost and Budget Impact of Malaria Vaccine Introduction in Uganda.
articles.authors: Ochanda PN, Ekirapa-Kiracho E, Mayora C, Ssempala R, Nakimuli B, Namirembe B, Ssennyonjo A, Ssengooba F
articles.details: This budget impact analysis estimated the cost of introducing the RTS,S/AS01 malaria vaccine into Uganda's expanded immunization program, drawing on prospective studies and literature-based unit costs, with malaria cases averted in eligible children as the primary outcome. Treatment costs for complicated malaria in children under 5 were projected to fall from $1.1 million to $297,465. Annual rollout cost was about $29 million (24% of the immunization budget, ~3.5% of the total health budget); over five years the total budget impact reached $145.5 million (net cost $141 million), ranging from $106.3 million to $175.6 million by coverage. Authors urge prioritized funding and cost-effective targeting.

articles.headline: Modeling shows SP chemoprevention coverage drives spread of resistant dhps A581G
articles.title_original: Modeling the Impact of Malaria Chemoprevention with sulphadoxine-pyrimethamine on the spread of Plasmodium falciparum dhps A581G mutation.
articles.authors: Mziray N, Okell LC, Kiware S, Cuomo-Dannenburg G, Ainea N, Ishengoma DS, Nyerere N
articles.details: This study used a deterministic age-structured compartmental model of human and mosquito populations to assess how sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP) chemoprevention (IPTp, IPTi, SMC, MDA) influences spread of the fully SP-resistant P. falciparum dhps A581G mutation in settings where dhps K540E is near fixation. Comparing three versus five monthly cycles per season at 0–80% coverage, at 45% coverage A581G prevalence rose from 11% to 20.6% (three cycles) and 27.8% (five cycles). ANOVA with partial eta-squared showed coverage level explained 58% of resistance spread (ηp²=0.58, 95% CI 0.57–1.00) versus 9.3% for cycle number, making coverage the dominant factor. Authors highlight a trade-off between immediate gains and long-term resistance, urging molecular surveillance.

articles.headline: Low R21 malaria vaccine uptake in Sudan driven by poor awareness
articles.title_original: Suboptimal uptake of the malaria vaccine in Sudan: what's behind the blinds?
articles.authors: Malik EM, El-Hassan HM, Ali MAA, Alsoni KAA, Alnair NMA, Noureddin AA, Aladani ISA, Adam MAB, Saad FM, Elkhidir TA, Alshareif BA, Khalafalla M, Al-Haddad NFK, Mohammed NYE, Eltayeb DIH, Ahmed N
articles.details: A cross-sectional mixed-methods study in Sudan's Gedarif and Blue Nile States surveyed 416 mothers/caregivers of children under five (99.0% response rate; median age 27.0 years), with focus group discussions and key-informant interviews, to assess R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccine utilization. Of 256 eligible children, only 36.7% (95% CI 31.1-42.8%; 94 children) received the vaccine, with no difference by state or residence. Awareness was low: 57.7% knew of the vaccine, 36.7% knew the required doses, and 53.1% did not view malaria as a significant problem; stockouts and geographic access were additional barriers. Authors conclude suboptimal uptake reflected limited education rather than hesitancy.

articles.headline: Hemozoin-associated proteins disrupt brain endothelial barrier in cerebral malaria model
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum hemozoin-associated biomolecules induce brain endothelial cell barrier disruption in an in vitro model of cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Crotty KA, Clotea I, Ueberheide B, Cammer M, Sall J, Liang A, Rodriguez A
articles.details: In an in vitro model of cerebral malaria using human brain microvascular endothelial cells, the endothelial barrier-disrupting activity of Plasmodium falciparum-infected red blood cells was found entirely in the hemozoin fraction. This disruption was not caused by the hemozoin crystal itself but by the biomolecules associated with it. Treating purified hemozoin with proteases inhibited the barrier disruption, indicating an important role for proteins, whereas nuclease treatment had no effect. The authors identify hemozoin-associated proteins as a key mechanism of brain endothelial barrier breakdown and a possible therapeutic target.

articles.headline: Modelling supports flexible timing for RTS,S fourth dose in perennial settings
articles.title_original: Timing of the fourth dose of RTS,S/AS01 malaria vaccine in perennial settings: a modelling study.
articles.authors: Figueroa-Downing D, Kelly SL, Cavelan A, Penny MA, Malinga J
articles.details: This modelling study used OpenMalaria, an individual-based stochastic transmission model, to evaluate timing (6-, 9-, 12-, 15-, 18-month intervals after the primary series) and coverage of the RTS,S/AS01 fourth dose on uncomplicated malaria, severe malaria, and deaths across transmission archetypes. At a baseline PfPR2-10 of 20%, the primary series accounted for roughly 73-93% of uncomplicated and 74-92% of severe cases averted regardless of fourth-dose timing in perennial settings. A fourth dose averted an additional 8-38% of uncomplicated and 9-36% of severe cases. Authors conclude a fourth dose at 15-21 months of age (6-12 months after dose three) with high coverage maximizes impact and can be tailored to country health touchpoints.

articles.headline: Extreme weather lowers malaria preventive treatment in pregnancy via disrupted antenatal care
articles.title_original: Extreme weather events and malaria prevention in pregnancy: a mediation analysis of antenatal care and sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine use in two malaria-endemic counties of Kenya.
articles.authors: Matanda DJ, Mchenga M, Amadi JA, Nakuya Z, Okoth O, Otieno B, Obare F
articles.details: A cross-sectional household survey in Kenya's Kisumu and Migori counties studied women who had given birth in the prior 24 months, using counterfactual mediation analysis to assess how self-reported extreme weather events affect completion of three or more IPTp-SP doses (IPTp3+) through attendance of four or more antenatal visits (ANC4+). IPTp3+ coverage was 49.5% among shock-exposed versus 58.8% unexposed. Extreme weather reduced ANC4+ (-0.23, 95% CI -0.41 to -0.05), and ANC4+ strongly predicted IPTp3+ (1.71, 95% CI 1.42-2.00). The total effect on IPTp3+ was -0.45 (76.6% mediated via ANC) but attenuated to -0.27 (95% CI -0.56 to 0.02) and became non-significant after adjustment, with stronger effects in Kisumu.

articles.headline: Ischemic stroke in young woman with P. vivax-dengue coinfection
articles.title_original: Ischemic stroke during dengue-Plasmodium vivax coinfection in a young woman: a case report.
articles.authors: Nati-Castillo HA, Saavedra-Torres JS, Cardenas JM, Gaibor-Pazmiño A, Ortiz-Prado E, Izquierdo-Condoy JS
articles.details: This case report describes a 23-year-old Colombian woman who developed acute right-leg monoparesis after 7 days of fever, presenting with hypotension and jaundice. Laboratory testing confirmed Plasmodium vivax malaria (8200 parasites/μL) and acute dengue (NS1 antigen positive; IgM-positive/IgG-negative), and MRI revealed an acute left anterior cerebral artery infarct with no traditional stroke risk factors. She was treated with artesunate-primaquine, supportive dengue care, and aspirin after thrombocytopenia resolved, improving with rehabilitation. The authors propose P. vivax-dengue coinfection can cause ischemic stroke through synergistic endothelial injury, underscoring early recognition and coinfection screening.

articles.headline: Unique Plasmodium CoA enzyme complex is a drug target
articles.title_original: The Plasmodium falciparum PPCS is a unique heteromeric complex with prokaryote-like activity and is a target of pantothenate analogs.
articles.authors: Liu X, Jin R, Domingo R, Kotzé TJ, Guan J, Hamann AR, Chu A, Hart-Smith G, Spry C, Sexton AE, Crooke Z, Venkadeswaran A, Yuan X, Creek DJ, Auclair K, Corry B, Strauss E, Saliba KJ
articles.details: This laboratory study characterized phosphopantothenoylcysteine synthetase (PPCS), which catalyzes the second of five steps in coenzyme A biosynthesis, essential for the intraerythrocytic stage of Plasmodium falciparum. Unlike most apicomplexans with one PPCS, P. falciparum expresses two that associate into a single functional heteromeric complex; unlike any other reported eukaryotic PPCS, it cannot use ATP and instead shows specificity for CTP, conferred by a prokaryote-like helical component. The complex is the target of multiple antiplasmodial pantothenate analogs, supporting design of inhibitors that exploit its unique features while sparing the human enzyme.

articles.headline: Stakeholders favor safer, food-flexible, long-acting malaria prevention in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Improving malaria chemoprevention coverage in pregnancy: Surveying stakeholder preferences for new product profiles and community-delivery approaches across five African countries.
articles.authors: Audibert C, Borghini-Fuhrer I, El Gaaloul M, Ramachandruni H, Tchouatieu AM, Majeres Lugand M
articles.details: Surveys in five malaria-endemic countries assessed stakeholder preferences for next-generation intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp), given limitations of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP), the only WHO-recommended option. Respondents included pregnant women (n=75), nurses/community health workers (n=53), clinicians (n=75), and policymakers (n=51), plus a discrete choice experiment among clinicians and policymakers. The most important attribute was safety in all trimesters, followed by short therapy duration, fewer tablets, and food requirements; birthweight impact was least influential. All groups felt a long-acting injectable with at least three months' coverage would improve adherence, and the DCE most strongly favored products without food restrictions, though three-quarters of pregnant women preferred antenatal clinic delivery.

articles.headline: Protocol for systematic review of pediatric malaria in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Burden, risk factors, and clinical outcomes of pediatric malaria in Nigeria: A systematic review and meta-analysis protocol.
articles.authors: Ibrahim OR, Abdulkareem J, Issa A, Alao MA
articles.details: This is a protocol for a systematic review and meta-analysis to pool laboratory-confirmed pediatric malaria data across Nigeria using WHO case definitions and random-effects modeling. Searches will cover six databases (PubMed, Embase, EBSCOhost, Google Scholar, Web of Science, Scopus) for observational and interventional studies from inception through June 30, 2025, with no language restriction. The primary outcome is pooled prevalence of pediatric malaria (overall, and severe vs. uncomplicated); secondary outcomes include factors affecting presentation and outcomes, with moderators such as age, sex, socioeconomic status, and geography. Risk of bias will use the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale and RoB 2.0. No results are reported. PROSPERO: CRD420251163322.

articles.headline: Mitochondrial gene expression differs in severe falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Differential expression of mitochondria-associated genes in clinical samples of Plasmodium falciparum showing severe manifestations.
articles.authors: Gujarati S, Singal BR, Gupta D, Kochar SK, Kochar DK, Das A
articles.details: Researchers profiled mitochondrial sense transcripts and natural antisense transcripts (NATs) in 22 Plasmodium falciparum clinical isolates grouped as uncomplicated malaria (UNC, n=6), cerebral malaria (CM, n=4), and hepatic dysfunction (HD, n=12), using a custom strand-aware 60K microarray validated by strand-specific RNA-seq. Compared with uncomplicated controls, the CM and HD cohorts showed distinct disease-specific sense and antisense transcripts from the mitochondrial and nuclear genomes; the CM cluster showed upregulation of tricarboxylic-acid-cycle and electron-transport genes, indicating disrupted mitochondrial bioenergetics in severe disease. Correlated sense-antisense pairs suggest a regulatory role for NATs, nominating them as non-conventional antimalarial targets.

articles.headline: In silico HLA coverage of falciparum epitopes for multi-epitope vaccine
articles.title_original: Population HLA coverage of experimentally characterized P. falciparum CD8 (+) T cell epitopes as potential components of a multi-epitope malaria vaccine construct: an in silico insight.
articles.authors: Siame C, Abdul-Mumin R, Agyekum G, Akuffo L, Afari E, Yankey CAB, Kusi KA
articles.details: This in silico study assessed global HLA population coverage of 16 experimentally validated P. falciparum CD8+ T cell epitopes from three antigens (CSP, AMA1, TRAP) for a multi-epitope malaria vaccine, using the Immune Epitope Database tools across 14 subregions and 78 countries. Eight epitopes (including CSPep1, CSPep2, TRAPep1) were 100% conserved across 16 strains, others ≥55%. The CSP epitope IQNSLSTEW (CSPep5) showed the highest global coverage (>85%) and 93.35% in West Africa, while ILSVSSFLF (CSPep4) showed minimal coverage (<5% in Africa, <2% elsewhere); LLACAGLAYK (TRAPep1) and STEWSPCSV (CSPep10) exceeded 50% coverage. Authors conclude combining conserved epitopes with broad HLA coverage promotes wide immune recognition.

articles.headline: First confirmed anti-NMDAR encephalitis case after falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Anti-NMDAR encephalitis following malaria: expanding the spectrum of post-malarial neurological syndromes.
articles.authors: Deng S, Wang W, Lu W, Wang S
articles.details: This case report describes a 40-year-old man who developed anti-NMDAR encephalitis after recovery from Plasmodium falciparum malaria. Despite confirmed parasite clearance, he presented eleven days later with seizures, altered consciousness, and acute psychosis. Anti-NMDAR antibodies were detected in cerebrospinal fluid by both immunofluorescence and cell-based assay, with no concurrent infection or malignancy. The authors report this as the first antibody-confirmed case following P. falciparum infection and note the patient improved rapidly after first-line immunotherapy, expanding the spectrum of post-malarial neurological syndromes.

articles.headline: Cytokine profiles linked to malaria infection in first-time mothers
articles.title_original: Cytokine-Driven Immune Phenotypes at Delivery as Indicators of Malaria Infection Among Primigravidae in Burkina Faso: An Exploratory Analysis.
articles.authors: Traore O, Rouamba T, Zango SH, Sorgho H, Valea I, Traore-Coulibaly M, Schallig HDFH, Tinto H
articles.details: This exploratory study assessed 33 primigravid women from Nanoro, Burkina Faso (mean age 19 years; range 18-20.5) at delivery, measuring antibody responses to P. falciparum antigens (PfCSP, PfAMA-1, EBA-175) and plasma cytokines (IL-4, IL-10, IL-6, TNF-α, IFN-γ). Principal component analysis and hierarchical clustering identified three immune profiles: a low-inflammatory cluster, a TNF-α-dominant cluster, and a highly pro-inflammatory cluster with elevated IL-6 and TNF-α (cluster stability AU ≥ 92%). All women in the most inflammatory cluster were P. falciparum-positive at delivery (Fisher's exact test, p = 0.04). The authors conclude cytokine profiling reflects concurrent infection and is a hypothesis-generating tool.

articles.headline: Neural network classifies malaria ring forms with 97% accuracy
articles.title_original: Automated Malaria Ring Form Classification in Blood Smear Images Using Ensemble Parallel Neural Networks.
articles.authors: Pongpanitanont P, Suttidate N, Nuinoon M, Khampeeramao N, Laymanivong S, Janwan P
articles.details: This study developed an automated binary classifier to detect malaria ring-form infections in thin blood smear single-cell images using a parallel neural network with a dual-branch fusion of a convolutional network and a multi-head self-attention branch. It used a balanced Kaggle dataset of 27,558 erythrocyte crops standardized to 128×128 pixels, evaluated by 10-fold stratified cross-validation and a 10% hold-out test set. All models achieved ROC-AUC of approximately 0.99; the primary model reached 0.9567 mean cross-validation accuracy and 0.97 accuracy (macro F1 0.97) on the test set, while a more complex model declined to 0.95 from higher false positives. The authors note clinical use is limited pending external validation.

articles.headline: Intravenous drug abuse raises falciparum malaria and HIV concern
articles.title_original: Intravenous drug abuse - a concern for falciparum malaria along with HIV.
articles.authors: Makkar A, Panda PK
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Co-creating community malaria care innovations in conflict-hit Cameroon
articles.title_original: Co-creation of community-based innovations to improve access to malaria treatment in conflict-affected regions of Cameroon.
articles.authors: Omam LA, Tanue EA, Omam EN, Metuge A, Nganmou I, Epie C, Kebam G, Ako S, Bessem ME, Hawkings H, Maxwell K, Zoungrana Y, Berryman E, Jarman E, Noukeme BM
articles.details: This operational research describes a participatory co-creation workshop held in 2021 bringing together government health services, community leaders, internally displaced persons, and community health workers to design interventions improving malaria treatment access in conflict-affected communities of Cameroon, building on prior formative research across 80 communities in the South West and Littoral regions. Three community-based innovations were co-created: the Community Health Participatory Approach replacing top-down dialogue with an inclusive model; a Health Voucher System covering malaria testing, treatment, and transport; and a Supportive Supervision Model to strengthen community health worker capacity. The authors conclude these warrant pilot implementation, while noting concerns about scalability and sustainability.

articles.headline: Ethiopia malaria resurgence: national review of system and operational gaps
articles.title_original: Resurgence of malaria in Ethiopia: a national landscape analysis of systemic and operational gaps.
articles.authors: Sasie SD, Aragaw FM, Assele DD, Argaw ZM, Afele MA
articles.details: A national landscape analysis using a convergent mixed-methods approach reviewed peer-reviewed literature and policy documents (published 2016–2025) plus structured consultations with malaria programme professionals, assessing six operational domains in Ethiopia. From 246 records, 24 malaria-specific primary studies met inclusion criteria. National insecticide-treated net ownership averaged about 64% (lower in high-burden and urban-adjacent areas), indoor residual spraying covered roughly half of targeted high-risk zones, and digital surveillance ran in about 80% of facilities but with delayed/incomplete reporting. Diagnostics were undermined by stock-outs, expired RDTs, and widespread pfhrp2/3 deletions; only about half of facilities reported timely commodity replenishment within three months. Authors conclude the programme has foundational capacity but needs stronger surveillance, resistance-adapted vector control, recovery mechanisms, and community-centred prevention.

articles.headline: Malaria and helminth coinfection rare in northern Uganda schoolchildren
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Coinfection with Malaria and Intestinal Helminths among School-Aged Children in Nwoya District, Northern Uganda: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: McGowan K, Bongomin F, Otika D, O'Connell C, Matejak N, Hamer DH
articles.details: A school-based cross-sectional study at four randomly selected schools in Nwoya District, northern Uganda (June 2–10, 2025) assessed coinfection with malaria and intestinal helminths among 190 school-aged children (median age 13 years, aged 5–18). Helminths were diagnosed by modified Kato-Katz and malaria by P. falciparum HRP2 RDTs and blood smears. While 75.3% (143) were RDT-positive for P. falciparum, only 13.2% (25) had microscopic parasitemia. Intestinal helminths were uncommon (3.2%, n=6: Ascaris lumbricoides 0.5%, Hymenolepis nana 1.6%, Schistosoma mansoni 1.1%), and all six helminth-infected children were malaria RDT-positive. Authors attribute the RDT–smear discrepancy to persistent antigenemia or low-grade parasitemia, and the low coinfection rate to effective mass drug administration for helminths.

articles.headline: High malaria parasitemia tied to blood and liver changes in Ghana
articles.title_original: Plasmodium Parasitemia and Its Relationship With Hematological, Liver, and Kidney Function of Patients With Falciparum Malaria in the Eastern Region, Ghana.
articles.authors: Badu A, Darban I, Mensah K, Antwi MH, Appeaning M, Kwakye AS, Buckman TA, Owusu VK, Mintah DT, Agyei RA, Yeboah C, Acorlor D
articles.details: A cross-sectional study at a public and a private facility in Ghana's Eastern Region (August–September 2023) examined relationships between Plasmodium parasitemia and hematological, liver, and kidney parameters in children under 5 and adults 18+. Among 362 participants (71.8% adults, 69.3% female), parasites were detected by microscopy and parameters measured on automated analyzers. No significant differences in parasitemia by gender or age category were found, though 51.9% (53/102) of children under 5 had high parasitemia. Adults with high parasitemia had significantly lower Hb, RBC, MCV, MCH, and platelets but higher ALT and AST than those with lower/moderate parasitemia; urea and creatinine did not differ significantly. Authors conclude high parasitemia is linked to hematological and hepatic alterations, with adult–child differences, supporting routine monitoring.

articles.headline: Data-driven model of imported malaria and migration in Morocco
articles.title_original: Data-driven modeling of imported malaria in Morocco and the impact of population migration.
articles.authors: Toufga H, Benrhmach G, Lhous M, Tridane A
articles.details: This modeling study proposes a deterministic compartmental model of human-to-mosquito malaria transmission that explicitly incorporates human immigration, with humans classed as susceptible, asymptomatic, infected, and recovered and vectors as susceptible or infected. Mathematical analysis proves positivity and uniqueness of solutions and shows the model admits a globally stable endemic equilibrium. Using Morocco as a case study, parameters were estimated from imported malaria data spanning 1990–2023 via three methods: least-squares fit, Extended Kalman Filter, and Long Short-Term Memory neural networks. An optimal control problem evaluated awareness, prophylactic treatment, treatment of infected individuals, and insecticide-based vector control. Numerical results show combined control strategies substantially reduce human and vector infections, underscoring surveillance and migration-aware control policies to prevent re-establishment.

articles.headline: Low uptake of malaria prevention among pregnant women in Ile-Ife, Nigeria
articles.title_original: Factors influencing the utilisation of malaria preventive services during pregnancy in Ile Ife, Nigeria: a mixed method approach.
articles.authors: Inyangudo GN, Ojo TO, Oluwabunmi F, Stanley E, Olaniyi B
articles.details: A mixed-method cross-sectional study among 200 pregnant women across ten healthcare facilities in two LGAs of Ile-Ife, Nigeria assessed factors influencing use of malaria preventive services, with quantitative questionnaires and qualitative key informant interviews and focus group discussions analyzed by logistic regression and thematic analysis. Only 34% had good knowledge of malaria preventive services; 62.5% owned long-lasting insecticide nets but only 47.5% currently used them. IPTp-SP uptake was 50.8% for at least one dose and 7% for three or more doses. IPTp-SP uptake was significantly associated with occupation, good knowledge, and gravidity. Barriers included commodity stock-outs, lack of money, transport costs, distance, late/irregular antenatal attendance, and preference for faith-based maternity homes. Authors recommend stable commodity supply, integrating unconventional providers, education, and provider training.

articles.headline: Ghana data show seasonal malaria chemoprevention with SPAQ generally safe in children
articles.title_original: Incidence and trends of adverse drug reactions during seasonal malaria chemoprevention among children: evidence from Ghana's pharmacovigilance database, 2015-2023.
articles.authors: Otoo BAF, Opoku-Mireku M, Seaneke SK, Darko DM, Sabblah GT, Bonful HA
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of sulphadoxine-pyrimethamine plus amodiaquine (SPAQ) adverse drug reactions reported to Ghana's Food and Drugs Authority (FDA) database between 2015 and 2023 during seasonal malaria chemoprevention in children aged 3-59 months. Among 2,097 ADRs analyzed, 51.2% were in male children and 60.3% occurred in those aged 13-59 months; the most common were fever (22.7%), vomiting (21.5%), and diarrhoea (16.9%). Serious reactions made up 8.6% of cases, most often involving prolonged hospitalization. ADR incidence fell from 220.63 per 100,000 doses in 2015 to 0.34 in 2023, and caregivers/non-healthcare professionals filed two-thirds of reports; the authors conclude SMC with SPAQ is generally safe but that professional reporting and active surveillance need strengthening.

articles.headline: COX1 barcoding reveals cryptic Anopheles diversity in Indonesian malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Malaria Vector Surveillance in Indonesia: COX1 Phylogenetic Reveals Monophyletic Clades and Cryptic Diversity in Anopheles Mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Senjarini K, Hasanah LNU, Wathon S, Oktarianti R, Labes A
articles.details: COX1 DNA barcoding of Anopheles mosquitoes collected by human landing catches at two Indonesian sites (Bangsring, Banyuwangi and Hargowilis, Kulonprogo) mostly agreed with morphological identification, but specimens identified as An. aconitus and An. minimus from Hargowilis were molecularly confirmed as An. flavirostris. Phylogenetic analysis (Neighbor-Joining, Kimura-2-Parameter, MEGA11) revealed eight monophyletic clades with strong bootstrap support (≥99% for six), and An. minimus from Hargowilis clustered more closely with An. flavirostris than with An. minimus from other Asian regions. An. sundaicus dominated in Bangsring (68%) and An. flavirostris in Hargowilis (13%). The authors conclude that integrating molecular tools into surveillance supports more accurate species identification and vector control.

articles.headline: Pfgch1 copy number rises with antifolate resistance mutations in Northeast India
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum GTP-cyclohydrolase 1 (Pfgch1) Gene Copy Number Variation and Its Implications in antimalarial resistance from Northeast India.
articles.authors: Sen A, Sharma P, Sharma S, Srivastava B, Singh K, Anvikar A, Bharti PK
articles.details: Quantitative PCR study of GTP-cyclohydrolase 1 (Pfgch1) gene copy number variation in 101 Plasmodium falciparum isolates from Northeast India, examining its association with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance markers in the Pfdhfr and Pfdhps genes. Most isolates carried a single Pfgch1 copy, but higher copy numbers were significantly associated with Pfdhfr and Pfdhps mutations, showing a statistically significant positive correlation. Authors conclude Pfgch1 amplification may compensate for antifolate-resistance mutations and that further work is needed; the finding could aid detection and control of antifolate-resistant P. falciparum.

articles.headline: Plasmodium protease PbSERA3 is essential for liver-stage parasite egress
articles.title_original: Plasmodium berghei serine repeat antigen 3 (PbSERA3) is required for hepatic merozoite egress.
articles.authors: Singh D, Patri S, Veeda N, Verma CK, Kavati A, Segireddy RR, Kolli SK, Arun Kumar K
articles.details: Using a yeast-based Flp/FRT conditional mutagenesis system in the rodent malaria parasite Plasmodium berghei, researchers obtained PbSERA3-edited sporozoites that invaded hepatocytes and developed normally in late liver stages but failed to initiate a blood-stage infection, pointing to a role in hepatic egress. PbSERA3, a serine repeat antigen and ortholog of P. falciparum PfSERA6, localized to the surface of non-permeabilized ookinetes and sporozoites, likely indicating an extracellular nature. It was proteolytically processed into two products in these stages, as reported in blood stages, consistent with being a SUB1 substrate. The authors propose that inhibiting the maturation of PbSERA3/PfSERA6 may have therapeutic implications for preventing clinical malaria.

articles.headline: Commentary argues malaria vector control faces structural, not just financial, crisis
articles.title_original: Musings from a Vectosaur: Malaria in 2026.
articles.authors: Lluberas MF
articles.details: An opinion commentary contends that modern malaria programmes depend too heavily on two indoor tools—long-lasting insecticidal nets and indoor residual spraying—neither designed to carry vector control indefinitely. It argues mosquito populations are responding as evolutionary biology predicts, through rising insecticide resistance and behavioural shifts in feeding and resting that bypass indoor interventions, while climate change and the spread of Anopheles stephensi into African urban settings add complexity. Citing the 2025 Africa Malaria Progress Report's warning that efforts remain off-course, the author calls for re-established integrated vector management combining surveillance, larval source management, environmental control, and targeted adult mosquito control under coordinated national programmes.

articles.headline: Malaria and G6PD deficiency worsen anaemia in young Nigerian children
articles.title_original: Evaluation of the impact of malaria and erythrocyte G6PD deficiency on anaemia outcomes among children in Jos Northcentral Nigeria.
articles.authors: Okoli CA, Wamu DI, Deffi F, Leshak PD
articles.details: This cross-sectional study in 2023 enrolled 100 children aged 1–5 years in Jos, Northcentral Nigeria, using Giemsa-stained thick blood films for malaria and colorimetric assay for erythrocyte G6PD activity. Of the 100 children, 55% had malaria parasitaemia, 49% were G6PD deficient, and 28% were anaemic; 24/55 (43.6%) of malaria-positive children were G6PD deficient, and 9 (37.5%) of G6PD-deficient malaria-positive children were anaemic. Malaria and G6PD deficiency had a significant combined impact on anaemia outcomes (F=7.327; p=0.002). The authors recommend mandatory G6PD screening before antimalarial use in endemic areas.

articles.headline: Severe malaria killed 9% of admitted children in Ethiopian study
articles.title_original: Treatment outcome and associated factors of severe malaria among admitted children in the Gambella Region, Southwest Ethiopia: a retrospective study design.
articles.authors: Animut A, Bekana T, Siraj SZ, Ponpetch K, Tasew G, Kinfe E, Mekonnen Z
articles.details: In a retrospective study of 417 children aged one month to fourteen years admitted with severe malaria at Gambella General Hospital in the Gambella region of Ethiopia between May 2022 and May 2024, the overall case fatality rate was 9.11%. Higher case fatality rates were observed among children with severe anemia (31.8%), convulsions (27%), pulmonary edema (19.7%), coma (18.5%), acute kidney injury (18.4%), and comorbid illnesses (16.9%). Plasmodium falciparum accounted for 91.7% of admissions and 34 (89.5%) of the deaths. Convulsion, severe anemia, and comorbid illnesses were significantly associated with the outcome, underscoring the need for prevention and early detection.

articles.headline: Drug pressure drives antifolate resistance in human but not zoonotic malaria
articles.title_original: Contrasting evolutionary trajectories of pyrimethamine resistance in human and zoonotic malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Putaporntip C, Somkuna J, Rojrung R, Tia T, Kuamsab N, Jongwutiwes S
articles.details: Researchers compared complete dhfr-ts gene sequences governing pyrimethamine resistance (DHFR residues N51I, C59R, S108N/T, I164L) across human and zoonotic malaria parasites from symptomatic patients in Thailand (P. falciparum n=241; P. vivax n=115) and sympatric zoonotic infections, mainly in macaques (P. knowlesi n=18; P. inui n=64). Among 356 human and 145 zoonotic sequences, zoonotic parasites showed markedly higher mutation counts, haplotype diversity, and nucleotide diversity. P. falciparum showed strong directional selection via the high-prevalence quadruple IRNL mutant, P. vivax showed incomplete fixation, and zoonotic parasites evolved under purifying selection and genetic drift. The authors conclude antifolate pressure has driven human dhfr-ts toward fixation while zoonotic orthologues remain under a mutation-selection-drift balance.

articles.headline: Review of unconventional iron metabolism in blood-stage malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Fifty shades of iron: Unorthodox mechanisms of iron acquisition and utilization in blood-stage Plasmodium parasites.
articles.authors: Loveridge KM, Sigala PA
articles.details: This review synthesizes current knowledge of how blood-stage Plasmodium falciparum acquires, distributes, and regulates iron during infection of human red blood cells. The authors note the parasite genome lacks many canonical iron transporters, storage proteins, reductases, and regulatory circuits essential in model organisms, and that emerging evidence points to unconventional strategies for maintaining iron homeostasis. They highlight key proteins and pathways shaping parasite iron metabolism and identify major unanswered questions to guide future efforts to therapeutically target this essential aspect of Plasmodium biology.

articles.headline: Antibody Fc functions linked to protection from P. vivax malaria in children
articles.title_original: A longitudinal study of children identifies antibody Fc-mediated functions and antigen targets of immunity to Plasmodium vivax malaria.
articles.authors: Opi DH, Longley RJ, Takashima E, Spelman T, Tayipto Y, Schoffer K, Brewster J, Reiling L, Wines BD, Kiniboro B, Siba P, Harbers M, Hogarth M, Tsuboi T, Robinson LJ, Mueller I, Beeson JG
articles.details: This longitudinal cohort study of children at risk of Plasmodium vivax malaria in Papua New Guinea used a new platform to quantify antibody functions against multiple P. vivax antigens. The authors identified antigens targeted by multiple functional antibodies, including Fcγ receptor interactions mediating cellular effector functions and complement fixation, and pinpointed specific antigens whose antibodies were associated with protection from P. vivax malaria. By evaluating thousands of antigen combinations, they identified subsets in the most protective combinations, providing leads for multi-antigen P. vivax vaccines that elicit multi-functional antibody responses.

articles.headline: Synthetic benzopyranone attractants selectively trap human-biting malaria mosquitoes in Madagascar
articles.title_original: Field evaluation of efficacy and selectivity of select benzopyranone attractants for anthropophilic malaria vectors in Madagascar.
articles.authors: Ralimanana SV, Ramarosandratana NH, Andrianjafy MT, Rambala Rakotomena NAH, Duclos MC, Ravaomanarivo LH, Mavingui P, Ramiharimanana FD, Mbaye A, Métay E, Vestalys Ramanandraibe V, Lemaire M
articles.details: Field study in the highlands of Madagascar tested synthetic attractants 4-hydroxycoumarin and 7-decyloxychromone, alone and combined with CO2, using CDC light traps to selectively capture anthropophilic Anopheles. 4-hydroxycoumarin attracted anthropophilic Anopheles efficiently and selectively (KI=80%; SI=5.1), while 7-decyloxychromone had similar attraction but lower selectivity (KI=69%; SI=1.6). Product mixtures showed no synergistic effect on selectivity. Anthropophilic Anopheles numbers decreased or were eliminated over consecutive trapping nights, suggesting selective mass trapping could complement vector control.

articles.headline: Cameroon survey: high malaria awareness but widespread net misuse persist
articles.title_original: Awareness, attitudes, and control measures regarding malaria and mosquitoes in the North Region of Cameroon.
articles.authors: Danga SPY, Ngadvou D, Yonki B, Ezeike AK, Djonga A, Nukenine EN, Esimone CO
articles.details: A community-based cross-sectional survey in the North Region of Cameroon used structured questionnaires with 500 households across 50 villages. Malaria awareness was universal (100%) and 99.6% identified mosquito bites as the main transmission route, though 42.4% wrongly attributed transmission to cold weather. Net ownership was nearly universal (99.6%; 96.2% used one the previous night), yet 90.4% reported misusing nets (tying animals 35.8%; fishing 7.4%). Self-medication was the most common first response (43.2%), and only 19.0% sought treatment within 24 hours. Authors urge targeted health education.

articles.headline: HRP2 RDTs remain effective for asymptomatic malaria despite pfhrp2/3 deletions in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Diagnostic challenges of histidine-rich protein 2-based rapid diagnostic tests due to pfhrp2 and pfhrp3 gene deletions in asymptomatic malaria in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Mazigo E, Jun H, Lee WJ, Louis JM, Fitriana F, Syahada JH, Muh F, Chun W, Park WS, Lee SJ, Na S, Han ET, Lu F, Kidima W, Han JH
articles.details: A cross-sectional community survey in twelve Tanzanian villages (8 high- and 4 low-transmission) evaluated HRP2-based RDTs and light microscopy against qPCR for asymptomatic P. falciparum, and assessed pfhrp2/pfhrp3 gene deletions. Among 3489 participants, RDT detected 710 (77.6%) of 915 qPCR-positive cases versus 492 (53.8%) by microscopy; overall accuracy was 90.0% for RDT and 86.2% for LM, with substantial RDT agreement (κ=0.736). Deletions occurred in 93 (11.8%) of 787 samples, including 19 (2.4%) dual deletions (all RDT-negative), more frequent in high-transmission villages. Authors support continued RDT use plus ongoing molecular surveillance.

articles.headline: Multi-centre testing sets broflanilide discriminating concentration for Anopheles gambiae
articles.title_original: Determination of the preliminary discriminating concentration of broflanilide against malaria vector mosquito Anopheles gambiae by multi-centre susceptibility testing.
articles.authors: Ando Y, Mori K, Bradley J, Snetselaar J, Small G
articles.details: To enable susceptibility monitoring for the new IRS product VECTRON T500 (containing broflanilide), WHO bottle bioassays were conducted at nine research facilities to collect dose-response data against susceptible Anopheles gambiae s.s. Kisumu strain females. After statistical analysis and validation per WHO guidelines, the preliminary discriminating concentration of broflanilide was determined to be 18 μg/bottle. This benchmark will support susceptibility monitoring of wild Anopheles populations in sub-Saharan Africa where the product is being introduced.

articles.headline: Uganda women: high net use but low IRS uptake for malaria
articles.title_original: Utilization of malaria control interventions and associated factors among women of reproductive age in Lira City, Northern Uganda.
articles.authors: Apio E, Angwech H, Opio B, Okwir G, Opoke R, Malinga GM, Opiro R
articles.details: A cross-sectional study with 629 randomly selected women of reproductive age in Lira City, Uganda, assessed knowledge, attitudes, perceptions, and use of malaria control interventions, with focus group discussions. ITN utilization was high (84.1%), IPT uptake moderate (68%), and IRS coverage low (32.8%); 96.8% were knowledgeable about ITNs and attitudes were positive (mean 4.34, SD=0.67). Although 91.9% perceived IRS as effective, concerns included bad odor/discomfort (51.7%), safety doubts (21.9%), and effectiveness (17.3%). Age, house type, challenges with nets, marital status, and exposure to health education were significant predictors of utilization.

articles.headline: Temperature and rainfall lags shape malaria risk across Colombia
articles.title_original: Lagged effect of temperature and rainfall on malaria incidence in Colombia (2013-2023): An approach with Bayesian spatiotemporal adjustment.
articles.authors: Gutiérrez JD
articles.details: This ecological spatiotemporal study analyzed weekly malaria case data from 970 municipalities in Colombia (2013-2023) combined with satellite climate data, using distributed lag non-linear models within a Bayesian hierarchical framework (INLA) and adjusting for forest cover, poverty, altitude, population, and prior cases. Malaria risk rose non-linearly with temperature, peaking around 28°C (minimum-risk exposure at 16.43°C), with significant effects at lags of 0-6 weeks. Lower weekly rainfall was associated with higher risk (minimum-risk exposure at 0.85 mm). The authors conclude these climate thresholds can support early-warning systems and targeted elimination efforts.

articles.headline: Odor-based attractant blends lure Anopheles stephensi in lab tests
articles.title_original: Comparative evaluation of synthetic attractants against an important malaria vector, Anopheles stephensi (Diptera: Culicidae) mosquitoes in laboratory conditions.
articles.authors: Hosseini SZ, Basseri HR, Zaim M, Azam K, Khalaj MR, Salari E
articles.details: This laboratory olfactometer study tested attraction of non-blood-fed female Anopheles stephensi mosquitoes to four chemical components of vertebrate host odors (at 5 dilutions) and their blends. When combined with CO2, three of four compounds showed significant attractancy (P<0.05), with hexanoic acid most attractive, followed by 3-methyl-1-butanol and L-lactic acid. Of five blends created, two significantly attracted mosquitoes, the most promising containing 3-methyl-1-butanol plus hexanoic acid.
