The authors note potential for odor-baited traps but call for field testing.

articles.headline: Microscopy and RDTs miss many malaria infections in Senegal
articles.title_original: Malaria misdiagnosis among febrile patients in Senegal: implications for the underestimation of Plasmodium species diversity and malaria burden.
articles.authors: Sambe BS, Diagne A, Diatta AS, Diaw SOM, Sarr I, Diatta HAM, Sané R, Guigma PYB, Senghor B, Diouf B, Sembene PM, Vigan-Womas I, Niang M
articles.details: This study compared standard malaria diagnostics (RDT and microscopy) against quantitative and qualitative PCR references in 948 febrile patients across three high-incidence regions of Senegal. PCR detected malaria in 75.29% (716/948) of patients, of which 70.25% involved non-falciparum species including P. ovale, P. vivax, and P. malariae. Microscopy and RDT missed 31.5% and 18.92% of PCR-positive infections respectively, and half of non-falciparum mono-infections were misdiagnosed as P. falciparum by both methods. The authors warn that sole reliance on microscopy and RDT may leave infections untreated and sustain transmission.

articles.headline: Quinine-loaded nanoparticles boost potency against drug-resistant malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Fabrication and evaluation of quinine-loaded chitosan-polycaprolactone core-shell polymeric nanoparticles for the treatment of malaria: an in vitro and in silico study.
articles.authors: Amos Y, Montenegro ED, Rubaka C, Swai H
articles.details: This in vitro and in silico study developed chitosan-functionalized poly(ε-caprolactone) core-shell nanoparticles (QN-PCL/CS NPs) for quinine delivery, characterized as 312.9 nm diameter, +30.8 mV zeta potential, and 81.8% encapsulation efficiency. The nanoparticles raised the cytotoxicity IC50 in Vero cells from 131.93 to 345.93 µg/mL and lowered the antiplasmodial IC50 against chloroquine-resistant P. falciparum (FCR3 strain) from 130.12 to 32.56 ng/mL, yielding an roughly ten-fold higher selectivity index (1.014 to 10.658) and doubled quinine penetration into infected erythrocytes. Molecular docking predicted strong quinine binding to chitosan (-4.02 kcal/mol) and PCL (-3.99 kcal/mol). The authors conclude the formulation is a promising platform for drug-resistant malaria.

articles.headline: Conserved intracellular protein tested as P. falciparum vaccine in mice
articles.title_original: Immunological response of using emulsifying elongation factor 1 alpha of Plasmodium falciparum-based vaccines in complete Freund's adjuvant.
articles.authors: Hong H, Eom TH, Lee E, Lee E, Lee HY, Won S, Lee DS, Kim MI, Park H, Yeo SJ
articles.details: This study evaluated the conserved intracellular antigen elongation factor-1 alpha of P. falciparum (PfEF-1α) emulsified in complete Freund's adjuvant as a malaria vaccine candidate in a rodent model, assessing in vivo efficacy, antibody production, and ex vivo T-cell cytokines. The rPfEF-1α-plus-CFA group showed a 50% increase in survival rate, with half of survivors never becoming patent, while all BSA-plus-CFA controls were patent. Immunization elicited a dominant CD4+IFN-γ+ response and a 16-fold higher antigen-specific IgG endpoint titer versus controls, and a validated epitope (410-FAIRDMRQTI-419) induced IFN-γ secretion. The authors suggest conserved intracellular antigens as viable malaria vaccine targets.

articles.headline: Sierra Leone operational research course supports HIV, TB, malaria response
articles.title_original: Strengthening operational research to complement Global Fund grants for HIV, TB and malaria in Sierra Leone.
articles.authors: Kenneh S, Kamau EM, Thorson A, Halleux C, Ameh G
articles.details: This introduces the first in a Public Health Action series of articles providing evidence-based answers to questions raised by health workers on HIV, TB and malaria on the frontlines in Sierra Leone, conducted as part of a SORT IT course led by TDR with various partners. The Sierra Leone course brought together 19 institutions to foster global engagement, partnerships and communities of practice. No quantitative study results are reported; SORT IT aims to make countries 'data rich, information rich and action rich' to improve health care delivery and outcomes.

articles.headline: Transcriptomics reveals adhesion genes linked to P. vivax rosetting
articles.title_original: Transcriptomics of Plasmodium vivax rosetting.
articles.authors: Bourgard C, Ferraboli JW, Lopes SCP, Lacerda MVG, Sunnerhagen P, Albrecht L, Costa FTM
articles.details: Using RNA-seq, researchers compared transcriptomes of 10 field Plasmodium vivax isolates from the Brazilian Amazon with high versus low rosetting rates. Of 492 differentially expressed genes, 172 (34.96%) were conserved genes of unknown function, while 320 (65.04%) highlighted integral and membrane-associated adhesive proteins, including PHIST proteins (53 genes, 10%); about 4% (19 genes) were kinases and 50% (248 genes) other proteins. High-rosetting isolates showed upregulation of the 6-cysteine gene family, including TRAG16, the 41-3 protein, and MSP7-like. The authors conclude these findings advance understanding of the molecular basis of P. vivax rosetting.

articles.headline: WWII soldiers faced prolonged disability from relapsing vivax malaria
articles.title_original: Post-infection symptoms in U.S. soldiers with malaria during the Second World War: major limitation to return to duty.
articles.authors: Shanks GD
articles.details: This historical analysis examines post-infection symptoms in U.S. soldiers with malaria during the Second World War, noting over 100,000 cases reported among U.S. military in the Southwest and South Pacific in 1943. Death rates were very low, but the main challenge was returning recovered soldiers to duty; relapsing Plasmodium vivax malaria was especially problematic, with many soldiers at Guadalcanal or New Guinea suffering more than 10 relapses. Around 1% of patients were repatriated for 'chronic malaria.' The authors argue future conflicts must anticipate diffuse post-infection symptoms to prevent war-fighter attrition.

articles.headline: Algae-made silver nanoparticles kill malaria parasites and curb inflammation
articles.title_original: Green synthesis of silver nanoparticles using Padina tetrastromatica against malaria and deciphering the mechanism through machine learning-driven metabolomics and network pharmacology.
articles.authors: Yaladanda N, Budige YK, Veeragoni D, Josyula JVN, Rodda R, Singh V, Sripadi P, Mutheneni SR
articles.details: Researchers green-synthesized silver nanoparticles using brown marine algae Padina tetrastromatica (Ag-PT) and evaluated them through in vitro, in vivo, metabolomics, network pharmacology, and in silico approaches. Ag-PT showed potent antiplasmodial activity with significantly lower IC50 values and superior parasite suppression versus chemically synthesized nanoparticles. Metabolomics showed Ag-PT restored disrupted fatty acid, arginine, and arachidonic acid metabolism, elevating pro-resolving mediator precursors (DHA, 14-HDHA, 18-HEPE) and replenishing l-arginine. Network pharmacology identified COX-2 (PTGS2) as a key hub gene, with the phytochemical eriodictyol binding strongly to COX-2. The authors conclude Ag-PT offers a multifaceted, dual host-and-parasite-targeting antimalarial strategy.

articles.headline: RALP1 protein essential for P. falciparum invasion of red cells
articles.title_original: RALP1 is essential for schizont maturation and erythrocyte invasion in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Wu J, Zhang Z, Pang J, Yang W, Ngim C, Li P, Ye J, Tian B, Cheng X, Wang F, Zhang Q, Shang X
articles.details: Researchers generated a 3×HA-tagged conditional knockdown line (ralp1-ha-glmS) of Plasmodium falciparum using CRISPR-Cas9 to study the rhoptry neck protein RALP1. GlcN-induced knockdown reduced RALP1 most prominently in schizonts, decreasing parasite proliferation, impairing schizont maturation, reducing merozoite numbers, and lowering erythrocyte invasion efficiency. RNA-seq showed limited effects in early rings but widespread downregulation of invasion- and host-parasite interaction-related genes in schizonts. RALP1 contains extensive intrinsically disordered regions with B- and T-cell epitope hotspots in its C-terminal RBC-binding domain (AlphaFold3 pTM=0.23). The authors conclude RALP1 is required for invasion and is a potential therapeutic or vaccine target.

articles.headline: Flooding triggers malaria outbreak in Kidal, Mali; extended SMC cut cases
articles.title_original: Extreme rainfall, flooding and malaria in the Sahara: outbreak analysis in Kidal, Mali 2024.
articles.authors: Dolo AM, Cissoko M, Teme A, Keita K, Sanogo M, Traoré CAT, Magassa M, Koné A, Roy FA, Sagara I, Gaudart J
articles.details: A historical cohort study covering the entire population of the Kidal region, northern Mali, analyzed weekly malaria cases (May 2024–February 2025) alongside TRMM rainfall data using generalized additive models on an interrupted time series. After 15 weeks of rainfall starting in week 25, the epidemic began in week 30, peaked in week 39 with 1,014 cases, lasted 28 weeks, and reached 1.688 cases per 1000 person-weeks (95% CI 1.687–1.690), with incidence highest in children under five. Extended seasonal malaria chemoprevention (to children under 15) and mobile health teams significantly reduced morbidity (SIR 0.50, 95% CI 0.33–0.75; and SIR 0.48, 95% CI 0.28–0.82).

articles.headline: Anopheles in Malawi sugar plantations prefer guava over sugarcane
articles.title_original: Exploring the importance and preference of sugar feeding behaviour of malaria vectors in sugar plantations of southern Malawi.
articles.authors: Zembere K, Coleman S, Chirombo J, Mbewe R, Tangena JA
articles.details: Researchers assessed sugar-feeding preferences of malaria vectors in the Illovo sugar plantations of Chikwawa, southern Malawi, to inform attractive targeted sugar baits (ATSBs). Using CDC light traps, Prokopack aspirators, and barrier screens they collected 187 adult anophelines and ran cold anthrone tests, plus an olfactory choice experiment with 810 insectary-reared Anopheles gambiae s.l. across six plant sugar sources. Over 40% (n=74) of collected mosquitoes—including An. gambiae s.l., An. funestus, An. coustani, and An. tenebrosus—had fed on natural sugars. Guava was twice as attractive as sugarcane (IRR 1.97, 95% CI 1.49–2.62, p<0.001), followed by banana (IRR 1.68), then mango and melon (IRR 1.49 and 1.45).

articles.headline: Antimalarial SC83288 targets PfDNMT2; PfATP6 mutations confer costly resistance
articles.title_original: Mechanisms of PfDNMT2 inhibition and PfATP6-mediated resistance to the antimalarial candidate SC83288 in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Sanchez CP, Duffey M, Celada RV, Kucharski M, Hoarau M, Saeyang T, Kamchonwongpaisan S, Sohraby F, de Buhr S, Nunes Alves A, Lucky AB, Miao J, Regnault C, Dziekan JM, Bozdech Z, Barrett MP, Lanzer M
articles.details: This laboratory study characterized the mechanism of action and resistance for SC83288, a chemically distinct antimalarial active against Plasmodium falciparum in vivo and in vitro, including artemisinin- and partner-drug-resistant strains. SC83288 disrupted blood-stage development by blocking DNA replication and arresting karyokinesis, with the DNA and tRNAAsp methyltransferase PfDNMT2 identified as a primary target, linked to impaired epigenetic regulation and altered S-adenosylmethionine fluxes. Resistance arose through mutations in the SERCA-type Ca²⁺ ATPase PfATP6 that transport the compound into the endoplasmic reticulum away from nuclear targets, but at a substantial fitness cost. The authors conclude SC83288 is a promising clinical development candidate.

articles.headline: Encapsulated sea cucumber reduces organ damage in malaria mice
articles.title_original: Hepatorenal protective role of encapsulated Holothuria atra in a mice model of Plasmodium berghei ANKA inoculation.
articles.authors: Utami PD, Setianingsih H, Sari DRT
articles.details: This experimental study tested the hepatorenal protective effects of encapsulated Holothuria atra in BALB/c mice infected with Plasmodium berghei ANKA. Forty-eight mice were randomly assigned to eight groups: an untreated negative control, a chloroquine-treated positive control, and six groups receiving chloroquine plus encapsulated H. atra with varying gum Arabic/soy protein isolate (GA/SPI) ratios and dosages, assessed by renal and hepatic histopathology. The chloroquine-only group showed the highest renal damage scores, while combination therapy—particularly the 4:1 GA/SPI ratio at 100 mg/kg—significantly reduced renal and hepatic damage, approaching negative-control levels for several parameters. The authors conclude encapsulated H. atra is a promising adjunct to antimalarial therapy.

articles.headline: Unified culture medium enables cross-species Plasmodium and Babesia drug screening
articles.title_original: A Standardized Culture Medium for Comparative Drug Efficacy Evaluation Across Plasmodium and Babesia Species.
articles.authors: Vydyam P, Ben Mamoun C
articles.details: This methods study describes DFS20, a standardized erythrocytic culture medium (DMEM/F12 with 20% fetal bovine serum) for harmonized in vitro drug efficacy testing across intraerythrocytic parasites. DFS20 supported robust asexual growth of multiple Plasmodium falciparum strains (3D7, Dd2, HB3, V1/S), Babesia duncani, Babesia divergens (Rouen 87), and Babesia MO1, with proliferation and morphology comparable to established species-specific media while removing reliance on undefined or discontinued components. Using a SYBR Green I assay on this platform, the authors screened dihydrotriazines and biguanides targeting conserved DHFR-TS enzymes and identified potent antifolate candidates with broad-spectrum activity against Babesia and Plasmodium species.

articles.headline: Modeling finds R21 malaria vaccine highly cost-effective in Zambia
articles.title_original: Provincial stratification and equity-weighted cost-effectiveness of malaria vaccines in Zambia under climate variability.
articles.authors: Phiri AE
articles.details: A modeling study built a province-stratified SEIR-mosquito transmission model for Zambia, calibrated to routine surveillance, to compare RTS,S/AS01 and R21 malaria vaccines over a 5-year horizon (2024-2028) with 3% discounting from the Ministry of Health perspective, benchmarked against a US$500/DALY willingness-to-pay threshold. RTS,S was estimated to avert a median 9.80×10^5 cases (95% UI 8.88×10^5-1.08×10^6) and ~1960 deaths, while R21 averted 1.09×10^6 cases (95% UI 9.91×10^5-1.21×10^6) and ~2188 deaths. Impact varied by province; authors conclude RTS,S is borderline cost-effective while R21 is highly cost-effective and potentially dominant, supporting phased, equity-weighted provincial roll-out.

articles.headline: Sudan parents know malaria well but few aware of vaccine
articles.title_original: Assessment of parental knowledge, attitude, and acceptance toward malaria vaccine for children under five years in Sudan, 2025.
articles.authors: Hassan AAE, Ali NAH, Elshareef MAA, Sidahmed TSM, Elobied HES, Ali AEO, Salih DAD, Al Rahma AMM, Abdallha AWA, Hussain AMS, Hussain AMS, Mohammed EYM, Ahmed KAH, Awadallah SYM
articles.details: A descriptive cross-sectional study surveyed 706 parents in high malaria-burden regions of Sudan (April-June 2025) using a piloted questionnaire (Cronbach's alpha 0.741) to assess knowledge, attitudes, and acceptance of the malaria vaccine for children under five. Nearly all (98.2%) were aware of malaria, but only 268 (38.0%) had heard of the vaccine; fathers were more aware than mothers (19.0% vs 10.7%, p=0.002), and awareness was higher among respondents from Kassala (30.6%), those with postgraduate education (11.9%), public-sector employees (13.1%), and higher earners (26.9% for >500K SDG; p=0.010). Acceptance was high; authors call for targeted awareness campaigns.

articles.headline: Two malaria cases of splenic rupture needing emergency splenectomy
articles.title_original: Malaria-associated splenic haemorrhage requiring emergency splenectomy: a rare complication managed in a resource-constrained setting.
articles.authors: Kumar A, Agatrao BA, Prakash A, Pogatyanatti B, Uppaluri D, Krishna VS, Farook AA, Sharma A, Patel A
articles.details: This report describes two patients working in a malaria-endemic region of South Sudan who presented with acute febrile illness of two days and palpable hepatosplenomegaly, and were diagnosed with mixed Plasmodium falciparum-Plasmodium vivax infection. Both suffered spontaneous splenic rupture causing gross hemoperitoneum and hemodynamic instability, requiring emergency exploratory laparotomy as a life-saving measure in a resource-constrained Eastern African setting. One patient survived after timely surgery and supportive care, while the other died despite emergency management.

articles.headline: Plasmodium tubulin adaptations alter microtubule structure and drug response
articles.title_original: Adaptations in Plasmodium tubulin determine distinct microtubule architectures, mechanics and drug susceptibility.
articles.authors: Bangera M, Wu J, Beckett D, Fachet D, Ferreira JL, Voth GA, Reber S, Moores CA
articles.details: Using parasite-purified tubulin and cryo-electron microscopy, researchers determined Plasmodium falciparum microtubule structures to understand how microtubule architecture varies. Parasite-specific sequences alter the tubulin dimer structure, suggesting modified drug susceptibility and polymer properties; within the P. falciparum microtubule, lateral contacts are smaller but stronger and the lattice is stiffer than in brain microtubules. Non-canonical architectures seen in parasites closely matched those formed in vitro, validating their physiological relevance. The authors conclude that evolutionary adaptation of tubulin modulates the material properties of the parasite microtubule cytoskeleton.

articles.headline: Improved QSAR models predict HDAC-inhibitor antimalarials, find nanomolar hits
articles.title_original: Improved quantitative structure-activity relationship (QSAR) models to predict the activity of hydroxamic acids as HDAC inhibitors against malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Dawood WA, Macdonald JR, Skinner-Adams TS, Hesping E, Gorse AJP, Andrews KT
articles.details: To accelerate antimalarial discovery, researchers built QSAR classification models (B1-B9) from 572 hydroxamic acid structures paired with P. falciparum growth-inhibition (PfIC50) values targeting histone deacetylases. Tripartite split models B5-B9 achieved high accuracy (AUC 0.953-0.984); external validation on 106 quisinostat-derived compounds showed B7 and B8 markedly outperformed first-generation model A7 (>67% vs ~5%). Testing 60 compounds from a virtual screen of >36,000 structures, model B7 was most accurate (~81%) versus B8 (~68%) and A7 (~56%), and yielded three potent antiplasmodial hits (PfIC50 8, 14, and 80 nM), two of which hyperacetylated P. falciparum histone H4, confirming deacetylase inhibition.

articles.headline: Deep learning models CRISPR gene drive mosquitoes for malaria suppression
articles.title_original: A simulation-based deep learning framework for spatially explicit malaria modeling of CRISPR suppression gene drive mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Allegretti YH, Sun W, Champer J
articles.details: This study built a deep learning model to understand how different parameters affect Anopheles mosquito suppression and human malaria prevalence following release of CRISPR suppression gene drives. Results suggest that reducing embryo resistance, reducing functional resistance, and increasing drive conversion efficiency can contribute to mosquito and malaria suppression. The parameter space for eliminating malaria was substantially larger than that for eliminating mosquitoes, suggesting an imperfect drive may still accomplish its objective despite chasing or functional resistance. The authors conclude suppression gene drives may be highly effective at locally eliminating malaria, even in challenging conditions.

articles.headline: Declining P. vivax genetic diversity tracks falling transmission in Cambodia
articles.title_original: Temporal trends in Plasmodium vivax diversity in eastern Cambodia evidence declining transmission.
articles.authors: Eam R, Hoon KS, Sutanto E, Rai A, Trimarsanto H, Rumaseb A, Thin S, Hor S, Chhorvann C, Bunkea T, Price RN, Popovici J, Auburn S
articles.details: Researchers genotyped 182 symptomatic Plasmodium vivax isolates from eastern Cambodia collected in 2014, 2015, 2019 and 2023, using a panel of 93 microhaplotype markers (vivaxGEN) sequenced on Illumina, to assess infection diversity and relatedness (identity-by-descent, IBD) as a transmission proxy. Polyclonal infections fell to 5% in 2023 versus 22-48% in earlier years (p<0.05), aligning with accelerated primaquine use in 2021, while infections with IBD >0.95 to one or more others rose to 81.4% in 2023 versus 8.9-10.8% in 2014-2019, indicating inbreeding after a population bottleneck. A 2019 spike in polyclonal infections (48%) followed interruption of malaria control services. The authors conclude microhaplotype genotyping can inform P. vivax transmission and support widespread radical cure.

articles.headline: Yellow fever-driven ecological collapse linked to reduced malaria in Brazil
articles.title_original: When yellow fever silenced the forest: Ecological collapse and reduced malaria transmission in the Rio de Janeiro Atlantic Forest, Brazil.
articles.authors: de Pina-Costa A, Lourenço-de-Oliveira R, Brasil P, Daniel-Ribeiro CT
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Correction to article on Yanomami malaria outbreak and gold mining
articles.title_original: Correction: 'Investigating the Yanomami malaria outbreak: gold mining and malaria' (2026), by de Angeli Dutra et al.
articles.authors: de Angeli Dutra D, Fontes CJ, Braga ÉM, Mordecai EA
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Malaria resurgence in Amhara, Ethiopia driven by spatial not warming factors
articles.title_original: Climate, environmental, and programmatic correlates of malaria resurgence in Amhara, Ethiopia (2018-2024): a Bayesian spatiotemporal analysis.
articles.authors: Lake MW, Asemahagn MA, Gelaye KA, Muchie KF, Nigussie TZ, Engedaw HA, Yenesew MA
articles.details: This ecological district-level panel analysis used Bayesian hierarchical negative binomial regression with spatial (BYM2) and temporal (AR1) random effects across 13,944 district-month observations from 166 districts (January 2018-December 2024) to examine climate, environmental, and programmatic correlates of malaria resurgence in Amhara, Ethiopia. Among 5,746,571 confirmed cases (64.3% P. falciparum, 35.7% P. vivax), mean monthly incidence rose 5.5-fold from 1.19 to 6.53 per 1,000 (2018 to 2024). Higher lagged maximum temperature and rainfall were associated with higher incidence, elevation was protective, and IRS protection, higher ITN ownership and larval source management were associated with lower incidence. Climate-incidence associations were predominantly spatial rather than temporal, and the authors conclude the resurgence was not consistent with a warming-driven explanation, favoring spatially targeted interventions.

articles.headline: Maternal parasitemia is main predictor of neonatal malaria in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Socioeconomic determinants and maternal malaria: impact on neonatal parasitemia in Osun State, Southwest, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Odediji SA, Busari LO, Olawuyi AO, Amoo KO, Amoo-Adeboye BO, Surakat OA, Adeleke MA
articles.details: A cross-sectional study of 85 mother-infant pairs at a hospital in Osogbo, Osun State, Nigeria used microscopy on maternal, cord, and neonatal blood to assess predictors of neonatal peripheral parasitemia. Neonatal parasitemia prevalence was 8.2% (7/85); all infected neonates were born to mothers with positive microscopy (7/19) and none to parasitemia-negative mothers (p<0.001). No significant associations were found with maternal education (p=0.912), occupation (p=0.183), ITN use (p=0.413), or IPT uptake (p=1.000). The authors conclude maternal parasitemia is the primary risk factor for vertical transmission.

articles.headline: Cameroon caregivers show low malaria vaccine knowledge despite awareness
articles.title_original: Knowledge, attitudes and factors associated with the awareness of caregivers of under-five children regarding the malaria vaccine in the Tiko Health District, Cameroon: A community-based cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Idang MA, Nsobinenyui D, Ukah CE, Yunika LK, Ngu CN, Fuanghene Wefuan R, Dang SZ, Ndip NE, Feguem MP, Shei CM, Nsagha DS
articles.details: A community-based cross-sectional study surveyed 410 caregivers of children aged 0-5 in the Tiko Health District, Cameroon about the malaria vaccine (RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M) using a structured questionnaire scored at a 60% cut-off. Although 60.7% had heard of the vaccine, only 26.6% had adequate knowledge and 25.1% positive attitudes, with healthcare workers the main information source (35.4%). Awareness was lower among caregivers whose child had a prior malaria episode (AOR 0.55; 95% CI 0.28-0.97), and higher among those trusting health workers (AOR 3.02; 1.83-4.99) and attending routine immunization (AOR 3.57; 2.27-5.60). Authors urge stronger health-worker engagement.

articles.headline: Correction: targeting prolyl-tRNA synthetase kills malaria mosquito vectors
articles.title_original: Correction to: Chemical Targeting of Prolyl-tRNA Synthetase Stalls Ovarian Development and Kills Malaria Vectors.
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Genomic surveillance maps fragmented antimalarial resistance across India
articles.title_original: Targeted Genomic Surveillance Unveils Genetic Variations Linked to Regional Malaria Drug Resistance Dynamics in India.
articles.authors: Khilari A, Sharma S, Bajpai M, Viswan K A, Chaturvedi R, Mirdha BR, Rahi M, Sharma A, Shanmugam D
articles.details: Researchers analyzed 238 P. falciparum clinical samples from 6 Indian states, using nanopore sequencing of 15 resistance-associated gene amplicons from dried blood spots via a PfMDR15 surveillance panel. The Northeast showed persistent high-frequency chloroquine resistance (Pfcrt K76T, CVIET haplotype; Pfaat1 S258L) and quintuple/sextuple Pfdhfr-Pfdhps haplotypes conferring complete sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance, while Central India retained largely wild-type Pfcrt but showed emerging lumefantrine tolerance (Pfmdr1 Y184F, Pfaat1 S258L). No WHO-validated Pfk13 artemisinin resistance mutations were detected, supporting continued ACT efficacy. Authors stress integrating molecular surveillance into drug policy.

articles.headline: Co-risk mapping prioritizes newborn sickle cell screening hubs in Uganda
articles.title_original: A maximal-coverage approach to prioritizing newborn sickle cell screening sites in Uganda using a sickle cell-malaria co-risk surface.
articles.authors: Paasi G, Ndeezi G, Kiyaga C, Namazzi R, Mupere E, Munabi IG, Kiguli S, Idro R, Olupot-Olupot P
articles.details: This modeling study combined kriged sickle haemoglobin (HbS) allele frequency with 2015-2024 mean P. falciparum prevalence in children 2-10 (PfPR2-10) on a 5km grid to compute an HbS×PfPR2-10 co-risk surface, then used greedy selection to place 50 candidate newborn sickle cell screening hubs in Uganda. Median HbS frequency was 6.5% and area-weighted mean PfPR2-10 was 26.0%; cumulative captured co-risk reached 19.7%, 33.6%, 45.2%, 57.5%, and 65.6% after placing 10-50 hubs. Captured co-risk was highly concentrated across districts (Gini 0.81), and 28/50 hubs (56.0%) fell within 60-minute facility catchments, offering a transparent siting tool.

articles.headline: Anopheles gambiae dominates Benin vectors amid widespread pyrethroid resistance
articles.title_original: Entomological surveillance of malaria vector: species, behaviors, and insecticide resistance in southern and central Benin.
articles.authors: Hougbe SZ, Ossé RA, Akpodji S, Ahouandjinou MJ, Koumodji KD, Affolabi ZK, DePina AJ, Oussou O, Bocco EO, Souler MI, Idrissou S, Alohoutade SV, Adjottin B, Mokuba A, Chitou S, Totin F, Ossah M, Traore I, Cisse MBM, Baba-Moussa L, Akogbeto M
articles.details: An entomological and molecular survey across six districts in southern and central Benin sampled mosquitoes over dry and rainy seasons using human landing catches and pyrethrum spray catches, with P. falciparum detection by qPCR and WHO insecticide susceptibility tests plus kdr L1014F and ace-1R G119S analyses. Anopheles gambiae s.l. predominated (>98%), biting both indoors and outdoors with high anthropophagy; EIRs were highest in Ifangni and Toffo, including the dry season. kdr allele frequencies exceeded 65% at all sites, while pirimiphos-methyl susceptibility was generally higher but reduced in some districts. Authors support stratified, locally tailored vector control.

articles.headline: P. malariae AMA-1 vaccine candidate highly diverse in Vietnam isolates
articles.title_original: Genetic polymorphism and natural selection of apical membrane antigen-1 in Plasmodium malariae isolated from Vietnam.
articles.authors: Nguyễn ĐTD, Lê HG, Võ TC, Nguyễn TH, Kang JM, Nguyễn KO, Trinh NTM, Van Khanh C, Hanh NTL, Van TTH, Trung NK, Cho M, Son TT, Quang HH, Na BK
articles.details: Researchers amplified and sequenced the apical membrane antigen-1 gene (pmama-1) from 95 P. malariae clinical isolates from Vietnam and analyzed polymorphism and natural selection using BioEdit, MEGA4, and DnaSP. A total of 117 sequences grouped into 19 distinct haplotypes, with most amino acid polymorphisms concentrated in domains I and II and predicted to sit on the protein surface. Polymorphism profiles broadly mirrored other Southeast Asian countries, though the positions and frequencies of changes varied by country, and evidence of natural selection was observed. The authors conclude pmama-1 is substantially heterogeneous and call for larger global studies.

articles.headline: Gene-drive mosquitoes with compound effectors suppress P. falciparum infection
articles.title_original: Compound effector genes suppress malaria parasite infections in gene-drive population modification strains of the African malaria mosquitoes, Anopheles gambiae and Anopheles coluzzii.
articles.authors: Carballar-Lejarazú R, Dong Y, Pham TB, Tushar T, Tavadia M, Dimopoulos G, James AA
articles.details: Researchers built four population-modification gene-drive strains in Anopheles gambiae s.s. and An. coluzzii carrying compound effector gene modules TP24 and TP43, introduced into TP13-based strains using Cas9 and dual guide RNAs (AgTP24, AgTP43, AcTP24, AcTP43). Gene conversion and inheritance were high, with 95–100% inheritance of the constructs, and life-table analyses showed mixed fitness impacts depending on species and copy number. After P. falciparum challenge, the compound effectors significantly reduced both parasite prevalence and infection intensity in both species. The authors highlight the potential of compound effector strategies for durable malaria transmission control.

articles.headline: Insecticidal net campaigns cut malaria incidence up to 35% in Zambia
articles.title_original: Malaria incidence before and after long-lasting insecticidal net distribution in four Zambian provinces, 2010-2021.
articles.authors: Mwenya RM, Ngulube ID, Richardson S
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: Using binomial regression and interrupted time series, researchers analyzed monthly rapid-diagnostic-test-confirmed malaria incidence across four Zambian provinces (Eastern, Luapula, Northern, Northwestern; combined population 5,965,257 across 1,357 health facility catchment areas) during January 2010–December 2021, assessing three long-lasting insecticidal net distribution campaigns. Malaria incidence declined significantly following LLIN distribution, with up to a 35% reduction in seasonally adjusted incidence (rate ratio 0.65, 95% CI 0.50–0.85; p=0.002). The authors emphasize that regular, timely net replacement is essential to maintain protective benefits.

articles.headline: Mouse cerebral malaria model poorly predicts human treatment success
articles.title_original: Cerebral malaria: of mice and men.
articles.authors: Weerasekera CJ, White NJ
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: This systematic review examined human and murine cerebral malaria publications since 2010, focusing on the P. berghei ANKA/C57BL/6 mouse model, which differs markedly from human disease in histopathology and intervention response. Since 2010, 149 interventions (118 adjuvants) were tested in mice, of which 142 (95%) were reportedly successful, yet only 26% were evaluated after the cerebral syndrome had developed and 65% of adjuvants were tested without a concomitant antimalarial. Meanwhile clinical research declined and no adjuvant intervention has proved effective in humans. The authors conclude the murine model's predictive value for human cerebral malaria is very poor.

articles.headline: Seasonal chemoprevention SPAQ shows low antimalarial efficacy in northern Mozambique
articles.title_original: Parasitological efficacy of seasonal malaria chemoprevention in Nampula, northern Mozambique.
articles.authors: Bonnington C, Sitoe M, Pulido Tarquino IA, Enosse SM, Sararat C, Suwannasin K, Proux S, Koesukwiwat U, Tarning J, Imwong M, Theiss-Nyland K, Nosten FH, White NJ
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: A pilot pharmacometric study nested in a seasonal malaria chemoprevention deployment gave monthly sulphadoxine-pyrimethamine-amodiaquine (SPAQ) to 460 healthy children in two villages in a high-transmission area of Nampula, northern Mozambique, with quantitative PCR, microscopy, and drug levels measured before and 7 and 28 days after the first dose. Parasitaemia prevalence fell from 68% to 41% overall (71% to 44% among those followed to 28 days), with preventive efficacy of 97% for P. ovale but only 42% for P. falciparum. Reinfections (N=50) and recrudescences (N=3) grew through high desethylamodiaquine concentrations, yet all 250 P. falciparum isolates were Pfcrt 76K (susceptible); 21/64 breakthrough infections had patent gametocytaemia. The authors conclude amodiaquine had low parasitological efficacy and sulphadoxine and pyrimethamine did not contribute significantly.

articles.headline: Review finds no consensus on CYP450 genes and vivax treatment failure
articles.title_original: Polymorphisms in Cytochrome P450 genes: implications for Plasmodium vivax malaria treatment in Brazil.
articles.authors: Cerilo-Filho M, Almeida MNG, Sampaio MP, Mello AGNC, Fernando DJV, Braz RP, Miceli LA, Reis NF, Guimarães RJP, Silva JRS, Storti-Melo LM, Baptista ARS, Machado RLD
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: A PRISMA systematic review examined how host Cytochrome P450 gene polymorphisms affect Plasmodium vivax malaria treatment in Brazil, where P. vivax accounts for about 85% of annual malaria cases. Articles published up to August 2024 were screened, and of 296 studies retrieved, eight met inclusion criteria, most focusing on CYP2D6 polymorphisms and using genotype-based prediction of metaboliser phenotypes. The review found a lack of consensus regarding the relationship between CYP450 single nucleotide polymorphisms and antimalarial drug response, and discusses potential implications for therapeutic failure and treatment safety.

articles.headline: PCR detects subpatent malaria in febrile migrant populations in Punjab
articles.title_original: Detection of subpatent malaria among febrile mobile and migratory population individuals: a strategy for malaria elimination.
articles.authors: Kaura T, Mewara A, Devi S, Singh N, Takkar J, Kumar N, Sehgal R, Grover GS
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: Case investigation with reactive active case detection (RACD) was used to detect subpatent malaria among febrile mobile and migratory population (MMP) individuals around index cases in Punjab, India, using microscopy, rapid diagnostic tests, and nested PCR. Of 119 malaria cases reported, 93.3% (111/119) were P. vivax and 6.7% (8/119) were P. falciparum. RACD around 28 index cases collected 176 fingerprick samples, identifying 11 infected individuals (6.3%): eight detected by all methods and three (3/11; 27.3%) subpatent cases detected by nPCR only. No positive patients were G6PD-deficient. The authors conclude subpatent malaria in MMP individuals challenges elimination and RACD may reduce spillover risk.

articles.headline: Uganda sustained malaria services during COVID-19 pandemic
articles.title_original: Ensuring continuity of malaria services in times of crisis: lessons learned in Uganda during the COVID-19 pandemic.
articles.authors: Manning EK, Magumba G, Tibenderana JK, Nuwa A
articles.details: This case study describes how Uganda's National Malaria Control Division and partners maintained essential malaria control interventions during the COVID-19 pandemic despite national pandemic restrictions, rapidly introducing updated operational protocols. Partners secured funding to supply more than 33,000 health workers and village health teams with infection prevention and control equipment to ensure safe service delivery. The authors highlight lessons on proactive risk planning, timely data reporting, and sustainable funding and commodity supplies.

articles.headline: Modeling optimizes mass drug administration timing for malaria in southern Senegal
articles.title_original: Mathematical modeling for analyzing mass drug administration operational factors for efficient malaria incidence reduction in southern Senegal.
articles.authors: Ndiaye K, Diop EHS, Slater HC, Loum MA, Ndiaye JA, Diallo I, NDiop M, Kaly SN
articles.details: Researchers developed a mathematical model based on impulsive differential equations incorporating asymptomatic carriers to analyze how operational factors affect mass drug administration (MDA) for malaria reduction in southeastern Senegal, following a pilot in the Tambacounda health district. Four factors were explored: number of MDA rounds, interval between rounds, campaign coverage, and start date. The model predicts successful campaigns require high coverage, multiple rounds per year, intervals of five to six weeks between rounds, and starting in the first month of the transmission period (mid-to-late July), with start date and interval highly correlated. The authors conclude MDA outcomes can be improved by optimizing operational factors for specific endemic settings.

articles.headline: Male sex and androgens weaken liver-stage immunity to malaria in mice
articles.title_original: Host biological sex directs immune control of the Plasmodium parasite liver stage in mice.
articles.authors: Duncombe CJ, Poehlman AS, Conrad EN, Sen N, Ayenew A, Blyn RC, McElfresh GW, Glennon EKK, Boey K, Kalata AC, Kaushansky A, Minkah N, Shears MJ, Murphy SC
articles.details: In male and female BALB/cJ mice—including orchiectomized (ORX) and androgen-treated males—infected with Plasmodium yoelii sporozoites, researchers assessed hepatic parasite burden, immune cell composition, and gene expression by immunofluorescence, RT-PCR, and spatial transcriptomics. Androgens increased parasite survival by reducing elimination of infected hepatocytes, while females and ORX males had fewer infected hepatocytes by 44 hours post-infection. Intact males showed lower baseline Kupffer cell, monocyte, and dendritic cell densities, reduced antigen presentation, and blunted Type I interferon responses. The authors conclude biological sex and androgens shape innate hepatic immunity and parasite survival during the liver stage.

articles.headline: Age, dose, and booster timing shape B/T cell responses to malaria vaccines
articles.title_original: B and T cell responses to pre-erythrocytic R21/Matrix-M and blood-stage RH5.1/Matrix-M malaria vaccines in endemic settings.
articles.authors: Bundi CK, Muñoz O, Kibwana E, Bellamy D, Stockdale L, Barrett JR, McHugh K, Mtaka I, Kalinga W, Kimani D, Nyamako L, Keter K, Ogwang R, Hill AVS, Bejon P, Hamaluba M, Silk SE, Minassian AM, Draper SJ, Olotu AI, Kapulu MC, Ewer KJ, Nielsen CM
articles.details: Using pre- and post-vaccination PBMC from two Phase 1b trials of R21/Matrix-M (NCT03580824) and RH5.1/Matrix-M (NCT04318002), antigen-specific memory B cell and circulating Tfh responses against P. falciparum were measured by flow cytometry. For R21, NANP-specific activated IgG+ memory B cells were higher in infants than adults and with a 50μg versus 25μg Matrix-M dose, correlating with anti-NANP serum IgG. For RH5.1, higher prior malaria exposure increased RH5-specific cTfh frequencies, and RH5-specific IgG+ memory B cell responses were greatest with delayed booster dosing (10-10-10μg at 0-1-6 months), correlating with serum antibody 2 years after final vaccination. Authors conclude age, adjuvant dose, vaccine dose, and booster timing influence cellular responses, informing multi-stage vaccine design.

articles.headline: Yeast RNAi pesticide kills invasive malaria vector Anopheles stephensi
articles.title_original: Targeting the Highly Invasive Malaria Vector Anopheles stephensi using Yeast RNAi Pesticides.
articles.authors: Njoroge TM, Hamid-Adiamoh M, Mysore K, Stewart ATM, Sun L, Akaiso DD, Duman-Scheel M
articles.details: This laboratory study evaluated Sh.463, a modified Saccharomyces cerevisiae baker's yeast RNAi pesticide targeting the Anopheles stephensi Shaker gene, delivered to adults via an attractive targeted sugar bait (ATSB) system and to larvae via a yeast-based system. Treatment with Sh.463-56.10R silenced the Shaker gene and caused high mortality in larvae and adults. Female-specific RNAi yeast larvicides targeting putative female-specific genes produced significant female mortality in cup bioassays, yielding higher male:female offspring ratios. The authors conclude Shaker-targeting RNAi yeast may be a novel biorational tool for integrated A. stephensi control and male mosquito sorting.

articles.headline: Gender-sensitive malaria vulnerability mapping in Zamfara State, Nigeria
articles.title_original: Geospatial mapping of gender-sensitive malaria vulnerability and ITN usage barriers in Zamfara State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Atijosan A, Atijosan A, Salau AO
articles.details: This study developed a Composite Gender-Sensitive Malaria Vulnerability Index (CGMVI) for Zamfara State, Nigeria, integrating malaria incidence/mortality data (Malaria Atlas Project), population data (WorldPop), ITN access and usage, maternal education, and household wealth using a Pentagonal Fuzzy Analytic Hierarchy Process, with Getis-Ord Gi* hotspot analysis. A west-to-east vulnerability gradient emerged, with hotspots in Gummi (3.86) and Bukkuyum (3.62) marked by low ITN usage (82-85%), maternal education deficits (89-91%), and poverty (85-90%); northern/eastern LGAs showed low vulnerability with ITN coverage ≈92%. Western clustering was significant (z-scores 2.11-4.89), with coldspots in the north/east (z-scores -1.69 to -3.39). The authors conclude the CGMVI can guide gender-sensitive ITN distribution and malaria control planning.

articles.headline: Structure-based discovery and validation of a P. falciparum DPAP-1 inhibitor
articles.title_original: Structure-based discovery of Plasmodium falciparum DPAP-1 inhibitor and its experimental validation as an antimalarial agent.
articles.authors: Verma D, Tripathi M, Rathore S, Ahmad I, Saeed M, Dwivedi VD, Tripathi RC
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Imported malaria in Portugal: prior infection linked to milder disease
articles.title_original: Imported Malaria in Portugal: A Retrospective Analysis from a Tertiary Public Hospital.
articles.authors: Valença Ferreira T, Alves Silva G, Duque V
articles.details: A retrospective study of 88 adults with imported malaria, mostly male expatriates exposed primarily in Angola, characterized epidemiology and severity biomarkers. Previous malaria history was reported in 52.3% of cases, and severe malaria occurred in 25.0% of patients, significantly more often among those without prior infection (p=0.027). Patients with severe disease had higher creatinine (p=0.009) and lactate dehydrogenase (p=0.038), while urea and parasitemia were independently associated with longer hospital stay (p<0.001 and p=0.016). The authors conclude these findings support semi-immunity in previously exposed individuals and the value of laboratory biomarkers for risk stratification.

articles.headline: Review: pfhrp2/3 gene deletions threaten HRP2-based malaria rapid tests
articles.title_original: Are HRP2/3 deletions silently crippling malaria rapid diagnostic tests?
articles.authors: S R S, Holla R, Rao M, Unnikrishnan B, B M S, Chalageri VH
articles.details: A literature review gathered 148 studies from PubMed and Google Scholar and, after screening, included 28 published studies plus WHO documents on pfhrp2/pfhrp3 gene deletions that cause false-negative HRP2-based rapid diagnostic tests for P. falciparum. Reported deletion rates ranged from 2.4% to 57.8%, with low levels in Chhattisgarh, India (3.8%), roughly 18.3% dual deletions in Brazil, and extreme levels in Southern Ethiopia (57.8%). RDT performance declines substantially where deletion prevalence exceeds the WHO 5% threshold. The authors conclude that alternatives to HRP-based tests and novel surveillance tools such as PCR, whole-genome sequencing, and digital droplet PCR are essential in high-deletion regions.

articles.headline: Drought indices predict lagged, site-specific malaria trends in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Hydroclimatic extremes indexed by SPI and malaria case dynamics in Oyo State, Nigeria: evidence for lagged, site-specific effects.
articles.authors: Odjegba EE, Akanji OE, Bankole AO, Makinde AA, Aromolaran AK, Olagoke VO
articles.details: This study examined drought and temperature influences on malaria incidence in Saki and Sepeteri, Oyo State, Nigeria, using monthly climate and health data from 2013 to 2023 and standardized precipitation indices (SPI-1, SPI-3, SPI-12) in regression models. Transmission was strongly seasonal, peaking July-November, with mean monthly cases of 1,134 in Saki and 869 in Sepeteri. In Saki, a one-unit increase in SPI-12 was associated with a 267% rise in cases (β=1.299, p=0.011), moderated by temperature (β=-0.049, p=0.011), with 1-2 month lagged models improving fit (ΔAIC≈47-59). The authors support integrating lagged drought and temperature indicators into malaria early-warning systems.

articles.headline: Non-lethal Plasmodium infection causes reversible thymic atrophy in mice
articles.title_original: Thymic Atrophy Development in a Non-Lethal Plasmodium Infection.
articles.authors: Corral-Ruiz GM, Pérez-Vega MJ, Mancilla-Herrera I, Barrios-Payán J, Fabila-Castillo L, Hernández-Pando R, Sánchez-Torres LE
articles.details: This study investigated mechanisms of thymic atrophy during non-lethal Plasmodium chabaudi AS infection across parasitemia stages. Atrophy was marked by decreased thymus weight and cellularity, especially at 20%-30% parasitemia, driven mainly by loss of double-positive (DP) and single-positive (SP) thymocytes, with cortical shrinkage, DNA fragmentation, and increased apoptosis. Elevated serum cytokines and chemokines (IFN-γ, TNF-α, IL-6, CXCL9) correlated with atrophy severity, and early release of thymocytes to the spleen worsened cellularity loss. After parasitemia resolved, the thymus recovered its structure and thymocyte populations, indicating prolonged parasitic burden can cause severe but reversible thymic dysfunction.

articles.headline: CCND3 enhancer variant associated with reduced malaria risk
articles.title_original: CCND3 enhancer variant reduces malaria risk.
articles.authors: Faial T
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Malarial retinopathy as a clinical marker of severe malaria
articles.title_original: A Study on Prevalence of Malarial Retinopathy among Malaria Cases and to Determine Its Presence as a Marker of Severe Malaria: Observations from Eastern India.
articles.authors: Chatterjee R, Bhadra TR, Chatterjee N, Mukherjee S, Halder P, Sarkar K, Chatterjee I, Saha B, Karmakar PS, Pramanik N, Samajdar SS
articles.details: This observational study of adult malaria patients (12 severe, 59 uncomplicated; severe cases mostly P. vivax) assessed direct-ophthalmoscopy retinopathy against WHO severity criteria, with febrile-illness and healthy controls. Retinopathy features appeared in 9/12 severe cases (75%) versus 2/59 uncomplicated (3.4%), and its presence was significantly associated with severe malaria (p<0.05). Sensitivity was 75% and specificity 96.6%, with positive predictive value 81.8%. Authors conclude malarial retinopathy may be a useful clinical biomarker for predicting severe malaria.

articles.headline: Optimized nested PCR detects Plasmodium malariae at low parasitemia
articles.title_original: Optimization of Nested-PCR for the detection of Plasmodium malariae in laboratory settings in Southern Vietnam.
articles.authors: Nguyen KT, Nguyen NH, Dinh QL, Nguyen HLV, Nguyen MAT, Huynh HQ, Van Thi Tran H
articles.details: This laboratory methods study optimized a nested-PCR protocol targeting the P. malariae 18S rRNA gene using PLU5/PLU6 and MAL1/MAL2 primers. The MAL1/MAL2 pair was specific for P. malariae with no cross-amplification of P. falciparum, P. vivax, P. ovale, or P. knowlesi. Optimal conditions were 35 cycles in the first round, 25 in the second, and a 54°C annealing temperature, achieving a limit of detection of 0.5 parasites/μL. Authors conclude the protocol is suited for laboratory use.

articles.headline: Climate shocks, not just warming, threaten African malaria control
articles.title_original: Climate shocks, not just warming, threaten malaria control efforts in Africa.
articles.authors: Messina J, Carrel M
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Genomic surveillance shows distinct mosquito resistance evolution under PBO nets
articles.title_original: Genetic Surveillance Reveals Differential Evolutionary Dynamic of Anopheles gambiae Under Contrasting Insecticidal Tools Used in Malaria Control.
articles.authors: Njoroge HN, Namuli L, Nagi SC, Hernandez-Koutoucheva A, McDermott DP, Knight E, Gonahasa S, Lynd A, Oruni A, Maiteki-Sebuguzi C, Opigo J, Yeka A, Katureebe A, Kyohere M, Kamya MR, Dorsey G, Hemingway J, Staedke SG, Clarkson C, Miles A, Lucas ER, Donnelly MJ
articles.details: This genomic study examined demographic and evolutionary effects on Anopheles gambiae of deploying standard pyrethroid-only ITNs versus pyrethroid-PBO nets during a clinical trial. Despite large drops in indoor mosquito density, nucleotide diversity and linkage disequilibrium showed no decline in effective population size. A Cyp9k1 gene duplication rose in frequency under pyrethroid-only nets but fell under PBO nets, while a 2La-inversion variant near UDP-glucose 6-dehydrogenase rose under PBO nets, and pyrethroid-only nets selected an X-chromosome diacylglycerol kinase locus. Authors stress genomic surveillance for adaptive resistance management.

articles.headline: Scoping review: malaria, dengue and antimalarials harm reproductive health
articles.title_original: Consequence of malaria and dengue on reproductive vigor. A scoping review.
articles.authors: Chatterjee PK, Chatterjee P, Dutta B, Anupama N, R M, Dhar S, Ks R, Bijoor SN, Tomar A, Tripathy A
articles.details: This review of English-language studies examined the effects of malaria, dengue, and antimalarial drugs on reproductive health. It reports malaria is associated with decreased semen quality, oligozoospermia, and necrozoospermia/azoospermia, plus higher rates of miscarriage, intrauterine death, premature delivery, low birth weight, neonatal and maternal deaths in pregnancy. Antimalarial drugs (chloroquine, quinine, quinacrine, pyrimethamine) are linked to male infertility, while acute dengue causes temporary sperm alterations without long-term infertility and raises the risk of premature delivery, fetal death, and hemorrhagic complications. The authors conclude malaria and dengue affect reproductive health, with an indirect effect being infertility due to antimalarial drugs.

articles.headline: Structure-guided PfCyRPA epitope mimic improves blood-stage malaria vaccine
articles.title_original: Structure-guided design of a PfCyRPA-based vaccine against blood-stage malaria.
articles.authors: Alam N, Wolfle C, Butkeviciute E, Quinkert D, King LDW, Higgins MK
articles.details: This pre-clinical vaccine-design study targeted PfCyRPA, a component of the Plasmodium falciparum PfPCRCR erythrocyte-invasion complex and a six-bladed β-propeller. Using structure-guided design, the authors built PfCyRPA-EM, a thermostable epitope mimic containing blades 1 and 2, which carry the epitopes of the most potent known growth-inhibitory antibodies. In a pre-clinical model, PfCyRPA-EM elicited antibodies that inhibited parasite growth at lower concentrations than those elicited by PfCyRPA. Its higher thermostability and improved expression as an I53-50 nanoparticle fusion make it well-suited for clinical development, alone or with other immunogens.

articles.headline: Zoonotic Plasmodium common in Malaysian long-tailed macaques
articles.title_original: Molecular and phylogenetic analysis of zoonotic Plasmodium in long-tailed macaques: insights from wildlife-human conflict zones in Selangor, Malaysia.
articles.authors: Zamri SZ, Aziz NAA, Mazlan M, Saidu YS, Zulhisam A, Che-Amat A, Sipin Q
articles.details: This cross-sectional study assessed prevalence, genetic diversity, and phylogeny of three zoonotic malaria parasites (Plasmodium cynomolgi, P. inui, P. knowlesi) in 100 long-tailed macaques (Macaca fascicularis) culled in wildlife-human conflict zones of Petaling and Hulu Langat districts, Selangor, Malaysia, between September 2022 and April 2023. Nested PCR found prevalences of 24% for P. cynomolgi, 23% for P. inui, and 4% for P. knowlesi, with 51% single, 18% dual, and 2% triple infections. Phylogenetic analysis (Maximum Likelihood, Kimura 2-parameter, 1,000 bootstraps) identified major S-type and A-type lineages for all three species. The authors emphasize the need for ongoing surveillance to mitigate zoonotic malaria risk.

articles.headline: Host protein CD44 acts as coreceptor for P. falciparum invasion
articles.title_original: With a little help from my friend: engaging CD44 facilitates Plasmodium falciparum invasion of erythrocytes.
articles.authors: Rénia L
articles.details: This commentary highlights work by Kongsomboonvech et al. identifying the host transmembrane protein CD44 as a critical coreceptor for Plasmodium falciparum invasion of human erythrocytes. When CD44 is crosslinked, it significantly enhances the parasite's ability to invade red blood cells, providing a molecular link between early merozoite attachment and the final, essential invasion steps.

articles.headline: Metabolic NAD+ shift triggers mosquito-stage rRNA switch in malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Shifting metabolism alters rRNA expression in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Hadjimichael E, Deitsch KW
articles.details: This commentary describes findings by Couble et al. on how malaria parasites switch the distinct ribosomal RNAs they express at different points in their transmission cycle. The authors determined that derepression of the mosquito-specific rDNA loci is initiated by altered NAD+/nicotinamide (NAM) ratios, which result from the metabolic shift parasites undergo as they transition into the mosquito stages.

articles.headline: How taurine helps keep asymptomatic malaria infections in check
articles.title_original: How taurine keeps asymptomatic malaria infections in check.
articles.authors: Taylor TE
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Ivermectin mass drug administration is cost-effective for malaria control in Kenya
articles.title_original: Cost and cost-effectiveness of ivermectin mass drug administration for malaria control in Kwale county, Kenya: a modelling analysis of a cluster-randomised trial.
articles.authors: Xie K, Otuko R, Marathe A, Gutierrez AS, Kariuki M, Musyoka L, Yaa E, Mbarak J, Omondi I, Kasiwa L, Rabinovich R, Maia M, Chaccour C, Deng X, Rist C
articles.details: A cost-effectiveness analysis, from a societal perspective, of the BOHEMIA cluster-randomised trial of ivermectin mass drug administration (iMDA) in Kwale county, Kenya, where iMDA given once monthly for 3 months at ~64% coverage reduced malaria incidence by 26%. Using a decision tree model, intervention cost was US$11.83 per person; household out-of-pocket costs averaged $5.85 (uncomplicated) and $52.23 (severe). The base-case ICER was $905.23 per DALY averted, below the threshold of 0.5× Kenya's GDP per capita ($974.65); the probabilistic median ICER was $1107.51 (50% credible interval 770.05-1606.77). The authors conclude iMDA can be a cost-effective supplementary vector control tool in moderate-transmission settings with good bednet coverage.

articles.headline: Commentary on a new malaria tool amid complex decision making
articles.title_original: A new tool at a complex time for malaria decision making.
articles.authors: Devine A, Kamya C
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Taurine rises in low-transmission season and blocks infected-cell adhesion
articles.title_original: Longitudinal plasma metabolomics reveals a role for taurine in asymptomatic malaria and seasonal persistence.
articles.authors: Diffendall G, Millot P, Chen P, Fogang B, Dorin-Semblat D, Aprahamian F, Durand S, Gamain B, Claessens A, Scherf A
articles.details: A longitudinal plasma metabolomics study (n=199) of individuals in the Gambia, West Africa, comparing high and low Plasmodium falciparum transmission periods to understand asymptomatic persistence. Metabolome changes were associated mainly with seasonality and less with malaria pathogenicity, and plasma levels of taurine increased longitudinally during low-transmission periods. Taurine had no effect on cultured parasite development in vitro but strongly inhibited and prevented infected red blood cell adhesion via PfEMP1 to the receptors CD36 and endothelial protein C receptor, which is linked to cerebral malaria. The authors propose taurine reduces cytoadhesion, potentially informing strategies to reduce symptomatic malaria.

articles.headline: Gene variant lowering cyclin D3 in red-cell precursors confers malaria resistance
articles.title_original: Reduced cyclin D3 expression in erythroid cells protects against malaria.
articles.authors: Marini MG, Mingoia M, Steri M, Tsamesidis I, Idda ML, Manca A, D'Avino C, Virdis F, Lodde V, Mulas A, Asunis I, Li X, Steiner MC, Loi A, Caria CA, Marongiu MF, Floris M, Marongiu M, Manunza L, Pitzalis M, Orrù V, Fiorillo E, Zoledziewska M, Moi P, Turrini FM, Pala M, Sidore C, Schlessinger D, Novembre J, Pantaleo A, Cucca F
articles.details: A mechanistic study of the variant rs112233623-T, located in an erythroid enhancer of CCND3 (encoding cyclin D3), which is associated with increased haemoglobin A2, larger erythrocyte size, and reduced erythrocyte number. The variant disrupts a SMAD3 transcription-factor binding site, weakens enhancer activity, reduces CCND3 expression in erythroblasts, and inhibits the G1-S cell-cycle transition. Population genetic methods showed signatures of positive selection for rs112233623-T in Sardinia, where malaria was once prevalent. P. falciparum growth was impaired in cultured erythrocytes from carriers, correlating with reactive oxygen species levels, mirroring G6PD deficiency and pointing to a common ROS-based mechanism of malaria resistance.

articles.headline: Repressing a cell-cycle protein hinders malaria infection
articles.title_original: Malaria is hindered by repression of a cell-cycle protein.
articles.authors: Band G
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Some Cambodian P. vivax parasites clear slowly after artesunate
articles.title_original: Parasite clearance in patients with Plasmodium vivax monoinfection treated with artesunate in Cambodia: an observational secondary analysis of trial data.
articles.authors: Tebben K, Eng V, Seng D, Tat B, Feufack-Donfack LB, Orban A, Yeat R, Salvador J, Sin S, Ko K, Khim N, Flamand C, Sommen C, Lek D, Serre D, Popovici J
articles.details: This study characterised how Plasmodium vivax responds to artesunate using clinical, genomic, and transcriptomic data from 161 P. vivax infections in 87 patients (median age 20) treated with 2 mg/kg/day of artesunate for 7 days in a drug efficacy study in Cambodia. All infections cleared by day 3, but 49 cleared slowly, including 9 (6%) with a parasite clearance slope half-life greater than 5 h, with no significant association to patient or infection characteristics. RNA sequencing of 31 infections linked slow clearance to a delayed gene-expression response, notably downregulation of genes associated with haemoglobin endocytosis and digestion. The authors warn this slow clearance could foster resistance to the artemisinin-combination therapy partner drug.

articles.headline: mRNA vaccine restores whole-parasite malaria vaccine protection in mice
articles.title_original: mRNA vaccination overcomes haemozoin-mediated impairment of whole-parasite malaria vaccines in mice.
articles.authors: Hassert M, Drewry LL, Pewe LL, Hancox LS, He R, Arumugam S, Mix MR, Salem AK, Harty JT
articles.details: Using mouse models, researchers showed that prior blood-stage Plasmodium yoelii exposure compromised Plasmodium berghei radiation-attenuated sporozoite (RAS)-induced CD8+ T cell responses and protection against liver-stage infection. The persisting malarial pigment haemozoin impaired antigen uptake by dendritic cells, reducing T cell activation. A lipid nanoparticle-encapsulated mRNA vaccine encoding a string of Plasmodium CD8+ T cell epitopes overcame the defective response and restored protection in malaria-experienced mice, and a combined RAS-plus-mRNA regimen enhanced liver-resident memory T cells. Authors identify haemozoin as a potential obstacle to vaccine efficacy in endemic areas.

articles.headline: G6PD Viangchan does not block P. falciparum in erythroblasts
articles.title_original: Invasion and development of Plasmodium falciparum in erythroblasts of humans carrying G6PD viangchan.
articles.authors: Boonpeng K, Palasuwan A, Ketprasit N, Kulkeaw K, Palasuwan D
articles.details: This in vitro study examined invasion and development of Plasmodium falciparum in human erythroblasts derived from CD34-positive hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells from subjects carrying the G6PD Viangchan (871G>A) variant, the most common in Southeast Asia. P. falciparum invaded polychromatic erythroblasts and developed into mature trophozoites, with no difference in percentages or stages of parasitized erythroblasts between G6PD-normal, heterozygous, or hemizygous subjects. The sample size (n=6) limited statistical analysis, but these preliminary findings suggest no malaria protection in this erythroblast model, warranting validation in larger cohorts.

articles.headline: New ddPCR assays quantify P. vivax and genotype G6PD Viangchan
articles.title_original: Development and implementation of droplet digital PCR assays for accurate quantification of Plasmodium vivax parasitemia and G6PD viangchan genotyping.
articles.authors: Srisutham S, Ausit K, Boonpeng K, Suksuwanont M, Kijprasong K
articles.details: Researchers developed, validated, and implemented duplex droplet digital PCR (ddPCR) assays for quantifying Plasmodium vivax parasitemia (targeting parasite tubulin and human RHCE genes) and genotyping the G6PD Viangchan (871G>A) variant, applied to clinical samples in Kanchanaburi Province, Thailand. The parasitemia assay had a limit of detection of 3.2 parasites/µL and limit of quantification of 400 parasites/µL with accuracy over 90% and CVs below 20%; genotyping achieved 100% sensitivity and specificity. Clinical densities ranged 315–82,429 parasites/µL, chloroquine produced 99.2–99.9% reduction by day 2 with clearance by day 3, and G6PD Viangchan was rare (98.9% wild-type; one heterozygous female, 1.1%).

articles.headline: Triple-drug combinations match standard therapy for uncomplicated malaria
articles.title_original: Artesunate-pyronaridine-atovaquone-proguanil and artesunate-fosmidomycin-clindamycin compared with standard artesunate-pyronaridine for the treatment of uncomplicated malaria (MultiMal): a randomised, controlled, clinical, phase 2 trial in Gabon and Ghana.
articles.authors: Agobé JCD, Maïga-Ascofaré O, Adegnika AA, Pfaffendorf C, Boaheng JM, Edoa JR, Agyiri ID, Bayode Adegbite R, Bart-Plange EY, Ahenkan E, Ekoka Mbassi D, Sarpong FN, Placca E, Kanin DK, Bakari P, Loag W, Kettenbeil J, Krishna S, Lell B, Agnandji ST, Amuasi JH, Zoleko-Manego R, Mombo-Ngoma G, Kremsner PG, Wicha SG, Ramharter M, Mischlinger J
articles.details: The MultiMal open-label, randomised, controlled phase 2 trial in Lambaréné, Gabon, and Kumasi, Ghana, compared two novel multidrug regimens, artesunate-pyronaridine-atovaquone-proguanil (APAP) and artesunate-fosmidomycin-clindamycin (AFC), against standard artesunate-pyronaridine (AP) in 100 patients with uncomplicated malaria across three age groups. PCR-corrected ACPR (per-protocol) at day 28 was 100% (AP), 100% (APAP), and 97% (AFC); at day 42 it was 87.5% (AP), 85.3% (APAP), and 94.4% (AFC), with no evidence of differential efficacy. All regimens were safe; treatment-emergent adverse events did not differ across groups (p=0.37), and the few severe events were haematological.

articles.headline: Climate change could add 123 million malaria cases in Africa by 2050
articles.title_original: Projected impacts of climate change on malaria in Africa.
articles.authors: Symons TL, Moran A, Balzarolo A, Vargas C, Robertson M, Lubinda J, Saddler A, McPhail M, Harris J, Rozier J, Browne A, Amratia P, Bertozzi-Villa A, Bhatt S, Cameron E, Golding N, Smith DL, Noor AM, Rumisha SF, Palmer MD, Weiss DJ, Desai N, Potere D, Sukitsch N, Woods W, Gething PW
articles.details: This modeling study integrated 25 years of African data on climate, malaria burden, control, socioeconomic factors, and extreme weather using a geotemporal model linked to an ensemble of climate projections under the SSP 2-4.5 scenario. Under current control levels, climate change could cause 123 million additional malaria cases (range 49.5–203 million) and 532,000 additional deaths (195,000–912,000) in Africa between 2024 and 2050. Extreme weather events were the primary driver, accounting for 79% (50–94%) of additional cases and 93% (70–100%) of additional deaths, with most increases stemming from intensification in existing endemic areas rather than range expansion. The authors call for climate-resilient malaria control and emergency response systems.

articles.headline: Cytokine-induced killer cells kill P. falciparum-infected red cells in vitro
articles.title_original: Cytokine-induced killer (CIK) cells inhibit Plasmodium falciparum parasitemia through cytolytic effector activity in vitro.
articles.authors: Vaseq R, Ferkamchwi B, Weiher H, Lukacs-Kornek V, Mahleqa N, Hübner MP, Sharma A, Schmidt-Wolf IGH
articles.details: This in vitro study showed that cytokine-induced killer (CIK) cells, which have dual NK- and T-cell traits, inhibited Plasmodium falciparum growth and significantly reduced parasitemia after 24 hours. Cytotoxicity against infected red blood cells depended mainly on secreted lytic agents (granzyme B, granulysin, perforin) rather than cell-to-cell contact, with granulysin and perforin synergistically facilitating granzyme B entry to cause parasite death. Priming CIK cells with dendritic cells pulsed with P. falciparum lysate antigen reduced cytotoxicity. CIK cells showed efficacy comparable to chloroquine (no synergy with it), while artemisinin combined with effector cells showed slightly enhanced cytotoxicity over artemisinin alone. The authors propose CIK cells as a potential cell-therapy approach against malaria.

articles.headline: 27% sub-microscopic Plasmodium infections, 8% mixed, missed by microscopy in Central India
articles.title_original: High Burden of Febrile Sub-microscopic Plasmodium Mixed Infections in Central India: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Deora N, Pande V, Pal DK, Sharma H, Dube S, Sinha A
articles.details: This study recruited febrile patients from four patient-care settings (June to November 2020), collecting dried blood spots with paired microscopy and/or rapid diagnostic test data, and used PCR to detect P. falciparum and P. vivax. Of 1030 samples, 27% (280) were infected: 188 (18%) mono-P. falciparum, 6 (0.5%) mono-P. vivax, and 86 (8%) mixed; none were detected by microscopy or RDT, making all 27% febrile sub-microscopic infections. Microscopy slide quality was unsatisfactory when a subsample was cross-examined, and none of nine followed mixed-infection patients reported recurrences or clinical development over 12 months, with no significant difference between mono- and mixed infections. The authors conclude this high 27% sub-microscopic burden, including 8% mixed infections, represents a significant challenge for malaria elimination.

articles.headline: New protein EMAP3 found exposed on P. berghei infected red cell surface
articles.title_original: Erythrocyte Membrane Protein 3 (EMAP3) Is Exposed on the Surface of the Plasmodium berghei Infected Red Blood Cell.
articles.authors: Hernandez SRC, Rashpa R, Jonsdottir TK, Paoletta MS, Ter Beek J, Díaz MR, Krol JMM, Chevalley-Maurel S, Ishizaki T, Berntsson RP, Janse CJ, Franke-Fayard B, Brochet M, Bushell ESC
articles.details: This experimental study identified a new Plasmodium berghei protein, EMAP3, and demonstrated its export to the infected red blood cell membrane, where it likely interacts with EMAP1, with only EMAP3 exposed on the outer iRBC surface. Parasites lacking EMAP3 showed no significant reduction in growth or sequestration, indicating EMAP3 is not a major CD36-binding protein. The authors propose that EMAP3's outer-surface location offers a new scaffold for displaying P. falciparum proteins on the P. berghei iRBC surface, providing an in vivo platform to screen for putative inhibitors of P. falciparum cytoadherence.

articles.headline: Trust and shared values strengthen community malaria response in Dominican Republic
articles.title_original: The importance of values in a community-based response to malaria in Santo Domingo, Dominican Republic: results from a longitudinal, mixed-methods study.
articles.authors: Keys H, Ureña K, Bardosh K, Taveras D, Desir L, Noland G
articles.details: This exploratory, mixed-methods study examined the role of trust and other values in a community-based malaria control intervention in Santo Domingo, Dominican Republic. It began in 2020 with a cross-sectional survey of 489 households measuring trust for neighbors, neighborhood associations, health system actors, and the national government, followed by a two-year qualitative phase of 66 interviews from 39 key informants, analyzed with grounded theory. Trust-module reliability was acceptable (ordinal α = 0.70); participants rated public and private institutions as 'very trustworthy' while neighbors and neighborhood associations were most often 'somewhat trustworthy.' Companionship, reciprocity, and 'showing face' fostered a virtuous circle linking participation, morale, and timely diagnosis and treatment, leading the authors to place values at the center of community malaria initiatives.

articles.headline: Only 59% of P. vivax patients complete 14-day primaquine course
articles.title_original: Suboptimal primaquine adherence in Plasmodium vivax malaria: Evidence from high-burden tribal districts in Odisha.
articles.authors: K AV, Thiruvengadam K, Sharma R, Perumalsamy N, Arumugam RD, Srirama S, Rahul A, Rahi M
articles.details: This prospective cohort study (January–December 2024) enrolled 269 laboratory-confirmed Plasmodium vivax patients aged over one year in two high-burden tribal districts of Odisha, India (Malkangiri, Koraput), assessing 14-day primaquine adherence via self-report, pill counts, and blister pack inspection. While adherence to chloroquine (3 days) and artesunate-SP exceeded 93%, only 58.7% (95% CI: 52.6–64.9) completed the primaquine course. Discontinuation was driven by symptom resolution (61.9%) and forgetfulness (21.4%); only one patient stopped due to an adverse event. Older age and household malaria history predicted better adherence. Authors conclude behavioral drivers of early cessation must be addressed to interrupt relapse transmission.

articles.headline: P. knowlesi Pk32 antigen shows conserved epitopes for blood-stage vaccine
articles.title_original: In silico profiling of the plasmodium knowlesi 32 kDa antigen: Diversity, epitope prediction, and structural modeling.
articles.authors: Saif A, Baruah P, Alghamdi JM, Acharyya R, Lau YL, Han JH, Ahmed MA
articles.details: This in silico study analyzed the genetic diversity, natural selection, and 3-D structure of Pk32, a predicted GPI-anchored protein of Plasmodium knowlesi and a homolog of the relatively conserved P. vivax Pv32, using 39 full-length sequences primarily from Malaysian Borneo. Sequences showed low nucleotide diversity (π = 0.0061), limited polymorphism, no geographical clustering, and strong evidence of purifying selection (codon-based Z-test, Tajima's D, FEL, MEME, FUBAR, SLAC). B-cell epitope prediction identified three common epitopes, two of which (145PKERES150 and 167DIGKKQNS174) were conserved across all sequences. The authors conclude that the limited variation combined with stable, conserved epitopes makes Pk32 a compelling blood-stage vaccine candidate against P. knowlesi.

articles.headline: Cold-chain-free LAMP test detects P. knowlesi at point of care
articles.title_original: A cold-chain free loop-mediated isothermal amplification test for detection of human malaria in point-of-care settings.
articles.authors: Rosli NN, Lau YL, Lai MY
articles.details: Researchers developed a refrigeration-independent loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) assay for Plasmodium knowlesi detection using a sugar-based enzyme preservation approach. Sucrose most effectively maintained Bst DNA polymerase activity during room-temperature storage, outperforming equivalent concentrations of glucose and galactose, with consistent performance for 12 days. The optimized assay achieved analytical sensitivity of 10 copies/μL and 100% specificity against Plasmodium species and human DNA. The authors conclude this chemical preservation method is a cost-effective alternative to lyophilization, with 12-day room-temperature stability suiting field deployment for malaria surveillance and remote clinics.

articles.headline: Clathrins involved in host cytosol endocytosis in malaria parasite
articles.title_original: Clathrins Are Involved in the Endocytosis of Host Cytosol in the Malaria Parasite.
articles.authors: Miao J, Lucky AB, Ning G, Xia X, Liang X, Siddiqui F, Min H, Wang C, Li X, Zhou ZH, Cui L
articles.details: This study characterized the clathrin heavy chain (PfCHC), clathrin light chain (PfCLC), and AP-1 γ subunit (PfAP-1 γ) in Plasmodium falciparum to investigate clathrin roles in endocytosis. Interactome analyses confirmed clathrins associate mainly with AP-1 components alongside cytostome-formation proteins, and imaging localized them to the parasite cytoplasm, periphery, and cis-Golgi. Ascorbate peroxidase 2-based electron microscopy confirmed their presence at coated vesicle-like structures and, unexpectedly, at cytostome collars. Knockdown of PfCHC produced abnormally long cytostome tubes and impaired hemoglobin uptake, leading the authors to conclude clathrins participate in cytostome processes in P. falciparum.

articles.headline: Three novel K-13/Pfmdr1 mutations found in Nigerian P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Three new single nucleotide polymorphisms identified in Plasmodium falciparum isolates from Kogi State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Akoma ON, Ike AC, Ayogu EE, Agbo MC, Ezeh CC, Okoh FN, Arome SO, Ezeonu IM
articles.details: This study screened for molecular markers of antimalarial drug resistance in symptomatic patients in Kogi State, Nigeria. Blood from 600 patients was tested by rapid diagnostic test; genomic DNA from 40 RDT- and PCR-confirmed P. falciparum samples was screened for Pfmdr1 and Kelch-13, with selected K-13 amplicons Sanger-sequenced. Pfmdr1 and K-13 amplicons were obtained from 45% (18/40) of positive samples; K-13 sequences showed 99.88% similarity to wild-type 3D7. Three non-synonymous mutations (K430E, W706C, Q707K) were observed in three samples. The authors conclude none of these mutations has been previously associated with artemisinin resistance.

articles.headline: Review finds 11% of East African ACTs are poor quality
articles.title_original: Artemisinin-Based Combination Therapies in East Africa: A Systematic Review of Quality, Clinical Efficacy, and Regulatory Progress Towards Sustainable Malaria Control.
articles.authors: Mekasha YT, Tegegne AA, Mashe T, Girma M, Hasen G, Belew S
articles.details: A PRISMA systematic review (PROSPERO CRD420251135109) of 40 studies on artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) from Ethiopia, Kenya, Tanzania, and Uganda. Of 3363 ACT samples tested, 375 failed at least one physicochemical quality specification, an overall failure rate of 11.2% (375/3363), with country-specific rates highest in Uganda (33.3%, 16/48), then Kenya (14.1%, 10/71), Tanzania (11.6%, 333/2859), and Ethiopia (4.2%, 16/385). Despite substandard products, most therapeutic trials reported PCR-corrected cure rates ≥90%, with artemether-lumefantrine showing strong outcomes and dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine extended prophylaxis; emerging K13 mutations linked to delayed parasite clearance threaten ACT effectiveness.

articles.headline: Amodiaquine inhibits malaria parasite enzyme plasmepsin V in lab assays
articles.title_original: Inhibiting catalytic activity of Plasmodium falciparum aspartate protease plasmepsin V: A biochemical approach to malaria intervention.
articles.authors: Prajapati AK, Kumar Y, Choudhary S
articles.details: A biochemical study repurposing the quinoline drugs amodiaquine (AQ) and clioquinol (CQ) to inhibit the catalytic activity of Plasmodium falciparum aspartate protease plasmepsin V (PfPlmV).
