Fluorescence quenching showed both compounds had moderate binding affinity, and docking indicated AQ binds the substrate-binding site engaging catalytic dyad residues Asp118 and Asp365, while CQ does not contact the dyad. Enzyme kinetics with a fluorogenic substrate showed AQ inhibits PfPlmV competitively with an IC50 of 0.42 ± 0.04 μM, whereas CQ showed no inhibitory effect. The authors conclude these insights support new biochemical approaches to targeted antimalarial therapy.

articles.headline: Ilex umbellulata bark extract suppresses malaria in infected mice
articles.title_original: Sub-acute toxicity studies and dynamics-based molecular insights on the in vivo antimalarial activity of Ilex umbellulata (Wall.) Loes bark in Plasmodium berghei-infected mice.
articles.authors: Zothantluanga JH, Tayeng D, Zonunmawii, Rahman O, Abdalla M, Chetia D
articles.details: A study assessing sub-acute oral toxicity and in vivo antimalarial activity of an 80% methanol bark extract of Ilex umbellulata in Plasmodium berghei-infected mice using Peter's 4-day suppressive test, with in silico mechanistic analysis. The extract's LD50 exceeded 2000 mg/kg and was safe orally for up to 28 days. A 200 mg/kg dose gave the lowest parasitaemia (5.00 ± 0.97%), highest parasite suppression (85.65 ± 2.77%), and longest survival (15.33 ± 2.82 days). Docking, MD simulations, and MM-GBSA suggested cyanidin-3-O-rutinoside binding to enoyl-acyl-carrier-protein reductase (PbEACPR) may underlie the activity. Authors call it a promising antimalarial candidate.

articles.headline: Ugandan medicinal plants screened for antimalarial activity in vitro
articles.title_original: In vitro antiplasmodial activity, acute toxicity and phytochemical quantification of selected medicinal plants used for the management of uncomplicated malaria in eastern Uganda.
articles.authors: Obakiro SB, Nabitandikwa C, Kiyimba K, Ocan M, Gavamukulya Y, Andima M, Lukwago TW, Maseruka R, Chebijira M, Opio M, Hokello JF, Kibuule D, Nabatanzi A, Orena S, Waako P, Owor RO
articles.details: This in vitro study validated five medicinal plants used for malaria in Uganda by evaluating their phytochemistry, acute toxicity (Wistar rats, OECD 423), and antiplasmodial activity (72-h SYBR Green assay) against chloroquine-sensitive (3D7), chloroquine-resistant (Dd2), and clinical P. falciparum isolates. Zanthoxylum chalybeum was the most potent extract (IC50 0.81–1.28 μg/ml), followed by Albizia coriaria and Entada abyssinica (IC50 10–50 μg/ml), while Maytenus senegalensis showed weak activity and Kigelia africana was largely inactive (IC50 >100 μg/ml). Alkaloids, flavonoids, tannins, and terpenoids were present in all samples; A. coriaria and M. senegalensis showed moderate acute toxicity (LD50 <2000 mg/kg). The authors conclude the results support traditional use but more studies are needed.

articles.headline: Network meta-analysis: monthly IPTp-SP cuts malaria in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Comparative effect of chemoprevention in malaria infection during pregnancy: A network meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Naing C, Ni H, Htet NH, Aung HH, Anamalai Y, Ahmed SI, Whittaker MA
articles.details: A network meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials evaluating antimalarial drugs for chemoprevention of malaria in pregnancy (IPTp), with evidence certainty rated by GRADE. The analysis included 22 RCTs (N = 14,705), eight rated high risk of bias, using placebo or dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP) as common comparators. There was moderate-certainty evidence that monthly single-dose IPTp with sulphadoxine-pyrimethamine (IPTp-SP) produced greater reductions in maternal parasitaemia and anaemia than placebo, and low-certainty evidence of fewer low-birth-weight cases with IPTp-SP than with combined DP plus azithromycin. Authors call for head-to-head trials and data from endemic regions outside Africa.

articles.headline: Rare cholecystitis and post-artesunate hemolytic anemia in severe malaria
articles.title_original: Acute Acalculous Cholecystitis and Post-artesunate Hemolytic Anemia: Two Rare Complications in Severe Malaria.
articles.authors: Nunes CF, Dias JV, Nunes BS, Gouveia C, Varandas L
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Mitochondrial exonuclease Exomit1 essential for malaria blood-stage infection
articles.title_original: Plasmodium mitochondrial exodeoxyribonucleases, Exo(mit1) and Exo(mit2), are conserved within alveolates with Exo(mit1) essential for the establishment of blood-stage infection.
articles.authors: Mishra S, Chatterjee T, Mehra P, Gahlawat A, Pradhan S, Gupta R, Bhattacharyya MK, Mishra S, Habib S
articles.details: This laboratory study identified two mitochondrial exonucleases, PfExomit1 and PfExomit2, in Plasmodium falciparum, with homologs only in certain alveolates. Both localized to the mitochondrion and showed distinct substrate specificities: PfExomit1 cleaved single-stranded DNA in both polarities, while PfExomit2 acted as a bipolar exonuclease on dsDNA with 3'-5' activity on ssDNA. PfExomit2 interacted with the mismatch-repair protein PfMutS and the double-strand-break-repair protein PfRad51. In rodent P. berghei, conditional knockout of Exomit1 in sporozoites allowed hepatocyte invasion and liver development but blocked transition to blood stage, indicating Exomit1's mtDNA-processing role is essential for establishing blood-stage infection.

articles.headline: Hydrogel microarray patches deliver primaquine and chloroquine transdermally
articles.title_original: Formulation and evaluation of hydrogel-forming microarray patches for transdermal primaquine and chloroquine delivery in malaria therapy.
articles.authors: Anjani QK, Volpe-Zanutto F, Miatmoko A, Adhami M, Novais JT, Gaitán XA, Hariawan BS, Cahyani DM, Octavia RT, Mubarok AS, Setyaningsih WAW, Bargieri DY, Donnelly RF
articles.details: Researchers developed and optimized lyophilised reservoirs of primaquine (PQ) and chloroquine (CQ) incorporated into hydrogel-forming microarray patches (MAPs). Reservoirs dissolved within 7 s (CQ) and 16 s (PQ), and in vitro permeation through neonatal porcine skin delivered about 40 mg (70% efficiency) of each drug over 24 h. In rats, MAPs raised Cmax from 0.68 to 1.68 ng/mL for CQ and 14.32 to 47.20 ng/mL for PQ versus oral dosing, with AUC0-72h 4.4-fold higher (CQ) and 1.3-fold higher (PQ). In a murine malaria model, MAP-delivered drugs reduced parasitaemia by 99.8%. The authors conclude MAPs are a promising minimally-invasive sustained-delivery platform.

articles.headline: Lumefantrine exposure lower in pregnant women with falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Population pharmacokinetics of lumefantrine in pregnant and non-pregnant women with uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Juma E, Ding J, Ongas M, Koskei N, Onyango K, Oloo F, Aman R, Kokwaro G, Tarning J, Ogutu B
articles.details: This population pharmacokinetic study enrolled 75 women with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria (25 non-pregnant, 30 in second trimester, 20 in third trimester) given artemether-lumefantrine (80/480 mg) twice daily for 3 days, analyzed by nonlinear mixed-effects modelling. Pregnancy increased lumefantrine elimination clearance by 23% (95%CI 10.8-34.8%) and central volume of distribution by 28% (95%CI 7.3-51.8%). Pregnant women had lower exposure, with geometric mean ratios of 0.76 (AUC), 0.79 (Cmax), and 0.69 (Day 7 concentration). The 28-day PCR-corrected cure was 100% in both groups. The authors conclude lower exposure in pregnancy may raise treatment-failure risk and warrants continued efficacy monitoring.

articles.headline: Review of Plasmodium-mosquito molecular interactions for malaria control
articles.title_original: Deciphering the molecular targets of Plasmodium and Anopheline interactions for malaria control.
articles.authors: Janjoter S, Kataria D, Dahiya N, Yadav M, Singh H, Garg S, Sehrawat N
articles.details: This review examines the molecular interactions between Plasmodium parasites and Anopheles mosquitoes to identify new targets for malaria control. It discusses how ookinetes interact with mosquito midgut proteins during midgut invasion and how sporozoites interact with salivary gland proteins, the roles of Plasmodium genes in male and female gametogenesis and transmission, and how the mosquito immune system defends against the invading parasite. The authors argue that better understanding of this biology could support the design of efficient vector-control strategies. No quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: Malaria-resistance gene variants surveyed in Colombian Pacific population
articles.title_original: Prevalence of the Main Human Genetic Variants Related to Resistance to Malaria in a Population of the Colombian Pacific Coast.
articles.authors: Ortega DC, Arango MP, Cañón S, Cárdenas H, González R, Barreto G
articles.details: This molecular study characterised five malaria-resistance-related genetic variants (Duffy, HbS, HbC, G6PD, and β-thalassemia) in 819 randomly selected individuals from 12 communities in the urban area of Buenaventura, Colombia, using PCR-RFLP and allele-specific PCR. Resistance allele frequencies were 3.1% (HbS), 2.2% (HbC), 72.2% (Duffy), 2.1% (β-thalassemia-29), 2.8% (β-thalassemia-88), and 11% (G6PD). Adolescents and young adults (13–26 years) and communities in the insular zone showed the highest proportions of resistance genotypes. The authors say these findings identify age groups and communities for targeted public health attention.

articles.headline: Review: bacterial larvicides cut larvae but malaria-impact evidence thin
articles.title_original: Systematic literature review of bacterial larvicides Bacillus thuringiensis var israelensis and Bacillus (Lysinibacillus) sphaericus for malaria control in Africa.
articles.authors: Mataba GR
articles.details: This systematic review searched multiple databases for evidence on whether the microbial larvicides Bacillus thuringiensis var israelensis (Bti) and Bacillus sphaericus (Bs) reduce clinical malaria burden in Africa, with quality assessed via the CONSORT 2010 checklist, funnel plot, and Egger's test. Of 54 eligible studies (mostly small-scale field trials), most reported Bti/Bs reduced primary vector larval density by 50%–100%, but only 14 reported a subsequent reduction in malaria incidence, ranging 31%–62%. Only one study evaluated inclusion in a large-scale countrywide routine control program (São Tomé and Príncipe). The authors conclude there is a serious lack of large-scale studies, impeding evidence-based policy.

articles.headline: Weakening test-rate/positivity link signals India malaria elimination progress
articles.title_original: Weakening TPR-MBER correlation: Marker of success in India's malaria elimination progress.
articles.authors: Prajapat K, Libny YS, Syed Ashish EA, Prasad P, Yalavarthi A, Das A, Chaudhary K, Jain I
articles.details: Using 2020–2024 Monthly Malaria Information System data from Madhya Pradesh, the authors analysed trends in transmission and the relationship between the Monthly Blood Examination Rate (MBER) and Test Positivity Rate (TPR). They observed a progressively weakening correlation between MBER and TPR, which they interpret as improved surveillance saturation and reduced underlying transmission. The authors propose the MBER–TPR correlation as a dynamic indicator for monitoring progress toward malaria elimination.

articles.headline: 40.5% malaria prevalence among febrile patients in northwest Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Prevalence and associated risk factors of malaria among febrile patients at Kidist Hana Health Center Fogera District, Amhara Regional State, Northwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Alebele S, Abate A, Sisay M, Asnake T, Alehegn M, Adisu D, Munshea A
articles.details: This facility-based cross-sectional study of febrile patients at Kidist Hana Health Center in Ethiopia tested blood by microscopy and First Response P.f/P.v rapid diagnostic tests, with risk factors assessed by logistic regression. Malaria prevalence by microscopy was 40.5%, with P. falciparum predominant (78.4%). Infection was significantly associated with being widowed, age below five years, low household income, lack of ITN ownership, stagnant water near the residence, and outdoor sleeping. Microscopy detected slightly more than RDT (40.5% vs 38.9%); RDT sensitivity was 95.91%, specificity 91.24%, PPV 88.16%, and NPV 97.04%, and the authors recommend universal ITN coverage and targeted interventions.

articles.headline: Biocredit LDH rapid test shows high accuracy for P. falciparum, P. vivax
articles.title_original: Diagnostic accuracy of novel Biocredit species-specific LDH-based immunochromatographic assay for Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax detection: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Gebremeskele BT, Getu E, Demeke AD, Bogale A, Churiso G
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated the pooled diagnostic accuracy of the non-HRP2 Biocredit Pf/Pv RDT, which detects species-specific lactate dehydrogenase isomers, against microscopy and PCR using a random-effects bivariate model. Nineteen datasets from eight studies (3,923 participants) were included. Pooled sensitivity was 91% (95% CI 77–97) for P. falciparum and 96% (88–99) for P. vivax; pooled specificity was 99% (95–100) and 100% (96–100), respectively. Against PCR, P. falciparum sensitivity fell to 81% (64–91), while in a transmission-setting subgroup analysis P. falciparum sensitivity was 94% (81–98) in high-transmission and 73% (61–83) in moderate-transmission areas; the authors conclude the assay is suitable where HRP2 deletions are prevalent but may be limited in elimination settings.

articles.headline: Plasmodium falciparum genetic diversity along Myanmar border areas
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum genetic diversity in border areas of Myanmar assessed by genotyping merozoite surface protein genes.
articles.authors: Ma J, Wu Y, Zeng W, Chen X, Xiang Z, Li C, Zhang Y, Zhao H, Duan M, Liang T, Mou Y, Tang X, Liu C, Kyaw MT, Thu AN, Kyaw MP, Cui L, Yang Z
articles.details: This study genotyped the merozoite surface protein 1 (Msp1) and 2 (Msp2) genes by nested PCR in 176 P. falciparum-infected blood samples from Paletwa Township and the Myitsone and Bhamo regions of Myanmar, including migrant workers. Msp1 was genotyped in 176 samples and Msp2 in 162. MAD20 was the most frequent Msp1 allele (89% in Myitsone, 85% in Bhamo, 67% in Paletwa) and 3D7 the most frequent Msp2 allele. Multiplicity of infection (Msp1) was 1.87 in migrants versus 1.33 and 1.56 in residents, with multi-infections more frequent in migrants (72% vs 43% and 45%). Authors conclude Msp1 showed higher diversity and migrants had higher MOI, possibly from low immunity or higher transmission.

articles.headline: Review: heme oxygenase-1's dual role in malaria pathogenesis
articles.title_original: Heme oxygenase-1 and malaria pathogenesis.
articles.authors: Wakai T, Chinedu S, Afolabi I
articles.details: This review examines heme oxygenase-1 (HO-1), an inducible heme-degrading enzyme, in the host response to Plasmodium infection. The authors summarize experimental and clinical evidence that HO-1 is strongly induced during malaria and regulates inflammation, oxidative damage, and tissue injury. In murine models, HO-1 induction protects against severe complications including cerebral malaria and organ dysfunction by modulating pro-inflammatory cytokines and vascular permeability, whereas elevated HO-1 in certain human immune cells is associated with heightened inflammation and disease severity. They highlight HO-1's context-dependent protective and pathological functions and its potential as a diagnostic/prognostic biomarker and therapeutic target.

articles.headline: In silico screening of deep-sea metabolites against PfDHODH
articles.title_original: Structure-Based Screening of Deep-Sea Microbial Metabolites Against Plasmodium falciparum Dihydroorotate Dehydrogenase.
articles.authors: Singh A, Rengasamy KRR, Benjakul S
articles.details: This computational drug-discovery study screened 1549 deep-sea marine microbial metabolites against Plasmodium falciparum dihydroorotate dehydrogenase (PfDHODH; PDB 7KZ4) using structure-based virtual screening, MM-GBSA binding free-energy estimation, ADME/Tox profiling, and 100 ns molecular dynamics simulations. Five top compounds had Glide scores from -12.02 to -10.61 kcal/mol, better than the reference primaquine (-6.920 kcal/mol), with MM-GBSA binding energies of -63.28 to -31.37 kcal/mol. Leads included (±)-puniceusine P, aspergilol F, tersaphilone C, a diphenyl ether, and 15-O-methyl ML-236A; (±)-puniceusine P and 15-O-methyl ML-236A showed stable complexes (backbone RMSD ~0.8-1.0 Å). Authors conclude these are promising PfDHODH inhibitor candidates requiring experimental validation.

articles.headline: qPCR estimates true malaria-attributable fever fraction in Uganda
articles.title_original: Estimating malaria attributable fraction using quantitative PCR in a longitudinal cohort in Eastern Uganda.
articles.authors: Martin AC, Wang Q, Babirye S, Arinaitwe E, Zedi M, Ssewanyana I, Namirimu FN, Nayebare P, Olwoch P, Tukwasibwe S, Jagannathan P, Nankabirwa JI, Kamya M, Dorsey G, Greenhouse B, Briggs J, Rodriguez-Barraquer I
articles.details: This longitudinal cohort study used qPCR parasite-density distributions to estimate the malaria attributable fraction (MAF) of fevers among 659 individuals followed up to 3 years across three zones in eastern Uganda. P. falciparum prevalence by qPCR ranged 47-63%, with over 95% parasitemic at least once. Overall MAF was 54-64%, highest in under-fives (72%), then 5-15 year-olds (56%), and lowest in adults over 16 (45%); nearly 50% of low-to-moderate-density fevers (10-100 parasites/µL) were attributed to malaria. MAF-corrected incidence exceeded the common threshold definition (fever plus ≥5000 parasites/µL) by 18% in under-fives, 7% in 5-15 year-olds, and 70% in adults. Authors conclude density thresholds underestimate true clinical malaria incidence.

articles.headline: TGF-β–Tim-3 axis sustains macrophage defense in malaria mice
articles.title_original: TGF-β regulated Tim-3 sustains macrophage phagocytic function and confers protection in Plasmodium yoelii NSM-infected mice.
articles.authors: Xie X, Liang G, Chen M, Luo L, Yuan H, Chen S, Lu K, Peng W, Xu L, Xie H, Li L, Zhao S, Wei H, Pan X, Huang J
articles.details: Using a Plasmodium yoelii NSM murine model, in vitro co-culture, scRNA-seq, flow cytometry, and functional assays, this study investigated the immune checkpoint Tim-3 on splenic macrophages. Infection significantly downregulated macrophage Tim-3; Tim-3+ macrophages showed enhanced antigen presentation and a proinflammatory phenotype with elevated ROS and cytokines. In vivo Tim-3 blockade worsened disease severity, increased parasitemia, and impaired phagocytosis without directly affecting T cells. TGF-β was identified as an upstream regulator: its signaling was suppressed during infection, and TGF-β-induced Tim-3 upregulation potentiated phagocytosis of infected red blood cells and protected against iRBC-induced cell death. Authors conclude a protective TGF-β–Tim-3 axis maintains macrophage function, marking Tim-3 as a potential therapeutic target.

articles.headline: GIS and remote sensing map malaria vulnerability hotspots in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Mapping malaria vulnerability hotspots using multi-criteria decision analysis, GIS, and remote sensing: a case study in Abaya Woreda, West Guji Zone, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Diriba D, Demise D, Kenate B, Gobena D, Lemi M, Teferi N, Karuppannan S, Churiso G
articles.details: This geospatial study mapped malaria vulnerability in Abaya Woreda, West Guji, Ethiopia, combining GIS and remote sensing. Ten climatic, topographic, environmental, and demographic factors were derived from sources including Landsat 8, the SRTM DEM, GPS field surveys, and CHIRPS precipitation data, weighted using the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) and integrated via weighted overlay analysis in ArcGIS 10.8. Results classified 38.3% of the area as highly vulnerable, 49.1% as moderately vulnerable, and 12.6% as low vulnerability, with high vulnerability linked to lower elevations, wetlands, water bodies, greater distance from health facilities, and high population density.

articles.headline: Iron deficiency protects against Plasmodium infection in women and mice
articles.title_original: Host iron deficiency protects against Plasmodium infection and drives parasite molecular reprofiling.
articles.authors: Clucas D, Bennett C, Harding R, Pettikiriarachchi A, Baldi A, Randall LM, Steel R, Mellin R, Hobbs M, Caiazzo S, Mwangi MN, Fielding KL, Hickey PF, Baldwin TM, Amann-Zalcenstein D, Emery-Corbin SJ, Mzembe G, Moya E, Braat S, Jex A, Demir AY, Verhoef H, Phiri KS, Biggs BA, Tham WH, Boddey JA, Pasricha SR, Ataíde R
articles.details: Using clinical data from Malawian pregnant women (n=711) in the REVAMP trial, a Tmprss6-knockout mouse model, and in vitro P. falciparum cultures, researchers examined iron–malaria interactions. Iron deficiency was associated with 50% reduced P. falciparum parasitemia in pregnant women (95% CI 30–64%, P<0.0001), while iron-deficient mice showed improved survival against P. berghei (median 15.5 vs 7.0 days) and greater protection from cerebral malaria (83% vs 17% survival). Iron chelation altered the parasite transcriptome and proteome, and intravenous iron supplementation did not increase parasitemia when paired with malaria prevention. Authors conclude iron deficiency protects against Plasmodium and support WHO iron supplementation when combined with adequate malaria prevention.

articles.headline: Vascular and immune biomarkers in malaria–COVID-19 coinfection in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Elevated ANG2/ACE2 and immune responses associated with Plasmodium falciparum and SARS-CoV-2 coinfection in Cameroon.
articles.authors: Tchoupe EB, Kameni MN, Eric MB, Taya JB, Kamdem SD, Numfor Nkah L, Ama Moor V, Tepa A, Neba FR, Afum-Adjei Awuah A, Amuasi JH, Netongo PM
articles.details: This study assessed clinical, biochemical, and immunological changes across 96 participants aged 15–64 years grouped into COVID-19 (n=28), malaria (n=28), co-infection (n=16), and healthy controls (n=24), using rapid tests, microscopy, RT-PCR, spectrophotometry, and ELISA. Co-infected participants had significantly elevated ALT, AST, urea, creatinine, and erythropoietin, plus increased IFN-γ and IL-1β, while malaria alone raised IL-6, IL-4, and IL-10. ANG2 was highest in COVID-19 and co-infected groups and ACE2 was markedly elevated in COVID-19 (p<0.01). ACE2 showed strong discriminatory power for disease severity (AUC 0.77 for malaria, 0.85 for COVID-19), supporting its diagnostic and prognostic value.

articles.headline: Diagnostic-resistant hrp2/3-deleted P. falciparum spread after malaria control in Peru
articles.title_original: Malaria control and the unexpected spread of diagnostic-resistant Plasmodium falciparum in Peru.
articles.authors: Gyuricza IG, Fola AA, Simkin A, Thwai KL, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Johri P, Henry CM, Cabrera-Sosa L, Porras-Laymito G, Cheng Q, Watson OJ, Gamboa D, Valdivia HO, Parr JB
articles.details: Researchers analyzed 1,215 P. falciparum samples collected 2003–2018 in Loreto, Peru, where the PAMAFRO control program sharply reduced transmission, using molecular inversion probe sequencing across >2,000 genome-wide loci. They found reduced genetic diversity, increased clonality, and fixation of parasites with both hrp2 and hrp3 deletions (hrp2-/3-), with identity-by-descent analysis showing rapid expansion of a single dominant hrp2-/3- lineage post-PAMAFRO. Simulations indicated fixation by genetic drift alone (s=0) was unlikely, requiring a selection coefficient of s≥0.03, while the PAMAFRO bottleneck increased the likelihood of fixation by drift 4.5- to 17-fold. Authors conclude fixation reflected combined demographic change and selective advantage unrelated to RDT use.

articles.headline: Systematic review of post-transfusion malaria in non-endemic African countries
articles.title_original: Post-transfusion Malaria in Morocco and Non-endemic African Countries: A Systematic Review of Reported Cases.
articles.authors: Harandou M, Nejjari MA, Naciri M, Yahyaoui A, Hami A
articles.details: This systematic review summarized published post-transfusion malaria (PTM) cases in non-endemic African countries, searching PubMed/MEDLINE, Google Scholar, and gray literature in English and French with no date restrictions (last search 30 December 2025), appraising quality with the JBI Critical Appraisal Checklist. Twenty PTM cases were identified, but only six were described in sufficient detail for full extraction, most involving immunocompromised or medically complex recipients. P. falciparum predominated among fully described cases, though P. malariae was also documented; fever days after transfusion was the commonest presentation, with diagnosis usually by peripheral blood smear. Authors conclude PTM is rare but clinically important, urging stronger hemovigilance, clinical suspicion, and refined donor risk assessment.

articles.headline: UAV image dataset trains AI to map mosquito breeding sites
articles.title_original: High-resolution UAV dataset for identification of breeding sites for malaria and dengue vectors in rural and peri-urban Tanzania: An experimental approach with Gemma 3N E4B architecture.
articles.authors: Nyambo DG, Lyimo TM, Sauli E
articles.details: This paper presents a high-resolution drone (UAV) imagery dataset collected from Mtwara and Tabora regions of Tanzania to develop surveillance models for potential mosquito breeding sites of malaria and dengue vectors. From 585 images collected in Igunga and Masasi Districts, 471 (80.5%) were retained and labeled across 19 classes of breeding sites. The dataset was used to train and validate the instruction-tuned multimodal Gemma 3N E4B model, which converged with a stabilization loss of 0.0319 and a 96.7% loss reduction (from 9.620 to 0.319) within 117 steps. The authors note that in Tanzania, malaria prevalence reaches up to 24% in under-5 children in rural and peri-urban settings.

articles.headline: Review: docking and machine learning for antimalarial drug discovery
articles.title_original: Gene Expression, Docking and Machine Learning in Malaria Drug Discovery: A Systematic Review.
articles.authors: Dangana RS, Ebhohimen IE, Malgwi SA, Ugbaja SC, Okpeku M
articles.details: This PRISMA-guided systematic review integrated 64 studies (2014–2024) on gene expression profiling, molecular docking, and machine learning for Plasmodium drug discovery, focusing on herbal compounds. Molecular docking was the dominant technique (32.37%), followed by in vitro antiplasmodial assays (14.39%), ADMET profiling (10.79%), and gene expression studies (3.60%). Isorhamnetin and myricetin 3-O-glucoside showed high binding affinity to Plasmepsin II and P. falciparum lactate dehydrogenase (PfLDH) (ΔG < -13 kcal/mol), while random forest and SVM models reached AUC up to 0.87 for bioactivity and resistance prediction, highlighting flavonoids (quercetin) and terpenoids (eugenol). The authors conclude that multiomics, docking, and ML integration improves target identification but that gaps in in vivo data and methodological inconsistency limit clinical translation.

articles.headline: Tanzanian communities link climate change to higher malaria risk
articles.title_original: Community perceptions on climate change and its impacts on malaria transmission in South-eastern Tanzania.
articles.authors: Nshatsi NC, Mponzi WP, Mwalugelo YA, Msaky DS, Simbeye S, Rite E, Okumu FO, Philbert A, Angelo T, Kaindoa EW
articles.details: This explanatory mixed-methods cross-sectional study explored community knowledge, perceptions, and practices on climate variability and malaria in malaria-endemic villages of south-eastern Tanzania. Data came from structured questionnaires with 384 community members, 11 key informant interviews, and 12 focus group discussions involving 72 participants. Among respondents, 86% reported experiencing climate-related changes, 67.5% recognized a link between climate change and malaria, 59.5% perceived increased vector-borne disease risk, and 70% reported higher malaria occurrence during the rainy season; only 26.6% regularly received climate and health updates despite 96.6% wanting them. The authors conclude that integrating community perspectives is important for locally relevant climate-adaptation and malaria-control strategies.

articles.headline: Health workers describe barriers to severe malaria guideline adherence
articles.title_original: Facilitators and barriers to adherence to severe malaria treatment guidelines in children at Mbale Regional Referral Hospital, Uganda: a health workers' perspective.
articles.authors: Chelangat SC, Nakawuki A, Orech S, Oboth P, Nekaka R, Iramiot JS, Mbwali I, Chalo RN, Epuitai J, Adongo PR, Ssenyonga LVN, Olowo S
articles.details: This qualitative descriptive study explored facilitators and barriers to healthcare providers' adherence to severe malaria treatment guidelines for children at Mbale Regional Referral Hospital, eastern Uganda. A total of 21 healthcare providers (doctors, nurses, pharmacists, laboratory technicians, blood bank workers, and clinical officers) were purposively recruited, with data collected through key informant interviews and analyzed thematically using the Donabedian model. Facilitators fell under structure (resources, drugs, training), process (manageable workloads, monitoring tools), and patient-related factors (caretaker cooperation, trust). Barriers included resource and staffing constraints, delayed referrals, lab and pharmacy delays, low CME attendance among nurses, and patient financial limitations. The authors conclude that addressing resource shortages, staffing, and patient education is crucial for better adherence.

articles.headline: Human-biting in Anopheles vectors evolved with early hominins in SE Asia
articles.title_original: Early hominin arrival in Southeast Asia triggered the evolution of major human malaria vectors.
articles.authors: Singh US, Harbach RE, Hii J, Chang MS, Somboon P, Prakash A, Sarma D, Broomfield BS, Morgan K, Albert S, Das A, Linton YM, Carlton JM, Walton C
articles.details: This study used phylogenomic analysis to reconstruct the evolutionary history of the Leucosphyrus Group of Anopheles mosquitoes in Southeast Asia, some highly anthropophilic vectors of human malaria and others feeding on non-human primates. Analysis drew on 2,657 high-confidence nuclear single-copy orthologous genes and 13 mitochondrial protein-coding genes from 40 individuals across 11 species. Molecular dating and ancestral state reconstruction indicated monkey-feeding is ancestral, with anthropophily most likely evolving once via adaptive introgression in the early Pleistocene in Sundaland. The authors conclude this origin coincided with the arrival of early hominins (Homo erectus) rather than modern humans, providing independent non-archaeological evidence for hominin colonization of Southeast Asia around 1.8 Mya.

articles.headline: Plasmodium kinase ARK1 drives spindle formation in atypical mitosis
articles.title_original: Plasmodium ARK1 regulates spindle formation during atypical mitosis and forms a divergent chromosomal passenger complex.
articles.authors: Nagar A, Yanase R, Zeeshan M, Ferguson DJP, Abel S, Pashley SL, Mishra A, Eze A, Rea E, Brady D, Bottrill AR, Vaughan S, Le Roch KG, Guttery DS, Holder AA, Tromer EC, Sharma P, Tewari R
articles.details: Laboratory study in Plasmodium identifying Aurora-related kinase 1 (ARK1) as a key component of inner microtubule organising centre and spindle formation, controlling kinetochore dynamics and mitotic progression during schizogony and male gametogony. Conditional ARK1 depletion disrupted spindle biogenesis, kinetochore segregation, karyokinesis and cytokinesis in both stages and affected parasite transmission. Interactome analysis showed ARK1 forms the catalytic core of a non-canonical chromosomal passenger complex with two divergent inner centromere proteins (INCENP-A, INCENP-B), lacking Survivin and Borealin. Authors propose the ARK1-INCENP interface as a potential multistage antimalarial target.

articles.headline: Modelling: pairing seasonal chemoprevention with multi-stage therapeutics improves protection
articles.title_original: Combining seasonal malaria chemoprevention with novel therapeutics for malaria prevention: a mathematical modelling study.
articles.authors: Braunack-Mayer L, Malinga J, Nekkab N, Kelly SL, Möhrle JJ, Penny MA
articles.details: Individual-based malaria transmission modelling study estimating the health impact of combining new P. falciparum therapeutics (vaccines, monoclonal antibodies, long-acting injectables) with seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) in children under five. For three SMC cycles in a high-transmission setting, a pre-liver stage therapeutic with >50% initial efficacy required a protection half-life >230 days to reduce cumulative severe cases by >23% during interventions and >5% five years after deployment stopped; longer protection was needed with four or five cycles. Combining SMC with multi-stage therapeutics increased impact, and authors conclude such candidates should be prioritised for development.

articles.headline: PfATP4 inhibitor compound 15 shows transmission-blocking and mouse efficacy
articles.title_original: Identification of Compound 15 (MMV1581361) as a PfATP4 Inhibitor with Transmission-Blocking Activity and In Vivo Efficacy in a SCID Mouse Model of Malaria.
articles.authors: Onyame HA, Brand S, Baud D, Basarab GS, Hawryluk N, Canan S, Togoh MD, Abdulai R, Eduku R, Awal M, Owoare RB, Chatterjee B, Singha Roy D, Mahapatra AKR, Bhowmik S, Putatunda P, Crespo B, Sanz L, Calvo D, Wittlin S, Tumwebaze PK, Borrows J, Amewu RK
articles.details: Structure-activity-relationship study identifying compound 15 (MMV1581361), derived from MMV020136, as a PfATP4 inhibitor against P. falciparum. It retained nanomolar activity against drug-sensitive, drug-resistant and field blood-stage isolates, showed a moderate rate-of-kill similar to pyrimethamine, and was efficacious in the humanized Pf3D70087/N9 NODscidIL2Rγnull mouse model at a single 25 mg/kg dose. It achieved 90% inhibition of male gamete exflagellation, a 98% reduction in mean oocyst intensity and an 88% transmission block, acting by disrupting Na+ homeostasis. Further development was deprioritised due to resistance risk and more advanced clinical candidates.

articles.headline: Corrigendum: P. falciparum Raf kinase inhibitor regulating PfCDPK1
articles.title_original: Corrigendum to "Plasmodium falciparum Raf kinase inhibitor is a lipid binding protein that interacts with and regulates the activity of PfCDPK1, an essential plant-like kinase required for red blood cell invasion" [Biochem. Biophys. Res. Commun. 749 (2025) 151350].
articles.authors: Sharma M, Krishnan D, Singh A, Negi P, Rani K, Revikumar A, Munde M, Bansal A
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Microscopy and nested PCR reveal malaria misdiagnosis in Ethiopian children
articles.title_original: Unmasking Malaria: Microscopy and nPCR Reveal the Hidden Misdiagnosis of Plasmodium Infections Among Febrile Pediatric in Northwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Getie S, Mekonnen GG, Worku L, Birhanie M, Meda AL, Abere A, Noedl H
articles.details: Institution-based cross-sectional study of 302 malaria-suspected febrile pediatric participants in Northwest Ethiopia assessing Plasmodium prevalence by microscopy and nested PCR. Malaria prevalence was 22.2% by microscopy and 18.2% by nPCR; among nPCR-positive cases, 19.2% were male and 17.3% female, with the highest prevalence (25.6%) in those aged ≥5 years. Age, not using bed nets, and awareness of malaria signs and symptoms were significantly associated with infection (p<0.05). Authors call for improved routine microscopy skills, quality control and adherence to standardized operating procedures.

articles.headline: Atlantic Forest malaria vector Anopheles cruzii is five cryptic species
articles.title_original: Anopheles (Kerteszia) cruzii, the main malaria vector in the Brazilian Atlantic Forest, is a complex of at least five cryptic species.
articles.authors: Voges K, Dias GR, Dupim EG, Pitaluga AN, Vanderlinde T, Carvalho Pinto CJ, Rezende HR, Uno F, Forrester SJ, Chong J, Carvalho AB, Rona LDP
articles.details: This genomic study sequenced 55 mosquito genomes (35 Anopheles cruzii s.l., 12 An. bellator s.l., and 8 An. homunculus; four via Nanopore and the rest via Illumina) to test for cryptic speciation in vectors of human and simian malaria in the Brazilian Atlantic Forest. Phylogenomic analysis revealed at least five cryptic species within An. cruzii (labelled A-E, with sympatry in some locations) and at least three lineages within An. bellator, all showing high genetic differentiation (FST 0.4-0.7) typical of distinct species. In contrast, An. homunculus showed low differentiation (FST ~0.2), consistent with a single widespread species. The authors note vector competence may differ among these cryptic species.

articles.headline: Host kinases regulate P. vivax dormant and replicating liver stages
articles.title_original: Host kinase regulation of Plasmodium vivax dormant and replicating liver stages.
articles.authors: Glennon EKK, Wei L, Roobsoong W, Primavera VI, van Zyl EM, Tongogara T, Yee CB, Sattabongkot J, Kaushansky A
articles.details: Using an approach called kinase regression on multiple P. vivax patient isolates, researchers assessed how host kinase activity influences liver-stage parasites, which form either replicating schizonts or dormant hypnozoites that reactivate weeks to months later. They identified a subset of host kinases that regulate the numbers of schizonts and hypnozoites as well as schizont size, and characterized both overlap and variability in host phosphosignaling dependencies between the two parasite forms. The data point to variability in host dependencies across isolates, which the authors suggest may be one origin of the field-observed heterogeneity in P. vivax relapse timing and frequency.

articles.headline: Bradykinin drives brain swelling in experimental cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Bradykinin Contributes to Vasogenic Edema in Murine Experimental Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Pinheiro AS, Teixeira DE, Silva-Aguiar RP, Shim YJ, Merkulova AA, Silbak S, Skomorovska-Prokvolit Y, Midem D, Ogolla S, Burckhardt BB, Gangnus T, Scharfstein J, Caruso-Neves C, McCarty OJT, Gailani D, Bader M, Rosenthal PJ, Dent AE, Janse CJ, McCrae KR, Pinheiro AAS, Kazura JW, Schmaier AH
articles.details: This study examined the role of bradykinin (BK)-induced vasogenic edema in cerebral malaria using Kenyan children with central nervous system malaria and C57BL/6J mice infected with P. berghei ANKA. Cleaved plasma high molecular weight kininogen (cHK), a marker of prior BK release, was present in 40% of children with central nervous system malaria versus 18% with uncomplicated malaria, and infected wild-type mice showed circulating cHK, elevated BK, and reduced HK and prekallikrein. Mice lacking kininogen (Kng1-/-) or BK B1/B2 receptors were protected from neurologic deterioration and brain edema, whereas mice lacking prekallikrein (Klkb1-/-) or prolylcarboxypeptidase (Prcp hypomorphs and brain endothelial conditional knockouts) had reduced neurologic deterioration and brain edema, and F12-/- mice were not protected. Adjuvant plasma kallikrein inhibition combined with artesunate reversed neurologic deterioration and brain edema and prolonged survival versus artesunate alone, supporting a contribution of BK-induced vasogenic edema to human and murine cerebral malaria.

articles.headline: Live-cell fluorescent assay measures P. falciparum proteasome activity
articles.title_original: Profiling 26S Proteasome Activity of Plasmodium falciparum Monitored by a Live-Cell Assay.
articles.authors: Gonçalves AF, Lima-Pinheiro A, Sampaio-Marques B, Ferreira PE
articles.details: Researchers developed a live-cell tool to monitor 26S proteasome function in P. falciparum, a drug target that synergizes with artemisinin (which damages parasite proteins, promoting their ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation). They generated a parasite line expressing GFP fused to a destabilization domain (GFP-DD) combined with MitoTracker staining; without the stabilizing ligand Shield-1, the reporter is rapidly degraded, so fluorescence provides a quantitative inverse readout of proteasome activity. Using microscopy and flow cytometry, fluorescence increased upon Shield-1 stabilization or pharmacological inhibition with MG132 (dose-dependent), and Shield-1 titration identified an optimal stabilization range. The authors present this as a practical platform for phenotypic screening and antimalarial resistance studies.

articles.headline: Scoping review protocol on urban Bamako malaria trends
articles.title_original: Trends in malaria indicators in urban and peri-urban areas of Bamako: a scoping review protocol.
articles.authors: Toure M, Keita S, Keita B, Arou AZ, Diallo I, Keita M, Kane F, Sanogo D, Diallo M, Tangara CO, Kone A, Magassa MH, Sogoba N, Diakite M, Doumbia S
articles.details: This is a protocol for a scoping review (not yet conducted) aiming to describe trends in malaria epidemiology in urban and peri-urban Bamako from 2002 to 2025, focusing on prevalence, incidence, clinical forms, mortality/morbidity, transmission, and burden, to inform control strategies adapted to rapid urbanization and emerging threats such as Anopheles stephensi. The planned methods include searching PubMed, Web of Science, ADHL Mali Home, and Google Scholar, plus National Malaria Control Program reports and Bamako academic theses, with data extracted and synthesized on malaria trends and clinical manifestations. As a protocol, it reports no epidemiological results. The review is registered at osf.io/q8upv.

articles.headline: Falciparum dominates but non-falciparum malaria common in West Cameroon
articles.title_original: Distribution of falciparum and non-falciparum malaria among symptomatic malaria patients in Dschang, West Region of Cameroon.
articles.authors: Tchuenkam PVK, White SJ, Potlapalli V, Keming EM, Muller MS, Kaunda DBN, Kharabora O, Dinglasan RR, Parr JB, Tume CB, Lin JT, Juliano JJ, Ali IM
articles.details: Cross-sectional facility-based study of 431 clinically suspected malaria cases from three health centres in Dschang, West Region of Cameroon (2020), using real-time PCR of 18S rRNA to detect P. falciparum, P. ovale, P. malariae, and P. vivax. Overall malaria prevalence was 54.8% (236/431): 53.4% P. falciparum, 4% P. ovale, and 0.9% P. malariae, with no P. vivax detected; 8.9% of infections were mixed-species. Of 12 genotyped P. ovale infections, 6 were P. ovale curtisi, 3 P. ovale wallikeri, and 3 a mixture. Authors conclude non-falciparum species, including both P. ovale subspecies, circulate and warrant continued monitoring.

articles.headline: Eight or more antenatal visits raise malaria-prevention uptake in Ghana
articles.title_original: Impact of eight or more antenatal care visits on intermittent preventive treatment of malaria uptake during pregnancy and facility-based delivery in Ghana: a propensity score matched analysis.
articles.authors: Agbi DM, Mwebesa E, Jimmy AI, Valère SK, Kerich GK
articles.details: Analysis of the 2022 Ghana Demographic and Health Survey using propensity score matching to estimate the effect of eight or more antenatal care visits (ANC8+) on facility-based delivery and uptake of at least three IPTp-SP doses among women who had given birth within the preceding three years. Only 38.7% met the ANC8+ threshold; overall 85.4% delivered in a facility and 66.5% received three or more IPTp-SP doses. After matching, ANC8+ increased facility delivery by 8.2 percentage points (ATT=8.2, 95% CI 4.6–11.9) and optimal IPTp-SP uptake by 11.1 points (ATT=11.1, 95% CI 6.8–15.4). Authors conclude adherence to WHO ANC8+ significantly improves malaria prevention in pregnancy.

articles.headline: Malaria parasite RNA enters host immune cell nuclei to hijack splicing
articles.title_original: Nuclear import of malaria RNA rewires splicing in host immune cells.
articles.authors: Abou Karam P, Kiper E, Ziv T, Yadid S, Kozela E, Zharoni N, Nevo R, Alfandari D, Otesh H, Cruz Camacho A, Lubelsky Y, Rotkopf R, Weizman E, Cossin M, Rosenhek-Goldian I, Petrovich-Kopitman E, Porat Z, Shoshani O, Ulitsky I, Levy C, Melamed Z, Regev-Rudzki N
articles.details: Mechanistic study describing how P. falciparum manipulates host immunity via an RNA-based strategy. Parasite-encoded mRNAs of the early transcribed membrane protein (ETRAMP) family, exported from infected red blood cells, evade cytoplasmic degradation in host immune cells and are imported into their nuclei, where they bind the host RNA-binding proteins ACIN1 and PNN, components of the splicing machinery associated with the exon junction complex. This interaction disrupts host splicing regulation, causing widespread transcript misprocessing and altered expression of immune-function proteins. No quantitative outcomes are reported; the authors conclude pathogen-derived transcripts exploit host splicing to reshape immune signaling.

articles.headline: Review of diagnostic methods for malaria, leishmaniasis, and Chagas disease
articles.title_original: Diagnostic methods for protozoan diseases: a review focused on leishmaniasis, Chagas disease and malaria.
articles.authors: Matos ADS, Rodrigues-da-Silva RN, Toledo TS, Ataides LS, da Motta ND, Rodolphi CM, Soares IF, Ribeiro FN, Alencar ALC, Maia FM, Lima-Junior JDC, Morgado FN
articles.details: Narrative review of the epidemiological landscape and diagnostic tools for three protozoan diseases—malaria, leishmaniasis, and Chagas disease—with a focus on Brazil, where diverse ecological settings and social inequality drive high prevalence. The review summarizes established immunological and molecular diagnostic approaches and the fundamental concepts for evaluating them, emphasizing integration of clinical and epidemiological information with sensitive, specific techniques. No primary quantitative results are presented; the authors highlight diagnosis as a strategic public-health tool for early detection, monitoring, and control of infections in endemic areas.

articles.headline: Malnourished children on SMC face higher malaria risk in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Malaria and malnutrition in children under the Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention (SMC) coverage in the health district of Nanoro, Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Compaore EW, Sondo P, Kabore B, Rouamba T, Kouevi AFC, Bayala IMC, Kambou SAE, Millogo KS, Bouda I, Ouedraogo DF, Hien SF, Kazienga A, Derra K, Rouamba E, Gneme A, Tinto H
articles.details: Longitudinal survey of children aged 6–59 months receiving seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) in the Nanoro health district, Burkina Faso (2020–2022), comparing malnourished children with those of adequate nutritional status (ANS); malaria was diagnosed by rapid diagnostic test and microscopy and modeled with a negative binomial model. Of 425 children, 260 were malnourished and 165 had ANS. Malaria incidence per 1000 child-months was higher in undernutrition (974.47) than ANS (214.47), and malnourished children were 1.41 times more likely to have a clinical malaria episode than children with ANS (IRR 1.42; 95% CI 1.03–1.94; p=0.028). The authors suggest combining SMC with nutrient supplementation in food-insecure areas.

articles.headline: Self-reported malaria affects 44.5% of Chinese workers in Uganda
articles.title_original: Epidemiological characteristics, prevention and control status, and factors associated with malaria infection among overseas Chinese workforce in Uganda.
articles.authors: Ji J, Gao X, Lei L, Yang T, Zhou A, Wang Y, Chen L, Cui Q, Zhu Y
articles.details: This cross-sectional study used an online questionnaire completed by 798 overseas Chinese workforce members residing across 32 districts in Uganda to identify factors associated with malaria infection. Malaria was defined as self-reported history during the stay, and 44.5% (355) reported prior infection. Multivariable logistic regression found increased odds for being male (AOR=1.92, 95% CI 1.27–2.91), negative prevention attitudes (AOR=2.49, 95% CI 1.41–4.40), higher local case presence (AOR=1.95, 95% CI 1.51–2.51), and longer residence (AOR=1.38). Bachelor's degree or higher (AOR=0.65) and living with a spouse (AOR=0.57) were protective. Authors call for an integrated control strategy.

articles.headline: Fatal thrombotic DIC in severe falciparum malaria after Angola travel
articles.title_original: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation With a Predominant Thrombotic Phenotype in Severe Plasmodium falciparum Malaria: A Case Report.
articles.authors: Gisca E, Santos MG, Costa S, Santos CR, Araújo A
articles.details: This case report describes a 61-year-old man with recent travel to Angola who presented with severe Plasmodium falciparum malaria complicated by disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) with a predominant thrombotic phenotype. He had profound thrombocytopenia, markedly elevated D-dimer, metabolic acidosis, shock, multiorgan dysfunction, and parasitemia exceeding 25%. Despite intravenous artesunate, anticoagulation, and ICU organ support, he developed progressive peripheral ischemia requiring right above-knee amputation; parasitemia declined but he died after nosocomial infections. The authors highlight this underrecognised thrombotic-predominant presentation and the challenges of managing severe imported malaria.

articles.headline: Scoping review finds scant research on malaria control during COVID-19
articles.title_original: Implementation of malaria control programmes during the COVID-19 pandemic in the Southern African Development Community Elimination 8 countries: A scoping review.
articles.authors: Muzamhindo DN, Chironda G, Tsoka-Gwegweni JM
articles.journal: Afr J Prim Health Care Fam Med
articles.details: This review, conducted using Joanna Briggs Institute methodology with a population-concept-context (PCC) framework, mapped and synthesised evidence on malaria control programmes during the COVID-19 pandemic in the Southern African Development Community Malaria Elimination 8 (E8) countries. Of 658 articles retrieved, only 7 met the inclusion criteria; half were published in 2021 and none in 2020. The authors conclude there is limited research on malaria programmes during the pandemic in E8 countries and emphasize the need for further research and policies that sustain malaria programmes during future pandemics.

articles.headline: Parasite genetic metrics track malaria prevalence across Uganda
articles.title_original: Accuracy of Plasmodium falciparum genetic data for estimating parasite prevalence and malaria incidence in Uganda.
articles.authors: Kiyaga S, Mbabazi M, Katairo T, Kabbale KD, Asua V, Nsengimaana B, Wiringilimaana I, Semakuba FD, Mwubaha C, Nakasaanya J, Watyekele E, Ayitewala A, Tukwasibwe S, Mulondo J, Nsobya SL, Agaba B, Maiteki-Sebuguzi C, Kamya MR, Kateete DP, Nabende JN, Jjingo D, Mboowa G, Batte C, Ssewanyana I, Aranda-Díaz A, Dorsey G, Rosenthal PJ, Conrad M, Greenhouse B, Briggs J
articles.details: This study assessed whether Plasmodium falciparum genetic metrics can estimate transmission intensity, using dried blood spots from 3,563 symptomatic malaria cases at 26 sentinel health facilities across Uganda during two 2023 collections. Amplicon deep sequencing of 165 polyallelic microhaplotypes was performed with MAD4HatTeR to derive within-host diversity and relatedness metrics. Complexity of infection (COI) and effective COI showed strong positive associations with prevalence (pooled cross-validated r=0.79), while between-host relatedness best predicted incidence; incidence models performed weaker (pooled r=0.37). Authors conclude microhaplotype metrics reliably capture spatial prevalence variation but have more limited ability to predict incidence.

articles.headline: Cross-border movement minor in malaria cases in Mudzi, Zimbabwe
articles.title_original: Role of cross-border movement on the importation of malaria cases in Mudzi district, Zimbabwe.
articles.authors: Fambirai T, Chimbari M
articles.details: This study used a newly developed tool to capture cross-border movement history among malaria-positive RDT cases at five health facilities and the community level in Mudzi district, a border district in Zimbabwe with persistent transmission despite indoor residual spraying. Of 619 recorded malaria cases, 53.47% were female and only 101/619 (16.32%) reported cross-border movement, with 96% of those aged 10–49 years and the highest proportions at facilities near the border. Health facilities provided most treatment (522/619, 84.33%), and only 49/619 (7.92%) were Mozambique residents.

articles.headline: Correction notice for blood-purification rescue of severe falciparum malaria case
articles.title_original: Correction: The blood purification therapy-based strategy effectively rescued the severe falciparum malaria patient experiencing cytokine storm-driven MODS.
articles.authors: Sun Y, Huang X, Hong B, Song J, Lu Y, Ma J, Wang X, Hu W, Zhang Y, Xuan H
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: High SP-resistance dhfr/dhps mutations in P. falciparum, Western Kenya
articles.title_original: Persistent and Circulating Plasmodium falciparum dhfr and dhps Mutations in Busia County, Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Ndung'u L, Thiong'o K, Karani L, Gitahi S, Kimani F, Ngugi MP, Kiboi D
articles.details: A molecular surveillance study analyzed 66 P. falciparum isolates from patients at Matayos Sub-County Hospital in Busia County, Kenya (November 2024–January 2025), using PCR and Sanger sequencing of dhfr and dhps codons linked to sulphadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP) resistance relevant to IPTp. Common dhfr substitutions were N51I (85.2%) and C59R (75.4%), with S108N (32.8%), S108T (19.7%), and I164L (18.0%, found exclusively on a non-canonical S108T background). Dhps was dominated by A437G (92.3%) and K540E (40%). Classical triple-mutant and quintuple haplotypes circulated, and authors call for continued molecular and phenotypic surveillance to inform IPT policy.

articles.headline: Low temperature reshapes chromatin in P. falciparum ring stage
articles.title_original: Integration of ATAC-seq and RNA-seq reveals temperature-responsive regulatory regions in Plasmodium falciparum asexual stages.
articles.authors: Zhang Y, Zhao Y, Zheng G, He S, Xiao J, Ma H, Huang J, Qi Y
articles.details: Researchers integrated ATAC-seq and RNA-seq to map chromatin accessibility and gene expression in synchronized P. falciparum ring (45 h post-invasion) and trophozoite (70 h) stages at human (37 °C) versus mosquito-mimicking (26 °C) temperatures, with qPCR validation. Low temperature induced 1083 differentially accessible regions in the ring stage (1081 gains, 2 losses), enriched in promoters, while trophozoites showed no significant changes. DAR-associated genes were enriched in host cell membrane interactions and antigenic variation; 41 genes showed concordant accessibility and expression changes. Authors conclude temperature selectively remodels ring-stage chromatin, suggesting targets for chromatin-directed antimalarial strategies.

articles.headline: Silent submicroscopic P. vivax case found before Argentina malaria-free status
articles.title_original: Asymptomatic and submicroscopic Plasmodium vivax infection: a study previous to malaria-free certification in Argentina.
articles.authors: Dantur-Juri MJ, Coello-Peralta RD, Calle-Atariguana DE, Arevalo-Bozada MM, Duque PL, González-Piñeres N, Rougeron V, Degrugillier F, Dentice-Maidana S, Liria-Salazar J, Zaidenberg MO
articles.details: This molecular surveillance study analyzed human blood samples from Argentina's border regions, including 92 samples preserved on Whatman FTA paper in 2022 (post-2019 WHO malaria-free certification), by amplifying and sequencing a fragment of the Plasmodium cytochrome b gene. A submicroscopic, asymptomatic P. vivax infection was identified in a 66-year-old individual from Salvador Mazza, Salta province (northwestern Argentina) with no travel history to endemic regions. The authors report this as the first molecularly confirmed submicroscopic P. vivax infection prior to certification in Argentina and the Southern Cone, urging strengthened surveillance and sensitive diagnostics to detect silent circulation.

articles.headline: Malaria knowledge-practice gap among nomadic Fulani pregnant women, Ghana
articles.title_original: Knowledge of malaria and preventive practices among Fulani pregnant women in the savannah Region of Northern Ghana.
articles.authors: Salifu Y, Nantomah B, Lasong J
articles.details: A community-based cross-sectional study (April–June 2022) assessed malaria knowledge and preventive practices among 159 nomadic Fulani pregnant women in the Savannah Region of Northern Ghana, using census and snowball sampling with logistic regression in SPSS. Overall, 61.0% had good malaria knowledge but 71.1% showed poor preventive practices; 54.7% owned at least one insecticide-treated net (ITN), yet only 28.9% had slept under one the previous night. Antenatal care attendance was associated with knowledge (AOR=0.31, 95% CI 0.12–0.83, p=0.020) and number of children with preventive practice (AOR=0.29, 95% CI 0.10–0.90, p=0.032). Authors recommend ANC-based education and culturally tailored outreach.

articles.headline: Climate-based model predicts Anopheles peaks before Zanzibar malaria surge
articles.title_original: Spatio-temporal modelling and prediction of Anopheles mosquito abundance in Tanga and Unguja, Tanzania: climatic drivers and insights for malaria early warning and vector control strategies.
articles.authors: Njotto LL, Kulaya NB, Derua YA, Malongo BB, Francis F, Schiøler KL, Hansson H, Wang CW, Saleh F, Baraka V, Alifrangis M, van der Deure T, Senyoni W, Cronie O, Stensgaard AS
articles.details: This study used spatio-temporal Generalized Additive Mixed Effects Models (GAMMs) to link meteorological factors to adult Anopheles abundance, sampling monthly across 11 sites in Tanga and 4 shehias in Unguja, Tanzania, collecting 4,312 mosquitoes in Tanga and 1,450 in Unguja. Drivers were region-specific: in Tanga abundance rose with higher maximum temperatures but fell with higher minimum temperatures, while in Unguja it peaked below 27.5 °C; precipitation (including a two-month lag) and higher humidity increased abundance. Models showed strong accuracy (Theil's U < 1, correlation > 0.8) and forecast an Unguja abundance peak in November 2023, preceding the late-2023/early-2024 Zanzibar malaria surge, supporting use as an early warning system.

articles.headline: Mosquito xenomonitoring in South Ethiopia finds Plasmodium DNA, goat-dominant blood meals
articles.title_original: Molecular xenomonitoring for identifying Plasmodium parasites and blood meal sources of mosquitoes in malaria endemic villages adjacent to the southern Rift Valley Lakes, South Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Eligo N, Woldeyes D, Tamiru G, Jima B, Wudneh F, Wegayehu T, Wondale B, Lindtjørn B, Massebo F
articles.details: A cross-sectional entomological survey across 12 malaria-endemic Kebeles near Lakes Abaya and Chamo in South Ethiopia used CDC light traps in 416 houses, serving as baseline for a trial of house screening and/or animal ivermectin. PCR on abdomens of fed Anopheles detected Plasmodium vivax in 2.0% (9/446) and P. falciparum in 0.2% (1/446). Of 446 specimens, 85% (378/446) had fed on vertebrate hosts: goat blood was most common at 67% (299/446), followed by human 23% (102/446), cow 19% (84/446), and dog 13% (57/446); the human blood meal index was 23%. Anopheles arabiensis dominated at 85% (380/446). Authors suggest interventions may prioritise animal blood sources.

articles.headline: Rainfall, not temperature, linked to under-5 malaria risk in Mali
articles.title_original: Associations between precipitation, temperature, and malaria prevalence in children under 5 in Mali.
articles.authors: Hayden NJT, Syer J, Yanow SK, Yamamoto SS
articles.details: This exploratory cross-sectional study pooled Mali Demographic and Health Surveys and Malaria Indicator Surveys from 2012/13, 2015, 2018, and 2021, examining RDT-diagnosed malaria in children aged 6–59 months against precipitation and temperature with one-, two-, and three-month lags using multilevel modelling. At a three-month lag, precipitation was significantly positively associated with malaria odds (OR 1.005, 95% CI 1.002–1.009), while minimum (0.761), maximum (0.871), and average (0.822) temperature were significantly negatively associated; maximum temperature was also negative at two months (0.863) and minimum temperature positive at one month (1.110). Authors conclude precipitation may be a risk factor, while temperature alone is likely not a key driver.

articles.headline: Plasmodium Complex III has essential second role in protein processing
articles.title_original: Dual function of mitochondrial complex III in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Rell RS, Shukla A, Morrisey JM, Okoye IC, Mather MW, Vaidya AB
articles.details: Building on the role of the mitochondrial electron transport chain (mtETC) in regenerating ubiquinone for pyrimidine biosynthesis in blood-stage Plasmodium falciparum, this laboratory study tested whether Complex III also processes imported mitochondrial proteins. Using transgenic parasites expressing yeast DHOD (which bypasses mtETC) and conditional knockdown of mitochondrial processing peptidase alpha (MPPα, a Complex III component), the authors found MPPα remained essential even when electron transport was bypassed. MPPα knockdown caused hypersensitivity to proguanil, and pulldown proteomics showed MPPα associated with multiple mitochondrially targeted proteins plus all Complex III components, indicating Complex III serves dual roles in mtETC function and protein processing.

articles.headline: Genomics shows most P. falciparum cases in Malaysia are imported
articles.title_original: Whole genome sequencing to inform the epidemiology of Plasmodium falciparum malaria in the elimination setting of Malaysia.
articles.authors: Tan MKI, Billows N, Divis PCS, Daneshvar C, Edgeworth J, Singh JC, Campino S, Clark TG
articles.details: To support malaria elimination in Sarawak, Malaysia, researchers applied nanopore sequencing and population genomic analyses to 21 Plasmodium falciparum isolates from archived whole blood samples collected 2008–2010, comparing them with 9,518 publicly available isolates from Central Africa, East Africa, Horn of Africa, Oceania, South America, South Asia, Southeast Asia, and West Africa. Most cases (13/15) likely originated outside Malaysia, supported by patient travel histories, high multiplicity of infection, and drug-resistance profiles consistent with the suspected source regions; two cases showed genomic origins inconsistent with reported travel. Identity-by-descent analysis revealed clustering in only two cases, indicating isolated introductions rather than sustained local transmission, underscoring the value of genomics in distinguishing imported from locally acquired infections.

articles.headline: Transmembrane protein BTP2 drives daughter cell separation in malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Transmembrane proteins mediate basal complex assembly and individual daughter cell formation in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Back PS, Wagner E, Llopis PM, de Oliveira RG, Mottinelli M, Ferrins L, Dvorin JD
articles.details: Using expansion microscopy and DNA-PAINT nanoscale imaging, researchers studied three transmembrane basal complex proteins in Plasmodium falciparum: BTP1, BTP2, and basolateral expansion boundary (BLEB). Parasites lacking BTP2 remained enveloped by membranes but failed to separate from each other, producing multiorganellar mutants, with the defect localized to a specific step of basal complex development while contractile ability stayed intact. They identified BLEB as a distinct plasma membrane region excluded from daughter cells. The authors propose a three-step model for basal complex biogenesis, highlighting transmembrane proteins' role in simultaneous formation of multiple daughter cells.

articles.headline: Review of carbamate insecticide resistance mechanisms in Anopheles gambiae
articles.title_original: Mechanisms of carbamate resistance in the malaria vector: Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Cleanclay WD, Akanni MH, Bajepade TI, Ajeoge JK, Zakari S
articles.details: This review synthesizes existing knowledge on the molecular and metabolic pathways of carbamate resistance in the female Anopheles gambiae, the primary African malaria vector. Carbamates act by reversibly inhibiting acetylcholinesterase, causing acetylcholine buildup, paralysis, and mosquito death, and are recommended by the WHO for alternative or rotational use in pyrethroid-resistant areas. The authors describe two main resistance mechanisms: target-site mutations in the acetylcholinesterase gene and increased detoxification of carbamates by enzymes including esterases and cytochrome P450s. They emphasize that understanding these mechanisms is crucial for sustaining IRS effectiveness and guiding resistance management.

articles.headline: PCR outperforms RDTs and microscopy for malaria diagnosis in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Diagnostic challenges in malaria detection: A comparative diagnostic performance of HRP2-based rapid diagnostic tests, microscopy, and PCR at Bichena primary hospital, Northwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Minwuyelet A, Yewhalaw D, Atenafu G
articles.details: This facility-based cross-sectional study of 274 participants at Bichena Primary Hospital, Northwest Ethiopia, compared HRP2-based RDTs, microscopy, and nested PCR. Plasmodium prevalence was 23.4% by PCR, 20.1% by microscopy, and 19% by HRP2 RDT. The RDT showed sensitivity 79.1%, NPV 94.1%, and accuracy 94.9%, while microscopy had 100% specificity and PPV but 85.9% sensitivity. Agreement was κ=0.904 between PCR and microscopy and κ=0.847 between PCR and RDTs, and PCR-confirmed cases had significantly lower platelet counts (P<0.05). The authors conclude RDTs and microscopy are less sensitive than PCR, possibly due to low parasitemia and pfhrp2 deletions warranting further investigation.

articles.headline: Qualitative study of perceived drivers of malaria resurgence in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Understanding malaria resurgence in the Amhara Region, northwestern Ethiopia: a qualitative study of perceived drivers from stakeholder and community perspectives.
articles.authors: Lake MW, Gelaye KA, Asemahagn MA, Muchie KF, Yenesew MA
articles.details: This qualitative study explored perceived drivers of malaria resurgence in the Amhara Region of Ethiopia, where confirmed cases rose from 1.0 million in 2018 to more than 7.3 million by October 2024. Researchers conducted 28 semi-structured key informant interviews, two focus group discussions (n=20), and 12 in-depth interviews with frontline health workers and community members, analyzed inductively using reflexive thematic analysis. Participants perceived near year-round transmission, expansion into previously low-risk highland areas, and a rising burden among children under five and pregnant women, attributing resurgence to ecological change (irregular rainfall, land use change, irrigation), health system fragility (antimalarial and diagnostic stockouts), and behavioral factors, exacerbated by conflict. The authors call for locally tailored, equity-focused interventions.

articles.headline: Expired malaria RDTs stay valid up to 9 months past expiration
articles.title_original: Test Performance of Expired Malaria Rapid Diagnostic Tests: A pilot diagnostic accuracy study conducted in Western Uganda.
articles.authors: Ndizeye R, Gunderson AK, Baguma E, Giandomenico DA, Ntaro M, Mulogo EM, Boyce RM
articles.details: This study in western Uganda evaluated the diagnostic agreement of expired malaria RDTs (SD Bioline Ag Pf/Pan and First Response Ag pLDH/HRP2) against a non-expired mRDT reference, administered in parallel to each participant. All expired tests up to 9 months post-expiration were valid and consistent with the reference, whereas all mRDTs 10 to 12 months post-expiration were invalid. Of 200 participants, 21 (10.5%) tested positive and 179 (89.5%) tested negative. The authors conclude these preliminary findings support further research into using expired mRDTs in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Bed net use cuts malaria infection 13% in young Ghanaian children
articles.title_original: Evaluating the effectiveness of insecticide-treated net use in preventing malaria among children under five: a quasi-experimental study.
articles.authors: Osei KM, Adiak AA, Han W
articles.details: In this analysis of nationally representative, georeferenced survey data from Ghana, sleeping under an insecticide-treated net (ITN) was associated with a 13% reduction in the odds of malaria infection (aOR 0.87; 95% CI 0.81–0.93) and an absolute risk reduction of 2.2% (RD −0.022; 95% CI −0.037 to −0.006) among children under five. The study included 11,481 children and used multilevel regression (conventional and Bayesian), propensity-score inverse probability of treatment weighting, and a linear probability model. Spatial analyses revealed misalignment between intervention coverage and malaria burden. The authors conclude ITNs offer meaningful but limited protection.

articles.headline: Deep-learning YOLO framework benchmarked for automated P. vivax detection
articles.title_original: Enhanced YOLO-based framework and benchmarking for automated Plasmodium vivax detection.
articles.authors: Prathap VM, Yadav S
articles.details: This methods study compared YOLO object-detection variants (v3, cascade v3, scaled v4, v5, and v8) for automated detection of Plasmodium vivax in thick blood smear images, evaluating precision, accuracy, F1-score, recall, and mean Average Precision (mAP). The authors propose a novel framework combining YOLOv3 with a modified MobileNetV2 backbone augmented by a Transformed Convolutional Layer (TCL) for multi-scale texture sensitivity and efficient feature extraction. No specific performance values are reported in the abstract. The authors present this as guidance toward scalable, interpretable AI diagnostic tools for early malaria detection across clinical and field settings.

articles.headline: Fluoroquinolone moxifloxacin potently inhibits P. falciparum plasmepsin II
articles.title_original: Physico-Chemical Insights into the Catalytic Inhibition of Plasmodium falciparum Plasmepsin II Activity by Repurposing Fluoroquinolone Drugs.
articles.authors: Syed SF, Thakur P, Patil M, Choudhary S
articles.details: This biochemical and computational study repurposed the fluoroquinolones ofloxacin, levofloxacin, and moxifloxacin to inhibit the catalytic activity of mature Plasmodium falciparum plasmepsin II (mPlmII), an aspartic protease essential for hemoglobin degradation, using thermodynamic analyses, enzyme kinetics, molecular docking, and QM/MM (ONIOM, B3LYP/6-31G*:UFF) calculations. Moxifloxacin showed the most potent inhibition (IC50 0.15 ± 0.02 μM), acting primarily through hydrogen bonding with the catalytic dyad Asp34 and Asp214, with donor-acceptor distances of 2.8–3.3 Å. Methylation of the NH group disrupted these interactions and reduced inhibitory efficacy. The authors conclude precise hydrogen bonding with Asp34/Asp214 is essential for effective inhibition.

articles.headline: Adding primaquine to chloroquine cuts P. vivax transmission in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Efficacy of primaquine-chloroquine combination on Plasmodium vivax transmission and parasite clearance in Ethiopia: a randomized controlled trial.
articles.authors: Melaku M, Wondale B, Tamiru G, Getu R, Kumsa T, Tesfaye Y, Lindtjørn B, Massebo F
articles.details: This open-label randomized controlled trial tested whether adding primaquine (0.25 mg/kg daily for 14 days) to chloroquine reduces Plasmodium vivax transmission and improves parasite clearance versus chloroquine alone, in patients aged 15 or older with microscopy-confirmed P. vivax mono-infection, using membrane feeding assays with colony Anopheles arabiensis and midgut dissections. After one day of treatment, reductions in infectiousness (P<0.0001), oocyst counts (P<0.001), CSP positivity (P=0.002), and the number of gametocyte-positive patients (P=0.02) were significantly greater with CQ-PQ than with CQ alone, while asexual parasite clearance at three days did not differ (P=0.42). Fifty-six patients completed the feeding experiments and 49 the 28-day follow-up; before treatment, 57% (16/28) in the CQ group and 82% (23/28) in the CQ-PQ group were infectious (P=0.08). Adding PQ reduced transmission to mosquitoes, though some patients remained infectious up to 24 hours post-treatment.

articles.headline: Anopheles gambiae dominant malaria vector in Hadiya Zone, Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Species Occurrence and Seasonal Variation of Malaria Vectors in Hadiya Zone, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Assemie A, Mulu D, Mekuriaw E, Muluken W
articles.details: This entomological survey characterized malaria vector species occurrence and seasonal variation across larval habitats in four purposively selected villages from October 2023 to June 2024, with morphological identification under a stereomicroscope. Of 721 larvae collected, Anopheles gambiae s.l. was dominant (43.82%, n=316) and An. pharoensis least abundant (7.9%, n=57), with An. funestus s.l. and An. coustani also present. Shemo Boyo had the most larvae (45.50%, n=328) and Kemecho Borara the fewest (9.57%, n=69); ditches had the highest mean larval density (2.61 per sample), followed by swamps (1.5) and riverbeds (0.8), with water pans lowest (0.14) and an overall mean of 1.11 that differed significantly across habitats (p=0.012). The authors conclude An. gambiae s.l. likely plays a primary role in local transmission.

articles.headline: Rural, poor households drive under-five malaria risk in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Sociodemographic and healthcare determinants of malaria infection among children under five in Burkina Faso: analysis of the 2021 Demographic and Health Survey data.
articles.authors: Kinda R, Gansane A, Traore TI, Kaboré NF, Debe S, Sore H, Nonkani WG, Guelbéogo MW, Tougri G, Tarama CW, Ilboudo SR, Sanou GS, Savadogo LGB
articles.details: Among 5674 children aged 6-59 months in the 2021 Burkina Faso Demographic and Health Survey, overall malaria prevalence by positive microscopy was 14.1% (95% CI: 13-15), with substantial regional disparities ranging from 5.1% in the Centre to 29.3% in the Sahel. Using two-stage stratified cluster sampling and modified Poisson regression, the risk of malaria increased with age (aPR 1.46 at 13-23 months, up to 2.07 at 24-35 months) and was higher among children in rural areas (aPR 2.21), households without electricity (aPR 2.01), and poor households (aPR 1.60). The authors conclude that achieving malaria elimination requires equitable access to electricity, poverty reduction, and targeted interventions for vulnerable groups in rural areas.

articles.headline: Computational analysis links Plasmodium retrocopies to virulence pathways
articles.title_original: Comprehensive computational insights into Plasmodium retrocopies reveals potential roles in virulence-associated functions.
articles.authors: Das S, Ghosh S, Thakur S
articles.details: A computational genomics study systematically characterized retrocopies (reverse-transcribed gene copies) across 23 pathogenic Plasmodium genomes to assess their evolutionary origins and functional roles in virulence. Using a stringent framework, the authors identified 724 high-confidence retrocopies with canonical retroposition signatures; many were intact, under purifying selection, and transcribed during intraerythrocytic stages, with some PPIase, PIR, and actin-related family members showing RNA-seq FPKM comparable to or exceeding parental genes. Enrichment analyses tied retrocopies to cytoadhesion, chromatin remodeling, stress response, and cytoskeletal organization, with notable expansions in primate-infecting lineages. The authors conclude retroposition is an underappreciated mechanism of genomic plasticity contributing to virulence-associated gene family diversification.

articles.headline: α-Thalassemia not linked to asymptomatic P. falciparum carriage in Ghana
articles.title_original: α-Thalassemia has no association with asymptomatic Plasmodium falciparum carriage in three ecological zones of Ghana.
articles.authors: Donkor AB, Bernasko FG, Abdulai A, Eyeson NAS, Abban RL, Boadu EA, Kusi KA, Afrane YA, Amoah LE
articles.details: This study genotyped α-thalassemia (multiplex PCR) and assessed Plasmodium falciparum carriage (RDT, microscopy, PCR) among 1401 asymptomatic individuals aged 1-60 years across Ghana's coastal, forest, and Sahel savanna zones, using archived dried blood spots. Malaria prevalence by PCR was highest in the forest zone (36.5%) versus Sahel savanna (27.6%) and coastal (23.5%) zones (p < 0.0001). Genotype distributions varied by zone (e.g., coastal: 54.3% αα/αα, 41.8% -α/αα, 3.8% -α/-α). Homozygous recessive individuals had higher odds of asexual parasites (OR 1.23) and gametocyte carriage (OR 1.46), but no statistically significant association was found between α-thalassemia genotype and asexual P. falciparum stages (p = 0.447).

articles.headline: Community awareness of zoonotic P. knowlesi malaria suboptimal in Thailand
articles.title_original: Community awareness and health providers' perspectives on zoonotic Plasmodium knowlesi malaria in Thailand: A mixed-methods assessment.
articles.authors: Sripoorote P, Inthitanon N, Wattanagoon Y, Cui L, Nguitragool W, Fornace K, Sattabongkot J, Parker DM, Aung PL
articles.details: An explanatory sequential mixed-methods study assessed awareness of zoonotic Plasmodium knowlesi malaria via a cross-sectional survey of 300 adults in three endemic districts of southern Thailand (eight-item awareness questionnaire) followed by semi-structured interviews with 28 healthcare providers. Overall awareness was moderate (mean 10.7/23; SD ± 2.9; range 3.0-20.0). Being female, living farther from health facilities, receiving malaria health education, and higher attitude scores were significantly associated with greater awareness (p < 0.05). Providers reported declining incidence but persistent forest-related transmission risk, with largely reactive health education. The authors conclude awareness remains suboptimal—especially among men—and call for proactive, male-focused health promotion.

articles.headline: pir gene expression profiled in severe Plasmodium vivax malaria
articles.title_original: Comparative clinical transcriptome of pir genes in severe Plasmodium vivax malaria.
