articles.authors: Boopathi PA, Singh S, Roy P, Tahbildar S, Kochar SK, Kochar DK, Das A
articles.details: Using custom microarrays, this transcriptomic study compared pir gene (including vir subfamily) expression in Plasmodium vivax parasites isolated from 23 patients in India—6 uncomplicated, 12 with hepatic dysfunction, and 5 with cerebral malaria—to examine genes potentially involved in cyto-adherence and splenic clearance evasion. A wide range of pir subfamilies were differentially expressed: 24 pir genes were upregulated in the cerebral malaria group and 28 in the hepatic dysfunction group, across different subfamilies in at least 50% of patients. In cerebral malaria, vir subfamily E (8 genes) and pvpir subfamily H (6 genes) predominated, whereas hepatic dysfunction featured vir subfamily E (9) and C (6).

articles.headline: Cochrane review: folic acid and malaria risk on antifolate antimalarials
articles.title_original: Folic acid supplementation and malaria susceptibility and severity among people taking antifolate antimalarial drugs in endemic areas.
articles.authors: Qi YP, Crider KS, Gutman J, Centeno-Tablante E, Fothergill A, Daniels K, Yeung LF, Mai CT, Mehta S, Williams JL, Finkelstein JL
articles.details: This Cochrane systematic review and meta-analysis (searched September 13, 2024) examined whether folic acid supplementation affects malaria susceptibility, severity, parasite clearance, or treatment failure among people taking antifolate antimalarials in endemic areas. Eight randomised trials with 3486 participants were included (three prevention, five treatment); antimalarials were sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (five trials), sulfisoxazole plus pyrimethamine, atovaquone-proguanil, and proguanil, and most treatment trials used doses above the 1.0 mg/day upper limit. For prevention, one trial found little to no difference in parasitaemia among pregnant women receiving folic acid plus iron versus iron alone (RR 1.21; 95% CI 0.56-2.62; P=0.63; 643 individuals). Uncomplicated and severe malaria outcomes were not reported.

articles.headline: Cribra orbitalia maps possible malaria burden in medieval Netherlands
articles.title_original: Mapping medieval malaria in the Netherlands: A bioarchaeological approach using cribra orbitalia as a proxy.
articles.authors: Schats R
articles.details: This bioarchaeological spatial-epidemiology study used cribra orbitalia prevalence as a non-specific skeletal proxy for malaria across 28 archaeological medieval sites in the Netherlands. Global Moran's I showed significant positive spatial autocorrelation, and LISA analysis identified High-High clusters in the Southwest and Low-Low clusters in the East. Broad-scale environmental regression models failed to explain these patterns, but prevalence correlated significantly with historical (1938) density estimates of the local mosquito Anopheles atroparvus, which breeds in brackish water. The authors conclude malaria may have contributed to cribra orbitalia in some regions, though small non-adult samples and uneven preservation limit interpretation.

articles.headline: Model: immunity creates reproduction-survival tradeoff for malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Immunity can impose a reproduction-survival tradeoff on human malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Patterson DD, Childs LM, Stopard IJ, Chitnis N, Serrato-Arroyo S, Greischar MA
articles.details: This theoretical study developed a within-host mathematical model incorporating immunity to track human malaria infection dynamics over infection age and to test how parasite investment in transmission (sexual) versus multiplication (asexual) stages affects fitness. With constant transmission investment, higher investment reduced infection duration and parasite fitness, and optimal investment occurred around 5%, lower than predicted by models lacking feedback between investment and immunity. When strategies varied with infection age, parasites benefited from delaying transmission investment to allow faster early multiplication. The authors show adaptive immunity can impose a survival-reproduction tradeoff as an emergent model property, with implications for the timing of infectiousness and parasite evolution.

articles.headline: P. ovale rises as P. falciparum recedes in coastal Tanzania
articles.title_original: Seasonal Variation and Interspecies Dynamics Among Plasmodium falciparum and ovale Species in Bagamoyo, Tanzania.
articles.authors: Carey-Ewend K, Marten A, Muller J, Peter EE, Odas M, Dominick MC, Muller M, Chhetri S, Amagai K, Rutha I, Kisandu F, Beka L, Kharabora O, Popkin-Hall ZR, Bailey J, Edwards JK, Gower EW, Juliano JJ, Ngasala BE, Lin JT
articles.details: Real-time 18S rRNA PCR screening of 7173 asymptomatic individuals over 5 years of age in coastal Tanzania (2018-2022) found P. falciparum prevalence of 27.4% (associated with younger age, male sex, and higher recent cumulative rainfall) and P. ovale prevalence of 11.5%. P. ovale infections peaked during months with lower P. falciparum prevalence, especially in the long wet season, with fewer Pf-Po co-infections than expected; this antagonism reversed in the short wet season, when Pf-Po co-infections were comparatively enriched. Mixed P. ovale curtisi/wallikeri infections appeared across all seasons, composing 23% of P. ovale isolates in which a specific species could be detected.

articles.headline: Most submicroscopic P. vivax infections clear without treatment in Thailand
articles.title_original: Spontaneous Clearance of Polymerase Chain Reaction-Positive Plasmodium vivax Parasitemia in Northwestern Thailand: An Epidemiological Perspective.
articles.authors: Aung PL, Sripoorote P, Petchvijit P, Suk-Aum K, Htwe KZZ, Lawpoolsri S, Nguitragool W, Cui L, Kaewkungwal J, Sattabongkot J
articles.details: This longitudinal cohort study (2018-2023) in northwestern Thailand used 14 rounds of mass blood surveys covering about 5000 individuals, screening for Plasmodium vivax by microscopy and PCR and following PCR-positive submicroscopic infections to assess natural clearance. Of 210 P. vivax infections detected, 154 were PCR-only, and 101 participants were analyzed after exclusions; 72 (69.9%) cleared spontaneously within 3 subsequent surveys. Turnbull survival analysis estimated the probability of persistent infection fell to 25.0% (95% CI 4.0-100.0) by day 47 and 7.0% (95% CI 1.0-38.0) by day 232, with no significant association between clearance and age, sex, nationality, occupation, or education. The authors present this as the first field evidence that untreated submicroscopic P. vivax often resolves, implicating naturally acquired immunity.

articles.headline: Human mobility spreads P. vivax across Brazilian Amazon, threatening elimination
articles.title_original: The Source-Sink Dynamics of Plasmodium vivax May Undermine Malaria Elimination Efforts in the Amazon: An Epidemiological and Population Genomic Study.
articles.authors: de Albuquerque NRM, Corder RM, Johansen IC, Ladeia WA, Rodrigues PT, Ladeia-Andrade S, Silva JC, Ferreira MU
articles.details: This epidemiological and population genomic study analyzed 5.5 million malaria case notifications (2003–2023) and whole-genome sequence data from 408 P. vivax isolates across South America to map parasite source-sink dynamics in the Brazilian Amazon, where 163,000 new cases (>80% P. vivax) were recorded in 2023. Nearly one-third of P. vivax infections in residents were acquired outside their locality or municipality, but only 1.7% were imported from other South American countries (mostly the Guiana Shield). Large cities like Manaus and Porto Velho act as receptive sinks surrounded by high-risk rural source areas, and parasites more than 1000 km apart often remained genetically connected. The authors conclude that targeting mobile populations, source localities, and receptive sinks is crucial for elimination.

articles.headline: Chimeric peptide vaccine targets parasite CSP and mosquito saliva protein
articles.title_original: A Multivalent Peptide Vaccine Against Malaria, Targeting Plasmodium Circumsporozoite Protein and Mosquito AgTRIO.
articles.authors: Chuang YM, Ledizet M, Mattessich M, Fikrig E
articles.details: This preclinical mouse study tested PfAg, a chimeric peptide vaccine combining regions of Plasmodium falciparum circumsporozoite protein (PfCSP) and the mosquito saliva protein AgTRIO. Mice given PfAg generated robust humoral responses against both PfCSP and AgTRIO. After exposure to PfCSP-expressing Plasmodium berghei-infected mosquitoes, PfAg-immunized inbred C57BL/6 and outbred CD-1 mice had significantly improved survival compared with control animals. The authors conclude these data will aid development of a new malaria vaccine.

articles.headline: Wealth and water storage shape malaria prevention in urban India
articles.title_original: Socioeconomic and household water management determinants of malaria and other vector-borne disease prevention in Urban Gujarat, India.
articles.authors: Batheja D, Samria D, Wimberly MC, Pascual M, Baharia RK, Mohanty AK, Desai V, Vaishnav K, Sharma R, Kohli V, Sharma S, Anvikar A, Murdock C, Nandi A
articles.details: This socioeconomic and health survey, conducted in Ahmedabad and Surat, India, between September and November 2023, collected data from 4,074 households (15,484 individuals) to examine associations between socioeconomic factors, water availability and storage practices, and mosquito-borne disease using logistic regression. Self-reported prevalence was low, with 77 cases of malaria, dengue, and chikungunya (18.90 cases per 1,000 households). Wealth, caste, and family size were significantly associated with disease reporting and prevention; the richest quintile adopted more prevention measures but perceived less risk, and storage in clean large containers was associated with increased symptoms and prevention. The authors conclude that socioeconomic disparities and water management significantly influence malaria incidence and prevention behaviors.

articles.headline: Meta-analysis finds DSM265 safe but raises adverse event risk
articles.title_original: Evaluating the safety, pharmacokinetics, and antiparasitic activity of DSM265, a novel antimalarial plasmodium dihydroorotate dehydrogenase inhibitor: a systematic review and meta-analysis of early clinical trials.
articles.authors: Sula I, Jihwaprani MC, Ahmad I, Khan TA, Al Abdin MG, Ali MM, Ming LC
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis (PROSPERO CRD42024499167) pooled seven early-phase clinical trials of DSM265, an antimalarial that targets P. falciparum DHODH, to evaluate safety, pharmacokinetics, and antiparasitic activity. DSM265 was associated with an increased risk of adverse events (RR 1.46, 95% CI 1.15-1.84), significant in the low-dose (25-250 mg; RR 1.86) and high-dose (600-1200 mg; RR 1.67) subgroups; given as a 400 mg oral suspension it achieved Cmax 9.3 μg/mL, Tmax 5.4 h, T1/2 112.1 h, and AUC0-∞ 1,601.9 h·μg/mL. It targeted P. falciparum DHODH with greater selectivity than P. vivax and showed no anti-gametocyte activity. The authors conclude DSM265 had a favorable safety profile after a single 400 mg dose, though preclinical signals of teratogenicity and testicular toxicity have halted further development.

articles.headline: Bloodless diet sustains mosquito rearing without harming malaria research traits
articles.title_original: Effects of a bloodless diet on fitness and malaria susceptibility in Anopheles mosquitoes from Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: de Sales Hien DF, Sare I, Sib AMAP, Poda BS, Velez R, Marques J, Silveira H, Diabate A, Bilgo EM
articles.details: This study evaluated the effects of a BLOODless™ diet on wing size, longevity, and Plasmodium falciparum susceptibility in Anopheles coluzzii and An. gambiae from Burkina Faso and the Kisumu An. gambiae strain, comparing blood- and bloodless-reared mosquitoes across seven generations. Wing size was unaffected by diet in the 3rd and 7th generations, with a diet-species interaction in the 7th. P. falciparum infection rates were similar in the 3rd generation, but by the 6th, blood-fed mosquitoes showed higher oocyst prevalence (intensity unchanged), and longevity increased significantly in bloodless-fed mosquitoes by the 7th generation. The authors conclude a blood-free diet enables sustained mosquito rearing while preserving traits essential for malaria research, offering an ethical alternative for vector control programs.

articles.headline: Sulfadoxine-resistance Pfdhps mutations common in P. falciparum, N'Djamena
articles.title_original: Resistance profile of the Plasmodium falciparum dihydropteroate synthase gene in three hospitals in the city of Ndjamena, Chad.
articles.authors: Cedric Y, Djakbé DL, Ngaryedji T, Nadia NAC, Pacome TK, Anaud NKJ, Esther DD, Alain DC, Ismail IA, Saleh B, Abel D, Hu H
articles.details: A cross-sectional analytical study genotyped the Plasmodium falciparum dihydropteroate synthase (Pfdhps) gene in 220 consenting patients across three health facilities in N'Djamena, diagnosing infection by RDT and thick blood smear and identifying the A437G and A581G mutations by semi-nested PCR and RFLP. Overall P. falciparum prevalence was 39.54% (highest in the 0-5-year age group), and the Pfdhps resistance gene was detected in 37.93% of positive cases, most frequently in those aged 5-35 years (45.61%). The A437G mutation was found in 9.09% of samples and A581G in 54.54%, with A581G more prevalent in men (30%) and in those reporting Fansidar use (30%). The authors conclude these high mutation frequencies underscore the need to strengthen monitoring of antimalarial drug resistance.

articles.headline: Severe malaria predominates in HIV-positive adults with low CD4 counts
articles.title_original: Clinical and biological features of complicated and asymptomatic malaria among PLHIV and HIV-negative adults: An observational study in Libreville, Gabon.
articles.authors: Moutombi Ditombi BC, Manomba Boulingui C, Mbang Nguema OA, Mihindou CJ, Pongui Ngondza B, Ndong Ngomo JM, Essomeyo Mebale M, Ntsame Owono MM, Mabicka Moussavou AD, Mayandza C, Mbondoukwé NP, Kono HN, Bouyou Akotet MK
articles.details: This cross-sectional study at the Centre Hospitalier Universitaire de Libreville, Gabon (September 2021–October 2022) compared malaria presentation in people living with HIV (PLHIV) and HIV-negative adults. Of 1,192 individuals tested (513 PLHIV, 599 HIV-negative), 56 P. falciparum-infected PLHIV and 66 HIV-negative individuals were enrolled. Asymptomatic malaria was more frequent in HIV-negative participants (60.6% vs 25.0%), while severe malaria was significantly more common in PLHIV (48.2% vs 12.1%, p<0.01), with prostration, seizures, impaired consciousness, severe anaemia and hypoglycemia predominating. Cotrimoxazole prophylaxis was associated with reduced parasitemia (p=0.03), and median CD4 count was lower in PLHIV with severe malaria (150 vs 304 cells/µL, p=0.018), underscoring the importance of early detection and management of co-infection.

articles.headline: Correction notice for P. vivax population genetics study
articles.title_original: Correction: Population genetics of Plasmodium vivax with transmission decline and rebound in two endemic areas of Papua New Guinea.
articles.authors: Fola AA, Mehra S, Razook Z, Lautu-Gumal D, Nate E, Lee S, Kattenberg JH, Koepfli C, Kazura J, Ome-Kaius M, Laman M, Robinson LJ, Mueller I, Barry AE
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Plant extracts show in vitro antiparasitic activity against P. falciparum
articles.title_original: In Vitro Antiparasitic Activity and Phytochemical Profiling of Rhaponticum repens and Achillea millefolium Methanolic Extracts Against Plasmodium Falciparum and Leishmania major.
articles.authors: Singh K, Siyadatpanah A, Garba A, Singh J, Norouzi R, Ben Said M, Belkahia H
articles.details: This in vitro study evaluated methanolic extracts of Rhaponticum repens and Achillea millefolium against Plasmodium falciparum and Leishmania major clinical isolates, with chloroquine and amphotericin B as positive controls. Antiparasitic activity was measured via JC-1 staining and flow cytometry, cytotoxicity via MTT assay on Vero cells, and composition by GC-MS. Both extracts showed dose- and time-dependent inhibition: R. repens was more active against L. major (81% inhibition at 2.0 mg/mL, IC₅₀=1.18 mg/mL, SI=10.6), while A. millefolium was more effective against P. falciparum (94% inhibition at 2.0 mg/mL, IC₅₀=0.52 mg/mL, SI=26.3). Cytotoxicity was low, and nonadecane and docosane were major constituents. The authors conclude the extracts are promising leads for natural-product antiparasitic drug discovery.

articles.headline: Computational framework predicts antimalarial activity of Malaria Box compounds
articles.title_original: Hybrid Computational Framework Integrating Ensemble Learning, Molecular Docking, and Dynamics for Predicting Antimalarial Efficacy of Malaria Box Compounds.
articles.authors: Moreno M, Cuesta SA, Mora JR, Márquez Brazon EA, Paz JL, Agüero-Chapin G, Pérez-Pérez N, García-Jacas CR
articles.details: This computational study combined ensemble machine learning, molecular docking, and molecular dynamics to predict antimalarial activity of compounds from the Malaria Box database against drug-resistant Plasmodium falciparum. After global regression of pEC50 performed poorly, a classification approach categorized compounds as 'active' or 'very active'; the best ensemble classifier reached Acc10-fold=0.738 and Accext=0.675, and within-class regression achieved Q²10-fold of 0.810 (very active) and 0.793 (active). Docking against P. falciparum Cytochrome B showed strong binding, often via π-π stacking and hydrogen bonds with Glu272, and 200 ns molecular dynamics confirmed stability of several complexes, including compound M31 despite poor docking scores. Binding free energy calculations validated several promising candidates, and the authors present the approach as a platform to accelerate antimalarial drug discovery.

articles.headline: Helminth coinfection shifts antimalarial antibody responses by species and antigen
articles.title_original: The association of helminth infection against antimalarial antibody responses in malaria infection and vaccination: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Pinandyo SH, Ali S, Aurelio B, Amoah AS, Kaisar MMM
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis (PROSPERO CRD420251055839) of human and animal studies examined how helminth infection affects antimalarial antibody responses during malaria infection and vaccination; 29 of 2705 screened articles were eligible, with eight human studies analyzed using standardized mean differences (SMD). Pooling four studies, any helminth infection was associated with higher antimalarial IgG (SMD -0.44; 95% CI -0.61 to -0.27; I2=79.7%; p<0.0001), while four studies showed marginally lower IgG with Schistosoma haematobium (SMD 0.40; 95% CI 0.00 to 0.80; p=0.0492). Three studies suggested better IgG3 (SMD -1.08; 95% CI -1.75 to -0.42; p=0.0015) in S. haematobium infection. The authors conclude modulation depends on helminth species and malaria antigen, with no definitive conclusion given study limitations.

articles.headline: pan-pLDH biomarker flags severe imported P. falciparum malaria via smartphone
articles.title_original: pLDH to identify severity in imported malaria: Implementing smartphone video analysis for rapid clinical decision-making.
articles.authors: Pedreira-Rincón J, Balerdi-Sarasola L, Villanueva G, Fleitas PE, Jimenez A, Mayor A, Muñoz J, Subirà C, Alvarez-Martinez MJ, Petrone P, Camprubí-Ferrer D, Parolo C
articles.details: This retrospective cohort study of patients with imported Plasmodium falciparum malaria and non-malarial fevers evaluated two parasite biomarkers—PfHRP2 and pan-specific lactate dehydrogenase (pan-pLDH)—for diagnosis and severity assessment, using a bead-based multiplex immunoassay and commercial lateral flow assays (LFAs) read by smartphone image and video analysis. The bead-based assay showed excellent diagnostic performance for both biomarkers and indicated both as promising severity markers. On LFAs, PfHRP2 maintained perfect diagnostic accuracy, but pan-pLDH outperformed PfHRP2 at identifying severe malaria. Smartphone video analysis cut testing time to under 6 minutes while preserving pan-pLDH's severity detection. The authors conclude pan-pLDH is valuable for severity assessment in non-endemic settings requiring rapid decisions.

articles.headline: P. vivax and arboviruses co-circulate at Brazil–French Guiana border
articles.title_original: Co-circulation of Plasmodium vivax and Arboviruses in the Brazil-French Guiana Border.
articles.authors: Cerilo-Filho M, Medeiros EDS, Arouca ML, Sampaio MP, Menezes RAO, Gomes MDSM, Silva JRS, Baptista ARS, Storti-Melo LM, Machado RLD
articles.details: This study analyzed 300 serum samples collected between 2014 and 2015 from the Brazil–French Guiana border, including 102 malaria cases and 198 controls, using rapid tests for IgM, IgG, and NS1 to assess co-circulation of Plasmodium vivax and arboviruses. High IgG reactivity was observed for dengue and chikungunya viruses, with frequent co-reactivity, while Zika virus detection was minimal. The authors conclude that overlapping transmission of P. vivax and arboviruses highlights the need for integrated surveillance to address co-circulation and co-infection risks.

articles.headline: Model: adding primaquine to DP chemoprevention cuts malaria in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Agent-based simulation of seasonal malaria chemoprevention strategy in Southern Tanzania: comparing dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine with or without primaquine.
articles.authors: Mfala CT, Nyambo DG, Mwaiswelo RO, Tchuenche JM, Clemen T
articles.details: An agent-based model (AnyLogic), built from a cluster-randomized DP-based seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) study, simulated three monthly rounds of dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP) with and without single low-dose primaquine (SLDPQ) over 180 days in under-five children across Masasi and Nanyumbu Districts, Tanzania; 2275 participants (1135 intervention, 1140 control) were modeled. Baseline prevalence was 11.5% (130/1135) intervention and 16.3% (186/1140) control. With DP alone, prevalence fell to 4.1% (47/1135) by day 125 then rebounded to 7.1% (80/1135) by day 180; adding SLDPQ lowered it further to 1.4% (16/1135) and 3.9% (44/1135). Effects held under ±1°C temperature scenarios. The authors conclude SLDPQ can be added to DP-SMC and scaled out in Tanzania.

articles.headline: Education and wealth linked to safer-sex negotiation among Tanzanian women
articles.title_original: Safer Sex Negotiation Among Women in Sexual Unions in Tanzania: Analysis of the 2022 Demographic and Health Survey and Malaria Indicator Survey.
articles.authors: Kessy SA, Stephano EE, Tibenderana JR, Mtoro MJ, Kessy IP, Majengo VG, Oguma ED, Mwalingo TP, Bago MH, Nyundo AA
articles.details: This analytical cross-sectional study used the 2022 Tanzania Demographic and Health Survey and Malaria Indicator Survey (conducted February–July 2022) with a two-stage stratified sampling design, applying modified Poisson regression to identify factors associated with safer sex negotiation among women in sexual unions. Overall prevalence of safer sex negotiation was 75.8% (95% CI 74.0–77.5). In adjusted analysis, higher likelihood was associated with primary (APR=1.16) and secondary/higher education (APR=1.21), middle (APR=1.10) and rich (APR=1.12) household quintiles, media exposure (APR=1.14), employment (APR=1.06), STI awareness (APR=1.17), internet use (APR=1.04), and partner education, plus geographic zone. The authors conclude education, socioeconomic status, media access, internet use, and geography enhance safer sex negotiation, supporting context-specific empowerment interventions.

articles.headline: Climate change could hinder malaria eradication efforts
articles.title_original: Climate change could hinder malaria eradication efforts.
articles.authors: O'Leary K
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Rainfall and temperature forecast malaria surges in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Modelling the association of rainfall and temperature with malaria incidence in Adamawa State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Bakare EA, Dukundane D, Salako KV, Kakaï RG, Okoronkwo C, Nelson E
articles.details: This time-series modeling study used monthly malaria, rainfall, and temperature data from January 2015 to April 2024 in Adamawa State, applying a Box-Jenkins ARIMA/SARIMAX framework with lagged temperature and rainfall, selected by AIC/BIC and evaluated by RMSE, MAE, and MAPE. The best-fitting model captured annual seasonality and climatic drivers of transmission. Forecasts for May 2024–December 2025 project pronounced seasonal surges between June and October, reaching approximately 67,052 cases in August 2024 and peaking at about 80,004 cases in October 2025. The authors conclude that climate variability strongly influences malaria dynamics and that SARIMAX forecasting can strengthen early warning systems.

articles.headline: Author correction to study on Ethiopian highland malaria trends
articles.title_original: Author Correction: Malaria trends in Ethiopian highlands track the 2000 'slowdown' in global warming.
articles.authors: Rodó X, Martinez PP, Siraj A, Pascual M
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Community accepts livestock ivermectin for malaria control in Indonesia
articles.title_original: Community perceptions and acceptance of ivermectin for malaria control on Sumba Island, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Timoria D, Dewi C, Bøgh C, Baskoro T, Nurcahyo W, Testamenti VA, von Seidlein L, Kobylinski K, Chambers M
articles.details: This qualitative social science study, conducted alongside the Sumba Livestock Ivermectin for Malaria (SLIM) trial between November 2022 and September 2023 across four villages in Southwest Sumba, Indonesia, explored community perceptions of ivermectin treatment of livestock (ITL) and potential human mass drug administration (MDA). It included 75 individuals (>18 years) in four focus group discussions (59 individuals) and 16 in-depth interviews, with about 650 people engaging in community events; data were thematically analyzed in NVivo. Malaria was ranked the most pressing health concern. Initial skepticism about livestock ivermectin was mitigated through trust-building; participants accepted it and were open to human MDA but raised safety concerns, especially for children. The authors conclude sustained engagement and partnership with local leaders are essential.

articles.headline: Malaria infection linked to reduced gut microbiota diversity in juncos
articles.title_original: Microbial community composition variation in relation to malaria infections in Junco hyemalis.
articles.authors: Amaya-Mejia WS, Sehgal RNM, Yeh PJ
articles.details: This study characterized and compared the gut microbiota of eight wild Oregon Juncos (Junco hyemalis) infected with Plasmodium relictum GRW04 and eight non-infected individuals. Alpha diversity was significantly lower in infected birds, and beta-diversity turnover occurred primarily among rare taxa, with Muribaculaceae, Acidobacteriales, Pseudomonas, and Escherichia-Shigella depleted in infected birds. Co-occurrence networks in infected birds had fewer connections and lacked hub taxa. The authors conclude these results provide stronger evidence for parasite-mediated changes in microbiota composition, consistent with dysbiosis.

articles.headline: Six-pronged strategy controls 22-month P. vivax outbreak in Suriname
articles.title_original: A six-pronged approach to manage a Plasmodium vivax outbreak in a low- and middle-income country on the road to malaria elimination.
articles.authors: Labadie-Bracho M, Baldewsingh GK, Hiwat van Laar H, Adhin MR
articles.details: Descriptive study of a Plasmodium vivax outbreak in Suriname, a low-middle income country, using surveillance and intervention data from November 2019 to August 2021. The outbreak lasted 22 months and caused 179 infections among 153 patients. An adaptive six-pronged approach combined "1-3-7" surveillance, community engagement, and vector control with mass screen-and-treat, mass drug administration (MDA), parasite genotyping, and targeted treatment. Mass screen-and-treat was less effective than MDA at reducing parasite prevalence; directly observed treatment reached 95.1% efficacy by day 180, but relapses fueled the outbreak. Implementing the "Radical Cure" marked the end of the outbreak.

articles.headline: Low RTS,S malaria vaccine uptake among young Cameroonian children
articles.title_original: Uptake of malaria vaccine (RTS,S/AS01) among children aged 6-24 months: A cross-sectional survey conducted in the Western region of Cameroon, 1 year following the vaccine introduction.
articles.authors: Ateudjieu J, Nana Djapou MC, Bekoa Onana BK, Tatsabong Tiomeni AA, Okoumokath Mpande GL, Kapso Nanguep D, Buh Nkum C, Ateudjieu Kenfack DW, Bissek AC
articles.details: Community-based cross-sectional survey of 399 children aged 6-24 months across 55 clusters in the Foumbot and Foumban health districts of Cameroon, more than a year after RTS,S/AS01 introduction. Vaccination coverage was 31.20% (95%CI 30.38-32.02) for dose 1, 22.61% for dose 2, and 17.70% for dose 3; among children receiving dose 1, 56.49% completed the 3-dose schedule, and timeliness ranged 50-57%. Parental perception was strongly associated with dose 1 receipt (aOR 15.54, 95%CI 12.63-19.10, p<0.001), dose 1 timeliness (aOR 2.36), and dose 2 completeness (aOR 9.81, p<0.001). Authors conclude performance indicators remain below expectations.

articles.headline: Review of gender interventions in HIV/TB/malaria finds none on malaria
articles.title_original: Gender-responsive and -transformative interventions in HIV, TB and malaria programmes: a review of evidence.
articles.authors: Bhushan A, Mishra S, Orth Z, Riha J
articles.details: Rapid review synthesizing evidence on gender-responsive and -transformative interventions across HIV, TB, and malaria programmes, searching PubMed and Scopus for English-language studies from low- and middle-income countries published between 2004 and 2024. Of 11,844 articles identified, 42 were included—all focused on HIV, with none on TB or malaria. Interventions fell into five categories: resilient health systems (n=6), gender norms change (n=9), social empowerment (n=8), economic empowerment (n=18), and law/policy reform (n=1). Multi-component, multi-level interventions using a socio-ecological framework showed more sustained improvements than single-component efforts, and couples-based approaches changed harmful norms more than individual-focused ones.

articles.headline: Chloroquine and mefloquine resistance persists in Colombian P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Antimalarial drug resistance in Plasmodium falciparum isolates from the Pacific Coast of Colombia.
articles.authors: Michie K, Chiu B, Briggs C, Corredor V, Matta-Cortés L, Rayner JC, Echeverri DF
articles.details: Study assessing antimalarial resistance genotypes and phenotypes in a panel of Plasmodium falciparum isolates collected across 23 years from the Pacific Coast of Colombia, focusing on the historically used drugs chloroquine, mefloquine, pyrimethamine, and sulfadoxine. Sensitivity was predicted using quantitative PCR and sequencing of resistance-associated loci including the artemisinin resistance gene PfKelch13, then confirmed phenotypically via in vitro culture. Multiple resistance-associated genotypes and phenotypes, particularly for chloroquine and mefloquine, persisted across the region, but no artemisinin resistance-associated polymorphisms were found. The authors argue against using these drugs as partner therapy for artemisinin.

articles.headline: IPTp-SP fails and selects for drug resistance in pregnant Ugandan women
articles.title_original: Recurrent Plasmodium falciparum parasitemia and drug resistance mutations during intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy in Uganda.
articles.authors: Kizza J, Katairo T, Kakuru A, Nsengimaana B, Esilu T, Wiringilimaana I, Semakuba FD, Gerlovina I, Hathaway N, Briggs J, Tukwasibwe S, Kiwuwa SM, Kamya MR, Nankabirwa JI, Dorsey G, Rosenthal PJ
articles.details: Genetic analysis of 1377 samples from pregnant women with asymptomatic parasitemia enrolled in a Busia, Uganda trial comparing monthly sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP), dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP), and DP+SP for intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp). Among 771 baseline samples, five PfDHFR and PfDHPS resistance mutations were nearly 100% prevalent, and the high-level PfDHFR I164L plus PfDHPS A581G combination reached 26.5%. The 28-day cumulative risk of recurrent parasitemia was far higher with SP (57.8%) than DP (4.1%) or DP+SP (3.9%), with symptomatic malaria and recrudescence also greatest in the SP arm, especially in primigravidae. SP receipt raised the I164L/A581G prevalence from 24.9% to 35.2%. Authors conclude IPTp-SP had poor preventive efficacy and selected for increased resistance.

articles.headline: Faster chemiluminescent assay measures antimalarial parasite-killing speed
articles.title_original: Using Next Generation Chemiluminescent Probes to Improve the Plasmodium falciparum in vitro Parasite Reduction Ratio (PRR) Assay.
articles.authors: Hellingman A, Göttle NL, Walz A, Brancucci NMB, Wittlin S, Mäser P, Rottmann M
articles.details: Methods study developing an optimized parasite reduction ratio (PRR) assay using a chemiluminescence-based lacZ/β-galSENSOR readout to assess antimalarial pharmacodynamics. The new protocol cut the standard 14–28 day incubation to 7 days while preserving key parameters including lag phase, parasite clearance time, parasite reduction ratio, and maximum killing effect. By contrast, the [3H]-hypoxanthine incorporation method failed to reliably detect viable parasites and consistently overestimated drug activity under the shortened protocol. The authors conclude the assay enables labs without radioactive facilities to evaluate compounds with higher throughput and lower equipment needs.

articles.headline: Review: nanotechnology and herbal nano-formulations for malaria treatment
articles.title_original: Nano-Enabled Herbal Remedies for Malaria Treatment: A Review of Recent Advances.
articles.authors: Xu C, Fatima A, Fatima M, Aqib AI, Zaheer T, Rab SO, Saeed M, Arif Z, Li K
articles.details: Narrative review of nanotechnology-based and herbal approaches to malaria therapy and control. It notes that widely used treatments include chloroquine, artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT), and quinine, but face drug resistance, misdiagnosis, low water solubility, and poor bioavailability. The authors describe nanoparticle advantages such as high drug-loading capacity, targeted delivery, improved biocompatibility, and reduced host-cell toxicity, and cover the malarial life cycle, traditional herbal remedies, nanoparticle delivery systems, herbal nano-formulations, drug-resistant malaria, and green-nanomaterial vector control. They conclude nanotechnology-based therapeutics are promising candidates with commercialization potential.

articles.headline: Mali field isolates stay sensitive to frontline antimalarials
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum field isolates drug susceptibility in Mali.
articles.authors: Maiga FO, Dembele L, Dama S, Diakite O, Diallo F, Sogore F, Maiga M, Dao F, Dara N, Alhousseini ML, Traore OB, Djimde AA
articles.details: Ex vivo susceptibility study of 26 Plasmodium falciparum field isolates from patients with uncomplicated malaria at three endemic sites (Faladje, Kolle, Bougoula-Hameau) in Mali, tested against 14 antimalarials using SYBR Green I and Mitotracker fluorescence assays. Frontline drugs remained highly potent: dihydroartemisinin (median IC50 <6 nM), lumefantrine (<50 nM), and pyronaridine (<10 nM); atovaquone and amodiaquine inhibited all isolates (<10 nM). Chloroquine showed marked variability (IC50 ~50–1300 nM), tafenoquine was weak (>1500 nM), and sulfadoxine/pyrimethamine had high IC50s (>1000 and >2000 nM). Next-generation cabamiquine and GNF179 were strongly active (<10 nM). Authors support continued resistance surveillance.

articles.headline: Iran's malaria progress toward elimination and recent resurgence
articles.title_original: Iran's Journey Through Malaria: From Past Challenges to Future Elimination-A Narrative Review.
articles.authors: Hanifian H, Nateghpour M
articles.details: This narrative review of WHO World Malaria Reports, national surveillance summaries, and PubMed/Scopus publications (2000–2025) found that Iran reduced indigenous malaria cases from thousands in the early 2000s to fewer than 300 annually by the mid-2010s, later recording multiple consecutive years of zero indigenous transmission. Key achievements included integrated vector management, community engagement, and strengthened cross-border initiatives. However, interruptions during the COVID-19 pandemic and a 2022 resurgence—largely associated with imported infections, operational disruptions, and emerging vector threats—highlighted vulnerabilities, while insecticide resistance and the spread of Anopheles stephensi further complicate the elimination trajectory. Authors conclude that WHO certification will require transparent surveillance metrics, reinforced cross-border collaboration, and sustained political and financial commitment.

articles.headline: Machine learning finds kinase inhibitors hitting two malaria targets
articles.title_original: Small Molecule Kinase Inhibitors with In Vitro β-Hematin Formation and Plasmodium falciparum Protein Kinase G Inhibitory Activity Identified Using Machine Learning.
articles.authors: Thibaud JL, Salomane N, Harries S, Wicht KJ, Coulson LB, Kuter D, Chibale K, de Villiers KA
articles.details: Using molecular docking and machine learning, researchers screened the BioVision Protein Kinase Inhibitor library for compounds with dual activity against two Plasmodium falciparum targets: β-hematin (synthetic hemozoin) formation and cGMP-dependent protein kinase (PfPKG). Three hits were identified. Derazantinib was most potent, with IC50 values of 88 µM against β-hematin formation (versus 22 µM for chloroquine) and 0.160 µM against PfPKG. Pazopanib (β-hematin IC50 219 µM; PfPKG IC50 0.330 µM) and afatinib (234 µM and 2.61 µM) showed more moderate activity against both targets.

articles.headline: Avian malaria parasites found in Iberian vultures and kites
articles.title_original: Unraveling Occurrence Patterns and Diversity of Avian Malaria Parasites in Iberian Obligate and Facultative Scavenger Birds.
articles.authors: Oliva-Vidal P, Muriel J, Marzal A, López Del Aguila GM, Martínez JM, Margalida A
articles.details: Active monitoring collected 383 blood samples from free-living obligate (European vultures) and facultative (red and black kites) scavenger birds in northeastern and central Spain, using nested-PCR to detect haemosporidian parasites and lineages. Overall haemosporidian prevalence was 3.4% (13/383), with Leucocytozoon at 2.3% and Plasmodium at 1.6%; among positives, Leucocytozoon (69.2%) exceeded Plasmodium (46.1%), including two co-infections. Black kites had the highest prevalence (8.3%). The authors report 10 new host-parasite interactions, describe a novel Leucocytozoon lineage (lGYPBAR01) infecting all European vultures except griffon, and detect Plasmodium relictum pSGS1 in an adult red kite, highlighting an overlooked conservation threat.

articles.headline: SARIMAX model forecasts rising malaria in Bahir Dar, Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Forecasting malaria incidence in a resource-limited urban setting with climate variables as exogenous regressors: time series analysis using a SARIMAX model in Bahir Dar, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Gelaw TT, Abera MA
articles.details: A time-series study built a seasonal autoregressive integrated moving average model with exogenous weather regressors (SARIMAX) using 90 monthly malaria data points from Bahir Dar, Ethiopia, spanning January 2018 to June 2025, with the first 80% for training and final 20% for testing. The SARIMAX(1,2,2)(1,2,2,12,exog) model fit the data well and projected a consistently increasing trend through 2026-2030, though no weather variable showed a statistically significant predictive relationship (p > 0.05). Predictive accuracy was reasonable (MAPE 28.3%). The authors conclude the upward forecast suggests a potential breakdown in current strategies and underscores the need for a localized early warning system.

articles.headline: AI-assisted miLab outperforms microscopy for malaria detection
articles.title_original: Evaluating the diagnostic performance of miLab™ for detection of malaria parasites using nPCR as reference standard.
articles.authors: Addae EK, Awortwe-Quaicoe T, Sackey B, Ansah RO, Larbi R, Dazangapai KDE, Frimpong JO, Amoako TOO, Asamoah A, Ayisi-Boateng N, Nkrumah B, Bekoe FA, Owusu M
articles.details: A hospital-based cross-sectional study enrolled 300 suspected malaria patients (168 females, 132 males; aged 1-87 years, median 24) across three malaria-endemic communities in Kumasi, Ashanti region, Ghana, evaluating the AI-assisted automated miLab platform against nested PCR as reference, with adjudicated microscopy by two mid-level microscopists and a WHO expert. Against nPCR, miLab showed sensitivity 94.23%, specificity 98.98%, and accuracy 97.33%, versus adjudicated microscopy's sensitivity 85.58%, specificity 97.96%, and accuracy 93.67%. miLab parasite densities ranged 0.95-5.34 log parasites/µL (median 3.52). The authors conclude miLab outperforms microscopy and could offer a reliable, rapid diagnostic alternative for resource-limited endemic settings.

articles.headline: Care-seeking barriers for child severe malaria in DR Congo
articles.title_original: Young children with severe malaria in the Kapolowe health zone: an analysis of their parents' health-seeking behaviors and the challenges they face.
articles.authors: Kanyamukenge DB, Maseko T, Khote FL, Makenga G, Mavoko HM, Tevuzula VM, Mulangu AM, Sompwe EM, Rietveld H, Manyando C, Van Geertruyden JP, Kakudji A, Bastiaens H
articles.details: A qualitative study in the Kapolowe health zone, Democratic Republic of Congo, conducted 30 focus group discussions across 30 villages, with 9 groups selected by pre-established criteria and analyzed inductively using an adapted three-delays model to explore parents' health-seeking behaviors for children under five with severe malaria. Findings indicate mothers often delay seeking care and frequently resort to self-medication with both modern medicines and traditional remedies. Barriers included geographical distance, impassable roads, lack of transportation and facilities, and direct and indirect financial costs, alongside cultural factors favoring waiting, traditional practices, or prayer. The authors conclude care-seeking is shaped by structural, financial, and cultural barriers, underscoring the need to strengthen the local health system.

articles.headline: SD Bioline RDT reliably rules out malaria in Nigerian children
articles.title_original: Field performance of the SD Bioline Malaria Ag P.f. rapid diagnostic test among children under five in Nigeria: insights from the 2021 Malaria Indicator Survey.
articles.authors: Olubiyi B, Alabi A, Isiko I
articles.details: A secondary analysis of the nationally representative 2021 Nigerian Malaria Indicator Survey evaluated the SD Bioline Malaria Ag P.f. rapid diagnostic test against microscopy in children aged 6-59 months, analyzing an unweighted sample of 9067 (weighted n=9131) with complete RDT and microscopy results. The RDT showed sensitivity 88.6%, specificity 75.2%, positive predictive value 49.5%, negative predictive value 96.0%, and accuracy 78.1%, with moderate agreement (Cohen's κ=0.496) and weighted ROC AUC 0.816. The authors conclude the test's high sensitivity and NPV confirm reliability for ruling out malaria, but moderate specificity and low PPV risk overestimating prevalence, underscoring the need for confirmatory testing and ongoing postmarketing evaluation.

articles.headline: Severe vs uncomplicated malaria burden in hospitalized patients, Jacobabad, Pakistan
articles.title_original: Burden of Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium falciparum Malaria: clinical insights from a prospective study in Jacobabad, Sindh, Pakistan.
articles.authors: Noor M, Junaid M, Furqan A
articles.details: A prospective clinical observational study of 260 malaria patients admitted to Pakistan Airforce Hospital, Shahbaz Base, Jacobabad, Sindh (2024–2025) compared the clinical and haematological profiles of severe versus uncomplicated cases classified by WHO criteria. Uncomplicated cases numbered 217 (83.5%) and severe cases 43 (16.5%). Severe malaria was significantly associated with jaundice, bleeding, pallor, respiratory distress, fits, cerebral malaria, anaemia, and thrombocytopenia. Overall case fatality was 16.2%, occurring in the paediatric group (0.1–15 years), with cerebral malaria the most fatal complication; authors conclude these parameters serve as prognostic indicators warranting close monitoring.

articles.headline: CYP450 gene variants and chloroquine-primaquine response in P. vivax patients
articles.title_original: Pharmacogenetic Profiling of Cytochrome P450 Enzymes in Plasmodium vivax Patients Treated with Chloroquine and Primaquine: Implications for Personalized Malaria Therapy.
articles.authors: Phompradit P, Muhamad P, Cheoymang A, Na-Bangchang K
articles.details: This pharmacogenetic study genotyped CYP2C8, CYP2C9, CYP2C19, CYP2D6, and CYP3A5 polymorphisms in 99 DNA samples from Burmese patients with microscopically confirmed P. vivax monoinfection treated with standard chloroquine (25 mg/kg) and primaquine (15 mg/kg/day for 14 days), using PCR-RFLP. All patients achieved complete parasite clearance and resolution of fever without recurrence. The most frequent alleles were CYP3A5*3 (71.7%) and CYP2D6*10 (30.6%). CYP3A5*3 was significantly associated with altered desethylchloroquine levels and CYP2C19*3 with faster parasite clearance; no significant associations were found for CYP2D6 or CYP2C8 with primaquine levels or efficacy. Authors conclude CYP variants modulate pharmacokinetics without compromising clinical outcomes and underscore the value of pharmacogenetic surveillance.

articles.headline: Systematic review: engineered symbiotic bacteria block Plasmodium in mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Evaluating paratransgenesis using engineered symbiotic bacteria for Plasmodium inhibition in mosquito vectors: A systematic review.
articles.authors: Cleanclay WD, Kernyuy FB, Kintung IF, Yensii NG, Chick JA, Obi AMM
articles.details: A PRISMA-guided systematic review evaluating paratransgenesis, which uses engineered symbiotic microbes to deliver antiplasmodial molecules into the Anopheles mosquito midgut as a transmission-blocking strategy. Searches of PubMed, ScienceDirect and Web of Science yielded 1,289 records, with ten studies included. Studies used bacterial and fungal symbionts (Asaia, Serratia, Pantoea, Enterobacter, Aspergillus oryzae) engineered to produce effector proteins (Scorpine, EPIP, Defensin, SM1-2 peptides). Oral sugar meals achieved midgut colonization with high inhibition of oocysts or sporozoites; Scorpine was the strongest effector with up to 97.8% inhibition of P. falciparum oocysts, and multi-effector combinations exceeded 89% inhibition. Authors conclude paratransgenesis is a promising complementary control tool, though ecological safety, microbial stability and field validation remain key obstacles.

articles.headline: Nanopore multiplex assay IDs mosquito species, bloodmeal, and Plasmodium
articles.title_original: A multiplex assay to detect mosquito species, bloodmeal host source and Plasmodium in malaria vectors using Nanopore amplicon sequencing.
articles.authors: Rogers EA, Derilus D, Sandi C, Reimer L, Lenhart A, Impoinvil LM
articles.details: This study tested the Oxford Nanopore MinION sequencer as a single-run assay to simultaneously identify mosquito species, bloodmeal host source, and detect parasites in malaria vectors. Researchers sequenced 150 insectary-reared mosquitoes (nine species), 150 blood-fed mosquitoes (five vertebrate blood sources), and 40 P. falciparum-infected samples; a combined assay integrated all three with 32 samples per amplicon (96 total), compared against Sanger sequencing. The platform accurately identified all nine mosquito species, five bloodmeal hosts up to 48 h post blood feed, and detected P. falciparum in all 40 positive controls, with high concordance to Sanger sequencing. Authors conclude the protocol is a novel, precise, cost-effective combined solution.

articles.headline: Genomic insecticide resistance in Anopheles arabiensis varies across Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Spatial and temporal signatures of genomic insecticide resistance in the Anopheles arabiensis mosquito malaria vector from Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Eukubay A, Bennett KL, Tekie H, Hernandez-Koutoucheva A, Gemechu F, Miles A, Abera D, Clarkson CS, Golassa L
articles.details: This study used whole-genome sequencing of the major malaria vector Anopheles arabiensis sampled across regions of Ethiopia to characterize insecticide resistance genes, finding high geographic and temporal variability. The Vgsc-L995F target-site substitution was highly prevalent in northern Ethiopia but less common elsewhere; metabolic resistance in western Ethiopia was indicated by high-frequency copy number variants at the Cyp6aa/p cluster and carboxylesterase Coeae2-7g. Markers were generally lower in the Central Rift Valley, though CNVs at Gste2 and Cyp9k1 were frequent. Seasonal shifts included rising Vgsc-L995F and cytochrome P450 frequencies during the major transmission season. Selection scans revealed signals on chromosome 2L (Coejhe1-5e genes) and chromosome 3L. Authors stress integrating genomic surveillance into entomological monitoring.

articles.headline: Understudied Anopheles in southern Zambia bite humans, sustaining residual malaria
articles.title_original: Species composition and blood feeding patterns of understudied Anopheles mosquitoes in southern Zambia, an area of low malaria transmission.
articles.authors: Mudenda T, Simubali L, Mwaanga G, Lubinda M, Katowa B, Musonda M, Hamapumbu H, Chibambo P, Chooli S, Ali RLMN, Gebhardt ME, Thuma PE, Simulundu E, Moss WJ, Saili K, Norris DE, Stevenson JC
articles.details: This vector survey collected 5697 female anopheline mosquitoes using CDC light traps from 12 households at 5 km intervals along a 100 km transect in Southern Province, Zambia, an area of low malaria transmission. Nine Anopheles species were identified, dominated by An. rufipes (26.8%) and An. pretoriensis (25.1%). Blood-meal analysis of 401 fed mosquitoes showed feeding mainly on goats (62.5%), humans (19.4%), and cows (10.2%), with a human blood index of 0.22. One An. rufipes specimen was positive for P. falciparum sporozoites. Authors conclude understudied, often exophagic secondary vectors may sustain residual transmission via peri-domestic human activity despite high ITN and IRS coverage.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic school-age children carry most transmissible malaria infections in Malawi
articles.title_original: Asymptomatic school-age children carry the majority of transmissible Plasmodium falciparum infections.
articles.authors: Buchwald AG, Vareta J, Nwagbata O, McCann RS, Sixpence A, Matengeni A, Kamzati M, Mangani C, Seydel KB, Wilson ML, Taylor TE, Mathanga DP, Valim C, Laufer MK, Cohee LM
articles.details: In a year-long cohort study in rural Malawi, researchers used molecular methods to detect all P. falciparum infections and those containing gametocytes (the transmissible stage) at routine surveillance and sick visits. Gametocytes were detected in only 23% of the population and were not randomly distributed; school-age children had significantly higher incidence of gametocyte-containing and high-density gametocyte infections than other groups and drove household gametocyte frequencies even after adjusting for infection levels. The authors estimate that clearing infections from asymptomatic school-age children in the rainy season would reduce population gametocyte abundance by 67%, concluding that interventions targeting school-age children are needed to reduce transmission.

articles.headline: Photonic crystal fibre optical sensor detects malaria parasite stages
articles.title_original: Machine learning assisted malaria detection using photonic crystal fibre optical sensors.
articles.authors: Abdullah-Al-Shafi M, Sen S, Mubassera M
articles.details: This study presents a photonic crystal fibre optical sensor with a hollow central core and five concentric heptagonal cladding layers designed to detect malaria via refractive index variations in red blood cells. Operating over a refractive index range of 1.373 to 1.402 at 2.2 THz, the sensor converts changes across Plasmodium developmental stages into distinct wavelength shifts to discriminate ring, trophozoite, and schizont stages. Numerical results showed high relative sensitivity of 97.45% for healthy cells, 96.89% (ring), 96.22% (trophozoite), and 95.45% (schizont), with confinement losses on the order of 10⁻⁸ dB/m. Authors conclude the platform is a cost-effective, high-performance option for early malaria detection.

articles.headline: Seasonal labour migration may sustain dry-season malaria in Sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Rural-Urban Migration and Low-Transmission Endemicity of Malaria in Sub-Saharan Africa: A Statistical Inference Approach to Uncover Possible Mechanisms.
articles.authors: Harikripal, Zabre SP, Sie A, Ouedraogo A, Danquah I, Bhattacharyya S
articles.details: This modeling study examined why low but persistent malaria hotspots (3 to 150 cases per 10,000 person-weeks) persist through the dry season in peri-urban and rural-urban fringe zones, focusing on Ouagadougou, Burkina Faso. The authors developed a data-driven malaria model and used statistical inference with empirical incidence and mobility data to compare seasonal, regular, and permanent migration patterns. Seasonal rural-urban labour migration most accurately explained low-transmission endemicity in dry months and its amplification into rainy-season outbreaks, maintaining persistent transmission that prevented local fade-out. Authors conclude that incorporating human mobility, especially seasonal migration, into transmission models and control strategies can strengthen malaria elimination efforts.

articles.headline: Zein nanocarrier sustained-release artemisinin overcomes drug-resistant malaria
articles.title_original: Development and evaluation of novel zein-based artemisinin sustained-release formulation for treating drug-resistant malaria.
articles.authors: Wang Y, Yu X, Zhang X, He X, Tang Y, Fang L, Culleton R, Zhang Q, Dong W, Cao J
articles.details: Researchers developed and evaluated a zein-based (natural maize protein) sustained-release nanoformulation encapsulating artemisinin (ART) to address its poor solubility and short (~1 h) half-life. The formulation achieved a 200-fold enhancement in ART water solubility and provided sustained release with good dispersibility in acidic and basic environments. In vitro, it showed potent inhibition of ART-resistant P. falciparum in ring survival and recrudescence assays; in vivo studies using rodent malaria and humanized erythrocyte mouse models showed prolonged ART half-life and superior efficacy versus free ART in reducing parasitemia, preventing recrudescence, and overcoming ART resistance. Authors conclude the zein nanocarrier is a promising strategy to optimize ART therapies against drug-resistant malaria.

articles.headline: Acute malaria weakens Ebola vaccine protection in mice via lymph node macrophages
articles.title_original: Acute malaria dysregulates specialized lymph node macrophages to suppress vaccine-elicited protection against the Ebola virus.
articles.authors: Elliff J, Grady L, O'Donnell KL, Messingham C, Rogers KJ, Akther J, Thurman A, Vijay R, Pezzulo A, Randall T, Marzi A, Butler NS, Maury W
articles.details: In a mouse model, acute blood-stage murine Plasmodium infection at the time of rVSV/EBOV (ERVEBO) vaccination reduced vaccine-mediated protection against mouse-adapted Ebola virus challenge. The reduced protection was associated with a Plasmodium-induced, interferon gamma-mediated decrease in rVSV/EBOV replication in lymph node macrophages, lowering primary anti-EBOV glycoprotein antibody responses. Higher vaccine doses partially overcame the antibody deficit and elicited protective responses. The authors conclude that this negative impact on low-dose rVSV/EBOV efficacy supports using higher antigen doses when managing Ebola outbreaks.

articles.headline: Two endothelial biomarkers distinguish cerebral malaria from other coma causes
articles.title_original: Specific Biomarkers Differentiate Cerebral Malaria from Other Causes of Coma in African children.
articles.authors: Mwangi S, Gomes C, Abdi AI, Rodriguez A
articles.details: This retrospective study measured plasma Angiopoietin-like 4 and Inhibin-βE, biomarkers secreted by endothelial cells in response to P. falciparum-infected erythrocytes, in a cohort of 379 Kenyan children with severe malaria (cerebral malaria, respiratory distress, or severe anemia) and with non-traumatic coma of unknown cause. Both markers showed high specificity for severe malaria versus non-malarial coma (AUC 0.82 for cerebral malaria, 0.86 respiratory distress, 0.85 severe anemia), rising to AUC 0.96 when combined with platelet levels, and predicted death from cerebral malaria (AUC 0.85). The authors conclude these markers could be developed to differentiate cerebral malaria from other coma causes.

articles.headline: Ivermectin sugar rings drive lethal effect on Anopheles mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Lethal effects of ivermectin structures on malaria vectors and in silico analysis of interactions with their glutamate-gated chloride ion channels.
articles.authors: Nguyen MN, Jones AK, Hotwagner D, Khemrattrakool P, Hongsuwong T, Hanboonkunupakarn B, Jittamala P, Sriwichai P, Tarning J, Kobylinski KC
articles.details: This study tested the mosquito-lethal effects of ivermectin (two sugar rings), monosaccharide (one sugar ring), and aglycone (no sugar rings) on Anopheles dirus and Anopheles minimus, finding full, partial, and no effect respectively, since ivermectin acts on the glutamate-gated chloride channel (GluCl) causing flaccid paralysis. Anopheles GluCl protein sequences were determined and used to build 3-D docking models, which identified a new hydrogen bond between the second sugar ring hydroxyl group (4″-OH) and THR304 of the M2-M3 loop, made possible by a substitution from C. elegans ILE273 to Anopheles THR304. The authors say this clarifies GluCl-ivermectin interactions and how resistance could arise.

articles.headline: Pathogen load variability broadens avian malaria's host range and spread
articles.title_original: Variation in pathogen load and the pathogen load-infectiousness relationship broaden avian malaria's distribution.
articles.authors: Seidl CM, Parise KL, Ipsaro IJ, Leach S, Hays D, Morimoto R, Banister K, Ferreira FC, Abraham EC, Foster JT, Paxton EH, Kilpatrick AM
articles.details: Using experimental infections in canaries (Serinus canaria), the authors quantified the relationship between Plasmodium relictum parasitemia and infectiousness to biting Culex quinquefasciatus mosquitoes, then estimated infectiousness of 17 bird species across 11 communities in Hawai'i. Infectiousness rose with parasitemia, temperature, and time since feeding; the gradual low-parasitemia slope left a wide range of parasitemias partly infectious, and high within-species variability produced extensive overlap in infectiousness among hosts. Although a few host species contributed disproportionately, broad overlap yielded similar total infectiousness across most communities. The authors conclude that the pathogen load-infectiousness shape and within-species variability help determine host range, transmission intensity, and spatial spread.

articles.headline: Climate variables linked to vivax and falciparum malaria in Madagascar highlands
articles.title_original: Association of Climate Variables with Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium falciparum Malaria Cases in Mandoto, Madagascar: A Statistical Modeling Study.
articles.authors: Rajaonarimirana E, Ravaoarimanga M, Lockwood S, Cramer E, Rajesy M, Drakeley C, Randremanana RV, White M
articles.details: This statistical modeling study analyzed monthly malaria case data from 27 health centers in Mandoto District, central highlands of Madagascar, between 2019 and 2024, alongside meteorological data, using descriptive, cross-correlation, and seasonal ARIMA forecast models. Across 6 years, 276,318 rapid diagnostic tests yielded a 39.6% positivity rate and 109,428 cases; after multispecies RDTs became available in 2021, 71.5% of cases were P. falciparum and 28.5% P. vivax, with both species co-endemic and higher risk in the western region. Cases peaked in January with a second peak from April to June, and P. falciparum showed stronger associations with precipitation and temperature variability. The authors conclude that combining time-series modeling with climate data can help predict cases and support planning.

articles.headline: IL-10-dominant cytokine profile linked to postpartum malaria in first-time mothers
articles.title_original: Postpartum cytokine shifts and IL-10-mediated immune suppression in malaria-infected primigravid women.
articles.authors: Traore O, Rouamba T, Zango SH, Sorgho H, Valea I, Traore-Coulibaly M, Schallig HDFH, Tinto H
articles.details: This cohort study enrolled 33 Burkinabè primigravid women at delivery and followed them at 1 and 3 months postpartum, quantifying plasma IL-4, IL-6, IL-10, TNF-α, and IFN-γ by ELISA, with malaria detected by PCR and microscopy. At delivery, 48.5% tested positive for malaria by PCR. Infected women had significantly elevated IL-10 and a decreased IL-6:IL-10 ratio versus non-infected women (p=0.005), an anti-inflammatory shift persisting into early postpartum; IL-10 correlated strongly with infection (σ=0.9, p<0.001), and IL-4 also showed a significant effect. The authors conclude an IL-10-dominant profile at delivery is associated with postpartum malaria susceptibility.

articles.headline: Heme and hemozoin trigger platelet death pathways in vivax malaria thrombocytopenia
articles.title_original: Heme and hemozoin induce platelet cell death through UPR-induced apoptosis and ferroptosis in vivax malaria.
articles.authors: Gomes MT, Medeiros-de-Moraes IM, Vieira-de-Abreu A, de Azevedo-Quintanilha IG, Fontes TM, de Brito Carneiro A, Azambuja P, Tolley ND, Rowley JW, Carpinter BA, Ferreira MU, Gonçalves RM, Rocha VN, Rosa Nascimento AL, Carvalho WA, Scopel KKG, Campbell RA, Bozza PT, Weyrich AS, Zimmerman GA, Lacerda MVG, Castro-Faria-Neto HC, Hottz ED
articles.details: Using biochemical, ultrastructural, pharmacological, and molecular approaches, this study examined stress and cell death pathways elevated in the platelet transcriptome during P. vivax malaria and markers of hemolysis correlating with thrombocytopenia. Heme from plasma of thrombocytopenic vivax malaria (but not nonthrombocytopenic vivax or falciparum malaria) induced platelet cell death ex vivo. Heme and hemozoin triggered apoptotic and necrotic features, with stronger necrosis from hemozoin; both activated apoptotic caspases, while only heme induced calpain-dependent BcL-xL degradation. The authors identified a caspase-independent intrinsic apoptosis program via the ER stress sensor IRE1α, and ferroptosis (lipid peroxidation, regulated by DGAT1/2) as the main pathway for hemozoin-induced thrombocytopenia in vitro.

articles.headline: Post-artemisinin delayed hemolysis after artesunate in pediatric returning traveler
articles.title_original: Severe Plasmodium falciparum Malaria Complicated by Post-artemisinin Delayed Hemolysis in a Non-immune Pediatric Returning Traveler.
articles.authors: Galadari S, Kalsekar M, Iqbal A, Daoud Z, Balila M, Nyanga O
articles.details: This case report describes a previously healthy nine-year-old boy who presented with severe P. falciparum malaria following travel to Tanzania, with multiorgan dysfunction including thrombocytopenia, acute kidney injury, hepatic dysfunction, and hypoxemic pneumonia. He was treated in the pediatric ICU with intravenous artesunate followed by oral artemether-lumefantrine and discharged after parasite clearance. Ten days after treatment initiation he re-presented with pallor, jaundice, and dark urine; labs showed hemoglobin decline, indirect hyperbilirubinemia, elevated LDH, reticulocytosis, low haptoglobin, and absent parasitemia, confirming post-artemisinin delayed hemolysis (PADH). He required packed red blood cell transfusion with full hematological recovery. The authors stress follow-up hemolysis testing 7–30 days after treatment, especially with high initial parasitemia.

articles.headline: Narrative review of malaria history, eradication, and re-emergence in Greece
articles.title_original: Malaria in Greece: From Ancient Scourge to Eradication.
articles.authors: Christodoulou MK
articles.details: This narrative review traces the long-term trajectory of malaria in Greece from antiquity through periods of endemicity, the national eradication campaign, and re-emergence of locally acquired cases in the early 21st century. It describes strategies that achieved elimination, including vector control, antimalarial treatment, environmental interventions, and sustained surveillance, and examines factors behind temporary re-establishment of transmission, emphasizing the vulnerability of malaria-free regions amid global mobility and persistent competent vectors. The authors conclude that elimination is achievable through coordinated, long-term public health efforts but requires continuous surveillance and rapid response to prevent re-establishment.

articles.headline: Narrative review of malaria burden and control in Nigerian under-fives
articles.title_original: Burden, challenges, and control of malaria among under-5s in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Okoro LN, Ayesiga I, Yeboah MO, Bulus NG, Gmanyami JM, Ismaila EL, Ahgu O, Otokpa EO, Edet EN, Dogo JM, Ediamu TD, Atimango L, Isiko I
articles.details: This narrative review synthesized peer-reviewed literature, national reports, and policy documents published since 2010, searching PubMed, Google Scholar, Scopus, and grey literature from WHO, UNICEF, and the Nigerian Ministry of Health, following SANRA guidelines, focused on malaria epidemiology, interventions, and control in children under five in Nigeria. Findings revealed a high malaria burden with regional disparities, highest prevalence in northern states and rural areas, driven by socioeconomic deprivation, climate variability, and suboptimal intervention coverage. Key challenges included diagnostic limitations, drug resistance, and inconsistent preventive measure use; while LLINs, intermittent preventive therapy, and indoor residual spraying were effective, coverage and implementation gaps remain. The authors call for a coordinated, multisectoral response.

articles.headline: Review finds most malaria interventions in Africa cost-effective
articles.title_original: Strategic resource allocation for malaria elimination in endemic settings: a systematic review of cost-effectiveness evidence.
articles.authors: Firdaus MH, Ho Imran DS, Puteh SEW, Zhueng TJ, Hamid MHA, Idris ZM, Manaf MRA
articles.details: This systematic review (PROSPERO CRD42024546911) synthesised 18 full economic evaluations of malaria prevention and treatment interventions, mainly randomised trials conducted largely in Sub-Saharan Africa. It assessed interventions including ITNs/LLINs, indoor residual spraying, diagnostics, and combined strategies using cost-effectiveness, cost-utility, and cost-benefit analyses with incremental cost-effectiveness ratios. LLINs and IRS were highly cost-effective, with costs per DALY averted below national GDP thresholds. Overall, the interventions deemed highly cost-effective included LLINs (with PBO nets), IRS (Actellic/3GIRS), combined LLIN+IRS, SMC/PDMC, IPTp, and ACT+RDT, while RTS,S vaccination and RDT+microscopy were moderately cost-effective and G6PD screening with tafenoquine and traveller chemoprophylaxis were context-specific or less cost-effective.

articles.headline: No G6PD A- (G202A) variant found in Nigerian malaria patients
articles.title_original: No G6PD A- (G202A) variant detected among Plasmodium falciparum-positive patients in Awka, Southeast Nigeria: a hospital-based study.
articles.authors: Ikegbunam M, Uchechukwu N, Abone H, Grace AE, Ezeunala M, Joy N, Immaculata NC, Igwe JO, Okeke O, Nworji F, Ihekwereme P
articles.details: This hospital-based study screened malaria-positive patients in Awka, Anambra State, Nigeria for the G6PD A376G and G202A variants using PCR and Sanger sequencing. Of 100 malaria-positive participants, 64 with complete genotyping and clinical data were analysed. The normal B variant predominated (83% of participants), and none carried the A- (G202A) variant associated with G6PD deficiency. The B and A+ variants showed no significant impact on haemoglobin or parasitaemia levels, and authors recommend broader genotyping or enzyme-activity testing before informing treatment policy.

articles.headline: Faster parallel tool speeds up Plasmodium identity-by-descent analysis
articles.title_original: hmmibd-rs: an enhanced hmmIBD implementation for parallelizable identity-by-descent detection from large-scale Plasmodium genomic data.
articles.authors: Guo B, Schaffner SF, Taylor AR, O'Connor TD, Takala-Harrison S
articles.details: Researchers developed hmmibd-rs, a multi-threaded reimplementation of the IBD-detection tool hmmIBD for Plasmodium genomic surveillance, adding support for user-defined non-uniform recombination rate maps and direct BCF input. Using 128 threads, it cut IBD detection time from 5.2 days to 1.3 hours for 220 million pairs of simulated P. falciparum-like chromosomes, roughly a 100× speedup that scales nearly linearly with CPU threads. Incorporating non-uniform recombination rates improved accuracy by reducing overestimation of IBD breakpoints in cold spots and underestimation in hot spots, lowering false positives. Applied to MalariaGEN Pf7 (~10,000 monoclonal samples), it completed IBD detection within hours.

articles.headline: Trial protocol tests community pre-referral treatment for severe malaria
articles.title_original: Severe malaria treatment with rectal artesunate and artemisinin-based combination therapy in remote settings: an effectiveness-implementation hybrid type 3 study protocol (SEMA ReACT).
articles.authors: Hachizovu S, Manyando C, Nambozi M, Khondowe S, Hamainza B, Mukumbuta D, Rietveld H, Mukomena Sompwe E, Kikunda G, Muhindo-Mavoko H, Luzolo Khote F, Kanyamukenge D, Bastiaens H, Kakudji A, Maseko T, Makenga G, Baraka V, Mutombo A, Duparc S, Van Geertruyden JP
articles.details: This effectiveness-implementation hybrid Type 3 study protocol, conducted in Nchelenge, Zambia and Kapolowe, Democratic Republic of Congo using phased rollout of upgraded integrated community case management (iCCM), evaluates community health workers and health facilities providing pre-referral rectal artesunate (RAS) followed by ACT to children aged 6 months–5 years where injectable artesunate or referral is not feasible. Primary outcomes include time from symptom onset to treatment initiation, 28-day PCR-corrected cure rates following RAS+ACT or RAS+injectable artesunate+ACT, and the proportion of the study population using formal healthcare services within the preceding 6 months. The study aims to provide evidence on the feasibility and effectiveness of community-based pre-referral treatment for severe malaria to inform policy and guide scalable strategies in high-burden regions.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence 33% among young DRC children in national survey
articles.title_original: Prevalence and factors associated with malaria among children aged 6-59 months in the Democratic Republic of the Congo: a nationwide cross-sectional survey.
articles.authors: Adam J, Mukuku O, Tawi JM, Luoga P, Mutombo AM
articles.details: Among 10,013 children aged 6–59 months who underwent malaria rapid diagnostic testing in the 2023/24 DRC Demographic and Health Survey, overall malaria prevalence was 33% (95% CI 30.2–35.6), ranging from 5% in Kinshasa to 61% in Bas-Uele province. This secondary analysis of weighted survey data used modified Poisson regression. Significantly associated factors included age 24–59 months (APR 1.25), mothers with no education (APR 1.15) or primary education (APR 1.13), poor (APR 1.52) and middle (APR 1.42) wealth quintiles, never sleeping under an ITN (APR 1.18), no household television (APR 1.73), and unimproved floor (APR 1.54) or roof (APR 1.27) material. The authors call for scaling up ITN distribution, caregiver education, and timely diagnosis and treatment.

articles.headline: Adding primaquine sharply cuts P. vivax recurrence in Pakistan
articles.title_original: Efficacy of artemether lumefantrine vs chloroquine for the treatment of Plasmodium Vivax infection in Pakistan.
articles.authors: Khan S, Muqtadir J, Abbas SA, Ayoob K, Wahab A, Zaidi SHM, Das B, Bhatti S, Vohra EA
articles.details: In a prospective observational cohort of 354 malaria patients in Karachi, Pakistan, primaquine-containing regimens had lower recurrence than chloroquine (CQ) or artemether–lumefantrine (AL) monotherapy. By Day 28, recurrent P. vivax infection occurred in 10/98 (10.2%) with CQ, 17/84 (20.2%) with AL, 1/93 (1.1%) with CQ+PQ, and 3/79 (3.8%) with AL+PQ. By six months, recurrence reached 15/98 (15.3%), 27/84 (32.2%), 1/93 (1.1%), and 5/79 (6.3%), respectively. In unadjusted Cox models, AL monotherapy carried a higher recurrence hazard than CQ, while primaquine-containing regimens had lower hazards than the corresponding monotherapies.

articles.headline: Study protocol: multicentric malaria elimination research across India
articles.title_original: Multicentric longitudinal study on malaria burden, vector bionomics and health system assessment in diverse eco-epidemiological settings in the context of malaria elimination in India: study protocol.
articles.authors: Rahul A, Thiruvengadam K, Sharma R, Jabir M, Viswan A, Jency PJ, Bharadwaj N, Muthukumaravel S, Shriram AN, Raju HK, Kannan S, Singh M, Bhuyan PJ, Jain T, Rahi M
articles.details: This is a study protocol for a longitudinal, multicentric study across 12 districts in 10 states of India, covering about 25,000 individuals in varied ecological settings (urban, periurban, rural, forest-foothill, coastal). Two village clusters of 1000 people per district will be enrolled. A baseline mass survey will estimate prevalence using bivalent RDTs and microscopy, with PCR for low-density parasitaemia in RDT-negative subsets. Participants are followed one year with monthly screening, plus sociobehavioural, health-system, entomological (vector bionomics, insecticide resistance), and environmental data collection. No results are reported; the aim is context-specific evidence for India's 2030 elimination target.

articles.headline: P. falciparum stomatin-like protein forms mitochondrial complex with metalloprotease
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum stomatin-like protein forms a putative complex with a metalloprotease in distinct mitochondrial loci.
articles.authors: Verhoef JMJ, Bekkering ET, Boshoven C, Hannon M, Evers F, Proellochs NI, Spruijt CG, Kooij TWA
articles.details: This laboratory study characterized the stomatin-like protein (STOML), an SPFH-family protein localizing to the Plasmodium falciparum mitochondrion. Deletion of P. falciparum STOML caused a significant growth defect and slower asexual blood-stage development, while sexual-stage development was unaffected; knockouts were not more sensitive to respiratory chain-targeting drugs. Endogenous epitope tagging revealed punctate localization at branching points and endings of the mitochondrial network. STOML resides in a large protein complex, and pulldown experiments identified the zinc-dependent metalloprotease FtsH as a likely interaction partner. AlphaFold2 predicts STOML resembles bacterial HflK/C, suggesting a STOML-FtsH complex facilitates blood-stage development.

articles.headline: No true association between loiasis and malaria in Gabon
articles.title_original: There Is No Association Between Loiasis and Malaria: Findings from a Secondary Analysis of a Cross-Sectional Survey in Rural Gabon.
articles.authors: Werner J, Zoleko-Manego R, Mombo-Ngoma G, Ramharter M, Mischlinger J
articles.details: A secondary analysis of a cross-sectional survey conducted in Gabon (2015-2016), enrolling 947 participants of all ages, assessed the association between malaria and loiasis. In crude analyses, malaria showed a borderline protective association with loiasis (OR 0.67; 95% CI 0.45-1.01; p=0.0521). After adjustment for confounders, particularly age, this disappeared (adjusted OR 1.31; 95% CI 0.81-2.11; p=0.276). Malaria predominantly occurred in children and loiasis mainly in older individuals, so the apparent crude association likely reflects differing age distributions rather than a true biological interaction. Findings do not support an association between the two diseases in this setting.

articles.headline: Imported malaria cases analyzed in malaria-free Tizi-Ouzou, Algeria
articles.title_original: Imported Malaria in a Malaria-Free Region: A Retrospective Analysis of Epidemiological, Clinical, and Therapeutic Patterns in Tizi-Ouzou, Algeria (2018-2024).
articles.authors: Yousfi S, Belaid D, Salhi O, Touhami NAK, Ouchene N, Elias N, Moussaoui L, Saidani S, Silem C, Seklaoui N, Bouchaib H
articles.details: A retrospective descriptive study at the University Hospital of Tizi-Ouzou, Algeria (2018-2024) examined laboratory-confirmed imported malaria diagnosed by Giemsa-stained smears. Among 106 individuals screened, 16 were confirmed (positivity rate 15.1%), with cases rising notably from 2022 to 2024 (40% positivity in 2024). Most patients were men (87.5%) and adults aged 20-50 (87.5%), mainly Algerian nationals returning from West Africa. P. falciparum accounted for 87.5% of cases, often with low parasitemia (<4% in 68.8%); none used chemoprophylaxis. Uncomplicated malaria was 78.6% and severe 21.4%; common abnormalities were anemia (92.8%), thrombocytopenia (42.8%), and hepatic cytolysis (43%). Mefloquine was most prescribed (71.4%).

articles.headline: Malaria cases rising again in Haryana, India despite elimination progress
articles.title_original: Malaria Surge in Haryana - an early warning to relook ongoing elimination efforts.
articles.authors: Kumari A, Kant R
