articles.details: A literature review of malaria epidemiology in Haryana State, India over five decades (134 articles found, 18 relevant) combined with NCVBDC annual data from 2018-2024. Cases fell from 3,149 to 52 (a 98.34% decline) by 2022 but then rose to 270 cases in 2024. The authors interpret this as an early-warning rising trend, possibly driven by climate-related factors affecting mosquito proliferation or gaps in health-system vector control, and call for strengthened surveillance, clinical management, and vector control to sustain the 2030 elimination goal.

articles.headline: Imported P. vivax malaria dominates in South-West Delhi fever clinic
articles.title_original: Imported Malaria in South-West Delhi: Five-year Epidemiological Trends (2020-2024) from a Fever Clinic and Implications for Urban Malaria Elimination.
articles.authors: Kumar M, Pal O, Saini S, Savargaonkar D, Anvikar A, Bharti PK, Nitika N
articles.details: Analysis of clinical records from a Fever Clinic at ICMR-NIMR, New Delhi, screening febrile patients by rapid diagnostic test and microscopy, with cases classified as imported or indigenous using travel history. Among 7,257 fever cases, 58 (0.8%) were malaria-positive; P. vivax accounted for 94.8% (55/58) and P. falciparum 3.4% (2/58). Males were 70.7% and ages 18-45 years 60.3%. Imported cases were 51.7% (30/58), rising from 52.2% in 2020 to 66.7% in 2024 as indigenous cases declined. Authors urge cross-notification, case-based surveillance, and inter-state collaboration.

articles.headline: ITN use gaps persist among Nigerian children and pregnant women
articles.title_original: Insecticide-Treated Net Utilization Among Children and Pregnant Women: 2021 Nigeria Malaria Indicator Survey Analysis.
articles.authors: Omole EB, Ndukwe OS
articles.details: Secondary analysis of the 2021 Nigeria Malaria Indicator Survey examining sociodemographic factors associated with insecticide-treated net (ITN) use among children under five and pregnant women, using bivariate and multivariate analyses. The survey covered 13,727 households, 14,476 women aged 15-49, and 12,742 children under five. ITN ownership was 56% of households, while 50% of women and 41.2% of children slept under an ITN. Use was higher in rural areas (p=0.001), the North West and North East regions (p<0.001), lower wealth quintiles (p<0.001), children under 12 months (p<0.001), and less-educated pregnant women. Gaps remain among older children, wealthier households, and more-educated women.

articles.headline: Rainfall drives malaria incidence in Yumbe District, Uganda
articles.title_original: Climate variability and malaria incidence trends in Yumbe District, West Nile Sub-region of Uganda (2017-2021).
articles.authors: Ninsiima LR, Musiitwa R, Nanyunja Z, Muleme J, Maasaba C, Anule T, Musoke D
articles.details: A retrospective ecological time-series study analyzing malaria incidence (2017-2021) in Yumbe district, Uganda using District Health Information System reports and national meteorological data (daily temperature and rainfall), with Kendall's tau correlations, multiple linear and Poisson regressions with lags, seasonal decomposition, cross-correlation, and ARIMAX modelling. A total of 2,066,711 malaria cases were reported. Rainfall was positively associated with incidence at a one-month lag (β=0.38, p<0.05) and strongly led cases by one month (r=0.759, p<0.001), while minimum temperature was inversely associated (β=-0.29, p<0.05); maximum temperature showed no significant effect. Authors recommend integrating climate data into surveillance and early-warning systems.

articles.headline: Inpatient test-and-treat malaria care falls short in Kano hospitals
articles.title_original: Quality of inpatient test-and-treat malaria case-management in public and private hospitals in Kano State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Getachew D, Ogbulafor N, Shekarau E, Musa B, Yusuf A, Haruna S, Oladosu OO, Oresanya O, Maxwell K, Zurovac D
articles.details: A cross-sectional assessment at 18 public and private hospitals in Kano State, Nigeria, including hospital assessments, 72 health-worker interviews, review of August 2024 paediatric and medical ward admission files, and re-checking of 211 archived malaria slides against expert readings, covering 2,814 suspected malaria admissions. Most hospitals (94.4%) offered parasitological diagnosis and stocked injectable artesunate and ACT. Composite test-and-treat performance was 39.3%, higher for children than adults (45.7% vs 26.5%) and public vs private (39.8% vs 30.8%). 73.7% were tested, 90.2% of severe malaria received artesunate, but only 30.0% of artesunate-treated patients were prescribed ACT and non-compliance with negative results was 75.8%. Routine microscopy sensitivity, specificity, PPV and NPV versus expert readings were 93.2%, 42.5%, 29.9% and 95.9%. Clinicians can trust negative slides but should be cautious with positive results.

articles.headline: Global review maps P. falciparum drug-resistance markers and rising artemisinin resistance
articles.title_original: The Global Landscape of Plasmodium falciparum Drug Resistance Markers, 2005-2025: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
articles.authors: Habarugira F, Batamuriza J, Ndahimana R, Minega JN, Dieng MM, Mutsaka-Makuvaza MJ, Daba TM, Idaghdour Y, Mutesa L
articles.details: A PRISMA systematic review and meta-analysis (PROSPERO: CRD4202511098991) searched PubMed, Web of Science, Scopus, Google Scholar, and gray literature for studies on P. falciparum antimalarial resistance markers. From 1972 records, 261 studies from 64 countries were included. Pooled prevalence was highest for pfdhfr (85.7%), then pfcrt (78.0%), pfdhps (73.7%), pfmdr1 (60.5%), and pfk13 (45.0%), with high heterogeneity (I2>95%). Rising pfk13 since 2012 signals emerging artemisinin resistance, while persistent pfdhfr/pfdhps mutations reflect ongoing sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine pressure. The authors call for integrating molecular surveillance into national malaria programs.

articles.headline: Correction notice for liver-stage Plasmodium transcriptome profiling article
articles.title_original: Correction: Transcriptome profiling of intrahepatocytic Plasmodium and their host hepatocytes based on the infection phase and the zonation of the liver.
articles.authors: Mo Z, Chen Y, Qin Y, Song J
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: New imaging platform tracks single-parasite drug killing and artemisinin dormancy
articles.title_original: High throughput quantitative tracking of Plasmodium falciparum clonal blood stage parasite growth and applications for antimalarial drug discovery.
articles.authors: Cheng Z, Ru L, Hoang A, Wang M, Ganjikunta S, Maher SP, Kyle DE
articles.details: Researchers developed Quantitative Tracking After Chemotherapy Exposure (qTRACE), a high-throughput, AI-assisted platform that measures cytotoxic and cytostatic drug effects at single-parasite resolution by tracking individual P. falciparum parasites growing into colonies. Applying qTRACE to artemisinin exposure, they found that up to 50% of viable parasites arose directly from recovery of artemisinin-induced dormancy. A next-generation version integrated deep-learning segmentation to link time-lapse phenotypes of label-free live parasites. Results confirmed viability of dormant ring stages and that recovery rates differ between artemisinin-susceptible and -resistant P. falciparum.

articles.headline: Cambodian P. falciparum relatedness shaped by geography and occupation
articles.title_original: Genetic relatedness of Cambodian Plasmodium falciparum isolates is driven by geography and occupation.
articles.authors: Rowley E, Guo B, Wojnarski M, Spring MD, Sriwichai S, Vesely BA, Silva JC, Waters NC, Thaloengsok S, Saingam P, Chaisatit C, Lertsethtakarn P, Sok S, Prom S, Chaudhury S, Lon C, Lek D, Saunders D, O'Connor TD, Takala-Harrison S
articles.details: Using identity-by-descent (IBD) from whole-genome sequencing, researchers estimated pairwise genetic relatedness among 107 P. falciparum isolates collected from military personnel and farmers in Cambodia between 2014 and 2016. Mean IBD sharing was high (40%), with greater relatedness in Oddar Meanchey than Kratie province (p<2.2e-16). Within Oddar Meanchey, different-occupation pairs shared 7.2% less genome IBD than farmer pairs. Highly related clusters (>90% IBD) were associated with occupation, K13 mutation (C580Y vs R539T), and PfCRT mutation (p=0.0025, 0.0001, 0.0001). Authors suggest occupation-specific transmission sources and tailoring interventions to local micro-epidemiology.

articles.headline: Spatial modeling identifies central Anhui as malaria re-transmission risk area
articles.title_original: Assessing the risk of malaria re-transmission in Anhui Province, China: an integration of spatiotemporal scan statistics and ecological niche modeling.
articles.authors: Liu B, Zhang T, Xue J, Huo S, He H, Tan X, Li W, Li S
articles.details: Combining spatiotemporal scan statistics and ecological niche modeling, this study identified the central region of Anhui Province as being at highest risk for malaria re-transmission, using imported case and demographic data from 2017–2023 and 2023 vector surveillance. Malaria hotspots stayed largely consistent across years (except 2021, P<0.05), with persistent clusters in Yaohai, Luyang, Baohe, Feidong, Changfeng, Feixi, and Shushan. The ensemble niche model performed well (AUC 0.943, TSS 0.759), placing suitable An. sinensis habitat mainly in central and part of the southern regions, driven by the annual vegetation index, night-light brightness, and wettest-month precipitation. The authors recommend strengthened surveillance and seasonal vector control in epidemic spots during the mosquito activity season.

articles.headline: Pediatric malaria linked to high rate of lasting neurological damage
articles.title_original: Malaria in Children is Associated with a High Rate of Neurological Sequelae and Need for Rehabilitative Therapies in Germany.
articles.authors: Adolph JE, Pentek C, Rink L, Held CJ, Della Marina A, Gangfuß A, Kölbel H, Dziobaka J, Rath PM, Verhasselt HL, Felderhoff-Müser U, Dohna-Schwake C, Goretzki SC
articles.details: This retrospective single-centre study analyzed 11 pediatric malaria patients (median age 9.5 years) treated at a tertiary hospital in 2023, with P. falciparum in 91% of cases. Eight (72.7%) had severe malaria and artesunate was first-line therapy in 64%. Despite prompt guideline-appropriate treatment, residual neurological symptoms occurred in 82% of patients and neurocognitive deficits in 44% of those tested; EEG abnormalities appeared in four patients, three developed epilepsy, and MRI showed cytotoxic corpus callosum and trigonal lesions in three. At 12 months 77.8% showed clinical improvement. Authors stress the need for coordinated follow-up even in non-endemic settings.

articles.headline: Out-of-pocket payments hinder malaria care in developing countries
articles.title_original: Implications of out-of-pocket payments for the control of malaria in developing countries: A narrative review.
articles.authors: Obeagu EI, Obeagu GU
articles.details: This narrative review synthesized peer-reviewed articles, reports, and policy documents to assess how out-of-pocket (OOP) payments affect malaria treatment in developing countries, examining household economic impacts, barriers to care, and alternative financing. The authors found that OOP payments significantly delay treatment-seeking and disproportionately burden low-income and rural households, contributing to underuse of insecticide-treated nets and diagnostics and worsening health inequities. They conclude that reducing OOP payments through government subsidies, community health insurance, donor-funded programs, and public-private partnerships is critical to improving malaria control and equitable access.

articles.headline: Diabetes-malaria comorbidity tied to higher parasitaemia and stronger antibody response
articles.title_original: Malaria-diabetes comorbidity is linked to higher parasitaemia and enhanced IgG response to malaria vaccine candidate antigens.
articles.authors: Agyare G, Courtin D, Asamoah Sakyi S, Nakotey GK, Adjeley Frempong N, Barnie PA, Tchum SK, Victor Nuvor S, Amoani B
articles.details: This hospital-based case-control study in the Central Region of Ghana enrolled 144 participants (40 with diabetes, 25 with diabetes plus malaria, 41 with malaria only, 38 controls) and measured IgG to vaccine candidate antigens (GLURP-R2, GLURP-R0, MSP3, MSP1, AMA-1, CSP) by ELISA. The malaria-diabetes comorbidity group had significantly higher parasitaemia than malaria alone (median 1702 vs 932, p=0.02) and elevated IgG to GLURP-R2 (1.60-fold, p=0.01), GLURP-R0, and MSP1; responses to MSP3, AMA1, and CSP did not differ. Authors suggest type 2 diabetes may enhance parasite multiplication while augmenting antibody responses, with implications for vaccine development.

articles.headline: Dry-season mass drug administration prevents chronic malaria in schoolchildren
articles.title_original: Impacts of Seasonal Mass Drug Administration in School-Aged Children on Chronic Malaria Infections in Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Paré IGL, Da DF, Guigma F, Bazié T, Somé BM, Djègbè NDC, Cissé F, Ouédraogo K, Somé AF, Kaboré J, Churcher TS, Dabiré RK
articles.details: This study in rural Burkina Faso conducted monthly dry-season parasitological surveys in two cohorts of school-aged children: one uninfected at baseline and untreated, and one parasitemic asymptomatic group given a full course of artemether-lumefantrine, with microscopy, PCR, msp1/msp2 typing, pfcrt/pfmdr1 genotyping, and entomological monitoring. Among untreated uninfected children 93% remained infection-free, and 91% of treated children stayed negative, while persistent infections were stable at 5-9% in both cohorts; the same haplotypes persisted within participants and drug-resistance markers were at low frequency. Authors conclude transmission is interrupted during the dry season and early MDA can effectively prevent infection.

articles.headline: Low R21/Matrix vaccine awareness but high acceptance among Lagos caregivers
articles.title_original: R21/MATRIX malaria vaccine: awareness, perception, acceptability, and willingness to pay among caregivers of under-five children in endemic communities in Lagos, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Agboola P, Precious DN, Adebisi YA, Kingsley OO, Olaniyi P, Ezekiel A, Ode OP, Aderounmu B, Olusogo AP, Opeyemi FI, Adeyemi-Benson OS
articles.details: This community-based cross-sectional survey of 372 caregivers of under-five children across five malaria-endemic communities in Lagos, Nigeria assessed awareness, perception, acceptability, and willingness to pay for the R21/MATRIX malaria vaccine, using questionnaires and binary logistic regression. Awareness was low at 9.7% (mainly via the internet), yet 98.1% believed the vaccine safe and effective, 76.6% would definitely vaccinate their child, and 71.2% were willing to pay—51.9% between ₦1,001 and ₦5,000 per dose. Gender, marital status, and relationship to the child were associated with awareness, while higher income and guardianship increased willingness to pay. Authors call for targeted community awareness campaigns.

articles.headline: Most Ethiopian antenatal-care facilities lack full malaria service readiness
articles.title_original: Malaria service readiness and associated factors among health facilities that provide antenatal care services in Ethiopia: a cross-sectional study using generalised estimating equation analysis.
articles.authors: Bogale KA, Asemahagn MA, Gelaye KA, Muchie KF, Engedaw HA, Azage M
articles.details: Only 15.7% (95% CI 13.8–18.0) of 1156 public and private health facilities providing antenatal care (ANC) in Ethiopia demonstrated full malaria service readiness, while 14.3% had none of the key components. This nationally representative cross-sectional study used data from the 2021/2022 Ethiopia Service Provision Assessment. Readiness varied markedly by region (Somali region more likely ready, AOR 1.53, 95% CI 1.19–1.95), and governance factors were strong predictors: a client suggestion box (AOR 1.60), recent report submission (AOR 2.79), and monthly staff meetings (AOR 1.64). The authors conclude readiness is low and unevenly distributed, recommending stronger supply chains, staff training, and governance.

articles.headline: Low malaria infection but notable serological exposure across Ethiopian towns
articles.title_original: Heterogeneity of malaria transmission in urban settings in Ethiopia: A seroprevalence and risk factor analysis.
articles.authors: Teka H, Fekadu S, Ejigu LA, Sisay S, Abdo M, Demisse M, Mamo H, Drakeley C, Byrne I, Medhin G, Golassa L, Gadisa E, Tadesse FG
articles.details: A community-based cross-sectional survey of 912 individuals from 196 randomly selected households in three Ethiopian towns (Adama, Metehara, Awash Sebat Kilo) used RDTs, qPCR and serology to assess P. falciparum and P. vivax exposure. Prevalence was low at 0.78% by RDT and 4% by qPCR (1.7% P. falciparum, 2.3% P. vivax), while seroprevalence was 19.4% (149/767) for P. falciparum and 18% (139/767) for P. vivax. Awash Sebat Kilo showed the highest recent-marker exposure (36.5% Pf; 28% Pv) versus Adama (2.8% each). Proximity to breeding sites (OR=2.8) and age over 15 years (OR=4.0) were significant risk factors. Authors highlight marked heterogeneity and the need for serological surveillance amid An. stephensi expansion.

articles.headline: Temperature and rainfall thresholds shape malaria incidence in Colombia
articles.title_original: Integrating causal inference and machine learning to quantify climate-malaria relationships: Evidence of temperature and rainfall thresholds from Colombian municipalities.
articles.authors: Gutiérrez JD
articles.details: An ecological observational study quantified causal effects of rainfall and temperature on malaria incidence across 969 Colombian municipalities below 1,600 meters from 2007 to 2023, computing monthly Standardized Incidence Ratios and applying Targeted Maximum Likelihood Estimation guided by directed acyclic graphs. Across 1,075,112 reported cases, the relationship was non-linear, with an optimal temperature of 25°C and approximately 37 mm of rainfall yielding the highest incidence. Negative control tests revealed residual confounding (p<0.05) and E-values indicated low-to-moderate tolerance to unmeasured confounders (1.14-1.48). Authors support integrating these thresholds into early-warning systems.

articles.headline: Health-system gaps challenge malaria elimination in Greater Mekong Subregion
articles.title_original: Strengthening the health systems at national level for malaria elimination in the Greater Mekong Subregion countries: a qualitative study.
articles.authors: Htike W, Oo WH, Bennett CM, Agius PA, Barry AE, Fowkes FJI
articles.details: A multi-country qualitative study in the Greater Mekong Subregion conducted semi-structured interviews with 39 stakeholders—national policymakers (n=5), Ministry of Health basic health staff (n=12), implementing-partner managers and field supervisors (n=16) and technical-agency personnel (n=6)—analyzed thematically against the WHO six health system building blocks. Stakeholders emphasized political commitment, targeted interventions for high-risk groups, reliable forecasting and supply chains, skilled workforce, quality assurance, expanded diagnostics and medicines, and mandatory G6PD testing. Identified barriers included incomplete private-sector reporting, inexperienced workforce, supply-chain constraints, declining donor funding and poor regulatory compliance. Authors conclude comprehensive systems-thinking assessment is essential for transitioning from control to elimination.

articles.headline: PvCyRPA C-terminal region proposed as P. vivax vaccine candidate
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity, natural selection, and immunological features of the Plasmodium vivax CyRPA protein: Implications for vaccine development.
articles.authors: Garzón-Ospina D, Buitrago SP, Cepeda-Riaño N, Castro-Cavadía CJ, Yasnot-Acosta MF
articles.details: This study analyzed the genetic diversity, natural selection and immunological features of the Plasmodium vivax CyRPA (Cysteine-Rich Protective Antigen) locus using 950 pvcyrpa sequences, including 42 newly obtained isolates from Colombia. The gene showed high nucleotide diversity shaped by natural selection and recombination. In-silico predictions identified B- and T-cell epitopes in mainly polymorphic regions with strong predicted binding to multiple HLA alleles, overlapping regions previously shown to elicit immune responses, and immune simulation of a multiepitope C-terminal construct predicted a robust humoral memory profile. Authors highlight the conserved C-terminal region of PvCyRPA as a broadly reactive vaccine candidate warranting in-vitro and in-vivo validation.

articles.headline: Review: molecular control of Plasmodium gametocyte formation
articles.title_original: Molecular mechanisms involved in Plasmodium gametocytogenesis.
articles.authors: Scovino AM, Oliveira-da-Silva RS, Almeida-da-Silva J, Mendes KD, da Silva-Junior EB, Decote-Ricardo D, Freire-de-Lima L, Freire-de-Lima CG, Totino PRR, Morrot A
articles.details: This review consolidates recent advances on the molecular mechanisms regulating gametocytogenesis—the differentiation of asexual blood-stage parasites into transmissible gametocytes—with a focus on P. falciparum. It describes how sexual commitment is triggered by environmental stressors such as depletion of lysophosphatidylcholine (LysoPC) and orchestrated by the AP2-G transcription factor as a master switch, regulated epigenetically via histone H3K9 trimethylation (H3K9me3), heterochromatin protein 1 (HP1), and GDV1. It further covers post-transcriptional control by RNA-binding proteins PfPuf1 and PfPuf2 and epitranscriptomic mRNA methylation (m5C and m6A). The authors argue this understanding is key to identifying transmission-blocking drug targets and vaccines.

articles.headline: Non-falciparum species contribute to malaria in Zanzibar
articles.title_original: Understanding the Epidemiology of Malaria in Zanzibar Through Molecular and Serological Analysis of Samples Collected During Reactive Case Detection.
articles.authors: Goel V, Hassan W, Murphy C, Choloi BB, Ali M, Mohamed B, Zacharia A, Dominick MC, Thwai K, Mohammed S, Shija SJ, Bailey JA, Björkman A, Ngasala BE, Rogier E, Juliano JJ, Lin JT
articles.details: This serological and molecular study examined falciparum and non-falciparum malaria epidemiology in Zanzibar using plasma from dried blood spots collected during reactive case detection across Unguja island. IgG responses to Plasmodium MSP1-19kD antigens were measured by multiplex bead-based immunoassay, and active infections detected by species-specific real-time PCR in 1618 participants across 35 reactive case detections. Overall, 35.3% had exposure to any malaria species, predominantly P. falciparum (29.8%), with lower seroprevalence for P. ovale (5.8%), P. malariae (5.9%), and P. vivax (5.9%); active infections occurred in 6.0% (P. falciparum 4.6%). Travel to mainland Tanzania was a dominant risk factor for seropositivity to all species. The authors conclude non-falciparum species contribute significantly to local epidemiology and may be inadequately addressed by P. falciparum–focused control.

articles.headline: Proline transporter PfApiAT2 essential for mosquito-stage transmission
articles.title_original: PfApiAT2 is a proline transporter essential for the transmission of Plasmodium falciparum by the mosquito vector.
articles.authors: Wirth D, Khushu M, Kissel RC, Kauffman J, Taccheri C, Singh N, Summers R, Plant L, Catteruccia F, Bopp S
articles.details: This experimental study identifies PfApiAT2, an amino acid transporter, as a proline-specific transporter essential for early P. falciparum oocyst development in Anopheles gambiae. Halofuginone-resistant pfapiat2-mutant parasites formed stunted oocysts severely defective in sporozoite production, and PfApiAT2-knockout parasites showed a complete block in sporogony, forming oocysts that stalled and degenerated. This growth defect was rescued by nutrient supplementation to the mosquito vector. The authors conclude PfApiAT2 represents a nutritional vulnerability in P. falciparum transmission, revealing the parasite's critical dependence on proline scavenged from its mosquito host.

articles.headline: Kdr resistance allele linked to higher Microsporidia MB in mosquitoes
articles.title_original: The L1014F Kdr mutation is associated with a higher prevalence and load of the Plasmodium-blocking symbiont Microsporidia MB In Anopheles Gambiae s.l. In Benin.
articles.authors: Sovi A, Ahouandjinou MJ, Aïkpon R, Totongnon GS, Yovogan B, Syme T, Dembélé A, Koukpo CZ, Sagbohan H, Ossè R, Agbo-Ola A, Gnanguenon V, Toffodji U, Megido RC, Hougbe SZ, Zoungbédji DM, Konkon AK, Cook J, Padonou GG, Protopopoff N, Akogbéto MC, Messenger LA, Adoha CJ
articles.details: This field study in southern Benin examined the association between the L1014F kdr insecticide-resistance mutation and the Plasmodium transmission-blocking endosymbiont Microsporidia MB in Anopheles gambiae s.l. collected by human landing catches across 60 villages, using PCR genotyping, ELISA, and qPCR. The complex (N=1040) was 93.7% An. coluzzii; Microsporidia MB prevalence was 1.6% in An. coluzzii and 2.2% in An. gambiae s.s., and the P. falciparum sporozoite rate was 2% in An. coluzzii. Microsporidia MB was absent in susceptible kdr-SS mosquitoes but present in kdr-RS and kdr-RR (1.9%), with higher mean DNA load in kdr-RR (23.23 ng/µl) than kdr-RS (10.13 ng/µl); elevation and soil explained habitat suitability (78% and 20%). The authors conclude the resistant allele is associated with higher Microsporidia MB prevalence and load.

articles.headline: Review of malaria epidemiology and control, focused on Africa
articles.title_original: Malaria: a review on its current epidemiological status and management strategies.
articles.authors: Okello G, Aucamp M
articles.details: This review summarizes the current epidemiological status and management of malaria, with Africa bearing the highest burden and P. falciparum the leading cause of malaria mortality. It notes a rise in clinically atypical malaria and asymptomatic carriers increasing misdiagnosis risk, while describing control gains from insecticide-treated nets, indoor residual spraying, rapid diagnostic tests, intermittent preventive treatment, artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs), and pediatric vaccines. The WHO African Region reported a 40% reduction in malaria incidence and 60% decline in mortality between 2000 and 2022. The authors highlight threats including drug and insecticide resistance, climate change, and changing mosquito behavior, and recommend cross-border control, seasonal malaria chemoprevention, resistance markers, new antimalarials, and scaled-up RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M vaccines.

articles.headline: E205D mutation in CYP6P3 drives pyrethroid resistance in Anopheles gambiae
articles.title_original: The E205D mutation in CYP6P3 drives pyrethroid insecticide resistance in the African malaria mosquito vector Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Kengne-Ouafo JA, Kouamo MFM, Muhammad A, Tepa A, Ntadoun S, Mugenzi LMJ, Tekoh T, Hearn J, Tchouakui M, Wondji M, Ibrahim SS, Wondji CS
articles.details: This molecular study identified a Glu205→Asp (E205D) point mutation in the CYP6P3 P450 gene as a driver of pyrethroid insecticide resistance in the malaria vector Anopheles gambiae, mapped to a CYP6 haplotype on chromosome 2R near the sodium channel locus. In vitro, the 205D variant metabolized the pyrethroid permethrin with 3.7 times higher catalytic efficiency than the E205 variant, with similar results for α-cypermethrin, and transgenic flies overexpressing 205D showed greater pyrethroid resistance. In field populations the variant correlated with resistance (OR 26.4; P<0.0001) and reduced bed net efficacy, with homozygous resistant mosquitoes surviving PermaNet 3.0 exposure more than susceptible ones (OR 6.1; P=0.011). The 205D variant predominates in West and Central Africa.

articles.headline: Study protocol for malaria and dengue serosurvey in Indonesia's new capital
articles.title_original: A study protocol for a baseline community-based cross-sectional study to investigate malaria and dengue transmission dynamics in a development area of new capital city, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Pradana A, Oktaviani M, Prameswari H, Supriyanto D, Waluyo P, Ndoen E, Rosadi D, Lubis I, Suwarti S, Subekti D, Kusumaningrum T, Carroll M, Djaafara B, de Alwis R, Mishra S, Drakeley C, Leder K, Lechner A, Fornace K, Elyazar I, Surendra H
articles.details: This is a study protocol for a community-based cross-sectional study investigating malaria and dengue transmission in the development area of Indonesia's new capital, Ibu Kota Nusantara (IKN), in East Kalimantan, a malaria and dengue hotspot where land-use changes and population movements pose new vector-borne disease risks including Plasmodium knowlesi. Approximately 2,000 individuals aged over 1 year will be enrolled, with finger-prick blood collected for multiplex bead-based serological assays (malaria species and dengue virus serotypes) and malaria RDTs, alongside demographic, clinical, environmental, and geolocation data. Statistical and geostatistical models will assess seroprevalence, spatial patterns, and risk factors. No results are reported.

articles.headline: Case report: severe P. vivax malaria with pulmonary involvement
articles.title_original: Severe Malaria Due to Plasmodium vivax With Pulmonary Involvement: A Case Report.
articles.authors: Valido T, Goncalves A, Sanches M, Costa Silva T, Pereira C
articles.details: This case report describes a 46-year-old woman from India who had traveled to Portugal for six months and presented with three days of asthenia and fatigue, found febrile and hypoxemic with severe anemia, thrombocytopenia, and ground-glass opacities on chest tomography. She was admitted to the ICU on high-flow oxygen, and P. vivax was identified with a parasitemia of 50% and parasite density of 8,645 parasites/µL. Because IV artesunate was unavailable, she was treated with artemether-lumefantrine and doxycycline, with progressive improvement, oxygen weaning, and cleared parasitemia by the third day. The authors stress high clinical suspicion for P. vivax in patients with relevant exposure.

articles.headline: P. vivax population genetics during decline and rebound in Papua New Guinea
articles.title_original: Population genetics of Plasmodium vivax with transmission decline and rebound in two endemic areas of Papua New Guinea.
articles.authors: Fola AA, Mehra S, Razook Z, Lautu-Gumal D, Nate E, Lee S, Kattenberg JH, Koepfli C, Kazura J, Ome-Kaius M, Laman M, Robinson LJ, Mueller I, Barry AE
articles.details: This population genetic study used a genome-wide SNP barcode to genotype 336 Plasmodium vivax isolates from serial cross-sectional surveys conducted over a decade in East Sepik (2005, 2012, 2016) and Madang Province (2006, 2010, 2014) in Papua New Guinea, examining changes after nationwide LLIN distribution that initially reduced then saw P. vivax resurge. The two provinces showed contrasting patterns: in Madang, complexity of infection, genetic diversity, and population structure varied with prevalence, with a possible bottleneck and clonal expansion at low transmission and rapid recovery on resurgence, whereas East Sepik showed less impact and ongoing transmission of multiple residual lineages. Genetic differentiation between areas increased during decline and reduced with resurgence. The authors conclude long-term sustained control is essential and SNP barcodes provide valuable transmission insights.

articles.headline: GI mucosal disruption in ART-treated SIV/Plasmodium fragile co-infected macaques
articles.title_original: Gastrointestinal Mucosal Disruptions During ART-Treated SIV/Plasmodium fragile Co-Infection.
articles.authors: Nemphos SM, Green HC, Prusak JE, Fell SL, Midkiff C, Rodgers A, Perret J, Goff K, Miller J, Varnado M, Didier K, Valencia N, Moström MJ, Tatum C, Barnes MB, Krzykwa CE, Rowe LA, Allers C, Grasperge B, De Paris K, Maness NJ, Kaur A, Londono-Renteria B, Blair RV, Manuzak JA
articles.details: This animal study examined gastrointestinal mucosal immune disruption during antiretroviral therapy (ART)-treated SIV/Plasmodium fragile co-infection in six adult male rhesus macaques, inoculated with SIVmac239, started on ART at week 8 post-infection, inoculated with P. fragile at week 12, and followed to week 20, with longitudinal assessment of viral loads, parasitemia, and immune cell frequencies. Significant CCR5+ CD4+ T cell decline occurred in the periphery, colon, and duodenum after SIV infection, while neutrophil frequencies were unchanged. Duodenal NET-forming granulocyte frequencies were significantly positively associated with peripheral SIV burden after co-infection but not with parasitemia or CD4+ T cell frequencies. The authors conclude P. fragile co-infection may cause mucosal disruptions contributing to peripheral SIV replication despite ART.

articles.headline: PbSR1 splicing factor binds nuclear and rhoptry proteins in P. berghei
articles.title_original: Identification of serine/arginine-rich splicing factor 1-interacting proteins in Plasmodium berghei.
articles.authors: Niikura M, Koyama Y, Fukuda Y, Kato K, Fukutomi T
articles.details: This laboratory study set out to identify proteins interacting with serine/arginine-rich splicing factor 1 (SR1), which is essential to asexual development of P. falciparum and P. berghei. Transgenic P. berghei ANKA expressing GFP-fused PbSR1 were generated for immunoprecipitation-mass spectrometry, and parasites co-expressing PbSR1::GFP and RhopH2::mCherry were used for western blot and live-cell imaging. PbSR1::GFP signal was stronger in nuclei than cytoplasm, and IP-MS showed PbSR1 interacts with nuclear proteins (RNA-binding protein, small nuclear ribonucleoprotein) and cytoplasmic rhoptry proteins RhopH1/Clag3, RhopH2, and RhopH3. Cytoplasmic co-localization of PbSR1 and RhopH2 was seen in trophozoites and gametocytes but not mature schizonts or merozoites. The authors conclude PbSR1 interacts with nuclear proteins and rhoptry proteins.

articles.headline: Trial protocol: transfluthrin spatial repellent against malaria in Ugandan children
articles.title_original: Evaluation of the protective efficacy of a transfluthrin-based spatial repellent product to reduce malaria prevalence in Uganda: study protocol for a cluster-randomised double-blinded control trial-the Mossie-GO trial.
articles.authors: Dennehy J, Dyall W, Jenkins A, Bradley J, Sserwanga A, Kigozi R, Bwanika JB, Nuwa A, Mawejje H, Turner MA, Wallace R, Lyle FGA, Hiscox A, Livesey AK, Logan JG, Achan J, Jones RT
articles.details: This is a study protocol for a placebo-controlled, double-blinded cluster-randomised controlled trial (Mossie-GO) evaluating a transfluthrin-based spatial repellent device (Mossie-GO™) to reduce malaria in Uganda. Fifty-six clusters are randomly assigned 1:1 to intervention or placebo-control arms, with the primary endpoint being malaria infection prevalence in children ≤5 years; 100 children at baseline and 60 children per cluster at 6 and 12 months will be sampled, each from a different household. Secondary objectives include entomological transmission correlates, user acceptance, and airborne transfluthrin concentration. No results are reported as this is a protocol; the trial is registered as NCT06232954.

articles.headline: SIMPLseq: sensitive P. falciparum genotyping with PCR contamination detection
articles.title_original: SIMPLseq: a high-sensitivity Plasmodium falciparum genotyping and PCR contamination tracking tool.
articles.authors: Schwabl P, Amaya-Romero JE, Kelley KA, Manrique P, Murphy SC, Crompton PD, Neafsey DE
articles.details: This methods paper describes SIMPLseq, a new 6-locus P. falciparum amplicon-sequencing 'miniplex' optimized for high-sensitivity, low-parasitemia genotyping, with an inline-barcode contamination detection system applied during first-round PCR. Panel diversity was assessed using public whole-genome sequencing data, and mock samples were used to estimate sensitivity and precision relative to the 4CAST miniplex. SIMPLseq distinguished 96.0% of randomly drawn sample pairs from 12 subnational sample sets, achieved 100% average locus detection at ≥0.5 parasites/μl and ≥50% at 0.25 and 0.125 parasites/μl, with zero false-positive haplotypes at a 1% detection limit across 25 replicates. Sentinel inline barcoding correctly identified all 24 deliberate contaminations and 39 unintentional contaminations in a 1420-sample run of dried blood spots from Mali. The authors conclude SIMPLseq extends the malaria genomic epidemiology toolkit using open-source reagents without a costly pre-amplification step.

articles.headline: Anopheles funestus distribution and malaria transmission role in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Updating Anopheles funestus group members in Burkina Faso: species distribution and contribution to malaria transmission.
articles.authors: Zongo ON, Toé I, Kientega M, Yemien SOG, Sawadogo G, Kaboré H, Agolinou AAM, Traore N, Kiendrebeogo E, Millogo AA, Niang A, Namountougou M, Lanfrancotti A, Nolan T, Maiga H, Diabaté A
articles.details: This entomological survey investigated the spatial distribution of the Anopheles funestus group and its contribution to malaria transmission in Burkina Faso, sampling 67 sites monthly from October to December 2022, with An. funestus detected at 22 sites. Mosquitoes were collected by pyrethrum spray catches and identified by morphology, with PCR used for species identification, blood meal sources, and P. falciparum infection. Of 617 An. funestus group members collected across eight regions, An. funestus s.s. was most prevalent at up to 95% (589/617), concentrated in Hauts-Bassins (30%, 177/589) and Sud-Ouest (55%, 329/589); An. rivulorum/An. rivulorum-like hybrids were 3.57% (22/617). Zoophilic behaviour was high (64.3% of blood sources), and P. falciparum infection was detected in 5.76% (33/572), with 5.6% (32/572) in An. funestus s.s. and 0.174% (1/572) in An. rivulorum-like. The authors emphasize strengthening vector surveillance and incorporating An. funestus into vector control.

articles.headline: Rivastigmine plus arteether improves survival in mouse cerebral malaria model
articles.title_original: Evaluation of clinically approved neuroprotective drugs as adjunct therapies in experimental cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Joshi P, Shaham SH, Shivahare R, Yadav K, Sharma A, Parul, Shukla S, Tripathi R
articles.details: This study evaluated six clinically approved neuroprotective drugs (topiramate, rasagiline, vinpocetine, cilostazol, piracetam, rivastigmine) as adjunct therapies for cerebral malaria, testing antimalarial and anti-cerebral malaria activity in vitro against P. falciparum and in a P. berghei ANKA-infected C57BL/6 mouse model of experimental cerebral malaria. Vinpocetine, cilostazol, and rivastigmine inhibited parasite growth without toxicity to Vero or BB19 cells or human red blood cells. Early oral treatment with cilostazol, piracetam, or rivastigmine delayed cerebral malaria symptom onset and significantly improved survival versus untreated controls; when started after the first neurological signs, only rivastigmine combined with α/β-arteether markedly improved parasite clearance and survival compared with either alone. The authors conclude that combining rivastigmine with α/β-arteether may be a practical, low-cost adjunct therapy for cerebral malaria, pending further mechanistic and clinical study.

articles.headline: Cerebral malaria and severe anemia linked to lasting cognitive impairment in children
articles.title_original: Cerebral Malaria and Severe Malarial Anemia, but not Other Forms of Severe Malaria, Are Associated With Long-Term Cognitive Impairment.
articles.authors: Bangirana P, Bond C, Namazzi R, Datta D, Mellencamp KA, Opoka RO, John CC
articles.details: A prospective cohort study followed 600 Ugandan children aged 6 months to 4 years with one or more of the five most common forms of severe malaria (cerebral malaria, respiratory distress, multiple seizures, severe malarial anemia, or prostration), assessing overall cognition, attention, and associative memory 12 months after discharge versus community children. Children with cerebral malaria (mean difference -0.66, P=.001) or severe malarial anemia (-0.71, P<.001) had significantly lower cognition scores, with cognitive impairment in 10.6% (5/47) and 12.1% (17/140) respectively versus 1.9% (2/104) of community children. Other severe malaria forms, and attention and memory domains, showed no significant differences.

articles.headline: Commentary: not all severe malaria forms impair long-term cognition
articles.title_original: Not All Forms of Severe Malaria Impair Long-term Cognition-More Characterization, More Questions.
articles.authors: Seydel KB, Ray STJ
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Qualitative study of malaria patients' symptom experiences in Limpopo, South Africa
articles.title_original: Experiences of people diagnosed with malaria regarding signs and symptoms at a selected village in the Vhembe District, Limpopo province, South Africa.
articles.authors: Kwinda W, Mudzweda AD, Luhalima T
articles.details: A qualitative exploratory, descriptive, and contextual study used semi-structured interviews with purposively selected participants diagnosed with malaria at Mhinga Village in the Vhembe District, Limpopo province, South Africa, to explore their experiences of signs and symptoms. Thematic analysis identified two subthemes: experiences with signs and symptoms, and circumstances prompting immediate consultation. Participants experienced symptoms according to body systems, and certain circumstances drove them to consult immediately. The authors concluded the study increased awareness of malaria prevention strategies that may lower transmission.

articles.headline: Higher socioeconomic status linked to lower malaria burden in coastal Tanzania
articles.title_original: Socioeconomic health equity with malaria burden: quantifying slope (Relative) index of inequity in malaria infection outcomes in the Southeastern-coastal Tanzania.
articles.authors: Liu L, Lu S, Ding W, Wang Z, Abdulla S, Lv S, Li S, Chaki PP, Zhou XN, Mlacha YP, Wang D
articles.details: Using 2019 baseline survey data from the China-Tanzania Demonstration Project on Malaria Control in three Southeastern-coastal districts (Rufiji, Kilwa, Kibiti), researchers applied the Slope Index of Inequality (SII) and Relative Index of Inequality (RII), with socioeconomic status derived via Principal Component Analysis, to assess equity in malaria outcomes through logistic and linear regression. Higher socioeconomic status was associated with lower infection risk (OR=0.9975, 95% CI 0.9972–0.9978), lower treatment costs (coefficient -3.13, P<0.05), and fewer days off work (coefficient -0.0017, P<0.05). Rufiji showed the most significant socioeconomic equality across outcomes. The authors emphasize the need for equity-focused interventions.

articles.headline: MRI shows occipital and peri-Rolandic sparing of cortical edema in pediatric cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Peri-Rolandic and Occipital Sparing Cortical Edema: A Prevalent MRI Finding in Pediatric Patients with Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Vidal L, Zandifar A, Kim JDU, Tierradentro-García LO, Heng S, Meneses M, Small D, Chetcuti K, O'Brien N, Postels D, Taylor T, Goyal MS
articles.details: A retrospective review assessed admission brain MRIs from 190 Malawian children with clinical cerebral malaria admitted at a single center from 2013-2019, with two neuroradiologists characterizing cortical edema patterns on T1, T2, and DWI and scoring overall brain volume (BVS) against clinical outcomes at discharge. Cortical edema was found in 163 scans; the predominant pattern was diffuse cortical involvement sparing the occipital and peri-Rolandic areas (103, 63.2%), with 22.7% sparing only the occipital cortex and 14.1% showing generalized edema. Occipital and peri-Rolandic sparing inversely correlated with BVS (β=-0.26, P<.001) and worse outcomes (OR 0.3, 95% CI 0.1-0.6, P=.002), becoming progressively involved with more severe disease.

articles.headline: Modelling shows scaled-up bed-net use could avert millions of malaria cases in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Modelling the effect of long-lasting insecticidal nets on malaria transmission dynamics in Kebbi State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Bakare EA, Olasupo II, Imoudu M, Abidemi A, Daniel DO, Osikoya SA, Mogbojuri OA, Nwana AO, Oniyelu DO, Olorunfemi RD, Olagbami OS, Agboola DO, Ikediashi SI, Adedeji A, Ikuseka O, Fadairo M, Odewale O, Onwuka IG, Hansinon A, Bakare DA, White LJ, Chitnis N, Oresanya O, Okoronkwo C, Nelson E, Kosoko S, Ogban GI
articles.details: This modelling study built a deterministic compartmental model of malaria transmission fitted to routine confirmed uncomplicated malaria case data from Kebbi State, Nigeria (January 2015 to May 2024), using least-squares estimation in R. It derived the effective reproduction number and simulated long-lasting insecticidal net (LLIN) usage scenarios of 38.2%, 50%, and 80%. The authors estimate that sustained 80% LLIN usage since 2015 would have averted about 5.2 million cases (97.98% reduction), and that achieving 80% usage going forward would avert about 3 million cases by May 2029 (78.93% reduction). They conclude policymakers should prioritise scaling up LLIN distribution and usage.

articles.headline: Vaccine candidate PfRIPR shows high genetic diversity in Senegal isolates
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity of the malaria vaccine candidate PfRIPR in a high transmission region of Senegal.
articles.authors: Nair M, Geering G, Agarwal A, Li R, Qiao Y, Xiao Q, Pouye MN, Thiam LG, Ba A, Hagadorn KA, Cisse A, Guerra N, Tawaldemedhen Y, Mangou K, Moore AJ, Diallo F, Sene SD, Sadio BD, Zhang E, Shapiro L, Patel SD, Mbengue A, Vigan-Womas I, Sheng Z, Bei AK
articles.details: Researchers used deep amplicon sequencing to characterize genetic diversity of the malaria vaccine candidate PfRIPR (RH5-interacting protein) in 89 Plasmodium falciparum clinical isolates from Kédougou, a high-transmission region of Senegal. Non-synonymous single-nucleotide polymorphisms were found in 64/89 (71.9%) of samples, totaling 26 non-synonymous SNPs including 15 novel ones. Of 16 SNPs mapped onto existing PfRIPR crystal structures, 7 were predicted to destabilize the protein and 2 to increase its stability. Three SNPs (Q737K, T738K, V840L) were identified in the EGF5-8 domains where neutralizing antibodies bind.

articles.headline: P. falciparum prefers Duffy-positive red cells, may shape Duffy negativity spread
articles.title_original: Invasion preferences suggest a possible role for Plasmodium falciparum parasites in the expansion of Duffy negativity in West and Central Africa.
articles.authors: Manneh B, Introini V, Reed J, Rotariu M, Antrobus R, Cicuta P, Weekes MP, Penman BS, Rayner JC
articles.details: Using flow cytometry and microscopy-based invasion assays, researchers tested whether Plasmodium falciparum preferentially invades specific Duffy red blood cell phenotypes, examining biophysical properties and surface protein composition. P. falciparum showed a consistent preference for Duffy-positive RBCs, with some biophysical and surface protein differences between Duffy-positive and Duffy-negative cells. The in vitro invasion data parametrized an evolutionary-epidemiological model incorporating exposure-acquired immunity, predicting that the FYBES allele frequency increases at low P. falciparum transmission but decreases at high transmission. The authors suggest DARC may be directly or indirectly involved in P. falciparum invasion, helping explain Duffy negativity distribution in sub-Saharan Africa.

articles.headline: Under-five malaria fell but northern Nigeria disparities persist, 2010-2019
articles.title_original: Spatial and temporal inequalities in malaria incidence and mortality among children aged 0-4 years in Nigeria: a subnational analysis, 2010-2019.
articles.authors: Oso TA, Okesanya OJ, Adebayo UO, Ayelaagbe OB, Ahmed MM, Obadeyi KB, Eshun G, Saliu OM, Lucero-Prisno DE 3rd
articles.details: This subnational analysis examined temporal and spatial inequalities in malaria incidence and mortality among children aged 0-4 years across Nigeria's 36 states and the Federal Capital Territory from 2010 to 2019, using IHME estimates analyzed with the WHO Health Equity Assessment Toolkit and five inequality metrics. National incidence declined from 103 to 74.5 cases (27.7% reduction) and mortality fell from 477.3 to 237.6 deaths per 100,000 (50.2% reduction). Northern states (Zamfara, Kano, Katsina) retained the highest burden while southern states (Lagos, Delta, Anambra) had lower burdens; incidence coefficient of variation fell from a 2013 peak of 46.3% to 28.1% in 2019. The authors call for geographically targeted interventions.

articles.headline: Lessons from a faculty training program in applied malaria modeling
articles.title_original: Building contextually-relevant training programs for scientific development: process and lessons learned in implementing two iterations of a faculty enrichment program in applied malaria modeling.
articles.authors: Onyango L, Ouédraogo-Ametchie G, Stahlfeld A, Holden TM, Richter R, Runge M, Toh KB, Agorinya I, Mensah B, Gerardin J
articles.details: This qualitative study reports on two iterations (2022 and 2023) of a 4-month faculty enrichment program in applied malaria modeling implemented at Northwestern University, USA, covering technical skills, communication skills, and malaria epidemiology. Two cohorts of participants and instructors were interviewed at baseline, midline, and endline about their expectations, experiences, and challenges. Participants valued growth in technical expertise, research, and communication skills plus knowledge-transfer opportunities, but reported challenges with cross-disciplinary learning, balancing program components, and adapting to new teaching styles. The authors conclude that technical training programs should be informed by participant needs and include continuous feedback mechanisms.

articles.headline: G6PD deficiency affects 6% of malaria patients in Ethiopia, meta-analysis
articles.title_original: Pooled prevalence of Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency among malaria patients in Ethiopia: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Dessie G, Getnet M, Getahun AB, Mengstie TA, Asefa T, Enyew EF, Yewodiaw TK, Endale HT, Endalew HL
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis pooled data from studies (searched in PubMed, Cochrane, Hinari, and Google Scholar) to estimate glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency (G6PDd) prevalence among malaria patients, using a random-effects model. The overall pooled prevalence of G6PDd was 6% (95% CI 0.04-0.08), with the highest at 23% (95% CI 0.06-0.41) in Jimma and the lowest at 1% (95% CI 0.00-0.03) across three malaria-endemic regions. Most G6PDd cases were associated with A376G and A376T variants, with G202A detected at low distribution and several emerging mutant variants reported in specific study areas.

articles.headline: Household, WASH factors and malaria in young Ghanaian children
articles.title_original: Household characteristics, water, sanitation and hygiene (WASH) and malaria prevalence among children aged 6-59 months in Ghana: an analysis of the 2022 Ghana Demographic and Health Survey.
articles.authors: Klu D, Alhassan A, Vidzro ES, Aberese-Ako M
articles.details: Using a weighted sample of 3255 households with children aged 6-59 months from the 2022 Ghana Demographic and Health Survey, this cross-sectional analysis used RDT testing and multivariate logistic regression to assess household and water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) factors. Malaria prevalence was 3.7%; ITN ownership was 78.9% but only 51.5% of children slept under ITNs, and 41.5% were anaemic. Lower odds of malaria were seen with wealthier households (aOR=0.15; CI 0.03-0.71), improved sanitation (aOR=0.59; CI 0.39-0.90), and untreated drinking water (aOR=0.47), while anaemic children (aOR=2.03; CI 1.36-3.04) and outside toilets (aOR=16.64; CI 2.06-134.57) had higher odds. Authors call for targeted WASH and ITN interventions.

articles.headline: Why DRC providers distrust negative malaria rapid diagnostic tests
articles.title_original: Positive tests are all alike, every negative test is negative in its own way: lack of confidence in negative malaria rapid diagnostic tests in the Democratic Republic of the Congo.
articles.authors: Sugg KN, Mpata FW, Humes MD, Aliango Marachto D, Rajan R, Winch PJ
articles.details: This qualitative study explored why healthcare providers in the Democratic Republic of the Congo do not adhere to malaria RDT results, noting that test positivity rates reported through the national HMIS were higher than those recorded by an automated Deki reader, especially in the dry season. Researchers conducted 64 semi-structured interviews at 16 primary-level health facilities in Kasai Central and Haut Katanga provinces, plus 12 key informant interviews and 24 community focus groups. Providers trusted positive RDTs more than negative ones, often equating fever with malaria and rationalizing negative results as false negatives; financial incentives favored microscopy. Authors suggest sharing RDT efficacy data and improving understanding of how RDTs work to boost confidence.

articles.headline: Case report: post-malaria neurological syndrome and acute disseminated encephalomyelitis
articles.title_original: Post malaria neurological syndrome and acute disseminated encephalomyelitis: a case highlighting an ongoing debate.
articles.authors: Rodrigues BA, Magriço M, Krupka D, Serrazina F
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: How malaria parasites control apicoplast inheritance during cell division
articles.title_original: Independent nuclear and organellar mechanisms determine apicoplast fate in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Shahar M, Qasem A, Shamay E, Tissawak A, Maron Y, Florentin A
articles.details: Using a dynamic live-imaging platform to track subcellular structures across the 48-hour intraerythrocytic life cycle of Plasmodium falciparum, researchers characterized apicoplast development and its link to the cell cycle. They identified four morphological stages correlating with nuclear replication, including a critical "Crown" stage required for nucleus-apicoplast attachment near centriolar plaques, and measured DNA ploidy and replication dynamics of nuclear and apicoplast genomes. Inhibiting nuclear DNA replication blocked apicoplast biogenesis early (showing dependence on S-phase initiation), while inhibiting apicoplast genome replication minimally affected development but disrupted the Crown stage and proper organelle segregation. The findings establish independent nuclear and organellar mechanisms governing apicoplast fate.

articles.headline: First malaria knowledge-attitudes-practices survey in Djibouti City
articles.details: A Knowledge, Attitudes and Practices (KAP) survey of 1,344 household heads across nine neighbourhoods in Djibouti City used analysis of variance, multinomial logistic regression, and Local Indicators of Spatial Association to identify predictors and spatial clustering of KAP scores. No significant association was found between sociodemographic or economic factors and malaria knowledge; prevention practices varied by place of residence, mother tongue, and education, while attitudes were linked to gender and income. Despite high awareness, 60% of respondents misidentified transmission routes and nearly two-thirds failed to adopt effective preventive behaviours, with LLIN ownership far exceeding correct use. Authors conclude information-deficit approaches have reached their limits and recommend motivational, norm-based interventions targeting high-risk neighbourhoods.
articles.title_original: Evaluation of household knowledge, attitudes and practices concerning malaria in the city of Djibouti.
articles.authors: Abdillahi Guedi O, Brou YT, Delmelle E, Briolant S

articles.headline: Saudi Arabia malaria now import-driven after 15-year decline
articles.title_original: Malaria: Examining Persistence at the Margins of Endemicity over 15 Years in Saudi Arabia.
articles.authors: Alruwaili Y
articles.details: This analysis of national Ministry of Health surveillance data from 2010 to 2024 described malaria trends across 20 health regions in Saudi Arabia, using ARIMA models to project trends through 2030. A total of 52,627 cases were reported, with interannual variability and an increase in incidence after 2020. Burden became largely restricted to the Aseer and Jazan regions; P. falciparum and P. vivax/P. ovale predominated, mostly in those aged ≥10 years. From 2021 onward no indigenous transmission was recorded, with most infections classified as imported. Forecasts indicated stable trends through 2030, underscoring the need for sustained surveillance to prevent re-establishment of local transmission.

articles.headline: Airport malaria cluster strikes malaria-free Libya, one death
articles.title_original: Airport Malaria Cluster in Certified Malaria-Free Country, Libya, 2024.
articles.authors: Alarbi AM, Elhaddad AB, Almehdawi NM, Saadawi WK, Aqeehal H, Elalem M
articles.details: In November 2024, an autochthonous cluster of 4 Plasmodium falciparum cases (1 fatal) was identified near Benina International Airport in Benghazi, Libya. Epidemiologic and entomologic investigation ruled out local transmission and pointed to airport malaria as the cause. The authors highlight the vulnerability of malaria-free regions to imported vectors.

articles.headline: Model maps mosquito habitat and biting risk for malaria control
articles.title_original: Spatial-temporal coupling of malaria vector habitat suitability and biting probability.
articles.authors: Aduvukha GR, Abdel-Rahman EM, Mutanga O, Odindi J, Tonnang HEZ
articles.details: This modelling study integrated remotely sensed, climatic, and topographic variables with presence-only vector data in MaxEnt to map Anopheles habitat suitability, then layered modelled vector presence, human availability, confirmed insecticide resistance, and bed net usage into fuzzy logic rule-based models to assess biting risk probability from 2000 to 2018. Two scenarios were tested (Model 1 using OR combinations, Model 2 using AND combinations) and validated against An. gambiae complex biting observations (n=25) for 2017-2021, yielding 91% mean accuracy for both. Increased bed net usage reduced biting probability, mirroring reduced P. falciparum prevalence over the period, supporting use as an early-warning system for targeted vector management.

articles.headline: Review: diabetes and malaria worsen each other in Africa
articles.title_original: Diabetes Mellitus and Malaria Co-Existence as Deadly Burden in Sub-Saharan Africa: A Review of Clinical Outcomes and Molecular Interactions.
articles.authors: Erhabor OG, Abdullahi AD, Sani A, Gabdo MB, Saad RS, Abubakar M, Isah MB, Simelane MBC, Umar IA, Ibrahim MA
articles.details: This review examined clinical studies and molecular interactions underlying the coexistence of diabetes mellitus and malaria in malaria-endemic areas. The authors describe a bidirectional relationship: malaria-related inflammation, oxidative stress, and immune activation disrupt insulin signaling and beta-cell function to worsen hyperglycemia in diabetics, while chronic hyperglycemia damages the endothelium, raises oxidative stress, and impairs immunity, creating an environment that facilitates malaria parasite survival and growth. TNF-α and insulin signaling pathways emerge as a pivotal link, and these observations were related to higher subclinical infection rates and prolonged parasitemia among diabetic patients in malaria-endemic areas. The authors suggest this relationship could be exploited to develop novel therapies targeting both diseases in tandem, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa, where the two diseases co-exist and are endemic.

articles.headline: Benzoxaborole compounds inhibit Plasmodium carbonic anhydrase enzyme
articles.title_original: Benzoxaboroles Are Inhibitors of the η-Class Carbonic Anhydrase From Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Bonardi A, Wein S, Mustafa M, Cerdan R, de Luca V, Capasso C, Gratteri P, Supuran CT, Nocentini A, Winum JY
articles.details: This study evaluated benzoxaborole 4 and its 6-substituted triazolyl derivatives (5-18) for inhibition of Plasmodium falciparum η-class carbonic anhydrase (PfaCA) and in vitro antiplasmodial activity against the P. falciparum 3D7 strain. Compounds 13 and 14 were the most potent enzyme inhibitors (KI = 0.45 and 0.43 µM) with antiplasmodial activity (IC50 = 34 and 47 µM), while the ethyl ester 7 showed the strongest antiplasmodial activity (IC50 = 2.5 µM). Molecular docking supported favorable binding within the PfaCA active site. The authors conclude benzoxaborole-based scaffolds represent a promising new chemotype for antimalarial drug development.

articles.headline: Civil war and COVID disruption drive malaria resurgence in Karen State
articles.title_original: Re-emergence of malaria in Karen State, Myanmar: has the battle of Burma been lost?
articles.authors: Pateekhum C, Thu AM, Lwin KM, Nyaw SB, Tun SW, Daniel S, Khin A, Aung WL, Kain K, Imwong M, Day NP, White NJ, Nosten FH
articles.details: This observational study reviewed malariometric and genomic data from a malaria clinic and village health worker network serving an estimated 350,000 people in eastern Karen State, Myanmar, in the context of the COVID-19 pandemic and the military coup. Between 2020 and 2024, P. falciparum cases rose 12-fold and P. vivax cases threefold, with increased transmission of P. falciparum (RR 1.72, 95% CI 1.68-1.76) and P. vivax (RR 1.82, 95% CI 1.80-1.84), greatest in northern areas where access to timely diagnosis and treatment fell. Authors conclude service disruption plus insecurity, population movement, and clonal expansion of a parasite lineage drove the resurgence, though the 2024 decline and near-absence of deaths show control efforts contained the crisis.

articles.headline: Malaria
articles.title_original: Malaria.
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Why whole-sporozoite malaria vaccines lose efficacy in endemic field trials
articles.title_original: The endemic dilemma: why whole sporozoite malaria vaccine efficacy falls in the field.
articles.authors: Hassert M, Harty JT
articles.details: This perspective argues that the sharp decline in durable efficacy of whole sporozoite vaccines (the most effective malaria vaccines to date) when moving from homologous CHMI studies to field trials in endemic regions reflects the complex deployment context rather than a single cause. The authors discuss the antigen mismatch hypothesis (limited vaccine genetic diversity failing to capture field parasite diversity) and the host-context hypothesis (genetics, infection history, microbiome, and nutrition affecting outcomes). They propose that future progress depends on integrating parasite genomics, systems immunology, and more representative animal models.

articles.headline: P. ovale co-infection shows minor lab differences in imported P. falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Comparison of routine clinical profiles of patients with imported P. falciparum malaria and co-infection with Plasmodium ovale wallikeri or Plasmodium ovale curtisi.
articles.authors: Balsa-Vázquez J, Rubio-Muñoz JM, Duvignaud A, Alcedo S, de Ayala AP, Salas-Coronas J, Elía-López M, Cattaneo P, Arévalo-Cañas C, García-Bujalance S, Ruiz-Giardín JM, Norman FF, Rojo-Marcos G
articles.details: This retrospective multicentre cohort study analysed 28 imported Plasmodium ovale wallikeri/P. falciparum mixed infections and 11 P. ovale curtisi/P. falciparum infections versus 117 matched P. falciparum mono-infections, all diagnosed by PCR. There were no significant differences in clinical presentation, severity, or hospitalization. P. ovale wallikeri co-infections had higher leukocyte counts (6.25 vs 4.85 ×10⁹/L; p=0.010) and lower parasitemia index (0.6% vs 1.0%; p=0.025); P. ovale curtisi co-infections had lower ALT (18.0 vs 27.3 IU/L; p=0.022), more frequent thrombocytopenia (100% vs 51%; p=0.003), and lower platelets (105 vs 148 ×10⁹/L; p=0.036). Authors deem the significance uncertain and suggest a negative interaction between P. falciparum and P. ovale curtisi.

articles.headline: Male sex linked to delayed blood-stage malaria after experimental infection
articles.title_original: Sex-based differences in Plasmodium infection in the control groups of controlled human malaria infection trials in malaria-naive populations in the USA and the Netherlands: a pooled analysis.
articles.authors: Duncombe CJ, Hergott DEB, Staubus W, Balke-Buijs M, Kublin JG, Duffy PE, Healy SA, Talley A, Jackson L, Sim BKL, Hoffman SL, Sauerwein RW, Roestenberg M, Murphy SC
articles.details: This pooled analysis of malaria-naive control participants from controlled human malaria infection (CHMI) studies used 18S rRNA nucleic acid amplification tests to compare time to blood-stage positivity by sex, adjusting for challenge route, P. falciparum strain, and study site. Among 102 control participants (48 female, 54 male) across 13 studies, mean time to first detectable parasitaemia was slightly longer in males (7.59 vs 7.17 days), with positivity occurring 8% later in males (time ratio 1.08, 95% CI 1.03-1.16). Males were more likely to have detectable infection after day 7 (35% vs 13%), with adjusted odds 5.20 times higher (95% CI 1.52-17.70). Authors conclude P. falciparum infection likely involves a sex-specific host-pathogen interaction in the liver.

articles.headline: New thermal profiling method maps dynamic P. falciparum protein complexes
articles.title_original: MAP-X unveils the shapeshifting interactome of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Li Q, Chen Q
articles.details: Pazicky et al. introduced MAP-X (meltome-assisted profiling of protein complexes), a thermal profiling method combined with machine learning, to map protein complex dynamics within intact Plasmodium falciparum parasites. This in situ approach revealed a highly dynamic interactome, identifying novel complexes and 'moonlighting' proteins. The authors conclude this shifts understanding of the parasite from static protein lists to temporal interaction networks. No quantitative results are reported in the abstract.

articles.headline: Commercial qPCR assay outperforms microscopy for P. vivax detection
articles.title_original: Promising diagnostic performance of a commercial qPCR assay for Plasmodium vivax detection: Contributing to malaria control and elimination.
articles.authors: Cerilo-Filho M, Sampaio MP, Almeida MNG, A Silva A, Montoia G, Storti-Melo LM, Baptista ARS, Di Santi SM, Machado RLD
articles.details: A commercial qPCR assay for Plasmodium vivax detection showed greater sensitivity than microscopy across 167 samples, detecting three additional positive cases. It also demonstrated high specificity with no cross-reactivity. The authors conclude it is a reliable tool for malaria diagnosis and surveillance.

articles.headline: PCR detects more malaria than microscopy in Peshawar; blood markers altered
articles.title_original: Integrated diagnostic and host biomarker assessment of plasmodium infections in malaria-endemic Peshawar, Pakistan.
articles.authors: Khan MA, Fayaz S, Saeed M, Murad R, Murad A, Khan RU, Naz S, Perillo A, Alhidary IA
articles.details: This cross-sectional study in District Peshawar, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa screened 2,500 suspected patients during peak transmission, using Giemsa microscopy and 18S rRNA PCR (reference standard) for P. vivax and P. falciparum. PCR detected significantly more cases than microscopy (p=0.001); microscopy had perfect specificity, 86.4% accuracy, κ=0.692, and AUC=0.823. Prevalence peaked in September, with higher rates among males aged 21–30 (p=0.04). Positives showed reduced hemoglobin, hematocrit, and RBC indices with leukocyte alterations, plus elevated ALT, AST, bilirubin and ALP and reduced albumin; parasite density correlated negatively with hemoglobin, hematocrit and platelet counts and positively with liver enzymes and bilirubin.

articles.headline: Commentary on beta-thalassemia and the missing malaria landscape
articles.title_original: Beta-thalassemia and a missing malaria landscape.
articles.authors: Elmakhzen B, El Makhzen N, Abriel H
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Review: Wolbachia bacteria as a potential tool for malaria control
articles.title_original: Wolbachia for malaria control.
articles.authors: Zhang X, Dimopoulos G
articles.details: This review discusses the potential of Wolbachia bacteria for malaria control, building on the success of Wolbachia-based dengue control and the establishment of Plasmodium-blocking Wolbachia transinfections in Anopheles mosquitoes. It covers experimental systems for studying Wolbachia–Anopheles interactions and the challenges of generating Wolbachia-transinfected Anopheles gambiae strains. The authors note that parasite challenge experiments in transiently and stably infected Anopheles demonstrated that Wolbachia-induced Plasmodium suppression is feasible, and outline mechanisms, challenges, and opportunities for moving from proof-of-concept to field application.

articles.headline: Cool roofs plus screening cut indoor heat and mosquitoes in rural houses
articles.title_original: Housing modifications for heat adaptation, thermal comfort and malaria vector control in rural African settlements.
articles.authors: Abong'o B, Kwaro D, Bange T, Moshi V, Simwero J, Otima J, Mendt S, Ochomo E, Maggioni MA
articles.details: This randomized pilot field study allocated 40 houses to four arms (cool-roof, cross-ventilation, mat-ceiling, control) to test passive-cooling and vector-proofing housing modifications; intervention houses had doors, windows, and eaves screened. Cool-roof houses had the lowest Heat Index (daytime -3.3 °C, P<0.001; nighttime -2.4 °C, P<0.01), while mat-ceiling houses lowered daytime but raised nighttime HI and cross-ventilation showed no difference. Screening reduced female Anopheles funestus by 77% and Culex by 58% versus control, and 85% of households were willing to invest their own resources. Authors conclude cool-roofs plus vector proofing are effective and sustainable.

articles.headline: MSRP1 marker enables isolating sexually committed P. falciparum parasites for proteomics
articles.title_original: Defining the Proteome of Sexually Committed Parasites in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Venugopal K, Achcar F, Wolski WE, Nanni P, Lemgruber Soares L, Wright GJ, Marti M
articles.details: This laboratory study addresses the difficulty of isolating sexually committed Plasmodium falciparum parasites (those destined to form transmission-stage gametocytes) due to a lack of markers. The authors demonstrate that MSRP1 is a highly specific sexual commitment marker and use it to generate and validate reporter parasite lines for FACS-based isolation of sexually versus asexually committed parasites. Proteomics of the isolated populations defined distinct protein signatures, including several merozoite surface proteins, indicating functional differences between the two populations. The work provides a blueprint for characterizing this stage of the life cycle.

articles.headline: Delayed hemolytic anemia after oral artemether-lumefantrine in severe falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Delayed hemolytic anemia after artemether-lumefantrine in Plasmodium falciparum malaria: A case report.
articles.authors: Usta O, Güllü D, Alhan Ö, Ergönül Ö
articles.details: This case report describes delayed hemolytic anemia (DHA) following an extended oral course of artemether-lumefantrine in a 50-year-old man with follicular lymphoma (treated with rituximab 10 months earlier) and severe Plasmodium falciparum malaria (parasitemia >10%). He developed significant hemolysis eight days post-treatment, with reticulocytosis and a notably positive direct Coombs test suggesting a possible immune-mediated component, distinguishing it from the typical non-immune mechanism. Immunosuppression, pre-existing hepatosplenomegaly, and the prolonged regimen likely contributed. The authors emphasize that DHA can follow oral AL, not just intravenous artesunate, and urge hemoglobin monitoring for 2-4 weeks in high-risk patients.

articles.headline: TIRAP S180L variant protects against severe malaria, meta-analysis finds
articles.title_original: Immunomodulatory effects of the TIRAP/MAL rs8177374 (S180L) variant: Meta-Analytic evidence from severe and Plasmodium falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: González-Castillo NI, Prado-Rodríguez SG, Iñiguez-Mosqueda O, Íñiguez-Gutiérrez L, Silva-Jara JM, Díaz-Zaragoza M, Graciano-Machuca O
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis examined the TIRAP/MAL rs8177374 (S180L) polymorphism and malaria susceptibility, including ten studies with 4103 malaria cases and 2460 controls, using pooled odds ratios under six genetic models. No significant association was found with overall malaria susceptibility, but the T allele and heterozygous (TC) genotypes were consistently associated with lower risk of severe malaria (T vs. C: OR=0.51, 95% CI 0.40-0.65, p<0.001). In the P. falciparum subgroup, the variant was linked to increased infection risk (T vs. C: OR=1.47, 95% CI 1.09-1.98, p=0.012). Authors conclude the polymorphism has a protective effect against severe malaria, supporting a modulatory role in pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Scoping review finds widespread pyrethroid resistance in Tanzanian malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Insecticide Resistance Management in Malaria Vectors in Tanzania Mainland (2015-2024): A Scoping Review.
articles.authors: Nkya TE
articles.details: This PRISMA-ScR scoping review synthesized evidence on insecticide resistance patterns, mechanisms, and management in Tanzanian malaria vectors from 2015-2024; of 910 records, ten met inclusion criteria, all using WHO susceptibility bioassays with some molecular or biochemical assays. Pyrethroid resistance was consistently reported, largely driven by knockdown resistance (kdr) mutations and metabolic mechanisms, and DDT resistance was common, while moderate carbamate resistance appeared in about half the studies. Organophosphates (pirimiphos-methyl) remained effective, and new insecticides clothianidin and chlorfenapyr showed high efficacy with no resistance detected. Authors conclude resistance is widespread, with surveillance and operational gaps limiting management effectiveness.

articles.headline: Immune factors and vesicles differ in bacterial vs malaria fever
articles.title_original: Profiling of circulating immune factors and host-derived extracellular vesicles in response to bacterial or malaria acute fever.
articles.authors: Tiberti N, Bertoli G, Mazzi C, Vezzelli E, Zanzoni S, Alladio F, Bisoffi Z, Tamarozzi F, Piubelli C, Buonfrate D, Gobbi FG
articles.details: This retrospective study analyzed serum from febrile patients with bacterial infection (n=52) or malaria (n=73), measuring circulating immune mediators and 37 surface markers of extracellular vesicles (EVs). Endothelial-activation markers (CD29+, CD62P+, CD9+ EVs) and MMP-2/MMP-9 were elevated in bacterial illness (q<0.05), while antigen-recognition markers (CD8+, CD81+, HLA-DR/DP/DQ+ EVs) and cytokine-storm mediators (IFNγ, CXCL10, TNFα) were prominent in malaria (q<0.005). HLA-DR/DP/DQ+ EVs distinguished bacterial from malaria fever with an AUC of 0.87, versus platelets' 0.92. The authors suggest EVs as potential diagnostic markers and mediators of inflammation.

articles.headline: Fat and vitamin A diet linked to less malaria in young children
articles.title_original: Associations Between Climate-Sensitive Nutrients, Clinical Malaria, and Anaemia Among Young Children in Rural Burkina Faso: An Analysis of Baseline Data From a Cluster-Randomised Controlled Trial.
articles.authors: Kurniawan AL, Borga MV, Zerbo F, Abidha CA, Gonnet A, Sorgho R, Coulibaly B, Sié A, Danquah I
articles.details: This cross-sectional study recruited children aged 6-23 months in Nouna, Burkina Faso, deriving dietary nutrient patterns via Principal Component Analysis and relating them to clinical malaria and anaemia (Hb<11 g/dL) by logistic regression. Among 452 children (median age 17 months, 52% male), 25.0% had clinical malaria and 88.5% had anaemia. A 'fat and vitamin A' pattern was inversely associated with clinical malaria (OR quintile 3 vs 1: 0.50, 95% CI 0.26-0.96) but not anaemia; a 'fibre and micronutrient' pattern showed no association. The authors call for comprehensive management strategies addressing both malaria and anaemia.

articles.headline: Rare mixed P. falciparum/P. vivax infections found in febrile patients
articles.title_original: Integrated diagnostic approach for mixed Plasmodium infection among acute febrile patients: rare incidence from Eastern Uttar Pradesh.
articles.authors: Bhardwaj P, Agrawal A, Tiwari S, Kumar B, Kumar N, Singh R, Joshi HS, Dwivedi GR
articles.details: This study investigated malaria among acute febrile illness patients in eastern Uttar Pradesh, India, using RDT, microscopy, and PCR. Four Plasmodium-positive cases were confirmed by RDT and three by microscopy as mono-infection; PCR revealed three of four samples had mixed P. falciparum and P. vivax infections, while one had P. falciparum only. One malaria case also had varicella-zoster virus, and another had Japanese encephalitis and scrub typhus alongside malaria and died during hospitalization. The authors note mixed Pf/Pv infections can cause life-threatening complications and urge considering cerebral malaria even in co-infection.

articles.headline: High malaria and typhoid co-infection in febrile hospital patients
articles.title_original: Malaria and salmonella typhi co- infection and antibiotic susceptibility in febrile patients at the Buea Regional Hospital.
articles.authors: Ebini-Ako TA, Tanih NF, Djakissam W
articles.details: This cross-sectional study tested 152 febrile patients at Buea Regional Hospital for malaria (blood) and typhoid (stool), with antimicrobial susceptibility testing on Salmonella typhi isolates. Malaria prevalence was 52.0% (79/152) and typhoid 37.5% (57/152); 73.7% (112/152) had either, and 22.3% (25/112) of confirmed patients had both. Vomiting was statistically associated with typhoid (P=0.008). Most antibiotics were effective against Salmonella isolates, but all isolates were resistant to sulfamethoxazole.
