The authors recommend independent laboratory-based diagnosis and treatment of malaria and typhoid to reduce antimicrobial resistance.

articles.headline: Adding amodiaquine doesn't alter artemether-lumefantrine pharmacokinetics
articles.title_original: Population pharmacokinetics of artemether-lumefantrine plus amodiaquine in patients with uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Ding J, Hoglund RM, van der Pluijm RW, Callery JJ, Peto TJ, Tripura R, Das S, Châu NH, Promnarate C, Mukaka M, Dysoley L, Fanello C, Onyamboko MA, Anvikar AR, Mayxay M, Smithuis F, von Seidlein L, Dhorda M, Amaratunga C, Faiz MA, Nghia HDT, White NJ, Day NPJ, Dondorp AM, Tarning J
articles.details: This pharmacokinetic analysis used data from two randomized controlled trials (conducted 2015-2020 in one African and two Southeast Asian countries) in which patients with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria received artemether-lumefantrine alone (n=443) or with amodiaquine (n=442), with concentration-time data analyzed via nonlinear mixed-effects modelling. Coadministered amodiaquine was not a significant covariate; for example, artemether Cmax was 256 ng/mL for artemether-lumefantrine alone versus 230 ng/mL when amodiaquine was added. All geometric mean ratios for drug-drug interaction effects on artemether, dihydroartemisinin, and lumefantrine fell within 0.80-1.25, indicating no clinically relevant interactions. The authors conclude amodiaquine's effect on artemether-lumefantrine pharmacokinetics is minimal, though further large-scale trials are needed to confirm this.

articles.headline: History of malaria fever and other invasive therapies at Vienna psychiatric clinic
articles.title_original: In the shadow of the "psychopharmacological revolution": Malaria fever, insulin coma, cardiazol and electroconvulsive therapy at the Vienna Psychiatric University Clinic, 1951-1969.
articles.authors: Heiss G
articles.details: This 2-year historical research project examined four invasive somatic psychiatric treatments — electroconvulsive therapy, cardiazol-induced convulsion therapy, insulin coma therapy, and malaria fever therapy — practiced at the University Clinic of Vienna under Hans Hoff from 1951 to 1969. Malaria fever therapy was used beyond its traditional indication for neurosyphilis. The analysis covered 14,919 medical records of patients aged 15 and older diagnosed with neurosyphilis, intellectual disabilities, schizophrenia, affective disorders, or psychopathy, and reviewed how these therapies were discussed in professional publications during the transition to new pharmacological treatments.

articles.headline: Anti-CSP monoclonal antibody MAM01 safe in first-in-human malaria trial
articles.title_original: Human monoclonal antibody MAM01 for protection against malaria in adults in the USA: a first-in-human, phase 1, dose-escalation, double-blind, placebo-controlled, adaptive trial.
articles.authors: Lyke KE, Berry AA, Laurens MB, Winkler J, Joshi S, Koudjra AR, Butler L, Billingsley PF, Pascini T, Patil A, Sim BKL, Fitzgerald G, Riegel J, Andrews K, Levi M, Anderson AB, Wells CD, Liu H, Huleatt J, Miller RS
articles.details: This phase 1, dose-escalation, double-blind, placebo-controlled adaptive trial tested MAM01, a monoclonal antibody directed against the NANP central repeat region of the P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein, in malaria-naive adults aged 18–50. Of 37 randomized participants, 29 received MAM01 at 1.5, 5, 10, or 40 mg/kg intravenously or 5 mg/kg subcutaneously, and six received placebo; participants underwent controlled human malaria infection via five P. falciparum NF54-infected mosquito bites 18–26 weeks later. MAM01 was well tolerated with no treatment-related serious adverse events. After challenge, 6 of 6 control participants and 18 of 22 MAM01 recipients developed parasitaemia.

articles.headline: Commentary on a new monoclonal antibody against malaria
articles.title_original: A new monoclonal in the arMAMentarium against malaria.
articles.authors: Lorenz FR, McCall MBB
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Case report: labyrinthitis ossificans after Plasmodium malaria infection
articles.title_original: Postmalaria Labyrinthitis Ossificans: A Rare Complication of Plasmodium Infection.
articles.authors: Batista FS, Couto R, Pinto D, Matias AM, Neves L, Castelão F
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Review: red blood cell invasion by malaria parasites guides vaccine design
articles.title_original: Erythrocyte invasion in malaria: from molecular mechanisms to rational vaccines.
articles.authors: Alam N, Farrell B, Jamwal A, Higgins MK
articles.details: This review describes how Plasmodium parasites invade and remodel human red blood cells through a temporally organized sequence of molecular processes, including membrane bridging by the P. falciparum PCRCR complex during invasion. It notes that a vaccine targeting a component of the erythrocyte invasion machinery has recently shown efficacy against malaria. Structural studies of vaccine- and infection-induced human antibodies have revealed neutralizing epitopes and mechanisms by which antibodies potentiate one another, and these insights are guiding rational, structure-based vaccine design, with the first rationally designed blood-stage immunogen now entering clinical trials.

articles.headline: Congenital malaria pooled prevalence 12.7% in Nigeria meta-analysis
articles.title_original: Congenital malaria in Nigeria: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Odeniran PO, Paul-Odeniran KF, Ademola IO
articles.details: This systematic review and quality-effects meta-analysis (PRISMA) assessed congenital malaria prevalence in neonates aged 0–7 days in Nigeria, drawing on literature published between January 2000 and December 2024. From 483 studies identified, 35 studies comprising 9503 neonates were included. The pooled prevalence of congenital malaria was 12.7% (95% CI: 7.3–19.2) with high heterogeneity (I²=97.3, P<0.0001), and the average parasite count was 56.6/μl. Co-occurring malaria and septicaemia affected 4.8% (3.4–6.7), while maternal, placental, baby peripheral, and cord blood parasitaemia prevalences were 25.5%, 20.3%, 12.9%, and 11.1%, respectively. Authors conclude congenital malaria is a substantial public health burden requiring enhanced surveillance and targeted interventions.

articles.headline: Surface adhesin TLP boosts traction force in malaria sporozoite motility
articles.title_original: Traction force generation in Plasmodium sporozoites is modulated by a surface adhesin.
articles.authors: Ripp J, Probst D, Singer M, Schwarz US, Frischknecht F
articles.details: This laboratory study used traction force microscopy to measure forces generated by motile Plasmodium sporozoites on their ventral surface during gliding, comparing wild-type with sporozoites lacking the thrombospondin-related anonymous protein–like protein (TLP). Sporozoites use myosin motors to drive retrograde actin flow linked through membrane-spanning adhesins to enable forward gliding essential for migration in skin. Both wild-type and tlp(-) sporozoites showed distinct foci of force generation, but tlp(-) sporozoites generated overall lower forces. The authors conclude TLP is an important element of the force-generating machinery during sporozoite gliding motility.

articles.headline: Renal and neurological sequelae frequent after severe imported malaria
articles.title_original: Assessment of Long-Term Sequelae After Severe Malaria: A Retrospective Study.
articles.authors: Cardona F, Héritier L, Cortaredona S, L'Ollivier C
articles.details: This single-centre retrospective cohort with prospective follow-up at the University Hospital of Marseille, France (January 2018–December 2024) assessed long-term renal and neurological outcomes in adults with severe imported P. falciparum malaria. Among 474 malaria cases, 66 (13.9%) were severe and 57 were included; all received intravenous artesunate with oral step-down and 35% required ICU care. At day 28, 6/41 (14.6%) had sequelae, associated with neurological failure, severe metabolic acidosis, and renal impairment at admission; at remote follow-up, 6/33 (18.2%) had sequelae, associated with older age, D3 blood smear positivity, neurological failure, and renal impairment. No deaths or relapses occurred, and 8/14 reported persistent symptoms at one year, chiefly fatigue and cognitive complaints.

articles.headline: Five imported falciparum malaria cases with acute kidney injury, all survived
articles.title_original: Malaria-Associated Acute Kidney Injury in a Low-Endemic Region: A Case Series of Five Imported Plasmodium falciparum Infections.
articles.authors: Mujuni DP, Sadiq AM, Luhwago E, Mutalemwa D, Mwanga A, Leonard L, Mkwizu EW, Shao ER, Kilonzo KG
articles.details: This case series describes five patients with imported Plasmodium falciparum malaria complicated by malaria-associated acute kidney injury (MAKI) treated at Kilimanjaro Christian Medical Center in northeastern Tanzania between December 2023 and January 2024. The cohort comprised four males and one female with a mean age of 38.2 ± 18.6 years, all presenting with fever and recent travel to high-endemic regions. Mean serum creatinine and urea at admission were 736 ± 346.4 μmol/L and 23.1 ± 14.1 mmol/L, respectively. Four patients required hemodialysis and one experienced malaria recrudescence, with 100% overall survival. The authors highlight the underrecognized burden of MAKI in a resource-limited, low-endemic region.

articles.headline: Malaria-associated kidney injury: pooled 26.5% incidence, 19.2% mortality in adults
articles.title_original: Clinical characteristics and outcome of malaria-associated acute kidney injury in adult patients: a systematic review and meta analysis.
articles.authors: Meena P, Singla V, Deshpande A, Kasturi U, Khan EF, Kumar VRK, Kalyesubula R
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis (PubMed, EMBASE, Cochrane CENTRAL; studies 2000–2024) synthesized data on malaria-associated acute kidney injury (MAKI) in adults, including 25 studies with 2,094 patients predominantly from South Asia (especially India, then Pakistan and Thailand). P. falciparum was most common, though severe AKI from P. vivax was increasingly reported. MAKI incidence ranged from 2.4–45.5% (up to 77% in severe malaria), with a pooled AKI incidence of 26.5% (95% CI 19.8–34.4; I²=97%); dialysis was required in 20–90% of cases. Pooled mortality was 19.2% (95% CI 12.9–27.7%), higher with delayed presentation, cerebral malaria, sepsis, ventilation, acidosis, and multiorgan failure. Acute tubular necrosis predominated among 95 biopsies, while thrombotic microangiopathy and cortical necrosis were associated with P. vivax; 5–20% developed chronic kidney disease.

articles.headline: Low-prevalence P. falciparum clone drives malaria outbreak via clonal expansion
articles.title_original: A previously low prevalence Plasmodium falciparum clone expands in an outbreak in the Pacific Coast of South America.
articles.authors: Cartagenova A, Zapata-Berrones K, Boboy J, Cuero W, Neafsey DE, Early AM, Sáenz FE
articles.details: This genetic surveillance study analyzed 72 clinical Plasmodium falciparum samples collected in three localities of northwest Ecuador, plus samples from the bordering Nariño department of Colombia, using seven microsatellite markers and whole genome sequencing, with relatedness measured as identity by descent. All infections were monoclonal, and parasites were highly clonal and shared between Ecuador and Colombia. A previously low-frequency clone from the Pacific Coast of Colombia, not before seen in Ecuador, caused new outbreaks through clonal expansion and showed high genetic differentiation from prior regional parasites. The authors conclude low-prevalence clones can become epidemic and hamper elimination in low-transmission areas.

articles.headline: Amodiaquine kills P. falciparum by disrupting glycolysis enzymes
articles.title_original: Chemoproteomics-based profiling elucidates the antimalarial effects of amodiaquine through disruption of glycolysis process in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Wang J, Wang C, Zhuge R, Tang H, Xia F, Zhang Y, Zhang J, Liu C, Wu J, Chen X, Gao P, Wang J
articles.details: Using an amodiaquine-derived activity probe and activity-based protein profiling combined with integrative proteomic and transcriptomic analyses, researchers identified the molecular targets of the 4-aminoquinoline antimalarial amodiaquine (AQ) in Plasmodium falciparum. They found three glycolysis-associated enzymes as potential targets and confirmed that AQ binds these proteins and disrupts glycolytic processes in the parasite. They also showed amodiaquine and artesunate (AS) have complementary effects on shared molecular targets, suggesting a mechanism for the enhanced efficacy of AS-AQ combination therapy. The authors conclude AQ acts by targeting parasite glycolytic metabolism, deepening understanding of ACT mode of action.

articles.headline: Evolutionary rate covariation predicts malaria parasite protein interactions
articles.title_original: Evolutionary rate covariation across malaria parasite species enables inference of protein interactions.
articles.authors: Hopson HD, Omelianczyk RI, Ramirez A, Little JH, Clark N, Sigala PA, Leffler EM
articles.details: This computational study calculated relative evolutionary rates across the genome on a phylogeny of 22 Plasmodium species and scored each protein-protein pair by the strength of evolutionary rate covariation (ERC) to infer co-functional proteins, addressing that one-third of P. falciparum genes remain functionally unannotated. Known pathways and interacting proteins across lifecycle stages showed strong ERC signals, and genome-wide scanning identified candidate functions enriched for stage expression and physically interacting pairs. As a proof of concept, the uncharacterized protein PF3D7_0811600 showed high ERC with and co-localized with high molecular weight rhoptry proteins RhopH2 and RhopH3. The authors present ERC as a tool to prioritize genes for functional discovery.

articles.headline: Low-density asymptomatic parasitemia not linked to clinical malaria or household spread
articles.title_original: Low-density asymptomatic parasitemia in southern Zambia does not lead to clinical malaria and is not associated with household transmission: results from a two-year cohort study.
articles.authors: Schue JL, Martin AC, Matoba J, Sing'anga C, Lubinda M, Katowa B, Musonda M, Berube S, Shields T, Kobayashi T, Hamapumbu H, Simulundu E, Norris DE, Wesolowksi A, Moss WJ
articles.details: In a pre-elimination setting in Southern Province, Zambia, an intensive longitudinal cohort of 197 households captured clinical malaria and asymptomatic Plasmodium falciparum parasitemia (by monthly qPCR) between 2018 and 2020, with passive surveillance for clinical cases. Of 1,071 individuals enrolled, 144 (13%) were qPCR-positive at least once; monthly prevalence never exceeded 4% and parasite density was very low (median four parasites/μL). Self-reported clinical malaria incidence was 46.7 cases per 1,000 person-years. There was no association between asymptomatic parasitemia and subsequent clinical malaria within individuals, nor with parasitemia in household members beyond one month. The authors conclude low-level asymptomatic parasitemia did not lead to clinical cases or household transmission.

articles.headline: Severe cerebral malaria with multi-organ failure cured by blood purification
articles.title_original: Successful treatment of severe cerebral malaria with artificial liver blood purification technology: A case report.
articles.authors: Fan C, Dai Y, Du H, Su T, Guo X, Yan Z, Fang H, Yao Y, Zhou X
articles.details: This case report describes a 43-year-old male with a history of frequent business travel to Nigeria who presented with high fever and confusion; a peripheral blood smear diagnosed Plasmodium falciparum infection. He developed severe cerebral malaria complicated by acute liver and kidney impairment, severe thrombocytopenia, coagulation dysfunction, cerebral edema, heart failure, and anuria. He was treated with artemether for the parasites plus artificial liver blood purification combined with continuous renal replacement therapy and ICU support, and was successfully cured. The authors suggest combined extracorporeal support may be an effective rescue therapy for severe malaria with multi-organ failure, noting cerebral malaria fatality rates of 20% to 30%.

articles.headline: Niger malaria burden rising in older children beyond SMC age range
articles.title_original: Burden of malaria among children aged 5-10 years in the Sahelian area: do we need to adapt seasonal malaria chemoprophylaxis?
articles.authors: Djedanem M, Yacoubou MAY, Zakari A, Issa I, Yahaya M, Zaneidou M, Mody I, Onana DB, Testa J, Jambou R
articles.details: Retrospective study of routine monthly district data (2018-2021) from 35 dispensaries across seven provinces in Niger, using SARIMA forecasting and multiple linear regression to assess malaria by age and ecology. Transmission was highly seasonal, peaking July-October. During peaks, children 12-59 months accounted for 33.2% of cases, those 10-14 years 26.0%, and 2-11 months 22.6%, with children 5-14 years together over one-third of cases. Southern districts near irrigated agriculture reported 3,000-7,500 cases/month (Niamey V peaked at 11,000) versus under 2,500 in savanna/Saharan districts. SARIMA projected increases after 2022, supporting expanded chemoprophylaxis beyond under-fives.

articles.headline: Mesenchymal stem cells reduce experimental cerebral malaria severity in mice
articles.title_original: Mesenchymal stem cells alleviate experimental cerebral malaria disease severity by inducing RoRγt(+) Foxp3(+) T regulatory (Tr 17) cells and modulating the dysregulated Th17/Treg axis.
articles.authors: Sharma I, Thakur RS, Chaudhary A, Chauhan R, Singh K, Sadhu S, Awasthi A, Das J
articles.details: Experimental study testing mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in Plasmodium berghei ANKA-infected C57BL/6 mice, an experimental cerebral malaria model, with parasitemia, survival, flow-cytometry immunophenotyping, and cytokine assays. MSCs reduced parasitemia, increased survival, decreased splenic hemozoin, and protected the blood-brain barrier by preventing vascular leakage. They rescued lymphocytes from apoptosis (downregulating PD-1/PD-L1 and ROS), corrected the Th17/Treg imbalance by lowering IL-6 and IL-17 and raising IL-10, and induced a suppressive RoRγt+Foxp3+ Tr17 effector Treg subset that downregulated IL-17 in Th17 cells. Authors conclude MSCs could serve as adjunct therapy for cerebral malaria immunopathology.

articles.headline: Feretia apodanthera plant extracts show antiplasmodial activity in lab and mice
articles.title_original: Harnessing the potential of medicinal plants in the malaria fighting: In vitro and in vivo antiplasmodial activities of Feretia apodanthera Delille (Rubiaceae).
articles.authors: Boni SI, Hien DFS, Koama KB, Youba M, Vaissayre V, Kabre Z, Dussert S, Diabate A, Lefevre T, Yerbanga RS, Haddad M, Meda NR
articles.details: Study evaluating Feretia apodanthera leaf, stem bark, and root extracts in vitro (SYBR Green assay) and in vivo in Plasmodium berghei NMRI-infected mice via suppressive, curative, and prophylactic tests, with toxicity and phytochemical (UHPLC-HRMS, GC-MS) analyses. Extracts were non-toxic (LD50 > 5000 mg/kg) with strong trophozoitocidal activity (IC50 0.140-0.620 μg/mL) and minimal hemolysis (<1%). Leaf showed the highest chemosuppression: suppressive 32.33% (125 mg/kg), 68.01% (250 mg/kg), and 76.31% (500 mg/kg); curative 68.41% (Day 5) and 63.41% (Day 7); prophylactic 63.60% (Day 5) and 57.10% (Day 7). Identified terpenoids, polyphenols, flavonoids, and alkaloids, with leaf richest in polyphenols/alkaloids, supporting traditional antimalarial use.

articles.headline: Plasmodium infection activates antitumor immunity in mouse lung cancer model
articles.title_original: Plasmodium infection fully activates the immune system in peripheral blood and tumor microenvironment in a murine Lewis lung cancer model.
articles.authors: Huang Q, Yang D, Tao Z, Yu Z, Ding W, Tao N, Liao X, Lin H, Cheng Z, Gao S, Kang Z, Xie J, Hu W, Qin L, Chen X, Yu G
articles.details: Experimental study using a subcutaneous Lewis lung cancer model in C57BL/6J mice treated with intraperitoneal Plasmodium yoelii, analyzing peripheral blood and tumor immune cells by high-dimensional flow cytometry plus Western blotting for pSTAT3 and TGFβ. Plasmodium infection inhibited tumor growth and increased CD3+, CD4+, and CD8+ T cells and multiple memory/effector subsets in both blood and tumor tissue. It also lowered PD-1 on CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, reduced polymorphonuclear MDSCs, raised the M1/M2 macrophage ratio, and decreased tumor pSTAT3 and TGFβ. Authors conclude Plasmodium infection remodels and activates the immune system against the tumor ecosystem, proposing it as a distinct form of cancer immunotherapy.

articles.headline: P. falciparum CelTOS vaccine antigen shows high haplotype diversity in Saudi Arabia
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity and haplotype structure of the CelTOS gene in Plasmodium falciparum from Jazan Province, Saudi Arabia: implications for vaccine development and malaria elimination.
articles.authors: Alothaid H, Alghnnam F, Al-Qahtani AA, Al Marzan A, Abu Alola M, Bohol MFF, Alhamlan F, Shafeai MI, Rudiny FH, Motaen AM, Al-Qahtani AA
articles.details: Population-genetics study characterizing the Plasmodium falciparum CelTOS gene from 202 high-quality coding sequences from 201 patients in Jazan Province, Saudi Arabia, using diversity indices, neutrality tests, recombination and codon-based selection models, and population-structure analyses. The 726-bp alignment had 18 segregating sites and 27 haplotypes with high haplotype but low nucleotide diversity (Hd = 0.921; π = 0.0059). Gene-wide ω ≈ 0.36 indicated predominant purifying selection with few episodically diversifying codons, and pairwise FST values were essentially zero. In logistic modeling for very high parasitaemia, age (OR 1.03; 95% CI 1.00-1.06) and non-Saudi nationality (OR 2.21; 95% CI 1.02-4.81) showed modest associations. Authors conclude CelTOS belongs to a largely panmictic, recombining population, supporting cross-border surveillance and its use as a vaccine and molecular marker.

articles.headline: Antibodies to P. vivax PvRipr protein found in exposed people
articles.title_original: B-cell epitope mapping and characterization of antibody responses to recombinant PvRipr in malaria-exposed individuals.
articles.authors: Soares IF, Rodolphi CM, Alencar ALC, Matos ADS, Rodrigues-da-Silva RN, Baptista BO, Martorano RM, de Souza HADS, Riccio EKP, de Barros JP, Totino PRR, Daniel-Ribeiro CT, Pratt-Riccio LR, Lima-Junior JDC
articles.details: This immunology study characterized the naturally acquired humoral response to recombinant PvRipr (a P. vivax homolog of the P. falciparum erythrocyte-invasion protein Ripr) using ELISA on plasma from individuals naturally exposed to malaria in the Brazilian Amazon, alongside in silico linear B-cell epitope prediction. More than 60% of individuals had IgM or IgG antibodies against PvRipr, with IgG1 and IgG2 most frequent among subclasses. Of four identified linear epitopes, B-PvRipr(879-888) and B-PvRipr(923-958) showed the highest seropositivity and reactivity; IgG to these correlated strongly with anti-PvRipr IgG and especially cytophilic IgG3. Authors conclude PvRipr is naturally immunogenic and a potential vaccine candidate.

articles.headline: Aluminum metasurface plasmonic biosensor detects malaria pLDH protein
articles.title_original: Experimental plasmonic sensing of malaria using an aluminum metasurface.
articles.authors: Kiyumbi AS, Tame MS
articles.details: This engineering study designed and experimentally tested an optical surface plasmon resonance (SPR) biosensor for label-free detection of plasmodium lactate dehydrogenase (pLDH), a metabolic biomarker present in all Plasmodium species. The sensor used an aluminum metasurface of nanoholes exploiting extraordinary optical transmission, operating in the visible range to detect P. falciparum LDH (pfLDH) spiked in phosphate-buffered saline. It achieved a spectral sensitivity of 360 nm per RIU and a limit of detection of 1.3 nM, equivalent to 45.6 ng/mL of pfLDH. The authors conclude this approach may offer a cost-effective, high-sensitivity method for diagnosing active infection.

articles.headline: PfHRP2 parasite biomass tracks malaria severity better than blood counts
articles.title_original: Reevaluating malaria severity metrics: insights from PfHRP2-derived biomass estimates in Ghanaian children.
articles.authors: Konadu EA, Obese AO, Danso-Coffie CK, Addison T, Kyei-Baafour E, Obiri D, Amoah LE, Kusi KA, Gyan B, Oduro D
articles.details: This study analyzed data from 118 children with cerebral malaria (CM, n=58), severe malarial anemia (SMA, n=28), or uncomplicated malaria (UM, n=32) at five referral hospitals in Accra (2012-2016) to compare PfHRP2-derived total parasite biomass (PTot), circulating burden (PCir) from peripheral density, and sequestered burden (PSeq=PTot-PCir). PTot and PSeq were consistently higher than PCir in severe syndromes, while in UM they were similar; median PCir was lower in CM than UM, and higher PSeq was associated with coma. Prior antimalarial treatment reduced PCir but not PTot. Authors conclude PfHRP2-based total biomass associates more strongly with severe disease and should inform severity assessment.

articles.headline: Escalating pyrethroid resistance in Cameroon Anopheles funestus amid high transmission
articles.title_original: Four-year monitoring of the malaria vector Anopheles funestus in central-west Cameroon reveals an escalation of pyrethroid resistance combined with high malaria transmission.
articles.authors: Tazokong HR, Tchouakui M, Wondji M, Boyomo O, Wondji CS
articles.details: This four-year (2020-2023) surveillance of Anopheles funestus s.s. in Mibellon, Cameroon assessed sporozoite infection and insecticide resistance using WHO bioassays, TaqMan/nested-PCR, RT-qPCR, and allele-specific genotyping, compared with 2015-2018 data. Plasmodium sporozoite infection rates ranged 4-21%, predominantly P. falciparum with P. malariae and P. ovale often as mixed infections. Permethrin mortality fell from 77.7% (2015) to 23.2% (2023) and deltamethrin from 46.6% (2016) to 8.5% (2023), with full organophosphate susceptibility; G454A-CYP9K1 and 4.3 kb SV alleles rose rapidly while CYP9K1 and CYP6P9a/b were overexpressed. PBO-based nets stayed highly effective versus complete failure of pyrethroid-only nets. Authors recommend PBO nets and organophosphate IRS.

articles.headline: Anopheles maculatus implicated as vector of zoonotic malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Evidence incriminating Anopheles maculatus as a potential vector of Plasmodium knowlesi and Plasmodium inui.
articles.authors: Sebayang BF, van de Straat B, Kurniawan A, Garjito TA, Haq AM, Westaway JAF, Lubis IND, Grigg MJ, Russell TL, Burkot TR
articles.details: This entomological study in Ujung Bandar Village, Langkat Regency, North Sumatra, Indonesia investigated Maculatus Subgroup mosquitoes collected by human landing catches (July 2022-June 2023), using ITS2 PCR, phylogenetics, and RT-qPCR targeting 18S rRNA with species-specific nested PCR for Plasmodium. All 234 specimens morphologically identified as An. maculatus s.l. were confirmed as An. maculatus s.s. Four specimens (1.7%; 95% CI 0.5-4.3%) were positive for Plasmodium genus DNA, with one positive for both Plasmodium knowlesi and Plasmodium inui—the first detection of zoonotic malaria parasite DNA in wild-caught An. maculatus. Authors conclude An. maculatus may be a potential vector of zoonotic and human malarias in North Sumatra.

articles.headline: Pregnant women's attitudes toward TB, malaria, GBS, RSV vaccines
articles.title_original: Pregnant women's attitudes and intentions toward tuberculosis, malaria, group B streptococcus, and respiratory syncytial virus vaccines in pregnant: Findings from a cross-sectional study of pregnant women living in Brazil, Ghana, Kenya, and Pakistan.
articles.authors: Limaye R, Schue J, Fesshaye B, Singh P, Miller E, Souza R, Jessani S, Temmerman M, Badzi CD, Sauer M, Brizuela V, Karron R
articles.details: A cross-sectional survey of 1,603 pregnant women recruited at primarily urban health facilities in Brazil, Ghana, Kenya, and Pakistan examined attitudes toward tuberculosis, malaria, group B streptococcus (GBS), and respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) vaccines. Vaccine safety for the baby was rated the most important decision factor, followed by efficacy for the baby and safety for the mother. Intention to definitely receive a vaccine was highest for GBS (30%), then malaria (26%), RSV (25%), and TB (19%). Regarding hesitancy, about 40% agreed vaccines are unnatural, 38% that the body should develop natural immunity, and 19% had delayed a recommended vaccine.

articles.headline: Hepcidin lower in malnourished children with asymptomatic malaria
articles.title_original: Hepcidin is low in children with moderate acute malnutrition and asymptomatic malaria: secondary analysis of a 2×2×3 factorial randomised trial in Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Helt TW, Kurtzhals J, List KK, Styrishave B, Yaméogo CW, Fabiansen C, Iuel-Brockdorf AS, Ritz C, Briend A, Filteau S, Michaelsen KF, Friis H, Christensen VB
articles.details: This secondary analysis of a randomised intervention trial in Burkina Faso assessed correlates of serum hepcidin (s-hepcidin) in 1,019 children with moderate acute malnutrition (MAM), who received 12 weeks of 500 kcal/d as corn-soy blend (CSB) or lipid-based nutritional supplements (LNS). Using Tobit regression, children with iron deficiency had 82% (95% CI 76, 87) lower s-hepcidin, while those with symptomatic malaria had 103% (95% CI 32, 210) higher s-hepcidin and those with recent or asymptomatic malaria had 51% (95% CI 35, 63) lower s-hepcidin. S-hepcidin rose 61% (95% CI 38, 87) after supplementation, 22% higher (95% CI 2, 45) with LNS than CSB. The authors call for further studies to corroborate the decreased s-hepcidin in malnourished children with asymptomatic malaria.

articles.headline: Growth-inhibitory epitope mapped in malaria vaccine candidate PfRipr5
articles.title_original: Identification of a growth-inhibitory epitope in PfRipr5, a malaria vaccine candidate against Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Nagaoka H, Fukushima A, Tsuboi T, Takashima E
articles.details: This laboratory study investigated PfRipr5, a truncated fragment (EGF-like domains 5–9) of the Plasmodium falciparum Rh5-interacting protein (PfRipr), a component of the PCRCR erythrocyte-invasion complex and a blood-stage vaccine candidate. Using Western blotting with cysteine-substituted mutants and surface plasmon resonance with a single-site kinetics model, the authors identified the epitope recognised by the growth-inhibitory monoclonal antibody 29B11. They defined a 20-amino-acid cysteine-associated epitope whose recognition depends on cysteine integrity. The authors conclude this provides a molecular entry point for dissecting PfRipr function and supports epitope-focused rational design of subunit vaccines against blood-stage malaria.

articles.headline: Imported malaria dominates near-elimination sites in southwestern Uganda
articles.title_original: Imported malaria predominates in near-elimination settings in Southwestern Uganda.
articles.authors: Mbabazi M, Kiyaga S, Katairo T, Kabbale KD, Asua V, Kagurusi BA, Wiringilimaana I, Nsengimaana B, Semakuba FD, Nakasaanya J, Ayitewala A, Watyekele E, Nabende I, Kayondo TM, Arinaitwe E, Mulondo J, Tukwasibwe S, Nsobya SL, Agaba B, Maiteki C, Jjingo D, Kateete DP, Kamya MR, Ssewanyana I, Aranda-Diaz A, Conrad MD, Murphy M, Gerlovina I, Epstein A, Rodriguez-Barraquer I, Rosenthal PJ, Dorsey G, Greenhouse B, Briggs J
articles.details: This study combined epidemiologic surveillance with parasite genomics at three health facilities in southwestern Uganda (Maziba and Muko, very low transmission; Kamwezi, low-to-moderate) between January 2023 and June 2024, enrolling microscopy-confirmed malaria cases with travel histories and dried blood spots. P. falciparum was genotyped using the MAD4HatTeR amplicon panel (165 diversity markers, 38 drug-resistance loci), with MOIRE, Dcifer, and the Plasmotrack Bayesian framework. Recent overnight travel was common in Maziba (87%) and Muko (96%) but infrequent in Kamwezi (12%); network inference identified most Maziba/Muko infections as imported, while Kamwezi showed sustained local transmission. Artemisinin partial-resistance markers K13 P441L and R561H were more prevalent in Kamwezi. The authors recommend tailored interventions addressing travel-associated and local transmission.

articles.headline: Computational screening finds natural-product inhibitors of mutant PfDHFR
articles.title_original: Computational exploration of natural product inhibitors against mutant Plasmodium falciparum DHFR through pharmacophore modeling, docking, and MD simulations.
articles.authors: Gogoi D, Gogoi B, Dutta PP, Kalita JM, Khataniar A, Sharma MP, Mandhadi JR, Phukan MM, Verma AK
articles.details: This in silico study used pharmacophore modeling, virtual screening, molecular docking, and 200 ns molecular dynamics simulations to identify natural-product inhibitors of mutant Plasmodium falciparum dihydrofolate reductase (pfDHFR), a target compromised by drug-resistance point mutations. A ligand-based pharmacophore (H1; r=0.949, r²=0.883) from 28 active compounds and a structure-based hypothesis (P1) were used to screen the TCM database after drug-likeness and ADME/Tox filtering. Three leads emerged: ZINC70454408 had the highest H-bond energy (−14.204 kcal/mol), predicted Ki of 0.04 µM, and stable RMSD averaging 2.1 Å, while ZINC04096650 and ZINC85631105 showed binding energies of −122.64 and −118.92 kcal/mol and Ki <0.1 µM. The authors highlight ZINC70454408 as the most promising candidate warranting experimental validation.

articles.headline: Urban Anopheles coluzzii in southeastern Nigeria resist pyrethroids and DDT
articles.title_original: Urban evolution of insecticide resistance and susceptibility patterns of Anopheles coluzzii in Southeastern Nigeria: implications for malaria vector control.
articles.authors: Ezeike AK, Danga SPY, Ezenwa VC, Chukas CF, Ogudu EO, Nwosu CM, Ebenebe IN, Chukwunwejim CR, Oli AN, Esimone CO
articles.details: This study tested phenotypic and genotypic insecticide resistance in Anopheles coluzzii from three urban centres (Awka, Onitsha, Nnewi) in Anambra State, southeastern Nigeria, using WHO susceptibility tests and screening for kdr L1014F and ace-1 G119S mutations. All specimens were An. coluzzii. Deltamethrin mortality was low (63%, 61%, 45%) and DDT susceptibility markedly reduced (56%, 25%, 20%), while bendiocarb and pirimiphos-methyl gave 100% mortality. The kdr 1014F allele was high (0.72–0.81) and ace-1 G119S low (0.07–0.12). Authors conclude pyrethroid/DDT resistance is driven by kdr, with carbamates and organophosphates still effective.

articles.headline: Photovoice study explores community views of monkey malaria in Sabah
articles.title_original: What does monkey malaria mean to you? A participatory photovoice study in rural Malaysian Borneo.
articles.authors: Naserrudin NA, Rahman AB, Manah AM, Donnie EM, Jamaji L, Lin PYP, Miller A, Krishnan M, Hassan MR
articles.details: This participatory photovoice study explored how rural communities in Sabah, Malaysia perceive zoonotic Plasmodium knowlesi (monkey) malaria. From January to May 2024, twenty participants aged 18 to 62 from three rural villages photographed their experiences over a 20-day period using their smartphones, followed by focus group discussions analysed with reflexive thematic analysis. Five interrelated themes emerged: monkey malaria as an embedded everyday risk, community resilience through locally derived practices, structural and socio-ecological challenges, the human-wildlife-environment interface, and cultural knowledge and blended practices for coping. The authors conclude effective malaria control should go beyond biomedical models to include local priorities, cultural practices, and lived experience.

articles.headline: Comparing three climate-based models of malaria transmission dynamics
articles.title_original: Comparing three approaches to modelling the effects of temperature and rainfall on malaria incidence in different climatic regions.
articles.authors: Madito GT, Brooke BD, Silal SP
articles.details: This modelling study built a vector-host compartmental model to compare three published approaches for incorporating temperature and rainfall effects on malaria transmission, simulated across three climate regions and varying annual vector control coverage. The first approach (biting and larval/adult mortality) indicated sustained seasonal transmission with lower cases per 1,000 in tropical versus semi-arid and sub-tropical regions; the second (temperature on life-cycle) projected extended peaks in tropical and semi-arid regions; the third (temperature and rainfall on behaviour, carrying capacity, aquatic survival) projected multiple irregular peaks ceasing in winter. Authors conclude climatic events like heatwaves or flooding can trigger outbreaks even in previously malaria-free areas, but sustained vector control plus larval source management and weather-informed timing can substantially reduce incidence.

articles.headline: Submicroscopic infections and gametocytes sustain malaria in Ethiopian community
articles.title_original: Community reservoirs of malaria parasites and gametocytes in Arba Minch district, southern Rift Valley, Ethiopia: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Zewde Z, Eligo N, Tesfaye Y, Lindtjørn B, Massebo F
articles.details: This reactive case detection study (July-October 2022) in Sile village, Gamo Zone, Southern Rift Valley, Ethiopia screened household and neighbouring contacts of index cases by microscopy with nested PCR confirmation. Of 2434 individuals visited following 142 PCR-confirmed index cases, 2009 were analysed: PCR-corrected microscopy-based prevalence was 3.6% (72/2009), and submicroscopic prevalence was 10.1% (33/326), comprising 4.6% P. vivax, 4.3% P. falciparum, and 1.3% mixed infections. Submicroscopic infections accounted for 31% (33/105) of PCR-confirmed cases. Index cases had higher asexual parasite density (16,177 vs 1900/uL; P<0.001) but lower gametocyte carriage, and gametocyte carriage was higher among P. vivax (22/32; 69%) than P. falciparum (6/27; 22%) reactive cases; the authors conclude community-based infections may help sustain transmission.

articles.headline: Nanobodies against Pfs48/45 block P. falciparum transmission
articles.title_original: Pfs48/45 nanobodies block Plasmodium falciparum transmission.
articles.authors: Lyons FMT, Chmielewski J, Gabriela M, Chan LJ, Tong J, Adair A, Zeglinski K, Gouil Q, Dietrich MH, Tham WH
articles.details: Researchers identified nanobodies against Pfs48/45 that recognise gametocytes and have strong transmission-reducing activity. Pfs48/45 and Pfs230 are two leading transmission-blocking vaccine candidates that form a complex on the surface of sexual-stage malaria parasites and are essential for male fertility. A crystal structure of the most potent nanobody bound to Pfs48/45 showed it binds a distinct epitope from the transmission-blocking monoclonal antibody TB31F but similar epitopes to RUPA-44 and RUPA-117. The authors conclude nanobodies are a versatile antibody format with potential to reduce malaria transmission.

articles.headline: Single DNA ligase essential for malaria blood and liver stages
articles.title_original: Plasmodium DNA ligase I is essential for parasite blood- and liver-stage development.
articles.authors: Pandey E, Mishra S, Varshney A, Habib S, Mishra S
articles.details: This experimental parasite-genetics study examined Plasmodium DNA ligase I (Lig1), the sole DNA ligase encoded by the malaria parasite nuclear genome, in human (P. falciparum) and rodent (P. berghei) parasites. Lig1 localized primarily to the nucleus throughout the life cycle, with some distribution across the apicoplast and mitochondrion. Attempts to disrupt PbLig1 in blood stages failed, indicating it is essential; using an Flp/FRT conditional knockout in sporozoites, PbLig1-deficient parasites invaded hepatocytes but arrested at mid-liver stage with limited nuclear division and reduced DNA content. The authors conclude Lig1 is essential for blood- and liver-stage development.

articles.headline: Review of current and emerging malaria prevention strategies
articles.title_original: The Evolving Landscape of Malaria Prevention Strategies: A Review of Recent Developments.
articles.authors: Charavanamuttu Y, Agyeman Wamba A, Taylor-Robinson AW, Lampejo T
articles.details: This review synthesises current and emerging malaria prevention approaches across vector control, chemoprevention, and immunoprophylaxis. It notes insecticide-treated nets and indoor residual spraying remain cornerstones of vector control but are increasingly compromised by widespread insecticide resistance, while chemoprevention (intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy and seasonal malaria chemoprevention in children) is highly efficacious yet falls below WHO uptake targets. The authors highlight the RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M vaccines as landmark achievements, with large-scale rollouts reducing severe disease and mortality, and discuss novel approaches such as monoclonal antibodies and genetically modified mosquitoes. They conclude that adaptive, multifaceted strategies are needed to achieve malaria elimination.

articles.headline: New sporozoite-specific rhoptry protein identified in malaria parasite
articles.title_original: Identification of a novel rhoptry protein expressed predominantly in Plasmodium sporozoites.
articles.authors: Oundavong S, Sekine T, Torii M, Ozawa T, Kosako H, Shinzawa N, Ishino T
articles.details: Using proximity-dependent biotin identification (UltraID) combined with genome editing in the rodent malaria parasite Plasmodium berghei, researchers screened for sporozoite-specific rhoptry proteins. Rhoptry neck protein 12 (RON12), which had the highest transcript levels in sporozoites, was tagged with UltraID as bait, and several secretory proteins including known rhoptry proteins were biotinylated during parasite maturation in mosquitoes. A novel rhoptry molecule, PBANKA_1363400, was identified and localized to sporozoite rhoptries, expressed predominantly in sporozoites rather than merozoites. The authors conclude the UltraID strategy enables sensitive, stage-specific protein identification in Plasmodium sporozoites.

articles.headline: Potato inhibitor blocks mosquito enzyme key to malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Inhibition of carboxypeptidase b1 from Anopheles stephensi by the potato carboxypeptidase inhibitor: a foundational step toward paratransgenesis-based malaria control.
articles.authors: Monshizadeh Z, Rismani E, Abdi F, Pakdel JD, Ghanbarnejad N, Raz A
articles.details: This laboratory study evaluated the Potato Carboxypeptidase Inhibitor (PCI) as an effector molecule for paratransgenesis-based malaria control, targeting carboxypeptidase-B1 from Anopheles stephensi (CPBAs1), an enzyme important for sexual development of Plasmodium in the mosquito midgut. The cpbas1 and pci genes were cloned into a pET-23a expression vector, expressed, and purified, and PCI's inhibitory effect was tested alongside the commercial inhibitor 1,10-phenanthroline. PCI efficiently inhibited CPBAs1 enzymatic activity at low concentrations. The authors conclude PCI's strong inhibitory activity and structural features make it a promising effector molecule for future paratransgenesis approaches.

articles.headline: Conserved PTRAMP–CSS–Ripr complex enables erythrocyte invasion across species
articles.title_original: PTRAMP, CSS and Ripr form a conserved complex required for merozoite invasion of Plasmodium species into erythrocytes.
articles.authors: Seager BA, Lim PS, Xiao X, Lai KH, Feufack-Donfack LB, Dass S, Jung NC, Abraham A, Grigg MJ, Anstey NM, William T, Sattabongkot J, Leis A, Longley RJ, Duraisingh MT, Popovici J, Wilson DW, Cowman AF, Scally SW
articles.details: This structural and biochemical study investigated PTRAMP, CSS, and Ripr orthologs across three Plasmodium species to define conserved features of the erythrocyte-invasion complex. Unlike Rh5 (restricted to P. falciparum and close relatives), these orthologs are present genus-wide; PTRAMP and CSS formed a disulfide-linked heterodimer in P. vivax and P. knowlesi, and all three species formed a PTRAMP-CSS-Ripr (PCR) complex by binding Ripr's C-terminal region. Cross-reactive antibodies differentially inhibited merozoite invasion, and a crystal structure revealed an inhibitory epitope on the C-terminal tail of PvRipr. Cryo-EM of the P. knowlesi PCR complex confirmed a core invasion scaffold with implications for multi-species malaria vaccines.

articles.headline: Rainfall-lagged models forecast under-five malaria trends in Ghana
articles.title_original: Malaria incidence, severity and mortality in children under five in Ghana: evidence from generalised additive models.
articles.authors: Bosson-Amedenu S, Eyiah-Bediako F, Anafo AY
articles.details: Using 132 monthly surveillance observations (2013–2023) from Tarkwa-Nsuaem, Ghana, this study fitted negative-binomial generalized additive models linking under-five malaria incidence, severe cases, and deaths to rainfall and temperature. Median monthly incidence was 847 cases, while severe cases (median 2) and deaths were rare. Adding rainfall 'memory' (1–3-month lags, 3–6-month accumulations) improved the incidence model (AIC 1,269→1,234; deviance explained ≈80%; adjusted R²≈0.88), with trend, Rain_lag1 (p=.002) and Rain_roll6 (p=.002) significant. Authors conclude transmission reflects recent rainfall history, supporting interpretable early-warning forecasts (12-month projection ~600–670 cases/month).

articles.headline: Splenic abscess with falciparum malaria and sickle cell trait
articles.title_original: Pyogenic Splenic Abscess With Concurrent Active Plasmodium falciparum Malaria and Sickle Cell Trait: A Case Report.
articles.authors: Gumbs S, Gikunda W, Das H, Douchee J, Wilkinson CA
articles.details: This case report describes a young male from West Africa who developed a pyogenic splenic abscess alongside concurrent Plasmodium falciparum malaria and sickle cell trait, with data from retrospective chart review and a literature review. He presented with abdominal pain and fever after starting antimalarial treatment; imaging showed a large splenic abscess, and percutaneous drainage yielded purulent fluid culturing Fusobacterium species. Treatment combined antimalarial therapy and broad-spectrum antibiotics with anaerobic coverage, and he recovered fully. The authors urge suspicion for anaerobic bacterial pathogens, particularly Fusobacterium, in splenic abscesses among patients from malaria-endemic regions.

articles.headline: Digital gangrene complicates cerebral falciparum malaria despite artesunate
articles.title_original: Purpura Fulminans With Digital Gangrene in Severe Plasmodium falciparum Malaria: A Case Presentation.
articles.authors: Palmares I, Morgado C, Dá Mesquita Faustino F, Silva J, Freitas P
articles.details: This case report describes a 66-year-old woman who returned from Angola without antimalarial chemoprophylaxis and presented with cerebral malaria due to Plasmodium falciparum. Despite early intravenous artesunate, hyperbaric oxygen therapy, and supportive care, she developed purpuric foot lesions that progressed to gangrene, though systemic recovery was achieved. The authors note purpura fulminans and peripheral gangrene are rare severe malaria complications from microvascular thrombosis, and that prompt effective antimalarial therapy is the most important management step since no strategies reliably prevent progression to gangrene.

articles.headline: Community-based malaria messaging boosts bed net use in Uganda
articles.title_original: Differentiated effects of sources of malaria messages on mosquito bed net use among women of reproductive age in Uganda: a propensity score matched analysis.
articles.authors: Mwebesa E, Agbi DM, Iha DT, Kogei DK, Agasa LO, Kerich GK, Mwangi A
articles.details: This secondary analysis of the 2018–2019 Uganda Malaria Indicator Survey used one-to-one propensity score matching among 3,488 women aged 15–49 who reported exposure to at least one malaria message, estimating effects of nine communication platforms on mosquito bed net use. Overall, 73.2% reported using a bed net the previous night. Messages from community health workers (ATT=0.322, 95% CI 0.111–0.533), community events (ATT=0.296, 95% CI 0.085–0.507), and social mobilization (ATT=0.185, 95% CI 0.008–0.362) significantly increased use, while television (ATT=0.051, 95% CI −0.062–0.164) did not. Authors conclude community-based and interpersonal channels are more strongly associated with bed net use than some mass media.

articles.headline: Gut barrier loss drives purinergic immunosuppression in severe malaria
articles.title_original: Intestinal Barrier Loss Enables Microbiota-Mediated Purinergic Suppression During Malaria.
articles.authors: Polidoro RB, Bednarski OJ, Lehman S, Oliveira FMS, Fusco EM, Bower L, Rangel-Ferreira M, Kour S, Namazzi R, Opoka RO, John C, Brault JJ, Tilston-Lunel A, Alves-Filho JC, Schmidt NW
articles.details: Using a murine model, this study found susceptibility to Plasmodium yoelii hyperparasitemia was associated with elevated regulatory T cells and diminished IFN-γ, and showed the IgA-coated microbiota fraction transfers this susceptibility. Plasmodium infection disrupted the intestinal barrier regardless of microbiota composition, with barrier loss linked to systemic adenosine persistence and expansion of CD39+ plasmablasts. Ugandan children with severe malaria showed a distinct purinergic immune signature versus asymptomatic community children. Reinforcing the gut barrier or blocking the adenosine A2A receptor improved germinal centers and reduced disease severity independent of parasite burden, defining a purinergic-dependent immunosuppression pathway driving pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Survey finds 24.5% malaria positivity in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan
articles.title_original: Determinants of malaria infection across different districts of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Haq IU, Mehmood Z, Muhammad A, Akhtar S, Aboagarib EAA, Zaib A, Ahmed SAM, Huwaikem M, Khan GA, Albugami HF, Ahmed B, Qu S
articles.details: This cross-sectional study surveyed 768 people across northern, central, and southern districts of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (KP) province, using a structured questionnaire and a combo rapid diagnostic test to identify determinants of malaria. The overall malaria proportion was 24.5%. In the final multivariate model, independent risk factors included male gender (AOR 1.8), age 16–30 (AOR 4.2) and 31–50 years (AOR 8.3), joint family type (AOR 2.6), low education (AOR 3.9), income 10,000–50,000 rupees (AOR 17.8), large family size (AOR 11.3), distance >3 km to healthcare (AOR 3.1), and lack of modern toilets (AOR 4.8). The authors call for tailored interventions in education, healthcare, sanitation, and bed nets.

articles.headline: Plasmodium malariae latent up to 50 years in seven Guangdong cases
articles.title_original: Unraveling the origins and features of long-latency Plasmodium malariae infections: a comprehensive epidemiological, clinical, and genetic study of seven cases in Guangdong Province (2014-2022).
articles.authors: Zhang JY, Mao Q, Wu D, Deng ZH, Liu J, Liao YH, Lu WC, Zhang GT, Zhang XC, Chen JD
articles.details: A retrospective study analyzed seven long-latency Plasmodium malariae infections in Guangdong Province (2014–2022) using clinical, epidemiological, and circumsporozoite protein gene data to trace origins. Cases showed female predominance (85.7%) and mean age 75.3 years, dispersed across seven counties; 85.7% (6/7) had resided in historically endemic regions. Infection sources were historical local transmission (6 cases) or Southeast Asian importation (1 case), with latency periods of 7 to 50 years. Phylogenetic analysis placed cases in the Asian lineage (95–97% homology), distinct from African strains (92–95%). Diagnostic delays had a median of 4 days. Authors urge enhanced surveillance among older people with unexplained fever in formerly endemic areas.

articles.headline: Engineered tandem antigen vaccine boosts malaria transmission blocking
articles.title_original: A stabilized tandem antigen chimera that elicits potent malaria transmission-reducing activity.
articles.authors: Ivanochko D, Miura K, Hailemariam S, Ravichandran R, Song Y, Huang WC, Stoter R, Teelen K, van Gemert GJ, Leaf EM, Chan S, Men C, Semesi A, Shiu C, MacGill RS, Long CA, Jore MM, King NP, Lovell JF, Julien JP
articles.details: Researchers developed a stabilized tandem antigen chimera (STAC) transmission-blocking vaccine candidate combining the most potent epitopes of Plasmodium falciparum Pfs230 domain 1 and Pfs48/45 domain 3 in a single construct, masking non-functional epitopes via engineered pseudo-native domain arrangement. Structure-guided optimization improved antigen yield and stability, and nanoparticle multimerization enhanced function. In female mice, STAC genetically conjugated to self-assembling protein nanoparticles elicited antibodies with transmission-reducing activity comparable or superior to the separately multimerized Pfs230-D1 and Pfs48/45-D3. Authors propose STAC as a promising next-generation transmission-blocking vaccine and a strategy for cost-efficient multi-component vaccine engineering.

articles.headline: Ugandan caregivers aware of R21 malaria vaccine but lack knowledge
articles.title_original: Caregivers awareness, knowledge, attitudes, and perceptions towards malaria vaccine in Kole and Kwania Districts, Northern Uganda.
articles.authors: Puleh SS, Kalyango JN, Ocan M, Yeka A, Siu G, Idro R, Akech SI, Acen J, Ejalu DL, Nankinga Z, Afayo R, Nankabirwa JI
articles.details: A sequential explanatory mixed-methods study assessed awareness, knowledge, attitudes, and perceptions of the R21 malaria vaccine among 574 randomly selected caregivers of age-eligible children in Kole and Kwania districts, Northern Uganda, with follow-up focus group discussions. Awareness was high at 90.1% (95% CI 87.3–92.4), but only 13.9% (95% CI 11.2–17.0) showed factual knowledge, including number of doses (16.4%), dosing intervals (21.0%), and age eligibility (34.2%). Positive attitudes were reported by 57.4% and positive perceptions by 57.5%, though nearly half voiced safety concerns. Authors recommend regular nationwide, locally tailored community education campaigns.

articles.headline: Ethiopian hospital malaria positivity rose to 17.7%, vivax dominant
articles.title_original: A five-year (2020-2024) malaria surveillance data analysis of Mattu Karl Specialized Comprehensive Hospital, Oromia region, Southwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Endalu T, Mohammed M, Bulcha I, Ushu L, Befkadu Z, Tamiru E, Ketema T, Dereje G, Teferi S
articles.details: Of 100,760 patients tested for malaria by microscopy at Mattu Karl Comprehensive Specialized Hospital in Ethiopia from 2020 to 2024, 14,303 (14.2%) were positive, with the positivity rate rising from 9.2% in 2020 to 17.7% in 2024. Plasmodium vivax accounted for 49.1% of cases, P. falciparum 37.8%, and mixed infections 13.1%. Females represented 51.4% of suspected patients and 51.8% of confirmed cases. This retrospective record review concluded that malaria incidence increased over the period with a concerning rise in mixed infections, underscoring the need for control strategies tailored to species dynamics.

articles.headline: Essay on malaria's overlooked role in early Palestine history
articles.title_original: Malaria in Palestine: the elephant in the room.
articles.authors: Alexander A
articles.details: This is a historical commentary/opinion paper arguing that malaria's role has been overlooked when considering the origins of the Arab-Israel conflict in the Middle East. The author contends that boycotting topics linked to Zionism or Israel has caused the malaria community to miss studying the events leading to the first sustained malaria control efforts. The piece asserts that over 100 years ago many rural areas of Palestine were desolate or uninhabitable due to the severity of the disease. No study design, population, or quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: New Python tool predicts multiplicity of infection from malaria genomic data
articles.title_original: Malaria-MOI: A flexible and scalable tool for predicting multiplicity of infection in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Billows N, Phelan J, Thorpe J, Vanheer LN, Tan MK, Campino S, Clark TG
articles.details: Methods/benchmarking study presenting Malaria-MOI, a fast, species-agnostic Python tool that infers multiplicity of infection (MOI) directly from standard genomics files (BAM, VCF) from whole genome sequencing, without training data or curated panels. Benchmarked on 27 P. falciparum mixed-clone samples with known MOI, it achieved a root mean squared error of 0.38, mean absolute error of 0.15, and correlation of 0.78 across 165 loci, exceeding median performance of existing Bayesian and likelihood methods. Applied to 8,208 field samples it correlated strongly negatively with FWS (ρ=−0.76), with sensitivity 0.99 for monoclonal infections and specificity 0.76 versus estMOI's 0.58. Authors conclude it suits genomic surveillance and elimination, supporting Illumina and Nanopore platforms.

articles.headline: Bed net use and malaria highest gaps in school-aged children, Côte d'Ivoire
articles.title_original: Disparities in long-lasting insecticidal bed net usage and malaria burden 2 years after a mass distribution campaign in central Côte d'Ivoire: A cross-sectional survey prior to a cluster randomised trial.
articles.authors: Sih C, Assi SB, Dangbenon E, Talbot B, Biggs J, Koffi AA, Alou LPA, Messenger LA, Zoh MG, Camara S, Kulkarni MA, Protopopoff N, N'Guessan R, Cook J
articles.details: Cross-sectional baseline survey of 1,672 individuals of all ages across 33 clusters in Côte d'Ivoire (July 2023), two years after a mass LLIN distribution and prior to an LLIN trial, using rapid diagnostic tests and multivariable logistic regression. LLIN ownership was 66.3% and access 27.8%; usage was 50.0% (aOR 0.50), 44.3% (aOR 0.41) and 56.9% (aOR 0.72) at ages 5-9, 10-14 and ≥15 years versus 63.2% in under-fives, and lowest in females aged 10-14 (41.0%). Malaria infection prevalence was 41.1% overall, peaking at 75.0% in ages 5-9 (aOR 1.86), while LLIN users had lower infection (38.5%, aOR 0.74) than non-users (44.3%). The authors recommend school-based net distribution, malaria education, routine screening and treatment, and chemoprophylaxis in schools to protect school-aged children.

articles.headline: Expert consensus on factors shaping P. knowlesi malaria prevention behaviours
articles.title_original: Exploring influencing factors affecting preventive behaviours of Plasmodium knowlesi malaria among at-risk communities: an application of the nominal group technique.
articles.authors: Md Hanif SA, Hassan MR, Safian N, Alabed AAA, Rafi'i MR, Naserrudin NA, Hussain NAMA
articles.details: Consensus-building study using the Nominal Group Technique (NGT) with a panel of experts experienced in malaria programme implementation to identify and prioritize factors affecting preventive behaviours against zoonotic Plasmodium knowlesi malaria in at-risk Southeast Asian communities. NGT sessions were conducted virtually in October 2024, with consensus defined as 80% agreement. Of 21 items presented, 20 were retained for final ranking, with a 100% voting response rate and consensus reached in a single round; items were grouped into four themes: environmental risks, human-related risks, policy and organizational factors, and vector-related risks. Authors conclude the findings provide a foundation for refining P. knowlesi prevention strategies.

articles.headline: Nanobodies and first structure target malaria transmission-blocking protein PfHAP2
articles.title_original: Crystal structure and nanobodies against domain 3 of the malaria parasite fusogen Plasmodium falciparum HAP2.
articles.authors: Lyons FMT, Chmielewski J, Chan LJ, Gabriela M, Chan RWB, Adair A, Tong J, Pymm P, Zeglinski K, Gouil Q, Tham WH, Dietrich MH
articles.details: Structural and antibody-discovery study targeting Plasmodium falciparum fusogen PfHAP2, which is essential for gamete fusion during fertilisation in the mosquito midgut and a proposed transmission-blocking target. The authors identified nanobodies binding specifically to domain 3 of PfHAP2 with nanomolar affinities, two of which showed some cross-species reactivity with HAP2 of other Plasmodium species. They solved the crystal structure of one nanobody in complex with domain 3, providing the first structural insights into this target, for which no monoclonal antibodies or structures had previously been generated.

articles.headline: New tetrahydrocarboline compound inhibits malaria parasite HDAC enzyme
articles.title_original: Discovery of γ-Tetrahydrocarboline Derivatives as Plasmodium falciparum Histone Deacetylase Inhibitors for Treatment of Malaria.
articles.authors: Ni H, Qian Y, Dai Z, Cai Y, Cao L, Li J, Lai Z, Hu X, Huang Z, Jiang L, Cui S
articles.details: Medicinal-chemistry study synthesizing and screening 33 new γ-tetrahydrocarboline derivatives as Plasmodium falciparum histone deacetylase 1 (PfHDAC1) inhibitors, building on the toxic lead Quisinostat via scaffold hopping and linker optimization. Lead compound 5ac showed potent activity against P. falciparum 3D7 (IC50 = 4.1 nM) with favorable safety (HepG2 IC50 = 1.5 μM, SI = 358; HEK293T IC50 = 15 μM, SI = 3573), improved physicochemical properties, and in vivo antimalarial activity. Mechanistically, 5ac downregulated malaria invasion-related genes. The authors conclude these derivatives are promising PfHDAC-targeted antimalarial agents.

articles.headline: Proteomic profiling of circulating microparticles in malaria patients
articles.title_original: Integrative proteomic profiling of malaria-derived microparticles: A mass spectrometry-based study.
articles.authors: Antwi-Baffour S, Adjei JK, Agyemang-Yeboah F, Annani-Akollor M, Kyeremeh R, Asare GA, Gyan B
articles.details: Mass spectrometry (2D LC-MS) proteomic study comparing plasma membrane-derived microparticles (MPs) from 43 malaria-diagnosed patients, 10 healthy controls, and parasite culture supernatant, with MPs confirmed by flow cytometry. A total of 1806 proteins were identified: 1729 in malaria-positive MPs, 333 in culture supernatant, and 234 in controls; the mean protein count in malaria MPs was significantly higher than controls. Haemoglobin subunits were significantly more abundant in malaria and culture MPs. The authors conclude that haemoglobin loss via MPs may initiate and progress anaemia in P. falciparum infection, with some proteins up- and others down-regulated.

articles.headline: Review of mosquito-borne arboviral and parasitic diseases including malaria
articles.title_original: Review of selected mosquito-borne diseases: arboviruses (dengue, chikungunya, Zika, West Nile, Japanese encephalitis, yellow fever) and parasitic diseases (malaria, lymphatic Filariasis).
articles.authors: Wei YL, Wu Z, Li RL, Tang F
articles.details: This review of mosquito-borne diseases transmitted by Aedes, Anopheles, and Culex mosquitoes reports they account for 17% of infectious diseases and roughly 700,000 annual deaths. It covers viral (dengue, Zika, chikungunya), parasitic (malaria, lymphatic filariasis), and zoonotic (Japanese encephalitis) pathogens, summarizing their clinical features, global distribution—concentrated in the tropics but expanding due to climate change and urbanization—and transmission dynamics shaped by temperature, humidity, and urban breeding sites. Control strategies discussed include vector management, vaccines (R21 for malaria, IXCHIQ for chikungunya), chemoprophylaxis, and novel technologies. Challenges highlighted include insecticide resistance and socioeconomic costs ($318 billion for Aedes-borne diseases since 1975).

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria in blood donors: 0.32% global pooled prevalence
articles.title_original: Global burden of asymptomatic malaria among blood donors: systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Malede B, Amare GA, Setegn A, Fenta A, Mengist A, Belew H, Muche Y, Abebaw D, Melkamu A, Ashagre A, Jemal M, Teffera ZH, Belayneh M, Abebaw A, Getinet M, Wondmagegn M, Abeje G, Almaw A, Berhan A, Adugna A, Admasu A, Wondimu H, Misganaw T, Wondmagegn YM
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis (PRISMA 2020) of 15 eligible studies from Africa, Asia, and South America estimating the pooled prevalence of asymptomatic malaria among blood donors as a transfusion-transmission risk. The overall pooled prevalence was 0.32% (95% CI: 0.25–0.39; I2 = 99.2%). By region, Africa was highest at 15.89% (95% CI: 9.48–22.31), followed by South America at 0.79% (95% CI: 0.30–1.89) and Asia lowest at 0.21% (95% CI: 0.15–0.27). By diagnostic method, prevalence was 21.86% by microscopy, 5.03% by PCR, and 0.21% by rapid diagnostic test. The authors conclude region-specific donor screening, especially in Africa, is needed for transfusion safety.

articles.headline: Proline transporter PfApiAT2 essential for malaria mosquito transmission
articles.title_original: PfApiAT2 is a proline transporter essential for the transmission of Plasmodium falciparum by the mosquito vector.
articles.authors: Khushu M, Kissel RC, Kauffman J, Taccheri C, Singh N, Summers RL, Plant LD, Wirth DF, Catteruccia F, Bopp S
articles.details: Molecular/genetic study showing the amino acid transporter PfApiAT2 is a proline-specific transporter essential for early Plasmodium falciparum oocyst development in Anopheles gambiae mosquitoes, building on its known role regulating proline homeostasis and halofuginone resistance in blood-stage parasites. Halofuginone-resistant pfapiat2-mutant parasites formed stunted oocysts defective in sporozoite production, while PfApiAT2-knockout parasites showed a complete block in sporogony, with oocysts stalling and degenerating. This growth defect was rescued by nutrient supplementation to the mosquito. The authors conclude PfApiAT2 represents a transmission vulnerability reflecting the parasite's nutritional dependence on its mosquito vector.

articles.headline: Zanthoxylum chalybeum rootbark extract clears malaria parasites in mice
articles.title_original: Antimalarial efficacy of aqueous crude rootbark extracts of Zanthoxylum Chalybeum: An in vivo experimental study in Wistar Albino Mice infected with Plasmodium berghei.
articles.authors: Michael O, Lwanira CN, Ocan M, Alele PE
articles.details: In vivo experimental study in Wistar albino mice infected with Plasmodium berghei NK65, testing aqueous rootbark extracts of Zanthoxylum chalybeum at 400, 200, and 100 mg/kg versus an artemether-lumefantrine (4 mg/kg) positive control and distilled water (10 mL/kg) negative control, given over five days from day three post-infection (ANOVA). The extracts achieved parasite clearance up to 76%, with significantly higher clearance than the negative control at 100 mg/kg (MD=15.6; p<0.001), 200 mg/kg (MD=16.42; p<0.001), and 400 mg/kg (MD=17.24; p<0.001), plus significantly lower temperature, but no remarkable difference in haemoglobin/haematocrit. The authors conclude Z. chalybeum is effective for parasite clearance and maintaining body temperature.

articles.headline: New scalable CRISPR-Cas9 system for high-throughput Plasmodium berghei gene editing
articles.title_original: Plasmodium berghei High-Throughput (PbHiT): a CRISPR-Cas9 System to Study Genes at Scale.
articles.authors: Jonsdottir TK, Paoletta MS, Henriksson J, Bushell ESC
articles.details: Methods/protocol paper presenting PbHiT (Plasmodium berghei High-Throughput), a scalable CRISPR-Cas9 genome-editing system for rodent malaria parasites. A single cloning step generates vectors linking a guide RNA to short (100 bp) homology arms for precise integration, with the gRNA also serving as a barcode enabling pooled transfections and mutant identification by gRNA sequencing. The system reliably recapitulates known mutant growth phenotypes and supports both knockout and tagging strategies, with an accompanying free online resource covering the entire P. berghei protein-coding genome. Authors present it as a reproducible tool for targeted functional studies and high-throughput genetic screens.

articles.headline: Pediatric mixed P. vivax–P. falciparum infection with disparate parasitemias
articles.title_original: Pediatric Mixed Plasmodium vivax-P. falciparum Infection with Disparate Parasitemias: Diagnostic and Surveillance Challenges.
articles.authors: Estela-Zape JL
articles.details: Case report of a 9-year-old boy from an endemic area with a six-day febrile syndrome. Thick smear and peripheral blood film microscopy with rapid diagnostic tests (pan-Plasmodium and HRP2) confirmed mixed infection with P. vivax (5500 parasites/µL) and P. falciparum (562 parasites/µL); he was hemodynamically stable without severe malaria criteria. After confirming normal G6PD activity, he was treated with artemether-lumefantrine followed by primaquine for hypnozoite eradication, with favorable evolution and documented parasite clearance. Authors highlight the risk of underestimating P. falciparum in mixed infections with disparate parasitemias and the value of integrated diagnostics in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Afebrile falciparum malaria presenting as severe hemolytic anemia in traveler
articles.title_original: Afebrile falciparum malaria with severe hemolytic anemia in a returning traveler: a case report.
articles.authors: Liu J, Chen X, Yang J, Xie J, Liu Z
articles.details: Case report of a 45-year-old Chinese man with a 12-day history of dizziness, fatigue, and poor appetite who developed acute confusion and quadriparesis; he remained afebrile throughout. Labs showed severe hemolytic anemia (hemoglobin 48 g/L), thrombocytopenia, and intravascular hemolysis, with neuroimaging ruling out acute intracranial pathology. Diagnosis was confirmed by peripheral blood smear showing P. falciparum (0.9% parasitemia, ~1746 parasites/µL), supported by HRP-2 antigen and PCR. Treatment with intramuscular artesunate followed by oral dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine led to full recovery and parasite clearance. Authors stress vigilance for afebrile malaria in returning travelers in elimination settings.

articles.headline: Anopheles population genetics across Uganda islands and mainland for vector control
articles.title_original: Assessing the population genetic structure and demographic history of Anopheles gambiae and Anopheles arabiensis at island and mainland sites in Uganda: implications for testing novel malaria vector control approaches.
articles.authors: Mwima R, Hui TJ, Lukyamuzi E, Bodde M, Makunin A, Birungi K, Lukindu M, Nanteza A, Muhanguzi D, Lawniczak M, Burt A, Kayondo JK
articles.details: Population genetics study assessing genetic structure, diversity, and demographic history of Anopheles gambiae and Anopheles arabiensis at six Uganda sites (three Lake Victoria islands and three mainland), genotyping 2918 An. gambiae and 173 An. arabiensis via targeted amplicon sequencing of 62 loci. Analyses showed clear species separation but little within-species differentiation; An. gambiae pairwise FST was low (0.00054–0.028) but often significant, with greater island isolation, An. arabiensis mainland populations appearing panmictic, negative Tajima's D indicating recent expansion, and small island effective population sizes (146–249) versus large mainland (4054–8190). Authors conclude geographic and ecological factors shape vector structure, informing design and monitoring of genetic-based control interventions.

articles.headline: One in seven adults with severe malaria had concurrent bacteremia in Uganda
articles.title_original: Prevalence and factors associated with concomitant bacteremia among adults admitted with severe malaria at Kayunga Regional Referral Hospital, Uganda.
articles.authors: Abdi FD, Hirsi AM, Ali M, Karshe AH, Sandeyl AA, Hersi AG, Yusuf AA, Bekele HK, Mohamed AA, Jayte M, Ezera A
articles.details: In this cross-sectional study of 207 adults with severe malaria at Kayunga Regional Referral Hospital in Uganda, 14.5% had concomitant bacteremia. The most common isolates were Salmonella typhi (33.3%), Staphylococcus aureus (30%), and Streptococcus spp. (16.7%). CNS symptoms, low peripheral oxygen saturation (SPO2), hyperparasitaemia, and leucocytosis were significantly associated with bacteremia. The authors conclude these features mark high-risk patients and that, absent culture and sensitivity results, a combination of ciprofloxacin and a penicillin can provide acceptable empirical coverage.

articles.headline: No Pfmdr1 N86Y mutant alleles found in P. falciparum from Mbouda, Cameroon
articles.title_original: Molecular surveillance of the Plasmodium falciparum Pfmdr1 N86Y mutation linked to antimalarial drug resistance in Mbouda, Cameroon.
articles.authors: Nadia NAC, Ngouana V, Cedric Y, Djakbe DL, Innes W, Pacome TK, Armand GNG, Edna GDM, Merveille TT, Fuyou Z, Hu H
articles.details: This molecular surveillance study recruited 302 patients across three health facilities in the Mbouda Health District, diagnosed by rapid diagnostic test and thick blood smear, to assess the prevalence of the P. falciparum Pfmdr1-N86Y mutation using nested PCR and RFLP with the AflIII enzyme. P. falciparum infection prevalence was 28.8%, with the highest burden among males, children aged 0–5 years, and adults over 35 years; the Pfmdr1 gene was detected in 95% of isolates. Across samples, 94% of alleles were wild-type (N86) and 1% were mixed (N86/Y86), with no mutant Y86 alleles observed, while 5% tested negative for the gene. The authors highlight the importance of evaluating other Pfmdr1 mutations and taking measures to delay the emergence of resistance.

articles.headline: Admixture-driven Duffy-null malaria adaptation found in Sudanese Copts
articles.title_original: Sudan's complex genetic admixture history drives adaptation to malaria in Sudanese Copts.
articles.authors: Vilà-Valls L, Garcia-Calleja J, Prado-Martinez J, Bosch E, Andrés AM, Netea MG, Comas D, Hassan HY
articles.journal: Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A
articles.details: This whole-genome sequencing study analyzed high-coverage (~30×) genomes from 125 individuals representing five ethnolinguistic groups across three language families in Sudan, revealing deep population structure with Nilo-Saharan, West Eurasian, Northern African, and Western African ancestral components. The authors report over one million novel variants, including population-specific deleterious alleles. Local ancestry inference revealed a strong signal of adaptive admixture on chromosome 1 in Sudanese Copts, with a peak of Nilo-Saharan ancestry introduced 1,000–1,500 years ago. They estimate a strong selection coefficient (s = 0.0996) for SNP rs2814778 in the ACKR1 gene responsible for the Duffy-null blood group, which confers resistance to P. vivax malaria.

articles.headline: Mark-recapture shows Anopheles farauti disperses farther than documented in Australia
articles.title_original: Estimating the dispersal of the malaria vector Anopheles farauti through a natural ecosystem in north Queensland, Australia using mark release and recapture experiments.
articles.authors: Chow WK, Cooper RD, Pagendam D, Trewin BJ, Beebe NW
articles.details: This study used fluorescent dust-based mark release recapture experiments to quantify dispersal of the malaria vector Anopheles farauti through a non-village natural ecosystem in tropical north Queensland, Australia. Across 51 releases of 19,889 marked mosquitoes during wet and dry seasons, 308 (1.55%) were recaptured, mostly (78%) by a bespoke mosquito catch barrier system. The longest flight distance was 3.51 km, with an estimated travel of about 73 m per day; parity dissections showed daily survival of 0.68 in the wet season and 0.75 in the dry season. The authors conclude An. farauti flies longer distances than documented, informing transmission modelling, gene drive spread, and vector control.

articles.headline: Baseline P. falciparum antigenic diversity before vaccine rollout in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Complexity of Infection and Plasmodium falciparum Circumsporozoite Protein Diversity Prior to Malaria Vaccine Implementation in Kaelé Health District, Cameroon, 2022-2023.
articles.authors: Ali IM, Martinez BV, Tchuenkam VPK, Sadler JM, Gorman CC, Nsango SE, Voundi V, Marcellin AJ, Wanda GY, Bailey JA, Dinglasan RR, Lin JT, Juliano JJ
articles.details: This study analyzed 100 P. falciparum-positive dried blood spots from Kaele Health District, Cameroon, using the 4CAST amplicon sequencing assay targeting csp, ama, sera2, and trap genes to characterize antigenic diversity and complexity of infection (COI) before RTS,S/AS01 vaccine implementation. The csp gene was genotyped in 35% of samples, yielding 22 haplotypes (He = 0.908; π = 0.021), with the vaccine-matched haplotype present in 20% of genotyped infections and signs of balancing selection in csp TH2/TH3 epitopes. Mean COI was highest for ama (2.8), followed by sera2 (2.1), csp (1.3), and trap (1.2). The authors conclude vaccine-matched strains and high epitope diversity may influence vaccine efficacy, warranting continued molecular surveillance.

articles.headline: Residual antimalarial drugs found pre-treatment, signaling drug pressure
articles.title_original: Residual antimalarial drug concentrations before treatment in malaria patients in Asia and Africa: A systematic review and meta-analysis depicting its implication for drug pressure.
articles.authors: Marwa KJ, Zinga M, Mnkungwe HR, Mutagonda R, Kapesa A, Mwaiswelo RO, Mmbando B, Lusingu JPA, Kamugisha E
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis pooled 15 studies of 3,522 P. falciparum malaria patients to estimate how often patients had residual antimalarial drug levels in their blood before treatment. Using a random-effects model (heterogeneity I2 >95%, p<0.01), the overall pooled proportion with pre-treatment drug levels was 24.0% (95% CI 16.0%-34.0%), higher in Africa (31.0%, 95% CI 19.0%-44.0%) than Asia (11.0%, 95% CI 5.0%-18.0%). By drug, proportions were highest for chloroquine (55.0%) and lumefantrine (34.0%), and 10.0% for mefloquine. The authors conclude this substantial drug pressure could promote antimalarial resistance.

articles.headline: Severe falciparum malaria case recovers despite multiple complications
