articles.title_original: Severe falciparum malaria remains a threat in East Kalimantan: A case report.
articles.authors: Gunawan CA, Mangiri HP
articles.details: This case report describes a 31-year-old male with severe P. falciparum malaria treated at a hospital in Samarinda, East Kalimantan Province, Indonesia, a region where malaria has become rare. He presented with acute kidney injury, impaired consciousness, jaundice, liver dysfunction, high parasitemia, and severe thrombocytopenia. He was treated with injectable artesunate plus symptomatic and supportive care and, despite a poor prognosis from multiple complications, made a full recovery. The authors emphasize that early diagnosis and prompt, proper treatment are critical, noting that reduced clinical experience in low-incidence areas risks misdiagnosis or delayed diagnosis.

articles.headline: Two microRNAs downregulate immune genes linked to cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Functional evidence of hsa-miR-21-5p and hsa-miR-3158-3p in regulating immune pathways relevant to cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Gupta A, Gupta H
articles.details: This in vitro functional study examined how hsa-miR-21-5p and hsa-miR-3158-3p, previously found elevated in cerebral malaria, regulate immune genes. MCF7 and HepG2 cell lines were transfected with synthetic mimics, and after 48 hours mRNA levels of genes including NF-κB, CD46, THBS1, TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-2, IL-6, IL-8, IL-10, and TLR4 were measured by RT-qPCR (2⁻ΔΔCt method). The hsa-miR-3158-3p mimic significantly downregulated NF-κB (p<0.05), while hsa-miR-21-5p significantly reduced NF-κB, IL-10, and TLR4 (p<0.05); other targets were unchanged. The authors conclude these miRNAs may regulate immune pathways implicated in cerebral malaria.

articles.headline: Correction: community IPT for malaria in pregnancy acceptability study
articles.title_original: Correction: Initiating community intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy in Senegal: qualitative evaluation of stakeholder acceptability and experience.
articles.authors: Ndoye T, Lugand MM, Diallo AM, Sakho S, Pouye MM, Dieng K, Faye SLB
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: PfCyRPA malaria vaccine antigen highly conserved in Senegal isolates
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity in the Plasmodium falciparum next-generation blood stage vaccine candidate antigen PfCyRPA in Senegal.
articles.authors: Ba A, Thiam LG, Pouye MN, Guo Y, Patel SD, Sene SD, Diallo F, Li R, Cisse A, Guerra N, Laqqa S, Mangou K, Moore AJ, Sadio BD, Ndiaye JLA, Mbengue A, Vigan-Womas I, Sheng Z, Shapiro L, Bei AK
articles.details: This study used targeted deep amplicon next-generation sequencing to assess genetic diversity of the blood-stage vaccine candidate antigen PfCyRPA in 93 P. falciparum clinical isolates from Kédougou, a high seasonal transmission area in Senegal. The wild-type reference (3D7) allele predominated, but 15 single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) were identified, only five previously reported and most present as singletons; population prevalence ranged from 1.1% (singletons) to 9.7% for the most prevalent SNP, V292F. Structure-based modeling predicted most SNPs have minor effects on PfCyRPA antibodies, while some alter its structure, stability, or interaction with PfRH5. The authors conclude PfCyRPA epitopes are largely conserved, informing structure-guided vaccine design.

articles.headline: More invasive house sparrows carry higher avian malaria infection
articles.title_original: Avian malaria and invasion success: Contrasting infection patterns in two introduced Eurasian sparrows in North America (Passer domesticus and Passer montanus).
articles.authors: Trigueros AC, Robertson BQ, Parker PG
articles.details: Researchers compared Plasmodium (haemosporidian) infection in 306 introduced sparrows—house sparrows (HOSP) and Eurasian tree sparrows (ETSP)—sampled across eight sites, assessing prevalence, relative parasitemia by qPCR, and lineage diversity. Contrary to expectations, the more invasive HOSP had higher Plasmodium prevalence (24.1%, 32/133; 95% CI 17.6%–32.0%) than ETSP (11.6%, 20/173; 95% CI 7.6%–17.2%), with host species the strongest predictor and nearly two-fold higher odds of greater parasitemia in HOSP. Lineage richness was similar (three shared lineages, one unique each; PHPAT01 newly reported in ETSP). The authors conclude host-parasite dynamics shift across invasion stages.

articles.headline: Host heme 'bait-and-kill' strategy resensitizes resistant malaria to artemisinin
articles.title_original: Targeting Infected Host Cell Heme Metabolism to Kill Malaria Parasites.
articles.authors: Siddiqui FA, Adapa SR, Li X, Miao J, Cui L, Jiang RHY
articles.details: This in vitro study developed a host-directed 'bait-and-kill' approach exploiting red blood cell heme metabolism to sensitize Plasmodium falciparum-infected RBCs to artemisinin, using proteomics, transcriptomics, flow cytometry, and culture assays. The heme precursor 5-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) induced selective porphyrin accumulation in infected RBCs (which had increased membrane permeability) while sparing impermeable uninfected RBCs; dihydroartemisinin (DHA) was the killing agent. ALA alone caused no cytotoxicity or ROS, but ALA+DHA acted synergistically, completely clearing artemisinin-resistant P. falciparum isolates from the Greater Mekong Subregion with no recrudescence over three weeks. The authors conclude this offers a resistance-bypassing treatment framework.

articles.headline: Single AP2-G amino acid switch blocks malaria sexual development
articles.title_original: A single valine to leucine switch disrupts Plasmodium falciparum AP2-G DNA binding and reveals GDV1's role in ap2-g activation.
articles.authors: Prajapati SK, Dong JX, Morahan BJ, Dotrang T, Barbeau MC, Williams AE, Hupalo D, Wilkerson MD, Dalgard CL, Kafsack BFC, Llinás M, Williamson KC
articles.details: Investigating sexual commitment required for malaria transmission, researchers identified a single valine-to-leucine mutation (V2163L) in the P. falciparum transcription factor AP2-G that abolishes gametocytogenesis. The mutant AP2-G.L2163 fails to bind the ap2-g consensus motif (GnGTAC), does not stimulate transcription, and loses autoregulation. GDV1-dependent expression of the mutant showed GDV1 is critical for initial activation of the silent ap2-g locus even without functional AP2-G, while wild-type AP2-G.V2163 is required for MSRP1 expression, a marker distinguishing early from late sexually committed schizonts. The authors conclude V2163 is essential for DNA binding and transmission.

articles.headline: Climate and government policy shape malaria transmission in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Climatic and governance determinants of malaria transmission in Rivers State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Egbom SE, Nduka FO, Nzeako SO, Nwafor GO, Bartholomew DC, Nwaigwe CC, Iwu HC, Ogbonna CJ, Nwokike CI, Opara MC, Ezenwaka CO, Anyanwu EO, Nwachukwu JI
articles.details: This study examined how meteorological conditions and government policy shifts affected malaria incidence in Rivers State from 2007 to 2021. Poisson, quasi-Poisson, and negative binomial models were used to identify environmental predictors, then compared with a SARIMAX model for forecasting. The quasi-Poisson model fit best among count models, but SARIMAX showed superior predictive accuracy; the SARIMAX(1,1,0)(1,1,1)₁₂ model found monthly incidence significantly influenced by seasonality, rainfall, and changes in government malaria-control policy periods. The authors conclude that integrating climatic and governance indicators strengthens malaria early-warning systems and surveillance.

articles.headline: S1PR1 receptor overexpressed in brain of fatal cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Expression of sphingosine-1-phosphate receptor 1 in the brain of fatal cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Srisook C, Nintasen R, Punsawad C, Glaharn S, Techarang T, Viriyavejakul P
articles.details: To explore the S1P/S1PR1 signaling pathway in cerebral malaria (CM), researchers examined S1P receptor 1 (S1PR1) expression by immunohistochemistry in archived brain tissues from fatal P. falciparum malaria and measured blood S1P by ELISA, correlating with severity. S1PR1 expression was intense in cerebral blood vessels and neurons of fatal CM patients compared with controls and non-CM patients (all p<0.001). Blood S1P levels decreased significantly in the CM group and were negatively correlated with S1PR1 expression in vessels and neurons. The authors conclude the S1P/S1PR1 pathway is involved in malaria pathogenesis and represents a potential therapeutic target to reduce sequestration.

articles.headline: COI sequencing identifies Anopheles vector species complexes in Guyana and Venezuela
articles.title_original: Anopheles species complexes from Guyana and Venezuela malaria endemic areas using COI sequences.
articles.authors: Rubio-Palis Y, Corredor-Medina C, Lenhart AE, Ruiz-López F
articles.details: This molecular taxonomy and phylogenetic study analyzed Anopheles mosquitoes collected in the Potaro-Siparuni Region of Guyana and five municipalities in Venezuela, using mitochondrial cytochrome C oxidase (mtCOI) gene data with MrBayes, TCS, P ID, Rosenberg's P(AB), and P(Randomly Distinct) species delimitation methods to characterize the Albitarsis, Nuneztovari, and Oswaldoi complexes. Four distinct taxa were identified: An. albitarsis F, An. goeldii, An. oswaldoi A, and An. oswaldoi B, with An. oswaldoi A and B occurring sympatrically in Boca de Nichare, Bolívar State, Venezuela. An. goeldii is reported for the first time from Guyana and Venezuela, An. oswaldoi A for the first time from Venezuela, and An. albitarsis F is confirmed as the only widely distributed Albitarsis-complex species in Venezuela.

articles.headline: Afghanistan malaria forecast: elimination by 2035 unlikely under current policy
articles.title_original: Malaria time series analysis, forecasting, and development of curated intervention strategies for the elimination of malaria from Afghanistan.
articles.authors: Rahimi BA, Habib MN, Wafa MH, Sahrai MS, Singh MP, Lal AA
articles.details: This time-series analysis used district-level monthly malaria incidence data from 2018 to 2023 from the Afghanistan Ministry of Public Health and annual case data from 1990 to 2023 from WHO to model trends, forecast, and stratify district endemicity. From 2018 to 2023 the test positivity rate was 15.2%, with 96.5% of cases being P. vivax, 3.2% P. falciparum, and 0.3% mixed; mean annual cases were 153,295. Nearly one-quarter of cases occurred in districts along the Pakistan border, while districts bordering Iran, Turkmenistan, Uzbekistan, Tajikistan, and China together contributed under 1%; cases peaked in 2002. Forecasting suggests elimination by 2035 is unlikely under current policy, though about 43% of districts with zero or low endemicity are ready for sub-national elimination.

articles.headline: Antibodies to CSP C-terminal epitope linked to malaria vaccine protection
articles.title_original: Vaccine-Induced Antibodies to a C-Terminal Plasmodium falciparum Circumsporozoite Protein Epitope Associated With Protection Against Malaria Challenge.
articles.authors: Friedman-Klabanoff DJ, Jensen TL, Gelber CE, Pinapati RS, Tan JC, Deye GA, Regules JA, Bergmann-Leitner ES, Laurens MB, Travassos MA, Goll JB, Takala-Harrison S, Berry AA
articles.details: Using serum from controlled human malaria infection experiments, this study used a peptide array covering P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein (CSP) genetic variants to quantify anti-CSP antibody responses in unprotected adults given full-length recombinant CSP (rCSP), protected adults given RTS,S, and unprotected adults given RTS,S, comparing breadth and intensity across groups. rCSP recipients had lower CSP antibody responses than the protected RTS,S group. Protected RTS,S vaccinees showed higher breadth of responses to junctional, central repeat, and C-terminal peptides, higher intensity to two C-terminal peptides (including part of Th3R), and higher breadth of IgA responses to variants of the C-terminal epitope PNDPNRNV. The authors conclude protection-associated responses to the junctional region not in RTS,S suggest a cross-reactive vaccine-induced response providing additional benefit.

articles.headline: Interleukin-6 elevated during adverse events in sporozoite malaria vaccine trial
articles.title_original: Cytokines Associated With Moderate and Severe Adverse Events During a Sporozoite Malaria Vaccine Trial With Controlled Human Malaria Infection.
articles.authors: Jatt LP, Gillespie KM, Van P, Hoffman SL, Jackson LA, Murphy SC, Heath JR, Kublin JG
articles.details: In a malaria vaccine trial with a higher-than-expected adverse event (AE) rate and frequent sampling, interleukin-6 was elevated on days when individuals experienced moderate and severe AEs. This study leveraged that trial to examine cytokine profiles associated with moderate and severe AEs, which are rarely investigated. The authors conclude that more research on immune responses associated with AEs is warranted to identify biomarkers of systemic reactogenicity and accelerate vaccine development.

articles.headline: CCR5Δ32 allele frequency low in malaria-exposed Amazonian women
articles.title_original: Updating the frequency of CCR5Δ32 in Brazil: Descriptive analysis of malaria cases and controls from Acre State, Amazon region.
articles.authors: Ziliotto M, Ellwanger JH, Dombrowski JG, Marinho CRF, Pontillo A, Chies JAB
articles.details: CCR5Δ32 allele frequencies were 2.88% in Plasmodium-infected women and 1.04% in uninfected women from the state of Acre in the Brazilian Amazon, a region where the Δ32 allele and malaria occur concurrently. The work updated data on the distribution of CCR5Δ32 allele frequency across populations from Brazilian states. The authors suggest the low Δ32 frequencies in Acre, relative to other Brazilian regions, may reflect the particular ancestry patterns of present-day Amazonian populations influenced by past European colonization.

articles.headline: Nigeria malaria surveillance: low drug resistance, chloroquine sensitivity now dominant
articles.title_original: Molecular Surveillance of Malaria in Nigeria Reveals a Low Frequency of Antimalarial Resistance Markers and Clonal Expansion of Chloroquine-Sensitive Infections.
articles.authors: Ajibaye O, Oboh-Imafidon M, Ajibola O, Olusola F, Egwu CO, Amambua-Ngwa BN, Diop MF, Mohammed N, D'Alessandro U, Falade C, Meremikwu M, Olukosi AY, Oriero EC, Amambua-Ngwa A
articles.details: Targeted amplicon sequencing of 895 P. falciparum clinical isolates from Nigeria's 6 geopolitical zones (mostly 2017-2021) profiled complexity of infection, genetic diversity, and resistance markers across 101 SNPs and 36 nonsynonymous mutations. About 30% of infections carried more than one distinct clone; 55.1% had wild-type Pfcrt at codons 74-76, though the chloroquine-resistant CVIET haplotype persisted in 40.9%. Sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance alleles at Pfdhfr codons 51, 59, 108 exceeded 50%, and Pfdhps A437G reached 87.3%. Most were wild-type at Pfkelch13. Authors conclude chloroquine sensitivity is now dominant while antifolate resistance persists.

articles.headline: Machine-learning sea-surface-temperature index improves long-lead malaria prediction in Peruvian Amazon
articles.title_original: A Machine Learning-Based Dynamic SST Index for Long-Lead Malaria Prediction in the Peruvian Amazon.
articles.authors: Pan M, Hu S, Janko MM, Zaitchik BF, Takahashi K, Lescano AG, Munayco CV, Pan WK
articles.details: This study proposes a machine-learning method using tropical sea surface temperature (SST) variability to predict malaria in the Peruvian Amazon. Significant correlations between tropical SST anomalies and malaria occurrence existed across seasons and time lags. Using a self-organizing map, the authors derived a dynamic SST index that outperformed the traditional El Niño-Southern Oscillation index (higher correlation, lower RMSE) at lead times exceeding 3 months. The index captures the ENSO life cycle from the Pacific Meridional Mode and appears to influence malaria via local near-surface air temperature and specific humidity. Open-source code is provided.

articles.headline: NLM smartphone malaria screener performs poorly in Ghana blood donors
articles.title_original: Diagnostic Accuracy of the National Library of Medicine (NLM) Screener Application in Screening for Malaria Parasites Among Blood Donors at the Korle-Bu Blood Bank, Ghana: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Appiah SB, Osei S, Brown NK, Amoah LE, Owusu EDA
articles.details: A cross-sectional study of 300 blood donors at Korle-Bu Teaching Hospital, Accra, Ghana, evaluated the National Library of Medicine (NLM) machine-learning smartphone screener against microscopy, RDT, and PCR (reference standard). PCR identified P. falciparum in 18/300 donors (6.0%). The NLM app showed sensitivity 38.9% (7/18), specificity 60.6% (171/282), PPV 5.9%, NPV 94.0%, with negligible agreement (κ = -0.001). RDT and microscopy had lower sensitivities (44.4% and 27.8%) but 100% specificity. Applied to the pool, the app would have caused 111 unnecessary discards and 11 missed infections. Authors conclude it needs substantial improvement before clinical transfusion use.

articles.headline: Apicortin marks Plasmodium apical conoid body but is dispensable
articles.title_original: Apicortin defines the Plasmodium apical conoid body but is dispensable for the parasite life cycle.
articles.authors: Zeeshan M, Mishra A, Pashley SL, Markus R, Brady D, Holder AA, Moores C, Tewari R
articles.details: Researchers investigated the microtubule-associated protein Apicortin, which contains a doublecortin domain and partial TPPP domain, in Plasmodium berghei. Live-cell imaging of a transgenic line expressing GFP-tagged Apicortin localized it to the apical end of invasive parasite stages within the mosquito vector. Super-resolution and expansion microscopy revealed Apicortin forms a distinct ringlike structure in the apical complex region. However, deletion of the Apicortin gene had no effect on parasite development, indicating the protein is not essential. The authors suggest redundancy or compensatory mechanisms stabilize the apical complex.

articles.headline: Systematic review: poor community knowledge of asymptomatic malaria in endemic areas
articles.title_original: To what extent do people in malaria-endemic countries know asymptomatic malaria infections? A systematic review.
articles.authors: Bouwe T, Ahissou NCA, Kikuchi K, Iwagami M, Nonaka D
articles.details: This PRISMA-guided systematic review (PROSPERO CRD42024508104) assessed knowledge of asymptomatic malaria in endemic countries, searching PubMed, Google Scholar, and Web of Science for peer-reviewed articles published 2010-2024. Of 483 articles retrieved, 11 were included. Across four general-public studies, the proportion knowledgeable about asymptomatic malaria ranged from 14.2% to 79.8%; among health personnel it was 88% (one study). Qualitative studies showed varied and lacking knowledge plus reluctance to adhere to interventions targeting asymptomatic carriers. Authors conclude there is a lack of knowledge and a shortage of studies, and that education and further research are needed to support elimination.

articles.headline: Ensuring lasting malaria vaccine impact in high-transmission areas
articles.title_original: Ensuring the Public Health Impact of Malaria Vaccination in High-Transmission Settings.
articles.authors: Bell GJ, Emch ME, Moormann AM, Bailey JA
articles.details: This perspective discusses the WHO-recommended P. falciparum vaccines RTS,S and R21, both shown safe and effective in trials, with early RTS,S pilot data suggesting reductions in severe disease and death. The authors note RTS,S efficacy was lower in higher-transmission settings, partly because control groups developed greater natural immunity, producing temporary periods of negative efficacy starting roughly three years after the primary series. Because delayed malaria cases of unknown severity are expected in vaccinated individuals during high-transmission implementation, they recommend enhanced surveillance and supplementary interventions alongside vaccination.

articles.headline: Ubiquitin ligase TEX1 essential for liver-stage malaria parasite development
articles.title_original: The Putative E3 Ubiquitin Ligase TEX1 Is Required for Nuclear Biology and Developmental Progression of Plasmodium berghei in the Liver.
articles.authors: Schmid M, Golomingi R, Franke-Fayard B, Caldelari R, Rehmann R, Roques M, Heussler VT
articles.details: Using gene knockout, tagging, and imaging in Plasmodium berghei, researchers functionally characterized the putative E3 ubiquitin ligase Trophozoite Exported Protein 1 (TEX1; PBANKA_0102200) across the mosquito and liver stages. TEX1-knockout parasites showed impaired mosquito-stage development with significant reductions in ookinete formation, oocyst numbers, and sporozoites reaching the salivary glands, and in liver stages displayed reduced growth, abnormal nuclear division, impaired maturation, and a dramatic decline in detached cell formation and blood-stage infectivity. Endogenous C-terminal tagging with GFP and 3×HA revealed a discrete subnuclear localization. The authors conclude TEX1 is required for nuclear replication and division, making it a promising target for liver-stage antimalarial interventions.

articles.headline: Inland Liberian under-fives had higher malaria prevalence than coastal
articles.title_original: Geographic variation in malaria prevalence among children under five in coastal and inland counties of Liberia: analysis of the 2022 Malaria Indicator Survey.
articles.authors: Tugbeh RS, Veliah G, Jennifer HG, Shaw TEB, Tchakondo S, Gayi KS, Assogba AS, Kandjoni Y
articles.details: This analysis of secondary data from the 2022 Liberia Malaria Indicator Survey (weighted sample of 2,189 children aged 6–59 months) compared malaria prevalence between coastal and inland counties using a two-proportion Z-test and logistic regression. Prevalence was 8.3% in coastal versus 12.7% in inland regions, a significant difference (Z=3.33, p=0.001). Predictors of infection included not sleeping under ITNs (1.5 times higher odds), severe anaemia (AOR=6.2; 95% CI 2.34–14.81), and poor households (AOR=6.4; 95% CI 3.06–13.27), while infants 0–11 months (AOR=0.04) and urban residence (AOR=0.6) had lower odds. The authors recommend targeting inland counties and high-risk groups.

articles.headline: GIS model reconstructs malaria risk on ancient Anatolian roads
articles.title_original: A 'useless marsh … breeding fever and insect pests': assessing malaria risk on roads through Cilicia Pedias (modern Türkiye) and its historical impact in antiquity and the Middle Ages.
articles.authors: Browning DC Jr, Nicovich JM
articles.details: This study applied a GIS-based multilayer malaria risk model, developed for antiquity, to the lowlands of Cilicia Pedias (southeastern Anatolia, modern Türkiye), a historical transit zone where malaria-like symptoms were frequently reported among travellers. Combined with a new method for mapping ancient road paths, the model assigned risk figures to travel routes used by merchants, pilgrims, and armies. Modelled risk data correlated extremely well with historical accounts of malaria-like illness linked to victims' known itineraries from the 4th century BCE through the 12th century CE. The authors conclude the results support interpreting these reported illnesses as malarial infection and demonstrate a replicable method combining GIS and text-based approaches.

articles.headline: Correction: metabolic rigidity as mechanical barrier to malaria
articles.title_original: Correction to "Metabolic Rigidity as a Mechanical Barrier to Malaria: Flickering Loss in PKLR-Deficient Erythrocytes".
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: IDO-1 inhibitor boosts artemether-lumefantrine cure in mouse malaria
articles.title_original: 8-Fluorotryptanthrin, an immunomodulatory IDO-1 inhibitor enhances the efficacy of artemether-lumefantrine in the treatment of malaria.
articles.authors: Katnoria S, Kumar R, Suri M, Salunke DB, Kaur S
articles.details: Lab and mouse study testing synthetic IDO-1 inhibitors against malaria. 8-Fluorotryptanthrin (C1) showed strong in vitro activity against P. falciparum strains 3D7 and Dd2 and P. berghei ANKA with high selectivity and minimal cytotoxicity/haemolysis. In P. berghei-infected BALB/c mice, C1 at 20 mg/kg significantly reduced parasitaemia, restored liver and kidney function, improved survival, and modulated T- and B-cell populations. Combining C1 with artemether-lumefantrine produced complete parasitaemia suppression and full survival by days 7 and 14, with increased IL-10 and IL-4 and reduced TNF-α and IFN-γ. Authors propose C1 as an immunochemotherapy lead against drug-resistant malaria.

articles.headline: Epigenetics and chromatin control var2csa placental-binding in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Epigenetics and chromatin structure regulate var2csa expression and the placental binding phenotype in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Lenz T, Sirel M, Hoppe H, Shafeeq S, Roch KL, Ribacke U
articles.details: Molecular study of how P. falciparum activates var2csa, the gene encoding the VAR2CSA adhesin responsible for placental cytoadhesion in pregnancy malaria. Using phenotypic and systems-biology assays, the authors established homogeneous VAR2CSA-expressing, placenta-binding parasites that exclusively transcribed var2csa while other var genes stayed silenced. Activation was strongly associated with depletion of repressive H3K9me3 marks and, via chromatin conformation capture, with a small but significant repositioning of var2csa relative to heterochromatic telomeric clusters. 5-methylcytosine occupancy was present across all var genes but independent of transcriptional repression or switching.

articles.headline: Rare genetic variants bias malaria parasite population-genetic estimates
articles.title_original: Rare variation in malaria parasites biases population-genetic inference.
articles.authors: Goldberg A
articles.details: Population-genetics reanalysis of genomic data from 18 worldwide P. falciparum populations addressing why effective population size estimates vary by orders of magnitude and why many genes show elevated πN/πS. The author shows these patterns follow directly from variation enriched for rare variants, with genealogies more consistent with a multiple-merger coalescent than a Kingman coalescent, biasing common summary statistics. The abundance of rare variants interacts with ratio-statistic properties to inflate gene-level πN/πS even without selection. Filtering rare variation reveals previously masked selection candidates, including merozoite surface proteins. Proposed mechanisms include host expansions, lifecycle dynamics, population structure, or selection.

articles.headline: Metabolomics reveals permethrin-resistance pathways in Anopheles gambiae
articles.title_original: Metabolomic changes underpinning permethrin resistance in the Anopheles gambiae malaria vector from Cameroon.
articles.authors: Djova SV, Christopher MW, Ibrahim SS, Kouamo M, Dinglasan RR, Garrett TJ, Wondji CS
articles.details: Metabolomic study of permethrin resistance in field-collected Anopheles gambiae malaria vectors from Cameroon, using UHPLC-HRMS/MS to compare resistant and susceptible mosquitoes. Resistant mosquitoes showed upregulated inosine, nicotinic acid, dipeptides, amino acids, fumarate, uracil, and aldopentose, suggesting altered detoxification, target-site and metabolic shifts, and enhanced energy production. Susceptible mosquitoes had increased N-acetyl-aspartic acid, xanthurenic acid, 2-hydroxyglutarate, 3-hydroxykynurenine, propanoylcarnitine, and L-pipecolic acid, linked to neurotoxicity, energy disruption, and tryptophan and lysine catabolism. The authors say these findings elucidate permethrin-resistance pathways and mechanisms that could drive pyrethroid cross-resistance.

articles.headline: TNF-iNOS-HIF-1α axis drives glycolysis aiding malaria resistance
articles.title_original: Reprogramming of host energy metabolism mediated by the TNF-iNOS-HIF-1α axis plays a key role in host resistance to Plasmodium infection.
articles.authors: Matteucci KC, Leite NPS, Assis PA, Hirako IC, Pioto F, Ojelabi O, Toller-Kawahisa JE, Vaz LG, Costa DL, DA Silva JS, Alves-Filho JC, Gazzinelli RT
articles.details: Mouse study of how TNF shapes host defense in P. chabaudi infection. TNF signaling reduced physical activity, food intake, and energy expenditure while enhancing liver and spleen glucose uptake and controlling parasitemia. TNF was required for iNOS expression, HIF-1α stabilization, GLUT1 expression, and enhanced glycolysis in monocytic cells. P. chabaudi-infected iNOS-/-, TNFRΔLyz2, and HIF-1αΔLyz2 mice showed impaired TNF release and monocyte glycolysis, with increased parasitemia and greater disease tolerance. The authors conclude that TNF-iNOS-HIF-1α-induced glycolysis in monocytes is critical for host defense and sickness behavior.

articles.headline: Imported malaria in Sri Lanka driven by work travel, 2013–2023
articles.title_original: The profile of imported malaria in Sri Lanka from 2013 to 2023.
articles.authors: Chulasiri P, Fernando D, Seneviratne S, Wickremasinghe R, Aluthweera C, Fernando T, Gunasekera K, Mendis K
articles.details: A descriptive analysis of 532 imported malaria cases from Sri Lanka's National Malaria Database (2013–2023). Over half (68.5%) occurred in people traveling for low- or high-wage employment. P. falciparum (48.1%, mostly acquired in Africa) and P. vivax (40.5%, mostly from India) predominated, with all four human species imported. P. ovale took longer to manifest (median 95 vs 14 days) and P. malariae longer to diagnose (15.5 vs 4–6 days). Patients sought care faster (geometric mean 2.42 days) than physicians took to diagnose (3.41 days, p<0.001). Seven P. falciparum recrudesced, three P. vivax relapsed; 46 cases were severe (all but one P. falciparum) with one death. Diagnostic delay was attributed to physicians, not patients.

articles.headline: Anopheles gambiae feeding and resting behaviour in southwestern Senegal
articles.title_original: Feeding and resting behaviours of natural Anopheles gambiae s.l. populations in an area of low malaria transmission in south-western Senegal: A comparative study between mainland and island settings.
articles.authors: Diop M, Coulibaly Y, Lo C, Dia AK, Ndoye NA, Tine EG, Diagne NS, Coulibaly YI, Sangaré M, Thiaw O, Faye MB, Tall MF, Ciss O, Faye O, Niang A, Konaté L, Lobo NF, Tine RCK, Niang EHA
articles.details: A longitudinal entomological study comparing mainland and island settings collected indoor and outdoor resting mosquitoes using Pyrethrum Spray Catches and Prokopack aspirators. Of 765 Anopheles collected (181 mainland, 584 island), An. gambiae s.l. was predominant (91.1%) with seasonal variation. Indoor resting density did not differ significantly between mainland (0.41/room) and island (4.09/room) (P=0.082). On the mainland, the human blood index was higher in indoor (76.2%) than outdoor (47.6%) females (P=0.035), whereas on the island it was lower with no indoor/outdoor difference (25.1% vs 31.1%, P=0.51). Endophilic populations had higher HBIs on the mainland (P=2.63×10⁻⁵).

articles.headline: Scoping review of malaria inequities in Madagascar
articles.title_original: Understanding inequities in the malaria landscape of Madagascar: a scoping review of current evidence.
articles.authors: Rajaofera MJN, Liu W, Reziky AM, Tomboanona S, Kuang D, Xia Q
articles.details: A comprehensive literature review of studies published between 2015 and 2024 from PubMed, Web of Science, Google Scholar, and EBSCOhost, supplemented by online reports, examined inequities in malaria prevention, diagnosis, and treatment in Madagascar. It found urban–rural disparities, with remote regions bearing a higher burden and insufficient rural healthcare infrastructure for timely diagnosis and treatment. Key interventions included insecticide-treated nets, indoor residual spraying, and seasonal malaria chemoprevention, with case management using artemisinin-based combination therapies and rapid diagnostic tests, while drug resistance, diagnostic failures, and socioeconomic inequalities hindered control. The authors recommend strengthening healthcare systems, improving supply chains, targeting vulnerable groups, and scaling up IRS, ITN distribution, and social and behavior change communication.

articles.headline: Severe malaria and case-management gaps in hospitalized Kenyan patients
articles.title_original: Using sentinel surveillance system data to characterize severe malaria illness and quality of malaria case management among hospitalized patients in Kenya, 2017-2024.
articles.authors: Itoh M, Lucchi N, Schultz J, Agogo GO, Munyua P, Chege D, Mwenda DN, Akoth S, Sumbi V, Shieshia M, Kandie R, Onyango EO, Hines JZ
articles.details: Analysis of Integrated Facility-based Surveillance sentinel data from twelve facilities (June 2017–July 2024) assessed risk factors for severe illness/death, microscopy accuracy versus PCR, and adherence to severe malaria treatment guidelines. Among 8,487 inpatients, 2,197 (25.9%) were malaria-positive; of these, 713 (32.5%) had severe disease and 16 (0.7%) died. Infants had the highest odds of severe illness. Fever lasting ≥5 days raised odds of severe illness and death (ORs 3.67 and 8.00), as did referral from another facility (ORs 3.01 and 3.15). Microscopy was PCR-negative in 21% of positives. Only 15% of severe cases received both IV artesunate and artemether-lumefantrine, and 17% received IV quinine, highlighting diagnostic and treatment-adherence gaps.

articles.headline: New molecular surveillance network launched for malaria in the Americas
articles.title_original: MMS Américas: a regional network for malaria molecular surveillance.
articles.authors: MMS Américas Network
articles.details: A meeting report describing a gathering of international researchers and public health professionals in Panama in August 2025 to discuss malaria molecular surveillance strategies for accelerating elimination in the Americas. A new network, MMS Américas, was established to promote sharing of expertise, protocols, and data. No quantitative study results are reported.

articles.headline: Co-designed community interventions improve malaria care in conflict-hit Cameroon regions
articles.title_original: The role of community co-designed interventions for malaria prevention and control in conflict-affected communities in Cameroon's Southwest and littoral regions.
articles.authors: E O EBM, Yankam BM, Ogundeko-Olugbami O, Donovan L, Counihan H, Hawkings H, Maxwell K, Baker K, Zoungrana Y, Tanue EA, Noukeme BM, Metuge A, Nganmou I, Divine A, Coril E, Gilbert K, Sachop N, Ako S, Pensha J, Tabong AO, Itoe BT, Wanji S, Berryman E, Jarman E, Omam EN, Omam LA
articles.details: A mixed-methods qualitative study in conflict-affected Cameroon evaluated three community-based, co-created interventions: a community dialogue approach called the Community Health Participatory Approach (CoHPA), community health worker (CHW) supportive supervision, and health vouchers for treatment and transportation. The endline evaluation involved 189 host, displaced, and returnee participants across 19 focus group discussions and 18 in-depth interviews, analyzed thematically using NVivo 14. Participants perceived increased malaria prevention knowledge and greater participation, increased trust in CHW services that reduced use of roadside drug sellers, and improved treatment access and timely referrals via vouchers, though insecurity reduced facility-based services and community leaders questioned voucher sustainability. The authors conclude the interventions were perceived to improve prevention and health-seeking awareness, service utilization, and treatment access.

articles.headline: Erythrocyte PMCA4b expression shapes oxidative stress and artemisinin sensitivity
articles.title_original: Decoding the multifaceted role of erythrocyte PMCA4b in oxidative stress-mediated malaria protection and artemisinin resistance.
articles.authors: Agrohi P, Garg S, Biswas S, Maurya P, Kumar V, Sharma J, Goswami B, Sil SK, Singh MP, Chand SK, Shalini S, Singh OP, Dhangadamajhi G, Mishra N, Ramalingam S, Mallick PK, Singh S
articles.details: This study examined ATP2B4 genotypes and erythrocyte PMCA4b Ca²⁺-ATPase expression in malaria susceptibility and artemisinin sensitivity, comparing severe malaria, uncomplicated malaria, and healthy controls in an Indian population, with functional assays of intra-erythrocytic calcium, oxidative stress, Gardos channel activity, and Plasmodium falciparum growth. ATP2B4 genotypes showed no significant association with malaria protection, but regardless of genotype, low PMCA4b expression correlated with increased intracellular calcium, oxidative stress, and reduced in-vitro parasite growth; the PMCA inhibitor resveratrol validated this role. Notably, low PMCA4b expression also reduced P. falciparum artemisinin sensitivity. Authors conclude host redox-modulating RBC variation influences drug efficacy and could serve as a biomarker for therapeutic response.

articles.headline: Protein atlas reveals new microtubule-associated proteins in P. falciparum gametocytes
articles.title_original: An atlas of novel microtubule-associated proteins in the malaria parasite Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Niedermüller K, Piwon N, Mesen-Ramirez JP, Zink L, Lemcke S, Liffner B, Fernandes CF, Farias GB, Alder A, Löw C, Dahan N, Wilson DW, Cubillán-Marín J, Gilberger TW
articles.details: Researchers used proximity-dependent biotinylation (BioID) with the subpellicular microtubule-associated protein PfSPM3 as bait to identify proteins associated with the subpellicular microtubule (SPMT) network of falciform Plasmodium falciparum gametocytes. Mass spectrometry revealed 37 high-confidence proteins in the PfSPM3 'proxiome,' and GFP tagging of 11 highly enriched candidates produced a spatial localization atlas, with many showing distinct localization within the SPMT network. Functional knockout of one protein, PF3D7_1003400, located near the IMC suture/SPMT boundary, interfered with gametocytogenesis and prevented falciform gametocyte development. Authors present the first SPMT-network atlas and suggest these proteins offer new avenues for blocking gametocyte development and malaria transmission.

articles.headline: Avian malaria kills zoo penguins; widespread haemosporidian infection across Thai zoos
articles.title_original: Malaria outbreaks in Humboldt penguins and haemosporidian infections in avian hosts in zoos across Thailand.
articles.authors: Rahmah SA, Jairak W, Thanee S, Saedan A, Mahadthai P, Novianto D, Saiwichai T, Kaneko O, Pattaradilokrat S, Kaewthamasorn M
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: Prompted by avian malaria deaths in Humboldt penguins (Spheniscus humboldti) at a Thai zoo, this study screened 406 blood and tissue samples from 146 Humboldt penguins and 260 birds (ten avian orders, 38 species) across six zoos using nested PCR of the mitochondrial cytochrome b gene with Sanger sequencing and phylogenetic analysis. Haemosporidian DNA was detected in 40 of 146 penguin samples (27.4%) and 125 of 260 other-bird samples (48.0%), including IUCN Red List species; exoerythrocytic meronts were seen in Kupffer cells. Parasites included four Plasmodium species (P. collidatum, P. relictum, P. juxtanucleare, P. gallinaceum) plus seven lineages, Haemoproteus sacharovi and four lineages, and Leucocytozoon caulleryi. Authors urge surveillance, vector control, and biosecurity in ex situ conservation.

articles.headline: Antibodies to P. falciparum surface antigens predict lower parasite burden in children
articles.title_original: Antibody levels to variant and conserved Plasmodium falciparum antigens predict reduction in parasite burden in Malian children.
articles.authors: Damena D, Dang L, Barry A, Renn JP, Gaoussou S, Mahamar A, Attaher O, Issiaka D, Morrison RD, Misal SA, Dicko A, Duffy PE, Fried M
articles.details: High antibody levels to four Plasmodium falciparum proteins significantly reduced the risk of high parasite density infection: DBLγ11 [OR 0.74 (95%CI 0.63–0.86)], DBLζ5 [0.80 (0.69–0.93)], and two helical interspersed subtelomeric proteins, PF3D7_0201600 [0.81 (0.70–0.94)] and PF3D7_0532300 [0.79 (0.68–0.92)]. These targets were identified by applying immunoprecipitation–mass spectrometry (IP-MS) to plasma from susceptible and semi-immune children, then testing whether antibody levels to membrane-associated proteins or PfEMP1s predicted reduced malaria disease. Opsonic phagocytosis of DBLγ11- and DBLζ5-coated beads was significantly higher in samples with top-tertile antibody levels (p<0.0001), and the relative phagocytosis index correlated with antibody levels (r²=0.61 for DBLζ5 and r²=0.49 for DBLγ11). The authors conclude antibodies to these four proteins reduce future parasite burden, possibly via opsonic phagocytosis.

articles.headline: AI matches microscopy and PCR for malaria diagnosis in meta-analysis
articles.title_original: Diagnostic Accuracy of Utilizing Artificial Intelligence for Malaria Diagnostic: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
articles.authors: Faratisha IFD, Yunita KC, Rahmawati HR, Fitri LE, Winaris N, Muflikah L
articles.details: This PRISMA systematic review and meta-analysis pooled 10 studies with external validation, totaling 6754 patients, to assess artificial intelligence-based systems for malaria diagnosis; study quality was rated with QUADAS-2. Pooled AI sensitivity was 87.7% (95% CI: 78.2-93.4) and specificity 91.4% (95% CI: 77.3-97.1). Against microscopy AI showed 87.7% sensitivity and 91.4% specificity, and against PCR 90.7% sensitivity and 88.3% specificity. The authors conclude AI offers high accuracy, automation, and lower cost, serving as a promising complementary tool comparable to reference methods.

articles.headline: Urine malaria rapid test less sensitive than blood tests in children
articles.title_original: Comparative Performance of Blood and Urine Malaria Rapid Diagnostic Tests in Detecting Malaria in Febrile Children: A Cross-Sectional Analysis.
articles.authors: Babatola AO, Adeniyi AT, Ojo TO, Ilori BF, Olatunya OS
articles.details: In febrile children, a urine-based malaria rapid diagnostic test (Fyodor UMT) showed limited standalone utility, with lower malaria positivity (45.3%) than the blood-based SD Bioline (67.4%) and CareStart (65.3%) RDTs. All participants gave blood and urine samples, and performance was assessed by sensitivity, specificity, PPV, and NPV. The two blood-based RDTs agreed excellently (Cohen's kappa 0.859), while the Fyodor UMT showed only moderate agreement with them (kappa 0.491, P<.001). The Fyodor UMT had 58.6% sensitivity, 100.0% specificity, 100.0% PPV, and 40.0% NPV, indicating limited value as a standalone diagnostic owing to its relatively low sensitivity.

articles.headline: Malaria infection lowers adipsin's diagnostic specificity for preeclampsia
articles.title_original: Impact of Malaria Infection on the Diagnostic Performance of Adipsin for Preeclampsia in Pregnancy: A Case-Control Study.
articles.authors: Opoku Mensah B, Anim EO, Ahenkorah Fondjo L, Manu AO
articles.details: This case-control study enrolled 200 pregnant women (20-42 weeks gestation) in four groups of 50: normotensive without malaria, normotensive with malaria, preeclamptic with malaria, and preeclamptic without malaria, with malaria confirmed by Giemsa smear and plasma adipsin, C3a, C5a, TNF-α, IL-6, IL-8, and IFN-γ measured by ELISA. Adipsin was significantly elevated in malaria-infected and preeclamptic women (p<0.001), highest with coexisting disease, and correlated with C5a (ρ=0.695), IL-6 (ρ=0.687), TNF-α (ρ=0.645), and parasite density (ρ=0.553). ROC specificity for preeclampsia fell in malaria-infected women (62.1%, AUC=0.719) versus malaria-negative (87.9%, AUC=0.823). The authors conclude P. falciparum alters adipsin, warranting malaria-adjusted thresholds.

articles.headline: P. falciparum drove malaria outbreak in Arba Minch, Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Epidemiological investigation of a possible malaria outbreak in Arba Minch town, Gamo Zone, Southern Ethiopia, 2023.
articles.authors: Dana DD, Belay MM, Ango TS, Zegene GM, Getachew S, Deressa W
articles.details: A descriptive epidemiological analysis followed by a 1:1 unmatched community-based case-control study (94 RDT-confirmed cases and 94 neighbourhood controls) investigated a malaria outbreak in Arba Minch Town, Gamo Zone, Southern Ethiopia, conducted from October 2022 to December 2023 using structured questionnaires and logistic regression. A total of 5181 confirmed cases were reported, with a 22.7% positivity rate and an attack rate of 41.2 per 1000 population; P. falciparum accounted for 71% of infections, with the outbreak peaking in epidemiological week 3 of 2023. Factors significantly associated with infection included residence proximity to mosquito breeding sites (AOR=5.45, 95% CI 2.11–14.08), presence of stagnant water (AOR=5.69, 95% CI 2.22–14.55), poor housing (AOR=4.40, 95% CI 1.83–10.54), inconsistent LLIN utilization (AOR=0.30, 95% CI 0.13–0.71), and poor knowledge (AOR=0.04, 95% CI 0.02–0.12). The authors call for improved vector control, environmental management, community education, and robust surveillance.

articles.headline: Computational screening finds natural compounds targeting P. falciparum kinases
articles.title_original: Computational screening of natural inhibitors against Plasmodium falciparum kinases: Toward novel antimalarial therapies.
articles.authors: Alruwaili M, Younas S, Elderdery AY, Alruwaili I, Millis J, Khan MU, Ejaz H
articles.details: This in silico study used a multi-targeted strategy to identify naturally occurring compounds as potential antimalarials against multidrug-resistant Plasmodium falciparum, focusing on the therapeutic targets casein kinase 2 (CK2) and cGMP-dependent protein kinase (PKG). Pharmacophore-based virtual screening selected the top 20 compounds including reference drug RY-1-65, which were docked using Maestro (Schrödinger 2020-3). The best-scoring compounds were Ligand-9 against PKG (-7.490 kcal/mol) and Ligand-13 against CK2 (-11.468 kcal/mol). Density functional theory, molecular dynamics simulations, and MMGBSA binding free energy calculations confirmed Ligand-13's reactivity and stability, leading the authors to suggest Ligand-13 as a promising antimalarial candidate.

articles.headline: Examining malaria outbreaks in endemic settings
articles.title_original: Malaria outbreaks in endemic settings?
articles.authors: Steketee RW
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Glutathione redox system as a drug target against Plasmodium
articles.title_original: Glutathione redox system as a therapeutic target for Plasmodium: Opportunities, Challenges, and future perspectives.
articles.authors: Mahato RP, Kumbham S, Kumar S, Ch S
articles.details: This review examines the glutathione (GSH) redox system in malaria parasites as a potential therapeutic target, noting that GSH creates a reducing environment protecting parasites from oxidative stress generated during hemoglobin breakdown and mitochondrial respiration. Because parasite GSH metabolic pathways differ markedly from those of the human host, they are presented as attractive targets against multidrug-resistant parasites. The authors discuss inhibitors of key enzymes (GST, GR, GS, and GP) and the potential of combination therapies, while highlighting challenges of selective drug targeting and drug resistance. They conclude these pathways offer promising targets for novel antimalarial strategies.

articles.headline: Civil society views on gene drive mosquitoes for malaria in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Exploratory conversations with biodiversity-oriented civil society groups on the potential applications of gene drive-modified mosquitoes for malaria control in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Finda MF, Sambo M, Malika G, Njalambaha R, Salum S, Okumu FO
articles.details: This exploratory qualitative study examined the perspectives of biodiversity-oriented civil society organizations (CSOs) on the governance, testing, and potential use of gene drive-modified mosquitoes (GDMMs) for malaria control in Tanzania. It involved eight in-depth interviews, one focus group discussion, and three large group discussions with representatives from ten biodiversity-focused CSOs, with transcripts analyzed thematically using NVivo 12 Plus. Participants expressed cautious support for GDMM research but raised concerns about scientific uncertainty, limited local expertise, transparency, transboundary effects, technological dependency, and accountability. Aligned with the precautionary principle, they called for robust context-specific evidence, ethical and regulatory frameworks, sustained community engagement, in-country capacity building, and national sovereignty before any environmental release.

articles.headline: Cost-effectiveness of XN-31 analyzer plus microscopy for diagnosing malaria
articles.title_original: A comparison of the cost-effectiveness of the current standard of care and of the haematology analyser XN31-microscopy combination for diagnosing malaria in a nonendemic country.
articles.authors: Picot S, Bienvenu AL
articles.details: This cost-effectiveness analysis compared malaria diagnosis scenarios in a cohort of patients receiving care at Lyon University Hospital for suspected malaria during 2023. Four scenarios were assessed: microscopy alone, rapid diagnostic test plus microscopy, LAMP plus microscopy (controls), and the XN-31 haematology analyser plus microscopy (intervention), with direct costs based on prices paid in France for one sample among 1000 tests per year and indirect costs based on staff salaries and time. The authors report that the diagnostic accuracy and short time-to-result of the haematology analyser combined with microscopy were key to its cost-effectiveness, though generalisability is limited by France-specific costs and the limited panel of methods tested.

articles.headline: Seasonal malaria chemoprevention linked to fewer pediatric infections and antibiotic use
articles.title_original: Indirect benefits of seasonal malaria chemoprevention for non-malarial pediatric infections and routine antibiotic use in real-world programmatic settings: a pre-post study using positive and negative controls.
articles.authors: Gebreegziabher EA, Ouattara M, Bountogo M, Coulibaly B, Boudo V, Ouedraogo T, Lebas E, Hu H, O'Brien KS, Hsiang MS, Glidden DV, Arnold BF, Lietman TM, Sié A, Oldenburg CE
articles.details: This pre-post study assessed benefits of seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC; monthly sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine plus amodiaquine) beyond malaria prevention in rural Burkina Faso, comparing non-malarial diagnoses (pneumonia, diarrhea, acute malnutrition) and antibiotic prescriptions during SMC administration weeks versus a three-week post-intervention period, using data from 51 health facilities and Poisson regression with positive and negative controls. During the post-SMC period, modest absolute declines per 1,000 person-weeks were seen for pneumonia (0.7), diarrhea (0.2), acute malnutrition (0.05), and antibiotic prescriptions (0.90), with IRRs of 0.86, 0.83, 0.71, and 0.88. Positive controls (malaria diagnoses, antimalarial prescriptions) fell by 3.7 and 3.6 (IRRs 0.62, 0.63), while injury (negative control) stayed stable. The authors conclude SMC may reduce common pediatric infections and routine antibiotic use.

articles.headline: Microsatellite study maps Anopheles funestus gene flow across Tanzania
articles.title_original: Microsatellite analysis of genetic differentiation among populations of the malaria vector Anopheles funestus across mainland Tanzania reveals contrasted patterns of geographic isolation and gene flow.
articles.authors: Mapua SA, Samb B, Nambunga IH, Kweyamba PA, Mkandawile G, Bwanaly H, Kaindoa EW, Odero JO, Masalu JP, Kahamba NF, Hape EE, Galizi R, Govella NJ, Okumu FO, Tripet F
articles.details: Using 12 microsatellite DNA markers, researchers analyzed genetic diversity, isolation, and gene flow among ten Anopheles funestus s.s. populations (n=654) and one An. parensis outgroup population (n=28) across ten administrative regions in mainland Tanzania. Allelic richness and genetic diversity did not differ significantly among populations, and there was no isolation by distance. FST values exceeded 0.2 between An. parensis and An. funestus s.s., while the highest divergence among An. funestus s.s. populations reached 0.288 between southeastern coastal and inland populations. Clustering identified three groups, with the Mtwara population highly differentiated; the authors conclude moderate divergence persists despite geographic separation and that anthropogenic gene flow may shape population structure.

articles.headline: Cambodia's real-time digital surveillance system supports malaria elimination
articles.title_original: A bespoke, real-time surveillance system for malaria elimination in Cambodia: architecture, applications and impact.
articles.authors: Ngor P, Rekol H, Sovannaroth S, Eng T, Ry R, Sok K, Yoem R, Ou V, Hem V, Chang P, Try R, Ringwald P, White LJ, Maude RJ
articles.details: This descriptive report presents Cambodia's locally built, real-time, case-based Malaria Information System (MIS), a digital platform integrating surveillance, diagnostics, treatment, logistics, entomology, and operational monitoring into one system across all health system levels. Mobile and web applications let village malaria workers and health facility staff report cases and interventions in real time, including offline, and support the 1-3-7 surveillance model, integrated drug efficacy surveillance, and incidence-based stratification. The authors conclude that locally developed digital health systems can transform disease surveillance and accelerate elimination when integrated with community networks and strong governance, while noting challenges such as limited technical support and a need for predictive tools.

articles.headline: Qualitative synthesis of community views on knowlesi malaria in Malaysia
articles.title_original: Synthesis of qualitative evidence on community experiences and perceptions of Plasmodium knowlesi malaria and factors influencing prevention and healthcare-seeking behaviours in Malaysia.
articles.authors: Htay MNN, Mohd NHN, Oo MMT, San LPP, Abas AL
articles.details: This qualitative evidence synthesis (PROSPERO CRD420251045457) used Thomas and Harden's thematic synthesis method to consolidate community experiences and perceptions of Plasmodium knowlesi malaria and factors influencing prevention and healthcare-seeking behaviours in Malaysia. Five qualitative and mixed-method studies published between 2022 and 2024 were included. Three analytical themes emerged: knowledge and lived realities shaping risk perception (including fear of hospitalization and income loss); environmental, structural, and social barriers constraining engagement with prevention and healthcare; and prevention practices reflecting local knowledge and availability of formal measures. The authors conclude that prevention behaviours are shaped by social, environmental, and cultural context, and that integrating local perspectives could improve sustainability and equity of control programs in rural areas.

articles.headline: Baseline household survey before RTS,S vaccine rollout in Ghana
articles.title_original: The Malaria Vaccine Implementation Programme study area in Ghana: results of a household survey prior to the introduction of the RTS,S/AS01 vaccine.
articles.authors: Welaga P, Gyan T, Koram K, Hodgson A, Odei-Lartey E, Agbokey F, Gyaase S, Awini E, Adu-Gyasi D, Amenuvegbe GK, Dadzie D, Adam AR, Hammond J, Sarfo A, Nuhu AR, Peprah PT, Twumwaa L, Boateng E, Snell P, Fogelson A, Okine RNA, Hamel MJ, Milligan P, Moore KA, Binka FN, Afari EA, Asante KP
articles.details: This baseline household survey, conducted 25 February to 18 March 2019 across 6778 households in 66 districts (33 implementing, 33 comparator) in Ghana before RTS,S/AS01E vaccine introduction, characterized malaria prevalence, ITN coverage, nutrition, and health-seeking among caregivers of children aged 5–48 months. Among 7768 children, 21% (95% CI 18–23) were RDT-positive for malaria; 87% of households owned at least one ITN and 62% of children slept under an ITN the prior night. Of children with fever in the prior two weeks (22%), 72% sought care, 40% were tested, and 42% took an antimalarial; 17% had MUAC ≤13.5 cm and EPI vaccine coverage exceeded 90%. Indicators were comparable between areas, establishing baseline comparability for future RTS,S evaluation.

articles.headline: 50-year-old Plasmodium falciparum genomes reveal long-term parasite adaptation
articles.title_original: Major features of parasite adaptation revealed by genomes of Plasmodium falciparum population samples archived for over 50 years.
articles.authors: Amambua-Ngwa A, Diop MF, Drakeley CJ, d'Alessandro U, Kwiatkowski DP, Conway DJ
articles.details: Researchers sequenced Plasmodium falciparum genomes from placental blood archived in the Gambia between 1966 and 1971, obtaining high-quality data from 54 infected samples to study parasites before widespread drug selection. Within-infection genomic complexity was high, most infections were genetically unrelated, and no drug resistance alleles were detected. Strong positive selection signatures appeared at multiple loci, mostly encoding erythrocyte-binding surface proteins targeted by acquired antibodies. Comparison with samples collected over the following nearly 50 years revealed major directional allele frequency changes at several loci beyond drug resistance genes, notably at gdv1 (regulating sexual conversion for transmission) and the Pfsa1 and Pfsa3 loci linked to sickle-cell trait infection. The authors say identifying these long-term adaptations is important for understanding and managing future parasite evolution.

articles.headline: Mathematical model shows spleen drives red cell loss in malaria
articles.title_original: The role of the spleen in red blood cell loss caused by malaria: A mathematical model.
articles.authors: Moss R, Dini S, Kho S, Barber BE, Buffet PA, Rajasekhar M, Price DJ, Anstey NM, Simpson JA
articles.details: Researchers built a mathematical model of red blood cell (RBC) dynamics that, for the first time, includes the spleen as a compartment retaining large numbers of infected and uninfected RBCs in P. falciparum and P. vivax infections. The model was calibrated to data from splenectomy patients in Papua, Indonesia, where asymptomatic infections showed the spleen harbored far more infected RBCs than circulated peripherally. They found splenic retention of uninfected RBCs attributable to malaria substantially exceeds circulating loss from parasitisation: in chronic infections the ratio of uninfected RBCs lost to splenic retention versus parasitisation was 17:1 for P. falciparum and 82:1 for P. vivax, larger than prior estimates for acute infections.

articles.headline: Review: Wolbachia bacteria as a tool to block malaria transmission
articles.title_original: The symbiotic Wolbachia in Anopheles and its role in reducing the transmission of Plasmodium: updates and prospects.
articles.authors: Nehra R, Dhanda S, Singh K, Singh H, Bharti PK
articles.details: This review summarizes evidence on the endosymbiotic bacterium Wolbachia pipientis in Anopheles mosquitoes and its potential for vector control via cytoplasmic incompatibility (CI) and pathogen blocking. Natural Wolbachia has been detected across roughly 31 Anopheles species, with reported field infection rates including 8–24% in An. gambiae, 4% prevalence (wAnga) in Ghana, 91% (wAnsA) in the DRC, and ~1.4% in An. culicifacies in India. In transinfected An. stephensi, the wPip strain showed stable maternal transmission with 100% infection frequency across generations, nearly complete CI, and moderate fitness costs, while wAlbB established CI and partial protection against Plasmodium. The authors conclude further research on host–symbiont interactions, genetic tools, and long-term field trials is needed.

articles.headline: High malaria prevalence found in onchocerciasis-endemic districts of Ghana
articles.title_original: Malaria in onchocerciasis-endemic populations: patterns in two districts in the Oti and Volta regions of Ghana.
articles.authors: Ampadu B, Osei S, Ashong Y, de Souza DK, Owusu EDA
articles.details: This retrospective case-control study analyzed 345 archived blood samples (1:2 ratio of Ov16 RDT onchocerciasis seropositives to seronegatives) from Nkwanta North District (Oti region) and Adaklu District (Volta region) of Ghana, detecting malaria with the OSCAR P.f/Pan antigen test and conventional PCR. Overall malaria prevalence was 42.6% (38.7% in Adaklu, 53.26% in Nkwanta North), with 81.7% onchocerciasis/malaria co-infection in Nkwanta North. A weak but significant association between malaria and onchocerciasis was seen (OR=2.03 overall; OR=3.52 in Adaklu after adjusting for age and sex). Against nested PCR, the RDT showed 89.2% sensitivity and 71.9% specificity. Authors attribute the high prevalence to environmental and socio-economic factors rather than direct disease correlation.

articles.headline: Cameroonian medicinal plant bark fractions show potent antiplasmodial activity
articles.title_original: Targeting the intra-erythrocytic life cycle of Plasmodium falciparum unveils antimalarial drug discovery starting points from Drymaria cordata and Macaranga monandra.
articles.authors: Youbi KA, Tali MBT, Ngouana V, Wendji Monkam DS, Souleyman H, Dongmo YKM, Dongmo KJJ, Nana O, Woutouoba Ntieche D, Dize D, Mbouna CDJ, Tsouh Fokou PV, Boyom FF
articles.details: This study tested aqueous, methanolic, ethanolic, and hydroethanolic extracts and fractions of Drymaria cordata (whole plant) and Macaranga monandra (bark) against chloroquine-sensitive (Pf3D7) and multidrug-resistant (PfDd2) P. falciparum, with selectivity assessed on Vero cells. D. cordata ethanolic extract was moderately active (IC50 PfDd2 18.9, Pf3D7 24.51 μg/mL), while the M. monandra methanolic bark extract was highly potent and selective (IC50 PfDd2 2.46, Pf3D7 1.02 μg/mL; SI >203 and >487). Fractions F2 and F3 reached IC50 of 0.60–3.42 μg/mL, arrested ring-stage development, lysed trophozoite-infected RBCs, and blocked merozoite egress. UHPLC-MS identified 17 metabolites; lecanoric acid was predicted as a promising candidate. Authors conclude the bark fractions target multiple asexual blood stages.

articles.headline: Review: hepatocyte autophagy in malaria liver-stage infection and therapy
articles.title_original: Hepatocyte Autophagy in Malaria: Current Concepts, Emerging Mechanisms, and Future Therapeutic Directions.
articles.authors: Berbudi A, Khairani S, Setyowati EY, Kwarteng A
articles.details: This review synthesizes recent advances on how hepatocytes combat Plasmodium liver-stage infection through noncanonical autophagy rather than canonical xenophagy. It describes the conjugation of ATG8 to single membranes (CASM), which lipidates LC3 onto the parasitophorous vacuole membrane via a V-ATPase-ATG16L1-dependent mechanism to activate the Plasmodium-associated autophagy-related (PAAR) response limiting early parasite development. Parasites counter this using PVM proteins UIS3 and UIS4 to sequester LC3 and remodel perivacuolar actin, and exploit GABARAP paralogs to activate TFEB and boost lysosomal biogenesis for nutrient access. The authors argue that selective modulation of noncanonical autophagy offers a promising host-directed therapy that restricts liver-stage development while limiting antimalarial resistance.

articles.headline: Antibody markers reveal continued P. falciparum exposure near elimination
articles.title_original: Serological evidence of continued exposure to Plasmodium falciparum among residents in a near-elimination setting.
articles.authors: Daud MM, Rahayu AA, Ilyas M, Hitipeuw D, Fitriana F, Syahada JH, Nuranindita R, Amirudin FA, Saputra EY, Jeon BY, Cahyani NKD, Pandupuspitasari NS, Han JH, Muh F
articles.details: A community-based cross-sectional study (May–June 2025) analyzed 120 malaria-exposed individuals and 24 malaria-negative controls across four villages (Sedayu, Kemejing, Kembaran, Wadas) in the Menoreh Hills, using protein microarray to measure antibodies to the P. falciparum blood-stage antigens PfEBA175 and PfRh5. PfEBA175 seroprevalence ranged from 10–40%, significantly higher in Kembaran (40%, p<0.001) and Wadas (23%, p=0.003) than controls, reflecting focal transmission; PfRh5 showed universal immunogenicity (53–70%, p<0.001), highest in Sedayu (70%) linked to nocturnal livestock activity (p=0.006). Authors conclude these complementary patterns reveal micro-epidemiological heterogeneity and may inform multi-antigen vaccine and sero-surveillance strategies.

articles.headline: Most Cameroonian caregivers willing to accept malaria vaccine
articles.title_original: Determinants of caregivers' acceptance of the malaria vaccine: The case of Bamenda and Bamenda III Health Districts in Cameroon.
articles.authors: Aboki LN, Nlinwe NO, Ebai CB, Niba LL, Atanga MBS
articles.details: A cross-sectional survey of 1000 caregivers of children aged five and below in Bamenda and Bamenda III Health Districts, Cameroon, assessed determinants of malaria vaccine (RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M) acceptance using a structured questionnaire and Chi-square tests with odds ratios. Overall, 89.6% of caregivers were willing to vaccinate their children. Factors significantly associated with acceptance included source of information (community events vs social media, OR=1.663, p=0.037), perception (very rejecting vs very accepting, OR=0.098, p<0.001), sex (male vs female, OR=0.704, p=0.028), religion (other vs Christian, OR=0.386, p=0.001), and occupation (NGO-employed vs self-employed, OR=2.92, p=0.045). Authors conclude acceptance is high but misinformation remains a barrier requiring targeted communication.

articles.headline: Case series of transient neuropsychiatric symptoms after falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: A Case Series of Transient Neuropsychiatric Manifestations Following Plasmodium Falciparum Malaria.
articles.authors: Bhattacharjee D
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Malaria funding linked to lower under-five deaths in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Funded investments contributed to the reduction of malaria morbidity and mortality in children under five: fifteen years retrospective study from 2009 to 2023 in Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Akotionga EA, Yaro JB, Kote B, Bakiono F, Ouedraogo T, Nassa S, Batiene V, Nitiema AP, Kouyate B, Nebie SP, Yonaba S, Laokri S, Bazira L, Sirima SB
articles.details: A retrospective study (2009–2023) in Burkina Faso assessed the impact of malaria funding on under-five mortality and morbidity, using secondary analysis of routine DHIS2 health-facility data alongside malaria programme and health-account financial data. Over fifteen years the country invested USD13,545 million in health, 15.3% directed against malaria. Distance to health facilities fell from 7.2 to 6.0 km, new contacts per capita for under-fives rose from 0.8 to 2.1 per year, and the malaria case fatality rate in children under five dropped from 3.4% to 1.3%. Three of thirteen regions—Sahel (IRR=4.1), Boucle du Mouhoun (IRR=3.5), and Nord (IRR=3.4)—had roughly threefold higher mortality risk than Centre. Authors conclude funding reduced malaria burden amid local variation in death risk.

articles.headline: Case report: sepsis from malaria, dengue, and COVID-19 co-infection
articles.title_original: Sepsis secondary to co-infection with malaria, dengue and COVID-19: a case report.
articles.authors: Guibla I, Zabramba A, Traore SIS, Tankoano AI, Ilboudo CS, Ouédraogo RFA, Ouédraogo MD, Poda A, Traoré IA
articles.details: This case report describes a 36-year-old woman admitted to intensive care in Burkina Faso with acute respiratory distress and a triple co-infection of Plasmodium falciparum malaria, dengue fever (DEN3 serotype), and COVID-19, complicated by sepsis and multiple organ dysfunction. She had severe hypoxemia, hepatic dysfunction (cytolysis and cholestasis), and a SOFA score of 7. Initial focus on malaria and dengue led to a 10-day delay in diagnosing COVID-19. The authors highlight the diagnostic complexity of overlapping, non-discriminatory presentations and advocate systematic assessment and multiplex PCR testing for early, accurate diagnosis.

articles.headline: Dengue detected in non-malaria febrile patients in two Kenyan counties
articles.title_original: Molecular diagnosis of arboviruses (dengue, Chikungunya, yellow fever, Rift Valley fever, Mayaro, West Nile, and Zika viruses) in non-malaria acute febrile illnesses in Kenya's Garissa County, July 2023, and Mombasa County, July 2024.
articles.authors: Roba A, Kiiru J, Kirui E, Langat P, Mbeneka M, Wanyonyi M, Nasengo J, Lagu HI, Achol E, Affara M, Gehre F, Hassan H, Odiambo E, Dugay F, Hussein A, Okemwa F, Swaleh S, Thani S, Gichana R, Karissa I, Ndia M
articles.details: This prospective arboviral surveillance study used a deployed mobile molecular laboratory to test blood from patients with non-malaria febrile illness (negative malaria microscopy) at health facilities in Garissa County and Mombasa County, Kenya. Using RT-PCR panels for dengue, Chikungunya, yellow fever, Rift Valley fever, Zika, Mayaro, and West Nile viruses, 4 of 326 Garissa patients were dengue-positive (1.23%, 95%CI 0.36%-3.23%) and 26 of 289 Mombasa patients were dengue-positive (9.00%, 95%CI 6.17%-12.90%). No other arboviruses were detected. The authors conclude that scaling up dengue diagnostic capacity in peripheral health centres is urgently needed.

articles.headline: Review highlights Plasmodium malariae as neglected, drug-resistance-prone parasite
articles.title_original: Plasmodium malariae is an overlooked malaria parasite with emerging challenges.
articles.authors: Shankar H, Kumar G, Ahmed N, Florentin A
articles.details: This review synthesizes current knowledge on Plasmodium malariae, described as the least studied of the five human malaria species despite its widespread global distribution. The authors note control challenges including the lack of P. malariae-specific diagnostics, chronic low-grade parasitemia, suboptimal clinical features, and emerging evidence of increasing antimalarial drug resistance and reduced susceptibility to first-line antimalarials. They argue its chronic-infection capacity and diagnostic difficulties threaten malaria elimination, and call for enhanced molecular diagnostics, targeted therapies, and improved surveillance to prevent resurgence as other species are controlled. No quantitative study results are reported.

articles.headline: Malaria cases surged in Jimma town, Ethiopia, amid surveillance gaps
articles.title_original: Evaluation of the malaria surveillance system and trends in Jimma town, Southwest Ethiopia, 2024: a mixed-method study.
articles.authors: Nuh AY, Fikru C, Regassa D, Serba HA, Abreham A, Mattew B, Nasir E, Fikadu H, Mulugeta H, Abubekir H, Christine L, Adam MA, Oloock R, Gizaw T
articles.details: This facility-based cross-sectional mixed-method study evaluated the malaria surveillance system and case trends across 10 public and private health facilities in Jimma Town, Southwest Ethiopia, combining DHIS2/register data with interviews of 8 key informants. Malaria cases rose dramatically from 432 in 2012 EFY to 11,824 in 2016 EFY, with Plasmodium falciparum accounting for 56.4% of infections; the largest increases were among school-aged children and adults, and private-facility contribution to reporting grew from 47% to 66%. The system was generally simple and flexible but limited by incomplete reporting, weak feedback, and poor private-sector integration.

articles.headline: End-users welcome non-invasive malaria tests but doubt their accuracy
articles.title_original: "It doesn't require blood!": Perceptions around non-invasive malaria testing tools in Indonesia, Peru, and Rwanda.
articles.authors: Fargnoli V, Calarco S, Thomas C, Thomas C, Mone Iye CN, Paz Soldan VA, Morrison AC, Serumondo J, Nshimiyimana L, Nsaba Uwera YD, Marbán-Castro E, Shilton S, Tetteh KKA
articles.details: This qualitative study explored end-user perceptions of non-invasive malaria diagnostics (saliva, exhaled volatile organic compounds, transdermal detection) through 24 semi-structured interviews and 16 focus group discussions (139 participants total) in Indonesia, Peru, and Rwanda. Participants valued the comfort of avoiding blood draw (seen as painful for children), ease of use, rapid real-time diagnosis, and portability, especially in remote areas. Major concerns were the lack of information on accuracy versus established methods, distrust of non-blood specimens, and inability to differentiate malaria species. The authors stress early stakeholder engagement to ensure such tools meet community needs.

articles.headline: Cochrane review: iron for children does not raise malaria risk
articles.title_original: Oral iron supplements for children in malaria-endemic areas.
articles.authors: Itzkovich M, Neuberger A, Harris I, Yahav D, Paul M, Gilboa M
articles.details: This systematic review of 40 randomised controlled trials (33,785 children) evaluated oral iron supplementation, with or without folic acid, in children in hyperendemic or holoendemic malaria areas. Compared with placebo or no treatment, iron resulted in little or no difference in clinical malaria (RR 0.93, 95%CI 0.87-1.00; high-certainty) and slightly reduced severe malaria (RR 0.90, 95%CI 0.81-0.98; high-certainty), with little or no difference in mortality (RR 1.15, 95%CI 0.76-1.74; low-certainty) or hospitalisations/clinic visits. Iron plus folic acid showed little to no difference in severe malaria requiring admission (RR 1.11, 95%CI 0.96-1.28; low-certainty).

articles.headline: Paracetamol fails to speed kidney recovery in children with severe malaria
articles.title_original: A phase I/II randomized controlled clinical trial to assess the feasibility, safety, and preliminary effectiveness of paracetamol in resolving acute kidney injury in children with severe malaria.
articles.authors: Paasi G, Namayanja C, Ongodia P, Okalebo CB, Okiror W, Abongo G, Masifa G, Amorut D, Muhindo R, Olupot-Olupot P
articles.details: This phase I/II open-label randomized controlled trial enrolled 40 hospitalized children (>6 months to <12 years) with malaria-induced acute kidney injury in eastern Uganda, randomized 1:1 to oral paracetamol 20 mg/kg every 6 hours for 48 hours or tepid sponging. Renal recovery at 48 hours, assessed by restricted mean survival time, was not improved: mean time to recovery was 0.491 h longer in the paracetamol group (95% CI -9.265 to 10.248; p=0.921), and 1.04 h after adjusting for age and weight (p=0.832). Paracetamol was safe with no significant difference in adverse events or hepatotoxicity. The authors conclude paracetamol did not improve renal recovery and larger studies are needed.

articles.headline: COVID-19 vaccination linked to higher adult malaria vaccine intention in Senegal
articles.title_original: Exploring factors affecting malaria vaccination intention and COVID-19 vaccine uptake: evidence from a joint analysis in rural Senegal.
articles.authors: Cortaredona S, Sokhna C, Maradan G, Ba EH, Seror V
articles.details: This cross-sectional telephone survey of 795 adults in rural Niakhar, Senegal, used the Health Belief Model and a multivariable bivariate probit model to jointly assess intention to receive a hypothetical free adult malaria vaccine and actual COVID-19 vaccine uptake. Among participants, 35.6% had received at least one COVID-19 dose and 58.6% expressed favourable intention toward a malaria vaccine. COVID-19-vaccinated individuals were 60% more likely to report favourable malaria vaccine intentions (p<0.001). Perceived severity more strongly predicted COVID-19 uptake while perceived susceptibility more strongly predicted malaria vaccine intention; women were more likely to support malaria vaccination (p=0.005). The authors conclude most determinants are shared across vaccines.

articles.headline: Synthesized thymol derivatives show antimalarial activity targeting PfCRT
articles.title_original: Thymol Derivatives as Antimalarial Agents: Synthesis, Activity Against Plasmodium falciparum, ADMET Profiling, and Molecular Docking Insights.
articles.authors: Ali AH, Retnosari R, Jamil SNH, Norazmi NAZ, Zuhaidi ND, Lam SD, Chieng S, Agustar HK, Chandru K, Md Iqbal N, Yee Ling L, Latip J
articles.details: Researchers synthesized thymol derivatives via esterification and conjugation with hydroxybenzoic and cinnamic acid derivatives, testing them against resistant and sensitive P. falciparum strains and Plasmodium knowlesi, with PfCRT molecular docking and ADMET analysis. Of seven derivatives, compounds 4 and 6 showed notable potency against P. falciparum 3D7 (EC50 = 6.01 ± 1.7 µM and 6.8 ± 1.1 µM) with high selectivity indices (16.5 and 14.6). Most derivatives were non-toxic to HCF mammalian cells (CC50 >99 µM), except compounds 3 (71.4 µM) and 1 (58.4 µM). Compounds 3, 4, and 6 showed strong PfCRT binding affinities (around -8.3 to -8.4 kcal/mol). The authors highlight compounds 4 and 6 as lead antimalarial candidates.

articles.headline: Adding single-dose primaquine to artemether-lumefantrine blocks malaria transmission
articles.title_original: The Effect of Artemether-Lumefantrine Combined with a Single Dose of Primaquine on Plasmodium falciparum Gametocyte Clearance and Post-Treatment Infectivity to Anopheles arabiensis.
articles.authors: Minwuyelet A, Yewhalaw D, Petronio Petronio G, Di Marco R, Atenafu G
articles.details: This prospective cohort and entomological study in Northwest Ethiopia assigned 96 microscopically confirmed P. falciparum gametocytemia mono-infection cases to artemether-lumefantrine (AL) alone or AL plus single low-dose primaquine (SLD-PQ), with follow-up on days 3, 7, 14, and 28 and membrane feeding assays against Anopheles arabiensis. Gametocyte prevalence declined faster with AL+SLD-PQ (15.2% day 3; 0% by day 7) versus AL alone (28.9% day 3, p=0.001; 12.2% day 7, p=0.033). On day 3, confirmed mosquito infection occurred in 28.6% of AL-only cases versus 6.8% of AL+SLD-PQ (p=0.001); by day 7, 7.3% of AL-only remained infectious versus none with SLD-PQ (p=0.01). The authors conclude adding SLD-PQ accelerates gametocyte clearance and completely blocks post-treatment transmission.

articles.headline: Apical polar ring protein PfAPR1 essential for malaria parasite invasion
