articles.title_original: The essential function of the apical polar ring during the blood stage of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Gurung P, Back PS, Ali I, Hernandez GM, Dvorin JD
articles.details: This molecular study identifies and characterizes PfAPR1 as an apical polar ring (APR)-resident protein in Plasmodium falciparum. Conditional knockout revealed PfAPR1 is essential for asexual replication, and iterative ultrastructure expansion microscopy showed it predominantly localizes to the basal APR ring. Loss of PfAPR1 caused defects in daughter cell segmentation and subpellicular microtubule organization while largely preserving IMC formation and apical polarity; knockout parasites contacted host red blood cells but failed to form a tight junction, resulting in a complete block in invasion. Using PfAPR1 as bait, additional APR proteins were identified, and the authors conclude the APR is a promising antimalarial target.

articles.headline: P. falciparum MSP1/MSP2 diversity and severe malaria alleles in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Genetic Diversity of Plasmodium falciparum Merozoite Surface Protein 1 and 2 Genes and Their Allele Association With Malaria Severity, Seasons, and Sex in Bamenda, North West Cameroon.
articles.authors: Achungu CR, Anong DN, Faustin K, Shey RA, Tabe CJ, Djouosseu CA
articles.journal: Can J Infect Dis Med Microbiol
articles.details: Genotyping of Plasmodium falciparum from people with microscopy/RDT-confirmed malaria in Bamenda, North West Region, Cameroon, found all family-specific alleles present, with RO33 the most prevalent Msp1 allele (44.7%) and 3D7 the most prevalent Msp2 allele (56.7%), while K1 was linked to severe malaria. Using nested PCR, Msp1 Block 2 was genotyped in 281 samples and Msp2 Block 3 in 194 samples to assess genetic diversity and associations with severity, sex, and season. Msp2 Block 3 alleles were associated with sex (χ²=8.856, p=0.012), but no allele was more prevalent in any specific season.

articles.headline: Co-creating community malaria services with stakeholders in northeastern Thailand
articles.title_original: Sustaining community-based malaria services through stakeholder engagement: lessons from co-creation in northeastern Thailand.
articles.authors: Jongdeepaisal M, Sirimattayanant M, Prasert O, Maneenet S, Sopa P, Napchit S, Yuenyao N, Singkham N, Charoensup I, Kanjanasuwan J, Thongard T, Asarath SA, Khirikoekkong N, Osterrieder A, Maude RR, Pell C, Cheah PY, Maude RJ
articles.details: Implementation/co-creation study in Buntharik district, Ubon Ratchathani province, northeastern Thailand, working with community members and malaria/health program implementers to design engagement activities sustaining community-based malaria care. Using co-creation and theory-of-change frameworks, the team ran 36 in-person engagement activities with approximately 550 participants between April 2023 and June 2024, producing a co-created 2024 Buntharik health calendar integrating malaria information with local health priorities. Authors conclude the co-creation process showed strong potential for sustaining community-based care amid declining malaria awareness, with responsiveness to local concerns as the key enabler, though future efforts need additional funding.

articles.headline: Bionomics and insecticide resistance of Indian malaria vectors across states
articles.title_original: A multicentric study on understanding the bionomics of Indian malaria vectors across diverse eco-epidemiological settings.
articles.authors: Mohanty AK, Eapen A, Singh H, Singh K, Baharia RK, Jain V, Mazumdar D, Sharma S, Shriram AN, Vidhya PT, Sharma A, Thiruvengadam K, Rahi M
articles.details: Multicentric entomological study across 14 districts in eight Indian states between 2021 and 2023, characterizing Anopheles vector bionomics, blood-meal preference, Plasmodium infection (by PCR), and WHO insecticide susceptibility. Anopheles culicifacies occurred in all states, with highest indoor man-hour density 11.95 (Kanker, Chhattisgarh) and outdoor 27.16 (Bareilly, Uttar Pradesh); An. minimus showed high anthropophagy (human blood index 0.94 in South Tripura) and An. baimaii the highest sporozoite rate (5.88). An. culicifacies was resistant to alpha-cypermethrin and deltamethrin in several districts, and An. stephensi resistant to multiple insecticides in North Goa. Authors flag changing resting behavior and pyrethroid resistance as concerns for India's pre-elimination efforts.

articles.headline: School-based preventive malaria treatment cuts absenteeism in Tanzania
articles.title_original: The impact of intermittent preventive treatment for malaria on school absenteeism among primary school-aged children in Handeni District Council, Tanzania: quasi-experimental study.
articles.authors: Kidumule FM, Omary H, Zacharia A, Mringo B, Shonde A, Ngasala B, Ottaru TA
articles.details: Quasi-experimental pre-post study of intermittent preventive treatment for schoolchildren (IPTsc) using dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine in Handeni District Council, Tanzania, comparing the same 346 pupils from 13 schools across January–May 2024 (pre) and January–May 2025 (post). Most pupils were aged 11–14 (55.5%) and 91.9% received all three IPTsc doses. Overall absenteeism fell from 13% to 10% (p=0.001), malaria-related absences declined from 27.8% to 11.3%, and attendance rose 6.7%; pupils not engaged in economic activities had higher absenteeism (β=0.26; 95% CI 0.17–0.36; p<0.001). Authors conclude IPTsc was associated with reduced overall and malaria-related absenteeism.

articles.headline: Seven-year malaria prevalence trend at an Ethiopian hospital
articles.title_original: Analysis of a seven-year trend in malaria prevalence at Pawe General Hospital, Northwestern Ethiopia: a retrospective cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Legesse F, Berhane N
articles.details: A retrospective analysis of laboratory records from Pawe General Hospital in northwestern Ethiopia (2015–2021) found that 11,495 of 155,669 suspected malaria cases (7.4%, 95% CI 7.2–7.5%; mean 1,642 ± 434 annually) were microscopically confirmed, of which 90.8% were P. falciparum, 6.2% P. vivax, and 3.0% mixed. Prevalence was significantly higher in males (8.8%, 6,178/70,021) than females (6.2%, 5,317/85,648), with males having 1.48 times higher odds of infection (OR=1.48, 95% CI 1.43–1.54; p<0.001); cases were highest among 25–54 and 15–24 year-olds. The authors note a resurgence after 2018 and call for strengthened surveillance.

articles.headline: High-resolution climate model shows bed nets cut malaria incidence 41%
articles.title_original: High resolution physically based modelling reveals malaria incidence reduction by vector control measures.
articles.authors: Dieng MDB, Munga S, Tompkins AM, Zornoza MG, Caminade C, Fersch B, Arnault J, Khagayi S, Schwarz M, Kariuki S, Bigogo G, Kunstmann H
articles.details: Researchers developed a high-resolution hybrid modeling framework linking WRF/WRF-Hydro and VECTRI, integrating atmospheric, hydrological, ecological, and intervention processes at 1 km and 50 m resolutions, with a new compartment for insecticide-treated net (ITN) coverage. Using 2007–2022 data from western Kenya during large-scale ITN deployment, the model reproduced observed malaria trends within ±100–150 cases monthly. Simulations showed ITN coverage reduced the entomological inoculation rate by 58% and malaria incidence by 41%, with highest efficacy under warm and moderately wet (150–250 mm) conditions. The authors conclude that integrating environmental process modeling with targeted control offers a cost-effective framework for sustainable malaria management.

articles.headline: Provider training boosts delivery of malaria preventive treatment in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Effectiveness of antenatal care delivery of intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine in the context of an integrated strategy with seasonal malaria chemoprevention in Mali and Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Koita K, Kayentao K, Worrall E, Huyen Ton Nu Nguyet M, Coulibaly O, Bognini JD, Tinto H, Rouamba T, Dembele M, Biébo B, Diarra S, Briand V, Hill J
articles.details: This nested cross-sectional study within a cluster-randomised trial assessed an integrated strategy delivering intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (IPTp-SP) through seasonal malaria chemoprevention alongside antenatal care (ANC) in Mali and Burkina Faso. Surveys ran November 2023 to January 2024 across 20 facilities per country (10 intervention/10 control), with ANC exit interviews, facility audits, and provider interviews. The primary outcome was IPTp-SP delivery by directly observed therapy. Enhanced (trained) facilities had higher delivery effectiveness in both Mali (79.2% vs 68.7%, p=0.006) and Burkina Faso (52.3% vs 28.7%, p<0.0001). Providers found the strategy feasible and acceptable.

articles.headline: Splenic rupture linked to accumulation of P. ovale-infected red cells
articles.title_original: Splenic accumulation of intact Plasmodium ovale sensu lato-infected red blood cells in a patient presenting with splenic rupture.
articles.authors: Benabdelmoumen G, Itani O, Joste V, Houzé S, Hardy D, Bavozet F, Sylla M, Kho S, Anstey NM, Consigny PH, Sissoko A, Buffet P
articles.details: This case report describes a 35-year-old man who suffered splenic rupture just after starting antimalarial treatment with atovaquone-proguanil followed by artesunate for acute Plasmodium ovale sensu lato infection. Spleen histology showed a 10-fold accumulation of intact P. ovale s.l.-infected erythrocytes in the spleen parenchyma compared to the general circulation. Infected erythrocytes also accumulated in small extra-splenic blood vessels, suggesting cytoadherence.

articles.headline: Protocol for systematic review of microRNAs in malaria-arbovirus coinfection
articles.title_original: MicroRNAs profiling in malaria and arbovirus coinfection: A systematic review protocol.
articles.authors: Deutou Wondeu AL, Nka AD, Ka'e AC, Ngoufack Jagni Semengue E, Masso S, Fisemou P, Mobolaji AT, Marymag Z, Fomboh Tah C, Nsen Bughe R, Mbu Nji A, Galgani A, Pirrò S, Colizzi V, Fon Mbacham W
articles.details: This is a protocol for a systematic review profiling microRNAs (miRNAs) in coinfections between malaria and arboviruses such as Dengue, chikungunya, and Zika viruses. Following PRISMA guidelines, the review will search multiple databases from January 2000 onwards with no language restrictions, including observational studies and clinical trials reporting miRNA expression data. Two reviewers will screen records, extract data, and assess risk of bias using ROBINS-I, ROB-2, and NIH tools, with a narrative synthesis and GRADE assessment of evidence. No results are reported. It is registered with PROSPERO (CRD42024600379).

articles.headline: Interviews examine social and health-system drivers of China's malaria elimination
articles.title_original: Malaria elimination practices in China from the perspective of health system and social development.
articles.authors: Song X, Liu Z, Li N, Chen L, Liu M, Liu J, Zhang Y, Wang M, Ren M
articles.details: This qualitative study examined China's malaria elimination practices from a health-system and social-development perspective, following WHO certification of China as malaria-free on June 30, 2021. Semi-structured interviews were conducted from July 2023 to July 2025 with key informants including national program managers, scholars, and technical personnel from China, international organizations, and high-burden African countries, analyzed using the thematic framework method. Among 42 participants, experts emphasized the health system (100%), living environment (71.43%), health education (66.67%), working environment (64.29%), water and sanitation (59.52%), agricultural crop types (57.14%), and employment opportunities (52.38%). The authors conclude social development is closely intertwined with malaria elimination.

articles.headline: Scoping review maps hemozoin-based next-generation malaria diagnostic technologies
articles.title_original: Hemozoin as a Diagnostic Biomarker: A Scoping Review of Next-Generation Malaria Detection Technologies.
articles.authors: Berbudi A, Khairani S, Kwarteng A, Otuonye NM
articles.details: This PRISMA-ScR/Joanna Briggs scoping review synthesized 24 studies on hemozoin-based malaria detection, a reagent-free crystalline biomarker produced by Plasmodium, spanning eight technology classes: magneto-optical platforms, magnetophoretic microdevices, photoacoustic detection, Raman/SERS spectroscopy, optical/hyperspectral imaging, NMR relaxometry, smartphone microscopy, and flow cytometry. Magneto-optical systems (Hz-MOD, Gazelle, RMOD) showed the highest operational readiness with robust specificity but reduced sensitivity at low parasitemia, while Raman/SERS achieved sub-100 infected cell/mL sensitivity but remained lab-bound; detection of early ring forms was reduced across modalities. Authors conclude magneto-optical devices are closest to field deployment but need further clinical validation and improved low-density detection.

articles.headline: Community home management of childhood malaria improves care in Senegal
articles.title_original: How the One Health approach can make effective the World Health Organization's recommendations on integrated management of childhood illnesses: Case of community-based home management of malaria in the Ferlo of Matam, Senegal.
articles.authors: Kaly JSN, Keita IM, Doucoure E, Sall FB, Ba AM, Mango AA, Gomis BL, Dieng MT, Ndao M, Diallo SM, Diop A, Fall KA, Coly O, Cisse O, Faye M, Diallo I, Thiongane A
articles.details: This cross-sectional study described a community-based home management (CBHM) strategy integrating One Health principles for transhumant herder children under five in the Ferlo region of Matam, Senegal, using DHIS2 document and activity-report data. Community providers selected from within herder communities saw 2,307 cases including 1,464 fever cases, with a malaria testing rate of 98.9% and positivity of 21.1%, of which 96.4% were treated with artemisinin-based combination therapies. Additionally, 209 diarrhea cases (67.9% treated with ORS and zinc) and respiratory infections were managed, and 1,156 children received vitamin A, 443 were dewormed, and 129 were referred for immunization. Authors conclude the strategy improved childhood illness care in 2024 and highlights the value of One Health approaches.

articles.headline: Illegal gold mining drives roughly 300% malaria rise in Yanomami land
articles.title_original: Investigating the Yanomami malaria outbreak: gold mining and malaria.
articles.authors: de Angeli Dutra D, Fontes J, Braga ÉM, Mordecai E
articles.details: Using satellite imagery and panel regression analyses, researchers quantified the effect of land-use change on malaria incidence in Yanomami Indigenous territory in the Amazon from 2016 to 2023. They observed an approximately 300% increase in malaria cases over the period, associated with rising illegal gold mining: a 1 standard-deviation increase in gold mining was associated with a 20–46% rise in malaria incidence 1–2 years later, and each 1 SD increase in forest-edge perimeter was associated with a 55% rise in cases. The authors conclude that illegal gold mining and resulting forest fragmentation are major drivers of the high malaria burden among the Yanomami.

articles.headline: Most Ethiopian malaria patients delay seeking treatment, meta-analysis finds
articles.title_original: Determinants of delay in treatment-seeking among malaria patients attending health facilities in Ethiopia from 2010 to 2024: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Mekonen EG, Aemro A
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis pooled 12 studies including 4,511 subjects to assess delay in treatment-seeking among malaria patients attending health facilities in Ethiopia, using random-effects modeling due to significant heterogeneity. The pooled prevalence of delay in treatment-seeking was 62.00% (95% CI 46.00–77.00). Determinants included lack of malaria knowledge, living far from a health facility, expensive care, lack of transportation, being a farmer, self-medication, no prior malaria history, low family income, lack of community-based health insurance, large family size, no formal education, and fear of drug side effects. Authors recommend strengthening malaria education, expanding community outreach, and reducing out-of-pocket costs.

articles.headline: Placental malaria linked to reduced child height and higher BMI in Mali
articles.title_original: Association between placental malaria, postnatal linear growth, and body mass index in the Dogon Longitudinal Study, Mali.
articles.authors: Merritt C, Vincenz C, Dolo Z, Shedden K, Strassmann BI
articles.details: In the Dogon Longitudinal Study in the District of Bamako and Bandiagara Escarpment, central Mali, 317 F2-generation participants had placental samples evaluated by histology for placental malaria (PM) infection stage and parasite density, with height, weight, and BMI measured roughly twice yearly from birth to a median age of 6.8 years; linear mixed models assessed growth to age 5. Infants from placentas with severe parasite density had lower height at 6 months (−0.83 cm; 95% CI −1.63, −0.03; p=0.042), with a continuing trend, and significantly higher BMI at ages 2 (0.56 kg/m²; p=0.011), 3, 4, and 5 years (1.35 kg/m²; 95% CI 0.68, 2.01) controlling for birth weight; no significant weight associations were found. Authors conclude severe placental parasite density is associated with decreased linear growth and, for the first time, increased postnatal BMI.

articles.headline: Malaria RDT results recorded fairly accurately in Côte d'Ivoire registers
articles.title_original: Accuracy of recording of malaria rapid diagnostic tests in Côte d'Ivoire.
articles.authors: Bedia-Tanoh VA, Konaté-Touré A, Kangah-Kouakou OMA, Mian ANN, Tanoh AM, Humes M, Griffith K, Aponte JJ, Hilton E, Cooper S, Lindblade KA, William Y
articles.details: A mixed-methods observational study across 16 primary health care facilities in two regions of Côte d'Ivoire captured digital images of RDT cassettes linked to register entries, with an independent panel interpreting the images as the reference standard. Of 11,161 matched RDT images and register entries, 57.9% were panel-interpreted as positive; agreement was strong (κ=0.83, 95% CI 0.77–0.88), with PPV 90.7% and NPV 94.8%. Negative or invalid results were more often incorrectly recorded as positive (5.9%) than the reverse (2.0%), suggesting systematic bias. The authors conclude recording is fairly accurate but misclassification of negatives may distort surveillance and test positivity rates.

articles.headline: IL-10 and IFN-γ signatures mark symptomatic childhood malaria in Togo
articles.title_original: Distinct systemic cytokine signatures define symptomatic malaria in children: insights from a longitudinal study in Togo.
articles.authors: Bara FD, Simfele HC, Koudema T, Bako S, Sakyi ME, Kudakpo AY, Mensah C, Donkor E, Malm FP, Ritter M, Awandare GA, Katawa G, Amenga-Etego LN
articles.details: A longitudinal cohort enrolled 60 children aged 6–10 years, all treated with artemether-lumefantrine and albendazole, with weekly monitoring over 12 weeks using microscopy and qPCR for P. falciparum and cytokines measured by sandwich ELISA. Of the 60 children, 25% (n=15) were qPCR-positive and 15% (n=9) microscopy-positive; 33.3% (n=5) progressed to symptomatic malaria. Symptomatic cases showed significantly elevated IL-6, IFN-γ, and IL-10; IL-10 strongly correlated negatively with Ct values (r=−0.973, p=0.001) and positively with parasite density (r=0.911, p=0.001). The authors conclude elevated IL-10 and IFN-γ characterize symptomatic malaria and may serve as prognostic biomarkers.

articles.headline: Bioclimatic zones shape An. coluzzii gene flow and insecticide resistance
articles.title_original: Genomic population structure and insecticide resistance mechanisms in the malaria vector An. coluzzii across contrasting bioclimatic zones in West Africa.
articles.authors: Amoako EK, Bennett KL, Hernandez-Koutoucheva A, Debrah I, Morang'a CM, Binaansim S, Asoala VA, Ariana C, Malm KL, Awandare G, Miles A, Clarkson CS, Amenga-Etego LN
articles.details: This study used whole-genome SNPs and inversion karyotypes to examine population connectivity of the malaria vector An. coluzzii across bioclimatic zones of West Africa including Ghana, Mali, and Côte d'Ivoire, with Ghana as a case study; reads were aligned to AgamP4 using BWA 0.7.15 with SNPs called via GATK 3.7.0 and selection scanned using the H12 statistic. Analyses identified restricted gene flow between northern savannah and southern forested regions and marked differences in resistance profiles, with greater target-site pyrethroid (Vgsc) and metabolic (carboxylesterases, Gste2) resistance in the North. A distinct signal at the Cyp12f cytochrome P450 gene and a UDP glucuronosyltransferase appeared in coastal Greater Accra. The authors conclude environmental conditions restrict gene flow and shape the distribution of resistance.

articles.headline: Mapping chromatin state dynamics across P. falciparum blood-stage cycle
articles.title_original: Chromatin state dynamics during the Plasmodium falciparum intraerythrocytic development cycle.
articles.authors: Brown AS Jr, Llinás M, Mahony S
articles.details: This study examined the interplay between chromatin states and transcription factor (TF) binding in Plasmodium falciparum across its 48-hour asexual intraerythrocytic developmental cycle, leveraging genome-wide profiles of chromatin accessibility, histone modifications, and Heterochromatin Protein 1 (HP1) occupancy. The authors found that several chromatin states remain stable throughout the lifecycle while others are dynamic and linked to gene activation or repression, and they grouped TFs by their chromatin preferences based on binding-site analyses. They provide a global overview of the chromatin state dynamics coordinating asexual blood-stage development.

articles.headline: Community testing approach narrowed wealth gaps in malaria prevention in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Development of a new wealth index for Tanzania: the moderated effect of the implementation of 1,7- malaria reactive community-based testing and response (1,7-mRCTR) by socioeconomic position (SEP) with malaria prevention.
articles.authors: Gao C, Huang S, Yin H, Lu S, Liu L, Mlacha YP, Chaki P, Zhou XN, Xiao N, Richardson S, Wang D
articles.details: Using two cross-sectional household surveys (2015 baseline, 2018 endline) covering 19,686 households, researchers built a 12-item wealth index via Mokken scale analysis and multiple correspondence analysis, then used logistic regression in a Difference-in-Differences framework to assess associations with LLIN ownership, LLIN use, and antimalarial drug use during 1,7-malaria reactive community-based testing and response (1,7-mRCTR) implementation. The poorest tertile had significantly lower odds of owning (OR=0.62, 95% CI 0.54–0.70) and using (OR=0.53, 95% CI 0.45–0.62) LLINs versus the wealthiest. The DID analysis showed a greater increase among the poorest tertile in owning (OR=1.26, 95% CI 1.03–1.56) and using (OR=1.88, 95% CI 1.25–2.82) LLINs. The authors conclude 1,7-mRCTR disproportionately benefited poorer households, reducing wealth-based inequalities.

articles.headline: Review proposes new definition and diagnostic algorithm for congenital malaria
articles.title_original: Congenital Malaria in Non-Endemic Settings: A Literature Review and Update on Diagnosis and Management.
articles.authors: Patterson CA, Nabarro LE, Kumar S, Nolder D, Chiodini PL
articles.details: This scoping literature review searched PubMed and Embase for English-language articles on congenital malaria (CM), neonatal malaria, and related MeSH terms, identifying 26 papers, and was prompted by a delayed-diagnosis UK case. CM typically presents in asymptomatic neonates with symptoms appearing in the first weeks after birth, and is often missed in non-endemic settings or clears spontaneously in endemic ones. The authors propose defining CM as asexual Plasmodium spp. forms or Plasmodium DNA in a peripheral blood smear of an infant under 28 days (or under 1 year with no endemic travel), and present a novel follow-up algorithm emphasizing PCR to detect very low parasitaemia below microscopy's limit.

articles.headline: Adding azithromycin to IPTp cuts parasitemia at delivery in pregnant women
articles.title_original: Safety and efficacy of azithromycin-containing regimen as an intermittent preventive therapy for malaria in pregnant women: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials.
articles.authors: Lin IF, Sulaiman H, Tran VK, Basri S, Pham CT, Nguyen LMD, Tey MY, Wu Mph CL, Tam KW, Loh EW
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis (pairwise and frequentist network meta-analysis, Mantel-Haenszel method) included eight randomized controlled trials conducted in moderate sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP) resistance areas, comparing azithromycin (AZ)-containing intermittent preventive therapy in pregnancy (IPTp) regimens against AZ-lacking regimens. AZ-containing regimens reduced peripheral parasitemia at delivery (odds ratio 0.71, 95% CI 0.57–0.88), with no significant differences in secondary outcomes such as preterm birth, low birth weight, neonatal death, fetal loss, small for gestational age, or maternal anaemia. Safety was similar to SP-based regimens (OR 1.11, 95% CI 0.76–1.62). The authors conclude adding AZ holds promise given escalating SP resistance.

articles.headline: Review: drug stockouts and treatment delays worsen malaria outcomes in Africa
articles.title_original: Drug stockouts and treatment delays in African health systems: impact on malaria morbidity and mortality.
articles.authors: Obeagu EI, Abdulrahman AO, Anetor JI
articles.details: This review examines how drug stockouts and treatment delays in African health systems undermine malaria control, particularly for children and pregnant women who rely on timely artemisinin-based combination therapies. It attributes the problem to multifactorial causes including weaknesses in procurement, supply chain management, health infrastructure, and human resources, and describes how treatment interruptions worsen disease outcomes and facilitate the spread of drug resistance. The authors argue these failures also erode public trust and push patients toward ineffective alternatives, and they call for strengthened supply chains, improved forecasting and inventory management, innovative technologies, partnerships, and community engagement to support elimination goals.

articles.headline: Case report: imported falciparum malaria in toddler returning from Sudan
articles.title_original: Alert for Imported Malaria in Non-Endemic Areas: A Case Report of Atypical Falciparum Malaria in a Young Child and Diagnostic Experience.
articles.authors: Feng J, Zhou Y, Zhang B, Huang M
articles.details: This case report describes a 2-year-11-month-old boy who returned from Sudan presenting with fever and diarrhea as initial symptoms. The case was flagged through blood routine re-examination rules informed by the patient's epidemiological history, and Plasmodium falciparum malaria was confirmed by rapid diagnostic testing and microscopic examination. The authors highlight that imported pediatric malaria is extremely rare and prone to diagnostic delay in non-endemic areas due to atypical presentations, and present the case as a closed-loop model of clinical suspicion, targeted laboratory testing, and definitive pathogen identification to guide early detection.

articles.headline: School surveys map malaria and schistosomiasis baseline in Chikwawa, Malawi
articles.title_original: Repeated biannual cross-sectional surveys in primary schools set baseline seasonal and spatial surveillance for malaria and schistosomiasis in the Shire Valley Transformation Programme (SVTP), Malawi.
articles.authors: Kapolo P, Chiepa B, Mbewe RB, Kapumba B, Kambewa E, Kaunga L, Coleman S, Chirombo J, Mzilahowa T, Jones CM, Stanton MC, Russell Stothard J
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: Four cross-sectional surveys across two wet and two dry seasons examined 4176 children aged 7–13 years in 21 primary schools in Chikwawa District, Malawi, using rapid diagnostic tests for malaria, urine reagent strips with egg-filtration microscopy for urogenital schistosomiasis, and urine-CCA dipsticks for intestinal schistosomiasis, to establish baseline data ahead of the Shire Valley Transformation Programme irrigation scheme. Overall prevalence was 10.8% (95% CI 9.8–11.7) for malaria, 36.5% (95% CI 35.1–38.0) for urogenital schistosomiasis, and 1.9% (95% CI 1.5–2.4) for intestinal schistosomiasis, with malaria–urogenital schistosomiasis co-infection at 5.2%. Malaria exceeded 25% and urogenital schistosomiasis surpassed 50% in some schools, showing strong spatial heterogeneity and unmet needs.

articles.headline: Polyalthia longifolia leaf extract shows antimalarial, antioxidant, spleen-protective effects in mice
articles.title_original: Spleen protective role, Antiplasmodial and Antioxidant activity of Polyalthia longifolia in mice infected with Plasmodium berghei parasite.
articles.authors: Onobrudu DA, Okpoghono J, Acha JO, Dunkwu CC, Enyi KC, Onyesom I
articles.details: This experimental study used 36 BALB/c mice, 30 inoculated with Plasmodium berghei (NK 65 strain), to test ethanol extract of Polyalthia longifolia leaves (EEPL) at 100, 200, and 400 mg/kg/day against the standard artemether-lumefantrine (lonart, 2 mg/kg/day). All EEPL doses and lonart steadily reduced parasitaemia, with significant increases in spleen antioxidants (GSH, GPx, CAT, SOD, TAC) and ferritin versus control, and reversal of spleen structural disorganization. Lonart gave the highest survival rate, followed by 400 mg/kg/day EEPL. Authors conclude EEPL controlled the macrophage response in infected erythrocytes; further work is needed to identify bioactive compounds.

articles.headline: Digital platform improves seasonal malaria chemoprevention delivery in Nampula, Mozambique
articles.title_original: Lessons from using SALAMA (DIGIT HCM-health campaign management platform) to implement and optimize seasonal malaria chemoprevention in Nampula, Mozambique.
articles.authors: Mussa A, Rodrigues M, Enosse SM, Cook L, Bunce L
articles.details: In 2024, Mozambique transitioned from paper-based to digital data collection using the DIGIT Health Campaign Management platform for seasonal malaria chemoprevention with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine plus amodiaquine, improving campaign efficiency, transparency, and supervision quality. Cascade training and real-time dashboards supported data monitoring, stock tracking, and supervision; in Moma district, first-day SP+AQ wastage exceeding 10% (the threshold for unusual wastage being >5%) triggered targeted supervision that reduced wastage on subsequent days. Digital administrative coverage (75–109%) aligned more closely with survey-based household coverage (~74%) than the paper-based campaign (93–105% vs ~79%). The authors conclude that well-coordinated, locally adapted digital innovations can enhance malaria prevention campaigns.

articles.headline: pfhrp2/3 deletions drive RDT-negative P. falciparum infections in Mizan Aman, Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Molecular, Transcriptomic, and Proteomic Characterization of Plasmodium Infections that Evade Detection by Rapid Diagnostic Tests in Mizan Aman, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Nikulkova M, Kessler A, Wang Z, Patel A, Tolessa T, Teka T, Tesfaye D, Lukas B, Lemessa D, Zemede M, Legesse F, Srivastava H, Sullivan SA, Yan G, Yewhalaw D, Carlton JM
articles.details: This study enrolled 148 individuals with suspected malaria in Mizan Aman, Ethiopia, using RDTs plus molecular, RNA-seq, and LC-MS proteomic methods to characterize infections evading rapid diagnostic tests. 29/148 (27.9%) were P. falciparum PCR-positive, of which 26/29 (89.7%) were false-negative by a P.f/Pan RDT; RDT-positive infections had higher parasite densities. Of 29 infections, 27 (93.1%) had both pfhrp2 and pfhrp3 gene deletions and 22 (75.9%) had negligible pfhrp2 transcripts. Ten samples had detectable PfLDH peptides, but none had detectable PfHRP2/PfHRP3 peptides. Authors conclude a heterogeneous array of factors underlies the observed RDT failure.

articles.headline: GPT-4o outperforms probabilistic models at assigning malaria child deaths
articles.title_original: Comparison of verbal autopsy using a large language model to biologically confirmed causes of death for malaria and other communicable diseases among children in six sub-Saharan African countries.
articles.authors: Carshon-Marsh R, Wen R, Ng TKS, Kamadod R, Bogoch I, Bondy SJ, Witek TJ Jr, Jha P
articles.details: This study compared three computer models (GPT-4o, InSilicoVA, InterVA-5) for assigning causes of death against Minimally Invasive Tissue Sampling (MITS) as reference, using 3129 under-five deaths from six sub-Saharan African CHAMPS sites enrolled December 2016 to December 2022. By MITS, malaria-attributed post-neonatal deaths were highest in Sierra Leone (30.3%, 67/221), then Kenya (17.3%, 42/243), Mozambique (13%, 18/138), and Mali (5.5%, 3/55). GPT-4o correctly classified 60/130 (46.2%) malaria deaths versus 30.0% (InSilicoVA) and 23.1% (InterVA-5), with higher cause-specific mortality fraction accuracy (0.36 vs 0.07 and 0.08). Authors conclude LLM-driven tools could enhance verbal autopsy systems.

articles.headline: Uganda fellowship program's contributions to malaria control, 2015-2022
articles.title_original: Uganda Public Health Fellowship Program's Contributions to Malaria Control Programs 2015-2022: Strategies, Implementation Challenges, and Opportunities.
articles.authors: Ario AR, Kwiringira A, Migisha R, Kwesiga B, Bulage L, Kadobera D, Kisaakye E, Asio A, Zalwango MG, Zalwango JF, Rutazaana D, Opigo J, Harris JR, Rollins K, Niang M, Boore AL, Nelson LJ, Belay K
articles.details: This descriptive report documents how the Uganda Public Health Fellowship Program (UPHFP), a 2-year post-Master's field epidemiology training program supported by PMI and the U.S. CDC, contributed to malaria control programs. From 2015 to 2022, UPHFP led or supported 50 malaria projects, including 14 surveillance projects, 11 outbreak investigations, 7 epidemiological studies, 5 case studies, 6 quality improvement projects, 3 policy briefs, and 4 training and mentorship projects. These informed policy decisions and strengthened surveillance, coordination, and outbreak response. A key challenge identified is single-source funding vulnerability; authors conclude the program enhances epidemiologic workforce capacity and strengthens surveillance.

articles.headline: LILRB1 intronic variant linked to lower malaria risk in Senegalese
articles.title_original: A Functional Intronic Variant of LILRB1 Associated with Clinical Malaria in the Senegalese Population.
articles.authors: Dieppois C, Chambonnier G, Gallardo F, Torres M, Thiam A, Mbengue B, Dieye A, Rihet P, Paul P
articles.details: This genetic association study genotyped four LILRB1 regulatory/coding variants in 267 Senegalese individuals (116 severe malaria, 74 mild malaria, 77 healthy controls), with age-adjusted logistic regression. Of 10,110 candidate eQTLs, 49 were associated with LILRB1 expression and three overlapped open chromatin near the distal promoter. Only rs7246537 was significant: allele A carriers had lower risk of clinical malaria (OR=0.50, 95% CI 0.28-0.88, p=0.0165) and cerebral malaria (OR=0.44, 95% CI 0.22-0.86, p=0.0176). The variant colocalized with a YY1-binding site, and luciferase assays showed the A allele drove twofold lower promoter activity. Authors conclude rs7246537 is a functional variant reducing susceptibility by modulating LILRB1 expression.

articles.headline: NK cells show reduced IFN-γ in children with severe malaria
articles.title_original: Altered natural killer cell function in children with severe malaria.
articles.authors: Turyasingura G, Mellencamp KA, Namazzi R, Opoka RO, John CC, Hart GT
articles.details: This flow-cytometry study characterized natural killer (NK) cell phenotype (CD56bright, CD56dim, CD56neg) and antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity in Ugandan children under 5 with severe malaria (n=21) versus community controls (n=19), across low and moderate transmission areas. Total NK counts were similar, but severe-malaria children had a higher proportion of LILRB1+ NK cells. In the low-transmission area only, severe-malaria children had more degranulating-only (CD107a+/IFN-γ-) NK cells; across both areas they had lower IFN-γ production and fewer IFN-γ-only (CD107a-/IFN-γ+) cells. Authors conclude that despite transmission-dependent differences, reduced NK cell IFN-γ production is a consistent feature of severe malaria.

articles.headline: Spontaneous splenic rupture from P. vivax managed without surgery
articles.title_original: Spontaneous splenic rupture as a rare complication of Plasmodium vivax: a case report.
articles.authors: Mouharir M, Jouabri B, Chahbi Z, Fihri MF, Baba H, Kaddouri S, Elbarni R, Qacif H, Zyani M
articles.details: This case report describes a thirty-year-old Moroccan soldier presenting with fever six months after deployment to a malaria-endemic country, despite mefloquine chemoprophylaxis. Plasmodium vivax was confirmed by blood film and treated with oral artemether-lumefantrine. Within 24 hours he developed acute abdominal pain, hemodynamic instability, and falling hemoglobin; CT revealed a subcapsular splenic haematoma with moderate haemoperitoneum consistent with spontaneous splenic rupture. He was managed conservatively with bed rest, monitoring, IV rehydration, and transfusion as needed, without splenectomy, with a favourable outcome. Authors note splenic rupture should be considered in malaria patients with abdominal pain and hypovolaemia, and that conservative management can preserve splenic function.

articles.headline: Transformer model forecasts malaria and dengue from climate data
articles.title_original: Application of a Temporal Fusion Transformer and Long-Term Climate and Disease Data to Assess the Predictive Power and Understand the Drivers for Malaria and Dengue.
articles.authors: Pillay MT, Le MTQ, Takamatsu Y, Phong TV, Kgalane N, Minakawa N
articles.journal: Int J Environ Res Public Health
articles.details: This modeling study applied Temporal Fusion Transformers (TFTs) to long-term high-resolution climate datasets and weekly malaria and dengue case records from South Africa and Vietnam, using multi-horizon forecasting with novel loss functions and physics-based constraints. The best malaria model achieved R2 of 0.95 and MAE of 4.98, while leading dengue models reached R2 up to 0.90. Variable-importance analyses found extreme temperature and rainfall metrics were the strongest predictors, with ENSO and the Indian Ocean Dipole improving longer-range malaria forecasts. The authors identified temperature and rainfall thresholds linked to elevated transmission and non-stationary relationships across decades, concluding TFTs offer accurate, interpretable early-warning forecasting.

articles.headline: Chalcogen-phosphorane compounds kill drug-sensitive and resistant P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Fast-Acting Chalcogen-Phosphoranes Inhibit Sensitive and Resistant Plasmodium falciparum Strains.
articles.authors: Moura IMR, Barbosa CS, Mendes GR, Aguiar ACC, Cruz FC, Menezes PH, Guido RVC
articles.details: This drug-discovery study evaluated eight sulfur-containing chalcogen-phosphorane analogues against Plasmodium falciparum 3D7, finding submicromolar potency (IC50s 0.7-1.9 μM) and reasonable selectivity over HepG2 and HEK293 cells (SI ≥12). Electron-withdrawing aromatic substituents improved potency two- to threefold. Representative compound 5 was fast-acting, rapidly induced pyknotic nuclei, showed no cross-resistance with standard antimalarials, and had an additive interaction with artesunate; it was most active against ring and trophozoite stages but less effective against schizonts. In a murine Plasmodium berghei model, compound 5 showed modest in vivo activity with slightly improved survival. Authors conclude the series is attractive for hit-to-lead development.

articles.headline: DHA-piperaquine and artesunate-pyronaridine highly effective for uncomplicated malaria in Ghana
articles.title_original: Therapeutic efficacy of dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine and artesunate-pyronaridine combinations in the treatment of uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Ghana, 2023.
articles.authors: Abuaku B, Boateng P, Peprah NY, Asamoah A, Duah-Quashie NO, Matrevi SA, Amoako EO, Quashie N, Owusu-Antwi F, Koram KA, Malm KL
articles.details: In this one-arm prospective efficacy study following the WHO protocol, children aged 6 months to 9 years with uncomplicated malaria were treated with dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DHAP) at five sentinel sites and artesunate-pyronaridine (AP) at four sites, achieving day-42 PCR-corrected cure rates of 100% at all DHAP sites and 91.9%–100% for AP (overall 97.3%, 95% CI 94.3–98.8). The 3/3 PCR-genotyping approach (msp1, msp2, glurp) distinguished reinfection from recrudescence. Day-3 parasitemia was rare (one DHAP site 1/60; one AP site 1/65), with no early treatment failures. The authors conclude that both DHAP and AP remain highly efficacious (>90%) and warrant continued use for treating uncomplicated malaria.

articles.headline: Replication stress raises de novo copy-number variations in P. falciparum genome
articles.title_original: Replication stress increases de novo CNVs across the malaria parasite genome.
articles.authors: Brown N, Luniewski A, Yu X, Warthan M, Liu S, Zulawinska J, Ahmad S, Prasad N, Congdon M, Santos W, Xiao F, Guler JL
articles.details: Using low-input genomics and specialized computational tools, researchers measured the rate of de novo copy number variations (CNVs) in the AT-rich Plasmodium falciparum genome under sub-lethal stress. Treatment with an antimalarial compound that inhibits replication produced a significant increase in genome-wide de novo CNVs, which encompassed genes from various cellular pathways involved in human infection. The authors conclude these findings highlight the link between replication stress, DNA repair, and CNV generation, mechanisms that facilitate rapid adaptation in this parasite.

articles.headline: AI-based automated microscopy matches conventional microscopy for malaria diagnosis
articles.title_original: Automated microscopy for malaria diagnosis in a reference laboratory in nonendemic settings.
articles.authors: Martín-Ramírez A, Lanza-Suárez M, Berzosa Díaz P, Benito A, Antón-Berenguer V, Rubio JM
articles.details: This prospective reference-laboratory study in a nonendemic setting evaluated the miLab™ deep-learning automated microscopy platform against conventional microscopy and nested-multiplex PCR (NM-PCR) on 400 samples. Versus thin-smear microscopy, miLab™ showed 90.8% concordance (kappa 0.8), sensitivity 92.1% and specificity 89.4%, with parasite-density correlation of 0.77 though counts ran 11.6% lower. Versus NM-PCR, sensitivity was 62.8% and specificity 95.4% (concordance 68.9%, kappa 0.4); 63.4% of P. falciparum infections were correctly identified. One P. vivax, three P. ovale, and one P. malariae were correctly classified only after expert review. Authors conclude it is as sensitive as conventional microscopy without expert microscopists, but species identification and quantification need improvement.

articles.headline: Mosquito species distribution and climate linked to malaria prevalence in Kenya
articles.title_original: Mapping mosquito diversity in Kenya correlating species distribution with malaria prevalence across varied climatic parameters.
articles.authors: Zablon JO, Goel V, Giesbrecht D, Kariuki L, Mbogo C, Goedel WC, Bailey JA, Matoke-Muhia D
articles.details: This study analyzed data from 23 Kenyan counties using mixed-effect regression, spatial and correlation analysis, and LASSO variable selection to relate climate variables and mosquito species distribution to malaria prevalence. Prevalence was highest in the Lake Endemic zone (19%), followed by Coastal Endemic (5%), semi-arid seasonal (2%), and low-risk (0.9%) zones in 2020. Anopheles gambiae dominated coastal and semi-arid areas while Anopheles funestus was highest in highland and lake zones. Regression indicated a combination of environmental factors and interventions, including insecticide-treated net coverage, predicted prevalence, though relationships varied across regions and time. Authors say findings can inform targeted control.

articles.headline: Outdoor malaria transmission risk in urban southern Benin
articles.title_original: An underestimated risk? Analysis of outdoor malaria transmission in an urban area of Southern Benin.
articles.authors: Konkon AK, Zoungbédji DM, Sovi A, Hoyochi I, Salako AS, Konkon C, Dangnon B, Yahoue G, Lokossou AS, Adjovi RV, Baba-Moussa L, Akogbéto M, Padonou GG
articles.details: This study compared indoor versus outdoor malaria transmission by Anopheles gambiae s.l. across neighborhoods of Porto-Novo using human landing catches, PCR species identification, CSP-ELISA for Plasmodium infection, and a 250-household survey. Of 6,201 mosquitoes collected, Cx. quinquefasciatus made up 55.6%, Ma. africana 25.7%, and An. gambiae s.l. 16.2%. Biting rates were similar indoors and outdoors in three neighborhoods but higher outdoors in Tokpota and Djegan-Daho, indicating exophagy. P. falciparum infection was found indoors in Tokpota (4.41%) and Djegan-Daho (3.57%) and outdoors in Djassin (1.26%). Although 86% slept indoors and most owned and used nets, both indoor and outdoor biting indicate residual transmission, warranting context-specific control.

articles.headline: Healthcare workers face gaps managing chikungunya and non-malarial fevers in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Practices and challenges related to management of chikungunya and non-malarial acute febrile illnesses in malaria-endemic areas of Afar region, Northeast Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Zerfu B, Kassa T, Mamo G, Legesse M
articles.details: This mixed-methods study assessed healthcare workers' practices and challenges in managing chikungunya (CHIK) and non-malarial acute febrile illnesses across six facilities in the Afar Region, Northeast Ethiopia, using questionnaires from 82 HCWs, in-depth interviews with 10 HCWs, and one year of retrospective febrile-case data from four facilities. Although 93.6% of HCWs had prior CHIK information (mean knowledge score 10.7, fair understanding at 56.3%), only 23.4% showed good knowledge. Malaria was the primary diagnosis (95.1%), yet 59.2% of febrile cases were non-malarial. Authors conclude diagnostic capacity, training, and public awareness need strengthening.

articles.headline: Secondary Anopheles vectors sustain malaria transmission in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Beyond Anopheles gambiae sensu lato: exploring the impact of non-dominant Anopheles species on malaria persistence in high-transmission endemic areas of Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Ngaffo KL, Hien AS, Soma DD, Maiga S, Sougue E, Ouédraogo COW, Delphine KO, Kaboré DPA, Namountougou M, Diabaté A, Josiane ED, Dabiré RK
articles.details: This study surveyed secondary (non-An. gambiae s.l.) malaria vectors across three ecological zones of Burkina Faso using human landing catches and pyrethroid spray catches, with molecular detection of Plasmodium and blood-meal analysis. A total of 1718 Anopheles were collected (688 in 2023, 1030 in 2024), comprising five species: An. nili, An. coustani, An. pharoensis, An. funestus, and An. rufipes, with An. nili dominant (87.5% in 2023; 93% in 2024). Most were collected outdoors with anthropophilic tendencies; P. falciparum was detected in all species except An. funestus. Authors conclude secondary vectors play an increasing role and indoor-focused control needs broadening.

articles.headline: Cambodia's mobile malaria workers sustained services during COVID-19
articles.title_original: Progress towards malaria elimination: insights from Cambodia's mobile malaria workers during the COVID-19 pandemic.
articles.authors: Manning EK, Huy R, Siv S, Ly P, Tibenderana JK, Vernaeve L
articles.details: This descriptive report discusses how Cambodia maintained malaria case detection and treatment by mobile malaria workers (MMWs) among hard-to-reach populations during the COVID-19 pandemic, as the country pursues elimination of all species by 2025. Pandemic movement restrictions threatened logistics and communication for MMWs reaching remote regions where infections are increasingly concentrated. Through locally tailored implementation and close coordination with health authorities, Malaria Consortium's MMWs minimized disruption to the malaria control programme while protecting staff and beneficiaries.

articles.headline: Pyronaridine-artesunate slower than artemether-lumefantrine for SARS-CoV-2 clearance
articles.title_original: Artemether-lumefantrine versus pyronaridine-artesunate for the treatment of malaria in patients with mild to moderate COVID-19 in Kenya and Burkina Faso: a randomised open-label trial (MALCOV).
articles.authors: Tangara B, Barsosio HC, Marlais T, Kabore JMT, Tiono AB, Otieno K, Wanjiku M, Achieng M, Onyango ED, Ondieki ED, Aura H, Odawo T, Allen DJ, Hannan L, Tetteh KK, Soulama I, Ouedraogo A, Serme SS, Soulama BI, Barry A, Badoum ES, Matthewman J, Brazal-Monzó H, Canizales J, Drabko A, Wu W, Kariuki S, Lesosky M, Sirima SB, Drakeley C, Ter Kuile FO
articles.details: This open-label randomised trial compared standard 3-day pyronaridine-artesunate (PA) versus artemether-lumefantrine (AL) in 143 patients aged ≥6 months with confirmed non-severe malaria and SARS-CoV-2 infection in Kenya and Burkina Faso (median age 19). SARS-CoV-2 clearance by day 7 was 41% (22/54) with PA versus 58% (33/57) with AL (aRR 0.78, 95% CI 0.45–1.35, p=0.37); day-7 viral load was higher with PA (855 vs 81 copies/mL, p=0.023) and time to clearance over 28 days was slower (aHR 0.55, p=0.004). Symptom resolution was similar, and parasitological cure by day 42 was 100% (PA) and 99% (AL). The authors conclude both remain highly effective antimalarials and should still be considered for uncomplicated malaria in patients with mild-to-moderate COVID-19.

articles.headline: New web tool maps genomic variation across Plasmodium species
articles.title_original: Malaria-GENOMAP: a web-based tool for exploring genomic variation of malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Thorpe J, Billows N, Ngwana-Joseph GC, Ibrahim A, Nolder D, Sutherland CJ, Nguyen THN, Nguyen THB, Nguyen QT, Dombrowski JG, Di Santi SM, Marinho CRF, Phelan JE, Kurowski T, Mohareb F, Campino S, Clark TG
articles.details: The authors developed Malaria-GENOMAP, a web-based tool integrating genomic variant data (such as allele frequencies) with geographical maps and chromosome-wide to gene-level views to explore Plasmodium genomic variation. It includes data from P. knowlesi (n=139), P. malariae (n=158), P. ovale curtisi (n=36), P. ovale wallikeri (n=47), P. simium (n=38), and P. vivax (n=1359), enabling investigation of population structure, geographic associations of mutations, and putative drug-resistance markers. The tool is available at https://genomics.lshtm.ac.uk/malaria-genomaps.

articles.headline: RDTs overestimate malaria prevalence in Ghanaian children versus microscopy
articles.title_original: False-positive and false-negative malaria rapid diagnostic test results: evidence from a nationally representative survey of children in Ghana.
articles.authors: Asare KK, Aboagye YO, Mohammed MW, Darko M, Adjei C, Emmanuels HF, Boakye AN, Asiedu E
articles.details: This study analyzed 4,417 children under five from the 2022 Ghana Demographic and Health Survey, comparing HRP2-based rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) against light microscopy for P. falciparum. Malaria prevalence was 20.3% by RDT versus 9.9% by microscopy; RDTs showed 89.7% sensitivity and 87.4% specificity, but a positive predictive value of only 43.9% (NPV 98.7%). False positives were uncommon (1.0%), while false negatives were more likely in insecticide-sprayed dwellings (aOR=1.56) and rural areas (urban aOR=0.54), with higher odds in Upper East (aOR=1.93). Authors conclude RDTs reliably rule out malaria but their low PPV may overestimate prevalence.

articles.headline: Cambodia stakeholder meeting on high-dose primaquine, tafenoquine for P. vivax
articles.title_original: Moving towards high-dose primaquine or single-dose tafenoquine for Plasmodium vivax treatment in Cambodia: a meeting report from dissemination of results of the EFFORT trial to stakeholders.
articles.authors: Dysoley L, Cassidy-Seyoum S, Adhikari B, Dahal A, Phoumen N, Srey S, Phalla T, Vonn P, Engrkruy B, Sovanarath O, Mengkea H, Rathana P, Makara I, Heng PK, Heng Y, Khlok H, Pong K, Moeng T, Vantha S, Soviet U, Ravuth R, Bunreth V, Vannak K, Sokhann M, Kimmen K, Dy K, Samol S, Lo K, Sokunthea N, Kry H, Kanha L, Naren L, Oum S, Sovann Y, Channavuth R, Bunmeng C, Hughes J, Brindle H, Hu Y, Peng S, Mardi P, Chanry I, Preap S, Heoy S, Sabawoon F, Sokha T, Ringwald P, Lynch CA, Price RN, Tripura R, von Seidlein L, Devine A, Thriemer K
articles.details: This meeting report describes the March 27, 2025 national dissemination of the EFFORT trial results by Cambodia's National Center for Parasitology, Entomology and Malaria Control. The EFFORT trial, conducted in four endemic countries including Cambodia, assessed the safety and effectiveness of a 7-day unsupervised high-dose primaquine course (7 mg/kg total) and single-dose tafenoquine (300 mg) versus 14-day unsupervised low-dose primaquine (3.5 mg/kg total) for P. vivax malaria, alongside feasibility and cost-effectiveness data. The report notes that current low-dose 14-day primaquine, rolled out in 2021 with point-of-care G6PD testing, is limited by poor adherence and suboptimal efficacy. No trial efficacy results are reported in the abstract.

articles.headline: Iron, copper, zinc isotope signatures in malaria-infected mice
articles.title_original: Iron, copper and zinc isotope ratios in malaria infection reveal signatures of the Plasmodium biology and host mice response to the infection.
articles.authors: Costas-Rodríguez M, Abou-Zeid L, Hastuti AAMB, Silva MDCB, de Morais AS, Quadros HC, Moreira DRM, Vanhaecke F
articles.details: Researchers examined the metallome of asexual blood-stage Plasmodium berghei in infected mice by measuring Fe, Cu, and Zn concentrations and isotope ratios. Isotopic compositions of all three metals were altered in parasitized red blood cells, suggesting parasites efficiently acquire and metabolize host metals. Hepatic iron overload was accompanied by depletion of heavier Fe isotopes, indicating mobilization of heavier Fe stores to compensate for anemia, while a hepatic Cu deficiency with lighter Cu isotopes and ceruloplasmin-mediated Cu release suggested Cu aids Fe release. Hepatic Zn concentration was unchanged but isotopically lighter. The authors argue Fe is central and Cu auxiliary in malarial anemia, while Zn reflects a host immunity response.

articles.headline: Transcription factor PbAP2-TR represses genes essential for Plasmodium blood stage
articles.title_original: Precise gene regulation through transcriptional repression is essential for Plasmodium berghei asexual blood stage development.
articles.authors: Nishi T, Kaneko I, Iwanaga S, Yuda M
articles.details: This study investigated the AP2-family transcription factor PbAP2-TR in the rodent malaria parasite Plasmodium berghei during asexual blood-stage development. Conditional knockout of pbap2-tr caused developmental arrest at the trophozoite (cell growth) stage, and target gene expression was upregulated in knockout parasites, while mutating the binding motifs increased target promoter activity—establishing PbAP2-TR as a transcriptional repressor. Time-course transcriptome analysis showed PbAP2-TR drives transcriptional downregulation from early to late trophozoite development, and it recruits a putative chromatin remodeler, PbMORC, as a co-factor. The authors conclude the PbAP2-TR–PbMORC complex provides precise control of transcription peak patterns during asexual blood-stage development.

articles.headline: IPTp with SP reduces chlamydia and GBS in Ugandan pregnancies
articles.title_original: Impact of intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine on sexually transmitted and reproductive tract infections: results from a randomized trial in Uganda.
articles.authors: Adrama H, Dela Cruz EJ, Ozarslan N, Kakuru A, Odongo B, Gaw SL, Benjamin-Chung J, Kizza J, Aguti M, Ategeka J, Olwoch P, Nakalembe M, Opira B, Clark TD, Kamya MR, Rosenthal PJ, Dorsey G, Roh ME
articles.details: This secondary analysis of a randomized controlled trial in Uganda compared monthly intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp) with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP), dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP), or DP+SP among 2,265 pregnant women, testing vaginal swabs at delivery for STIs/RTIs. Compared with the IPTp-DP reference arm, IPTp-SP had an 80% lower prevalence of Chlamydia trachomatis (2.5% vs 12.4%) and a 35% lower prevalence of Group B Streptococcus (7.7% vs 11.7%). C. trachomatis was associated with increased preterm delivery (PR=1.86) and GBS with increased term low birthweight (PR=2.08). The authors conclude monthly IPTp-SP may reduce adverse birth outcomes through non-malarial antimicrobial activity.

articles.headline: Microscopy misidentifies 15% of malaria species in India, missing most mixed infections
articles.title_original: Plasmodium mono and mixed-infections in India: a tale of see-saw in species misidentification.
articles.authors: Deora N, Pande V, Sinha A
articles.details: This pooled analysis of previously published reports compared 2,706 microscopy–PCR sample pairs from 16 locations across 11 Indian states to quantify Plasmodium species misidentification by microscopy. Microscopy showed a 15% misidentification rate (408/2,706) and misclassified over 98% of mixed infections (400/405) as mono-infections, almost all as P. falciparum. Jharkhand and Madhya Pradesh were identified as major contributors (>20%) to species misidentification. The authors conclude microscopy overestimates P. falciparum burden and underestimates non-falciparum species, potentially wasting elimination resources, and propose an analysis approach for misidentification and sub-microscopic infection data.

articles.headline: Plasma exchange plus hemodiafiltration rescues severe malaria patient with cytokine storm
articles.title_original: The blood purification therapy-based strategy effectively rescued the severe falciparum malaria patient experiencing cytokine storm-driven MODS.
articles.authors: Sun Y, Huang X, Hong B, Song J, Lu Y, Ma J, Wang X, Hu W, Zhang Y, Xuan H
articles.details: This case report describes a 63-year-old patient with severe falciparum malaria, cytokine storm, and multiple organ dysfunction syndrome (MODS) treated with therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) combined with continuous venovenous hemodiafiltration (CVVHDF). After the first session, interleukin-6 fell from 729.65 to 26.42 pg/mL and interferon-gamma from 170.38 to 42.47 pg/mL, with the parasite controlled and the patient recovering from MODS. A literature review identified 29 patients given antimalarials plus blood purification adjuvant therapy and six small cohort studies; 19 patients receiving TPE or blood purification all had good prognosis. The authors conclude the TPE+CVVHDF strategy is a promising approach for cytokine storm in severe falciparum malaria.

articles.headline: Four cases of P. vivax congenital and neonatal malaria in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Plasmodium vivax congenital and neonatal malaria with atypical presentation in Western Ethiopia: four cases highlighting diagnostic pitfalls.
articles.authors: Adugna NB, Adugna NB, Geleta DB, Mitike BA, Terefa YA
articles.details: This descriptive case series reported four neonates (two girls; median age at presentation 18 days, range 1–21) admitted with congenital or neonatal malaria. Fever and poor feeding occurred in three infants each, tachypnea in two; one was afebrile with omphalitis. Two had thrombocytopenia and one anaemia. Plasmodium vivax was confirmed on Giemsa-stained thick/thin smears (parasitaemia +1 in two cases, +2 in one, +3 in one). All received IV artesunate (3 mg/kg) plus empiric antibiotics and were discharged by hospital day 7 with no deaths. Maternal malaria was documented in two pregnancies (one vivax, one falciparum), and one infant presented on day 1, consistent with congenital infection. The authors stress heightened clinical suspicion for P. vivax CNM in endemic regions.

articles.headline: Plant triterpenic esters inhibit PfA-M17 aminopeptidase in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Natural triterpenic phenolic esters target PfA-M17 in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Mamede L, Rangel GW, Schmitt M, Albrecht S, Spichty M, Bero J, Beaufay C, Florent I, Llinás M, Frédérich M, Quetin-Leclercq J
articles.details: This study investigated the mode of action of a mixture of eight triterpenic esters (8TTE), previously linked to the antiplasmodial activity of Keetia leucantha twigs, against Plasmodium falciparum using phenotypic analysis, enzymatic tests, molecular docking, and metabolomic profiling. The compounds showed an antiplasmodial profile with activity onset in the early-ring stage, inhibition of the aminopeptidase PfA-M17 (PF3D7_1446200), and perturbations in parasite haemoglobin metabolism. The authors conclude that further structure-activity and medicinal chemistry studies are warranted to explore 8TTE-related molecules as future antimalarials.

articles.headline: Chlorfenapyr bednets outperform pyrethroid nets against resistant Anopheles in Uganda
articles.title_original: Chlorfenapyr bednets effectively overcome pyrethroid resistance escalation in highly resistant Anopheles malaria vectors in Uganda.
articles.authors: Oruni A, Menze BD, Fotso-Toguem YG, Ngannang-Fezeu VB, Thiomela RF, Tchouakui M, Hearn J, Kayondo J, Wondji CS
articles.details: Experimental hut trials in Uganda evaluated bednets against highly resistant free-flying wild Anopheles and F2 hybrids of FANG and Uganda An. funestus. The Interceptor G2 (chlorfenapyr) net showed superior efficacy versus the pyrethroid-only Interceptor net (mortality OR 18.7, 8.05–48.6, P<0.0001), achieving 70.6% mortality against An. funestus and 63.2% against An. gambiae. PBO nets (Olyset Plus, PermaNet 3.0) and pyriproxyfen Royal Guard gave lower mortality against An. funestus (36.1%, 31.0%, 37.6%) but moderate effect against An. gambiae, while Interceptor gave the lowest (~25%). Royal Guard showed no significant oviposition reduction (63.9% vs 63.3%, P>0.05), and pyrethroid resistance markers (4.3 Kb-SV, G454A-Cyp9K1) showed no significant association with Interceptor G2 survival. The authors support Interceptor G2 for areas with highly resistant vectors.

articles.headline: Serum metabolomics of asymptomatic P. falciparum infection in children
articles.title_original: Exploratory Serum Metabolomic Profiling of Asymptomatic Malaria Children Infected with Plasmodium falciparum in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Zinga MM, Kassim Mohamed M, Ojo D, Mohamed Abdoul-Latif F, Mkassy FI, Wilfred D, Marwa KJ, Cacciatore S
articles.details: This exploratory study used proton NMR (1H NMR) spectroscopy and multivariate analysis to profile serum metabolomes of 48 asymptomatic school-aged children from a high-transmission area in Tanzania (27 malaria-positive, 21 malaria-negative), matched by age, sex, and residence. Group-level differences were modest and heterogeneous, with malaria-positive children showing lower lactate, pyruvate, branched-chain amino acids, and threonine. The KODAMA algorithm identified a metabolically distinct subgroup (18.5%) of malaria-positive children with reduced phenylalanine and alanine and increased glycerophospholipids, resembling symptomatic malaria. Urinary tract infections showed more consistent, malaria-independent signatures involving mannose and carbohydrate metabolism, and the authors stress accounting for coinfections and intragroup variability.

articles.headline: Pv3Rs: software to classify P. vivax recurrence from genetics
articles.title_original: Pv3Rs: Plasmodium vivax relapse, recrudescence, and reinfection statistical genetic inference.
articles.authors: Foo YS, White MT, Taylor AR
articles.details: This is a software/methods report introducing Pv3Rs, an R package that performs statistical genetic inference to distinguish the cause of Plasmodium vivax recurrence: relapse (hypnozoite activation), recrudescence (blood-stage treatment failure), or reinfection (new mosquito inoculation). Under simplifying assumptions, it models genetic relationships between parasites within and across infections to compute posterior probabilities of each recurrence state, aiding molecular correction in clinical trials estimating antimalarial efficacy. It supports multiple genetic data types, including length-polymorphic loci and amplicon sequencing. Pv3Rs is freely available on CRAN under the GNU license (CRAN DOI 10.32614/CRAN.package.Pv3Rs).

articles.headline: Review: Plasmodium PI4Kβ as dual antimalarial and immune target
articles.title_original: Plasmodium Phosphatidylinositol 4-Kinase Beta (PI4Kβ): An Emerging Target at the Interface of Parasite Biology and Host Immunity.
articles.authors: Nimbarte VD, Sonak SS, Mohapatra N, Ishwarkar S, Kumar SR, Mustajar S, Pinto R, Sharanya ST
articles.details: This review examines phosphatidylinositol 4-kinase beta (PI4Kβ), an essential lipid kinase in Plasmodium species involved in intracellular signaling, vesicular trafficking, and parasite development. The authors argue PI4Kβ also modulates host immune responses through regulation of protein export, immune evasion, and extracellular vesicle formation. They propose that inhibiting PI4Kβ both disrupts parasite survival and may enhance immune recognition by impairing virulence factor trafficking and improving antigen presentation, offering a dual antiplasmodial and immunomodulatory strategy. The review highlights its therapeutic potential and calls for further study of its role in antigen processing, vaccine responsiveness, and combinatorial immunotherapy.

articles.headline: Hypothesis: Hsp70 and granzyme B in severe malaria pathology
articles.title_original: Is the Association of Granzyme B and the Exported Parasite Hsp70 a Possible Trojan Horse of Severe Malaria?
articles.authors: Dongola TH, Shonhai A
articles.details: This review/hypothesis article explores whether the parasite-exported chaperone PfHsp70-x and granzyme B (GrB) contribute to severe malaria. The authors note that in some tumor cells about 15–20% of total Hsp70 is released extracellularly, associating with cholesterol-rich surface microdomains and recruiting natural killer cells that release GrB; a 14-mer TKD motif (KDNNLLGRFELSG) of human Hsp70 is implicated in NK activation. Because Plasmodium falciparum exports PfHsp70-x to the infected red blood cell membrane and GrB is elevated in severe malaria, they propose PfHsp70-x on the infected RBC may stimulate NK cells to release GrB, and discuss prospects for Hsp70–GrB-mediated antimalarial therapy.

articles.headline: Review of global malaria status and Gulf Cooperation Council countries
articles.title_original: Malaria Public Health Status: Global Context and Update on Gulf Cooperation Council Countries.
articles.authors: Ahmad T, Alhammadi BA, Almaazmi SY, Arafa S, Blatch GL, Dutta T, Gestwicki JE, Keyzers RA, Meskiri A, Sharafeldin A, Shonhai A, Singh H
articles.details: This review assesses worldwide malaria status with a focus on Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) countries. It notes the global malaria death toll fell from 864,000 in 2000 to 576,000 in 2019, after which progress slowed and mortality rose since 2020 amid the COVID-19 pandemic. The authors highlight widespread Plasmodium falciparum resistance to first-line drugs and the promise of the first malaria vaccines (RTS,S/AS01 and R21/MM). Since 2015 the Eastern Mediterranean Region has seen rising cases and deaths; Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar, and the UAE are declared malaria-free, while Saudi Arabia and Oman (and neighboring Yemen) remain endemic. Threats include drug resistance, invasive mosquito species, rising temperatures, and imported malaria.

articles.headline: Case report: unusual Plasmodium forms on blood smear in febrile child
articles.title_original: An unusual presentation of Plasmodium spp. in peripheral blood smear.
articles.authors: Ansari N, Gupta K, Nandwani S, Bir R
articles.details: A case report of a 7-year-old boy who presented with 6-7 days of abdominal pain and fever and was admitted and started on intravenous ceftriaxone, paracetamol, pantoprazole, and Ringer lactate. Laboratory testing showed normocytic normochromic red cells with leukocytosis and a left shift (total leukocyte count 16.3 × 10³/µL), haemoglobin 9.2 g/dL, marked thrombocytopenia (platelets 35,000/µL), and elevated C-reactive protein (144.8 mg/L), with normal liver function. A peripheral blood smear performed in view of thrombocytopenia and persistent fever revealed unusual microscopic findings whose clinical significance is discussed in the report.

articles.headline: Severe imported falciparum malaria in 32 untreated travelers
articles.title_original: Clinical and epidemiological profile of severe imported Plasmodium falciparum malaria in travelers at hospital admission: A retrospective single-center study in Moscow, Russia.
articles.authors: Emerole K, Tokmalaev A, Mvuania V
articles.details: A single-center retrospective study analyzed records of patients treated for severe imported malaria at an infectious diseases hospital between 2020 and 2023. In total, 32 patients met WHO criteria for severe malaria; median age was 41 (IQR 36-52), 89% were male, and 91.2% were of Russian descent, with nearly all infections acquired in sub-Saharan Africa and no patient using chemoprophylaxis. Plasmodium falciparum was the sole agent in every case. At admission, 99% had high parasitemia, 98% altered consciousness, 78.1% profound weakness, and 52% jaundice. Authors emphasize prompt diagnosis, treatment, and improved pretravel counseling.

articles.headline: Corrigendum to study on malaria and inflammatory biomarkers in children
articles.title_original: Corrigendum to "Analysis of the Relationship Between Malaria and Inflammatory Biomarker Levels in Children in Rural Iraq: Impact of Nutritional Deficiencies, Anemia, and Hematological Disorders" published in Trop Parasitol. 2025 Jul-Dec;15(2):110-117. doi: 10.4103/tp.tp_26_25. Epub 2025 Oct 25. PMID: 41244076; PMCID: PMC12617583.
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Commentary on integrating malaria vaccines into Southeast Asian elimination
articles.title_original: Integrating malaria vaccines into Southeast Asian elimination strategies: A cautious path forward.
articles.authors: Parija SC, Poddar A
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Malaria drives maternal anaemia in Africa; preventive treatment averts cases
articles.title_original: The burden of malaria-attributable maternal anaemia and the impact of preventive treatment across sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Leuba SI, Verity R, Gutman JR, Weiss DJ, Cairns M, Williams JE, Khairallah C, Bojang K, Dodd J, Noor AM, Taylor SM, Otieno K, Coulibaly SO, Kayentao K, Kariuki S, Greenwood B, Desai M, Chandramohan D, Tagbor H, Ter Kuile FO, Madanitsa M, Walker PGT
articles.details: A modeling study combined individual-level data on haemoglobin, gravidity, gestational age, and PCR-confirmed P. falciparum infection from 12,608 pregnancies in 7 African countries with a gravidity-specific exposure-immunity model. For 2023, an estimated 13.1 million pregnancies were exposed to P. falciparum; without preventive measures this would cause 2.41 million (95% CrI 1.98-3.04 million) moderate or severe anaemia cases (Hb <9 g/dL), including 600,000 severe (Hb <7 g/dL). Intermittent preventive treatment with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine averted about 1.10 million moderate/severe and 330,000 severe cases. Authors warn that interrupting IPTp-SP risks rapid resurgence of severe maternal anaemia.

articles.headline: Routine pre-eclampsia diagnosis often misclassified in malaria-endemic antenatal care
articles.title_original: Diagnostic Concordance and Misclassification of Pre-Eclampsia in a Malaria-Endemic Setting: A Comparative Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Opoku Mensah B, Serwaa AA, Dugbatey MK, Anim E
articles.details: A comparative cross-sectional study of 618 pregnant women clinically diagnosed with malaria, pre-eclampsia, or both across primary, secondary, and tertiary facilities compared routine diagnoses against predefined reference criteria. Overall concordance was 50.2% (Cohen's κ = 0.233, p < 0.001), and routine pre-eclampsia diagnosis had a sensitivity of 61.8%, specificity of 62.2%, PPV of 65.9%, and NPV of 57.9%. Malaria parasite density was associated with higher ln(uPCR) (β = 0.17, p < 0.001) but was not independently linked to blood pressure or to diagnostic discordance. The authors conclude routine pre-eclampsia diagnosis shows only moderate performance with considerable misclassification when malaria is concurrent.

articles.headline: Malaria in pregnancy linked to maternal anemia in Northern Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Prevalence, Associated Risk Factors, and the Impact of Malaria on Anemia in Pregnant Women Attending ANC in North Gondar Zone, Northern Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Alula GA, Endale T, Yibeltie G
articles.details: A facility-based cross-sectional study of 308 pregnant women attending antenatal care in North Gondar Zone, Northern Ethiopia, used Giemsa-stained smears for malaria diagnosis and a Hemocue Hb 201+ analyzer for hemoglobin. Overall malaria prevalence was 13.6%, with higher odds among women aged ≥40 years (AOR = 4.8; 95% CI: 1.4–16.0), rural residents (AOR = 2.8; 95% CI: 1.2–6.5), low education (AOR = 2.6; 95% CI: 1.0–6.7), living within 1 km of water (AOR = 3.0; 95% CI: 1.4–6.5), unscreened houses (AOR = 3.6; 95% CI: 1.6–8.2), and inconsistent/nonuse of insecticide-treated nets (AOR = 7.2; 95% CI: 2.9–18.0). Among infected women, 40% were anemic; the authors conclude malaria significantly increases anemia risk and emphasize ITN use and integrated screening.

articles.headline: Modular recombination framework explains DBLMSP antigen diversity in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Module-level recombination drives DBLMSP polymorphism and functional conservation in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Duan YW, Chen SB, Wang TY, Yang WX, Kassegne K, Shen HM, Chen JH
articles.details: Analyzing Plasmodium falciparum DBLMSP1 sequences from samples collected along the China-Myanmar border, researchers found haplotypes that standard population genetic models could not explain, and through comparative alignment and BLAST proposed that DBLMSP1 and DBLMSP2 consist of discrete recombinable sequence modules flanked by conserved regions. Global mining identified nine genotypes each for DBLMSP1 and DBLMSP2, with some modules shared across paralogs (e.g., identical DBLMSP1 module 1M3c and DBLMSP2 module 2M3a), suggesting historical inter-locus recombination. In the dominant DBLMSP1-1 genotype, nucleotide diversity and Tajima's D peaked in variable modules while conserved elements (receptor-binding cleft, SPAM domain) were under purifying selection. The authors propose this modular framework aids understanding of structural diversity, with implications for immunogen design and surveillance.

articles.headline: Review of mosquito immunity and genetic strategies for malaria vector control
articles.title_original: Advancing Malaria Vector Control: Insights Into Mosquito Immunity and Genetic Strategies.
articles.authors: Cleanclay WD, Adedoyin ED, Zakari S, Ogunlana OO, Iweala EEJ, Chinedu SN
articles.details: This review examines the interaction between Plasmodium parasites and Anopheles mosquito vectors, focusing on mosquito innate immunity and genetic approaches to malaria vector control amid persistent insecticide resistance. It describes mosquito immune defenses—pathogen recognition receptors, signaling pathways, and effectors such as antimicrobial peptides and melanization—and Plasmodium evasion mechanisms. The review explores genetic strategies including CRISPR-Cas9 gene drive, Wolbachia, RNA interference, symbiont-based approaches, and self-limiting methods such as RIDL and pgSIT, and highlights mosGILT as an emerging immune regulator relevant to blocking transmission. The authors call for further research into mosquito immune genes and pathways to develop innovative and acceptable genetic vector control approaches.

articles.headline: First-year RDT surveillance shows P. falciparum dominance in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium Species Using Rapid Diagnostic Tests in Cameroon: First-Year Data From Routine Malaria Surveillance.
articles.authors: Tabue RN, Neh F, Evina E, Bomba DA, Voundi JV, Fosso J, Zeh AM
articles.details: A nationwide routine malaria surveillance program in Cameroon used P.f/Pan rapid diagnostic tests over one year (January–December 2024) across health facilities at all care levels, with data compiled through the National Health Information System. Of 3,843,507 individuals tested, 43% were malaria-positive; P. falciparum monoinfection accounted for 82% of cases, mixed P. falciparum + Pan infections for 15%, and Pan infection for 3%. P. falciparum monoinfection prevalence was highest in the Far North region (92.1%), followed by North (85.7%), Central (82.5%), West (81.7%), and East (80.6%), with positive cases peaking between June and July. The authors confirm non-falciparum and mixed infections exist but note RDT limitations in species differentiation, underscoring the need for molecular methods and genomic surveillance including HRP2/3 deletion monitoring.

articles.headline: cTfh cell activation and BTLA/LAG-3 changes linked to placental malaria outcomes
articles.title_original: cTfh Cell Activation and Changes in BTLA and LAG-3 Expression Differentially Associate With Poor Pregnancy Outcome in Plasmodium falciparum Placental Malaria.
articles.authors: Zambo Bitye BM, Mbianda Nana CM, Kwanou Tchakounte BD, Fogang B, Kenfack Tekougang Zangue B, Medouen Ndeumou RS, Ayong L, Megnekou R
articles.details: This cross-sectional study analyzed peripheral, placental (intervillous), and cord blood from 49 women at delivery, using multiparametric flow cytometry to profile circulating T follicular helper (cTfh) cells and ICOS-/ICOS+ subsets in P. falciparum placental malaria (PM).
