PM was associated with increased cTfh frequencies in peripheral and intervillous blood; PM-negative women had higher ICOS- cTfh, while PM-positive women had elevated ICOS+ cTfh correlating with parasitemia. BTLA was downregulated in bulk and ICOS- cTfh cells during PM. Intervillous cTfh frequencies positively correlated with baby weight, whereas intervillous BTLA and LAG-3 expression negatively correlated with birth weight, and cTfh frequencies negatively associated with maternal hemoglobin.

articles.headline: Anti-gSG6-P1 IgG biomarker shows gaps for assessing Anopheles exposure in Mekong region
articles.title_original: Immunological surveillance using anti-gSG6-P1 IgG biomarker reveals spatio-temporal dynamics of Anopheles exposure and gaps in malaria risk assessment in the Greater Mekong Subregion.
articles.authors: Saeung M, Jupatanakul N, Jampeesri N, Afelt A, Chareonviriyaphap T, Manguin S
articles.details: This study evaluated the anti-salivary biomarker anti-gSG6-P1 IgG as a serological marker of Anopheles exposure, sampling the same 184 participants via finger prick across three seasons (rainy August 2022, cool-dry December 2022, hot-dry April 2023). IgG levels were measured by ELISA. Factor Analysis of Mixed Data found seasonality exerted the strongest influence on IgG levels, likely driven by human activities such as rubber tapping where Anopheles dirus is present. Higher rubber-plot entry frequency (5-7 days/week) significantly increased IgG responses (1.08 ± 0.36) versus lower frequency (0-4 days/week) (0.96 ± 0.35). The authors highlight limitations of this biomarker where local vectors have low salivary peptide homology to An. gambiae, calling for region-tailored serological tools.

articles.headline: Insecticide supply chain functions reactively, weakly linked to malaria risk
articles.title_original: Evaluation of the insecticide custody chain and its relationship with malaria burden in the Brazilian Amazon: a process and exploratory impact assessment (2019-2023).
articles.authors: Dourado ML, Silva RAE, Castro MC, Peterka CRL, Kluge CR, Castro D, Lima JBP
articles.details: This mixed-methods process and exploratory impact assessment evaluated the indoor residual spraying (IRS) insecticide custody chain (Etofenprox 20% PM) and its relationship to malaria burden nationally and in three high-incidence Amazonas municipalities (Barcelos, Tefé, São Gabriel da Cachoeira) from 2019 to 2023. Pearson correlation, linear regression with time-lag, and ARIMA models were used alongside a qualitative risk matrix. Nationally, insecticide volume was significantly associated with malaria cases (β = 0.161; p = 0.038; R² = 0.74) and deforestation (β = 0.626; p = 0.034), with allocation lagging behind incidence peaks. Municipal models lacked statistical significance. The authors conclude the custody chain operates reactively, lacks integration with surveillance and predictive environmental data, and needs structural reform toward risk-based, anticipatory planning.

articles.headline: Computational analysis of Plasmodium tandem-repeat proteins and their functions
articles.title_original: Structural and functional insights from detailed computational analysis of Plasmodium repetome.
articles.authors: Kaur S, Kumar V, Singh K, Beh A, A A, Mishra PC, Hora R
articles.details: This computational study analyzed the tandem repeat (TR) content of multiple Plasmodium species proteomes, finding P. falciparum and P. vivax exceptionally rich in TR regions. P. falciparum carried longer TRs, mainly in exported proteins important for survival and immune evasion, with short and intermediate TRs biased toward asparagine usage, suggesting replication slippage and positive selection. Gene ontology analysis showed most TR-containing P. falciparum proteins were involved in binding nucleic acids, proteins, and small molecules. Surprisingly, variable surface antigen (VSA) families had low repeat content, yet all TR-containing VSAs were sero-reactive with antigenic peptides within repeats. Three-dimensional predictions showed several repeats forming ordered super-secondary structures that may facilitate intermolecular interactions.

articles.headline: Quality improvement raised malaria case-reporting sensitivity to 100% in five weeks
articles.title_original: Quality Improvement Initiative to Improve the Sensitivity of Malaria Reporting under Integrated Health Information Platform, Raigarh, Chhattisgarh, 2023.
articles.authors: Patel Y, Kaviprawin M, Patel K, Ramasamy A, Gahwai DK
articles.details: This quasi-experimental time-series quality improvement (QI) project used the Plan-Do-Study-Act method to improve the sensitivity of malaria reporting in the Integrated Health Information Platform (IHIP), which had captured only 44% of laboratory-tested malaria cases from primary health centers in September 2023. Targeting four PHCs with poor sensitivity, root causes were identified as inadequate laboratory technician knowledge of IHIP data entry and poor feedback from the district surveillance unit. After implementing refresher training and a weekly feedback mechanism, the project achieved 100% sensitivity within 5 weeks across the selected PHCs. The authors conclude QI methods can feasibly identify and address setting-specific root causes to improve reporting outcomes.

articles.headline: Malaria declining in Kanker district but two blocks remain hotspots
articles.title_original: Epidemiology of Malaria Cases Reported from Government Health Facilities in District Kanker, Chhattisgarh, January 2018 - December 2022.
articles.authors: Jain T, Ramasamy A, Khare A, Ramkete DK, Nagarajan R
articles.details: This descriptive analysis of National Vector-Borne Disease Control Programme surveillance data from all government health facilities in Kanker examined malaria epidemiology over 2018-2022. The Annual Parasite Index fell from 3.0 to 1.3 and the Annual Falciparum Index from 2.5 to 1.1 between 2019 and 2022, while the Test Positivity Rate and Test Falciparum Rate halved from 2018 to 2022 with the Annual Blood Examination Rate held around 30%. The proportion of Plasmodium falciparum cases rose to over 90% from 2020 to 2021. In 2022, nearly 2/7 blocks had an API above 2 and 1/7 blocks an AFI above 2; the authors conclude reasons for the higher API in these blocks need to be explored.

articles.headline: Malaria resurgence in Koraput district with sharp 2023 rise
articles.title_original: Epidemiology of Malaria in Koraput District, Odisha, 2019-2023: A Descriptive Analysis of National Vector Borne Disease Control Programme Surveillance Data.
articles.authors: Jena S, Bicholkar A, Jacob QM, Baral P, Murali S
articles.details: This descriptive epidemiological study analyzed National Vector Borne Disease Control Programme surveillance data for Koraput district, Odisha, from 2019 to 2023, calculating program indicators by time, place, and person. Koraput reported an Annual Blood Examination Rate of 28% and an Annual Parasite Incidence of 2.7 per 1000, which rose sharply to 4.5 per 1000 in 2023. Narayanpatna had the highest API at 38 per 1000, followed by Bandhugaon at 25 per 1000 in 2023, and API was consistently higher in males than females. Authors conclude the consistent seasonal peak offers an opportunity for pre-emptive pre-monsoon measures and that surveillance needs strengthening, aided by migration to the Integrated Health Information Platform.

articles.headline: Chhattisgarh malaria surveillance system has low sensitivity and data quality
articles.title_original: Evaluation of Malaria Surveillance System in Government Health Facilities in Six Districts of State Chhattisgarh, India, 2023.
articles.authors: Gahwai DK, Kaviprawin M, Panigrahi DK, Das JK, Patel K, Roy M, Tigga S, Jain T, Patel Y, Ramasamy A
articles.details: This descriptive cross-sectional study evaluated the malaria surveillance system in six districts of Chhattisgarh, India, in 2023 using Centers for Disease Control evaluation guidelines, sampling 1276 syndromic, 110 presumptive, and 100 laboratory reporting units and interviewing surveillance staff at state and district levels. Among 1442 P-forms the system captured only 84 (5.8%) of cases, and among 11,510 L-forms it captured 3334 (29%). Valid entries in key variables were present in 44/84 (52.4%) of P-forms and 2461/3418 (72%) of L-forms. Authors conclude that malaria surveillance has inadequate sensitivity and data quality, requiring identification of factors behind low sensitivity and quality improvement initiatives.

articles.headline: First Bahrain case of neonatal P. knowlesi malaria with congenital CMV
articles.title_original: Co-infection of Neonatal Plasmodium knowlesi Malaria With Congenital Cytomegalovirus: The First Reported Case From Bahrain.
articles.authors: Mohroofi AD, Ben Turkia H, Aljowder F, Althawadi D, Loni R
articles.details: This case report describes the first documented case of congenital malaria in Bahrain caused by Plasmodium knowlesi, a zoonotic species mainly found in Southeast Asia. A 26-day-old Pakistani male infant presented with a one-day history of fever, jaundice, lethargy, and hepatosplenomegaly, with laboratory findings of pancytopenia and elevated inflammatory markers. Despite a negative rapid malaria antigen test, the peripheral blood smear confirmed P. knowlesi while the mother's malaria PCR and smear were negative; the infant also tested positive for cytomegalovirus with high blood and urine viral loads. He was treated with intravenous artesunate and ganciclovir followed by oral valganciclovir, and authors emphasize considering congenital malaria in neonates with nonspecific symptoms even in non-endemic regions.

articles.headline: Could NETosis be a new therapeutic target in severe malaria?
articles.title_original: [Malaria: Could NETosis, neutrophils' defense mechanism involved in severe cases of the disease, be a new therapeutic target?].
articles.authors: Kociánová L, Larribau S, Veltz N
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Vietnamese P. vivax shows high PvMSP-1 ICB 5-6 genetic diversity
articles.title_original: Genetic polymorphisms of merozoite surface protein-1 ICB 5-6 in Vietnamese Plasmodium vivax isolates.
articles.authors: Nguyễn TH, Nguyễn ĐTD, Lê HG, Võ TC, Trinh NTM, Cho M, Van Khanh C, Quang HH, Na BK
articles.details: Molecular epidemiology study analyzing polymorphism of the pvmsp-1 ICB 5-6 region in Plasmodium vivax isolates from 4 Vietnamese provinces (Dak Lak, Dak Nong, Gia Lai, Khanh Hoa) collected 2018–2022. From 117 isolates, 139 sequences were obtained, clustering into 34 distinct amino-acid haplotypes, with recombinant types predominant and diverse poly-glutamine variants. Central Highlands sequences showed high polymorphism while Khanh Hoa was less polymorphic; phylogenetic analysis showed no significant country- or region-specific clustering. Authors conclude substantial genetic diversity with regional variation, underscoring the need for continued molecular surveillance.

articles.headline: Imported P. falciparum treatment failure with partial artemisinin resistance
articles.title_original: Treatment Failure and Post-Artesunate Delayed Haemolysis in a Returned Traveller From Uganda With Partially Drug-Resistant Severe Plasmodium falciparum Malaria.
articles.authors: Travis J, McCarthy K, Chapman P, Huang L, Tan A, Cheng Q, Barber BE
articles.details: Case report of a man in his 40s recently returned from Uganda, hospitalized with P. falciparum malaria and hyperparasitaemia of ~1.5 × 10^6 parasites/μL (26%). He received intravenous artesunate followed by artemether-lumefantrine, but parasite clearance was delayed; despite a negative blood film, he was readmitted 3 weeks later with recurrent parasitaemia. Testing showed reduced lumefantrine susceptibility, an A675V mutation in pfk13, and increased ring-stage survival, consistent with partial artemisinin resistance. The authors highlight the high risk of treatment failure in patients with hyperparasitaemia and partial drug resistance.

articles.headline: Gene drives tested against real-world malaria diversity
articles.title_original: Gene drives tested against real-world malaria diversity.
articles.authors: Marchal I
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Rainfall linked to malaria deaths in 19th-century Ontario
articles.title_original: Ecologies of Risk: Malaria and Settler Landscape Transformation in 19th-Century Ontario.
articles.authors: Cooke A, Brickley MB
articles.details: Historical ecological study examining how settler-driven environmental change shaped malaria transmission and mortality in 19th-century southern Ontario. Researchers analyzed 2702 deaths attributed to probable malaria from 1831 to 1900 using civil, cemetery, parish, and municipal records, coded for age, sex, occupation, region, and season, alongside Toronto temperature and rainfall data. Malaria mortality declined over time but persisted; children under 5 accounted for over half of deaths, and rural western Ontario had the highest mortality. A generalized additive model showed a strong nonlinear association between rainfall and malaria deaths (p < 0.001), while temperature was not significant. Authors conclude disease risk was structurally embedded in settler-transformed landscapes.

articles.headline: Commentary urges caution on CXCL10-high microglia in cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: CXCL10(high) Microglia in Cerebral Malaria: Toward Translational Validation.
articles.authors: Rattanapitoon NK, Gordon CN, Charoenphon N, Rattanapitoon SK
articles.details: Commentary responding to Wang et al., who identified CXCL10high TNFαhigh Ki67+ microglia as drivers of CD8+ T cell recruitment in experimental cerebral malaria (ECM). The authors propose reframing CXCL10high microglia as a candidate "neuroimmune endotype" requiring prespecified, testable validation in human disease rather than a fully validated taxonomy. They recommend single-nucleus and spatial transcriptomics of human postmortem tissue, paired plasma/CSF biomarker correlation, and mechanistic microglial antigen-presentation assays, cautioning against strong therapeutic inferences from ECM alone before reproducible human signatures and clinical correlations are established.

articles.headline: Social media sentiment on RTS,S malaria vaccine rollout in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Assessing RTS, S malaria vaccine rollout perception in Cameroon: Sentiment analysis from X and facebook using hugging face.
articles.authors: Cynthia AN, Njuaka M, Koomson N, Fogap NZ
articles.details: This study used natural language processing and machine learning (Hugging Face Transformer library) to analyze sentiment in Twitter (X) and Facebook posts about the RTS,S (Mosquirix) malaria vaccine rollout in Cameroon. Of the reactions analyzed, 42.0% were negative, 40.0% were positive, and 18.0% were neutral, showing a nearly even split between skeptical and supportive views. The authors conclude that significant public skepticism must be addressed through targeted, evidence-based communication on vaccine safety and efficacy, and that sentiment analysis can give policymakers real-time insight into attitudes toward immunization.

articles.headline: R21 vaccine protects against skin but not venous malaria sporozoites
articles.title_original: Malaria vaccine protection against intradermal or venous parasites: a randomized phase 2b human challenge trial.
articles.authors: Kapulu MC, Orenge F, Kimani D, Kibwana E, Kibet H, Mutahi M, Datoo MS, Bellamy D, Musembi J, Ngoto O, Rashid H, Akinyi S, Mwatasa MH, Nyamako L, Keter K, Gatheru R, Mutiso A, Musyoki J, Mwacharo J, Abebe Y, James ER, Billingsley PF, Ngetsa C, Mosobo M, Makale J, Tawa B, Wamae K, Ochola-Oyier LI, Lawrie A, Ramos-Lopez F, Roberts R, Richie TL, Sim BKL, Hoffman SL, Ewer KJ, Hill AVS, Hamaluba M, Bejon P
articles.details: In a phase 2b open, randomized controlled trial in Kenya, healthy volunteers received the R21/Matrix-M vaccine (n=38), ME-TRAP-based vaccines (n=24), or control (n=18), and 37 underwent controlled human malaria infection by intradermal (ID) or direct venous injection (DVI) of P. falciparum sporozoites with PCR monitoring. After ID challenge, 7/8 (88%) controls and 11/12 (92%) ME-TRAP volunteers met treatment criteria for parasitemia, but none of 12 R21 volunteers did; however, 5/5 R21 volunteers given DVI sporozoites met the endpoint (P<0.0005 by log rank). No serious adverse events occurred. The authors conclude R21/Matrix-M was highly protective against ID but not DVI sporozoites, and that efficacy correlates should be assessed by separate ID and DVI challenges.

articles.headline: Audit of malaria treatment appropriateness by WHO severity criteria
articles.title_original: Appropriateness of malaria treatment according to World Health Organization criteria for severe malaria in a non-endemic country.
articles.authors: Pérez-Jacoiste Asín MA, Navarro Flores A, Alonso Crespo A, Pérez-Ayala A, Losada Galván I, de Dios B, Hernández-Jiménez P, Gil-Mosquera M, Jiménez-Morillas F, Lumbreras C, Lizasoain M, Herrero-Martínez JM
articles.details: This single-centre retrospective study at Hospital 12 de Octubre (Madrid) reviewed all symptomatic malaria patients over 16 years old from January 2015 to December 2023, classifying severity by WHO criteria and assessing treatment adequacy (parenteral artesunate for severe; oral artemisinin combination or atovaquone/proguanil for non-severe). Among 64 patients (median age 39.5 years, 75% Visiting Friends and Relatives), malaria was severe in 15 (23.4%), and treatment matched severity in 46 (71.8%). Three severe cases received oral treatment (later found misclassified) and 15 non-severe cases received parenteral artesunate, with the main reason for artesunate overuse being thrombocytopenia (median platelet count 33,000/mm3). The authors conclude treatment was mostly adequate and that thrombocytopenia, not a WHO severity criterion, drove artesunate overuse.

articles.headline: Mutagenesis screen finds genes controlling malaria parasite sexual development
articles.title_original: Large-Scale Mutagenesis Screening for Genetic Determinants of Plasmodium falciparum Sexual Development.
articles.authors: Aringbangba O, Pires CV, Chawla J, Prakash P, Sollelis L, Marti M, Adams JH
articles.details: This methods paper describes a scalable forward genetic screen using single-insertion mutants generated by random piggyBac mutagenesis to identify genes regulating P. falciparum sexual development (gametocytes), the stage capable of surviving in the mosquito vector. The approach identifies genes essential for asexual parasite survival or tolerance to in vivo phenotypes such as febrile temperature, antimalarial drugs, and oxidative stress, and was extended to categorize genes by their effect on gametocyte production as hypo-producers (reduced) or hyper-producers (increased). The authors present this large-scale phenotypic screen methodology as a tool to identify essential gametocyte genes for transmission-blocking vaccines and drugs.

articles.headline: Competitive ELISA predicts transmission-blocking activity of Pfs230D1 vaccine
articles.title_original: Epitope-specific competitive ELISA predicts malaria transmission-blocking vaccine Pfs230D1 activity measured in standard membrane feeding assay.
articles.authors: Meehan CA, Cowles MV, Morrison RD, Yi Y, Gu J, Hume JC, Peyton MP, Sagara I, Healy SA, Renn JP, Duffy PE
articles.details: Researchers developed an epitope-specific competitive ELISA platform (P230Compete) for the malaria transmission-blocking vaccine candidate Pfs230D1, using single-chain variable fragments against epitopes recognized by high-activity human monoclonal antibodies, to predict transmission-reducing activity (TRA) measured by the standard membrane feeding assay (SMFA). They tested 171 serum samples from two postvaccination time points in a phase I Pfs230D1-EPA/AS01 trial, measuring total IgG (EUF), IgG subclasses (IgG1F, IgG3F, IgG4F), and bound complement C1q (C1qF). EUF and IgG1F strongly correlated with and predicted TRA ≥80% (AUC 0.81 for both after dose 3; 0.80 and 0.76 after dose 4), with combined EUF+IgG1F performing better. The authors conclude P230Compete is a promising proxy to replace SMFA in late-stage Pfs230D1 trials.

articles.headline: Plasmodium yoelii lethal vs non-lethal strains shape malaria immune outcomes
articles.title_original: Immune Modulation by Plasmodium yoelii: Insights From Lethal and Non-Lethal Strains.
articles.authors: Lim SYM
articles.details: This review uses the Plasmodium yoelii model—comparing lethal 17XL and non-lethal 17XNL strains—to examine how host immune responses determine malaria severity. It reports that 17XL infections trigger excessive pro-inflammatory cytokine release and immune exhaustion, while 17XNL infections sustain regulated Th1/Th2 responses that enable parasite control and survival. Pathways involving MIF, TLR7 signalling, and immune checkpoints (PD-1, LAG-3) distinguish the strains. The authors conclude that malaria severity reflects the timing and resolution of host immune responses rather than parasite load, and highlight humanised models, single-cell profiling, and immunomodulatory interventions for future therapeutic and vaccine strategies.

articles.headline: Review of barriers to malaria vaccine rollout in Africa
articles.title_original: Challenges to malaria vaccine availability and successful implementation of malaria vaccine program in Africa.
articles.authors: Ojo RJ, Igbayilola YD, Gyebi GA
articles.details: This review draws on academic and health databases to identify obstacles to malaria vaccine availability and program implementation in Africa. It finds supply constraints to be the major factor, as current manufacturing capacity is insufficient to meet demand across endemic regions, compounded by limited national health budgets affecting acquisition, transport, storage, and immunization activities. Additional barriers include low confidence in healthcare systems, misinformation and conspiracy theories about vaccine efficacy and safety, cold chain requirements, multiple-dose schedules, shortages of skilled health workers, weak pharmacovigilance, and inequitable allocation. The authors call for scaled-up manufacturing, stable funding, stronger health systems, and community trust-building.

articles.headline: Malaria burden among military recruits in Niger: 11% prevalence
articles.title_original: Burden of malaria in young adults in Niger: A prospective study in a military setting.
articles.authors: Aminou YY, Hamidou B, Djedanem M, Ramatoulaye HL, Moustapha WA, Testa J, Jambou R
articles.details: This prospective cohort study monitored 2840 military recruits (median age 27 years; 91.5% male, aged 17–55) across three military health centers in Niger from September to December 2023, during two 17-day periods. Among 3193 recorded consultations, respiratory infections predominated in Zinder and digestive disorders in Agadez. There were 353 confirmed malaria cases (prevalence 11%), with Niamey showing the highest attack rate. Of patients, 74.5% were anemic, 44% were hospitalized, and 55.8% were temporarily unfit for duty; 82% reported using mosquito nets. The study estimates nearly 2000 workdays lost annually in this group (potentially 12 million days nationally), highlighting the need for integrated control.

articles.headline: Systematic review: trace elements, immunity, and malaria severity
articles.title_original: Essential Trace Elements and Their Impact on Immune Response and Disease Severity in Malaria Infection: A Systematic Review.
articles.authors: Hanifian H, Nateghpour M, Naderi M
articles.details: This PRISMA-guided systematic review synthesized evidence on serum and plasma concentrations of essential trace elements (iron, zinc, copper, magnesium) in malaria-infected humans and experimental models, searching PubMed, Web of Science, Scopus, and Google Scholar for studies published from January 2010 to November 2025, with CASP quality appraisal. Malaria infection was consistently associated with reduced iron, zinc, and magnesium, while copper showed variable trends, often elevated in acute infection reflecting inflammation. These alterations were linked to dysregulated cytokine production (notably increased TNF-α and IL-10), higher parasitemia, and worse clinical outcomes. Zinc supplementation improved micronutrient status but had limited impact on malaria incidence. The authors suggest combining targeted micronutrient interventions with antimalarial therapy.

articles.headline: First artemisinin-resistant imported P. falciparum case detected in Italy
articles.title_original: Recrudescence of Plasmodium falciparum malaria imported from Sub-Saharan Africa: a report of two cases and the first detection of artemisinin resistance in Italy.
articles.authors: L'Episcopia M, Poloni A, Corbellino M, Boccolini D, Gigantiello A, Covizzi A, Bernasconi D, Colombo V, Severini C, Antinori S
articles.details: This case report describes two Italian travelers from sub-Saharan Africa with severe Plasmodium falciparum malaria that recrudesced after standard treatment with intravenous artesunate and oral dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine. Molecular characterization of resistance-associated genes (Pfk13, Pfcrt, Pfmdr1, Pfdhfr, Pfdhps, PfCytB) was performed on blood samples. In one case, the validated artemisinin partial-resistance marker R561H in Pfkelch13 was identified. In the second patient, no Pfkelch13 mutations were detected, and recrudescence was probably due to low blood drug concentration administered by nasogastric tube, though Pfkelch13-independent pathways or reduced artemisinin susceptibility could not be ruled out. The authors stress continuous surveillance and adapting treatment protocols.

articles.headline: Commentary: tools to beat malaria exist, but is there the will?
articles.title_original: We have the means to beat malaria, do we have the will?
articles.authors: The Lancet Microbe
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: PCR finds P. falciparum infections missed by RDTs and microscopy
articles.title_original: Molecular Detection of Plasmodium falciparum Infections Missed by Microscopy and HRP2-Based RDTs in the Dominican Republic.
articles.authors: Cabrera Federo C, Cuevas F, Gonçalves AM, Freitas B, Miguel I, Baptista V, Conte M, Santos-Pereira A, Aquino E, Disla M, Tabata A, Osório NS, Cruz M, Veiga MI
articles.details: In the Dominican Republic, a WHO E2025 elimination-target country, researchers re-examined 1095 suspected malaria cases initially classified as negative by HRP2-based rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) and microscopy. Nucleic acids extracted from used RDT cassettes were screened by real-time PCR for Plasmodium spp., with pfhrp2 deletion analysis on P. falciparum positives. Extraction succeeded in 969/1095 (88.5%) samples, and P. falciparum DNA was detected in 18/969 (1.9%); 16.7% (3/18) of these carried pfhrp2 deletions. The authors conclude that more sensitive diagnostics are needed to sustain elimination efforts.

articles.headline: Plasmodium-protein nanoparticle vaccine gives full protection in mice
articles.title_original: A Plasmodium-derived nanoparticle vaccine elicits sterile protection against malaria in mice.
articles.authors: Shi D, Ma R, Gupta R, Dickey TH, Patel PN, Salinas ND, Tang WK, Queen A, Singleton M, Delbe N, Conteh S, Lambert LE, Duffy PE, Tolia NH
articles.details: Researchers engineered a P. falciparum pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP) synthase into a nanoparticle displaying a designed P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein (CSP) and the P. vivax cell-traversal protein CelTOS, creating a multivalent malaria vaccine. The vaccine elicited high anti-CSP and anti-CelTOS antibody titres, and three doses provided complete sterile protection against malaria in a mouse model. Cryo-electron microscopy resolved the PLP nanoparticle structure at 2.95 Å. Because PLP synthase has no identifiable human ortholog, the authors note low risk of autoimmunity or pre-existing immunity, establishing it as a vaccine platform.

articles.headline: One-step injectable artesunate faster and cheaper for severe malaria in children
articles.title_original: Single-step versus conventional injectable artesunate for severe malaria in children: an open label, non-inferiority randomized clinical trial, Democratic Republic of the Congo and United Republic of Tanzania.
articles.authors: Gesase S, Onyamboko M, Fanello C, Abdul O, Kayembe DK, Bakomba SB, Minja DT, Nzambiwishe BK, Ekombolo PE, Malabeja A, Mbwana JR, Deen J, Mtove G, Adhikari B, Mapondela M, Taya C, Mutinda B, Waithira N, Lusingu JP, von Seidlein L, Mukaka M, Dondorp AM, Peto TJ
articles.details: An open-label randomized trial at hospitals in Kinshasa (DRC) and Korogwe (Tanzania) enrolled 200 patients aged 3 months to 16 years with severe malaria, randomized 1:1 to a new one-step injectable artesunate formulation or the conventional two-step formulation, with 4 weeks of follow-up. Mean administration time was 2 min 22 s versus 3 min 41 s (P<0.0001), and mean syringe/needle cost was US$0.53 versus US$0.84 (P=0.0001). Parasite clearance half-lives were 2.1 h versus 2.0 h (P=0.173), with one severe adverse event in each arm (P=1.000). The authors conclude the one-step formulation is quicker and cheaper with equivalent safety and efficacy.

articles.headline: Chloroquine-resistant P. vivax pooled at about 7% in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Chloroquine-resistant Plasmodium vivax malaria in Ethiopia: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Wondmagegn YM, Setegn A, Girmay G, Assefa M, Getnet B, Cherkos T, Abebe W, Dessie N, Derso A, Yiheyis Z, Adugna A, Misganaw T, Abere A, Tamir M, Belayneh M, Amare A, Ashagre A, Malede B, Amare GA
articles.details: This PRISMA-guided systematic review and meta-analysis (PROSPERO CRD42024564745) synthesized evidence on chloroquine-resistant Plasmodium vivax in Ethiopia, where P. vivax accounts for about 40% of malaria cases and chloroquine is first-line treatment. Thirteen studies with 2,190 participants gave a pooled resistance prevalence of 7.32% (95% CI: 2.55-12.08; I²=95.1%, p<0.001). Prevalence was higher during the 2003-2009 period (10.70%), in the Oromia region (9.58%), and with 42-day follow-up (17.91%). The authors conclude resistance is present and clinically significant, underscoring the need for ongoing surveillance and alternative therapies.

articles.headline: Scoping review of P. vivax circumsporozoite protein vaccines in mice
articles.title_original: Plasmodium vivax circumsporozoite protein and vaccine strategies in murine models: A scoping review.
articles.authors: Sampaio MP, Cerilo-Filho M, Almeida MNG, Matos MAT, Silva AA, Fernando DJV, Bras RP, Baptista ARS, de Castro TX, Machado RLD
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Antimalarial monoclonal antibodies show strong protection and transmission-blocking
articles.title_original: Efficacy and safety of Monoclonal Antibodies in Malaria Protection and Prevention of Transmission: A Systematic Review.
articles.authors: Verma K, Singhal R, Anvikar A, Bharti PK, Nitika N
articles.details: A PRISMA systematic review of seven Phase I/II trials (n=776) evaluating antimalarial monoclonal antibodies CIS43LS, L9LS, MAM01, and TB31F. Against P. falciparum, CIS43LS achieved 75–88% protective efficacy at doses ≥10 mg/kg intravenous, and L9LS 66–77% via subcutaneous administration, both lasting up to 6 months with only grade 1–2 adverse events. Half-lives were extended (CIS43LS 56–80 days; L9LS 46.1 days; MAM01 71.1 days). For MAM01, 18 of 22 intervention participants developed parasitemia; 1 at 5 mg/kg subcutaneous and 3 at 40 mg/kg intravenous were protected. TB31F showed transmission-reducing activity exceeding 80% at doses ≥1 mg/kg, sustained up to 160 days. Authors conclude mAbs are promising scalable prevention tools needing larger trials.

articles.headline: Falcilysin proposed as new antimalarial drug target
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum falcilysin as an emerging potential drug target for antimalarial drug discovery.
articles.authors: Dalal A, Malik J, Singh P, Rathi B
articles.details: This review presents Plasmodium falciparum falcilysin (FLN), a conserved zinc metalloprotease essential for parasite survival via hemoglobin degradation and apicoplast transit peptide processing, as an emerging antimalarial drug target. Recent work identified a druggable hydrophobic allosteric pocket and clarified FLN's dynamic conformations, and multiple small-molecule classes showed potent inhibition in biochemical, cellular, and in vivo models. Key challenges include dual-organelle localization, selectivity over human homologs, and clinical translation; authors suggest expanding chemical diversity, covalent and PROTAC modalities, multitarget strategies, and cryo-EM and AI-driven modeling.

articles.headline: Gene-drive mosquitoes block patient-derived malaria parasites in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Gene-drive-capable mosquitoes suppress patient-derived malaria in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Habtewold T, Lwetoijera DW, Hoermann A, Mashauri R, Matwewe F, Mwanga R, Kweyamba P, Maganga G, Magani BP, Mtama R, Mahonje MA, Tambwe MM, Tarimo F, Chennuri PR, Cai JA, Del Corsano G, Capriotti P, Sasse P, Moore J, Hudson D, Manjurano A, Tarimo B, Vlachou D, Moore S, Windbichler N, Christophides GK
articles.details: Researchers engineered Anopheles gambiae in Tanzania to express two exogenous antimicrobial peptides and equipped them with non-autonomous gene drive capability. The transgenic strain robustly inhibited sporogonic development of genetically diverse P. falciparum isolates obtained from naturally infected children. The modifications were efficiently inherited by progeny when Cas9 endonuclease was supplied by another locally engineered strain. Authors conclude this locally tailored approach brings gene drive technology a critical step closer to application for malaria eradication.

articles.headline: Duffy gene diversity and emerging P. vivax in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Genetic dynamics of the Duffy antigen receptor for chemokines gene and Plasmodium vivax circulation within sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Adeloye F, Lambe KO, Oboh JA, Abdulsalam H, Enyinnnaya C, Nwuba R, Ogundahunsi O, D'Alessandro U, Amambua-Ngwa A, Meremikwu MM, Hughes GL, Heinz E, Oboh MA
articles.details: This population-genetics study analyzed 223 DARC (Duffy antigen receptor for chemokines) gene sequences retrieved from the NCBI database across Nigeria, Cameroon, Ethiopia, Madagascar, and South Africa, examining gene dynamics relative to emerging P. vivax infections in a historically P. falciparum–endemic region. South Africa showed the most haplotype and nucleotide diversity, with various selection pressures in Western Africa and the Central African Republic. Population structure clustered Cameroon, Nigeria, and Ethiopia (suggesting shared ancestry), while South Africa and Madagascar formed a distinct lineage. Authors conclude there is minimal genetic diversity within sSA with evidence of selection, and note detection of P. vivax among Duffy-null individuals as a public health concern.

articles.headline: P. cynomolgi Duffy binding proteins fail to bind human red cells
articles.title_original: Characterisation of erythrocyte binding activity of Plasmodium cynomolgi duffy binding protein haplotypes from Macaca fascicularis in Peninsular Malaysia.
articles.authors: Latif ENM, Adlan ASA, Lau YL, Cheong FW
articles.details: This in vitro study characterized erythrocyte binding by two Plasmodium cynomolgi Duffy binding proteins, PcDBP1(II) and PcDBP2(II), whose two haplotype groups (groups 1 and 2) have been reported in Peninsular Malaysia. Four isolates were tested per protein: group 1 (a recent field isolate and the old reference Strain B) and group 2 (a recent field isolate and the old reference Berok), each cloned into the pDisplay-AcGFP1 vector, transfected into COS-7 cells, and incubated with human erythrocytes/reticulocytes and macaque erythrocytes at 1% haematocrit, with binding scored by counting rosette formations. Group 1 isolates differed significantly (P<0.05) in PcDBP1(II) binding to macaque erythrocytes, the recent isolate binding more strongly than the old one, while PcDBP2(II) showed no such difference. Neither protein bound human erythrocytes or reticulocytes, suggesting additional parasite-host interactions may be required for human erythrocyte or reticulocyte invasion.

articles.headline: PCR detects far more malaria than microscopy or RDT in Papua
articles.title_original: Performance comparison of rapid diagnostic test, microscopy, and polymerase chain reaction for Malaria detection in high-transmission area, Keerom Regency, Papua Province, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Rohkmad MF, Sandy S, Widiyanti M, Adiningsih S, Kesuma AP, Maladan Y, Anggraeni YM, Ramadhani T, Ipa M, Hidajat MC, Kawulur H, Kurniawan CM, Kopo AD, Dharmayanti NI, Garjito TA, Oktavian A, Hendarwan H
articles.details: This study compared rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs), microscopy, and PCR for malaria detection among 234 participants suspected of uncomplicated malaria in the high-transmission area of Keerom Regency, Papua Province, Indonesia. PCR identified 42.3% of cases, versus 26.5% by microscopy and 22.6% by RDTs, with PCR detecting many cases missed by the other methods, particularly in low-parasitemia situations. Sensitivity and specificity of microscopy and RDTs were assessed against PCR as the reference standard, revealing significant discrepancies. The authors conclude enhanced diagnostic strategies are needed to improve case detection and support elimination efforts.

articles.headline: Climate warming raises avian malaria prevalence in wild birds
articles.title_original: Hidden effects of avian malaria under climate warming.
articles.authors: Braga ÉM, Vieira LMC
articles.details: This commentary discusses a long-term study by Theodosopoulos et al. on a wild bird population in a temperate region of Northern Europe, which found a significant increase in avian malaria parasite prevalence under a climate-warming scenario. The piece frames disruption of the temperature-humidity balance as a threat to ecosystems and infectious disease spread.

articles.headline: NAT screening finds rare asymptomatic malaria in Amazon blood donors
articles.title_original: Improving blood safety: NAT-based detection of Plasmodium spp. in blood donors in endemic areas of Brazil.
articles.authors: Gomes de Almeida AC, Shuan Laco AC, Rodrigues MG, Godinho de Siqueira VG, de Moura Brito FB, Gato CM, Mota Cordeiro JS, Hipólito Carneiro JM, Lopes Albuquerque SR, Chaves YO, de Fátima Ferreira da Cruz M, Baptista PA, Addas-Carvalho M, Tarragô AM, de Melo GC
articles.details: This retrospective observational study used HemoSys database records to describe malaria positivity by the NAT PLUS HIV/HBV/HCV/Malaria assay (Bio-Manguinhos) among blood donors at a reference blood center in the Brazilian Amazon. Among 67,114 donors tested (median age 35, IQR 25-43; 65.1% male), malaria-related factors accounted for 2.6% of deferrals, and NAT PLUS positivity was 0.01% (8/67,114), all reactive donors being asymptomatic at screening. Most positive cases (6/8) occurred during the low-transmission rainy season, and one donor (12.5%) later developed symptomatic P. falciparum malaria confirmed by thick blood smear. The authors highlight the need for standardized protocols and molecular testing to identify at-risk donors.

articles.headline: Hypofuran B analogs show moderate activity against P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Synthesis and In Vitro Activity of Hypofuran B and Analogs Against Plasmodium Falciparum and Trypanosoma Cruzi.
articles.authors: Franco CA, Varejão JOS, Ceravolo IP, Machado VM, Krettli AU, de Melo Resende D, Murta SMF, Martins FT, Pilau EJ, Montes VR, Kohlhoff M, Varejão EVV
articles.details: This study reports the synthesis and in vitro biological evaluation of hypofuran B and a series of analogs against Plasmodium falciparum and Trypanosoma cruzi, made via crossed aldol condensation of phenylacetaldehyde and furfural derivatives (yields 20-83%, E/Z ratios 89:11 to 98:2). The most active analogs showed IC50 values of 5.35-10.35 µg/mL, with cytotoxicity assessed in L929 cells; the most promising compound had a CC50 above 400 µg/mL, indicating lower cytotoxicity than chloroquine, and a clear structure-activity-toxicity relationship was observed for P. falciparum. In silico predictions supported favorable drug-like profiles, leading the authors to highlight these drynaran derivatives as promising antimalarial leads.

articles.headline: Malaria PCR screening in transplant donors and sickle cell recipients
articles.title_original: Malaria Screening and Treatment in Hematopoietic Cell Transplant Donors and Sickle Cell Disease Candidates/Recipients: A Case Series Using Malaria Polymerase Chain Reaction Testing and a Literature Review.
articles.authors: Czech MM, Das S, Limerick E, Fitzhugh C, Hsieh M, Cuellar-Rodriguez J
articles.details: A single-center retrospective review examined malaria screening and outcomes in sickle cell disease patients undergoing allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplant (HCT) and their related donors, alongside a literature review. Among 57 HCT donors tested, three asymptomatic cases were found; two were detected before donation by smear and PCR, while one screened by smear alone was diagnosed retrospectively after transmitting malaria to the recipient, with retrospective PCR of the cell product positive. Among 52 recipients tested, two developed peri-HCT malaria (one pre-HCT, one donor-derived). The literature review identified 10 detailed HCT malaria cases. Authors conclude malaria PCR offers greater sensitivity and may prevent unnecessary donor deferrals.

articles.headline: Bone marrow microscopy and PCR diagnose occult malaria case
articles.title_original: Out of Sight, Should not be out of mind: A perplexing case of Malaria.
articles.authors: Saxena S, Das R, Khurana S, Mirza SA, Joshi KD, Kaur H
articles.details: A case report of a 30-year-old male presenting with high-grade fever, chills, and vomiting; on admission he was afebrile, pale, with progressive splenomegaly. Multiple peripheral blood smears and malaria rapid diagnostic tests were negative, but bone marrow examination revealed rare red blood cells with Plasmodium spp., confirmed by PCR from the marrow aspirate slide. He fully recovered after treatment with artemether-lumefantrine and primaquine. The authors emphasize the value of bone marrow microscopy and PCR for diagnosing malaria in patients with unexplained cytopenias and splenomegaly.

articles.headline: Could IPTp with SP reduce small vulnerable newborns beyond malaria?
articles.title_original: Beyond malaria: can intermittent preventive treatment with sulphadoxine-pyrimethamine reduce the number of small vulnerable newborns globally?
articles.authors: Unger HW, Ataide R, Roh ME, Rahman A, Price RN, van Eijk AM, Dorsey G, Ter Kuile FO, Rogerson SJ
articles.details: This perspective discusses whether intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy with sulphadoxine-pyrimethamine (IPTp-SP), originally designed to protect pregnant women from malaria, could reduce small vulnerable newborns (SVNs) globally. The authors note SP appears to retain efficacy against low birthweight even when Plasmodium infection is absent or the parasite is highly resistant to SP, possibly via antimicrobial activity against maternal genitourinary and gut infections, effects on gut physiology reversing environmental enteric dysfunction, or anti-inflammatory actions. They conclude mechanistic studies and placebo-controlled randomised trials of IPTp-SP in non-malarious settings are needed to confirm its potential for reducing SVNs.

articles.headline: Corrigendum: alpha-gal immune responses and R21/MM vaccine testing
articles.title_original: Corrigendum to "Naturally acquired immune responses to alpha-gal in malaria endemic settings and pre-clinical efficacy testing with R21/MM" [Vaccine 68 (2025) 127897].
articles.authors: Mukhopadhyay ES, Bellamy DG, Tinto H, Hamaluba M, Truby A, Salman AM, Hill AVS
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Mosquito blood-meal preferences and avian malaria in Balkans
articles.title_original: Feeding Preferences of Mosquitoes (Culicidae) from the Eastern Balkans and Their Role in Transmission of Avian Malaria.
articles.authors: Bobeva A, Marinov MP, Klayn S, Ilieva M, Dimitrov D, Zehtindjiev P
articles.details: Mosquitoes were collected between April and October across two years (2017 and 2019) in a deciduous forest and reedbeds to study feeding preferences and avian haemosporidian transmission. Vertebrate hosts were identified in 75 engorged individuals: 44 blood meals (58.7%) were of avian origin (25 bird species), 30 (40.0%) from mammals (13 species), and one (1.3%) from a reptile. Culex pipiens fed on the most bird hosts, Culex modestus on the greatest variety of sources, while Aedes vexans, Anopheles maculipennis, and Anopheles hyrcanus were predominantly mammophilic. Avian haemosporidian parasites were found in 11 abdominal (11.6%) and four head-thorax (4.2%) samples; the most prevalent lineage in abdomens was SGS1 (Plasmodium relictum, n=4).

articles.headline: Review: Amazon deforestation drives persistent malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Deforestation, Mosquito Ecology, and Malaria Elimination in the Amazon Region.
articles.authors: Grillet ME, Sallum MAM, Conn JE
articles.details: This review examines how degradation and deforestation of the Amazon rainforest acts as a main environmental driver of persistent malaria epidemics in the region. It focuses on how forest change alters anopheline vector ecology—including mosquito abundance and the rate of human-vector contact—as a mediator between environmental transformation and malaria risk, and discusses the growing role of regional zoonotic malaria. The authors argue that sustaining malaria elimination in the Americas requires better understanding of mosquito adaptation to anthropogenic pressures on the forest.

articles.headline: RTS,S/AS01E vaccine cuts malaria burden in real-world phase 4 study
articles.title_original: Effectiveness of the RTS,S/AS01(E) malaria vaccine in a real-world setting over 1 year of follow-up after the three-dose primary schedule: an interim analysis of a phase 4 study in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi.
articles.authors: Ndeketa L, Haine V, Debois M, Asante KP, Agyapong PD, Kaali S, Devadiga R, Harrison SBE, Boahen O, French N, Kayan K, Ogutu B, Adeniji E, Kariuki S, Owusu-Agyei S, Olewe F, Jere TM, Maleta K, Mategula D, Mzanga P, Phiri VK, Ansah PO, Orimbo J, Ansah NA, Orsini M, Ong'echa JM, Oduro AR, Sifuna PM, Azongo DK, Otieno W, Bangre O, Kaburise MB, Ababio LO, Oyieko JN, Sing'oei V, Amoit SK, Nyangulu W, Schuerman L, Awuni D, Ochieng BO, Onyango I, Odera-Ojwang P, Oguk EA, Mendoza YG, Cherop RY, Okoth GO, Cravcenco C, Chipatala R, Roman F, Oneko M, Savic M
articles.details: EPI-MAL-003 was a phase 4 disease-surveillance study with prospective cohort event monitoring at 12 sites in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi, enrolling children younger than 18 months in vaccine-exposed and unexposed clusters. Comparing vaccinated children in exposed clusters with unvaccinated children in unexposed clusters over 1 year after three RTS,S/AS01E doses, country-adjusted incidence rate ratios were 0·70 (95% CI 0·67-0·73) for any malaria, 0·42 (0·30-0·60) for severe malaria, 0·64 (0·56-0·72) for malaria-related hospitalisations, 0·79 (0·74-0·84) for all-cause hospitalisations, and 0·83 (0·64-1·09; p=0·18) for all-cause mortality; the adjusted odds ratio for anaemia among hospitalised children was 0·81 (0·73-0·90). Authors conclude vaccination significantly reduced malaria burden, supporting continued use.

articles.headline: Review urges serology to support malaria elimination in India
articles.title_original: India should consider sero-epidemiology as an additional tool to bolster malaria elimination.
articles.authors: Rahi M, Sharma R, Thiruvengadam K, Perumalsamy N
articles.details: This review argues that India should add malaria sero-epidemiology to its elimination toolkit alongside rapid diagnostic tests and microscopy, citing other countries that have integrated serology. It notes India accounts for over half of Southeast Asia's malaria burden, mainly P. falciparum and P. vivax, and that cases rose from 161,753 (2021) to 176,522 (2022) and 255,500 (2024), about a 58% increase since 2021. The authors describe serology's potential to determine true burden, verify elimination, assess transmission in high-risk groups, detect low-density or dormant infections, and measure intervention impact, particularly in low-transmission settings.

articles.headline: Plant extract shows antiplasmodial and bone-protective effects in malaria rats
articles.title_original: Osteoprotective Effects and In Silico Profiling of Parinari curatellifolia Planch. Ex Benth. (Chrysobalanaceae) Extract on Acute Plasmodium berghei-Compromised Rat Bone.
articles.authors: Jouonzo J, Kamkumo RG, Ngueguim FT, Efange NM, Tsila SS, Ambamba BDA, Ngondi JL, Ayong L, Lenta BN, Sewald N, Dimo T
articles.details: This study tested an aqueous Parinari curatellifolia leaf-and-bark mixture extract for antiplasmodial and osteoprotective effects. In vitro antiplasmodial activity was assessed against P. falciparum 3D7 and Dd2; LC-MS characterized 12 compounds; and in vivo effects were evaluated using a Plasmodium berghei ANKA-induced malaria and bone disorder model, with hematological, biochemical, immunological, and histopathological assessment, plus molecular docking on Pf20S, TyrRS, MyD88, and RANKL. The extract showed in vitro antiplasmodial activity, reduced parasitemia (p<0.001), improved hematological parameters, and corrected malaria-induced bone damage by increasing bone osteocalcin, TGF-β1, ALP activity, and mineralization (p<0.001) while reversing inflammation. The authors conclude it acts as an antiplasmodial, osteoprotective, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant agent.

articles.headline: Hospital study: malaria found in 2% of febrile patients in Delhi
articles.title_original: Malaria epidemiology, clinical characteristics, and concurrent infections in Delhi, India: A hospital-based study.
articles.authors: Hawadak J, Goel A, Matlani M, Haider M, Kojom Foko LP, Singh V
articles.details: This longitudinal hospital-based study screened 4259 febrile patients at a Delhi government hospital using rapid malaria antigen testing and light microscopy, with serological tests for concurrent infections. Malaria was confirmed in 87 patients (2.04%), caused by P. vivax and P. falciparum, with common presentations including chills (80.46%), hepatosplenomegaly (64.37%), myalgia (56.32%), generalised body ache (54.02%), and jaundice/icterus (51.72%); severe anaemia was the most frequent severe form. Nearly 45% of malaria cases were co-infected with dengue, chikungunya, or Salmonella typhi. The July–September climatic period (aOR=2.18; p<0.05) and male sex (aOR=1.42; p<0.05) were risk factors for malaria infection.

articles.headline: Drug-resistance markers in P. falciparum from Kumasi, Ghana, 2023
articles.title_original: Molecular Markers of Antimalarial Drug Resistance in Plasmodium falciparum From Kumasi, Ghana, 2023.
articles.authors: Kegya AD, Oppong E, Darko E, Boateng KA, Phillips RO, Heinemann M, Ramharter M, Igiraneza C, Ndoli JM, Mockenhaupt FP, van Loon W
articles.details: This molecular surveillance study genotyped Plasmodium falciparum isolates from 200 falciparum malaria patients in Kumasi, Ghana, collected in 2023, examining mutations in pfk13, pfcoronin (P76S), pfmdr1 (N86Y, Y184F, Y1246D), and pfaat1 (S258L). Non-synonymous pfk13 mutations occurred in 2.2% of isolates, including the candidate artemisinin partial-resistance marker P527H plus S522C, C532S, and I590V. Pfcoronin P76S was present in 29.7% and associated with lower parasite density, the pfmdr1 NFD pattern predominated (suggesting impaired lumefantrine sensitivity), and pfaat1 S258L was common at 64.7%. Markers were not associated with each other or with day-3 parasite positivity. Authors conclude ACT efficacy remains high but continuous molecular monitoring is needed.

articles.headline: Systematic review: cannabis-derived compounds show antimalarial activity in preclinical studies
articles.title_original: Cannabis-Derived Compounds Against Plasmodium sp.: A Systematic Review of Preclinical Studies.
articles.authors: de Mendonça Lima T, de Sena LWP, de Sousa ACC, de Freitas GRM, Rotta I, Visacri MB
articles.details: This review of preclinical in vitro and in vivo studies evaluated the antimalarial activity and toxicity of cannabis-derived compounds against Plasmodium sp., searching Web of Science, PubMed, Scopus, and LILACS through December 2024, with two reviewers independently performing study selection, data extraction, and methodological assessment. Eight studies published between 2001 and 2022 were included, most conducted in North America (n=5). In vitro IC50 values ranged from 0.16 to 4.1 μg/mL, indicating mild to high activity, and all in vivo studies reported positive effects including moderate antimalarial activity and disease tolerance. The toxicity profile was not extensively studied, and most studies had an unknown or unclear risk of bias; the authors call for more comprehensive study design and further validation, especially regarding toxicity.

articles.headline: 4-nitrobenzoate boosts atovaquone efficacy by blocking parasite ubiquinone synthesis
articles.title_original: 4-nitrobenzoate inhibits 4-hydroxybenzoate polyprenyltransferase in malaria parasites and enhances atovaquone efficacy.
articles.authors: Verdaguer IB, Santos MF, Filho MM, Castro GO, Hernández A, Peres MA, Katzin AM, Crispim M
articles.details: This laboratory study investigated 4-nitrobenzoate (4-NB) as an inhibitor of 4-hydroxybenzoate polyprenyltransferase (4-HPT; PfCOQ2 gene), an enzyme in ubiquinone biosynthesis essential for the Plasmodium falciparum electron transport chain. The authors show that 4-NB inhibits Pf4-HPT, enhances atovaquone efficacy and selectivity, and preserves proguanil synergy; in Saccharomyces cerevisiae expressing PfCOQ2, 4-NB inhibited ubiquinone biosynthesis, and in Plasmodium berghei-infected mice 4-NB improved atovaquone efficacy. Structure-activity studies with 4-HB analogs further defined chemical features for potentiation. The authors conclude PfCOQ2 is a druggable target to boost atovaquone-based combination therapy and address emerging atovaquone/proguanil resistance.

articles.headline: P. falciparum protein GEP1 confirmed as transmission-blocking drug target
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum gametogenesis essential protein 1 (GEP1) is a transmission-blocking target.
articles.authors: Huppertz F, Caturelli MS, Lehmann LS, Kurth F, Maier AG, Matuschewski K
articles.details: Researchers characterized gametogenesis essential protein 1 (GEP1) in Plasmodium falciparum using CRISPR-Cas9 to create PfGEP1 loss-of-function lines. These lines failed to undergo male or female gametogenesis even when stimulated by xanthurenic acid (XA) or a temperature drop, and the defect persisted despite treatment with the phosphodiesterase inhibitor Zaprinast. Analysis of field samples revealed two GEP1 SNPs, V241L and S263P, in 12% and 20% of 49 cases. The authors confirm GEP1's essential role in gamete activation, highlight an XA-independent function, and support its potential as a transmission-blocking drug target warranting targeted drug development.

articles.headline: New avian Plasmodium species described in Brazilian fire-eye bird
articles.title_original: A New Species of Plasmodium of the Subgenus Novyella Infecting White-Shouldered Fire-Eyes (Pyriglena leucoptera) (Aves: Thamnophilidae) in Brazil.
articles.authors: Alves LGM, Pereira PHO, Batista VL, Lopes LE, Braga ÉM
articles.details: This study described a new species of Plasmodium (subgenus Novyella) infecting a white-shouldered fire-eye (Pyriglena leucoptera) in Brazil. Molecular analysis associated the new species with lineage PYLEU01, closely related to Plasmodium (Novyella) homopolare with a genetic divergence of 4.18%, but distinguished by many mature amoeboid trophozoites and laterally located young meronts plus a smaller average number of merozoites in mature erythrocytic meronts. Its blood-stage morphology most resembles Plasmodium vaughani and Plasmodium rouxi but differs by predominantly 4 merozoites (atypical for P. vaughani) and 5-6 merozoites in some meronts (atypical for P. rouxi). Integrative analyses confirm it as a distinct Plasmodium species.

articles.headline: Volume electron microscopy maps Plasmodium ookinete development
articles.title_original: Cellular Hallmarks From Volume Electron Microscopy Reveal Developmental Progression of Plasmodium Ookinetes.
articles.authors: Darif N, Rheinnecker M, Hildenbrand K, Chookajorn T, Dorner LP, Hériché JK, Henriksson S, Funaya C, Hentzschel F, Sandblad L, Billker O, Schwab Y, Frischknecht F
articles.details: Using three complementary volume electron microscopy (vEM) techniques with cluster analysis of morphometric parameters, this study mapped the subcellular transformation of fertilized Plasmodium zygotes into motile ookinetes. The authors observed intimate mitochondrion-nucleus interactions, distinct microtubule arrangements, and elongated micronemes closely interacting with the apicoplast. They provide an open-access subcellular atlas of ookinete development intended to support mechanistic insights from reverse genetic studies and to serve as a framework for ultrastructural study of other parasite stages and developmental transitions.

articles.headline: Radiation-attenuated PfSPZ malaria vaccine fails to protect Gabonese children
articles.title_original: Safety, tolerability, and protective efficacy of a radiation-attenuated, whole sporozoite malaria vaccine in children in Gabon: a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled, phase 2 trial.
articles.authors: Agnandji ST, Bok J, Alabi A, Kabwende AL, Mbouna A, Bie J, Moukiti E, Lalremruata A, Esen M, Kreidenweiss A, Kc N, Sim BKL, Richie TL, Preston Church LW, McCall MBB, Hoffman SL, Kremsner PG, Mordmüller B
articles.details: In a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled phase 2 trial in children aged 1-12 years in Gabon, 200 children were allocated 2:1 to receive 9.0 × 10^5 PfSPZ Vaccine or saline placebo by direct venous inoculation on days 1, 8, and 29, with artemether-lumefantrine before the third dose; 192 received all three vaccinations. Systemic adverse events were similar between groups (25% vaccine vs 23% placebo), with no treatment-related deaths. P falciparum infection occurred in 19% (25/129) of vaccine versus 23% (14/63) of placebo recipients, giving an age-adjusted vaccine efficacy of 9% (95% CI -75 to 53; p=0.78). The vaccine was safe but did not prevent infection.

articles.headline: Malaria epidemiology in Meta department, Colombia
articles.title_original: Malaria in the department of Meta, eastern Colombia: 2017-2021.
articles.authors: Sánchez Lerma L, Rojas Gulloso A, Pavas Escobar NC, Bernal Otalora JC, Mahecha Bohórquez N, Angulo Ceballos O
articles.details: This study of malaria data from 2016 to 2021 in Meta in Colombia found a significant relationship between age category and acquiring malaria, with most cases occurring in Puerto Gaitán, Villavicencio, and Mapiripán. Although Meta is not part of the malaria endemic zone, its municipalities located near rivers have enhanced contact with the vector. The authors conclude that no eradication programme will succeed without the cooperation of its inhabitants.

articles.headline: Perspective on India-Africa collaboration for malaria elimination
articles.title_original: Can India-Africa synergies accelerate malaria elimination? A perspective on South-South Collaboration.
articles.authors: Gupta A, Gupta H
articles.details: This perspective article argues that South-South collaboration among low- and middle-income countries can accelerate malaria research and control, noting that African-led research receives only a small fraction of global R&D funding despite sub-Saharan Africa bearing the greatest malaria burden. Drawing on initiatives such as ANDI, the Elimination 8 (E8) group, and the India-Africa Health Sciences Platform, the authors describe how such partnerships can foster innovation, build local research capacity, improve diagnostics, and strengthen surveillance. They discuss barriers including limited funding and fragmented systems and propose regional investment and regulatory harmonization.

articles.headline: Artemether-lumefantrine underperforms other ACTs in Ugandan children
articles.title_original: Efficacies of artemether-lumefantrine, artesunate-amodiaquine, dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine, and artesunate-pyronaridine for the treatment of uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in children aged 6 months to 10 years in Uganda: a randomised, open-label, phase 4 clinical trial.
articles.authors: Kamya MR, Nankabirwa JI, Ebong C, Asua V, Kiggundu M, Orena S, Okitwi M, Tukwasibwe S, Agaba B, Kyabayinze D, Opigo J, Rutazana D, Binagwa B, Mugwanya E, Babirye S, Sebikaari G, Condo PM, Appiah G, Nsobya SL, Conrad MD, Rosenthal PJ, Moriarty LF, Yeka A
articles.details: In a randomised, open-label, phase 4 trial at three sites (Agago, Arua, Busia) in Uganda, 808 children aged 6 months to 10 years with uncomplicated P falciparum malaria received artemether-lumefantrine or a site-specific comparator (dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine, artesunate-amodiaquine, or artesunate-pyronaridine), with 42-day follow-up. Uncorrected adequate clinical and parasitological response for artemether-lumefantrine was low (51.8% in Arua, 51.8% in Busia, 79.4% in Agago) versus 98-99% for dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine and artesunate-amodiaquine and 73.7% for artesunate-pyronaridine. PCR-corrected 28-day efficacy for artemether-lumefantrine ranged 81.5-97.0%, lower than comparators (92.4-100%). Parasite clearance half-lives were prolonged with PfK13 Cys469Tyr (median 4.2 h) and Ala675Val (4.9 h) mutations; all regimens were well tolerated.

articles.headline: Diversifying antimalarial treatment for uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria in Uganda
articles.title_original: Diversifying antimalarial treatment for uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Uganda.
articles.authors: Dondorp AM
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Mini-review on nuclear medicine imaging for malaria
articles.title_original: Mini-review and situation report on the role and usefulness of nuclear medicine imaging for malaria.
articles.authors: Duvenhage J, Zeevaart JR, Sathekge MM, Ebenhan T
articles.journal: Eur J Nucl Med Mol Imaging
articles.details: This review presents the clinical nuclear imaging techniques used in malaria, focusing on severe malaria as an understudied multisystem disease that can lead to life-long neurological and cognitive sequelae. The authors note that the molecular mechanisms underlying severe malaria pathogenesis are poorly understood and that research has relied largely on post-mortem studies and animal models, both of which lack translatability to human malaria. They report that although the presented radiopharmaceuticals have added value, development of more malaria-specific radiopharmaceuticals has stagnated, and they highlight current limitations and how these challenges might be overcome.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic and symptomatic malaria survey in Northcentral Nigeria
articles.title_original: A cross-sectional survey of malaria in asymptomatic and symptomatic individuals in an endemic area of Northcentral Nigeria.
articles.authors: Akoma ON, Okoh FN, Amoke OC
articles.details: This cross-sectional survey in Lokoja, Northcentral Nigeria, screened 328 consenting participants by light microscopy for Plasmodium parasitemia during the high transmission season (April to October) over a three-year period (2018–2021). Asymptomatic infection was found in 12.4% (25/202) and symptomatic infection in 85.7% (108/126), with no significant difference (p>0.05) between mean distributions of cases across the two groups. All cases were uncomplicated and falciparum-specific, and overall P. falciparum prevalence with hyperparasitemia was 40.5% (133/328). The authors call for regular prevalence monitoring with better diagnosis and control strategies such as mass antimalarial administration targeting the asymptomatic reservoir.

articles.headline: Global malaria and NTD burden trends, 1990–2030 projection
articles.title_original: The past, present and future of global malaria and neglected tropical diseases: a disease burden assessment from 1990 to 2030.
articles.authors: Li D, Shi Y, Wang R, Hong X, Shi T, Zhu S
articles.details: Using Global Burden of Disease Study 2019 data, this study applied joinpoint regression, Spearman's correlation, and ARIMA modeling to assess past, present, and projected trends of malaria and neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) toward WHO 2030 control targets. Western Sub-Saharan Africa had the highest malaria and NTD case burden in 2019, accounting for about half of global cases, while age-standardized incidence rose from 1990 to 2019 in high SDI and World Bank high-income regions. Dengue was the highest-burden NTD in 2019 with a rising trend, and children under 5 bore the most serious burden. The model predicted a slow upward trend in age-standardized mortality from 2020 to 2030.

articles.headline: Ten-year SP/amodiaquine resistance marker trends in Chad SMC
articles.title_original: Trends in Plasmodium falciparum resistance markers to sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine and amodiaquine over ten years of seasonal malaria chemoprevention in Moissala Health District, Chad.
articles.authors: Grandesso F, Diar MSI, Kouassi F, Sayyad-Hilario J, Ouedraogo P, Djimde AA, Singh S, Dama S
articles.details: This study monitored molecular markers of P. falciparum resistance to sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP) and amodiaquine (AQ) across three surveys (2014, 2021, 2023) of 136, 256, and 219 blood samples from children with clinical malaria in Moissala Health District, southern Chad, where seasonal malaria chemoprevention has been used since 2013. Triple dhfr mutants were very high in 2014 and 2021 (100% and 96.9%) but lower in 2023 (83.9%, p<0.001), while quadruple mutants (triple dhfr + dhps A437G) rose from 28.0% to 41.0% to 47.9% (p<0.001); quintuple mutants stayed low (7.6%, 2.8%, 5.9%). The pfcrt K76T mutation fell from 44.6% to ~11%, and pfmdr-1 N86Y remained consistently low. The authors conclude SP and AQ remain effective but the rise in quadruple mutants is concerning and warrants sustained surveillance.

articles.headline: VAR2CSA antibodies mark exposure and protect against placental malaria harms
articles.title_original: VAR2CSA-specific IgG and IgM antibodies are markers of exposure and protection against adverse malaria pregnancy outcomes.
articles.authors: Onwuka AM, Aitken EH, Hasang W, Madanitsa M, Mwapasa V, Phiri K, Ter Kuile FO, Rogerson SJ
articles.details: In a malaria-prevention trial, pregnant Malawian women enrolled at 16-28 weeks' gestation who were P. falciparum-infected at enrolment (n=321) or later in pregnancy (n=145) had plasma IgG and IgM against the VAR2CSA DBL1X-ID2a domain measured at enrolment and delivery. Infected women had higher antibody levels, which declined by delivery. Enrolment IgG showed no association with birth outcomes, but in women uninfected at enrolment, higher delivery IgG was linked to lower low-birth-weight risk (aOR 0.43, 95% CI 0.19-0.97, p=0.04); in infected women, one log higher enrolment IgM was associated with 23% less maternal anaemia at delivery (aOR 0.77, 95% CI 0.60-0.99, p=0.04). Authors conclude these antibodies mark infection and protection.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria pooled at 33% in Nigeria, highest in children
articles.title_original: Prevalence and associated risk factors of asymptomatic malaria in Nigeria: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Mukhtar A, Ismail M, Usman A, Odetokun IA, Ibrahim MA, Salman AA, Bawa-Sani HG, Nass SS, Goni BW, Shehu MN, Nuhu A, Zhang X, Isah MB
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis (PROSPERO CRD42024591788) searched PubMed, Google Scholar, and Scopus for observational studies reporting asymptomatic malaria prevalence in Nigeria, pooling 25 studies with a random-effects model in Stata 15.0. The pooled prevalence was 33% (95% CI 26-41), varying from 25% in the general population to 52% in children. Risk-factor-related prevalence estimates were 19% for education level, 36% for sex, and 39% for insecticide-treated net usage. Authors conclude asymptomatic malaria is highly prevalent, especially in children, sustaining transmission, and emphasize prioritizing proven transmission-reduction tools before addressing the asymptomatic reservoir.

articles.headline: OpIE2-DsRed marker blocks blood-feeding, shortens lifespan in Anopheles gambiae
articles.title_original: An OpIE2-DsRed marker disrupts female blood-feeding and shortens lifespan in the malaria vector Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Sarig A, Sar-Shalom E, Kolley ESM, Yonah ES, Lamdan LB, Lewin AR, Partosh T, Pines G, Bohbot JD, Papathanos PA
articles.details: While generating CRISPR-based gene drive strains of the malaria vector Anopheles gambiae carrying an OpIE2-DsRed fluorescent marker, researchers unexpectedly found transgenic females could not blood-feed and were sterile, while males appeared fertile but had reduced lifespan. Control strains confirmed the OpIE2-DsRed cassette caused the defect. Behavioral assays showed females were attracted to a membrane feeder but failed to initiate feeding, repeatedly probing and grooming before leaving unfed; microscopy revealed a distally curved proboscis in both sexes. A second variant (OpIE2b), differing in flanking sequences at the IE-2 junction, drove strong expression without impairing feeding or longevity, providing a phenotypically neutral marker.

articles.headline: Livelihood activities shape malaria exposure in qualitative Uganda study
articles.title_original: Livelihoods as a key social determinant of malaria: Qualitative evidence from Uganda.
articles.authors: Deane K, Atusingwize E, Musoke D
articles.details: This qualitative study explored perceptions of the social determinants of malaria in Uganda using 14 key informant interviews, 10 focus group discussions with community members, and 11 in-depth interviews with recently affected households. Participants identified livelihoods as key determinants, including maize-focused crop production, livestock husbandry, construction, dusk/night activities, and gendered work. Mechanisms linking these to malaria included increased mosquito exposure at dusk/night, creation of new breeding sites, attracting mosquitoes to housing, providing feeding sites, working near breeding sites, and gendered care responsibilities exposing children. Authors recommend integrating malaria prevention into socio-economic development strategies and engaging with social determinants.

articles.headline: Mathematical model of malaria-COVID-19 co-infection with optimal control
articles.title_original: Optimal control analysis of a mathematical model of malaria and COVID-19 co-infection dynamics.
articles.authors: Diabaté AB, Sangaré B, Koutou O
articles.details: This paper analyzes a deterministic mathematical model of malaria and COVID-19 co-infection in a homogeneous population, first studying each single-infection model then the co-infection dynamics. The authors calculate each model's basic reproduction number and study existence and stability of steady states, showing that under certain conditions both the malaria single-infection and co-infection models exhibit backward bifurcation. They analyze conditions for extinction, competitive exclusion, and co-existence, perform local sensitivity analysis of the reproduction numbers, and apply Pontryagin's maximum principle to determine optimal control strategies. Numerical simulations support the theoretical findings.

articles.headline: Higher malaria parasitemia linked to liver enzyme rises; ABO blood group not
articles.title_original: The Association between Parasitemia and Liver Enzyme Alterations in Malaria Patients: ABO Blood Group as A Non-Contributory Factor.
articles.authors: Aminizadeh S, Shokri E, Mohebali M, Hanifian H, Zafari S, Edalati M, Bahadory S, Hosseinifard H, Nateghpoor M
articles.details: A case-control study compared 50 malaria-positive patients with 50 healthy controls (mean age 38.10±16.40 years), analyzing ABO blood group and liver enzymes (ALT, AST, ALP) with severity classified by WHO criteria. Malaria patients had significantly higher AST, ALT, and ALP than controls (p=0.001), and liver enzyme levels correlated significantly with parasitemia (p<0.001), with higher parasite loads linked to greater hepatic dysfunction. No significant association was found between ABO blood group and liver enzyme levels (p>0.05). The authors conclude liver enzyme profiles may serve as biomarkers of malaria severity.

articles.headline: Baseline study to guide seasonal malaria chemoprevention in semi-nomadic Turkana
articles.title_original: A Baseline Mixed-Methods Study to Inform Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention Delivery in a Semi-Nomadic Population in Turkana, Kenya.
articles.authors: Kostandova N, Kafu C, Chepkwony TJ, Ambani G, Abel L, Rotich B, Kipkoech Kirui J, Kimachas E, Robie E, Lowoi K, Ekai D, Lokoel G, Orinda O, Van Hulle S, Mgeni R, O'Meara WP, Wesolowski A, Genberg B, Menya D
articles.details: This mixed-methods baseline study used stratified cluster sampling to enroll 198 households with children aged 6 months to 5 years, testing individuals six months and older by RDT and PCR for P. falciparum, plus 78 key informant interviews, 31 focus group discussions, and 60 in-depth interviews. P. falciparum prevalence was 21% by both RDT and PCR, varying across villages up to 73% positivity by PCR. Challenges to seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) included physical access, stigma, and acceptability. Authors conclude SMC is feasible but should combine centralized and door-to-door delivery using existing grassroots structures.

articles.headline: Real-world malaria vaccine evaluation needs better African vaccination records
articles.title_original: Need for Real-World Evaluation of Malaria Vaccines and Reliable Vaccination Records in Africa.
articles.authors: Ko YK, Alfvén T, Okomo GO, Kagaya W, Shioda K, Kaneko A, Gitaka J
articles.details: Evaluating the real-world effectiveness of the newly approved malaria vaccines RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M is critical for informing vaccine policy, especially in areas not represented in the original clinical trials. Observational designs such as cohort studies using the target trial emulation framework and the test-negative design offer promising approaches, but both require accurate, individual-level vaccination data, which remains a major challenge in many African countries. The authors argue that strengthening electronic immunization registries and improving the quality and completeness of paper-based immunization records is essential both for evaluating current vaccines and for preparing for future malaria vaccines, to support robust vaccine monitoring and decision-making.

articles.headline: Plant-produced antibodies bind native circumsporozoite protein on malaria sporozoites
articles.title_original: Plant-produced monoclonal antibodies recognize the native circumsporozoite protein on Plasmodium falciparum sporozoites.
articles.authors: Thiganta K, Rattanapisit K, Suwanchaikasem P, Roobsoong W, Bulaon CJI, Phoolcharoen W
articles.details: Researchers produced the anti-PfCSP monoclonal antibody MS-1797, which targets the conserved NANP repeat region of the circumsporozoite protein, in glycoengineered Nicotiana benthamiana ΔXF plants. Two variants, MS-01 (original) and MS-02 (MS-1797-SEKDEL), yielded up to 457.3 µg/g and 415.7 µg/g fresh weight at 6 days post-infiltration, at >90% purity, with either human core glycans or high mannose structures. Both retained antigen specificity in vitro and bound native PfCSP on sporozoites by immunofluorescence. The authors conclude plant-based production of functional anti-malarial mAbs is feasible for research, diagnostics, and prophylaxis.

articles.headline: School-age children at highest malaria risk in Ho Municipality, Ghana
articles.title_original: Malaria test positivity, Plasmodium species distribution, and risk factors in Ho Municipality, Ghana: a retrospective analysis of seasonal and demographic trends (2020-2022).
articles.authors: Squire DS, Afuah GM, Dika ND, Dogbey P, Bohli JK, Logosu PE, Tanson PA, Avorkliya R, Donaldy W, Ablordey K, Kwadzokpui PK
articles.details: This retrospective cross-sectional census analyzed all malaria microscopy records from three healthcare facilities in Ho Municipality, Ghana over 36 months (January 2020–December 2022), using log-linear and Poisson regression adjusted for age, sex, facility, and year. Among 27,171 tests, the overall test positivity rate was 8.8%, declining from 9.9% in 2020 to 7.2% in 2022; school-age children (5–12 years) had the highest TPR (18.0%) and transmission peaked in August (13.9%). P. falciparum dominated (79.9% of confirmed cases). School-age children (aPR=3.26) and adolescents (aPR=3.49) were highest-risk, with elderly females also elevated (aPR=2.15). Authors urge targeted interventions including school-based chemoprevention.

articles.headline: Young primigravida and traditional housing raise malaria-in-pregnancy risk in Uganda
articles.title_original: Maternal and household risk factors for malaria in pregnancy and low birthweight: a prospective cohort study from Uganda.
articles.authors: Aguti M, Kizza J, Kakuru A, Nakalembe M, Nankabirwa JI, Gaw SL, Opira B, Ssemukuye T, Ozarslan N, Ranjit A, Dela Cruz E, Clark TD, Roh ME, Jagannathan P, Rosenthal PJ, Adrama H, Olwoch P, Mayende J, Odongo B, John A, Kamya M, Dorsey G
articles.details: This prospective cohort, drawn from a randomized trial of intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp) in Busia District, Uganda, followed 2,728 women through delivery with monthly microscopy, placental histopathology, and birth weight. At enrolment 38.1% had parasitaemia, 6.5% after starting IPTp, 6.4% had high-grade placental malaria, and 6.8% delivered low-birth-weight (<2500 g) infants. Parasitaemia at enrolment was higher in those aged 16–21 (RR 1.62), primigravidae (RR 1.86), and traditional houses (RR 1.17). Primigravida strongly predicted high-grade placental malaria (RR 4.20) and low birth weight (RR 2.14).

articles.headline: Malaria incidence clusters by temperature and highland terrain in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Spatial analysis of malaria incidence and environmental determinants in Hadiya Zone, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Abdo SS
