articles.details: This geostatistical study analyzed malaria incidence across woredas of the Hadiya Zone, Ethiopia, in 2022 and 2023 using spatial autocorrelation and regression methods. Moran's I (0.558 in 2022, 0.483 in 2023; p<0.01) and Geary's C (0.63, 0.69) confirmed significant clustering, with hot spots in Shashogo, Soro, and Misrak Badawacho and cold spots in Misha, Duna, and Gombora. A spatial autoregressive (SAR) model fit better than OLS and identified average maximum temperature (β=0.945, p=0.017) and proportion of highland terrain (β=0.543, p=0.040) as key predictors. Authors support geographically targeted interventions; limitations include the short study period and absence of socioeconomic variables.

articles.headline: Arts-based engagement boosts community acceptance of Sterile Insect Technique
articles.title_original: From song to stories: assessing the impact of exposure to arts-based community engagement tools in shaping knowledge, attitudes, and acceptability toward the Sterile Insect Technique for malaria control in South Africa.
articles.authors: Manana PN, Jewett S, Munhenga G
articles.details: This cross-sectional survey of 614 community members in uMkhanyakude District, KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa, assessed whether exposure to arts-based community engagement tools (music, drama, radio stories) influenced knowledge, attitudes, and acceptance of the Sterile Insect Technique (SIT) for malaria control. Only 26.2% (n=161) had been exposed. Those exposed more often correctly identified that female mosquitoes feed on blood (95.0% vs 85.8%, p=0.008) and supported upcoming SIT releases (98.1% vs 89.4%, p=0.003); exposure remained a significant predictor of acceptance (OR 0.65, 95% CI 0.45–0.94). Authors conclude arts-based tools improved knowledge and acceptance but reach was limited.

articles.headline: XN-31 analyzer accurately diagnoses malaria in vivax-dominant Thailand
articles.title_original: Performance evaluation of the automated haematology analyzer XN-31 for malaria diagnosis in Plasmodium vivax-dominant regions of Thailand.
articles.authors: Kawaguchi M, Komaki-Yasuda K, Nakatsu M, Kerdsakundee N, Hamana M, Tougan T, Kamei M, Konishi A, Krudsood S, Kano S
articles.details: This study evaluated the automated haematology analyzer XN-31, which uses flow cytometry to detect parasites and report % parasitaemia (MI-RBC%), in 349 suspected malaria patients in Thailand where P. vivax predominates, comparing it with thick/thin film microscopy, RDTs, and PCR. Of 349 samples, 125 were microscopy-positive (103 P. vivax, 17 P. falciparum, 3 P. knowlesi, 1 P. ovale, 1 P. malariae). The XN-31 showed 98.4% sensitivity versus thick film, outperforming the RDT (88.0%); all XN-31-positive samples were PCR-confirmed, species flags matched microscopy 100%, and quantitative results correlated strongly with thin film (r=0.903). Authors conclude it is a useful diagnostic tool in P. vivax–dominant regions.

articles.headline: Plasmodium vivax genomes highly conserved in Duffy-negative Sudanese individuals
articles.title_original: Highly conserved Plasmodium vivax genomes in Duffy-negative individuals from Sudan.
articles.authors: Schroeder RE, Ahmed S, Ford A, Elfaki M, Hamad SO, Elfaki TM, Mohamed S, Manko E, Clark TG, Campino S, Hamid MMA, Lo E
articles.details: This study compared P. vivax infections in Duffy-positive and Duffy-negative individuals in Sudan using epidemiological data and parasite genome sequencing. Duffy-positive individuals had significantly higher parasitemia, and their parasite genomes were much more diverse across all 14 chromosomes and 44 erythrocyte-binding gene candidates, with merozoite surface protein family genes accounting for much of the diversity. Many erythrocyte-binding gene candidates were under both positive and negative selection pressure. In the DBP and RBP genes and TRAg38, amino-acid changes to structurally different residues in the binding regions could affect erythrocyte binding affinity and antigenic conformation.

articles.headline: Correction notice for malaria gametocyte imaging study
articles.title_original: Correction: Machine learning-based phenotypic imaging to characterise the targetable biology of Plasmodium falciparum male gametocytes for the development of transmission-blocking antimalarials.
articles.authors: Tsebriy O, Khomiak A, Miguel-Blanco C, Sparkes PC, Gioli M, Santelli M, Whitley E, Gamo FJ, Delves MJ
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: New genetic tool enables study of malaria virulence factor PfEMP1
articles.title_original: A system for functional studies of the major virulence factor of malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Cronshagen J, Allweier J, Mesén-Ramírez JP, Stäcker J, Vaaben AV, Ramón-Zamorano G, Naranjo-Prado I, Graser M, López-Barona P, Ofori S, Jansen PWTC, Hornebeck J, Kieferle F, Murk A, Martin E, Castro-Peña C, Bártfai R, Lavstsen T, Bruchhaus I, Spielmann T
articles.details: This laboratory methods study used selection-linked integration (SLI) to engineer Plasmodium falciparum parasites that all express the same single PfEMP1 variant and to genome-edit the expressed locus, overcoming the expression heterogeneity that normally hampers study of this variant surface antigen. Moving the system from reference strain 3D7 to IT4 produced parasites with effective receptor binding, and a second version (SLI2) allowed additional genome edits. Using these tools, the authors studied PfEMP1 trafficking, generated cell lines binding common endothelial receptors, surveyed the functional PfEMP1 protein environment, and identified new proteins required for PfEMP1-mediated sequestration.

articles.headline: Antimalarial drugs cause acute hepatitis in treated patient
articles.title_original: Deciphering Hepatic Dilemmas: A Case of Acute Hepatitis Following Malaria Treatment.
articles.authors: Lovrien S, Quist E, Maita Coronel D, Singal AK, Xie C
articles.details: This case report describes a 55-year-old African woman with P. falciparum malaria, recently treated in Cameroon, who presented with jaundice and transaminitis; liver biopsy showed cholestatic hepatitis. Diagnosis was complicated because only one of four follow-up thick and thin blood smears was positive for P. falciparum. After more than 17 months of follow-up, her liver enzymes and bilirubin returned to baseline without further malaria treatment, and the findings were attributed to drug-induced liver injury from antimalarial medications.

articles.headline: Portable PCR test outperforms RDT and microscopy for P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Field evaluation of the miniature direct-on-blood PCR nucleic acid lateral flow immunoassay (mini-dbPCR-NALFIA) for the detection of Plasmodium falciparum in high seasonal malaria transmission setting in Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Kiemde F, Rouamba T, Some DY, Kabore B, Valia D, Natama HM, Bere AW, Mens PF, Tinto H, Schallig HDFH
articles.details: A phase 3 diagnostic accuracy study in a high P. falciparum transmission setting in Burkina Faso evaluated a miniature direct-on-blood PCR with lateral flow readout (mini-dbPCR-NALFIA) against PfHRP2-based RDT and microscopy, using qPCR (varATS gene) as reference, in 438 febrile patients of all ages. Malaria prevalence by qPCR was 65.5% (287/438). The dbPCR-NALFIA showed sensitivity 96.5% and specificity 98.0%, outperforming RDT specificity (80.8%) and microscopy sensitivity (90.2%), detected 67.9% of submicroscopic qPCR-positive samples missed by microscopy, and had the highest agreement with qPCR (kappa 0.90 vs 0.80 microscopy, 0.71 RDT). Authors conclude it shows excellent field performance for case detection.

articles.headline: Gold miners in Zimbabwe show good malaria knowledge but poor prevention
articles.title_original: An assessment of knowledge, practices, and malaria parasitaemia among artisanal gold miners and community members in Mudzi, Zimbabwe.
articles.authors: Fambirai T, Chimbari MJ
articles.details: A mixed-methods cross-sectional study in Mudzi district, Zimbabwe assessed malaria knowledge, practices, and parasitaemia among 128 artisanal gold miners (AGMs) and 113 community members (CMs), with 277 individuals tested by RDT and blood smear microscopy. Participants showed good knowledge of malaria cause and transmission but low risk perception and misconceptions, with no significant difference between groups in education, knowledge, or perceptions; AGMs predominantly lived outdoors without protective measures. Malaria prevalence across groups was less than 1%. Authors conclude that high knowledge is not translating into preventive behaviour and call for integrated interventions addressing both livelihoods and prevention.

articles.headline: Repurposed human kinase inhibitor kills malaria parasites by blocking hemozoin
articles.title_original: Repositioning a Human Kinase Platelet-Derived Growth Factor Receptor Alpha Type II Inhibitor for Malaria and Inhibition of Hemozoin Formation.
articles.authors: Mmonwa MM, Audu O, Maepa K, Dziwornu GA, Govender P, Tshaka L, Burrows J, Taylor D, Masike K, Njoroge M, Wicht KJ, Coulson LB, Chibale K
articles.details: Screening the Kinase Chemogenomics Set, a collection of narrow-spectrum human kinase inhibitors, identified GSK190937, a type II PDGFRA inhibitor, with activity against asexual blood-stage Plasmodium falciparum (PfNF54 IC50 = 0.22 μM). Medicinal chemistry progression aimed to improve potency, selectivity, and ADME properties, yielding compounds 20, 23, and 29 with improved microsomal metabolic stability and asexual blood-stage antiplasmodium activity. Mechanism-of-action studies showed the series inhibits hemozoin formation, killing late-stage trophozoites.

articles.headline: Experimental evolution of murine malaria in single vs helminth-coinfected mice
articles.title_original: Experimental evolution of Plasmodium yoelii in single and helminth-coinfected mice.
articles.authors: Dusuel A, Bourbon L, Groetz E, Rialland M, Roche B, Faivre B, Sorci G
articles.details: Using experimental evolution, researchers serially passaged the murine malaria parasite Plasmodium yoelii either in singly infected mice (SI-lines) or in mice previously infected with the nematode Heligmosomoides polygyrus (COI-lines), then compared infection dynamics and virulence to the ancestral population after five and seven passages. Serial passage increased parasitemia and virulence by favoring fastest-replicating genotypes, but SI-lines and COI-lines had similar replication rates and virulence, and hosts with evolved lines were less tolerant. COI-lines replicated more slowly early when used in mismatched (single-infection) environments. The authors conclude there was no strong virulence divergence between lines, possibly reflecting a cost of virulence, but parasites rapidly adapted to their host environment.

articles.headline: 16-year malaria surveillance in Isfahan, Iran shows imported P. vivax dominance
articles.title_original: Epidemiological and clinical profile of malaria patients in Isfahan Province, Iran: a retrospective analysis from 2009 to 2025.
articles.authors: Lesani S, Boozhmehrani MJ, Golban M, Ghayour Najafabadi Z
articles.details: A retrospective observational analysis of microscopically confirmed malaria cases from the Isfahan Center for Disease Control (January 2009 to January 2025) examined epidemiological and clinical characteristics, using descriptive statistics and multivariable logistic regression for severe-malaria risk factors. Among 569 cases, incidence declined sharply after 2009 with a resurgence in 2024; most patients were male (96.1%), aged 15–24 years (53.6%), and Afghan nationals (80.8%), and 80.8% were imported. Plasmodium vivax accounted for 88.0% of cases, while P. falciparum (4.6%) was strongly associated with severe disease (adjusted OR 22.6; 95% CI 1.24–410.8; p = 0.035). The authors conclude imported cases among migrant workers remain the central challenge to elimination, requiring species-specific strategies and cross-border collaboration.

articles.headline: Water and sanitation access strongly linked to lower malaria incidence
articles.title_original: Investigating the relationship between malaria incidence and public health infrastructure in sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Shin J
articles.details: This country-level analysis used global health database data to assess how access to clean water, sanitation, and childhood vitamin A supplementation relate to malaria incidence in sub-Saharan Africa, applying Kruskal-Wallis tests with Dunn's post hoc comparisons, Cliff's delta effect sizes, and tree-based machine learning and logistic regression models. Significant differences in incidence were found across water and sanitation access groups, with the lowest-access groups having the highest incidence and large effect sizes; vitamin A supplementation showed significant but small differences. Tree-based models had moderate predictive performance and outperformed logistic regression. The authors conclude clean water and adequate sanitation are strongly associated with lower malaria incidence.

articles.headline: Insect-specific viruses infect mosquitoes but don't block Plasmodium development
articles.title_original: Systemic infection with insect-specific viruses does not affect Plasmodium sporozoite formation in Anopheles mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Schinkel M, Overheul GJ, Ploeger E, van Gemert GJ, Junglen S, Miesen P, Bousema T, van Rij RP
articles.details: This study tested whether insect-specific viruses affect Plasmodium development in Anopheles mosquitoes, screening a panel of 15 viruses for replication in two Anopheles gambiae cell lines and in vivo. Six viruses replicated efficiently in cells; after intrathoracic injection into Anopheles stephensi, Herbert herbevirus, Agua Salud alphavirus, Gouléako goukovirus, and invertebrate iridescent virus 6 replicated well without high mortality, while Flock House virus caused high mortality. In coinfection experiments with Plasmodium falciparum, sporozoite formation and infection rates did not differ between virus-infected and control mosquitoes. The authors conclude none of the tested viruses affected Plasmodium development, but the work provides a resource for studying Anopheles immunity.

articles.headline: Consensus framework developed to assess post-elimination malaria surveillance capacity
articles.title_original: Building a tool to assess malaria surveillance and response capacity in malaria post-elimination contexts: a modified and dual-weighted Delphi approach.
articles.authors: Lu G, Xu E, Cao Y, Chai L, Liao Z, Wang J, Sha T, Wang Y, Müller O, Zhao J, Zhu G, Cao J
articles.details: Using two systematic reviews, expert panels, and a modified two-round Delphi process with 30 experts in epidemiology, disease control, and public health, the authors developed a standardized framework to assess malaria surveillance and response capacity in post-elimination settings. From 45 candidate indicators, 34 indicators across six domains were retained. The highest-weighted domains were quality and use of surveillance data (0.371) and surveillance system coverage and performance (0.240); the top indicators were the proportion of confirmed cases with completed epidemiological investigations and reports (0.1153) and the interval from first medical visit to diagnosis (0.1131). Expert authority coefficients were 0.777 and 0.895. The authors conclude the tool can help countries sustain elimination and mitigate reintroduction risks.

articles.headline: Malaria parasite cytochrome c has unusually low redox potential
articles.title_original: A Divergent Cytochrome c in Malaria Parasites with an Anomalously Low Redox Potential.
articles.authors: Loveridge KM, Olsen OD, Scherer SR, Espino-Sanchez TJ, Wienkers H, Hill CP, Kay MS, Sigala PA
articles.details: This biochemical study characterized a newly identified divergent lineage of eukaryotic cytochrome c homologs in Apicomplexa, exemplified by Plasmodium falciparum cyt c-2 (Pfcyt c-2). Despite conserved axial His/Met ligands, Pfcyt c-2 has an unusual pentacoordinate heme and a redox potential of -278 mV, over 500 mV lower than canonical cyt c homologs (+250 mV) and lower than any natural monoheme c-type cytochrome, contradicting a conventional electron transport chain role. The protein nonetheless showed canonical thermostability, low-level peroxidase activity, and elevated structural heterogeneity. The authors conclude this represents a new clade of cyt c variants with divergent properties, offering scaffolds for mechanistic discovery and redox engineering.

articles.headline: Malaria RDT matches microscopy accuracy among febrile Ethiopian patients
articles.title_original: Prevalence of malaria and diagnostic performance of malaria rapid diagnostic tests compared to microscopy among febrile patients at Pawe General Hospital, Northwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Legesse F, Berhane N
articles.details: In a hospital-based cross-sectional study of 422 febrile patients suspected of malaria at Pawe General Hospital, Ethiopia (November–December 2022), malaria prevalence by Giemsa-stained microscopy was 27.0% (114/422), with P. falciparum accounting for 84.2%, P. vivax 7.9%, and mixed infections 7.9%. The Bioline Malaria Ag Pf/Pv mRDT showed 99.1% sensitivity, 99.3% specificity, 98.3% PPV, and 99.7% NPV, with almost perfect agreement with microscopy (κ=0.98). Male sex (AOR=1.88; 95% CI 1.16–3.04; p=0.011) and households of five or more members (AOR=2.19; 95% CI 1.36–3.54; p=0.001) were significant risk factors. The authors conclude the mRDT can be reliably used for routine malaria diagnosis in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Bradford assay validated for protein quantification of R21 malaria vaccine
articles.title_original: Systematic Development and Validation of a Bradford-Based Protein Quantification Method for Novel Multi-Dose R21 Malaria Vaccine Formulated with 2-Phenoxy Ethanol (2-PE).
articles.authors: Jena R, Ranade D, Chaudhari P, Salunke A, Mahamuni A, Gairola S
articles.details: This methods study developed and validated a protein quantification assay for the R21 malaria vaccine formulated with Matrix-M1 adjuvant and 2-PE preservative. Micro-BCA, BCA, and Bradford assays were compared, and the Bradford assay was selected based on precision, accuracy, and linearity, then optimized for buffer systems and validated per ICH Q2(R2) guidelines. The validated assay was specific, accurate, precise, and repeatable, with a limit of quantitation of 2 µg/mL, performed comparably to ELISA, and was sensitive enough to detect antigen loss from adsorption to vial surfaces. The authors conclude the rapid, high-throughput, cost-effective method establishes a new quality-control benchmark for VLP-based vaccines with complex formulations.

articles.headline: Cambodia province nears malaria elimination despite persistent P. vivax
articles.title_original: Assessing progress and challenges towards malaria elimination in Kampong Speu, Cambodia: analysis of Plasmodium vivax and mixed infections, 2019-2023.
articles.authors: Ly K, Ung S, Roces MC, Lek D, Ly P
articles.details: This epidemiological analysis used Cambodia's national Malaria Information System and radical cure treatment records to assess malaria cases and P. vivax and mixed-species infection treatment outcomes in Kampong Speu Province from 2019 to 2023. Malaria screening increased substantially, largely by village malaria workers, while confirmed cases declined: annual parasite incidence fell from 23.8 per 1000 at-risk individuals in 2019 to 0.7 per 1000 in 2023, and radical cure completion among eligible cases rose from 78% in 2019 to 98% in 2023. Males aged 15–49, particularly forest-goers, remained the most at-risk group. The authors recommend enhanced prevention, increased screening, and 100% radical cure for eligible high-risk individuals to support elimination.

articles.headline: Procedural errors degrade malaria RDT accuracy, especially at low parasitaemia
articles.title_original: The effects of introduced procedural errors on malaria rapid diagnostic test performance in a laboratory setting.
articles.authors: Wilson S, Ah Y, Aidoo M
articles.details: Laboratory study testing six malaria RDT products using cultured Plasmodium falciparum diluted to a range of parasitaemia, run per manufacturers' instructions (baseline) then with deliberate deviations in buffer volume, blood volume, and incubation time. Increasing sample volume beyond the recommended 5µL impaired performance, with 4 of 6 products showing 83.3% (30/36) invalid results from faulty sample migration. Insufficient buffer caused at least one invalid result in over half the products (10/27, 37%), while excess buffer gave 0/45 invalid. The shortest 5-min incubation caused the most deviations. At 1,000 parasites/µL with 5µL sample, three products achieved 100% agreement with baseline. Authors conclude errors adversely affect results, especially in low-parasitaemia samples.

articles.headline: Housing modification costs and willingness to pay for malaria prevention in Uganda
articles.title_original: Housing modification to prevent malaria in Uganda: an analysis of costs, willingness to pay, and equity.
articles.authors: Snyman K, Ochieng W, Gonahasa S, Aber J, Katureebe A, Staedke SG, Kamya MR, Westercamp N, Pitt C
articles.details: Cost and willingness-to-pay (WTP) analysis alongside a cluster-randomized controlled trial of two housing modifications—screening and eave tubes—in Eastern Region, Uganda, covering roughly 4000 homes (20000 people), with WTP measured via three cross-sectional surveys of 1500 households each using modified structured haggling. Screening cost a mean of $116 (95%CI $112-120, 2022 USD) per house and eave tubes $50 (95%CI $48-52); annualized over 5 years this was $4.22 and $3.03 per person protected per year. Installation cost more in the wealthiest versus poorest quintiles (screening $151 vs $69; eave tubes $95 vs $31). Over 75% would pay something, but amounts covered only a small fraction of costs (screening 12% vs 7%; eave tubes 18% vs 14% wealthiest vs poorest). Authors conclude equitable scale-up needs additional financing.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic, drug-resistant P. falciparum in HIV patients in Brazzaville
articles.title_original: Characterization of Plasmodium falciparum infections among people living with HIV under antiretroviral and co-trimoxazole chemoprophylaxis in Brazzaville, Republic of Congo.
articles.authors: Djontu JC, Baina MT, Ntabi JDM, Mapanguy CCM, Batchi-Bouyou AL, Elenga VA, Kouikani FY, Ndala ACM, Mouanga AM, Ntoumi F
articles.details: Health facility-based cross-sectional study characterizing malaria in 683 people living with HIV (aged 1 month to 84 years) on antiretroviral therapy and co-trimoxazole prophylaxis, conducted January to September 2022 at an outpatient center in Brazzaville, Republic of Congo, using microscopy, nested PCR, RFLP-PCR and sequencing. All infected participants were asymptomatic; microscopy detected 1.2% (8/683) and PCR detected 9.2% (62/675) submicroscopic infections, with P. falciparum mono-infection predominant (69/70, 98.6%) and mean multiplicity of infection 2.1±1.09 (68.1% polyclonal). High mutation prevalence was found in Pfdhfr (51I 78.2%, 59R 84.2%, 108N 84.0%), Pfdhps (437G 72.0%, 540E 30.8%, 581G 48.5%), Pfcrt 76T (46.9%) and Pfmdr1 184F (42.1%); no artemisinin partial-resistance K13 mutations were detected. Authors report low malaria prevalence and comparatively low genetic diversity.

articles.headline: IFN-γ and IL-4 mark protection from attenuated malaria vaccine
articles.title_original: IFN-γ and IL-4 correlate with protection induced by an attenuated malaria vaccine.
articles.authors: Robinson TM, Bolton JS, Duncan EH, Bergmann-Leitner ES, Mura M
articles.details: Immunological study of controlled human malaria infection after immunization via mosquito bites with radiation-attenuated Plasmodium falciparum sporozoites, profiling circulating innate mononuclear cell phenotypes and PBMC responses to in vitro sporozoite stimulation. The authors identified correlates of protection spanning baseline immune responses (IFN-γ secretion and HLA-DRhigh expression) and vaccine-induced factors (IL-4 secretion and CD57- γδ T cell frequency).

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria common in rural adults in Gabon
articles.title_original: Burden and determinants of asymptomatic malaria among adults living in urban and rural areas in Gabon in 2023: a community-based cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Moutombi Ditombi BC, M'Bondoukwé NP, Ndong Ngomo JM, Kono HN, Pongui Ngondza B, Ada Mengome M, Ndong Akomezoghe L, Moussavou Mabika DA, Mihindou CJ, Moutongo R, Mawili-Mboumba DP, Bouyou-Akotet MK
articles.details: Community-based cross-sectional survey conducted January to December 2023 in Bitam (rural), Libreville, and Owendo (urban), Gabon, assessing asymptomatic P. falciparum infection by microscopy among 1,496 adults aged ≥18 years with no symptoms or recent treatment, with logistic regression for determinants. Overall prevalence was 15.3%, significantly higher in rural (22.4%) than urban areas (4.1%; p<0.01), with higher rural parasite densities. Independent risk factors included rural residence (aOR 7.1; 95% CI 4.4-9.8), being a worker (aOR 5.2; 95% CI 3.4-7.8), and attending school (p<0.01). ITN use was low (32.7%) and not significantly protective. Authors conclude malaria control should be broadened to include adults and occupational exposure.

articles.headline: Review of integrated malaria control approaches in Madagascar
articles.title_original: Integrated Malaria Control in Madagascar: From Vector Dynamics to Community Interventions.
articles.authors: Ramboarina S, Kapesa L, Razafindrakoto J, Rasolofo V, Solofoharivelo MC
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: 8-year blood-donor screening for malaria and Chagas in Tuscany
articles.title_original: Prevention of Transfusion-Transmitted Malaria and Chagas Disease in Non-Endemic Countries: An 8-Year Study of Seroprevalence Among Donors at Risk in Tuscany (Central Italy).
articles.authors: Mangano VD, Pinto B, Marotta R, Galli L, Moscato GA, Lupetti A, Bruschi F
articles.details: This 8-year seroprevalence study screened blood donors at risk in northwestern Tuscany, Italy, for transfusion-transmissible Plasmodium and Trypanosoma cruzi using ELISA, with confirmatory LAMP, CLIA, and Western blot. Among 3,754 donors tested for anti-Plasmodium antibodies, seroprevalence was 6.8% (95% CI 6.1–7.7%), highest in donors from Sub-Saharan Africa (42.9%) and Southeast Asia (10.6%); none of 20 seropositive donors tested were positive by Plasmodium DNA LAMP. Euroimmun ELISA gave lower seropositivity (4.8%) than DRG ELISA (8.2%). Among 4,285 donors tested for anti-T. cruzi, seroprevalence was 0.7%, with no confirmed chronic Chagas cases. High seroreversion after one year suggests re-screening seropositive donors to reduce deferral.

articles.headline: Mapping spread of artemisinin-resistance k13 markers across Africa
articles.title_original: Mapping the prevalence of molecular markers of Plasmodium falciparum artemisinin partial resistance in Africa: a spatial-temporal modelling study.
articles.authors: Young NW, Meier-Scherling CPG, Cuomo-Dannenburg G, Tollefson GA, Connelly SV, Marglous J, Gyuricza IG, Carey-Ewend K, Kyong-Shin R, Popkin-Hall ZR, Zeleke AJ, Ishengoma DS, Fola AA, Simkin A, Niaré K, Parr JB, Conrad M, Okell LC, Ruybal-Pesántez S, Watson OJ, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Verity R
articles.details: This spatial-temporal modelling study mapped Plasmodium falciparum kelch13 (k13) artemisinin partial-resistance and ACT partner-drug markers across Africa, using a Gaussian process model on an integrated dataset of 3,806 molecular surveys and 182,071 genotyped samples (literature 2014–2025 plus WWARN, MalariaGEN Pf7, and WHO data). Resistance increases were driven by distinct emergences of k13 561H (Rwanda), 675V (Uganda), and 622I (Ethiopia/Eritrea). From 2012 to 2024, predicted k13 prevalence rose 1.81%/year in Northern Province, Uganda (reaching 26.36%) and 3.49%/year in Northern Province, Rwanda (reaching 39.44%). The mdr1 86Y amodiaquine marker is fading while crt 76T persists in the Horn of Africa. Authors warn of multicentric expansion threatening ACT efficacy and urge targeted molecular surveillance across East Africa.

articles.headline: Insecticide resistance trends in Indian malaria vectors, 2000–2024
articles.title_original: Spatio-temporal trends of insecticide resistance in Indian malaria vectors (2000-2024): a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Mullick R, Yadav CP, Singhal R
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 51 studies (published 2000–2024) evaluated spatial-temporal resistance to DDT, malathion, and deltamethrin among Indian Anopheles malaria vectors. DDT resistance was widespread across almost all states except the north-east; Anopheles culicifacies showed very low DDT susceptibility. For deltamethrin, average percent mortality was 89% (95% CI 79–94%) in An. culicifacies, 93% (78–98%) in An. stephensi, and 100% (99–100%) in An. fluviatilis, with deltamethrin resistance mainly in Chhattisgarh and Madhya Pradesh. An. culicifacies and An. stephensi were highly malathion-resistant, while An. fluviatilis was fully susceptible. Authors conclude north-eastern vectors remain largely susceptible and stress continued surveillance.

articles.headline: COVID-19's indirect effect on Guinea's HIV, malaria, TB, vaccine services
articles.title_original: The impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on HIV, malaria, tuberculosis and childhood vaccination services in Guinea: an interrupted time series analysis.
articles.authors: Diallo A, Sidikiba S, Cissé AS, Camara M, Kaba M, Diallo BD, Carlos-Bolumbu M, Sako FB, Delamou A
articles.details: This interrupted time series analysis used national surveillance data (January 2018–December 2022) on nine HIV, malaria, tuberculosis, and childhood vaccination indicators in Guinea, fitting a Poisson segmented regression with seasonality to estimate COVID-19's impact via incidence rate ratios. Immediate decreases occurred in three indicators, from 6% for pentavalent vaccine (IRR 0.940, 0.906–0.974) to 15% for TB case notification (IRR 0.850, 0.785–0.920). Pandemic trends declined monthly for four indicators, from 0.8% for in-patient malaria cases (IRR 0.992) to 8% for infant PCR testing (IRR 0.920), while TB-positive microscopy rose 2.5%/month. Authors conclude malaria indicators were generally maintained, but HIV infant testing, vaccines, and TB indicators were heavily impacted.

articles.headline: Mosquito metabolism and gut microbes shape P. vivax infection outcomes
articles.title_original: Metabolic reprogramming and gut microbiota ecology drive divergent Plasmodium vivax infection outcomes in Anopheles darlingi.
articles.authors: Carlos BC, Voges K, Affonso PHA, Jaye A, Tong Rios C, Tinoco-Nunes B, Alonso DP, Cai JA, MacCallum RM, Moreno M, Vlachou D, Souza-Neto JA, Christophides GK
articles.details: Experimental infections of the Amazonian vector Anopheles darlingi with field-derived Plasmodium vivax gametocytaemic blood produced two phenotypes: low and high oocyst burdens. Midgut transcriptomic profiling showed low-infection mosquitoes mounted early, sustained activation of detoxification, redox regulation, aromatic amino acid catabolism, and purine depletion, alongside reduced bacterial diversity and enrichment of Enterobacteriales and Pseudomonadales (taxa linked to anti-Plasmodium activity). High-infection mosquitoes showed limited metabolic reprogramming and expansion of Flavobacteriales. Low-infection outcomes arose from the lowest gametocyte-density bloodmeals, framing vector competence as a dynamic metabolic-microbial outcome and a target for transmission-blocking strategies.

articles.headline: Anopheles gambiae gut bacteria differ by P. falciparum infection in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Microbiota diversity of Anopheles gambiae in Bankeng, southern Cameroon, and its association with Plasmodium falciparum infection.
articles.authors: Sandeu MM, Maffo Tatsinkou CG, Dada N, Kouhoué Feukam F, Njiokou F, Hughes GL, Wondji CS
articles.details: Using 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing, this study compared the bacterial microbiota of 120 naturally collected Anopheles gambiae mosquitoes (60 infected and 60 uninfected with Plasmodium falciparum) in Bankeng, Cameroon, across wet and dry seasons. Of 99 bacterial taxa detected, 97 were shared; two (Acetobacteraceae and Enterococcus) were unique to uninfected mosquitoes and none to infected, while 14 genera were more abundant in uninfected mosquitoes versus two in infected. Bacterial diversity differed significantly, being higher in uninfected mosquitoes during the wet season and in infected mosquitoes during the dry season. The authors note possible links to susceptibility or resistance but cannot establish causality.

articles.headline: Engineered Serratia AS1 bacterium spreads through mosquitoes for malaria control
articles.title_original: Environmental persistence and transmission dynamics of Serratia AS1 in mosquito habitats: advancing paratransgenesis for malaria control.
articles.authors: Kamel Urmia H, Koosha M, Yakhchali B, Moosa-Kazemi SH, Oshaghi MA
articles.details: This semi-field study in Bandar Abbas, Iran evaluated Serratia AS1-mCherry, a paratransgenesis candidate, in Anopheles stephensi habitats. The bacterium colonized mosquito midguts and ovaries, persisted in larval breeding water for 14 days, and remained stable on sugar-soaked cotton pads for 4-6 days. Transmission occurred via transstadial, venereal, and vertical routes plus adult acquisition from larval habitats (sipping), with water-based delivery effectively disseminating it among mosquito populations. The authors conclude Serratia AS1 with effector molecules is feasible for field use as a sustainable paratransgenesis-based vector-control strategy.

articles.headline: Symmetrical foot gangrene complicating severe falciparum malaria in a teenager
articles.title_original: Symmetrical lower extremity gangrene in a patient with severe malaria: a case report and literature review.
articles.authors: Aman ST, Belay EH, Jirata TD, Hussen SH, Isa SF, Abagojam AH, Kebede ML, Bezabih KT, Tukeni KN
articles.details: A 14-year-old Ethiopian girl developed symmetrical peripheral gangrene of the lower limbs during severe Plasmodium falciparum malaria. She presented with fever, vomiting, and diarrhea, and despite prompt antimalarial therapy showed progressive darkening of both feet. Clinical evaluation revealed stable vital signs, preserved organ function, and palpable peripheral pulses, supporting a diagnosis of severe malaria complicated by peripheral gangrene; management was conservative with continued antimalarial treatment. The authors emphasize early recognition and addressing underlying etiologies to optimize outcomes.

articles.headline: Call for an evolutionary baseline model of P. falciparum genomes
articles.title_original: Towards an evolutionary baseline model of Plasmodium falciparum for population-genomic inference.
articles.authors: Henry CM, Marsh JI, Daigle A, Crescenzi J, Lin JT, Bailey JA, Johri P
articles.details: This perspective argues for building a baseline evolutionary model of Plasmodium falciparum that jointly accounts for simultaneously acting evolutionary processes, to enable population-genomic inference. The authors note malaria caused over 15.7 million deaths in the 21st century and roughly 600,000 globally in 2023, with recurrent resistance to frontline drugs and multiple resistance alleles at intermediate-to-high frequency in specific Asian and African populations. Leveraging tens of thousands of genomes from efforts such as MalariaGEN, they identify key components needed for a null model to test evolutionary hypotheses, improve inference of evolutionary parameters, and identify new drug-resistance gene candidates.

articles.headline: Dysfunctional memory B cells linked to waning malaria vaccine immunity
articles.title_original: Dysfunctional memory B cell responses to the protective repeat region of the Plasmodium circumsporozoite protein are associated with waning humoral immunity.
articles.authors: McDougal CE, Mkindi CG, Rodda LB, Lucarelli CV, Langowski MD, King NP, Jongo S, Daubenberger C, Pepper M
articles.details: This cross-species, comprehensive analysis examined B cell responses to the Plasmodium circumsporozoite protein (CSP), focusing on the repeat and C-terminus domains used in malaria subunit vaccines, after either Plasmodium infection or immunization. The authors report that CSP-based vaccines show limited durability in malaria-naïve individuals and are even less effective in malaria-experienced individuals. They conclude that two forces predominantly impair proper memory B cell differentiation: inhibition of germinal center formation by infection, and skewed B cell differentiation caused by the repetitive nature of the protective CSP repeat region.

articles.headline: Biliverdin reductase enzymatic activity is essential for malaria resistance
articles.title_original: Biliverdin Reductase Catalytic Activity Is Essential for Malaria Resistance.
articles.authors: Mesquita M, Figueiredo A, Rastogi ST, Ramos S, Pagnotta S, Carlos AR, Cardoso S, Nóvoa A, Mallo M, Paul B, Jentho E, Soares MP
articles.details: Using mouse models, researchers generated Blvra G17A and Blvra E97A mice carrying missense mutations in the BVRA NAD(P)H-binding domain and reductase motif to separate BVRA's catalytic activity from its non-canonical kinase and transcription-factor functions. Both mutant strains had reduced enzymatic activity and succumbed to malaria that was non-lethal to wild-type mice. Quantification of circulating unconjugated bilirubin showed a dose-response effect in which mutants failed to reach the bilirubin threshold needed for protection. The authors conclude BVRA's antimalarial effect depends on its catalytic activity and define a minimal bilirubin threshold required for parasite control and malaria resolution.

articles.headline: Rosettes marginate weakly in blood flow, keeping infected cells circulating
articles.title_original: Rosette margination in blood flow during malaria pathogenesis.
articles.authors: Dasanna AK, Papagrigorakes M, Lanzer M, Schwarz US, Gompper G, Fedosov DA
articles.details: Using mesoscopic hydrodynamics simulations and microfluidic experiments, this study investigated margination of malaria rosettes—structures of one infected red blood cell (iRBC) surrounded by adhered healthy RBCs—at various flow rates, healthy RBC volume fractions, and iRBC–hRBC binding strengths. Rosette margination toward vessel walls was significantly weaker than that of single iRBCs, mainly due to the deformability and dynamics of the rosette clusters. Simulation predictions were confirmed by microfluidic experiments. The authors suggest the main function of rosette formation is not to enhance cytoadhesion to the endothelium but to keep iRBCs in the blood flow, at least along straight vessel segments.

articles.headline: High-dose primaquine cuts P. vivax relapse in children, shorter course works
articles.title_original: Safety, tolerability, and efficacy of high versus low-dose, short versus long-course daily primaquine for the radical cure of uncomplicated Plasmodium vivax malaria in children under 15 years of age: an open-label, non-inferiority, randomized controlled trial (CHILDPRIM).
articles.authors: Pacheco ALO, Omena AG, Baía-da-Silva DC, Jardim TAP, Silva DCB, Lopes APB, Barbosa LRA, Souza IG, Araujo RF, Nogueira LOS, Vale SCN, Melo GC, Cordeiro JSM, Bassat Q, Sampaio VS, Lima VDS, Martinez-Espinosa FE, Mourão MPG, Monteiro WM, Alecrim MGC, Brito-Sousa JD, Lacerda MVG
articles.details: CHILDPRIM was an open-label, non-inferiority randomized trial in children under 15 with uncomplicated P. vivax malaria in the Brazilian Amazon, conducted from August 2021 to January 2025. After G6PD screening, 100 participants were randomized 1:1:1 to standard 3.5 mg/kg primaquine over 7 days (0.5 mg/kg/day), high-dose 7.0 mg/kg over 14 days (0.5 mg/kg/day), or high-dose 7.0 mg/kg over 7 days (1.0 mg/kg/day), and followed 180 days. Kaplan-Meier recurrence-free estimates at day 180 were 50% (3.5 mg/kg/7d), 82.3% (7.0 mg/kg/14d), and 79.4% (7.0 mg/kg/7d) (log-rank p=0.0065); the trial was halted after the second interim analysis. Higher doses caused more adverse events (methaemoglobinaemia, anaemia, gastrointestinal symptoms) but no more serious adverse events. The authors conclude high-dose regimens were safe and effective, with the shorter course favored for adherence.

articles.headline: Genetic diversity and selection across the full P. vivax Duffy binding protein gene
articles.title_original: Complete gene sequence diversity, amplification, and natural selection of the Plasmodium vivax Duffy binding protein locus.
articles.authors: Putaporntip C, Kuamsab N, Tia T, Takashima Y, Jongwutiwes S
articles.details: Researchers analyzed the complete Pvdbp gene sequence, copy number variation, and duplication genotypes in 232 P. vivax isolates from northwestern, northeastern, eastern, and southern Thailand, also determining patients' Duffy blood-group genotypes. Pvdbp duplication (all Cambodian type) was found in 22 isolates (9.48%), with identical copies (n=17) or microheterogeneity (n=5). Across 254 sequences, nucleotide diversity was highest in PvdbpII, and positive selection occurred in both the 5' region and PvdbpII, with 26 positively selected codons of which 22 overlapped predicted B cell and/or T cell epitopes. No significant difference in Duffy phenotype frequencies was found between patients and healthy blood donors. The authors conclude sequence diversity extends beyond PvdbpII and that duplications may enhance immune evasion via increased gene dosage.

articles.headline: Two-thirds of P. knowlesi patients carry viable gametocytes
articles.title_original: Plasmodium knowlesi gametocyte carriage and load among malaria patients at Kapit Hospital in Sarawak, Malaysian Borneo.
articles.authors: Theivindran D, Hu TH, Mohamad DSA, Rosli N, Hii KC, Singh B, Siner A
articles.details: In a study of 295 hospital patients with PCR-confirmed single Plasmodium knowlesi infections, mature female gametocytes were detected by real-time PCR of pks25 mRNA transcripts. Viable gametocytes were present in 67.5% (199/295) of patients. Gametocyte load correlated positively with total parasitaemia (ρ=0.32, p=0.01), but showed no significant association with duration of illness before admission (ρ=0.28, p=0.7). Among gametocyte-positive patients, 40 (20%) had fewer than 500 transcript copies/µL, while 25 (12.5%) had elevated levels — 13 (10.8%) at 10,001–100,000 and 12 (6%) above 100,000. Authors conclude humans could potentially serve as infectious hosts, though this is not direct evidence of human-to-human transmission.

articles.headline: Malaria risk higher in villages near a dam in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Epidemiological profile and environmental determinants of Plasmodium falciparum infection in villages surrounding the Soum Agropole, Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Ouare N, Sorgho H, Hansson H, Rouamba T, Ilboudo H, Derra K, Kaboré B, Tahita MC, Rouamba E, Alifrangis M, Zida A, Magnussen P, Tinto H
articles.details: Monthly household-based surveys across five villages located 3–40 km from the Soum dam assessed Plasmodium falciparum infection by microscopy, with 13,786 participants. Asymptomatic carriage (24.5%) exceeded clinical cases (19.5%), giving a 12-month combined prevalence of 39.6% (95% CI 38.79–40.42). Prevalence was highest in Soum near the dam (42.1%) and lowest in Séguédin farther away (33.2%). Participants aged 5–19 years had the highest infection prevalence (43.6%–69.4% across villages). Unexpectedly, monthly rainfall was associated with decreased infection risk (OR=0.77, 95% CI 0.66–0.9). Authors highlight the impact of dam construction on malaria epidemiology.

articles.headline: Blocking Wnt signaling boosts P. falciparum liver-stage growth in vitro
articles.title_original: Inhibition of Wnt signaling in primary human hepatocytes promotes Plasmodium falciparum liver stage development.
articles.authors: Kanyal A, van Gemert GJ, Wu H, van der Starre A, de Wilt JHW, Bousema T, Sauerwein RW, Bártfai R, Yang ASP
articles.details: Researchers improved an in vitro model of Plasmodium falciparum liver-stage development by culturing primary human hepatocytes in medium with serum-replacement and signal transduction inhibitors to delay loss of parasite permissiveness. Integrating hepatocyte transcriptomic profiles with parasite phenotypes identified host signaling pathways affecting development. Inhibition of the Wnt pathway significantly increased the size and maturity of liver-stage schizonts while preserving hepatocyte metabolic activity and epithelial character. The authors present this as an improved liver-stage model to aid discovery of therapies targeting P. falciparum liver stages.

articles.headline: Caregiver awareness of RTS,S malaria vaccine explored in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Awareness, perception and influences on uptake of the RTS,S/AS01 malaria vaccine among caregivers for children under 5 years in South West region, Cameroon.
articles.authors: Anye JC, Niba LL, Njimanted O, Fang EE, Itoe BT, Fung EG, Ewi-Kang HG, Kimbi HK
articles.details: This exploratory qualitative study used two focus group discussions with 20 mothers and caregivers of children aged 11–30 months to examine awareness, perceptions, and influences on uptake of the RTS,S/AS01 malaria vaccine in urban and rural communities of the South West Region of Cameroon. Urban caregivers had high but often limited awareness, sometimes confusing the vaccine with other malaria interventions, while rural caregivers showed uneven awareness but strong trust in health workers. Perceived benefits like reduced episode severity boosted confidence; barriers included communication gaps, misinformation, gender dynamics, long waiting times, and distance to facilities. Authors stress clear communication and trust-building to support equitable uptake.

articles.headline: MRI reveals brain injury in fatal adult cerebral malaria case
articles.title_original: Radiological findings in fulminant cerebral malaria: a rare neuroimaging presentation.
articles.authors: Nayak B, Mohanty S, Das S, Panda S, Mishra SB
articles.details: This case report describes a 54-year-old woman with hypertension and hypothyroidism who presented with fever, seizures, and altered sensorium, and was in shock with severe metabolic acidosis and multiorgan failure. Rapid diagnostic test and peripheral smear confirmed Plasmodium falciparum infection. CT brain was normal, but MRI showed bilateral T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in the basal ganglia, thalami, centrum semiovale, and periventricular white matter with patchy diffusion restriction, suggestive of cytotoxic oedema. Despite timely artesunate, dialysis, and organ support, the patient died of refractory multiorgan failure. Authors emphasize the diagnostic value of MRI in adult cerebral malaria when CT is inconclusive.

articles.headline: Serratia bacteria found in Burkina Faso malaria mosquitoes as transmission-blocking candidate
articles.title_original: Molecular characterization of potential Plasmodium-Blocking Serratia spp. bacteria in field-caught malaria mosquito in Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Traoré H, Gnambani EJ, Hien DFS, Yao RK, Konkobo M, Sodré AF, Somda MB, Ouedraogo AS, Diabaté A, Bilgo E
articles.details: This field study isolated and characterized Serratia bacteria from Anopheles gambiae complex mosquitoes collected at three sites in Houet Province, Burkina Faso, using PCR targeting the luxS gene and 16S rRNA sequencing. Overall Serratia prevalence among malaria vectors was 13.3%, varying by mosquito developmental stage, locality, and organ. The local strains were distinct from Serratia previously reported in Ghana and Nigeria, and only one Anopheles coluzzii mosquito was co-infected with Serratia and Plasmodium falciparum. Authors conclude these wild Serratia spp. may have potential for malaria control.

articles.headline: Perspective: One Health lessons from China's malaria elimination
articles.title_original: China's malaria elimination: One Health lessons for vector-borne disease governance.
articles.authors: Liu J, Liu Y, Cheng G
articles.details: This perspective interprets China's WHO certification as malaria-free in 2021 through a One Health governance framework, drawing on a British Medical Journal series documenting regional case studies in Hainan Province, the Huai River Basin, and Yunnan Province, plus national strategies and larval management. It emphasizes four pillars—integrated surveillance, regional cooperation, ecological intervention, and technological innovation—as a pathway for sustainable vector control and future prevention of vector-borne diseases amid climate change. No quantitative outcomes are reported.

articles.headline: Geohelminth-malaria co-infection low but rises in third-trimester pregnancy, Kinshasa
articles.title_original: Geohelminthiasis and Malaria Co-Infection in Pregnant Women in Kinshasa: Case of Lisungi and Lukunga Hospitals in Democratic Republic of the Congo.
articles.authors: Dianzenza C, Tshiongo JK, Kuseke L, Muswele F, Kasereka G, Mangungulu D, Laken EK, Tevuzula VM, Kayentao K, Mavoko HM
articles.details: This cross-sectional study of 336 pregnant women at two hospitals in Kinshasa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, assessed geohelminth-malaria co-infection using thick smears, stool examinations, and hemoglobin measurements. Co-infection occurred in 5.7% and geohelminthiasis alone in 7.7%, with Ascaris lumbricoides most frequent (6.3%), followed by Trichuris trichiura (1.5%) and Ancylostoma duodenale (0.3%). The third trimester was significantly associated with increased risk of co-infection and geohelminthiasis (adjusted OR = 5.8; 95% CI 1.36-9.23; p = 0.0077), but no significant association was found with maternal anemia. Authors recommend systematic screening during pregnancy.

articles.headline: Fluorescent dye tracks mitochondrial changes during artemisinin-induced parasite growth arrest
articles.title_original: Fluorescence-based bioassay for the detection of changes in mitochondrial physiology during artemisinin-induced growth arrest of blood-stage Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Whangviboonkij N, Kulkeaw K, Imwong M, Kobpornchai P
articles.details: This laboratory study used the mitochondrion-specific, pH-sensitive fluorochrome Mtphagy to monitor mitochondrial physiology in blood-stage Plasmodium falciparum during dihydroartemisinin (DHA) exposure, relevant to artemisinin resistance and ring-stage survival. After DHA exposure, Mtphagy fluorescence increased in early ring-form trophozoites (indicating decreased pH), and colocalization with MitoTracker was slightly reduced following growth arrest. Inhibiting mitochondrial fission and the PI3K-mediated pathway significantly reduced Mtphagy-positive parasites and delayed recrudescence. Authors conclude the Mtphagy assay is a useful tool for detecting mitochondrial changes and that targeting mitochondrial dynamics may help overcome ART resistance.

articles.headline: Sudanese health workers show major knowledge gaps on malaria vaccination
articles.title_original: Preliminary insights into knowledge and attitudes toward malaria vaccination among Sudanese healthcare workers: an exploratory cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Hamza DAA, Suliman OKB, Sam AAK, Abdelrahman AOO, Elemam TAA, Fadul EMAM
articles.details: This exploratory cross-sectional online survey of 222 healthcare workers across Sudan assessed knowledge and attitudes toward malaria vaccination (RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M), with adequate knowledge and positive attitudes each defined as scores ≥70%. Only 9% (n=20) demonstrated adequate knowledge and 21.6% (n=48) had positive attitudes; while 66.2% had heard of malaria vaccines, 71.2% were unaware of WHO recommendations and 76.6% did not know the target parasite. Negative attitudes (78.4%) centered on safety, affordability, and trust. Positive attitudes were more common among males, those with >5 years' experience, and private-sector workers (all p<0.05). Authors warn of vaccine hesitancy risk and urge targeted training.

articles.headline: Brazilian federal intervention in Yanomami lands: malaria cases rose, hospitalizations fell
articles.title_original: What impact did the Brazilian Federal Government's intervention have on malaria cases in the Yanomami Indigenous lands?
articles.authors: de Aguiar Barros J, Granja F, Siqueira PEL, da Silva Epifânio I, Coelho RR, Ferreira-da-Cruz MF
articles.details: This descriptive retrospective study used Brazil's Health Information System data on reported malaria cases, hospitalizations, and deaths in Roraima from 2021 to 2024 to assess the 2023 federal government intervention in the Yanomami Indigenous Land. Roraima reported 117,214 indigenous malaria cases overall. After the intervention, cases increased significantly (52,210 to 65,004; p=0.0005), hospitalizations decreased (787 to 638; p=0.0304), and deaths remained steady (111 total; p=0.9089). P. vivax was dominant. Exported cases dropped sharply to Maranhão (-95.5%), Pará (-86.8%), and Amazonas (-71.5%). Authors conclude case detection improved while hospitalizations declined and mortality was unchanged.

articles.headline: Anopheles species distribution across Burkina Faso's three climatic zones
articles.title_original: Spatial Distribution and Biodiversity of Anopheles Mosquito Species Across Climatic Zones in Burkina Faso: Implications for Malaria Vector Control.
articles.authors: Zongo ON, Kiendrebeogo E, Sow BBD, Kientega M, Toé I, Sanou R, Yemien SOG, Sawadogo G, Kaboré H, Agolinou A, Traore N, Epopa PS, Millogo AA, Niang A, Namountougou M, Maiga H, Diabaté A
articles.details: This study assessed Anopheles biodiversity and spatial distribution across the three climatic zones of Burkina Faso to guide vector control. Indoor resting mosquitoes were collected from 67 health districts across 13 regions between September and December 2022 using pyrethroid spray catches. Of 30,521 Anopheles identified, An. gambiae s.l. dominated (94.4%), with highest abundance in the Sudano-Sahelian zone and greatest diversity in the Soudanian zone. Molecular analysis of 2,026 specimens showed An. coluzzii predominant in Sahelian (74.9%) and Sudano-Sahelian (71.2%) zones, while An. gambiae s.s. was most frequent in the Soudanian zone (53.8%). Authors emphasize locally adapted control strategies.

articles.headline: Periostin (OSF-2) has dual effect on phagocytosis of malaria-infected red cells
articles.title_original: Effect of periostin (OSF-2) on phagocytosis of Plasmodium-infected erythrocytes.
articles.authors: Chin JY, Mohammed-Syafiei MN, Lim YJ, Chong GX, Suhaimi MN, Phong ZY, Ng YL, Lau YL, Sam IC, Rénia L, Lee WC
articles.details: This in vitro study investigated phagocytosis of P. falciparum- and P. knowlesi-infected erythrocytes by human monocytic THP-1 cells in the presence of recombinant human periostin (OSF-2), a secretory protein that stimulates rosette formation. Using culturable parasite isolates with and without uninfected erythrocytes, and antibody blocking assays, the authors found OSF-2 had a dual role: when rosetting was prevented by purifying infected cells, OSF-2 increased phagocytosis in a CD36-dependent, rapidly reversible manner, including when THP-1 cells were pre-primed. However, when rosetting was induced by adding uninfected erythrocytes, OSF-2 reduced phagocytosis. Findings highlight complex parasite-host interactions in pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Drug-resistance markers in P. falciparum from two western Kenya regions
articles.title_original: Comparative molecular surveillance of polymorphisms in chloroquine resistance transporter, multidrug resistance 1, and Kelch 13 genes associated with antimalarial drug resistance in Plasmodium falciparum isolates from Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Bungei J, Ouma C, Zhong D, Oyweri J, Lee MC, Zhou G, Atieli H, Githure J, Wang C, Yan G
articles.details: This cross-sectional study genotyped Pfcrt, Pfmdr1, and Pfk13 single-nucleotide polymorphisms in P. falciparum from symptomatic patients in the Lake-endemic Nyando sub-County (Kisumu) and highland epidemic-prone Marani sub-County (Kisii) of western Kenya. Detected mutations included Pfcrt M74I, N75E, K76T (each 5.4%); Pfmdr1 N146I (0.9%), Y184F (48.2%), D1246Y (3.6%); and Pfk13 S522C (0.9%), R561H (0.9%), A675V (12.8%). Kisii showed significantly higher Pfcrt mutant alleles (11.5%) and Pfk13 A675V (27.5%) (p<0.05). The Pfcrt CVIET haplotype persisted, and novel Pfmdr1 N146I and rare Pfk13 S522C/R561H mutations emerged in Kisumu. Given artemether-lumefantrine is first-line, authors stress continued surveillance.

articles.headline: Malaria RDT self-testing feasible and acceptable in three African communities
articles.title_original: Feasibility of self-testing for acute malaria using rapid diagnostic tests in three peri-urban sub-Saharan African community settings.
articles.authors: Brumwell A, Bemer M, Lauff A, Morton JF, Thomas KK, Moe CA, Cooper S, Humphries H, Sharma A, Pollard D, Kwena Z, Sang N, Winters A, van Heerden A, Bukusi E, Rech D, Drain P
articles.details: This feasibility study evaluated rapid diagnostic test (RDT) self-testing for malaria in 100 households each in Migori County, Kenya; KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa; and Copperbelt, Zambia, surveying usability, acceptability, and preferences. Among 225 participants in Kenya, 80 in South Africa, and 163 in Zambia, 25 (11.5%), 0 (0.0%), and 3 (1.8%) tested positive, respectively. Participants interpreted RDTs with 100% sensitivity and 99.7% specificity versus trained staff, with only one incorrect interpretation, and over 96% across sites found the RDT easy to use. Perceived comfort for future self-testing was high in Kenya (92%) and Zambia (86%) and moderate in South Africa (66%), and the authors conclude RDT self-testing is highly acceptable and feasible.

articles.headline: Biometric fingerprinting improves malaria patient ID in Papua, Indonesia
articles.title_original: Enhancing unique identification of patients with malaria using biometric fingerprinting in Papua, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Fransisca L, Kenangalem E, Ubra RR, Ley B, Price RN, Poespoprodjo JR, Douglas NM
articles.details: A three-phase before-and-after study at Wania clinic in Timika, Papua, Indonesia, over 16 months tested whether biometric fingerprinting added to an existing 'malaria card' system improved unique identification of malaria patients; 7471 patients attended the 'Malaria Corner'. The proportion linkable to a pre-existing malaria card number rose from 44.4% (660/1487) in Phase 1 to 48.9% (1348/2756) in Phase 3 (difference 4.5%, 95% CI 1.4–7.7%); among fingerprinted patients in Phase 3 it was 55.8% (1117/2001). Refusal of fingerprinting was rare (0.39% and 0.26%). Authors conclude biometrics can improve identification in resource-limited settings, though unreadable fingerprints, especially in children, were a challenge.

articles.headline: Poly-α microsatellite shows high P. falciparum diversity in two Kenyan sites
articles.title_original: Characterizing Plasmodium falciparum genetic diversity and complexity of infections in clinical malaria infections in Western and Coastal Kenya using the poly-alpha microsatellite marker.
articles.authors: Abade NA, Mgawe C, Ndwiga L, Kimenyi KM, Ochola-Oyier LI
articles.details: Researchers genotyped the neutral poly-α microsatellite marker in dried blood spots from 93 participants in a therapeutic efficacy study in Busia County (2016) and 92 from a monitoring study in Kilifi (2020), Kenya, to characterize P. falciparum genetic diversity and complexity of infection (COI) before applying it to molecular correction in efficacy studies. Genotyping succeeded in about 97% (Busia) and 87% (Kilifi). Busia infections were mainly polyclonal (80%) with mean COI 2.9 (p<0.0001); Kilifi were mostly monoclonal (52.5%) with mean COI 1.7. Expected heterozygosity was similar (Busia 0.92; Kilifi 0.90), with effective alleles 10.8 vs 9.3. Authors conclude poly-α is a useful tool for therapeutic efficacy studies and intervention evaluation.

articles.headline: Optimized selective whole-genome amplification speeds P. falciparum surveillance
articles.title_original: Enhancing whole genome DNA amplification of Plasmodium falciparum for advanced molecular surveillance in malaria control.
articles.authors: Tan MKI, de Jesus MCS, Phelan JE, Thorpe J, Ward D, Batra R, Patel A, Nolder D, Chiodini P, Sutherland CJ, Edgeworth J, Clark TG, Campino S
articles.details: This study optimized selective whole genome amplification (SWGA), using phi29 DNA polymerase, for routine genomic surveillance of P. falciparum. Thirteen clinical whole blood samples from returning travellers underwent SWGA at 2, 8, and 16 h incubations, with three additional samples run at 3 h using the newer EquiPhi29 polymerase; conditions were compared by amplification/sequencing yield, parasite DNA recovery, genome coverage, and SNP concordance (Cohen's Kappa). The 8 and 16 h durations gave comparable yields with >90% SNP concordance. The new protocol achieved over 63% genome coverage in three hours while cutting reagent costs by nearly 60%, and drug resistance profiles (Malaria-Profiler) stayed consistent. Authors conclude this enables faster, cheaper malaria genomic surveillance.

articles.headline: Explainable deep learning achieves ~98% accuracy for malaria blood smear diagnosis
articles.title_original: Trustworthy deep learning for malaria diagnosis using explainable artificial intelligence.
articles.authors: Parveen R, Qui B, Song W, Al-Kahtani N, Jamjoom MM, Mostafa SM, Sultan N, Fatima J
articles.details: This study evaluated deep learning models for detecting malaria from blood smear images. Among four CNNs (MobileNetV2, VGG19, InceptionV3, ResNet18), ResNet18 achieved the highest F1-score of 96.33%. Two hybrid architectures, Xception and Inception-ResNetV2, were fine-tuned on 27,090 images from the Kaggle malaria collection, reaching about 98% accuracy on validation and test sets, with accuracy remaining 97–98% on an independent Harvard Dataverse thick-smear dataset under varied staining/imaging. Three explainable-AI techniques (Grad-CAM, LIME, SHAP) provided spatial, superpixel, and pixel-level interpretability and revealed morphological/staining artifacts behind misdiagnoses. Authors present this as an accurate, interpretable, scalable diagnostic framework for resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Metabolism-chromatin link controls ribosomal RNA gene switching in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: A metabolism-chromatin axis promotes differential ribosomal RNA transcription in the human malaria parasite.
articles.authors: Couble JE, Vignolini T, Doré G, Mussgnug S, Lombard B, Richard M, Loew D, Büttner M, Dueñas-Sánchez R, Poschet G, Bryant JM, Baumgarten S
articles.details: This study examined how P. falciparum differentially silences individual rDNA (ribosomal RNA) genes in the human host that are de-repressed after transmission to the mosquito vector. Defining epigenetic signatures of rRNA transcription, the authors found that rDNA silencing depends on aerobic glycolysis, and that disrupting NAD+ regeneration during lactate fermentation promotes rDNA de-repression. They identified the sirtuin histone deacetylase Sir2a as the mediator linking fluctuating NAD+ levels to transcriptional output. The authors conclude that rDNA activation is coupled to the parasite's metabolic state as it transitions from aerobic glycolysis to mitochondrial respiration during host-to-vector transmission.

articles.headline: Pre-infection gut microbiome signatures predict malaria parasite levels
articles.title_original: Distinct gastrointestinal microbial signatures predict parasite levels in controlled Plasmodium infections in both rhesus macaques and humans.
articles.authors: Gustin AT, Broedlow CA, Hager K, Coronado E, Wangari S, Iwayama N, Ahrens CY, Garrison WD, Guerriero KA, De Paris K, Londono-Renteria B, Gale M Jr, Klatt NR, Kublin JG, Manuzak JA
articles.details: Using 16S rRNA sequencing of pre-infection gastrointestinal samples, researchers experimentally tested whether gut microbiome composition predicts acute Plasmodium parasitemia in rhesus macaques infected with P. fragile and in adult humans from a controlled human malaria infection trial (NCT04072302, originally testing the antimalarial KAF156) infected with P. falciparum. Distinct pre-infection microbial signatures were associated with increased risk of above-median parasitemia in both species, and a Bifidobacterium feature set stratified parasitemia risk. The authors conclude pre-infection microbiome composition indicates Plasmodium risk and that the macaque model mirrors human dynamics, supporting microbiome-targeting prevention approaches.

articles.headline: Histone lactylation identified as new epigenetic mark in Plasmodium
articles.title_original: Histone lactylation: A new epigenetic mark in the malaria parasite Plasmodium.
articles.authors: Jabre I, Andoh NE, Naldoni J, Gregory W, Mbye H, Yoon CE, Cunnington AJ, Georgiadou A, Blagborough AM, Merrick CJ
articles.details: This laboratory study characterised histone lactylation for the first time in malaria parasites, detecting it in the human parasites P. falciparum and P. knowlesi and in vivo in the rodent models P. yoelii and P. berghei. Histone lactylation increased with elevated lactate levels in vitro and in vivo, and mass spectrometry showed lactylation of lysines on several parasite histones and many non-histone chromatin proteins. The mark was less abundant and less inducible in P. knowlesi than P. falciparum. The authors propose it acts as a metabolic sensor, noting severe falciparum malaria causes hyperlactataemia, which in P. yoelii correlated with transcriptomic programmes suggesting metabolic 'dormancy'.

articles.headline: Dengue, malaria, and typhoid co-infections common among febrile Nigerian patients
articles.title_original: Dengue virus infection amongst malaria and typhoid fever suspected acute febrile patients in the Niger river basin of Nigeria.
articles.authors: Okpanachi RO, Omatola CA, Abraham JO, Okolo MO, Conco-Biyela T, Onoja AB, Adamu AM
articles.details: In a cross-sectional study, blood samples from 200 consenting febrile patients at three health facilities in the Niger River Basin area of Nigeria were tested for dengue virus (NS1 antigen and IgG/IgM antibodies), malaria parasites (parasite examination), and typhoid (Widal test). Anti-DENV antibodies were detected in 38 (19.0%) patients: 27 (13.5%) IgG, 8 (4.0%) IgM, and 1 (0.5%) each for NS1 alone, combined IgG/IgM, and IgG/IgM/NS1. Malaria and typhoid positivity were 104 (52%) and 125 (62.5%); monoinfections were dengue 27 (13.5%), malaria 37 (18.5%), and typhoid 58 (29%), with 7 (4%) triple infections. Risk factors included age 41-50, farming, marriage, and uncovered or stagnant water, with back pain and nausea as associated symptoms; the authors stress differential diagnosis to avoid inappropriate treatment.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence 13.4% among pregnant women in Northwest Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Prevalence and associated factors of malaria among pregnant women in Maraki Health Center, North West, Gondar, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Dagnaw M, Indracanti M, Jan A, Ayalew J
articles.details: A facility-based cross-sectional study of 271 pregnant women attending antenatal care at Maraki Health Center, Northwest Ethiopia, used structured interviews and laboratory testing (RDT and microscopy) for malaria diagnosis. Malaria prevalence was 13.4%. Factors independently associated with infection included not using insecticide-treated nets (AOR=3.2; 95% CI: 1.4-7.5), not receiving IPTp with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (AOR=2.8; 95% CI: 1.1-6.9), fewer than four ANC visits (AOR=4.6; 95% CI: 1.9-11.2), living near stagnant water (AOR=2.5; 95% CI: 1.1-5.8), and attending ANC during the rainy season (AOR=1.9; 95% CI: 1.0-3.7). Authors recommend strengthening ITN distribution, IPTp-SP uptake, ANC attendance, and environmental sanitation.

articles.headline: Protocol to sort antigen-specific B cells from malaria-infected mice
articles.title_original: Protocol to identify and sort antigen-specific B cells from Plasmodium-infected mice.
articles.authors: Calôba C, Minns AM, Lindner SE, Vijay R
articles.details: This methods protocol describes how to identify and sort an antigen-specific B cell population using SpyCage reagents tailored to bind the MSP1-19 antigen in a rodent malaria model. Steps cover preparation of splenic single-cell suspension, B cell enrichment, and staining to sort live cells for downstream applications including single-cell RNA, V(D)J, and ATAC sequencing. No quantitative results are reported as this is a protocol article.

articles.headline: Long-read sequencing protocol profiles P. falciparum antigen diversity
articles.title_original: Protocol for genomic surveillance of Plasmodium falciparum antigens using amplicon-based PacBio long-read sequencing.
articles.authors: Plaza DF
articles.details: This laboratory protocol identifies and classifies structurally diverse variants of the Plasmodium falciparum antigen genes msp1, msp2, glurp, and csp using an amplicon-based long-read sequencing platform. It describes PCR barcoding, PacBio circular consensus sequencing (CCS), in silico PCR-based size variant calling, and advanced data analysis in Galaxy. By resolving full-length sequences for each antigenic clone, the approach measures infection complexity, constructs isolate phylogenies, and supports vaccine design.

articles.headline: Forest data boosts cost-effective risk-based malaria surveillance
articles.title_original: Integrating forest data and health facility surveys to optimise risk-based malaria surveillance in the Philippines.
articles.authors: Fornace KM, Reyes RA, Macalinao MLM, Lim JS, Bareng APN, Luchavez JS, Hafalla JCR, Espino FEJ, Matthiopoulos J, Drakeley CJ
articles.details: This study integrated health facility surveys with remote-sensing environmental data to optimize malaria surveillance across a forested district in the Philippines, using convenience surveys of facility attendees whose residences were geolocated via tablet apps. Infection was assessed by microscopy, rapid diagnostic tests, and molecular methods. Health facility-based surveys increased detection by screening more individuals over wider areas, while routine diagnostic sensitivity was lower in forests. Risk-based surveillance incorporating forest data increased the probability of detecting malaria foci over three-fold compared with routine surveillance and was highly cost-effective.

articles.headline: Correction notice for paper on P. falciparum immune evasion
articles.title_original: Correction to "Immune Response and Evasion Mechanisms of Plasmodium falciparum Parasites".
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Hemozoin directly damages neurons in experimental cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Hemozoin induces neuronal injury primarily characterized by axon rupture and mitochondrial damage in experimental cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Li T, Dang D, Shen Y, Wang J, Huang Y, Zhu Q, Wang Y, Yang C, Li G, Sun J, Zhang A, Li P, Liang J, Zhao Y
articles.details: Using C57BL/6J mice infected with Plasmodium berghei ANKA to induce experimental cerebral malaria, plus in vitro primary cortical neurons stimulated with purified hemozoin (pHz), this study tested whether hemozoin (Hz) directly injures neurons. In ECM mouse cortex there was significant Hz deposition and reduced neuronal nuclei (NeuN) expression. pHz adhered to and was internalized into neurons, causing reduced dendritic arborization, axon rupture, and membrane disruption, plus mitochondrial structural damage, lowered membrane potential, reduced ATP, elevated ROS, increased nNOS activity, and decreased glutamate. The authors conclude Hz directly causes neuronal injury, informing adjuvant therapy development.

articles.headline: Review maps antimalarial resistance genes across Nigeria
articles.title_original: Molecular surveillance of antimalarial drug resistance genes in Nigeria: a systematic review and roadmap to malaria elimination.
articles.authors: Bamikole OJ, Fayehun AF, Uthman YA, Salako IA, Adedeji BA, Olufeagba MB, Amodu OK
articles.details: This systematic review summarized molecular surveillance of antimalarial drug resistance markers (Pfcrt, Pfmdr, Pfdhfr, Pfdhps, Pfk13) in Nigeria, searching PubMed, ScienceDirect, Ovid, and Scopus for studies published from 2010 to 2024. A total of 26 studies met inclusion criteria, revealing high overall prevalence of mutations linked to chloroquine and sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance, particularly in Pfcrt and Pfdhfr, while artemisinin-resistance Pfk13 mutations were less common. Regional disparities were evident, with generally lower Pfcrt prevalence in northern Nigeria than the south. The authors suggest chloroquine might be reconsidered only where resistance remains consistently low, contingent on confirmatory trials and continued surveillance.

articles.headline: 2025 funding cuts threaten African malaria programmes, demand domestic action
articles.title_original: African leaders and regional institutions need to take the bull by the horns: a perspective on the impact of the 2025 funding cuts to malaria programmes.
articles.authors: Osoro CB, Hill J
articles.details: This perspective examines the impact of sharp 2025 reductions in global malaria funding following the US government's termination of most global health programmes and declines in development aid from the UK, France, Germany, Canada, Switzerland, and others. It notes Africa carried 94% of the 263 million malaria cases in 2023, with malaria reducing GDP by up to 1.3% annually and causing half a billion lost workdays. Bed net distribution, seasonal malaria chemoprevention, and malaria indicator surveys have been severely disrupted. The authors argue African governments must increase domestic funding and build public-private partnerships, with leadership from the African Union, Africa CDC, and African Leaders Malaria Alliance.

articles.headline: Geospatial mapping pinpoints high-risk urban malaria zones in Accra, Ghana
articles.title_original: Fine-scale spatial mapping of urban malaria prevalence for microstratification in an urban area of Ghana.
articles.authors: Oppong SK, Dosoo DK, Peprah NY, Adu GA, Mohammed W, Rozier J, Kayan K, McPhail M, Amratia P, Alene KA, Asante KP, Gething PW, Malm KL
articles.details: This geospatial modeling study combined environmental, climatic, and urban covariates with malaria prevalence data from 12,371 surveyed children aged 6 months to 10 years to predict malaria risk across the Greater Accra Region of Ghana. Predicted prevalence in this age group ranged from 0 to 49%, with satellite-derived tasselled cap brightness, enhanced vegetation index, and urban covariates being predictive. The authors produced maps quantifying the probability of prevalence exceeding 10% (exceedance probability), identifying high-risk areas within districts earmarked for elimination. They conclude the maps can support national and subnational targeting of malaria interventions.

articles.headline: Molecular testing finds hidden asymptomatic malaria reservoir in Ecuadorian Amazon
articles.title_original: Asymptomatic parasitaemia in a malaria pre-elimination setting in the Putumayo area of the Ecuadorian Amazon basin.
articles.authors: van der Ende J, Dávila MV, Menting S, Sáenz FE, Hanscheid T, Schallig HDFH, Grobusch MP
articles.details: In a repeated cross-sectional study during the 2023 rainy season, researchers surveyed 293 asymptomatic individuals across four riverine communities in Putumayo, in Ecuador's Amazon border region, using RDTs, expert microscopy, and real-time qPCR targeting the 18S rRNA gene. RDTs detected no infections (0/293), microscopy found three P. vivax infections (1.0%, 95% CI 0.2-2.4%), and qPCR detected five cases (1.7%, 95% CI 0.6-3.9%), revealing two additional submicroscopic infections. High Cq values (mean 32.6) indicated very low parasite densities, with infections found only in mid-to-late rainy season, predominantly in males. The authors conclude this hidden, low-density reservoir is a blind spot requiring sensitive molecular surveillance and cross-border collaboration for elimination.

articles.headline: Malaria-infected red cells impair platelet production, explaining thrombocytopenia
articles.title_original: Interactions between Plasmodium falciparum-infected red blood cells and their extracellular vesicles with megakaryocytes: implications for platelet-like particle formation.
articles.authors: Nunthanasup N, Palasuwan A, Palasuwan D, Combes V
articles.details: This in vitro study examined how P. falciparum-infected red blood cells (pRBCs) and their extracellular vesicles (EVs) affect megakaryocytic differentiation and platelet-like particle (PLP) formation, using the human MEG-01 megakaryoblastic cell line differentiated with PMA and co-cultured with pRBCs, normal RBCs, or their EVs. Intact pRBCs transiently adhered to MEG-01 cells but suppressed megakaryocytic markers, downregulated NOTCH3, and altered apoptosis- and stress-related genes; PLP production rose under some conditions but clotting activity was impaired. RBC-EVs were internalized but caused minimal changes, while pRBCs and EVs selectively increased IL-8, RANTES, and MCP-1. The authors conclude defective platelet production is a novel mechanism contributing to malaria-associated thrombocytopenia.

articles.headline: Falling falciparum malaria tracks declining Burkitt's lymphoma in coastal Kenya
articles.title_original: A "Familiar Foe Revisited": examining the relationship between endemic Burkitt's lymphoma and changing malaria admissions in the coastal region of Kenya.
articles.authors: Mwaniki MK, Mohammed S, Kariuki N, Wamalwa DC, Mutisya F, Newton CR
articles.details: This study analyzed three decades (1990-2020) of pediatric admission data (ages 0-14) in coastal Kenya to examine trends in endemic Burkitt's lymphoma (BL) incidence relative to declining malaria admissions. Among 95 BL patients identified (72, 75.8%, male), cases fell across epochs: 29 cases (10-year cumulative incidence 93.1 per 100,000) in 1990-1999, 62 cases (130.3 per 100,000) in the second decade, and only 4 cases (10.2 per 100,000) in 2010-2020; the decline was significant (one-way ANOVA p<0.0001). Median parasite density also fell from 13,966 to 7,224 (Kruskal-Wallis p=0.0021), and BL correlated positively with P. falciparum infection (r=0.53, p=0.0024). The authors interpret the BL decline as plausibly explained by the sustained decline in falciparum malaria.

articles.headline: Review: experimental models for liver-stage malaria, with organoids as a path forward
articles.title_original: Experimental models of liver-stage malaria: Progress, gaps, and challenges.
