articles.authors: Nitaramorn N, Kulkeaw K, Imwong M
articles.details: This review examines in vitro experimental platforms for studying Plasmodium liver-stage infection, the asymptomatic phase that precedes erythrocytic disease and, in P. vivax, persists as dormant hypnozoites in hepatocytes that reactivate to cause relapse. It notes that current therapies are effective but pose risks for people with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency, motivating better models for drug development. The authors discuss strengths and limitations of existing intrahepatic culture systems and argue that three-dimensional, self-organizing organoids recreating native liver tissue could address key gaps, aiding preventive strategies and radical cures.

articles.headline: Dioscorea dumetorum tuber butanol fraction suppresses malaria in mice
articles.title_original: n-Butanol Fraction of Dioscorea dumetorum Tuber Aqueous Extract Exhibited Potent Antimalarial Activity in Plasmodium berghei-infected Mice.
articles.authors: Okeme U, Yakubu MT
articles.details: In a murine model, the n-butanol fraction of Dioscorea dumetorum (Esuru) tubers and its sub-fraction CCF1 produced the strongest, significant (p<0.05) parasite growth inhibition—49.13% and 40.80%, respectively—suppressing parasitemia in a dose-dependent manner. Researchers had screened the n-hexane, ethyl acetate, and n-butanol fractions, identified n-butanol as most potent, fractionated it by column chromatography into three sub-fractions, and chemically profiled the most potent (CCF1) by HPLC. CCF1 contained 11 compounds, most abundantly allantoin and protodioscin. The authors conclude these fractions are a promising source for novel antimalarial therapies or lead compounds.

articles.headline: Distance and education shape malaria treatment-seeking across ages in rural Malawi
articles.title_original: Predictors and characteristics of malaria treatment-seeking in Malawi: a longitudinal cohort study.
articles.authors: Wan C, Katsabola H, Sixpence A, Mangani C, Matengeni A, Taylor TE, Wilson ML, Valim C, Mathanga DP, Laufer MK, Cohee LM, Buchwald AG
articles.details: This community-based, open-enrollment longitudinal cohort study in rural Malawi assessed predictors of malaria treatment-seeking using monthly active case detection and passive case detection visits, with mixed-effects logistic and Poisson regression. Among 962 participants contributing 7293 visits (6794 ACD, 499 PCD), 82% (720/880) of treatment-seeking occurred in the formal sector. Longer walking time (OR=0.28 for >90 vs <30 min) and higher household head education (OR=0.41 for secondary vs none) reduced odds of formal-sector care. Older age, male sex, and greater facility distance lowered PCD attendance; fever raised it (OR=1.60, 1.16–2.21), but P. falciparum infection and parasite density did not. Overall PCD rate was 70 per 100 person-years. The authors call for more equitable community-based care across all ages.

articles.headline: Submicroscopic, mostly asymptomatic malaria common in Peruvian Amazon communities
articles.title_original: Hidden burden: submicroscopic Plasmodium spp. infections in indigenous populations of the Peruvian Amazon.
articles.authors: Saavedra-Samillán M, López-Ugarte LC, Sandoval-Bances J, Rojas LM, Udhayakumar V, Valdivia HO, Sanchez JF, Chenet SM
articles.details: This cross-sectional study in four indigenous communities of the Río Santiago District, Condorcanqui Province, Peru, used active case detection during the wet season with microscopy and qPCR to measure submicroscopic Plasmodium prevalence and associated factors. Of 926 individuals, 157 (17.0%) were Plasmodium-positive, with P. vivax most frequent; submicroscopic infection prevalence was 8.5% (79/926), highest in Caterpiza (20.3%, 35/172), and 74.7% (59/79) of these were asymptomatic. In multivariate analysis, asymptomatic status (aPR=2.32, 95% CI 1.63–3.29) and living in Caterpiza versus Alianza Progreso (aPR=2.34, 1.68–3.24) raised prevalence, while household animals lowered it 36% (aPR=0.64, 0.48–0.85). The authors urge molecular diagnostics in surveillance to detect hidden transmission.

articles.headline: Anxiety widespread among guardians of children with severe malaria in Malawi
articles.title_original: Prevalence of anxiety and associated factors among guardians of children admitted with severe malaria at Thyolo District Hospital in Malawi.
articles.authors: Chidzere N, Munthali C, Chapweteka G, Munthali T, Kumwenda P, Chikasema B, Mkwinda E, Chironda G
articles.details: This cross-sectional study at the paediatric ward of Thyolo District Hospital in Malawi assessed anxiety prevalence, severity, and correlates among 187 guardians of children admitted with severe malaria, using a structured questionnaire and the GAD-7 scale analyzed with Chi-square tests and logistic regression. Mild anxiety was most common (79.7%), followed by moderate (10.7%) and minimal (9.6%), with no severe cases. Higher anxiety was significantly associated with age, guardian type, education level, area of residence, and income source (all p<0.001), as well as longer hospital stays and repeated admissions (both p<0.001). The authors recommend routine anxiety screening and integrated psychological support in paediatric care.

articles.headline: Luminal tubulin acetylation not essential for Plasmodium, Toxoplasma survival
articles.title_original: Luminal acetylation of microtubules is not essential for Plasmodium berghei and Toxoplasma gondii survival.
articles.authors: Kumar T, Röver K, Stortz JF, Binder AM, Spreng B, Konert M, Meissner M, Frischknecht F, Jimenez-Ruiz E
articles.details: This study generated and characterized genetic mutants to test whether α-tubulin K40 luminal acetylation matters in apicomplexan parasites. In Plasmodium berghei, introducing a Q40K mutation in α1 tubulin did not affect parasite development or infectivity. In Toxoplasma gondii, αTAT was confirmed to drive microtubule acetylation, but contrary to previous reports its deletion had no impact on parasite growth in vitro. The authors conclude luminal K40 acetylation is not essential for microtubule function in either species, indicating functional redundancy and cytoskeletal plasticity.

articles.headline: Income, employment, insurance predict breast exam uptake in Tanzanian women
articles.title_original: Factors associated with the uptake of clinical breast examination for early detection of breast cancer: interaction analysis of the 2022 Tanzania Demographic and Health Survey and Malaria Indicator Survey.
articles.authors: Nyamhanga T, Luoga P, Paulo H, Wangwe P, Adam J, Eustace R
articles.details: A secondary analysis of nationally representative survey data examined factors associated with uptake of clinical breast examination (CBE) for early breast cancer detection among women aged 15-49, using weighted descriptive statistics and multivariate binary logistic regression. Women with low incomes, who were unemployed, and those without health insurance had lower odds of undergoing CBE, with interactions between age and residence and between age and education. Overall CBE uptake was very low at 5%. The authors call for targeted breast health awareness education across all health system levels.

articles.headline: Fatal severe malaria with nearly all WHO complications in endemic-area woman
articles.title_original: We Still Have to Fear Malaria: A Case Report of Severe Malaria With Almost all the Listed WHO Complications in a Patient Living in a Sub-Saharan Endemic Area.
articles.authors: Simeni Njonnou SR, Tsiazok Dongmo M, Djilo Kouonang LO, Tsafack Tapondjou B, Ndembe Djeni E, Pierre Choukem S
articles.details: This case report describes a 67-year-old woman with hypertension and bilateral knee osteoarthritis who presented with confusion and abnormal movements in a febrile context; both a rapid diagnostic test and thick blood smear were positive for malaria. She developed hemolytic anemia, severe hypoglycemia, status epilepticus, deep coma, diffuse ecchymoses, digital ischemia, respiratory failure, hepatocellular failure, acute kidney injury, and hyperkalemia (6.24 mmol/L). Despite therapeutic improvements in consciousness and organ function, she ultimately succumbed to sepsis. The authors emphasize malaria's severe complications, its potential fatality, and the importance of prevention.

articles.headline: PfENT1 and PfENT4 purine transporters highly conserved, viable drug targets
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity of Plasmodium falciparum equilibrative nucleoside transporters PfENT1 and PfENT4: Implications for purine-based antimalarial drug development.
articles.authors: Tashie W, de Koning HP, Duah-Quashie NO, Quashie NB
articles.details: This study characterized the genetic diversity of the Plasmodium falciparum equilibrative nucleoside transporters PfENT1 and PfENT4 in over 7,000 field isolates worldwide, filtering VCF files to detect SNPs and InDels and analyzing Illumina-generated sequences for copy number variations. Only SNPs were found, with missense variant frequencies of 4.5% in PfENT1 and 6.2% in PfENT4. Three common PfENT1 variants (F36S, V129I, L133F) had minor allele frequencies of 4.0%, 4.1%, and 3.0%, and four PfENT4 variants (I188F, N209I, A260T, S383A) had MAFs of 2.2%, 1.6%, 1.5%, and 38.9%. Allele frequencies were similar in rural and urban areas (p>0.05), leading the authors to conclude both genes are highly conserved and well suited as drug targets.

articles.headline: 24-SNP barcodes track malaria transmission shifts in western Kenya
articles.title_original: Assessing the impact of temporal changes in transmission on Plasmodium falciparum strains in Asembo, western Kenya (1996-2017) using within-host metrics via 24-SNP barcodes.
articles.authors: Vestal G, Zhou Z, Sergent S, Sheth M, Lee J, Otieno K, Kariuki S, Hill A, Ter Kuile FO, Lindblade KA, Slutsker L, Hamel MJ, Desai M, Gimnig JE, Samuels AM, Vigfusson Y, Shi YP
articles.details: Using a 24-SNP NGS barcode assay, this study genotyped smear-positive malaria samples from cross-sectional surveys in Asembo, western Kenya (1996, 2001, 2007, 2012, and 2017), applying StrainRecon and STIM to measure multiplicity of infection (MOI) and population genetic metrics (FST, IBD, Hs, Ne). FST, Hs, Ne, and population-level strain-relatedness showed no significant differentiation over time, but average MOI significantly decreased from 4.32 in 1996 to 3.34 in 2012, then rose to 3.49 in 2017. Within-host strain relatedness (IBD) was inversely correlated with MOI, and a novel slope metric suggested a shift toward co-transmission from 2001 to 2012 and a return to 1996-like superinfection levels by 2017. The authors present this as the first simple slope-based method using 24-SNP barcodes to distinguish superinfection from co-transmission in high-transmission settings.

articles.headline: Dual-AI bed nets cut malaria mosquito density over two years in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Three-year trends in malaria transmission parameters under deployment of Interceptor®G1, Interceptor®G2 and PermaNet®3.0 insecticide-treated bed nets in South-Western Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Tapsoba M, Guelbeogo WM, Sanou A, Zongo S, Gogue C, Debe S, Arnett K, Davis K, Shannon J, Digre P, Mwesigwa J, Tynuv K, Fornadel C, N'Falé S, Robertson M, Challenger JD, Tougri G, Gansané A, Ranson H, Olivier G, Wagman J
articles.details: A longitudinal entomological study from August 2019 to February 2022 compared three insecticide-treated net types across three health districts in South-Western Burkina Faso: pyrethroid-only (Interceptor G1), pyrethroid-chlorfenapyr (Interceptor G2), and pyrethroid-PBO (PermaNet 3.0), using Human Landing Catches and CDC light traps with P. falciparum detection by PCR. Of 73,101 female Anopheles collected, An. gambiae s.l. dominated (>59%). In Banfora (Interceptor G2), Anopheles density fell 88.7% over three years; pyrethroid-only nets in Gaoua showed rebound by year 3, and PBO nets in Orodara stayed stable. Sporozoite and entomological inoculation rates mirrored density, declining in year 2 but resurging by year 3. Authors conclude dual-AI nets sustained reductions for two years but durability and complementary control are needed.

articles.headline: Decade of data shows 96% malaria decline in Jimma Zone, Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Unraveling the spatiotemporal clustering of malaria incidence and modeling a decade of epidemiological data in Jimma Zone, Oromia, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Biruksew A, Demeke A, Birhanu Z, Golassa L, Sisay AL, Kemal J, Yewhalaw D
articles.details: This study modeled spatiotemporal patterns of malaria using ten years of data (2012-2021) from 15 districts of Jimma Zone, Oromia, Ethiopia, calculating annual parasite incidence (API) and standardized incidence ratio, with Moran's I for spatial clustering and ARIMA time-series for 2022-2026 forecasting. Of 1,718,471 individuals screened, 359,655 (20.93%) were confirmed positive; P. falciparum, P. vivax, and mixed infections accounted for 52.71%, 43.62%, and 3.67%. Incidence fell 96.13%, with API dropping from 87.97 to 3.40 per 1,000 by 2021. Distribution was random across districts, with seasonal peaks August-November (max September) and March-June. The ARIMA (0,1,3)x(1,1,0) forecast suggests stable incidence with seasonal fluctuations but no significant trend; authors stress continued surveillance in persistent areas such as Chora Botor and Shebe Sonbo.

articles.headline: Commentary questions whether Cabo Verde is truly malaria-free
articles.title_original: "Cabo Verde: is it really malaria-free?".
articles.authors: Diogo R, Carvalho BT, Mendonça L, da Cunha MR, Pedro DM, Paramés F, Marques T, Paulo SE, Cristino JM, Pereira ÁA
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Delayed diagnosis and severe outcomes in imported malaria in Vietnam
articles.title_original: Delayed diagnosis and severe outcomes in imported malaria in Vietnam highlighting systematic gaps in detection.
articles.authors: Nguyen TTT, Do CD, Nguyen BHT, Pham HTT, Nong VM, Van Vu G, Doan TT
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Room-temperature LAMP test detects knowlesi malaria with 100% accuracy
articles.title_original: Rapid Detection of Knowlesi Malaria Using Room-Temperature Stable and Ready-to-Use Colorimetric Loop-Mediated Isothermal Amplification Reagents.
articles.authors: Lai MY, Lau YL
articles.details: This study developed a room-temperature-stable, ready-to-use dried-format colorimetric loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) assay for detecting P. knowlesi malaria, eliminating the cold-chain requirement of traditional LAMP; positive samples turn yellow and negatives remain pink, readable without instruments. Tested on 75 clinical blood samples from district and state hospitals in Malaysia against nested PCR as reference, the dehydrated LAMP showed 100% sensitivity (95% CI: 92.1-100.0%) and 100% specificity (95% CI: 88.4-100.0%). Reagents remained stable at room temperature for up to 15 days. Authors conclude it is well suited for resource-limited settings where knowlesi malaria is endemic.

articles.headline: Home tablet neurorehab and brain-signal monitoring feasible for child cerebral malaria survivors
articles.title_original: A Pilot Feasibility Study of a Home Tablet-Based Neurorehabilitation Program and Serial Brain Vital Sign Monitoring for Survivors of Pediatric Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Macoun SJ, Silverman JM, Brunet PR, Lewis JM, Kafoteka E, Ziba T, Kirby ED, Ighalo J, D'Arcy RCN, O'Brien N
articles.details: This feasibility study enrolled children aged 3-12 years who had survived cerebral malaria, in which lasting sequelae are identified in 50% of survivors. Participants completed a 6-month in-home, tablet-based neurorehabilitation program (Dino Island) alongside serial brain-health assessments using event-related potentials measured with the Brain Vital Signs system. Both programs were feasible and easy to implement, with low attrition (5%), positive family ratings (100%), and parent-reported positive behavioral changes (60%); adequate ERP data acquisition and favorable affordability indicators were also found. The authors conclude the approach was feasible, well accepted, and appropriate in sub-Saharan Africa, justifying further research.

articles.headline: Transfer-learning CNNs classify malaria-infected blood cells with up to 89% accuracy
articles.title_original: Malaria Parasite Cell Classification Using Transfer Learning with State-of-the-Art CNN Architectures.
articles.authors: Laghari AA, Muhammad W, Memon ML, Hussain A, Kumar A
articles.details: This study applied deep learning with transfer learning to automatically detect and classify malaria parasites from microscopic blood smear images, addressing the slowness and variability of traditional microscopy. Eight pretrained CNN architectures (VGG16, VGG19, Inception-v3, ResNet-18/34/50/101, and Xception) were fine-tuned on labeled datasets of parasitized and uninfected cells and evaluated by precision, recall, F1-score, and support. ResNet-50 and ResNet-101 each reached about 89% accuracy and Xception about 88%, while VGG-16 reached about 80% due to a recall trade-off despite high precision. The authors conclude these models show strong balanced performance, validating the transfer-learning approach.

articles.headline: Modeling shows operational factors shape next-generation bednet effectiveness against malaria
articles.title_original: Cascades of effectiveness of next-generation insecticide-treated nets against malaria, from entomological trials to real-life conditions.
articles.authors: Champagne C, Lemant J, Assenga A, Kibondo UA, Lekundayo RG, Mbuba E, Moore J, Muganga JB, Ntabaliba WS, Odufuwa OG, Swai JK, Alexa M, Goers R, Golumbeanu M, Chitnis N, Ross A, N'Guessan R, Moore S, Pothin E
articles.details: Using a mathematical model parameterized with entomological data and validated against randomized controlled trial results, researchers quantified the impact of two next-generation insecticide-treated nets—Interceptor G2 (chlorfenapyr-pyrethroid) and Olyset Plus (piperonyl butoxide-pyrethroid)—from entomological efficacy to population-level effectiveness against malaria amid spreading insecticide resistance. They found that beyond entomological factors, operational factors including functional survival, net use, and in-bed exposure critically influence overall and product-specific effectiveness. The authors present results for Tanzania and provide a dashboard allowing users to explore product selection based on setting-specific factors.

articles.headline: No validated artemisinin-resistance K13 mutations found in Iranian P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Absence of Artemisinin Resistance-Associated Mutations in the Plasmodium Falciparum Kelch 13 (K13) Propeller Domain Gene Following Widespread Use of Artemisinin-Based Combination Therapy in Iran.
articles.authors: Mohammadi S, Forouzesh F, Mehrizi AA, Shafi ZSM, Pakdel JD, Raeisi A, Pirahmadi S
articles.details: This molecular surveillance study assessed pfk13 gene point mutations linked to artemisinin resistance in Iranian P. falciparum isolates, 18 years after the nationwide introduction of artemisinin-based combination therapies. Sixty finger-prick blood samples were collected from patients with symptomatic, uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria during 2022-2023; nested PCR confirmed infection via the 18S ssrRNA gene and amplified pfk13 for sequencing. Of 50 successfully sequenced samples, 98% (49/50) carried the wild-type allele, and one harbored a novel nonsynonymous mutation, R513C (C1537T). The authors conclude that no validated resistance mutations were detected, suggesting artemisinin remains effective, and that continued surveillance is warranted.

articles.headline: Recombinant-protein VAR2CSA antibody assays unreliable for pregnancy malaria exposure in Brazil
articles.title_original: VAR2CSA-reactive IgG in Brazilian women exposed to Plasmodium falciparum or P. vivax infection during pregnancy.
articles.authors: Dos Santos MI, Dombrowski JG, Hoffmann-Veltung H, Quintana MdP, Marinho CRF, Hviid L, Lopez-Perez M
articles.details: This study measured levels and Fc-afucosylation of VAR2CSA-reactive IgG in plasma from pregnant Brazilian women at delivery, using full-length VAR2CSA expressed in insect cells (FV2BIC) or Chinese hamster ovary cells (FV2CHO) and the native antigen (IT4VAR04) on infected-erythrocyte surfaces, alongside GLURP-specific (P. falciparum) and PvDBP-specific (P. vivax) IgG. FV2CHO-specific IgG levels were lower than FV2BIC-reactive levels, and only FV2CHO-specific IgG was restricted to women exposed to P. falciparum during pregnancy; FV2CHO IgG was markedly Fc-afucosylated. PvDBP-specific IgG was higher in P. vivax-exposed women but did not correlate with FV2-specific IgG. The authors caution against using insect-cell recombinant proteins to measure VAR2CSA exposure and find no support for PvDBP-induced cross-reactivity protecting against placental malaria.

articles.headline: In silico analysis finds druggable Hsp90 features in P. knowlesi
articles.title_original: Plasmodium knowlesi Heat Shock Protein 90s: In Silico Analysis Reveals Unique Druggable Structural Features.
articles.authors: Daniyan MO, Singh H, Blatch GL
articles.details: This computational study used protein sequence and structural analysis, phylogenetics, protein network mapping, protein-ligand interaction, and small-molecule docking to characterize the heat shock protein 90s of the zoonotic malaria parasite Plasmodium knowlesi (PkHsp90s). Four isoforms were identified (cytosolic, endoplasmic reticulum, mitochondrial, and apicoplast), and the glycine-rich helix loop (GHL) motif of cytosolic PkHsp90 was predicted to adopt a straight conformation forming a plasmodial-specific hydrophobic extension of the ATP-binding site lid domain, absent in human Hsp90s HSPC1 and HSPC3. Virtual screening identified compounds (ZINC22007970, ZINC724661072, ZINC724661078) predicted to bind strongly to the GHL pocket with weak or no binding to HSPC1. The authors conclude this supports rational antimalarial drug design targeting PkHsp90s.

articles.headline: Review of non-falciparum malaria species circulating in West Africa
articles.title_original: Circulation of non-falciparum malaria species in West Africa: A literature review from 2000 to 2024.
articles.authors: Garba MN, Moustapha LM, Diallo MA, Diongue K, Seck MC, Doutchi M, Ndiaye M, Ibrahim ML, Ndiaye D, Badiane AS
articles.details: This literature review examined the circulation of non-falciparum malaria species in West Africa using articles published between 2000 and 2024, selected via Google Search, PubMed Central, and the National Library of Medicine. A total of 31 articles were reviewed, distributed across Benin, Burkina Faso, Ivory Coast (2 each, 6.5%), Ghana and Nigeria (4 each, 12.9%), Mali (5, 16.1%), Niger (3, 9.7%), and Senegal (9, 29.0%). P. falciparum was reported in 29 papers (93.5%), P. malariae in 25 (80.6%), P. ovale in 24 (77.4%), and P. vivax in 11 (35.5%). RDTs were used in 17 papers (54.8%), of which 12 (70.6%) were PfHRP2-based and 5 (29.4%) P.f/Pan-based. The authors underscore the need for more sensitive diagnostics and modified control strategies addressing asymptomatic infections.

articles.headline: Imported malaria cases profiled in Pernambuco, Brazil, 2001–2022
articles.title_original: Clinical and epidemiological profiles of confirmed malaria cases in the state of Pernambuco, Brazil, from 2001 to 2022.
articles.authors: Guedes DL, Guimarães HMR, Padilha MCG, Pinto MCP, Silva MRND, Andrade EVO
articles.details: This descriptive observational study analyzed 350 malaria cases reported in Pernambuco, Brazil, a nonendemic area, during 2001–2022. Most patients were men (75.1%), Mixed-race (51.1%), urban residents (81.1%), with a median age of 32 years; infections mostly originated in Brazil (52%) or Angola (33%). Median time from symptom onset to testing was 10 days for Indigenous, 5 for Black, and 4 for Mixed-race and White individuals, and longer for those with lower education (6.5 vs. 3 days), but multivariate analysis found ethnicity, education, and geographic displacement were not significant predictors of diagnostic delay. Cases showed a significant seasonal pattern (higher in the first half of the year, p=0.0382), the declining annual incidence trend was not significant (p=0.138), and P. vivax (49.7%) and P. falciparum (47.5%) predominated.

articles.headline: Burkina Faso free healthcare policy linked to lower child malaria
articles.title_original: Evaluating the effects of the free healthcare policy on clinical visits and malaria among children in Burkina Faso: a modeling study of past trends and future forecasts.
articles.authors: Ouédraogo AL, Offosse MJ, Zhang J, Toé S, Yaméogo P
articles.details: This modeling study evaluated the effects of Burkina Faso's 2016 free healthcare policy on clinical visits, malaria prevalence, and severe malaria in children under five, using routine EMR data analyzed with Generalized Linear Mixed Effects Models in a Differences-in-Differences design (baseline 2015 vs. 2018) and Bayesian hierarchical forecasts through 2020. Across 344,935 clinical visits from 199,664 children in 192 villages, visits rose from 1.1 to 2 per child (p<0.001, 82% increase), though children within 5 km of a facility accessed more care than remote ones (2.2 vs. 1.7, p<0.001). Malaria prevalence fell from 98% to 80% and severe malaria from 6% to 2.2% (both p<0.001), with forecasts of further declines (prevalence to 64%, severe malaria to 1.02%) by 2020. Authors highlight improved access but persistent inequities.

articles.headline: Donor red blood cells drive variability in P. falciparum culture
articles.title_original: Improving reproducibility of Plasmodium falciparum culture: a large-language-model-driven literature review and a parasite growth variability assessment of donor red blood cells.
articles.authors: Mahomed SM, Hanscheid T, Almeida Y, Grobusch MP, Rebelo M
articles.details: This dual investigation combined a large language model-assisted systematic review of 963 PubMed-indexed articles (2000–2025) on red blood cell (RBC) reporting in malaria culture with an experimental analysis of parasite growth variability using P. falciparum strain 3D7 cultured with RBC from 69 malaria-naïve volunteers (24 with detailed haematological profiling). Of 314 studies giving RBC source, 61.1% omitted donor origin, 72.6% omitted blood group, and only 6.0% reported anticoagulant; Type O RBC were used in 54.7% of those reporting blood group. Experimentally, parasite growth after 96 h varied 6.4-fold across volunteers (range 3.0–19.3), reproducible over three years; low-growth volunteers had lower haemoglobin (12.3 vs. 14.8 g/dL, p=0.03), more anaemia history (p=0.01), and greater malaria-endemic ancestry (p=0.03), though no consistent predictor emerged. The authors propose the STRuBeC minimum reporting standard.

articles.headline: Mitochondrial gene regulation signatures in severe P. vivax malaria
articles.title_original: Integrative transcriptomic and machine learning approaches to decipher mitochondrial gene regulation in severe Plasmodium vivax malaria.
articles.authors: Roy P, Aggarwal Y, Kochar SK, Kochar DK, Das A
articles.details: This study profiled mitochondrial gene expression in severe Plasmodium vivax malaria, integrating transcriptomic data with machine learning to examine both protein-coding sense transcripts and long non-coding natural antisense transcripts (NATs) across the parasite's intra-erythrocytic development cycle. The authors report distinct, clinically relevant expression signatures in which NATs appear to act as regulators tightly linked to mitochondrial pathways and translational machinery, suggesting a strategy to fine-tune mitochondrial function under severe disease. They emphasize these are preliminary findings from a limited clinical cohort requiring validation in larger populations, and position mitochondrial NATs as potential antimalarial drug targets. No quantitative effect sizes are reported in the abstract.

articles.headline: Abbott-Bioline malaria RDT highly insensitive for P. vivax vs microscopy
articles.title_original: The underperforming Abbott-Bioline Malaria Ag P.f/P.v rapid diagnostic test: a whiter shade of pale - where the truth is not plain to see.
articles.authors: Thein KS, Soe K, Tun SKS, Kuipers R, Janssen S, Tarning J, Htun Y, Day NP, Kyaw TT, Dondorp AM, White NJ, Smithuis FM
articles.details: A field evaluation compared the WHO-approved Abbott-Bioline Malaria Ag P.f/P.v rapid diagnostic test with microscopy in 187 febrile persons during a P. vivax outbreak in Myanmar. Microscopy diagnosed 46 with P. vivax while the Abbott RDT detected only 11, giving a sensitivity of 0.24 (95% CI 0.12–0.36) and specificity of 1.0. Among the 28 (61%) patients with parasite counts ≥200/µL (the WHO lower detection limit), only 11 were RDT-positive, a sensitivity of 0.39 (95% CI 0.21–0.57). The positivity colour band was often absent or faint (confirmed via ImageJ). The authors conclude the RDT is dangerously insensitive, causing treatment delays, and note deployment continues.

articles.headline: IgE antibody binding to malaria PfCSP antigen characterized
articles.title_original: Biophysical and functional characterization of IgE interactions with a malaria antigen.
articles.authors: Adam AI, Sutton BJ, McDonnell JM
articles.details: This study characterized interactions between the Plasmodium falciparum circumsporozoite protein (PfCSP) and CIS43ε, an IgE variant of the neutralizing antibody CIS43, using surface plasmon resonance and functional assays. SPR showed biphasic binding of CIS43ε to PfCSP, with higher affinity for a core PfCSP peptide (Peptide 21) than other NANP repeat motifs, especially in bivalent interactions. In FcεRI-expressing cells, multimeric Peptide 21 (but not monomers) induced significant degranulation, plateauing at the trimeric state, while full-length PfCSP triggered greater degranulation than tetrameric Peptide 21. The authors conclude these multivalent interactions activate effector cells and highlight the therapeutic potential of IgE antibodies against malaria.

articles.headline: Aurora kinase inhibitors target P. falciparum across multiple stages
articles.title_original: Targeting Aurora Kinases as Essential Cell-Cycle Regulators to Deliver Multi-Stage Antimalarials Against Plasmodium Falciparum.
articles.authors: Langeveld H, Maepa K, Maree M, Thibaud JL, Salomane N, Bridgwater R, Famodimu MT, Godoy LC, Pasaje CFA, Boonyalai N, de Souza ML, Fong J, Rabie T, van der Watt M, Theart RP, Ghidelli-Disse S, Niles JC, Lee MCS, Winzeler EA, Delves MJ, Chibale K, Wicht KJ, Coulson LB, Birkholtz LM
articles.details: Researchers evaluated human Aurora kinase inhibitors against the Aurora-related kinase (Ark) family in Plasmodium falciparum as multistage antimalarial targets. Several human AurB inhibitors showed multistage potency (<250 nM) across asexual blood stages, liver schizonts, and male gametes, with the most potent compounds—hesperadin, TAE684, and AT83—showing >1000-fold selectivity toward the parasite. They identified PfArk1 as the principal vulnerable target, with hesperadin specifically inhibiting it; inhibition blocked mitotic completion, producing unsegregated, multi-lobed nuclei from aberrant microtubule organization. The authors conclude PfArk1 is the main Aurora mitotic kinase in proliferative stages and validate hesperadin as a tool compound for drug-discovery campaigns.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccine candidate PfARMA antibodies may not confer protection
articles.title_original: The N-terminal region of malaria vaccine candidate Plasmodium falciparum asparagine-rich merozoite antigen is immunodominant and targeted by polyreactive antibodies.
articles.authors: Garza R, Marchioni JM, Honeycutt JD, Hurlburt NK, Torres C, Garcia A, Loranc E, Yemington E, Towers D, Ssewanyana I, Pancera M, Lavinder JJ, Jagannathan P, Greenhouse B, Bol S, Bunnik EM
articles.details: Using samples from P. falciparum-exposed individuals, this study examined naturally acquired B cell and antibody responses to the blood-stage vaccine candidate asparagine-rich merozoite antigen (PfARMA). Responses were dominated by IgM+ B cells recognizing the N-terminal intrinsically disordered region 1 (IDR1); a human monoclonal antibody to IDR1 was non-neutralizing, while one binding the folded domain showed weak neutralizing activity. Plasma IgM and IgG predominantly targeted IDR1 and increased with exposure but, contrary to prior reports, did not correlate with protection in age- and exposure-matched children. About 30% of unexposed individuals had polyreactive IgG targeting IDR1. The authors conclude antibodies to PfARMA may not directly contribute to protection.

articles.headline: Gold miners can self-perform malaria rapid tests after training
articles.title_original: Practicability of a malaria rapid diagnostic self-test among artisanal gold miners in French Guiana: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Le-Querriou R, Lambert Y, Petit-Sinturel M, Plessis L, Bardon T, Carboni C, Suarez-Mutis M, Vreden S, Muller J, Bordalo-Miller J, Sanna A, Douine M
articles.details: This cross-sectional study assessed the practicability of the Bioline Malaria Ag P.f/P.f/P.v rapid diagnostic test (RDT) self-testing among 40 artisanal and small-scale gold mining workers enrolled during Malakit training sessions in Oiapoque, Brazil (May–June 2024); an independent observer scored knowledge, ability to perform the test, and result interpretation. After training, mean scores were 83.9% for knowledge, 84.6% for practice without assistance (100% with guidance), and 77.2% for interpretation; all RDTs yielded valid results. Correct interpretation was 96.0% for positive strips, 70.0% for negative, and 65.8% for invalid strips, with an overall practicability score of 81.9%. The authors conclude self-testing is practicable after structured training and supports community-based diagnosis in elimination programmes.

articles.headline: Anopheles species diversity shifts by season in southern Odisha, India
articles.title_original: Seasonal dynamics and species diversity of Anopheles mosquitoes in malaria endemic districts of Southern Odisha India.
articles.authors: Baig MM, Koppula DT, Panigrahi DK, Vijayakumar B, Choudhary D, Acharya P, Patnaik M, Gunasekaran K, Kumar A, Rahi M, Shriram AN
articles.details: This entomological survey, conducted from March 2021 to February 2023 across 11 traditionally hyper-endemic districts in southern Odisha, assessed seasonal variation in Anopheles diversity, composition, and abundance using indoor and outdoor manual collection. A total of 10,807 Anopheles mosquitoes were collected, with morphological identification revealing 18 species. An. subpictus predominated in summer 2021 (328; 42.99%) and the rainy season (1151; 46.60%); the primary vector An. culicifacies was consistently present (780; 31.58% in rainy 2021 and 798; 38.35% in rainy 2022); An. varuna peaked in winter (730; 35.56% in 2021-2022). Ganjam had the highest species diversity, and seasonal variation significantly affected diversity (p<0.001), exceeding district-level influence.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria in 3.4% of Ethiopian schoolchildren surveyed
articles.title_original: Asymptomatic Plasmodium infection and predictors among schoolchildren in Bahir Dar Zuria District, Northwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Muhye A, Abate MA, Ayehu A, Dejen P
articles.details: This school-based cross-sectional study (March–May 2024) determined the prevalence and predictors of asymptomatic Plasmodium infection among 416 systematically sampled schoolchildren in Bahir Dar Zuria District, Northwest Ethiopia, using rapid diagnostic tests, capillary buffy coat, and blood film, with multilevel mixed-effect logistic regression. Composite prevalence was 3.4% (95% CI: 1.7–6.3), with P. falciparum accounting for 64.3% of cases. Significant predictors included age (AOR=3.53), gender (AOR=2.87), stagnant water near the household (AOR=3.76), never using long-lasting insecticidal nets (AOR=2.90), and a history of malaria in the last year (AOR=2.23). The authors conclude asymptomatic schoolchildren are significant reservoirs sustaining silent transmission and recommend active case detection, improved net coverage, environmental management, and seasonal chemoprevention.

articles.headline: Mapping altitude and ecoregion distribution of Colombia's malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Ecoregional and altitudinal distribution of the principal malaria vectors in Colombia.
articles.authors: Carrasquilla MC, González R, Pineda SR, Ocampo CB, Montoya-Lerma J, Pareja-Loaiza P, Ahumada ML
articles.details: This study characterized the ecoregional and altitudinal distribution of Colombia's principal malaria vectors (Anopheles albimanus, An. darlingi, An. nuneztovari s.s., An. neivai s.l., and An. calderoni) using a database of records dating 1929 to 2023, with elevation and ecoregion data from USGS and the Ecoregions2017 shapefile. A total of 2,371 records were obtained. An. albimanus and An. neivai s.l. were highly represented in coastal regions, while An. darlingi (strongly forest-associated), An. nuneztovari s.s., and An. calderoni were mainly inland; vectors were predominantly below 500 m, though An. albimanus and An. calderoni occurred above 1,000 m, and An. albimanus and An. neivai s.l. suggested elevation increase over time. The authors conclude the mapping will help define receptivity areas for malaria stratification and control prioritization.

articles.headline: Quinine linked to lower death risk than artesunate in South Sudan children
articles.title_original: A Cox Proportional Hazards Model Approach to Identifying Malaria Risk Factors in Children in South Sudan.
articles.authors: Jumi LGL, Mohmmed AOA
articles.details: This hospital-based retrospective study at Al Sabah Children's Hospital in Juba, South Sudan, used a Cox proportional hazards model to identify risk factors affecting survival of 6,410 children (aged 1 month to 15 years) admitted with malaria from January 1 to December 31, 2021. Males were 56.08%; 4.73% died and 95.27% were censored, with mean age 23.66 months. Increased age raised the hazard of death (HR 1.0223), while higher body weight lowered it (HR 0.8513); quinine treatment was associated with lower death risk than artesunate (HR 0.7107), and absence of comorbidity reduced risk (HR 0.5530), all significant at p<0.01. The authors note this quinine finding is contrary to studies showing artesunate is superior, and that quinine remains the drug of choice in South Sudan.

articles.headline: Daytime mosquito biting times poorly matched by entomological surveillance schedules
articles.title_original: Malaria Mosquito Host-Seeking Activity Times in Manhiça District, Rural Mozambique, and the Need to Better Match Entomological Surveillance Strategies to Daylight Cycles.
articles.authors: Jobe NB, Máquina M, Opiyo MA, Martí-Soler H, Malheia A, Marrenjo D, Cuamba N, Pino D, Saúte F, Paaijmans KP
articles.details: This entomological study assessed diurnal host-seeking activity of malaria mosquitoes indoors and outdoors in two villages in the Manhiça district, then compared sunrise/sunset times across African longitudes and latitudes against current surveillance approaches. The most abundant species was Anopheles tenebrosus, and daytime host-seeking (06:00–18:00) was observed in only a single Anopheles ziemanni, which may actually have been collected after sunset (17:06). Sunrise and sunset times varied by over 2 hours across the year by location. The authors conclude surveillance strategies should be adjusted to match local daylight cycles and human behaviors to yield more actionable data.

articles.headline: Nine-year hospital data show fluctuating malaria burden in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Malaria Trends, Burden, Seasonal Variation, and Interventions in Western Tigray, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Kassahun GB, Berhe AM, Brhanu MA, Aregawi BB
articles.details: A hospital-based retrospective cross-sectional study analyzed microscopically confirmed malaria cases recorded from 2011 to 2019 at Kahsay Abera General Hospital in Western Tigray, North Ethiopia, also assessing intervention activities via checklist, communication, and observation. Of 36,438 recorded cases, there were 50 hospital deaths (0.14%) and 2,016 admissions (5.5%); Plasmodium falciparum predominated with 22,621 cases (62.1%). The highest mortality was in those aged ≥15 years (38 cases, 0.10%) and during October–December (21 cases). The fluctuating case trend was statistically significant (p=0.001) with no species shift, while the mortality trend was not (p=0.62). Malaria remains a major health problem despite control progress.

articles.headline: Outdoor activity at peak biting hours raises malaria risk in Meghalaya
articles.title_original: Malaria risk, treatment-seeking, and prevention in Northeastern India: clinic-based surveillance of persistent transmission.
articles.authors: Lyngdoh P, Sarkar R, Wilson ML, Lyngdoh I, Kessler A, Carlton JM, Albert S
articles.details: A multi-site, facility-based cross-sectional study enrolled individuals presenting with malaria-like symptoms at five primary health centres and one district hospital across three districts of Meghalaya, northeastern India, between 2018 and 2021, detecting infection by microscopy, RDT, and PCR. Of 1,031 participants providing blood samples, 45 (4.4%) had a Plasmodium infection, most (60%) P. falciparum, with prevalence ranging from 1.8% in West Khasi Hills to 12.3% in South Garo Hills. Diagnosis within the past 12 months (OR=9.2; P<0.001), and outdoor activities before bedtime (OR=2.0; P=0.023) or early morning (OR=3.1; P=0.010) were significantly associated with infection. The authors highlight local risk factors sustaining transmission.

articles.headline: Malaria RDT reliably detects PfHRP2 in stored dried blood spots
articles.title_original: The reliability of malaria rapid diagnostic test kit for detecting P. falciparum PfHRP2 in dried blood spot samples preserved under different conditions and durations.
articles.authors: Aninagyei E, Apekey J, Boatey CA, Banono E, Adu JO, Boakye AA
articles.details: This study tested the reliability of a malaria rapid diagnostic test for detecting Plasmodium falciparum PfHRP2 in dried blood spots (DBS) preserved under different conditions and durations. From 60 whole blood samples from patients with uncomplicated malaria, 900 DBS were made and stored at room temperature (25°C), 6°C, and -60°C for 30, 60, 90, 120, and 150 days. PfHRP2 detection was reproducible from day 30 to 120 regardless of storage condition; after 150 days the positivity rate fell to 90%, 88.3%, and 80% respectively (p=0.236). The authors conclude room temperature offers a cost-effective, equipment-free method for preserving PfHRP2 for up to 120 days.

articles.headline: High-quality reference genome generated for zoonotic Plasmodium simium
articles.title_original: Novel zoonotic cases of Plasmodium simium from São Paulo with a reference genome of the Brazilian strain.
articles.authors: Manko E, Billows N, Higgins M, Ward D, Ibrahim A, Campino S, Di Santi SM, Clark TG
articles.details: To support study of zoonotic malaria caused by Plasmodium simium, researchers used Oxford Nanopore and Illumina sequencing to generate a high-quality reference genome (Ps_SãoPaulo, 21.8 Mb). Comparative genomic analyses confirmed known differences from P. vivax, including mitochondrial mutations, deletions in reticulocyte-invasion genes (RBP2a, DBP), and variants in blood-stage antigen genes (msp3.1, msp3.2). Genome-wide analysis of 38 P. simium samples from Brazil revealed expected divergence from P. vivax and potential unique signatures of selection and host adaptation. The authors present this as the most comprehensive P. simium reference genome, with insights for malaria control in Brazil.

articles.headline: Intradermal attenuated-sporozoite malaria vaccine triggers PD-L1 immune suppression in mice
articles.title_original: Intradermal immunization with Plasmodium berghei late-arresting genetically attenuated sporozoites induces PD-L1 expression on regulatory macrophages and dendritic cells.
articles.authors: van Schuijlenburg R, Naar CM, de Bes-Roeleveld HM, Chevalley-Maurel S, Zigó LA, Houlder EL, Lambooij JM, Baalbergen E, Roestenberg M, Franke-Fayard B
articles.details: Using a Plasmodium berghei late-arresting genetically attenuated sporozoite (GA2 LA-GAP) rodent model, researchers compared intradermal (ID) versus intravenous (IV) immunization across multiple organs. ID immunization produced lower frequencies of CD8+ tissue-resident memory T cells in spleen (1.3%, p=0.3), lungs (8.1%, p=0.005) and liver (2.5%, p=0.07), with reduced activation markers; in liver, Granzyme B and perforin were significantly decreased (both p<0.0001). ID delivery induced a regulatory myeloid phenotype (low CD86, high PD-L1) in skin, draining lymph nodes and liver. Authors conclude this regulatory profile may impair T-cell priming and that optimizing ID administration could improve efficacy.

articles.headline: Monthly prophylaxis beats screen-and-treat for malaria in Cambodian soldiers
articles.title_original: Evaluating malaria elimination strategies among military forces in Cambodia: a multi-arm clinical trial comparing monthly prophylaxis and focused screening and treatment.
articles.authors: Wojnarski M, Chaudhury S, Boonchan T, Bun R, Chann S, Gosi P, Soveasna K, Song S, Buathong N, Ittiverakul M, Sriwichai S, Arsanok M, Kuntawunginn W, Saingam P, Chaisatit C, Ponlawat A, Fansiri T, Vanachayangkul P, Jaichapor B, Sinoun M, Chuor CM, Kheangheng T, So M, Wanja E, Davidson S, Spring M, Rekol H, Dysoley L, Saly K, Livezey JR, Lin JT, Smith PL, Satharath P, Manning JE, Sok S, Saunders DL
articles.details: A multi-arm randomized trial among eight military cohorts (1,050 volunteers) along the Cambodian-Thai border compared three months of monthly malaria prophylaxis (MMP: dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine plus weekly 22.5 mg primaquine for 12 weeks) versus focused screening and treatment (FSAT), with cohorts also assigned permethrin-treated or sham clothing. MMP reduced P. falciparum positivity by 90% (10% to 1%; ARR 9%) versus 66% for FSAT (7.6% to 2.7%; ARR 4.9%), though 32% of baseline DP-treated Pf cases recrudesced. MMP cut P. vivax to 0% at three months but it rebounded to 6.7% by six months. The low-dose weekly primaquine was safe even for the 15% who were G6PD-deficient. Authors conclude MMP was safe and superior to FSAT for elimination.

articles.headline: Chemoproteomics confirms peptidomimetics kill malaria parasites by targeting plasmepsin V
articles.title_original: Deconvolution of the On-Target Activity of Plasmepsin V Peptidomimetics in Plasmodium falciparum Parasites.
articles.authors: Su W, Nguyen W, Siddiqui G, Dziekan JM, Marapana D, Penington JS, Mehra S, Razook Z, McCann K, Ngo A, Jarman KE, Barry AE, Papenfuss AT, Gilson PR, Creek DJ, Cowman AF, Sleebs BE, Dans MG
articles.details: To resolve inconsistencies between biochemical potency and cellular activity, researchers used chemoproteomic approaches—solvent-induced protein precipitation and intact-cell thermal profiling—to demonstrate that peptidomimetic inhibitors engage plasmepsin V (PMV), an essential aspartyl protease that enables protein export during the asexual blood stage of Plasmodium infection. They generated parasite lines with reduced sensitivity to the peptidomimetics; whole-genome sequencing revealed a single nucleotide variant within the pmv gene, validated by reverse genetics as mediating resistance. Together, these data confirm the peptidomimetics act directly on PMV, supporting it as a validated antimalarial drug target.

articles.headline: Humanized-metabolism mouse model improves antimalarial drug discovery testing
articles.title_original: Humanization of Drug Metabolism in the Plasmodium berghei Mouse Model for Antimalarial Drug Discovery.
articles.authors: MacLeod AK, Merino C, Viera-Morilla S, Frame L, Tavendale A, Duncan C, Ferguson L, Pinto EG, Simeons FRC, Gomez-Jimenez V, García AB, Riley J, Shishikura Y, Martínez-Martínez MS, Wolf CR, Read KD
articles.details: Researchers evaluated the '8HUM' mouse line, extensively genetically humanized for cytochrome P450 enzymes and their regulators, to better predict human drug metabolism in antimalarial discovery. Engrafting human erythrocytes into an immunocompromised 8HUM/Rag2-/- version was insufficient to permit Plasmodium falciparum infection, even after natural killer cell depletion, but 8HUM mice could be infected with Plasmodium berghei to assess drug efficacy. Approved antimalarials were generally more metabolically stable in 8HUM than wild-type mice, and major human-mouse species differences in quinine derivative metabolism were removed. Authors conclude the 8HUM P. berghei model improves translational accuracy early in antimalarial drug discovery.

articles.headline: Eucalyptus and lemongrass essential oils repel malaria mosquitoes in lab tests
articles.title_original: Spatial Exposure Responses of Malaria Vectors to Eucalyptus grandis (W. Hill ex Maiden) and Cymbopogon citratus (DC.) Stapf Essential Oils.
articles.authors: Kaddumukasa MA, Mutekanga NM, Kula F, Batume C, Kato AB
articles.details: This laboratory study examined spatial behavioral responses of female Anopheles gambiae (Kisumu strain) to Eucalyptus grandis and Cymbopogon citratus essential oils, extracted by steam distillation, using a Y-tube olfactometer at various concentrations with three replicates; constituents were identified by gas chromatography-mass spectrometry. The main components were L-α-terpineol and eucalyptol for Eucalyptus grandis, and lavandulol, methyl ether and citral for Cymbopogon citratus. Mosquito response curves indicating effective repellency concentrations are reported. Authors conclude both essential oils show promise as botanical spatial repellents and warrant further characterization as new vector-control tools amid insecticide resistance.

articles.headline: P. vivax infection alters sterol carrier protein, linking metabolism to mosquito reproduction
articles.title_original: Plasmodium vivax infection-driven modulation of sterol carrier protein reveals a metabolic link to reproductive physiology in Anopheles stephensi.
articles.authors: Kumari S, Yadav P, Sankhala N, Rani J, Sharma G, Chauhan C, Tevatiya S, Dixit R
articles.details: RNAi-mediated knockdown of sterol carrier protein (SCP) significantly reduced mosquito fecundity, underscoring its vital role in reproductive physiology. Mosquitoes rely exclusively on blood-derived sterols transported by SCP, whose expression is strongly induced after blood feeding and dynamically modulated during Plasmodium vivax infection—upregulated in the midgut and salivary glands but suppressed in hemocytes. The authors conclude SCP links nutrient uptake to reproductive outcome, that infection-driven modulation may facilitate parasite growth, and that SCP is a possible transmission-blocking target in integrated malaria control strategies.

articles.headline: Splenic CD103+CD8+ T cells decline and stay quiescent in malaria-infected mice
articles.title_original: Characteristics of CD103(+)CD8(+) T cells in the spleen of Plasmodium yoelii NSM-infected mice.
articles.authors: Pan X, Shi F, Tang S, Liu M, Pan L, Liang G, Li L, Xie H, Zhao S, Huang J
articles.details: In a mouse model, C57BL/6 mice received intraperitoneal injection of 10^6 Plasmodium yoelii NSM-infected red blood cells, and splenic CD45+ cells were analyzed by single-cell RNA sequencing, flow cytometry, qPCR, and dual-luciferase assays. A substantial CD103+CD8+ T cell subset in naïve spleens declined rapidly in proportion and count after infection. These cells showed higher CD62L but lower CD44, CD69, and TIGIT, rarely secreted IFN-γ or granzyme B, and were enriched in translation/ribosome pathways, suggesting a pre-activation state. The authors implicate transcription factor LEF1 in regulating Itgae (CD103) expression and conclude these cells play a limited role in infection.

articles.headline: Malaria, typhoid, and brucellosis burden mapped across Uganda, 2016–2023
articles.title_original: Spatial and Temporal Distribution of Febrile Illnesses in Uganda: Case Studies of Brucellosis, Malaria and Typhoid Fever (2016-2023).
articles.authors: Aceng FL, Arinaitwe ES, Muruta A, Deza-Cruz I, Ekiri AB
articles.details: This retrospective analysis used Uganda's national disease surveillance data from 2016 to 2023, reporting 110,134,705 malaria cases, 1,572,162 typhoid fever cases, and 361,563 brucellosis cases, with spatial mapping in QGIS. National prevalence per 100,000 ranged from 43,316 to 29,271 for malaria, 538 to 445 for typhoid, and 151 to 89 for brucellosis. Over the period, malaria prevalence fell 4%, typhoid 2%, and brucellosis 8%. The northern region had the highest malaria and brucellosis prevalence and the central region the lowest, while the central region had the highest typhoid prevalence. The authors call for reviewing the national malaria control program and strengthening multisectoral control.

articles.headline: Qualitative study explores perennial malaria chemoprevention acceptability in Osun State, Nigeria
articles.title_original: Investigating factors enabling the acceptability of perennial malaria chemoprevention implementation in Osun State, Nigeria: evidence from a qualitative process evaluation.
articles.authors: Ekholuenetale M, Aransiola JO, Ujuju C, Ogbulafor N, Oroge O, Garba RA, Rahman SA, Maxwell K, Awosanya RA, Adekola MA, Ilesanmi BB, Ajayi A, Ogundun OA, Oresanya O, Tibenderana JK
articles.details: Perennial malaria chemoprevention (PMC) for children under 24 months was found acceptable in Osun State, Nigeria, in a qualitative study using focus group discussions and key informant interviews with caregivers, community leaders, fathers of children under 24 months, and health workers, analyzed thematically with ATLAS.ti 24. Acceptability was influenced by perceived effectiveness, child-friendliness, free health services, and delivery through the expanded programme on immunization (EPI) platform. Barriers included lack of transportation funds and fear of side effects, while health worker attitudes and religious leaders' acceptance also mattered. The authors conclude programmes should identify context-specific factors to maintain high acceptability and uptake.

articles.headline: Sudanese caregivers show high malaria vaccine acceptance despite low awareness
articles.title_original: Awareness and acceptance of the malaria vaccine among caregivers attending primary health care centres in Sudan: a mixed-methods study.
articles.authors: Hamid A, Elsir R, Abbas E, Ibrahim N, Ahmed F, Mohamed S, Adam M, Abdalla R, Abuzaid M, Alameen F, Ahmed M, Bakhit A, Abdalla E
articles.details: This descriptive cross-sectional mixed-methods study, conducted from November 2024 to January 2025 in two public primary healthcare centres in Sudan, surveyed 159 caregivers via consecutive sampling on malaria vaccine awareness and acceptance, with thematic analysis of hesitancy reasons. Acceptance was high at 86.2% despite limited prior awareness of 11.3%. Acceptance was significantly associated with perceived malaria severity (p<0.001), caregivers' own vaccination history (p=0.034), and children's complete immunization (p=0.047). Vaccine safety concerns predominated, alongside scepticism toward healthcare systems; trusted information came mainly from healthcare providers (66%). The authors conclude introduction should prioritize targeted safety communication and integration into existing immunization frameworks.

articles.headline: Modeling shows behavior-tailored ITN strategies key to malaria elimination targets
articles.title_original: Insecticide-treated net use and elimination of malaria in Sub-Saharan African countries: assessing the Global Technical Strategy using an evolutionary game approach.
articles.authors: Laxmi, Oraby T, Tyshenko MG, Danquah I, Bhattacharyya S
articles.details: This modeling study built a behaviour-incidence model fitted to historical malaria case reports and insecticide-treated net (ITN) usage data across Sub-Saharan African countries to assess progress toward the Global Technical Strategy (GTS) 2025 and 2030 milestones. Using clustering and optimisation methods, the authors classified countries by transmission rates, mosquito prevalence, and socioeconomic conditions. They found some countries can meet GTS targets through ongoing ITN efforts while others require enhanced social awareness campaigns, economic assistance, and improved ITN efficacy. The authors conclude that country-specific behavioural interventions addressing proper ITN use are essential for accelerating elimination.

articles.headline: Geostatistical model identifies persistent high-risk malaria villages in Eastern Myanmar
articles.title_original: Spatiotemporal trends in P. falciparum malaria and identification of high-risk villages in Eastern Myanmar: an 8-year observational study.
articles.authors: Rae JD, Parker DM, Maude RJ, Thu AM, Devine A, Nosten FH, Simpson JA
articles.details: This 8-year observational study fitted a geostatistical model to routine surveillance data (2014–2021) from 507 village-based malaria posts in Hpapun Township, Myanmar, to track P. falciparum incidence and identify high-risk villages, accounting for elevation, prior interventions, and spatial correlation. Between 2014 and 2019, P. falciparum incidence decreased by 93.9%. Villages receiving targeted interventions had higher pre-intervention incidence (IRR 4.72, 95% CI 4.56–4.90) and lower post-intervention incidence (IRR 0.26, 95% CI 0.24–0.27). In 2021, 12 high-risk villages were identified, eight persistently high-risk. The authors conclude geostatistical modelling can efficiently identify persistent high-risk villages to support elimination.

articles.headline: Meta-analysis finds variable malaria RDT performance across sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Performance and challenges of malaria rapid diagnostic tests in endemic regions of Africa.
articles.authors: Ojeniyi FD, Ayoola AO, Ibitoye O, Opaleye OO, Olowe OA, Ehigie LO, Thomas BN, Ojurongbe O
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis pooled data from 48 studies to evaluate the diagnostic sensitivity and specificity of various malaria rapid diagnostic test (RDT) brands across sub-Saharan Africa. The results revealed considerable variability influenced by antigen persistence, cross-reactivity with other infections, and genetic polymorphism in the HRP2 gene, which can cause false positives and negatives. The authors underscore the need for region-specific diagnostic strategies and advanced tools capable of detecting low-level parasitemia and differentiating Plasmodium species, recommending emerging technologies and multi-platform approaches to improve accuracy.

articles.headline: Kelch13 R622I resistance mutation and HRP2-RDT negativity high in northern Ethiopia
articles.title_original: High prevalence and emerging positive association of kelch13 R622I and HRP2-based RDT negativity in Plasmodium falciparum in northern Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Zeleke AJ, Fola AA, Tollefson GA, Niaré K, Leonetti A, Taropawala O, Marglous J, Crudale R, Brhane BG, Assefa A, Kiyuka P, Parr JB, Hailu A, Tegegne MA, Bailey JA
articles.details: This year-long genomic surveillance study sequenced 903 P. falciparum infections using molecular inversion probe panels in two districts (Gondar Zuria and Tach Armachiho) of northwestern Ethiopia to track artemisinin partial resistance mutations. The validated k13 R622I mutation was found in 44.3% of samples, more frequent in Gondar Zuria than Tach Armachiho (52% vs 35%; p<0.001), and persisted year-round. HRP2-based RDT negativity was 39.3%, and R622I parasites were more often HRP2-RDT negative than wild-type (48.3% vs 30.7%; p<0.05); the two co-occurred in 22% of samples. The k13 C580Y mutation was detected at 0.4% in Gondar Zuria—the first Horn of Africa report—with haplotypes suggesting local de novo African emergence. The authors warn of a double threat to control and elimination.

articles.headline: Anopheles funestus drove malaria resurgence in Eastern Uganda
articles.title_original: Disentangling the roles of different vector species during a malaria resurgence in Eastern Uganda.
articles.authors: McClure M, Oruni A, Arinaitwe E, Musiime A, Kyagamba P, Otto G, Adiga J, Rwatooro JA, Kilama M, Krezanoski P, Briggs J, Rosenthal PJ, Nankabirwa JI, Kamya MR, Dorsey G, Greenhouse B, Rodriguez-Barraquer I
articles.details: This cohort study followed 422 children in 94 households during 2021–2023 in Tororo District (where indoor residual spraying formulation changed) and neighboring Busia District (no IRS), Uganda, using passive and monthly active PCR surveillance for P. falciparum and biweekly CDC light-trap mosquito collection. Each doubling of annualized household entomologic inoculation rate (aEIR) was associated with a 29% increase in P. falciparum hazard (adjusted HR 1.29, 95% CI 1.25–1.33). This effect was primarily driven by An. funestus (HR 1.29, 1.25–1.34), with a much smaller association for An. gambiae (1.04, 1.01–1.08), and was stronger in Tororo. The authors conclude that replacement of An. gambiae by An. funestus drove the resurgence.

articles.headline: Widespread insecticide resistance in Ghana's malaria mosquitoes over five years
articles.title_original: Trends and patterns of insecticide susceptibility of Anopheles gambiae between 2015 and 2020: implications for malaria vector control interventions in Ghana.
articles.authors: Asafu-Adjaye A, Chabi J, Malm K, Akrofi OO, Coleman S, Dery DB, Yihdego Y, Frempong KK, Pi-Bansa S, Asigbaase MA, Pwalia R, Joannides J, Sakyi KY, Akuoko OK, Osei JHN, Athinya DK, Baffoe-Wilmot A, Bart-Plange C, Boakye DA, Dadzie SK
articles.details: Annual WHO tube susceptibility assays were conducted on adult female Anopheles gambiae from 30 sentinel sites across Ghana from 2015 to 2020, testing seven insecticides across four classes, with PCR on 40 mosquitoes per site to identify species and detect kdr-west (L1014F) and ace-1 (G119S) mutations. High resistance to pyrethroids (deltamethrin, permethrin) and DDT was found at all sites, with declining susceptibility over the years, and reduced susceptibility to carbamates (bendiocarb, propoxur) and organophosphates (malathion, pirimiphos-methyl). L1014F frequency was significantly higher than G119S. Authors call for continuous resistance monitoring to guide vector control.

articles.headline: Diagnostic delay drove severe imported malaria during COVID co-circulation
articles.title_original: Diagnostic delay inducing severe imported falciparum malaria: a time-sensitivity comparison of two imported cases during COVID-19 co-circulation.
articles.authors: Gong QH, Wan SB
articles.details: This comparative case report describes two male travelers returning from Africa (Nigeria and Côte d'Ivoire) who developed fever during COVID-19 quarantine in Shanghai, China (2021); both initially tested negative for SARS-CoV-2. Case 1 had a 96-hour diagnostic delay and progressed to severe falciparum malaria with delirium, thrombocytopenia (33 × 10⁹/L), and acute kidney injury (creatinine 278 μmol/L), requiring intensive care. Case 2 was diagnosed within 24 hours by rapid diagnostic test and microscopy and received immediate artesunate without severe complications. Authors conclude diagnostic delay directly raises severe malaria risk and urge parallel testing of febrile travelers.

articles.headline: Genomic surveillance of drug resistance and vaccine targets in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Molecular epidemiology of Plasmodium falciparum drug resistance and vaccine targets in high-transmission settings in Africa.
articles.authors: Aliyu IA, Bala JA, Musa BM, Amole TG, Ahmed AB, Kovalenko G, Umar A, Garba Y, Gwarzo SA, Mansur A, Hidaya MG, Aliyu MH, Galadanci HS
articles.details: Using Oxford Nanopore amplicon sequencing, researchers profiled five resistance genes (crt, dhfr, dhps, mdr1, k13) and the vaccine target csp in Plasmodium falciparum clinical isolates from northern Nigeria. They found high frequencies of dhfr-IRNI (90%) and dhps-SGKAA (45%) haplotypes, but no quintuple or sextuple dhfr/dhps combinations linked to SP-IPTp failure, supporting continued preventive use in pregnancy. The chloroquine-resistant Pfcrt-76T allele persisted at ~25%, and no WHO-validated Pfk13 artemisinin-resistance mutations were found, though novel variants were detected. Some csp polymorphisms were predicted to affect protein stability. Resistance haplotypes clustered locally, notably in Kano and Yobe.

articles.headline: Review: how blood group antigens shape malaria susceptibility
articles.title_original: Blood group antigens and malaria susceptibility.
articles.authors: Liu FF, Liang J, Gao M, Li K
articles.details: This review synthesizes evidence on how erythrocyte surface blood-group antigens modulate malaria susceptibility, covering effects on parasite invasion, cytoadherence, immune evasion, and disease severity. It focuses on a curated set of systems including ABO, Duffy, MNS, Gerbich, Knops, and Diego, outlining their molecular mechanisms. The authors also discuss population heterogeneity, parasite-host adaptations and counter-adaptations, and clinical implications for risk stratification, transfusion safety, and vaccine design.

articles.headline: Healthcare-associated falciparum malaria traced to blood-borne hospital transmission
articles.title_original: Case Report and Epidemiological Investigation of Healthcare-associated Plasmodium falciparum Malaria Transmission in Westchester County, New York-2023.
articles.authors: White JL, Southwick K, Madison-Antenucci S, Piedmonte NP, Gayen S, Clemons B, Quinn M, Barrett K, Backenson PB, Antwi M, Slavinski S, Lee E, Huang A, Smith K, Guzzardi E, Hunter JC, McKinney KL, Mace KE, Ridpath AD, Williams S, Barratt JLN, Jacobson D, Pierre-Louis E, Bajic M, Kelley J, McElroy PD, Raphael BH, Bulbin A
articles.details: This case report and epidemiological investigation describes a 65-year-old Westchester County, New York resident diagnosed with Plasmodium falciparum on 2 October 2023 despite no recent international travel to an endemic area. The investigation used syndromic surveillance, healthcare facility investigations, and genetic analysis of the patient and epidemiologically linked malaria patients to assess potential exposure routes. Genetic and epidemiologic results implicated blood-borne transmission in a healthcare setting from a concurrently hospitalized traveler with confirmed malaria, likely due to an infection-control lapse, though the exact mechanism remains unknown. No mosquito-transmitted or additional induced cases were identified.

articles.headline: Opinion: Suriname's malaria elimination strategy as Amazon model
articles.title_original: Is malaria elimination in the Amazon rainforest feasible? The case of Suriname.
articles.authors: Vreden S, Eersel M, Bretas G, Cairo H, van Eer E, Adhin M
articles.details: This opinion paper summarizes Suriname's malaria elimination strategy and argues it is feasible for similar Amazon rainforest settings. The approach was applied in both stable and nonstable communities and included robust surveillance, maximized access to early diagnosis and treatment, retreatment strategies, and ample distribution of insecticide-treated nets, supported by decades of sustained community engagement. The authors believe similar strategies could be effectively adapted and applied elsewhere in Amazon regions. No quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: Qualitative study of malaria control perceptions in Burundi
articles.title_original: Understanding perceptions of malaria control measures in Burundi: a qualitative descriptive study using a socio-ecological approach.
articles.authors: Niyonsaba T, Ndayisaba R, Dumont C, Muhoza C, Lampaert E, Alfred AD, Pellecchia U, Uwimana F, Bucumi A, Irabaheta N, Marisasi D, Bigirimana I, Ndayiragije S, Rwimo A, Nishemezwe D, Nimubona D, Niyomwungere L, Ndihokubwayo F, Iradukunda O, Irambona E, Nicobaharaye C, Sindayikengera J, Van Bortel W, Colombe S, Meudec M
articles.details: A qualitative descriptive study conducted from March to May 2023 in the Kinyinya and Ryansoro health districts of Burundi, combining 60 individual interviews and 28 focus group discussions, mostly with farmers and with a slight female predominance (53% of individual interviews, 57.5% of FGDs). Using a socio-ecological approach, thematic analysis explored perceptions and practices around four interventions: LLIN distribution, indoor residual spraying, IPTp-SP, and environmental management. The authors identified influencing factors at intrapersonal, organizational, and policy/environmental levels and conclude that effective prevention requires a multifaceted approach addressing communication, contextual adaptation, adverse effects, local priorities, and socio-economic constraints.

articles.headline: Genetically engineered mosquitoes block circulating malaria strains
articles.title_original: Genetically engineered mosquitoes block development of circulating malaria strains.
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Inositol phosphate kinase IPMK proposed as new malaria drug target
articles.title_original: Unveiling the Plasmodium inositol (pyro)phosphate pathway: Highlighting inositol polyphosphate multikinase as a novel therapeutic target for malaria.
articles.authors: Obuobi A, Quashie NB, Duah-Quashie NO, Sayers JR
articles.details: This exploratory in silico and molecular study investigated the Plasmodium falciparum inositol phosphate signaling pathway (IPP) as a novel antimalarial drug target, characterizing the sequences and structures of putative inositol phosphate kinases. Experimental validation across laboratory strains and a clinical isolate confirmed the p.Pro375Gln substitution in IPMK1, the first evidence of this variant in field isolates. The authors provide molecular evidence of IPP genes in P. falciparum and identify inositol polyphosphate multikinase (IPMK) as a promising drug target owing to its unique sequence and structural features, intended to guide future experimental validation.

articles.headline: Angola: relying only on RDTs limits malaria diagnosis
articles.title_original: The risks of exclusive reliance on rapid diagnostic tests for malaria in Angola.
articles.authors: Chitumba HO
articles.details: In Angola, where malaria remains a major burden, reliance on rapid diagnostic tests limits species identification and parasite quantification. The authors argue that strengthening microscopy and integrating molecular diagnostics are essential to improve clinical management, surveillance capacity, and detection of emerging resistance. No quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: Retrospective donor blood screening during 2023 Florida P. vivax malaria cluster
articles.title_original: Heightened Nontesting Risk Mitigation Blood Donor Screening with Retrospective Transcription-Mediated Amplification Testing during a 2023 Autochthonous Florida Malaria Cluster.
articles.authors: Sullivan DJ, Lanteri MC, Bres V, Hanhan M, Pagan M, Rey A, Thomas K, Reik RA
articles.details: This report describes a blood center's nontesting risk-mitigation response to an autochthonous Plasmodium vivax outbreak in which symptomatic malaria was detected in seven individuals without recent malaria travel history. With no approved donor screening test available, the center retrospectively performed transcription-mediated amplification (limit of detection two to seven infected erythrocytes per milliliter) on 258 donor samples from four Sarasota zip codes and 178 samples from eight Miami zip codes. All donor samples were nucleic acid test nonreactive, and no local transfusion-transmitted malaria cases were reported. The authors conclude that escalating donor screening, pathogen reduction, and surveillance helped minimize transfusion-transmission risk.

articles.headline: Comparing adjuvants to boost antibody responses for nanoparticle malaria vaccine
articles.title_original: A Comparative Evaluation of Adjuvants for Enhancing Antibody Responses to a Nanoparticle-Based Malaria Vaccine.
articles.authors: Nepal Y, Francian A, Roberts BT, Khan R, Wondra G, Demierre A, Apel F, Chackerian B
articles.details: This immunization study evaluated different classes of adjuvants for enhancing antibody responses to a virus-like particle (VLP)-based pre-erythrocytic Plasmodium falciparum vaccine targeting circumsporozoite protein (CSP) epitopes. Five formulations significantly increased anti-CSP IgG titers, and several also enhanced antibody durability. The authors conclude they identified promising adjuvants that improve both the magnitude and longevity of VLP-induced immunity.

articles.headline: Parasite genetic metrics track malaria prevalence better than incidence in Uganda
articles.title_original: Accuracy of Plasmodium falciparum genetic data for estimating parasite prevalence and malaria incidence in Uganda.
articles.authors: Kiyaga S, Mbabazi M, Katairo T, Kabbale KD, Asua V, Nsengimaana B, Wiringilimaana I, Semakuba FD, Mwubaha C, Nakasaanya J, Watyekele E, Ayitewala A, Tukwasibwe S, Mulondo J, Nsobya SL, Agaba B, Maiteki-Sebuguzi C, Kamya MR, Kateete DP, Nabende JN, Jjingo D, Mboowa G, Batte C, Ssewanyana I, Aranda-Díaz A, Dorsey G, Rosenthal PJ, Conrad M, Greenhouse B, Briggs J
articles.details: This surveillance study assessed whether Plasmodium falciparum genetic metrics can estimate transmission intensity, using dried blood spots from 3563 symptomatic malaria cases across 26 sentinel health facilities in Uganda over two collections in 2023, with amplicon deep sequencing of 165 polyallelic microhaplotypes (MAD4HatTeR). In Round 1, parasite prevalence ranged from 5.0% to 49.23% and incidence from 91–1062 cases per 1,000 person-years, while Round 2 incidence ranged from 33–1667 cases per 1,000 person-years. Complexity of infection (COI) and effective COI were strongly positively associated with prevalence (pooled cross-validated r = 0.79), while incidence models performed weaker (r = 0.37), with between-host relatedness the strongest incidence predictor. The authors conclude microhaplotype diversity metrics reliably reflect spatial prevalence variation but have limited ability to predict incidence.

articles.headline: Plasmodium kinase NEK4 drives meiosis and zygote maturation
articles.title_original: A divergent Plasmodium NEK4 acts as a key regulator driving the early events of meiosis.
articles.authors: Yanase R, Hair M, Zeeshan M, Ferguson DJP, Brady D, Pasquarello C, Bottrill A, Bhanvadia S, Neal A, Tromer EC, Le Roch KG, Hainard A, Holder AA, Vaughan S, Guttery DS, Tewari R
articles.details: This molecular study identifies the Plasmodium berghei NIMA-related kinase NEK4 as a key regulator coupling post-zygotic meiotic initiation with zygote morphogenesis. Using ultrastructure expansion microscopy, the authors show NEK4 accumulates at the microtubule-organising centre and apical polar complex shortly after fertilisation, and that deletion of nek4 causes complete developmental arrest, with blocked MTOC duplication and microtubule formation, uncondensed chromatin, and failed nuclear migration and cell polarity. Transcriptomic and phosphoproteomic analyses showed reduced expression and phosphorylation of HOP1, REC8, and AP2-O, establishing NEK4 as a driver of meiotic entry and zygote maturation.

articles.headline: Fractional-order malaria model adds memory effects and non-vector transmission
articles.title_original: Analytical and data-driven fractional-order malaria transmission model with vector and non-vector pathways.
articles.authors: Ahman QO, Okpara PA, Agbata BC, Senewo EO, Didigwu NE
articles.details: This modeling study develops a Caputo fractional-order malaria transmission model integrating both vector and non-vector exposure pathways while embedding memory effects in human and mosquito dynamics, with analytical properties (positivity, boundedness, disease-free equilibrium, fractional local stability) derived and an Adams–Bashforth–Moulton predictor–corrector scheme used for simulation. Numerical experiments showed that decreasing the fractional order q delayed epidemic peaks, prolonged oscillatory persistence, and amplified long-term infection memory, while adding non-vector pathways increased infection persistence and improved fit to field data. Parameter estimation using weekly malaria incidence from the Nigeria Centre for Disease Control confirmed the model reproduced outbreak patterns, and the authors conclude it provides a unified framework supporting malaria forecasting and control.

articles.headline: Malaria rapid tests show suboptimal sensitivity versus microscopy in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Performance of rapid diagnostic tests for malaria diagnosis in Nigeria: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Ismail M, Mukhtar A, Odetokun IA, Ibrahim MA, Salman AA, Bawa-Sani HG, Nass SS, Goni BW, Nuhu A, Zhang X, Usman A, Isah MB
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 15 studies compared malaria rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) against microscopy, the gold standard, in Nigeria, pooling estimates with a random-effects model and assessing bias via QUADAS-2. Pooled RDT sensitivity was 69.7% (95% CI 67.7-71.6%) and specificity 81.5% (95% CI 79.9-83.1%), with a diagnostic odds ratio of 21.87 (95% CI 7.45-64.21) and an SROC area of 0.902, though heterogeneity was high (I2=96.8%) and publication bias was suggested; performance was better in urban settings. The authors conclude RDTs fall below WHO benchmarks and that diagnostic algorithms integrating RDTs with microscopy or molecular methods are needed.

articles.headline: AI-designed scaffolds present a protective PfCSP epitope to antibody L9
articles.title_original: Machine learning enables de novo multiepitope design of Plasmodium falciparum circumsporozoite protein to target trimeric L9 antibody.
articles.authors: Nelson JAD, Garfinkle SE, Lin ZJ, Park J, Kim AJ, Bayruns K, McCanna ME, Konrath KM, Agostino CJ, Kulp DW, Odom John AR, Pallesen J
articles.journal: Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A
articles.details: Currently approved malaria vaccines give only partial protection because the antibodies they induce vary in quality; they present the unstructured central repeat region and the C-terminal domain of the Plasmodium falciparum circumsporozoite protein (PfCSP). The protective monoclonal antibody L9 recognizes three structured copies of the PfCSP minor repeat and, like other potent antimalarial antibodies, depends on homotypic antibody-antibody interactions for its efficacy. The authors designed antigens scaffolding a copy of the PfCSP minor repeat able to bind L9, and developed a machine-learning-driven structural antigen design pipeline (MESODID) to present multiple structure-based epitopes in one scaffold, using it to design scaffolds presenting three copies of the minor repeat.
