articles.headline: Acyclic Nucleoside Phosphonates As Potential Inhibitors of Plasmodium Falciparum Egress
articles.title_original: Acyclic Nucleoside Phosphonates As Potential Inhibitors of Plasmodium Falciparum Egress.
articles.authors: Cheviet T, Wein S, Lionne C, Badji Y, Venturini N, Cerdan R, Peyrottes S
articles.details: Researchers synthesized and evaluated a new family of acyclic nucleoside phosphonates (nucleotide analogues) designed to target an essential step in the development of the malaria parasite, with two series modifying either the purine scaffold or the acyclic chain. All compounds were tested against chloroquine-sensitive (3D7) Plasmodium falciparum, and a few showed in vitro antimalarial activity with IC50 values ranging from 11.3 to 38.8 µM. A molecular docking study revealed key interactions with the putative target that supported the biological data, and the authors conclude these analogues are promising antimalarial scaffolds warranting further optimization.

articles.headline: Mapping Malaria Susceptibility in the Oti Region of Ghana Using Remote Sensing and GIS: A Retrospective Observational Study
articles.title_original: Mapping Malaria Susceptibility in the Oti Region of Ghana Using Remote Sensing and GIS: A Retrospective Observational Study.
articles.authors: Okyere G, Abakah A, Kwarteng A, Kwarteng EVS
articles.details: Mapping Malaria Susceptibility in the Oti Region of Ghana Using Remote Sensing and GIS: A Retrospective Observational Study

articles.headline: Pyrethroid Resistance in Anopheles gambiae s.l.: A Focus on Permethrin and Deltamethrin for Malaria Vector Control
articles.title_original: Pyrethroid Resistance in Anopheles gambiae s.l.: A Focus on Permethrin and Deltamethrin for Malaria Vector Control.
articles.authors: Adesoye OA, Awoniyi AM, Adeogun AO
articles.details: Pyrethroid Resistance in Anopheles gambiae s.l.: A Focus on Permethrin and Deltamethrin for Malaria Vector Control

articles.headline: Smartphone location data tool maps malaria transmission hotspots
articles.title_original: Smartphone-based retrospective analysis for malaria hotspot detection.
articles.authors: da Silva BM, Giddaluru J, Cardozo LE, Martins FM, Antolini APR, Bargieri DY, de Lacerda MVG, Monteiro WM, Sampaio VS, Nakaya HI
articles.details: This study developed the Sickness Positioning System, a tool that applies density-based clustering to passively collected, anonymized smartphone location-history data (via Google Takeout) from over 200 malaria-infected individuals to identify transmission hotspots. The tool pinpointed multiple elevated-risk areas, and field validation confirmed two previously unrecognized mosquito breeding sites as active transmission sources. Compared with Brazil's annually updated federal surveillance system, it provided more precise and timely spatial assessment. The authors conclude it is a cost-effective, objective, and timely method that could enhance disease surveillance and enable more targeted interventions.

articles.headline: Low-dose primaquine safe for P. vivax radical cure without G6PD testing
articles.title_original: Haematologic responses to primaquine used for radical cure of Plasmodium vivax in public health facilities in Ethiopia: a prospective observational study.
articles.authors: Shifraw T, Abdelmenan S, Zewde A, Worku A, Abera A, Mohammed H, Etana K, Keating J, Chibsa S, Girma S, Teka H, Mumba P, Murphy M, Karmarkar E, Price RN, Hwang J, Berhane Y
articles.details: In a prospective observational study, 879 patients with P. vivax or mixed malaria were enrolled at four health centres and given 14-day low-dose primaquine (0.25 mg/kg/day) for radical cure, with haemoglobin and adverse-event monitoring on days 3, 7, 13, and 27 and G6PD testing on day 13. No patient had G6PD deficiency; 3 of 298 females tested (1.0%) had intermediate deficiency. Median fractional haemoglobin fall was −0.9% on day 3 and 0.8% on day 7; nine patients dropped >25% from baseline, but none had clinical haemolysis, and 91.9% had haematological recovery by day 13 with no serious adverse events. The authors conclude the regimen was well tolerated, supporting the policy of low-dose primaquine without prior G6PD testing where adverse-event monitoring exists.

articles.headline: Human and mosquito behavior drive residual malaria exposure in Kenyan highlands
articles.title_original: Human and vector behavioural determinants of malaria transmission in the Nandi highlands, western Kenya.
articles.authors: Machani MG, Sunkara A, Awandu SS, Ombok M, Ouko A, Abel L, Turnbull LB, Rodriguez I, Kim J, Clemens JC, Ayodo G, Ochomo EO, John CC, Lobo NF
articles.details: This study monitored Anopheles biting rates indoors and outdoors (18:00–06:00 over five nights, ten houses per village) using human landing catches across three ecological settings, alongside hourly human-behavior observations, during long (May 2018) and short (October 2018) rainy seasons. High vector diversity showed site-specific dominance (An. arabiensis, An. christyi, and a novel Anopheles spp. 14 BSL-2014), with most specimens collected indoors at two sites (67%, 52.9%) and outdoors at the swamp-adjacent site (58.3%). Exposure peaked outdoors in early evening (1800–2100h) and indoors in the first half of the night; most exposure occurred indoors for unprotected sleepers and awake individuals (53–55%). LLINs prevented 24.5–44.9% of bites depending on site and season. The authors conclude residual transmission persists, requiring behavior-informed interventions such as spatial repellents and larviciding.

articles.headline: Solar geoengineering may reduce but redistribute South Asian malaria
articles.title_original: Malaria transmission dynamics under climate change and solar geoengineering in South Asia: a GLENS-based assessment.
articles.authors: Hussain A, Shoaib M, Latif M
articles.details: Using the VECTRI malaria model, this study simulated transmission from 2020 to 2097 across seven South Asian countries (Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Iran, India, Nepal, Pakistan) under a stratospheric aerosol injection geoengineering scenario (GLENS-SAI, stabilizing temperatures at 2020 levels) versus an unmitigated high-emissions RCP 8.5 control, assessing vector density, entomological inoculation rate (EIR), and cases. Compared with the control, GLENS-SAI reduced overall transmission intensity, with significant EIR declines in India, Nepal, Bangladesh, northern Pakistan, southern Iran, and the Afghanistan–Pakistan border, but localized increases in southeastern Pakistan, western Afghanistan, parts of Iran, and northern Nepal. The annual transmission cycle shortened in amplitude and duration. The authors conclude region-specific strategies are needed given spatially varied effects.

articles.headline: Computational screening identifies candidate pfGSK3β inhibitors against malaria
articles.title_original: In silico investigation of novel Plasmodium Falciparum glycogen synthase kinase (pfGSk3β) inhibitors for the treatment of malaria infection.
articles.authors: Adebambo KF, Otify S
articles.details: This in silico drug-repurposing study targeted Plasmodium falciparum glycogen synthase kinase (pfGSK3β), mining 53 candidate inhibitors from the CHEMBL database and evaluating them via molecular docking, 100-ns molecular dynamics simulations (GROMACS), and MMPBSA. Three compounds were identified (S20, S39, S56), with S56 outperforming the co-crystallized control ligand S1 on several metrics. Binding affinities were −7.12 for S1 and −5.64 for S56; MD gave average RMSDs of 4.5 nm (S1) versus 1.0 nm (S56), and MMPBSA total energies were comparable (S56 −14.45 kJ/mol, S1 −13.03 kJ/mol). ProTox-III toxicity analysis flagged possible toxicity. The authors propose S39, S20, and S56 as potential repurposed antimalarial candidates.

articles.headline: Pará, Brazil malaria cases fell then rose around COVID-19 pandemic
articles.title_original: Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on malaria transmission in the state of Pará, Brazil.
articles.authors: de Sá CAF, Sardinha DM, de Paula Souza E Guimarães RJ, Vallinoto ACR, Dos Santos EJM, Viana GMR, do Socorro Souza Chaves T
articles.details: This observational, descriptive, and analytical study analysed secondary surveillance data on reported malaria cases in the state of Pará, Brazil, divided into pre-pandemic (2018-2019), critical (2020-2021), and less critical (2022-2023) periods, comparing variables with Chi-square tests. Of 170,245 total cases, 79,125 occurred before, 44,830 during the critical period, and 47,502 in the less critical period. Cases among miners rose from 17.2% to 58%, and Plasmodium falciparum cases rose from 2.6% to 11.3%; imported cases from other states were 3813 (2.2%). The authors conclude seasonality was maintained, the critical-period decline reflected control actions, and the later rise was associated with resumed social interaction and greater internal mobility.

articles.headline: CD44 cross-linking promotes P. falciparum invasion of red blood cells
articles.title_original: CD44 cross-linking promotes Plasmodium falciparum invasion.
articles.authors: Kongsomboonvech AK, Scally SW, Le Guen Y, Valissery P, Salinas ND, Cowman AF, Tolia NH, Egan ES
articles.details: This laboratory study investigated the host transmembrane protein CD44, previously identified as a host factor for Plasmodium falciparum invasion of red blood cells through a forward genetic screen. An anti-CD44 monoclonal antibody, BRIC 222, significantly promoted invasion through CD44 cross-linking, which changed phosphorylation of RBC cytoskeletal proteins and increased accessibility of surface proteins including the essential invasion receptor Basigin. The parasite ligand EBA-175, which interacts with CD44, similarly enhanced invasion and induced membrane changes; both BRIC 222 and EBA-175 increased binding of the PfRH5/PCRCR invasion complex to Basigin. The authors propose CD44 cross-linking coordinates ligand-receptor interactions, making RBCs more permissive to invasion.

articles.headline: Genomic toolkit released for surveillance of Southeast Asian malaria vectors
articles.title_original: A genomic toolkit for surveillance and elimination of the principal malaria vectors in Southeast Asia.
articles.authors: Dennis TPW, Vantaux A, Kobylinski KC, Alam MS, Witkowski B, Al-Amin HM, Lobo NF, Wilson AJ, Drury E, Goncalves S, Harding N, Clarkson CS, Hernandez-Koutoucheva A, Kwiatkowski D, Miles A, Donnelly MJ, St Laurent B
articles.details: This resource paper releases Adir1.0, a curated, analysis-ready catalog of 540 Anopheles dirus whole-genome sequences accessible via the malariagen-data-python API, alongside an updated Amin1.0 API for Anopheles minimus, the principal malaria vectors in Southeast Asia. The authors demonstrate the resources with the first exploratory population genomic analysis of An. dirus from Bangladesh, Thailand, and Cambodia, revealing population structure, candidate regions of physiological insecticide resistance, and genomic structural variation. They conclude these open resources provide a foundation for genomic surveillance to inform vector control and malaria elimination strategies in Southeast Asia.

articles.headline: Commentary on ivermectin's role in malaria control
articles.title_original: Ivermectin's role in malaria control: the simplicity and the complexity.
articles.authors: Schwartz E, Orshan L, Schlagenhauf P
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Three-compound Artemisia combination tested against malaria in mice
articles.title_original: Proteomics-driven screening of artemisinin-based combination ratios and mechanistic insights into Plasmodium berghei infection in mice.
articles.authors: Hao L, Sun J, Li J, Li Z, Yu Z, Huang H, Liu G, Shen Z, Huo H, Yuan Q, Li H, Huang L
articles.details: This study systematically screened antimalarial compound ratios in murine malaria models to devise a strategy against artemisinin resistance, optimizing a formula comprising arteannuin B, artemisinic acid, and scopoletin—bioactive compounds from Artemisia annua. Using integrated proteomic profiling and western blot validation, the authors found the formula strengthened host defense by modulating phagocytic activity in splenic macrophages, dendritic cells, and natural killer cells via Fcγ receptor-mediated pathways. They conclude these findings provide mechanistic insights into artemisinin-associated immune potentiation and propose a novel ACT strategy targeting host-parasite interactions to circumvent emerging artemisinin resistance. No quantitative efficacy figures were reported in the abstract.

articles.headline: Review: host defense peptides as antimalarial agents and immune modulators
articles.title_original: Host defense peptides in malaria infection: their contributions, significance and constraints.
articles.authors: Alfaki DA, Elbasheir MM
articles.details: This review examines host defense peptides (HDPs), components of innate immunity, in malaria infection. It describes how HDPs—including defensins, cathelicidins, NK-2 peptide, platelet factor 4, macrophage inflammatory protein-3α, and hepcidin—target multiple stages of Plasmodium development through direct antimicrobial activity and immunomodulation. The authors discuss recent findings on HDP interactions with Plasmodium at various infection stages and their roles in modulating host immune responses, while noting that the specific roles and targets of HDPs remain incompletely understood and highlighting current limitations.

articles.headline: Scoping review of malaria control strategies in Northern Ghana
articles.title_original: Malaria control in Northern Ghana: a scoping review.
articles.authors: Zankawah MM, Issakah MA, Yakubu E, Tiwaa-Boateng NA, Baatiema L, Moomin A, Banchani JS, Chimdi IE, Edusei AK, Ordinioha B
articles.details: This review searched four databases (Medline, Embase, Scopus, Cochrane Library) and identified 1,782 studies on malaria control interventions in Northern Ghana published between 1 January 2013 and 10 June 2023, of which 38 met the eligibility criteria. Most studies (44.7%) were conducted in the Upper East region and included pregnant women (19%), children (14%), and malaria patients (14.3%), focusing mainly on case management (28.9%), intermittent preventive treatment (IPTp, 23.7%), and long-lasting insecticidal nets (LLINs, 15.8%), while malaria vaccines were the least studied (2.6%). The authors conclude there was no regional or intervention balance and call for further studies on combined intervention effects, larval source management, and malaria vaccines.

articles.headline: Systematic review: malaria treatment in neonates is sparse and inconsistent
articles.title_original: Management of malaria in newborns: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Sultanli A, Beck JS, Kremp M, Sondermann H, Bonitz M, Honkpehedji YJ, Remppis J, Adegnika AA, Esen M, Bélard S
articles.details: This systematic review searched PubMed and Web of Science for studies reporting confirmed Plasmodium infection and antimalarial treatment in neonates (age ≤28 days). Of 4,028 reports, 26 publications (22 case reports, three case series, one cross-sectional study) covering 40 neonates were included; 19 cases were from African countries. Median age at symptom onset and diagnosis was 16 and 19 days. P. falciparum and P. vivax accounted for 45% and 43% of cases; 70% were initially misdiagnosed with sepsis. Only 28% of P. falciparum cases received artemisinin-based therapy (56% got oral amodiaquine or chloroquine monotherapy), and P. vivax was mostly treated with oral chloroquine (64%). All patients survived, with parasitaemia clearance in 2–7 days. The authors call for prospective interventional studies to inform age-specific guidelines.

articles.headline: Review of intervention options against outdoor-biting malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Outdoor biting by malaria vectors: what are the options for intervention?
articles.authors: Braack L, Macdonald MB
articles.details: This literature review assessed classes of interventions to reduce outdoor malaria transmission, particularly in the Asia-Pacific region, including attractive targeted sugar baits, endectocides, outdoor residual spraying, fogging, mass trapping, genetic modification, larval source management, repellents, raised platforms, and zooprophylaxis. Larval source management is effective in some contexts and has WHO endorsement as a supplemental method; spatial emanators/repellents recently received WHO recommendation for indoor application; topical repellents work for short-term exposure; endectocides offer focal potential; and gene drive remains a longer-term vision. The authors conclude no universally applicable tool exists and recommend integrating complementary techniques with community engagement under the WHO Integrated Vector Management framework.

articles.headline: Assessment finds Cambodia's malaria surveillance system strong overall
articles.title_original: Key findings and recommendations from assessment of Cambodia's national malaria surveillance system.
articles.authors: Sovannaroth S, Kugler AM, Ngor P, Brindle H, Chhun B, Filip E, Hem V, Khy V, Matoy RJJ, Ou V, Ran S, Ringwald P, Sok K, Yoem R, Zhang Z, Rekol H
articles.details: Using the WHO Malaria Surveillance Assessment Toolkit, Cambodia's National Center for Parasitology, Entomology, and Malaria Control evaluated 68 indicators across four objectives (Performance, Context and Infrastructure, Technical and Process, Behavior) using 2021–2023 data, with subnational surveys, field and national data quality assessments, and a desk review. Cambodia reported only 1,384 malaria cases in 2023 and aims to eliminate all human malaria by 2025. Of core indicators met: 82% of Performance (14/19), 77% of Context and Infrastructure (8/13), 79% of Technical and Process (4/7), and 100% of Behavior (3/3). Key improvement areas include revising case investigation and classifications, enhancing data cleaning/verification, and upgrading the national malaria information system with unique patient identifiers and interoperability.

articles.headline: Alpha-gal antibodies linked to malaria protection; BSA-α-gal vaccine fails in mice
articles.title_original: Naturally acquired immune responses to alpha-gal in malaria endemic settings and pre-clinical efficacy testing with R21/MM.
articles.authors: Mukhopadhyay ES, Bellamy DG, Tinto H, Hamaluba M, Truby A, Salman AM, Hill AVS
articles.details: This study measured anti-α-gal IgG and IgM antibodies in sporozoite-challenged malaria-naïve UK volunteers and in naturally exposed/infected Kenyan adults and Burkina Faso infants, and tested a BSA-linked α-gal vaccine with Matrix-M adjuvant in alpha-gal transferase knock-out mice. UK volunteers showed no significant antibody increase 90 days post-challenge, while Burkina Faso infants showed rising IgG and IgM over time; protection correlated with higher anti-α-gal IgM (and possibly IgG) levels. BSA-linked α-gal with Matrix-M elicited IgG and IgM responses but failed to protect mice against sporozoite challenge.

articles.headline: Review of population-level interventions to control falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Advances in population-based interventions to control falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Glossop SE, Peto TJ, Adhikari B
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: This review surveys population-level prevention strategies for malaria, noting that case declines since 2000 plateaued after 2015. Insecticide-treated nets contributed most to reducing mortality but face suboptimal distribution, access, and use as well as insecticide resistance, while chemoprevention protects tens of millions of people, primarily children in sub-Saharan Africa, but is threatened by drug resistance. The authors highlight that the R21/Matrix-M vaccine offers greater efficacy, lower cost, and scalable production versus RTS,S/AS01, that next-generation monoclonal antibodies could enable seasonal chemoprophylaxis, and that gene drives may eradicate malaria-transmitting mosquito populations. They argue a multifaceted approach combining these new tools with traditional ones could reinvigorate progress toward malaria elimination.

articles.headline: Submicroscopic knowlesi, vivax and falciparum malaria detected by PCR in North Sumatra
articles.title_original: Submicroscopic Burden of Zoonotic Plasmodium knowlesi Malaria on Mursala Island and Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax Transmission in Mainland North Sumatra, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Lubis IND, Permatasari R, Siahaan L, Cahyadi RAD, Nainggolan IRA, Syafutri RD, Sinambela MN, Jauharah S, Lestari A, Theodora M, Prameswari H, Piera KA, Barber BE, Anstey NM, Grigg MJ
articles.details: Using ultrasensitive reverse-transcriptase real-time PCR, this study screened febrile patients across three mainland districts and Mursala Island in North Sumatra, Indonesia for zoonotic malaria. Among 64 Mursala participants, 7 (10.9%) adults had Plasmodium knowlesi (including 5 agricultural workers and 2 with severe WHO anemia) and 2 (3.1%) had P. vivax; all were negative by microscopy and pan-parasite lactate dehydrogenase rapid tests. No P. knowlesi was found among 947 mainland participants, but PCR detected Plasmodium in 26% (P. vivax 17.5%, P. falciparum 7.5%), with 30% submicroscopic; no P. cynomolgi was found. The authors conclude P. knowlesi transmission is low but can cause severe malaria, and molecular diagnostics remain crucial for surveillance.

articles.headline: Malaria rapid test accuracy varies by transmission level and demographics in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Varying Malaria Rapid Diagnostic Test Accuracy by Regional Transmission Level and Demographics in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Wiener D, Seth MD, Mandara CI, Madebe RA, Popkin-Hall ZR, Giesbrecht D, Bakari C, Lyimo B, Pereus D, Francis F, Mbwambo D, Aaron S, Lusasi A, Lazaro S, Sheahan TP, Parr JB, Bailey JA, Ishengoma DS, Juliano JJ
articles.details: This study compared three national standard malaria rapid diagnostic tests (mRDTs) and a research mRDT against qPCR using samples from a 2021 cross-sectional study in Tanzania, examining how performance varied by age, sex, and regional transmission rate. Differences in mRDT results versus qPCR were found across these factors, and the research test underperformed overall with poor sensitivity across transmission strata. Odds ratios indicated that transmission intensity may affect mRDT agreement and diagnostic performance. The authors conclude these findings inform test accuracy and help reduce outcome misclassification for malaria prevalence estimates.

articles.headline: Uridine supplementation reduces malaria parasitemia in mice and in vitro
articles.title_original: Temporal multi-tissue profiling reveals metabolites linked to immune response and malaria parasitemia control.
articles.authors: Teklemichael AA, Wu J, Bohrnsen E, Xu F, Stadler RV, Obiorah T, Brandes NT, LaCroix IS, Bosio CM, Schwarz B, Su XZ
articles.details: This experimental study infected C57BL/6n mice with four P. yoelii strains and P. berghei ANKA, collecting blood, spleen, and liver on days 4 and 6 post-infection and measuring metabolites by LC-MS/MS. Tissue- and strain-specific changes in glycolysis intermediates, amino acids, and nucleotides correlated with cytokine levels and parasitemia; glycolysis intermediates including bisphosphoglycerate, phosphoenolpyruvate, and dihydroxyacetone phosphate were negatively correlated with parasitemia. Supplementing P. berghei ANKA-infected mice with uridine significantly reduced parasitemia and MCP-1, and uridine also lowered P. falciparum parasitemia in vitro. The authors suggest uridine and metabolic interventions could enhance malaria treatment strategies.

articles.headline: Malaria cases and clusters concentrate in South Korea's capital region, 2024
articles.title_original: Individual Malaria Case and Case Cluster Status in the Capital Region of the Republic of Korea, 2024.
articles.authors: Ko M, Seo YJ, Kim H, Hwang K
articles.details: Surveillance analysis of all malaria cases reported in the Integrated Disease Control Information System in the capital region of the Republic of Korea from January 2022 to December 2024, with spatial analysis in QGIS and clusters defined as multiple cases within 14 days and 1 km. In 2024, 620 cases were reported in the capital city, 94.1% of the national total—a slight decrease from 2023 but a 78.7% increase from 2022. Of 47 identified clusters, five (10.6%) occurred in military units, concentrated in high-risk areas near the North Korean border. Main risk factors were residence (57.8%), work (12.9%), military service (12.9%), and outdoor leisure. Authors call for targeted measures for military personnel and outdoor workers to reach the 2030 elimination goal.

articles.headline: Barriers to adopting multiple first-line malaria therapies in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Barriers and facilitators to the adoption of multiple first-line therapies for management of uncomplicated malaria in Tanzania: a multi-method qualitative study.
articles.authors: Ntissi HH, Itaeli A, Sangeda RZ, Bwire GM
articles.details: This multi-method qualitative study examined barriers, facilitators, and strategies for adopting WHO-recommended multiple first-line therapies (MFT) for uncomplicated malaria in Tanzania, where P. falciparum accounts for 96% of infections, combining a desk review of national data (2020–2024), ACT importation data (2021–2025), and 12 semi-structured interviews with policymakers, regulators, supply chain managers, and providers, analyzed thematically in NVivo. Cases fell from ~8.9 million to 7.2 million before resurging to 8.1 million in 2023; children under five were 34% of cases and 46% of deaths, with declining incidence (p=0.011) but no mortality improvement (p=0.802). Artemether-lumefantrine made up 73.5% of antimalarial imports versus 12% artesunate, 6% artemether, and 2.6% dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine. Barriers included high cost of alternative ACTs, limited provider training, and weak supply chains; the authors conclude MFT is promising but needs import diversification, stronger supply chains, capacity-building, and surveillance-informed policy.

articles.headline: Imported malaria from mainland Tanzania threatens elimination on Pemba Island
articles.title_original: Epidemiological and entomological analysis of imported malaria in Pemba Island, Zanzibar: a study from 2020-2022.
articles.authors: Ali AO, Ali AM, Mwema MF, Mlacha YP
articles.details: Of 2646 malaria cases reported on Pemba Island, Zanzibar between 2020 and 2022, 1411 (53.3%) were classified as imported, with significantly higher proportions among males (57.3%, p<0.0001) and adults over 18 (68%, p<0.0001). Most imported cases came from mainland Tanzania—mainly Tanga (61%), Dar es Salaam (12%), and the Coastal Region (10%)—with only 2% traced to three neighbouring countries. Using negative binomial and univariate/multivariate regression models, importation was lower in Micheweni (IRR=0.48, p=0.001) and during the rainy season (IRR=0.83, p=0.049), higher in Mkoani (IRR=2.45, p<0.001), and significantly higher in 2021 (OR=3.58) and 2022 (OR=5.62) than in 2020; Human Biting Rate was associated with imported cases (p=0.021) while Anopheles biting density was not (r=0.12, p=0.5). The authors call for enhanced cross-border surveillance and spatially targeted interventions.

articles.headline: IPTp-SP use during pregnancy critically low in Somalia
articles.title_original: Factors associated with intermittent preventive treatment (IPTp-SP) use for malaria during pregnancy in Somalia: a multilevel analysis of the 2020 demographic and health survey.
articles.authors: Ali AO, Abdillahi HA, Nadarajah S, Muse AH, Hared YA
articles.details: Cross-sectional multilevel analysis of the 2020 Somalia Demographic and Health Survey, including 7124 women aged 15–49 with a live birth in the preceding 5 years, assessing self-reported use of any sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP/Fansidar) during the last pregnancy, stratified by antenatal care (ANC) attendance. Overall reported SP use was just 6.3%; 69.0% of women reported no ANC visits, and use was highest with 1–3 visits (10.5%), lower with 4+ visits (4.2%), and lowest with no visits (1.8%). Nulliparous women had lower odds (AOR=0.6, 95% CI 0.4–0.9), as did women attending 4+ ANC visits versus none (AOR=0.38, 95% CI 0.25–0.58); nomadic women had higher odds than rural women (AOR=3.7, 95% CI 2.2–6.4). Community-level factors explained 7.4% of residual variance. Authors conclude uptake falls far short of WHO goals, driven by very low ANC attendance (31%).

articles.headline: Machine learning predicts childhood malaria risk in Nigerian displacement camps
articles.title_original: From risk factors to predictive modelling: applying machine learning to childhood malaria surveillance in resource-limited settings.
articles.authors: Ashaolu JO, Akanji TS, Ayansola VI, Olawale-Succes OO, Sunday AJ, Some SYM
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 693 under-5 children in Nigerian Internally Displaced Persons camps, collecting sociodemographic, household, and malaria-knowledge data plus Rapid Diagnostic Test outcomes, and training four machine learning models (Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting Machine) on a 70:30 split. Malaria prevalence was 68.5%. Key risk factors included a caregiver with no education (aOR=3.23, p=0.026), while female caregivers were associated with lower risk (aOR=0.53, p=0.024). Random Forest performed best (AUC=0.892), with caregiver occupation and residential camp the most significant predictors. A notable knowledge-practice gap was found: 60.3% of caregivers knew prevention methods but bed net usage was only 2%. Authors conclude ML can improve malaria risk prediction and support precision public health interventions.

articles.headline: Indonesia and Papua New Guinea launch joint One-Island malaria effort
articles.title_original: Two Nations, One Front: Indonesia and Papua New Guinea forge a One-Island approach to fight persistent malaria on New Guinea.
articles.authors: Prameswari HD, Kisomb J, Mapira P, Keven JB, Susanto H, Isa R, Supriyanto D, Garjito TA, Syafruddin D, Ndoen E, Iswahyudi, Firmansyah, Permana F, Chin N, Lobo NF, Walker ED, Laihad FJ, Das S, Hawley WA, Elyazar IR
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Malaria and dengue show linked timing and location in Peru's Loreto
articles.title_original: Spatiotemporal co-distribution and time lagged cross correlation of malaria and dengue in Loreto, Peru.
articles.authors: Carrasco-Escobar G, Carcamo PM, Kaplan SR, Quispe JM, McCord GC, Benmarhnia T
articles.details: Using district-level time series from the Loreto region of Peru (2000-2021), researchers applied time-lagged cross-correlation (TLCC) analysis to assess temporal and spatial relationships between malaria and dengue incidence. A growing number of districts reported both diseases over time, and maximum TLCC coefficients varied in magnitude, direction, and lag across districts. In the Northwest, malaria increases often preceded dengue increases, while in the Northeast malaria increases preceded dengue decreases; some Northwest areas showed spatial correlation. The authors conclude these patterns can guide targeted integrated vector-control interventions.

articles.headline: Computational screening finds fungal compounds targeting malaria GluRS enzyme
articles.title_original: Rational design of novel Plasmodium falciparum glutamyl-tRNA synthetase inhibitors for the development of next-generation antimalarial drugs.
articles.authors: Nofal W
articles.details: This in silico study screened 1,830 compounds from the MeFSAT medicinal-fungi chemical library against Plasmodium falciparum glutamyl-tRNA synthetase (GluRS) using structure-based virtual screening (PyRx v0.8) and 100-ns molecular dynamics simulations, with chloroquine as control. Two compounds, MSID000152 and MSID000974, showed the strongest binding affinities of -10.4 and -10.1 kcal/mol respectively, with stable complexes (mean RMSD 3.94 Å, 2.35 Å, versus 2.39 Å for chloroquine). MSID000152 also showed favorable MMPB/GBSA binding free energy. The authors propose both as promising antimalarial leads warranting in vitro and in vivo testing.

articles.headline: Placental malaria affects 25% of pregnancies in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Prevalence, predictors and adverse perinatal outcomes of placental malaria in sub-Saharan Africa: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Alemayehu A, Kiya GT, Berhanu S, Zeynudin A, Beyene J, Yewhalaw D
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis pooled 178 observational studies (published 2000-2024) covering 93,809 parturient women from 23 sub-Saharan African countries to estimate placental malaria (PM) prevalence, predictors, and perinatal outcomes. Overall pooled PM prevalence was 25.1% (95% CI 22.0-28.3), ranging from 16.9% by RDT to 34.8% by histopathology. Predictors included primigravidae (OR 2.65), no antenatal care (OR 7.43), HIV infection (OR 2.67), and not using ITNs (OR 2.11). PM increased odds of maternal anaemia (OR 2.18) and low birth weight (OR 1.71). The authors conclude PM is a multifactorial problem requiring multidimensional interventions targeting identified predictors.

articles.headline: Review urges India to shift P. vivax treatment from chloroquine to ACTs
articles.title_original: Plasmodium vivax elimination from India may need therapeutic efficacy studies - Informed shift to artemisinin-based treatment.
articles.authors: Kathpalia M, Kojom Foko LP, Rawat P, Chaturvedi R, Rahi M, Sharma A
articles.details: This review synthesizes clinical, molecular, and pharmacological evidence to argue that India should shift first-line Plasmodium vivax treatment from chloroquine (CQ) to artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) to protect malaria elimination gains threatened by potential CQ resistance. ACTs are cited as offering faster parasite clearance, better tolerability, effectiveness against mixed-species infections, and reduced relapse when combined with primaquine. The authors note the WHO recommends changing first-line treatment when total failure exceeds 10%, and contend that India's therapeutic efficacy study (TES) coverage and molecular resistance-marker data are sparse. They recommend expanding TES and adopting an ACT-based regimen.

articles.headline: Plasmodium berghei proteasome subunit Rpt3 activates protein phosphatase PP1
articles.title_original: Characterization of the Regulatory AAA-ATPase Subunit Rpt3 in Plasmodium berghei as an Activator of Protein Phosphatase 1.
articles.authors: Lainé C, De Witte C, Martoriati A, Farce A, Metatla I, Guerrera IC, Cailliau K, Khalife J, Pierrot C
articles.details: This molecular study characterized the Plasmodium berghei 26S proteasome AAA-ATPase regulatory subunit Rpt3 (PbRpt3) and showed it binds the Protein Phosphatase 1 catalytic subunit (PP1c), an essential parasite phosphatase. PbRpt3 enhanced PP1c activity in vitro and in a Xenopus oocyte heterologous model, with the interaction possibly occurring outside the proteasome and depending on PbRpt3's RVxF motifs and ATP-binding capacity. Reverse genetic studies suggested PbRpt3 is essential during the erythrocytic cycle, and interactome analysis confirmed it belongs to the 19S regulatory particle and may interact with phospholipid-binding proteins.

articles.headline: Malaria pigment hemozoin pushes macrophages toward pro-inflammatory M1 state
articles.title_original: Malaria pigment hemozoin drives M1 pro-inflammatory macrophage polarization in vitro.
articles.authors: Perego F, Parapini S, Calvo-Alvarez E, Dolci M, Delbue S, Ghezzi S, Vicenzi E, Poli G, Basilico N, D'Alessandro S
articles.details: This in vitro study tested whether hemozoin (HZ), the malaria pigment byproduct of heme detoxification, modulates M1/M2 macrophage polarization relevant to severe malaria inflammation. Primary human monocyte-derived macrophages (MDM) and THP-1-derived macrophages (dTHP-1) were stimulated with M1 or M2 signals plus native HZ, with cytokine gene expression and secretion (TNF-α, IL-1β, CXCL8, IL-6, IL-10, PPARG) and STAT6 phosphorylation measured. In non-polarized cells, HZ induced an M1 phenotype, increasing CXCL8, TNF-α, and IL-1β; under polarizing stimuli it further raised CXCL8 and IL-1β in MDM but reduced TNF-α in dTHP-1. HZ did not affect M2 markers. Authors conclude HZ promotes M1 pro-inflammatory differentiation, with model-specific variation.

articles.headline: Nationwide PCR survey finds 63% Plasmodium positivity in Sierra Leone
articles.title_original: Molecular investigation of Plasmodium spp. in Sierra Leone: recommendations on diagnostic methods for malaria control in high-epidemic regions - a nationally representative cross-sectional epidemiological survey.
articles.authors: Zhang M, Mi Z, Wang S, Jalloh MB, Lyu P, Song X, Zhao Y, Zhang J, Xie Y, Wu S, Zheng M, Li X, Yang S, Wang B, Zhao Z, Sevalie S, Sahr F, Hou L, Chen W
articles.details: A nationally representative, age-stratified cross-sectional survey enrolled 2112 individuals aged 2 years or older across Sierra Leone during the rainy season, testing whole blood by qPCR with species identification on 400 positive samples. The Plasmodium qPCR positivity rate was 63.0% (95% CI 61.0–65.1), with a regionally weighted rate of 62.9%. Among the 400 typed samples, 97.75% were P. falciparum and 20.5% carried multiple species; 18.6% of positives had relatively low parasitaemia and 93.5% of infected individuals self-reported as asymptomatic. Positivity decreased in those aged 60+, was higher in males, and showed no significant urban-rural difference. Authors emphasize molecular diagnosis for surveillance and a sustained transmission reservoir.

articles.headline: Maternal blood proteins linked to newborn size in pregnancy malaria
articles.title_original: Association between peripheral markers in women with malaria in pregnancy and small newborns: A cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Dos Santos MI, Carvalho Júnior AR, de Oliveira GM, Gomes LC, Prates Syed WA, Separovic EPM, Epiphanio S, Wunderlich G, Marinho CRF, Dombrowski JG
articles.details: Among 386 pregnant women with and without malaria, drawn from a prior cohort in the Amazon region of Brazil between 2013 and 2015, maternal peripheral Tie2, sFlt1, sENG, and leptin were associated with newborn growth parameters in mothers who had malaria during pregnancy. ELISAs quantified Ang-1, Ang-2, Tie2, VEGF, sFlt1, VEGFR2, PlGF, sENG, and leptin in plasma at delivery, with associations assessed by ROC curves and multivariable logistic regression. sFlt1 was associated with newborn weight and length and sENG with length in Plasmodium vivax-infected women, while Tie2, sFlt1, and leptin were associated with length in Plasmodium falciparum-infected women. The authors suggest these markers may help clarify the biology of malaria in pregnancy in areas of low transmission.

articles.headline: HBV and P. falciparum co-infection in pregnant women in Gabon
articles.title_original: Hepatitis B Virus and Plasmodium falciparum Co-Infection Among Pregnant Women in Gabon: Prevalence, Biochemical Impact, and Antagonistic Replication Dynamics.
articles.authors: Andeme Eyi AS, Mikelet Boussoukou IP, Omouessi ST, Ondh Obame JA, Makoyo Komba O, Djoba Siawaya JF, Ndeboko B
articles.details: A prospective cross-sectional study in Libreville, Gabon (May 2022–May 2023) enrolled 222 pregnant women to assess HBV–P. falciparum co-infection prevalence and biochemical impact, using HBsAg rapid tests with ELISA and ECLIA quantification, parasitemia by rapid test, microscopy and Lambaréné thick film, and liver enzymes (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT). HBV was detected in 9 cases (4.05%): 6 (2.7%) mono-infected and 3 (1.35%) co-infected with P. falciparum; P. falciparum parasitemia occurred in 58 cases (26.1%). HBV mono-infected women showed elevated AST, while co-infected women had normal liver enzymes. An inverse, non-significant relationship between HBsAg levels and parasitemia was observed (Spearman's ρ = -0.5, p = 0.67). Authors suggest possible biological antagonism and a need for integrated screening.

articles.headline: Diet chemicals alter American mosquitoes' susceptibility to malaria parasite
articles.title_original: Dietary para-aminobenzoic acid, uric acid, and antibiotics modulate the susceptibility of Anopheles darlingi and Anopheles albimanus to Plasmodium berghei.
articles.authors: Costa BA, Godoy RSM, Henrique-Gomes L, Santana RAG, Justiniano SCB, Lopes SCP, Monteiro WM, de Melo GC, Secundino NFC, Pimenta PFP
articles.details: This laboratory study tested how post-infection dietary treatments affect susceptibility of the New World vectors Anopheles darlingi and Anopheles albimanus to the rodent malaria parasite Plasmodium berghei. Mosquitoes received para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA), uric acid, or penicillin/streptomycin after infection. Dietary PABA, alone or combined, influenced susceptibility in both species. P. berghei sporozoites were detected in the hemolymph but not in the salivary glands up to 28 days post-infection. The authors report this as the first demonstration that post-infection diet may influence American vectors' susceptibility to P. berghei.

articles.headline: No K13 artemisinin-resistance mutations found in southern Ethiopia parasites
articles.title_original: Preliminary analysis of the conserved Plasmodium falciparum k13 gene in Arbaminch and Mirab Abaya, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Jote K, Gebrehiwot Y, Abebe A, Kipayko C, Dieng CC, Lo E, Golassa L, Chala B
articles.details: This cross-sectional study assessed Plasmodium falciparum Pfkelch13 (k13) propeller-domain polymorphisms in Arbaminch and Mirab Abaya, Ethiopia, analysing 52 PCR-confirmed mono-infection samples by Sanger sequencing, with phylogenetic and haplotype-network comparison to six other African countries. Of 52 participants, 28 (52.8%) were male and 24 (47.2%) female; median age 19 years (IQR 15–32). No k13 polymorphisms were detected (pairwise similarity index 0.982), indicating high genetic conservation, with Ethiopian haplotypes contributing to a dominant African haplotype. A negative Fu and Li's D suggested a possible selective sweep. The authors conclude artemisinin-based combination therapy remains effective and urge continued molecular surveillance.

articles.headline: Designed multi-epitope antigen shows promise for falciparum malaria diagnosis
articles.title_original: In silico design and validation of a Plasmodium falciparum multi-epitope diagnostic antigen (PfMEDA1) for the diagnosis of falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Moyeh MN, Shey RA, Mbulli AI, Dieudonne NL, Sofeu-Feugaing DD, Yega VA, Talla RN, Soh-Bang C, Sherinyuy BC, Ngengang AC, Chick JA, Mbacham WF
articles.details: This study designed and validated PfMEDA1, a chimeric multi-epitope antigen built in silico from linear B-cell epitopes of five immunogenic Plasmodium falciparum proteins joined by GSGSG linkers, then expressed in E. coli and tested by indirect ELISA against human sera from infected (n=223) and non-infected (n=81) cases in Fako Division, South West Region, Cameroon. The protein was predicted soluble, stable, and immunogenic. Mean OD differed significantly between infected and non-infected samples (P<0.0001), giving diagnostic sensitivity of 89.69% (95% CI 84.93–93.35) and specificity of 75.31% (95% CI 64.47–84.22), with Cohen kappa 0.64 versus PCR reference. The authors conclude PfMEDA1 shows diagnostic potential but needs field validation.

articles.headline: Erosion-control pits drove rain-linked malaria outbreak in Uganda
articles.title_original: Malaria outbreak facilitated by increased vector-breeding sites from erosion control pits sustained by intermittent rainfall, Mbale District, Uganda, January-June 2019.
articles.authors: Bahizi G, Monje F, Kadobera D, Bulage L, Ario AR
articles.details: This outbreak investigation in Bumbobi and Nyondo Sub-Counties, Mbale District, Uganda, used descriptive epidemiology and a matched case-control study (150 cases, 150 age- and village-matched asymptomatic controls) plus environmental and entomological assessment, covering confirmed cases from 1 January to 30 June 2019. There were 8,827 case-patients (attack rate 33%), higher in females (36%) than males (27%), peaking 4 weeks after the last rainfall peak. Soil erosion control pits holding stagnant water near homes were strongly associated with illness (OR 18, 95% CI 7.0–50), while wearing long sleeves (OR 0.19) and sleeping under a bed net (OR 0.17) were protective; Anopheles gambiae predominated. Authors recommend draining pits and expanding bed-net coverage.

articles.headline: Metagenomic tool detects multiple viruses in non-malaria fever outbreaks
articles.title_original: NOMAD: metagenomic characterisation of the viral pathogen composition in outbreaks of non-malaria acute febrile illness cases.
articles.authors: Kulohoma BW, Ng'eno I
articles.details: Applying profile Hidden Markov Models derived from Swiss-Prot viral reference sequences to simulated and existing infectious-disease metagenomic datasets (Ebola, hepatitis C, chikungunya, and mosquito-borne arboviruses), this study identified combinations of sequences from multiple viral etiological agents within the same disease sample. The approach targets non-malaria acute febrile illness (NM-AFI) in patients with a negative parasitological test, where little is known about viral causes. It provides a granular view of the multiple viral agents present within a single intra-host disease episode and highlights prevalent strains that could be routinely detected with directed diagnostic tests to improve disease surveillance in endemic regions.

articles.headline: Pyrimidine biosynthesis enzymes are essential in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Genetic analysis of pyrimidine biosynthetic enzymes in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Rajaram K, Sievert ML, Elahi R, Dillard LB, Blauwkamp J, Absalon S, Prigge ST
articles.details: This laboratory genetic study examined three enzymes in the de novo pyrimidine biosynthesis pathway of Plasmodium falciparum: aspartate transcarbamoylase (PfATC), carbamoyl phosphate synthetase II (PfCPSII), and dihydroorotase (PfDHO). Despite a predicted apicoplast trafficking peptide, PfATC showed no apicoplast localization; its knockdown was lethal and could not be rescued by apicoplast metabolic bypass, while N-terminal truncation impaired growth. PfCPSII was likewise essential for proliferation. Deletion of PfDHO in a uracil-salvaging parasite line caused uracil auxotrophy, confirming its essential function. The authors conclude these enzymes represent multiple vulnerable drug-target nodes.

articles.headline: New heterospirocyclic compounds kill drug-resistant P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Novel heterospirocyclic antimalarials with activity against artemisinin- and multidrug-resistant P. falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Theodoridis L, Giannangelo C, El-Saafin F, Prabhath MR, Macraild CA, Sharma P, Merino D, Creek DJ, Carvalho TG
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: Compounds 25 and 26, members of a new class of heterospirocyclic compounds, showed antimalarial activity within 24 hours against a panel of drug-resistant strains of Plasmodium falciparum. Untargeted metabolomics of infected red blood cells suggested compound 25's mechanism may involve disruption of the pyrimidine biosynthesis pathway and haemoglobin catabolism. Neither compound induced major toxicity in kidney- and hepatic-derived human cell lines, indicating specificity. The authors conclude these compounds are a promising opportunity for antimalarial drug development against drug-resistant malaria.

articles.headline: Cerebral malaria in boy after unprotected travel to Indonesia
articles.title_original: [Cerebral malaria after travel to Indonesia].
articles.authors: Pollstergaard KK, Vestergaard LS, Aftab H, Poulsen A
articles.details: This case report describes a young Danish boy who developed severe Plasmodium falciparum malaria after travel to Indonesia without chemoprophylaxis or mosquito protection. Initial flu-like symptoms were misinterpreted as influenza, delaying diagnosis. He presented with cerebral malaria and multiorgan failure requiring intensive care. Rapid treatment with intravenous artesunate led to full recovery. The authors emphasize correct travel advice and early recognition and rapid testing of malaria after travel to endemic areas, even where perceived local risk is low.

articles.headline: Geospatial analysis maps malaria hotspots in under-five children
articles.title_original: Malaria hotspots and risk factors among children under-five years of age across eight West African countries: A geospatial analysis of DHS data.
articles.authors: Aidé ES, Kazienga A, Adegboye O, Sondo P, Tinto H
articles.details: This geospatial analysis used recent Demographic and Health Surveys data from eight West African countries, with malaria infection determined by microscopy among children under five. Generalised Linear Mixed and Geostatistical Models assessed sociodemographic predictors and spatial variation, predicting prevalence at 10×10 km resolution and mapping areas exceeding 30% prevalence. Insecticide-treated net use was significantly associated with reduced odds of infection in four of eight countries, and younger age (<2 years) with lower risk across all countries. Marked spatial heterogeneity was seen, with high predicted prevalence in Benin and Côte d'Ivoire and lower in Ghana and Liberia. The authors stress geospatial approaches for tailoring control.

articles.headline: Humidity and temperature linked to malaria in The Gambia
articles.title_original: Evaluation of the significant association between Climatic Variables and Malaria Cases in the Upper River Region of the Gambia.
articles.authors: Okafor UA, Yaffa S, D'Alessandro U, Ojeh VN
articles.details: This study examined how climatic variables correlate with malaria incidence in the Upper River region of The Gambia, using monthly case data for individuals under five and over five from 2011 to 2022 alongside meteorological data (maximum and minimum temperatures, rainfall, and relative humidity). A Kendall rank correlation and a negative binomial model assessed the associations. The odds of malaria increased by 3–4% in under-fives and 6–9% in over-fives with relative humidity, while decreasing by 20–14% in under-fives and 21–15% in over-fives with maximum temperature (>1% significance level, in the unadjusted and adjusted models respectively). The authors concluded that relative humidity is a major influencing factor and recommend aligning malaria control and prevention programs with key climatic indicators.

articles.headline: P. vivax genomes show distinct adaptations in Duffy-negative East African hosts
articles.title_original: Adaptive evolution of Plasmodium vivax in Duffy-negative hosts: insights from East African genomes.
articles.authors: Ford A, Dieng CC, Abagero BR, Yan G, Yewhalaw D, Janies DA, Lo E
articles.details: Genome-wide SNP analysis of 110 Plasmodium vivax isolates from Ethiopia (38 from Duffy-negative and 72 from Duffy-positive infections) examined adaptations enabling DARC-independent erythrocyte invasion. Duffy-positive parasites showed markedly higher genetic variation (477,561 SNPs) than Duffy-negative ones (197,461 SNPs), with TRAg3, TRAg34, and MSP3-family loci showing the greatest nucleotide diversity. Positive selection differed by host genotype: drug-resistance and erythrocyte-binding genes (MAEBL) in Duffy-positive parasites versus organellar maintenance and vesicle-trafficking genes in Duffy-negative ones. Invasion-ligand substitutions (PvDBP1, PvEBP/DBP2, PvRBP2b) were common in Duffy-positive but largely absent in Duffy-negative parasites. Authors conclude Duffy-negative P. vivax maintains a genetically conserved background reflecting strong selective constraints.

articles.headline: Future climate scenarios cut mosquito survival but not P. vivax infection
articles.title_original: Predicted future climate scenarios impact survival and immune response but not Plasmodium vivax infection in Anopheles aquasalis (Diptera, Culicidae), the primary vector of coastal Central and South America and the Caribbean Islands.
articles.authors: Silva AS, Leda RL, Cella W, Mwangi VI, Santana RAG, Silva-Neto AV, da Silva MNP, Pereira RS, Lopes SCP, Melo GC, Pimenta PFP, Almeida-Val FF, Val AL, Baía-da-Silva DC, Monteiro WM
articles.details: Laboratory study tested IPCC AR5 climate projections (RCP 4.5, 6.0, 8.5) on Anopheles aquasalis survival, P. vivax infectivity, and immune-gene expression in climate-controlled rooms, with mosquitoes infected via artificial membrane feeding. All conditions supported parasite development, with no significant differences in infection rates (54–68%) or oocyst intensity. However, survival declined significantly under RCP 6.0 and 8.5, with day-7 post-infection survival probabilities of 18.6% and 6.5% respectively versus 40% in non-blood-fed controls (p<0.001). Attacin and Defensin expression varied across scenarios, suggesting reduced immune activation under higher heat and CO2; Cecropin showed no significant change. Authors conclude future climatic stress may compromise vector fitness despite retained infectivity.

articles.headline: Review: treating P. falciparum malaria in travelers amid toxicity and resistance
articles.title_original: Treatment of Plasmodium falciparum malaria in the nonendemic setting: a narrative review of toxicity, failure, and emerging resistance.
articles.authors: Bottieau E, Mutsaers M, Balerdi-Sarasola L, Yansouni CP, Rosanas-Urgell A
articles.details: For more than 15 years, artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT) and atovaquone-proguanil have been recommended for treating Plasmodium falciparum malaria in most nonendemic countries. This review covers the epidemiology, pattern, and risk factors for artemisinin-related hemolysis and for treatment failure with ACT or atovaquone-proguanil among travelers, including clinical features, pharmacokinetic parameters, and resistance-conferring mutations in failing cases. The authors note that travelers differ from residents of endemic areas (lack of prior immunity, older age, overweight, comorbidities, and comedication) and may be more vulnerable as reduced susceptibility to key antimalarial drugs spreads. They propose adaptations to the management of malaria in travelers emphasizing structured clinical follow-up to detect toxicity and failure, plus systematic genomic surveillance.

articles.headline: Survey finds high belief in traditional malaria remedies in an Ethiopian town
articles.title_original: Assessment of knowledge, attitudes, and practices regarding the use of traditional medicine for treating malaria among residents of Woreta Town, Amhara Regional State, Northwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Tadesse TY, Ayele TM, Eshetie A, Yazie TS, Hammesso WW, Dagnew FN, Zelalem AE
articles.details: Community-based cross-sectional study assessed knowledge, attitudes, and practices regarding traditional medicine for malaria among 390 residents of Woreta Town, Amhara Regional State, Northwest Ethiopia, using face-to-face interviews. All 390 (100%) responded; 259 (66.4%) believed visiting a modern medicine practitioner soon after using traditional medicine could be beneficial, and 254 (65.1%) reported awareness of antimalarial plants including Bisana (27.9%), Girawa (26.7%), Nechshinkurt (14.6%), Shifarraw (20.1%), and papaya (4.6%). Some reported animal byproducts such as goat bile (19.28%) and preparations like Yekoye Kibe (3.1%). Authors conclude there is high community awareness and belief in traditional remedies, with many recognizing benefits of integrating traditional and modern medicine.

articles.headline: AL efficacy low in Burkina Faso children; DP and AS-pyronaridine perform better
articles.title_original: Efficacy of artemether-lumefantrine, dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine, and artesunate-pyronaridine for the treatment of uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Burkina Faso, 2020-2021.
articles.authors: Gansane A, Tarama C, Lingani M, Debe S, Tiendrebeogo F, Kinda R, Sagnon A, Ganou A, Rouamba T, Sombie S, Some H, Kiendrebeogo S, Bonko MA, Tougri G, Kompaore CS, Wiesner L, Eddis C, Horton B, Kennedy C, Bajic M, Pierre-Louis E, Kahn A, Rosillo S, Patel DS, McCaffery JN, Plucinski MM, Laird V, Ahmed J, Tinto H, Valea I, Moriarty LF
articles.details: Therapeutic efficacy study enrolled children aged 6 months to 12 years with microscopically confirmed uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria at three sites, treated with artemether-lumefantrine (AL), dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP), or artesunate-pyronaridine (AS-PYR) and followed for 28 or 42 days; 1078 participants reached endpoint. Uncorrected 28-day efficacy was 57.1–64.4% for AL, 96.6–98.9% for DP, and 82.2–90.7% for AS-PYR. PCR-corrected efficacy was above the 90% WHO threshold for all drugs and sites except AL in Nanoro (per-protocol 89.4%). No artemisinin-resistance molecular markers were found, and one sample harbored a pfhrp3 single deletion. Authors suggest a multiple first-line therapy approach using non-AL ACT to preserve lumefantrine.

articles.headline: Conference abstracts from 2025 Plasmodium vivax research meeting
articles.title_original: Abstracts of the 9th International Conference on Plasmodium vivax Research (ICPvR) 2025.
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Highly sensitive RDT misses most P. falciparum infections in case detection
articles.title_original: Assessment of a highly sensitive rapid diagnostic test for reactive case detection of Plasmodium Falciparum infections in Myanmar.
articles.authors: Khine SK, Aung PP, Linn NYY, Monti F, Khin NN, Awabdeh D, Topcuoglu E, Hwang J, Tun KM
articles.details: A prospective community-based reactive case detection study in 50 villages of Ann Township, Rakhine State, Myanmar, enrolled 1,990 contacts (aged ≥5 years) of 51 index P. falciparum cases, comparing a highly sensitive RDT (NxTek Eliminate Malaria Ag P.f) and a conventional RDT (SD Malaria Ag P.f/P.v) against ultra-sensitive PCR. P. falciparum positivity was 1.4% by cRDT, 3.9% by hsRDT, and 8.6% by usPCR. hsRDT sensitivity was 27.9% (specificity 98.8%) versus cRDT sensitivity 13.7% (specificity 100%), both differences significant (p<0.01). Authors conclude hsRDTs detect more infections but have low overall sensitivity, so their use should weigh field feasibility and cost-effectiveness.

articles.headline: Malaria in young children costs Burundi households nearly half a month's income
articles.title_original: Economic burden of malaria in children under the age of five in Burundi.
articles.authors: Niyibitegeka F, Manirakiza A, Hezagira N, Sinarinzi P, Abraham P, Devine A
articles.details: A micro-costing study at 15 sites across all five geographic regions of Burundi estimated the cost per malaria episode in children under five from household, health system, and societal perspectives, using caregiver interviews and review of medical and financial records. The average societal cost per episode was US$12.1, with households bearing US$3.3 in out-of-pocket costs and US$4.7 in productivity losses from eight days of caregiving. Health system costs were US$2.6 per outpatient visit and US$44.0 per inpatient stay, varying from US$1.0 (community) to US$11.0 (regional hospital outpatient) and US$42.8–50.8 for admissions. Household costs equaled 44% of monthly individual income; authors call for greater investment in prevention and vaccines.

articles.headline: Fractional-order mathematical model simulates malaria transmission dynamics
articles.title_original: Fractal fractional approach to investigate the forecasting and dynamics of malaria disease for early control precaution.
articles.authors: Farman M, Hurdoganoglu U, Hincal N, Gokbulut N, Guler E, Suer K, Sambas A, Hincal E
articles.details: This modeling study developed a fractional-order malaria transmission model using the Mittag-Leffler kernel, formulated as a deterministic eight-compartment system with human and mosquito subpopulations, to study dynamics and control in Northern Cyprus. The authors established existence and uniqueness of solutions via Lipschitz criteria, derived equilibrium points and the reproductive number for sensitivity analysis, and demonstrated local and global stability using a Lyapunov function. Numerical simulations via the Newton Polynomial method at varying fractal orders showed an asymptotically stable endemic equilibrium, with rising susceptible human populations and declining infectious mosquito numbers, underscoring the need for major control efforts.

articles.headline: Mapping district-level malaria risk and drivers across India, 2019–2023
articles.title_original: Mapping malaria risk in India between 2019 and 2023: a tool for the public to track malaria.
articles.authors: Sam AK, Keshri N, Bhowmick IP, Anvikar A, Phuleria HC
articles.details: Researchers mapped malaria across India's 700+ districts for 2019–2023 using NCVBDC annual district data, National Family Health Survey socio-economic data, and MERRA-2 meteorological and Sentinel land-use satellite data, building region-specific spatiotemporal ensemble models with Getis-Ord Gi* clustering. From 0.33 million cases in 2019, the COVID-19 pandemic significantly reduced reported cases; P. falciparum regions were widespread in North-eastern and Central India, while P. vivax showed post-pandemic geographic expansion into north-eastern areas. Scheduled caste/tribe and economically marginalised populations were most vulnerable, with drinking water practices, maternal education, and healthcare access associated with transmission. A public digital dashboard was developed to track district-level indicators.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence clustered geographically among young Niger children by RDT
articles.title_original: Exploring spatial variation and multilevel modeling of malaria prevalence among children aged 6-59 months based on RDT in Niger: Insights for public health decision-making.
articles.authors: Mulugeta SS, Masresha BM, Ejigu LA
articles.details: Using data from the 2021 Niger Malaria Indicator Survey, this study applied spatial analysis and multilevel logistic regression to RDT-confirmed malaria among children aged 6–59 months. Of 4,724 children examined, 1,121 (23.7%) were RDT-positive. Prevalence was significantly spatially clustered (Moran's I = 0.434, p < 0.001), revealing distinct hotspots and cold spots. Malaria was associated with child age, anemia level, maternal education, the number of children sleeping under bed nets, insecticide-treated net use, the number of children aged 5 and under, residence, and region; the authors emphasize improved ITN coverage and locally tailored interventions.

articles.headline: Ethnicity, occupation, and species drive malaria care-seeking delays
articles.title_original: Drivers of delay in reported malaria care-seeking in Myanmar and Thailand.
articles.authors: Htwe KZZ, Aung PL, Sripoorote P, Soe MT, Petchvijit P, Aung PP, Khamsiriwatchara A, Lawpoolsri S, Cui L, Parker DM, Kyaw MP, Sattabongkot J, Kaewkungwal J
articles.details: This longitudinal study assessed reported time to care-seeking among febrile patients suspected of malaria attending community-based facilities in Myanmar and Thailand, using log-logistic Accelerated Failure Time models to estimate Time Ratios (TRs). In Myanmar (n=2,960), mean fever duration was 1.8 days (SD 1.3); longer delays were linked to Shan ethnicity (TR 1.48), uncertain malaria history, farming, and P. falciparum (TR 1.10) or P. vivax (TR 1.23) diagnosis. In Thailand (n=15,576), mean fever duration was 2.9 days (SD 1.8), with longer delays for farmers, uncertain history, and P. falciparum (TR 1.27) or P. vivax (TR 1.20). Bed net use shortened delays. Authors recommend targeted interventions to promote timely care-seeking.

articles.headline: New method maps Plasmodium protein complex dynamics in intact cells
articles.title_original: MAP-X reveals distinct protein complex dynamics across Plasmodium falciparum blood stages.
articles.authors: Pazicky S, Tjia S, Farias GB, Piwon N, Philip N, Sobota RM, Waters AP, Gilberger TW, Bozdech Z
articles.details: Researchers introduced MAP-X (meltome-assisted profiling of protein complexes), which maps the complexome via thermal proteome profiling in intact cells, and applied it across seven timepoints of the P. falciparum intraerythrocytic developmental cycle. MAP-X predicted more than 20,000 interactions, resolving conserved protein complexes, reproducing previously identified interactions, and finding previously unreported associations. The parasite's protein complexes showed distinct dynamic alterations, and the method predicted moonlighting subunits that dissociate from their native complex to assume different functions. The authors present MAP-X as a resource for uncovering Plasmodium biology at a proteome-wide level.

articles.headline: Conserved RIFIN motif binds LILRB1, linked to lower severe malaria risk
articles.title_original: A subset of Plasmodium falciparum RIFINs is linked to severe malaria risk reduction and engages LILRB1 through a conserved structural motif.
articles.authors: Kassegne K, Shen HM, Chen SB, Xu SJ, Li J, Xu B, Deitsch KW, Wang Y, Zhou XN, Chen JH
articles.details: Using protein microarrays, this immunoproteomic study profiled anti-RIFIN antibody responses in individuals with malaria and correlated them with disease severity, validating candidates by logistic regression and assessing function/structure via surface plasmon resonance, structural modeling, and LILRB1-RIFIN crystallography. The authors identified a RIFIN subset associated with reduced risk of severe malaria. These RIFINs engage the inhibitory receptor LILRB1 through a conserved structural motif anchored by disulfide-bonded cysteines despite extensive sequence diversity, with LILRB1 binding RIFINs and MHC-I via overlapping interfaces. They describe a bifunctional RIFIN subset balancing clinical immunity and immune evasion, offering a blueprint for vaccine design.

articles.headline: Hemoglobin S allele frequency measured in Chad malaria patients
articles.title_original: Allele frequency of Hemoglobin S among patients with uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in N'Djamena, Chad.
articles.authors: Yamti SN, Akouya A, Dezoumbe K, Tawe L, Kgakatsi SRT, Djibagaou AD, Routoubé M, Cappelli G, Atturo S, Dongho GBD, Russo G, Dzomo GRT, Colizzi V, Paganotti GM
articles.details: This study determined the hemoglobin S (HbS) allele frequency and assessed possible protection among patients with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria in N'Djamena, Chad, addressing a scarcity of data from Chad. Infections were identified by microscopy, and human DNA from dry blood spots was analyzed by PCR-RFLP. Among 320 uncomplicated falciparum cases (two-thirds female), the HbS variant allele frequency was 6.72%. No significant association was found between HbS genotypes and parasite density. The authors report a non-negligible HbS frequency—the first such data from Chad—but caution the malaria-survey sample is not representative of the wider population.

articles.headline: Ivermectin mass drug administration cuts scabies in malaria trial
articles.title_original: Direct and indirect protection against scabies through ivermectin mass drug administration designed for malaria in Mozambique: a substudy nested within a cluster-randomised, controlled trial.
articles.authors: Furnival-Adams J, Houana A, Saute F, Elobolobo E, Rudd M, Nicolas P, Montaña J, Martinho S, Mundaca H, Mbanze J, Soares A, Imputiua S, Ruiz-Castillo P, Ribes M, Sanz A, Mamudo Salé M, Macucha A, Casellas A, Lopez V, Vegove V, Mutepa V, Munguambe H, Xerinda A, Materula F, Rabinovich NR, Engelman D, Chaccour C
articles.details: This substudy nested within the open-label, cluster-randomised BOHEMIA malaria trial enrolled 1951 participants from 39 clusters in a malaria and scabies co-endemic community in Mozambique, comparing single-dose ivermectin 400 μg/kg (alone or with livestock dosing) against 400 mg albendazole control, given monthly for 3 months. Scabies prevalence in the ivermectin group fell from 8.10% at baseline to 2.11% at 3 months, with lower odds of scabies than albendazole clusters (aOR 0.18, 95% CI 0.07-0.45, p=0.0002). Reductions occurred in both directly exposed participants and indirectly exposed children under 5 (3 months aOR 0.17; 6 months aOR 0.21). Authors conclude ivermectin MDA for malaria may also benefit scabies control, possibly community-wide.

articles.headline: Review of immunity to the Plasmodium liver stage
articles.title_original: Exposing the hidden: establishing immunity to Plasmodium liver stage infection.
articles.authors: Quin J, Urrutia Iturritza M, Kina ÜY, Matuschewski K, Ankarklev J
articles.details: This review discusses immune responses to the pre-erythrocytic (liver) stage of Plasmodium, the obligatory phase before symptomatic blood-stage infection and a target for blocking both illness and onward transmission. It notes that robust immunity against pre-erythrocytic Plasmodium falciparum fails to develop during natural exposure in endemic settings. The authors highlight how expanding knowledge of key liver-stage immune mechanisms is shaping current and future malaria intervention strategies. No quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: Cerebral malaria case: sudden coma without prior fever
articles.title_original: When malaria hides: a case report of sudden coma without prior fever in cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Muskananfola FV, Purnama A
articles.details: This case report describes a 19-year-old woman who presented with sudden unconsciousness and rigor, with fever denied before admission but developing during hospitalization. A rapid diagnostic test was positive and microscopy later confirmed Plasmodium falciparum infection. She was treated with intravenous artesunate followed by primaquine and dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine, improved with treatment, and follow-up microscopy confirmed parasite clearance. The authors highlight that cerebral malaria can present as sudden coma without a preceding fever history.

articles.headline: Survey finds malaria knowledge gaps across Tanzanian population
articles.title_original: Evaluating the General Public's Knowledge of Malaria: A Nationally Representative Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Thalib HI, Khan S, Pereira M, Abouelmagd F, El Said M
articles.details: This cross-sectional study analyzed secondary data from the 2021-2022 Tanzania Demographic and Health Survey, covering 18,747 individuals aged 15 years and older, to evaluate malaria knowledge and associated demographic factors. Malaria knowledge varied significantly by age, gender, education, and location (p < 0.05), with awareness highest among those aged 20-24 and lowest among those over 50. Urban residents, more educated individuals, and male-headed households showed greater knowledge, and media exposure and mobile phone ownership were associated with increased awareness. The authors conclude that targeted education, digital tools, and improved rural health infrastructure are needed for effective malaria control.

articles.headline: HbS and HbC variants protect Saudi children from severe malaria
articles.title_original: Distribution of HbS, HbC variants, ABO blood groups in relation to malaria infection among Saudi children in Jazan region.
articles.authors: Qahtani SAA, Saleh NM, Alsubaie RS, Almutairi R, Hamid O, Elhassan LA, Abushouk A, Ahmed MS, Nasr A
articles.details: This study of 587 Saudi children in the Jazan region, Saudi Arabia, classified as malaria-free controls (n=196), uncomplicated malaria (n=215), and severe malaria (n=176), examined haemoglobin variants and ABO blood groups in relation to Plasmodium falciparum infection using PCR and ELISA. HbS allele carriers had reduced odds of severe malaria versus controls (OR=0.40, 95% CI 0.22-0.72, P=0.002), and HbC carriers showed even stronger protection against severe malaria (P<0.01). Blood group A was more frequent in malaria cases and linked to higher susceptibility but did not reach statistical significance, while uncomplicated cases had higher total IgG and IgG1-IgG4 levels (P<0.001). Authors conclude HbS and HbC offer significant protection against severe malaria and host genetics shape malaria severity.

articles.headline: High-throughput assay measures antibodies blocking placental malaria parasite adhesion
articles.title_original: A high-throughput assay to measure antibodies that block adhesion of Plasmodium falciparum infected erythrocytes to chondroitin sulfate A.
articles.authors: Dube Y, Hasang W, Teo A, Madanitsa M, Nielsen MA, Ter Kuile FO, Aitken EH, Rogerson SJ
articles.details: Researchers developed a simplified, low-cost adhesion inhibition assay using diaminofluorene to measure haemoglobin release from P. falciparum infected erythrocytes bound to chondroitin sulfate A (CSA), a potential correlate of protection against placental malaria. The assay showed strong reproducibility (r=0.82) and correlated with a published assay (r=0.53). In 466 Malawian pregnant women (321 infected, 145 uninfected), inhibitory antibodies were higher in infected multigravidae (46.3%) than primigravidae (9.7%, p<0.001). Among 162 women infected at mid-pregnancy, those without placental malaria had more inhibitory antibody (33.8%) than those with past placental malaria (12.5%, p=0.002).

articles.headline: Modelling: chemoprevention for school-age children cuts malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Effect of malaria chemoprevention for school-age children across transmission archetypes: a modelling study.
articles.authors: Suresh J, Zimmermann M, Maiteki C, Stahlfeld A, Pratt A, Mathanga DP, Staedke SG, Laufer MK, Drakeley C, Bever C, Cohee LM
articles.details: A modelling study used an established malaria transmission model with three African epidemiological archetypes (Sahelian, Central, Southern) across transmission levels (PfPR2-10 5-40%) to estimate the effect of intermittent preventive treatment (IPT) of school-age children on P. falciparum transmission. Comparing dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine, artesunate-amodiaquine, and sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine-amodiaquine via school- or community-based delivery, frequent monthly long-acting drug campaigns averted 70-90% of cases in school-aged children and 20-60% in younger children and adults, with greater community benefit at lower transmission (PfPR2-10 5-10%). Shorter-acting or less frequent regimens averted 40-60% in school-aged children and 15-50% in others.

articles.headline: Parasite gene families fikk, phist, surf linked to severe malaria
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum gene expression signatures and antibody profiling implicate the fikk, phist, and surf multigene families in severe malaria syndromes.
articles.authors: Cummings BE, Stucke EM, Coulibaly D, Lawton JG, Sobota RS, Kone AK, Kane B, Guindo B, Tangara B, Sissoko M, Maiga F, Traore K, Diawara A, Traore A, Thera A, Dara A, Niangaly A, Daou M, Diarra I, Tolo Y, Sebastian S, Jain A, Nakajima R, Jasinskas A, Silzel EK, Shrestha B, Ouattara A, Munro JB, Laurens MB, Lyke KE, Tebben K, Bogale H, Plowe CV, Sissoko MS, Kouriba B, Takala-Harrison S, Felgner PL, Silva JC, Doumbo OK, Thera MA, Travassos MA
articles.details: In a Mali case-control study, researchers used RNA-seq to compare P. falciparum gene expression in cerebral malaria, severe malarial anemia, and concurrent disease against matched uncomplicated malaria controls. Severe malarial anemia comparisons showed comparatively low differential expression, while cerebral malaria and concurrent disease showed significant differential expression of the fikk, phist, and surf multigene families, implicated in cytoadherence and erythrocytic remodeling. Custom microarrays probed with acute and convalescent sera showed children developed antibody responses to subsets of these proteins after severe malaria, suggesting exposure during illness and potential as targets for natural protective immunity.

articles.headline: Review of Plasmodium falciparum molecular markers of drug resistance
articles.title_original: A review on molecular markers of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Abebe W, Woldesenbet D
articles.details: This review summarizes molecular markers of antimalarial drug resistance in P. falciparum. It describes artemisinin resistance from point mutations in the Kelch13 beta-propeller domain; atovaquone resistance from cytochrome b mutations affecting the ubiquinol binding site; 4-aminoquinoline resistance from mutations in chloroquine resistance transporter (pfcrt), multidrug resistance 1 (pfmdr1), and increased plasmepsin II/III copy numbers; antifolate resistance correlated with substitutions in dihydrofolate reductase and dihydropteroate synthase; and amino alcohol resistance from pfmdr1 and Na+/H+ exchanger 1 mutations. The authors call for transcriptomic, genomic, and proteomic research, combination therapies, and stronger global surveillance.

articles.headline: Review: Pfhrp2/3 deletions threaten rapid malaria diagnosis
articles.title_original: Overcoming the challenges of Plasmodium falciparum histidine-rich protein 2 and 3 deletions in malaria diagnosis and control.
articles.authors: Enyi EO, Nnanna JC
articles.details: This literature review examines Plasmodium falciparum histidine-rich protein 2 and 3 (Pfhrp2/3)-deficient parasites, which pose significant challenges to malaria diagnosis and control. Searching ScienceDirect, Springer, Google Scholar, and PubMed for English-language studies published between 2018 and 2024, the authors selected 83 studies based on predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria. The review highlights the challenges these deletions pose to malaria diagnosis and control and identifies potential solutions, including alternative diagnostic approaches and novel rapid diagnostic tests targeting multiple antigens.

articles.headline: Pakistani P. vivax vaccine candidate gene shows high genetic diversity
articles.title_original: Genetic polymorphisms of merozoite surface protein-3α in Plasmodium vivax isolates from Pakistan.
articles.authors: Nguyễn KO, Kang JM, Võ TC, Lê HG, Akhtar S, Nguyễn TH, Nguyễn ĐTD, Cho M, Afridi SG, Na BK
articles.details: Researchers analyzed genetic polymorphisms and natural selection of the merozoite surface protein-3α gene (pvmsp-3α), a P. vivax vaccine candidate, in 129 Pakistani P. vivax isolates from Khyber Pakhtunkhwa province collected between 2020 and 2021. The gene was amplified, cloned, and sequenced. Diversity arose mainly from size polymorphisms in block I and amino acid changes plus recombination in block II. Compared with populations from other countries, Pakistani pvmsp-3α showed the highest genetic diversity, with distinct natural selection trends and substantial haplotype heterogeneity, informing understanding of this vaccine target globally.

articles.headline: P. vivax CyRPA protein binds erythrocytes, supports vaccine potential
