articles.title_original: Plasmodium vivax cysteine-rich protective antigen (PvCyRPA), an important element for vaccine strategies targeting P. vivax, interacts with human erythrocyte surface.
articles.authors: Cebrián-Carmona J, Arévalo-Pinzón G, Barreto-Santamaria A, Gutiérrez-Ortegón DE, Gómez M, Sánchez-Mirón A, Mesa-Valle CM, Molina-Miras A, Garrido-Cárdenas JA, Patarroyo MA
articles.details: This study characterized the cysteine-rich protective antigen of Plasmodium vivax (PvCyRPA) as a vaccine candidate, examining its expression, localization, and function. The pvcyrpa gene was transcribed and translated in late VCG-1 strain schizonts, with subcellular location suggesting membrane association. Recombinant PvCyRPA produced in baculovirus and COS-7 systems bound both CD71+ reticulocytes and normocytes, indicating a role in erythrocyte interaction. Over 90% of sera from malaria-exposed individuals recognized the protein, confirming strong antigenicity. The authors conclude these findings support PvCyRPA's potential as a target for multi-antigen anti-P. vivax vaccine strategies.

articles.headline: Plasmodium yoelii infection shrinks colon tumors in mice
articles.title_original: Plasmodium yoelii infection inhibits colon cancer in mice via modulation of hypoxia-inducible factor-1α expression.
articles.authors: Jiao Y, Ni Y, Ge L, Zhan H, Xie F, Hao N, Xuan L, Xia H, Fang Q, Tao Z
articles.details: In a mouse study, CT26.WT colon cancer cells were inoculated subcutaneously into BALB/c mice, divided into a tumor control group and a group also infected intraperitoneally with 0.2 mL (5×10^6/mL) of P. yoelii-infected red blood cells. By day 24 of tumor bearing, the P. yoelii-infected group showed significantly downregulated HIF-1α (P<0.01) and VEGFA (P<0.01) and upregulated VHL (P<0.01) and p53 (P<0.01) versus controls. By tumor day 21, infected-group tumors stopped growing and shrank significantly (P<0.001). The authors conclude P. yoelii inhibits tumor development by modulating HIF-1α and related factors.

articles.headline: Malaria and neglected infections common in pregnant migrants, Italy
articles.title_original: Malaria and other neglected infections in pregnant migrants: A retrospective study in Northern Italy.
articles.authors: Todaro L, Sforza A, Zaffagnini A, Cattaneo P, Mazzi C, Rizzolo F, Scarso S, Angheben A, Zammarchi L, Gobbi F, Buonfrate D, Ursini T
articles.details: A retrospective observational study at a referral center in Verona, Italy (February 2023–March 2025) assessed neglected infectious diseases among pregnant migrant women referred for screening, using risk-based serological, molecular, and parasitological assays beyond routine antenatal tests. Eighty-five women (median age 28) from 22 countries underwent extended testing. Malaria was detected in 5/29 tested (17.2%), all asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic; Schistosoma serology was positive in 10/36 (27.8%), Strongyloides in 5/84 (5.9%), and HTLV-1/2 in 3/60 (5.0%). No HIV or Trypanosoma cruzi cases were found. The authors recommend integrating tailored infectious disease screening into antenatal care for migrants.

articles.headline: ART-heme adduct proposed as artemisinin resistance biomarker in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Elucidating artemisinin (ART)-heme as a biomarker for ART resistance and ATP-dependent transport of ART in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Liu H, Zhou H, Cai Y, Du S, Huang Z, Xu K, Xing J
articles.details: This in vitro study characterized artemisinin (ART) metabolism and transport in Plasmodium falciparum, using ART-sensitive strains (Pf3D7/PfDd2) and an ART-resistant strain (Pf6320 carrying K13 mutation C580Y) synchronized at ring and trophozoite stages. The ART-heme adduct was identified as a major ART metabolite with weak antimalarial activity (µM level). In sensitive parasites ART-heme formation was rapid and concentration-dependent (50–450 nM), whereas in resistant parasites it was independent of ART concentration. Formation was susceptible to orthovanadate (a P-type ATPase inhibitor), and ART showed synergy with verapamil. The authors conclude ART-heme may serve as a resistance biomarker and that ART transport is ATP-dependent.

articles.headline: Review of T follicular helper cell biology during malaria
articles.title_original: T follicular helper cell development during malaria.
articles.authors: Antonetti RM, Shah M, Butler NS
articles.details: This review examines how Plasmodium infection shapes CD4 T follicular helper (Tfh) and Th1 cells, which drive phagocyte activation and humoral immunity. The authors note that malaria induces unique host physiologic changes that may generate nonconventional Tfh subsets with mixed phenotype and function and inefficient memory, helping explain why sterilizing, protective immune memory rarely develops. They summarize current understanding of conventional Tfh differentiation, the phenotype and functions of Tfh subsets and memory cells during malaria, and highlight key knowledge gaps that remain.

articles.headline: Single-cell map of Plasmodium development inside Anopheles mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Mapping Plasmodium transitions and interactions in the Anopheles female.
articles.authors: Yan Y, Verzier LH, Cheung E, Appetecchia F, March S, Craven AR, Du E, Probst AS, Rinvee TA, de Vries LE, Kauffman J, Bhatia SN, Nelson E, Singh N, Peng D, Shaw WR, Catteruccia F
articles.details: Using single-cell RNA sequencing of both P. falciparum parasites and Anopheles mosquito cells across developmental stages and metabolic conditions, the authors mapped key transitions and mosquito-parasite interactions in the midgut. Functional analyses identified processes regulating oocyst growth and showed the transcription factor PfSIP2 is essential for sporozoite infection of human hepatocytes. Shared barcode analysis with confocal microscopy revealed parasites preferentially interact with midgut progenitor cells during epithelial crossing and that late oocysts form tight connections with surrounding muscle cells. Findings were confirmed across several mosquito-parasite combinations, including field-derived parasites, and identify transmission-blocking candidates.

articles.headline: New compound kills P. falciparum by blocking protein synthesis
articles.title_original: A Novel Antimalarial Agent that Inhibits Protein Synthesis in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Bravo P, Diamanti E, Hamed MM, Bizzarri L, Wiedemar N, Passecker A, Brancucci NMB, Albisetti A, Gumpp C, Illarionov B, Fischer M, Witschel M, Schehl T, Hahne H, Mäser P, Rottmann M, Hirsch AKH
articles.details: Researchers identified Compound 31, a novel antimalarial with a 2-hydroxyphenyl benzamide scaffold, showing potent activity against blood-stage and mature sexual stages of Plasmodium falciparum and no toxicity in human cells. Resistance selection identified a previously unknown point mutation in the P. falciparum multidrug-resistance protein 1 (pfmdr1) gene, confirmed as the primary resistance mediator by CRISPR/Cas9 editing, with no cross-resistance to first-line antimalarials. Proteomics indicated the compound's primary mode of action is direct binding to cytosolic ribosomal subunits, inhibiting parasite protein synthesis. The authors conclude it is a promising next-generation antimalarial starting point.

articles.headline: Sickle cell trait raises P. falciparum infection risk in older individuals
articles.title_original: Association between sickle cell trait and Plasmodium falciparum infection: evidence for age-specific protection in a cross-sectional study in Guinea-Bissau.
articles.authors: Silva R, Rodrigues A, Arez AP, Medeiros MM
articles.details: Genotyping of β-globin in DNA from 601 dried whole blood samples collected in the 2017 national malaria prevalence survey in Guinea-Bissau assessed sickle cell trait (SCT) frequency and its association with Plasmodium falciparum infection. SCT carriers (32.0%), concentrated in island regions and their corresponding ethnic groups, were more likely to have microscopic P. falciparum infection than non-carriers overall (OR=2.3, 95% CI 1.4-3.5) and in age-stratified analyses (<5 years OR=2.3, 95% CI 1.0-5.2; 15-24 years OR=5.0, 95% CI 1.8-13.8). However, among infected children under 5, carriers had lower parasitemia and higher hemoglobin. The authors conclude SCT protects young children from severe outcomes while increasing microscopic infection risk in older individuals.

articles.headline: Review of sex determination in malaria gametocytes
articles.title_original: Pink or blue: sex determination in malaria gametocytes.
articles.authors: Pradel G, Petter M
articles.details: This review discusses molecular mechanisms governing sex determination in the malaria parasite Plasmodium, proposing it is decided at two checkpoints. First, chromatin remodeling activates the master regulator AP2-G, triggering a transcriptional cascade that commits parasites to sexual development and drives expression of genes for male and female gametocytes. Second, post-transcriptional processes—including stabilization and suppression of transcripts via RNA-binding proteins—guide branching into male or female gametocytes through threshold-trait-dependent mechanisms. The authors highlight recent advances in understanding these two checkpoints.

articles.headline: Review: GFAP as a biomarker for cerebral malaria neuroinflammation
articles.title_original: Current status of the GFAP's potentials in cerebral malaria, a systematic review.
articles.authors: Tulantched DSM, Min Z
articles.details: A systematic review (PRISMA 2020; PROSPERO CRD42024558265) assessed glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP) as a diagnostic and prognostic biomarker in cerebral malaria from Plasmodium falciparum. From 2,147 records, 24 studies were included (9 human, 15 experimental). In experimental models, GFAP was consistently elevated in brain and retinal tissues, correlating with astrocyte activation and neuroinflammation, but human studies were variable, with several reporting no significant increase in plasma GFAP in patients versus controls. Authors conclude GFAP shows promise but lacks diagnostic specificity and consistency, and standardized, longitudinal studies are needed.

articles.headline: Review: how malaria parasites hijack red blood cell proteins
articles.title_original: Crosstalk between malaria and host proteome during the intraerythrocytic developmental cycle.
articles.authors: Pazicky S, Bozdech Z
articles.details: This review examines how Plasmodium falciparum modifies the host red blood cell (RBC) proteome during its asexual intraerythrocytic developmental cycle. The parasite remodels the RBC cytoplasm and surface and alters deformability, and increasingly is shown to engage, modify, and import specific RBC proteins to support functions essential for its growth and development. The authors note that most mechanistic details remain poorly understood and discuss the potential of targeting host proteome interactions to design host-directed antimalarial therapies.

articles.headline: Vesicle adaptor proteins AP-1, AP-3, AP-4 essential for malaria parasite survival
articles.title_original: Vesicle adaptors in malaria parasites show conservation and flexibility of protein sorting machinery.
articles.authors: Cubillán-Marín J, Fröhlke U, Ramón-Zamorano G, Mainye S, Mesén-Ramírez JP, Farias GB, Höhn K, Gilberger TW, Bártfai R, Spielmann T
articles.details: This study characterized vesicle adaptor complexes AP-1, AP-3, and AP-4 in malaria parasites, showing all three are important for parasite survival. AP-1 was needed for intracellular growth, biogenesis of specialized invasion organelles, and formation of invasive progeny, acting through the multi-ligand receptor sortilin, while AP-3 and AP-4 were both required for host cell invasion, with AP-4 sorting multi-transmembrane proteins. Proximity proteomics from live cells revealed the sorting machinery is largely conserved with distant model organisms but includes unconventional features such as tepsin functioning with AP-1 and clathrin with AP-4, indicating unexpected exchangeability of conserved sorting elements.

articles.headline: Malaria chloroquine resistance transporter expressed across multiple life stages
articles.title_original: Stage-dependent expression and vacuolar localization of Plasmodium berghei chloroquine resistance transporter (CRT).
articles.authors: Korbmacher F, Rauch M, Kenthirapalan S, Kooij TWA, Maier AG, Matuschewski K
articles.details: Using CRT-mCherry endogenously tagged Plasmodium berghei parasites, researchers profiled the spatio-temporal expression of the chloroquine resistance transporter (CRT), a digestive vacuole membrane protein. CRT was expressed during asexual blood stage growth and localized to the hemozoin-containing digestive vacuole, and was also abundant in gametocytes and ookinetes up to mosquito midgut colonization. Expression was switched off during sporogony and early liver infection but reappeared in mature liver stages, likely in preparation for blood infection. The work shows CRT and the digestive vacuole compartment persist beyond asexual propagation in erythrocytes.

articles.headline: Clinical risk score predicts artesunate treatment failure in malaria patients
articles.title_original: Development of a clinical risk scoring system for artesunate treatment failure prediction in malaria patients.
articles.authors: Kim D, Lee DH, Song Y, Kim JS, Gwak HS
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: This retrospective study developed a clinical risk scoring system to predict failure of intravenous artesunate therapy in adult patients (≥18 years), using WHO criteria for early or late clinical failure and logistic regression with backward elimination. Among 98 patients, treatment failure occurred in 12 (12.2%). Female sex, parasitemia >5%, and impaired consciousness were independent risk factors; predicted failure probabilities for scores of 0–4 points were 5%, 16%, 41%, 72%, and 90% (AUROC = 0.768, 95% CI 0.605–0.931). Authors conclude the tool helps identify high-risk patients needing intensified monitoring or alternative treatment.

articles.headline: Review: mechanisms and global threat of drug-resistant malaria
articles.title_original: The evolving threat of drug-resistant malaria in a globalized world.
articles.authors: Shukla S, Mani A
articles.details: This review examines drug resistance in malaria, focusing on Plasmodium falciparum, the most lethal species and the leading cause of malaria deaths. It discusses the molecular mechanisms of action of antimalarial drugs and the genetic basis of resistance, the global impact of resistant parasites, and the challenges they pose to malaria control programs. The authors emphasize the urgent need for real-time surveillance systems and argue that a deeper understanding of parasite drug resistance could support improved treatment and more effective control strategies.

articles.headline: Review: how malaria infection drives telomere shortening
articles.title_original: Malaria infection and telomere length: A review.
articles.authors: Wakai TN, Fiamitia C, Oba EB, Chinedu SN, Afolabi IS
articles.details: This review explores the relationship between malaria infection and telomere length, an area the authors say remains underexplored. It describes how acute malaria initiates rapid telomere degradation, promotes accelerated cellular senescence, and suppresses telomerase expression, with possible partial recovery as the parasite clears during treatment, while prolonged Plasmodium exposure in highly endemic regions may further accelerate telomere shortening and increase susceptibility to age-related ailments. The authors discuss roles for oxidative stress and inflammatory mediators, and consider telomere length as a potential biomarker for malaria severity and treatment outcomes.

articles.headline: Malawi isolates show CQ-sensitivity return but rising partner-drug resistance markers
articles.title_original: Emergence of chloroquine-sensitive Plasmodium falciparum and rising resistance to first-line artemisinin partner drugs in Malawi.
articles.authors: Mazigo E, Jun H, Lee WJ, Louis JM, Syahada JH, Fitriana F, Muh F, Ahmed MA, Lu F, Han JH, Kwon TH, Lee SJ, Na S, Chun W, Park WS, Han ET, Kidima W, Han JH
articles.details: This study assessed molecular markers of antimalarial resistance in 1582 blood samples from individuals seeking diagnosis in Mzuzu and Lilongwe, Malawi, between December 2020 and June 2021, with P. falciparum confirmed by nested and quantitative PCR and resistance genes (pfmdr1, pfcrt, pfk13, pfatp6, pfdhfr, pfdhps) sequenced by Sanger sequencing. No pfk13 or pfcrt mutations were found, supporting continued artemisinin and chloroquine susceptibility and suggesting re-emergence of chloroquine-sensitive parasites. The pfmdr1-NFD haplotype (reduced lumefantrine susceptibility) was present in 159/371 (42.9%), and the quadruple pfdhfr-pfdhps mutant haplotype (AIRNVI-SGEAA, high-level SP resistance) in 287/328 (87.5%). The authors stress continued molecular surveillance.

articles.headline: FIKK kinase chimeric antigen as novel P. falciparum diagnostic target
articles.title_original: Antigenic determinant analysis of FIKK Kinase family to identify novel candidates for diagnosis of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Rajendra Prasad M, Anil Kumar D, Dodwani E, Singh S, Trivedi V
articles.details: This study designed a chimeric FIKK (C-FIKK) protein as a diagnostic target for Plasmodium falciparum by selecting highly antigenic, species-specific B- and T-cell epitopes from six FIKK kinases using IEDB tools, taken from N-terminal regions with no homology to human proteins. Antibodies raised against C-FIKK showed high sensitivity with a detection limit of 3 μM for FIKK9.1 and were selective for the parasite, with no cross-reactivity to host blood components or to bacterial, viral, fungal, and algal organisms. Validation with mock patient samples semi-quantitatively detected parasite loads with an average accuracy of 95.45%. The authors propose FIKK-based rapid diagnostic tests as a robust next-generation diagnostic.

articles.headline: Once-daily arterolane-piperaquine matches artemether-lumefantrine in Nigerian malaria trial
articles.title_original: Arterolane and piperaquine Vs. artemether and lumefantrine in uncomplicated malaria: A randomized study in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Awodele O, Bello IS, Akodu BA, Ahmed AA, Usman S, Bolarinwa RA, Akinyede A, Adelabu HA, Akande EO, Dey J, Kudrigikar V, Rao PS, Mehta S
articles.details: This phase IV, randomized, multicenter, open-label, active-controlled study in Nigeria compared arterolane maleate-piperaquine phosphate (AMP, 150 mg+750 mg once daily) with artemether-lumefantrine (AL, 80 mg+480 mg twice daily), each for 3 days, in 350 patients aged 12-65 with acute uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria. The primary endpoint, PCR-corrected adequate clinical and parasitological response (ACPR) on Day 28, was 100% in both groups and sustained to Day 42. PCR-uncorrected ACPR was comparable (Day 28: AMP 98.2%, AL 99.4%, P=0.352; Day 42: AMP 98.2%, AL 98.8%, P=0.674), with no significant differences in fever or parasite clearance times and no drug-related or severe adverse events. The authors conclude once-daily AMP is comparable to twice-daily AL.

articles.headline: PBPK model explains how malaria infection alters pyronaridine pharmacokinetics
articles.title_original: Importance of Lysosomal Trapping and Plasmodium Parasite Infection on the Pharmacokinetics of Pyronaridine: A Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Model-Based Study.
articles.authors: Chu WY, Schouten WM, Mavoko HM, Tshiongo JK, Yobi DM, Kabasele FA, Kasereka G, Maketa V, Sevene E, Vala A, Shin J, D'Alessandro U, Kayentao K, Huitema ADR, Dorlo TPC
articles.details: This study built a physiologically based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) model in PK-Sim and MoBi to explain pyronaridine disposition, adding lysosome compartments and Plasmodium parasite compartments within red blood cells scaled by parasitemia, drawing on data from a phase I clinical trial and the PYRAPREG study. Model optimization used data from 52 healthy individuals and 25 patients with malaria. Incorporating lysosomal sequestration was essential to capture distribution, and accounting for low hemoglobin plus drug accumulation in the parasite compartment described whole-blood pharmacokinetics. Free pyronaridine in the parasite compartment was over 10-fold higher than in whole blood; higher parasitemia raised AUC0-24h and Cmax mainly on day 1, while the subsequent hemoglobin decline lowered them.

articles.headline: Proteasome inhibitor ONX-0914 shows potent antimalarial activity in vitro and in mice
articles.title_original: Dual-targeting proteasome inhibitor ONX-0914 demonstrates potent antiplasmodial activity for malaria treatment.
articles.authors: Van Truong N, Nguyen TK, Thau NS, Chu TH, Sanaullah B, Venkataramaiah C, Han JH, Na SH, Park WS, Chun WJ, No JH, Han ET
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: Researchers tested the proteasome inhibitor ONX-0914 against Plasmodium in vitro and in vivo. It inhibited chloroquine- and artesunate-sensitive and -resistant P. falciparum strains at low nanomolar IC50 (<50 nM), synergized with artesunate, and showed low human-cell toxicity (CC50 >100 μM), acting via dual inhibition of hemoglobin metabolism and the ubiquitin–proteasome system. In vivo against P. berghei ANKA, treatment suppressed parasites by >95% after 4 days, while a single dose given by oral, intraperitoneal, or intravenous routes increased survival rate and mean survival time (ED50 7.62 mg/kg oral, 6.52 mg/kg intraperitoneal). Treatment also produced <2% recrudescence at one month, reduced organ lesions, and 23.83% bioavailability, leading the authors to conclude ONX-0914 is a promising antimalarial agent.

articles.headline: Brain endothelial adhesion receptors mapped in fatal pediatric cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Mapping the expression of endothelial adhesion receptors for Plasmodium falciparum-infected erythrocytes in fatal cerebral malaria in Malawian children.
articles.authors: Dorovini-Zis K, Li H, Zhe C, Zhang B, Small D, Taylor TE
articles.details: This post-mortem study examined expression of 5 cytoadhesion receptors for PfEMP1 across 12 brain regions in 50 Malawian children—27 with cerebral malaria and 23 with non-malarial causes of death—and quantified colocalization with sequestered infected red blood cells (iRBC). ICAM-1 was the most widely expressed receptor, followed by CD36, VCAM-1, E-selectin, and thrombospondin; receptor-expressing microvessels were most numerous in the frontal lobe and fewest in brainstem and cerebellum. Colocalization was highest for ICAM-1 and CD36 and greatest in the frontal lobe, with sequestration ratios near 100% across regions. Receptor expression was greater in brain than peripheral organs, potentially underpinning regional iRBC sequestration in cerebral malaria.

articles.headline: TLR4 and TLR9 gene variants linked to P. vivax parasitaemia and symptoms
articles.title_original: New Evidence of TLR4 and TLR9 Variants Influencing Parasitaemia and Symptoms of Plasmodium vivax Infection.
articles.authors: Silva MMRI, Correia GMA, Lindenau JD, Cunha MG, Ohnishi MDO, Ventura AMRS, Ribeiro-Dos-Santos Â, Hutz MH, Sortica VA
articles.details: This study genotyped 148 individuals with symptomatic uncomplicated Plasmodium vivax malaria from the Amazon region of Pará, Brazil, for variants in TLR1, TLR4, TLR7, TLR8, TLR9, and TIRAP (rs4833095, rs1927911, rs179008, rs3764880, rs352140, rs8177374), testing associations with parasitaemia, gametocytaemia, and clinical index via generalised linear models. TLR9 rs352140 TT homozygotes had higher parasitaemia than C allele carriers (p=0.034), and TLR4 rs1927911 GG homozygotes had a higher clinical index than A allele carriers (p=0.018). The authors report the first association of the intron 1 TLR4 rs1927911 variant with P. vivax malaria symptoms in this population.

articles.headline: Review: ACKR1/Duffy antigen roles extending beyond malaria
articles.title_original: The emerging roles of ACKR1/Duffy antigen beyond malaria.
articles.authors: Shaikh R, Ghosh K, Gorakshakar A
articles.details: This perspective reviews atypical chemokine receptor 1 (ACKR1), formerly the Duffy antigen/receptor for chemokines (DARC), known for its role in Plasmodium vivax invasion of erythrocytes. The authors highlight emerging functions beyond malaria, including involvement in inflammation, cancer biology, hematopoiesis, and several diseases, and discuss potential therapeutic applications. No quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: Designed STEVOR multiepitope fusion antigen shows broad malaria vaccine promise
articles.title_original: Conserved multiepitopes in Plasmodium falciparum STEVORs enable rational design of a fusion antigen vaccine construct with broad immunogenicity.
articles.authors: Sun ZS, Yin JX, Zhao HQ, Zhu YS, Chen SB, Shen HM, Xu B, Zhou XN, Wang TY, Yang WX, Duan YW, Chen JH, Kassegne K
articles.details: Using computational immunology, researchers identified conserved overlapping B- and T-cell epitopes (7–27 amino acids) in the semiconserved domain of four Plasmodium falciparum STEVOR antigens linked to severe malaria, with conservation confirmed across 138 clinical isolates (Togo and Brazil) and 342 global strains. The designed fused multiepitopes showed high IgG reactivity in infected individuals' sera, had no allergenicity/toxicity, and induced CD4+ and CD8+ T-cell proliferation and IgG antibodies in BALB/c mice. On this basis, a multiepitope fusion antigen (MEFA) construct achieved 97.15% global combined HLA coverage and elicited both cellular and humoral immunity in silico. Recombinant MEFA expressed in E. coli was recognized by significantly more anti-STEVOR IgG in nonsevere than severe malaria sera, suggesting association with milder disease and potential as a severe malaria vaccine candidate.

articles.headline: Lepidobotrys staudtii extracts kill drug-sensitive and resistant P. falciparum in vitro
articles.title_original: In vitro antiplasmodial activities of extract and fractions from Lepidobotrys staudtii against sensitive and resistant blood-stage parasites of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Magoudjou Pekam JN, Efange NM, Mishro L, Keumoe R, Ndjakou BL, Ayong L, Njayou FN, Moundipa PF, Rajendran V
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: This in vitro study tested crude extract and fractions from the stem bark of Lepidobotrys staudtii against blood-stage Plasmodium falciparum (Pf3D7 drug-sensitive and PfINDO chloroquine-resistant). All preparations showed IC50 below 10 μg/mL; the hexane and ethyl acetate fractions were most potent (IC50 3.73 and 3.4 μg/mL against Pf3D7), with no cytotoxicity up to 500 μg/mL. Ring-stage parasites were particularly susceptible (IC50 2.17–4.87 μg/mL for Pf3D7; 2.27–6.27 μg/mL for PfINDO), and combining a fraction with standard antimalarials at sub-inhibitory concentrations significantly reduced IC50 values. The ethyl acetate fraction arrested parasite development and merozoite egress, supporting the plant as a potential antimalarial source.

articles.headline: Computational study models PfCRT and PfDHFR mutation effects on drug binding
articles.title_original: Computational investigation of mutations in PfCRT and PfDHFR proteins for emerging resistance of Plasmodium falciparum to antimalarial drugs.
articles.authors: Ghosh S, Joshi D, Ponnusamy CS, Sridharan B, Velusamy M
articles.details: This computational study used molecular docking and molecular dynamics simulations to assess how mutations in PfCRT (chloroquine resistance transporter) and PfDHFR (dihydrofolate reductase) affect interactions with nine antimalarial drugs, including piperaquine, in Plasmodium falciparum. The PfCRT-K76A–piperaquine complex showed strong binding affinity (−9.5 kcal/mol) with moderate structural deviation, while the PfDHFR-N51I–piperaquine complex bound even more strongly (−10.8 kcal/mol) but with higher structural fluctuation (RMSD 4.491 ± 1.462 nm) and increased compactness, which may reflect restricted ligand accommodation and possible resistance. The authors conclude these findings clarify how the mutations contribute to drug resistance and provide a foundation for designing more effective antimalarial strategies, pending experimental validation.

articles.headline: Cryptic C-terminal ATP site found on P. falciparum Hsp90 chaperone
articles.title_original: Investigation of a cryptic ligand binding site on Plasmodium falciparum Hsp90.
articles.authors: Mansfield CR, Taggart EL, Chirgwin ME, Derbyshire ER
articles.details: This study characterized the ligandability of the less-conserved C-terminus of the Plasmodium falciparum chaperone PfHsp90 as a potential alternative antimalarial drug target, since inhibitors of the highly conserved N-terminal ATP site are lethal but hard to make selective over the human homolog. Using in silico and in vitro methods, affinity experiments with a lysine-reactive nucleotide analog and an ATP resin supported a specific interaction between ATP and a C-terminal truncation of PfHsp90. Limited proteolysis suggested association with ATP, dATP, and ADP, but not AMP or GTP, and computational analyses plus mutagenesis probed the molecular basis. The authors conclude the work lays a foundation for developing C-terminal Hsp90 inhibitors against Plasmodium parasites.

articles.headline: Intron variant linked to emerging pfdhps resistance haplotypes in Africa
articles.title_original: A novel intron variant is associated with emerging pfdhps mutant haplotypes in West and Central African Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Hocke EF, Hansson H, Chopo-Pizarro A, Adegbola AJ, Bolaji O, Ngwa Niba PT, Ali IM, Nji A, Mbacham W, Baraka V, Kulaya NB, Kahunu GM, Mavoko HM, Nkoli PM, Sompwe EM, Mbongi D, Mitashi P, Bédia VA, Gnagne PA, Konaté A, Yavo W, Menan H, Beshir K, Mousa A, Sutherland CJ, Alifrangis M, Roper C
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: Targeted amplicon sequencing of the pfdhps first intron and second exon in 929 Plasmodium falciparum-positive blood samples from Nigeria, Cameroon, Tanzania, the Democratic Republic of Congo, and Côte d'Ivoire identified a non-coding mutation, a548383t, that expands a di-nucleotide repeat in the first intron of pfpppk-dhps. Found in Nigeria, Côte d'Ivoire, and Cameroon in association with the 431V mutation, the intron mutation was most strongly associated with the VAGKGS haplotype (OR = 211.7, P < 0.001), followed by VAGKAS (OR = 39.2, P < 0.001) and VAGKAA (OR = 33.6, P < 0.001), with reduced di-nucleotide repeat diversity in 431V-positive variants. The authors conclude the variant is significantly associated with 431V, consistent with selective sweeps, but cannot yet distinguish genetic hitchhiking from a functional advantage.

articles.headline: DRC community case management mostly follows malaria guidelines for under-fives
articles.title_original: Assessment of adherence to the National Malaria Guidelines in treatment management of malaria among children under five in integrated community case management in the DRC.
articles.authors: Ibrahimi S, Kadiobo L, Embeke N, Diarra H, Tshiswaka G, Mukumena E, Brunner B
articles.details: This study assessed adherence to the National Malaria Guidelines in Integrated Community Case Management (ICCM) of malaria in children under 5, using 2,326 children from 30 ICCM sites in the DRC, with chi-square tests and multiple logistic regression. About 63.8% of children had malaria; 94.8% (n=1,407) of those clinically diagnosed received a rapid diagnostic test versus 2.8% (n=42) who did not (p=0.0001), and children with malaria were more likely to receive treatment (93.4% vs 6.6%, p=0.0001), though 18.5% without malaria still received treatment. Compared to Lualaba, Kasai Oriental was more likely to adhere to RDT testing (AOR=1.89, CI 1.51-2.37) and Tanganyika less likely (AOR=0.40). The authors conclude the findings can inform programs to improve guideline adherence and quality of care.

articles.headline: Novel HDAC inhibitor kills P. falciparum at 20 nM in vitro
articles.title_original: Antiplasmodial activity of pentyloxyamide-based histone deacetylase inhibitors against Plasmodium falciparum parasites.
articles.authors: Dawood WA, Fisher GM, Kremeyer J, Fischer F, Home JL, Goodman CD, Lam KS, Maier AG, Kurz T, Andrews KT
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: This in vitro study screened a panel of 24 histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitors bearing a hydroxamic acid zinc-binding group, a pentyloxyamide linker, and substituted 4-phenyl/4-(pyridinyl)thiazole caps against asexual intraerythrocytic-stage P. falciparum. The most potent compound, 4o, had a P. falciparum IC50 of 20 nM with >250-fold selectivity over human cells and was also active against exoerythrocytic-stage parasites (IC50 24 nM), but lacked potent activity against late-stage gametocytes (IC50 >2 μM). Compound 4o caused hyperacetylation of P. falciparum histone H4 and stabilised PfHDAC1, indicating deacetylase inhibition. The authors present these compounds as therapeutic starting points with novel mechanism for malaria.

articles.headline: Indigenous P. vivax cases rising across three WHO regions
articles.title_original: Indigenous Plasmodium vivax upsurge in the Eastern Mediterranean, Western Pacific, and South East Asia regions - beyond the constant culpability of climate change, COVID-19, and armed conflicts.
articles.authors: Kojom Foko LP, Sharma A
articles.details: This analysis of epidemiological data examined indigenous Plasmodium vivax cases in the Eastern Mediterranean, Southeast Asia, and Western Pacific regions in recent years. Significant upsurges were seen in more than half of the countries; in Papua New Guinea and Yemen the increase has been consistent since 2015, while Indonesia and Pakistan experienced sharp rises between 2020 and 2023 (e.g., +100% and +83.4%). The authors note Thailand is unlikely to meet the WHO E-2025 elimination target, that factors such as COVID-19, armed conflicts, and climate change do not fully explain the resurgence (chloroquine resistance also matters in PNG), and call for a compartmentalized approach.

articles.headline: Microbial natural product screen finds antiparasitic hits versus malaria and toxoplasma
articles.title_original: Screening and hit evaluation of a microbial metabolite library against the pathogenic Plasmodium falciparum and Toxoplasma gondii parasites.
articles.authors: Gancheva MR, Mao EY, Romeo O, Vuong D, O'Handley R, Page SW, Lacey E, Wilson DW
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: Researchers screened the BioAustralis Discovery Plates Series I library of 812 microbial natural product compounds against erythrocytic-stage Plasmodium falciparum and Toxoplasma gondii tachyzoites. They identified 219 compounds inhibiting P. falciparum growth by at least 80% at 2 μg/mL and 149 with equivalent activity against T. gondii, spanning more than 50 compound classes. After triaging for low mammalian cytotoxicity and defining IC50 values, four compound groups showed low-nanomolar activity; pladienolide B and cryptopleurine were very potent but also cytotoxic, while monocyclic thiazole peptides (e.g., micrococcin P1, thiocillins) and pleuromutilins showed both low antiparasitic IC50 and low cytotoxicity. The authors highlight these as potential starting points for novel antiparasitic development.

articles.headline: NMR backbone assignments reported for P. falciparum flap endonuclease
articles.title_original: (1)H, (15)N and (13)C backbone resonance assignments of flap endonuclease from Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: do Aido-Machado R, Baxter NJ, Rowe ML, Pohare MB, Vitovski S, Sayers JR, Waltho JP
articles.details: This work reports the 1H, 15N and 13C backbone resonance assignments of residues 2-349 of flap endonuclease (FEN) from Plasmodium falciparum (PfFEN349) in its substrate-free state, using heteronuclear multidimensional NMR spectroscopy. About 90% of all backbone resonances were assigned, with 298 of 337 theoretically detectible backbone amide resonances identified in the 1H-15N TROSY spectrum. Predicted solution secondary structure from chemical shift analysis (TALOS-N) mostly agreed with an AlphaFold model. The authors note PfFEN349 is a potential target for inhibitor development against malaria, and the assignments provide a pathway for drug discovery.

articles.headline: Heme detoxification protein resists soluble recombinant expression in E. coli
articles.title_original: Recombinant expression and purification of Plasmodium heme detoxification protein in E. coli: Challenges and discoveries.
articles.authors: Singh R, Makde RD
articles.details: Lab study attempting to express the Plasmodium Heme Detoxification Protein (HDP)—which converts toxic heme into hemozoin and is a target of many antimalarials—in a native soluble form using an E. coli system. Strategies included ortholog expression, consensus sequence design, solubility-enhancing fusion partners, chaperone co-expression, and N-terminal truncations. Most constructs were insoluble or formed soluble aggregates; only one construct with a 44-residue N-terminal truncation and a C-terminal 6-His tag (HDPpf-C10) was soluble, but it showed no detectable heme-to-hemozoin transformation activity. The authors conclude the flexible, unstructured N-terminal region is essential for both heme binding and conversion.

articles.headline: SP resistance erodes IPTp benefit against malaria in pregnancy
articles.title_original: The impact of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance on the effectiveness of intermittent preventive treatment for the prevention of malaria in pregnancy in Africa: an updated systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: van Eijk AM, Stepniewska K, Khairallah C, Rodriguez E, Ahn J, Gutman JR, Ter Kuile FO
articles.details: Updated aggregated-data systematic review and meta-analysis of 122 studies (148,693 participants) matching IPTp-SP outcome data with molecular markers of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance, using Poisson mixed-effects models adjusted for transmission intensity, HIV, paucigravidae percentage, and bednet use. Effectiveness of three versus two SP doses against malaria parasitaemia at delivery declined as resistance rose. In west/central Africa the adjusted risk ratio was 0.71 (95% CI 0.65–0.78) at very low resistance versus 0.83 (0.72–0.95) at low resistance (p=0.0144). In east/southern Africa it was 0.63 (0.57–0.69) at moderate, 0.89 (0.82–0.96) at high, and 0.93 (0.85–1.01) at very high resistance (p<0.0001). This pattern was not seen for low birthweight.

articles.headline: Stiffened pyruvate-kinase-deficient red cells may resist malaria parasite
articles.title_original: Metabolic Rigidity as a Mechanical Barrier to Malaria: Flickering Loss in PKLR-Deficient Erythrocytes.
articles.authors: Hernando-Ospina N, Calero M, Solís G, Azcárate IG, Herráez-Aguilar D, Caselli N, Moleiro LH, Segovia JC, Bautista JM, Monroy F
articles.details: Biophysical study using high-speed flickering spectroscopy and non-invasive microrheology to examine how PKLR (pyruvate kinase) deficiency, which lowers glycolytic ATP in red blood cells, alters membrane mechanics. Compared with healthy controls, PKLR-mutant erythrocytes showed marked reductions in ATP-dependent flickering amplitude and membrane fluidity. When infected with Plasmodium yoelii in mouse RBCs, these rigidified cells retained mechanical properties that appeared to hinder parasite-induced membrane remodeling. The authors propose glycolytic insufficiency creates a biomechanical barrier against malaria and that label-free flickering analysis is a useful tool for studying enzymopathies and infection susceptibility at the single-cell level.

articles.headline: Fetal Doppler reassuring during ventilation for severe malaria in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Severe Malaria and Mechanical Ventilation in Pregnancy: The Crucial Role of Fetal Doppler in Monitoring Fetal Well-Being.
articles.authors: Olaya S, Angulo SD, Miranda J, Garay M
articles.details: Case report of a 33-year-old multiparous woman at 33 weeks of gestation who presented with a five-day history of fatigue, arthralgia, chills, and abdominal pain, then rapidly developed respiratory failure requiring intubation and mechanical ventilation. Continuous fetal monitoring including Doppler velocimetry showed reassuring parameters, with normal umbilical artery and middle cerebral artery flow and no signs of fetal hypoxia or centralization. A literature review found fewer than a dozen reports of mechanical ventilation in pregnancy, mostly for pneumonia, ARDS, or trauma. The authors highlight the feasibility and importance of fetal Doppler surveillance during invasive ventilation, alongside individualized maternal ventilatory management.

articles.headline: Broflanilide IRS product outperforms Actellic against resistant mosquitoes in Kenya
articles.title_original: Community evaluation of VECTRON™ T500 (broflanilide) for indoor residual spraying for malaria vector control in Siaya county, Kenya.
articles.authors: Wekesa CN, Polo B, Muchoki M, Omondi S, Kipsum M, Abong'o B, Machani MG, Bange T, Abbey I, Kiptoo P, Mburugu P, Keitany K, Sang D, Ramaita E, Ochomo E
articles.details: Two-arm non-inferiority community trial in Bar Olengo, Siaya County, Kenya (June–November 2024) comparing indoor residual spraying with VECTRON™ T500 (broflanilide, 100 mg a.i./m²) versus Actellic™ 300CS (pirimiphos-methyl, 1 g a.i./m²), 25 structures per arm plus five water-sprayed controls, assessed by WHO cone bioassays over six months. Against pyrethroid-resistant Anopheles gambiae s.s. Bungoma strain, VECTRON™ T500 achieved 98.73±3.51% mortality versus 80.22±11.23% for Actellic™ (p<0.001); against susceptible Kisumu strain, 100% versus 89.60±6.34% (p<0.001). Actellic™ fell below 80% after month 4 while VECTRON™ stayed >95%. No cross-resistance was detected, and no adverse events occurred in VECTRON™ households versus 8% (12/150) for Actellic™.

articles.headline: Review maps 45 Anopheles malaria vector species across Eastern Mediterranean
articles.title_original: Diversity and spatial distribution of malaria vectors in the WHO Eastern Mediterranean region from 1900 to 2024: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Rashti R, Chavoshi E, Tapak L, Nikpoor F, Ayubi E, Asakereh H, Mohammadi Y
articles.details: This systematic review searched PubMed, Web of Science, and Scopus through August 2024 for field studies on Anopheles vectors in the WHO Eastern Mediterranean region, including 276 of 3,637 identified publications. It documented 45 Anopheles species across 18 countries, with research concentrated on five species: An. stephensi (102 studies), An. superpictus (57), An. dthali (49), An. culicifacies (47), and An. maculipennis (45). Iran contributed the most studies (122), followed by Pakistan (35), Sudan (31), and Egypt (30). The authors stress the need for ongoing vector surveillance to detect distribution shifts and guide control.

articles.headline: Artemisinin-resistance K13 mutations spreading eastward in northwestern Tanzania
articles.title_original: Temporal Trends in Artemisinin Partial Resistance and Other Antimalarial Drug Mutations in Plasmodium falciparum from Kagera Region, Northwestern Tanzania, 2021-2023.
articles.authors: Simkin A, Mandai S, Fola AA, Young NW, Pereus D, Bakari C, Madebe RA, Seth MD, Mrengela RB, Kisambale AJ, Chacha GA, Mandara CI, Francis F, Mbwambo D, Garimo I, Chacky F, Aaron S, Lusasi A, Molteni F, Njau RJA, Kajange S, Nhiga SL, Mohamed A, Juliano JJ, Ishengoma DS, Bailey JA
articles.details: Longitudinal molecular surveillance in Tanzania's Kagera region (2021–2023) genotyped 2,826 P. falciparum isolates from seven districts using molecular inversion probes to track artemisinin partial resistance and other markers. The WHO-validated kelch13 R561H mutation persisted in Karagwe and Kyerwa (14–26%) and appeared for the first time in Muleba (5.0%) and Bukoba rural (0.7%) in 2023, indicating eastward spread; regional R561H prevalence rose from 5.5% (2021) to 11.3% (2022) then 6.9% (2023). MDR1 N86Y stayed near fixation while CRT K76T fell from 5.9% to 2.4%; antifolate markers DHFR I164L and DHPS A581G peaked at 38.1% and 48.1%. Authors urge district-level surveillance to preserve ACT efficacy.

articles.headline: Two RNA-binding proteins block Plasmodium vivax liver-stage replication
articles.title_original: Two Plasmodium vivax hypnozoite-expressed RNA-binding proteins inhibit liver stage replication.
articles.authors: Vo KC, van Biljon R, Zanghi G, Zavala-Martinez B, Betz W, Vaughan AM, Painter HJ, Kappe SHI
articles.details: Investigating how P. vivax hypnozoites maintain dormancy, researchers hypothesized that hypnozoites must actively suppress schizogony, and used differential transcriptome prospecting to identify two hypnozoite-expressed transcripts encoding putative RNA-binding proteins. They assessed each protein's function by expressing it individually in liver stages of Plasmodium yoelii, a rodent parasite that naturally does not form hypnozoites; each protein blocked liver-stage schizogony, leaving parasites small and uninucleate. A screen of RNA sequences interacting with the proteins' RNA-binding domains showed enrichment of distinct, highly specific motifs, suggesting they may block schizogony by binding RNAs containing those motifs. The authors named them IESI-1 and IESI-2, providing functional evidence for a possible molecular mechanism of hypnozoite formation.

articles.headline: Review of unusual DNA replication during P. falciparum schizogony
articles.title_original: Unusual replication dynamics during Plasmodium falciparum schizogony.
articles.authors: Berners-Lee R, Smith TK
articles.details: This review discusses the unusual asexual replication of Plasmodium falciparum during the erythrocytic cycle, in which the parasite proliferates via schizogony—alternating asynchronous rounds of DNA replication and nuclear division within a shared cytoplasm, followed by a single mass cytokinesis producing many daughter cells. The authors note that despite advances in high-throughput single-molecule techniques, the parasite's replication dynamics remain unclear and are likely shaped by its highly AT-rich genome and the pressures of schizogony. They argue that clarifying these dynamics could reveal parasite-specific vulnerabilities and inform new antimalarial development.

articles.headline: Malaria knowledge high but unrelated to burden in rural Mozambique
articles.title_original: Knowledge, attitude and practice in high endemic settings for malaria: a cross-sectional study in a rural community in central Mozambique.
articles.authors: Lequechane JD, Chale F, Azevedo N, Abílio AP, Muanido AG, Gonçalves L, Manuel JL, Silveira H
articles.details: This cross-sectional KAP study in Tambai community, Nhamatanda district, Sofala province, surveyed all household heads before (May 2021) and after (June 2022) an intervention using a semi-structured questionnaire, analyzed in SPSS v29.0. Knowledge of malaria symptoms, transmission, prevention, and treatment was high both before (149; 96.8%) and after (143; 91.1%), with no differences between intervention and control groups or before versus after. The authors conclude the REPELMALARIA project had neither positive nor negative impact on KAP scores, suggesting the area's high malaria burden is not driven by gaps in residents' knowledge, attitudes, or practices. Linked to ClinicalTrials.gov NCT04419766.

articles.headline: Sociodemographic and ecological factors tied to childhood malaria in Kenya
articles.title_original: Sociodemographic and ecological factors associated with malaria and malaria recurrence risk in children in Msambweni, Kwale County, Kenya.
articles.authors: Amugongo JS, LaBeaud AD, Bosire C, Bisanzio D, Nzaro M, Ngari M, Vu DM, Ndenga BA, Mutuku FM
articles.details: A cohort of children aged 1-18 years presenting with acute febrile illness at Msambweni County Referral Hospital, Kwale County, Kenya (2014-2018), with a nested case-control component, was assessed using caregiver interviews and household observations; malaria was diagnosed by Giemsa microscopy. Across 2610 visits by 2371 children, 40.6% (1059/2610) had parasitaemia, 41.8% (992/2371) experienced malaria, and 2.5% (60/2371) had recurrent malaria. Malaria risk rose with older age (≥5 vs <2 years aRR 1.57, 95%CI 1.23-2.00); protective factors included higher maternal education (aRR 0.66), piped/well water in the compound (aRR 0.78), and electricity/solar lighting (aRR 0.61). Recurrent malaria was linked to poor-quality housing and proximity to rice fields. Authors conclude investments in housing, maternal education, and WASH can strengthen control.

articles.headline: Carboxylesterase Coeae6g drives insecticide cross-resistance in Anopheles gambiae
articles.title_original: Predictive chemoproteomics and functional validation reveal Coeae6g-mediated insecticide cross-resistance in the malaria vector Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Balaska S, Grigoraki L, Lycett G, Weetman D, Oladepo F, Colman F, Vontas J, Paine MJI, Ismail HM
articles.details: This laboratory study presents a predictive functional chemoproteomic framework combining competitive activity-based protein profiling with functional validation to identify enzyme-mediated insecticide resistance in the malaria vector Anopheles gambiae. In a susceptible strain, fluorophosphonate probe profiling with pirimiphos-methyl-oxon (the bioactive metabolite of the organophosphate pirimiphos-methyl) revealed 18 active serine hydrolases. The carboxylesterase Coeae6g, previously associated with pyrethroid and carbamate resistance, was functionally validated and confirmed to confer resistance to pirimiphos-methyl and mediate cross-resistance to malathion, bendiocarb, and permethrin. The authors conclude this scalable approach bridges genotype-phenotype gaps and can complement genomic surveillance to guide insecticide management.

articles.headline: Placental malaria meta-analysis: global prevalence and risk factors
articles.title_original: Placental malaria: a systematic review and meta-analysis of global burden, risk factors, and maternal and foetal outcomes.
articles.authors: Menon S, D'Alessio F, Chaudhuri N, Onwuchekwa C, Kaur M, Nkumama I, Olesen OF
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis examined placental malaria (PM) due to Plasmodium falciparum, its risk factors, and maternal/foetal outcomes, including studies since 2013 and assessing quality with the Newcastle-Ottawa scale. It included 50 studies, 45 from sub-Saharan Africa, with 15 (33%) using histological diagnosis. Global PM prevalence was 17% (95%CI 12-21), rising to 23% in histology-based studies, and was higher in SSA (19%) than other regions (4%). One study of HIV-positive women reported 45% PM prevalence versus 17% in HIV-negative women; one stillbirth study showed an OR of 3.81 (95%CI 1.22-11.94), and primigravidae had pooled ORs of 1.61 (95%CI 0.91-2.84) versus multigravidae. Authors conclude PM burden is high, especially among primigravidae and HIV-positive women, but wide CIs precluded conclusions on outcomes.

articles.headline: Modelling age-structured migration's effect on malaria burden
articles.title_original: Impact of age-structured migration on malaria burden: A modelling-empirical analysis in sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Harikripal, Pascal Zabre S, Danquah I, Bhattacharyya S
articles.details: This modelling study formulates a generalized compartmental model incorporating human and vector populations with three age groups to capture heterogeneity in susceptibility, exposure, and occupation-driven rural-to-urban migration in sub-Saharan Africa. The basic reproduction number R0 was derived via the next-generation matrix approach, and local and global stability of equilibria were analyzed using Lyapunov methods and a graph-theoretic approach. The model was validated against multiyear reported malaria case data from Ouagadougou combined with annual migration records from Kossi province, Burkina Faso, and reproduced age-cohort incidence patterns. Simulations indicate nonlinear amplification and synchrony in infection prevalence partially driven by age-specific migration, offering insights to support targeted interventions in regions with seasonal migration.

articles.headline: Toward climate-smart malaria control in a warming Kenya
articles.title_original: Climate, Interventions, and Malaria Outcomes in a Warming World: Towards Climate-Smart Malaria Control in Kenya.
articles.authors: Nyawanda BO, Ochomo E, Otieno JD, Keitany K, Machini BK, Vounatsou P
articles.details: This perspective argues, drawing on recent Kenyan evidence, that climate variability already exerts effects on malaria outcomes comparable to or stronger than commonly measured interventions at local scales, with transmission showing non-linear, lagged relationships to temperature and rainfall. The authors note that climate change is expected to reshape the geography and seasonality of malaria risk, while socio-economic development and vector control such as insecticide-treated bed nets and timely case management continue to reduce incidence and deaths, especially in young children. They propose a "climate-smart" agenda for Kenya featuring dynamic targeting of interventions to climate lags and thresholds, sustained protection for the youngest, attention to school-age infection reservoirs, and embedding short-term local forecasts into routine planning.

articles.headline: Submicroscopic congenital malaria affects 12% of cord samples in Guatemala
articles.title_original: Prevalence of submicroscopic congenital malaria and associated risk factors in an area of low endemicity of Guatemala.
articles.authors: Franco RG, Ortiz L, Bardají A, Castañeda D, Menendez C, Padilla N, Castellanos ME
articles.details: A prospective cohort and nested case-control study in Fray Bartolomé de las Casas, Guatemala (2009-2011) tested umbilical cord blood that was negative by microscopy using qPCR for submicroscopic Plasmodium sp. infection. Of 442 cord samples analysed (92% of 480 randomly selected), 54 were positive, giving a submicroscopic cord-blood malaria prevalence of 12.2% (95% CI 9.3%-15.6%). In the case-control analysis (54 cases, 51 controls), submicroscopic maternal Plasmodium sp. infection (aOR 7.55, 95% CI 1.84-51.5) and gravidity (aOR 1.49, 95% CI 1.13-2.04) were associated with higher risk. Authors emphasize prompt diagnosis and treatment of malaria in pregnancy.

articles.headline: Review: climate and land-use change shaping Plasmodium knowlesi transmission
articles.title_original: Plasmodium knowlesi and global changes: socio-ecological drivers and challenges.
articles.authors: Muh F, Qanita NG, Yuwanita MR, Wusono AD, Rahayu AA, Amirudin FA, Firdaus MER, Cahyani NKD, Han JH
articles.details: This review examines how climate change and related socio-ecological factors influence transmission of Plasmodium knowlesi, a malaria species found predominantly in Southeast Asia. It discusses how temperature and precipitation variations affect mosquito vectors and parasites, how deforestation and land-use change facilitate zoonotic transmission from simian hosts to humans, and how human-macaque-mosquito interactions and community behaviour (including the gap where awareness does not translate into action) alter exposure risks. The authors highlight these interconnections to guide future research on controlling P. knowlesi in regions where human, monkey, and mosquito interactions are intensifying. No quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: Adding bed nets to IPTp-SP is cost-effective for malaria in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Cost-effectiveness of intermittent preventive therapy and insecticide-treated bed nets for malaria prevention among pregnant women in Jos North, Plateau State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Okoro LN, Zoakah AI, Bupwatda P, Anyamene EL, Bulus NG, Kumbak FD, Sabano MC, Mukisa KA, Gmanyami JM, Amoakoh MY, Isiko I
articles.details: A cross-sectional cost-effectiveness analysis in Jos North, Plateau State, Nigeria, compared IPTp-SP alone with combined IPTp-SP plus insecticide-treated nets (ITNs) among 1000 pregnant women attending antenatal care at five primary healthcare facilities (2019-2020), using DALYs lost (from low birth weight and maternal anaemia at 36-40 weeks) as outcomes. Total DALYs lost were higher for IPTp-SP alone (101.53) than IPTp-SP/ITN (85.9), an incremental 15.63 DALYs averted, at an incremental cost of US$100.19 (US$231.98 vs US$131.79), giving an ICER of US$2.12 per DALY averted. At a willingness-to-pay of US$150/DALY, the probability IPTp-SP/ITN was more cost-effective was about 60%.

articles.headline: Multi-country study of why malaria RDT results are misrecorded
articles.title_original: Characteristics of healthcare workers and health facilities associated with inaccurate recording of malaria rapid diagnostic test results: a multi-country study.
articles.authors: Atobatele S, Mpimbaza A, Ngufor C, Yavo W, Konate-Toure A, Ahogni I, Ssewante N, Orya E, Akpiroroh E, Okoro O, Agaba B, Kpemasse A, Agnon J, Tanoh AM, Affoukou C, Opigo J, Ntadom G, Okagbue H, Eugene EC, Aponte JJ, Hilton E, Galles N, Soebiyanto R, Cooper S, Okoronkwo C, Humes M, Griffith K, Lindblade KA, Sampson S
articles.details: A multi-country, mixed-methods observational study in 64 public health facilities across Benin, Côte d'Ivoire, Nigeria, and Uganda (June-December 2023) assessed factors associated with inaccurate recording of malaria rapid diagnostic test (RDT) results, comparing panel-interpreted photographed RDTs against facility registers. Among more than 100,000 RDTs performed by 499 healthcare workers, 5.1-7.3% of results were misrecorded as positive and 0.7-3.7% as negative; test positivity ranged from 45.3% (Nigeria) to 59.7% (Côte d'Ivoire), and overall agreement ranged from 90.2% (Nigeria) to 94.3% (Benin). Misrecording was driven by differing individual, contextual, and systemic factors by country, with distinct patterns for false-positive versus false-negative recording.

articles.headline: Forest pack" bite-prevention tools acceptable in Cambodia elimination effort
articles.title_original: Evaluating the acceptability and feasibility of new mosquito bite prevention tools in a "forest pack" to support malaria elimination in Cambodia.
articles.authors: McIver DJ, Doum D, Men C, Neukom J, Lobo NF, Richardson JH, Macdonald M, Shete PB, Sovannaroth S, Tatarsky A
articles.details: This implementation study evaluated the acceptability and feasibility of a "forest pack" containing a volatile pyrethroid spatial repellent (VPSR) and topical repellent, plus insecticide-treated clothing (ITC) and behaviour-change communication, among forest goers, dwellers, and rangers in two high-burden operational districts in Cambodia. A total of 28,000 packs were distributed over four rounds; 2935 individuals from 18 villages were enrolled. Distribution reached 85% of the target in Sen Monorom and 72-102% in Phnom Srouch, though ITC coverage was low (1-40% and 1-5% respectively). Reported acceptability rose over time, reaching up to 96% for the spatial repellent, 93% for topical repellent, and 100% for ITC; the authors call for better implementation and studies of epidemiological impact.

articles.headline: P. vivax dbp and ebp2 gene amplification varies across Amazon regions
articles.title_original: The pattern of gene amplification of members of the Plasmodium vivax erythrocyte binding-like proteins family across the Amazon rainforest.
articles.authors: Guedes KS, Fernandes GM, Sanchez BAM, Ntumngia FB, Adams JH, Kano FS, Fontes CJF, de Sousa TN, Carvalho LH
articles.details: This study analyzed 191 Plasmodium vivax isolates from three malaria-endemic Amazon sites (eastern, western, southwestern) for copy number variation (CNV) in the dbp and ebp2 erythrocyte binding-like genes, measured by qPCR with β-tubulin as reference, alongside host DARC genotyping by real-time PCR. In the western region, 25% of isolates showed dbp amplification (up to 8 copies) versus 2-9% (2-3 copies) in southwestern and eastern regions; ebp2 amplification was detected in 15% of western isolates versus 1-4% elsewhere. All individuals were DARC positive, and no association was found between gene CNVs and DARC genotypes. The authors conclude regional CNV variation exists without DARC-genotype association, warranting further study of its biological and immunological significance.

articles.headline: Mosquito repellent use for malaria prevention low despite awareness in Uganda
articles.title_original: Knowledge, attitudes, practices and determinants of mosquito repellent use for malaria prevention in Nangabo Ward, Wakiso District, Uganda.
articles.authors: Kemigisha G, Musoke D, Walekhwa AW, Yeka A
articles.details: This cross-sectional mixed-methods study assessed knowledge, attitudes, practices, and determinants of mosquito repellent use among 185 household heads in Nangabo Ward, Wakiso District, Uganda, with 15 in-depth interviews. While 63.8% were aware of repellents and 59.5% held positive attitudes, only 8.2% used them consistently; 77.3% had ever used them and 44.9% used them occasionally. Knowledge gaps included only 38.4% knowing repellents are safe for children under 5 and 35.7% for pregnant women. Age 60+ was associated with non-use (AOR=2.50, 95% CI 1.43-4.38, P=0.001), tertiary education with use (AOR=0.42, 95% CI 0.22-0.79, P=0.007), large households (8-10) with non-use (AOR=2.59, 95% CI 1.28-5.24, P=0.008), and positive attitudes with use (AOR=3.28, 95% CI 1.15-9.34, p=0.036). Authors recommend subsidies and community education.

articles.headline: Large-scale larviciding reduced mosquito densities across risk strata in Tanzania
articles.title_original: A large-scale mosquito larviciding in Tanga Region, Tanzania, reduced mosquito densities to varying degrees across malaria transmission risk strata.
articles.authors: Gavana T, Kailembo D, Kasagama E, Lilolime NS, Mwamlima TG, Mwalimu CD, Bernard J, Yoram B, Kajange S, Lazaro S, Kisoka N, Chaki P, Lengeler C
articles.details: This pilot project evaluated six eight-week rounds of mosquito larviciding using Bacillus thuringiensis israelensis and Bacillus sphaericus across three councils in the Tanga Region representing high, moderate, and low malaria risk strata, with longitudinal entomological monitoring in 120 villages (60 intervention, 60 control paired councils). Larviciding was associated with lower late-stage Anopheles larval densities, with Incidence Rate Ratios of 0.20 (95% CI 0.12-0.35) in high-risk, 0.51 (95% CI 0.26-1.01) in moderate-risk, and 0.40 (95% CI 0.24-0.68) in low-risk areas. Adult Anopheles gambiae sensu lato densities were reduced in moderate- and low-risk strata, while no significant reductions occurred in Anopheles funestus. Authors conclude larviciding can reduce mosquito densities across ecological settings, though effectiveness varies by species and context.

articles.headline: Iso-mukaadial acetate alters P. falciparum metabolic and replication gene expression
articles.title_original: The impact of Iso-mukaadial acetate on Plasmodium falciparum transcriptional gene regulation.
articles.authors: Salomane N, Mafuna T, Allie F, Mahlangu JS, van Zyl RL, Pooe O, Simelane M
articles.details: This study used RNA sequencing to investigate the antiplasmodial mechanism of Iso-mukaadial acetate (IMA), extracted from Warburgia salutaris, comparing gene expression in IMA- and chloroquine-treated P. falciparum NF54 strain during the intraerythrocytic stage using PlasmoDB, ShinyGO, and g:Profiler. Both IMA and chloroquine upregulated genes for parasite biological processes and cell adhesion, including PfEMP1, RIFIN, and STEVOR. IMA downregulated peptidyl-proline modification and glycolytic pathways, with KEGG analysis suggesting glycolysis-gluconeogenesis and pentose phosphate enzymes (GAPDH, G6PD-6-phosphogluconolactonase) as theoretical targets, whose suppression could reduce ATP and NADPH; IMA also inhibited DNA replication components (MCM complex, topoisomerases). Chloroquine specifically downregulated DNA replication licensing factors MCM3 and DNA helicase. Authors suggest IMA's effects on energy metabolism, redox balance, and DNA replication warrant further biochemical validation.

articles.headline: PfAnchor protein essential for apicoplast fission and inheritance in malaria
articles.title_original: An essential adaptor for apicoplast fission and inheritance in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Blauwkamp JA, Rajaram K, Staggers SR, Harrigan O, Doud EH, Xu W, Ke H, Prigge ST, Sun SY, Absalon S
articles.details: This study identified a previously uncharacterized P. falciparum protein (Pf3D7_0613600), named PfAnchor, as a key regulator of apicoplast fission in blood-stage parasites. Using Ultrastructure Expansion Microscopy, PfAnchor was shown to localize to the apicoplast throughout the asexual blood stage, and conditional depletion disrupted apicoplast fission, causing incomplete cytokinesis and parasite death. Loss of the apicoplast's elongated branched structure via azithromycin treatment rescued these defects, indicating a specific role in fission. Immunoprecipitation identified interaction with the dynamin-like GTPase PfDyn2, establishing PfAnchor as the first apicoplast-specific dynamin adaptor protein. The authors conclude PfAnchor is essential for apicoplast fission and inheritance, highlighting parasite-specific organelle division as a potential therapeutic vulnerability.

articles.headline: Mefloquine resistance undermines mefloquine-piperaquine triple therapy efficacy
articles.title_original: Tolerance of Plasmodium falciparum mefloquine-resistant clinical isolates to mefloquine-piperaquine with implications for triple artemisinin-based combination therapies.
articles.authors: Roesch C, Cosson A, Mairet Khedim M, Khim N, Kloeung N, Ke S, Srun S, Eam R, Khean C, Kul C, Adoux L, Popovici J, Leang R, Ringwald P, Ariey F, Coppée R, Witkowski B
articles.details: Using Cambodian clinical isolates of P. falciparum with distinct resistance profiles, this lab study assessed the efficacy and resistance potential of the mefloquine-piperaquine (MQ-PPQ) partner-drug combination proposed for triple artemisinin-based combination therapies (TACTs). MQ resistance conferred significant cross-tolerance to MQ-PPQ, while PPQ-resistant and -sensitive strains stayed susceptible. Under repeated MQ-PPQ pressure for four months, parasites rapidly acquired tolerance driven by pfmdr1 amplification, and MQ inhibited PPQ accumulation dose-dependently. The authors conclude MQ resistance alone can undermine MQ-PPQ TACT efficacy, questioning this combination's rationale.

articles.headline: Community engagement drove malaria elimination push in Laos forest populations
articles.title_original: Early and sustained community engagement to reach unreached populations for malaria elimination in Lao People's Democratic Republic.
articles.authors: Roy SR, Banouvong V, Hoban E, Khamlome B, Chindavongsa K, Inthirath I, Inthivong S, Sibounheuang K, Akavong K, Huong TTG, Yadav RPH, Ringwald P, Kelley JF, Xangsayarath P, Shortus MS, Reyburn R
articles.details: This descriptive program report details "Accelerator Strategies" implemented in 2022 in the Lao People's Democratic Republic to reach unreached high-risk groups—forest goers, farmers on forest fringes, and mobile populations—in the Southern region. Activities included targeted drug administration and intermittent preventive treatment as chemoprevention regardless of infection status. Three enablers for community participation were identified: service delivery by community members themselves; strong advocacy and political commitment from local leaders and influencers; and people-centered services beyond the village using granular data on risk behaviors, population movement, and GIS mapping. The authors report early, sustained engagement yielded high intervention coverage, greater acceptance, and decreased malaria burden.

articles.headline: Dengue and malaria hospital deaths mapped across Ecuador's provinces
articles.title_original: Dengue and malaria mortality in Ecuador: A population analysis of hospital deaths across 24 provinces, 2015-2023.
articles.authors: Acosta-España JD, Morales-Gualotuña M, Pazmiño-Villaseñor J, Dueñas-Espín I
articles.details: This cross-sectional ecological study analyzed anonymized hospital discharge data (INEC) using ICD-10 codes to calculate age-standardized hospital mortality rates per million for dengue and malaria across Ecuador's 24 provinces from 2015 to 2023. Dengue accounted for 125 hospital deaths, concentrated in Amazonian and coastal provinces. Malaria caused five adult deaths (3 in 2019, 1 in 2021, 1 in 2023), with P. falciparum predominating; the highest malaria mortality rate was in Napo (16.3 per million in 2021). The authors conclude both diseases showed fragmented patterns with persistent underreporting, highlighting gaps in surveillance and the need for sustained clinical vigilance and integrated mortality monitoring.

articles.headline: Gestational malaria linked to poorer quality of life in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Health-related quality of life among pregnant women exposed to malaria in an endemic region of Colombia.
articles.authors: Cardona-Arias JA, Higuita-Gutierrez LF
articles.details: A convergent triangulation mixed-methods study in an endemic region of Colombia assessed health-related quality of life (HRQoL) among 400 pregnant women (WHOQOL-BREF), with a grounded-theory qualitative subsample of 30. Among the 400 women, 6% reported malaria during the current pregnancy and 44% had had malaria at some point. The psychological domain scored highest (median 78) while physical health was lowest (median 43). Multivariate models showed gestational malaria, comorbidities, and rural residence were associated with poorer physical and environmental health. The authors conclude HRQoL is significantly impaired in physical, environmental, and social domains, with gestational malaria's impact socially normalized and underrecognized.

articles.headline: Chinese social media malaria discussion is event-driven, Africa-focused
articles.title_original: Event-driven dynamics, regional disparities, and netizen engagement: characterizing and exploring malaria discourse on Sina Weibo for enhanced health communication.
articles.authors: Wang N, Zhao X, Li C, Mao Y
articles.details: This study analyzed 81,010 malaria-related posts collected from Sina Weibo between January 2019 and August 2024, using descriptive analysis and natural language processing (topic modelling, sentiment analysis, named entity recognition) across temporal, spatial, and user-type dimensions. Netizen attention was event-driven, peaking around China's health assistance to Africa, public health incidents, and malaria awareness days (about 20% of annual posts); negative events sustained engagement roughly 1.5 times longer than positive ones. Africa-related discussion exceeded 60% of region-specific posts annually. Official health accounts posted infrequently. The authors conclude tailored communication strategies are needed to enhance public malaria awareness.

articles.headline: IRS household visits used to reach pregnant women for malaria prevention
articles.title_original: Indoor residual spraying household visits as a platform for engaging pregnant women in malaria prevention on Bioko Island, Equatorial Guinea.
articles.authors: Galick DS, Eyono JNM, Rivas MR, Phiri WP, Guerra CA, García GA
articles.details: This implementation study on Bioko Island, Equatorial Guinea, used indoor residual spraying (IRS) campaign data to identify households with pregnant women for follow-up visits assessing knowledge and uptake of LLINs and IPTp. Of 1,567 households visited, 589 pregnant women were identified. Awareness of LLINs was high (76.9% cited them as prevention) but IPTp awareness was low (34.5% could cite a benefit; 37.1% aware of facility availability). Regular nightly LLIN use was 54.8% versus 35.2% on track for three IPTp doses. Ordinal regression linked LLIN use to awareness it prevents malaria, and IPTp uptake to awareness of required doses. Authors conclude complementary interventions can be leveraged to reach pregnant women and that boosting IPTp awareness could improve coverage.

articles.headline: Seasonal migration drives P. falciparum gene flow between highlands and lowlands
articles.title_original: High connectivity and low differentiation of Plasmodium falciparum parasite populations in a setting with high seasonal migration.
articles.authors: Esayas E, Louie W, Routledge I, Murphy M, Demeke NN, De Amaral F, Aranda-Díaz A, Greenhouse B, Kassa FA, Nigusse T, Assefa M, Ashine T, Getachew A, Ntuku H, Golassa L, Gadisa E, Bennett A, Smith JL
articles.details: This genomic study in Ethiopia examined Plasmodium falciparum diversity and connectivity between low-transmission highlands and endemic lowlands to assess seasonal agricultural migration's role in sustaining transmission. qPCR-positive dried blood spots from highland health facilities and lowland worksites underwent multiplexed amplicon sequencing. Lowland populations had higher complexity of infection and polyclonal rates (mean COI 2.62, 62%, n=581) than highland residents (mean COI 2.00, 40%, n=599), with similar expected heterozygosity (He≈0.4). Worksite infections showed high genetic connectivity and extensive parasite sharing, including 27 related clusters in highland cases and 10 in seasonal workers. Authors conclude agricultural mobility likely drives parasite diversity and gene flow, sustaining malaria transmission.

articles.headline: Methylene blue reverses brainstem damage in primate cerebral malaria model
articles.title_original: Methylene blue treatment of fatal cerebral malaria and identification of potential blood biomarkers.
articles.authors: Hang JW, Leong YW, Narang V, Sunyakumthorn P, Im-Erbsin R, Foo S, Lum J, Lee B, Brown AE, Rénia L, Turner GDH, Wassmer SC, Lombardini ED, Russell B, Malleret B
articles.details: Using Plasmodium coatneyi-infected male rhesus macaques as a non-human primate model of human cerebral malaria (CM), researchers used differential gene expression analysis to show methylene blue reversed detrimental infection effects on the brainstem. Comparing the brainstem dataset with two transcriptomic datasets (P. coatneyi-infected M. mulatta blood and P. falciparum-infected human blood), they identified nine genes associated with CM severity, most expressed in neutrophils, suggesting potential as blood biomarkers for diagnosing P. falciparum-induced fatal CM. Authors highlight the need for new CM treatments and promising biomarkers to improve diagnosis and prognosis.

articles.headline: Adjunctive ruxolitinib lowers inflammation, boosts immunity in malaria challenge trial
articles.title_original: Adjunctive ruxolitinib attenuates inflammation and enhances immunity in volunteers experimentally infected with Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Webster R, Oyong DA, Llewellyn S, Abd-Rahman AN, Potter AJ, Mukhiya R, Sahai N, Leelasena I, Ujvary E, Mathison S, Andrew DW, Bukali L, de Labastida Rivera F, Engel J, Soon MSF, Frame T, Hamelink J, Nalubega M, Dooley NL, Loughland JR, Nguyen T, Rosenberg-Hasson Y, Maysel-Auslender S, Sigal N, Foygel K, Gower J, Peters J, Woo R, Amante F, Wells TNC, Chalon S, Moehrle JJ, McCarthy JS, Birrell GW, Edstein MD, Leipold M, Obermoser G, Maecker H, Engwerda CR, Barber BE, Boyle MJ
articles.details: This double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial assessed whether the JAK1/2 inhibitor ruxolitinib reduces inflammation and enhances antiparasitic immunity in malaria-naïve volunteers inoculated with blood-stage Plasmodium falciparum. Twenty participants were inoculated and, on day 8, randomized to artemether-lumefantrine with either ruxolitinib or placebo, then re-inoculated 90 days later. Ruxolitinib was safe and well tolerated and attenuated inflammatory responses, reducing post-treatment increases in C-reactive protein and severity markers angiopoietin-2 and intercellular adhesion molecule-1. It also enhanced immune memory after the second infection, with elevated HLA-DRA and 4-1BB consistent with increased T cell activation. Authors support further evaluation of ruxolitinib as an adjunctive malaria treatment.

articles.headline: Systematic review of AI applications for malaria elimination
articles.title_original: The role of artificial intelligence (AI) in the elimination of malaria: a systematic literature review.
articles.authors: Kumar SM, Nath SN, Vani HC, Shrinivasa BM, Ahmed N, Tomer V, Bankoti K, Negi G, Ali SSB, Jaiswal MK, Warkade V
articles.details: This systematic literature review explored the capability of artificial intelligence (AI) to aid India's goal of eliminating malaria by 2030. A total of 62 research articles published between 2014 and 2025 were reviewed, covering AI applications in diagnosis (e.g., image-based parasite detection), treatment optimization (predictive analytics), and outbreak prediction. Main findings indicate AI models achieved better diagnostic accuracy and significantly decreased diagnostic time and human error. Identified limitations include poor data interoperability, limited rural infrastructure, and gaps in healthcare worker training. The review analyzes globally employed AI techniques adaptable for India and suggests areas for future research.

articles.headline: Non-falciparum malaria common in febrile patients, southern Senegal
articles.title_original: Common Molecular Detection of the Neglected Human Malaria Parasite Among Febrile Patients in Southern Regions in Senegal.
articles.authors: Sambe BS, Diaw SOM, Diagne A, Diatta AS, Diatta HAM, Sarr I, Sané R, Guigma PYB, Senghor B, Diouf B, Sembene PM, Vigan-Womas I, Niang M
articles.details: A molecular survey tested blood from 1990 febrile patients across four southern Senegal regions (Kedougou, Kolda, Tambacounda, Ziguinchor) during the 2020, 2021, and 2022 transmission seasons, using Plasmodium genus-specific qPCR and species-specific nested PCR. The Plasmodium positivity rate was 73.43%, with 67.92% single-species infections and 32.08% co-infections by two or three species. Non-falciparum species (P. vivax, P. ovale, P. malariae) circulated in three of four regions; P. vivax and P. malariae were not detected in Ziguinchor, while Kolda and Tambacounda showed first evidence of non-falciparum infections and Ziguinchor the first P. ovale report. Authors urge clinicians and the control programme to account for these species.

articles.headline: Malaria–helminth co-infection linked to anemia in pregnant women, Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Malaria and Soil-Transmitted Helminth Co-Infection and Its Association With Anemia Among Pregnant Women Visiting Mizan-Tepi University Teaching Hospital, Southwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Duguma T, Assefa S, Tesfaye T, Alemayehu B
articles.journal: Can J Infect Dis Med Microbiol
