articles.details: This institution-based cross-sectional study from September 1 to December 30, 2024 enrolled 332 pregnant women attending antenatal care at Mizan-Tepi University Teaching Hospital, collecting blood and stool samples and using logistic regression. Prevalence was 24.4% (81/332) for malaria, 33.7% (112/332) for soil-transmitted helminths (STH), and 12.7% (42/332) for co-infection; overall anemia prevalence was 57.5%. Rural residence (AOR=2.459, p=0.001) and handwashing before meals (AOR=12.748, p=0.010) were significantly associated with malaria-STH co-infection. The authors conclude malaria and STH infection showed a strong statistical association with anemia.

articles.headline: Symptom score helps triage malaria testing in adults with respiratory symptoms
articles.title_original: Syndromic presentation by patients with malaria and respiratory symptoms in a malaria-endemic urban-and-peri-urban setting.
articles.authors: Muwonge H, Akugizibwe R, Bbosa G, Damani AM, Nakidde B, Mugahi R, Byakika-Kibwika P
articles.details: Using screening data from an ongoing malaria treatment study, researchers assessed 332 adults presenting with upper respiratory symptoms at six outpatient clinics in an urban and peri-urban Ugandan setting. Malaria was confirmed in 16.0% by both rapid diagnostic test and microscopy. Fever and cough were the strongest independent predictors, and positivity rose with symptom count, from 0.8% with one symptom to 100% with four. A four-point symptom score (fever, cough, nasal symptoms, sore throat) at a threshold of two or more had 98.1% sensitivity and 47.3% specificity. Authors suggest incorporating the score into triage to optimize testing in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Qualitative study on community IPTp delivery acceptability in Senegal
articles.title_original: Initiating community intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy in Senegal: qualitative evaluation of stakeholder acceptability and experience.
articles.authors: Ndoye T, Majeres Lugand M, Diallo AM, Sakho S, Pouye MM, Dieng K, Faye SLB
articles.details: This qualitative study used 111 semi-structured interviews and 16 focus group discussions to explore stakeholder perspectives on community delivery of intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy (c-IPTp) with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP) in high-endemic districts of Kolda and Kedougou, Senegal. Findings were synthesized as strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats. Providers viewed c-IPTp positively for reducing workload and improving remote access, though some felt it undermined antenatal care attendance; SP and pregnancy-test stockouts and community health worker incentives were key barriers. Pregnant women found it acceptable but raised concerns about adverse events and socio-cultural issues. Recommendations include securing supplies, improving reporting, incentivizing workers, and consistent funding.

articles.headline: Review tracks P. falciparum diversity and drug-resistance shifts in Kenya
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity, population structure, and drug resistance of Plasmodium falciparum in Kenya: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Nyataya JN, Mutsaers M, Masakhwe CA, Laigong AC, Kattenberg JH, Rosanas-Urgell A, Waitumbi JN
articles.details: A systematic review and meta-analysis included 58 Kenyan articles published between 2002 and 2023 (21 on genetic diversity, 37 on drug resistance) from an initial 737 articles, genotyping markers including Pfcrt, Pfdhfr/Pfdhps, Pfmdr1, and Pfk13. Expected heterozygosity was high (0.79 Lake Basin, 0.80 Highlands, 0.96 Coast), infections were multiclonal (34%–80%) with mean MOI 2–4.8. The Pfcrt CVIET haplotype fell from 96% (1999) to 1% (2017) while CVMNK rose from 6.8% (1998) to 98.8% (2017); Pfdhfr-51I, Pfdhfr-59R, Pfdhps-437G, and Pfdhps-540E increased over time (Mann-Kendall p<0.05). Pfk13 mutations (R539T, N458Y, R561H, A675V, V568G) were reported in Lake Basin and Highland regions (2018–2024). Authors conclude findings suggest ongoing transmission and potential parasite-population connectivity.

articles.headline: Global burden and inequality of neglected tropical diseases and malaria, 1990–2021
articles.title_original: Global, regional, national burden, trends and health inequality of neglected tropical diseases and malaria from 1990 to 2021.
articles.authors: Feng L, Mo G, Liu Z, Liu Q
articles.details: This secondary analysis of the Global Burden of Disease 2021 database evaluated burden, trends, and health inequities of neglected tropical diseases and malaria (NTDM) by age, sex, region, and Socio-Demographic Index, using joinpoint regression, Concentration Index, Slope Index of Inequality, decomposition analysis, and a Bayesian age-period-cohort model projecting to 2050. From 1990 to 2021, age-standardized incidence rose 0.59%, while prevalence, death, and DALY rates fell 58.05%, 25%, and 32.36%. Western Sub-Saharan Africa bore the highest burden; children under five had the highest incidence and death rates. Lower-SDI regions carried higher burden, with population growth worsening it. Projections to 2050 suggest continued prevalence decline but rising incidence, death, and DALY rates.

articles.headline: CNN classifies malaria parasite stages from blood smears at 92.4% accuracy
articles.title_original: Mobile phone-based plasmodium parasites stage detection from Giemsa stained blood smear by convolutional neural networks.
articles.authors: Bedir H, Arslan MÖ, Akıner MM, Öztürk M, Uygun ZO
articles.details: A convolutional neural network (CNN) pipeline for the automated detection and staging of malaria from Giemsa-stained blood smear images achieved an overall classification accuracy of 92.4% (±2.1%), with precision (93.1% ±1.8%), recall (92.8% ±1.9%), and F1-scores all exceeding 0.90 across classes. The dataset was annotated into four classes—Ring Form, Trophozoite, Schizont, and uninfected red blood cells—and split into training (70%), validation (15%), and testing (15%) subsets. The Schizont stage had the highest accuracy (94.7%), while the Ring Form showed slightly lower performance (91.2%), likely due to morphological overlap with early trophozoites. Cross-validation indicated reliability across dataset splits, and the authors conclude the approach is scalable for resource-constrained diagnostic settings.

articles.headline: Malaria resurgence in Amhara, Ethiopia, with rising P. vivax dominance
articles.title_original: Resurgence of malaria in the Amhara Region, Ethiopia (2014-2024): trends, spatial expansion, and control challenges.
articles.authors: Lake MW, Asemahagn MA, Nigussie TZ, Gelaye KA, Muchie KF, Engedaw HA, Yenesew MA
articles.details: This retrospective analysis examined 11 years (January 2014–December 2024) of weekly malaria surveillance data from 166 districts in the Amhara Region, Ethiopia, using joinpoint regression, ARIMA modelling, and Getis-Ord Gi* hotspot statistics. A total of 7,710,733 cases and 162 deaths were reported, with adults (≥15 years) contributing 62%. A trend inflection in late 2018 marked a shift from decline (APC −13.2%) to resurgence (APC +12.6%); Annual Parasite Incidence rose from 10.9 to 74.8 per 1000 between 2018 and 2024. P. vivax contribution rose from 25% to 43% (p<0.001), with its incidence surging 11-fold (2.7 to 32.4 per 1000). In 2024, resurgence occurred in 83% of districts, expanding into urban and highland areas.

articles.headline: Review of strategies to control imported malaria in elimination zones
articles.title_original: Imported malaria: a silent and forgotten threat in malaria free zones?
articles.authors: Lusiyana N, Ahmad RA, Elyazar I, Murhandarwati EEH
articles.details: This PRISMA-ScR systematic review searched PubMed, Scopus, and ProQuest to identify strategies and lessons for controlling imported malaria and preventing reintroduction in regions that have achieved elimination. Of 149 articles reviewed, 24 were selected for in-depth analysis. Reintroduction events were primarily driven by imported cases from endemic areas, with contributing factors including cross-border population movement, conflict, and international travel, alongside competent vectors and expanding breeding habitats. All studies implemented imported case surveillance: case detection (21 articles), case management (4), mass drug administration (3), chemoprophylaxis (3), vector surveillance (6), and vector control (7). Authors conclude that case-based surveillance alone is insufficient and must be complemented with context-specific interventions to sustain elimination.

articles.headline: Low asymptomatic P. falciparum prevalence in pregnant women, Mogadishu
articles.title_original: Prevalence of asymptomatic malaria and associated factors among pregnant women in Mogadishu, Somalia: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Abdi MY, Mohamud FA, Abdullahi SA
articles.details: This cross-sectional study at Ayaan Hospital in Mogadishu, Somalia, enrolled 171 asymptomatic pregnant women via random sampling between February and March 2025, using questionnaires, rapid diagnostic tests, and microscopy, with chi-square, Fisher's Exact test, and odds ratios for analysis. Asymptomatic Plasmodium infection prevalence was 2.9%, with P. falciparum the only species detected in all positive cases. No significant associations were found, though younger age (OR=0.24, 95% CI 0.03–2.23), urban residence (OR=0.12, 95% CI 0.012–1.32), and ITN use (OR=0.06, 95% CI 0.002–1.82) suggested possible protective effects. Authors conclude prevalence was low and call for larger studies to confirm these observations.

articles.headline: Terminalia plant compound eschweilenol C blocks P. falciparum blood stages
articles.title_original: Eschweilenol C and subfractions from Terminalia plants disrupt haemoglobin metabolism, inhibiting Plasmodium falciparum growth at rings and trophozoite stages.
articles.authors: Tali MBT, Mbouna CDJ, Zehra A, Wouamba SCN, Mouthe GPK, Kamche AY, Dize D, Kouam SF, Kamdem BP, Tchouankeu JC, Khan F, Boyom FF
articles.details: This in vitro drug-discovery study fractionated active extracts from Terminalia ivorensis (TiWEa) and T. brownii (TbMMeOH:Ea), isolating the compound eschweilenol C, and screened the subfractions and compound against P. falciparum strains (Dd2, 3D7, Dd2-GNF156), stage V gametocytes, and mammalian cells. All subfractions showed activity against susceptible and multidrug-resistant strains (3D7, Dd2) with IC50 of 0.15-5.73 µg/ml; eschweilenol C had IC50 of 490.74 nM (Dd2) and 379.20 nM (3D7). Top hits Ti03, Ti04, and eschweilenol C had IC50 < 1 µg/ml, selectivity index > 10, reduced haemozoin production, killed ring and trophozoite stages, and bound PfM1AAP and PfM17LAP. Authors identify eschweilenol C as a promising candidate amid artemisinin resistance in Africa.

articles.headline: Malaria-anaemia double burden affects 6.4% of young Ghanaian children
articles.title_original: The double burden: co-occurrence of malaria and anaemia in children under five in Ghana - a multilevel mixed-effects logistic regression analysis.
articles.authors: Karikari JK, Bedu-Addo K, Hassan YSA, Konadu E, Addae M, Amponsah OKO, Opoku DA
articles.details: Using data from the 2022 Ghana Demographic and Health Survey and mixed-effects multilevel binary logistic regression, this study estimated a 6.4% prevalence (95% CI: 5.5-7.5) of co-occurring malaria and anaemia among children under five. The double burden was associated with being a fifth-born child (aOR=3.92; 95% CI 1.60-9.62), fever within the preceding two weeks (aOR=2.49; 95% CI 1.46-4.24), and having a mother with primary education (aOR=1.87; 95% CI 1.07-3.28). Lower odds were seen for children in households headed by someone aged 60 or older (aOR=0.33; 95% CI 0.14-0.79) and in poorer (aOR=0.51), middle-income (aOR=0.31), and richer (aOR=0.31) households. The authors call for integrated malaria, nutrition, and anaemia interventions, particularly in high-burden regions.

articles.headline: Reversible paralysis in pregnancy from malaria-schistosomiasis co-infection
articles.title_original: Reversible paralysis in pregnancy, thinking beyond malaria: a case report.
articles.authors: Sous M, Boti M, Mose B, Sous W
articles.details: This case report describes a 17-year-old pregnant woman (gravida 1 para 0) in Malawi presenting with fever, headache, neck pain, and abdominal pain, diagnosed with malaria and treated with artemisinin-based oral combination therapy. Twelve days later she developed progressive bilateral lower-extremity weakness; blood film showed malaria parasites, and despite IV artesunate her weakness worsened with neurogenic bladder. Urine studies revealed ova and parasites suggestive of schistosomiasis, and presumptive neuroschistosomiasis treatment was started with prednisone before praziquantel. After physiotherapy she was ambulatory at discharge three weeks later. Authors highlight the dual burden and the danger of anchoring bias when malaria co-exists in low-resource settings.

articles.headline: PfPI3K inhibitor idelalisib reverses artemisinin resistance in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: PfPI3K inhibition reversed artemisinin resistance by increasing ROS and ubiquitinated protein levels in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Shi N, Wang R, Yu T, Yang J, Chen L, Li Q, Li Y, Zhang S
articles.details: This laboratory study tested combining a PfPI3K inhibitor (idelalisib), which lowers phosphatidylinositol-3-phosphate (PI3P) levels, with dihydroartemisinin (DHA) against artemisinin-resistant P. falciparum. The combination produced synergistic antimalarial effects and reversed artemisinin resistance. The decline in PI3P inhibited reactive oxygen species (ROS) degradation, causing ROS accumulation, parasite apoptosis, and buildup of ubiquitinated proteins, while downregulating molecular chaperone gene expression and increasing eIF2α phosphorylation, leading to a dysregulated antioxidant system and disrupted proteostasis that sensitized parasites to artemisinins. Authors conclude targeting PfPI3K with idelalisib is a viable strategy to control artemisinin resistance.

articles.headline: IL-10-secreting T cells dominate response in children's uncomplicated malaria
articles.title_original: CD4 and CD8 T-cell response is dominated by IL-10-secreting cells in children with uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Ariera BO, Guyah B, Onditi I, Waomba K, Koech E, Sabourin KR, Samayoa-Reyes G, Rochford R, Ogolla S
articles.details: This flow-cytometry study tested whether acute clinical P. falciparum malaria alters EBV T-cell immunity, relevant to endemic Burkitt lymphoma risk, by comparing 10 Kenyan children with acute malaria at baseline and matched 4-week recovery with 10 healthy community controls after stimulation with EBV- and cytomegalovirus-specific peptides. The frequency of activation-induced marker cells was comparable across stimulations, but children with acute malaria showed a shift in cytokine secretion favoring IL-10 in both T-cell subsets during active disease and at 4 weeks post-recovery. The authors conclude that clinical malaria did not impair T-cell activation but induced IL-10-favoring shifts, and that these alterations are systemic rather than EBV-specific.

articles.headline: Acute kidney injury common and deadly in African children with severe malaria
articles.title_original: Identification of acute kidney injury in African children with severe malaria: a multinational individual participant data meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Bond C, Batte A, Afolayan F, Anstey N, Bassat Q, Bejon P, Berkley JA, Evans RDR, Goldstein SL, Hawkes MT, Ibrahim O, Imani P, John CC, Kain KC, Liepmann C, Marsh K, Namazzi R, Nakuya M, O'Brien N, Oluwadare LP, Opoka RO, Sserunkuuma J, Wynkoop H, Conroy AL
articles.details: This individual participant data meta-analysis pooled 18 studies (8 countries, 13,528 children aged 3 months to 16 years) with severe P. falciparum malaria and serum creatinine measurements; there were 951 deaths (7%). KDIGO-defined AKI occurred in 47% (95% CI 46–48%; Stage 1 46%, Stage 2 30%, Stage 3 23%) and was associated with 2.87 increased odds of in-hospital death (95% CI 2.28–3.61), rising monotonically with stage. The WHO threshold (creatinine >3 mg/dL) flagged only 3.4% (238) of children and missed 93% (3059/3297) of AKI cases. Authors conclude simplified age-based creatinine thresholds can enable early AKI recognition.

articles.headline: Plasmodium kinase NEK4 drives meiosis onset after fertilization
articles.title_original: A divergent Plasmodium NEK4 acts as a key regulator driving the early events of meiosis.
articles.authors: Yanase R, Hair M, Zeeshan M, Ferguson DJP, Brady D, Pasquarello C, Bottrill A, Bhanvadia S, Neal A, Tromer EC, Le Roch KG, Hainard A, Holder AA, Vaughan S, Guttery DS, Tewari R
articles.details: Using P. berghei, researchers identified the NIMA-related kinase NEK4 as a regulator coupling meiotic initiation with zygote-to-ookinete morphogenesis. Ultrastructure expansion microscopy showed NEK4 accumulates at the microtubule-organising centre and apical polar complex shortly after fertilisation, and zygotes undergo MTOC-driven nuclear migration resembling fission yeast horsetail movement. Deleting nek4 caused complete developmental arrest: blocked MTOC duplication and microtubule formation, uncondensed chromatin, and failed nuclear migration and polarity. Transcriptomic and phosphoproteomic analyses showed NEK4 loss collapsed meiotic and cytoskeletal regulatory networks, reducing expression and phosphorylation of HOP1, REC8, and AP2-O. NEK4 is established as a key driver of meiotic entry and zygote maturation.

articles.headline: Locally acquired falciparum malaria case in non-endemic Lebanon
articles.title_original: Locally Acquired Plasmodium falciparum Malaria in a Non-endemic Region of Lebanon: A Case Report.
articles.authors: Hashem S, Mahfouz T, Berry A, Salameh H, Tarhini M
articles.details: This case report describes locally acquired P. falciparum malaria in a 70-year-old Lebanese woman with no history of travel to endemic regions, who presented with intermittent fever, chills, headache, nausea, and abdominal pain. Laboratory tests showed anemia, thrombocytopenia, and leukopenia; peripheral blood smear revealed intraerythrocytic ring forms and a rapid diagnostic test confirmed P. falciparum. Given her age and high parasitemia, she was treated with intravenous artesunate followed by oral artemether-lumefantrine and recovered fully. With no travel, transfusion, or transplant exposure, the authors highlight the need for sustained malaria surveillance and clinician awareness in historically non-endemic areas.

articles.headline: Computational screening finds candidate inhibitor of resistance transporter PfMDR1
articles.title_original: In-silico identification of antimalarial compounds targeting PfMDR1 of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Preetha TL, Yadav V, Subbarao N
articles.details: This in-silico study screened antimalarial compounds with unknown targets from the ChEMBL and IUPHAR databases against P. falciparum PfMDR1 (PDB 8JWI), a transporter that expels drugs such as chloroquine and contributes to drug resistance, using molecular docking with GLIDE and GOLD. The top compound achieved a GLIDE docking score of −12.169 kcal/mol versus −5.435 for the benchmark, with strong hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions at key active-site residues. Molecular dynamics simulations (GROMACS) showed stable binding with minimal fluctuation, and MM/PBSA binding free energy was −130.971 ± 14.283 kJ/mol. Authors propose it as a promising candidate for overcoming PfMDR1-mediated resistance and structure-based drug design.

articles.headline: Three years of clothianidin indoor spraying cut malaria transmission in Côte d'Ivoire
articles.title_original: Entomological impact of three years of clothianidin-based indoor residual spraying in two high malaria endemic districts in Côte d'Ivoire.
articles.authors: Chabi J, Edi CVA, Coleman S, Gbalegba GCN, Ouattara AF, Broudje BRN, Kouame RMA, Yokoly FN, Soro D, Konan LY, N'Tamon RN, Tia E, Yapo RM, Konan GK, Tea AS, Akre MA, Kpan DSM, Tanoh AM, Cissé NG, Flatley C, Kouassi BL
articles.details: Three rounds of clothianidin-based indoor residual spraying (IRS) in two endemic districts of Côte d'Ivoire (Sakassou and Nassian) reduced the human biting rate by about 60% by the third round (IRR=0.40; p=0.002), with no significant change in two control districts (Beoumi and Dabakala). Monthly mosquito collections by human landing catches and pyrethrum spray catches identified Anopheles coluzzii and An. gambiae as the main vectors, and the odds of sporozoite infection fell 49% after the first (OR=0.51; p=0.023) and second (OR=0.51; p=0.043) rounds. The entomological inoculation rate dropped four-fold, from 3.92 to 1.25 infective bites per person per night, versus a marginal decline in controls (0.91 to 0.58). The authors conclude IRS is a useful tool for malaria control in highly endemic settings.

articles.headline: Severe malaria treatment guideline adherence low in Northern Uganda hospitals
articles.title_original: Adherence to severe malaria treatment guidelines in Northern Uganda: a mixed-methods study.
articles.authors: Akello Z, Oloro J, Mukanyangezi MF, Asingizwe D
articles.details: A convergent parallel mixed-methods study assessed adherence to severe malaria treatment guidelines among healthcare workers in public hospitals in the Acholi sub-region of Northern Uganda, reviewing 384 patient records across three hospitals and conducting key informant interviews with nine healthcare workers. Overall adherence was 33.07%, with non-adherence to the recommended IV-then-oral drug sequence in 38.3% and treatment of only confirmed malaria in 25%. Medical doctors were more likely to adhere than other professionals (aIRR 1.7, p=0.035). The authors conclude adherence is suboptimal and recommend routine refresher training, mentorship, and support supervision.

articles.headline: Modelling adds seasonal SP doses to perennial chemoprevention for children
articles.title_original: Rethinking a hybrid malaria chemoprevention delivery strategy for children in sub-perennial settings: a modelling study integrating age- and seasonally-targeted delivery.
articles.authors: Sen S, Schellenberg D, Penny MA
articles.details: Using the OpenMalaria individual-based model combined with pharmacological drug-action models, this study evaluated a hybrid malaria chemoprevention (HMC) strategy combining existing perennial malaria chemoprevention (PMC) with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine plus additional monthly SP doses during the higher-risk rainy season for children aged 3-24 months, and an age-expanded HMC+ for children 3-36 months, across prevalence (5-70%), drug sensitivity, and coverage assumptions. HMC and HMC+ averted 2.1 and 2.9 times more clinical cases and 2.0 and 3.3 times more severe cases than PMC alone, with median protective efficacy of 31.8% and 44.9% against clinical and 16.1% and 26.4% against severe cases. Authors conclude seasonally-targeted chemoprevention added to PMC could substantially increase benefits in sub-perennial settings.

articles.headline: Secondary Anopheles vectors may sustain malaria in Assam, India
articles.title_original: Evidence of secondary anopheline vectors in sustaining malaria transmission in Kokrajhar District, Assam, Northeastern India.
articles.authors: Singh K, Mohanty A, Malla WA, Ranjha R, Gam J, Ali R, Bharti PK, Anvikar AR, Singh H
articles.details: This study investigated sporozoite positivity of secondary anopheline species in the high-endemic Kokrajhar District, Assam, northeastern India, where P. vivax and P. falciparum coexist and primary vectors An. minimus and An. baimaii have declined. Using CDC light traps, indoor resting mouth aspiration, and pyrethrum spray captures, 1794 female mosquitoes spanning five genera were collected, with Anopheles maculatus group confirmed by PCR targeting the ITS2 region. Plasmodium positivity was highest in An. maculatus (4%; 5/80), followed by An. minimus (4.8%; 1/21) and Anopheles kochi (4.6%; 1/22). The authors conclude secondary vectors beyond traditional primary vectors may sustain transmission and should be integrated into control programmes.

articles.headline: Commentary celebrates progress in scaling up malaria diagnostic testing
articles.title_original: A reason to celebrate in a time of uncertainty. Response to: Linblade et al. "Assessing the accuracy of the recording and reporting of malaria rapid diagnostic test results in four African countries-methods and key results".
articles.authors: Barat LM
articles.details: This commentary responds to Linblade and colleagues' publication assessing the accuracy of recording and reporting malaria rapid diagnostic test results in four African countries. The author argues the original work missed an opportunity to interpret its findings within the historical context of malaria diagnosis and treatment. Viewed historically, the author contends the findings are cause to celebrate the success of country and global efforts to scale up malaria diagnostic testing over the past 15 years. No new quantitative results are presented.

articles.headline: Novel compound UA2239 blocks malaria parasite egress via cGMP inhibition
articles.title_original: An acyclic nucleoside phosphonate effectively blocks the egress of the malaria parasite by inhibiting the synthesis of cyclic GMP.
articles.authors: Ali M, Dura R, Guery MA, Colard-Itté E, Cheviet T, Robresco L, Berry L, Lionne C, Lavazec C, Claessens A, Peyrottes S, Wengelnik K, Wein S, Cerdan R
articles.details: This laboratory study characterizes UA2239, an acyclic nucleoside phosphonate with a guanine nucleobase, as an antimalarial candidate. UA2239 rapidly and irreversibly blocks the egress of merozoites and gametes from infected erythrocytes by decreasing cyclic GMP levels, with P. falciparum guanylyl cyclase α strongly suggested as its primary target. Parasites exposed to the drug over prolonged periods developed resistance by mutating downstream effectors rather than the target itself. The authors highlight its dual-stage activity against both parasite growth and transmission, positioning it as a first-in-class candidate for further development.

articles.headline: Household decisions and poverty drive delayed severe-malaria care in children
articles.title_original: Influence of Sociodemographic Factors and Household Decision-Making on the Delayed Presentation of Childhood Severe Malaria at a Secondary Facility in North-Central Nigeria.
articles.authors: Issa A, Lawal AF, Folorunsho M, Ibrahim OR
articles.details: A cross-sectional study of 146 children admitted with severe malaria at a secondary facility in north-central Nigeria examined how sociodemographics and household decision-making affected presentation timing, with delay defined as arrival >24 hours after symptom onset. 69.2% (101/146) presented late; most were male (59.6%, 87/146) and under five (65.1%, 95/146). Common reasons for delay were failure to recognize the child's illness (44.6%, 45/101) and lack of funds (38.6%, 39/101). Waiting for the father's decision was associated with timing, and socioeconomic class independently predicted delay (AOR 3.05, 95% CI 1.10–8.48).

articles.headline: Migratory, larger, more colorful birds show higher avian malaria risk
articles.title_original: Host Traits and Avian Malaria Parasites: Understanding the Intrinsic Connections.
articles.authors: Pinzón-Cárdenas PA, González-Quevedo C, Rúa-Uribe GL, Pérez-Tris J, Rivera-Gutiérrez HF
articles.details: This phylogenetically informed comparative study assessed which host traits influence the probability of haemosporidian infection in birds, compiling published diagnoses from Colombia alongside ecological, morphological, coloration, and sexual-selection (dimorphism and dichromatism) traits plus a habitat-specialization index. Using phylogenetic generalized linear models on 115 bird species from different families, the analysis found that migratory species, larger-bodied birds, and those with more colorful plumage had a higher probability of infection, while habitat specialization was negatively associated with infection risk. The authors interpret these patterns through increased exposure to haemosporidian vectors and call for further study of sexual-selection traits.

articles.headline: Author correction: male-drive female-sterile self-limiting control of Anopheles gambiae
articles.title_original: Author Correction: A male-drive female-sterile system for the self-limited control of the malaria mosquito Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Strampelli A, Willis K, Gulliford HR, Gribble M, Fasulo B, Burt A, Crisanti A, Bernardini F
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Plasmodium sporozoites damage mosquito salivary glands, mixing saliva and hemolymph proteins
articles.title_original: High-resolution proteomics unveils salivary gland disruption and saliva-hemolymph protein exchange in Plasmodium-infected mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Alves E Silva TL, Kanatani S, Barletta Ferreira AB, Schwartz CL, Liffner B, Talyuli OAC, Olivas J, Nagata BM, Pala ZR, Pascini T, Alves DA, Zhao M, Suzuki M, Dorner LP, Frischknecht F, Coppens I, Barillas-Mury C, Absalon S, Ribeiro JMC, Sinnis P, Vega-Rodriguez J
articles.details: Using high-resolution proteomics, gene expression, and morphological analysis, researchers examined how Plasmodium infection affects Anopheles gambiae salivary glands. Infected glands showed differential protein expression, including enrichment of hemolymph-derived humoral proteins that diffuse in through structural damage caused by invading sporozoites, while saliva proteins diffuse out into circulation. Saliva from P. berghei- or P. falciparum-infected mosquitoes showed altered composition with a pronounced reduction of immune proteins, likely from association with sporozoite surfaces or changes in saliva properties. Saliva profiles were remarkably similar across both Plasmodium species, suggesting a conserved sporozoite–salivary gland interaction.

articles.headline: Novartis drug ganaplacide succeeds in malaria clinical trial amid resistance worries
articles.title_original: 'A sigh of relief': New malaria drug succeeds in clinical trial as existing treatments falter.
articles.authors: Kupferschmidt K
articles.details: Ganaplacide, developed by Novartis, eases worries about drug resistance, but hard choices remain about how best to use it.

articles.headline: Hidden low-density malaria infections persist in malaria-free Malaysian areas
articles.title_original: Persistent low-density infection in areas at risk of malaria reintroduction in Malaysia.
articles.authors: Jiram AI, Hisam S, Abdul Hamid MH, Jelip J, Noordin R, Musa KI, Ogu Salim N, Md Yusuf NA, Amir NF, Rasyidina A, Ismail NP, Hazwani Aziz DI, Abdullah NH, Kep A, Hussin Z, Ismail A
articles.details: A community-based cross-sectional survey screened 3,322 asymptomatic residents in 23 high-receptivity localities across Sabah, Perak, Kelantan, and Johor (June–October 2020) using microscopy and nested PCR targeting Plasmodium 18S rRNA. Microscopy detected no infections, but nPCR revealed 1.86% prevalence (62/3,322), comprising P. malariae (40.3%), P. vivax (29.0%), P. knowlesi (24.2%), P. falciparum (1.6%), P. cynomolgi (1.6%), and mixed P. vivax/P. knowlesi (3.2%). All positives came from Sabah and an Orang Asli settlement in Perak, with no significant difference by gender or malaria history (p>0.05). Authors conclude these hidden reservoirs threaten elimination and require sensitive diagnostics.

articles.headline: Plasmodium detected in 24% of febrile patients in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Epidemiology of Plasmodium spp. Detection Among Acute Febrile Illness Patients in Two Regions of Nigeria.
articles.authors: Quiner CA, Ephraim AZ, Kwaghe V, Erameh C, Samuels J, Courtney LP, Kim JH, Edeawe OI, Vongdip N, Ejike OJ, Odia I, Asman K, Chebu P, Agbukor J, Matthew OD, Orok V, Owolagba F, Okhiria B, Ogbaini-Emovon E, Odion WM, Obagho BA, Fayomade R, Adams J, Oga EA
articles.details: Patients with acute febrile illness at two tertiary hospitals were enrolled (August 2023–September 2024) and screened for 25 pathogens with a multi-pathogen assay. Plasmodium spp. was detected in 293 (24.4%) enrollees. Increased risk was associated with larger household size (COR=3.70, 95% CI 1.12–12.28), being a healthcare worker (COR=3.39, CI 1.22–9.41), children aged 11–14 (COR=1.86, CI 1.15–3.02), enrollment from FCT (COR=1.30, CI 1.00–1.69), and start of the dry season. Coinfections occurred in 37.2% (109) of Plasmodium-positive patients, including nine biodefense pathogens in 105 enrollees. Climatic predictors showed no statistical significance. Authors call for updated diagnostics and expanded surveillance.

articles.headline: Review finds malaria, measles, meningitis vaccines cost-effective in Africa
articles.title_original: Cost-effectiveness and budget impact of malaria, measles, and meningitis vaccines in Africa: a scoping review.
articles.authors: Anosike C, Ojiakor IM, Etiaba EI, Uguru NP, Ezenduka CC, Onwujekwe O
articles.details: A scoping review (PRISMA-ScR) searched PubMed, HINARI, and EBSCO Host for studies published 2004–2024 on cost-effectiveness and budget impact of malaria, measles, and meningitis vaccines in Africa, including 41 of 885 retrieved articles. Cost per fully vaccinated child ranged US$4.20–$52.35 for malaria and US$1.66–$4.31 for measles. Cost per DALY averted ranged US$22.63–$3,314.01 (malaria), US$1.60–$1,239.35 (measles), and US$66.64–$4,586.72 (meningitis). Most studies found the vaccines more cost-effective than no intervention or alternatives. The budget impact of introducing the malaria vaccine across 41 African countries is about US$185 million. Authors note evidence remains limited but vaccines were largely cost-effective.

articles.headline: Invasive and native malaria mosquitoes share urban habitats in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Co-occurrence of native and invasive malaria vectors in anthropogenic habitats in Metehara, Ethiopia: Opportunities for urban malaria control.
articles.authors: Ashine T, Kochora A, Assefa M, Negash N, Gidisa B, Hailemeskel E, Alemayehu M, Assefa C, Ebabu M, Mamo Ayana G, Nigusse T, Aberra Kassa F, Esayas E, Eyasu A, Chali S, Epstein A, Jaiteh F, Bradley J, Doumbe-Belisse P, Reynolds AM, Massebo F, Wilson AL, Weetman D, Donnelly MJ, Gadisa E
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: A bioecological assessment in Metehara town, Ethiopia (November 2023–October 2024) mapped larval habitats and collected immature and adult mosquitoes, with PCR for species ID, blood-meal analysis, and sporozoite detection. Of 767 potential larval habitats, 98.3% (754) were anthropogenic; among Anopheles-positive anthropogenic habitats (55.3%, 417), one-third contained both An. stephensi and An. arabiensis. Habitat positivity varied seasonally for An. arabiensis (LR=46.96, P<0.01) but not An. stephensi (LR=13.06, P=0.11). Of 2,078 adults, An. arabiensis was most abundant (63.7%, 1,323), then An. pharoensis (26.4%, 549). Sporozoite rate was highest in An. pharoensis (4.2%, 23/548), then An. arabiensis (0.4%, 5/1,321), with none in An. stephensi (0/173). Authors recommend integrated vector control targeting anthropogenic habitats.

articles.headline: Ultrasensitive ELISA detects P. falciparum at 0.5 parasites per microliter
articles.title_original: Ultrasensitive ELISA for Accurate Detection of Plasmodium falciparum Infection.
articles.authors: Kobayashi Y, Okita K, Chen PK, Hasunuma M, Tsuneki T, Hayakawa ESH, Yoshimura T, Ito E
articles.details: Researchers developed an ultrasensitive detection system for P. falciparum HRP2 (PfHRP2) and pan-lactate dehydrogenase (pLDH) by combining ELISA with thio-NAD cycling (TN-cyclon), testing recombinant protein in BSA buffer and Pf-parasitized erythrocytes in buffer and in non-infected human whole blood; pLDH was targeted to capture HRP2-deficient strains. The assay detected recombinant PfHRP2 at 0.782 pg/mL and pLDH at 1.33 pg/mL, and parasitemia in vitro culture as low as 2.57×10⁻⁵% (PfHRP2) and 2.41×10⁻⁵% (pLDH). In human whole blood, it detected 0.5 parasites/μL for both targets. Sensitivity is about 10 times greater than current ultrasensitive kits, requires no erythrocyte separation, and can detect pfhrp2-deletion strains via pLDH.

articles.headline: Guillain-Barré syndrome after P. vivax malaria, full recovery
articles.title_original: Guillain-Barré Syndrome Following Malaria: A Rare Case Report From Rural Ethiopia and Literature Review.
articles.authors: Abdela HA, Kimo NH, Woyimo TG
articles.details: Case report of a 21-year-old male who developed Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) following Plasmodium vivax malaria. He presented with ascending paralysis, areflexia, dysphagia, dysarthria, and autonomic dysfunction; cerebrospinal fluid showed albumin-cytologic dissociation supporting the diagnosis. He was treated with intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIg) and supportive care and achieved full recovery 8 weeks after weakness onset. The authors highlight considering GBS in patients with recent malaria and neurological deficits in endemic regions.

articles.headline: Catenated malaria VAR2CSA protein boosts CAR-T cells against tumors
articles.title_original: Protein catenation potentiates antitumor activity of malaria VAR2CSA navigation CAR-T cells in a mouse model of hematological malignancies.
articles.authors: Hu W, Tao Z, Guo W, Ding W, Huang S, Li J, Peng H, Zeng L, Chen Z, Qiu R, Kang Z, Yao Y, Chen X
articles.details: Preclinical study developing a navigable CAR-T cell system using the recombinant malaria protein VAR2CSA (rVAR2), which binds placental-like chondroitin sulfate A (pl-CSA) on many cancers. Researchers made an interlocking rVAR2 catenane (AXVB) as a navigator and built a third-generation CAR-T with CD28 and 4-1BB co-stimulatory domains via an anti-rVAR2 antibody. Versus the rVAR2 monomer, AXVB showed enhanced thermostability and target affinity. In vitro and in hematologic xenograft mouse models, AXVB-navigation CAR-T cells eliminated CD19-positive and CD19-negative pl-CSA+ tumor cells and significantly prolonged survival.

articles.headline: ITN use among pregnant women 63.8% in high-burden sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Pooled prevalence and factors associated with insecticide-treated net use among pregnant women in malaria high-burden countries in sub-Saharan Africa: a multilevel mixed-effects analysis.
articles.authors: Mwebesa E, Musinguzi B, Legason ID, Opoke R, Agaba BB, Kananura RM, Mwangi A
articles.details: Secondary analysis of 2017–2021 Malaria Indicator Survey data from seven malaria high-burden sub-Saharan African countries pooled 4,950 pregnant women aged 15–49, using multilevel mixed-effects logistic regression. Pooled prevalence of insecticide-treated net (ITN) use was 63.8% (95% CI 61.8–65.9), and was low in Ghana (49.2%) and high in Niger (90.5%). Primary education (aOR 1.36, 95% CI 1.21–1.53) and having one to two births (aOR 1.24, 95% CI 1.06–1.44) were associated with higher ITN use, while 14% of the variation was attributable to differences between countries (ICC 0.14) and 32% to differences between regions within countries (ICC 0.32). The authors conclude that ITN use is suboptimal with substantial regional variation.

articles.headline: Review: securing artemisinin production and managing resistance
articles.title_original: Safeguarding artemisinin: integrating bioproduction innovation and resistance management for malaria eradication.
articles.authors: Farmanpour Kalalagh K, Rezaei A, Hazrati S, Nicola S, Moradi S, Davarpanah Dizaj S, Tariverdizadeh N, Beyraghdar Kashkooli A
articles.details: This review consolidates the dual challenges of artemisinin (ART) production and Plasmodium falciparum resistance to ART, which undermines malaria elimination efforts. It covers biotechnological advances in ART biosynthesis—including metabolic engineering in native and heterologous hosts and approaches to agricultural constraints in Artemisia annua cultivation—and examines the molecular mechanisms of ART resistance, emphasizing pfk13 mutations. It critically evaluates artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT) and novel triple and quadruple ACTs (TACTs/QACTs), weighing their advantages, drawbacks, and geographical suitability, and advocates an interdisciplinary roadmap to sustain ART's efficacy.

articles.headline: Pyrethroid resistance and high malaria transmission in Bobo-Dioulasso vectors
articles.title_original: Insecticide resistance and malaria transmission indicators in Anopheles gambiae s.l. in Bobo-Dioulasso, Burkina Faso: implications for vector control strategies.
articles.authors: Amara MF, Namountougou M, Konaté H, Kouadio KWU, Yaméogo KB, Kagoné ST, Diabaté A, Gnankine O
articles.details: Entomological study in Bobo-Dioulasso, Burkina Faso, assessing insecticide resistance and transmission indicators in Anopheles gambiae s.l. from six neighborhoods using WHO susceptibility bioassays for six insecticides, PBO synergist tests, and PCR. Populations showed high resistance to DDT and pyrethroids with moderate-or-higher knockdown mutation frequencies (L995F, L995S), but susceptibility to bendiocarb and pirimiphos-methyl; PBO restored pyrethroid susceptibility, suggesting metabolic resistance. Of 622 specimens, An. arabiensis predominated (90.8%). Sporozoite rates were 45% in An. coluzzii, 27.4% in An. arabiensis, 16.2% in An. gambiae s.s. The entomological inoculation rate was 10.6 infectious bites/person, mostly from An. arabiensis (91.2%).

articles.headline: No kdr mutations found in four malaria vectors' organochlorine resistance
articles.title_original: Knockdown resistance (kdr) and organochlorine insecticide resistance in malaria vectors: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Abbasi E, Daliri S
articles.details: This PRISMA systematic review (six databases searched to June 2024, STROBE quality assessment, five eligible studies) examined knockdown resistance (kdr) mutations linked to organochlorine insecticide resistance in Anopheles albimanus, An. darlingi, An. dirus, and An. punctipennis. No kdr mutations (at VGSC codons 1010, 1013, or 1014) were identified in any of the four species. Resistance was attributed primarily to metabolic mechanisms—elevated mixed-function oxidases (MFO) and non-specific esterases (NSE)—rather than target-site insensitivity. The authors conclude kdr does not contribute to organochlorine resistance here and call for broader genetic screening.

articles.headline: Modelling vector control combinations under varying pyrethroid resistance in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Modelling the impact of different intervention packages for malaria control under varying intensities of pyrethroid resistance.
articles.authors: Gervas HE, Mayengo MM, Mlacha YP, Ngowo HS, Okumu FO, Selvaraj P
articles.details: A compartmental transmission model for Tanzania, incorporating Anopheles funestus and An. arabiensis, assessed insecticide-treated nets (ITNs), indoor residual spraying (IRS), and biolarvicides—alone or combined—under varying pyrethroid resistance, plus case management with artemisinin-based combination therapy and immunization (RTS,S/AS01, R21/Matrix-M). At moderate resistance (50%), ≥71% ITN coverage with either 50% IRS or 32% biolarvicide reduced the effective reproduction number (Re) below 1; at high resistance (>75%), Re remained above 1 regardless of vector control combination. Adding immunization (≥40%) to 80% ITNs and 80% treatment cut infectious individuals to <20% and Re below 1 even at high resistance.

articles.headline: Scoping review: mobile-app malaria surveillance linked to vector control
articles.title_original: Mobile app-based malaria surveillance and vector control integration: a scoping review of evidence from endemic settings.
articles.authors: Okereke PU, Okereke OW, Ogunyinka BO, Aluele SI, Tiamiyu RA, Afolabi MA, Umeh CV, Nwibo EU, Ibekwe SN, Ogbu AC, Okwor EO, Onwurah CC, Ogamba N
articles.details: This PRISMA-ScR scoping review synthesized evidence on mobile-app-based malaria surveillance platforms and their integration with vector control. Searching PubMed, Web of Science, EMBASE (Ovid), and Google Scholar for studies published 2010–2024, 152 articles were identified and 20 met inclusion criteria, analysed across timeliness, integration, community engagement, challenges, and policy alignment. Systems such as MCBR (Myanmar), SOCH and MCS (India), and MRRS (Zambia) improved case-reporting timeliness and enabled more precise vector control, while GIS tools aided hotspot identification and FeverTracker enhanced case detection. Challenges included poor internet access, low digital literacy, and limited national policy integration; the authors urge institutionalization within national health systems.

articles.headline: High P. falciparum genetic diversity and multiplicity of infection in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Genetic polymorphism of Plasmodium falciparum using merozoite surface proteins 1 and 2 (msp-1 and msp-2) genes in Oveng and Mintom, South region of Cameroon.
articles.authors: Nanssong-Vomo CT, Feufack-Donfack LB, Bouopda-Tuedom AG, Ibrahima-Ibrahima, Kiam CB, Youmsi Fotso GJ, Kemeni MN, Fotso BT, Abate L, Eboumbou C, Ayong L, Mbida-Mbida JA, Nsango SE
articles.details: Using dried blood spots from infected individuals in Oveng and Mintom, southern Cameroon, this study genotyped Plasmodium falciparum via multiplex and nested PCR targeting msp1 (K1, MAD20, RO33) and msp2 (FC27, 3D7) allelic families with fragment analysis. Among 315 isolates, 12 msp1 alleles (172–300 bp) and 28 msp2 alleles (200–800 bp) were detected. For msp2, FC27 was found in 100% and 3D7 in 98.4%; for msp1, K1 in 87.8%, MAD20 75.2%, RO33 74.9%. Clones per infection ranged 1–7, with 99% polyclonal; mean multiplicity of infection was significantly higher for msp2 than msp1 (4.85 vs 2.40, p<0.05). Expected heterozygosity was 0.83 (msp1) and 0.98 (msp2), indicating high diversity and intense transmission.

articles.headline: Policy brief supports school-based preventive malaria treatment in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Implementation of the intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in school-aged children (IPTsc) in moderate and high endemic areas in Tanzania: a policy brief.
articles.authors: Makenga G, Baraka V, Minja DTR, Chiduo M, Mmbando BP, Seth MD, Lusasi A, Chacky F, Aaron S, David A, Mohamed A, Lazaro S, Molteni F, Bastiaens H, Van Geertruyden JP, Lusingu JPA
articles.details: This policy brief, following a clinical trial and large-scale implementation research in Tanzania, supports intermittent preventive treatment for school-aged children (IPTsc, ages 5–15). In high-transmission settings, up to 70% of school-aged children harbour parasites (mostly asymptomatically), contributing to school absenteeism (13–50%) and anaemia (61%), while acting as a transmission reservoir. School-based delivery of dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP) proved safe, cost-effective, acceptable, and led to significant reductions in malaria prevalence. The authors recommend IPTsc as a complementary control strategy for areas with moderate to high transmission and encourage adoption by countries with similar epidemiological profiles.

articles.headline: Rituximab linked to higher malaria risk in lymphoma patients
articles.title_original: Rituximab is associated with an increased risk of malaria among adult lymphoma patients in Malawi: a prospective observational cohort.
articles.authors: Eastburg L, Makhijani SA, Forconi CS, Moore D, Kharabora O, Kasonkanji E, Kampani C, Chikasema M, Roush SM, Kimani SM, Fedoriw Y, Juliano JJ, Moormann AM, Gopal S, Painschab MS
articles.details: This post hoc analysis of a prospective observational cohort of 96 adults (≥18 years) with diffuse large B-cell lymphoma compared CHOP chemotherapy versus rituximab plus CHOP (R-CHOP). Fourteen developed symptomatic malaria: 5/59 (8%) on CHOP versus 9/37 (24%) on R-CHOP (OR 3.5, 95% CI 1.1–12; p=0.039), with no association with age, performance status, or HIV. Among HRP2-positive cases, antigenemia was higher with R-CHOP (3.6 vs 0.92 ng/mL; p=0.011); malaria-specific antibody levels did not differ. Authors suggest rituximab may increase symptomatic malaria risk.

articles.headline: Lessons on barriers to four-dose RTS,S malaria vaccine delivery
articles.title_original: Recommendations to mitigate barriers to uptake and delivery of a four-dose malaria vaccine schedule: insights from the MVIP's qualitative evidence.
articles.authors: Price J, Collymore Y, Bawa JT, Mkisi RE, Buell C, Jalang'o RE, Chisema M, Amponsa-Achiano K, Gordon WS
articles.details: This qualitative analysis drew on published and unpublished data from the 2019–2023 Malaria Vaccine Pilot Evaluation of the four-dose RTS,S/AS01E vaccine in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi, including a longitudinal Healthcare Utilization Study that gathered caregivers' perceptions and interviewed health workers, with technical experts and implementation managers separately providing insights to validate the findings. Despite good vaccine acceptance, many children missed some or all doses due to overlapping acceptance and delivery challenges with the four-dose schedule. The authors present recommendations, guidance, tools, and materials to help programme staff address the schedule's complexity and track individual children's doses to support vaccine scale-up.

articles.headline: Five decades of malaria trends across Rajasthan's ecological regions
articles.title_original: Echoes of resilience: spatio-temporal analysis of five-decade battle against malaria in Rajasthan, India.
articles.authors: Singh H, Singh S, Saroha P, Singh K, Sharma RS, Sharma RP, Gupta J, Gupta SK
articles.details: This spatio-temporal study analyzed malaria patterns over five decades (1975–2023) across four ecogeographical regions of Rajasthan, India (Desert, Aravalli, Gangetic plain, Hadoti Plateau), assessing trends, Anopheles species diversity, insecticide susceptibility, and rainfall correlation. Malaria peaked in 1976 and 1996, then declined sharply after 2000; median API ranged from 1 (Gangetic plain) to 1.93 (Hadoti Plateau). Rainfall correlated positively with API in the Desert (r=0.39; p=0.006) and Gangetic Plain (r=0.35; p=0.014). Anopheles stephensi and An. culicifacies predominated, resistant to organochlorides and organophosphates but susceptible to synthetic pyrethroids; Aravalli had the highest P. falciparum prevalence.

articles.headline: High acceptance but low awareness of R21 vaccine in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Awareness, acceptability, and willingness to pay for the R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccine: a cross-sectional study among pregnant women and nursing mothers in Enugu State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Dim OF, Chima UE, Iloabuchi FC, Anene-Okeke CG, Nwachuya CA, Onyehalu JC, Umeh AU, Agbo CA, Agbo JE, Isah A
articles.details: This descriptive cross-sectional study used a 30-item questionnaire to assess awareness, acceptability, and willingness to pay (WTP) for the R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccine among 310 pregnant women and nursing mothers in Enugu State, Nigeria. Most were unaware of the vaccine (262; 84.5%), yet 292 (94.2%) agreed to vaccinate their child and 247 (79.7%) were willing to pay, with mean maximum WTP of US$0.82±1.41 and median US$0.38. Age (p=0.005) and health insurance (p=0.021) were significantly associated with WTP; insurance predicted lower WTP (OR 0.417, 95% CI 0.19–0.92). Authors conclude increased awareness could boost coverage.

articles.headline: mRNA CSP epitope vaccine gives strong protection in mice
articles.title_original: mRNA delivery of circumsporozoite protein epitope-based malaria vaccines induces protection in a mouse model.
articles.authors: Wu NR, Beutler N, Hu X, Skog PD, Liguori A, Flores-Garcia Y, Maiorino L, Terada S, Lu D, Lai YC, Ndihokubwayo J, Schiffner T, Cottrell CA, Eskandarzadeh S, Alavi N, Kubitz M, Phelps N, Tingle R, Hodges S, Youhanna JE, Amirzehni S, Irvine DJ, Himansu S, Zavala F, Rogers TF, Burton DR, Schief WR
articles.details: Researchers developed self-assembling nanoparticle immunogens displaying four classes of P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein (PfCSP) epitope regions—NANP repeats, junctional-region repeats, and N- and C-terminal domains—and tested them in a mouse malaria infection model. N- and C-terminal domains gave little protection, while combining junctional and NANP repeat epitopes strongly reduced liver burden. mRNA-delivered nanoparticle and membrane-anchored immunogens displaying both junctional and NANP epitopes were most effective, achieving 99% reduction in liver burden and sterilizing immunity from parasitemia in some mice. Authors propose these mRNA candidates for rapid translation to human challenge studies.

articles.headline: PBO-treated bed nets show non-significant extra malaria protection in Kenya
articles.title_original: Do Piperonyl Butoxide Long-Lasting Insecticide Treated Nets Provide Additional Protection Against Malaria Infections Compared with Conventional Nets in an Operational Setting in Western Kenya?
articles.authors: Rascón-García K, Lapp Z, Markwalter CF, Kimachas E, Abel L, Obala A, Taylor SM, O'Meara WP, Mangeni JN
articles.details: This operational cohort study followed 768 participants via monthly active and passive surveillance, recording use of LLINs distributed in 2017 and 2021 and detecting symptomatic malaria by rapid diagnostic test in Bungoma County, Western Kenya. It compared piperonyl butoxide (PBO) LLINs against conventional pyrethroid-only LLINs using a multilevel logistic regression model. PBO LLINs provided greater individual-level protection (odds ratio 0.70; 95% CI 0.47-1.03), but the finding was not statistically significant. The authors conclude that in areas with pyrethroid resistance, PBO-synergist LLINs can add protection and should be considered for scale-up despite added cost.

articles.headline: Poor healthcare access tied to higher malaria deaths in Colombia
articles.title_original: Inequitable Access to Healthcare and the Malaria Burden in Colombia: A Value-Based Policy Approach for Sustainable Elimination.
articles.authors: Olivera MJ, Magali Caro H, Ricardo Gómez J
articles.details: This longitudinal ecological study examined disparities in healthcare access and malaria burden across Colombian municipalities from 2010 to 2023, using national surveillance, statistics, and provider-registry data with indicators including a Potential Access Index, Annual Parasite Index, and malaria case fatality rate. Multivariable logistic and correlation models adjusted for rainfall and temperature. Lower access municipalities had higher case fatality and parasite indices; the strongest predictors were diagnostic delay >48 hours (aOR 4.1; 95% CI 2.24-7.11) and very low access (aOR 3.5; 95% CI 1.94-6.37). Extended travel >2 hours (aOR 2.8) and age <5 years (aOR 1.9) were also associated; only 39.7% of cases were diagnosed within 48 hours. The authors conclude persistent inequities in diagnostic timeliness and availability impede elimination.

articles.headline: Pregnant women face frequent, genetically diverse vivax malaria recurrences in Colombia
articles.title_original: Parasite genotypes and host immune mediators in primary and recurrent episodes of vivax Malaria in Colombia.
articles.authors: Duque-Isaza YY, Carmona-Fonseca J, Arango-Flórez EM
articles.journal: Rev Peru Med Exp Salud Publica
articles.details: Two cohorts of patients with uncomplicated P. vivax malaria—41 pregnant women and 46 non-pregnant individuals—were followed for 120 days in Puerto Libertador and Tierralta, Cordoba, Colombia, all given standard treatment. Parasite microsatellite genotypes (Pv3.27, Pv3.502, Pv1.501) and host immune mediators (IL-13, IL-10, TNF-alpha, IFN-gamma, IL-8, TGF-beta, Fox-P3, PD-L1) were compared between primary and recurrent infections. Recurrence frequency was higher in pregnant women (41.6%) than non-pregnant individuals (8.7%), with greater genetic diversity; recurrences were genetically homologous in only 23% and 33% of cases respectively. Only Fox-P3 varied significantly, higher in the primary episode. The authors conclude pregnant women face high recurrence and parasite variation, warranting strengthened vivax prevention and treatment in pregnancy.

articles.headline: Emberá communities in Chocó view malaria as a Western disease
articles.title_original: Between their own and the western: the experience of Emberá communities in Chocó with Malaria.
articles.authors: Duque-Ortiz C, Osorno-Sánchez S, Gutierrez-Asprilla SD, Garcia-Tirado MJ, Nieto-Betancurt J, Guzman-Sánchez LF, Montoya-Cedula S, Pérez-Villa M, Botero-Palacio LE, Vera-Marin C, Hernández-Sarmiento JM
articles.journal: Rev Peru Med Exp Salud Publica
articles.details: This ethnographic study explored how Emberá indigenous communities of Chocó, Colombia coexist with malaria, using 250 hours of participant observation in six communities and 25 interviews with members, authorities, and Jaibaná between October 2022 and November 2023, analyzed via Constructivist Grounded Theory in NVIVO. The central category framed malaria as "a disease that accompanies indigenous communities," with subcategories of disease from the West, the mosquito as master of the disease, and health care beginning with the Jaibaná. Communities perceive malaria as Western and attribute transmission to mosquitoes without recognizing humans as the outbreak source. The authors conclude the Jaibaná is central to care, addressing spiritual conditions complementing Western treatment, though ancestral practices are incorporated empirically.

articles.headline: Antibody mismatch to malaria parasite genotypes linked to severe disease
articles.title_original: Antibody responses to polymorphic Plasmodium falciparum merozoite antigens in Malawian children with severe and uncomplicated malaria.
articles.authors: Dobaño C, Rogerson SJ, Cavanagh DR, Taylor TE, McBride JS
articles.details: This study examined concordance between IgG responses to polymorphic and dimorphic regions of vaccine candidates MSP-1 and MSP-2 and PCR-detected infecting P. falciparum genotypes in 269 paediatric patients with cerebral malaria, severe malarial anaemia, or uncomplicated malaria in Blantyre, Malawi. Antibody specificities generally matched infecting genotypes, more so at convalescence, though levels fell after parasite clearance. At presentation, severe-malaria children did not have lower genotype-matched antibodies (MSP-1 block 2) than uncomplicated cases, but lower IgG to MSP-2 type B (FC27) correlated with cerebral malaria and lower response to type A (IC1/3D7) with severe anaemia. Discordant antibody-genotype responses were associated with neurological sequelae after cerebral malaria. The authors conclude blood-stage antigenic diversity could contribute to immune escape and malaria severity.

articles.headline: Satellite weather data forecasts malaria outbreaks months ahead in Zambia
articles.title_original: Malaria outbreak prediction at the sub-district level in Zambia using remote sensing satellite data.
articles.authors: Ippolito MM, Kvit A, Xu T, Nande A, Bond C, Matoba J, Lubinda M, Shields T, Thuma P, Shiff C, Curriero FC, Moss WJ, Truelove S
articles.details: This study built and validated a negative binomial regression model to forecast seasonal malaria outbreaks in a low-transmission sub-district catchment in Choma District, Zambia, using 15 years of weekly health-facility case data plus remotely sensed rainfall (CHIRPS) and MODIS/Terra land surface temperature. Trained on 2010-2016 data and validated 2017-2024, the model found cases rose with higher November-January nighttime temperature (β=2.1, 95% CI 0.7-3.5, P=0.003) and December rainfall (β=23.5, 95% CI -0.7 to 47.8, P=0.057), with a negative interaction (β=-1.2, P=0.052). It reproduced the 2020 outbreak within 4% (2367 observed vs 2467 predicted) four months ahead, though it overestimated in two seasons. Authors conclude this weather-driven approach can support stock management and resource mobilization.

articles.headline: Time series models forecast malaria cases across eight Indian states
articles.title_original: Forecasting Malaria in Indian States: A Time Series Approach with R Shiny Integration.
articles.authors: Ghosh SK, Ananthakumar U, Chougale PD, Somaraj AB
articles.details: This modeling study developed and evaluated time series approaches to forecast malaria cases across eight Indian states (Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, Maharashtra, Meghalaya, Mizoram, Odisha, Tripura, and Uttar Pradesh). Methods compared polynomial regression with seasonal components, log-transformed polynomial regression, lagged difference models, and ARIMA, assessed by RMSE and MAPE with residual analysis and rolling forecast validation. The log-transformed polynomial regression model consistently outperformed the others in accuracy and robustness across all states. The authors also built an interactive R Shiny tool letting users input data, select models, and visualize predictions to support real-time malaria forecasting and decision-making.

articles.headline: P. falciparum merozoite gene diversity differs by malaria severity in children
articles.title_original: Genotypic and antigenic diversity of Plasmodium falciparum merozoite proteins in Malawian children with severe and uncomplicated malaria.
articles.authors: Dobaño C, Rogerson SJ, Taylor TE, McBride JS
articles.details: Using polymorphic merozoite surface proteins MSP-1 and MSP-2, this study characterized genetic and serological diversity of P. falciparum infecting Malawian children across clinical presentations over two transmission seasons: 93 with cerebral malaria (CM), 50 with severe anaemia (SMA), 26 with both CM and SMA, and 92 with uncomplicated malaria (UM). Parasite genetic diversity was higher in SMA (2.70±1.20) than CM (2.25±0.89; p<0.01). The MSP-1 K1-type was more frequent in SMA but nearly absent in CM (p=0.03), and MAD20 block 2 was less common in CM than UM (p<0.05). MSP-2 FC27-type associated with SMA (66%) and IC1-type with CM (61.5%; p<0.025). Authors conclude parasite antigenic variability contributes to malaria pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Model finds LLINs plus treatment best controls malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Optimal control analysis of malaria infection in children under 5 years and pregnant women under the influence of temperature.
articles.authors: Robert PK, Irunde JI, Mtunya AP
articles.details: This study developed a mathematical model to evaluate three malaria control measures—treatment of infected individuals, long-lasting insecticidal nets (LLINs), and indoor residual spraying (IRS). The effective reproduction number was derived using the next-generation matrix approach, and optimal control theory was applied to identify the most effective combination of strategies. In single-strategy simulations, treating infectious humans was more effective than spraying or LLINs alone; among combinations, LLINs plus treatment was most effective, and adding all three controls gave no significant improvement over LLINs plus treatment. The authors conclude that combining LLINs with prompt treatment is the most cost-effective approach, particularly in high-transmission settings.

articles.headline: CNN with Otsu segmentation reaches 97.96% accuracy detecting malaria in blood smears
articles.title_original: Optimized CNN framework for malaria detection using Otsu thresholding-based image segmentation.
articles.authors: Singh R, Prabha C, Abdulla S
articles.details: This work proposes an optimized convolutional neural network (CNN) enhanced by Otsu thresholding-based image segmentation to detect malaria-infected cells in peripheral blood smear images. Using 43,400 blood smear images split 70:30 for training and testing, a baseline 12-layer CNN achieved 95% accuracy, rising to 97% with EfficientNet-B7 in a hybrid parallel feature-fusion model, and to a peak 97.96% with Otsu-based segmentation (about a 3% gain over baseline). On a manually annotated 100-image subset, Otsu masks achieved a mean Dice coefficient of 0.848 and Jaccard Index (IoU) of 0.738; five-fold cross-validation gave consistent results (94.8%, 96.9%, 97.8%). Authors conclude simple preprocessing can substantially boost CNN classification for scalable, low-cost malaria diagnostics.

articles.headline: Value profile for malaria vaccines and monoclonal antibodies
articles.title_original: Value profile for Malaria vaccines and monoclonal antibodies.
articles.authors: Birkett AJ, Ansah E, Gordon S, Gyapong M, Hall S, Kelly SL, Laurens M, Penny MA, Shirey M, Slutsker L, Sparrow E, Ethelston S, Westercamp N, Wu L, Hamel MJ
articles.details: This expert working-group value profile reviews malaria vaccines and prophylactic monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) for a disease causing over 0.5 million annual deaths. Since 2021, two WHO-recommended vaccines have gained Gavi financing and high demand across sub-Saharan Africa; pilot introduction was associated with a 13% decline in all-cause mortality and a 22% drop in severe malaria hospitalizations. The authors note continued interest in improved next-generation vaccines aligned with WHO preferred product characteristics, and emerging mAb prophylaxis, prioritizing prevention of disease and death in young African children while increasingly targeting transmission and elimination.

articles.headline: Homology modeling of malaria parasite type-P5 ATPases
articles.title_original: Homology Modeling of Type-P5 ATPases from the Malaria Parasite: Insight into Their Functions and Evolution, and Implications About the Effect and Role of Intrinsically Disordered Protein Structure.
articles.authors: Wiser MF
articles.details: This computational structural study characterized haemosporidian type-P5 ATPases by homology modeling, using yeast Spf1 (subtype-P5A) and human ATP13A2 (subtype-P5B) as templates. Malaria parasites have both subtype-P5A and P5B genes whose modeled structures recapitulate known features; an additional α-helix was detected in the P-domain of subtype-P5A ATPases, proposed as a distinguishing signature. The parasite proteins also contain inserted variable, low-complexity intrinsically disordered loops that may form a protective shroud-like structure. Modeling suggested the subtype-P5A ATPase is likely an ER transmembrane dislocase, while subtype-P5B ATPases are unlikely to be lysosomal polyamine transporters, indicating lineage-specific functional divergence.

articles.headline: Piper essential oil and 6-ishwarone show anti-P. vivax activity
articles.title_original: First Evidence of Anti-Plasmodium vivax (Plasmodiidae): Activity of the Essential Oil and 6-Ishwarone Isolated from Piper alatipetiolatum Yunck. (Piperaceae).
articles.authors: Ramos GQ, de Azevedo RG, de Oliveira AC, da Costa MLL, da Silva FMA, Sá ISC, Melo GC, Pinto Lopes SC, Pontes GS, Nunomura SM, Nunomura RCS, Roque RA
articles.details: This in vitro and in silico study evaluated Piper alatipetiolatum essential oil and its major constituent 6-ishwarone against Plasmodium vivax, with cytotoxicity testing in Vero cells and PBMCs and molecular docking on DHFR and LDH. Both inhibited P. vivax dose-dependently, with IC50 values of 9.25 µg/mL (oil) and 3.93 µg/mL (6-ishwarone), and no cytotoxicity at 24 h (cell viability 94.7–98.3%). Docking showed selective binding of 6-ishwarone to DHFR (−7.7 kcal/mol; Ki = 2.27 µM) versus weaker LDH binding (−6.2 kcal/mol; Ki = 28.10 µM), suggesting DHFR as the primary target. ADMET predictions indicated favorable, non-mutagenic, non-hepatotoxic drug-like properties.

articles.headline: Subnational stratification tailors malaria interventions in Liberia
articles.title_original: Epidemiological stratification and sub-national tailoring of malaria interventions in Liberia.
articles.authors: Koko VS, Hinneh DL, Kumeh O, Washington T, Alade JO, Duokie DS, Wesseh CS, Bawo L, Davis T, Zawolo J, Konwloh P, Jackson D, Kollah TC, Jones L, Janafo AS, Jeuronlon MK, Galatas B, Temu EA
articles.details: This subnational tailoring (SNT) analysis stratified malaria transmission risk at the district level in Liberia using routine health-facility data, the Malaria Indicator Survey, entomological data, demographic and health surveys, and modeled burden metrics. Epidemiological stratification based on modeled parasite prevalence (PfPR), incorporating the 2022 Malaria Indicator Survey, found a median PfPR of 29% (SD = 4.8%) across 98 health districts, ranging from 17 to 37%. Of these, 84 districts were classified as moderate transmission and 14 as high transmission, with none low. The analysis informed targeting of eight key interventions, revision of the national operational plan, and scale-up of dual-active nets and vaccination.

articles.headline: Automated reports on primaquine dose for vivax malaria
articles.title_original: Automated reporting of primaquine dose efficacy, tolerability and safety for Plasmodium vivax malaria using a systematic review and individual patient data meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Edler P, Rajasekhar M, Price DJ, Adam I, Awab GR, Barber BE, Brasil LW, Chamma-Siqueira NN, Chu CS, Cui L, Daher A, Gomes MDSM, Gonzalez-Ceron L, Grigg MJ, Karunajeewa H, Lacerda MVG, Ladeia-Andrade S, Leslie T, Ley B, Lidia K, Llanos-Cuentas A, Longley RJ, Manning L, Mekonnen DA, Monteiro WM, Moore BR, Nosten F, Pasaribu AP, Pereira DB, Poespoprodjo JR, Pukrittayakamee S, Rijal KR, Saravu K, Siqueira AM, Sutanto I, Taylor WRJ, Thanh PV, Thriemer K, Vieira JLF, White NJ, Yeshiwondim AK, Zuluaga-Idarraga LM, Guerin PJ, Simpson JA, Price RN, Commons RJ
articles.details: This living systematic review pooled individual patient data from efficacy studies of uncomplicated Plasmodium vivax malaria treated with daily primaquine regimens, published since January 1, 2000, to assess how primaquine mg/kg dose affects antirelapse efficacy, hematological safety, and gastrointestinal tolerability. As of January 16, 2025, 9,270 individual patient data records from 41 studies were collated into a standardized repository, and an R Shiny app integrating R and Stata analyses generated open-access automated reports for user-selected countries or regions. The authors conclude these location-specific reports can inform national and regional primaquine policy decisions and research priorities in vivax-endemic areas.

articles.headline: Malaria recording weak at Ghana's formal drug retail shops
articles.title_original: Exploring documentation and reporting practices in malaria tracking: a mixed-methods study among formal drug retail shops in Hohoe Municipal, Ghana.
articles.authors: Loglo V, Gmayinaam VU, Ampomah MA, Boateng P, Ansah EK
articles.details: A concurrent mixed-methods study assessed malaria documentation and reporting among 57 formal drug retail facilities (9 community pharmacies, 48 over-the-counter medicine sellers), with 7 in-depth interviews. Participants' median age was 26 years (IQR 24-42); 40 (70.2%) were Counter Medicine Assistants and 38 (66.7%) had 1-5 years' experience. 51 (89.5%) lacked documentation and reporting tools, and only 5 (10.5%) used paper-based methods, with a significant difference between pharmacies and OTCMS (p=0.004). Digital reporting was preferred by 35 (61.4%). Authors conclude documentation was low and recommend infrastructure investment and training.

articles.headline: Blighia sapida leaf extract shows antimalarial activity in mice
articles.title_original: Antimalarial, Antioxidant and Hepatoprotective Activities of Ethanol Extract of Blighia sapida K.D. Koenig (Sapindaceae) in Plasmodium berghei-infected Mice.
articles.authors: Akinyemi DO, Olorunfemi AB, Akinola O, Abiodun OO, Afolabi OK, Thomas BN, Gbotosho GO, Ojurongbe O
articles.details: This in vivo study evaluated ethanol leaf extract of Blighia sapida using Peter's 4-day suppressive and Rane's curative tests in Swiss mice infected with chloroquine-resistant ANKA, at doses of 50, 200, 400, and 800 mg/kg, with piperaquine/dihydroartemisinin (18/4 mg/kg) as positive control. The extract was non-toxic up to 5000 mg/kg and gave dose-dependent maximum suppressive and curative activities of 77.02% and 71.94% at 800 mg/kg. Hepatoprotection was strongest at 400 mg/kg (AST 84.93 U/L, ALT 68.48 U/L vs negative control 133.88 and 93.92). Antioxidant markers improved (MDA 0.22 nmol/mg, nitrite 3.79 µmol/mg at 800 mg/kg; p<0.05). Authors conclude promising therapeutic potential.

articles.headline: Smoke from native plants knocks down, repels malaria mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Field evaluation of ethnomedicinal plants' smoke knockdown and repellent efficacy against pre-bedtime biting malaria vectors in Mazowe district, Zimbabwe.
articles.authors: Nyasvisvo DS, Sande S, Sithole R, Nhiwatiwa T
articles.details: A field study tested knockdown and repellent efficacy of smoke from Vitex payos, Peltophorum africanum, and Adansonia digitata against malaria vectors, using a 4x4 Latin square design and CDC light traps. Three-to-five-day-old An. gambiae s.l. were exposed to smoke over 150 minutes. V. payos and P. africanum produced 100% knockdown, with faster kdT50/kdT90 for V. payos (37.8/69.7 min) and P. africanum (44.8/89.7 min) than A. digitata (77.8/146.9 min); 24-hour mortality was 91.7%, 80%, and 71.6% respectively. Smoke from V. payos (86%, p=0.008) and P. africanum (73%, p=0.028) significantly reduced An. funestus s.l. biting. Authors recommend further investigation as a complement to ITNs.

articles.headline: New β-carboline compounds resist resistance, hit dormant P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Characterization of β-Carboline Derivatives Reveals a High Barrier to Resistance and Potent Activity against Ring-Stage and DHA-Induced Dormant Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Haney RS, Butler JH, Wardlaw LA, Merino EF, Mendiola V, Cooper CA, Mathew J, Tumwebaze PK, Rosenthal PJ, Cooper RA, Kyle DE, Rizopoulos Z, Baud D, Brand S, Totrov M, Carlier PR, Cassera MB
articles.details: This study characterized β-carboline antimalarials PRC1584 and its more potent analog PRC1697 against Plasmodium falciparum. PRC1584 showed a high resistance barrier and retained potent activity against fresh Ugandan P. falciparum isolates, and both compounds had strong in vitro potency against actively proliferating ring stages and dihydroartemisinin-induced dormant stages. The PfKelch13-C580Y mutation was associated with increased susceptibility to PRC1584, whereas PfKelch13-R549T and Pfcoronin-R100K-E107V mutations were not. Authors conclude the class supports a possible novel mechanism of action and future development of ACTs targeting DHA dormancy in resistant parasites.

articles.headline: Review: histone lysine methyltransferases as P. falciparum drug targets
articles.title_original: Yet to SET: Plasmodium falciparum Histone Lysine Methyltransferases.
articles.authors: Shah D, Karmodiya K
articles.details: This review examines the role of Plasmodium falciparum histone lysine methyltransferases in transcriptional regulation and the histone modification landscape, framing the parasite's epigenome as a promising antimalarial target. It notes that the AT-rich genome, relative paucity of specific transcription factors, and limited heterochromatin make epigenetic control important to parasite survival and virulence, and that the parasite's epigenetic signature differs from its host. The authors highlight that regulators of these histone marks remain understudied compared with histone acetylation, and argue new antimalarials with distinct modes of action and multistage, multispecies activity are needed.

articles.headline: Platelet-derived TGF-β1 explored as cerebral malaria treatment target
articles.title_original: Platelet-Derived Transforming Growth Factor-β1: A New Hope for Cerebral Malaria Treatment.
articles.authors: Liu S, Guo B, Song L, Chen G, Zhang T, Du Y
articles.details: This review examines platelet-derived transforming growth factor (TGF)-β1 as a therapeutic target in cerebral malaria, a fatal neurological complication of P. falciparum infection. It argues platelets drive immunopathology and vascular blockage, and that platelet-derived TGF-β1 induces endothelial cell apoptosis, promoting microangiopathy and compromised blood-brain barrier integrity. The authors note reduced TGF-β1 levels in mouse/human tissue and peripheral circulation correlate with cerebral malaria severity. They propose brain-targeted delivery of TGF-β1 antibodies via surface-modified exosomes as a promising approach. No clinical results are reported.

articles.headline: Cell-permeable fluorinated nucleotide blocks P. falciparum growth in vitro
articles.title_original: Membrane-Permeable 5-Fluorodeoxyuridine Triphosphate Derivatives Inhibit the Proliferation of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Nikolova V, Linnemannstöns K, Bendel ME, Machado M, Ganter B, Budimir P, Vogts M, Fischer C, Ganter M, Meier C, Dobbelstein M
articles.details: This laboratory study tested cell-permeable derivatives of 5-fluorodeoxyuridine triphosphate (cpFdUTP) against P. falciparum in infected human red blood cells, addressing the parasite's lack of nucleoside kinases that limits standard nucleoside analogues. cpFdUTP rapidly and potently inhibited parasite proliferation, arresting development at the trophozoite-to-schizont transition by stalling DNA replication. Although cpFdUTP also impaired human cell growth, supplementation with thymidine or cell-permeable deoxythymidine triphosphate (cpdTTP) selectively rescued human cells while maintaining parasite inhibition, identifying a potential therapeutic window for cpFdUTP combined with thymidine as a novel malaria treatment approach.

articles.headline: Recombinant AsSGU protein produced and tested as transmission-blocking vaccine candidate
articles.title_original: Cloning, expression and purification of AsSGU protein and its immunogenic response analysis as target candidate for transmission blocking vaccine against malaria.
articles.authors: Singh H, Kirar M, Janjoter S, Dahiya N, Yadav M, Sehrawat N
articles.details: This study characterized AsSGU (Anopheles stephensi Secretory Glycoconjugate of Unknown function), a GPI-anchored midgut protein expressed after blood feeding whose role in ookinete invasion and transmission-blocking activity has been reported in An. gambiae. The assgu gene's expression was analyzed at different time intervals after blood feeding, and in-silico analysis indicated the protein is immunogenic. The gene was cloned and transformed into E. coli BL21(DE3), and the secretory recombinant protein was isolated and purified, yielding 0.2 mg/L from bacterial culture, then injected into a rabbit for immunological response analysis.

articles.headline: Rainfall and humidity strongly linked to under-five malaria in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Spatio-temporal dynamics of malaria incidence in children under five years of age and correlation with meteorological variability in the North and Far North regions of Cameroon between 2015-2022.
articles.authors: Mouliom A, Ilorme F, Ateba JM, Ekoyol G, Fotso J, Kidwang JP, Tchopwe J, Djele S, Dzika JO, Essangui Same E, Ayong L, Ali IM, Eboumbou Moukoko EC
