articles.details: This spatiotemporal study examined malaria incidence in the North and Far North regions of Cameroon between 2015 and 2022 and its correlation with meteorological variables, using distributed lag nonlinear models and a generalized additive quasi-Poisson regression model. The maximum recorded incidence was 190.6 (189.5-191.8) per 1000 person-years in the North and 243.8 (242.9-244.7) in the Far North. From more than 1 mm/day rainfall, transmission risk rose 1.2-fold (RR=1.2, 95% CI 1.1-1.2) in the North and 1.4-fold (RR=1.4, 95% CI 1.3-1.5) in the Far North, while humidity at 70% raised risk 2-fold (RR=2, 95% CI 1.4-2.8) in the North and 3.7-fold (RR=3.7, 95% CI 2-4.6) in the Far North. Overall, the data showed strong associations between malaria incidence and humidity and rainfall.

articles.headline: Genome study reveals Pfsa1–PfCRT K76T drug and host selection in Mali
articles.title_original: Genome-wide SNP genotyping of Plasmodium falciparum isolates across Mali reveals major impacts of Pfsa1 and PfCRT K76T selections on parasite populations.
articles.authors: Niaré K, Dara A, Sissoko S, Crudale R, Koné A, Doucouré H, Sanogo V, Cissoko M, Sissoko MS, Tékété M, Koné A, Diarra B, Dembélé M, Koné B, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Djimdé AA
articles.details: Researchers performed molecular inversion probe sequencing of 458 clinical P. falciparum isolates collected in 2023 from 13 health districts across Mali, analyzing drug-resistance mutations, SNPs, and human β-globin to assess parasite transmission, relatedness, and selection. Polygenomic infection prevalence ranged from 13.0% in the north to 44.0% in the south; mean IBD within districts was <0.2, and IBD between districts inversely correlated with distance (P=0.006, R=-0.28). The strongest selection signal was around pfcrt. PfCRT K76T (55.8%, n=104) and sickle cell trait (8.4%, n=83) were more prevalent in Pfsa1-carrying samples versus wild type (26.6% and 1.3%; P<0.005). The authors confirm Pfsa1's association with sickle haemoglobin and a Pfsa1–PfCRT 76T linkage warranting surveillance.

articles.headline: P. falciparum uses distinct DNA repair pathways in subtelomeric vs core genome
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum DNA repair dynamics reveal unique roles for TLS polymerases and PfRad51 in genome diversification.
articles.authors: Vishwanatha A, Zhang X, Liu YJ, Leung A, Herring M, Visone J, Chan A, Calhoun S, Deitsch K, Kirkman L
articles.details: This laboratory study of the haploid malaria parasite Plasmodium falciparum dissected DNA repair pathways by disrupting the DNA repair enzyme PfRad51 and the translesion synthesis (TLS) polymerases PfPolζ and PfRev1. TLSΔ parasites showed irradiation hypersensitivity that varied across the cell cycle, while PfRad51Δ parasites showed uniform hypersensitivity. Using targeted double-strand breaks, the authors found PfRad51 is essential for homologous-recombination repair in the conserved core genome, whereas a Rad51-independent, TLS-independent homology-directed pathway operates in the hypervariable subtelomeric regions that contain var virulence genes. They propose these differential repair responses maintain P. falciparum's distinctive chromosome structure.

articles.headline: Review finds variable fidelity of reactive malaria surveillance across regions
articles.title_original: Implementation and fidelity of reactive surveillance and response strategies for malaria elimination: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Oo WH, Aye Tun N, Thu KM, Cutts JC, Htike W, Htwe EP, Oo MC, Zaw AK, Agius PA, Fowkes FJI
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis examined reactive surveillance and response strategies for malaria elimination, searching five databases for all years up to 16 June 2025. It included 69 studies (33 Asia-Pacific including 16 in the Greater Mekong Subregion, 34 Africa, 2 South America), with 43 in the meta-analysis. The '1-3-7' strategy developed in China was common in the Greater Mekong Subregion, while 19/34 African studies used reactive case detection alone. Implementation fidelity was 100% case-investigation completeness and 97% (95% CI 93–100%) timeliness in China, but timeliness of case investigation was lowest in Africa (56%, 95% CI 26–83%) and the Greater Mekong Subregion (57%, 95% CI 37–77%). The authors conclude fidelity varies and strategies should be tailored to health-system capacity.

articles.headline: Review: migrants in Middle East/North Africa disproportionately affected by NTDs and malaria
articles.title_original: The burden, clinical outcomes and risk factors related to neglected tropical diseases and malaria in migrant populations in the Middle East and North Africa: a systematic review and meta-analyses.
articles.authors: Elafef E, Maatoug T, Evangelidou S, Marti-Soler H, Adam A, Arisha AH, Hilali M, Hargreaves S, Bani I, Seedat F, Requena-Méndez A
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis assessed the burden, outcomes, and risk factors of neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) and malaria among migrants in the Middle East and North Africa, searching six databases for literature from 2000 to 28 August 2024. It included 39 NTD studies (81,678 migrants, 11 countries) and 16 malaria studies (12,823 migrants, 5 countries). Pooled NTD prevalence among migrants was 4.7% hookworm, 1.8% Trichuris trichiura, 1.75% Ascaris lumbricoides, and 1.8% taeniasis; migrants had higher prevalence than non-migrants (e.g., hookworm 1.8% vs 0.03%, dengue 26% vs 3.5%). Malaria case fatality was 0.2–1.5%, hospitalisation 25.8% and ICU 1.3%; severe malaria was higher in non-migrants than migrants in Qatar (50% vs 5.2%). The authors conclude migrants are disproportionately affected, with evidence gaps remaining.

articles.headline: Time-course study maps three phases of heme processing in P. falciparum digestive vacuole
articles.title_original: Heme processing in the malaria parasite, Plasmodium falciparum: a time-dependent basal-level analysis.
articles.authors: Garnie LF, Egan TJ, Wicht KJ
articles.details: This laboratory study characterized the native digestive vacuole (DV) of Plasmodium falciparum over time using confocal microscopy, immunofluorescence, immunoblotting, and cellular fractionation, since inhibiting hemozoin formation is the mechanism of many antimalarials. Measuring DV uptake and growth, abundance of plasmepsins I and IV, and basal hemoglobin, heme, and hemozoin, the authors identified three developmental phases in chloroquine-sensitive NF54 parasites: lag-type growth (20–28 h), rapid growth (28–40 h), and plateau (40–48 h), each with distinct characteristics. They also observed key differences between chloroquine-sensitive NF54 and chloroquine-resistant Dd2 parasites.

articles.headline: Case report: prolonged cerebral malaria in adolescent with sickle cell disease
articles.title_original: Protracted Febrile Illness and Coma in an Adolescent with Sickle Cell Disease and Malaria.
articles.authors: Judd L, Bevot A, Lalremruata A, Flesche C, Mayer A, Mezger M, Heideking K, Bélard S, Remppis J
articles.details: This case report describes a 15-year-old adolescent with sickle cell disease who presented at a tertiary children's hospital with severe hemolysis after returning from West Africa. Over 5 weeks the patient had recurrent hemolysis, fever, and neuropsychiatric symptoms that worsened despite immunosuppressive and broad-spectrum antibiotic treatment. Cerebral malaria was eventually diagnosed and treated, leading to prompt recovery, though another hemolysis episode occurred 12 days after artesunate treatment. The authors highlight the complex interplay among malaria, sickle cell disease, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, and treatments, and stress the importance of thorough travel history and timely malaria testing in non-endemic settings.

articles.headline: Metabolomic profiling of Plasmodium knowlesi blood stages reveals drug targets
articles.title_original: Mass spectrometry-based metabolomics uncovers distinct metabolic signatures and potential therapeutic targets in Plasmodium knowlesi.
articles.authors: Uthailak N, Chotirat S, Anatjitsupha A, Thongyod W, Tipthara P, Sattabongkot J, Tarning J, Nguitragool W, Reamtong O
articles.details: This mass spectrometry-based metabolomics study profiled Plasmodium knowlesi across three intraerythrocytic stages (ring, trophozoite, and schizont) to characterize parasite biology. The analysis revealed distinct metabolic profiles, particularly in the ring stage. Glycerophospholipid metabolism and sphingolipid de novo biosynthesis emerged as key pathways across all stages, while phosphatidylserine synthesis was specifically linked to ring-stage-biased metabolites. The authors highlighted CDP-diacylglycerol-inositol 3-phosphatidyltransferase as a promising drug target, concluding the profile could support development of novel therapeutics against P. knowlesi malaria.

articles.headline: Questioning SP/amodiaquine seasonal malaria chemoprevention suitability in East Africa
articles.title_original: Is Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention with Sulfadoxine-Pyrimethamine/Amodiaquine Appropriate in East Africa?
articles.authors: Rosenthal PJ
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine outperforms SPAQ for seasonal malaria chemoprevention in northern Uganda
articles.title_original: Pharmacometric evaluation of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine- amodiaquine and dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine seasonal malaria chemoprevention in northern Uganda.
articles.authors: Bonnington C, Nuwa A, Theiss-Nyland K, Kajubi R, Kamya MR, Nankabirwa J, Ebong C, Nabakooza J, Opigo J, Salandindi D, Odongo M, Sararat C, Watson JA, Suwannasin K, Proux S, Koesukwiwat U, Tarning J, Imwong M, Tibenderana J, Nosten FH, White NJ
articles.details: This prospective 28-day pharmacometric study in Karamoja, Northern Uganda assessed seasonal malaria chemoprevention, with children in two villages receiving sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine-amodiaquine (SPAQ) and a third village randomized to dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP) versus no SMC. Among 1250 children, baseline qPCR parasitemia prevalence was 46% (575/1250; P. falciparum 85%, other mainly P. ovale 25%). Breakthrough parasitemias occurred in 7% (33/496) of DP, 31% (158/504) of SPAQ, and 39% (98/250) of no-drug recipients. Adjusted protective efficacy against clinical malaria was 60% for SPAQ and 86% for DP. All 260 genotyped P. falciparum isolates were Pfcrt K76 and mostly quintuple Pfdhfr/dhps mutants. The authors concluded SPAQ had low chemopreventive efficacy whereas DP was effective.

articles.headline: Optimized selective proteasome inhibitors show antimalarial promise
articles.title_original: Optimization of Species-Selective Reversible Proteasome Inhibitors for the Treatment of Malaria.
articles.authors: Gahalawat S, Ray S, Zhang X, Deng X, Han Y, Chen Z, Lawong A, Shackleford DM, Katneni K, Chen G, Li P, Ng A, Zhong L, Hu M, McInerney M, Wang W, Saunders J, Collins D, Jayaseelan J, Noack CL, C Maity B, De N, Laleu B, Campbell SF, Phillips MA, Charman SA, Ready JM
articles.details: This drug-discovery study optimized a series of species-selective reversible inhibitors targeting the Plasmodium falciparum 20S proteasome, starting from a carboxypiperidine scaffold identified via a high-throughput viability screen. Iterative structure-activity relationship studies produced potent, selective inhibitors with good oral bioavailability, showing nanomolar potency against P. falciparum blood-stage parasites and selective inhibition of the parasite proteasome over the human counterpart. Cryo-EM confirmed binding at the β5 subunit, and in vivo pharmacokinetic studies identified promising candidates. The authors conclude proteasome inhibition is a viable strategy for novel antimalarial drug development.

articles.headline: Malaria and typhoid coinfection common among febrile patients in Yaoundé
articles.title_original: Malaria and Typhoid Fever Coinfection Among Febrile Patients Attending a Health Facility in Yaoundé, Cameroon: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Netongo PM, Tchoutang AM, Eric MB, Tepa A, Nzuno MC, Kamdem SD
articles.details: This cross-sectional study at the Etoug-Ebe Baptist Hospital in Yaoundé, Cameroon enrolled 288 febrile patients aged over 2 years to determine prevalence of malaria, typhoid fever, and coinfection and associated risk factors. Malaria was diagnosed by rapid diagnostic test followed by thick blood smear microscopy; typhoid by Widal, IgM/IgG rapid tests, and stool antigen detection. Of 288 participants, 179 (62.5%) tested positive for malaria and/or typhoid; prevalence was 44.79% (n=129) for malaria, 37.15% (n=107) for typhoid, and 19.79% (n=57) for coinfection. Coinfection was significantly associated with fever (p=0.008) and profuse sweating (p=0.05), while jaundice was linked to typhoid (p=0.029). The authors conclude malaria was the leading cause of febrile illness with notable coinfection levels.

articles.headline: Astrocyte and β-dystroglycan loss linked to blood-brain barrier breakdown in cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: β-Dystroglycan Downregulation and Astrocytic Alterations: A Possible Role in Blood-Brain Barrier Disruption During Experimental Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Karri S, Babu PP
articles.details: Using an experimental cerebral malaria (ECM) model, researchers compared the brain at 5 days post-infection (early asymptomatic stage) and 7 days post-infection (terminal stage), with early artemether (ARM) treatment as a recovery comparison. They measured brain water content and blood-brain barrier integrity and assessed protein expression and localization by immunoblotting, immunohistochemistry, and immunofluorescence. The terminal stage showed increased Evans blue extravasation, FITC-dextran leakage, and brain edema versus the ARM-treated group, alongside loss of β-dystroglycan, altered astrocyte morphology, and reduced ZO-1 and collagen IV. The authors propose that MMP-9 cleavage of β-dystroglycan disrupts astrocytic connections with the vasculature and dysregulates the dystrophin-glycoprotein complex, driving edema.

articles.headline: Plasmodium protein IMC1j organizes microtubules and drives ookinete motility
articles.title_original: Plasmodium berghei IMC1j interacts with δ-tubulin to orchestrate subpellicular microtubule organization in ookinetes.
articles.authors: He G, Feng Y, He D, Hu Y, Wang D, Wang S, Sun X, Shi S, Yang T, Hu X, Huang Y, Cui L, Cao Y, Zhu X
articles.details: This molecular study characterized the alveolin protein PbIMC1j in the malaria parasite Plasmodium berghei. PbIMC1j was found to be palmitoylated and predominantly expressed in ookinetes; gene knockout reduced asexual-stage fitness and virulence, altered ookinete morphology, and impaired motility and/or infectivity of ookinetes and sporozoites. Deletion analyses showed the C-terminal coiled-coil domain is required for proper IMC targeting and function, while the IMCp domain mediates interactions that stabilize the IMC protein ISC1 and the subpellicular microtubule component δ-tubulin (TubD). In ookinetes lacking TubD, the subpellicular microtubules were disorganized, mirroring the defects seen in PbIMC1j-deficient strains.

articles.headline: Rhoptry neck protein RON6 sustains malaria parasite infectivity and virulence
articles.title_original: Plasmodium berghei rhoptry neck protein 6 maintains parasite infectivity and virulence.
articles.authors: Narahari V, Patri S, Satpute MD, Vemuganti GK, Mishra DK, Kolli SK, Arun Kumar K
articles.details: Using the rodent malaria model Plasmodium berghei, researchers investigated PbRON6, a putative ortholog of the essential P. falciparum RON6, in invasion of red blood cells and hepatocytes and liver-stage development. PbRON6 localized to the sporozoite membrane with an extracellular C-terminal domain. RON6-depleted parasites failed to maintain infectivity and virulence, prolonging mouse survival, and induced chronic malaria and hyper-reactive malarial splenomegaly marked by decreased B and T lymphocytes and loss of lymphoid follicles. The authors propose targeting PbRON6 in pre-erythrocytic stages to prevent clinical malaria and using the mutant to study splenomegaly.

articles.headline: Ribosomal RNA is degraded during Plasmodium falciparum gametocyte development
articles.title_original: Degradation of ribosomal RNA during Plasmodium falciparum gametocytogenesis.
articles.authors: Grünebast J, Singhal R, Bromley R, Kanatani S, Watson K, Olson S, Dumetz F, Pascini TV, Tripathi A, Dunning Hotopp JC, Sinnis P, Llinás M, Serre D
articles.details: Using Oxford Nanopore direct RNA sequencing of tightly synchronized blood-stage Plasmodium falciparum and salivary gland sporozoites, researchers tracked the dynamics of the three ribosomal RNA types (A, S1, S2) across the life cycle. A-type rRNAs predominated during the intraerythrocytic cycle and gametocytogenesis, while S-type rRNAs increased in mosquito stages starting 3 days post-infection, and sporozoites showed an even proportion of all types. They detected extensive, specific rRNA degradation during gametocytogenesis, beginning in stage II gametocytes and continuing to late development. The authors hypothesize this degradation is linked to translational repression and metabolic quiescence in stage V gametocytes.

articles.headline: Measuring resistant-allele formation in a malaria mosquito gene drive
articles.title_original: Mutant allele formation and inheritance during Cas9/guide RNA-mediated gene drive in a population modification mosquito strain for human malaria control.
articles.authors: Carballar-Lejarazú R, Pham TB, Tushar T, James AA
articles.details: This study analyzed Cas9/guide RNA gene-drive-mediated target-site mutagenesis in outcross and testcross progeny of an Anopheles gambiae strain carrying the TP13-based gene-drive system, to estimate rates of cleavage-resistant mutant allele formation. Mutant target-site alleles arose in the germline at average rates per target gene per generation of 0.08% for paternal and 0.33% for maternal testcross lineages, with an overall average of 0.21%. Germline-originating mutations represented 47% of those inherited in testcross progeny, the rest coming from paternal- and maternal-effect mutations. Clustering effects amplified potential inheritance frequencies. The empirically derived values are intended to inform more realistic data-driven gene-drive modeling.

articles.headline: Complement inhibition proposed as new therapy for malarial anemia
articles.title_original: Inhibition of Complement Will Substantially Reduce the Anemia of Malaria.
articles.authors: Taylor RP, Lindorfer MA
articles.details: This review argues that complement activation, particularly C3b opsonization, is a major driver of anemia in Plasmodium falciparum and P. vivax malaria, with at least 10 nonparasitized erythrocytes cleared for every parasitized one destroyed. The authors link both childhood malarial anemia and post-artesunate delayed hemolysis to complement, activated by hemin/hematin, the digestive vacuole, and hemozoin released from lysed parasitized cells. They propose testing FDA-approved complement inhibitors that block C3 activation, first in vitro in P. falciparum/vivax blood cultures, then in a clinical trial combining artesunate with a complement inhibitor.

articles.headline: Review of how skin environment guides Plasmodium sporozoite movement
articles.title_original: Influence of biophysical and biochemical cues on Plasmodium sporozoite dynamics in skin tissue.
articles.authors: Gopakumar N, Chen H, Messenger LA, Park S
articles.details: This review summarizes literature on Plasmodium sporozoite motility after Anopheles mosquitoes inject the parasites into human skin, focusing on the biophysical and biochemical cues sporozoites must navigate to reach blood vessels. Drawing on intravital imaging and in vitro, in vivo, ex vivo skin models and engineered substrates, it concludes that sporozoite movement is strongly affected by extracellular matrix composition, tissue stiffness, and surface topography. The authors note major gaps in understanding how age, inflammation, or mechanical stress alter navigation, and argue that insight into skin-stage signaling and gene expression could reveal novel therapeutic targets.

articles.headline: Seasonal malaria chemoprevention halved malaria incidence in Nigerian children
articles.title_original: Impact of seasonal malaria chemoprevention: a plausibility evaluation of routine data from health facilities in three implementing states in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Ikechukwu EC, Okereke E, Ogunmola O, Chukwumerije J, Emeto D, Salifu E, Balogun A, Oguoma C, Shekarau E, Ogbulafor N, Cassidy E, Rassi C, Oresanya O, Nnaji CA
articles.details: A pre-post plausibility evaluation used routine health management information system data from 36 health facilities (12 each in Kogi, Oyo, and the Federal Capital Territory) to assess seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) in children aged 3-59 months, comparing a pre-SMC period (2021) with the SMC period (2022). Mean uncomplicated malaria incidence fell from 20 to 9 cases per 1000 children; after adjustment, incidence was 50% lower (95% CI 39-60) during SMC, and all-cause fever was 29% lower (95% CI 14-41). Impact varied, greatest in Oyo and absent in Kogi. Severe malaria and deaths were too few to assess.

articles.headline: Uganda launches 24/2-hour initiative to speed malaria treatment
articles.title_original: The 24, 2 hours initiative: a game changer in malaria mortality reduction.
articles.authors: Ranjbar M, Katureebe C, Musanhu CC, Maureen A, Nabakooza J, Rukundo GB, Maiteki C, Mpimbaza A, Opigo J
articles.details: This paper introduces Uganda's '24,2 Hours Initiative,' a malaria case-management strategy framed around treating uncomplicated malaria within 24 hours and giving severe cases pre-referral or injectable treatment and supportive care within 2 hours of facility arrival, plus starting post-discharge services within 24 hours after severe-malaria discharge. It cites that severe malaria accounts for 15-20% of hospital admissions and that about 6% of malaria cases were hospitalized in 2023. The initiative emphasizes patient-centered continuity of care and free community-based malaria case management for all ages, and suggests its principles may apply to other high-endemic sub-Saharan settings if locally adapted.

articles.headline: Pakistan stakeholders review tafenoquine, primaquine for vivax radical cure
articles.title_original: Effectiveness of tafenoquine and primaquine for radical cure of Plasmodium vivax: a meeting report from dissemination of results of the EFFORT trial to stakeholders in Pakistan.
articles.authors: Qurashi B, Zaidi SE, Mushtaque M, Faisal A, Haroon Z, Agha B, Hammal N, Hameed A, Raza A, Ali U, Khan A, Jabbar FA, Adam ZR, Mehmood T, Price RN, Ghanchi N, Thriemer K, Beg MA
articles.details: This meeting report disseminates results of the EFFORT trial, which evaluated 7-day high-dose primaquine (7 mg/kg) and single-dose tafenoquine (300 mg) against the standard 14-day low-dose primaquine regimen (3.5 mg/kg total) for radical cure of relapsing P. vivax, which causes roughly 85% of confirmed malaria in Pakistan. Conducted across four endemic countries including Pakistan, the trial found both regimens well tolerated and effective at preventing relapse; in Pakistan the added benefit of high-dose primaquine over standard was modest but consistent with other South Asian countries. A national dissemination meeting on 10 April 2025 discussed policy alignment, phased tafenoquine and G6PD testing rollout, and funding constraints.

articles.headline: Trial protocol: SP vs artesunate for malaria chemoprevention in Zambian children
articles.title_original: Parasite clearance and protection from Plasmodium falciparum infection (PCPI): a two-arm, parallel, double-blinded, placebo-controlled, randomised trial of presumptive sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine versus artesunate monotherapy among asymptomatic children 3-5 years of age in Zambia.
articles.authors: Martinez-Vega R, Chaponda M, Mousa A, Gwasupika J, Chaponda EB, Daka V, Mwanza S, Chipunga M, Beshir KB, Chopo-Pizarro A, Kaur H, Okell L, Hansson H, Hocke EF, Alifrangis M, Gosling R, Roper C, Sutherland C, Chico RM
articles.details: This is a protocol for a two-arm, parallel, double-blind, placebo-controlled randomised trial in Zambia evaluating how parasite genotypes affect the efficacy of single-dose sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP) in asymptomatic children aged 3-5 years. Children receive directly observed treatment: the SP group (n=400) gets placebo artesunate for 7 days then active SP on day 0, while the AS group (n=200) gets active artesunate for 7 days then placebo SP on day 0. Thick blood smears are collected on days 0, 2, 5, and 7 and weekly until day 28, while dried blood spots are collected on the same days and weekly from day 28 to day 63 for qPCR and genotyping. Planned outcomes include time-to-parasite-clearance, mean duration of SP protection against infection, and mean symptom-free duration; no results are reported as this is a protocol, which notes the dhps K540E mutation as possibly linked to reduced chemoprevention efficacy.

articles.headline: High dhps resistance mutations in P. falciparum, Mbouda, Cameroon
articles.title_original: Polymorphism of the dihydropteroate synthase resistance gene of Plasmodium falciparum in three hospitals in Mbouda, Western Cameroon.
articles.authors: Nadia NAC, Cedric Y, Line GAP, Pacome TK, Armand GNG, Anaud NKJ, Esther DD, Edna GDM, Yval KT, Mbida M, Fuyou Z, Hu H
articles.details: This study characterized the Pfdhps resistance gene of P. falciparum across three hospitals in Mbouda, Cameroon, collecting 296 samples tested by rapid diagnostic test and thick blood smear, with positive samples analyzed by nested PCR and RFLP (AvaII and BstUI) for the A437G and A581G mutations. P. falciparum prevalence was 25.34%, and the Pfdhps resistance gene prevalence was 74.66%, with A437G at 56% and A581G at 64%. Mutant alleles were more frequent than wild-type (A437G 57.14% vs 42.85%; A581G 91.66% vs 8.33%), and the gene was most carried by the 6-35 age group; females had higher overall prevalence (77.8%) than males (71.8%). The authors conclude resistance is prevalent and call for enhanced surveillance.

articles.headline: Review: malaria vaccine awareness and acceptance vary widely in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Public awareness and acceptance of malaria vaccine in Sub-Saharan Africa: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Ekhator E, Essien VG, Afolabi OE, Okundaye MI, Adewonbi T
articles.details: This PRISMA-guided systematic review searched nine databases for studies published January 2021 to March 2025 on public awareness and acceptance of the malaria vaccine in sub-Saharan Africa, appraising studies with JBI tools. A total of 33 studies were included, predominantly from West Africa and cross-sectional in design. Malaria vaccine awareness ranged from 7.26% in the Democratic Republic of Congo to 95.6% in Ghana, with healthcare workers the main information source, while acceptance ranged from 10.3% to 100% and was generally higher in Nigeria and Ghana. Education and occupation were consistently associated with greater awareness and acceptance, whereas age, gender, and income showed inconsistent influence. The authors conclude awareness and acceptance vary widely and that more concrete steps are needed to improve uptake.

articles.headline: Qualitative study of community views on ivermectin mass drug administration in Kenya
articles.title_original: Community Experiences and Perceptions of the Broad One Health Endectocide-Based Malaria Intervention in Africa Trial of Ivermectin Mass Drug Administration: A Longitudinal Qualitative Study in Kwale County, Kenya.
articles.authors: Wangari W, Onyango T, Kazungu K, Nuru K, Kasiwa L, Chaccour C, Rabinovich NR, Mwangangi J, Maia M, Jones C
articles.details: This longitudinal qualitative study, conducted alongside the BOHEMIA ivermectin mass drug administration (MDA) clinical trial, examined community experiences and perceptions of the trial and the ivermectin MDA. Using purposive maximum variation sampling, researchers selected five villages (two intervention, three control), lived in each for one month before the trial making observations and conducting in-depth interviews and focus group discussions, and remained throughout all three MDA rounds; 22 in-depth interviews and structured observations were completed, plus 15 focus group discussions in eight additional villages two months after the MDA. Findings indicate that addressing needs and confidence in the implementing institution fostered participation, while previous negative experiences with MDA and perceived exclusion from engagement activities reduced involvement. The MDA was widely perceived as effective at reducing mosquitoes and malaria in both arms, and as highly effective against bedbugs in the intervention arm, highlighting the role of trust, engagement, and prior experiences.

articles.headline: Dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine stays highly effective for malaria in Papua, Indonesia
articles.title_original: Treatment Efficacy of Dihydroartemisinin-Piperaquine for Uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax Malaria in Timika, Papua, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Kambuaya NN, Ramli MSS, Candrawati F, Kenangalem E, Prayoga P, Puspitasari AM, Noviyanti R, Trianty L, Indrawanti R, Simatupang M, Ubra RR, Hill J, Hafidz F, Poespoprodjo JR
articles.details: This study assessed dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP) efficacy for uncomplicated P. falciparum and P. vivax malaria in Timika, Papua, Indonesia, 15 months after DP was also introduced for intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp-DP). Between May and December 2023, 75 P. falciparum and 75 P. vivax patients received supervised 3-day DP, with follow-up to day 42 and molecular analysis for resistance markers and to distinguish recrudescence from reinfection. Among those completing day 42 (68 P. falciparum, 58 P. vivax), the cumulative risk of same-species recurrence was 1.5% (95% CI 0-7.9%, PCR-adjusted) for P. falciparum and 5.2% (95% CI 1.1-14.1%, unadjusted) for P. vivax; no patient had day-3 parasitemia, and no P. falciparum isolates carried kelch13 mutations or increased plasmepsin 2-3 copy numbers (0/75). The authors found no evidence of high treatment failure or resistance selection but stress continued monitoring.

articles.headline: Actin-like proteins Alp5a/Alp5b drive male gamete DNA segregation, blocking transmission
articles.title_original: Plasmodium actin-like proteins are essential for DNA segregation during male gametogenesis and malaria transmission.
articles.authors: Varshney A, Pandey E, Nirdosh, Mishra S
articles.details: This molecular study in P. berghei examined the Plasmodium-specific actin-like proteins Alp5a and Alp5b, which localize to the nucleus, interact with each other, and structurally superimpose on human Arp2 and Arp3. The proteins were individually dispensable for blood-stage development, gametocytemia, and actin nucleation, but were simultaneously indispensable for parasite viability, and an Arp2/3 inhibitor confirmed an essential blood-stage role. Knockout parasites invaded the mosquito midgut and formed oocysts, but these were significantly smaller and failed to mature, leading to death. Genetic crosses showed male gamete defects from impaired DNA segregation during male gametogenesis, identifying Alps as essential for transmission.

articles.headline: Cryo-EM of Pfs230:Pfs48/45 complex with antibodies clarifies transmission-blocking mechanisms
articles.title_original: Cryo-EM structure of endogenous Pfs230:Pfs48/45 complex with six antibodies reveals mechanisms of malaria transmission-blocking activity.
articles.authors: Bekkering ET, Yoo R, Hailemariam S, Heide F, Ivanochko D, Jackman M, Proellochs NI, Stoter R, van Gemert GJ, Maeda A, Yuguchi T, Wanders OT, van Daalen RC, Inklaar MR, Andrade CM, Jansen PWTC, Vermeulen M, Bousema T, Takashima E, Rubinstein JL, Kooij TWA, Jore MM, Julien JP
articles.details: Researchers used cryo-electron microscopy to determine the structure of the endogenous P. falciparum Pfs230:Pfs48/45 complex, a leading transmission-blocking vaccine target, bound to six transmission-blocking antibodies. Pfs230 consists of multiple domain clusters rigidified by insertion-domain interactions, while membrane-anchored Pfs48/45 forms a disk-like structure interacting with a short C-terminal Pfs230 peptide critical for membrane retention in vivo. This membrane retention was not essential for transmission to mosquitoes, suggesting complex disruption is not the antibodies' mode of action. The analyses identified conserved epitopes and a structural paradigm for complement-dependent activity of Pfs230-targeting antibodies, informing next-generation interventions.

articles.headline: Survey finds poor post-trial access planning in Sub-Saharan disease trials
articles.title_original: Post-trial access practices in conducted clinical trials for Malaria, Tuberculosis, and Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTDs) across Sub-Saharan African countries: A quantitative study.
articles.authors: Seralegne Y, Wangamati CK, Bernabe RC, Mdala I, Zewdie M, Adane HT
articles.details: This quantitative, cross-sectional study used a self-administered online questionnaire to assess post-trial access (PTA) planning and implementation among principal investigators, co-PIs, trial coordinators, and sponsors involved in malaria, tuberculosis, and neglected tropical disease clinical trials conducted across sub-Saharan African countries between 2008 and 2019. Of 300 invited potential participants, 37 provided complete responses. A large proportion (43%) did not provide PTA plans, and most participants (70.3%) expressed a need for PTA training, highlighting an overall lack of formalized PTA policies and commitments. The authors conclude there is an urgent need for structured PTA policy training programs, stakeholder collaboration, and effective training.

articles.headline: Severe malaria in Senegalese children aged 5-15: 4.5% mortality
articles.title_original: Characteristics and mortality factors of severe malaria in children and teenager aged from 5 to 15 years in Thiès region, Senegal.
articles.authors: Lawson ATD, Sidibé M, Mahmoud MA, Bahada BHB, Diop SA
articles.details: This retrospective multicenter study across four referral hospitals in Thiès, Senegal (July 2019-June 2024) included children aged 5 to 15 hospitalized for confirmed severe malaria (positive rapid diagnostic test and/or thick blood smear plus at least one WHO 2015 criterion). Among 109 cases (mean age 9.56 ± 2.87 years; sex ratio 1.95), 60% were from rural areas with admissions mainly in October (36.7%) and November (22.9%). Frequent features included prostration (27%), convulsions (21.10%), coma (17.43%), severe anemia (33%), and thrombocytopenia (61.62%). Injectable artesunate was first-line, followed by ACT (82%). Outcomes were favorable in 90%, with 5.5% sequelae and 4.5% mortality; mortality was significantly associated with respiratory distress and ≥2 WHO severity criteria.

articles.headline: Multigravidae antibodies recognize ten conserved VAR2CSA epitopes for placental malaria vaccine
articles.title_original: Identification of Plasmodium falciparum VAR2CSA peptides differentially recognized by IgG of multigravidae through epitope excision.
articles.authors: Misal SA, Renn JP, Morrison RD, Cowles MV, Mahamar A, Attaher O, Dicko A, Duffy PE, Fried M
articles.details: This study used epitope excision combined with multiplex isobaric labeling to identify and quantify P. falciparum VAR2CSA epitopes differentially recognized by naturally acquired IgG from multigravidae (who had acquired anti-adhesion antibodies) versus primigravidae (who had not). VAR2CSA is the leading pregnancy malaria vaccine candidate; functional anti-adhesion antibodies develop over 2-3 pregnancies. While both groups reacted similarly to most epitopes, multigravidae IgG differentially recognized ten epitopes (Log2 fold change > 1, p < 0.05) that were conserved across multiple VAR2CSA alleles. The authors conclude these preferentially recognized epitopes will be valuable for designing a VAR2CSA subunit vaccine.

articles.headline: IMC55 protein dispensable for P. falciparum blood-stage replication
articles.title_original: The inner membrane complex protein, IMC55, is dispensable for intra-erythrocytic development of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Vick GW, Fierro MA, Park L, Brooks C, Vasant M
articles.details: IMC55, a multi-transmembrane inner membrane complex protein in Plasmodium falciparum identified as a putative interacting partner of the ER-resident calcium-binding protein PfERC, is not required for asexual replication. Although a whole-genome transposon mutagenesis screen predicted IMC55 to be essential for the intra-erythrocytic asexual cycle, inducible expression of the gene using two conditional systems revealed no defect in replicative fitness during the asexual blood stage. The authors conclude that IMC55 function is either dispensable or redundant for the P. falciparum asexual blood stage.

articles.headline: Malaria burden varies across three ecological zones in Ghana
articles.title_original: Clinical malaria in three ecological zones in the Volta and Oti Regions of Ghana.
articles.authors: Axame WK, Dinko B, Kweku M, Binka FN
articles.details: A cross-sectional study of 459 participants across forest (Ho), coastal (Keta), and savannah (Nkwanta) zones assessed clinical presentation, parasitaemia, anaemia, and ITN uptake using RDTs and microscopy. Overall malaria prevalence was 15.2% by RDT and 9.7% by microscopy, highest in Nkwanta; most cases were uncomplicated (88.7%), with severe malarial anaemia most frequent in Nkwanta (24.4%). Geometric mean parasite density was highest in Keta (11,715.6 parasites/µL vs 7616.4 in Nkwanta and 5880.1 in Ho; p=0.0003). Anaemia prevalence was 79.3% overall (Nkwanta 94.1%, Keta 82.9%, Ho 69.8%; p<0.0001). ITN ownership was 69.7% but usage among owners only 61.3%. Authors support subnational tailoring of interventions.

articles.headline: Health workers' malaria RDT recording mostly accurate in Uganda
articles.title_original: Accuracy of recording of malaria rapid diagnostic test results in Uganda.
articles.authors: Ssewante N, Namuganga JF, Katahoire A, Musoke J, Mutesi N, Humes M, Griffith K, Aponte JJ, Soebiyanto R, Cooper S, Agaba B, Opigo J, Lindblade KA, Mpimbaza A
articles.details: A prospective study in 16 lower-level public health facilities across four districts in the Busoga and Lango regions of Uganda measured agreement between healthcare workers' RDT interpretations and a trained, external panel using the HealthPulse digital RDT reader. Of 40,049 RDT images captured, 37,137 (92.7%) were analysed, and the OPD test positivity rate was 61.8%. Overall agreement was strong (Cohen's κ 0.81, 95% CI 0.76–0.85), with workers more often misrecording results as positive (7.2%) than negative (1.9%); agreement was higher in Busoga (κ 0.86) than Lango (κ 0.78) and lower with certain RDT products and faint lines. The authors recommend RDT validation exercises and tailored refresher training.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence fluctuated in Sennar State, Sudan
articles.title_original: Epidemiological trends of malaria in Sennar State, Sudan.
articles.authors: Abubakr M, Mohamed NS, Sami H, Sabon K, AbdElbagi H, Osman OF, Ahmed A
articles.details: A descriptive cross-sectional study in Sennar State, Sudan surveyed 2027 households (503 in 2018, 516 in 2019, 1008 in 2020), covering 8372 individuals, assessing malaria-related fever and death via questionnaires and RDT-confirmed infection. Fever prevalence was 41.0% (2018), rising to 58.5% (2019), then 48.4% (2020). Overall malaria prevalence rose from 2.8% (2018) to 4.8% (2019) before falling to 3.3% (2020); Abu Hujar consistently had the highest prevalence (13.6% in 2019; 9.5% in 2020). Malaria-related deaths were 12 in both 2018 and 2019, rising to 22 in 2020. Authors highlight locality-specific fluctuations and the need for sustained surveillance and tailored interventions.

articles.headline: Copper-driven cuproptosis worsens experimental cerebral malaria via astrocytes
articles.title_original: Cuproptosis-driven astrocyte reactivity exacerbates experimental cerebral malaria pathogenesis.
articles.authors: Hou X, Mo X, Zhang X, Wang Q, Chen X, Lai C, Gao J, Wu L, Liu W, He J, Zeng X, Yin H, Deng Z, Liu T, Ye M, Liu Z, Jin X, Song J, Wang J, Huang B
articles.details: Using a P. berghei ANKA-induced experimental cerebral malaria (ECM) model in C57BL/6 mice plus in vitro astrocyte experiments, researchers examined cuproptosis (copper-dependent programmed cell death) in disease progression, comparing the copper ionophore disulfiram (DSF) against the copper chelator tetrathiomolybdate (TTM). ECM mice showed cerebral copper accumulation with upregulated cuproptosis markers (SLC31A1, FDX1, DLAT, DLST) and downregulated ATP7A. DSF exacerbated ECM with increased parasitemia, blood-brain barrier permeability, cerebral edema, neuroinflammation, and astrocyte reactivity (GFAP+/Serping1+), whereas TTM counteracted these effects. In vitro, DSF-CuCl2 augmented astrocyte reactivity, cuproptosis regulators, and proinflammatory mediators (CXCL10, TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6); TTM-CuCl2 reversed these. Authors propose copper homeostasis modulation as a potential therapeutic strategy.

articles.headline: Training boosts lab workers' competency in LAMP malaria diagnostics
articles.title_original: Evaluation of laboratory workers competency on loop mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) technology for malaria diagnostics: a technology transfer assessment study (LAMP-TTA) during a multi-site clinical trial.
articles.authors: Tegegne B, Kamaliddin C, Pillai DR
articles.details: This technology-transfer assessment evaluated knowledge and technical skills gained from training in loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) for molecular malaria diagnosis among laboratory technicians in the LAMPREG study. All 15 enrolled participants were assessed by questionnaire before and after initial training and during follow-up support visits; 14 had no prior LAMP experience. After training, the self-assessed technical knowledge median rating rose by four points (median 5/5, range 4/5–5/5) and LAMP chemistry knowledge increased by nine points (median 10/10, range 6/10–10/10). At follow-up, all reported confidence running reactions, with result-interpretation scores of 10/10. The authors conclude adequate training, support, and evaluation underpin successful technology transfer.

articles.headline: Malaria messaging linked to higher bednet use in Uganda
articles.title_original: Effect of exposure to malaria messages on insecticide-treated net use among women and under-five children in Uganda: a propensity score matched analysis.
articles.authors: Mwebesa E, Agbi DM, Iha DT, Agasa LO, Mwangi A
articles.details: This propensity score-matched analysis used Uganda Malaria Indicator Survey 2018–19 data to assess the effect of exposure to malaria messages on insecticide-treated net (ITN) use among 8,868 women of reproductive age and 6,915 children under five. The night before the survey, 69.3% (95% CI: 67.2–71.2) of women and 71.8% (95% CI: 69.1–74.4) of under-five children slept under a net. Exposure to malaria messages had a significant positive effect on ITN use, with an average treatment effect on the treated of 5.1% (95% CI: 1.5–8.9%) among exposed women and 4.3% (95% CI: 1.0–7.6%) among their under-five children. The authors conclude behavioural change communication effectively promotes ITN use.

articles.headline: Submicroscopic infections and two vectors drive residual Amazonian malaria
articles.title_original: Submicroscopic infections and two malaria vectors are major contributors to residual malaria in an Amazonian village in Peru.
articles.authors: Guzmán-Guzmán M, Saavedra MP, Rodriguez P, Acosta C, Gomez J, Valdivia HO, Sanchez JF, Vittet M, Manrique E, Herrera-Varela M, Morales J, Bickersmith SA, Vinetz JM, Gamboa D, Conn JE
articles.details: This year-long study in the focal village of Santa Rita in the Loreto region of Amazonian Peru combined a monthly longitudinal cohort, population screening, landscape stratification, satellite imagery, and vector biology to identify Plasmodium transmission risk. Among 353 enrolled inhabitants, qPCR detected a 17:1 ratio of submicroscopic to microscopic infections; microscopy-detected infections were seasonal (peaking with rising river levels and rainfall) while submicroscopic infections were perennial. Despite high net use, residents with outdoor occupations had significantly higher P. vivax infection rates than indoor or non-working residents (35% vs. 21%, p=0.012). Plasmodium-infected Nyssorhynchus darlingi were found across all five landscapes, and a putative secondary vector, Anopheles near costai G1, was infected with P. vivax and P. malariae—a first report in Peru. Degraded forest was the most pathogenic landscape.

articles.headline: New Bayesian tool improves treatment-failure classification in falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Probabilistic classification of late treatment failure in uncomplicated falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Mehra S, Taylor AR, Imwong M, White NJ, Watson JA
articles.details: This methods study addresses distinguishing recrudescence (treatment failure) from reinfection in uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria, normally done by comparing parasite allelic calls from PCR of polymorphic markers in acute and recurrent samples using match-counting methods (e.g., 3/3 or 2/3 matching alleles). The authors show that in polyclonal infections, common in high-transmission settings, current match-counting and model-based methods may have unacceptably high false-discovery rates, overestimating treatment failure. They developed software called PfRecur that provides analytical Bayesian posterior probabilities of treatment failure, and used data from a study in Angola to demonstrate its utility in resolving complex polyclonal infections and more accurately estimating treatment failure rates.

articles.headline: Non-pyrethroid indoor spraying cut malaria over 13 years in Zambia
articles.title_original: Impact of malaria vector control interventions implemented in Luangwa District southeastern of Zambia: A 13-year observational time series analysis of malaria trends.
articles.authors: Chinula D, Hamainza B, Chiwaula J, Mumba K, Zulu R, Ndhlovu K, Kiware S, Reed T, Killeen GF
articles.details: This 13-year observational time-series analysis used Health Management Information System data (2009–2021) from Luangwa District, Zambia, with generalized linear mixed models to assess effects of long-lasting insecticidal nets (LLINs) and indoor residual spraying (IRS) on severe-malaria inpatient admissions and total confirmed cases. IRS with micro-encapsulated pirimiphos-methyl (PM-CS) reduced inpatient admissions (RR 0.28, 0.46, 0.41 at ≤3, 4–6, 7–12 months post-spray) and total cases (RR 0.25, 0.66, 0.48) for a full year; a deltamethrin-clothianidin coformulation reduced severe admissions only for the first three months (RR 0.27). Overall there were >90% fewer admissions and >80% fewer cases by study end, attributed to scaling up IRS with PM-CS or DC in late 2014 and nearly doubling health facilities in mid-2016. No epidemiological effect was attributable to LLINs, presumably because coverage was already high throughout.

articles.headline: Community and health-worker views on rural malaria control in South Africa
articles.title_original: Experiences of Community Members and Health Workers Regarding Malaria Control Programmes in the Rural Ingwavuma Community, uMkhanyakude District Municipality, KwaZulu-Natal Province, South Africa.
articles.authors: Thembakazi MT, Herbert C, Nelisiwe K
articles.details: Community members in the rural Ingwavuma community (uMkhanyakude district, bordering Mozambique) generally viewed malaria control activities positively, particularly health education, indoor residual spraying (IRS) and vector surveillance, with strong, respectful rapport between control personnel and communities. This exploratory qualitative study used eight in-depth interviews with healthcare workers and eight focus group discussions with 88 community participants, conducted in isiZulu and analysed thematically. Healthcare workers cited surveillance challenges from illegal cross-border movement between South Africa and Mozambique, occasional social protests disrupting spraying, and some participants reported negative IRS experiences. The authors conclude continuous community engagement is needed and the adverse IRS experiences warrant further investigation.

articles.headline: Malaria microscopy infrastructure repurposed to diagnose skin diseases
articles.title_original: Leveraging malaria microscopy infrastructure to diagnose common and neglected skin diseases using direct microscopy in Sumba, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Siregar GO, Harianja M, Smith DJ, Ataupah MR, Laiskodat RD, Bøgh C, Soebono H, Grijsen ML
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Ivermectin mass drug administration failed to reduce malaria in Mozambique trial
articles.title_original: Ivermectin to reduce malaria transmission- safety and efficacy results from the BOHEMIA cluster randomized trial in Mozambique.
articles.authors: Chaccour C, Nicolas P, Martinho S, Mundaca H, Elobolobo E, Ruiz-Castillo P, Houana A, Montaña J, Mbanze J, Casellas A, Macucha A, Mael M, Soares A, Kiuru C, Gutierrez AS, Imputiua S, Constantino L, Vegove V, Cole G, Duthaler U, Ribes M, Mutepa V, Brew J, Munguambe H, Stanulovic S, Xerinda A, Materula F, Gorski N, Wanjiku C, Furnival-Adams J, Túnez L, Sam L, Collins L, Xia K, Hammann F, Rudd M, Rist C, Jones C, Maia M, Candrinho B, Rabinovich NR, Saute F
articles.details: The BOHEMIA cluster-randomized trial in Mopeia district, Zambezia province, randomised clusters to monthly ivermectin (400 mcg/kg) to humans for three months, the same plus injectable ivermectin to pigs (300 mcg/kg) and cattle (200 mcg/kg), or albendazole (400 mg) control, with malaria incidence by RDT measured monthly in children under five over six months. Cyclone-related floods caused loss of 57 clusters; 22,724 participants from 100 clusters were enrolled (March 17–October 18, 2022). At six months, infection incidence was comparable across arms (adjusted IRR 0.99, 95% CI 0.86–1.14, P>0.9 for human-only vs control; 1.05, 95% CI 0.87–1.27, P=0.6 for human-plus-livestock vs control), and severe adverse event rates did not differ (IRR 1.29, 95% CI 0.62–2.68). Operational challenges rendered efficacy data inconclusive.

articles.headline: Teacher-delivered DP chemoprevention cut schoolchildren's malaria in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Implementation and effectiveness of intermittent preventive treatment in school aged children using dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine to reduce malaria burden: an implementation research of a cluster randomised trial in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Makenga G, Mmbando B, Seth MD, Baraka V, Challe DP, Francis F, Mhina AD, Liheluka E, Minja DTR, Chiduo M, Mtove G, Mandara C, Gesase S, Segeja MD, Kamugisha M, Hayuma PM, Mbwana JR, Sebukoto H, Malabeja A, Tupa JB, Ngede SJ, Lusasi A, Chacky F, David A, Thawer SG, Mohamed A, Aaron S, Lazaro S, Molteni F, Nkayamba A, Bastiaens H, Van Geertruyden JP, Lusingu JPA
articles.details: This cluster-randomised implementation study in a high-endemic area of Tanzania (February 2020–July 2021) evaluated intermittent preventive treatment in schoolchildren (IPTsc) using dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP) delivered by schoolteachers over three annual rounds. Three rounds covered 127 schools and over 73,000 children, with coverage of 77%, 73% and 80% across rounds; effectiveness was assessed in 1,971 children in the DP arm and 1,781 controls. After one year, IPTsc reduced clinical malaria incidence by 41% overall (95% CI 31–49; p<0.001) and 54% in high-endemic strata, and reduced parasite prevalence by 81% overall (95% CI 56.3–100; p<0.001). Authors conclude large-scale IPTsc is feasible, well accepted, and supports national policy adoption.

articles.headline: Malaria vector P450 enzymes metabolise PAH pollutants, may drive insecticide resistance
articles.title_original: Investigating the potential role of metabolic resistance genes in conferring cross-resistance to pyrethroids and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon pollutants in the major malaria vector Anopheles coluzzii.
articles.authors: Muhammad A, Ibrahim SS, Ismail HM, Irving H, Paine MJI, Wondji CS
articles.details: This laboratory study tested whether metabolic resistance genes confer cross-resistance between pyrethroids and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) pollutants in Anopheles coluzzii, using microsomes from pyrethroid-resistant field mosquitoes and the susceptible Ngousso strain, plus recombinant P450s (CYP6M2, 6Z2, 6Z3, 9J5, 6P3, 6P4, 6P5, CYP9K1, and An. funestus CYP6P9a) against fluorene, fluoranthene and naphthalene. Resistant microsomes depleted fluorene by 73% and fluoranthene by 43%, with high fluorene affinity (Km 58.69 µM, turnover 37.0 min⁻¹). CYP6P3 and CYP6Z3 metabolised fluorene (47.9% and 52.8% depletion), while CYP6Z2 and CYP6Z3 metabolised fluoranthene (50.4% and 60.3%); naphthalene was not metabolised. Authors conclude vector P450s can metabolise PAHs, potentially selecting for insecticide resistance in the field.

articles.headline: High P. falciparum MSP2 genetic diversity signals intense malaria transmission in Chad
articles.title_original: Genetic polymorphism of MSP2 and its implications for Plasmodium falciparum resistance in N'Djamena, Chad.
articles.authors: Abel D, Christelle Nadia NA, Cedric Y, Pacome TK, Bismarck N, Ngaryedji T, Merveille TT, Hervé MM, Deborah DK, Hu H, Michel N
articles.details: This molecular study analyzed blood from 302 patients at four health facilities to characterize MSP-2 genetic diversity of Plasmodium falciparum. P. falciparum prevalence was 40.72%, with higher parasite intensity in women (317.81±109.93). Of samples analyzed, 101 carried the MSP-2 gene (78.21%), yielding 12 alleles and a genetic diversity of 9.37%. The multiplicity of infection was 1.26, with 73.26% monoclonal, 25.74% double-clonal, and 1% triple-clonal infections; the phylogenetic tree showed 11 clades. Authors conclude the high diversity reflects intense transmission and should guide antimalarial resistance studies.

articles.headline: Health workers misrecord some malaria rapid test results in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Accuracy of recording and reporting of malaria rapid diagnostic tests in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Atobatele S, Sampson S, Orya E, Okoro O, Akpiroroh E, Okagbue H, Gab-Deedam SD, Bademosi O, Atu G, Lindblade KA, Mukhtar AM, Ayodeji O, Eugene EC, Aponte JJ, Galles N, Humes M, Griffith K, Cooper S, Ogbulafor N, Okoronkwo C, Ntadom G
articles.details: A mixed-method evaluation in 16 health facilities across Oyo and Sokoto States compared malaria RDT results recorded by health workers in facility registers against an independent panel re-interpreting photographed tests. Of 19,586 RDTs captured, 18,319 were analyzed: 6.2% were misrecorded as positive and 3.7% as negative, giving a positive predictive value of 87.2% (95% CI 86.4–87.8%) and negative predictive value of 92.9%. Overall agreement was 90.2% with a pooled kappa of 0.80 (95% CI 0.75–0.85). Faint test lines (8.8% of RDTs) increased misrecording as negative. Authors call for improved training and supervision.

articles.headline: Survey finds high malaria knowledge and declining cases in The Gambia
articles.title_original: Evaluation of knowledge, perception, and attitude of malaria burden in the Upper River region of The Gambia.
articles.authors: Okafor UA, Yaffa S, D'Alessandro U, Ojeh VN, Filho WL
articles.details: A descriptive mixed-method survey in the Upper River Region assessed malaria knowledge, perceptions, and attitudes among 381 household heads, plus 31 key informants and 7 focus groups. Basic malaria knowledge was high (percentage index of 81), with statistically significant influences (<5%) from district, number of health centers, gender, distance to health centre, and occupation. Treated bed nets were the main prevention tool; hospital visits and herbs were common treatments. Reported malaria cases fell 87% in those over 5 and 45% in under-fives, with mortality down 98% and 89% respectively over 2011–2023. Authors say findings can inform elimination planning.

articles.headline: Adequate antenatal care linked to higher IPTp uptake in Ghana
articles.title_original: Association between adequacy of antenatal care and uptake of intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy among women in Ghana.
articles.authors: Bayuo FB, Antwi EO, Adu-Boahen N, Effah EA, Appiah SK, Opoku HA, Efiong MB, Acheampomaa G, Senyah MM, Bofa A, Afoakwah G, Haile ZT
articles.details: This cross-sectional study used 2022 Ghana Demographic and Health Survey data (n=3692) to examine whether adequacy of antenatal care (ANC) was associated with uptake of at least three doses of intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp-SP). Overall, 61.3% received at least three IPTp-SP doses. In multivariable analysis, the odds of three or more doses were higher among women with eight or more ANC visits (OR 1.50, 1.18–1.90; p=0.001), a first-trimester first visit (OR 1.74, 1.44–2.09; p<0.001), and receipt of all ANC components (OR 1.62, 1.29–2.03; p<0.001). Authors conclude adequate ANC supports IPTp-SP uptake.

articles.headline: Structural equation modelling identifies child and environment drivers of malaria in Uganda
articles.title_original: Structural equation modelling (SEM) for malaria prevalence and risk factors in Uganda.
articles.authors: Kakaire G, Chumoh EC, Salyungu M, Kerich G, Too RK, Kosgei M
articles.details: Using secondary data from the 2018–2019 Uganda Malaria Indicator Survey, researchers built a Structural Equation Model with four latent constructs—Socioeconomic Status, Environment, Maternal Health, and Child Health—to predict child malaria status. Child Health showed the strongest positive association with malaria (β=0.22, p<0.001), followed by a marginal negative association with Environment (β=−0.36, p=0.056); Socioeconomic Status and Maternal Health were not significant. Model fit was moderately acceptable (CFI=0.786, TLI=0.630, RMSEA=0.064, SRMR=0.053). Authors conclude SEM helps disentangle malaria drivers and highlights child-level health and environmental exposures for integrated interventions.

articles.headline: Modeling human movement to guide malaria elimination in Bangladesh
articles.title_original: Identifying malaria elimination strategies in the presence of human movement in Bangladesh.
articles.authors: Mahmud AS, Chang MC, Citron DT, Engø-Monsen K, Sayeed AA, Zaman SI, Uddin D, Rahman MM, Rahaman M, Islam MN, Maude RJ, Buckee CO, Chang HH
articles.details: This modeling study built a metapopulation malaria model parameterized with human mobility data and fit to epidemiological data for the Chittagong Hill Tracts (CHT) districts in Bangladesh, where parasite mixing and importation across districts are common. The authors found substantial heterogeneity in transmission intensity, with the estimated basic reproduction number varying greatly across places with similar observed incidence. Local vector control had the greatest impact in areas combining high transmission intensity and high connectivity with more populated western districts. They conclude that focusing only on high-incidence areas is insufficient, since local elimination in low or intermediate incidence areas has only moderate impact, and the framework can prioritize resource allocation.

articles.headline: Pfs230 domain 12 emerges as transmission-blocking malaria vaccine candidate
articles.title_original: Pfs230 domain 12 is a potent malaria transmission-blocking vaccine candidate.
articles.authors: Inklaar MR, de Jong RM, Da DF, Hubregtse LL, Meijer M, Teelen K, Bekkering ET, Grievink S, van de Vegte-Bolmer M, van Gemert GJ, Stoter R, Nagaoka H, Tsuboi T, Takashima E, Spruijt CG, Vermeulen M, Dabire RK, Arinaitwe E, Cohuet A, Bousema T, Jore MM
articles.details: Researchers produced eight single-domain fragments of the Plasmodium falciparum sexual-stage surface protein Pfs230 in Drosophila melanogaster S2 cells and tested their immunogenicity in mouse immunizations. Beyond the known Domain 1 (D1), antibodies raised against Domain 12 (D12) showed strong, complement-dependent transmission-reducing activity in membrane feeding assays with cultured parasites, and also reduced mosquito transmission of parasites from naturally infected carriers. The D12 antigen was recognized by sera from an all-age cohort naturally exposed to P. falciparum, with antibody levels increasing with age. The authors conclude that Pfs230 D12 is a promising transmission-blocking vaccine candidate.

articles.headline: Geo-epidemiological risk stratification selects malaria interventions in Mali
articles.title_original: Geo-epidemiological risk stratification to select malaria interventions, case of Mali.
articles.authors: Cissoko M, Magassa M, Cissé IA, Coulibaly S, Samaké DS, Sanogo V, Koné M, Thiam S, Ballo T, Diarra S, Fomba S, Koné A, Gaudart J, Sagara I
articles.details: To inform Mali's new National Strategic Plan, the authors updated malaria risk stratification using WHO recommendations, drawing on data from all 75 Health Districts for 2018-2022 plus national parasitology and entomology survey data. Adjusted incidence accounted for reporting, diagnostic positivity, and health facility attendance, and four strata were defined per the 2017 WHO Malaria Elimination Framework. Most districts (54) fell in highest and moderate transmission areas covering 84% of the population (about 18 million). Ten intervention packages were selected, with 61 of 75 districts eligible for LLIN mass distribution, 57 for SMC, 67 for IPTp, and 19 for vaccination; 55 districts had high burden requiring continued major interventions.

articles.headline: US babesiosis cases and deaths exceeded malaria, 2018-2022
articles.title_original: Babesiosis and Malaria in the United States: Epidemiology, Research Funding, Medical Progress, & Recommendations for Improvement.
articles.authors: Jajosky RP, Li W, Jajosky AN, Jajosky PG, Stowell SR
articles.details: This analysis compared babesiosis and malaria in the US using CDC case data, NIH funding figures, and a literature review of medical progress, given both are caused by intraerythrocytic parasites (Babesia, Plasmodium). Over 2018-2022 there were 9,799 confirmed babesiosis and 7,722 confirmed malaria cases, with babesiosis exceeding malaria in 4 of 5 years; babesiosis and malaria were the underlying cause of death for 70 and 32 residents, respectively. Most babesiosis was domestically acquired B. microti, while over 90% of malaria cases were imported (P. falciparum most common) and ~95% linked to not taking chemoprophylaxis. NIH funding was ~$4 million for babesiosis versus ~$169 million for malaria in 2024, and the authors call for increased funding for babesiosis, a rare and orphan disease.

articles.headline: Ensemble deep learning classifies malaria parasites in blood smears
articles.title_original: An optimized transfer learning approach integrating deep convolutional feature extractors for malaria parasite classification in erythrocyte microscopy.
articles.authors: Reddy CKK, Anisha PR, Almushharaf A, Talla R, Baili J, Cho Y, Nam Y
articles.details: An ensemble transfer-learning approach achieved a test accuracy of 97.93% with an F1-score and precision of 0.9793 each for automated malaria diagnosis from blood smear microscopy, outperforming a Custom CNN (97.20% accuracy, 0.9720 F1), VGG16 (97.65% accuracy, 0.9765 F1), and CNN-SVM (82.47% accuracy, 0.8266 F1) in classifying parasitized versus uninfected blood smears. The model combined outputs from VGG16, ResNet50V2, DenseNet201, and VGG19 with data augmentation, pre-processing, and hyperparameter tuning. The authors conclude the scalable, automated method improves diagnostic accuracy and reduces reliance on manual microscopy, particularly in resource-constrained settings.

articles.headline: First in-cell images of P. falciparum ribosomes making proteins
articles.title_original: Seeing is believing-Plasmodium falciparum translation in action.
articles.authors: Erath J, Pavlovic Djuranovic S
articles.details: This is a commentary on a study by Anton et al. that used cryoelectron tomography and cryoelectron microscopy to produce the first in situ visualization of Plasmodium falciparum ribosomes inside infected red blood cells. Ten ribosomal states were resolved, five of which had not previously been seen in eukaryotes, yielding a more complete picture of the parasite's translation elongation cycle. The work characterizes parasite-specific translation dynamics and shows how the antimalarial cabamiquine disrupts elongation.

articles.headline: Severe thrombocytopenia in 27% of hospitalized malaria patients in Rwanda
articles.title_original: Prevalence and factors associated with severe thrombocytopenia among hospitalized patients with malaria attending Kigeme District Hospital, Southern Province, Rwanda: cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Ndatumuremyi J, Kagimbangabo JMV, Nshimiyimana I, Gaspard B, Sendegeya JP, Nizeyimana V, Byukusenge C, Nsanzabera C
articles.details: A cross-sectional study of 124 randomly selected hospitalized malaria patients at Kigeme District Hospital, Southern Province, Rwanda (December 2023 to November 2024) assessed the prevalence and factors associated with severe thrombocytopenia, using binary logistic regression. Severe thrombocytopenia occurred in 27.4% of patients. Factors significantly associated included age ≤5 years (AOR=5.17, 95%CI 1.674-16.259), infecting Plasmodium species (AOR=3.4), positive C-reactive protein (AOR=3.45), jaundice (AOR=2.875), epistaxis (AOR=1.84), and high serum urea (AOR=1.73), whereas coma/cerebral malaria was associated with lower likelihood (AOR=0.189). Authors stress early identification of at-risk severe malaria patients.

articles.headline: Optimized artemether-lumefantrine dose effective in infants under 5 kg
articles.title_original: Pharmacokinetics, safety and efficacy of an optimized dose of artemether-lumefantrine in the treatment of acute uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in neonates and infants of less than 5 kg body weight: a multicentre, open-label, single-arm phase 2/3 study (CALINA).
articles.authors: Wounounou G, Tiono AB, Ogutu B, Manyando C, Sagara I, Schneitter S, Bassat Q, Gaaloul ME, Marrast AC, Demin I, Winnips C, Risterucci C, Hugot S, Hofstetter G, Qian Z, Su G, Zhang J, Renner KC, Cousin M, Venishetty VK, Sayyed S, Gandhi P, Kabore B
articles.details: CALINA used an exposure-matching approach to test a new 5 mg artemether:60 mg lumefantrine dispersible tablet (1:12 ratio) given twice daily for 3 days in patients under 5 kg with Plasmodium falciparum malaria, comparing drug exposures with historical data from patients 5–<15 kg. In Cohort 1 (patients >28 days; N=22), geometric mean artemether Cmax was 68.0 ng/mL (90% CI 45.1,103), meeting the primary endpoint; Cohort 2 (1–28 days; N=6) showed comparable exposure (62.2 ng/mL, 90% CI 33.6,115). PCR-corrected Day 29 ACPR was 95.5% in Cohort 1 and 100% in Cohort 2. Treatment was well tolerated, with developmental assessments at 12 months within the normal range.

articles.headline: Climate change linked to rising malaria in Chiredzi District, Zimbabwe
articles.title_original: Climate change and malaria in Chiredzi District, Zimbabwe: emerging evidence and pathways towards malaria prevention.
articles.authors: Musakwa W, Selamolela NB, Ndlovu A, Zanamwe C, Dhlandhlara B, Mandinyenya B
articles.details: A transdisciplinary study examined links between climate change, environmental factors, and malaria incidence from 2010 to 2022 in Chiredzi District, Zimbabwe, combining weather station data, malaria incidence data, focus group discussions, and participatory hot-spot mapping, with statistical analysis in MATLAB. Evidence of climate change included increased rainfall and temperature, changing seasons, and extreme weather, with a statistically significant relationship to malaria incidence (p=0.0479), though some years showed a negative relationship attributable to other factors such as poverty and migration. Authors call for longer-term datasets and climate-informed malaria prevention programs.

articles.headline: Seasonal malaria chemoprevention cuts malaria only for weeks, Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Trends in uncomplicated and severe malaria following seasonal malaria chemoprevention administration in Nouna, Burkina Faso: a quasi-experimental pre-post study.
articles.authors: Gebreegziabher EA, Ouattara M, Bountogo M, Coulibaly B, Boudo V, Ouedraogo T, Lebas E, Hu H, Glidden DV, Arnold BF, Lietman TM, Sié A, Oldenburg CE
articles.details: Seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) substantially reduced uncomplicated and severe malaria in children under 5, but only in the weeks immediately following administration, according to a pre-post analysis of real-world surveillance data from clinic visits in 285 villages in Nouna District, Burkina Faso, across four rounds in 2021. Using negative binomial regression with injury rates as a negative control, uncomplicated malaria rates were lower by 41%, 34%, and 22% in the first, second, and third weeks after administration, and severe malaria declined by 47%, 47%, and 34%, while injury rates did not change significantly. The gradual rise in malaria by the third week suggests a shorter duration of protection than previously observed, and the authors note that extending to five rounds of distribution may be necessary in regions with longer transmission seasons.

articles.headline: Malaria care utilization fell sharply one year into COVID-19 pandemic
articles.title_original: Malaria healthcare service utilization before, during, and 1 year into the COVID-19 pandemic in 20 sub-Saharan African countries using Generalized Estimating Equation.
articles.authors: Asnake AA, Eshun G, Osborne A, Sarfo M
articles.details: This longitudinal study analyzed secondary survey data (Premise Malaria COVID-19 Health Services Disruption Survey 2020 and 2021) from 4,726 participants aged 16 and above across 20 malaria-endemic sub-Saharan African countries, using Generalized Estimating Equations to assess malaria service utilization before, at the onset of, and 1 year into the COVID-19 pandemic. Malaria testing fell from 71.77% to 59.12%, provider visits from 80.61% to 1.25%, and treatment receipt from 97.97% to 15.23%. One year in, participants were 45% less likely to be tested (AOR=0.55, 95% CI 0.48–0.63), 99.7% less likely to visit a provider (AOR=0.003), and 99.6% less likely to receive treatment (AOR=0.004). Authors stress the need for resilient health systems during global health emergencies.

articles.headline: Awareness and socio-economic status shape malaria vaccine decisions in Ghana
articles.title_original: Untangling choices: how awareness and socio-economic status shape malaria vaccination decisions among Ghanaian women.
articles.authors: Ansah RK, Tackie S, Asosega KA, Tawiah K, Danquah BA, Appiah ST, Twum RA, Omane-Adjepong M
articles.details: Using the Multi-Population Curie-Weiss Model (a statistical mechanics framework) applied to data from the 2019 Ghana Malaria Indicator Survey covering 3,004 women, this study examined how awareness, education, and social interactions shape binary malaria vaccination decisions. Overall, 93.54% intended to vaccinate their children and 6.45% did not. Among those in favour, 72.10% were educated and 40.10% were aware of the vaccine; among those opposed, 78.35% were educated and 71.13% lacked prior awareness. Rural educated women showed a modest 1.62% increase in intrinsic motivation, while urban educated women showed a negative interaction strength (−4.06%), suggesting limited imitative effects. Authors suggest these insights can inform strategies to improve vaccine uptake.

articles.headline: Malaria messaging and net use among under-five children in West Africa
articles.title_original: A comparative analysis of the predictive influence of access to malaria messages on utilization of mosquito nets by under-five children in West Africa.
articles.authors: Ajaero ID, Nwachukwu CA, Oramah CP, Ajaero CK
articles.details: This study compared mosquito net use by under-five children and the predictive influence of exposure to malaria messages, using post-2020 Malaria Indicator Survey data on nationally representative samples of women aged 15–49 in four West African countries (Liberia, Mali, Niger, Nigeria), analyzed with descriptive statistics, chi-square tests, and multilevel logistic regression. The proportion of under-five children sleeping under nets ranged from 47% in Nigeria to 86% in Niger. In the full regression model, Liberia (OR=1.31), Mali (OR=9.90), and Niger (OR=3.67) had increased odds of net use, while Nigeria (OR=0.31) had reduced odds. Malaria information from health facilities, number of under-five children, household size, and region also significantly influenced net use. Authors recommend more enlightenment campaigns, especially via health workers, and region-specific interventions.

articles.headline: Avian Plasmodium exposure slightly alters Culex pipiens mosquito microbiota
articles.title_original: Effects of avian Plasmodium exposure on the microbiota of Culex pipiens.
articles.authors: Garrigós M, García-Ruiz O, Enkvist CR, García-López MJ, Moreno-Indias I, Ruiz-López MJ, Veiga J, Figuerola J, Videvall E, Martínez-de la Puente J
articles.details: Using 16S rRNA metabarcoding, researchers assessed whether exposure to avian Plasmodium affects the microbiota of its natural vector, Culex pipiens. Field-collected, lab-reared mosquitoes fed on house sparrows (Passer domesticus) naturally infected with Plasmodium relictum (lineage SGS1) or on uninfected birds, and abdominal microbiota was analyzed, yielding 2,006 Amplicon Sequence Variants. The microbiota was dominated by Wolbachia, followed by Stenotrophomonas and Faecalibacterium. There was no difference in alpha or beta diversity between exposed and unexposed mosquitoes, but exposed mosquitoes had higher relative abundance of family Bacteroidaceae and genus Bacteroides (and Rikenellaceae when Wolbachia was excluded). Pathway enrichment (KEGG) showed enrichment in biosynthesis and metabolism pathways. Authors conclude exposure is associated with slightly different microbiota, though causality needs further study.

articles.headline: Correction: EEG features distinguish cerebral malaria in Malawian children
articles.title_original: Correction to: Electroencephalogram Features Distinguish Cases of Cerebral Malaria Among Malawian Children With Fever and Coma.
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: EEG features distinguish cerebral malaria from other coma causes in children
articles.title_original: Electroencephalogram Features Distinguish Cases of Cerebral Malaria Among Malawian Children With Fever and Coma.
articles.authors: Andrews A, Harrar DB, Zelleke T, Zhang J, Zhang B, Barber J, Dasan R, Amoah N, Findorff O, Krieger S, Barrett E, O'Brien N, Fuller CE, Moxon CA, Taylor TE, Griffiths MJ, Ray STJ, Postels DG
articles.details: This retrospective case-control study compared EEG recordings of children with cerebral malaria to those with nonmalarial febrile coma (bacterial meningitis, viral encephalitis, or unknown cause), all with fever and Blantyre Coma Score ≤2. It included 203 children with cerebral malaria and 87 with nonmalarial coma. Cerebral malaria showed higher voltage, slower background frequency, more sleep elements, less variability, more abnormal organization, less continuity, and greater quantitative power. Qualitative and quantitative EEG each distinguished etiology (AUROC 0.86 and 0.85), and combining methods improved performance (AUROC 0.90). Authors conclude EEG may help differentiate the microbiologic cause of febrile coma.

articles.headline: Migrant arrivals linked to malaria importation risk in Trinidad and Tobago
articles.title_original: Assessing the Potential for a Malaria Outbreak in Trinidad and Tobago Due to Migrant Populations From Guyana and Venezuela for the Period 2009-2019.
articles.authors: Narinesingh-Motie R, Ivey M, Edwards J, Edwards RJ
articles.details: This study analyzed a census of 211 positive malaria cases over 2009-2019 alongside arrival data from Guyana and Venezuela to assess outbreak risk in Trinidad and Tobago. Cases had a mean age of 35 years (SD 14.3), and 74% were male; common parasites were Plasmodium vivax (68.2%) and Plasmodium falciparum (22.3%). Most cases (91.9%) were imported, 6.2% locally acquired, and 1.9% unknown; among 194 imported cases, origins were Guyana (39.7%), Venezuela (38.7%), and Sub-Saharan Africa (15.5%). Log-transformed Venezuelan arrivals correlated moderately with cases (r=0.642, R²=0.412, p=0.033). Authors conclude population movement, especially from Venezuela, significantly predicts malaria incidence.

articles.headline: Antimalarial drugs differently affect host liver mitochondria in infected mice
articles.title_original: Comparative effects of antimalarial drugs on oxidative phosphorylation, mitochondrial dynamics and mitophagy in Plasmodium berghei-infected mice.
articles.authors: Olanlokun JO, Yahaya OO, Babarinde CO, Oloke OM, Steenkamp P, Prinsloo G
articles.details: This experimental study examined effects of antimalarial drugs on host liver mitochondrial function in 35 Swiss mice infected intraperitoneally with chloroquine-resistant (ANKA) Plasmodium berghei, treated orally once daily with 10 mg/kg of amodiaquine-artesunate (AA), artemether-lumefantrine (AL), sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP), or artesunate (ART); livers were taken on day 6. AA, AL, and SP did not significantly open the mitochondrial permeability transition (mPT) pore, while ART caused 7-fold opening and enhanced FoF1 ATPase (P<0.01); SP and AA induced membrane lipid peroxidation (P<0.01) and silenced mitochondrial complex expression. Drugs variably altered fission/fusion and mitophagy genes (DRP1, OPA1, Mitofusin 2, PINK1, FUNDC1, PGC-1α, prohibitins). Authors conclude host mitochondria respond differently to antimalarial drugs.

articles.headline: Bioclimatic zones shape insecticide resistance in West African malaria vector
articles.title_original: Genomic population structure and insecticide resistance mechanisms in the malaria vector An. coluzzii across contrasting bioclimatic zones in West Africa.
articles.authors: Amoako EK, Bennett KL, Hernandez-Koutoucheva A, Debrah I, Morang'a CM, Binaansim S, Asoala VA, Ariana C, Malm KL, Awandare G, Miles A, Clarkson CS, Amenga-Etego LN
