articles.details: This population genomic study examined connectivity and insecticide resistance in the malaria vector An. coluzzii across contrasting bioclimatic zones of West Africa, using SNPs from whole genomes and inversion karyotypes associated with environmental adaptation. The authors identified restricted gene flow between northern savannah and southern forested populations. Using Ghana as a case study, they found marked differences in insecticide resistance profiles across zones, with greater target-site pyrethroid and metabolic cross-resistance in the North reflecting differing insecticide use, and distinct resistance mechanisms in coastal Greater Accra possibly linked to urban agriculture. They conclude environmental conditions restrict gene flow and shape the geographic distribution of molecular insecticide resistance.

articles.headline: Novel 4-quinolone compounds show multistage antimalarial activity
articles.title_original: Evaluation of the Activity of 4‑Quinolones against Multi-Life Stages of Plasmodium spp.
articles.authors: Annunciato Y, da Silva EM, Araújo JE, Martinez LDN, Santana S, Workneh EA, S Alvarez LC, Magalhães ML, Andrade AO, da Costa WJ, Campolina G, Nascimento Santana M, Barbosa CS, Peres EPML, Moura CS, Dos Santos NAC, Bastos ADS, Barros RRM, Gazarini ML, Medeiros JF, Teles CBG, Costa FTM, Alves AC, Pereira DB, Pinto J, Cravo PVL, Guido RVC, Prudêncio M, Araujo MDS, Corrêa AG, Cassiano GC, Aguiar ACC
articles.details: This study evaluated novel 4-quinolone derivatives as multistage antimalarials with predicted activity against asexual intraerythrocytic, liver, and transmission-blocking stages of Plasmodium. Compound 1, a natural 2-substituted-4-quinolone previously isolated from plants and microorganisms, and its derivatives 2-6 displayed significant activity against Plasmodium sexual stages in ex vivo and in vivo assays and prevented hepatic cell infection, with compound 1 showing the highest activity against both sexual and asexual stages. The authors conclude these compounds warrant further study toward developing new antimalarial drugs.

articles.headline: Drug-resistance and CSP vaccine SNPs in P. falciparum, Cameroon
articles.title_original: Targeted Sequencing Identifies SNPs Associated with Antimalarial Drug Resistance and the CSP Vaccine Antigen in Plasmodium falciparum from Southwest Cameroon.
articles.authors: Efeti MT, Fankem SN, Diallo MT, Lahngong MS, Acha NL, Shey RA, Demeyer K, Souopgui J, Ghogomu SM, Njemini R
articles.details: Targeted Oxford Nanopore sequencing of pfcrt, pfmdr1, pfdhfr, pfdhps, pfkelch13 and pfcsp genes in 72 Plasmodium falciparum blood samples from a community in the Southwest Region of Cameroon found a high prevalence of pfdhfr mutations (98.6% N51I, 98.6% C59R, 97.7% S108N) and the pfmdr1 Y184F mutation (76.1%). Mutations in pfdhps were also observed (54.2% S436A, 2.8% A437G, 38.9% A581G). No WHO-validated pfkelch13 artemisinin-resistance markers were found, though K189T (63.4%) and R255K (4.2%) variants were detected, and 19 non-synonymous SNPs were identified in pfcsp. The authors support the continued use of artemisinin-based combination therapies and sustained molecular surveillance of both antimalarial drug resistance and vaccine-related polymorphisms.

articles.headline: Travel and mobility drive imported malaria into Zanzibar
articles.title_original: Integrating mobility, travel survey, and malaria case data to understand drivers of malaria importation to Zanzibar, 2022-2023.
articles.authors: Muller JG, Dewalt K, Goel V, Ali M, Hassan W, Mohamed B, Choloi BB, Msolo D, Zacharia A, Nwange M, Kane R, Bisanzio D, Emch M, Björkman A, Reithinger R, Shija SJ, Ngasala BE, Juliano JJ, Lin JT
articles.details: This study integrated prospective facility and traveller surveys, Meta digital mobility data, and rainfall data to identify drivers of malaria importation to Zanzibar's island of Unguja. Surveys ran at 94 health facilities (May 2022–December 2023) and a Dar es Salaam ferry terminal (August–December 2024). Of 3,875 malaria cases, 1,172 reported mainland Tanzania travel within 30 days (283/511, 55% in 2022; 889/3,348, 27% in 2023), most often to Tanga, Dar es Salaam, and Morogoro. Children (aOR 3.2, 95% CI 2.2–4.8) and females (aOR 1.5, 95% CI 1.3–1.8) had higher odds of travel-associated malaria. Imported cases correlated with rainfall at mainland destinations, suggesting predictable patterns for targeting high-risk travellers.

articles.headline: Factors associated with insecticide-treated net use in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Factors associated with insecticide-treated mosquito nets utilization for malaria prevention in Burkina Faso: finding from cross-sectional household survey.
articles.authors: Badolo H, Hien H, Hien AS, Somda SMA, Bazié H, Bacyé FY, Diallo CO, Tierno A, Guira M, Meda N
articles.details: This quasi-experimental cross-sectional study in Burkina Faso used univariate and multivariate binary logistic regression (Stata 16.1) to assess factors associated with insecticide-treated net (ITN) use. Net textile type (polyester OR 1.23, 95% CI 1.13–1.33) and net age were influential, with older nets used less (6–11 months OR 0.91, 95% CI 0.83–0.997; ≥12 months OR 0.52, 95% CI 0.401–0.675). The level of ITN possession (excessive ITNs OR 0.66, 95% CI 0.597–0.74) and the household poverty quintile were also associated with ITN use (mean OR 0.791; rich OR 0.656; very rich OR 0.557). The authors conclude that strengthening health education on the benefits of ITNs will improve appropriate net use.

articles.headline: Ferulenol disrupts P. falciparum blood and sexual stages
articles.title_original: Disruption of mitochondrial function in blood and sexual stages of Plasmodium falciparum by ferulenol.
articles.authors: Niramolyanun G, Praikongkatham C, Nguitragool W, Sattabongkot J, Kangwanrangsan N
articles.details: This in vitro study tested ferulenol against multiple lifecycle stages of cultured Plasmodium falciparum, assessing asexual blood-stage, early- and late-stage gametocyte, and gamete development by light microscopy and mitochondrial membrane potential via JC-1 staining. The highest dose inhibited asexual blood-stage proliferation by 88%, early-stage gametocyte development by 82%, and stage V gametocyte maturation by about 90%. The effect was stronger on male than female gamete formation (81% vs 27% inhibition). The authors position ferulenol as a promising dual-target antimalarial that could disrupt both disease severity and transmission via its mitochondrial target.

articles.headline: Insecticide resistance management plans assessed in three African countries
articles.title_original: Managing insecticide resistance in malaria vectors in Africa: case studies from Cameroon, Côte d'Ivoire and Tanzania.
articles.authors: Tabue RN, Gbalegba CGN, Mwalimu CD, Tungu PK, Fodjo BK, Elanga-Ndille E, Menze B, Mosha J, Wolie RZ, Clarke SE, Lines J, Abeku TA
articles.details: This mixed-methods study evaluated national insecticide resistance management (IRM) plans for malaria vectors in Cameroon, Côte d'Ivoire, and mainland Tanzania, using document reviews, epidemiological and entomological data, and stakeholder discussions in 2023. Each country had a 5-year IRM plan aligned with national strategies and international guidelines, but the Côte d'Ivoire and mainland Tanzania plans were outdated. Implementation was hindered by inadequate domestic funding, heavy donor reliance, limited sentinel site coverage, and inactive monitoring sites. The authors recommend increased domestic financing, regular plan updates, and improved localized surveillance to sustain evidence-based vector control.

articles.headline: IL-2 levels show no consistent link to malaria or severity
articles.title_original: Association between interleukin-2 cytokine levels and Plasmodium infections: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Kwankaew P, Kotepui KU, Anabire NG, Wilairatana P, Kotepui M
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis synthesized 30 studies (from 3,023 records screened) reporting interleukin-2 (IL-2) levels in people with Plasmodium infection versus uninfected controls and across disease severity, using standardized mean differences (SMD) under a random-effects model. There was no significant difference in IL-2 between infected and uninfected individuals (SMD = 4.56, 95% CI [-3.16; 12.29], P = 0.25, I² = 98.6%, 1074 participants), nor between severe and non-severe malaria (SMD = 0.37, 95% CI [-0.91; 1.67], P = 0.57, I² = 97.4%, 694 participants). The authors conclude there is no consistent evidence that IL-2 is altered, though very high heterogeneity limits interpretation.

articles.headline: CNS malaria recovery shows prolonged coagulation and gene dysregulation
articles.title_original: Prolonged dysregulation of gene expression and biomarkers of coagulation, fibrinolysis, and endothelium in pediatric central nervous system malaria.
articles.authors: Dent AE, Rosa BA, Dobbs KR, Ogolla S, Midem D, Embury P, Skomorovska-Prokvolit Y, Hannon ER, Koech E, Schmaier AH, Otieno W, Mitreva M, Kazura JW
articles.details: This cohort study compared serial peripheral blood mononuclear cell transcriptomes (at 6 weeks) and plasma biomarkers (at 6 weeks and 6 months) during recovery in Kenyan children presenting with central nervous system malaria (CNS-M, n = 37), uncomplicated malaria (UM, n = 20), or acute febrile non-malaria illness (AFN, n = 12). Upregulated interleukin/cytokine signaling, neutrophil degranulation and innate immunity pathways were only partially reversed by 6 weeks; megakaryocyte/platelet production, IL-4/IL-13/IL-10 signaling and overexpression of LAG3, CCR5, PDGFRB and ICMT were CNS-M-specific, and the Hemostasis pathway was uniquely downregulated in CNS-M at 6 weeks. Biomarkers of coagulation, fibrinolysis, neutrophil activation and endothelial integrity, correlated with high cell-free DNA, persisted for 6 weeks in CNS-M but normalized in UM and AFN. The authors conclude CNS-M recovery involves prolonged dysregulation of coagulation, fibrinolysis and platelet activation.

articles.headline: Modeling urban malaria risk factors in Anopheles stephensi area, Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Modeling urban malaria infection in Anopheles stephensi hotspot area in Eastern Ethiopia: application of Structural Equation Modeling.
articles.authors: Merga H, Degefa T, Birhanu Z, Abiy E, Lee MC, Yan G, Yewhalaw D
articles.details: This facility-based cross-sectional study used Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) to assess direct and indirect effects of factors on urban malaria infection among 329 febrile patients visiting public health facilities in Dire Dawa city, Ethiopia, an area with the invasive vector Anopheles stephensi. Analysis used the lavaan package in R with diagonally weighted least squares estimation. Wealth index had a significant negative effect on insecticide-treated net (ITN) utilization (-0.66; p < 0.001) and on malaria knowledge (-0.63; p < 0.001); attitude positively affected ITN use (0.16; p = 0.049); and history of travel outside the city had a significant positive effect on infection (0.969; p = 0.01). Indirect effects via attitude/utilization were also significant. The authors conclude SEM effectively identifies these pathways and that urban-targeted interventions should be strengthened.

articles.headline: Review: silica nanoparticles to deliver sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine for malaria
articles.title_original: Advancing malaria treatment with magnetic and mesoporous silica nanoparticles loaded with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine: a review.
articles.authors: Egza TF, Kebede EM, Perumal RD
articles.details: This review examines magnetic and mesoporous silica nanoparticles as drug-delivery platforms for the antimalarial combination sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP), which faces reduced bioavailability, systemic toxicity, and resistance. It describes how a magnetic core enables targeted delivery via external magnetic fields while the mesoporous silica shell permits high drug loading and controlled release, and reviews advances in synthesis, surface functionalization, and encapsulation. The authors report that in vitro and in vivo studies have shown improved pharmacokinetics, reduced toxicity, and enhanced antimalarial efficacy of SP-loaded nanoparticles, particularly against resistant Plasmodium strains, while noting challenges of scalability, biocompatibility, and regulatory hurdles for clinical translation. No quantitative pooled results are provided.

articles.headline: New food-vacuole transporter mutations linked to artemisinin resistance
articles.title_original: Role of novel mutations in food vacuole transporters beyond K13-mediated artemisinin resistance in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Junaid IT, Panda A, Deshmukh A, Sardar R, Narwal M, Agrawal P, Prakash N, Akhtar A, Dey AK, Singh SS, Mridha S, Mochi J, Parveen S, Kumar M, Nagar R, Gaur N, Gupta D, Mohmmed A, Kaur I, Karmodiya K, Malhotra P
articles.details: This study isolated intact Plasmodium falciparum food vacuoles (FV), performed proteome analysis to identify FV transporters, and examined co-occurring mutations with known PfKelch13 and PfCRT polymorphisms using single-nucleotide polymorphism data from the Pf3K and MalariaGEN databases. Proteome analysis identified 16 transporter proteins; SNP analysis of 2,517 samples revealed co-association of six mutations in four transporter genes (PfCRT, PfNT1, PfCTR2, PfMDR2) with PfKelch13 polymorphisms (P < 0.0001). Functional complementation in Saccharomyces cerevisiae with wild-type and mutant proteins (PfNT1-F394L, PfMFR5-S278T, PfMFR5-Y570F) produced resistant phenotypes in the presence of dihydroartemisinin. The authors conclude additional parasite transporters contribute to chloroquine and artemisinin resistance evolution beyond K13.

articles.headline: ASHA workers follow malaria protocols but rarely first contact in North India
articles.title_original: Implementation Fidelity of Accredited Social Health Activist (ASHA) Workers in Malaria Case Management in a High-Burden District of North India.
articles.authors: Selvaraju E, Goel PK, Gour N, Kumar A
articles.details: A community-based cross-sectional study interviewed 440 mothers of under-five children and 30 ASHA workers in a high-burden district to assess implementation fidelity of community malaria case management across WHO-aligned domains. Half of children (222/440, 50.5%) had fever in the prior month, yet only 4 (1.8%) mothers first sought care from ASHAs (below the 80% benchmark, p<0.05) and only 9 (4.0%) were tested within 24 hours. Among 8 malaria-positive cases, ASHA treatment initiation and completion were 100% and follow-up 87.5%; though 92.1% of mothers knew mosquitoes transmit malaria, 73.6% preferred informal providers. Authors conclude ASHAs adhere well but are underutilized as first point of care.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic Plasmodium infections alter blood and immune markers in chimpanzees
articles.title_original: Hidden hematological, biochemical and immune costs of asymptomatic malaria infections in semi-wild chimpanzees.
articles.authors: Nowakowski A, Elguero E, Patterson C, Boissière A, Degrugillier F, Sidobre C, Arnathau C, Grentzinger P, Willaume E, Talman AM, Malleret B, Boundenga L, Ngoubangoye B, Prugnolle F, Wassmer SC, Rougeron V
articles.details: This study profiled hematological, biochemical, and immune effects of natural malaria infection in 27 semi-wild chimpanzees using MinION sequencing and species-specific PCR. Overall Plasmodium infection rate was 48.15%, with frequent multi-species co-infections (P. gaboni, P. reichenowi, and P. vivax-like); younger animals were more infected and had higher parasitemia, especially with triple infections involving P. vivax-like. Across 15 hematological markers and 8 cytokines/chemokines, infected animals showed elevated urea, reduced creatinine, and increased pro-inflammatory (TNF, IL-1β, CCL3) and anti-inflammatory (IL-10) cytokines, with higher IL-10 on ex vivo PBMC stimulation. Authors conclude chronic infections are not entirely benign and cause systemic immune modulation and physiological stress.

articles.headline: High genetic diversity in P. falciparum CSP vaccine antigen in Rwanda
articles.title_original: Genetic polymorphism of Plasmodium falciparum circumsporozoite protein in Kigali, Rwanda.
articles.authors: Fankem SN, Mbonimpa JB, Kalimba EM, Diallo MT, Souopgui J
articles.details: This study examined genetic diversity of the Plasmodium falciparum circumsporozoite protein (Pfcsp) gene in 245 clinical isolates from a referral hospital in Kigali, Rwanda, using PCR amplification and Oxford Nanopore sequencing (R10.4 chemistry). Forty-eight distinct haplotypes were found, indicating high haplotype diversity (Hd=0.8899) but moderate nucleotide diversity (π=0.00834), suggesting immune-driven balancing selection. The N-terminal region was highly conserved (including the KLKQP motif), while the central NANP/NVNP repeat region and the C-terminal Th2R/Th3R epitopes were highly polymorphic; fewer than 1% of sequences matched the 3D7 vaccine strain. Authors conclude genetic divergence of circulating csp variants could influence RTS,S/R21 vaccine performance and underscore the need for molecular surveillance.

articles.headline: eDelphi study ranks top research priorities for seasonal malaria chemoprevention
articles.title_original: Global medium-term research priority setting for seasonal malaria chemoprevention: results from an eDelphi study.
articles.authors: Richardson S, Steele A, Scott S, Ainsworth J, Eckert E, Tchouatieu AM, Sillah JS, Cuomo-Dannenburg G, Okereke E, Okeh DU, Tonks S, Ndiaye JL, Dentinger C, Rassi C, Baker K
articles.details: Using eDelphi methodology, the SMC Alliance ran an online consultation to identify medium-term research priorities for seasonal malaria chemoprevention with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine plus amodiaquine (SPAQ). Starting from 46 priorities compiled at a workshop, three survey waves (English, French, Portuguese) gathered 96 responses from 67 unique respondents in 20 countries, rating priorities on importance, feasibility, and extent already addressed. Of 25 priorities retained for ranking, the highest-ranked included evaluating the duration of prophylactic protection from SPAQ and other SMC medicines, and evaluating the effect of integrating SMC with other prevention interventions on antimalarial resistance. Authors conclude sustainability, resistance, integration, and delivery quality dominated priorities to guide future research and programme adaptation.

articles.headline: Knowledge gaps and incomplete IPTp limit malaria prevention in pregnant Ghanaians
articles.title_original: Assessing the knowledge, attitudes and practices towards malaria prevention and determinants of antenatal care utilization among pregnant women in Sekyere South district, Ghana; a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Zuuri CN, Gyamah DK, Buabeng L, Mensah R
articles.details: A cross-sectional survey of 422 pregnant women assessed knowledge, attitudes, and practices toward malaria prevention and determinants of antenatal care utilization, using structured questionnaires with descriptive statistics and logistic regression. Most respondents (78%) had adequate knowledge, but 56% wrongly believed malaria spreads person-to-person, only 48% knew the recommended ≥3 IPTp doses, and ITN use was 63% (non-use linked to heat and unavailability). Knowledge of IPTp dosage was significantly associated with uptake (p<0.001), while socio-demographic factors did not predict ANC attendance. Authors conclude prevention gaps stem from misconceptions and incomplete IPTp adherence rather than supply shortages, and recommend strengthening ANC counselling, correcting myths, and reminder prompts.

articles.headline: Conserved P. ovale wallikeri GAMA antigen shows strong immunogenicity
articles.title_original: Immunogenicity and gene polymorphism analysis of Plasmodium ovale wallikeri glycosylphosphatidylinositol-anchored micronemal antigen (GAMA).
articles.authors: Ling X, Chen F, Xu S, Tan Y, Sun Y, Han S, Wang Q, Cheng Y
articles.details: Researchers analyzed 56 Plasmodium ovale isolates imported from Africa (38 P. ovale wallikeri, 18 P. ovale curtisi) collected between 2012 and 2016, sequencing the powgama gene and assessing recombinant PowGAMA (amino acids 276–646) expressed in E. coli. The 38 wallikeri isolates showed 10 SNPs, nucleotide diversity (π) of 0–0.00435, and 13 haplotypes (Hd=0.812), with a conserved ~2.3 kb region. Immunized BALB/c mice mounted a Th1-polarized response with high IFN-γ, an endpoint antibody titer of 1:5,120,000, and 78.37% avidity. rPowGAMA detected natural infections with 83.3% sensitivity and 70% specificity, suggesting potential for protective immunity.

articles.headline: Temperature is main climate driver of malaria across African countries
articles.title_original: Climate change and malaria: an old enemy of Africa is back.
articles.authors: Filho WL, Gbaguidi GJ, Zuñiga RAA, Ibrahim U
articles.details: This study analyzed two decades of malaria incidence trends and climate influences across selected African countries using Pearson correlation, joinpoint regression, and linear regression. Mean temperature was the main climatic factor affecting transmission in Angola, Cameroon, Ivory Coast, Ghana, Mozambique, and Uganda; warmer temperatures generally raised malaria risk, except in Ghana where higher temperatures were linked to decreased risk. Precipitation showed a significant negative association with incidence in Burkina Faso and Uganda, indicating increased rainfall reduced transmission. The authors call for greater policymaker engagement to intensify malaria control in Sub-Saharan Africa.

articles.headline: High malaria–typhoid co-infection among febrile patients in Northern Ghana
articles.title_original: Malaria and typhoidal co-infection among febrile patients: a health facility-based cross-sectional study in Northern Ghana.
articles.authors: Dassah SD, Anati MI, Kulariba J, Alidu EA, Senoo DKJ, Adda RB, Agongo G, Motey GA, Amenga-Etego L, Abugri J
articles.details: A health facility-based cross-sectional study recruited 332 febrile patients across seven facilities in the Kassena Nankana Municipal and Nabdam Districts, Upper East Region, Ghana, during peak rainy seasons (July–November) of 2023 and 2024, using malaria antigen rapid tests and typhoid IgM serology. Prevalence of either or both infections was 60.0%, with malaria seroprevalence 37.7%, typhoid 36.7%, and co-infection 14.7%. Nabdam district had higher malaria odds (aOR=3.87; 95%CI 1.71–8.76; p<0.01), tertiary education raised typhoid odds (aOR=5.33), tap water reduced typhoid (aOR=0.36) and co-infection (aOR=0.07), and vomiting predicted malaria (aOR=2.45). Authors urge integrated febrile-illness diagnosis and treatment.

articles.headline: ASAQ and AL remain highly effective for uncomplicated malaria in Madagascar
articles.title_original: Efficacy and Safety of Artesunate + Amodiaquine and Artemether + Lumefantrine for the Treatment of Uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum Malaria in Madagascar, 2020.
articles.authors: Harimanana A, Randriamiarinjatovo DNAL, Rakotomanga TA, Irinantenaina J, Moriarty LF, Laird V, Patel D, Abdallah RA, Zhiyong Z, Svigel SS, Kapesa L, Razafindrakoto J, Ralinoro F, Rason MA, Andriamananjara MN, Razanatsiorimalala S, Razafinjato C, Randrianarivelojosia M
articles.details: A randomized parallel-group study at four health centers in Madagascar (2020) assessed artesunate+amodiaquine (ASAQ) versus artemether+lumefantrine (AL) in children aged 6 months to 14 years with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria, using the WHO protocol with 28-day follow-up. Of 765 enrolled, 709 (92.7%) reached the endpoint. PCR-corrected ACPR was 100% at all sites for ASAQ, and 97.6–100% for AL. Day 3 slide positivity was 0–1%, and adverse events (gastrointestinal issues, headache) occurred in 10.2% (72/709). No pfK13 mutations associated with artemisinin resistance were found among 727 samples, confirming both regimens remain safe and efficacious.

articles.headline: PCR reveals underestimated Trichuris in children in malaria-endemic Papua
articles.title_original: Molecular diagnosis of Trichuris trichiura: Prevalence and associated risk factors in children under five living in a malaria-endemic area in Papua, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Sukmana DJ, Kridaningsih TN, Mufidah H, Kenangalem E, Burdam FH, Susilawati TN, Poespoprodjo JR, Murhandarwati EEH
articles.details: A study of 181 children aged under five from a household survey across 16 villages in Timika, Papua, Indonesia (April–July 2013) compared Kato-Katz microscopy with qPCR for Trichuris trichiura. Prevalence was 13.8% (25/181) by microscopy versus 31.5% (57/181) by qPCR, a 17.7% increase (p=0.0001). Children over 3 years had triple the risk (aOR 3.29; 95%CI 1.42–7.63; p=0.006), and those with Hb ≤10 g/dL had higher risk (OR 2.46; p=0.020). Malaria prevalence was 9.9% (18/181), and malaria coinfection raised anaemia risk (OR 11.7; p=0.004). Authors recommend including soil-transmitted helminth prevention for young children in malaria-endemic areas.

articles.headline: Lupeol and betulin derivatives show potent in vitro antimalarial activity
articles.title_original: Hemisynthesis of lupeol and betulin derivatives and their in vitro antimalarial activity against Plasmodium falciparum FCR3.
articles.authors: Bafarawa ID, Abd Ghani MS, Ramadani AP, Fuadah S, Othman MBH, Ibrahim MN, Liew SY, Che Omar MT, A Wahab H, Azmi MN
articles.details: This in vitro study synthesized a series of lupeol and betulin derivatives and tested them against the Plasmodium falciparum FCR3 strain. Eight compounds, including lupenone, showed antiplasmodial potency with IC50 values of 0.03–9.9 µM; lupenone and derivatives 4a and 4b were most active (0.3 ± 0.3, 0.03 ± 0.05, and 0.7 ± 0.8 µM), compared with reference drugs artemisinin (0.7 ± 0.3 µM) and chloroquine (10.3 ± 2.9 µM). Molecular docking showed good affinity to the PfATP6 binding site (−9.8 to −8.4 kcal/mol). The authors conclude these compounds are promising leads for new antimalarial drug development.

articles.headline: Hydrogen peroxide propels hemozoin crystal motion in malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Chemical propulsion of hemozoin crystal motion in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Hastings EM, Skóra T, Carney KR, Fu HC, Bidone TC, Sigala PA
articles.journal: Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A
articles.details: This mechanistic laboratory study investigated the puzzling motion of hemozoin crystals inside the food vacuole of malaria parasites using quantitative image analysis, single-particle tracking, and Brownian dynamics simulations. Motion required the intact vacuole but not the parasite, was tightly confined yet much faster than thermal diffusion. Hydrogen peroxide, generated at high levels in the food vacuole, stimulated motion of isolated crystals, while suppressing peroxide formation slowed motion inside parasites. The authors conclude that surface-exposed metals on hemozoin catalyze peroxide decomposition to self-propel the crystals, likely reducing oxidative stress from hemoglobin digestion during blood-stage infection.

articles.headline: Artisanal mining communities show higher P. falciparum infection prevalence in Ghana
articles.title_original: Factors Associated With Plasmodium falciparum Infection Transmission in Artisanal Mining and Mining-Free Communities: The Case of Fanteakwa South District, Ghana.
articles.authors: Aninagyei E, Adedia D, Larbi G, Abbew GA, Tukwarlba I, Boatey CA, Sarfo-Bempong B, Acheampong DO
articles.details: Asymptomatic Plasmodium falciparum was significantly more common in artisanal mining communities than in a mining-free community in Fanteakwa South District, Ghana, where stagnant water created by mining promotes mosquito breeding. Using stratified sampling and nested PCR on dried blood spots from 341 participants (240 from mining communities, 101 from the mining-free community), overall asymptomatic prevalence was 53.1% and was higher in mining communities (64.6%) than in the mining-free community (27.7%, p<0.001). In mining areas, risk factors included staying outdoors at night for more than 2 hours (AOR=8.4, p<0.001), using nets from open markets (AOR=4.2), and net sharing (AOR=3.0). The authors call for targeted interventions such as quality-assured ITN distribution, behaviour change communication, and housing improvements.

articles.headline: EQ-5D value set choice strongly affects malaria quality-of-life estimates in children
articles.title_original: Determining health-related quality of life among children with malaria in Kenya: does the choice of EQ-5D value set matter?
articles.authors: Okech T, Thavorncharoensap M, Chaikledkaew U, Omondi GP, Nyokabi P, Thumbi SM, Thakkinstian A
articles.details: This cross-sectional study in western Kenya had caregivers complete the proxy-reported EQ-5D-5L questionnaire to assess health-related quality of life in children with malaria, comparing Ethiopian, Ugandan, and Dutch value sets. Of 95 children, 45 had uncomplicated and 50 had severe malaria, with pain/discomfort and usual activity most affected. Median utility for uncomplicated malaria was 0.82 (Ethiopian), 0.59 (Ugandan), and 0.73 (Dutch); for severe malaria it was lower at 0.30, −0.13, and 0.09 respectively, with significant differences across value sets. Vaccination status and malaria severity were significant determinants. The authors stress the need for a Kenyan-specific value set.

articles.headline: Vector and larval habitat data support larval source management in Kebbi, Nigeria
articles.title_original: Utilization of vector bionomics, remote sensing and larval habitat characterization data to inform larval source management decision in the high malaria burden state of Kebbi, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Oduola AO, Inyama PU, Samdi LM, Bunza MA, Bagudu A, Okeke I, Yina G, Esema M, Ntadom G, Ambrose K, Mihigo J, Yoshimizu M, Seyoum A
articles.details: This entomological study analyzed routine data across six monitoring sites in Nigeria, focusing on Kebbi State, and used GIS with satellite imagery to identify and characterize larval habitats in Argungu, Bunza, and Kalgo LGAs. Anopheles gambiae s.l. was the predominant vector (13,201; 100%) in Kebbi, with the highest human biting rates indoors and outdoors in dry (15.2, 8.7 b/p/n) and rainy (42.2, 14.3 b/p/n) seasons, and an 83% protection gap. Of 635 potential habitats, 460 (97.9%) were positive for Anopheles larvae, mostly rice fields. The authors conclude larval source management is warranted in Kebbi State.

articles.headline: Correction notice for Plasmodium pitheci mitochondrial genome paper
articles.title_original: Correction: The mitochondrial genome sequence of the Bornean orang-utan (Pongo pygmaeus) malaria parasite, Plasmodium pitheci.
articles.authors: Dharmayanthi AB, Pratama R, Sánchez KL, Nurillah A, Fadiah F, Komara, Nugraha RTP, Saputra S, Nurkanto A, Fahroni A, Irwanto A, Syafruddin D, Adnan IA, Saragih JR, Coutrier FN, Setiadi W, Lobo NF
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Multiple causes of death found in children with cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Cause-specific mortality in a cohort of paediatric cerebral malaria patients.
articles.authors: Wynkoop HJ, Bvalani-Kaunda R, Seydel KB, Moxon CA, Chimalizeni Y, Taylor TE, Birbeck GL, Postels DG, O'Brien NF
articles.details: A retrospective review of 393 children with cerebral malaria enrolled in interventional clinical trials at the Blantyre Malaria Project/Queen Elizabeth Central Hospital categorized the immediate cause of death for those who died. Of the cohort, 52 (13%) died; cerebral herniation was the most common immediate cause (46%), followed by status epilepticus with apnea (17%), while non-neurologic causes accounted for 37% (cardiac failure/shock 13%, respiratory failure 12%, renal failure 6%, hepatic failure 4%, other 2%). Lower admission coma score was linked to death from cerebral herniation or hepatic failure (p=0.001). The authors conclude that diverse causes of death may dilute the measured impact of brain-focused therapies and that future trial sample-size estimates may need adjustment.

articles.headline: Illegal mining drives surge in malaria among Yanomami people
articles.title_original: Mining and malaria in the Brazilian Amazon and in the Yanomami indigenous land.
articles.authors: Castro MC, Arisco NJ, Diniz CG, Ponmattam J, Peterka C, Basta PC, Ferreira MU
articles.details: This study examined temporal and spatial patterns of malaria and mining in Brazilian Amazon indigenous lands and quantified mining's impact on malaria among the Yanomami following expansion of illegal mining since 2018. A 1% increase in annual mining area was associated with a 24% (95% CrI: 17%, 32%) increase in monthly malaria cases among the Yanomami. The authors estimate malaria cases in 2022 were underreported by 83%, and that an excess of 102,870 malaria cases occurred from 2018 to 2023 due to mining (an added public health cost of about US$6.9 million), calling for intensified malaria control.

articles.headline: RDTs miss P. vivax in Indian febrile-patient malaria survey
articles.title_original: A cross-sectional survey of Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax in India using rapid diagnostic test and microscopy across 12 sites of varying transmission, 2023-2024.
articles.authors: Dahal P, Singh-Phulgenda S, Nema S, Tripathi PK, Malla WA, Sinha DP, Mohanty A, Chalageri VH, Shrinivasa BM, Singh SP, Singh PK, Singh K, Ranjha R, Baharia RK, Chhajed R, Nain M, Das A, Kumar RS, Gupta A, Rahi M, Flegg JA, Nitika N, Dhorda M, Anvikar AR, Guérin PJ, Bharti PK
articles.details: A cross-sectional study tested 10,290 febrile participants for malaria using rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) and microscopy across 12 sites of varying endemicity in India. RDT identified 1,516 cases (14.7%, 95% CI 7.7–21.8%) and microscopy 1,436 (14.0%); of RDT positives, 1,105 (72.9%) were P. falciparum mono-infection, 290 (19.1%) P. vivax mono-infection, and 121 (8.0%) mixed P. falciparum/P. vivax. RDT sensitivity/specificity were 95.0%/99% for P. falciparum, 83%/100% for P. vivax, and 88%/100% for mixed infections; 43 (0.4%) RDT-negative participants were positive by microscopy. The authors conclude low RDT sensitivity for P. vivax underscores an urgent need for better diagnostics.

articles.headline: Raman 2D-COS spectroscopy analyzes malaria parasitemia in red cells
articles.title_original: Two-dimensional correlation spectroscopy (2D-COS) in the analysis of parasitemia in Raman spectra of red blood cells of patients diagnosed with malaria.
articles.authors: Migdalski M, Czepiel J, Moskal P, Birczyńska-Zych M, Kucharska M, Biesiada G, Stokłosa J, Bociąga-Jasik M, Wesełucha-Birczyńska A
articles.details: This study used two-dimensional correlation spectroscopy (2D-COS) on Raman spectra of red blood cells from patients infected with Plasmodium falciparum at different parasitemia levels. A 442 nm laser line excited heme resonance, producing synchronous auto-peaks from ν4 and ν37 deoxy-heme marker vibrations at 1354 cm⁻¹ and 1580 cm⁻¹ (with cross-peaks characteristic of hemozoin), while a 785 nm line added information on proteins and lipids of infected erythrocytes. The authors discuss the potential of using the relative Raman intensity ratio I1370/I1354 (oxy- vs deoxy-heme) to correlate with malarial parasitemia levels in blood samples.

articles.headline: Review finds integrated vector management beats single methods for malaria control
articles.title_original: Integrated vector management for malaria control: a review of approaches and effectiveness.
articles.authors: Otolorin GR, Castellanos ME, Adegboye OA, McBryde ES
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: This review of 14 studies published between 2009 and 2024 found that integrated vector management (IVM) consistently outperformed single interventions for malaria control across different regions worldwide, drawing on the Web of Science, PubMed and Google/Google Scholar databases. The studies used observational, quasi-experimental and cluster-randomized controlled trial designs, with outcomes including malaria incidence, vector density, parasite prevalence, entomological inoculation rate and human biting rates; six reported statistically significant reductions in transmission (p<0.05). Longitudinal studies from Uganda, Ethiopia and Nigeria showed sustained reductions in malaria cases and vector populations, while large-scale programs in China and India illustrated long-term success. The authors conclude that IVM, combining traditional and emerging tools, offers a sustainable path toward global malaria control and eradication.

articles.headline: Commentary on the emerging threat of zoonotic malaria in India
articles.title_original: Tackling the Emerging Threat of Zoonotic Malaria in India.
articles.authors: Divyaparvathy J, Ramesh H
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Cross-species anti-CelTOS antibodies block Plasmodium liver stage and transmission
articles.title_original: [Cross-specific anti-CelTOS antibodies for different Plasmodium species inhibiting the liver stage and transmission to the vector].
articles.authors: Varloteau A, Fouquoire L, Ravier L
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Researchers characterize an endogenous cysteine protease inhibitor in P. vivax
articles.title_original: Partial characterization of a cysteine protease inhibitor of Plasmodium vivax.
articles.authors: Võ TC, Kang JM, Lê HG, Na BK
articles.details: This laboratory study identified and biochemically characterized an endogenous cysteine protease inhibitor of Plasmodium vivax (PvICP), as cysteine proteases are recognized antimalarial drug targets. PvICP shared highly similar structural features with orthologs from other Plasmodium species. Recombinant PvICP expressed in Escherichia coli showed broad inhibitory activity against falcipain-family cysteine proteases, including vivapain-3, vivapain-4, falcipain-3, malapain-2 and malapain-4, with more potent activity against vivapain-3 and vivapain-4. Inhibitory activity was not significantly affected by pH. The authors conclude these findings provide groundwork for exploring PvICP's biological role and potential as a therapeutic target.

articles.headline: Climate change expands suitable habitat for African malaria mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Climate Change Favors African Malaria Vector Mosquitoes.
articles.authors: van der Deure T, Nogués-Bravo D, Njotto LL, Stensgaard AS
articles.details: Using an extensive mosquito observation dataset, species distribution modeling, and climate and land-use data, this study estimated future human exposure to six dominant African malaria vector species across sub-Saharan Africa. Projections identified three species likely to substantially expand their suitable habitat: Anopheles gambiae, Anopheles coluzzii and Anopheles nili s.l. Combining these projections with human population density, the authors conservatively estimate approximately 200 million additional people could live in areas highly suitable for these three vectors by the end of the century, with new exposure hotspots in Central and East Africa. They conclude climate impacts on vectors are highly species-specific and that malaria control must adapt to shifting vector distributions.

articles.headline: Mosquito vector diversity shapes malaria prevalence depending on environment
articles.title_original: Vector diversity and malaria prevalence: global trends and local determinants.
articles.authors: Hoi AG, Gilbert B, Mideo N
articles.details: Using curated open-access datasets, researchers analyzed the relationship between mosquito vector species richness, malaria prevalence, and environmental attributes such as temperature. Globally, the association between vector species richness and malaria prevalence differed by latitude, indicating strong dependence on environmental conditions. In Africa, the environment exerted a top-down influence by shaping vector communities, whereas in Southeast Asia prevalence depended on more complex interactions between the physical and socioeconomic environment (rainfall and GDP) and vector diversity. The authors conclude vector diversity helps structure disease distribution.

articles.headline: Two HSP90 inhibitors share binding site but differ in resistance
articles.title_original: Shared Binding Site but Divergent Resistance Profiles Uncover Novel Resistance Mechanisms in Plasmodium HSP90 Inhibitors.
articles.authors: Ko FH, Lukens AK, de Souza ML, Foy A, Hsiao J, Ndiaye T, Okombo J, Yeo T, Park H, Uhlemann AC, Boonyalai N, Kumpornsin K, Girling G, Pasaje C, Godoy L, Ottilie S, Durst GL, Chatterjee A, Niles J, Lee M, Fidock DA, Wirth DF, Winzeler EA
articles.details: In P. falciparum (and yeast), two HSP90 inhibitors targeting the identical ATP-binding site showed divergent resistance profiles. Geldanamycin readily selected 10 distinct resistance mutations conferring up to 22-fold resistance, distributed throughout the binding pocket, while AUY-922 required 44 weeks to yield a single A41S mutation giving only 2-fold resistance to AUY-922 but not to geldanamycin. Unexpectedly, A41S enhanced AUY-922 binding affinity yet still conferred resistance, while hypersensitizing yeast to all inhibitors. The authors propose AUY-922's lower resistance risk stems from engaging multiple HSP90 family members, showing resistance risk cannot be predicted from binding-site identity alone.

articles.headline: Optimized low-volume protocol enriches Plasmodium RNA for transcriptomics
articles.title_original: Low-volume Plasmodium blood sample processing protocols for untargeted transcriptomics optimized using Plasmodium knowlesi.
articles.authors: Sauve E, Kattenberg JH, Moris P, Guetens P, Monsieurs P, Rosanas-Urgell A
articles.details: Using a Plasmodium knowlesi culture diluted in whole blood as a mock P. vivax natural infection, researchers compared white blood cell (WBC), rRNA, and globin depletion methods plus RNA-seq library preparation kits to optimize low-volume, low-parasite-density sample processing. WBC depletion increased mapped Plasmodium paired-end reads from 5.4% to 37.2–54.5% depending on method, and globin/rRNA depletion decreased combined human globin and rRNA gene counts by up to 90%. The total RNA library kit was dominated by P. knowlesi rRNA despite the highest mapped read percentage. The authors conclude combining WBC and globin depletion with an mRNA library kit best optimizes parasite sequencing output.

articles.headline: Historical essay on early rural malaria control in Palestine, 1922
articles.title_original: Why and when successful rural malaria control became a local problem - Palestine 1922.
articles.authors: Alexander A
articles.details: This historical perspective examines the first sustainable rural malaria control programme launched in Palestine more than 100 years ago, framed against recent events including the demise of USAID, recognition of a State of Palestine, and the 7 October 2023 attack by Hamas. The author discusses how the Zionists confronted obstacles of a similar nature when initiating the programme. No quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: New class of malaria treatment shows promise against drug resistance
articles.title_original: First new type of malaria treatment in decades shows promise against drug resistance.
articles.authors: Lenharo M
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: AI DeepSeek algorithm identifies malaria parasites in blood smears
articles.title_original: A Case of Plasmodium Identification in Peripheral Blood Smears based on the DeepSeek Intelligent Algorithm.
articles.authors: Zheng Z, Lu M
articles.details: This case report applied the DeepSeek AI model to analyze Plasmodium parasites in peripheral blood smears from a patient with imported malaria, comparing it against traditional smear microscopy and Plasmodium genetic testing. DeepSeek successfully identified the parasites, including different developmental stages, and completed image analysis in a few minutes versus the hours required by experienced microscopists. The authors conclude the tool offers high precision and efficiency, and propose an 'AI primary screening + manual review' model to support grassroots medical institutions in controlling imported malaria.

articles.headline: Weaker selection in sporozoite-stage genes of P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Heterogeneous constraint and adaptation across the malaria parasite life cycle.
articles.authors: Perkins SA, Neafsey DE, Early AM
articles.details: This population-genetics study examined how Plasmodium falciparum's multi-stage life cycle shapes evolution, categorizing genes by their stage of expression and comparing between- and within-species diversity across stages. The authors argue that stage-specific differences in cell ploidy, clonal competition, and transmission bottlenecks alter the drift-selection balance. They found signals of weaker negative selection in genes expressed exclusively in sporozoites, consistent with sporozoites not proliferating, showing limited clonal competition, and passing through a strong bottleneck. They discuss how timing therapeutic interventions to particular life stages might affect the rate at which parasites evolve resistance.

articles.headline: Avian malaria parasite responds to any mosquito bite, not just vectors
articles.title_original: Plastic responses of Plasmodium relictum to competent and non-competent mosquito bites.
articles.authors: Chauvin V, Berthomieu A, Loiseau C, N Tchana C, Angelier F, Rivero A
articles.details: Researchers experimentally infected birds with the avian malaria parasite Plasmodium relictum and exposed them to bites from a competent vector (Culex quinquefasciatus) or a non-competent species (Anopheles gambiae) to test whether parasites respond specifically to competent vectors. Both exposures raised parasitaemia, but the response was significantly stronger to the non-competent species, while host inflammatory, humoral, and stress markers were similar across treatments. No species-specific change in gametocyte conversion rate or sex ratio occurred. The authors conclude P. relictum does not discriminate between mosquito species, instead using a generalist, possibly bet-hedging strategy, highlighting the role of mosquito community composition in transmission.

articles.headline: Profile of Fitsum Tadesse and malaria research in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Fitsum Tadesse: shaping malaria research in sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Prasad A
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Anopheles aquasalis scale phenotypes affect P. vivax parasite load
articles.title_original: Phenotypic variation and vector competence in Anopheles aquasalis: A comparative analysis of susceptibility to Plasmodium vivax.
articles.authors: Rodrigues NB, Santana RA, Carvalho BFDS, Martinez EG, Bruce AD, Justiniano SCB, da Silva ÁC, Nacif-Pimenta R, Monteiro WM, de Melo GC, Secundino NFC, Pimenta PFP
articles.details: This laboratory study compared vector competence for Plasmodium vivax between two phenotypes of Anopheles aquasalis—30BS (30% black scales) and 50BS (50% black scales)—using membrane feeding assays with blood from P. vivax-infected patients in Manaus, Brazil, across five independent infections. Among 286 midguts examined (139 from 50BS, 147 from 30BS), prevalences were 67.63% for 50BS and 55.10% for 30BS, with no significant difference (IRR=1.11; 95% CI 0.67–1.84; p=0.68). However, infection intensity was significantly higher in 30BS mosquitoes (mean 72.91 oocysts vs 56.94; p=0.042). The authors conclude phenotypic variation influences susceptibility, with 30BS mosquitoes more permissive to parasite development.

articles.headline: Italian network reports on severe malaria care in non-endemic setting
articles.title_original: The Italian network for severe malaria treatment (NOMAL): epidemiology, clinical presentation, management and outcomes in a non-endemic setting.
articles.authors: Bartoli TA, Corpolongo A, D'Abramo A, Angheben A, Bertoli G, de Gaetano Donati K, Cauda R, Antinori S, Attard L, Madeddu G, Tomasoni LR, Pallotto C, Vulcano A, Maffongelli G, Zammarchi L, d'Ettorre G, Navarra A, Girardi E, D'Alessandro U, Nicastri E
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Chemical adjuvants boost whole-sporozoite malaria vaccine immunogenicity in lab tests
articles.title_original: A Trojan horse: Chemical boosting of Plasmodium falciparum whole sporozoite vaccine immunogenicity.
articles.authors: Naar CM, van Schuijlenburg R, van Meerbeek M, Almeida L, van Leeuwen FWB, Roestenberg M
articles.details: In a laboratory study, researchers chemically adjuvanted Plasmodium falciparum sporozoites (SPZ) with two positively charged dye-adjuvant complexes (Cy5-CL307 and Cy5-MDP) that accumulate in SPZ via mitochondrial potential, aiming to boost whole-sporozoite vaccine potency. On average 90% of SPZ were adjuvanted while remaining viable (84% vs 71%), motile (78.7% vs 83.6%), and as infective as controls. SPZCL307 (but not SPZMDP) increased macrophage activation markers (CD80, CD25, IL-6, IL-8, TNF, IL-1β, IL-10; p values 0.018–0.043) and raised CD137 expression by 18% (p=0.01) in circumsporozoite-specific memory CD8+ T cells, with a 1.6-fold perforin trend (p=0.064). Authors conclude SPZ can be adjuvanted without harming viability or infectivity, warranting further optimization.

articles.headline: CDC port stations deliver most emergency malaria/antitoxin drugs within 8 hours
articles.title_original: A Review of Malaria, Diphtheria, and Botulism Medication Distributions by CDC Port Health Stations From May 2006 to February 2024.
articles.authors: Rubin L, Fowler J, Ranga S, Kirley M, Tchan J, Pacheco M, Preston LE, Waltenburg MA, Akinkugbe O, Kinzer MH, Alvarado-Ramy F, Brown C, Gertz AM
articles.details: This analysis queried CDC port health station (PHS) records of distributions of biologic agents not commercially available in the US—botulism and diphtheria antitoxins and, until 2022, intravenous artesunate for severe malaria—from May 2006 to February 2024, using descriptive and multivariate analyses of time from release authorization to delivery. CDC PHS made 2912 shipments via 17 distribution sites to 54 US jurisdictions and 3 countries; of 850 shipments with available data (December 2019 or later), 83% (702/850) arrived within 8 hours of authorization. Commercial air transport (aOR 11.43, 95% CI 6.94–18.81, P<.001) and authorization outside routine work hours (aOR 5.10, 95% CI 3.31–7.86, P<.001) were significantly associated with delays. Authors conclude staffing, distribution locations, and expanded ground transport options could minimize delays.

articles.headline: Adults and teens carry high P. falciparum burden despite child chemoprevention
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum carriage in a population under long-term, intensive malaria control in Kedougou region, Senegal: a 1-year cohort study.
articles.authors: Legendre E, Ciré Ba EK, L'Ollivier C, Cissoko M, Katile A, Mehadji M, Serre P, Sokhna C, Ranque S, Danfakha F, Bendiane MK, Sagara I, Gaudart J, Landier J
articles.details: This 1-year open cohort study in Kedougou region, Senegal, enrolled all individuals older than 6 months from randomly selected households in four villages, detecting Plasmodium falciparum by quantitative PCR on capillary dried blood across four dry- and wet-season surveys to assess subclinical infection under intensive malaria control including seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC). Among 763 participants from 69 households (followed April 13, 2021 to March 30, 2022), prevalence was lowest in SMC-eligible children aged under 10 (below 10%); during the wet season prevalence rose in those aged 15–24 (32.1%) and 35–49 (24.7%). Clinical burden was highest at ages 10–14 (527 cases per 1000 person-years) and 15–19 (631 per 1000), over five-fold higher than children aged 6 months–4 years (93 per 1000). Outdoor night-time activity was associated with infection. Authors conclude elimination efforts must target the parasite reservoir, especially young adults.

articles.headline: Malaria burden in a region under long-term chemoprevention
articles.title_original: Malaria burden in a region under long-term chemoprevention.
articles.authors: Sow D, Wotodjo AN
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: DHA-piperaquine stays effective for imported African P. falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Effectiveness of dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine for treating Plasmodium falciparum malaria from sub-Saharan Africa: a retrospective study.
articles.authors: Ciminelli F, Buonfrate D, Mazzi C, Neumayr A, Camprubí-Ferrer D, Gobbi F
articles.details: A retrospective observational study in a single centre in Italy reviewed records of patients treated with a three-day regimen of dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DHA-PPQ) for imported uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria; 89 of 90 patients (after excluding one infected in Yemen) were infected in sub-Saharan Africa. Baseline median parasitaemia was 0.136% (IQR 0.029-0.750). On day 3, only 6 patients (6.7%) still had circulating parasites, with no early treatment failures, and follow-up (63 patients) excluded late parasitological failures. No significant trend in day-3 positivity was seen across study periods (p=0.339), indicating no decline in DHA-PPQ effectiveness over time.

articles.headline: Lab brain-vessel model shows P. falciparum cuts pericyte Ang-1
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum impairs Ang-1 secretion by pericytes in a 3D brain microvessel model.
articles.authors: Long RKM, Korbmacher F, Ronchi P, Fleckenstein H, Schorb M, Mirza W, Mallorquí M, Aguilar R, Moncunill G, Schwab Y, Bernabeu M
articles.details: Researchers engineered a human 3D microfluidics-based brain microvessel model containing primary brain microvascular endothelial cells and pericytes to study the angiopoietin-Tie axis implicated in cerebral malaria, where patients show elevated Ang-2 and reduced Ang-1. Exposure to P. falciparum-infected red blood cell egress products decreased Ang-1 secretion, increased vascular permeability, and caused minor pericyte ultrastructural changes. Barrier disruption was partially reversed by pre-treatment with recombinant Ang-1 and the Tie-2 activator AKB-9778. The authors suggest a mechanistic role for pericytes in cerebral malaria and potential for angiopoietin-Tie-targeting therapeutics.

articles.headline: Review: malaria and microgravity as models of aging red blood cells
articles.title_original: Exploring erythropoiesis: aging through the lens of malaria and microgravity.
articles.authors: Foucambert P, Hang JW, Malleret B
articles.details: This review examines erythropoiesis (red blood cell production) and major age-related changes in stem cells, transcription factors, and regulators such as erythropoietin and iron. It discusses mechanisms driving the high prevalence of anemia in the elderly—chronic disease, sepsis, and unexplained anemia—along with current therapies and management gaps. The authors propose two underexplored models for studying aging RBCs: malaria and microgravity, arguing that Plasmodium infection and spaceflight mirror key features of age-related erythropoietic dysfunction and RBC morphological changes.

articles.headline: Systematic review: nanoparticle delivery for blood-stage malaria drugs and vaccines
articles.title_original: Nanotechnology-based delivery strategies for drugs and vaccines targeting blood stage malaria: A systematic review.
articles.authors: Amos Y, Rubaka C, Sauli E, Swai H, Tumbo A
articles.details: This systematic review synthesized evidence on nanotechnology-based delivery systems aimed at improving targeting, antigen/drug stability, and therapeutic outcomes against the blood stage of malaria, where Plasmodium parasites invade red blood cells. Forty studies from 2005 to 2025 were analyzed, covering lipid, polymeric, inorganic, and protein-based nanoparticles. Functionalized nanocarriers with ligands targeting infected red blood cells enhanced drug efficacy and reduced systemic toxicity, while nanoparticle antigen carriers elicited strong immune responses, achieving up to 83.3% survival in in vivo preclinical models. The authors note persisting challenges in scalable production, clinical translation, and regulatory approval.

articles.headline: Seasonal Anopheles biting and infection persist in urban Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Influence of the dry and humid seasons on the assessment of human exposure to malaria vector bites in Bobo-Dioulasso, Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Amara MF, Kouadio KWU, Konaté H, Yao RK, Kouamé BNA, Karabinta A, Diabaté A, Gnankiné O, Namountougou M
articles.details: This entomological study assessed human exposure to malaria vector bites in urban Bobo-Dioulasso, using Human Landing Catches (HLC) and Pyrethrum Spray Catches (PSC) along riverbanks across seasons from 2023 to 2024. Of 1455 mosquitoes collected (922 by HLC, 533 by PSC), three An. gambiae complex species were identified: An. arabiensis (91.20%), An. coluzzii (5.09%), and An. gambiae (3.71%, absent in the dry season). P. falciparum infections were detected year-round, with 0.16% of mosquitoes carrying dual P. falciparum/P. malariae infections in July. Blood meals were predominantly mixed (59.63%), then zoophilic (24.41%) and anthropophilic (15.96%), and Entomological Inoculation Rates rose in the humid season, underscoring persistent residual transmission.

articles.headline: New avian malaria parasite Plasmodium aramidis described in wood rails
articles.title_original: Hidden in plain sight: Morphological and molecular description of Plasmodium aramidis n. sp. in blood and tissues from Brazilian Wood Rails.
articles.authors: Vieira LMC, Santos DOD, Pacheco MA, Escalante AA, Oliveira AR, Braga ÉM
articles.details: This taxonomic study describes Plasmodium aramidis n. sp., a new avian haemosporidian of the ARACAJ01 cytb lineage, identified in five Grey-necked Wood Rails (Aramides cajaneus) rescued from the wild in Southeastern Brazil. The authors provide morphological data, a near-complete mitochondrial genome phylogeny, and histopathology. The parasite appears pathogenic, causing pulmonary lesions (edema, hemorrhage) and hepatic hemosiderosis in all individuals analyzed; exoerythrocytic meronts were seen in lung, heart, and skeletal muscle in two of three individuals examined. The authors conclude it is a common parasite of A. cajaneus needing further host and pathogenicity study.

articles.headline: Anopheles diversity and microclimate in Indonesian highland rice fields
articles.title_original: Ecological diversity of Anopheline mosquitoes in highland agroecosystems in relation to microclimate dynamics: implications for malaria control.
articles.authors: Arisanti M, Yahya Y, Suryaningtyas NH, Sukendra DM, Maha MS, Gunawan G, Isnawati R, Ernawan B, Nurcahyo W, Kaliannagounder K, Pangaribuan HU
articles.details: This ecological survey examined the diversity, distribution, and habitat preferences of Anopheles mosquitoes in rice-field landscapes of Banding Agung District, South Sumatra, Indonesia. Adult mosquitoes were collected monthly from April to September using human landing catch, animal-baited traps, and resting collections, alongside larval surveys across eight aquatic habitat types with environmental measurements. Of 6899 mosquitoes collected, Culex spp. dominated (56.6%) and Anopheles spp. comprised 15.6%, with An. vagus most abundant. PCA showed pH and illumination positively influenced several Anopheles species. Human biting rate peaked at ~23.5 °C and >88% relative humidity (R²=0.656); animal biting rate models were weak (R²=0.081). The authors stress integrating ecological and climatic factors into vector surveillance.

articles.headline: Atovaquone-proguanil fully arrests P. falciparum sporozoites in vaccine trial
articles.title_original: Complete attenuation of Plasmodium falciparum sporozoites by atovaquone-proguanil.
articles.authors: Borrmann S, Sulyok Z, Müller K, Fendel R, Sulyok M, Friesen J, Lalremruata A, Sandri TL, Nguyen TT, Calle CL, Knoblich A, Sefried S, Ibáñez J, Lorenz FR, Heimann HL, Weller DM, Steuder R, Adukpo S, Granados Bayon P, Molnár Z, Esen M, Metzger W, James ER, Ruben A, Abebe Y, Chakravarty S, Manoj A, Kc N, Murshedkar T, Hafalla JCR, Woldearegai TG, O'Rourke F, Held J, Billingsley P, Sim BKL, Richie TL, Hoffman SL, Kremsner PG, Matuschewski K, Mordmüller B
articles.details: This study tested chemo-attenuation (CVac) of Plasmodium falciparum sporozoites with atovaquone-proguanil (AP) to improve vaccine safety. In mice, P. berghei sporozoites given with AP arrested early in hepatocytes and induced protection linked to parasite-specific effector-memory CD8+ T cells. In a clinical trial of PfSPZ-CVac (AP), three doses of 5.12×10⁴ or 1.5×10⁵ PfSPZ were given by direct venous inoculation with oral single-dose AP (1000/400 mg), fully preventing blood-stage infection during immunization. Only 2/8 and 2/10 vaccinees were protected on challenge with 3.2×10³ PfSPZ 10 weeks later, inferior to chloroquine-based CVac. Protection with the chloroquine regimen was associated with antibodies to LISP2, LSA1, and PfMSP5, but not PfCSP. The authors suggest single-dose AP could reduce chemoprophylaxis dosing frequency.

articles.headline: Review: defining the ideal pre-erythrocytic malaria vaccine
articles.title_original: Better late than never: defining the ideal vaccine that targets pre-erythrocytic malaria infection.
articles.authors: Rochfort Peters GM, Baum J
articles.details: The licensure of the first two subunit malaria vaccines—RTS,S/AS01 (Mosquirix, GSK) and R21/Matrix-M (Serum Institute of India), which received full WHO prequalification in 2022 and 2023 respectively—marks a landmark in efforts to develop a much-needed malaria vaccine. Both require a primary series of four doses, given over a 12–18-month period, to confer maximum protection to children against P. falciparum, eliciting a predominantly B-cell response (with a modest CD4+ T-cell response) against the circumsporozoite protein (CSP) to block the sporozoite from colonising the liver. The review notes that the level and durability of protection remain uncertain, especially in infants and in areas of perennial, high-intensity transmission, and argues this benchmark has bolstered efforts to find complementary or improved strategies, with over half a million children under 5 still dying each year from malaria.

articles.headline: Risk factors for malaria deaths in Brazil: delay, age, indigenous communities
articles.title_original: Towards Malaria Elimination: A Nationwide Case-Control Study to Assess Risk Factors for Severe Malaria-Related Deaths in Brazil.
articles.authors: Garcia KKS, Laporta GZ, Duarte EC, Soremekun S, Abrahão AA, da Silva AC, de Pina Costa A, Guimarães Lacerda MV, Drakeley C, Ramalho WM, Siqueira AM
articles.details: This nationwide case-control study used Brazil's Ministry of Health data (2011–2020) to assess risk factors for malaria-related deaths (ICD-10 B50–B54), linking epidemiological records (Sivep-Malaria, Sinan) with mortality records (SIM) and applying logistic regression. Cases were malaria deaths; controls were non-fatal malaria cases. Of 632 malaria-related deaths, 454 (71.8%) occurred in the Amazon region and 178 (28.1%) outside it. In the Amazon, risk factors included age (<6 months or >60 years; p≤0.01), delayed treatment >48h (p<0.001), illiteracy (p=0.01), and living in indigenous villages (p<0.001), while active case detection was protective (p=0.01). In the Extra-Amazon, risk factors included delayed treatment (p=0.049), P. falciparum or mixed infections (p<0.049), foreign-acquired infections (p=0.01), and higher education (p=0.03). The authors conclude deaths concentrate in socially vulnerable groups and that timely diagnosis and active surveillance are key to Brazil's 2030 zero-malaria-death target.

articles.headline: Plasmodium CSP repeats act as elastic spring guiding sporozoite motility
articles.title_original: The Plasmodium CSP repeats have elastic properties with a critical role in sporozoite motility.
articles.authors: Balaban AE, Kanatani S, Mitra J, Gregory J, Vartak N, Sinnis-Bourozikas A, Frischknecht F, Ha T, Sinnis P
articles.details: This laboratory study examined the central repeat region of the Plasmodium circumsporozoite protein (CSP) on the sporozoite surface. Sporozoite mutants with truncated or scrambled CSP repeats showed impaired motility caused by altered formation and dynamics of TRAP-containing adhesion sites. Using single-molecule fluorescence-force spectroscopy, the authors found that CSP repeats behave like a stiff, linear spring whose elastic properties depend on repeat length and are lost when the repeats are scrambled. They conclude the repeat region has a biophysical, functional role in parasite infection and motility.

articles.headline: Preventive treatment cuts malaria in HIV-positive pregnant women, not birth outcomes
articles.title_original: Intermittent preventive treatment regimens for malaria in HIV-positive pregnant women: Systematic review and meta-analysis of clinical trials.
articles.authors: Elgadi A, Noorallah T, Abdelazim E, Altraifi S, Altraifi H, Elhaj A, Elnaiem W, Hemmeda L
articles.details: This PRISMA systematic review and meta-analysis pooled 13 randomized clinical trials with 5,226 HIV-positive pregnant women receiving intermittent preventive treatment (IPT) for malaria. IPT reduced odds of malaria during pregnancy or at delivery by 55% (OR=0.45, 95% CI 0.36–0.56, P≤0.001), with IPTp-dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine most effective; it cut placental malaria by 48% (OR=0.52, 95% CI 0.39–0.70) and maternal anemia by 18% (OR=0.82, 95% CI 0.69–0.97, P=0.03). However, it showed no significant effect on low birth weight, fetal loss, stillbirth, or preterm labor. Authors conclude IPT reduces maternal complications but not adverse birth outcomes.

articles.headline: Imaging flow cytometry detects malaria-infected erythroblasts in spleen and marrow
articles.title_original: Quantification and visualization of malaria-infected erythroblasts by imaging flow cytometry.
articles.authors: Imai T, Miyazaki S, Miyazaki Y, Kagaya W, Nakashima M, Ito K, Sase M, Yoshioka-Takeda M, Shimokawa C, Hayashi K, Araki T, Annoura T, Itokawa K, Komagata O, Ha NT, Shimo A, Murakami T, Hisaeda H
articles.details: This study used imaging flow cytometry (ImageStream) to identify and quantify parasitized erythroblasts (pEBs) in mice infected with GFP-expressing Plasmodium yoelii 17XNL, compared with conventional FACS. At 14 days post-infection, splenic, bone marrow, and peripheral blood cells were stained for TER119, MHC class I, and Hoechst 33342. No pEBs were detected in peripheral blood, but significant populations appeared in spleen and bone marrow, with ImageStream frequencies closely matching FACS. The method also revealed doublets/triplets suggesting erythroblastic islands and blebbing indicating apoptosis-like processes, validating image-based detection of infected erythroblasts.

articles.headline: Review of AI deep-learning methods for diagnosing Plasmodium knowlesi malaria
articles.title_original: Insights into AI-Driven malaria diagnosis: A systematic review with implications for Plasmodium knowlesi.
articles.authors: Tiong JY, Hasikin K, Ngui R, Divis PCS, Loo CK, Lai KW, Cheong FW, Wan Sulaiman WY
articles.details: This systematic review evaluates artificial intelligence approaches for malaria blood-stage diagnosis, focusing on the zoonotic species Plasmodium knowlesi. It critically assesses datasets, preprocessing methods, and deep learning techniques including convolutional neural networks, transfer learning, ensemble learning, and object detection models such as YOLO and Faster R-CNN for blood-stage identification. The authors note that research on AI-based classification of P. knowlesi blood stages remains limited and highlight persistent challenges including limited annotated datasets, class imbalance, and interpretability concerns. They conclude that improved dataset curation, domain adaptation, and explainable AI will be crucial for advancing P. knowlesi diagnostics.

articles.headline: Twenty-year geospatial analysis maps shifting malaria hotspots in Kenya
articles.title_original: A multidimensional space-time geospatial analysis for examining the spatial trends of vector-borne diseases: 20 years of malaria in Kenya.
articles.authors: Blanford JI, Kioko C
articles.details: This study applied multidimensional space-time pattern mining to 20 years (2000–2020) of Plasmodium falciparum incidence, mortality, and prevalence data plus bed net and antimalarial use data from the Malaria Atlas Project for Kenya, analyzed over the full period and at 10-year intervals. Local and emerging hotspot analyses identified hotspots, coldspots, and outliers and tracked their directional shifts. Malaria prevalence generally declined, with a slight increase between 2016–2018; four key malaria zones and two additional increasing areas were identified, while bed net and antimalarial use rose over time. Authors conclude malaria persists mainly in cross-border regions and that these methods help refine elimination strategies.

articles.headline: Hemoglobin-degradation genes conserved in Indian P. vivax isolates
articles.title_original: Characterization of genes involved in hemoglobin degradation in Plasmodium vivax isolates from Chennai, India, and species of non-human primate malaria.
articles.authors: Shalini S, Mishra N, Verma S, Kale S, Mallick PK, Prajapati SK, Kumar A, Srivastava N, Joshi H, Eapen A, Singh OP
articles.details: This molecular study characterized three food-vacuole enzymes—plasmepsin IV (PM_IV), heme detoxification protein (HDP), and falcilysin (FLN)—in Plasmodium vivax, analyzing genomic DNA from 30 clinical isolates from India plus three chloroquine-resistant reference strains using homology modelling. Several nonsynonymous mutations were found, including Val165Ile in PM_IV, Asn513Asp and Gly676Asp in FLN, and a novel in-frame duplication c.28_33dup in HDP, but structural modelling showed these did not affect the enzymes' active binding sites. The authors conclude the genes are highly conserved, supporting their essential roles in parasite survival and potential as drug targets, and report these as the first findings from the Indian subcontinent offering insights into chloroquine action and resistance.

articles.headline: Review of malaria epidemiology and re-emergence risk in Spain and Europe
articles.title_original: Historical evolution of the epidemiology of Malaria in Spain and Europe. Future perspectives.
articles.authors: Marañés Antoñanzas I, López Gallardo Y, García de Oro Á, Gutiérrez González C, Peyró Sánchez R, Sánchez Martínez S
articles.journal: Eur J Clin Microbiol Infect Dis
articles.details: This review examined the historical and current epidemiology of malaria in Spain and Europe, drawing on English- and Spanish-language medical articles, official guides, and WHO and ECDC reports. Malaria was officially eradicated in Spain in 1964 and in Europe in the 1970s through health programs, drug therapy, insecticide spraying, and environmental engineering, but the disease is now mainly imported by international travelers and migrants, with autochthonous transmission in Europe being extremely infrequent due to early diagnosis and treatment. The authors conclude that although disease potential in Europe is currently low, warming climatic conditions and continued migration could favor re-emergence, so disease awareness, surveillance, and vector control must be strengthened.

articles.headline: NLRP1 variant protective against gestational malaria via inflammasome pathway
articles.title_original: Impact of NLRP1 Met1154Val and IL1B variants on gestational malaria: an unexplored role of NLRP1 in inflammasome activation by Plasmodium spp.
articles.authors: Leal VN, Ribeiro JA, Marra LG, Rio AT, Reis EC, Wunderlich G, Dombrowski JG, Marinho CR, Pontillo A
articles.details: This association study in a cohort of pregnant women from a malaria-endemic region in northern Brazil tested whether functional single-nucleotide variants in inflammasome genes affect response to Plasmodium infection and development of placental malaria. The NLRP1 p.Met1154Val variant was associated with protection against Plasmodium infection and correlated with a healthier placental state, while IL1B variants appeared more prevalent in severe cases. The authors also showed for the first time that infected red blood cells induce NLRP1- and caspase-1-dependent pyroptosis in BeWo trophoblast cells, identifying a novel inflammasome pathway in gestational malaria pathogenesis and suggesting NLRP1 is an under-explored inflammasome receptor in malaria.

articles.headline: Human malaria RDTs detect simian malaria at higher parasite densities
articles.title_original: Can malaria rapid diagnostic tests be used to detect simian malaria?
articles.authors: Madmanee W, Day NPJ, White NJ, Dondorp AM, Malaivijitnond S, Imwong M
articles.details: This diagnostic evaluation tested 14 commercial human malaria rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) detecting PfHRP2 and either pLDH or aldolase on 131 blood samples from non-human primates with confirmed malaria, plus 30 macaque samples without malaria as controls, using qPCR as reference. Infecting species included P. cynomolgi (73.2 parasites/μL), P. inui (272.8 parasites/μL), and P. coatneyi (158.4 parasites/μL). Overall RDT sensitivity ranged from 34.2% to 94.3% across brands and exceeded 90% in the best-performing test when parasite density exceeded 200 parasites/μL; pLDH- and aldolase-based tests performed similarly, sensitivity was highest for P. inui, no false positives occurred in controls, but PfHRP2 false positives appeared in 1–17% of RDTs among infected samples. The authors conclude RDT performance varies by brand, species, and density, with good detection above 200 parasites/μL.

articles.headline: Wolbachia found in Iranian Anopheles dthali, suggesting biocontrol potential
articles.title_original: Wolbachia Infection in Iranian Malaria Vectors: Prevalence and Biocontrol Implications.
articles.authors: Saeedi S, Karimian F, Moosa-Kazemi SH, Nejati J, Bavani MM, Koosha M, Choubdar N, Khosravi G, Oshaghi MA
articles.details: This survey assessed Wolbachia infection in 1094 Iranian mosquito specimens collected from nine provinces (2016–2019), covering seven malaria vectors, four non-malaria Anopheles species, and three Culex species, using PCR targeting eight genes. Wolbachia DNA was detected exclusively in Anopheles dthali and Culex species, with infection rates of 73.4% in An. dthali and 77.78%–96.77% in Culex, notably higher in males, and present in all regions except one in the north. Phylogenetic analysis placed the strains in supergroup B, closely related to strains in An. moucheti and An. demeilloni. The authors conclude the high Wolbachia prevalence in An. dthali is promising for malaria prevention, recommending future work to confirm cytoplasmic incompatibility and transmission-blocking potential.

articles.headline: Red cell sugar C1GALT1 found essential for P. falciparum invasion
articles.title_original: Sialyl-T Antigen: A Novel Red Blood Cell Determinant for Plasmodium falciparum Invasion.
articles.authors: Mikdar M, Shabani E, Grüring C, Chaand M, Kanjee U, Goldberg JM, Azouzi S, Tennessen JA, Elsworth B, Keutcha C, Barteneva NS, Doench JG, Duraisingh MT
articles.details: Using RNA interference screening in erythroid cells plus CRISPR/Cas9 knockout in primary human hematopoietic stem cells, researchers identified the glycosyltransferase C1GALT1 as a novel host determinant for Plasmodium falciparum red blood cell invasion. Studies of C1GALT1-deficient reticulocytes and glycophorin A/B-null MkMk RBCs showed that C1GALT1-dependent α(2-3) sialic acid structures within mucin-type O-glycans are crucial for efficient invasion by both sialic acid-dependent and sialic acid-independent P. falciparum strains, but not by Plasmodium knowlesi. Different P. falciparum strains showed variable dependence on distinct sialic acid configurations, highlighting a key role for RBC glycans in malaria.

articles.headline: NOX2-driven oxidative stress causes cardiac hypercontractility in malaria mice
articles.title_original: NOX2-induced oxidative stress triggers cardiac hypercontractility during severe malaria.
articles.authors: de Oliveira KCM, Silva AO, Bruno AS, Ito HN, Queiroz-Junior CM, Berg BB, Castor MGME, Machado FS, Ceron CS, Teixeira MM, Cau S
articles.details: In male Balb/c mice infected with Plasmodium chabaudi, researchers tracked heart function and cardiac redox balance over the course of infection, using apocynin and NOX2 knockout (NOX2-/-) mice to test the role of NOX2. Isolated Langendorff hearts from infected mice showed higher contraction indexes at baseline and under isoprenaline stimulation, alongside increased lipid peroxidation (TBARS) and reduced superoxide dismutase activity. Apocynin treatment (30 mg/kg/day) and NOX2 deletion reversed the cardiac hypercontractility without affecting parasitemia, and apocynin also reduced cardiac oxidative stress and pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1β and IL-6. The authors conclude NOX2 induces hyperdynamic hearts via local oxidative stress and inflammation.

articles.headline: Monocyte dysregulation marks symptomatic and asymptomatic P. vivax malaria
articles.title_original: Systems approach identifies monocyte imbalance in symptomatic and asymptomatic P. vivax malaria.
articles.authors: Studniberg SI, Bafit M, Ioannidis LJ, Worley MJ, Trianty L, Utami RAS, Puspitasari AM, Apriyanti D, Coutrier FN, Poespoprodjo JR, Kenangalem E, Andries B, Prayoga P, Price RN, Noviyanti R, Garnham AL, Hansen DS
articles.details: Using a systems approach integrating transcriptional profiling with mass cytometry phenotypes from individuals with symptomatic and asymptomatic Plasmodium vivax malaria, researchers examined immune responses. Symptomatic P. vivax malaria featured upregulation of anti-inflammatory pathways and checkpoint receptors, profound downregulation of monocyte-function transcripts, and significant depletion of CCR2+CXCR4+ classical monocytes. Asymptomatic infections also showed dysregulation of genes associated with monocyte activation and the inflammasome. The authors identify monocyte dysregulation as a key feature of the P. vivax response and argue asymptomatic infection is not innocuous, potentially failing to support immune processes needed to clear parasitemia or respond to vaccination.

articles.headline: WHO reference serum harmonises competition ELISA for anti-malaria antibodies
