articles.title_original: Evaluation of 1(st) WHO anti-malaria reference reagent for competition ELISA harmonisation and development of ADAMSEL analytical platform.
articles.authors: Khatri B, Riese P, Shkarlet H, Mortier D, McShane H, Bowyer PW, Remarque E
articles.details: This study harmonised the competition ELISA (cELISA) assay for Plasmodium falciparum across three laboratories using the 1st WHO reference reagent, the anti-malaria human reference serum 10/198, which served as an internal standard for calculating IgG concentrations against the Apical Membrane Antigen 1 (AMA1). Because AMA1 sequence diversity and cross-reactivity complicate antibody assessment, the cELISA was used to examine cross-reactive responses against different variants. A novel semi-automated analytical tool was developed in the R-statistics environment and made freely available. The authors demonstrate the reference reagent's effectiveness as a standard and present the ADAMSEL software and R-platform as accessible, no-cost analysis tools.

articles.headline: Simple phenolic compounds inhibit P. falciparum calcium pump PfATP6
articles.title_original: Small phenolic inhibitors of PfATP6, a Plasmodium falciparum calcium ATPase, as prototype antimalarials.
articles.authors: Sobota S, Devaraja S, Chung M, Arevalo J, Martinez-Aguirre J, Makeyenko J, Kellen-Yuen C, Eastman S, Derbyshire E, Saade C, Dieudonné T, Paula S
articles.details: Researchers characterized a small library of structurally simple phenolic compounds featuring hydroquinone and naphthoquinone scaffolds as candidate inhibitors of PfATP6, a Plasmodium falciparum calcium-transporting ATPase, using blood-stage parasite viability and PfATP6 activity inhibition assays. Several compounds inhibited parasite growth, including di-alkylated hydroquinones, a naphthoquinone disulfide, and the disinfectant hexachlorophene, and some also inhibited the purified enzyme, though the correlation between the two activities was not absolute. Molecular interactions were analyzed by homology modeling, ligand docking, and molecular dynamics, revealing hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions as main binding contributors. The authors present this as a first step toward designing improved small-molecule PfATP6 inhibitors.

articles.headline: Genetic screen finds P. falciparum factors for surviving sickle-trait cells
articles.title_original: Phenotypic screens reveal Plasmodium falciparum genetic factors associated with infection of sickle-trait cells.
articles.authors: Pires CV, Oberstaller J, Wang C, Gibbons J, Micchelli C, Zhang M, Otto TD, Rayner JC, Taylor S, Adams JH
articles.details: This in vitro study used a piggyBac transposon-mutant library of P. falciparum, culturing parasites in sickle cell trait (HbAS) versus control (HbAA) red blood cells and assessing growth by Quantitative Insertion Site Sequencing (QIseq) to identify mutants sensitive or tolerant to the HbAS environment. Mutants sensitive to growth in HbAS cells were associated with genes for signaling pathways, exported proteins, and host-interaction genes, which overlapped with genes involved in oxidative stress response and antimalarial drug sensitivity (artemisinin derivatives and proteasome inhibitor). The authors conclude these factors link sickle-trait infection to parasite stress responses including heat-shock, artemisinin, and oxidative stress.

articles.headline: CYP2D6 metabolizer variants and P. vivax relapse risk in Colombia
articles.title_original: Polymorphisms of the CYP2D6 gene and its relationship with Plasmodium vivax relapses after chloroquine-primaquine treatment in Turbo, Colombia.
articles.authors: Sierra-Cifuentes V, Zuluaga-Idárraga L, Aguirre-Acevedo D, Silva de Barros Puça MC, Nobrega de Sousa T, Lopera-Mesa TM
articles.details: This exploratory study determined CYP2D6 phenotypes and genotypes in 71 of 78 P. vivax patients on supervised chloroquine-primaquine treatment, analyzing nine polymorphisms plus copy number variation and exploring associations with relapse over six months and primaquine plasma levels. Most diplotypes (81.7%) corresponded to normal or ultrarapid metabolizers and 18.3% to poor/intermediate metabolizers; median day-2 plasma primaquine was higher in the impaired-activity group than normal metabolizers (660.4 vs 313.5 ng/ml; effect size r −0.51, 95% CI −0.82 to 0.03). No significant difference in relapse hazard was found between impaired and normal activity (adjusted HR 1.45; 95% CI 0.39–5.39). The authors call for further research.

articles.headline: Review compares metabolic footprints of Plasmodium and Babesia in red cells
articles.title_original: Shared host, distinct invaders: metabolomic footprints of plasmodium and babesia in host red cells.
articles.authors: Beri D, Rodriguez M, Lobo CA
articles.details: This review compares the metabolomic signatures of Plasmodium- and Babesia-infected red blood cells, both transfusion-transmitted parasites sharing the same host cell. Shared hallmarks include enhanced glycolytic rate and lipid scavenging from RBC membranes, along with perturbed sugars, amino acids, lipids, redox homeostasis, and heme utilization. Key differences relate to hemoglobin breakdown and the use of resulting amino acids for parasite propagation. The authors argue that understanding these metabolic similarities and differences will aid identification of new biomarkers and specific targeted therapies.

articles.headline: Uncertainty-guided attention network detects malaria parasites in thick smears
articles.title_original: Uncertainty-guided attention learning for malaria parasite detection in thick blood smears.
articles.authors: Xiong H, Wang Z, Sharan RV, Berkovsky S
articles.details: This work proposes an uncertainty-guided attention learning network for detecting malaria parasites in thick blood smear images, using a pixel attention mechanism to capture fine-grained features. Channel-wise uncertainty is estimated via a Bayesian channel attention reformulation, so features from high-uncertainty channels (treated as unreliable) are restrictively used to guide pixel attention learning. Compared against several state-of-the-art baselines on two public datasets using parasite-level and patient-level evaluation, the method showed superior performance on most metrics, notably achieving the highest average precision (AP) scores in both parasite- and patient-level scenarios.

articles.headline: Narrative review of AI opportunities and risks in African malaria control
articles.title_original: Artificial intelligence in African malaria control programs: opportunities and risks.
articles.authors: Obeagu EI, Ogenyi FC
articles.details: This narrative review synthesizes current applications of artificial intelligence in Sub-Saharan African malaria control programs, covering surveillance, outbreak prediction, diagnostics, treatment optimization, and resource allocation. It highlights case studies including Kenya's drone-assisted vector surveillance, Nigeria's mobile health platforms for real-time case reporting, and Tanzania's climate-informed forecasting models, plus AI-driven bioinformatics for Plasmodium genomic surveillance and vector-parasite molecular network analyses. The authors critically examine challenges such as data quality, ethics, infrastructure limitations, and potential inequities, providing guidance for policymakers and researchers seeking to leverage AI toward malaria elimination.

articles.headline: Dissolving microneedle patches deliver amodiaquine-artesunate through skin
articles.title_original: Polymeric microarray patches for transdermal delivery of amodiaquine and artesunate: A novel strategy against Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Anjani QK, Volpe-Zanuto F, Miatmoko A, Moreno-Castellanos N, Novais JT, Gaitán XA, Hariawan BS, Cahyani DM, Octavia RT, Mubarok AS, Wahyu Setyaningsih WA, Johnston C, Bargieri DY, Donnelly RF
articles.details: This study developed dissolving microarray patches (MAPs) for transdermal delivery of the ACT pair amodiaquine and artesunate, using Tween® 80 to boost solubility and skin permeability. In vitro, up to 40% of amodiaquine and 90% of artesunate were deposited and permeated into skin. Pharmacokinetics showed MAP delivery extended artesunate's half-life 8.5-fold (54.05 vs 6.37 h) and raised its Cmax by 21% (1796.02 vs 1421.93 ng/mL), and prolonged amodiaquine's half-life ~1.4-fold (57.72 vs 40.75 h). In a Plasmodium berghei-infected murine model, combined MAP treatment cut parasitaemia by 99.5% within seven days, comparable to oral dosing.

articles.headline: Age-structured model projects combined vaccine and net malaria impact
articles.title_original: An age-structured mathematical model to assess the combined effects of vaccine and non-vaccine interventions on malaria transmission and burden.
articles.authors: Glèlè Kakaï R, Djomatin LC
articles.details: Researchers built an age-structured mathematical model to assess combined effects of malaria vaccination (RTS,S and R21) with non-vaccine interventions on transmission and burden. The model integrates demographic parameters, long-lasting insecticide-treated nets, access to treatment, vaccine efficacy profiles, and waning immunity to simulate disease burden across age cohorts and disability-adjusted life years (DALYs) under different vaccination scenarios. It was calibrated using national demographic data for a low-transmission setting (Ethiopia) and a high-transmission setting (Nigeria). Preliminary findings suggest integrating vaccination with existing control strategies could significantly reduce malaria incidence and mortality over time, informing vaccination strategies and policy.

articles.headline: PSSP17 essential for mosquito transmission, proposed as RDT biomarker
articles.title_original: Evaluation of Plasmodium falciparum PSSP17 essentiality in human-to-mosquito transmission and its prospective candidacy as a biomarker.
articles.authors: López-Gutiérrez B, Hathaway N, Alonso-Palomares L, Nyasembe VO, Kato M, Hamerly T, Parr JB, Juliano JJ, Harbers M, Dinglasan RR
articles.details: To address pfhrp2/3-deletion strains that evade current PfHRP2-based rapid diagnostic tests, this study evaluated Plasmodium falciparum sexual stage protein 17 (PSSP17; PF3D7_1218800)—previously found in saliva of children with subclinical infections—as a candidate biomarker. The authors show pssp17 is dispensable in asexual stages but important for onward transmission by mosquitoes. Analysis of 19,280 publicly available P. falciparum genomes revealed no naturally occurring deletions. They conclude that should Δpssp17 mutants arise, mosquitoes cannot effectively transmit them, supporting further consideration of PSSP17 for non-invasive, saliva-based sensitive RDTs.

articles.headline: Plasma cell-free DNA test diagnoses vivax malaria after negative smears
articles.title_original: Travel-Related Malaria Diagnosis on Karius Test Despite Negative Blood Smear.
articles.authors: Weigold JE, Lal S, Youssef DA
articles.details: This case report describes a 59-year-old man who developed fever, rash, and myalgia after returning from the Amazon rainforest, with leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, transaminitis, and hyperbilirubinemia but negative malaria smears. A Karius test—a plasma-based microbial cell-free DNA sequencing assay—detected Plasmodium vivax and established the diagnosis. The patient required several regimens for recurrent attacks, including chloroquine and primaquine, artemether-lumefantrine, and finally quinine plus doxycycline with a prolonged primaquine course, after which symptoms and laboratory values normalized. The case highlights limitations of smear microscopy in P. vivax, the role of molecular diagnostics, and the importance of hypnozoite clearance to prevent relapse.

articles.headline: Cameroon survey finds high COVID, moderate malaria antibody seroprevalence
articles.title_original: Seroprevalence of anti-SARS-CoV-2 and anti-Plasmodium falciparum and vivax antibodies in urban and rural zones in the West Region of Cameroon.
articles.authors: Kamga RMN, Ajim NC, Soubgwi PF, Noyes H, MacLeod A, Simo G, Adamson WE
articles.details: This cross-sectional study estimated anti-SARS-CoV-2 and anti-Plasmodium antibody seroprevalence in urban (Bafoussam) and rural (Fongo-Tongo) areas of the West Region of Cameroon during April–May 2022, using rapid diagnostic tests on finger-puncture whole blood from 805 participants. Overall SARS-CoV-2 seroprevalence was 60.1% (urban 66.6%, rural 54.0%). Overall anti-Plasmodium seroprevalence was 22.2%, higher rurally (31.4%) than urban (14.8%); 21% was attributed to P. falciparum and 2.11% to P. vivax. A generalised linear binomial model found no association between anti-SARS-CoV-2 and anti-Plasmodium antibodies overall, though SARS-CoV-2 IgM was positively associated with Plasmodium antibodies.

articles.headline: Contact exposure to ivermectin kills malaria mosquitoes and blocks parasites
articles.title_original: Contact exposure to ivermectin induces acute mortality and inhibits parasite development in malaria vectors.
articles.authors: Jeena M, Goyal B, Lata S, Kumar G, Pradhan LK, Thakur Y, Kaur G, Verma V, Pandey KC, Pasi S, Kaur J
articles.details: Laboratory study testing ivermectin as a contact toxin against Anopheles stephensi and An. culicifacies via topical and bottle bioassays, plus Plasmodium berghei infection studies in An. stephensi to assess transmission blocking. Ivermectin caused 100% mortality in both species within 24-48 h at higher doses. At 48 h, topical LD50 values were 0.017 ng/mg (An. stephensi) and 0.002 ng/mg (An. culicifacies); bottle LD50 values were 4.245 and 1.768 µg/bottle respectively. Contact exposure reduced oocyst numbers by 71% at 0.01 µM. Authors conclude ivermectin is an effective contact mosquitocidal and transmission-blocking vector-control tool.

articles.headline: Gut bacterial dysbiosis in pediatric severe malaria linked to post-discharge death
articles.title_original: Gut bacterial dysbiosis in pediatric severe malaria associates with post-discharge mortality.
articles.authors: Bednarski OJ, Lehman SB, Mzinza D, Kazinga C, Namazzi R, Opoka RO, Ren J, Tran TM, Taylor TE, Seydel KB, John CC, Conroy AL, Schmidt NW
articles.details: Analysis of gut bacteria in stool samples from children with severe malaria and community children across two African studies. Children with severe malaria showed gut dysbiosis with increased Enterobacteriaceae abundance that associated with multiple clinical complications of severe malaria. Increased abundance of Escherichia coli predicted post-discharge mortality. Metagenome analysis identified elevated metabolic pathways and genes supporting utilization of host-derived molecules that could promote Enterobacteriaceae survival and growth. Authors suggest treatments targeting Enterobacteriaceae may reduce post-discharge mortality in children with severe malaria.

articles.headline: MALDI-TOF MS could cut malaria vector surveillance costs by 83%
articles.title_original: MALDI-TOF MS for malaria vector surveillance: A cost-comparison analysis using a decision-tree approach.
articles.authors: Karisa J, Rist C, Tuwei M, Ominde K, Bartilol B, Ondieki Z, Musani H, Wanjiku C, Mwangangi J, Mbogo C, Rono M, Bejon P, Maia M
articles.details: Cost-comparison analysis using a deterministic decision-tree model comparing current molecular methods versus MALDI-TOF MS for malaria vector surveillance, using the Kenyan national malaria program as a case study and assuming an annual sample size of 15,000 mosquitoes processed at a local laboratory in Kenya. Switching entirely to MALDI-TOF MS was estimated to yield a 74.48% net time saving, up to 84% savings on material costs, 77% on labour costs, and an overall 83% direct cost saving. Authors conclude adoption could bring substantial time and cost savings to routine entomological surveillance in Africa.

articles.headline: P. vivax relapse risk falls as transmission declines in Thailand
articles.title_original: Plasmodium vivax Malaria Relapse Risk Depends on Transmission Intensity: Evidence From a Longitudinal Study in Northwest Thailand.
articles.authors: Chu CS, Lwin KM, Burgoine K, Phyo AP, Turner C, San T, Aung AA, Win HH, Htoo K, Soe NL, Christina N, Than HH, Dah N, Pilaseng K, Wiladpaingern J, Proux S, Mukaka M, Parker DM, Carrara VI, Nosten F, White NJ
articles.details: Prospective observational rolling cohort study in northwest Thailand enrolling healthy nonpregnant G6PD-normal volunteers with symptomatic P. vivax infection in the previous 12-24 months who had not received radical cure; participants received supervised primaquine 0.5 mg base/kg/day for 14 days and were followed until P. vivax recurrence, maintaining a continuous cohort of 200. From March 2010 to September 2014, 380 adults and children enrolled; 92 developed P. vivax malaria, 25 within 4 months. Annual incidence fell from 0.19 (2010) to 0.09 infections per person-year (2014), and the primaquine failure rate was 75% lower than predicted. Authors conclude declining transmission reduces the hypnozoite reservoir, increasing apparent radical-cure efficacy in pre-elimination settings.

articles.headline: Host kinase NUAK1 is a target against P. falciparum in red cells
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum exploits NUAK1 to establish infection in human erythrocytes.
articles.authors: Navarrete DJ, Kim CY, McLelland K, Ramirez MG, Baro B, Salinas ND, Tolia NH, Doerig C, Ong SE, Golkowski MG, Egan ES
articles.details: Laboratory study identifying the AMPK-related kinase NUAK1 as a critical host factor for Plasmodium falciparum in human erythrocytes. NUAK1 was present in erythrocytes and showed increased phosphorylation in infected cells. Two selective NUAK1 inhibitors, HTH-01-015 and WZ4003, inhibited P. falciparum growth throughout the asexual life cycle, including erythrocyte invasion, with chemoproteomic profiling confirming selectivity for human NUAK1; inhibitors reduced phosphorylation of the NUAK1 substrate MYPT1, and genetic overexpression of NUAK1 partially rescued the signaling and invasion phenotypes. Authors conclude the NUAK1 pathway is a vulnerable target for host-directed malaria control.

articles.headline: Review: PfEMP1 antibodies and protection against cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Breaking the bind: PfEMP1-specific antibodies in cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Banda JP, Walker IS, Nyirenda T, Aitken EH, Rogerson SJ
articles.details: This review synthesizes seroepidemiological, functional, and mechanistic evidence on antibodies against Plasmodium falciparum erythrocyte membrane protein 1 (PfEMP1) on infected erythrocytes in cerebral malaria (CM). Antibodies to EPCR-binding CIDRα1 and DBLβ domains associated with dual EPCR and ICAM1 binding correlate with reduced CM risk, while responses to rosetting-associated domains (DBLα, CIDRγ) are less characterized. The authors describe antibody kinetics (early, durable responses to Group A variants versus delayed, transient responses to Groups B and C), effector mechanisms (opsonic phagocytosis, complement activation, Fc glycosylation), and methodological challenges, and call for epitope mapping, longitudinal cohorts, and broadly inhibitory monoclonal antibodies for vaccine and therapeutic design.

articles.headline: Deep-learning YOLOv3 tool detects P. falciparum on blood smears with 94% accuracy
articles.title_original: Rapid and accurate recognition of erythrocytic stage parasites of Plasmodium falciparum via a deep learning-based YOLOv3 platform.
articles.authors: He W, Zhu H, Geng J, Zhu D, Wu K, Xie L, Li J, Yang H
articles.details: This study developed and evaluated a YOLOv3 deep-learning object-detection tool to recognize erythrocytic-stage Plasmodium falciparum on thin blood film smears. Peripheral blood from malaria patients was smeared, stained, and microscopically examined, with manual confirmation and qPCR validation; images of infected red blood cells were captured and cropped via a sliding window into 20 small images (518 × 486 pixels). Across 262 tested images, the model detected 358 P. falciparum-containing infected RBCs with a 1.68% false negative rate (6 missed) and 3.91% false positive rate (14 misreported), giving 94.41% overall recognition accuracy. The authors conclude the tool offers feasible support for malaria control in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Anopheles coluzzii drives most P. falciparum transmission in Benin
articles.title_original: Spatial and temporal distribution of four malaria vector species and their relative contributions to Plasmodium falciparum transmission along the south-north transect of Benin, West Africa.
articles.authors: Akpodji S, Agbangla C, Padonou GG, Affolabi ZK, Koukpo ZC, Adoha C, Hougbe SZ, Sominahouin A, Tokponnon F, Osse RA, Oussou O, Adjottin B, Odjo EM, Yovogan B, Azondékon R, Salako A, Akogbéto M
articles.details: To update malaria vector distribution and transmission roles, mosquitoes were collected across 18 communes in Benin (two villages each) using human landing catches and pyrethrum spray catches. Female Anopheles gambiae s.l. and An. funestus were tested for P. falciparum circumsporozoite antigen by ELISA/CSP, identified to species by PCR, and sporozoite load quantified by real-time PCR, combining sporozoite load with the entomological inoculation rate (EIR). Anopheles coluzzii accounted for 63.01% of transmission (EIR 87.7 infective bites/person/year), An. gambiae for 36.7% (EIR 51.1), and An. funestus 0.24%. About 30% of An. gambiae carried over 10,000 sporozoites versus under 10% of An. coluzzii. Linking sporozoite load to EIR clarifies vectors' true transmission contribution.

articles.headline: Coustani Group mosquitoes appear minor malaria vectors in DR Congo
articles.title_original: Coustani Group mosquito (Diptera: Culicidae) diversity and malaria transmission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo.
articles.authors: Hendershot AL, Wat'senga FT, Mampangulu T, Lodi O, Nsungu IT, Manzambi EZ, Okito A, Nzila P, Aubourg MA, Yu X, Likwela J, Fornadel C, Oxborough RM, Lobo NF, Irish SR
articles.details: This study examined Coustani Group mosquito diversity, bionomics, and role in malaria transmission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo using field and molecular approaches. Human landing collections and pyrethrum spray catches were conducted in Lodja, Kapolowe, and once in Kenge; mosquitoes were morphologically identified, tested for Plasmodium sporozoites by ELISA and PCR, and identified by ITS2 and CO1 sequencing. Anopheles paludis (morphologically identified) carried no sporozoites in Lodja and only one specimen in Kapolowe. Sequencing revealed two species groups in Lodja and four in Kapolowe, with differing biting times. The authors conclude these mosquitoes did not appear to be major vectors given low sporozoite detection, though species misidentification complicates this, and further work is needed.

articles.headline: Evaluating Thailand's 1-3-7 reactive malaria surveillance for elimination
articles.title_original: Evaluating Thailand's malaria reactive surveillance and response strategies for malaria elimination: a mixed-method study.
articles.authors: Oo WH, Roobsoong W, Aye Tun N, Khant KM, Htike W, Kengganpanich M, Agius PA, Sattabongkot J, Fowkes FJI
articles.details: This cross-sectional mixed-method study assessed implementation of Thailand's 1-3-7 reactive surveillance and response (RASR) strategy (case notification within one day, investigation within three days, investigation survey within seven days) in Tak, Songkhla, and Yala provinces between January and April 2023. It combined quantitative surveys of malaria programme stakeholders (n=33) and frontline malaria service providers (n=41) with focus group discussions and in-depth interviews. About half of participants (40/74, 54.1%) perceived cases were notified within one day; nearly all (73/74, 98.7%) reported investigation and response activities met timeframes except in high-burden border areas, and all (74/74, 100%) viewed the strategy as efficient. Challenges included limited human resources and budget, low community participation, and difficult terrain and telecommunication. The authors conclude the 1-3-7 strategy is not one-size-fits-all and depends on health system capacity and local incidence.

articles.headline: Malaria prophylaxis in HIV adults did not raise resistance mutations
articles.title_original: Impact of Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole and Chloroquine Prophylaxis on Drug-Resistant Plasmodium falciparum Mutations in Adults with HIV in Malawi.
articles.authors: Fairhurst KJ, Mungwira RG, Divala TH, Nyirenda OM, Masonga R, Joshi S, Fern N, van Oosterhout JJ, Laufer MK, Laurens MB
articles.details: Using samples from a randomized controlled trial in Malawi comparing chloroquine (CQ) versus trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TS) prophylaxis among adults with HIV on successful antiretroviral therapy, researchers used pyrosequencing to assess P. falciparum resistance markers. Compared with no-prophylaxis controls, parasites from TS or CQ recipients showed no significant increase in antifolate resistance polymorphisms in pfdhfr and pfdhps or in the chloroquine resistance transporter pfcrt. No CQ-resistance mutation was detected in any breakthrough infection. The authors found no evidence that prophylaxis selected for these resistance mutations.

articles.headline: STING-IRF3 signaling controls IL-35 regulatory B cells in mouse malaria
articles.title_original: Distinct STING-IRF3 activation determines IL-35(+) Breg differentiation in rodent malaria.
articles.authors: Feng A, Li Q, Jiang N, Lv K, Yang Y, Liu T, Su Z, Zhang Y, Sang X, Feng Y, Chen R, Chen Q
articles.details: In a rodent study, C57BL/6 mice were infected with Plasmodium berghei ANKA (which causes cerebral malaria) or P. yoelii YM (which does not), and regulatory B cell (Breg) responses were compared. Distinct Breg subtypes differentiated via hemozoin-mediated Toll-like receptor 9 activation, with IL-35+ Breg expansion occurring in P. yoelii YM but not P. berghei ANKA infection. STING-mediated IRF3 phosphorylation suppressed IL-35+ Breg differentiation, potentially contributing to experimental cerebral malaria, whereas P. yoelii YM activated IRF3 STING-independently to promote IL-35+ Bregs. The authors highlight IL-35+ Bregs as key modulators of malarial immunopathology.

articles.headline: Fatal imported babesiosis misdiagnosed as falciparum malaria in Honduras
articles.title_original: First documented case of imported human babesiosis in Honduras: Diagnostic challenges with malaria falciparum.
articles.authors: Chaver L, Flores H, Padilla W, Rodríguez J, Zúniga C, Medina F, Matamoros G, Araujo ME, Ortiz B, Fontecha G
articles.details: This case report describes the first confirmed imported human babesiosis in Honduras. A 72-year-old man returning from Massachusetts developed fever, neurological symptoms, and ischemic stroke; thick smears suggested P. falciparum, and he was treated with chloroquine-primaquine then artemether-lumefantrine, but hyperparasitemia persisted and he developed multiorgan failure, dying on day 33. Molecular assays were negative for Plasmodium, while PCR confirmed Babesia 18S rRNA, and retrospective smear review identified the Maltese cross. The authors stress considering babesiosis in febrile travelers unresponsive to antimalarials and strengthening molecular diagnostic capacity in malaria-endemic regions.

articles.headline: mRNA vaccine restores malaria vaccine protection blocked by hemozoin
articles.title_original: mRNA vaccination overcomes hemozoin-mediated impairment of whole parasite vaccine efficacy for malaria.
articles.authors: Hassert M, Drewry LL, Pewe LL, Hancox LS, He R, Arumugam S, Mix MR, Salem AK, Harty JT
articles.details: Using a murine Plasmodium model, researchers showed that prior blood-stage exposure compromised Radiation Attenuated Sporozoite (RAS)-induced CD8+ T cell responses and protection, and identified the persisting malarial pigment hemozoin as the mediator. Mechanistically, hemozoin impaired antigen uptake by dendritic cells, reducing T cell activation. A lipid nanoparticle-encapsulated mRNA vaccine encoding a string of Plasmodium CD8+ T cell epitopes overcame this defect and restored protection in Plasmodium-exposed mice, and a combined RAS+mRNA regimen enhanced liver resident memory T cells and protection in malaria-experienced hosts. The authors propose hemozoin as an obstacle to vaccine efficacy in endemic settings.

articles.headline: Macaque infection rates may signal human P. knowlesi malaria risk
articles.title_original: Multi-pronged surveillance to understand the spatiotemporal correlations among macaques, vectors and humans in Plasmodium knowlesi malaria transmission.
articles.authors: Phang WK, Jeyaprakasam NK, Pramasivan S, Suli ZB, Tam JZ, Hamid MHBA, Abdullah MLB, Rohimly AABI, Ashrat NB, Chuang TW, Nguitragool W, Vythilingam I, Lau YL
articles.details: This surveillance study integrated field data from humans, macaques, and mosquito vectors collected in Peninsular Malaysia between 2019 and 2022 to study Plasmodium knowlesi transmission, using partial correlation analysis controlling for inter-host site proximity and temporal difference. The proportion of P. knowlesi-positive human cases correlated positively with average Anopheles Leucosphyrus-group mosquitoes per night within 10 km (rs = 0.228, P = 0.042), and P. knowlesi-positive macaques correlated with historical human cases within 6 km and beyond. Macaque samples were more often positive for P. knowlesi, P. inui, and P. coatneyi in peridomestic-agriculture than urban areas. The authors conclude macaque infection may serve as a proxy for persistent transmission and elevated human risk.

articles.headline: Novel Mologic Pf LDH rapid test matches HRP2 RDT and microscopy
articles.title_original: Clinical evaluation of a novel Mologic malaria Plasmodium falciparum LDH-dependent lateral flow rapid diagnostic test diagnostic accuracy: a cross-sectional study, Sudan.
articles.authors: Mohamed AO, Hamid MMA, Jebreel WMA, Elaagip A, Mohammed FO, Albsheer MM, Mustafa SA, Ahmed TME, Ahmed SBA, Chamai M, Dittrich S
articles.details: In a cross-sectional study at two rural primary care centres in Khartoum state, Sudan, 500 patients with suspected malaria were tested by microscopy, the index Mologic Pf LDH-dependent RDT, a comparator RDT, and PCR as reference. PCR was positive in 210/500 (42%), microscopy in 200 (40%), the index LDH RDT in 193 (38.6%), and the comparator in 199 (39.8%). Sensitivities were 95.24% for microscopy, 91.9% for the index RDT, and 93.81% for the comparator; all were nearly 100% specific, with almost perfect agreement (κ=0.929). Six HRP2-negative samples were detected by the Mologic LDH RDT. Authors conclude it is suitable for managing P. falciparum HRP2-negative malaria.

articles.headline: 60% of treated African children face over 24-hour malaria treatment delays
articles.title_original: Estimating rates of treatment delay for malaria fevers among children in Sub-Saharan Africa 2006-2022.
articles.authors: Lubinda J, Rumisha SF, Dzianach P, McPhail M, Saddler A, Browne A, Sanna F, Gelaw Y, Castle P, Kiss JB, Harris J, Rozier JA, Vargas C, Amratia P, Symons TL, Cameron E, Gething PW, Weiss DJ
articles.details: Using 177 nationally representative surveys, researchers estimated malaria treatment delay rates among African children treated between 2006 and 2022. They found 60% (95% UI 45.7-72.8) experienced delays exceeding 24 hours and 29% (95% UI 18.9-41.2) exceeded 48 hours, affecting 33 million and 16 million children respectively. Somalia had the highest delay rate (76%) and Tanzania the lowest (35.3%); initial improvements stagnated after 2015, with East Africa progressing most while Central and West Africa worsened, and poorer and rural populations facing higher delays. The authors note late treatment raises the odds of severe disease nearly 2.8 times.

articles.headline: nanosd gene drive efficiently modifies Anopheles gambiae malaria mosquitoes
articles.title_original: The nanosd integral gene drive enables population modification of the malaria vector Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Yen PS, Verkuijl SANR, Capriotti P, Del Corsano G, Yee Kim Guan C, Hoermann A, Inghilterra MG, Aramburu-Gonzalez I, Khan MA, Vlachou D, Christophides GK, Windbichler N
articles.details: This study describes creation of the nanosd integral gene drive, a CRISPR-Cas9 homing drive targeting and inserted into the nanos gene of the malaria vector Anopheles gambiae. It achieved high drive rates of 98.4% in females and 99.5% in males. Homozygous females (but not males) showed impaired fecundity and variable ovary underdevelopment, with decreased nanos transcripts. Susceptibility to Plasmodium spp. and O'nyong nyong virus remained similar to wild-type. The drive propagated efficiently in caged populations, persisted as a Cas9 source after drive-resistant alleles emerged, and mobilised a non-autonomous antiparasitic effector, showing promise for vector control via population modification.

articles.headline: Scoping review of machine learning in malaria vaccine research
articles.title_original: From data to immunity: the role of machine learning in advancing malaria vaccine research: a scoping review.
articles.authors: Khanday S, Sayeed M, Jafri NF, Jafri IF, Jafri RF, Ashraf G, Safwat S, Nasr DS
articles.details: This review synthesized peer-reviewed articles, reviews, and systematic analyses published between 2017 and 2025 on machine learning (ML) and AI applications in malaria vaccine development, identified through a targeted literature search. Thematic analysis identified five key areas: antigen discovery and prioritization using supervised and semi-supervised learning; immune signature identification and efficacy prediction; computational tool and framework development for data integration; broad reviews of AI/ML applications; and epidemiological modeling for policy support. Most studies were conducted in Europe and North America, often with collaborations in Africa. The authors conclude ML is accelerating antigen discovery, immune profiling, and prediction of vaccine efficacy, but data quality, model interpretability, and validation challenges must be addressed.

articles.headline: Plasmodium berghei protein PbGAC binds erythrocytes during merozoite invasion
articles.title_original: The Plasmodium berghei merozoite protein PbGAC is critically involved in erythrocyte binding during invasion.
articles.authors: Gao J, Jiang N, Zhang Y, Chen R, Feng Y, Sang X, Chen Q
articles.details: Using western blotting, immunofluorescence, flow cytometry, heparinase II treatment, mouse immunization, passive immunization, immunoprecipitation, and mass spectrometry, researchers characterized PbGAC (encoded by PbANKA_1137800), a previously uncharacterized Plasmodium berghei ANKA merozoite protein. PbGAC, located at the extreme apical region of the merozoite, bound heparin and the erythrocyte surface during invasion via the heparan sulfate-dependent pathway, and interacted with several secreted proteins involved in erythrocyte invasion. Mice immunized with recombinant PbGAC-His or passively immunized with sera from vaccinated mice showed enhanced immunity against lethal challenge. Authors conclude PbGAC is critically involved in erythrocyte binding during invasion.

articles.headline: Correction notice: human liver organoids support P. vivax infection
articles.title_original: Correction: Human liver organoids are susceptible to Plasmodium vivax infection.
articles.authors: Nitaramorn N, Kobpornchai P, Tongkrajang N, Chaisri U, Imwong M, Kulkeaw K
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: 24-SNP barcode can't distinguish P. falciparum imported to China from Central vs West Africa
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity and population structures of Plasmodium falciparum parasites imported to China from Central and West Africa determined using a SNP barcode.
articles.authors: Zeng W, Zhu W, Qin Y, Lan H, Cen Y, Sun K, Wu Y, Liang T, Mou Y, Liu C, Tang X, Xiang Z, Huang Y, Cui L, Yang Z
articles.details: This study applied a 24-SNP high-resolution melting (HRM) barcode to analyze the population structure of Plasmodium falciparum imported to China from Central and West Africa. A total of 181 samples yielded complete 24-SNP barcodes. There was no significant difference in the proportion of multi-clone infections among the four populations, and nucleotide diversity was low across all. Pairwise FST values ranged from 0.001 to 0.054, indicating low to moderate genetic differentiation, and principal component analysis and population structure assessment showed no prominent clustering. The authors conclude the barcode is insufficient to distinguish isolates from Central and West Africa, likely due to minimal genetic differentiation between these regions.

articles.headline: Olfactory gene catalog compiled for four malaria mosquito vectors
articles.title_original: Olfactory gene dynamics in invasive Indian and non-invasive African malaria vectors at the crossroads of development, infection and resistance.
articles.authors: Sharma A, Dixit B, Goyal B, Harit R, Kumar J, Biswas S, Goswami R, Pandey KC, Emami SN, Chakraborti S
articles.details: Using a genome-wide approach, researchers compiled the olfactory gene catalog for four malaria vectors: Anopheles stephensi and Anopheles culicifacies (Indian species) and Anopheles gambiae and Anopheles funestus (African species), cataloging odorant binding proteins (OBPs), chemosensory proteins (CSPs), odorant receptors (ORs), ionotropic receptors (IRs), and gustatory receptors (GusRs). African vectors had significantly higher numbers of OBP, OR, and IR genes than the Indian species. The invasive A. stephensi showed closer genetic ties to A. minimus and A. gambiae than to A. culicifacies. In A. stephensi larvae, pyrethroid-resistant mosquitoes showed elevated expression of SAP2 and SAP3, suggesting a potential role in insecticide resistance.

articles.headline: Public dataset of malaria seasonality timeseries for sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: A malaria seasonality dataset for sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Sanna F, Keddie SH, Boyhan T, Dzianach PA, McPhail M, Seitz J, Nguyen T, Redpath A, Chikolwa T, Browne AJ, Lubinda J, Saddler A, Hafsia S, Jayaseelen R, Baggen HS, Rozier JA, Symons TL, Harris J, Connor S, Vargas C, Whittaker C, Nguyen M, Gething PW, Weiss DJ
articles.details: This study assembled and publicly released a geolocated dataset of historical timeseries describing malaria seasonality published since 2000 for sub-Saharan Africa. Three approaches were used: an extensive literature review employing novel natural language processing to accelerate article screening, extractions from a routine surveillance dataset with geolocated data from all malaria-endemic countries, and cross-referencing with a key entomological dataset. The resulting data include malaria prevalence, incidence, mortality, and entomological timeseries, plus a novel assembly of qualitative descriptions of malaria seasonality extracted from published literature.

articles.headline: Poor antimalarial adherence among Rwandan febrile patients despite high malaria knowledge
articles.title_original: Understanding Malaria Treatment Adherence in Rwanda: Implications for Artemisinin Resistance.
articles.authors: Gashema P, Jordan A, Saramba E, Wernsman Young N, Iradukunda PG, Karema C, Mazarati JB, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Banek K
articles.details: A cross-sectional study in six health facilities in moderate- to high-transmission areas of Rwanda interviewed 406 febrile patients or caregivers using semistructured questionnaires and found that, despite high malaria knowledge, treatment adherence was poor. Among respondents, 71% (289/406) owned insecticide-treated nets and 51% (205/406) received indoor residual spraying, and knowledge was high (81% identified symptoms, 72% understood transmission, 74.6% aware of control measures). However, of the 44.3% (180/406) treated in the last 6 months, only 46% (83/180) completed the 3-day course, while 37% (66/180) stopped within 2 days and 11% (19/180) went over 3 days; among the 27% (109/406) who took antimalarials for fever, 54% (49/109) obtained medication from drug outlets/pharmacies. The authors conclude that this poor adherence exacerbates the risk of developing antimalarial resistance.

articles.headline: Review of Anopheles mascarensis, a malaria vector in Madagascar and Comoros
articles.title_original: Anopheles mascarensis, a Malaria Vector Endemic to Madagascar and the Comoros Archipelago: A Review.
articles.authors: Goupeyou-Youmsi J, Tantely LM, Andrianinarivomanana TM, Girod R, Bourgouin C
articles.details: This review compiles published and previously unpublished data on Anopheles mascarensis, a mosquito endemic to Madagascar and the Comoros Archipelago that was historically confused with the continental species Anopheles marshalli. The authors update the species' geographical distribution map (last published in 1966) and analyze its biology, geographic variants, and role in malaria transmission. An. mascarensis is the main vector in eastern and southeastern Madagascar and a secondary vector elsewhere. Behavioral and biogeographic differences between east coast and Central Highlands populations suggest it may be a complex of sibling species, a hypothesis the authors say is increasingly testable with molecular tools.

articles.headline: Parasite census population size tracks falciparum response to IRS and SMC in Ghana
articles.title_original: Measuring changes in Plasmodium falciparum census population size in response to sequential malaria control interventions.
articles.authors: Tiedje KE, Zhan Q, Ruybal-Pésantez S, Tonkin-Hill G, He Q, Tan MH, Argyropoulos DC, Deed S, Ghansah A, Bangre O, Oduro AR, Koram KA, Pascual M, Day KP
articles.details: The authors introduce 'census population size,' a parasite-centered endpoint derived from multiplicity of infection (MOIvar) estimated via a Bayesian approach using hyper-diverse var gene DBLα tags, and apply it across sequential malaria interventions in a high, seasonal transmission area of northern Ghana from 2012 to 2017. Following indoor residual spraying (IRS), which reduced transmission intensity by >90% and parasite prevalence by ~40-50%, var diversity, MOIvar, and population size dropped significantly across ~2000 people of all ages. These reductions were short-lived: 32 months after IRS stopped and seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) began, diversity and population size rebounded in all age groups except children aged 1-5 years targeted by SMC. The parasite population remained very large, demonstrating P. falciparum resilience to short-term interventions.

articles.headline: Red-emissive fluorescent probe detects and classifies malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Advanced Clinical Diagnosis of Malaria Using a Simple Red-Emissive Smart Fluorescent Probe.
articles.authors: Ye Y, Zhang S, Chen G, Liu Z, Chen X, Luo R, Zhang Z, Zhao X, Lian J, Yang Z, Samanta S, Yang Z, Xu K
articles.details: Researchers designed a hemicyanine dye, MR-1, with blue absorption and red emission that shows turn-on fluorescence upon binding AT-rich segments of double-stranded DNA, with control compounds MR-2 and MR-3 used to explain the design rationale. MR-1 showed excellent cell permeability and localized to the nucleus in live and fixed cells. Incubation with malaria parasite-infected red blood cells produced a marked fluorescence increase, with a detection limit of ~20/μL. Flow cytometric analysis of clinical infected red blood cells enabled precise detection and classification of malaria parasites and differentiation of malaria from Babesia infection, which has similar clinical symptoms.

articles.headline: Genomic surveillance finds widespread falciparum resistance markers in Kigali, Rwanda
articles.title_original: Genomic Surveillance of Plasmodium falciparum Drug Resistance Markers Between October 2021 and June 2023 in Kigali, Rwanda.
articles.authors: Fankem SN, Mbonimpa JB, Kalimba EM, Diallo MT, Teke ME, Souopgui J
articles.details: This genomic surveillance study analyzed 250 clinical Plasmodium falciparum isolates collected in Kigali, Rwanda between October 2021 and June 2023 using Oxford Nanopore and Sanger sequencing, focusing on pfcrt, pfmdr1, pfdhfr, pfdhps, and Pfkelch13 mutations. Resistance-associated mutations were common: pfcrt 76T (26%) and pfmdr1 184F (72.8%), indicating continued lumefantrine pressure; all isolates carried pfdhfr and pfdhps mutations, with IRNI-SAEAA and IRNI-SAEGA haplotypes in 45.6% and 24.8%. Pfkelch13 mutations occurred in 50.4% of isolates, including validated R561H (25.6%), A675V, and candidate P441L; a novel K189T + R561H haplotype (24.8%) and BTB/POZ domain H384R (6.4%) were also found. The authors stress the urgent need for continued molecular surveillance and functional validation.

articles.headline: Draft genome sequence and assembly of Anopheles gambiae strain G3
articles.title_original: Whole-Genome Shotgun Sequencing and Assembly of Anopheles gambiae G3, the Host of Malaria Parasite Plasmodium sp.
articles.authors: English E, Gulick C, Jane A, Kim M, Kpodonu N, Lee A, Lopez RKS, Patel K, Rajaram A, Unver L, Rankin AE, Ghazal SM
articles.details: The authors report a 216.3 Mbp draft genome sequence and assembly for Anopheles gambiae strain G3, the African malaria mosquito and main vector of human malaria, which was isolated from Gambiae in 1975. The draft genome comprises almost 82 thousand scaffolds, 53X coverage, and a G+C content of 44.5%.

articles.headline: Five-year malaria surveillance trends in former SNNPR, Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Retrospective analysis of malaria surveillance data in the former Southern Nations, Nationalities, and peoples' region, Ethiopia (2017-2021).
articles.authors: Dana DD, Ango TS, Getachew S, Deressa W
articles.details: A retrospective analysis of malaria surveillance data from the former Southern Nations, Nationalities, and Peoples' Region (SNNPR), Ethiopia, examined morbidity, mortality, species distribution, and geography. Of 6,292,147 suspected cases tested, 999,687 (15.89%) were laboratory-confirmed and treated, with 679,787 (68%) due to P. falciparum and 319,900 (32%) due to P. vivax; an additional 16,023 (1.6%) were clinically diagnosed. Cases rose from 214,095 (13 per 1,000) in July 2021 to 270,274 (16 per 1,000) in December 2021, with the highest burden in South Omo (22%), Gamo (19%), and Wolaita (17%) zones. Average annual incidence was 12 per 1,000 (moderate risk). Authors recommend enhanced surveillance and vector control in high-burden areas.

articles.headline: Self-limiting Male Drive Female Sterile system suppresses Anopheles gambiae
articles.title_original: A male-drive female-sterile system for the self-limited control of the malaria mosquito Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Strampelli A, Willis K, Gulliford HR, Gribble M, Fasulo B, Burt A, Crisanti A, Bernardini F
articles.details: This study describes a self-limiting gene drive strategy called Male Drive Female Sterile (MDFS) targeting the malaria vector Anopheles gambiae. The MDFS construct causes dominant sterility in females while transgenic males remain fertile, transmitting the female-sterility trait at super-Mendelian rates. Laboratory experiments showed that repeated releases of MDFS mosquitoes led to elimination of caged mosquito populations. Modelling based on these findings suggests MDFS could be a highly effective and self-limiting approach for suppressing wild malaria mosquito populations.

articles.headline: PfATP2 flippase drives P. falciparum drug sensitivity and growth
articles.title_original: PfATP2 is a flippase on the Plasmodium falciparum surface that is important for growth and influences parasite sensitivity to antiplasmodial compounds.
articles.authors: Qiu D, England E, Lehane AM
articles.details: Researchers characterized PfATP2, a putative phospholipid flippase in Plasmodium falciparum previously linked via pfatp2 gene amplification to resistance against compounds MMV007224 and MMV665852. They show PfATP2 localizes to the parasite surface and is important for growth. Parasites engineered to overexpress PfATP2 showed reduced sensitivity to MMV665794, MMV007224, and MMV665852, while PfATP2 knockdown parasites became hypersensitive to all three. PfATP2 expression did not affect cytosolic pH. Overexpressing parasites internalized a fluorescent phosphatidylserine analogue (NBD-PS) faster, and MMV665794 and MMV007224 reduced NBD-PS internalization. The data support PfATP2 as a major ATP-dependent phosphatidylserine internalization mechanism and a potential drug target.

articles.headline: New LDH/HRP2 rapid tests perform well for P. vivax and P. falciparum
articles.title_original: First field evaluation of novel LDH- and HRP2-based rapid tests for Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium falciparum malaria diagnosis.
articles.authors: Oduma CO, Lulu B, Ewnetu Y, Lown LA, Kayode TA, Hawaria D, Koepfli C
articles.details: This field evaluation enrolled 708 clinical patients presenting for malaria diagnosis in southern Ethiopia, testing four RDTs (two BIOCREDIT, one Bioline, and First Response) against qPCR and expert microscopy, with hrp2/3 deletion typing. By qPCR, 46.0% were positive (77 P. falciparum mono-infections, 198 P. vivax mono-infections, 51 mixed). At densities >20 parasites/µL, all RDTs reached >96% sensitivity for P. falciparum (vs 63% for health center microscopy) and >92% for P. vivax (vs 72%); specificity was >99% for P. falciparum and >98% for P. vivax. Only 2/53 typed P. falciparum infections carried hrp2/hrp3 deletions, both detected by LDH-based RDTs. The novel BIOCREDIT and Bioline RDTs showed high sensitivity and specificity.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence and risk factors in Kenyan under-five children
articles.title_original: Individual and community level factors of malaria among under-five children in Kenya: Based on the Kenya Demographic and Health Survey.
articles.authors: Lakew G, Tareke AA, Yigzaw ZA, Getachew D, Getachew E, Yirsaw AN
articles.details: Using 2022 Kenya Demographic and Health Survey data for 3,146 under-five children, a multilevel logistic regression model assessed factors associated with malaria. Prevalence was 22.72% (95% CI: 21.29–24.22). Protective factors included maternal age 25–34 years (AOR 0.75, 95% CI 0.57–0.99), richest family wealth (AOR 0.48, 95% CI 0.26–0.89), treated mosquito bed net (AOR 0.39, 95% CI 0.17–0.89), having no bed net (AOR 0.49, 95% CI 0.31–0.77), and breastfeeding mothers (AOR 0.66, 95% CI 0.52–0.85), while community-level poverty increased risk (AOR 1.82, 95% CI 1.19–2.77). Authors recommend promoting insecticide-treated bed nets, supporting low-income families, and strengthening maternal health education.

articles.headline: Higher temperatures and moderate rainfall raise under-5 malaria risk in Africa
articles.title_original: Associations of temperature and precipitation with malaria in children under 5: A multi-country study in Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Chombo S, Bachwenkizi J, Omary H, Paulo HA, Luoga P, Zacharia A, Mbishi JV, Andrew JD, Addo IY
articles.details: This analytical cross-sectional study examined associations of temperature and precipitation with malaria among 15,009 children under five across six Sub-Saharan African countries (Burundi, Burkina Faso, Malawi, Nigeria, Tanzania, Uganda), using ERA-5 climate data for the 12 months preceding surveys and weighted modified Poisson regression. Average malaria prevalence was 25.9%, highest in Nigeria (38.1%) and Burundi (38.0%). A one-degree Celsius temperature rise increased malaria risk 1.77-fold (95% CI 1.297-2.414, p<0.001), while squared temperature slightly reduced risk (1%, p=0.002). Children in areas with 250-500 mm annual precipitation had 72% higher risk than those with over 500 mm (95% CI 1.331-2.212, p<0.001). Authors conclude climate data should inform malaria control.

articles.headline: Blood-stage MSP1 malaria vaccine safe and immunogenic in pre-exposed adults
articles.title_original: Full-length merozoite surface protein 1 formulated with GLA-SE adjuvant in malaria pre-exposed adults: a randomised, controlled, double-blind, parallel-group, single-centre Phase Ib trial.
articles.authors: Kahatano AE, Mpina M, Vanobberghen F, Hassan O, Urasa N, Sasamalo I, Mswata S, Paris D, Gajewski S, Saxena M, Fürle K, Kiehl V, Böhnlein E, Aschenbrenner A, Lanzer M, Thomson-Luque R, Olotu A, Daubenberger C
articles.details: This randomized, controlled, double-blind, single-centre Phase Ib trial tested full-length recombinant PfMSP1 (3D7 strain, 150 μg) formulated in GLA-SE adjuvant (SUM-101) versus Verorab rabies vaccine in 40 healthy malaria pre-exposed adults aged 18-45, given as three monthly intramuscular doses (days 0, 28, 56) in Bagamoyo, Tanzania. 37 of 40 (93%) completed all vaccinations and follow-up. Within 7 days, 52 solicited adverse events at least possibly related to the product occurred: 26 events in 10 participants (50%) in the SUM-101 arm and 26 in 13 (65%) in the control arm, mostly mild-to-moderate (injection site pain, headache, fatigue). No severe or serious adverse events occurred. SUM-101 elicited robust MSP1-specific IgM and IgG, peaking on days 56 and 84 respectively.

articles.headline: Corrigendum to study on Indian malaria epidemics and climate
articles.title_original: Corrigendum to "Malaria epidemics in India: Role of climatic condition and control measures" [Sci. Total Environ. Volume 712, 10 April 2020, 136368].
articles.authors: Baghbanzadeh M, Kumar D, Yavasoglu SI, Manning S, Hanafi-Bojd AA, Ghasemzadeh H, Sikder IU, Kumar D, Murmu N, Haque U
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Antibodies to GLURP R2 and Rh5.1 linked to lower malaria risk in children
articles.title_original: A Multiplex Serological Assay to Evaluate the Antibody Responses to a Set of Plasmodium falciparum Antigens and Their Protective Role Against Malaria in Children Aged 1.5 to 12 Years Living in a Highly Seasonal Malaria Transmission Area of Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Ezinmegnon S, Nébié I, Marlais T, Ouattara D, Diarra A, Patterson C, Tetteh K, Ouédraogo A, Drakeley C, Tiono AB, Sirima SB
articles.details: In children aged 1.5 to 12 years from Burkina Faso, anti-GLURP R2 and anti-Rh5.1 antibodies were associated with reduced malaria risk (adjusted IRR = 0.52 and 0.40, respectively), and the combination of AMA1, GLURP R2, and Etramp5 Ag1 showed the best predictive performance (AUC = 0.70). IgG responses to 16 P. falciparum antigens were measured by multiplex assay using cross-sectional and longitudinal approaches, with associations assessed via incidence rate ratios (IRRs) and ROC analysis. Children aged 5-12 years had higher antibody levels than those aged 1.5-5 years, and uninfected children had higher antibodies to EBA181 RIII-V, Rh5.1, and SEA1.

articles.headline: TCF25 regulates gametocyte formation and ribosome biogenesis in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Transcription factor 25 modulates gametocytogenesis and ribosome biogenesis in the malaria parasite Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Wu J, Zhang Z, Ngim C, Yang W, Li P, Wang F, Ye J, Li B, Tian B, Zhang Q, Shang X
articles.details: This laboratory study investigated TCF25 — described as a transcriptional regulator and a key component of the Ribosome-associated Quality Control (RQC) complex — in Plasmodium falciparum by creating a tcf25 knockout strain and performing comparative transcriptomics at the ring and schizont stages, gametocyte induction experiments, ChIP-seq, and RT-qPCR. Knockout caused dysregulation of 168 downregulated and 24 upregulated genes at the ring stage, and 53 downregulated and 4 upregulated genes at the schizont stage. ChIP-seq identified 44 high-confidence TCF25-binding target genes, including the rDNA, and TCF25 deficiency increased rRNA expression, particularly 28S rRNA. The authors conclude TCF25 regulates gametocytogenesis via the ap2-g pathway and directly controls A-type rRNA expression and ribosomal subunit homeostasis, offering insight into transmission and ribosome biogenesis in malaria parasites.

articles.headline: PfEBA-175 region II shows diversity yet stays a vaccine candidate
articles.title_original: Micro-geographic variation in antigenic diversity of PfEBA-175 region II in asymptomatic Plasmodium falciparum infections in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Syahada JH, Lee WJ, Jun H, Louis JM, Fitriana F, Muh F, Lu F, Ahmed MA, Na S, Chun W, Park WS, Jeon BY, Han ET, Todd J, Manjurano A, Kidima W, Mazigo E, Lee SJ, Han JH
articles.details: Researchers sequenced PfEBA-175 region II (RII) from 172 asymptomatic P. falciparum isolates collected in Geita and Kigoma, Tanzania, assessing nucleotide diversity, selection, and antibody responses to a recombinant RII protein. They found high nucleotide diversity (π = 0.00359 ± 0.00012), evidence of immune-driven adaptive evolution (dN-dS = 3.15, p < 0.001), and recent population expansion (Fu's Fs = -30.614). Average seropositivity was 84.8% (ranging 95.4% in Rwantaba to 50.0% in Bunyambo) and correlated with age (ρ = 0.333, p < 0.001) but not parasitemia or gender. Glycan-binding residues remained conserved, supporting its continued evaluation as a blood-stage vaccine candidate.

articles.headline: Review: how System of Rice Intensification may affect African malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Climate change adapted rice production: does the system of rice intensification impact malaria vector ecology?
articles.authors: Hardy H, Hopkins RJ, Mnyone L, Hawkes FM
articles.details: This review discusses how the System of Rice Intensification (SRI), an alternative rice cultivation practice that alters the growing environment and reduces water inputs to boost yields, may affect the ecology and bionomics of Africa's dominant malaria vector species. The authors note established links between irrigated rice systems, increased malaria vector densities, and intensified transmission in rice-associated communities. They argue that growing adoption of SRI across Africa, driven by climate-change pressures and demand for higher rice production, could have important and as-yet-uncertain consequences for local malaria transmission dynamics.

articles.headline: Stakeholder views on post-discharge malaria chemoprevention in Malawi
articles.title_original: Assessing stakeholder perceptions and contextual factors in implementing post-discharge malaria chemoprevention in children with severe anaemia in Malawi.
articles.authors: Ndambo MK, Hoyt J, Nyirongo E, Phiri KS, Hill J, Manda-Taylor L
articles.details: This exploratory qualitative formative study used in-depth and key informant interviews with 31 purposively sampled stakeholders to assess perceptions of the WHO recommendation on post-discharge malaria chemoprevention (PDMC) for children with severe anaemia and considerations for its implementation in Malawi. Thematic analysis identified a continued high burden of malaria and anaemia, knowledge gaps among Health Surveillance Assistants, and caregiver barriers including traditional beliefs, financial constraints, and logistical challenges. The authors conclude effective implementation requires community engagement, strengthened supply chains, health worker training, and integration of PDMC into health systems in low-resource, high-burden settings.

articles.headline: AL and DHA-piperaquine both clear malaria in Nigerian children
articles.title_original: Parasite clearance of two commonly administered artemisinin-based combination therapies in under-five children with uncomplicated malaria in a comprehensive healthcare facility in South-Western Nigeria: a randomised controlled trial.
articles.authors: Ahmed AA, Bello IS, Adewole TO, Olowookere SA, Oyegbade OO, Ogundokun AO, Olajubu TO, Funwei RI, Ojurongbe O, Adepoju DA, Bello AB, Adegboyega AA, Anjorin AO, Anjorin AO, Akinola OO, Adewumi KM, Omenugha MK, Salami OT, Familusi AO, Akande EO
articles.details: This open-label randomised controlled trial enrolled 122 children aged 6–59 months with uncomplicated malaria at a comprehensive healthcare facility in Southwestern Nigeria, assigning them to three days of artemether-lumefantrine (AL, n=61) or dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine phosphate (DHAPQ, n=61) with 42-day follow-up and PCR genotyping. Mean fever clearance time was significantly shorter with AL (46.16 ± 3.31 h vs 48.83 ± 8.95 h; p = 0.032), while parasite clearance time did not differ significantly (61.30 vs 63.97 h; p = 0.541). Day 42 PCR-uncorrected cure rates were 94.7% (AL) and 92.9% (DHAPQ) (p = 0.679), rising to 100% in both after PCR correction; five participants in the DHAPQ group reported mild adverse events, compared with none in the AL group. The study concluded that both treatments had good parasite clearance.

articles.headline: AI hybrid ViT-CNN model diagnoses malaria from blood smears
articles.title_original: Ensemble-Based Early Detection of Malaria via Explainable ViT-CNN Feature Fusion and SHAP.
articles.authors: Özdemir EY, Koç C, Küçük K, Özyurt F
articles.details: This study proposes a hybrid, explainable-AI diagnostic model for detecting malaria from microscopic blood smear images, combining features from a Vision Transformer (ViT) and an EfficientNet-based CNN, filtering them by importance with SHAP, and retraining the top features with an ensemble of CatBoost, XGBoost, and Logistic Regression. The best configuration, combining ViT_Large_16 and EfficientNetB0, achieved 95.61% accuracy with a 1.6 s processing time, reported as roughly 204 times faster than traditional machine learning approaches. The authors conclude the approach offers explainability and speed advantages suitable for clinical integration in early infectious-disease diagnosis.

articles.headline: Correction notice for study on anticoagulants in placental and cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Correction: Anticoagulant therapy and altered tissue factor expression protect against experimental placental and cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Andrew AK, Bracken TC, Nazario-Maldonado N, Russ BN, Gonzalvo KT, Brainard BM, Mackman N, Moore JM
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Metabolomics reveals altered plasma metabolites in malaria-infected rats
articles.title_original: Application of a Non-Targeted Metabolomics Study in Plasmodium berghei-Infected Rats: Towards Unravelling Metabolic Alterations During Malaria Infection.
articles.authors: Mbuli ZN, Ndlovu IS, Masola B, Mukaratirwa S
articles.details: This experimental study used untargeted Gas Chromatography-Time-of-Flight Mass Spectrometry to profile serum metabolites in 20 male Sprague Dawley rats split into uninfected controls and Plasmodium berghei-infected animals (infected via intraperitoneal injection of parasitized red blood cells). Infected rats showed distinct metabolite profiles with elevated urea and reduced 1,5-anhydroglucitol, D-(+)-Talose, and arachidonic acid. Pathway analysis showed significant enrichment of the glucose-alanine cycle, alpha-linolenic acid metabolism, and linoleic acid metabolism. The authors interpret upregulated gluconeogenesis and amino acid catabolism as responses to parasite-induced energy demand, and highlight metabolomics as a potential diagnostic and prognostic tool.

articles.headline: Higher temperatures, especially minimum, linked to increased malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Association between temperature and malaria (1959-2019): a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Murugan Y, Shrivastav V
articles.details: This PRISMA-guided systematic review and meta-analysis included 11 studies from Africa and Asia spanning 1959-2019, using random-effects models to pool correlations between ambient temperature and malaria outcomes. Mean temperature showed a moderate positive correlation with malaria incidence (r = 0.417, 95% CI 0.098-0.735, p = 0.01), while minimum temperature had the strongest, most consistent association with test positivity rates (r = 0.454, 95% CI 0.358-0.549, p < 0.001), significant in all 11 studies; maximum temperature was weaker (r = 0.356). Heterogeneity was substantial (I2 > 99%). The authors conclude temperature, especially minimum temperature, significantly influences transmission and support integrating temperature monitoring into surveillance and early-warning systems.

articles.headline: Commentary on bold innovations for a changing malaria landscape
articles.title_original: Innovation under pressure: bold ideas for a changing malaria landscape.
articles.authors: Epstein A, Tangena JA
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Case report on post-artesunate delayed haemolysis diagnostic challenges
articles.title_original: The usual suspect-malaria? Diagnostic challenges in post-artesunate delayed haemolysis: a clinical laboratory perspective through a case report.
articles.authors: Molnar E, Cabral PM, Portal F, Guimarães AR, Santos L, Guimarães JT
articles.details: This mini review and case report examines a case of travel-associated malaria from a medical laboratory perspective, focusing on post-artesunate delayed haemolysis (PADH), a rare but potentially severe complication of artemisinin-derivative treatment. The authors note that malaria incidence in Europe is low and predominantly travel-related, requiring thorough travel history and high diagnostic proficiency. They emphasize that PADH, a predictable haemolytic phenomenon following effective artemisinin therapy, may be difficult to distinguish from manifestations of the disease itself. No quantitative outcomes are reported.

articles.headline: Bed net use among African children pooled at 70% in meta-analysis
articles.title_original: Utilization of insecticide-treated nets for malaria prevention among children in Africa: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Basiru A, Alozieuwa UB, Aboh MI, Usman UY, Nwaefulu ON, Okeke OP, Akinsolu FT, Sobande OO
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis (PROSPERO CRD42023474978) of 30 studies covering 76,045 children, 50 households, and 1009 caregivers assessed the prevalence of insecticide-treated net (ITN) utilization among children in Africa. Most studies (93.3%) were cross-sectional. Using a random-effects model, the pooled prevalence of ITN use was 69.50% (95% CI 64.5-74.5%) with very high heterogeneity (I2 = 100%, p = 0.001). Utilization was highest in Central Africa (85.44%) and Southern Africa (80.7%), and lowest in West Africa (67.0%). The authors conclude ITN use among African children is low, varies by region, and needs improvement.

articles.headline: Artemisinin-resistance and hrp2/3 deletion markers rare in South African surveillance
articles.title_original: Very Low Prevalence of Validated kelch13 Mutations and Absence of Histidine-Rich Protein 2/3 Double Gene Deletions in South African Malaria-Eliminating Districts (2022-2024).
articles.authors: Raman J, Mabona M, Nyawo Q, Mangena B, Kok G, Mathaba L, Malatje G, Lauterbach SB, Makhanthisa TI, Gwarinda H, Letinić BD, Ndadza A, Raswiswi E, Shandukani M, Baloyi E, Fekema Z, Ismail A, Featherston J, Greenhouse B, Smith JL, Aranda-Diaz A
articles.details: A molecular surveillance study analyzed 4,471 samples collected January 2022 to August 2024 from healthcare facilities and active surveillance in malaria-eliminating districts of KwaZulu-Natal and Mpumalanga Provinces, South Africa, using PCR and Sanger or targeted amplicon sequencing for resistance markers in kelch13, crt, mdr1, dhfr, and dhps and for hrp2/3 gene deletions. Validated kelch13 artemisinin partial-resistance markers were rare (P574L in two samples, P553L in one); the most common nonsynonymous mutations were A578S (30/999) and I494V (13/951). Nearly all parasites carried chloroquine-susceptible crtK76 (99.8%) and mdr1N86 (99.0%) alleles, while dhfr triple (99.9%) and dhps double (98.2%) mutations were near fixation. No hrp2/3 deletions were detected. Authors conclude current treatments and diagnostics remain effective but sustained surveillance is needed.

articles.headline: Clothianidin spray kills malaria mosquitoes for 8 months in Madagascar huts
articles.title_original: Efficacy of the Insecticide Formulation SumiShield® 50WG for Malaria Vector Control in Experimental Huts in Madagascar.
articles.authors: Nepomichene T, Randrenjarison R, Randriamanga J, Girod R
articles.details: This experimental hut study in Madagascar evaluated the residual efficacy of SumiShield 50WG, an indoor residual spray containing the neonicotinoid clothianidin, over 9 months after spraying on walls coated with different substrates, using wild anopheline mosquitoes and an insecticide-susceptible Anopheles arabiensis laboratory strain. Mortality in wild mosquitoes stayed above the WHO 80.0% threshold for up to 8 months post-spray when assessed up to 96 hours after capture, depending on wall surface. The formulation did not induce exophily or inhibit blood-feeding. In An. arabiensis the threshold was met up to 8 months, but during the ninth month it was not achieved even at 120 hours. Authors conclude residual efficacy of up to 8 months is sufficient to cover the transmission season in most endemic areas of Madagascar.

articles.headline: Mass-distributed bed nets in Madagascar lost efficacy within one year
articles.title_original: Early Bio-Efficacy Loss of Nets Mass Distributed for Malaria Vector Control in Madagascar in 2018: Implications for Malaria Prevention.
articles.authors: Nepomichene T, Randrenjarison R, Raharinjatovo J, Swamidoss I, Mapp C, Kapesa L, Razafindrakoto J, Bowen A, Belemvire A, Zohdy S, Poyer S, Girod R
articles.details: This study assessed the bio-efficacy of DawaPlus 2.0 and PermaNet 2.0 ITNs mass distributed across Madagascar in 2018, testing on arrival and at 12, 24, and 36 months after distribution, alongside chemical analysis of insecticide residue. On arrival, mosquito mortality exceeded the WHO 80.0% threshold for DawaPlus 2.0 (86.4%) and PermaNet 2.0 (83.6%), but at 12, 24, and 36 months mortality was below 56% across all districts. Knockdown was below the 95.0% threshold at all time points, even for new nets. Deltamethrin residue was below expected ranges (under 66 mg/m2 for DawaPlus 2.0 and under 36 mg/m2 for PermaNet 2.0), except new DawaPlus 2.0. Authors conclude both nets lost bio-efficacy within one year, possibly due to manufacturing, storage/transport, or use issues, warranting corrective action.

articles.headline: Outdoor-biting secondary vectors drove 2018 Madagascar malaria outbreak
articles.title_original: Malaria Outbreak in Farafangana District, Southeast Madagascar, 2018: Are Secondary Vectors a Threat to Current Malaria Control Approaches?
articles.authors: Nepomichene T, Harimanana A, Randrianaivo F, Randrenjarison R, Raherinjafy R, Rakotondramanga JM, Zohdy S, Razakamiadana S, Kapesa L, Andriamananjara MN, Baril L, Randremanana R, Randrianasolo L, Dentinger CM, Girod R
articles.details: Entomological and epidemiological investigations characterized a 2018 malaria outbreak in Farafangana District, Madagascar, that occurred shortly after ITN distribution and indoor residual spraying, across six villages in three communes; mosquitoes were collected by human landing catches, light traps, and pyrethrum spray catches, and household members were tested by RDT. The most frequent vectors were Anopheles coustani, An. gambiae s.s., An. funestus, and An. mascarensis, with high outdoor biting (exophagy 59.8% for An. gambiae s.s. up to 100.0% for An. coustani; An. funestus under 47.0%). Of 1,488 Anopheles, 25 (1.7%) carried P. falciparum sporozoites, 24 collected outdoors, 13 (54.2%) of them An. coustani. Of 226 people tested, 71 (31.4%) were RDT-positive, and 61 (85.9%) of these were asymptomatic, mostly children. Authors conclude highly infected secondary and primary vectors with predominant exophagy plus asymptomatic infections sustained transmission, and outdoor-biting control strategies should be explored.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria found in 6% of sub-Saharan migrants screened
articles.title_original: Burden of asymptomatic malaria in adult sub-Saharan migrants attending an outpatient clinic in Rome from February 2024 to January 2025.
articles.authors: Faraglia F, Vita S, Kontogiannis D, Bartoli TA, Rosati S, Bevilacqua N, Maffongelli G, Corpolongo A, Bartolini B, Vulcano A, D'Abramo A, Fontana C, Nicastri E
articles.details: Screening of 87 afebrile adult sub-Saharan African migrants (age ≥16) attending a mobile clinic in Rome (enrolled February 2024–January 2025) found a malaria prevalence of 6%, all in male migrants. Using RDTs, LAMP, thick smears, and RT-PCR, LAMP detected Plasmodium spp. in 5 cases (6.0%), confirmed by RT-PCR, while RDTs identified only 2 (2.3%); species included P. falciparum, P. malariae, P. ovale, and one mixed infection, and the longest time since arrival among positives was 181 days. The authors conclude that asymptomatic malaria can persist for months, is often missed by RDTs, and that adding RT-PCR to routine screening can improve early detection.

articles.headline: Hierarchical var gene switching helps malaria parasites evade immunity
articles.title_original: Hierarchical switching pattern in antigenic variation provides survival advantage for malaria parasites under variable host immunity.
articles.authors: Iragavarapu GP, Varsha HJ, Vembar SS, Ghosh B
articles.details: This theoretical study used a Markov chain model combined with single-cell RNA-seq, RT-qPCR, and RNA-seq data to examine the biased hierarchical switching of Plasmodium falciparum var genes (~60 members encoding PfEMP1 variants). The analysis established a hierarchical var expression pattern and showed, when host immune response was included, that the hierarchy is beneficial when variants are cleared at variable rates: variants cleared more efficiently by the immune system are expressed more stably and at higher levels, while slowly cleared variants are quickly turned off. Stable variants showed greater IgM binding affinity. The authors conclude this provides a mechanistic basis for the observed switching hierarchy.

articles.headline: Optimized naphthyridine PI4K inhibitor cuts parasitemia in malaria mouse model
articles.title_original: Optimization of 2,8-Diaryl-1,5-naphthyridines as Plasmodium falciparum Phosphatidylinositol 4-Kinase Inhibitors with Improved ADME Profiles and In Vivo Efficacy.
articles.authors: Dziwornu GA, Seanego D, Fienberg S, Sypu VS, Salomane N, Krugmann L, Taylor D, Masike K, Njoroge M, Boonyalai N, Lee MCS, Godoy LC, Pasaje CF, Niles JC, Basarab GS, Coulson LB, Ghorpade SR, Chibale K
articles.details: This medicinal-chemistry study optimized 2,8-diaryl-1,5-naphthyridine inhibitors of Plasmodium phosphatidylinositol 4-kinase IIIβ to improve their physicochemical and pharmacokinetic properties. A new frontrunner, compound 27, showed an improved pharmacokinetic profile and reduced parasitaemia by 91% at 4 × 50 mg/kg QD doses in the humanized NOD-scid IL-2Rγnull mouse model of Plasmodium falciparum malaria. Compound 27 caused no hERG channel inhibition at high concentrations and no mammalian cytotoxicity, but showed low selectivity against related human lipid kinases (PI3Kα and PI4Kβ), with significantly higher selectivity margins against the human MINK1 and MAP4K4 kinases.

articles.headline: New 4-aminoquinoline amodiachins active against drug-resistant P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Discovery of Amodiachins, a Novel Class of 4-Aminoquinoline Antimalarials Active against Multidrug-Resistant Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Handford MJ, Li Y, Riscoe T, Liebman KM, Zhang X, Kelly JX, Riscoe MK
articles.details: This structure-activity relationship study optimized amodiaquine analog antimalarials (amodiachins, ADCs) for metabolic stability and in vivo activity, testing them against drug-sensitive and drug-resistant P. falciparum strains and in Plasmodium yoelii murine models. Adding a piperidine ring and varied N-alkyl substitutions enhanced activity, stability, and efficacy. Compound 43 (ADC-028) showed nanomolar activity against drug-sensitive and multidrug-resistant P. falciparum, minimal cytotoxicity, and favorable murine microsomal stability (t1/2 = 48.2 min). In vivo it achieved a nonrecrudescence dose at 16 mg/kg/d and a single-dose cure at 50 mg/kg, with metabolic stability (T1/2 = 84 h) and oral bioavailability (F = 76%). The authors identify 43 as a candidate to overcome drug-resistant malaria.

articles.headline: Fluorescent marinoquinoline derivative inhibits malaria parasite protease activity
articles.title_original: Fluorescent Marinoquinoline Derivative as Inhibitors of Plasmodium falciparum: SAR Analysis, Mode of Action and In Vivo Studies.
articles.authors: Santos Barbosa P, Souza GE, Maluf SEC, Bonatto V, Moura CS, Mendes GR, Valdes TA, Annunciato Y, Rossetto BDS, Ventura PDS, Ortin GGD, da Silva W, Icimoto MY, Ferreira ADS, Cruz FC, Teles CBG, Pereira DB, Cassiano GC, Santana S, Prudêncio M, Barbosa CS, Moura IMR, Giampauli RM, De Sousa IL, Rocco SA, Gazarini ML, Correia CRD, Aguiar ACC, Guido RVC
articles.details: This study explored the mechanism of action of marinoquinolines (MQ) by synthesizing 20 new fluorescent analogues and performing structure-activity relationship analysis. Compound 19 showed favorable in vitro (IC50 3D7 = 0.28 μM; CC50 HepG2 = 53 μM), ex vivo (EC50 Pf = 1.2 μM; EC50 Pv = 0.53 μM), and in vivo activity, with a 3 × 50 mg/kg oral dose producing a 96% reduction in parasitemia in Plasmodium berghei-infected mice, plus favorable physicochemical and pharmacokinetic properties. Compound 19 selectively accumulates in infected erythrocytes, enters the digestive vacuole, and inhibits P. falciparum proteolytic activity, suggesting MQs act as protease inhibitors. The authors conclude MQs are promising antimalarial lead candidates.

articles.headline: Transcription factor PfAP2-06B controls P. falciparum erythrocyte invasion
articles.title_original: An ApiAP2 Family Transcriptional Factor PfAP2-06B Regulates Erythrocyte Invasion Indirectly in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Shi Q, Wan K, Gong Y, Pang J, Liu Y, Tang J, Zhang Q, Cao J, Shen L
articles.details: Lab study of the ApiAP2 transcription factor PfAP2-06B (PF3D7_0613800) in the intraerythrocytic cycle of Plasmodium falciparum. Conditional knockdown of PfAP2-06B caused defective asexual growth and impaired erythrocyte invasion. Bulk RNA-seq showed PfAP2-06B modulates invasion-related genes at the schizont stage, and single-cell RNA-seq indicated it influences invasion gene expression and contributes to stochastic cell-to-cell expression variation. The authors conclude PfAP2-06B is critical for erythrocyte invasion and a potential target for new malaria control strategies.

articles.headline: Blocking PKCθ protects mice from cerebral malaria brain injury
articles.title_original: Inhibition of PKCθ Abrogates CD8(+) T Cell-Mediated Neurotoxicity in Murine Cerebral Malaria.
