articles.authors: Albrecht-Schgör K, Stefan VE, Steinlechner M, Humer D, Siegmund K, Peer S, Gruber T, Überegger M, Zur Nedden S, Baier-Bitterlich G, Lackner P, Schmutzhard E, Thuille N, Klepsch V, Baier G
articles.details: Mouse study testing whether protein kinase C θ (PKCθ) drives CD8+ T cell-mediated injury in experimental cerebral malaria. Using PKCθ-null mice on a C57BL/6N background infected with Plasmodium berghei ANKA, PKCθ deficiency significantly improved survival without changing parasite burden in blood or brain. Loss of PKCθ skewed T cells toward central memory rather than effector memory phenotypes, reducing CD8+ T cell sequestration in the cerebral microvasculature, preventing neurovascular damage and alleviating neurological signs. The authors suggest repurposing clinical PKCθ inhibitors as host-targeted interventions for cerebral malaria.

articles.headline: Review of intracellular parasite burden, transmission, and vaccines
articles.title_original: Intracellular Parasitic Infections Caused by Plasmodium falciparum, Leishmania spp., Toxoplasma gondii, Echinococcus multilocularis, Among Key Pathogens: Global Burden, Transmission Dynamics, and Vaccine Advances-A Narrative Review with Contextual Insights from Armenia.
articles.authors: Sargsyan T, Stepanyan L, Tsaturyan A, Palumbo R, Vicidomini C, Roviello GN
articles.details: This review examines intracellular parasitic infections caused by protozoan, helminthic, and arthropod-borne parasites—such as toxoplasmosis, leishmaniasis, and tick-borne diseases—covering parasite classification, immune evasion, diagnostic challenges, transmission dynamics, and control strategies, with a focus on recent experimental and licensed vaccine advances and a 'One Health' approach. Armenia serves as a case study illustrating how local ecological conditions, vector distribution, public health capacity, and social determinants shape the national burden, highlighting challenges such as limited access to advanced diagnostics, underreporting, and the need for stronger surveillance.

articles.headline: Review of methods to induce malaria parasite gametocyte formation
articles.title_original: Induction of gametocytogenesis in human malaria parasites: from stress to genome editing.
articles.authors: Subramaniam R, Chiew ZY, Zuhaidi NDB, Lau YL, Cheong FW
articles.details: Mini review summarizing methods used to induce gametocytogenesis—the transition from asexual to sexual-stage gametocytes—in human Plasmodium species in vitro. It spans conventional approaches using environmental stressors through to genome-editing technology that makes precise modifications to sexual conversion-related genes. The authors note that despite major progress, scalability of gametocytogenesis induction remains limited, especially for non-falciparum species, and conclude that integrating both approaches is crucial for developing efficient induction methods relevant to malaria transmission control and eradication.

articles.headline: Mosquito metabolic pathways shape avian Plasmodium transmission
articles.title_original: Avian malaria and the overlooked metabolic pathways underlying mosquito-Plasmodium interactions.
articles.authors: Garcia-Longoria L, Berthomieu A, Hellgren O, Rivero A
articles.details: Review combined with transcriptomic analysis examining non-immune physiological and metabolic pathways underlying mosquito–Plasmodium interactions, focused on avian malaria. Comparing infected and uninfected mosquitoes at various infection stages, the authors identify differential expression of numerous metabolic pathways essential for Plasmodium development, including genes for the parasite's energetic needs, digestive enzymes aiding midgut barrier traversal, and salivary enzymes enabling blood meal uptake. They argue the parasite modulates these pathways to enhance and prolong infection and transmission, and call for an integrative approach to find novel targets for controlling transmission.

articles.headline: Adequate bed-net ownership linked to less child malaria in Kenya
articles.title_original: Long-lasting insecticidal net ownership and malaria infection by socio-economic status: a cross-sectional household study in an area along Lake Victoria, Kenya.
articles.authors: Iwashita H, Nagi S, Bahati F, Kagaya W, Larson PS, Kongere J, Kanoi BN, Hayasaka R, Sugishita T, Gitaka J, Kaneko A
articles.details: A household survey in the Lake Victoria region of Suba North Sub-County, Homa Bay County, Western Kenya (June–September 2021) found that sufficient LLIN ownership (one net per ≤2 people) was associated with lower PCR-confirmed Plasmodium infection in children, after stratifying by asset-based socio-economic status (SES). Using a generalized linear model, sufficient ownership was significantly associated with lower infection in middle-SES households (OR 0.32, 95% CI 0.12–0.92), with a non-significant trend in low-SES households (OR 0.59, 95% CI 0.15–2.91); combining low and middle SES gave OR 0.37 (95% CI 0.17–0.87). The authors conclude that promoting the WHO ratio of one net per two people can reduce malaria risk.

articles.headline: Stored malaria RDT cassettes stay accurate for a month
articles.title_original: Are malaria rapid diagnostic test results stable over time to support verification of surveillance data?
articles.authors: Ngufor C, Lindblade KA, Atobatele S, Mpimbaza A, Ahogni I, Ssewante N, Akpiroroh E, Kpemasse A, Okoro O, Agaba B, Cooper S, Griffith K, Humes M
articles.details: A prospective observational study across 48 health facilities in Benin, Nigeria, and Uganda (June–September 2023) found that stored RDT cassettes remained highly stable over one month and can reliably reflect initial test results. Using an AI-powered RDT reader (HealthPulse, Audere) to photograph tests immediately, at one week, and at one month—with images interpreted by a trained panel—45,155 of 54,251 RDTs (83.2%) had valid readings at all three time points; at one month, 95.1% of initially positive and 95.3% of initially negative results were unchanged, with a PPV of 96.3% (95% CI 96.1–96.5) and NPV of 93.8% (95% CI 93.4–94.1). Most result changes occurred within the first week, and faint lines were associated with higher rates of change in both directions. The authors conclude that stored cassettes can reliably verify health-facility surveillance records.

articles.headline: New P. falciparum PX-domain protein PfPX2 linked to vesicle trafficking
articles.title_original: Characterization of a phosphoinositide-binding protein containing a PHOX homology domain in the malaria parasite Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Roucheray S, Stastny D, Kaur H, Tahotna D, Dacks JB, Griac P, Richard D
articles.details: This molecular study characterized Pf3D7_0704400, a putative phosphoinositide (PIP)-binding protein with a PX domain in Plasmodium falciparum, named PfPX2. In silico structural analysis showed both canonical and non-canonical PIP-binding motifs and a positively charged binding pocket; lipid-binding assays showed the PX domain bound all PIP species with a preference for PI3P, PI5P, and PI(3,5)P2. Immunofluorescence localized PfPX2 to the Golgi apparatus and the micronemes in developing schizonts, and proximity labelling identified PfSortilin, the clathrin heavy chain, and PfDyn1 as potential interactors. The authors suggest PfPX2 participates in vesicular trafficking between the Golgi apparatus and the micronemes, with orthologues found across apicomplexans and other alveolates.

articles.headline: Nanopore sequencing tells malaria recrudescence from new infection
articles.title_original: Rapid multiplexed nanopore amplicon sequencing to distinguish Plasmodium falciparum recrudescence from new infection in antimalarial drug trials.
articles.authors: Holzschuh A, Lerch A, Nsanzabana C
articles.details: This methods study evaluated multiplexed nanopore amplicon sequencing (AmpSeq) targeting six P. falciparum microhaplotypes to distinguish recrudescence from new infection in antimalarial drug trials. Sensitivity and specificity were tested using defined mixtures of four laboratory strains (3D7:K1:HB3:FCB1), and genetic diversity was assessed with 20 paired patient samples from a clinical trial. The assay detected minority clones as low as 1:100:100:100, showed high specificity (false-positive haplotypes <0.01%), and reproducibility of 98% (intra-assay) and 97% (inter-assay); marker diversity was high (cpmp HE=0.99, 28 haplotypes). It distinguished recrudescence from new infection in 17/20 cases (85%) across all six markers. Authors conclude nanopore AmpSeq is a feasible, robust tool for rapid molecular correction of drug-efficacy estimates.

articles.headline: Correction notice for ESCRT-III extracellular vesicle study in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Correction: The ESCRT-III machinery participates in the production of extracellular vesicles and protein export during Plasmodium falciparum infection.
articles.authors: Avalos-Padilla Y, Georgiev VN, Lantero E, Pujals S, Verhoef R, Borgheti-Cardoso LN, Albertazzi L, Dimova R, Fernàndez-Busquets X
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Zanzibar malaria epidemic linked to temperature, urban high-risk groups
articles.title_original: The November-2023-March-2024 malaria epidemic in Zanzibar: a spatiotemporal epidemiological analysis.
articles.authors: Kooiman F, Ali MH, Alifrangis M, Shija SJ, Hassan WS, Schiøler KL, Saleh F
articles.details: A spatiotemporal epidemiological analysis of a 21-week malaria epidemic in Zanzibar comprising 23,569 confirmed cases. The study characterized affected individuals' risk profiles, identified spatial-temporal hotspots, and assessed associations with precipitation and temperature. Higher malaria risk was found among males, individuals aged 15-35, urban residents, and those not sleeping under insecticide-treated nets. One significant space-time cluster was identified in the urban southwest of Unguja. Weekly case counts were significantly associated with average weekly temperature at an 8-week lag. The authors conclude this represents a serious setback for malaria elimination, calling for intensified interventions targeting high-risk populations.

articles.headline: Antimalarial drugs and bradykinin identified as ligands of PfSR10
articles.title_original: Identification of antimalarial drugs and bradykinin as ligands of the Plasmodium membrane protein PfSR10.
articles.authors: Jiang D, Wen X, Han JF, Cheng QX, Zhang R, Zheng Y, Zheng K, Huang SH, Chen JY, Li SW, Yang ZS, Han B, Tie L, Yang F, Zhang PJ, Xiao P, Lin H, Yu X, Sun JP
articles.details: A molecular and structural study of the Plasmodium falciparum serpentine receptor PfSR10. The authors found that antimalarial drugs—chloroquine, dihydroartemisinin, piperaquine-tetraphosphate-tetrahydrate, and primaquine diphosphate—act as PfSR10 agonists inducing coupling with human Gi/Gq proteins, confirmed via biochemical reconstitution and cryo-EM. Using proteomic profiling, they identified bradykinin as an endogenous agonist activating PfSR10, with ligand binding and conformational changes characterized through mutagenesis and FlAsH-BRET assays. The findings establish PfSR10 as a receptor for both antimalarials and host peptides, highlighting its dual role in drug action and host-parasite communication, with implications for malaria pathogenesis and therapeutic development.

articles.headline: Review of cellular aging biomarkers in malaria host-parasite relationships
articles.title_original: Critical Understanding of the Influence of Cellular Aging Biomarkers on Host-Parasite Relationships Serving as a Key Platform for Malaria Eradication.
articles.authors: Anzaku DO, Afolabi IS
articles.details: A comprehensive review examining the interaction between Plasmodium parasites and their hosts, focusing on infection mechanisms, host immune response, and parasite immune-evasion strategies. It notes that P. falciparum causes the most severe infection, which can lead to chronic morbidity, anemia, and cerebral malaria. The review emphasizes understanding the temporal and spatial dimensions of host-parasite adaptation for forecasting parasite evolution and spread, and narrows its focus to the interplay between malaria infection and cellular aging biomarkers. The authors propose that understanding this dynamic could provide novel insights into disease pathophysiology and serve as a platform for malaria control efforts.

articles.headline: Pesticide residues in Amazon soils pose human health cancer risks
articles.title_original: Potential Risks to Human Health Caused by the Use of Pesticides in Soils of Three Municipalities Impacted by Localized Malaria in the Brazilian Amazon.
articles.authors: Santos LFD, Deus RJA, Sucupira IMC, Brasil DDSB, Mendes RA
articles.details: Soils from three municipalities in the Legal Amazon (Mazagão, Porto Velho, and Cantá) were analyzed by gas chromatography for DDT and alphacypermethrin, revealing DDT and its metabolites that indicate slow degradation, especially in Cantá (average 2.694 mg/kg total DDT). Alphacypermethrin was highest in Porto Velho (average 0.364 mg/kg), possibly due to long-lasting insecticidal net (LLIN) use. The incremental lifetime cancer risk reached 2.93 × 10⁻³ for DDT and 1.17 × 10⁻¹ for alphacypermethrin, and the total non-carcinogenic risk index reached an extreme 336.61. The authors conclude the pesticides pose no ecological risk but do pose human health risks (mainly through food intake), indicating irregular LLIN use and the need for continuous monitoring.

articles.headline: Pure red cell aplasia from falciparum malaria and parvovirus B19 coinfection
articles.title_original: Transient Pure Red Cell Aplasia in a Case of Plasmodium falciparum and Parvovirus B19 Coinfection.
articles.authors: John CV, Basu A, Bose D, Ray Y
articles.details: A case report of a 22-year-old man, a native of West Bengal, India, diagnosed with complicated P. falciparum malaria and treated with intravenous artesunate followed by artemether-lumefantrine. Despite parasitemia clearance, he developed persistent hypoproliferative anemia without hemolysis and positive antinuclear antibodies; bone marrow examination showed markedly decreased erythroid cells with giant pronormoblasts typical of pure red cell aplasia, and parvovirus B19 quantitative PCR showed 2.1 × 10³ IU/mL. He recovered fully after intravenous immunoglobulin and a tapering prednisone course. The authors highlight that acute B19V coinfection should be considered in malaria patients with persistent anemia despite parasitological clearance.

articles.headline: New sequencing panel detects full-gene P. falciparum drug-resistance mutations
articles.title_original: Multiplex long-amplicon sequencing for comprehensive molecular surveillance of Plasmodium falciparum resistance to artemisinin and partner drugs in artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs).
articles.authors: Li F, Zhang J, Xu S, Wang Y, Mao Z, Zhu G, Liu Y, Cao J
articles.details: This methodological study developed an optimized multiplex long-amplicon PCR panel for comprehensive molecular surveillance of P. falciparum resistance to artemisinin and ACT partner drugs. Six genes were amplified: four artemisinin-resistance markers (Pfk13, Pfcoronin, Pfap2μ, Pfubp1) and two partner-drug markers (Pfmdr1, Pfcrt), standardized to 2.5±0.2 kb, with full-length coverage of Pfk13, Pfcoronin and Pfap2μ. Validation used 11 mock samples (parasitemia 1% to 0.0001%) and 16 field venous-blood samples on Illumina sequencing. Sensitivity was 50 parasites/μL for dried blood spots and 5 parasites/μL for venous blood, all targets reaching 100% coverage, at $15.60 per sample. Authors conclude it is a scalable, cost-effective tool for resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Updated trial protocol: cash incentives to cut malaria in Kenya
articles.title_original: The second-round evaluation of financial incentives on malaria prevalence in the Lake Victoria Basin, Kenya: updated study protocol for a cluster-randomized controlled trial.
articles.authors: Matsumoto T, Nagashima M, Kagaya W, Okomo G, Kongere J, Oginga J, Opiyo V, Gitaka J, Kaneko A
articles.details: This is an updated protocol for a second-round cluster-randomized controlled trial testing demand-side malaria interventions in Suba South Sub-county, Homa Bay County, Kenya. The original 92 clusters will be re-randomized to conditional cash transfer, lottery incentive scheme, or control arms, each with updated malaria education and financial incentives linked to negative malaria test results (rewards adjusted for local inflation). Malaria prevalence will be reassessed by RDT, microscopy, and PCR at 3 and 6 months post-intervention; primary outcomes are changes in prevalence, LLIN usage, and malaria knowledge/perception, with data from both rounds combined to improve statistical power. No results are reported (protocol; trial UMIN000053284).

articles.headline: P. vivax dominates rising malaria burden in Shangla, Pakistan
articles.title_original: Fighting malaria in Shangla District, Pakistan: insight into epidemiology, risk factors, and strategic control measures (2020-2024).
articles.authors: Ullah I, Baig RM, Riaz A, Mukhtar M, Khan MA, Zaib A, Muhammad N, Khan S, Ali A, Ahmad A, Del Angel Nunez RM
articles.details: This 5-year retrospective epidemiological study analyzed 130,401 laboratory-confirmed malaria cases among 572,696 febrile individuals screened from 2020 to 2024 in District Shangla, Pakistan, using district surveillance data. Annual Parasite Incidence rose from 2 to 61 per 1000 and Test Positivity Rate from 3% to 32% through 2023, then declined in 2024 (API 46/1000; TPR 27%) after targeted interventions. P. vivax comprised 95% of cases, P. falciparum 4.6%, and mixed infections 0.4%; only 18% were microscopy-confirmed. Adults ≥15 years were 67% of cases with male predominance (54%); only 14% completed primaquine regimens. Authors call for an integrated, climate-adaptive elimination strategy including universal G6PD testing.

articles.headline: Most Nigerian caregivers show good malaria knowledge, better prevention
articles.title_original: Malaria knowledge and preventive practices among caregivers of under-five children in Southwest Nigeria.
articles.authors: Adeleke TO, Akinlosotu MA, Samson TK, Adeleke OV, Ajala DE, Olasinde Y, Oluwasanya AN, Esan DT
articles.details: This hospital-based cross-sectional study assessed malaria knowledge and preventive practices among 797 caregivers of children under five in Southwestern Nigeria, using a pretested interviewer-administered questionnaire and multiple logistic regression. Of those surveyed, 689 (86.4%) demonstrated good malaria knowledge. Caregivers from Ondo and Ogun States had significantly higher odds of good knowledge than those from Osun State. Good knowledge was significantly associated with better prevention practices (OR=2.347, 95% CI 1.251–4.406, p=0.008). Authors conclude these findings can guide targeted public health interventions for malaria control in Nigeria.

articles.headline: Computational study flags harmala alkaloids as antimalarial candidates
articles.title_original: Computational insights into harmala alkaloids as novel antimalarial candidates against Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Dipto KZ, Shariar R, Saha CK, Huzaifa A, Robin TB, Patil RB, Alam MT, Rafi MIH, Faruk MAZ, Moin AT, Zinnah KMA, Hasanuzzaman M, Islam T
articles.details: This in silico study evaluated harmala alkaloids, obtained from plants and microbes such as Peganum harmala, as candidate antimalarials against P. falciparum. The work builds on findings that harmane, an alkaloid from the mosquito gut endophyte Delftia tsuruhatensis TC1, can impede parasite transmission. Harmine and harmaline reportedly show antimalarial activity by inhibiting protein kinase 4 (PK4), an enzyme essential for parasite survival, with lower toxicity and inhibition of both blood-stage growth and transmission. Using ADME analysis, molecular docking, MD simulation and toxicity analysis, the authors identify harmala alkaloids as potential inhibitors of key P. falciparum proteins, warranting further in vitro and in vivo validation. No clinical results are reported.

articles.headline: Lightweight deep-learning network detects malaria parasites in blood smears
articles.title_original: DANet a lightweight dilated attention network for malaria parasite detection.
articles.authors: Ali A, Pal R, Dey I, Cuevas E, Perez-Cisneros M, Sarkar R
articles.details: This computational study introduces DANet, a lightweight dilated attention network (~2.3 million parameters) for detecting malaria parasites in red blood cell smear images. It was trained and tested on the NIH Malaria Dataset of 27,558 images (19,290 training, 2,756 validation, 5,512 test) from 150 infected and 50 healthy individuals. DANet achieved an F1-score of 97.86%, accuracy of 97.95%, and AUC-PR of 0.98, validated by 5-fold cross-validation and Grad-CAM visualizations. The authors conclude it offers superior efficiency over hybrid CNN-transformer models and can run on edge devices like the Raspberry Pi 4 for resource-constrained settings.

articles.headline: Caregiver factors linked to low malaria vaccine uptake in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Caregivers' practices and factors associated with malaria vaccine uptake among under-five children in the Tiko Health District, Cameroon: A community based cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Abiache IM, Nsobinenyui D, Ukah CE, Kumenyuy YL, Ngeha NC, Wefuan R, Dang SZ, Ndip NE, Feguem MP, Nsagha DS
articles.details: A community-based cross-sectional study of 410 caregivers of children aged 0–5 years in the Tiko Health District, Cameroon, assessed RTS,S/AS01 malaria vaccine uptake using a structured questionnaire and logistic regression, finding that only 32.2% (n=132) of children had received the vaccine. Of those vaccinated, 72.0% completed the recommended doses and 82.6% of caregivers maintained vaccination records. Higher uptake was associated with female caregivers (aOR 4.16, 95% CI 1.47–11.75), caregivers in health professions (aOR 2.87), biological parents (aOR 11.44), household income of 89–179 USD (aOR 2.76), trust in health workers (aOR 6.12), and information from healthcare providers (aOR 7.60), while prior malaria infection in children lowered odds (aOR 0.31). The authors conclude uptake is suboptimal and call for stronger caregiver education and trust-building.

articles.headline: Ceiling-mounted nets cut indoor mosquito biting in Peruvian Amazon
articles.title_original: Efficacy of ceiling-mounted mosquito nets for malaria vector control in a Peruvian Amazon riverine community: A stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial.
articles.authors: Quispe AM, Álvarez-Antonio C, Gutierrez Rodriguez F, Carey C, Rodríguez-Ferrucci H, Inti C, Kim ES
articles.details: A stepped-wedge cluster-randomized trial in the Llanchama riverine community (Loreto, Peru) tested ceiling-mounted mosquito nets for Anopheles vector control across 69 households randomized into three clusters receiving the intervention at staggered three-month intervals. Entomological indices were measured via human landing catches over 12-hour periods. Per-protocol analysis showed renovated households had a 55% reduction in indoor Anopheles counts (95% CI 33%–74%; p=0.004), 60% lower indoor bites per person per night (95% CI 27%–78%; p=0.003), and 61% lower indoor bites per person per hour (95% CI 15%–83%; p=0.018); peridomestic indices did not differ significantly. The authors conclude this structural modification is a promising strategy to lower malaria risk.

articles.headline: Tfh2 and CCR7+ Tfh1 cells linked to protective malaria antibodies
articles.title_original: Tfh2 and a subset of Tfh1 cells associate with antibody-mediated immunity to malaria.
articles.authors: Soon MS, Oyong DA, Dooley NL, Mukhiya R, Pava Z, Andrew DW, Loughland JR, McCarthy JS, Chan JA, Beeson JG, Engwerda CR, Haque A, Boyle MJ
articles.details: This immunology study mapped peripheral blood T follicular helper (pTfh) cell heterogeneity in healthy individuals before and during controlled human malaria infection (CHMI) using parallel single-cell RNA-Seq and VDJ-Seq. The authors identified two pTfh1 subsets distinguishable by CCR7 expression: Tfh1-CCR7− cells showed higher baseline inflammatory cytokine and cytotoxicity-associated gene expression, while Tfh1-CCR7+ cells had higher germinal center signatures. During CHMI, Tfh1-CCR7+, Tfh1-CCR7−, and Tfh2 cells all clonally expanded and activated, but only Tfh1-CCR7+ and Tfh2 cells positively associated with protective antibody production. The authors conclude these two subsets are linked to antibody-mediated immunity to malaria.

articles.headline: Nanozyme immunoassay detects malaria transmission-competent individuals
articles.title_original: Evaluation of Malaria Parasite Transmission Competency Using a Nanozyme-Based Immunodiagnostic Targeting Female Gamete Antigen Release.
articles.authors: Haghighi T, Najer A, Broto M, Dahalan FA, Churchyard A, Yahiya S, Famodimu MT, Tunnicliff M, Abdelwahed A, Tachibana M, Ishino T, Aniweh Y, Awandare GA, Mahamar A, Vanheer LN, Bousema T, Drakeley C, Dicko A, Stone W, Baum J, Stevens MM
articles.details: This study developed a nanozyme-based immunoassay to detect the Plasmodium falciparum female gametocyte activation antigen PfG377, which is released during gametogenesis, as a way to identify transmission-competent individuals. In cultured parasites, PfG377 levels were higher in supernatants of activated versus nonactivated cultures and those treated with transmission-blocking drugs. Using patient samples from a transmission-focused clinical trial, including patients treated with the gametocytocidal drug primaquine (PQ), PQ-treated samples showed significantly lower PfG377 signals two days after treatment, consistent with PQ-sterilized gametocytes failing to activate. The authors present this as a pathfinder for field rapid diagnostics supporting malaria elimination.

articles.headline: Monthly SP fails for malaria prevention in pregnancy, selects resistance
articles.title_original: Recurrent Plasmodium falciparum parasitemia and drug resistance mutations during intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy in Uganda.
articles.authors: Kizza J, Katairo T, Kakuru A, Nsengimaana B, Esilu T, Wiringilimaana I, Semakuba FD, Gerlovina I, Hathaway N, Briggs J, Tukwasibwe S, Kiwuwa SM, Kamya MR, Nankabirwa JI, Dorsey G, Rosenthal PJ
articles.details: This study sequenced 1377 samples from pregnant women with asymptomatic parasitemia in a trial comparing monthly sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP), dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP), and DP+SP for intermittent preventive treatment (IPTp) in Busia, Uganda. Among 771 day-of-initiation samples, five PfDHFR and PfDHPS resistance mutations were nearly 100% prevalent, and the high-level PfDHFR I164L plus PfDHPS A581G mutations had combined prevalence of 26.5%. The 28-day cumulative risk of recurrent parasitemia was 57.8% (SP), 4.1% (DP), and 3.9% (DP+SP); recrudescence was 40.1%, 2.0%, and 0.8%, with risks greatest in primigravidae. In the SP arm, combined I164L/A581G prevalence rose significantly from 24.9% to 35.2%. Authors conclude IPTp-SP had poor efficacy and selected for increased resistance.

articles.headline: Correction notice for paper on malaria-associated acute kidney injury
articles.title_original: Correction: Simião et al. Involvement of Inflammatory Cytokines, Renal NaPi-IIa Cotransporter, and TRAIL Induced-Apoptosis in Experimental Malaria-Associated Acute Kidney Injury. Pathogens 2024, 13, 376.
articles.authors: Simião GM, Parreira KS, Klein SG, Ferreira FB, Freitas FS, Silva EFD, Silva NM, Silva MVD, Lima WR
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Zanzibari residents unfamiliar with acquired immunity and rebound malaria
articles.title_original: Reconsidering rebound malaria: Zanzibari voices on acquired immunity, risk, and history.
articles.authors: Graboyes M, Lefevre D, Meta J, Minu-Sepehr A
articles.details: This qualitative study drew on 98 interviews with residents of Zanzibar to examine awareness of "acquired immunity" and "rebound malaria," outlining four periods of the island's malaria history including a rebound epidemic in the 1970s-1980s. Despite roughly 70 years of international malaria work, members of the lay public had little to no familiarity with these concepts, and there was no oral or archival evidence that researchers had ever explained them. People shown a brief visual tool and given a five-to-ten-minute conversation showed changed understanding and found the information valuable. Authors conclude ethical malaria interventions require fuller disclosure of risks, more robust consent, and greater public education.

articles.headline: A simple strategy to cut malaria infection rates in babies
articles.title_original: A simple strategy to cut malaria infection rates in babies.
articles.authors: O'Leary K
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: AI plus pharmacometrics to tailor malaria and TB dosing in Africa
articles.title_original: Artificial intelligence coupled to pharmacometrics modelling to tailor malaria and tuberculosis treatment in Africa.
articles.authors: Turon G, Mulubwa M, Montaner A, Njoroge M, Chibale K, Duran-Frigola M
articles.details: This methods paper proposes a pipeline coupling machine learning and artificial intelligence with physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) and non-linear mixed effects (NLME) modelling to identify drug-gene pairs of potential pharmacogenetic interest for African populations. Drug-gene pairs are ranked using knowledge embedding techniques on public structural and bioactivity data, refined with a large language model, then evaluated for sensitivity in PBPK analysis with relevant variants inspected via NLME for dose optimization. The authors focus on genes of potential clinical relevance in Africa and apply the approach to malaria and tuberculosis therapies, many of which remain pharmacogenetically uncharacterised. No quantitative outcome results are reported in the abstract.

articles.headline: Most Ugandans surveyed accept gene-drive mosquitoes despite low awareness
articles.title_original: "Have Scientists Done Enough?" Community Perspectives on Gene Drive-Modified Mosquitoes for Malaria Control in Northern Uganda: A Mixed-Methods Study.
articles.authors: Madraa G, Musoke P, Nantale R, Bongomin F
articles.details: This mixed-methods study assessed public awareness and acceptance of gene-drive modified mosquitoes (GDMMs) for malaria control, combining a structured survey of 301 participants (guided by the Health Belief Model and Theory of Planned Behaviour) with 22 in-depth interviews. Of 301 participants, only 60 (19.9%) were aware of GDMMs, yet 193 (64.1%, 95% CI 58.4-69.5) reported acceptance. Independent predictors of acceptance were favourable attitude (aPR 1.25, 95% CI 1.05-1.49, p=0.013), high subjective norm score (aPR 1.28, 95% CI 1.08-1.51, p=0.004), and high perceived behavioural control (aPR 1.23, 95% CI 1.05-1.46, p=0.013). The authors conclude acceptance is shaped by attitudes, social norms, and behavioural control.

articles.headline: Deep-learning tool predicts function of uncharacterized Plasmodium proteins
articles.title_original: PlasmoFP: leveraging deep learning to predict protein function of uncharacterized proteins across the malaria parasite genus.
articles.authors: Srivastava HR, Berenberg D, Qassab O, Wang Z, Bonneau R, Carlton JM
articles.details: The authors introduce PlasmoFP, deep learning models built to predict protein function across the genus Plasmodium, trained on structure-function relationships of phylogenetically relevant SAR supergroup proteins to overcome Plasmodium proteins' low similarity to model organisms. The models estimate epistemic uncertainty, control false discovery rates, and were validated against proteins with manually curated GO terms and experimentally characterized proteins. Integrating PlasmoFP predictions with existing annotations reduced the proportion of proteins lacking Gene Ontology terms from 15-59% to 3-28% across 19 Plasmodium species, and raised fully annotated proteins from 7-42% to 36-68%.

articles.headline: Plasmodium and Haemoproteus parasites found in 13.5% of British wild birds
articles.title_original: The lineage diversity, spatiotemporal distribution and pathological significance of Plasmodium and Haemoproteus spp. infection of wild birds in Great Britain.
articles.authors: Heaver JP, John SK, Seilern-Macpherson K, Spiro S, Wilkinson V, Cunningham AA, Lawson B
articles.details: In a 15-year wildlife disease surveillance scheme, liver and/or spleen samples from 857 post-mortem wild birds (62 species, 27 families, eight orders) were screened by nested PCR for Plasmodium and Haemoproteus. Overall 13.5% (116 birds) tested positive, with 8.9% (76) Plasmodium and 4.7% (40) Haemoproteus; highest Plasmodium rates were in Paridae (36.3%, 4/11) and Turdidae (34.5%, 51/148). A significant cluster of Plasmodium cases occurred in Southeast England. Of 30 detected lineages (20 Haemoproteus, 10 Plasmodium), 23 were new to Great Britain and four apparently novel. Among 13 Plasmodium-positive Eurasian blackbirds examined histologically, only one was diagnosed with acute malaria as a contributory cause of death.

articles.headline: Age-structured model explores malaria vaccination and transmission control
articles.title_original: Age-structured vaccination strategies in mathematical modeling of malaria transmission.
articles.authors: Haringo AT, Obsu LL, Bushu FK
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: This study presents an age-structured mathematical model of malaria vaccination capturing human and mosquito host interactions. The authors established the model's mathematical properties and analyzed equilibrium stability in relation to the effective reproduction number (Re). The system has a malaria-free equilibrium, locally and globally asymptotically stable when Re<1, and a malaria-endemic equilibrium, globally asymptotically stable when Re>1. A sensitivity analysis of Re identified parameters influencing transmission for prioritization. The authors conclude that combining effective vaccination, treatment, and insecticide vector control could reduce transmission toward near-elimination, informing integrated malaria control planning.

articles.headline: Meta-analysis finds high P. falciparum HRP2/3 gene deletions in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: High Prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum HRP2/3 Gene Deletions in Ethiopia: Implications for Malaria Diagnosis and Treatment-A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
articles.authors: Gebrie H, Abere A, Gashaw A, Gebremeskele BT, Bukayaw YA, Bayable A, Mekuriaw BY, Yimer EG, Demeke AD
articles.journal: Can J Infect Dis Med Microbiol
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis pooled studies on Plasmodium falciparum PfHRP2/3 gene deletions, which can cause false-negative results on HRP2-based rapid diagnostic tests. From 932 initially identified studies, 11 articles were included using a random-effects model. The overall pooled prevalence of PfHRP2/3 gene deletions was 35.64% (95% CI 21.43-49.85); PfHRP2 deletions were 8.48% (95% CI 0.96-16.01), PfHRP3 deletions 23.74% (95% CI 12.16-35.32), and concurrent deletions of both genes 8.14% (95% CI 0.67-15.61). The authors conclude this high prevalence threatens continued use of PfHRP2/3-based RDTs in Ethiopia and recommend phasing them out and adopting PCR.

articles.headline: Platelet distribution width predicts malaria in pregnant women
articles.title_original: Predicting malaria in sudanese pregnant women: reliability of complete blood count.
articles.authors: Dura MC, Gürsoy B, Aktürk H, Aslan Ö, Hergüner M, Ezirmik E
articles.details: Retrospective study of 174 pregnant women (78 with confirmed malaria, 96 malaria-negative controls) comparing complete blood count parameters to assess their diagnostic value for malaria in pregnancy. Body mass index, hemoglobin, platelet count, and platelet distribution width were significantly lower in the malaria group (p<0.05), but only platelet distribution width remained significant in multivariate logistic regression. Platelet distribution width showed the highest diagnostic accuracy (area under the curve=0.857). The authors conclude it may be a reliable, accessible marker for malaria in pregnancy in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Large-scale larviciding shows no clear malaria reduction in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Large-scale intermittent larviciding intervention and associations with key malaria epidemiological parameters in Tanga Region, Tanzania.
articles.authors: Kailembo D, Gavana T, Kasagama E, Bernard J, Molteni F, Kisoka N, Yoram B, Kajange S, Lazaro S, Dismas C, Ross A, Chaki P, Lengeler C
articles.details: Controlled before-after study evaluating intermittent biolarviciding across three councils in Tanga Region, Tanzania (population over 1.1 million) spanning high, moderate, and low malaria risk strata, conducted June 2022 to April 2024 with three rounds per year of Bacillus thuringiensis var. israelensis and Bacillus sphaericus. Compared with paired control councils, larviciding showed no significant association with clinical malaria incidence: incidence rate ratios were 1.16 (95% CI 0.98–1.37) high, 0.86 (0.72–1.02) moderate, and 0.96 (0.76–1.22) low stratum, with similar results for test positivity. The authors found no strong evidence of impact and call for continuous year-round, cluster-randomized studies.

articles.headline: Optimizing lab methods for testing ivermectin against Anopheles mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Optimizing endectocide and ectocide compound evaluation in Anopheles malaria vectors.
articles.authors: Kobylinski KC, Hongsuwong T, Khemrattrakool P, Kaewkhao N, Kullasakboonsri R, Phanphoowong T, Sriwichai P, Hanboonkunupakarn B, Jittamala P, Tarning J
articles.details: Three in vitro experiments with ivermectin in Anopheles dirus and Anopheles minimus to refine endectocide/ectocide evaluation methods, testing effects of sugar diet, meal type (plasma vs fresh or frozen blood), and ivermectin uptake measured by LC-MS/MS. The Multivitamin diet substantially increased An. dirus LC50 versus Sucrose and Mix diets; ivermectin lethality rose in a blood meal versus plasma meal for An. dirus but the inverse occurred for An. minimus. Roughly 20% of ivermectin was lost in the midgut compared with the blood meal. The authors recommend the Mix diet to minimize control mortality and note unexplained meal-related effects, possibly from mosquito excretion.

articles.headline: Proposed integrated strategy to prevent malaria rebound in Africa
articles.title_original: Integrated vector and parasite management for malaria control in high-endemic areas: pummel and pin strategy to prevent malaria setbacks.
articles.authors: Ranjbar M, Opigo J
articles.details: Perspective/conceptual article arguing that high-endemic sub-Saharan African areas often stagnate or rebound despite control efforts, putting WHO 2030 targets (≥90% reduction in malaria mortality and morbidity) out of reach. Drawing on literature focused on Uganda, it proposes Integrated Vector and Parasite Management (IVPM) and a "Pummel and Pin" implementation framework that reduces both the parasite reservoir and vectorial capacity, emphasizing the neglected "Pin" component to sustain gains. The authors stress that feasibility and cost-effectiveness across endemicity levels remain priorities for operational research, given the approach is resource-intensive amid funding shortfalls.

articles.headline: Many Nigerian physicians lack malaria vaccine knowledge, survey finds
articles.title_original: Physicians' knowledge of, and willingness to encourage caregivers to partake in the RTS S/AS01 and R21 matrix M malaria vaccines roll out - a national survey in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Oboh MA, Adeleke OT, Adeleke OV, Awotunde TA
articles.details: Cross-sectional online survey of physicians across Nigeria's six geopolitical zones assessing knowledge of the RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccines and willingness to encourage caregivers to vaccinate. Of respondents, 68.9% knew about the malaria vaccine while 29.9% (152) did not; 64% correctly identified five years as the appropriate administration age, while 13.8% gave a higher age (5–10 years) and 22.2% did not respond. Most physicians were very willing to recommend the vaccines and felt caregivers were likely to accept them. The authors call for educational interventions addressing concerns about efficacy, safety, doses, affordability, and availability.

articles.headline: Fractional-order math model of malaria transmission with treatment and vector control
articles.title_original: Analysis of fractional-order model for the transmission dynamics of malaria via Caputo-Fabrizio and Atangana-Baleanu operators.
articles.authors: Agbata BC, Dervishi R, Gümüş M, Smerat A, Mbah GCE
articles.details: This modeling study developed a fractional-order mathematical model of malaria transmission incorporating human treatment and mosquito population management, using three non-integer differential operators (Caputo, Caputo-Fabrizio with exponential decay, and Atangana-Baleanu with a Mittag-Leffler kernel). Using fixed-point theory, the authors proved existence and uniqueness of solutions and ran numerical simulations across varying fractional orders. They found that increasing the fractional order slowed malaria spread, reduced the peak number of infections, and prolonged outbreak duration, reflecting memory-dependent dynamics. The authors concluded fractional models capture long-term transmission dynamics more accurately than classical models and recommended sustained, combined treatment and vector-control strategies.

articles.headline: Imported malaria dominates in Saudi Arabia; P. falciparum most common
articles.title_original: Malaria in travelers and local populations: Comprehensive study of incidence patterns and origin-based classification in Saudi Arabia.
articles.authors: Alharbi B, Ahmed M
articles.details: This study analyzed Ministry of Health surveillance data on malaria cases across 13 administrative regions of Saudi Arabia from January 2022 to December 2023, using chi-square tests to assess case origins, Plasmodium species, and age-group incidence. Of 1,453,451 febrile cases examined, 0.7% (10,779) were positive (0.9%, 6,460 of 688,629 in 2022; 0.6%, 4,319 of 764,822 in 2023). Jazan had the highest incidence (59%), followed by Makkah (20%) (P=0.046), with higher incidence in patients aged ≥15 years. By species, P. falciparum was highest at 63.5%, P. vivax–P. ovale 33%, P. malariae 0.5%, and mixed infections 3% (P=0.002). The authors concluded imported malaria predominated and called for targeted interventions for travelers.

articles.headline: Hybrid time-series machine-learning models improve malaria incidence forecasting
articles.title_original: Hybrid models combining trend and seasonality components with machine learning algorithms provide accurate forecasting of malaria incidence.
articles.authors: Hussain SSA, Bedi S, Yadav CP, Mohanty AK, Mahatme K, Tyagi S, Krishnan NMA, Kota SH, Sharma A
articles.details: This study compared forecasting models for malaria incidence using climatic and malaria data from Goa, India, spanning 2010 to 2019. Multi-collinearity analysis and SHAP were used to identify the most important predictors among 15 climatic variables; three machine-learning models (SVM, Random Forest, XGBoost), three time-series models (ARIMA, SARIMA, SARIMAX), and three hybrid models (RF-ARMA, SVM-ARMA, XGB-ARMA) were trained and tested. Climatic extremes influenced transmission more than average values. Machine-learning models had lower accuracy (RMSE 13–37) but high precision, while time-series models were more accurate (RMSE 5–41) but less precise; hybrid models combining both achieved RMSE 0.5–15. The authors concluded integrating time-series components into machine-learning models substantially enhances forecast accuracy and precision for malaria elimination planning.

articles.headline: New click-chemistry DNA probes track Plasmodium DNA synthesis without copper
articles.title_original: Vinyl-DNADetect Probes to Assess DNA Proliferation in Plasmodium Parasites using Inverse-Electron-Demand Diels-Alder Click Reactions.
articles.authors: Braun AC, Fisher GM, Hilko DH, Macdonald JR, Thomas S, Andrews KT, Poulsen SA
articles.details: This chemical biology study developed novel 5-vinyl-2'-deoxyuridine (VdU)-based metabolic probes, designated vinyl-DNADetect, to study DNA synthesis in Plasmodium parasites, which lack thymidine kinase and cannot incorporate the standard EdU probe. The probes were designed to bypass thymidine kinase requirements and to be detected via a catalyst-free, fixation-free inverse-electron-demand Diels-Alder (IEDDA) click reaction with a fluorogenic tetrazine, avoiding cytotoxic copper catalysis. Using flow cytometry and fluorescence microscopy, the authors quantified and visualized incorporation into Plasmodium DNA, with several probes showing high-efficiency labeling, demonstrated labeling in live P. falciparum-infected erythrocytes, and performed the first orthogonal VdU- and EdU-based pulse-chase double-staining experiment in wild-type Plasmodium.

articles.headline: Anopheles darlingi drives malaria transmission in Yanomami Amazon villages
articles.title_original: Bionomics and malaria infection rates in anopheline species in Yanomami villages of the Brazilian Amazon Forest.
articles.authors: Sánchez-Ribas J, Silva-do-Nascimento TF, Pereira-Ribeiro C, Santos-Neves MSA, Barnwell JW, Lourenço-de-Oliveira R, Oliveira-Ferreira J
articles.details: This longitudinal entomological survey was conducted from March 2013 to March 2015 in five Yanomami villages of the Marari community in the Brazilian Amazon, using MosqTent human-baited traps, Nasci aspirators, and CDC light traps, with molecular detection of parasites in Anopheles. A total of 1,962 adult Anopheles from seven species were collected. Anopheles darlingi predominated in Alapusi (87.9%), Gasolina (81.9%), and Taibrapa 1 (52.5%), while An. nuneztovari s.l. dominated in Taibrapa 2 (80.0%). An. darlingi was infected with both P. vivax and P. falciparum, with infection rates up to 2.1%; outdoor entomological inoculation rates ranged from 49.3 to 79.3. The authors concluded village-specific vector control strategies are needed.

articles.headline: Risk factors for locally acquired malaria infections in Zanzibar
articles.title_original: Determinants of locally acquired malaria infections in Zanzibar: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Fakih BS, Seidahmed O, Holzschuh A, Abdul R, Irema I, Shija SJ, Aliy SM, Ali A, Fink G, Yukich J, Hetzel MW
articles.details: A rolling cross-sectional survey in five Zanzibar districts tested household members of clinical index cases and neighbouring households using rapid diagnostic tests and qPCR. Among 17,891 surveyed, 15,151 (85%) had not travelled outside Zanzibar in the prior 60 days; of 7286 of these tested by qPCR, 2.6% (95% CI 2-3%) were positive. Higher infection odds occurred in those aged 5-15 (aOR 3.5) and 16-25 (aOR 1.9), houses with open windows (aOR 2.1), and moderately wet areas (aOR 3.4); bed net-owning households had lower odds (aOR 0.5). Authors recommend promoting bed nets, housing improvements, and targeting high-risk individuals.

articles.headline: Nutrient stress boosts parasite clag2 copies to keep host cell permeable
articles.title_original: Nutrient stress dramatically increases malaria parasite clag2 copy number to increase host cell permeability and enable pathogen survival.
articles.authors: Potchen NB, Silva TM, Kalia I, Lack JB, Desai SA
articles.details: This in vitro study examined how P. falciparum maintains the plasmodial surface anion channel (PSAC) that increases erythrocyte permeability to nutrients, normally linked to CLAG3. A CLAG3-knockout parasite kept partial PSAC activity and grew in nutrient-rich media but could not expand under more physiological nutrient levels. In vitro selection of the CLAG3-null parasite yielded a mutant able to expand under nutrient limitation with restored solute uptake despite absent CLAG3. Whole-genome sequencing revealed a dramatically increased clag2 copy number, confirmed by qPCR and DNA transfection, implicating CLAG2 in nutrient channel formation and revealing genome plasticity in malaria parasites.

articles.headline: In vitro malaria hypnozoites release specific candidate diagnostic metabolites
articles.title_original: In vitro cultured malaria hypnozoites leave a footprint of specific metabolites.
articles.authors: Pasini EM, Hakimi H, Chaiyawong N, Klop O, Zeeman AM, Nieuwenhuis I, van der Werff N, Kihara K, Kami K, Kocken CHM, Kaneko O
articles.details: This proof-of-concept study sought diagnostic markers for dormant P. vivax liver-stage hypnozoites, which reactivate to cause relapse and currently cannot be detected. Researchers combined in vitro Plasmodium cynomolgi hypnozoite cultures (a close proxy to P. vivax) with sensitive metabolomics. Specific hypnozoite-related metabolites were identified in the supernatant of hypnozoite-enriched liver-stage cultures. The authors suggest that, following in vivo validation in the P. cynomolgi/rhesus model and then in P. vivax-infected individuals, these markers could enable a rapid diagnostic test to identify hypnozoite carriers for targeted treatment and spare G6PD-deficient individuals unnecessary primaquine exposure.

articles.headline: Review of malaria control and elimination strategies in DR Congo
articles.title_original: Malaria in the Democratic Republic of Congo: how support programs consolidate elimination gains and protect at-risk populations from recrudescence.
articles.authors: Akilimali A, Mufungizi I, Rasheed S, Tague C, Shams Z, Shah RJ, Wilczyńska W, Siddiqui HT, Atif MH, Tufail Z, Qadri M, Alhassan A, Scott GY, Wei CR, Mukherjee A, Muco Mugisha MJ, Bisimwa C, Bulondo D, Muhoza B, Langat AK
articles.details: This review consolidates evidence on malaria in the Democratic Republic of Congo, focusing on pregnant women and children under 5 who bear the highest burden. It outlines ongoing challenges including limited healthcare access, inadequate funding, insecticide resistance, surveillance gaps, and heavy reliance on international donors for vector control. The authors highlight innovations such as genomic surveillance, rapid diagnostic tests, and mHealth, and emphasize integrated approaches including RTS,S and R21 vaccines, long-lasting insecticidal nets, and maternal/child health integration. They argue sustainable financing, policy reinforcement, community engagement, and climate-adaptable interventions are essential to prevent resurgence and protect vulnerable populations.

articles.headline: Memory regulatory T cells switch to protective helpers in recurrent malaria
articles.title_original: Memory Regulatory T Cells Reprogram into Protective Tfh-like Effectors in Recurrent Malaria.
articles.authors: Charles-Chess NAE, Ruberto AA, Bowers C, Obeng-Adjei N, Hansen MR, Renuka Prasad DB, Traore B, Klonowski KD, Crompton PD, Kurup SP
articles.details: Using longitudinal studies in humans and mice, this study examined the role of regulatory T cells (Tregs) across repeated Plasmodium infections. During primary infection, Tregs suppress protective immunity by inhibiting germinal center reactions. In contrast, memory Tregs (mTregs) persisting after clearance underwent antigen-driven expansion and inflammation-induced epigenetic reprogramming during reinfection, transitioning from Foxp3+ immunosuppressive cells into Bcl6+ follicular helper (Tfh)-like effectors. These mTreg-derived Tfh-like cells enhanced germinal center responses and Plasmodium-specific antibody generation, facilitating parasite control; blocking this differentiation abolished protection. The findings reveal adaptive plasticity allowing mTregs to switch from immunoregulatory to protective roles during recurrent malaria.

articles.headline: Review compares TEP1 immune roles in malaria mosquitoes and schistosomiasis snails
articles.title_original: Comparative immunological roles of TEP1 in Anopheles gambiae and Biomphalaria glabrata: implications for malaria and schistosomiasis control.
articles.authors: Li H, Feng Y, Qian Y, Jiang W, Zhu Y, Xu J, Li X, Fei X, Wang R, Shao Y, Du L, Zhang X, Chen K
articles.details: This review compares the activation and function of thioester-containing proteins (TEPs) in the malaria vector Anopheles gambiae (AgTEP1) and the schistosomiasis snail host Biomphalaria glabrata (BgTEP1), focusing on pathogen opsonization and elimination. It discusses TEP interaction networks with co-factors including LRIM1/APL1C, BgFREPs, and Biomphalysin, and TEP-mediated immune pathways such as reactive oxygen species generation, phagocytosis, and melanization. The authors highlight conserved and species-specific features of the TEP system and their potential application to vector intervention and disease prevention strategies. No quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: Molecular markers distinguish imported from local malaria cases in Honduras
articles.title_original: Molecular Characterization of Plasmodium Species to Strengthen Malaria Surveillance in Migrant Populations in Honduras.
articles.authors: Godoy A, Euceda K, Pinto A, Valdivia HO, Chaver L, Ardon G, Fontecha G
articles.details: As Honduras approaches malaria elimination, this molecular surveillance study analyzed 14 markers—six from Plasmodium falciparum and eight from P. vivax—in local and migrant samples to differentiate local versus imported infections. All P. falciparum isolates carried the wild-type pfcrt haplotype associated with chloroquine susceptibility, while polymorphisms in pfmdr1, pfama1, pfglurp, and pfs47 distinguished migrant from local genotypes. For P. vivax, three novel pvcsp VK210 haplotypes and a first VK247 variant were detected in migrants, plus novel haplotypes in pvmsp1, pvmsp3α, pvmsp3β, pvs47, and pvs48/45. The authors conclude pfmdr1, pfs47, pvs47, and pvs48/45 were informative for inferring geographic origin, supporting molecular surveillance in low-transmission settings.

articles.headline: Symptom recognition and insurance drive timely malaria treatment in Kenyan children
articles.title_original: Determinants of timely malaria treatment among under-five children attending public health facilities in Kisumu East sub-county, Kenya: a health facility-based cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Ochieng G, Silumbwe A, Sampa M, Maritim P, Zulu JM, Kato J, Okungu J, Jacobs C
articles.details: This health facility-based cross-sectional study assessed determinants of timely malaria treatment (within 24 h of symptom onset) among under-five children at public facilities in Kisumu East sub-county, Kenya, using two-stage stratified-cluster sampling and multiple logistic regression. Among 434 caregivers, higher odds of timely treatment were associated with caregivers' ability to recognize malaria symptoms (AOR=2.9; 95% CI 1.4–6.3; p=0.006), belief in appropriate malaria drugs (AOR=6.9; 95% CI 2.5–18.9; p<0.0001), and having health insurance (AOR=2.1; 95% CI 1.3–3.6; p=0.005). Visiting herbalists first (AOR=0.1; 95% CI 0.1–0.3; p<0.0001) and fear of drug side effects (AOR=0.5; 95% CI 0.3–0.9; p=0.013) reduced timely treatment. The authors call for addressing misconceptions, affordable insurance, and caregiver education.

articles.headline: Single low-dose primaquine does not impair folate recovery in children
articles.title_original: Plasma folate dynamics in Plasmodium falciparum-infected African children treated with artemisinin combination therapy and single low-dose primaquine or placebo.
articles.authors: Ajayi S, Onyamboko MA, Olupot-Olupot P, Ayuen DS, Chimjinda N, Taya C, Williams TN, Uyoga S, Maitland K, Fanello C, Day NPJ, Mukaka M, Taylor WRJ
articles.details: This randomized, placebo-controlled trial measured serial plasma folate (Days 0, 3, 7, 28) in P. falciparum-infected Ugandan and Congolese children aged 6 months to 11 years treated with age-dosed single low-dose (0.25 mg/kg) primaquine (SLDPQ) or placebo plus artemether-lumefantrine or dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine, with G6PD (c.202T) genotyping. Of 408 children, 66 (16.2%) were G6PD-deficient and 51 (12.5%) heterozygous females. Mean baseline folate was 10.83 (SLDPQ) vs 10.92 (placebo) ng/ml, associated with baseline Hb and parasitaemia; folate fell a mean 1.65 ng/ml (p<0.0001) then rose to a Day-28 mean of 11.04 ng/ml. SLDPQ and G6PD status did not compromise post-treatment folate, supporting SLDPQ use without G6PD testing.

articles.headline: Abbott-Bioline malaria RDT fails to detect confirmed P. falciparum and P. vivax cases
articles.title_original: Investigating the Abbott-Bioline™ malaria antigen Pf/Pv rapid diagnostic test.
articles.authors: Aung MTT, Thu AM, Phyo AP, Aung WL, Pateekhum C, Proux S, Yotyingaphiram W, Archusuksan L, Wongaeka M, White NJ, Day NP, Nosten F
articles.details: This field and laboratory evaluation compared the Abbott-Bioline™ Malaria Ag Pf/Pv RDT (detecting Pf HRP2 and Pv LDH) against the First Response® Malaria Ag pLDH/HRP2 RDT and microscopy (gold standard) on the Thailand-Myanmar border, after reported false negatives. The Bioline RDT had a sensitivity of 0.18 and specificity 0.99 for P. falciparum (vs First Response 0.89 and 0.93), and sensitivity 0.44, specificity 0.99 for P. vivax (vs 0.59 and 0.98). Bioline showed fainter lines at parasite densities of 208–1993/µL and detected only 45.0% (9/20) of acute falciparum and 74.0% (37/50) of vivax cases, versus First Response's 90.3% (18/20) and 84.0% (42/50). The authors conclude the Bioline test is not fit for purpose and should be replaced.

articles.headline: Artesunate-amodiaquine stays effective for child malaria in Ghana
articles.title_original: Efficacy of artesunate-amodiaquine for the treatment of uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria among children aged 2-11 years in the Asante Akim North Municipality, Ghana: a single-arm open trial.
articles.authors: Hanson H, Marfo KS, Osei-Mensa J, Jaeger A, Sarpong N, Tsali DN, Martey A, Gyimah AK, Placca E, Oppong M, Foli G, Almoustapha NS, Amuasi J, May J, Maïga Ascofaré O
articles.details: A prospective single-arm trial enrolled 173 children aged 2-11 with RDT-positive uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria, treating them with weight-based artesunate-amodiaquine and assessing adequate clinical and parasitological response (ACPR) at day 28 per the 2009 WHO protocol. By day 3 all treated participants achieved total parasite clearance; 8.1% vomited after at least one dose. Day-28 ACPR was 92.07% (modified intention-to-treat) and 100% (Kaplan-Meier), with no treatment failures. Whole-genome sequencing found no pfk13 artemisinin-resistance mutations; the pfmdr1 NFD haplotype occurred in 27.1% and pfcrt K76T in 1.9% of isolates. Authors conclude AS-AQ remains effective.

articles.headline: Plasmodium male gametes show unique microtubule singlet-to-doublet transition
articles.title_original: A basal body microtubule singlet-to-doublet transition in Plasmodium male gametogenesis.
articles.authors: Yang S, Ma S, Yuan C, Li Z, Ji F, Yao L, Cui H, Guo Q, Yuan J
articles.details: Using iterative ultrastructure expansion microscopy and cryo-electron tomography, researchers resolved the native microtubule organization in the nuclear envelope-anchored basal body of male gametes from the rodent malaria parasite P. yoelii. Unlike the canonical triplet-to-doublet transition, the parasite exhibits a microtubule singlet-to-doublet transition. δ-Tubulin and ε-Tubulin are expressed in male gametocytes and regulate axoneme formation; δ-Tubulin localizes at the proximal end of the B-tubule and modulates B-tubule assembly of the microtubule doublet. The work reveals these tubulins' key role in axoneme assembly during male gametogenesis.

articles.headline: GDH and HDP show promise as malaria serological biomarkers
articles.title_original: Evaluation of GDH and HDP as novel serological biomarkers for Plasmodium falciparum malaria diagnosis across varying parasitemia and transmission settings in India.
articles.authors: Ahmad A, Nema S, Arora K, Sikka R, Singh A, Krishna S, Singh MP, Verma AK, Nitika N, Anvikar AR, Gupta H, Bharti PK
articles.details: This study evaluated antibody responses to novel biomarkers glutamate dehydrogenase (GDH) and heme detoxification protein (HDP) versus conventional antigens (HRP2, LDH, aldolase) using ELISA on plasma from 928 participants, including 886 P. falciparum-positive cases, across varying parasitemia and transmission settings. Antibody levels clearly differentiated malaria-positive from negative samples, with ROC area under the curve above 96% for detecting P. falciparum infection. GDH2 and HRP2 distinguished parasitemia levels, while GDH2, HDP2, and LDH showed promise distinguishing endemicity levels, though sensitivity/specificity for these categories was limited. Authors conclude GDH and HDP have potential as reliable serological biomarkers.

articles.headline: Impaired CYP2D6 linked to altered P. vivax infection dynamics
articles.title_original: How CYP2D6 Polymorphism Modulates the Community-Wide Risk of Plasmodium vivax Infection: A Panel Study in Amazonian Brazil.
articles.authors: Puça MCSB, Naziazeno IM, Santos VCFD, Rodrigues PT, Calil PR, Ladeia WA, Gil JP, Ferreira MU, Nobrega De Sousa T
articles.details: A community-based panel study genotyped roughly 1300 individuals for CYP2D6 — the enzyme metabolizing primaquine used for radical cure of P. vivax — and monitored them repeatedly by molecular diagnosis for submicroscopic and asymptomatic infections over a 4-year follow-up. Children with impaired CYP2D6 activity had more frequent P. vivax infections than those with normal activity, but this reversed in the second decade of life as prevalence rose in adolescents with normal activity (P=.0008). Parasite densities were lower in adults with impaired activity than in younger individuals with normal activity (P=.0383). Authors conclude CYP2D6 may shape infection dynamics and malaria immunity.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria doesn't impair COVID mRNA booster antibody response
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Parasitemia Does Not Diminish Neutralizing Antibody Responses After mRNA COVID-19 Booster Vaccination in HIV-infected Adults.
articles.authors: Samandari T, Achola M, Hutter JN, Mboya G, Otieno W, Kee JJ, Huang Y, Aponte JJ, Ockenhouse CF, Lee CK, Polakowski L, Yacovone M, Tapley A, Dadabhai S, Mkhize NN, Kaldine H, Bhebhe S, Moore PL, Hural J, Garrett N, Kublin JG
articles.details: Researchers evaluated neutralizing antibody responses in asymptomatic HIV-infected adults, with and without PCR-confirmed P. falciparum parasitemia, who received either monovalent mRNA-1273 or bivalent mRNA-1273.222 (WA-1 and BA.4/5) COVID-19 booster vaccines. Against a prior benchmark where an ID50 titer of 1000 correlated with 96% efficacy preventing COVID-19, both parasitemic and nonparasitemic participants showed ID50 geometric mean titers above 22,000 one month after boosting. Authors conclude COVID-19 mRNA vaccine antibody responses are unimpaired by concurrent asymptomatic parasitemia.

articles.headline: Novel IgG3 allele linked to fewer malaria infections in PNG children
articles.title_original: Association of a Novel IgG3 Allele With Malaria in Children From the Sepik Region of Papua New Guinea.
articles.authors: Saeed M, Aitken EH, Naung MT, Bourke C, Wu KW, Longley RJ, Chung AW, Damelang T, Kiniboro B, Mueller I, Rogerson SJ
articles.details: In a longitudinal cohort of 203 children aged 1-3 years from the East Sepik region of Papua New Guinea, IgG1 and IgG3 alleles were genotyped by PCR and Sanger sequencing, and linear regression tested associations with Plasmodium infection. A novel IgG3 allele, IGHG3*30 (G3m29) with a long 4-exon hinge region, was carried by 78% of children (40% heterozygous, 38% homozygous). Carriers had significantly fewer Plasmodium spp infections (β = -1.736; 95% CI, -3.39 to -0.079; P = .038), most notably about 1 fewer P. vivax asymptomatic infection over 18 months (β = -1.06; P = .028), plus lower IgG to P. vivax vaccine candidate proteins. Authors conclude the allele is highly prevalent and associated with fewer infections.

articles.headline: Malaria shifts children's neutrophil subsets, possibly linking to Burkitt lymphoma
articles.title_original: The Impact of Malaria-Induced Neutrophil Subset Shift and a Link to Burkitt Lymphoma.
articles.authors: Akinyi S, Tonui RK, Maina TK, Agwati E, Oduor CI, Njuguna FM, Keitany KK, Chepsiror D, Ayieko C, Moormann A, Kinyua AW, Forconi CS
articles.details: Using multiparameter flow cytometry, researchers profiled neutrophil subsets (markers CD15, CD16, CD10, CD11b, CD182, CD184, CD62L) in children from Western Kenya with differing P. falciparum malaria exposure, children diagnosed with Burkitt lymphoma (BL), and healthy malaria-naive Kenyan adults. Malaria-exposed children showed increased frequencies of aged neutrophil subsets and reduced mature subsets versus malaria-naive children, with profiles resembling adults. A positive correlation in immature neutrophils was seen between malaria-exposed healthy and BL children (rs = 0.7; p < 0.0001), suggesting a malaria-driven expansion of the immature subset that could foster a permissive environment for BL and contribute to malaria-induced immunopathology.

articles.headline: CryoEM reveals how Plasmodium apicoplast polymerase balances replication and lesion bypass
articles.title_original: Structural basis of multitasking by the apicoplast DNA polymerase from Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Kumari A, Enache T, Craggs TD, Pata JD, Lahiri I
articles.details: Using single-particle cryo-electron microscopy, researchers solved structures of the P. falciparum apicoplast DNA polymerase (apPol) bound to undamaged DNA and nucleotide substrates in five pre-chemistry conformational states. The apicoplast replisome uses this single polymerase for both replicative and lesion bypass synthesis. apPol could accommodate a nascent base pair with fingers in an open configuration, which might facilitate lesion bypass, and in the fingers-open state a nascent base pair checkpoint preferentially selected Watson-Crick base pairs as needed for accurate replication. The authors propose these structural features explain how apPol combines both activities in one protein.

articles.headline: Peru trial protocol tests focal mass drug administration for P. vivax elimination
articles.title_original: FocaL mass drug administration for Plasmodium vivax malaria elimination (FLAME): study protocol for an open-label cluster randomized controlled trial in Peru.
articles.authors: Fine SR, Soto Calle V, Altamirano Quiroz A, Rodriquez Ferruci H, Manrique P, Wu X, Carrasco Escobar G, Benjamin-Chung J, Bennett A, Auburn S, Price RN, Greenhouse B, Baird JK, Domingo GJ, Roh ME, Rosas A, Llanos-Cuentas A, Hsiang MS
articles.details: FLAME is a 3-year cluster randomized controlled trial in Loreto, Peru, evaluating focal mass drug administration (fMDA) to reduce P. vivax transmission. Thirty low-transmission communities (API < 250 cases/1000) will be randomized 1:1 to fMDA versus control, with all communities receiving Peruvian national standard malaria control. High-risk individuals (living within 200 m of a P. vivax case reported in the prior 2 years) will receive three fMDA cycles over 2 years, each with two directly observed therapy rounds 2 months apart, using 25 mg/kg chloroquine plus a single 300 mg dose of tafenoquine (age ≥16) or 25 mg/kg chloroquine plus 7 days of primaquine 0.5 mg/kg/day if younger. The primary outcome is the cumulative incidence of symptomatic P. vivax malaria, with 80% power to detect at least a 68% relative reduction.

articles.headline: Institutional quality and aid linked to malaria burden across sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Institutional quality, aid flows, and malaria burden: a geospatial analysis of sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Namubiru C
articles.details: This geospatial study examined relationships between institutional quality, development aid flows, and malaria burden across 38 sub-Saharan African countries during 2010-2022, using malaria cases and deaths per 1,000 population. Explanatory variables included development assistance for health per capita, government effectiveness indices, and health worker density, with spatial econometric models (Spatial Durbin, Spatial Lag of X, Spatial Durbin Error) and fixed-effects models with Driscoll-Kraay standard errors. Malaria burden declined overall but showed significant spatial autocorrelation (Moran's I). Unexpectedly, higher health worker density, institutional effectiveness, and aid levels were positively associated with burden, and burden was driven more by local conditions with limited cross-border spillover. Authors call for localized health systems strengthening plus regional coordination.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence 26% in under-five children, Nandom Municipality, Ghana
articles.title_original: Prevalence of malaria infection and associated factors among children under five years attending health facilities in the Nandom Municipality, Upper West Region, Ghana.
articles.authors: Boadu S, Azornu CK, Axame W, Bonsu EO, Wumnaya R, Yidana S, Abanga WA, Sarpomaa Asante L, Adjei MR, Kubio C
articles.details: A facility-based cross-sectional study sampled 404 febrile children under five and their caregivers across six health facilities. Both RDT and microscopy were performed. Positivity was 125/404 (30.9%; CI 26.6–35.6) by RDT and 105/404 (26.0%; CI 21.9–30.5) by microscopy. Independent predictors included residence in Gengenkpe sub-municipality versus Baseble (aOR 4.58; 95% CI 1.58–13.27), not sleeping under an ITN the prior night (aOR 2.01; 95% CI 1.02–3.93), and over thirty minutes' travel to a pharmacy (aOR 2.19; 95% CI 1.08–4.45). Authors note 26% exceeds the 2024 national target of 9.7%.

articles.headline: Post-introduction evaluation of RTS,S malaria vaccine in Ghana, Kenya, Malawi
articles.title_original: POST introduction evaluation (PIE) of the malaria vaccine introduced in three pilot countries (Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi) in 2021.
articles.authors: Walldorf JA, Mayers GF, Amponsa-Achiano K, Jalang'o R, Chisema M, Khalayi L, Njoroge J, Haji A, Tweneboah PO, Sally ET, Mategula D, Mhone B, Zimba B, Chirwa E, Mohammed NT, Adjei MR, Sillah J, Pellaux-Furrer E, Magafu MGMD, Okine RNA
articles.details: Using WHO's post-introduction evaluation methodology, researchers assessed RTS,S/AS01E malaria vaccine implementation in the three Malaria Vaccine Implementation Programme pilot countries at least a year after introduction, via semi-structured interviews with immunization staff, observation of sessions and storage, and caregiver interviews. Reported median coverage at least 2 years after pilot start ranged from 69–91% for dose 1, 62–82% for dose 2, and 58–81% for dose 3 by 24–30 months; dose 4 coverage was lower due to fewer eligible children. Identified best practices included early subnational stakeholder involvement, health-worker training, defaulter tracing, and community engagement.

articles.headline: Facilitators and barriers to malaria care for pregnant women, Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Facilitators and barriers of malaria prevention and treatment services to pregnant women in Ethiopia: a multi-level health system analysis, 2025.
articles.authors: Bogale KA, Yenesew MA, Alemu K, Muchie KF, Asemahagn MA, Enbiale W
articles.details: A qualitative study engaged 49 multi-level stakeholders—policymakers, programme managers, advocacy groups, and healthcare providers—using interviews and field notes analyzed by inductive thematic analysis. Facilitators included prioritizing pregnant women in bed net distribution, NGO partnerships, increased antenatal care contact time, expanded diagnostics, and community engagement. Barriers included chloroquine shortages, fragmented service delivery, staff shortages, bed net misuse, policy gaps such as delayed adoption of WHO-recommended IPTp, and inadequate data surveillance with underreporting of asymptomatic cases. Authors recommend strengthening supply chains, adopting one-stop-shop ANC models, piloting IPTp, and improving digital surveillance.

articles.headline: Correction: Anopheles species in malaria transmission, Colombian gold-mining areas
articles.title_original: Correction: Anopheles species associated with malaria transmission in gold-mining areas of northwest Colombia.
articles.authors: Rojas MA, Ahumada ML, Velasco-Pareja MC, Chaparro P, Yasnot-Acosta MF, Orjuela LI
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Micronutrient deficiencies linked to malaria infection in Rwanda case-control study
articles.title_original: Associations between undernutrition and malaria infection: a case-control study from Rwanda.
articles.authors: Uwimana A, Alexiou H, Mutoni JD, Robert A, Rujeni N, Cani PD, Coutelier JP, Mutesa L, Everard A
articles.details: A case-control study compared malaria cases (positive blood smear) against malaria-free controls in the same households, with data collected across 9 endemic districts in all four provinces and Kigali City. Diets were predominantly starchy staples (56.7% of total energy), and risk of vitamin A, B2, B12, calcium, zinc, and selenium deficiency ranged 50–80%. Severe chronic stunting was higher in cases (17%) than controls (10.6%) but not significant. After adjustment, vitamin E deficiency risk (aOR 7.46; 95% CI 4.43–12.58; p<0.001) and iron deficiency risk (aOR 1.80; 95% CI 1.11–2.93; p=0.017) were positively associated with malaria, while older age, female sex, and selenium deficiency were inversely associated.

articles.headline: Ghana health facilities tested and treated malaria but rarely tracked patients
articles.title_original: Adherence to the test, treat and track malaria policy among selected health facilities in Ghana: the clients' perspective.
articles.authors: Kyei C, Tawiah C, Agbokey F, Anim JA, Tivura M, Afari-Asiedu S, Kyeremateng CK, Kornu FM, Oppong J, Zuuri W, Nyampong I, Agana VA, Awimba PI, Ankonu AN, Awine E, Welega P, Gyaase S, Tetteh RJ, Mayers P, Harrison SBE, Asante KP
articles.details: This cross-sectional study assessed adherence to the WHO test, treat and track (T3) malaria policy through exit interviews with 590 patients and surveys of 30 health facility managers across 6 districts in three malaria epidemiological zones of Ghana. CHPS compounds made up 18 (60.0%) of facilities, and 29 of 30 facilities stocked RDT kits and antimalarials. Facilities in the southern zone had 2.87 increased odds of T3 adherence versus the middle zone (aOR=2.87, 1.7-4.8; p<0.001), and males were less likely to return for review or home visits (aOR=0.6, 0.3-0.9; p=0.018). Testing and treating were high, but patient tracking was very low across all zones.

articles.headline: Socio-economic factors drive malaria transmission hotspots across Indian districts
articles.title_original: Determinants of malaria transmission in Indian districts in 2018: insights from ensemble models.
articles.authors: Sam AK, Khatoon A, Phuleria HC
articles.details: This modeling study mapped malaria hotspots and identified covariates influencing transmission across Indian districts using district-wise malaria, socio-economic, meteorological, and land-use/land-cover data analyzed with an ensemble of Random Forest and Poisson regression models. Spatial clusters of annual parasite incidence (API) and annual falciparum incidence (AFI) appeared in central and northeastern districts, with additional non-AFI hotspots in northern districts. The models indicated that socio-economically marginalized communities with water inaccessibility, no mobile phone, and use of unprotected water sources were potentially vulnerable, while mother's education could reduce transmission. The authors recommend targeting clusters of high-burden districts for interventions.

articles.headline: 13-month mass treatment in Ghana did not lower EBA-175 antibody titres
articles.title_original: Increased Plasmodium falciparum EBA175RIII-V antibody titres despite a 13-month malaria mass testing, treatment, and tracking intervention in a high malaria endemic community in Ghana.
articles.authors: Tey PK, Brashear A, Kwapong SS, Acquah FK, Bredu DG, Busayomi A, Anang SF, Abban BC, Nagaoka H, Takashima E, Obiri-Yeboah D, Cui L, Amoah LE
articles.details: This study monitored total IgG titres against Plasmodium falciparum PfEBA-175RIII-V in 314 individuals aged 6 months-90 years participating in four quarterly mass testing, treatment, and tracking (MTTT) rounds in a high-transmission community in Ghana, using dried blood spots for PCR diagnosis and serology. PCR infection prevalence fell from 56.2% at baseline to 46.8% at the end, and 212 participants (67.5%) were infected at least once. Antibody titres fluctuated and were highest at study end, correlated positively with age, and were significantly higher in infected than uninfected participants. The 13-month MTTT did not significantly reduce PfEBA-175RIII-V antibody titres or naturally acquired immunity.

articles.headline: Meta-analysis links elevated ESR to malaria and severe disease
articles.title_original: A systematic review and meta-analysis of Plasmodium infections and erythrocyte sedimentation rate in relation to disease severity.
articles.authors: Kotepui M, Suwannatrai AT, Kotepui KU, Mahittikorn A, Masangkay FR, Thinkhamrop K, Wangdi K, Wilairatana P
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis (PROSPERO CRD42023482332, PRISMA-guided) examined erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) differences between malaria patients and non-malarial controls and between severe and non-severe malaria, including 19 studies and using random-effects standardized mean differences (SMD). ESR was significantly higher in malaria patients than controls (SMD 3.0037, 95% CI 0.5347-5.4726; p=0.0171; I²=97.3%, 11 studies) and higher in severe versus non-severe cases (SMD 2.2556, 95% CI 0.8759-3.6354; p=0.0014; I²=96.1%, 4 studies). However, very high heterogeneity and significant publication bias (Egger's test p=0.005) limit confidence, so the authors conclude ESR's independent diagnostic or prognostic utility remains uncertain.

articles.headline: Correction notice for study on CRP marker in febrile children
articles.title_original: Correction: Evaluation of CRP as a marker for bacterial infection and malaria in febrile children at the Douala Gyneco-Obstetric and Pediatric Hospital.
articles.authors: PLOS One Editors
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Pyronaridine-artesunate outperforms artemether-lumefantrine for uncomplicated falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Pyronaridine-artesunate for treating uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria.
