articles.authors: Fox T, Otieno JA, Pryce J, Hine P
articles.details: This systematic review evaluated pyronaridine-artesunate (PY-AS), a WHO-recommended ACT, against other ACTs for uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria, including 15 studies (14 RCTs) with 7295 participants plus 10 non-randomized safety studies with 11,783 participants. Versus artemether-lumefantrine, PY-AS probably reduced PCR-adjusted treatment failure at day 28 (RR 0.40, 95% CI 0.19–0.85) and unadjusted failure at day 28 (RR 0.27, 95% CI 0.14–0.52) and day 42 (RR 0.61, 95% CI 0.46–0.82), with little or no difference for PCR-adjusted failure at day 42 (RR 0.86). Versus artesunate-amodiaquine, PY-AS probably reduced unadjusted day-28 failure (RR 0.49, 95% CI 0.30–0.81).

articles.headline: Liver sinusoid structure lets few CD8 T cells find Plasmodium efficiently
articles.title_original: Liver sinusoid constraints make random search of CD8 T cells for Plasmodium parasites efficient.
articles.authors: Zenkov VS, Cockburn IA, Lin R, Griffith WN, Langston MA, Ganusov VV
articles.details: Using simultaneous imaging of Plasmodium-specific CD8 T cells, sporozoites, and liver sinusoids, plus a new method modeling sinusoids as graphs to simulate T cell movement, the authors re-examined whether T cells need attraction to locate liver-stage infection. An older open-space/3D metric suggested few T cells are attracted to the infection site, but the new graph-based metric detected no attraction when movement was constrained to sinusoids. Simulations showed that roughly 1 cell per imaging volume (<10^5 cells/liver), moving without attraction at observed speeds, suffices to find the infection within 24 hours. The authors conclude the constrained tissue structure makes random search effective and that apparent attraction may be an artifact of open-space metrics.

articles.headline: Systems biology reveals shared and species-specific host responses to malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Systems Biological Analysis of Immune-Metabolic Host Responses to Distinct Malaria Parasites.
articles.authors: Lima DV, Guimarães TP, Almeida ACG, Teixeira AJ, Monteiro WM, Melo GC, Gardinassi LG
articles.details: This systems-biology study integrated public blood flow cytometry, transcriptomics, and untargeted metabolomics from simian malaria models (P. coatneyi and P. cynomolgi in rhesus monkeys, modeling P. falciparum and P. vivax) and human malaria. Conserved dynamics were found in blood dendritic cells, effector memory CD8+ T cells, and PD-1+ effector memory CD8+ T cells, with increased HMOX1 and coagulation-related gene expression across simian infections and complement activation upregulated in rhesus and humans. Chemokine and T cell differentiation modules were mostly associated with P. cynomolgi and P. vivax. Conserved metabolite regulation included kynurenine and androgen/estrogen-derived metabolites. The authors conclude parasite biology substantially shapes host immune and metabolic responses, but systematic experimental comparisons are still needed.

articles.headline: Review confirms molecular techniques reliably detect Plasmodium in Anopheles mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Detection of Plasmodium in Anopheles spp (Diptera: Nematocera: Culicidae) using molecular biology techniques: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Fonseca EDC, David EDS, Santos KCD, Oliveira FP, Barbosa LMC, Komninakis SV, Souto RNP
articles.journal: Rev Inst Med Trop Sao Paulo
articles.details: This PRISMA 2020 systematic review searched five databases (LILACS, PubMed, SciELO, Scopus, Web of Science) plus Google Scholar to assess molecular biology techniques for detecting Plasmodium in Anopheles mosquitoes. Of 484 studies retrieved, 12 met inclusion criteria; all used molecular methods to determine parasite species in mosquito samples. The authors conclude that, despite challenges, molecular biology provides accurate and reliable results that contribute to effective epidemiological response and monitoring of Plasmodium spread in endemic areas.

articles.headline: Shifting malaria profile in declining low-endemic Brazilian Amazon area raises concern
articles.title_original: Changes in the malaria transmission profile in a low-endemic area of the Brazilian Amazon: a cause for concern.
articles.authors: Oliveira-Neto FM, Albuquerque HG, Silva ARD, Gonçalves EDGDR, Suárez-Mutis MC
articles.journal: Rev Inst Med Trop Sao Paulo
articles.details: This descriptive, retrospective, ecological study analyzed malaria surveillance data from Maranhao, a Brazilian state with declining cases, covering 2003 to 2022. Of 83,517 reported cases, 67.8% were autochthonous, and 83.1% of those occurred in rural areas; Annual Parasitic Incidence is now low risk in most municipalities. The epidemiological profile shifted toward imported cases (initially from abroad, more recently from other Brazilian states), and the proportion of P. falciparum fell from 17.7% in 2003 to 6.01% in 2022. Most cases were male, aged 15 to 45 (rising from 59.1% to 70.5%). The authors warn that these changes could reverse progress and call for strengthened surveillance to prevent reintroduction and resurgence.

articles.headline: Mathematical models predict recurrent malaria infection patterns for study planning
articles.title_original: How do recurrent malaria infections occur in clinical cohorts: a mathematical modelling study to support study planning.
articles.authors: Krumkamp R, Rautman LH, Maiga-Ascofaré O, May J, Lorenz E
articles.details: This methodological modelling study built five compartmental models (ordinary differential equations) to describe recurrent malaria in a malaria-naïve cohort under different transmission scenarios: constant infection risk (A), treatment plus reduced susceptibility after infection (B), vaccination effects (C), heterogeneous transmission (D), and seasonal variation (E). Treatment and immunity (Model B) lowered the number of recurrent events versus Model A despite equal infection counts, while vaccination (Model C) substantially reduced first and recurring infections. In Model D, nearly 100% of the high-transmission subgroup had more than one infection. The authors provide equations to estimate expected recurrent events and cohort sizes across settings.

articles.headline: Malaria and helminths lower hemoglobin in Cameroon military personnel
articles.title_original: The impact of malaria and intestinal helminths on haemoglobin concentrations among armed forces of the North West and South West Cameroon.
articles.authors: Che SB, Ntonifor HN, Tofel KH, Jugha VT, Wanyu BY, Taiwe GS
articles.details: This cross-sectional study enrolled 812 male armed-forces participants in the North West and South West Regions of Cameroon, testing malaria by microscopy, hemoglobin by an Hb meter, and intestinal parasites by direct smear and Kato-Katz. Two Plasmodium species (P. falciparum and P. vivax) gave an overall malaria prevalence of 13.6%, with P. falciparum most common and 86.6% of infected participants having mild malaria; overall intestinal parasite prevalence was 22.8%, and Loa loa microfilaria prevalence was 0.2% (2/812). Parasite density differed significantly across age groups, and malaria parasites, Trichuris trichiura, and Schistosoma mansoni significantly affected mean Hb. The authors conclude malaria and helminths remain public health issues affecting hemoglobin.

articles.headline: Wing shape morphometrics distinguishes cryptic Anopheles malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Unraveling Wing Shape Variation in Malaria Mosquitoes from the Arctic Edge: A Geometric Morphometric Study in Western Siberia.
articles.authors: Calderon X, Artemov G, Burlak VA, Alexeeva S, Hernández-P R, Suazo MJ, Pérez LM, Benítez HA, Correa M
articles.details: This geometric morphometric study analyzed wing venation patterns to differentiate cryptic Anopheles maculipennis subgroup vectors—An. messeae, An. daciae, An. beklemishevi, and the An. messeae × An. daciae hybrid (An. m-d)—in Western Siberia, Russia. Using Procrustes ANOVA, principal component analysis, and canonical variate analysis with anatomical landmarks, the analyses revealed significant differences in wing shape and size among the species, with hybrids showing intermediate morphologies. Landmarks on the radial and medial veins contributed most to species separation, no static allometry was detected, and shape differences were not explained by size. The authors conclude wing morphometrics combined with molecular identification provides a reliable framework for vector species delimitation and surveillance.

articles.headline: Low molecular weight PAH pollution assessed in Puerto Rico marsh
articles.title_original: Occurrence and Distribution of Three Low Molecular Weight PAHs in Caño La Malaria, Cucharillas Marsh (Cataño, Puerto Rico): Spatial and Seasonal Variability, Sources, and Ecological Risk.
articles.authors: Berríos-Rolón PJ, Márquez F, Cotto MC
articles.details: This environmental study assessed three low molecular weight polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons—naphthalene (NAP), phenanthrene (PHEN), and anthracene (ANT)—in surface waters of Caño La Malaria, the main freshwater source of Cucharillas Marsh, Puerto Rico, sampling four locations in wet and dry seasons with GC-MS quantification. NAP dominated; Σ3PAHs ranged from 7.4 to 2198.8 ng/L, higher in the wet season (mean 745.79 ng/L) than dry (mean 186.71 ng/L). No significant spatial differences were found (p=0.753), inter-site correlation was high (r=0.96), and diagnostic ratios with PCA indicated a predominantly pyrogenic combustion source (PHEN-ANT r=0.824). Ecological hazard quotients indicated negligible acute toxicity (<0.01), though benchmark exceedances suggest potential chronic risk. Note: this article concerns a place named 'Caño La Malaria' and is not about the disease malaria.

articles.headline: Malaria–helminth coinfection worsens parasitemia and anemia in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Integrated assessment of malaria parasite load, anemia, and associated factors in patients with and without soil-transmitted helminthes coinfection in Southern Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Bogale A, Tefera S, Taye B, Mitiku A, Kasse A, Getu E, Gemechu T, Misganaw T, Degefa T
articles.details: This comparative cross-sectional study enrolled 220 patients at health facilities in Dilla Zuria, Southern Ethiopia—110 with Plasmodium monoinfection and 110 coinfected with soil-transmitted helminths (STHs)—using Kato-Katz for STH identification, Giemsa-stained thick and thin films for parasitemia, and a HemoCue analyzer for hemoglobin. Coinfected patients had significantly higher mean parasitemia (40,093.54 parasites/µl) than monoinfected individuals, with hookworm and Trichuris trichiura intensities positively associated with parasitemia and Ascaris lumbricoides inversely associated. Hemoglobin was significantly lower in coinfected patients, strongly associated with hookworm (p<0.001) and T. trichiura (p<0.038) but not A. lumbricoides (p<0.838). The authors conclude integrating deworming into malaria case management may mitigate anemia.

articles.headline: Review: environmental and socio-economic drivers of malaria in West Africa
articles.title_original: Environmental and socio-economic determinants of malaria transmission in West Africa: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Gbaguidi GJ, Topanou N, Filho WL, Begedou K, Ketoh GK
articles.details: This systematic review screened articles from PubMed and DOAJ databases, ultimately selecting 68 articles after applying inclusion and exclusion criteria. It aimed to identify socio-economic and environmental determinants influencing malaria transmission and incidence in West Africa. The most common environmental predictors were air temperature, rainfall, relative humidity, and vegetation, while key socio-economic determinants were education level, place of residence, housing structure, poverty, and quality of information. The authors conclude these factors can serve as indicators for targeted malaria elimination and prevention programmes.

articles.headline: Pilot of cycling multiple first-line malaria treatments feasible in Kenya
articles.title_original: Health system challenges and facilitators associated with adaptive cycling deployment of multiple first-line treatment for uncomplicated malaria: a pilot study in a malaria-endemic region of Kenya.
articles.authors: Cole A, Chege T, Aman R, Githuka G, Muga R, Aspinall A, Kokwaro G
articles.details: This 2-year pilot study implemented adaptive cycling of four artemisinin-based combinations (artemether-lumefantrine, dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine, amodiaquine-artesunate, pyronaridine-artesunate) in adults with uncomplicated malaria in an implementation county versus a control county that continued artemether-lumefantrine, assessing health-system challenges as a mitigation strategy against artemisinin partial resistance. Multiple first-line treatment (MFT) was accepted by stakeholders, with one minor adverse effect (vomiting) and patient preference for once-daily dosing; economic cost was USD 3 per patient treated. Challenges included drug quantification/stock logistics, human-resource constraints, and diagnostic kit stock-outs. The control county showed 12.5 percentage points higher malaria positivity (23.3% vs 10.8%). Authors conclude adaptive cycling MFT is feasible with adequate planning.

articles.headline: Modeling projects mostly lower malaria transmission across India by 2080s
articles.title_original: Changing climate and its impacts on the dynamics of future malaria transmission over certain endemic regions in India.
articles.authors: Bal PK, Kumar DS, Parihar RS, Saini A
articles.details: Using the VECTRI vector-borne disease model forced with temperature, rainfall, and population density, this modelling study projected future malaria transmission across four climatological zones of India under the CMIP6 SSP5-8.5 scenario. Comparing the 2080s with a 1985-2014 baseline, results indicate an overall 10-30% decrease in Entomological Inoculation Rate (EIR) over most Indian regions alongside a 4-5 °C temperature rise and 10-40% rainfall increase, though parts of the western and peninsular regions showed increased EIR. These changes were predominantly controlled by temperature and rainfall during summer (decrease) and winter (increase) monsoon seasons. Authors suggest the results may aid policymakers and regional malaria risk assessments.

articles.headline: LAMP point-of-care test detects low-density P. falciparum infections
articles.title_original: Sensitive near point-of-care detection of asymptomatic and submicroscopic Plasmodium falciparum infections in African endemic countries.
articles.authors: Rakotomalala Robinson D, Pennisi I, Cavuto ML, Kiemde F, Chamai M, Some DY, Quigley E, Malpartida-Cardenas K, Ndiath MO, Correa S, Darboe B, Stewart LB, Georgiou P, Baldeh M, Tinto H, Cunnington AJ, Erhart A, D'Alessandro U, Rodriguez-Manzano J
articles.details: Researchers adapted a near point-of-care, LAMP-based Pan/Pf molecular diagnostic platform using capillary blood, with a limit of detection of 0.6 parasites/μL and sample-to-result under 45 minutes, and evaluated it on 672 community-level capillary blood samples including 146 qPCR-confirmed P. falciparum positives. The platform achieved 95.2% sensitivity (95% CI 90.4-98.1) and 96.8% specificity (95% CI 94.9-98.0), and detected 94.9% (130/137) of asymptomatic infections and 95.3% (41/43) of submicroscopic cases (<16 parasites/μL), outperforming expert microscopy (70.1% and 0%) and rapid diagnostic tests (49.6% and 4.7%). Authors conclude it offers a sensitive, scalable tool to support test-and-treat malaria elimination strategies.

articles.headline: Dengue and malaria coinfection plus diverse pathogens in febrile Nigerian patients
articles.title_original: Detection of dengue, malaria, and additional causes of acute febrile illness: The need for expanded testing, Bayelsa State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Akanbi DO, Abaye BB, Averhoff F, Berg MG, Orf GS, Lawan KM, Beckett GA, Omotoso AB, Mata M, Cloherty GA, Chabuka L, de Oliveira T, Mac-Fisi KW, Walter A, Mark II, Edeki G, Sinayoko A, Nasrullah M, Justina BI, Balogun MS
articles.details: This cross-sectional study tested blood from 443 patients aged ≥1 year presenting with acute febrile illness at four hospitals in Bayelsa State, Nigeria, using DENV serology (NS1, IgM, IgG) and RT-PCR, thick-smear microscopy for malaria, and metagenomic next-generation sequencing. Acute DENV seroprevalence was 14.5% (n=64), malaria prevalence was 42.4% (n=188), and 6.5% (n=29) had DENV-malaria coinfection, with an additional 17.6% (n=78) showing past DENV markers. Rural/suburban residence and age ≥31 years correlated with dengue seromarkers, while larger household size and borehole water-use predicted malaria; multiple DENV serotypes (serotype 3 dominant) and other pathogens (adenovirus, EBV, hepatitis A, Coxsackievirus A5, mpox clade IIb) were identified. This is the first documentation of human DENV infection in Bayelsa State, supporting expanded routine testing.

articles.headline: Survey finds low vivax and ovale, high falciparum malaria in Cameroon highlands
articles.title_original: Epidemiology of ovale, vivax and falciparum malaria in the highlands of Cameroon: An integrated community survey of human infection and vector abundance.
articles.authors: White SJ, Tchuenkam VPK, Mbouh M, Gaither C, Bouopda-Tuedom AG, Kiam BC, Popkin-Hall ZR, Sadler JM, Carey-Ewend K, Hand E, Ngum MN, Ngomsi YN, Bailey JA, Kaunda DB, Mafo LK, Lemogo GN, Dinka CL, Nsani CA, Noubom M, Goel V, Ibrahima I, Onguene JC, Lin FC, Lin JT, Nsango SE, Dinglasan RR, Juliano JJ, Ali IM
articles.details: A cross-sectional community survey of 3,661 participants from 871 households across seven health areas in Dschang, Cameroon (ReMA study) used qPCR to assess malaria prevalence. P. vivax prevalence was low at 0.1% and P. ovale spp. at 0.64%, while P. falciparum remained prevalent at 8.1%; co-infection of P. ovale spp. with P. falciparum occurred in 23.1% of ovale cases. Gametocytemia was common in falciparum but uncommon in ovale infections. Children and those with recent domestic travel had higher odds of P. falciparum infection. Entomological surveys found high densities of An. gambiae s.l. and An. funestus s.l. The authors conclude relapsing species are less widespread than previously thought in West Cameroon.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence 41% among febrile patients in Jawi District, Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Prevalence of malaria and associated factors among febrile patients in selected healthcare facilities of Jawi District, northwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Dagnew A, Yeshanew S, Nibret E, Atenafu G, Kendie FA, Assefa M, Habtamu A
articles.details: A cross-sectional study of 420 febrile patients seeking treatment at government healthcare facilities in Jawi District, northwest Ethiopia (April to November 2023) examined blood by microscopy. Malaria prevalence was 41.2% (95% CI: 0.365-0.459), with P. falciparum predominant (64.8%). Multivariable logistic regression identified lack of formal education (AOR=2.452, p=0.047), proximity to mosquito breeding sites (AOR=43.224, p=0.015), and awareness gaps about transmission (AOR=1.384, p=0.044) as significant factors. The authors conclude malaria infection was high and multifactorial, urging stronger education, improved housing, and sustained access to diagnosis, treatment, and prevention.

articles.headline: Review proposes targeting Plasmodium detoxification enzyme GST for new antimalarials
articles.title_original: Targeting phase II detoxification enzyme inhibition in Plasmodium for malaria treatment.
articles.authors: Onisuru O, Achilonu I
articles.details: This review explores inhibition of Plasmodium's glutathione S-transferase (GST), a phase II detoxification enzyme, as a novel antimalarial drug discovery route in the face of widespread resistance to existing antimalarials. The authors discuss GST's critical role in the parasite's xenobiotic metabolism, converting toxic molecules to less toxic, soluble compounds. They argue that inhibiting P. falciparum and P. vivax GSTs holds promise as a distinct mode of action for finding new antimalarials.

articles.headline: PfCelTOS vaccine candidate shows low genetic diversity in Vietnam isolates
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity and natural selection of cell-traversal protein for ookinetes and sporozoites (CelTOS) in Plasmodium falciparum isolates from Vietnam.
articles.authors: Võ TC, Lê HG, Kang JM, Minh Trinh NT, Cho M, Goo YK, Quang HH, Na BK
articles.details: This study analyzed genetic diversity and natural selection of the P. falciparum CelTOS gene (pfceltos) in 366 sequences from P. falciparum-infected patients in three malaria-endemic Vietnamese provinces (Dak Lak, Gia Lai, Phu Yen) between 2018 and 2022. Twenty-nine polymorphic amino acid changes were identified, with four substitutions (K100N, S104L, F117V, N119K) commonly detected. Overall genetic diversity was low, with Dak Lak showing greater diversity and a distinct population structure compared to Gia Lai and Phu Yen. The authors conclude the limited diversity and conservation of key amino acids support PfCelTOS as an attractive vaccine candidate, though global analysis is needed.

articles.headline: Plasmodium yoelii drives M1 macrophage polarization via TLR7/STAT3 pathway
articles.title_original: Plasmodium yoelii infection regulates M1 macrophage polarization and function through TLR7/STAT3 signaling pathway.
articles.authors: Mo L, Jiang Z, Hong C, Guo J, Qiu G, Wu Q, Deng G, Chen Y, Lu Y, Zhou H, Liu Q, Luo X, Zhao Y, Qi Y, Li Q, Ma X, Yang Q
articles.details: This mouse study investigated how Plasmodium yoelii infection regulates macrophage function and adaptive immunity. Infection triggered macrophages to polarize toward an M1 phenotype and enhanced phagocytosis, linked to upregulated TLR7 expression; the TLR7 agonist R848 alleviated infection symptoms. STAT3 expression also increased, and STAT3 inhibitors attenuated TLR7-induced effects, indicating regulation through a TLR7-STAT3 pathway. Depleting macrophages with Clodronate liposomes increased parasitemia and weakened T-cell and B-cell responses. The authors conclude Plasmodium infection promotes M1 polarization and phagocytosis via TLR7-STAT3 signaling, initiating innate and adaptive defenses.

articles.headline: Resistance to ZY19489 restores piperaquine and chloroquine susceptibility in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Collateral hypersensitivity between ZY19489 and piperaquine neutralizes PfCRT-mediated drug efflux and Plasmodium falciparum resistance.
articles.authors: Okombo J, Ndiaye T, Qahash T, Moura IMR, Gil-Iturbe E, Hagenah LM, Bridgford JL, Bonatto V, Ward KE, Yeo T, Narwal SK, Orta LV, Anderson I, Dhingra SK, Pasaje CFA, Park H, Kim J, Guido RVC, Angulo-Barturen I, Niles JC, Mancia F, Uhlemann AC, Mok S, Quick M, Winzeler EA, Leroy D, Llinás M, Thathy V, Fidock DA
articles.details: Laboratory study identifying a resistance mechanism to ZY19489, a triaminopyrimidine antimalarial in Phase IIb trials, in Plasmodium falciparum. Low-grade resistance was mediated by a novel mutation in the chloroquine resistance transporter PfCRT, which sharply reduced asexual blood-stage growth and carried a substantial fitness cost. Resistant parasites lost chloroquine resistance and became hypersusceptible to piperaquine. All three agents interfered with host hemoglobin catabolism, and uptake studies in PfCRT proteoliposomes showed ZY19489 blocks mutant PfCRT-mediated efflux of piperaquine and chloroquine. The authors propose pairing ZY19489 with piperaquine as a resistance-refractory combination.

articles.headline: AI decision-support tool sharply improves Sudan malaria protocol adherence
articles.title_original: Enhancing Physicians' Adherence to the 2023 Sudan Malaria Case Management Protocol Using AI as an Intervention Tool.
articles.authors: Muhammed A, Ibrahim S, Mohammed AAY, Mohamed Ahmed AK, Idris MYM, Yousif MMO, Omer ITA, Rababah A, Sherfeldin Mohammed HE, Ahmed SAE, Osman Ahmed Osman M, Musa Mohamed ZH, Mohamed Ibrahim LA, Musa Hussain EY, Ali HKM, Mohamed Mustafa FA, Eltahir SME, Elhag MES, Kanan Ahmed AA
articles.details: Two-cycle clinical audit evaluating an AI-based clinical decision-support system (CDSS) plus training on physician adherence to the 2023 Sudan Malaria Case Management Protocol at Al-Managil Teaching Hospital, with 50 physicians assessed by questionnaire. Recognition of the protocol rose from 38% to 100% (risk difference +62%, 95% CI 48–76; p<0.001), awareness of IV/IM artesunate as first-line treatment from 53% to 98% (+45%; p<0.001), and favorable attitudes toward severe malaria from 15% to 91% (+76%; p<0.001). Mean compliance increased from 50.7% to 96.8% (p<0.001). Authors note a possible Hawthorne effect and absence of clinical outcome data, with residual gaps in microscopy reporting and G6PD testing.

articles.headline: Computational multi-epitope vaccine designed against P. falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Immunoinformatics-Based Multi-Epitope Vaccine Design Against P. falciparum-Causing Malaria: A Computational Approach.
articles.authors: Larbi AA, Mensah C, Korankye R, Darkwah AA, Agyemang EB, Ayikpah IY, Kwarteng A
articles.details: Computational immunoinformatics study designing a multi-epitope subunit vaccine against Plasmodium falciparum. B-cell, cytotoxic T-cell, and helper T-cell epitopes were predicted from five highly conserved P. falciparum antigens and concatenated with adjuvants and linkers. The construct's T-cell epitopes were estimated to cover 87.07% of the world's population per the Allele Frequency Net Database, and it was predicted to be soluble with suitable physicochemical properties. Docking and molecular dynamics simulations indicated strong, stable interactions with TLR4, and immune simulation suggested development of long-lasting memory B and CD4+ T cells. The authors state the candidate requires further experimental validation.

articles.headline: First pfhrp2/3 gene deletions found in Malawi cause false-negative RDTs
articles.title_original: First report of pfhrp2 and pfhrp3 gene deletions compromising HRP2-based malaria rapid diagnostic tests in Malawi.
articles.authors: Louis JM, Mazigo E, Jun H, Lee WJ, Syahada JH, Fitriana F, Muh F, Chun W, Park WS, Lee SJ, Na S, Lu F, Han ET, Han JH
articles.details: Cross-sectional study enrolling 1582 participants from referral hospitals in Mzuzu (n=1186) and Lilongwe (n=396), with 391 P. falciparum-positive samples analyzed for pfhrp2/pfhrp3 deletions by multiplex qPCR, compared against microscopy and qPCR. Malaria prevalence was 45.2% in Lilongwe (predominantly asymptomatic, 94.2%) versus 22.9% in Mzuzu (mostly symptomatic, 97.1%). RDT sensitivity was 78.5% (95% CI 74.6–82.1) versus 64.8% for microscopy, with specificity 93.6% versus 95.4%. Dual pfhrp2/3 deletions occurred in 24 isolates (15.0%) from Lilongwe and 24 (10.4%) from Mzuzu; all dual-deleted samples were RDT false-negative but positive by microscopy and qPCR. This is the first report of these deletions in Malawi.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence highest in urban schoolchildren in Eastern Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Malaria among schoolchildren in urban, peri-urban and rural settings in Eastern Ethiopia: repeated cross section study.
articles.authors: Merga H, Degefa T, Birhanu Z, Abiy E, Lee MC, Yan G, Yewhalaw D
articles.details: Repeated cross-sectional study of primary schoolchildren in urban, peri-urban, and rural Eastern Ethiopia during high and low transmission seasons, using Giemsa-stained blood smears and microscopy. Malaria prevalence was 5.1% (95% CI 3.84–6.7) in the high season and 2.24% (95% CI 1.48–3.37) in the low season. During high transmission, asymptomatic prevalence was 7.3% urban, 4.97% peri-urban, and 2.9% rural (P=0.007). P. falciparum was dominant in both seasons (83% high, 59% low). Non-use of ITNs (AOR 2.2; 95% CI 1.2–3.99) and presence of under-five children in the household (AOR 1.9; 95% CI 1.1–3.38) predicted infection in the high season. Authors recommend targeting all school settings, especially urban and peri-urban areas.

articles.headline: Chrysosplenetin reverses artemisinin resistance in rodent malaria via redox, transporters
articles.title_original: Chrysosplenetin acts as a homeostasis stabilizer with dual-function in shattering Plasmodium berghei K173 resistance to artemisinin driven by both ABC transporters and heme-ROS/GSH axis.
articles.authors: Ji H, Huang Y, Tian J, Bo R, Heng X, Yang J, Huang Y, Wang X, Tan Q, Gao H, Chen L, Chen J
articles.details: This mechanistic mouse study compared artemisinin (ART) monotherapy versus ART plus chrysosplenetin (CHR) in Mdr1a wild-type and knockout mice infected with sensitive or resistant Plasmodium berghei K173, measuring ABC transporter (Mrp1/2/4/5, Bcrp) mRNA, hemoglobin, cytokines, ROS/GSH redox balance, and PI3K/AKT-mTOR and MAPK signaling. In drug-resistant mice, combination therapy gave the highest survival (100%) and inhibition (30%) and lowest parasitaemia (~20.0%) regardless of Mdr1a status, with fold changes spanning roughly 1.02 to 84.00 across markers. The authors conclude CHR acts as a homeostasis stabilizer disrupting resistance via ABC transporters and the heme-ROS/GSH axis, with P-gp efflux function not critical.

articles.headline: Online narratives reveal personal and economic toll of malaria in Africa
articles.title_original: Malaria in their words: thematic analysis of online narratives from Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Zhang SH, Nwankwoala O, Jakazi CS, D'Amiano AJ, Kabuya JB, Kapungu K, Laker M, Ippolito MM
articles.details: This qualitative study applied inductive thematic analysis, using a phenomenological lens and rapid ethnographic assessment, to 160 narrative accounts from GiveDirectly's online repository across five countries, most from Kenya (n=121) and Uganda (n=33). Two thematic domains emerged: perceived vulnerability contexts (linking malaria to poor housing, agricultural and pre-dawn labor) and experienced consequences (severe financial burdens, household triage between care, food, and schooling, lost workdays, liquidated assets, school dropout, and lasting disabilities including mobility, vision, and cognitive impairments). The authors conclude interventions should extend beyond public health to socioeconomic support and infrastructure improvements.

articles.headline: Indigenous knowledge shapes malaria prevention behaviors in Manicaland, Zimbabwe
articles.title_original: Indigenous knowledge systems and malaria prevention in Zimbabwe: a market basket analysis.
articles.authors: Chapoterera B, Marume A, Midzi N, Naidoo K
articles.details: This cross-sectional study surveyed 480 adults aged 18 and above in Manicaland Province, Zimbabwe, recruited by stratified random sampling, using structured face-to-face questionnaires and association rule mining (Apriori algorithm in R) to identify co-occurring health-seeking behaviors. Participants were predominantly female (69.0%) and aged 25-44 (57.5%); in the prior three months 36.7% of respondents and 54.0% of families had experienced malaria. Indigenous knowledge came mainly from older people (27.7%). Visiting church prophets was strongly associated with not visiting traditional healers (support=0.66, confidence=0.91, lift=1.08). The authors conclude health-seeking reflects complex spiritual, traditional, and biomedical interactions and call for culturally informed malaria control.

articles.headline: Correction notice for KAP survey on malaria among Chinese expatriates
articles.title_original: Correction: Survey on knowledge, attitudes and practices (KAP) of malaria prevention and control among Chinese expatriates in South Sudan.
articles.authors: Su Q, Yu Y, Chen H, Gao Y, Li J
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Cluster trial design requirements for detecting malaria vector suppression
articles.title_original: Requirements for designing cluster randomised control trials to detect suppression of malaria vector population densities.
articles.authors: Hancock PA, Hui TJ, Epopa PS, Milogo A, McKemey AR, Yao FA, Diabaté A, Burt A
articles.details: This methodological study developed statistical models to simulate cluster randomised control trials (CRCTs) for evaluating interventions (e.g., sterile or genetically modified mosquito releases) that suppress malaria vector populations, informed by a 5-year time series of Anopheles gambiae complex densities from four villages in western Burkina Faso. For a 50% suppression effect at 90% power, 21-22 clusters were needed targeting all An. gambiae complex species, 24-26 for An. coluzzii alone, and 60-66 for An. gambiae alone; a 90% suppression effect required only 9-10 clusters. The authors provide empirically based guidance for designing such CRCTs.

articles.headline: Severe falciparum malaria recurs after treatment in returning traveller
articles.title_original: Recrudescent severe malaria due to Plasmodium falciparum following treatment in a Taiwanese patient: a case report.
articles.authors: Tseng YC, Cheng MY, Lin PB, Hsu SF, Hsu JJ, Shen YC, Ho MW, Hsiao CT, Hsueh PR
articles.details: This case report describes a previously healthy 66-year-old Taiwanese man who developed fever, vomiting, diarrhoea, and jaundice after returning from multiple Central African countries, progressing to multi-organ failure (acute kidney injury, liver failure, thrombocytopenia, respiratory distress) with 20% P. falciparum parasitaemia confirmed by real-time PCR. He received intravenous artesunate and oral artemether-lumefantrine plus haemodialysis and plasma exchange, achieving parasite clearance, but recrudesced 4 weeks later with recurrent fever, anaemia, and parasitaemia despite no pfk13 mutations. A revised regimen of artesunate with doxycycline and clindamycin followed by artemether-lumefantrine cleared parasites within 2 weeks with complete recovery. The authors stress that recrudescence can occur in non-immune patients even with guideline-based therapy.

articles.headline: Ugandan health workers show poor knowledge, negative attitudes on malaria vaccine
articles.title_original: General knowledge and attitude of healthcare professionals and infrastructural readiness prior to implementation of malaria vaccine in selected hospitals in Tororo district, Uganda: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Asio L, Besigye I, Puleh SS, Nazziwa N, Ocan M
articles.details: This cross-sectional study assessed knowledge, attitudes, and infrastructural readiness among healthcare professionals before R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccine rollout at Tororo General Hospital and St. Anthony Hospital in Tororo district, Uganda, using self-administered questionnaires and an infrastructure inspection checklist. Among 211 participants, 70% (147/211, 95% CI: 63-76) had unfavorable attitudes versus 30% (64/211) positive, with a mean score of 3.30 (SD 0.489), and concerns about affordability, accessibility, and effectiveness. Knowledge was low, with 99% (208/211) demonstrating poor knowledge and only 1% (3/211) good knowledge. The authors found limited infrastructural preparedness and recommend targeted training and additional infrastructure before rollout.

articles.headline: Most Ghanaian herbal antimalarials show poor activity against P. falciparum
articles.title_original: The efficacy of selected Ghanaian herbal antimalarials against laboratory strains and clinical isolates of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Yeboah SN, Ansong M, Clotworthy D, Domie PE, Chirawurah JD, Awiaga CK, Mensah C, Sakyi ME, Adika E, Morang'a CM, Awandare GA, Aniweh Y, Amenga-Etego LN
articles.details: This in vitro study evaluated 20 commercially available liquid antimalarial herbal medicines obtained from drug stores and pharmacies in Madina, Accra, Ghana, testing their anti-plasmodial activity against five laboratory strains and two clinical isolates of P. falciparum using growth inhibition assays. Of the 20 products, 30.0% (n=6) showed potent activity across all six parasite strains with IC50 values below 50 ug/ml; 3 of the 6 potent formulations acted at the trophozoite stage while the rest varied across intraerythrocytic stages. The authors conclude most preparations had poor activity and emphasize the need to strengthen regulation, efficacy testing, and quality monitoring of traditional medicines.

articles.headline: Opsonic phagocytosis linked to higher malaria risk in low-transmission area
articles.title_original: A Novel Phagocytosis Assay Reveals the Association between Elevated Opsonic Phagocytosis Levels and an Increased Risk of Clinical Malaria in a Low-Malaria Transmission Area.
articles.authors: Odhiambo EO, Mellencamp KA, Holla P, Ayodo G, John CC
articles.details: In a low, unstable transmission Kenyan highland setting, blood samples from 5,753 individuals were collected and the cohort monitored for clinical malaria over roughly a decade. Using a nested case-control design, 317 cases who developed clinical malaria were matched 1:1 by age and village with 317 controls, and a novel opsonic phagocytosis (OP) assay was applied alongside IgG responses to P. falciparum antigens. OP levels rose with age but stayed positive in under half of individuals until age ≥15. After adjustment, higher OP was associated with increased clinical malaria risk in those aged 5-14 (aOR 3.32, CI 1.13-9.77) and ≥15 (aOR 4.85, CI 1.02-23.00), but not in children under 5 (aOR 0.57, CI 0.20-1.64), suggesting OP marks exposure rather than protection.

articles.headline: Model: malaria parasites need two host cues to optimize transmission
articles.title_original: Malaria parasites can optimize transmission investment by sensing two within-host cues.
articles.authors: Wang A, Greischar MA, Mideo N
articles.details: This theoretical modeling study extends prior optimal-investment theory for the rodent malaria parasite Plasmodium chabaudi to ask which within-host cues parasites should sense to maximize fitness, quantified as host infectiousness during acute infection. The results show that sensing a single parasite-associated cue (e.g., abundance of infected red blood cells or transmission stages) approaches optimal fitness only when perceived non-linearly, but no single cue reproduces the optimal time-varying strategy or enables terminal investment at infection's end. Sensing two cues—log-transformed infected and uninfected red blood cell abundance—lets parasites track infection progression, permits terminal investment, and recovers optimal fitness, but increases virulence and reduces robustness to environmental and developmental noise.

articles.headline: Scoping review: oxidative stress in malaria and typhoid co-infection
articles.title_original: Oxidative stress in malaria and typhoid fever: A scoping review of the pathogenic mechanisms and therapeutic implications.
articles.authors: Netongo PM, Eric MB, Kamdem SD, Tchoutang AM, Mbacham WF
articles.details: This scoping review synthesized studies published between January 2000 and May 2024 (searched via PubMed, ScienceDirect, and DOAJ) examining ROS production, antioxidant responses, and pharmacological effects on oxidative stress markers in malaria and typhoid fever. The authors report that both diseases elevate oxidative stress, which is further exacerbated in co-infections, and that the endogenous antioxidant response is activated but may be insufficient to fully neutralize ROS. They also note that commonly used antimalarial and antibacterial drugs influence oxidative and antioxidant dynamics, and conclude that targeting the oxidative-antioxidant balance may open avenues for adjunctive therapies and vaccines.

articles.headline: Molecular methods reveal malaria vector misidentified in Northeast India
articles.title_original: Diagnostic puzzle of Anopheles species using morphological vs. molecular approach: a case study in Tripura, a hyperendemic malaria state, Northeast India.
articles.authors: Sihag AK, Rajkonwar J, Gogoi P, Vezhavendan S, Chellappan S, Satpute SS, Malakar P, Debnath S, Baruah K, Kaur H, Subbarao SK, Bhattacharyya DR, Bhowmick IP
articles.details: In a longitudinal (2019-2024) entomological study in Tripura, Northeast India, morphological techniques identified 9 An. fluviatilis collected during winter months alongside 500+ An. minimus. Molecular investigation reclassified the An. fluviatilis specimens as An. minimus, revealing 3 new haplotypes. The authors attribute the misidentification to hypermelanization, absence of presector pale spots on the wing, and palpal banding pattern, suggesting An. fluviatilis may not be present in the region. They conclude that combined morphological and molecular methods are needed for correct Anopheles identification, as misidentification can mislead vector control policies.

articles.headline: Permethrin-treated baby wraps cut childhood malaria by two-thirds
articles.title_original: Permethrin-Treated Baby Wraps for the Prevention of Malaria.
articles.authors: Boyce RM, Shook-Sa BE, Ndizeye R, Baguma E, Giandomenico D, Cassidy CA, Eshun S, Siedner MJ, Staedke SG, Ntaro M, Juliano JJ, Reyes R, Mulogo EM
articles.details: In a double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial in Uganda, 400 mother-child pairs (children 6-18 months) were assigned 1:1 to permethrin-treated baby wraps or sham-treated wraps, with retreatment every 4 weeks; all received a new pyrethroid-only long-lasting insecticide-treated net and were followed for 24 weeks. The incidence of clinical malaria was 0.73 cases per 100 person-weeks in the intervention group versus 2.14 in the control group (incidence rate ratio 0.34; 95% CI 0.23-0.51; P<0.001). Rash was more common with permethrin (8.5% vs 6.0%). Maternal use of treated wraps significantly reduced clinical malaria in children already using bed nets.

articles.headline: New statistical model for recurrent events in malaria vaccine trial
articles.title_original: Generalized nonparametric temporal modeling of recurrent events with application to a malaria vaccine trial.
articles.authors: Heng F, Sun Y, Xu J, Gilbert PB
articles.details: This methods paper develops generalized nonparametric temporal models of intensity processes with multiple time scales, encompassing multiplicative and additive temporal intensity models through choice of link functions. The authors develop a maximum likelihood estimation procedure using local linear smoothing with double kernels, an adaptive algorithm for overlapping covariates, and a cross-validation bandwidth selection procedure, and investigate the estimators' asymptotic properties. Simulations showed satisfactory finite-sample performance. The methods were applied to the MAL-094/MAL-095 malaria vaccine efficacy trial to assess how new infection risk changes over time and how prior infection or vaccination modifies future infection risk and vaccine efficacy.

articles.headline: Antimalarial resistance markers low after chemoprevention in Sierra Leone
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Molecular Markers of Resistance to Antimalarial Drugs Three Years After Perennial Malaria Chemoprevention in Sierra Leone.
articles.authors: Chen H, Owusu-Kyei K, Fombah AE, Williams J, García-Fernández C, Rovira-Vallbona E, Bofill A, Quintó L, Figueroa-Romero A, Mac-Abdul F, Samai M, Mayor A, Menéndez C
articles.details: A cross-sectional survey conducted June-August 2021 in three districts of Sierra Leone screened 440 febrile children aged 2-5 years; 300 RDT-positive participants were enrolled and dried blood spots analyzed by qPCR and sequenced for resistance markers. Of 298 samples, 237 (79.5%) were qPCR-positive and 230 genotyped. The pfdhfr triple mutant was found in 99.5% (217/218); pfdhps A437G in 92.1% and K540E in 19.1%; the dhfr/dhps quintuple mutant in 4.6% (7/151), with no sextuple mutants. The pfcrt CVIET haplotype occurred in 36.6%, and no validated pfK13 artemisinin-resistance markers were detected. The sustained low quintuple-mutant prevalence supports continued SP-based IPTp and PMC and continued ACT use.

articles.headline: Vietnam P. falciparum vaccine antigen PfCSP shows regional and global diversity
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity and natural selection of circumsporozoite surface protein in Vietnam Plasmodium falciparum isolates.
articles.authors: Võ TC, Lê HG, Kang JM, Trinh NTM, Van Khanh C, Cho M, Quang HH, Na BK
articles.details: This study sequenced the pfcsp gene (circumsporozoite protein, a leading malaria vaccine candidate) from P. falciparum isolates collected in five malaria-endemic provinces of Central Vietnam, yielding 95 sequences, and compared them with 836 global pfcsp sequences. The N- and C-terminal non-repeating regions showed limited diversity, but notable polymorphisms appeared, including an A98G change and a 19-amino-acid insertion in the N-terminal region, plus polymorphisms in the C-terminal Th2R and Th3R regions; the central repeat region was highly polymorphic due to variation in peptide repeat motif numbers and types. The authors conclude these findings inform design of effective PfCSP-based vaccines.

articles.headline: Complete mitochondrial genome sequenced for orang-utan malaria parasite P. pitheci
articles.title_original: The mitochondrial genome sequence of the Bornean orang-utan (Pongo pygmaeus) malaria parasite, Plasmodium pitheci.
articles.authors: Dharmayanthi AB, Pratama R, Sánchez KL, Nurillah A, Fadiah F, Komara, Nugraha RTP, Saputra S, Nurkanto A, Fahroni A, Irwanto A, Syafruddin D, Adnan IA, Saragih JR, Coutrier FN, Setiadi W, Lobo NF
articles.details: Researchers sequenced the complete mitochondrial genome of Plasmodium pitheci, a malaria parasite infecting Bornean orang-utans (Pongo pygmaeus). The genome spans 5871 bp and encodes all essential mitochondrial genes in a conserved Plasmodium arrangement. Bayesian phylogenetic analyses confirmed that previously identified Plasmodium sp. VM, VS, and Pongo clades A and B correspond to P. pitheci, though Pongo clade C remains taxonomically unresolved. The authors conclude this genomic resource provides a foundation for species-specific molecular diagnostic tools to improve diagnosis in orang-utans, aid conservation, and potentially support zoonotic malaria control.

articles.headline: Chamomile-derived MgO nanoparticles inhibit chloroquine-sensitive and -resistant P. falciparum in vitro
articles.title_original: Antimalarial potential of Matricaria chamomilla-derived MgO nanoparticles against Plasmodium falciparum strains: an experimental study.
articles.authors: Farzaneh Z, Hanifian H, Nateghpour M, Hasanpour G, Raeisi A, Shabani M, Farivar L, Khezri A, Dehdast SA, Shahsavari S
articles.details: This experimental study evaluated green-synthesized magnesium oxide (MgO) nanoparticles, prepared using Matricaria chamomilla extract, for antiplasmodial activity against P. falciparum 3D7 (chloroquine-sensitive) and K1 (chloroquine-resistant) strains in vitro. Nanoparticles made with three water-to-ethanol solvent ratios (30:70, 50:50, 70:30) were quasi-spherical (30–80 nm) with hydrodynamic sizes of 183, 161, and 606 nm respectively. IC₅₀ values against 3D7 were 0.19, 0.21, and 0.22 mg/mL, and against K1 were 0.41, 0.45, and 0.42 mg/mL. The authors conclude the nanoparticles showed antiplasmodial activity against both strains and warrant further preclinical investigation.

articles.headline: Malaria positivity in pregnant women declined but stayed high, Savannah Region, Ghana
articles.title_original: Trend of malaria test positivity rate among pregnant women, Savannah Region, Ghana, 2018-2022.
articles.authors: Abesig WJ, Nindow JAC, Bagonluri AC, Sarfo AK, Issahaku GR, Odikro MA, Akowuah G, Bandoh DA, Kenu E, Kubio C
articles.details: This retrospective, descriptive, cross-sectional study used DHIMS 2 database records from 2018 to 2022 to describe the malaria-in-pregnancy test positivity rate in the Savannah Region of Ghana. The testing rate was 90.8% (46,734/51,479), and 54.5% (95% CI: 54.0–55.0) of pregnant women tested positive; positivity was highest among mothers below 20 years (59.3%), and Bole District had the highest rate (70.1%), accounting for 40.5% (10,315/25,463) of cases. Positivity declined from 60.4% in 2018 to 51.6% in 2022. The authors conclude positivity remains high with district disparities and call for strengthened prevention (IPTp with SP, ITNs).

articles.headline: Review: late-arresting attenuated parasites as a liver-stage malaria vaccine strategy
articles.title_original: The path from early- to late-liver stage arresting genetically attenuated parasites as a malaria vaccination strategy.
articles.authors: Lamers OAC, Franke-Fayard BMD, Roestenberg M, Krol JMM
articles.details: This review discusses genetically attenuated parasites (GAPs) that arrest during liver-stage development as a malaria vaccination strategy. It contrasts P. falciparum GAPs that arrest after 24–48 hours (early-arresters) with those arresting after 6–7 days (late-arresters), noting that late-arresters have shown superior efficacy and underscoring the importance of liver-stage immunity for sterile protection. The authors summarize GAPs tested in humans and the preclinical research behind their creation, focusing on the safety, efficacy, and large-scale implementation of existing GAPs as malaria vaccines.

articles.headline: Adding Vitamin A to seasonal malaria chemoprevention adds 24 cents per child
articles.title_original: A cost analysis comparing seasonal malaria chemoprevention with and without Vitamin A supplementation among under-5 children in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Oresanya OB, Arije O, Fadipe J, Rotimi K, Phillips A, Maxwell K, Shekarau E, Onwu N, Bazant ES
articles.details: This cost analysis compared a standalone seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) campaign using sulphadoxine-pyrimethamine plus amodiaquine (SPAQ) with an SMC campaign integrated with Vitamin A supplementation (VAS) among under-5 children in two Local Government Areas of Bauchi State, Nigeria (SMC-only Cycle 1 in July 2021 reaching 168,820 children; SMC+VAS Cycle 4 in October 2023 reaching 170,681 children). Total cost was US$158,934 for SMC-only versus US$186,426 for the integrated campaign, an additional US$27,492. Cost per child rose from $0.94 to $1.18, a $0.24 increment, with distribution and drug costs the largest contributors. Authors conclude the integration is feasible and modestly priced.

articles.headline: Review of ICU management and neuromonitoring for adult cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Management and monitoring strategies for severe cerebral malaria: a guide for the intensivist.
articles.authors: Vathi S, Corriero A, Rodríguez EE, Moreau A, Bogossian EG, Baggiani M, Hites M, Sonneville R, Taccone FS, Salvagno M
articles.details: This review examines the pathophysiology, clinical features, and intensive-care management of severe cerebral malaria (CM) caused by Plasmodium falciparum, defined by impaired consciousness (Glasgow Coma Score < 11) after excluding other encephalopathies. CM carries a mortality of 15-25% and leaves long-term neurological sequelae, with pathogenesis involving parasitized erythrocyte sequestration in cerebral microvasculature, immune hyperactivation, blood-brain barrier disruption, and cerebral edema raising intracranial pressure. Intravenous artesunate is the first-line antimalarial, supported by seizure and complication management, while adjunctive therapies remain experimental with no proven benefit. The authors argue invasive and non-invasive neuromonitoring is underutilized in adults and call for protocolized, multidisciplinary care.

articles.headline: Disrupting Plasmodium chaperone complex destabilizes PfR2 and arrests DNA replication
articles.title_original: Inhibition of the assembly of Plasmodium Hsp70-1 and Hsp40 complex blocks DNA replication by destabilizing ribonucleotide reductase subunit-2.
articles.authors: Alkhatib I, Garg D, Tabassum W, Tanishka K, Bhattacharyya MK, Bhattacharyya S
articles.details: This laboratory study identifies molecular determinants required for folding of the small ribonucleotide reductase subunit (PfR2) in Plasmodium falciparum. The authors show PfR2 interacts with the cytosolic Type-I Hsp40 cochaperone PfYdj1 (Pf3D7_1437900), which engages PfHsp70-1 via its His-Pro-Asp motif, with specificity for PfYdj1 rather than the Type-II cochaperone PfSis1. Disrupting the PfYdj1-PfHsp70-1 association with small molecule 116-9e destabilized PfR2 homeostasis, reduced dNTP production, and caused replication arrest. Combining 116-9e with the PfR2 catalytic inhibitor benzo-hydroxamate produced synergism in 3D7 parasites, proposing this chaperone-complex assembly as an antimalarial target.

articles.headline: Subcutaneous R21/Matrix-M and immune-complex formulation fully protect mice
articles.title_original: Subcutaneous administration of the malaria R21/Matrix M vaccine and immune complex formation with anti-circumsporozoite protein mAb 2A10 elicit protective efficacy in mice.
articles.authors: Mukhopadhyay E, Camacho CL, Hill AVS, Salman AM
articles.details: This preclinical study reports the immunogenicity and short-term protective efficacy of the R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccine given subcutaneously (SC), as an immune complex (R21 plus anti-NANP monoclonal antibody 2A10) with Matrix-M, and as co-administered R21/MM with pre-formed immune complex, using only two immunizations in BALB/c mice. SC administration was immunogenic and more efficacious (100% in BALB/c mice) than the intramuscular route. The R21:2A10 immune-complex/MM formulation was immunogenic and induced sterile protection, whereas co-administration of R21/MM with pre-formed immune complex was less protective than the immune complex alone. Authors describe this as the first application of an immune-complex vaccine approach to a parasitic disease.

articles.headline: Insulin-like peptides trigger egg formation in Anopheles stephensi mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Insulin-like peptides activate egg formation in the Asian malaria mosquito Anopheles stephensi.
articles.authors: Phipps BL, Brown MR, Strand MR
articles.details: This study characterized regulation of egg-producing gonotrophic cycles in Anopheles stephensi using assays for yolk deposition, ovary ecdysteroidogenesis, vitellogenin expression, nutrient storage, and oviposition, comparing mated and unmated females against prior Aedes aegypti findings. Yolk deposition depended on head-derived hormones, and two insulin-like peptides, AsILP3 and AsILP4, stimulated the vitellogenic phase, whereas ovary ecdysteroidogenic hormone (OEH) showed no activity. Nutrient stores were lower than in Ae. aegypti, and females were unresponsive to AsILP3 without a blood meal. Males transferred ecdysteroids to females, and mating before the first blood meal promoted oviposition, indicating gonotrophic regulation shares features with but differs from Ae. aegypti.

articles.headline: Review of malaria vaccine costs, delivery strategies, and implementation barriers
articles.title_original: Costs, delivery strategies and implementation challenges for malaria vaccines: a rapid review of literature.
articles.authors: Isah A, Obi C, Okeke I, Gwe M, Onwujekwe O
articles.details: This rapid review searched PubMed, Google Scholar, and Cochrane Library for studies on malaria vaccine delivery, costs, and logistical challenges. Thirty studies met inclusion criteria, 21 conducted in Africa. RTS,S/AS01 was the most commonly reported vaccine (n=23, 76.67%), with reported efficacy rates of 30%–75%. Cost-effectiveness estimates ranged from $20–$50 per DALY averted, with higher costs in some settings. Delivery largely used routine immunization (n=12, 40%), with drones showing promise in one study. Key barriers were logistical constraints, community acceptance, and healthcare infrastructure limitations.

articles.headline: Review of γδ T-cell responses in malaria and tuberculosis
articles.title_original: Exploring γδ T-Cell Responses in Malaria and Tuberculosis: Implications for Immunity, Coinfection, and Disease Management.
articles.authors: Mohamud YN, Chaijaroenkul W, Na-Bangchang K
articles.details: This systematic review examines the roles of γδ T cells in malaria and tuberculosis, emphasizing subset-specific dynamics and functional plasticity. In malaria, Vγ9Vδ2+ cells are rapidly activated upon Plasmodium falciparum infection, exerting antiparasitic effects via proinflammatory cytokines, cytotoxicity, and immune modulation, but chronic or repeated exposure leads to functional exhaustion of Vδ2+ cells with reduced cytokine responsiveness and a shift toward regulatory phenotypes. In TB, γδ T cells aid pathogen containment through interferon-γ and interleukin-17, macrophage activation, and granuloma formation. The authors conclude that understanding context-dependent γδ T-cell subset functions is critical for targeted immunotherapies and vaccines.

articles.headline: Rainfall raises malaria risk at 2–8 week lag in Eastern Uganda
articles.title_original: Quantifying the Lagged Effects of Climate Variables on Malaria Risk in Eastern Uganda.
articles.authors: Kim S, Nabukeera B, Taremwa Y, Ng'etich M, Otieno F, Cygu S, Kajungu D, Kiragga A, Tozan Y
articles.details: Using weekly malaria case data from July 2018 to February 2023 from a health facility in the Iganga-Mayuge Health and Demographic Surveillance System site in Uganda, plus remotely sensed temperature and rainfall data, researchers applied a distributed lag nonlinear model to quantify associations between climate variables and symptomatic malaria risk, and whether they varied by age. They found a 2- to 8-week lag between rainfall exceeding 200 mm/week and significantly increased risk; no significant lagged association was found with temperature. Risk in school-aged children was less sensitive to climate variables than other age groups. The authors stress context-specific, adaptive malaria control strategies.

articles.headline: Plasmodium fragile infection models human severe malaria in rhesus macaque
articles.title_original: Clinical and Pathological Outcomes of Experimental Plasmodium fragile Infection in a Rhesus Macaque.
articles.authors: Prusak JE, Nemphos SM, Green HC, Fell SL, Midkiff CC, Rodgers A, Perret J, Grasperge B, Vail K, Minkah N, Wilder B, Londono-Renteria B, Blair RV, Manuzak JA
articles.details: This study assessed the clinical and pathological effects of experimental Plasmodium fragile infection in nonhuman primates. A male rhesus macaque infected with P. fragile-infected erythrocytes developed clinical signs of malaria including anemia, changes in red and white blood cell distribution, altered red blood cell morphology, and gross and histopathological changes to the liver, lungs, heart, and brain. P. fragile-infected red blood cells were observed in the vasculature of major organs including the spleen, liver, lung, and brain. The authors conclude that experimental P. fragile infection of rhesus macaques is a translatable model of human malaria.

articles.headline: Acetyl-CoA synthetase mutations alter P. falciparum antimalarial drug susceptibility
articles.title_original: Acetyl-CoA synthetase mutations affect the susceptibility of Plasmodium falciparum to antimalarial drugs.
articles.authors: Zhao W, Xiang Z, Zeng W, Qin Y, Pan M, Wu Y, Duan M, Mou Y, Liang T, Zhang Y, Liu C, Tang X, Huang Y, Yang G, Cui L, Yang Z
articles.details: Researchers detected PfAcAS mutations S868G and V950I by whole-genome sequencing in recrudescent Plasmodium falciparum parasites after treatment with artesunate and dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine. Using CRISPR/Cas9, they engineered parasite lines carrying the S868G and V950I mutations in two genetic backgrounds and tested antimalarial susceptibility in vitro. The mutations, alone or combined, affected susceptibility to several drugs including artemisinin derivatives (dihydroartemisinin, artesunate, artemether) and chloroquine, though absolute changes in susceptibility were modest. The authors suggest PfAcAS may provide new molecular markers for monitoring drug resistance in P. falciparum.

articles.headline: Malaria parasite Complex III also processes mitochondrial proteins, beyond electron transport
articles.title_original: Dual Function of Mitochondrial Complex III in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Rell RS, Shukla A, Morrisey JM, Okoye IC, Mather MW, Vaidya AB
articles.details: This laboratory study of blood-stage Plasmodium falciparum tested whether mitochondrial Complex III, a validated antimalarial target, has functions beyond regenerating ubiquinone for pyrimidine biosynthesis. The authors generated transgenic lines conditionally expressing mitochondrial processing peptidase alpha (MPPα), a Complex III component, in parasites carrying a yeast DHOD bypass of the electron transport chain. MPPα was found essential even when electron transport was bypassed, and its knockdown made parasites hypersensitive to proguanil. Pulldown proteomics showed MPPα associated with multiple mitochondrially targeted proteins plus all Complex III components, indicating Complex III serves dual roles in electron transport and protein processing.

articles.headline: Hydropower dams not a primary driver of malaria transmission in West Africa
articles.title_original: Hydropower development and malaria transmission: A geospatial econometric study.
articles.authors: Thomas CJ
articles.details: This geospatial econometric study used multivariate Difference-in-Differences regression to assess whether hydropower development causally affects malaria transmission in communities within 15 km of dam sites in Ghana, Côte d'Ivoire, and Gabon, comparing pre- and post-dam periods. Outcomes were P. falciparum clinical incidence per person and parasite prevalence in children aged 2-10 years. The DiD estimator showed no statistically significant increase in malaria after dam implementation (Côte d'Ivoire incidence p=0.210, prevalence p=0.200; Gabon both p=0.990; Ghana incidence p=0.089, prevalence p=0.102). Environmental and socio-economic factors—urbanisation, elevation, and climate—were strongly associated with transmission (p<0.01). The authors conclude hydropower presence alone is not a primary driver of malaria.

articles.headline: Screen of Medicines for Malaria Venture compound boxes finds new anthelmintic leads
articles.title_original: Discovery of small molecules with anthelmintic potential in the Medicines for Malaria Venture's COVID and Global Health Priority Boxes using an infrared-based assay for Caenorhabditis elegans motility.
articles.authors: Sun YU, Liu LJ, Caffrey CR
articles.details: Using a Caenorhabditis elegans infrared-based motility assay, researchers screened 400 compounds from the Medicines for Malaria Venture's COVID Box and Global Health Priority Box for anthelmintic activity. Primary and secondary time- and concentration-dependent screens identified twelve hits, nine of which are known anthelmintics. Three additional bioactives—flufenerim, flucofuron, and indomethacin—were identified, with EC50 values ranging from 0.211 to 23.174 µM. Counter-toxicity screens in HEK293 cells showed varying toxicity (EC50 0.453 to >100 µM). The authors propose these three compounds as starting points for developing new anthelmintics.

articles.headline: AL and ASAQ both highly effective for uncomplicated malaria in Liberia
articles.title_original: Efficacy and safety of artemether-lumefantrine (AL) and artesunate-amodiaquine (ASAQ) for the treatment of uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Liberia, 2022-2023.
articles.authors: Koko VS, Skrip L, Kumeh O, Washington T, Diallo MO, Kafuko J, Inyang U, Ponce de León GF, Zhou Z, Laird VR, Zawolo J, Getahun B, Harmon-Gray WM, Plucinski M, Schultz JS
articles.details: This WHO-protocol therapeutic efficacy study evaluated artemether-lumefantrine (AL) and artesunate-amodiaquine (ASAQ) for uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria in children aged 6-59 months at Saclepea and Sinje, Liberia. Of 1,630 screened, 305 were enrolled (152 AL, 153 ASAQ) and monitored for 28 days, with 299 (98%) reaching an endpoint. Day-3 parasitaemia was 1.3% (2/153) for ASAQ and 0% (0/151) for AL. PCR-corrected adequate clinical and parasitological response at day 28 was 100% (Saclepea) and 95.9% (95%CI 88.0-98.7%, Sinje) for AL, and 100% (Saclepea) and 94.4% (95%CI 85.9-97.9%, Sinje) for ASAQ. No adverse events were reported. Both treatments exceeded the WHO 90% efficacy threshold.

articles.headline: Vector Guard mosaic PBO net outperforms comparators against resistant mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Efficacy of Vector Guard®, a mosaic alpha-cypermethrin and piperonyl butoxide-treated net, for the control of pyrethroid-resistant malaria vectors: a non-inferiority experimental hut evaluation in Benin.
articles.authors: Nounagnon J, Gbegbo M, Agbevo A, Vigninou E, N'dombidjé B, Ngufor C
articles.details: An experimental hut trial in Covè, Benin evaluated Vector Guard®, a novel mosaic alpha-cypermethrin-PBO insecticide-treated net, against wild pyrethroid-resistant Anopheles gambiae s.l., unwashed and after 20 washes, versus Olyset® Plus (permethrin-PBO) and Royal Sentry® 2.0 (alpha-cypermethrin only). PBO pre-exposure restored partial susceptibility to alpha-cypermethrin (34% vs 4% mortality) but not permethrin. Among 6,799 females collected, Vector Guard® mortality exceeded Olyset® Plus unwashed (36.4% vs 17.5%, p<0.001) and after washing (17.2% vs 8.7%, p<0.001), and met non-inferiority for mortality (OR 2.71, 95%CI 2.26-3.24) and blood-feeding protection (OR 0.53, 95%CI 0.45-0.62). Wash retention was higher (83% PBO, >94% alpha-cypermethrin). The authors support adding it to the WHO prequalified list.

articles.headline: Few Nigerian caregivers use health facilities for under-five malaria
articles.title_original: Care-seeking behaviour of caregivers for suspected malaria in under-five children in a Southwestern State of Nigeria.
articles.authors: Akinrinade OT, Isere EE, Ajayi IO
articles.details: A cross-sectional analytical study surveyed 301 caregivers of under-five children across three Local Government Areas in Ondo State, Southwestern Nigeria, assessing care-seeking behaviour for suspected malaria using a structured questionnaire. Although 94.0% suspected malaria in their child within the prior 12 months, only 9.3% visited a health facility, while 37.4% bought drugs from patent medicine vendors/pharmacies and 25.2% used herbs; only 51.2% had good malaria knowledge. Significant predictors of facility visits were being female (aOR=3.32, 95% CI 1.29-8.54), urban residence (aOR=4.25, 95% CI 1.72-10.48), living within 5 km of a facility (aOR=3.38, 95% CI 1.03-11.07), and good malaria knowledge (aOR=4.16, 95% CI 1.61-10.77). Authors recommend community awareness campaigns.

articles.headline: Monoclonal antibody targets PfEMP1 linked to cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: A monoclonal antibody selectively recognizing PfEMP1 proteins associated with cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Dalgaard N, Olsen RW, Adams Y, Mendez BL, Rudbaek JJ, Bisholm SAG, Mousiliou A, Walker MR, Hviid L, Tahar R, Ndam NT, Barfod L, Jensen AR
articles.details: Researchers functionally characterized a mouse monoclonal antibody, mAb02, raised against eight different DBLβmotif domains of Plasmodium falciparum erythrocyte membrane protein 1 (PfEMP1), a protein subset (A and B/A) with dual affinity for host receptors ICAM-1 and EPCR implicated in cerebral malaria. The antibody selectively recognized DBLβmotif-positive PfEMP1 proteins and inhibited their binding to ICAM-1, and also recognized infected erythrocytes expressing these proteins and inhibited their adhesion to ICAM-1. The epitope lies in an unfolded linker region of the ICAM-1-binding site and includes residues directly involved in the interaction. Authors conclude mAb02 targets a broadly conserved epitope, suggesting potential for antibody-based intervention against cerebral malaria.

articles.headline: NFATc1 drives LAG3 on CD8 T cells in malaria-infected mice
articles.title_original: NFATc1 regulates LAG3+CD8+ T cells in the spleen of mice infected with Plasmodium yoelii NSM.
articles.authors: Pan X, Mo F, Pan L, Xiao W, Liang G, Xie X, Yuan H, Wei H, Zhao S, Li L, Jia L, Xie H, Huang J
articles.details: Using C57BL/6 mice subcutaneously infected with Plasmodium yoelii NSM, researchers isolated splenic lymphocytes and analyzed them by flow cytometry and single-cell RNA sequencing to study LAG3+CD8+ T cells in anti-malarial immunity. LAG3 expression was significantly upregulated in splenic CD8+ T cells after infection, and these cells showed enhanced activation, responsiveness, proliferation, and cytokine production. Activated NFATc1 co-expressed with LAG3, and dual-fluorescence reporter assays in 293T cells identified NFATc1 as a transcription factor binding the LAG3 promoter; siRNA knockdown of NFATc1 reduced LAG3 expression. Authors conclude NFATc1 positively regulates LAG3 expression in these functionally enhanced T cells.

articles.headline: New compounds targeting PfSSB kill drug-resistant malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Targeting Plasmodium falciparum Single-Stranded DNA-Binding Protein: Discovery of New Scaffold Compounds Effective against Drug-Sensitive and Artemisinin-Resistant Strains.
articles.authors: Naik B, Bhalerao P, Shekhar S, Varghese SA, Makwana S, Mandal CC, Bhatt TK, Dhar SK, Prusty D
articles.details: This study identified two structurally novel compounds, PPG and 9-HPF, targeting Plasmodium falciparum single-stranded DNA-binding protein (PfSSB), essential for apicoplast DNA replication. Biolayer interferometry and gel retardation assays confirmed both compounds bind PfSSB and disrupt its ssDNA-binding ability. Against drug-sensitive P. falciparum (3D7), PPG and 9-HPF showed IC50 values of 54.95 and 9.13 μM respectively, and inhibited artemisinin-resistant P. falciparum (C580Y) with resistance indices of 0.79 and 1.26. MTT assays confirmed both are nontoxic, and structural analysis indicated distinctness from existing antimalarials. Virtual screening of 59,807 similar compounds identified candidates with higher PfSSB affinity. Authors conclude these are promising leads against drug-resistant parasites.

articles.headline: Antioxidant nanoparticles improve survival in mouse severe malaria
articles.title_original: Self-Assembling Antioxidant Nanoparticles Increase Survival and Reduce Disease Symptoms of Severe Malaria.
articles.authors: Yoshitomi T, Hayashi K, Araki T, Annoura T, Nagasaki Y
articles.details: Researchers developed a self-assembling redox nanoparticle (RNP) designed to resist rapid metabolism and excretion and, via its PEG shell and nanoscale size, limit entry into normal cells to preserve redox balance, then tested it in a Plasmodium berghei-induced malaria model in mice. After intraperitoneal administration, RNP entered the bloodstream and remained in circulation for over 24 hours. In untreated infected mice, blood reactive oxygen species (ROS) rose dramatically once a parasite-infection threshold was exceeded, causing lethal damage; low-molecular-weight TEMPOL only marginally reduced ROS, whereas RNP significantly decreased ROS, markedly improving disease severity and survival and suppressing erythrocyte membrane oxidation and fragility. Authors highlight RNP's therapeutic potential for malaria.

articles.headline: Quarter of healthy Nigerian adults carry asymptomatic P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Economic empowerment and various preventive strategies play a role in reducing asymptomatic malaria towards the end of the rainy season.
articles.authors: Okpala C, Umeh I, Anagu LO
articles.details: A community-based cross-sectional study surveyed 130 apparently healthy adults aged 18+ at the end of the rainy season, using a self-administered questionnaire and a P. falciparum rapid diagnostic test (mRDT) from a finger-prick sample, with binary logistic regression to find associated factors. Asymptomatic P. falciparum infection was confirmed in 26.15% (34/130) of participants. Lower odds of asymptomatic malaria were seen among those who agreed that the cost of prevention tools such as ITNs, insecticides and repellents is reasonable (OR 0.29/0.27, 95% CI 0.07/0.06–1.24). Authors conclude that financial means to access prevention resources can drive healthier behaviours that reduce transmission.

articles.headline: New phosphonate inhibitors target Plasmodium purine-salvage enzyme
articles.title_original: C1'-Branched Acyclic Nucleoside Phosphonates as Inhibitors of Plasmodium Falciparum 6-Oxopurine Phosphoribosyltransferase.
articles.authors: Frydrych J, Keough DT, Xia H, Slavětínská LP, Dračínský M, Česnek M, Travis J, Chavchich M, Edstein M, Hocková D, Guddat LW, Janeba Z
articles.details: This medicinal-chemistry study designed purine-based C1'-branched acyclic nucleoside phosphonates with diverse secondary linkers on the C1' atom as inhibitors of human hypoxanthine-guanine phosphoribosyltransferase and Plasmodium falciparum HGXPRT, an enzyme target for antiparasitic drugs. Compounds with a terminal phosphonate or hydroxyl group showed submicromolar to single-digit micromolar inhibition, with the lowest Ki values of 0.7 µM for the human enzyme and 0.4 µM for the parasite enzyme. A phosphonodiamidate prodrug of one compound had an IC50 of 4.3 µM against a drug-sensitive P. falciparum strain grown in human erythrocytes. Authors conclude these compounds show in vitro activity and merit as potential drug leads.

articles.headline: R21/Matrix-M vaccine antibodies target multiple CSP epitopes
articles.title_original: Repertoire, function, and structure of serological antibodies induced by the R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccine.
articles.authors: McDaniel JR, Voss WN, Bowyer G, Rush SA, Spencer AJ, Bellamy D, Ulaszewska M, Goike J, Gregory S, King CR, McLellan JS, Hill AVS, Georgiou G, Ewer KJ, Ippolito GC
articles.details: Researchers performed proteomic analysis of polyclonal IgG antibodies against the NANP tetrapeptide of the circumsporozoite protein (CSP), the core immunogen of the R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccine, in 10 malaria-naïve adult volunteers, including after a controlled human malaria infection challenge. The vaccine induced polarized anti-NANP IgG repertoires heavily skewed toward IGHV3-30/3-33 genes with minimal somatic mutation, which stayed static after challenge. These antibodies cross-reacted with the N-terminal junction CSP epitope despite its absence from the R21 construct, and NANP-specific IGHV3-30/3-33 monoclonals blocked sporozoite invasion in vitro and prevented parasitemia in vivo. Authors conclude R21/Matrix-M elicits minimally mutated polyclonal IgG that can target multiple protective CSP epitopes.

articles.headline: Malaria vectors bite indoors and outdoors before 10pm in Zambia
articles.title_original: Early-evening indoor and outdoor foraging by major malaria vectors in Nchelenge, Zambia.
articles.authors: Gebhardt ME, Markle HL, Krizek RS, Mbewe D, Mulenga FK, Lupiya JS, Barnett EE, Muleba M, Ippolito MM, Stevenson JC, Moss WJ, Norris DE
articles.details: This entomological study tested whether persistent malaria in holoendemic Nchelenge District is partly due to early-evening and outdoor foraging by local anophelines. Three replicate CDC light-trap collections were made at 24 households over 6 weeks during the 2019 dry season, set indoors, in animal pens, and in outdoor spaces from 16:00–22:00, plus overnight indoor traps in the third replicate. Twelve anopheline species were collected; the major vector Anopheles funestus dominated (n=1,865, 68.2%) and was caught primarily indoors (n=1,370, 73.4%). Human-blooded and P. falciparum-positive specimens occurred in all trap types, showing infected foraging before 22:00. Indoor An. funestus EIR reached 18.74 bites/person/6 months at inland sites, with little difference between evening and overnight traps.

articles.headline: Codesign workshops tailor perennial malaria chemoprevention across four countries
articles.title_original: Tailoring malaria control interventions to suit local context: codesign of perennial malaria chemoprevention (PMC) programmes through the Plus Project.
articles.authors: Center M, McGirr AL, Skjefte M, Ekobika LC, Bahibo H, Nhantumbo E, Wakpo B, Ogouyemi Hounto A, Yah Kokrasset C, Candrinho B, Malhotra K, Clarke SE, Gosling RD, Kouakou J
articles.details: This paper describes the Plus Project's use of a codesign approach to develop country-specific models of perennial malaria chemoprevention (PMC), an intervention aimed at reducing malaria and anaemia morbidity and mortality in children. Three-and-a-half-day codesign workshops were held in Benin, Cameroon, Côte d'Ivoire and Mozambique, bringing together national and subnational malaria, immunisation and child-health programme stakeholders plus development partners and research institutions, using participatory methods such as journey mapping and surveys to reach consensus. The process produced four different PMC strategies with four to eight contact points and varied codelivery interventions tailored to each country's health-system platforms, logistics and political context. Authors conclude the collaborative approach also gathered programmatic insights and increased stakeholder buy-in, with learnings applicable beyond malaria.

articles.headline: Anti-erythropoietin autoantibodies in children tied to younger age, not anemia
articles.title_original: Serum Anti-Erythropoietin Autoantibodies and Their Association With Younger Age in Paediatric Malaria Cases.
articles.authors: Amponsah FA, Addai-Mensah O, Antwi-Boateng L, Sackey B, Duneeh RV, Acheampong I, Adoba P, Awi DVA, Afriyie EY, Boateng R, Anokye AA, Agyemang V, Doe SK, Appiah SK
articles.details: A case-control study enrolled 90 children aged 1-10 years: 60 with malaria (30 with anaemia, 30 without) and 30 healthy controls, measuring serum erythropoietin and anti-epo antibodies by ELISA. Anti-epo antibodies were detected in 5.6% of malaria participants and none of the controls, a non-significant difference. Within malarial anaemia patients 5.0% were positive versus 3.37% in malaria without anaemia (p=0.640). Antibody positivity was significantly associated with elevated epo concentrations and younger age. The authors conclude anti-epo antibody production is not linked to P. falciparum infection or malarial anaemia but is strongly associated with younger age and elevated epo levels.

articles.headline: Review of current malaria vaccine developments and immune responses
articles.title_original: Malaria vaccines: Current developments and immunological insights.
articles.authors: Verma A, Ranjha R, Singh K, Yadav V, Bhaskar A, Dwivedi VP, Mohan M
articles.details: This review discusses malaria vaccine development, noting the disease affects nearly 263 million people globally with children under 5 most susceptible to morbidity and mortality, and emerging drug-resistant parasites threatening control and elimination. It covers the two malaria vaccines approved by WHO and future candidate vaccines targeting different life stages of Plasmodium. The review also highlights recent findings on the host's immune responses against malaria, novel vaccine candidates, and ideal vaccine requirements, while noting the parasite's genetic complexity and multiple life stages complicate vaccine development.
