articles.headline: Review of PvCSP as a Plasmodium vivax vaccine candidate
articles.title_original: Plasmodium vivax circumsporozoite surface protein (PvCSP) as a vaccine candidate: a brief review.
articles.authors: Alves KCS, Mourão EMV, Melo JM, Almeida MEM, Oliveira JGS, Mariúba LAM, da Costa VCA
articles.journal: Rev Inst Med Trop Sao Paulo
articles.details: This review examines the Plasmodium vivax circumsporozoite surface protein (PvCSP) as a vaccine candidate, noting vaccine research has historically prioritized P. falciparum, leaving P. vivax comparatively understudied due to its biology, including hypnozoite formation in the pre-erythrocytic phase, difficulties establishing in vitro culture, and lack of fully representative experimental models. PvCSP is highlighted for its immunogenic potential and partial protection in preclinical studies, but clinical trials with PvCSP-based vaccines have not yet shown a consistent protective response. The review provides a historical overview and explores recent innovative approaches to advance an effective P. vivax vaccine.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence 15% among pregnant women in Gambella, Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Malaria in Pregnancy and Predisposing Factors in Jor District of Gambella Region, Southwest Ethiopia: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Oguta O, Mohammed J, Umer U, Solomon ET
articles.details: A facility-based cross-sectional study assessed 446 pregnant women selected by systematic random sampling, using microscopy blood film examination and structured interviews. Malaria prevalence was 15.2% (95% CI: 11.4, 17.5), with P. falciparum at 8.96% (40/446), P. vivax at 2.7% (12/446), and mixed P. falciparum/P. vivax comorbidity at 3.6% (16/446). Significant associated factors included no formal education (AOR=1.32), lack of knowledge about transmission (AOR=2.7), stagnant water near residence (AOR=3.61), non-ownership of ITN (AOR=5.74), and low altitude (AOR=5.26). The authors conclude malaria prevalence was high and recommend ITN access, health education, and environmental measures.

articles.headline: Mining community knowledge of asymptomatic and P. vivax malaria in Amazon
articles.title_original: Malária incubada: a mixed methods analysis on knowledge and experiences on Plasmodium vivax and asymptomatic malaria infections in a hard-to-reach and mobile population in the Amazon.
articles.authors: Plessis L, Jimeno Maroto I, Lambert Y, Galindo M, Bardon T, Vreden S, Suarez-Mutis M, Figueira A, Bordalo JM, Douine M, Sanna A
articles.details: A mixed-methods pre-intervention assessment within the CUREMA project explored knowledge and perceptions of asymptomatic Plasmodium carriage and P. vivax malaria among artisanal and small-scale gold mining communities working across Suriname, French Guiana and Amapá, Brazil. The qualitative survey included 25 semi-structured interviews, 14 group interviews and 38 informal conversations; the quantitative cross-sectional survey included 539 participants, mostly men (73.5%), median age 38 years. Almost all recognized mosquitoes as vectors, and awareness of asymptomatic forms ("malária incubada") and recurrence risk was common, often attributed to inadequate treatment, but knowledge of P. vivax characteristics and radical treatment was limited. The authors stress context-specific strategies to improve elimination efforts (NCT05540470).

articles.headline: Mapping P. falciparum detergent-resistant membrane microdomains across blood stages
articles.title_original: Dynamics of detergent-resistant membranes of Plasmodium falciparum during blood stage development.
articles.authors: Sferra G, Fratini F, Birago C, Megliorini L, Mochi S, Lalle M, Paone S, Olivieri A, Ferreri C, Sansone A, Ponzi M, Currà C, Pizzi E
articles.details: Using quantitative proteomics, lipid analysis, and bioinformatics, this study characterized the composition and dynamics of Plasmodium falciparum detergent-resistant membranes (DRMs) in schizonts and gametocytes. Comparative flotation analysis of DRM-associated proteins and lipids revealed both conserved and stage-specific organizational features, and showed that after invasion host DRMs are remodeled, with erythrocyte junctional complex components internalized and repositioned in the parasitophorous vacuole membrane. Predicted DRM interactomes identified a core set of proteins shared between schizont and gametocyte stages forming a central, highly interconnected subnetwork, while stage-specific functions were peripheral.

articles.headline: TMV virus-like particle CSP vaccines match RTS,S in mouse challenge
articles.title_original: Comparison of major, minor and junctional circumsporozoite protein epitopes for malaria vaccine design.
articles.authors: Ryan E, Brown D, Harrison W, Foor S, Ashraf K, Bolton JS, Flores-Garcia Y, MacGill RS, Locke E, Bergmann-Leitner E, Roth AE, Robben PM, Matyas G, Soisson L, Miller R, Batchelor AH, Zavala F, Dutta S
articles.details: This study compared tobacco mosaic virus (TMV) capsid virus-like particles displaying minor repeat (NPNV), junctional (NPDP), and major repeat (NPNA) epitopes of P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein in an intravenous challenge model. Despite high cross-reactivity and in vitro inhibition, minor repeat candidates did not confer sterile protection in vivo, whereas constructs displaying NPNAx20, NPNAx5, and a junctional+minor repeat epitope induced sterile protection. Head-to-head comparisons of selected TMV vaccines and RTS,S showed equivalent in vivo liver burden reduction. TMV-NPNAx20 was selected for clinical-grade manufacture based on equivalent parasite burden reduction at lower antibody concentrations.

articles.headline: Relapses cause half of P. vivax illness despite immunity in children
articles.title_original: The probability of Plasmodium vivax acute illness following primary infection and relapse in Papua New Guinea.
articles.authors: Ross A, Koepfli C, Timinao L, Hardy D, Thüring T, Loeffel M, Kiniboro B, Felger I, Mueller I
articles.details: In Ilaita, Papua New Guinea, children aged one to three years were followed over 16 months for illness (fever with ≥500 parasites/µl) via fortnightly active and passive case detection and bimonthly blood-stage infection sampling, with primary infections and relapses estimated from prior genotyping analyses. A Bayesian model related observed P. vivax clinical cases to expected primary infections and relapses, using cumulative primary infections since birth as a proxy for immunity. The probability of illness decayed exponentially with cumulative primary infections, and illness following relapse was lower than following primary infection. Relapses contributed about half of P. vivax illness despite accounting for 80% of the force of blood-stage infection.

articles.headline: Pyronaridine-artesunate 92.5% efficacy in artemisinin-resistant Vietnam falciparum
articles.title_original: Therapeutic efficacy monitoring of pyronaridine-artesunate (Pyramax®) in treating uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Gia Lai province, Vietnam from 2022 to 2023.
articles.authors: Van Thanh N, Quang HH, San NN, Cuong NK, Trinh NTM, Van Khanh C, Edgel KA, Nguyen HC, Letizia AG, Birrell GW, Martin NJ, Edstein MD, Chavchich M
articles.details: In an open-label, single-arm trial in Gia Lai province, Central Vietnam (March 2022–December 2023), 120 adults and children with uncomplicated P. falciparum received pyronaridine-artesunate (Pyramax®) once daily for 3 days plus single-dose primaquine under direct observation. The PCR-adjusted adequate clinical and parasitological response at Day 42 was 92.5% (95% CI 85.5–96.2; 98/106). Median parasite and fever clearance times were 84 h and 36 h, parasite clearance half-life was 7.4 h (IQR 6.3–8.4), and 50.4% (60/119) remained microscopy-positive at 72 h, suggesting partial artemisinin resistance. The eight patients with recrudescence had lower Day 7 pyronaridine concentrations (median 33.3 ng/mL) than malaria-free patients (median 47.6 ng/mL; P=0.065).

articles.headline: Computational metric guides design of high-affinity anti-malaria antibodies
articles.title_original: A Single Structure-Derived Computational Metric Predicts High-Affinity Antibody Selection Against a Malaria Antigen.
articles.authors: Dale GA, Chen B, Koo JH, Wecera M, Kratochvil S, Batista FD
articles.details: This work derived a computational metric from predicted protein structures that captures binding-affinity information of variants of CIS43, an antibody targeting the repeat region of P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein (PfCSP). Using germinal-center principles, the metric was used to explore a sequence space exceeding 3×10^47 variants and to derive new high-affinity CIS43 variants. The framework was extended to generate high-affinity variants of an unrelated PfCSP-targeting antibody, L9, by maturing both homotypic and antigen-binding interactions. The authors conclude that coupling micro-evolutionarily selected mutations with in silico screening enables selection of high-affinity malaria antibodies.

articles.headline: Low malaria prevalence found among lymphedema patients in Western Uganda
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Malaria Among Individuals Living With Lymphedema in Kamwenge District, Western Uganda.
articles.authors: Mwesigye V, Tebulwa JB, Musinguzi B, Muzafaru T, Zamarano H, Nkubi Bagenda C, Mulogo E, Byarugaba F, Herbert I
articles.details: A cross-sectional study assessed malaria infection among 154 individuals with clinically confirmed lymphedema, recruited via purposive sampling from Rukunyu Hospital and surrounding communities in Kamwenge District, Western Uganda. Participants were 71.4% female with an average age of 54.7 years, and 96.8% had bilateral lower limb lymphedema. Malaria was diagnosed by microscopic examination of blood smears. Plasmodium falciparum was detected in 3.3% (n=5) of participants. The authors conclude that the co-occurrence of malaria and lymphedema underscores the need for integrated healthcare, continued surveillance, targeted interventions, and community education.

articles.headline: Rwanda deploys region-specific antimalarials to counter artemisinin resistance
articles.title_original: Strategies for mitigating emerging artemisinin-based antimalarial drug resistance in Rwanda: a promising approach for managing therapies in malaria-endemic countries.
articles.authors: Muvunyi CM, Gashema P, Mbituyumuremyi A, Iradukunda PG, Siddig EE, Niyonzima JD, Hakizimana E, Umulisa N, Noor AM, Mugabo Semahore J, Tuyishime A, Harelimana JD, Umuhire J, Butera Y, Nsanzimana S
articles.details: This policy-oriented analysis describes Rwanda's data-driven strategy of targeted multiple first-line therapies to mitigate rising Plasmodium falciparum antimalarial drug resistance, including significantly decreased artemether-lumefantrine (AL) efficacy linked to Pfkelch13 mutations. Because the A675V mutation associated with AL resistance was mainly reported in the western region, artesunate-pyronaridine was recommended there. Dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine was considered in eastern and central regions where R561H mutations predominated, while AL was maintained in the southern region where R561H prevalence was low. The authors suggest this model could inform other malaria-endemic countries facing emerging Pf genetic diversity.

articles.headline: Seasonal malaria chemoprevention costs driven by caregiver productivity losses
articles.title_original: Estimating the opportunity cost of seasonal malaria chemoprevention implementation in Burkina Faso, Mali and Senegal.
articles.authors: Owusu R, Gilmartin C, Diawara H, Bocoum F, Ndiaye O, Ruisch A, Aryeetey GC, Jainie D, Kokovena M, Walker D, Nonvignon J
articles.details: Using a repeated descriptive cross-sectional design with an ingredients costing approach, this study estimated the full opportunity costs of seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) for children under five in Mali, Burkina Faso, and Senegal, collecting data from April to November 2022 across two SMC cycles. It involved 376 caregivers in Mali, 398 in Senegal, and 373 in Burkina Faso, plus community health volunteers and supervisors. Financial costs per dose were US$0.99 (Mali), US$1.42 (Burkina Faso), and US$1.51 (Senegal); economic costs per dose were US$2.96–US$3.14. Indirect costs, mainly productivity losses, constituted 50%–66% of total economic costs. The authors call for comprehensive cost assessments to inform malaria control decisions.

articles.headline: Environmental, sociocultural, socioeconomic factors synergistically linked to malaria in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Malaria in pregnancy: a holistic exploration of synergistic association through environmental, sociocultural, and socioeconomic lenses in Pru east municipality, Ghana.
articles.authors: Bardoe D, Hayford D, Bio RB, Osei E
articles.details: A cross-sectional study of 456 pregnant women in the Pru East Municipality, Ghana (April to August 2024) used serological screening and a structured questionnaire, with analysis by chi-square tests, logistic regression, confirmatory factor analysis, and structural equation modelling (SEM). Malaria in pregnancy prevalence was 21.5% (95% CI 17.7–25.3), with Plasmodium falciparum most prevalent (14.7%), P. falciparum/P. malariae co-infection at 3.5%, and high parasitaemia in 6.4% of infected women. The SEM showed good fit (RMSEA 0.048) and confirmed that environmental, sociocultural, and socioeconomic domains were moderately to strongly correlated, exerting a synergistic influence. The authors urge multidimensional control strategies beyond biomedical interventions.

articles.headline: PCR high-resolution melting validated for distinguishing malaria species
articles.title_original: Optimization of the real-time PCR platform method using high-resolution melting analysis and comparison with sequencing and phylogenetic analysis for developing optimal malaria diagnostic methods.
articles.authors: Ahmadi O, Sharifi Y, Saeed M, Reihani A, Shamsian SA, Moghaddas E, Mirahmadi H, Etemadi S, Fotouhi-Ardakani R, Zarean M
articles.details: This study optimized a real-time PCR high-resolution melting (HRM) method targeting the 18S SSU rRNA region and compared it with sequencing and phylogenetic analysis using 300 samples from individuals with suspected malaria in Sistan and Baluchistan province, southeastern Iran. Microscopic slides were examined and analyzed on a Light Cycler 96 Instrument (Roche). PCR identified 9 cases (3%) of P. falciparum and 20 (6.66%) of P. vivax; HRM detected 15 (5%) P. falciparum and 14 (4.66%) P. vivax; sequencing found 13 (4.33%) P. falciparum and 16 (5.33%) P. vivax. HRM achieved 2.73-degree differentiation between species with complete agreement with sequencing. The authors conclude HRM is a reliable, sensitive, and specific diagnostic method.

articles.headline: Global review of artemisinin resistance across 100,000+ P. falciparum samples
articles.title_original: Understanding the global rise of artemisinin resistance: Insights from over 100,000 Plasmodium falciparum samples.
articles.authors: Balmer AJ, White NFD, Ünlü ES, Lee C, Pearson RD, Almagro-Garcia J, Ariani C
articles.details: This review aggregates spatiotemporal kelch13 marker data from 112,933 Plasmodium falciparum samples collected between 1980 and 2023 into a single resource, the most comprehensive overview of artemisinin partial resistance (ART-R) markers to date. It traces the history and current status of kelch13 mutations, focusing on their spread across Southeast Asia and recent emergence in East/Northeast Africa. The authors note that recent increases in ART-R in Africa mirror patterns seen in Southeast Asia 10-15 years ago, and examine fitness costs, treatment strategies, and local epidemiological dynamics that may influence further spread.

articles.headline: Green-synthesized MgO nanoparticles show antimalarial activity in vitro
articles.title_original: Antiplasmodial Activity of Green-Synthesized MgO Nanoparticles Using Achillea millefolium Against Chloroquine-Resistant and-Sensitive Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Khamsehpour N, Hanifian H, Nateghpour M, Raeisi A, Shabani M, Hassanpour G, Khezri A, Dehdast SA, Farivar L, Shahsavari S
articles.details: This in vitro study evaluated green-synthesized magnesium oxide nanoparticles (MgO NPs) made from Achillea millefolium extracts against chloroquine-sensitive (3D7) and chloroquine-resistant (K1) Plasmodium falciparum strains. Nanoparticles were synthesized with varying solvent ratios and characterized by UV-Vis, FTIR, FESEM, and DLS, with cytotoxicity assessed via MTT and hemolysis assays. The most effective formulation showed IC50 values of 0.17 mg/ml against 3D7 and 0.76 mg/ml against K1. The authors conclude the nanoparticles have potent antiplasmodial activity against both strains and promise as plant-based nanotherapeutics.

articles.headline: Serum proteomics of Plasmodium-infected wild macaques identifies zoonotic malaria biomarkers
articles.title_original: Comparative serum proteomics of plasmodium-infected free-living macaques in Thailand: Host immune responses and candidate biomarkers for zoonotic malaria.
articles.authors: Ruengket P, Roytrakul S, Tongthainan D, Udom C, Fungfuang W
articles.details: This comparative serum proteomics study analyzed 61 free-living macaques (25 Macaca leonina, 32 Macaca arctoides, 4 Macaca fascicularis) screened for Plasmodium by nested PCR, with serum proteomes profiled by liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry. Plasmodium infection was confirmed in 20.00% of M. leonina, 50.00% of M. arctoides (34.78% zoonotic), and 100% of M. fascicularis. Of 9,525 serum proteins identified, 698 were differentially expressed across species, and 36 formed PPI subnetworks linked to immune defense, apoptosis, and other pathways. Six of nine previously reported malaria-associated proteins were consistently expressed, supporting their potential as conserved biomarkers for zoonotic malaria.

articles.headline: Meta-analysis: arterolane-piperaquine non-inferior to artemether-lumefantrine for falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: A systematic review and meta-analysis of clinical trials comparing arterolane-piperaquine vs. artemether-lumefantrine for the treatment of uncomplicated falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Mondal A, Basu A, Modak DC, Goswami RP
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis of four randomized controlled trials compared arterolane-piperaquine with artemether-lumefantrine for uncomplicated falciparum malaria, using a random-effects model on risk differences. One RCT had high risk of bias and three had low risk. The 28-day PCR-corrected risk difference was 0 (95% CI -0.02 to 0.02), and the 42-day PCR-corrected risk difference was 0.01 (95% CI -0.01 to 0.03). The risk difference for at least one adverse effect was -0.01 (95% CI -0.02 to 0.00), with vomiting, anemia, and abdominal pain commonly reported for arterolane-piperaquine. The authors conclude arterolane-piperaquine is non-inferior to artemether-lumefantrine.

articles.headline: Engineered antibody L9_yd19 broadens malaria protection via new homotypic interactions
articles.title_original: Anti-Malaria Antibody Engineering Broadens Recognition Motifs and Reveals New Homotypic Interactions that Enhance Protective Breadth.
articles.authors: Chun J, Tripathi P, Flores-Garcia Y, Madan B, Lee GA, Fahad AS, Lei H, Teng IT, Hurlburt NK, Flynn BJ, Pancera M, Miura K, Zhou T, Idris AH, Zavala F, Seder RA, Kwong PD, DeKosky BJ
articles.details: Researchers engineered the protective anti-malaria monoclonal antibody L9, which binds the NVDP minor repeat on P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein (PfCSP), using site saturation mutagenesis and yeast display screening to improve affinity. One variant, L9_yd19, was modestly more potent in vivo against a chimeric transgenic Plasmodium encoding PfCSP with two widely spaced minor repeats from a Kenyan strain, with no loss of potency against the 3D7 benchmark. L9_yd19 also gained high affinity for NANP major repeats and protected against transgenic Plasmodium carrying only NANP major repeats (NANP12). Cryo-EM revealed it recognizes PfCSP through two distinct homotypic interfaces, a novel mechanism with potential for improved malaria prevention.

articles.headline: Review: splenic macrophages in malaria pathogenesis and immunity
articles.title_original: Emerging Role of Splenic Macrophage in Malaria Pathogenesis and Immunity.
articles.authors: Gupta A, Kalkal M, Das J
articles.details: This review synthesizes literature from PubMed Central and Google Scholar on the role of splenic macrophages in malaria caused by Plasmodium parasites. It describes how these cells contribute to parasite clearance by phagocytosing infected red blood cells, present antigens, produce cytokines, and regulate immunity. The authors emphasize that macrophage polarization during Plasmodium infection is not strictly M1 versus M2 but exists along a continuum, enabling dynamic immune modulation that balances effective immunity against excessive inflammation. They conclude that targeting specific macrophage functions and biomarkers could improve vaccine development, diagnostics, prognosis, and treatment.

articles.headline: Case report: IV artesunate for high-parasitemia severe P. falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: [Artesunate and Severe Malaria: The Importance of Proper Treatment Steps and Laboratory Monitoring].
articles.authors: Özer D, Çavuş İ, Özbilgin A
articles.details: This case report describes a 52-year-old man with travel history to Liberia, referred for thrombocytopenia and hematochezia, who developed severe P. falciparum malaria with metabolic acidosis and acute renal failure. Infection was confirmed by Giemsa-stained thin smear, the Abbott Bioline Malaria Ag P.f/P.v rapid diagnostic test, and real-time quantitative PCR. Labs included platelets of 11,000/μL, hemoglobin 18.3 g/dL, total bilirubin 8 mg/dL, and creatinine 1.69 mg/dL. He was treated with intravenous artesunate plus meropenem for secondary infection, with rapid response and declining parasitemia, then transitioned to oral therapy. The authors stress IV artesunate as first-line treatment and the value of parasitemia monitoring.

articles.headline: ACT compliance higher in Global Fund-supported clinics in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Compliance with the Use of Artemisinin-based Combination Therapy in the Treatment of Uncomplicated Malaria amongst Primary Healthcare Workers in Anambra State, Nigeria - The Role of Funding.
articles.authors: Chukwuka UB, Ibeh CC, Adogu PO, Chukwuka JO
articles.details: This cross-sectional comparative study in Anambra State, Nigeria, assessed health worker compliance with the National Guidelines for uncomplicated malaria treatment, comparing Global Fund-supported (GFS) versus government-supported (GS) primary healthcare centres. It reviewed 1,536 case records, surveyed 82 health workers, and assessed 32 PHCs. Overall compliance was 83.2%, significantly higher in GFS PHCs (89.1%) than GS PHCs (76.5%, P=0.00). ACT stock-out occurred in 43.8% of GFS versus 100.0% of GS PHCs (P=0.00), with greater guideline access, case management aids, and supervision in GFS facilities. The authors conclude that free ACT availability, guideline access, and supervision enhanced compliance.

articles.headline: Pregnant women know but underuse malaria prevention in Borno, Nigeria
articles.title_original: Knowledge and Utilisation of Malaria Prevention Strategy among Pregnant Women in Some Selected Primary Health Centres in Maiduguri, Borno State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Samuel G, Abdullahi M, Danladi SS, Jonah JH, Tweneboah E, Musa A
articles.details: This cross-sectional descriptive survey examined knowledge and utilization of malaria prevention strategies among 407 pregnant women attending selected primary health centres in Maiduguri Metropolitan Council and Jere, Borno State, Nigeria. Mean age was 26 ± 4.08 years. Most women (80.8%) had good knowledge of malaria prevention, but utilization was low, with 79.1% showing overall poor usage; 60.9% reported using long-lasting insecticide-treated nets and 20.6% had never used larval source management. Knowledge was associated with trimester (P=0.0044), gravidity (P=0.019), and education (P=0.001), but not religion (P=1.000). The authors recommend targeted health education and supportive monitoring.

articles.headline: Over half of pregnant women lost to follow-up in Northern Uganda
articles.title_original: Determinants of loss to follow-up among pregnant women living in a high malaria burden setting in Northern Uganda.
articles.authors: Ictho J, Segala FV, Novara R, Olung N, Patti G, Papagni R, Atim G, Totaro V, Olal L, De Vita E, Adongo J, L'episcopia M, Okori S, Dall'Oglio G, Putoto G, Saracino A, Di Gennaro F, Lochoro P
articles.details: This observational retrospective cohort study examined determinants of loss to follow-up (LTFU) among 1,558 pregnant women receiving malaria screening within the ERASE-Rise Against Malaria project across three facilities in Oyam and Kole districts, Northern Uganda, recruited July 2022 to June 2024. LTFU was defined as absent outcome data 30 days after expected delivery. 871 women (55.9%) were LTFU, reduced to 29.1% after recovery efforts. Protective factors included higher education (aOR=0.75), being primigravida (aOR=0.73, P=0.0275), and malaria during pregnancy (aOR=0.61, P<0.0001). Women at Aboke Health Center IV were over five times more likely to be LTFU than those at Aber Hospital (aOR=5.57, P<0.0001). The authors urge targeted interventions addressing structural barriers.

articles.headline: CPR gene mutations drive escalating pyrethroid resistance in Anopheles funestus
articles.title_original: Genomic Drivers of Pyrethroid Resistance Escalation in the Malaria Vector Anopheles funestus Across Africa.
articles.authors: Gadji M, Tazokong HR, Kouamo MFM, Tchouakui M, Wondji M, Mugenzi LMJ, Irving H, Hearn J, Ibrahim SS, Wondji CS
articles.details: This multi-omics study (7-year-gap temporal RNA-Seq, PoolSeq whole-genome, and functional analyses) investigated pyrethroid resistance escalation in the malaria vector Anopheles funestus across Africa. Spatiotemporal analyses (2014-2021) revealed massive overexpression of novel genes (V-ATPase, tubulin alpha-1, transposase) alongside canonical resistance genes (P450s, cuticular proteins, chemosensory), while epigenetic regulators were greatly overexpressed in highly resistant mosquitoes. P450 selective-sweep signatures appeared in rp1 and CYP9K1 in Central Africa, and at the cytochrome P450 reductase (CPR) gene an N70I mutation rose from 0% (2009) to 80% (2021) in Malawi, with a 5.9 kb promoter duplication in Ghana. Transgenic Drosophila confirmed CPR-70I enhances pyrethroid resistance when co-expressed with CYP6P9a, revealing a novel CPR-mediated resistance mechanism.

articles.headline: Modeling the diagnostic threat of pfhrp2/3 gene deletions in malaria
articles.title_original: Modeling the threat of pfhrp2/3 gene deletions for malaria diagnosis.
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Model predicts global spread risk of pfhrp2/3 deletions evading rapid tests
articles.title_original: Global risk of selection and spread of Plasmodium falciparum histidine-rich protein 2 and 3 gene deletions.
articles.authors: Watson OJ, Tran TN, Zupko RJ, Symons T, Thomson R, Visser T, Rumisha S, Dzianach PA, Hathaway N, Kim I, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Slater H, Okell L, Gething P, Ghani A, Boni MF, Parr JB, Cunningham J
articles.details: Using mathematical modeling informed by current pfhrp2/3 deletion prevalence and a literature review, this study assessed the global risk of Plasmodium falciparum histidine-rich protein 2 and 3 (pfhrp2/3) gene deletions, which reduce detection by widely used rapid diagnostic tests. Since 2010, parasites lacking pfhrp2 have been observed in 40 of 47 surveyed countries, prompting three countries to switch diagnostics. The authors identify ten priority countries for surveillance and predict primary spread in Africa will move southward from the Horn of Africa through East Africa within 20 years. Four countries yet to switch tests are consistently classified as high risk, and the authors call for refined surveillance and innovation.

articles.headline: US investigates first locally acquired malaria outbreaks in 20 years
articles.title_original: Public Health Response to the First Locally Acquired Malaria Outbreaks in the US in 20 Years.
articles.authors: DeVita TN, Morrison AM, Stanek D, Drennon M, Sarney E, Brennan W, Tomson K, Blackmore C, Broussard K, Duwell M, Blythe D, Rothfeldt L, Dulski T, Blount K, Ledford S, Blackburn D, Wallender E, Barratt JLN, Raphael BH, Lenhart AE, Ridpath AD, Mace KE, Williams SG, Beard CB, Parise ME, McElroy PD
articles.details: This qualitative study describes the interdisciplinary public health response to locally acquired mosquito-transmitted malaria outbreaks in the US (May-December 2023), the first in 20 years. It included 10 patients (mean age 39.5 years; 7 male [70%]) from Florida, Texas, Maryland, and Arkansas and 783 Anopheles spp mosquitoes across 4 states, none with recent international travel. P. vivax was detected in 3 Anopheles crucians in Florida; all Florida P. vivax cases shared one strain, while Texas and Arkansas cases were genetically distinct. All 9 P. vivax strains had signatures consistent with Central and South American origin, and Maryland's P. falciparum was consistent with African origin. Outbreaks remained contained to individual counties, and the authors conclude sustained US transmission is unlikely.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence highest in schoolchildren across three Tanzanian villages
articles.title_original: Prevalence and risk factors associated with infections caused by Plasmodium parasites at micro-geographic level in three villages of Muheza district in Tanga region, north-eastern Tanzania.
articles.authors: Challe DP, Francis F, Seth MD, Tupa JB, Madebe RA, Mandara CI, Kigadye ES, Makene VW, Ishengoma DS
articles.details: This cross-sectional community survey assessed Plasmodium infection prevalence and risk factors at micro-geographic levels in three villages (Magoda, Mpapayu, Mamboleo) of Muheza district, Tanga region, north-eastern Tanzania, in June 2021. Among 1134 individuals from 380 households (95.2% reporting bed net use), infection prevalence was 19.2% by microscopy and 24.3% by RDTs. Prevalence was significantly higher in school children aged 5 to <15 years (27.3% microscopy, 37.6% RDTs) than in under-fives and adults (p < 0.001). Households with closed eaves had lower infection odds by RDT (AOR = 0.91; 95% CI 0.85-0.97; p < 0.001). Microscopy prevalence varied significantly across villages (14.7%-24.6%, p = 0.001), indicating high micro-geographic heterogeneity to guide targeted interventions.

articles.headline: SP resistance markers near fixation, super-resistance emerging in western Kenya
articles.title_original: Regional variation in sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance genotypes and haplotypes of Plasmodium falciparum dihydrofolate reductase and dihydropteroate synthase genes in Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Bungei J, Ouma C, Zhong D, Oyweri J, Lee MC, Zhou G, Atieli H, Githure J, Wang C, Yan G
articles.details: This cross-sectional study genotyped Plasmodium falciparum antifolate resistance markers Pfdhfr and Pfdhps in 112 isolates from febrile patients in an endemic region (Kisumu, n=60) and an epidemic region (Kisii, n=52) of western Kenya. High-frequency mutations included Pfdhps 437G (99%), 540E (97.1%) and 436H (28.8%), and Pfdhfr 51I (100%), 59R (97.3%) and 108N (100%). The quintuple mutant SGEAA-IRNI was common (57.89% Kisumu vs 48.94% Kisii; p>0.05), while the sextuple HGEAA-IRNI was significantly higher in Kisii (44.68% vs 16.31%; p<0.05). The authors conclude fully resistant haplotypes have approached fixation and the dominant 164L allele in the endemic region signals emerging super-resistance, warranting review of SP efficacy.

articles.headline: Baseline malaria survey shows transmission hotspot in northern Côte d'Ivoire
articles.title_original: Malaria epidemiology in the Korhogo area, Northern Côte d'Ivoire: baseline assessment prior to a randomized controlled trial.
articles.authors: Tchiekoi NB, Zogo MB, Ahoua Alou LP, Somé A, Soma DD, Coulibaly I, Koné A, Fournet F, Taconet P, Dahounto A, Baba-Moussa L, Dabiré RK, Moiroux N, Koffi AA, Pennetier C, Assi SB
articles.details: This study characterized malaria epidemiology in the Korhogo health district, northern Côte d'Ivoire, ahead of deploying complementary vector control tools alongside LLINs, and found the area to be a transmission hotspot. Using passive case detection (398,424 person-weeks across 26 villages, July 2016-July 2017), three cross-sectional surveys in people under 21, and thick blood smears in six villages, mean weekly malaria incidence was 0.25 cases per 100 person-weeks (130 cases per 1000 person-years). Malaria case prevalence ranged from 13.4% in the dry season to 43.4% in the late rainy season, highest in children under 5, and smear parasite prevalence was 85.4-100%. P. falciparum accounted for 97% of single infections, with mixed P. malariae and P. ovale infections also detected, and the authors conclude the region is suited to evaluating supplementary vector-control tools alongside LLINs.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria in Ghanaian schoolchildren fell sharply from 2017 to 2023
articles.title_original: Reduction in Asymptomatic falciparum malaria infection amongst schoolchildren in three ecological zones of Ghana between 2017 and 2023.
articles.authors: Abban RL, Amenga-Etego L, Busayomi A, Acquah FK, Dzotefe GB, Kornu VE, Kusi KA, Amoah LE, Afrane YA
articles.details: This study compared asymptomatic P. falciparum infection in Ghanaian schoolchildren between 2023 (1,154 participants) and previously published 2017 data (1,109 participants) at the same sites, using microscopy and PCR during the dry and rainy seasons. Asymptomatic infection by microscopy fell from 16.05% in 2017 to 1.21% in 2023, and by PCR from 39.95% to 17.33%, with infection in the Sahel zone declining significantly by 60%. Submicroscopic infections were higher in 2017 (24.35%) than 2023 (16.46%), with the forest zone highest at both timepoints (47.01% in 2017, 28.28% in 2023). The authors conclude asymptomatic infection declined between 2017 and 2023 but continued surveillance and improved interventions are needed.

articles.headline: RTS,S malaria vaccine sustains protection over 50 months in children
articles.title_original: Sustained efficacy of the RTS,S/AS01(E) malaria vaccine over 50 months of follow-up when used in full-dose or fractional-dose regimens in young children in Ghana and Kenya: final results from an open-label, phase 2b, randomised controlled trial.
articles.authors: Osei-Tutu L, Kariuki SK, Lee CK, Fabre R, Bii DK, Adjei S, Oneko M, Attobrah Sarfo MA, Ockenhouse CF, Schuerman L, Buabeng PBY, Bakari A, Atieno C, Kotoh-Mortty MF, Otieno K, Ntiamoah Y, Sang T, Bollaerts A, Westercamp N, Ansong D, Agbenyega T, Samuels AM, Ofori-Anyinam O
articles.details: This open-label phase 2b randomized controlled trial in Agogo, Ghana and Siaya County, Kenya evaluated full-dose and fractional-dose (one-fifth) RTS,S/AS01E regimens versus rabies-vaccine control in children aged 5–17 months, reporting end-of-study results at 50 months. Of 1,609 children randomized (322 per RTS,S group, 321 control), vaccine efficacy against all clinical malaria episodes to month 50 was 36% (95% CI 19–50) for R012-20, 51% (37–61) for R012-14-26-38, 43% (28–55) for Fx012-14-26-38, and 41% (26–53) for Fx017-20-32 (p<0.001 for all). Cases averted per 1000 full-dose equivalents were highest for the fractional regimens (1151 and 1134). The vaccine was well tolerated with only five related serious adverse events and no related deaths; the authors conclude yearly doses maintain efficacy and fractional dosing could expand availability and cut cost.

articles.headline: Opinion: gut microbes may drive daily rhythms in malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Do microbes mediate rhythmic malaria parasite-mosquito vector interactions?
articles.authors: Riithi N, Mooney JP, Reece SE
articles.details: This perspective integrates chronobiology with evolutionary ecology to propose that mosquito gut microbes mediate daily rhythms in malaria parasite–vector interactions. The authors note that newly discovered rhythmicity in gut microbes, combined with microbes' known modulation of vector immunity and metabolism and their direct interactions with parasites, suggests microbes have an underappreciated influence on rhythms in mosquito susceptibility and parasite development. They argue that understanding how time of day of transmission shapes malaria spread is urgently needed, particularly as changes in mosquito biting rhythms likely affect microbiota rhythms. No quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: Droplet digital PCR detects asymptomatic low-density malaria infections
articles.title_original: High-sensitivity detection of asymptomatic malaria in high-risk Thai populations using droplet digital PCR.
articles.authors: Kramyoo M, Boonpeng K, Janchoo C, Siriwattanakul K, Phankhod K, Vongthanom N, Kritprayoch P, Yokanit W, Srisutham S
articles.details: This diagnostic study evaluated droplet digital PCR (ddPCR) for detecting asymptomatic Plasmodium infections in high-risk populations, comparing it against microscopy. Samples came from malaria-endemic regions of Thailand (n=403) and Thai workers returning from Sudan (n=270), tested using 18S rRNA genus-level screening and species-specific markers for the five human Plasmodium species. ddPCR detected infections in 0.25% of Tak, Thailand samples and 7.41% of Sudan-returnee samples, versus only 2.59% positive for P. falciparum by microscopy among returnees. Median genus-level concentration was 0.95 copies/μL (IQR 0.37–29.48). The authors conclude ddPCR can reveal hidden parasite reservoirs critical for elimination.

articles.headline: Artesunate nanoparticles improve parasite clearance in malaria mouse model
articles.title_original: Effects of artesunate-loaded nanoparticles on Plasmodium berghei treatment in a mouse model.
articles.authors: Wen H, Li Y, Luo J, Zhu W, Hide G, Feng X, Lun ZR, Lai DH
articles.details: This study tested artemisinin-derivative drugs (artesunate, dihydroartemisinin, artemisinin) encapsulated in zinc-based metal-organic framework (MOF) nanoparticles against Plasmodium berghei in a heavy-parasite-load mouse model. Artesunate nanoparticles at 16 mg/kg with three injections over a 24-hour interval significantly improved parasite clearance and combatted hyperparasitaemia (parasitaemia ≥5%), whereas the same dose over a 12-hour interval only delayed hyperparasitaemia. Using a 4-injection regime over 24 hours, 10 mg/kg dihydroartemisinin and artemisinin nanoparticles showed stronger clearance than non-encapsulated artemisinin at the same dose. The authors conclude nanoparticle encapsulation offers a promising approach against hyperparasitaemia.

articles.headline: Residual malaria transmission persists despite next-generation nets in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Residual malaria transmission in Western Burkina Faso: Vector Behavior, insecticide resistance, and the efficacy limits of next-generation LLINs.
articles.authors: Kouadio KWU, Amara MF, Soma DD, Dabiré RK, Diabaté A, Gnankiné O, Namountougou M
articles.details: This entomological surveillance study in two districts of western Burkina Faso assessed the impact of next-generation LLINs (PBO LLINs and Interceptor G2) on Anopheles gambiae s.l., using Human Landing Catch and Pyrethrum Spray Catch with hourly indoor/outdoor collections across four households per district. Seven Anopheles species were recorded, with An. coluzzii dominant followed by An. arabiensis, and both early and late biting behaviors observed. Entomological inoculation rates were 0.875, 0.437, and 0.063 infectious bites/person/month across the survey months. Kdr-west and kdr-east resistance mutations were detected across all An. gambiae s.l. members. The authors conclude substantial residual transmission persists despite LLINs and IRS.

articles.headline: Triazole amide compounds show antimalarial activity against P. falciparum
articles.title_original: 1,2,4-Triazole amides: Design, synthesis, characterisation, in vitro and in silico evaluation against blood stage parasites of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Tom AA, Rajendran V, Thottasseri AA, Gopan G, Mani M, Kannan T
articles.details: This study designed, synthesized, and characterized 30 1,2,4-triazole amide derivatives via molecular hybridisation and evaluated their blood-stage activity against P. falciparum 3D7 using the SYBR green assay. Meta-substituted derivatives were most active, with the 3,5-dimethyl (17), 3-Br (11), and 3-Cl (7) compounds showing IC50 values of 0.87, 1.27, and 2.17 μg/mL respectively. Against the chloroquine-resistant INDO strain, these had IC50 values of 1.55, 5.70, and 2.30 μg/mL. Compounds 17, 11, and 7 showed no hemolytic or cytotoxic effects on red blood cells, human Mo7e, or murine BA/F3 cells, and in silico docking with P. falciparum dihydrofolate reductase showed favourable interactions. The authors report the first 1,2,4-triazole amides as potent anti-plasmodial agents.

articles.headline: Review: how anti-CSP antibodies protect against malaria sporozoites
articles.title_original: Tissue-dependent protection mechanisms of antibodies targeting Plasmodium sporozoites.
articles.authors: Aguirre-Botero MC, Amino R
articles.details: This review examines how antibodies targeting the circumsporozoite protein (CSP), the main surface protein of Plasmodium sporozoites, confer protection against infection. It focuses on antibodies binding the CSP central repetitive region, which underlie protection from CSP-based vaccines and have shown efficacy in animal models and humans. The review explores how these antibodies neutralize sporozoites and mediate protection through tissue-specific mechanisms, arguing that understanding these multilayered mechanisms is critical for improving vaccines and antibody-based prophylaxis.

articles.headline: Review maps kelch13 artemisinin-resistance mutations across Africa
articles.title_original: Mapping Plasmodium falciparum mutations in Africa: A critical review of emerging drug resistance and implications for malaria control.
articles.authors: Gashema P, Kagame J, Iradukunda PG, Siddig EE, Tessema SK, Tegegne MA, Mazaba ML, Fallah M, Ngamije D, Harelimana JD, Muvunyi CM
articles.details: A critical review of Plasmodium falciparum genetic diversity reported in Africa between 2019 and 2024, focused on drug-resistance markers threatening artemisinin-based combination therapies. Validated kelch13 mutations clustered in East Africa, notably Eritrea (R622I, 68%) and Uganda (R561H, 52%), with emerging mutations in Rwanda (C469F, 36%), Uganda (C469F, 59%; P441L, 69%), and Tanzania (P441L, 20%). Partner-drug resistance markers (Pfcrt, Pfmdr1, Pfdhfr, Pfdhps) were prevalent in eastern (30-38%) and western (44-55%) regions. Contributing factors included drug pressure (23-35%), antimalarial misuse (10-12.5%), and cross-border movement (10%). Authors call for stronger drug regulation, community awareness, and molecular surveillance.

articles.headline: New potential malaria vector mosquito found infected in Venezuela
articles.title_original: First detection of Nyssorhynchus rondoniensis (Diptera: Culicidae) in southern Venezuela and its infection with Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Conn JE, Bickersmith SA, Moreno JE, Sallum MAM, Sanchez V, Moncada N, Grillet ME
articles.details: A field study in Bolivar state, southern Venezuela detected 88 specimens of Nyssorhynchus rondoniensis, a potential malaria vector, across several villages, identified by COI barcode sequencing. One mosquito tested positive for Plasmodium falciparum by real-time PCR, giving an infection rate of 1.14%. The species, previously known only from Acre and Rondônia states in western Brazil, was found in different years, in both rainy and dry seasons, in peridomestic and forest-fringe habitats, with anthropophilic behavior and early/late evening biting. Authors suggest it has likely been present but misidentified, and raise concern given the gold-mining environment.

articles.headline: Captopril reduces malaria-induced liver damage in mice
articles.title_original: Captopril reduces liver cytotoxicity in a murine model of Plasmodium chabaudi infection.
articles.authors: Ventura PDS, de Souza DV, Aguiar ACC, Carvalho CPF, Ribeiro DA, Nagaoka MR, Gazarini ML
articles.details: An experimental study in mice infected with Plasmodium chabaudi assessed liver histopathology after oral treatment with the antimalarial chloroquine, captopril (an ACE inhibitor), and losartan (an AT1 receptor blocker). Hematoxylin and eosin-stained liver sections were scored for sinusoid congestion, leukocyte infiltration, hemozoin deposition, and portal tract inflammation. Chloroquine alone or combined with vasodilators reduced these tissue-change scores. The authors report that P. chabaudi infection compromised hepatic histopathological parameters and that vasodilator treatment improved some alterations, with captopril in particular reducing infection-induced cytotoxicity in mouse liver cells.

articles.headline: Scoping review: using routine health data to gauge malaria interventions
articles.title_original: Use of Routine Health Data to Monitor Malaria Intervention Effectiveness: A Scoping Review.
articles.authors: Reithinger R, Bisanzio D, Cushnie A, Craig J
articles.details: A scoping review of analytical approaches for estimating malaria intervention effectiveness using routine health management information systems (HMIS) data. A PubMed search combining terms for malaria, intervention, effect/impact, and system/surveillance generated 957 records; 93 underwent full-text review and 49 met inclusion criteria, limited to studies from the past decade. Included studies were summarized by publication year, geography, outcome variables, target populations, interventions assessed, HMIS platform, and analytical approach (modelling and non-modelling). The authors conclude routine HMIS data can complement surveys and modelling to regularly assess intervention effectiveness and support countries' strategic malaria goals.

articles.headline: Adding primaquine to AL speeds gametocyte clearance in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Efficacy and gametocyte carriage in Plasmodium falciparum cases treated with artemether-lumefantrine alone versus with single-dose primaquine in Ethiopia: A randomized controlled study.
articles.authors: Kumsa T, Jima B, Tamiru G, Getu R, Melaku M, Tesfaye Y, Lindtjørn B, Massebo F
articles.details: A parallel two-arm, 1:1 randomized controlled trial in Ethiopia compared artemether-lumefantrine (AL) plus primaquine (PQ) versus AL alone for uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria over 28 days, enrolling 146 symptomatic patients with 106 analyzed. Both groups had no asexual parasites by day 2 on microscopy, but by day 7 PCR detected parasite DNA in 5.8% (3/52) of AL+PQ versus 18.5% (10/54) of AL-alone patients (P=0.09). PCR-uncorrected cure was 100% (AL) versus 98.1% (AL+PQ) (P=0.31), recrudescence was 2.8% (3/106), and gametocyte clearance was faster with PQ (100% by day 7 vs day 14), though only 9.4% had gametocytes at baseline. The authors conclude PQ may accelerate gametocyte clearance but low gametocyte carriage may limit its impact on transmission.

articles.headline: miRNA expression differs in lethal vs non-lethal mouse malaria
articles.title_original: Differential expression of miRNAs dictates the susceptibility/resistance during malaria pathogenesis.
articles.authors: Kataria P, Chaudhary A, Thakur RS, Sharma I, Kalkal M, Chauhan R, Malla WA, Singh K, Das J
articles.details: Small RNA sequencing compared miRNA profiles in splenocytes of mice infected with lethal Plasmodium yoelii Py17XL versus non-lethal Py17XNL strains, sampled on day 13 post-infection. Compared with uninfected controls, 171 and 220 miRNAs were differentially expressed in the Py17XL and Py17XNL groups respectively, with 79 commonly dysregulated, 53 unique to lethal and 86 unique to non-lethal infection. RT-qPCR confirmed selected patterns: miR-875-3p, miR-30a-3p, miR-499-5p and miR-212-3p were upregulated in Py17XNL, while miR-6992-5p, miR-704, miR-7676-3p and miR-1933-3p rose in Py17XL. Targeted pathways included MAPK, mTOR, FoxO, Th-17 differentiation and TGF-β, suggesting distinct regulatory mechanisms and potential therapeutic or biomarker targets.

articles.headline: Drug-resistance markers tracked under two malaria chemoprevention regimens in Mali
articles.title_original: Frequencies of molecular markers of drug resistance in the context of two different Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention (SMC) treatment regimens in the Koulikoro health district, Mali.
articles.authors: Traore B, Kane F, Toure M, Jørgensen MHM, Sanogo D, Keita S, Keita M, Sogoba N, Shaw-Saliba K, Diakite M, Shaffer JG, Alifrangis M, Hansson H, Doumbia S
articles.details: This molecular surveillance study analyzed 564 PCR-positive P. falciparum samples (218 in 2019, 346 in 2020) from Koulikoro, Mali, comparing SP+AQ and DHA+PQ as Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention regimens using next-generation sequencing of Pfmdr1, PfK13, Pfcrt, Pfdhfr, Pfdhps and Pfexo, plus qPCR for Pfmdr1 and Pfplasmepsin2 copy numbers. The Pfdhfr 51I-59R-108N haplotype exceeded 93% both years, and the Pfdhps 437G single mutant was most prevalent (~60%). In 2020, combined Pfdhfr/Pfdhps IRN/IAGKAS and IRN/VAGKGS haplotypes appeared at 9.6% and 1.2%. The Pfcrt wild-type haplotype rose significantly from 39% to 52% (P=0.01). PfK13 mutations Y493H and C580R were detected, and emerging Pfdhps-431V and the IRNI/IAGKAS quintuple mutant underscore the need for continued resistance surveillance.

articles.headline: RSL3 synergizes with pyrimethamine to block P. falciparum growth
articles.title_original: Synergistic activity of RSL3 and Pyrimethamine to inhibit the proliferation of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Nikolova V, Linnemannstöns K, Zahoruiko A, Ganter M, Lüder CG, Dobbelstein M
articles.details: This in vitro study showed that the ferroptosis-inducing compound RSL3 synergizes with pyrimethamine, an inhibitor of P. falciparum dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR), to suppress parasite proliferation in red blood cells. A similar synergistic effect occurred with cycloguanil, a pyrimethamine analog, but not with other DHFR inhibitors or alternative ferroptosis-inducing agents. The lipid-peroxidation antagonist Ferrostatin-1 largely failed to rescue parasite growth under RSL3, suggesting a mechanism distinct from canonical ferroptosis. The authors conclude that RSL3 and related compounds could act as adjuvants to enhance pyrimethamine's antimalarial efficacy and potentially overcome drug resistance.

articles.headline: Commentary on confronting malaria resurgence in Africa
articles.title_original: Malaria resurgence in Africa: confronting the challenges.
articles.authors: Ntoumi F, Aklillu E, Asogun D, Ansumana R, Mfinanga S, Yeboah-Manu D, Nachega JB, Zumla A
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: GIS and neural-network mapping locates malaria hotspots in northeast India
articles.title_original: Identification of malaria vulnerable zones in North East Region of India: Spatiotemporal assessment through geographical information system and self-organizing maps.
articles.authors: Mopuri R, Kadiri MR, Bhimala KR, Mutheneni SR
articles.details: This spatiotemporal study analyzed district-level reported malaria case data from 2011 to 2019 across the North East Region of India to identify vulnerable zones. Local Moran's I was used for cluster and outlier analysis, Getis-Ord Gi* statistics determined hotspot and coldspot intensity, and Self-Organizing Maps (an artificial neural network) identified disease clusters exported to a GIS to generate hotspot maps. Overall malaria incidence decreased consistently from 2011 to 2019, but significant and persistent hotspots were concentrated in Meghalaya, Mizoram and Tripura states. The authors conclude these findings provide a framework for targeting control resources toward elimination.

articles.headline: Astrocyte pyroptosis via GSDME drives cerebral malaria neuroinflammation in mice
articles.title_original: GSDME-dependent astrocyte pyroptosis promotes the progression of neuroinflammation in experimental cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Wang J, Zhu Q, Tong G, Yu K, Liang J, Huang Y, Wang Y, Yang C, Li T, Liang W, Li G, Liu X, Sun J, Li Y, Shen Y, Zhao Y
articles.details: Using a murine experimental cerebral malaria model, this study found that astrocytes mount a strong interferon-γ response that recruits brain-infiltrating CD8+ T cells, which trigger gasdermin E (GSDME)-dependent pyroptosis in astrocytes, amplifying neuroinflammation and neuronal injury. Genetic ablation of Gsdme or pharmacological inhibition of GSDME activation with mannose significantly attenuated brain inflammation and damage. The authors conclude that GSDME-dependent astrocyte pyroptosis exacerbates cerebral malaria and represents a novel therapeutic target.

articles.headline: Standard operating protocol for evidence-based malaria management
articles.title_original: Standard operating protocol for malaria management.
articles.authors: Pannu AK
articles.details: This standard operating protocol outlines a simplified, WHO-based approach to rapid clinical decision-making for malaria. It recommends parasitological confirmation by peripheral smear or rapid diagnostic test before treatment, except in suspected severe cases where testing is not immediately feasible. Artemisinin-based treatment is the mainstay: oral artemisinin-based combination therapy for uncomplicated malaria and intravenous artesunate for severe cases, including pregnant women in all trimesters and children. G6PD testing is not required for single-dose gametocytocidal primaquine but is essential before radical cure with primaquine or tafenoquine.

articles.headline: Wrightia plant extracts and tryptanthrin show antiplasmodial activity in vitro
articles.title_original: Phytochemical analysis and evaluation of antiplasmodial and cytotoxic activities of Wrightia dubia and Wrightia pubescens traditionally used for malaria treatment in Southeast Asia.
articles.authors: Abd Karim HA, Ilmi H, Widyawaruyanti A, Ismail NH, Zohdi RM, Osman CP
articles.details: This study analyzed phytochemicals and tested antiplasmodial, cytotoxic, and hemolytic activities of Wrightia dubia and Wrightia pubescens, traditionally used to treat malaria. Of ten extracts, three dichloromethane extracts (W. dubia wood, W. dubia twig, W. pubescens twig) showed significant antiplasmodial activity with IC50 values of 1.89, 6.38, and 1.79 μg/mL respectively. LC-MS tentatively identified 38 compounds, and chromatography yielded a new natural product plus nine known compounds. Tryptanthrin showed activity against P. falciparum 3D7 and Dd2 strains (IC50 2.50 and 3.14 μM). Active extracts and compounds were non-toxic to HepG2 cells and caused no erythrocyte hemolysis, supporting in vivo evaluation of tryptanthrin.

articles.headline: Imported malaria cases treated in a German emergency department
articles.title_original: The role of emergency departments in the treatment of imported severe and uncomplicated malaria - a retrospective monocentric observational study in a German university hospital.
articles.authors: Wörnle M
articles.details: This retrospective monocentric observational study examined adult emergency department patients with imported malaria at the LMU University Hospital Munich, Germany, from January 2019 to December 2023. Of 24 patients treated, 22 had P. falciparum infection, 1 had P. ovale, and 1 had P. vivax. Nineteen patients had uncomplicated malaria and 5 developed severe malaria; 20 received inpatient treatment (15 uncomplicated, 5 severe). There were no fatalities during hospital stay. The authors conclude that emergency departments play a crucial role in diagnosing and treating imported malaria.

articles.headline: CD47 drives NK cell recruitment to brain in mouse cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: CD47-mediated selective accumulation of mature Ly49h+ NK cells to the brain associates with the pathogenesis of cerebral malaria in mice.
articles.authors: Oakley MS, Malla P, Hayashi CTH, Majam V, Chorazeczewski JK, Okoth WA, Meredith S, KuKuruga M, Akue A, Rotstein DS, Kumar S
articles.details: This study compared Plasmodium berghei ANKA infection in wild-type versus CD47 knockout (CD47-/-) C57BL/6 mice to define CD47's role in experimental cerebral malaria (ECM). Absence of CD47 conferred partial but significant resistance to ECM (log-rank P < 0.001), with diminished brain sequestration of NK cells, CD4+ T cells, and CD8+ T cells, and lower circulating IL-10 and MIP1β. CD47 was critical for maturation, activation, and recruitment of brain-sequestered NK cells, associating with exclusive Ly49h expression by a subset of CD11b+CD49b+ NK cells. The findings reveal a previously undefined role of CD47 in NK cell-mediated ECM pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Review: how brain injury develops in pediatric cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Pathogenesis of brain injury in cerebral malaria: emerging mechanisms and implications for intervention.
articles.authors: Datta D, John CC
articles.details: This review summarizes recent advances in the pathogenesis of brain injury in pediatric cerebral malaria, a condition with high mortality and long-term cognitive impairment. It describes a cascade involving blood-brain barrier (BBB) impairment from local hypoxemia, ischemia, and endothelial activation after sequestration of infected erythrocytes, plus oxidative stress from free heme release. TNF-α, reactive oxygen species, CD8+ T cells, and parasite toxins cross the impaired BBB, causing neuronal injury via CNS immune activation and direct cytotoxicity; acute kidney injury and hyperuricemia may worsen BBB impairment and cerebral edema. The authors note neuroimaging, electrophysiology, in vitro BBB models, and experimental studies show promise for identifying interventions.

articles.headline: p-Cymene plus quinine cures malaria-infected mice in study
articles.title_original: Therapeutic Effects of Isopropyltoluene (p-Cymene) Alone and in Combination with Quinine Against Malaria Infection Through Modulation of Inflammation and Oxidative Stress.
articles.authors: Shater AF
articles.details: This experimental study tested p-cymene (CM) alone and combined with quinine (Qu) against Plasmodium berghei in 108 BALB/c mice across nine groups (six infected, three noninfected), with infected mice receiving normal saline, Qu 10 mg/kg, CM 5 or 10 mg/kg, or CM (5 or 10 mg/kg) plus Qu 10 mg/kg orally daily for 4 days after intraperitoneal infection with 1×10^6 infected erythrocytes. The CM plus Qu combination achieved 100% survival and 100% parasite growth suppression (p<0.001), the largest reductions in malondialdehyde and nitric oxide, upregulation of superoxide dismutase, glutathione peroxidase, and IL-10 (>fourfold), and reduced TNF (<1.3-fold) and IL-1β (<1.4-fold). The authors conclude the combination is effective via modulating oxidative stress and inflammation but call for clinical trials.

articles.headline: Malaria-on-a-chip model tests antimalarial drug clearance
articles.title_original: Translation of a Human-Based Malaria-on-a-Chip Phenotypic Disease Model for In Vivo Applications.
articles.authors: Rupar MJ, Hanson HM, Botlick BL, Sriram N, Rogers S, Zuniga J, Liu Z, Trimmer SJ, Ciurca JM, Long CJ, McAleer CW, Schmidt S, Favuzza P, Lowe P, Gobeau N, Hickman JJ
articles.details: A Malaria-on-a-Chip model incorporating human liver, spleen, and endothelium with P. falciparum-infected blood, maintained for 7 days in serum-free medium, sustained all intraerythrocytic life-cycle stages and allowed organ-organ interaction. Chloroquine, lumefantrine, or artesunate delivered as monotherapies to 3D7- or W2-infected systems each produced dose-dependent parasite clearance in both strains. In the 3D7 model, recrudescence occurred after chloroquine or lumefantrine but not artesunate, while in W2 systems chloroquine and lumefantrine stabilized parasitemia by day 7 and artesunate further reduced it. Population dynamics modeling of pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) outcomes was used to predict human in vivo efficacy and off-target toxicity parameters.

articles.headline: Quercetin inhibits P. falciparum small heat shock proteins
articles.title_original: Comparative Characterization of Plasmodium falciparum Small Heat Shock Proteins and Their Inhibition by Quercetin (3,3',4',5,7-Pentahydroxyflavone).
articles.authors: Timothy FM, Zininga T
articles.details: This study characterized three P. falciparum small heat shock proteins (PfHsp20a, PfHsp20b, PfHsp20c), each with a conserved α-crystallin domain, by recombinantly expressing and purifying them. Circular dichroism and thermal shift assays showed distinct conformations, with PfHsp20a most thermally and chemically stable, and malate dehydrogenase and citrate synthase assays confirmed all three have autonomous chaperone activity. The flavonoid quercetin disrupted their structure and function concentration-dependently, with PfHsp20c most sensitive, and inhibited growth of P. falciparum Nf54 and Dd2 strains in vitro with IC50 values of 5.4 μM and 7.8 μM, respectively. The authors present the first direct evidence of independent chaperone activity in these proteins and highlight them as potential antimalarial drug targets.

articles.headline: Review: extracellular vesicles in malaria pathogenesis and therapy
articles.title_original: Extracellular vesicles in malaria pathogenesis and Therapy: Emerging insights and future perspectives.
articles.authors: Abida, Kamal M, Alqurashi AA, Alhuthli SM, Alharbi MA, Alghamdi BJ, Alsharari SH, Khobrani MA, Almakrami JA, Albishri JO, Alshammari AK, Imran M
articles.details: This review examines the role of extracellular vesicles (EVs), particularly exosomes derived via the endosomal pathway, in malaria progression and immune modulation. It covers exosome biogenesis, their bioactive molecular cargo, and their functional roles in influencing innate and adaptive immunity and host-pathogen interactions in malaria pathogenesis. The authors discuss exosomes as therapeutic targets given their immunomodulatory potential and as biomarkers for diagnosis and prognosis, and highlight the use of 3D cell culture systems and in vivo models for studying exosome-mediated host-parasite interactions. They advocate for exosome-based interventions as a promising new paradigm in malaria therapy.

articles.headline: Call for a specific clinical definition of severe malaria in travellers
articles.title_original: Severe malaria in travellers-time to create a specific definition.
articles.authors: Barkati S, Libman M, Schwartz E
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Omics reveals insecticide resistance mechanisms in African malaria mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Impact of Omics Studies on Understanding Insecticide Resistance Mechanisms in Sub-Saharan Malaria Vectors: A Systematic Review.
articles.authors: Orogun YG, Ogunlana OO, Taiwo DI, Fadare OA
articles.details: A PRISMA systematic review searched PubMed, Web of Science, and Google Scholar for studies using genomic, transcriptomic, or multi-omics approaches to investigate insecticide resistance in Anopheles vectors in sub-Saharan Africa. Twenty-two studies met inclusion criteria. They identified key resistance mechanisms including metabolic resistance, target-site mutations, and cuticular changes, and multi-omics work uncovered novel markers such as CYP450 reductase (CPR), UDP-glycosyltransferases (UGTs), and salivary gland proteins. The authors conclude omics approaches enhance understanding of resistance and should be integrated into routine surveillance to guide region-specific vector control and elimination.

articles.headline: PfAnchor protein drives apicoplast fission, a potential drug target
articles.title_original: An Essential Adaptor for Apicoplast Fission and Inheritance in Malaria Parasites.
articles.authors: Blauwkamp J, Rajaram K, Staggers SR, Harrigan O, Doud EH, Xu W, Ke H, Prigge ST, Sun SY, Absalon S
articles.details: Using Ultrastructure Expansion Microscopy in blood-stage Plasmodium falciparum, researchers characterized an uncharacterized protein (Pf3D7_0613600), named PfAnchor, that localizes to the apicoplast throughout the asexual blood stage. Conditional depletion disrupted apicoplast fission, causing incomplete cytokinesis and parasite death; eliminating the apicoplast's elongated branched structure with azithromycin rescued these defects. Immunoprecipitation showed PfAnchor interacts with the dynamin-like GTPase PfDyn2, establishing it as the first apicoplast-specific dynamin adaptor protein. The authors conclude PfAnchor is essential for apicoplast fission and inheritance, highlighting parasite-specific organelle division as a therapeutic vulnerability.

articles.headline: Case: dengue may have triggered Plasmodium vivax relapse in traveler
articles.title_original: A Case of Intercurrent Dengue and Probable Relapsing Plasmodium vivax Malaria in a Returned Traveler to India: Case Report and Literature Review.
articles.authors: Kavanoor Sridhar K, Buskandar F, Dhaliwal M, Calloo GV, Boggild AK
articles.details: This case report describes a returned traveler from an endemic region with co-infection of dengue and Plasmodium vivax, in whom the epidemiology and clinical course suggested dengue triggered a P. vivax relapse. The authors note that dengue-malaria co-infection is underestimated because diagnostic approaches, particularly point-of-care rapid diagnostic tests, often stop once one diagnosis is made. They review the literature on co-occurrence of dengue and malaria in travelers and the evidence for dengue as a precipitant of relapsing malaria.

articles.headline: Antenatal-care data can track malaria prevalence and ITN coverage
articles.title_original: Antenatal care surveillance for monitoring malaria prevalence and intervention coverage: a multicountry analysis.
articles.authors: Munsey A, Digre P, Hicks J, Wagman J, Robertson M, Alao M, Ogouyemi Hounto A, Gansane A, Debe S, Candrinho B, Uhomoibhi P, Okoko OO, Lemwayi R, Aron S, Kangale CC, Kabamba BM, Miller J, Walker P, Gutman J
articles.details: This multicountry analysis assessed whether data from first antenatal care (ANC1) visits—rapid diagnostic test malaria prevalence, insecticide-treated net (ITN) ownership, and fever treatment-seeking—could monitor malaria burden. ANC1 prevalence in areas of six countries (Benin, Burkina Faso, Mozambique, Nigeria, Tanzania, Zambia) was compared with household survey prevalence in children. District-level ANC1 and child prevalence were correlated (Spearman r=0.79, 95% CI 0.65–0.88, p<0.001), and ANC1 prevalence predicted child prevalence (leave-one-out cross-validation mean absolute error 6.5%). ITN ownership estimates also correlated with household surveys, but treatment-seeking data did not appear useful. The authors conclude ANC1 data may help monitor prevalence and prevention.

articles.headline: Mosquito nutrition shifts malaria biting time, may undermine bed nets
articles.title_original: Biting time of day in malaria mosquitoes is modulated by nutritional status.
articles.authors: Oke CE, Rund SSC, Machani MG, Sabtiu ARM, Akuamoah-Boateng Y, Afrane YA, Reece SE
articles.details: Semi-field experiment with female Anopheles gambiae sensu lato given a blood meal on day 3 post-emergence and one of three feeding treatments: 0.5% sucrose, 10% sucrose, or 10% sucrose plus an additional blood meal on day 6. Mosquitoes were released on day 10 with human-mimic traps to track host-seeking timing. Low-resourced mosquitoes were 2-3-fold more likely to host seek overall and 5-10-fold more likely to host seek earlier in the night than well-resourced mosquitoes, which mostly sought hosts in the second half of the night. The authors conclude nutritional condition drives plasticity in biting time, an underappreciated contributor to residual transmission, and that targeting mosquito nutrition could affect ITN success.

articles.headline: Low pfhrp2/3 deletion rates support continued RDT use in Cubal, Angola
articles.title_original: Analysis of pfhrp2 and pfhrp3 gene deletions, and the structure and variability of PfHRP2 and PfHRP3 proteins: implications for the performance of malaria rapid diagnostic tests in Cubal, Angola.
articles.authors: Mediavilla A, Molina-de la Fuente I, Martínez-Vallejo P, Francisco K, Martins JF, Febrer-Sendra B, Maturana CR, Silgado A, Nindia A, Goterris L, Martínez-Campreciós J, Oliveira-Souto I, Molina I, Berzosa P, Sulleiro E
articles.details: Study of 100 dried blood samples from febrile patients in Cubal, western Angola, collected May to July 2022 and confirmed Plasmodium-positive by real-time PCR, assessing pfhrp2 and pfhrp3 deletions and PfHRP2/PfHRP3 protein variability affecting PfHRP2-based RDTs. Of these, 96% were P. falciparum; exon 1-2 deletions occurred in 7.29% (pfhrp2) and 11.46% (pfhrp3), with no exon 2 deletions and no double deletions. Histidine repeats varied significantly between RDT sensitivity groups, and epitopes 3A4 and C1-13 were present in 100% of samples (up to 15 and 18 times per isolate). The authors conclude PfHRP2-RDTs remain suitable in Cubal but require continued monitoring.

articles.headline: Plasmodium prevalence rose to 26.9% by 2023 in rural Tanzania
articles.title_original: Temporal and spatial trends of the prevalence of infections caused by Plasmodium parasites among rural community members in three regions with varying transmission intensities in Mainland Tanzania.
articles.authors: Challe DP, Petro DA, Francis F, Seth MD, Madebe RA, Mandai SS, Budodo R, Kisambale AJ, Chacha GA, Moshi R, Mbwambo RB, Pereus D, Bakari C, Mbata D, Lyimo B, Kanyankole GK, Aaron S, Mbwambo D, Kajange S, Lazaro S, Kapologwe N, Mandara CI, Makene VW, Ishengoma DS
articles.details: Community surveys conducted from 2021 to 2023 among 8166 individuals aged ≥6 in rural communities of Kigoma, Ruvuma and Tanga regions of Mainland Tanzania found an overall Plasmodium infection prevalence of 23.2% (n=1896) by rapid diagnostic test, varying significantly by year (22.9% in 2021, 20.6% in 2022, 26.9% in 2023; p<0.001). School children (5-<15 years) and males had significantly higher prevalence in all years, and prevalence among those with fever in the prior 48 hours rose from 40.1% (2021) to 45.7% (2022; p=0.049) to 58.6% (2023; p<0.001). Infection prevalence and odds were significantly higher among non-bed-net users, traditional-house residents, and low-SES households (assessed by modified Poisson regression). The authors call for targeted, adaptive malaria control strategies.

articles.headline: Cambodia stakeholders accept shorter P. vivax radical cure regimens
articles.title_original: Perceptions of shorter radical cure Plasmodium vivax treatment regimens and their implementation: a qualitative study among stakeholders in Cambodia.
articles.authors: Cassidy-Seyoum SA, Chheng K, Chanpheakdey P, Meershoek A, Hsiang MS, von Seidlein L, Adhikari B, Tripura R, Ley B, Price RN, Lek D, Engel N, Thriemer K
articles.details: In a qualitative study in a pre-elimination setting in Cambodia, acceptance of 7-day high-dose primaquine and single-dose tafenoquine as shorter treatment options for P. vivax malaria was high among 142 policymakers, healthcare providers, and malaria service recipients studied through interviews and focus group discussions analysed thematically. Policymakers and providers saw a need for shorter regimens given low adherence to longer treatments and attributed added value in quality, effectiveness, and reduced healthcare-worker burden, while patients valued faster recovery and quicker return to work and were willing to tolerate more adverse events over a shorter period. Some stakeholders raised adverse-event concerns and policymakers wanted further evidence generation. The authors conclude uptake is likely but will require confidence-building and enhanced adverse-event monitoring.

articles.headline: Malaria affects 22.47% of households across 19 sub-Saharan African countries
articles.title_original: Malaria prevalence and its determinants across 19 sub-Saharan African countries: a spatial and geographically weighted regression analysis.
articles.authors: Yitageasu G, Worede EA, Alemu EA, Tigabie M, Birhanu A, Angelo AA, Aweke MN, Demoze L
articles.details: Community-based cross-sectional study using Demographic and Health Survey data from 19 sub-Saharan African countries, covering 126,424 households and 11,594 clusters, with spatial analysis and Geographically Weighted Regression. Household malaria prevalence was 22.47% (95% CI 22.24%-22.70%), about one in five households, with significant spatial autocorrelation (Global Moran's I = 0.159; Z = 239.1; p<0.001) and hotspots in Benin, Burkina Faso, Togo, Uganda, Rwanda, parts of the Republic of the Congo, and Mozambique; SaTScan identified 34 significant clusters. Higher prevalence was linked to rural residence, rudimentary/natural roofs, younger household heads, poorest wealth quintile, lack of bed nets, and large households, while richest households, female-headed households, and natural/rustic walls had lower risk. The authors recommend targeted interventions in hotspot areas.

articles.headline: Survey finds attitude and behaviour gaps among at-risk groups near Thai-Cambodia border
articles.title_original: Estimating malaria risk behaviours and their determinants among at-risk populations in a pre-elimination setting, Sisaket Province, Thailand-Cambodia border.
articles.authors: Saeung M, Aung PL, Jupatanakul N, Manguin S, Chareonviriyaphap T, Phuanukoonnon S
articles.details: A KAP survey of 402 at-risk individuals in Sisaket Province in late 2022 used logistic regression to identify factors linked to poor knowledge, attitudes, and practices and to malaria risk behaviours. Over half showed good knowledge (51.5%), attitudes (64.7%), and practices (58.2%). Poor attitudes were more likely with lower education (primary or below aOR=2.2, 95% CI 1.2-4.0; senior primary aOR=2.5, 95% CI 1.4-4.8) and low socioeconomic status (aOR=1.8, 95% CI 1.1-3.0); poor practices were less common in agricultural workers (aOR=0.2, 95% CI 0.1-0.5). Risk behaviours were more frequent among males (aOR=2.8, 95% CI 1.2-6.8) and those with poor practices (aOR=3.5, 95% CI 1.6-8.1). Authors call for targeted behaviour change communication.

articles.headline: Barriers to malaria prevention in pregnancy in rural western Uganda
articles.title_original: Barriers to malaria prevention services in pregnancy: a multi-methods study in rural western Uganda.
articles.authors: Clubine Horowitz RA, Ngelese H, Baguma E, Nyangoma G, Shem B, Ntaro M, Mulogo EM, Boyce RM
articles.details: This multi-methods study screened pregnant women at their first antenatal visit at four lower-level health facilities for P. falciparum using rapid diagnostic tests, then held focus groups with women and interviews with midwives to explore barriers to prevention services like IPTp. From February 2021 to September 2022, 1,644 women were tested and 225 (13.5%) were RDT-positive; primigravid status and younger maternal age were associated with positivity. Identified barriers included delayed pregnancy/malaria testing, chemoprevention hesitancy, financial and transportation constraints, antenatal clinic operations, low bed net use, knowledge gaps, and spousal attitudes. Authors conclude young primigravid women are at highest risk and suggest decentralizing services into the community.

articles.headline: AI smartphone reader interprets malaria RDT images with 96.8% accuracy
articles.title_original: Evaluating the performance of an artificial intelligence-based electronic reader for malaria rapid diagnostic tests across Benin, Côte d'Ivoire, Nigeria and Uganda.
articles.authors: Lindblade KA, Ngufor C, Yavo W, Atobatele S, Mpimbaza A, Ssewante N, Akpiroroh E, Konaté-Toure A, Ahogni I, Kpemasse A, Tanoh AM, Ntadom G, Opigo J, Zobrist S, Griffith K, Humes M
articles.details: The MaCRA assessment evaluated the HealthPulse smartphone app, which uses an AI computer-vision algorithm to read malaria RDTs, against a trained human reference panel interpreting photographs in health facilities in Benin, Côte d'Ivoire, Nigeria, and Uganda in 2023. Of 110,843 RDT images, 106,877 (96.4%) were analysed; the AI achieved 96.8% accuracy (95% CI 96.7-96.9) and an overall F1 score of 96.6, with recall and precision >97% for positive and negative results but poor for invalid (recall 84.8%, precision 42.8%) and uninterpretable (recall 0.8%, precision 2.3%) classifications. Faint test lines sharply reduced recall (positive aOR 0.02, 95% CI 0.02-0.02; negative aOR 0.10, 95% CI 0.07-0.16). Performance varied by country, RDT product, and image quality.

articles.headline: RCT: airtime incentives did not boost citizen mosquito-sound recording in Tanzania
articles.title_original: "Fighting against malaria is everyone's concern": a randomized control trial assessing the role of incentives for encouraging local communities to record and upload mosquito sounds using the MozzWear application.
articles.authors: Mponzi WP, Dam R, Msaky D, Mwalugelo Y, Sinka M, Kiskin I, Herreros-Moya E, Roberts S, Willis K, Kaindoa EW
articles.details: This randomized controlled trial tested whether airtime incentives encourage community members to record and upload mosquito flight sounds using the MozzWear app with HumBug acoustic sensors and adapted bed nets ('HumBug Nets'). Participants were randomized to a control group (no incentive) or an incentive group (weekly airtime credit) and asked to record and upload data once weekly for four months, with an experience survey at the end. The control group performed well in nights spent recording and uploading compared with the incentive group, and intrinsic and extrinsic motivation varied by demographics. Feedback in rural Tanzania emphasized that fighting malaria was everyone's concern. Authors conclude citizens can contribute valuably to mosquito surveillance even without incentives.

articles.headline: Urban farming sites harbor most Anopheles larvae in Accra, Ghana
articles.title_original: Larval habitat diversity, physicochemical characteristics and their effect on the larval density of malaria vectors in the city of Accra, Ghana.
articles.authors: Sabtiu ARM, Hinne IA, Sraku IK, Halou DK, Doe RT, Attah SK, Aboagye-Antwi F, Afrane YA
