articles.details: Larval surveys at 15 locations in Accra during the dry (February-March) and rainy (June-July) seasons of 2022 used the WHO dipping method across five site categories (irrigated urban farming, low/middle/high socioeconomic status, and peri-urban) to assess larval habitats and physicochemical effects on Anopheles gambiae s.l. density. Of 727 potential habitats, 65.34% (475/727) were positive for Anopheles larvae, with drainage ditches most common (48.21%; 229/475). Irrigated urban farming sites held the highest An. gambiae s.l. abundance (27.24%; 6244/22,919), peaking in the rainy season (77.01%; 17,650/22,919; p=0.000). Polluted habitats had higher ammonium (3.4 vs 1.3 mg/L NH4-N). Anopheles coluzzii was dominant (54.4%; 368/677), and invasive Anopheles stephensi was detected.

articles.headline: Nanopore CYP2D6 genotyping to guide primaquine for P. vivax
articles.title_original: Enhancing CYP2D6 genotyping with nanopore sequencing to address allele diversity P. vivax malaria elimination.
articles.authors: Wongsurawat T, Jenjaroenpun P, Suwannasin K, Wankaew N, Sanguansakpakdee N, Amornpornviwat T, Dondorp AM, Day NPJ, Nosten F, White NJ, Imwong M
articles.details: This methods study developed a cost-effective DNA library preparation combined with nanopore long-read sequencing to profile CYP2D6 star alleles, which determine who benefits from primaquine therapy used to prevent P. vivax relapse. Using characterized DNA standards, the authors evaluated bioinformatics tools and proposed a new approach for resolving hybrid structural variants from long-read amplicon data. Genotyping of DNA from 90 individuals in Thailand's Greater Mekong Subregion achieved high resolution, identifying structural variants and a previously unidentified allele. The authors conclude the approach supports locally tailored testing to enhance P. vivax elimination.

articles.headline: EBV reactivation linked to altered cytokines in pregnant malaria patients
articles.title_original: Impact of epstein-barr virus reactivation on cytokine levels in pregnant women with malaria in the west region of cameroon.
articles.authors: Chatue IAD, Nyegue MA, Kamdem SD, Taya-Fokou JB, Djiyou ABD, Tchoupe EB, Tepa A, Neba RF, Junaid IT, Malhotra P, Netongo PM
articles.details: In a study of 220 pregnant women recruited from three hospitals in the West region of Cameroon, malaria was diagnosed by microscopy and nested PCR, and EBV reactivation was assessed by ELISA serology; cytokines (IL-6, IFN-γ, TNF-α, IL-10, TGF-β) were measured by ELISA. Women with reactivated EBV and severe malaria had significantly lower IL-6 (29.86 vs 55.83 pg/ml, p=0.02) and higher TGF-β (17.6 vs 6.1 pg/ml, p=0.01) than those with latent EBV and severe malaria, and IL-10 correlated strongly negatively with parasitemia (r=-0.9762, p=0.0004). The authors conclude EBV reactivation may impair inflammatory control of parasitemia and worsen P. falciparum malaria.

articles.headline: Age drives stronger inflammatory responses during malaria
articles.title_original: Age is an intrinsic driver of inflammatory responses to malaria.
articles.authors: Loughland JR, Dooley NL, Pava Z, SheelaNair A, Andrew DW, Tipping P, Bourke P, Engwerda CR, Lopez JA, Piera KA, William T, Barber BE, Grigg MJ, Anstey NM, Minigo G, Boyle MJ
articles.details: This study examined how age shapes inflammatory responses in malaria by measuring cytokines in non-immune children and adults during infection and testing innate cell responses in vitro to malaria parasites in naive children and adults. During malaria, increasing age was associated with higher inflammatory chemokines (CCL2, CCL3, CXCL8, CXCL9), CRP, and IDO, which correlated with symptoms. In naive individuals, adult classical monocytes and Vδ2+ γδ T cells produced more inflammatory cytokines and showed greater transcriptional activation; adult monocyte responses were dominated by CCL2, whereas children showed increased IL10 and IL10 signaling. The authors conclude age-dependent cellular mechanisms drive inflammatory responses in malaria.

articles.headline: Antibody responses differ by malaria exposure after controlled infection
articles.title_original: IgM, IgG, and IgG Subclass Antibody Responses to Plasmodium falciparum Proteins in Naïve, Malaria-Vaccinated and Semi-Immune Volunteers after Controlled Human Malaria Infection.
articles.authors: Gómez-Pérez GP, Vidal M, Campo JJ, Moncunill G, Jimenez A, Vázquez-Santiago M, Ruiz-Olalla G, Sanz H, Ayestaran A, Angov E, Dutta S, Chitnis C, Chauhan V, James ER, Billingsley PF, Sim BKL, Kremsner PG, Hoffman SL, Lell B, Mordmüller B, Dobaño C
articles.details: This study compared IgM, total IgG, and IgG subclass antibody responses to 21 pre-erythrocytic and erythrocytic P. falciparum proteins before and after controlled human malaria infection (CHMI) by direct venous inoculation of 3,200 PfSPZ across three groups: malaria-naïve (n=22), naïve immunized with PfSPZ-CVac (n=27), and lifelong malaria-exposed Africans (n=20, including 11 normal hemoglobin and 9 sickle cell trait). PfSPZ-CVac recipients had higher IgM/IgG to CSP and SSP-2/TRAP, while malaria-experienced individuals had broader blood-stage antibody responses, longer pre-patent periods, and fewer symptoms. Cytophilic IgG1/IgG3 to blood-stage antigens were positively associated with longer pre-patent periods and controlled parasitaemia better; the authors note noncytophilic IgG2/IgG4 elevation in exposed individuals could reflect regulatory responses that may hamper vaccine efficacy.

articles.headline: Historic P. vivax malaria fever therapy in psychiatric patients
articles.title_original: Clinical and Parasitological Characteristics of Plasmodium vivax Malaria in Malaria-Naïve Patients: A Review of Malaria Fever Therapy in Patients with Schizophrenia and Neurosyphilis during the 1950s and 1960s in Vienna, Austria.
articles.authors: Wolff S, Kainz RM, Reimold D, Lagler H, Mischlinger J, Ramharter M
articles.details: This study analyzed parasitological and clinical features of iatrogenically induced P. vivax malaria from malaria fever therapy in patients treated for schizophrenia or neurosyphilis at the Department of Psychiatry of the General Hospital of Vienna between 1951 and 1969. A total of 322 patients were included (schizophrenia n=147; neurosyphilis n=175); inoculation was mainly intravenous with doses of 4–8 mL of blood. First fever peaks appeared about 7 days post-inoculation, and the afebrile interval between paroxysms shortened progressively from 41 to 31 hours; after a mean of 5–6 fever peaks patients received quinine monotherapy or combination therapy. The authors conclude the extra-hepatic incubation of P. vivax is about 7 days and report a surprisingly short periodicity between fever paroxysms.

articles.headline: Transfluthrin emanator BiteBarrier cuts mosquito bites in semi-field trials
articles.title_original: The BiteBarrier transfluthrin emanator demonstrates significant protection against susceptible and resistant malaria and arbovirus vectors in semi-field trials in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Maasayi MS, Swai JK, Muganga JB, Moore J, Stevenson JC, Coleman M, Lobo NF, Moore SJ, Tambwe MM
articles.details: In a semi-field system simulating indoor and outdoor settings, the transfluthrin-based spatial emanator BiteBarrier provided over 93% (95% CI: 92-93) protection against mosquito bites indoors and 80% (95% CI: 78-81) outdoors, with mortality of 47% (95% CI: 43-53) indoors and 26% (95% CI: 22-30) outdoors, regardless of mosquito species or resistance status. It was tested over eight weeks of aging against five species—pyrethroid-susceptible Anopheles gambiae s.s. and Aedes aegypti, knockdown-resistant (KDR) An. gambiae s.s., and pyrethroid-resistant An. funestus and Culex quinquefasciatus—using landing rate and feeding success methods, with feeding giving more robust efficacy estimates. The authors conclude BiteBarrier shows potential for reducing mosquito bites and vectorial capacity over at least eight weeks of use.

articles.headline: Drug repurposing finds epigenetic inhibitors active against P. vivax hypnozoites
articles.title_original: A drug repurposing approach reveals targetable epigenetic pathways in Plasmodium vivax hypnozoites.
articles.authors: Maher SP, Bakowski MA, Vantaux A, Flannery EL, Andolina C, Gupta M, Antonova-Koch Y, Argomaniz M, Cabrera-Mora M, Campo B, Chao AT, Chatterjee AK, Cheng WT, Chuenchob V, Cooper CA, Cottier K, Galinski MR, Harupa-Chung A, Ji H, Joseph SB, Lenz T, Lonardi S, Matheson J, Mikolajczak SA, Moeller T, Orban A, Padín-Irizarry V, Pan K, Péneau J, Prudhomme J, Roesch C, Ruberto A, Sabnis SS, Saney CL, Sattabongkot J, Sereshki S, Suriyakan S, Ubalee R, Wang Y, Wasisakun P, Yin J, Popovici J, McNamara CW, Joyner C, Nosten FH, Witkowski B, Le Roch KG, Kyle DE
articles.details: Screening the ReFRAME repurposing library and a collection of epigenetic inhibitors against Plasmodium vivax liver stages identified inhibitors of epigenetic pathways—including DNA methyltransferase inhibitors and inhibitors of histone post-translational modifications—as selectively active against P. vivax and P. cynomolgi quiescent hypnozoites. Immunofluorescence showed a strong nuclear 5-methylcytosine signal, and bisulfite sequencing mapped DNA methylation across most coding genes in sporozoites, with methylation in proximal promoter regions and first exons potentially affecting gene expression. The approach was validated using MMV019721, an acetyl-CoA synthetase inhibitor that affects histone acetylation and was previously reported as active against P. falciparum blood stages. The authors conclude that several epigenetic mechanisms likely modulate hypnozoite formation or persistence, offering an avenue for improved radical-cure antimalarials, which must eliminate quiescent hypnozoites in the liver.

articles.headline: Commentary: sporogeny discovered in malaria transmission-blocking mosquito symbiont
articles.title_original: Discovery of sporogeny in the malaria transmission-blocking symbiont of Anopheles mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Herren JK
articles.details: This commentary discusses work by Parry et al. visualizing the sporogeny of Microsporidia MB, a transmission-blocking symbiont. The findings show Microsporidia MB localizes to mosquito germline tissues and undergoes octosporogony within developing oocytes, suggesting both vertical and potential horizontal transmission routes, with the identification of spores bearing hallmark microsporidian structures strengthening the case for environmental dissemination via egg deposition. The authors state these results fill key biological knowledge gaps, set new methodological standards for in vivo symbiont imaging, and advance Microsporidia MB's feasibility as a novel, sustainable malaria control tool.

articles.headline: PfRIPR malaria vaccine candidate shows genetic diversity in Senegal isolates
articles.title_original: Genetic Diversity of the Malaria Vaccine Candidate PfRIPR in a High Transmission Region of Senegal.
articles.authors: Nair M, Geering G, Agarwal A, Li R, Qiao Y, Xiao Q, Pouye MN, Thiam LG, Ba A, Hagadorn KA, Cisse A, Guerra N, Tawaldemedhen Y, Mangou K, Moore AJ, Diallo F, Sene SD, Sadio BD, Zhang E, Shapiro L, Patel SD, Mbengue A, Vigan-Womas I, Sheng Z, Bei AK
articles.details: To assess genetic diversity of the malaria vaccine candidate RH5-Interacting Protein (RIPR), deep amplicon sequencing was performed on 89 Plasmodium falciparum clinical isolates from Kédougou, a high-transmission region of Senegal. Nonsynonymous SNPs were found in 64/89 (71.9%) of samples; 26 nonsynonymous SNPs were identified, 15 of them novel. Of 26 SNPs, 16 were threaded onto existing RIPR crystal structures: 7/16 were predicted to destabilize RIPR and 2/16 to increase its stability. Three SNPs (Q737K, T738K, V840L) were identified in the EGF5-8 domains where neutralizing antibodies are known to bind.

articles.headline: Tanzania stakeholders weigh domestic funding for malaria, HIV, TB services
articles.title_original: Stakeholders' perspectives on funding malaria, HIV/AIDS and tuberculosis services in Tanzania through domestic resources mobilisation: a qualitative study.
articles.authors: Ngadaya FD, Isangula K, Kimambo H, Soka G, Kitinya C, Philbert D, Mwenda L, Kagaruki GB, Kimaro G, Ngadaya E, Kahwa A, Mfinanga S
articles.details: This qualitative descriptive study explored stakeholder perspectives on alternative domestic funding for malaria, HIV/AIDS and tuberculosis services in Tanzania amid declining donor funding. Data were gathered from 76 purposely selected stakeholders, including policymakers, programme managers, regional and district health managers, and healthcare workers, and analysed thematically. Four themes emerged: current funding sources, financial resources needed, proposed domestic mobilisation strategies, and implementation challenges. Proposed strategies included universal health insurance, reducing service-exemption beneficiaries, disease-specific funds, and product taxation; challenges included poor awareness, conflicting political promises, and accountability issues. Authors conclude the financing gap is significant and that strengthening insurance, reducing exemptions, and taxation could help, but community sensitisation and improved fund accountability are crucial.

articles.headline: Trial protocol: seasonal mass R21/Matrix-M vaccination to cut malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Seasonal mass vaccination with R21/Matrix-M for malaria elimination (SERVAL): protocol of the cluster randomised trial.
articles.authors: Dabira ED, Natama HM, Jaiteh F, Grietens KP, Bocoum FY, Ndiath MO, Mohammed N, Gibba B, Hill AVS, Ghani A, Erhart A, Tinto H, D'Alessandro U
articles.details: This is a multi-centre, open, cluster-randomised controlled trial protocol testing whether seasonal mass vaccination with the pre-erythrocytic R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccine reduces transmission in The Gambia (low-to-moderate transmission) and Burkina Faso (intense transmission). Thirty villages in The Gambia and 24 in Burkina Faso will be randomised 1:1 to intervention or control; all eligible residents in intervention villages receive three monthly R21 rounds (May-July 2024) before the transmission season, with a booster in June 2025. The primary outcome is malaria prevalence at peak transmission (November 2024); secondary outcomes include safety, clinical malaria incidence, coverage, acceptability and cost-effectiveness. No results are reported.

articles.headline: Correction notice: human-to-Anopheles dirus transmission of Plasmodium knowlesi
articles.title_original: Correction: Human-to-Anopheles dirus mosquito transmission of the anthropozoonotic malaria parasite, Plasmodium knowlesi.
articles.authors: Kumpitak C, Duangmanee A, Thongyod W, Rachaphaew N, Suansomjit C, Manopwisedjaroen K, Aung PL, Imad HA, Cui L, Sattabongkot J, Nguitragool W, Bantuchai S
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Malaria and helminth co-infection prevalence in Ethiopian pregnant women
articles.title_original: Malaria and soil transmitted helminth infection and associated factors among pregnant women attending public health facilities in Dilla town, South Ethiopia: cross sectional study design.
articles.authors: Tefera S, Bogale A, Kassie A, Abreham A, Bayih A, Ali MM
articles.details: A facility-based cross-sectional study of 331 pregnant women attending antenatal care at public health facilities in Dilla town, Southern Ethiopia (April 1-June 24, 2023) assessed prevalence and risk factors for malaria, soil-transmitted helminth (STH), and co-infection using microscopy of blood films and stool examination. Prevalence was 18.1% (60/331) for STH, 8.8% (29/331) for malaria, and 5.7% (19/331) for co-infection; Ascaris lumbricoides predominated (13.6%), and P. vivax (4.8%) exceeded P. falciparum (4.0%). Rural residence, living near stagnant water, and not using ITNs raised malaria odds 2.66, 3.09, and 3.67-fold respectively. Authors call for integrated antenatal screening for both infections.

articles.headline: Azithromycin's effect on child mortality by malaria chemoprevention season
articles.title_original: The role of seasonal malaria chemoprevention in the effect of azithromycin on child mortality: A secondary analysis of the CHAT cluster randomized clinical trial.
articles.authors: Gebreegziabher EA, Ouattara M, Bountogo M, Coulibaly B, Boudo V, Ouedraogo T, Lebas E, Hu H, O'Brien KS, Hsiang MS, Glidden DV, Arnold BF, Lietman TM, Sié A, Oldenburg CE
articles.details: A secondary analysis of the CHAT cluster-randomised, placebo-controlled trial of twice-yearly mass azithromycin (AZ) in 341 communities in Nouna District, Burkina Faso, examined whether AZ's effect on all-cause under-5 mortality varied by seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) season or coverage; all communities received SMC. Using Poisson regression, the AZ versus placebo mortality rate ratio was 0.77 (95% CI 0.60-0.98) during SMC season versus 0.89 (0.68-1.15) outside it, and 0.73 (0.56-0.96) in clusters with <80% SMC coverage versus 1.0 (0.59-1.69) at ≥80%. Interactions were not statistically significant; authors suggest further research on targeting high-transmission or low-coverage settings.

articles.headline: Deep-learning model detects Plasmodium vivax in blood smears
articles.title_original: Leveraging MobileNetV2 and deep learning innovation for high accuracy Plasmodium Vivax detection in blood smears.
articles.authors: Prathap VM, Yadav S, Qidwai T
articles.details: This study proposes a YOLOv3 object-detection model with a modified MobileNetV2 backbone—incorporating a Transformed Convolution Layer at the bottleneck—to classify infected versus uninfected Plasmodium vivax cells in thin blood smear images, aiming to improve speed and accuracy over traditional microscopy. The reported performance metrics were an accuracy of 1.00, precision of 0.98, recall of 0.98, F1 score of 0.97, and mean average precision (mAP) of 0.90. Authors conclude the approach offers a better automated diagnostic aid for medical professionals.

articles.headline: Alstonia boonei extract lowers parasitemia and blood glucose in malaria-diabetic mice
articles.title_original: Alstonia boonei stem bark aqueous extract ameliorates elevated parasitemia levels, normalises blood glucose, modulates inflammatory biomarkers and enhances antioxidant status in Plasmodium berghei-infected/diabetic mice.
articles.authors: Agunloye OM, Akintoye OF, Oboh G
articles.details: In a controlled animal study, male mice were divided into five groups: normal control, untreated Plasmodium berghei-infected/diabetic, chloroquine-metformin treated, and Alstonia boonei stem bark aqueous extract treated at 40 and 80 mg/kg. The extract produced a dose-dependent reduction in fasting blood glucose and parasitemia, inhibited α-amylase and α-glucosidase, enhanced antioxidant enzymes (SOD, catalase, GSH), restored red blood cell count, hemoglobin, and packed cell volume, reduced C-reactive protein, and raised interleukin-10. Effects were stronger at 80 mg/kg and comparable to the standard drug group. Authors conclude the extract has potential for managing malaria-diabetes comorbidity.

articles.headline: Water, sanitation, hygiene, and malaria factors predict diarrhea outcomes in Chad
articles.title_original: Association of Diarrhea Outcomes with Drinking Water Factors, Sanitation, Hygiene, and Malaria Practices in the Population of Béré, Chad.
articles.authors: Boutrin MC, Andersen M, Gately Z, McLarty C, Santos E
articles.journal: Int J Environ Res Public Health
articles.details: A survey of 484 respondents in the rural town of Béré, Chad, administered by trained community health workers using random sampling, assessed diarrhea outcomes and associated factors. Respondents were predominantly Nangtchéré (87%), evangelical (63%), male (88%), and aged 40-59 (43%). Drinking water treatment, transport and storage, malaria-related factors, sanitation and hygiene practices, and children's diarrhea experience and treatment were all significant predictors of adult diarrhea outcomes (p < 0.001). Similar factors, plus health advice source, predicted children's diarrhea outcomes (p < 0.001), and the authors conclude that future interventions targeting these factors are warranted.

articles.headline: Case report: tetanus with cerebral malaria and probable meningitis in child
articles.title_original: Generalized Tetanus Associated With Malaria and Probable Meningitis in a 4-Year-Old Patient: A Case Report in Limbe Regional Hospital, Cameroon.
articles.authors: Tchuisseu Ngapjang CD, Fotso C, Gobina R, Lingo PA, Shumbang Tchumukong QJ, Kouam BB, Effila M, Berinyuy Nyuydzefon E, Vincent Siysi V
articles.details: This case report describes a 4-year-old unvaccinated male referred from a nearby hospital for better management of severe malaria and probable enteric fever after presenting with febrile seizures, neck stiffness, and vomiting. Shortly after admission he developed trismus, recurrent generalized paroxysmal muscle spasms, and opisthotonos, leading to diagnoses of tetanus and cerebral malaria, with probable bacterial meningitis as a relevant differential. Owing to the caregiver's financial constraints, no specific investigations were carried out, and he was admitted to the ICU and empirically treated with anti-tetanus toxoid, antibiotics, and antimalarial. His condition improved considerably within the first 48 hours, and he spent 5 additional days in the hospital before being discharged.

articles.headline: Lifelong malaria exposure, not vaccination, alters B cell subsets after CHMI
articles.title_original: Effect of controlled human Plasmodium falciparum infection on B cell subsets in individuals with different levels of malaria immunity.
articles.authors: Requena P, Gómez-Pérez GP, McCall MBB, Barrios D, Aguilar R, Fernández-Morata J, Vidal M, Campo JJ, Sanchez C, Yazdabankhsh M, Sim BKL, Hoffman SL, Kremsner P, Lell B, Mordmüller B, Dobaño C, Moncunill G
articles.details: This controlled human malaria infection (CHMI) study compared B cell phenotypes in malaria-naïve, PfSPZ-CVac–immunized, and semi-immune (lifelong-exposed) volunteers. Compared to naïve individuals, semi-immune but not vaccinated volunteers had increased baseline frequencies of immature B cells, active naive B cells, active atypical and classical memory B cells, and CD1c+ B cells, but lower frequencies of some IgG+ B cells. During early blood-stage infection (day 11 after CHMI), resting classical memory B cells expanded in all three groups. Vaccination altered CHMI's effect, associating positively with resting classical memory B cells (β = 0.190, 95% CI 0.011-0.368) and negatively with CD1c+ resting atypical memory B cells (β = -0.328, 95% CI -0.621 to -0.032), and sickle cell trait also modulated effects.

articles.headline: Children with severe malaria sicker at health centres than community workers in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Treatment-Seeking for Children with Suspected Severe Malaria Attending Community Health Workers and Primary Health Centres in Adamawa State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Lee TT, Omoluabi E, Ayodeji K, Yusuf O, Okon C, Brunner NC, Delvento G, Signorell A, Lambiris MJ, Kwiatkowski M, Burri C, Lengeler C, Emedo E, Cheshi F, Fashanu C, Wiwa O, Hetzel MW
articles.details: In the context of introducing pre-referral rectal artesunate in Adamawa State, Nigeria, where case fatality was 19% at primary health centres (PHCs) versus 6% at community health workers (CORPs), researchers enrolled 589 children under 5 with fever and danger signs of severe malaria attending CORPs (N = 314) and PHCs (N = 275). After 28 days, caregivers were interviewed. Compared to CORP attendees, PHC children were more likely to live in urban areas, have ≥4 clinical danger signs (50% vs. 39%, p = 0.02), signs of central nervous system involvement (90% vs. 74%, p < 0.01), and received initial home treatment (42% vs. 33%, p = 0.04). Authors conclude PHCs may see sicker children and need health system strengthening.

articles.headline: Climate change projected to expand malaria range in Papua New Guinea
articles.title_original: Climate Change Is Expected to Expand Malaria Transmission Range and Population at Risk in Papua New Guinea.
articles.authors: Karl S, Skinner EB, McEwen S, Keven J, Kisomb J, Robinson LJ, Laman M
articles.details: Using satellite-derived temperature data and climate models, this study assessed malaria transmission suitability across Papua New Guinea from 1960 to 2019 and projected changes through 2040, applying a temperature-dependent basic reproduction number (R0). The proportion of people in suitable areas rose from 58% to 61% (an additional 249,125 people) over 1960–2019, and is projected to reach 74% by 2040 under a conservative model (an additional 2,802,709 people). Interventions had greater impact where R0 < 0.3. The population needing malaria control is expected to double to 13.4 million by 2040, with 2.8 million attributable to climate change, especially in densely populated highland regions.

articles.headline: Education, internet use linked to malaria vaccine awareness in Ghanaian women
articles.title_original: Determinants of Malaria Vaccine Awareness Among Women in Ghana: Findings From the 2022 Ghana Malaria Indicator Survey.
articles.authors: Sarfo M, Larbi-Ampofo AK, Lawal O, Bonnah SK, Eshun G
articles.details: This analysis of 38,459 women aged 15-49 from the 2022 Ghana Malaria Indicator Survey examined sociodemographic determinants of malaria vaccine awareness, defined as having heard of the vaccine in the past 6 months, using survey-weighted logistic regression. Awareness was higher among women with tertiary education (AOR = 2.89, 95% CI: 2.50-3.34), those who used the internet in the past 12 months (AOR = 1.85, 95% CI: 1.74-1.97), and Greater Accra residents (AOR = 1.75, 95% CI: 1.54-2.00). Awareness increased with age, peaking among women aged 45-49 years (AOR = 2.03, 95% CI: 1.60-2.57). The authors conclude that public health campaigns should prioritize equitable access to vaccine information for underserved populations.

articles.headline: Umbrella review: 10% malaria prevalence among vulnerable groups in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Burden of malaria infection among most vulnerable populations in Ethiopia: an umbrella review of systematic reviews and meta-analyses.
articles.authors: Demeke A, Kassa G, Gebrie H, Tefera S, Getu E, Dube SJ, Bayable A, Kassie A, Muleta B, Eba WW, Gebremeskele BT, Demeke AD
articles.details: This umbrella review synthesized six systematic reviews and meta-analyses (from 3,388 records) on malaria prevalence and risk factors among children, pregnant women, and migrant populations in Ethiopia, using a random-effects model. The overall pooled prevalence among vulnerable populations was 10.10% (95% CI: 0.36–19.85), with low heterogeneity (I² = 0.0%, P = 0.982). Not using an insecticide-treated net (pooled OR: 5.22, 95% CI: 3.93–6.94) and proximity to stagnant water (pooled OR: 4.21, 95% CI: 2.75–6.46) were independently associated with increased risk. Authors conclude these consistent risk factors can guide targeted prevention strategies.

articles.headline: P450 CYP6P9a/b gives Anopheles funestus cross-resistance to new insecticide
articles.title_original: The duplicated cytochrome P450 CYP6P9a/b confers cross-resistance to a mitochondrial complex I inhibitor in the African malaria vector Anopheles funestus.
articles.authors: Tekoh TA, Mugenzi LMJ, Menze B, Tchapga W, Wondji M, Tchouakui M, Small G, Wondji CS
articles.details: This laboratory study tested whether cytochrome P450 genes CYP6P9a/b, linked to pyrethroid resistance, confer cross-resistance to a novel mitochondrial complex I inhibitor insecticide (code-named Sherlock) in Anopheles funestus, using CDC bottle, WHO cone, and WHO tunnel bioassays plus genotyping and qRT-PCR. Field An. gambiae and An. funestus from Cameroon were fully susceptible to Sherlock, while the FUMOZ-R strain showed moderate resistance. Mosquitoes carrying one CYP6P9a_R allele had higher odds of surviving Sherlock (CDC 1xDC: OR = 5.3; 5xDC: OR = 18.6; cone: OR = 5.1; tunnel: OR = 6.6; all p < 0.0001), with elevated CYP6P9a expression in survivors. The L119F_GSTe2 marker did not confer cross-resistance.

articles.headline: Half of under-five malaria cases face treatment delay in Horn of Africa
articles.title_original: Prevalence and associated factors of delay in seeking malaria treatment among under five children in the Horn of Africa: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Agimas MC, Aweke MN, Mengistu B, Baffa LD, Fentie EA, Shewarega ES, Belew AK, Muhammad EA
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis assessed the prevalence and factors associated with delayed malaria treatment-seeking among under-five children in the Horn of Africa, using a random-effects model with assessment of heterogeneity and publication bias. The pooled prevalence of delayed treatment was 48% (95% CI: 34%–63%). Factors increasing delay included expensiveness (OR = 4.39, 95% CI: 2.49–7.76), drug side effects (OR = 2.94), distance >3000 meters (OR = 2.59), and history of child death (OR = 2.5), while formal education (OR = 0.69) and middle income (OR = 0.42) were protective. Authors emphasize improving financial and physical access to healthcare to reduce delays.

articles.headline: Vegetation near homes raises malaria risk in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Housing and household characteristics associated with malaria vectors abundance and clinical malaria incidence in a semi-urban and a rural area of Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Nikiema M, Dambach P, Kabore J, Some SYM, Ouedraogo A, Barry Y, Boudo V, Chabi BAM, Sie A, Badolo A, Gneme A
articles.details: Using CDC light traps, researchers sampled indoor mosquitoes in 355 randomly selected houses across semi-urban and rural areas of the Nouna health district, Burkina Faso, from July to October 2021, applying Poisson and logistic models to assess how housing and household characteristics affect vector abundance, mosquito infection, and malaria cases. Of 22,863 mosquitoes collected, Anopheles coluzzii, An. gambiae sensu stricto, and An. arabiensis predominated. Wall type, roof type, breeding sites, and LLIN use showed no significant effect, but surrounding vegetation was associated with mosquito abundance (RR 2.5, CI 1.43-4.15, p<0.001), vector infection (RR 2.5, CI 1.74-3.33, p<0.001), and malaria cases (RR 1.4, CI 1.03-2.0, p=0.048). Presence of children under five and female householders also predicted malaria cases, leading the authors to conclude that environmental management should complement vector control.

articles.headline: Plasmodium-altered skin flora and odor increase mosquito attraction
articles.title_original: [Skin flora, odor and malaria transmission].
articles.authors: Song X, Wang J
articles.journal: Zhongguo Xue Xi Chong Bing Fang Zhi Za Zhi
articles.details: This commentary discusses how host body odor attracts female Anopheles mosquitoes and how Plasmodium infection changes host odors to increase attractiveness, though mechanisms remain unclear. It highlights a recent study reporting marked changes in skin flora and volatile substances in mice following Plasmodium infection, with ethylbenzene identified as increasing host attractiveness to mosquitoes. The authors suggest these findings offer new insights for developing novel malaria control strategies.

articles.headline: P. vivax ligand RBP1a binds TfR1, prohibitin-2, and basigin
articles.title_original: The reticulocyte restriction: invasion ligand RBP1a of Plasmodium vivax targets human TfR1, prohibitin-2, and basigin.
articles.authors: Molina-Franky J, Röth D, Ararat-Sarria M, Patarroyo MA, Kalkum M
articles.details: In vitro study evaluating the erythroid cell lines JK-1 and BEL-A as reticulocyte surrogates for Plasmodium vivax invasion, combined with comparative membrane proteomics (LC-MS) of these lines, reticulocytes, and mature erythrocytes. Interactions between parasite ligand PvRBP1a (residues 158-650) and host receptors were mapped by TurboID proximity labeling and validated by ELISA. Both JK-1 and BEL-A supported invasion. Beyond known receptors TfR1/CD71, CD98hc, and basigin, candidates including prohibitin-2, CAT-1, ATB(0), CD36, integrin beta-1, and CNNM3 were identified; proximity labeling confirmed TfR1 and BSG interactions and newly implicated PHB2 as a co-receptor. Authors present these as platforms for dissecting invasion and developing targeted antimalarials.

articles.headline: Plasmodium ARK1 kinase drives atypical mitosis, divergent passenger complex
articles.title_original: Plasmodium ARK1 regulates spindle formation during atypical mitosis and forms a divergent chromosomal passenger complex.
articles.authors: Tewari R, Nagar A, Yanase R, Zeeshan M, Ferguson DJP, Abel S, Pashley S, Mishra A, Eze A, Rea E, Brady D, Bottrill A, Vaughan S, Roch KL, Guttery D, Holder A, Tromer E, Sharma P
articles.details: Mechanistic study identifying Aurora-related kinase 1 (ARK1) as a key component of inner MTOC and spindle formation in Plasmodium, controlling kinetochore dynamics and mitotic progression. Conditional ARK1 depletion disrupted spindle biogenesis, kinetochore segregation, karyokinesis, and cytokinesis during both schizogony and male gametogony, and affected parasite transmission. Interactome analysis revealed ARK1 as the catalytic core of a non-canonical chromosomal passenger complex containing two divergent INCENPs but lacking Survivin and Borealin. Comparative genomics suggests this architecture arose early in Apicomplexa. Authors identify the ARK1-INCENP interface as a potential multistage target for malaria therapeutics.

articles.headline: Cytokine levels track parasite density in Sudanese malaria patients
articles.title_original: Assessment of Cytokines TNF-alpha, IFN-gamma, TGF-beta-1, and IL-10 in Malaria Patients of the River Nile State , Sudan: A critical study of immune response.
articles.authors: Hamad MNM, Albadrani GM, Ghazwani AA, Abdelmola A, Suliman RS, Mahjaf GM, Fahmy SA, Badi S, Yassin HM, Othman GA, Altaher TA, Noor SK
articles.details: Study of 90 participants with microscopy-confirmed malaria in River Nile State, Sudan, measuring TNF-α, IFN-γ, TGF-β1, and IL-10 by ELISA alongside parasite density and COVID-19 co-infection. TNF-α, IFN-γ, and TGF-β1 were significantly associated with parasite density (P<0.05; r=0.42, 0.38, 0.51, P<0.01), but IL-10 was not. TGF-β1 was higher in P. vivax infections, while IL-10 was elevated in P. falciparum cases (560.0 vs 415.6 pg/mL, P=0.019). Uric acid was lower in those co-infected with COVID-19 (P<0.05). Authors conclude these cytokines shape the immune response and parasite clearance.

articles.headline: Repeat malaria infection builds clinical immunity to P. vivax, not parasite control
articles.title_original: Development of clinical immunity to Plasmodium vivax following repeat controlled human malaria infection.
articles.authors: Hou MM, Harding AC, Barber NM, Kundu P, Bach FA, Salkeld J, Themistocleous Y, Greenwood NM, Cho JS, Barrett JR, Nugent FL, Rawlinson TA, Hodgson SH, Khozoee B, Mac Lochlainn DJ, Cowan RE, Poulton ID, Baker M, Kingham L, Mitton CH, Platt A, Lopez Ramon R, Ramos Lopez F, Thomas M, Skinner K, Quinkert D, Pipini D, Lias AM, Bardelli M, Edwards NJ, Donnellan FR, Biswas S, Rayner JC, Nielsen CM, Silk SE, Draper SJ, Nahrendorf W, Spence PJ, Minassian AM
articles.details: In a multi-cohort controlled human malaria infection study, malaria-naïve adults underwent blood-stage CHMI up to three times with P. vivax PvW1, and some were rechallenged with P. falciparum 3D7. Nineteen had primary P. vivax CHMI, 12 a secondary homologous CHMI, 2 a tertiary, and 6 a heterologous P. falciparum rechallenge. Clinical immunity developed rapidly after a single infection, protecting against fever and laboratory abnormalities via attenuated inflammatory cytokines/chemokines and reduced coagulation and endothelial activation, but without anti-parasite immunity. This clinical immunity was species-specific and gave no protection against P. falciparum.

articles.headline: District assessment finds prevention, diagnostic, and supply gaps driving Uganda malaria rise
articles.title_original: From Endemic to Epidemic: A Qualitative District-Level Assessment of an Increase in Malaria Cases in the Elgon Region of Uganda.
articles.authors: Fuller B, Ssekitoleko R, Kyozira C, Makumbi I, Bakainaga A, Moore CC, Kiirya HI
articles.details: This qualitative district-level assessment examined gaps in malaria prevention, detection, and response in the high-burden Elgon region of Uganda, using DHIS2 data to select highest-incidence districts plus structured key-informant interviews of district health officers, malaria focal persons, and vector control officers, analyzed by immersion-crystallization. Uganda's malaria incidence rose from 206 to 271 cases per 1,000 between 2012 and 2022. Main themes were lapses in vector control, untimely logistical support, and limited chemotherapeutics. Of 10 districts, 7 (70%) had inadequate insecticide-treated nets, 3 (30%) had no indoor residual spraying, and 8 (80%) had limited or no rapid diagnostic tests; mean time since last antimalarial resupply was 117 (±9) days.

articles.headline: Proposed minimum and optimal reporting standards for malaria molecular surveillance
articles.title_original: Toward Setting Minimum and Optimal Data to Report for Malaria Molecular Surveillance with Targeted Sequencing: The "What" and "Why".
articles.authors: Juliano JJ, Meier-Scherling CPG, Wernsman Young N, Tollefson GA, Connelly SV, Parr JB, Conrad MD, Sadler JM, Hennelly CM, Assefa A, Okell L, Fola AA, Niaré K, Marglous J, Carey-Ewend K, Futila Kyong-Shin R, Gerdes Gyuricza I, Cuomo-Dannenburg G, Ruybal-Pesántez S, Watson OJ, Verity R, Bailey JA
articles.details: This perspective proposes minimal and optimal data that should be reported for malaria molecular surveillance, to support validation, comparability, reuse, and FAIR (findable, accessible, interoperable, reusable) principles. The authors focus on what should be reported and why rather than specific data formats, arguing that progressing to reporting individual infection-level polymorphism or microhaplotype data is central to maximizing impact for tracking challenges such as P. falciparum artemisinin partial resistance and deletions in the histidine-rich protein 2 and 3 (hrp2/hrp3) genes. They emphasize that reporting must adhere to local regulatory practices, ensure proper data oversight and management, and prevent data colonialism while preserving opportunities for data generators.

articles.headline: Causal modeling suggests further warming may reduce malaria in hot Colombian areas
articles.title_original: Stochastic treatment regimes in climate-health research: Reassessing malaria risk under warming scenarios in Colombia.
articles.authors: Gutiérrez JD
articles.details: This climate-health study used causal machine learning to estimate how incremental temperature increases affect malaria incidence across the 100 Colombian municipalities with the highest incidence from 2007 to 2023, while controlling for valid confounding variables. Transmission intensified at temperatures between 15 and approximately 23.5 °C but declined at higher temperatures, indicating an optimal range. The Average Treatment Effect on excess malaria cases progressively decreased, from −0.007 for a 0.5 °C increase to −0.063 for a 2.0 °C increase relative to current temperatures. The authors conclude that further warming could constrain transmission in already-hot regions and call for public health strategies tailored to local temperature profiles and socio-economic conditions.

articles.headline: Plant compound ellagic acid shows potent activity against drug-resistant P. falciparum
articles.title_original: A bio-guided investigation of Mitragyna inermis (Willd) O. Kuntze (Rubiaceae) unveils natural product isolates with potent cross-activity against sensitive and multidrug resistant Plasmodium falciparum strains in vitro.
articles.authors: Hassan S, Osei Bobbie S, Tchatat Tali MB, Dize D, Kamche Youbi A, Lawane AI, Kemgne Madiesse E, Mahamat Alio H, Baker B, Fekam Boyom F
articles.details: This bio-guided in vitro study screened crude extracts (water, ethanol/water, ethanol, methanol) from stem bark and twigs of Mitragyna inermis against P. falciparum strains Dd2 and 3D7 using a SYBR Green I fluorescence assay, with cytotoxicity tested on Vero cells and erythrocytes, followed by chromatographic purification and ADME prediction. Methanolic and ethanol/water extracts were highly potent (IC50 < 3 μg/mL) with selectivity index > 10. Among isolated compounds, ellagic acid showed potent cross-activity (IC50 0.84–1.62 μg/mL; 2.77–5.36 μM) against susceptible 3D7 and multidrug-resistant PfDd2, PfINDO, PfW2, and PfK1 strains, with a favorable predicted ADME profile. Unidentified compounds 5–8 (IC50 0.21–5.38 μg/mL) were also promising drug-discovery starting points.

articles.headline: Bitter leaf and Siam weed extracts reduce malaria testicular damage in mice
articles.title_original: Bitter leaf (Vernonia amygdalina) and siam weed (Chromolaena odorata) aqueous extracts alleviate testicular damage induced by Plasmodium berghei in male mice via modulation of oxidative stress pathways.
articles.authors: Oyeleye IS, Bolarinde AA, Ademiluyi AO, Oboh G, Ojo OR
articles.details: In an experimental study, 64 male mice were inoculated with chloroquine-sensitive Plasmodium berghei strain NK65 and treated for 5 days with aqueous extracts of bitter leaf (Vernonia amygdalina) and Siam weed (Chromolaena odorata) at 100, 200, and 400 mg/kg, with chloroquine 10 mg/kg as standard. Both extracts suppressed parasitemia and significantly (p<0.05) reduced testicular ROS and TBARS, inhibited enzyme activities, and increased antioxidant levels dose-dependently versus infected untreated mice. The authors conclude the extracts reverse malaria-induced testicular oxidative damage via modulation of oxidative stress pathways.

articles.headline: Olfactory gene variants linked to host-seeking in Anopheles minimus
articles.title_original: Genetic variation in olfactory pathways associated with host-seeking behavior in natural populations of Anopheles minimus, a primary malaria vector in western Thailand.
articles.authors: Pusawang K, Zhong D, Sriwichai P, Samung Y, Saeung A, Aupalee K, Somboon P, Junkum A, Wongpalee SP, Saingamsook J, Sattabongkot J, Cui L, Yan G
articles.details: Wild Anopheles mosquitoes were collected in Tha Song Yang district, Tak province, Thailand, using cow-baited traps, human landing catches, and CDC light traps, then characterized by cox1 sequencing and whole-genome sequencing. An. minimus females made up 25% (504/1,997) of samples; 287 specimens yielded 143 cox1 haplotypes forming two lineages, A (181) and B (106). Whole-genome sequencing of Lineage A grouped by host-seeking behavior (cow-baited, human landing indoor/outdoor) gave 12,659,785 variants and 68,975 non-synonymous SNPs after filtering, with 2,629, 2,948, and 4,369 significant nsSNPs (FDR p<0.001) per comparison. Gene Ontology analysis showed strong enrichment for olfaction-related genes, and Sanger sequencing confirmed candidate nsSNPs associated with host-seeking preference.

articles.headline: ARIMA beats FB-Prophet for predicting malaria incidence in Uganda
articles.title_original: Comparison of ARIMA and FB-Prophet time series models for the prediction of national and regional malaria incidence in Uganda.
articles.authors: Fuller B, Ssekitoleko R, Kyozira C, Colston JM, Makumbi I, Bakainaga A, Moore CC, Kiirya HI
articles.details: This time-series modeling study used aggregated DHIS2 data on national and regional malaria incidence in Uganda from 2020 through 2023 to compare ARIMA and FB-Prophet models, with 41-month training and 6-month validation sets evaluated by MAE, RMSE, and MAPE. National malaria incidence rose from 200.5 cases per 1000 persons in 2021 to 265.4 per 1000 in 2022, with the highest burden in West Nile, Acholi, and Busoga regions. Mean regional MAE, MAPE, and RMSE were 0.007, 31.2, and 0.01 for ARIMA versus 0.01, 47.8, and 0.01 for FB-Prophet; ARIMA outperformed FB-Prophet nationally and in 14 of 15 regions. The authors conclude both models can guide malaria resource allocation.

articles.headline: Review of human genetic variations conferring malaria resistance
articles.title_original: Human genetic variations conferring resistance to malaria.
articles.authors: Zhang X, Wu J, Peng Y, Luo L, Zhang L, Huang X, Chen G, Li Y, Yi H
articles.details: This review examines key human genetic variations associated with resistance to malaria, including hemoglobinopathies (sickle cell trait and thalassemia), glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency, blood group polymorphisms, and variants related to inflammation and immune regulation. It notes that the prevalence of these adaptations varies widely across geographic regions, reflecting historical malaria burden, and that the precise protective mechanisms remain incompletely understood. The authors argue that understanding these variations is crucial for advancing malaria epidemiology, improving diagnostics, and developing targeted prevention and control strategies.

articles.headline: Insecticide resistance and target-site mutations in China's malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Insecticide resistance and the role of target-site insensitivity mutations among malaria vectors in China: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: He Z, Wang D, Kou Y, Liu Y, Zhao D, Yang C, Zhou R, Zhang H, Deng Y
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis searched databases from 2000 to 2024 to assess insecticide mortality rates and frequencies of knockdown resistance (kdr) and acetylcholinesterase-1 (ace-1) mutations in Anopheles mosquitoes in China. Thirty articles covering 30,065 An. sinensis were included, with a pooled insecticide-resistance mortality rate of 61% (95% CI 53-68); mortality by insecticide ranged from 28% (beta-cyfluthrin) to 82% (fenitrothion), including DDT 49% and deltamethrin 47%. The kdr mutation frequency was 36% and ace-1 was 78%; for ace-1, 42% were homozygote resistant, 25% heterozygote, and 33% susceptible. The authors conclude these vectors are resistant to pyrethroids, organochlorines, carbamates, and organophosphates, urging novel insecticides or biocontrol.

articles.headline: Income and education shape malaria net use in rural Nigeria
articles.title_original: Socioeconomic and educational influences on malaria prevention and treatment behaviours in rural Nigeria.
articles.authors: Alao JO, Olowoshile OP, James TA, Okezie CC, Adebayo ZP, Ogbonna SC, Oyelayo EA
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of the 2021 Malaria Indicator Survey examined how household income, education, and media exposure relate to malaria control behaviours among rural populations, using three analyses (n=5,021 households for income/ITN; n=9,546 women aged 15-49 for education; n=893 for media exposure). ITN ownership was near-universal at 97.7% but use was 70.3%; low-income households had 3.6 times higher odds of ITN use (95% CI 3.07-4.29). Higher education predicted greater prevention awareness (p<0.001) yet was associated with lower ITN use, and media exposure did not significantly influence treatment-seeking. Authors conclude targeted behavioural interventions are needed beyond knowledge and distribution.

articles.headline: Adjuvants and MHCII shape antibody focus on P. vivax vaccine epitopes
articles.title_original: Adjuvants and MHCII modulate the immunogenicity of subdominant epitopes in Plasmodium vivax Duffy binding protein.
articles.authors: Vinals DF, Hoque MR, Oludada OE, Jansen EB, Mitran CJ, Enterina JR, Macauley MS, Hawkes MT, Yanow SK
articles.details: Using the Plasmodium vivax Duffy binding protein with a defined subdominant region, this immunization study investigated how to overcome epitope subdominance for strain-transcending antibody responses. Antigens carrying mutations in immunodominant epitopes combined with the adjuvants GLA-SE or alum overcame subdominance of the region, but these effects were largely negated by MHCII restriction. Robust antibody responses to subdominant epitopes depended on boosting, which altered the epitope hierarchy and recruited germinal center B cells specific to subdominant epitopes. The findings inform design of subunit vaccines targeting subdominant epitopes.

articles.headline: Suriname becomes first malaria-free country in the Amazon region
articles.title_original: Suriname: first malaria-free country in the Amazon region.
articles.authors: Das M
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Blood donor screening enables real-time malaria surveillance in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Blood donors as a sentinel population for real-time malaria surveillance using the Sysmex XN-31: a one-year review from the National Blood Transfusion Centre in Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Sawadogo S, Hwandih T, Saker J, Coetzer TL, Paszkowska A, Kompaoré SCB, Münster M
articles.details: This prospective study screened consecutive blood donations at the Ouagadougou and Bobo-Dioulasso Regional Blood Transfusion Centres in Burkina Faso (5 of 13 regions) for malaria using the Sysmex XN-31 automated analyser, analysing results with descriptive statistics, chi-squared, and logistic regression. Donor malaria prevalence was 5.91% (3164/53575); key predictors were age ≤30 years (OR 2.85, p<0.001), male sex (OR 1.47, p<0.001), and rural residency (OR 2.40, p<0.001), with strong seasonal variation lagging rainfall by 3 months. Gametocyte carriage in males rose from 1.39% (14/1006) in low season to 4.42% (66/1494) in high season (p<0.001). Authors conclude blood donor screening offers granular, real-time malaria surveillance.

articles.headline: REL2/NF-κB pathway controls mosquito midgut bacteria and parasite levels
articles.title_original: The major role of the REL2/NF-κB pathway in the regulation of midgut bacterial homeostasis in the malaria vector Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Zaković S, Rivera GE, Gomaid R, Martinez CG, Kappler C, Marois E, Levashina EA
articles.details: This study examined tissue-specific immune responses of Anopheles females to blood feeding and Plasmodium falciparum infection. REL2 was shown to regulate antimicrobial peptide gene expression and shape midgut bacterial composition after a blood meal; loss of REL2 caused midgut dysbiosis with overgrowth of Serratia spp. and mosquito lethality after blood feeding. Notably, Serratia-induced dysbiosis reduced P. falciparum prevalence in surviving mosquitoes. The authors conclude the immune system is critical for maintaining midgut bacterial homeostasis and reveal complex interactions among mosquito immunity, gut microbiota, and malaria parasites.

articles.headline: Draft whole-genome assembly of malaria mosquito Anopheles stephensi
articles.title_original: Whole genome Shotgun Sequence of Anopheles stephensi, The Host of Malaria parasite, Plasmodium sp.
articles.authors: Farah J, Jang J, Madhi K, Phan J, Pratt E, Sankrit A, Sharma NL, Suchak A, Thomas DW, Ghazal SM
articles.details: This study performed a genome assembly on a female Anopheles stephensi, a major malaria mosquito vector in Asia. The resulting genome assembly was 201 Mbp in size, comprising 32,280 contigs with an N50 of 21.1 kb and a GC content of 45%.

articles.headline: Editorial on genetic and evolutionary drivers of TB and malaria drug resistance
articles.title_original: Editorial: Mechanisms driving drug resistance in tuberculosis and malaria: genetic, environmental, and evolutionary insights.
articles.authors: Kushwaha AK, Bansal A, Arora G, Kushwaha D
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Case report: P. vivax exflagellation and mixed infection on blood smear
articles.title_original: Exflagellation of Plasmodium vivax and Dual Infection Diagnosed on Peripheral Blood Film: A Rare Case Report.
articles.authors: Patil BU, Ghongade P, Bhatkule M, Chavhan P
articles.details: This case report describes a male patient in his early twenties presenting with a mixed malaria infection identified on peripheral blood smear. The smear showed trophozoites and schizonts of Plasmodium vivax, trophozoites of Plasmodium falciparum, and multiple exflagellated microgametes of P. vivax. The authors note that the exflagellated form of P. vivax is difficult to identify due to similarity with other hemoparasites, and emphasize prompt, correct reporting to guide appropriate treatment and prevent transmission.

articles.headline: Structured P. vivax populations across Mexico's malaria foci
articles.title_original: Population Dynamics of Plasmodium vivax in Mexico Determined by CSP, Pvs25, and SSU 18S rRNA S-Type Polymorphism Analyses.
articles.authors: González-Cerón L, Gómez-Pérez DJ, Santillán-Valenzuela F, Ovilla-Muñoz M, Guzmán-Bracho C, Pech-May A, Amores GR, Montoya-Pérez A, Villarreal-Treviño C
articles.details: This genetic study analyzed Plasmodium vivax parasites from five malaria foci using three markers: the circumsporozoite gene (pvcsp, by PCR-RFLP and sequencing), pvs25 mutations, and 18S SSU rRNA S-type ribosomal variants, including prior data from Chiapas. Both VK210 and VK247 pvcsp types were detected, with VK210 showing greater haplotype diversity (0.860) than VK247 (0.198); two pvs25 mutations (Q87K and I130T) yielded three haplotypes. Haplotype frequencies varied among foci (p < 0.001), and AMOVA, FST, and Spearman's correlation indicated a structured P. vivax population. The Lacandon region harbored the broadest reservoir of P. vivax haplotypes, while the high adaptation of parasites in the northwestern region represents a challenge for malaria elimination.

articles.headline: Case report: Guillain-Barré syndrome after falciparum malaria in teen
articles.title_original: Guillain-Barré Syndrome Following Falciparum Malaria in an 18-Year Male Adolescent: A Rare Case Report and Literature Review.
articles.authors: Getahun GA, Arega G, Jemal MK, Woldie BG, Gudina EK
articles.details: This case report describes an 18-year-old male who developed progressive bilateral lower then upper extremity weakness over one week, with voice changes, facial drooping, difficulty swallowing, and high-grade fever. CSF analysis showed an elevated protein level of 172 mg/dL with no cells, and nerve conduction studies confirmed the acute motor and sensory axonal neuropathy (AMSAN) variant of Guillain-Barré syndrome; a peripheral blood film was positive for the trophozoite stage of Plasmodium falciparum. He was managed with intravenous artesunate and supportive care, his weakness stabilized around day 14, and he was able to walk with minimal support at 2 months.

articles.headline: India's reported malaria cases far below WHO estimates, study finds
articles.title_original: Time to address the drawbacks in estimation and reporting of malaria cases in India and beyond.
articles.authors: Deora N, Singhal R, Sinha A
articles.details: This analysis examined the gap between WHO-estimated and NMCP-reported malaria cases in India, calculating an estimated-to-reported ratio (ERR) from 2010 to 2022 and dissecting WHO's estimation assumptions. ERR stayed consistently high (WHO South-East Asia Region >6; India >10) across the period. Private-sector case reporting in India was virtually non-existent, contributing to the gap, and contemporary research showed microscopy-positivity 3 to 55 times higher than NMCP figures. The authors conclude that clarifying WHO estimation methods and improving private-sector reporting could narrow or eliminate the ERR gap.

articles.headline: Mouse model boosts detection of parasitized erythroblasts in blood
articles.title_original: Visual Detection of Malaria Parasite-Parasitized Erythroblasts in Peripheral Blood via Immunization-Based Model.
articles.authors: Ito K, Tateishi YS, Imai T, Miyazaki S, Miyazaki Y, Kagaya W, Nakashima M, Sase M, Yoshioka-Takeda M, Shimokawa C, Hayashi K, Itokawa K, Komagata O, Ngo-Thanh H, Shimo A, Araki T, Annoura T, Murakami T, Hisaeda H
articles.details: Using a murine model, researchers developed a method to increase circulating parasitized erythroblasts (pEBs)—recently identified as host cells for malaria parasites but normally too rare in peripheral blood to study. Mice were immunized with live Plasmodium yoelii 17XNL, then challenged with P. berghei ANKA. Immunized mice were protected from cerebral malaria and survived longer, during which pEBs appeared in circulation and were detected on Giemsa-stained smears; all blood-stage parasite forms, including enucleating erythroblasts, were identified within pEBs. The authors conclude the model enables in vivo/ex vivo study of pEB biology without bone marrow or spleen isolation.

articles.headline: Mosquito fauna and blood-meal preferences mapped in southern Iran
articles.title_original: Culicidae vector ecology in southern Iran: Integrating Faunistics and molecular analysis of blood-feeding preferences to strengthen malaria surveillance in transition zones.
articles.authors: Kalantari M, Azizi K
articles.details: This survey characterized Culicidae fauna and blood-feeding preferences in Shiraz, southern Iran, collecting 1249 adult mosquitoes from April 2023 to January 2024 using pyrethrum spray catches, CDC light traps, and aspiration. Dominant species were Culex pipiens (39.0%), Cx. quinquefasciatus (23.6%), and Aedes caspius (19.6%); Anopheles dthali (80.6% of Anophelinae) and An. sacharovi (19.4%) were the main Anophelines. PCR-RFLP of cytochrome b from 50 blood-fed females showed Anopheles fed mainly on rodents/dogs (41%) and humans (28%), while Cx. pipiens was markedly anthropophilic (54% human meals). An. sacharovi clustered in irrigation-rich northern Shiraz (χ²=12.7, p<0.01). Authors advocate integrated vector management and sustained molecular monitoring.

articles.headline: Wealth, mother's education, rural living tied to child malaria in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Sociodemographic Factors Associated with Malaria Infection among Under-Five Children in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Agboola HO, Ajibola O, Lasisi AJ, Adebayo F, Aremu O, Khan S
articles.details: This cross-sectional study analyzed 2018 Nigeria Demographic and Health Survey data from 10,352 under-five children to identify sociodemographic factors associated with malaria, using RDT results as the outcome and binomial logistic regression. Children's age (χ²=166.200, p=0.000), mother's education (χ²=762.530, p=0.000), and wealth index (χ²=1020.442, p=0.000) were all significantly associated with malaria. Children in rural areas were more likely to be infected than urban children (OR=1.633, p=0.000). The authors conclude that age, wealth, maternal education, and residence are key predictors and call for broad-based interventions.

articles.headline: Only a third of Nigerian children got full SMC course, survey shows
articles.title_original: Knowledge and Uptake of Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention among Caregivers of Under-five Children in a Tertiary Hospital in Kaduna State, North-western Nigeria.
articles.authors: Tom HM, Suleiman AG, Usman S, Darma MA, Yahaya U, Wakawa MM, Hamza AS, Babandi ZS
articles.details: This cross-sectional study assessed knowledge and uptake of seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) among 234 caregivers of under-five children at a tertiary hospital in Kaduna State, north-western Nigeria, using a systematically sampled questionnaire and chi-square testing. All respondents were female; 182 (76.8%) had heard of SMC and 128 (54.0%) had good overall knowledge. While 137 (58.4%) took at least one dose during the last campaign, only 65 (27.2%) received all four doses; among the 100 (41.6%) who missed it, reasons included not knowing about SMC (54.0%) and child absence (20.0%). Uptake was associated with education, occupation, ethnicity, religion, and SMC knowledge (p<0.05). Authors urge intensified community enlightenment.

articles.headline: Mixed P. falciparum and P. vivax malaria treated in G6PD-deficient patient
articles.title_original: Navigating Challenges in Mixed Malaria Infections: A Case of Mixed Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax Infection in a Patient With Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase (G6PD) Deficiency.
articles.authors: Lau CKS, Kek XY, Gan WF
articles.details: This case report describes an imported mixed malaria infection in a patient with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency, in whom Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax were both identified by malaria PCR. The authors note that mixed infections are rarely reported and often underestimated, and emphasize early diagnosis for timely treatment. The patient's condition improved after completing a full course of IV artesunate, oral Riamet (artemether/lumefantrine), and primaquine.

articles.headline: Plant extract inhibits P. falciparum Hsp70-1 chaperone activity in vitro
articles.title_original: Acanthospermum australe Extract Inhibits the Chaperone Activity of Plasmodium falciparum Heat Shock Protein 70-1.
articles.authors: Koza NA, Myeza NN, Hoppe H, Mosa RA, Kappo AP, Simelane MBC, Opoku AR
articles.details: This in vitro study tested a dichloromethane extract of Acanthospermum australe against the chloroquine-sensitive P. falciparum strain 3D7 using the pLDH assay, and assessed its effect on the chaperone activity of P. falciparum heat shock protein 70-1 (PfHsp70-1) via ATPase, thermally induced luciferase, and malate dehydrogenase (MDH) aggregation assays. The extract showed high antiplasmodial activity with an IC50 of 1.3 µg/mL. PfHsp70-1 alone reduced thermal aggregation of MDH and luciferase, but adding the extract increased protein aggregation, and the extract also inhibited PfHsp70-1 ATPase activity. The authors conclude the extract contains compounds that could target parasite Hsp70 functions.

articles.headline: Review: targeting the malaria parasite mitochondrion and yeast as a model
articles.title_original: Targeting Mitochondrial Function in Plasmodium falciparum: Insight into Antimalarial Drugs and the Emerging Role of Saccharomyces cerevisiae as a Model System.
articles.authors: Greco S, Assalve G, Lunetti P, Kayentao K, Dara A, Scaramuzzi D, Zara V, Ferramosca A
articles.details: This review discusses the mitochondrion of Plasmodium falciparum as a promising antimalarial target, citing its essential role across all parasite life-cycle stages and its structural and biochemical differences from the human counterpart, against the backdrop of rising drug-resistant strains. It highlights recent advances in compounds targeting mitochondrial function and argues for the budding yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae as a model system for antimalarial drug discovery, owing to its shared eukaryotic features, genetic tractability, and ability to heterologously express parasite mitochondrial proteins as a cost-effective platform for elucidating drug mechanisms.

articles.headline: Commentary on supporting the Global Fund to fight AIDS, tuberculosis, and malaria
articles.title_original: Supporting the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis, and Malaria.
articles.authors: Piot P, Abubakar I
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Commentary calls for new tools to prevent malaria in pregnancy
articles.title_original: A need for new tools for prevention of malaria in pregnancy.
articles.authors: Saito M, McGready R
articles.details: This commentary discusses intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp) with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP) as a chemoprevention strategy against malaria in pregnancy. Referencing a recent PLOS Medicine study, it highlights the need for a better understanding of the mechanism by which IPTp-SP reduces low birthweight, as well as the need for novel measures to prevent malaria earlier in gestation.

articles.headline: Review: how controlled human malaria infection studies advance vaccines and drugs
articles.title_original: Controlled human malaria infection studies: insights into recent advances and key immunological and ethical implementation lessons.
articles.authors: Ogwang R, Adan M, Bejon P, Kapulu MC
articles.details: This article reviews the history and rationale of controlled human malaria infection (CHMI) models for falciparum malaria. It describes how CHMI studies supported and accelerated clinical development of the first malaria vaccines, RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M, accurately predicting their efficacy. The authors highlight CHMI applications for advancing drugs and monoclonal antibodies and for characterising immunological and innate mechanisms that may mediate protection, and discuss lessons learned from studies in naïve and endemic populations, including transferring the models from high-income to low- and middle-income settings.

articles.headline: Persistent Plasmodium vivax hotspot districts challenge malaria elimination in India
articles.title_original: Hotspot districts of persistent Plasmodium vivax malaria pose a formidable challenge to malaria elimination in India.
articles.authors: Ramasamy A, Thiruvengadam K, Viswan K A, Sharma R, Nagarajan SA, Yadav CP, Sharma A, Rahi M
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: High P. falciparum/P. vivax co-infection in Duffy-negative Ethiopians
articles.title_original: High prevalence of mixed Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax co-infections in Duffy negative individuals in Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Tat B, Feufack-Donfack LB, Neneyoh Yengo B, Eng V, Tefera Shenkutie T, Abebe A, Tsigie M, Tacoli C, Khim N, Feleke SM, Lo E, Popovici J
articles.details: A cross-sectional study of 1,154 febrile patients across three Ethiopian regions (Gambela, Oromia, SNNPR) used PCR for Plasmodium species, TaqMan assay for Duffy genotype, and semi-nested PCR for PvDBP gene duplication. Plasmodium was detected in 46% of patients: 49% P. falciparum, 26% mixed P. falciparum/P. vivax, and 23% P. vivax mono-infections; 41% were Duffy negative. P. vivax mono-infections were rare in Duffy-negative individuals (2.4%), while 25% of mixed infections were Duffy negative. Of 155 samples, 78% showed PvDBP gene duplication (94% in Gambela). Authors suggest interactions between Duffy-negative P. vivax infection and P. falciparum co-infection, underscoring molecular surveillance needs.

articles.headline: Tr1 regulatory T cells both protect against and prolong malaria
articles.title_original: CXCR6(+) CD127(-) Tr1 Cells Balance Immunity and Persistence in Plasmodium falciparum Infection.
articles.authors: Nideffer J, Bach F, Strubbe S, Lopez L, Zedi M, Nankya F, Briggs J, van der Ploeg K, Musinguzi K, Kim S, Romero AG, Keya A, Camanag K, Lewis S, Abdelbasset M, Wang B, Boss A, Nansubuga E, Nankabirwa JI, Arinaitwe E, Takahashi S, Dorsey G, Greenhouse B, Rodriguez-Barraquer I, Kamya MR, Bacchetta R, Ssewanyana I, Haque A, Roncarolo MG, Jagannathan P
articles.details: Using scRNAseq and in vitro/in vivo analyses in children in malaria-endemic Uganda, researchers validated CXCR6+ CD127- as markers enriching for Plasmodium falciparum-specific type 1 regulatory T (Tr1) cells, which comprise nearly 90% of the Pf blood-stage antigen-specific CD4+ T cell pool. These Tr1 cells suppressed CD4+ T cell proliferation via IL-10, were the dominant subset responding to Pf-infected red blood cells, and expanded after malaria, expressing IL-10 and IFNg. Higher Tr1 frequencies correlated with lower probability of symptoms given parasitemia but also with delayed parasite clearance in untreated asymptomatic children, suggesting Tr1 cells mediate clinical immunity while facilitating parasite persistence.

articles.headline: Genomic study of African malaria vector Anopheles funestus reveals selection
articles.title_original: Genomic diversity of the African malaria vector Anopheles funestus.
articles.authors: Boddé M, Nwezeobi J, Korlević P, Makunin A, Akone-Ella O, Barasa S, Gadji M, Hart L, Kaindoa EW, Love K, Lucas ER, Lujumba I, Máquina M, Nagi SC, Odero JO, Polo B, Sangbakembi C, Dadzie S, Koekemoer LL, Kwiatkowski D, McAlister E, Ochomo E, Okumu F, Paaijmans K, Tchouassi DP, Wondji CS, Ayala D, Durbin R, Miles A, Lawniczak MKN
articles.details: Researchers sequenced 656 modern Anopheles funestus s.s. specimens (collected 2014–2018) and 45 historic specimens (collected 1927–1967) from 16 African countries to study evolution under vector control pressure. Despite high genetic diversity, the species showed stable but considerable continental population structure; one population showed little differentiation over a century and 4,000 kilometers, yet two genetically distinct ecotypes were found nearby. Vector control produced strong selection signals, with some resistance alleles shared across populations via gene flow and others arising independently. A promising Anopheles gambiae gene drive target was highly conserved in An. funestus, informing insecticide and gene drive strategies.

articles.headline: Adding DHA-piperaquine to SP doesn't improve malaria-in-pregnancy birth outcomes in Uganda
articles.title_original: Dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine plus sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine versus either drug alone for intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy: A double-blind, randomized, controlled phase 3 trial from Uganda.
articles.authors: Kakuru A, Kizza J, Aguti M, Adrama H, Ategeka J, Olwoch P, Nakalembe M, Nankabirwa JI, Opira B, Ozarslan N, Ranjit A, Dela Cruz E, Clark TD, Roh ME, Gaw SL, Jagannathan P, Rosenthal PJ, Kamya MR, Dorsey G
articles.details: This double-blind, randomized, controlled trial in Uganda assigned 2,757 pregnant women (1:1:1) to monthly IPTp with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP), dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP), or DP plus SP. The combination did not reduce the composite adverse birth outcome versus DP (30.0% vs 30.9%, RR 0.97, 95% CI 0.84-1.12, p=0.70) or SP (30.0% vs 26.4%, RR 1.14, 95% CI 0.98-1.33, p=0.10), and DP alone was worse than SP (30.9% vs 26.4%, RR 1.17, p=0.04). DP cut symptomatic malaria by 94% and microscopic parasitemia by 97% versus SP, with no significant difference in grade 3-4 adverse events.

articles.headline: Review examines drivers of resurging malaria cases in Rwanda
articles.title_original: Investigating the resurgence of malaria in Rwanda: factors contributing to rising cases amidst near-eradication success and strategies for sustained control.
articles.authors: Julius N, Diane BI
articles.details: This review of 22 sources (16 peer-reviewed papers, 6 reports) analyzes Rwanda's malaria resurgence after cases fell from 4.8 million in 2016/17 to 620,000 by 2023/24, followed by a 45.8% rise by 2024 and 87,000 cases in March 2024. Cited contributors include climate-driven expansion of Anopheles gambiae habitat, pyrethroid and artemisinin (K13) resistance, suboptimal non-pyrethroid indoor residual spraying, poverty, nonadherence, and human mobility enabling asymptomatic transmission. Suggested responses include community health worker strategies, drone-mediated larviciding (96.4% community acceptance), non-pyrethroid IRS, resistance screening, and new drugs (dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine, artesunate-pyronaridine); gaps include P. vivax strategies and funding.

articles.headline: Review traces P. falciparum's evolution into an obligate parasite
articles.title_original: Evolutionary Trajectory of Plasmodium falciparum: From Autonomous Phototroph to Dedicated Parasite.
articles.authors: Pikor D, Hurla M, Drelichowska A, Paul M
articles.details: This review synthesizes the co-evolutionary dynamics among human hosts, Anopheles vectors, and Plasmodium falciparum. It describes human immunogenetic adaptations such as hemoglobinopathies countered by the parasite's antigenic variation and immunomodulation, and mosquito adaptations including shifted host-seeking behavior and insecticide resistance via metabolic detoxification and target-site insensitivity that erode bed nets and indoor residual spraying. At the molecular level, it highlights P. falciparum's genome streamlining through gene loss, repurposing of the apicoplast for fatty acid, isoprenoid, and haem biosynthesis, and antigenic switching and epigenetic regulation driving immune escape and drug resistance. The authors argue these insights point toward next-generation therapeutic and vector-control strategies.

articles.headline: Young primigravida and traditional housing raise malaria-in-pregnancy risk in Uganda
articles.title_original: Maternal and household risk factors for malaria in pregnancy and low birthweight: a prospective cohort study from Uganda.
articles.authors: Aguti M, Kizza J, Kakuru A, Nakalembe M, Nankabirwa JI, Gaw SL, Opira B, Ssemukuye T, Ozarslan N, Ranjit A, Cruz E, Clark TD, Roh ME, Jagannathan P, Rosenthal PJ, Adrama H, Olwoch P, Mayende J, Odongo B, John A, Kamya MR, Dorsey G
articles.details: Drawn from a randomized controlled trial of intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp) in Busia District, Uganda (December 2020–July 2024), this analysis included 2,728 of 2,757 enrolled women (98.9%) followed through delivery with monthly parasitemia assessment, placental histopathology, and birth weight. At enrolment 38.1% had parasitemia, 6.5% had parasitemia after starting IPTp, 6.4% had high-grade placental malaria, and 6.8% of live births were low birthweight. Parasitemia at enrolment was more common in women aged 16–21 (RR 1.62), primigravida (RR 1.86), and those living in traditional houses (RR 1.17), with these associations persisting after IPTp initiation. Primigravida was strongly associated with high-grade placental malaria (RR 4.20) and low birthweight (RR 2.14), while maternal education and household wealth showed no significant associations.

articles.headline: Scoping review: climate change is reshaping malaria transmission in Zimbabwe
articles.title_original: Impact of Climate Change on Malaria Transmission and Management in Zimbabwe: A Scoping Review of the Literature.
articles.authors: Chivasa T, Nunu WN, Dhlamini M, Maviza A, Ndagurwa GN
articles.details: This scoping review, following PRISMA extension guidance, included 22 literature sources to synthesize evidence on climate change's impact on malaria transmission and management in Zimbabwe, where about half the population is at risk. Most reviewed studies indicate climate change is shifting the geographic distribution, trends, timing, and intensity of transmission, though others emphasize non-climatic human, ecological, health-system, and intervention-coverage factors. The authors note overstretched health systems and uncertain intervention effectiveness as challenges, and identify gaps including lack of longitudinal data, limited localized predictive modelling, and little focus on climate-parasite dynamics. They call for climate-informed programming, improved local funding, multi-sectoral collaboration, and climate-resilient health systems.

articles.headline: Protocol: do malaria controls affect progression to severe disease in children?
articles.title_original: Progression from uncomplicated to severe malaria among children in settings receiving different malaria control interventions in sub-Saharan Africa: a systematic review protocol.
articles.authors: Okek EJ, Lutwama J, Kinengyere AA, Asio J, Awor S, Le Doare K, Musinguzi B, Sande JO, Ocan M, Kayondo J
articles.details: This is a systematic review and meta-analysis protocol (PROSPERO CRD 42024619945) aiming to collate evidence on the extent of progression from uncomplicated to severe malaria among populations in sub-Saharan Africa receiving concurrent deployment of various malaria control measures. The authors will search PubMed, Web of Science, Embase, Scopus and Google Scholar, screen and extract data in duplicate, and use narrative and quantitative synthesis with the I2 statistic for heterogeneity plus subgroup analysis. No results are reported; the full review was expected by the end of December 2025.

articles.headline: Review of mosquito-based transmission-blocking vaccines for malaria
articles.title_original: Mosquito-based transmission-blocking vaccine candidates for malaria: progress, challenges, and innovations.
articles.authors: Dahiya N, Yadav M, Kataria D, Janjoter S, Sehrawat N
articles.details: This review surveys mosquito-based transmission-blocking vaccine (TBV) candidates for malaria, contrasting them with current parasite-based vaccines RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M, which the authors describe as having lower efficacy and only reducing disease severity and death risk. It covers antisera-raising methods used to evaluate transmission-blocking efficacy, the status of candidate mosquito-based TBV antigens, and challenges and promising approaches for improving efficacy. The authors argue TBVs could prevent the spread of infectious malaria and that mosquito-based TBVs can target a wide variety of parasites and reduce vector survival. No quantitative efficacy results are reported.

articles.headline: Different P. falciparum strains trigger differing antibody breadth after controlled infection
articles.title_original: Controlled human malaria infection with NF54 and 7G8 strains elicit differential antibody responses to Plasmodium falciparum peptides.
articles.authors: Friedman-Klabanoff DJ, Jensen TL, Lyke KE, Laurens MB, Silva JC, Stucke EM, Ouattara A, Ifeonu OO, Hodges T, Moser KA, Gelber CE, Goll JB, Hoffman SL, Patel JJ, Pinapati RS, Tan JC, Deye GA, Takala-Harrison S, Travassos MA, Berry AA
articles.details: Using a novel diversity-reflecting peptide microarray of 638,817 unique peptides representing 22,655 variants of 227 proteins, the authors probed sera from 38 malaria-naïve adults before and 28 days after controlled human malaria infection (CHMI) with one of two genetically distinct P. falciparum strains, NF54 (n=21) or 7G8 (n=17). Response intensity was similar across groups, but 20 proteins showed differential breadth, with 7G8-infected participants having higher breadth for 17 of them. Of 543 ABCPred-predicted epitopes, 66 overlapped with MEME-identified epitopes, six being highly cross-reactive (>95% of peptide variants serorecognized). The authors suggest ETRAMP10.2 (PF3D7_1033200) could be considered for a next-generation vaccine.

articles.headline: El Niño-Southern Oscillation and shifting malaria risks in the Amazon
articles.title_original: El Niño-Southern Oscillation and shifting malaria risks in the Amazon.
articles.authors: Laporta GZ
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Review of cerebral malaria pathophysiology and emerging biomarkers
articles.title_original: Neurovascular Pathophysiology and Emerging Biomarkers in Cerebral Malaria: An Integrative Perspective.
articles.authors: Pikor D, Hurła M, Banaszek-Hurła N, Drelichowska A, Paul M
articles.details: This review synthesizes the neurovascular pathophysiology of cerebral malaria, a life-threatening complication of Plasmodium falciparum infection defined clinically by coma with peripheral parasitemia and no alternative cause.
