It describes how cytoadherence of parasitized erythrocytes in the cerebral microvasculature, inflammation, and endothelial activation cause blood-brain barrier disruption, vasogenic and cytotoxic cerebral oedema, and raised intracranial pressure. It highlights high-resolution MRI findings (basal ganglia infarcts, brainstem lesions, corpus callosum abnormalities) that predict poor outcomes, and circulating extracellular vesicles as potential diagnostic and prognostic biomarkers. The authors note persistent neurocognitive impairment, behavioral problems, and epilepsy in survivors and discuss neuroprotective therapeutic targets.

articles.headline: Half of P. vivax malaria cases developed severe complications in study
articles.title_original: Clinical spectrum and complications of Plasmodium vivax malaria: A retrospective study from Delhi, India.
articles.authors: Singh D, Uddin A, Bajaj K, Chaturvedi A, Garg R, Chandra K, Das A, Kohli S, Jain V
articles.details: A retrospective study of 361 patients with P. vivax malaria at a South Delhi, India tertiary hospital classified cases as complicated or uncomplicated by WHO criteria. Mean age was 31 years with male predominance (64.6%), but complications were more frequent in females (42% vs 32%, P=0.039). Overall 167 (46.3%) had complications. Thrombocytopenia (OR 3.20, P<0.001), leucocytosis (OR 2.37, P<0.05), elevated creatinine (OR 6.07, P=0.001), and hyperbilirubinemia (OR 3.71, P<0.001) were significantly linked to severity. Authors conclude P. vivax frequently causes severe disease, challenging its benign reputation.

articles.headline: Opinion: increase Africa's representation on the Global Fund board
articles.title_original: It is time to increase Africa's governmental representation on the governing board of the global fund to fight AIDS, tuberculosis and malaria.
articles.authors: Nalugala RM, Kakoma J, Bridge G, von Agris JM, Hayes Badenhorst R, Fransen L, Brown GW
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Refined malarial retinopathy criteria improve cerebral malaria diagnostic accuracy
articles.title_original: Re-evaluating malarial retinopathy to improve its diagnostic accuracy in paediatric cerebral malaria: A retrospective study.
articles.authors: Wilson KJ, Muiruri Liomba A, Seydel KB, Banda OK, Moxon CA, MacCormick IJC, Harding SP, Beare NAV, Taylor TE
articles.details: A retrospective analysis of the Blantyre autopsy study in Blantyre, Malawi (1996–2010) tested whether malarial retinopathy predicts cerebral parasite sequestration in children with fatal cerebral malaria. Of 84 children, 65 met WHO clinical criteria for cerebral malaria; 18 (28%) of 65 lacked sequestration at autopsy. Malarial retinopathy had 89.4% sensitivity and 73.0% specificity for sequestration. Reclassifying patients whose only sign was 1–5 haemorrhages in a single eye as retinopathy-negative improved sensitivity to 94.3% and specificity to 88.0%. Authors conclude retinopathy is the most specific point-of-care test and propose a refined management algorithm.

articles.headline: HLA-G 14 bp polymorphism linked to severe malaria in iron-fortified children
articles.title_original: HLA-G 14 bp polymorphism: Implications and its associated risk to malaria severity among iron-fortified Ghanaian infants and young children.
articles.authors: Tetteh Danquah A, Courtin D, Asamoah Sakyi S, Nakotey GK, Tchum SK, Afriyie Duah N, Lagrave A, Sewor C, Amoani B
articles.details: A secondary analysis of a double-blinded cluster randomized controlled trial used 432 archival samples from Ghanaian infants and young children aged 6–36 months in the Tain District and Wenchi Municipal, Bono Region. Anaemia was measured by haematology autoanalyzer, malaria and parasitaemia by microscopy, and HLA-G 14 bp polymorphism by PCR. Among iron-fortified children, the 14 bp +/− and −/− variants were associated with higher risk of severe malaria, and the 14 bp +/− variant was linked to higher anaemia risk among iron supplement recipients. Authors conclude this polymorphism elevates severe malaria risk, predisposing to anaemia.

articles.headline: Next-gen sequencing reveals hidden avian malaria diversity in Vietnamese birds
articles.title_original: Hidden Plasmodium diversity revealed in southeastern Asian passerines using next-generation amplicon sequencing.
articles.authors: Damnjanović D, Nazarizadeh M, Nováková M, Linh NV, Tolstenkov O, Kerimov A, Bushuev A, Štefka J
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: Using next-generation amplicon sequencing of the haemosporidian cytochrome b gene, researchers investigated Plasmodium prevalence and diversity in three passerine bird species in Cat-Tien National Park, Vietnam. They identified two known and ten novel Plasmodium lineages, with high infection rates in the little spiderhunter, white-rumped shama, and blue-winged pitta. Species delimitation methods consistently identified five distinct operational taxonomic units, with each bird species infected by a specific subset of diversity. Sympatric Plasmodium OTUs were not closely related but had overlapping host preferences. Authors conclude avian malaria distribution is filtered by host identity and ecology rather than being uniformly opportunistic.

articles.headline: P. vivax malaria relapses twice in traveler despite primaquine
articles.title_original: Recurrent Malaria with Plasmodium vivax: A Case Report and Brief Review of the Literature.
articles.authors: Andrejkovits ÁV, Pop AV, Fejér M, Gîrbovan EC, Coșeriu RL, Vintilă C, Văsieșiu AM
articles.details: This case report describes a 41-year-old man from Romania who developed Plasmodium vivax malaria after travel through Southeast Asia without chemoprophylaxis, presenting with fever, chills, myalgia, headache, vomiting, abdominal pain, mild jaundice, and slight neurological signs; labs showed severe thrombocytopenia, elevated bilirubin, inflammatory markers, and borderline creatinine. Diagnosis was confirmed by rapid diagnostic test and blood smear, and he was treated with doxycycline and atovaquone-proguanil. He experienced two relapses confirmed as P. vivax by RT-PCR, the second occurring despite primaquine radical cure after the first relapse. The authors stress that accurate species identification is critical to guide long-term management given P. vivax's high relapse potential.

articles.headline: Correction: household factors and P. falciparum in young Ugandan children
articles.title_original: Correction: Household practices and infrastructure associated with high Plasmodium falciparum infection rates among children under five years old in Northern Uganda.
articles.authors: Echodu R, Nyeko C, Okot J, Iwiru T, Nakiwu V, Aryemo F, Ajolorwot S
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Review: seasonal malaria chemoprevention cuts malaria in Sahel children
articles.title_original: Status and prospects of seasonal malaria chemoprevention among children in Sahelian countries: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Djedanem M, Salé NM, Aminou EYYM, Testa J, Jambou R
articles.details: This PRISMA 2020 systematic review and meta-analysis (PROSPERO CRD42023413920) assessed the effectiveness of seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) — a monthly three-day course of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine plus amodiaquine during transmission season — in reducing malaria incidence and prevalence in children under five in Sahelian countries, ten years after introduction. Searching MEDLINE, Cochrane, and African databases for studies from 2013 to 2023, it included RCTs, non-randomized trials, cohort, intervention, and observational studies, using RoB 2 and ROBINS-I for bias assessment. The meta-analysis found SMC effectively reduces uncomplicated malaria, severe malaria, and mortality compared with controls, though it is always used alongside indoor residual spraying and insecticide-treated nets, and must adapt to changing epidemiology and cases in older children.

articles.headline: Gracilaria seaweed extract inhibits P. falciparum in vitro
articles.title_original: Antiplasmodial activity of Gracilaria sp. ethanol extract against Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Setyaningrum E, Septiani L, Wibowo H, Arifiyanto A, Suryaningkunti JA
articles.details: This in vitro study evaluated the antiplasmodial activity of Gracilaria sp. ethanol extract against Plasmodium falciparum strain D37. Dried, powdered seaweed underwent ethanol extraction, with constituents screened by phytochemical methods, infrared spectroscopy, and gas chromatography, revealing saponins, tannins, flavonoids, and alkaloids. Alkaloids, flavonoids, and derivative chemicals inhibited parasite growth with an IC50 of 43.38 μg/mL. The authors frame seaweeds as a potential affordable alternative raw material to address parasite drug resistance.

articles.headline: How infected red cells adhere under flow on ICAM-1, CD36
articles.title_original: Contact Dynamics of Cytoadhering Plasmodium falciparum-Infected Erythrocytes in Flow.
articles.authors: Scholz K, Papagrigorakes M, Lettermann L, Pennarola F, Patra P, Dasanna AK, Sanchez CP, Kehrer J, Cavalcanti-Adam EA, Schwarz US, Tanaka M, Lanzer M
articles.details: This biophysical study investigated the dynamic adhesion behavior of Plasmodium falciparum-infected erythrocytes on surfaces functionalized with ICAM-1, CD36, or both, using DNA-based molecular force sensors, high-speed reflection interference contrast microscopy, and computer simulations. Trophozoite-stage infected erythrocytes, which keep a discoidal shape, showed complex motions including flipping over the long axis and flipping with lateral sliding, with or without pinning, producing patchy adhesion footprints, whereas schizont-stage parasites displayed more uniform rolling consistent with their spherical, stiffened morphology. Sliding incidence increased on CD36-containing surfaces for both stages, and some adhesion footprints extended distances comparable to an endothelial cell's length. The findings offer more detailed insight into cytoadhesion mechanisms and microvascular pathology.

articles.headline: Transmission-blocking antibodies common in Tanzanian populations, rise with age
articles.title_original: Seroprevalence of antibodies to Plasmodium falciparum transmission-blocking target proteins Pfs230D1M and Pfs48/45 in Tanzanian populations of diverse malaria transmission intensity.
articles.authors: Mulamba C, Kalinga WF, Mtaka I, Lazaro LO, Kamage J, Nkumama I, Odufuwa OG, Kreppel K, Mekhaiel D, Miura K, Long CA, Olotu AI, Williams C
articles.details: A serosurvey of 290 Tanzanian participants (114 children aged 5-12, 44 adolescents 13-17, 132 adults 18-45) measured IgG against P. falciparum transmission-blocking antigens Pfs230D1M, Pfs48/45, and Pfs25 by ELISA, with qPCR for parasites and membrane feeding assays for transmission-reducing activity. Pfs230D1M antibodies were detected in 56% (157/281) and Pfs48/45 in 49% (141/290), with ~30% positive for both; Pfs25 IgG was undetectable in all 117 tested. Seroprevalence rose significantly with age (adults vs children: Pfs230D1M adjusted OR 3.16, 95% CI 1.81-5.53, p≤0.0001; Pfs48/45 OR 3.06, 95% CI 1.79-5.25, p≤0.0001). Significant transmission-reducing activity appeared in 2/10 highly reactive participants. Authors conclude these naturally acquired responses are prevalent and stable.

articles.headline: Malaria drug-library screen yields potent SARS-CoV-2 PLpro inhibitors
articles.title_original: Screening of Medicines for Malaria Venture Open Boxes Identifies Potent SARS-CoV‑2 Papain-like Protease (PL(pro)) Inhibitors.
articles.authors: Gawriljuk VO, Noske GD, Fernandes RS, Nakamura AM, Freire MCLC, Godoy MO, Bonatto V, Chelucci R, Andricopulo A, Bakowski MA, Wolff KC, Riva L, Burrows JN, Wells TNC, Laleu B, Martins R, Souza JP, Arruda E, Samby K, Laskar S, Guido RVC, Oliva G, Godoy AS
articles.details: A target-based screening campaign tested Medicines for Malaria Venture open-access libraries (malaria, pathogen, COVID, and pandemic response boxes; ~1400 molecules) against SARS-CoV-2 main protease (Mpro) and papain-like protease (PLpro). MMV1634397 emerged as a PLpro inhibitor with IC50 of 0.7 μM, and optimized analogs 5, 10, and 13 reached IC50 values of 0.16, 0.34, and 0.06 μM respectively. The most potent compound and its isomer showed antiviral activity in ACE2-HeLa cells (EC50 2.9 and 3.7 μM) with favorable pharmacokinetics. Authors conclude the MMV libraries are valuable for target-based antiviral drug discovery.

articles.headline: Historical review of malaria casualties and chemoprophylaxis in Vietnam War
articles.title_original: US Malaria Casualties and Chemoprophylaxis during the Vietnam War.
articles.authors: Shanks GD
articles.details: This historical account describes malaria's impact on US military personnel during the Vietnam War, when it caused as many hospitalizations as combat wounds in 1965, with front-line infantry rates exceeding 10 per 1,000 man-days. Chloroquine-primaquine chemoprophylaxis was inadequate; adding daily dapsone improved effectiveness but caused rare agranulocytosis and at least eight deaths. Severe malaria caused 78 known deaths in the US Army and 46 in the US Marines, but the principal military burden was lost days—over a month per case and at least 200,000 lost man-days per year. P. vivax relapsing malaria later caused thousands of US infections, though indigenous transmission was rare. The author warns malaria's mass-casualty potential persists for deployments to New Guinea or sub-Saharan Africa.

articles.headline: Helminth infection rare in Ugandan children with severe malaria
articles.title_original: Low prevalence of helminth infection in Ugandan children hospitalized with severe malaria.
articles.authors: Capone D, Jahan N, Namazzi R, Opoka RO, John CC
articles.details: This study analyzed 177 stool samples from children aged 6-48 months with laboratory-confirmed severe malaria enrolled at two Ugandan hospitals from 2014-2017, plus 25 age/sex/time-matched community controls negative by rapid diagnostic test. Nucleic acids were tested by sensitive qPCR for six helminth species. Prevalence of ≥1 intestinal helminth was low in severe malaria cases (5.1%, 9/177) and controls (4.0%, 1/25), and helminth infection did not alter severe malaria risk (adjusted RR 1.0, 95% CI 0.82-1.3, p=0.78). Authors conclude Uganda's national deworming campaign has been highly successful and helminths were not associated with severe malaria here.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic non-falciparum and falciparum malaria widespread in Rwandan adults
articles.title_original: Prevalence of asymptomatic non-falciparum and falciparum malaria in the 2014-15 Rwanda Demographic Health Survey.
articles.authors: Gaither C, Morgan C, Kirby R, Karema C, Gashema P, Zuromski J, White SJ, Topazian HM, Giesbrecht D, Thwai K, Young NW, Goel V, Boyter K, Munyaneza T, Muvunyi CM, Butera JD, Bailey JA, Mazarati JB, Juliano JJ
articles.details: Using the 2014-2015 Rwanda Demographic Health Survey, researchers performed quantitative real-time PCR for four Plasmodium species in 5,050 primarily adult individuals and assessed correlates of infection. Overall infection prevalence was 22.3% (95% CI 20.3-24.3), with P. falciparum at 16.3% (14.5-18.1) and non-falciparum at 8.0% (6.6-9.3); P. ovale spp., P. malariae, and P. falciparum were broadly distributed while P. vivax was rare. Parasitemias tended to be low and mixed-species infections were common, especially in high-transmission areas. P. falciparum was associated with lower wealth, rural residence, and low elevation. Authors conclude continued molecular monitoring of Plasmodium diversity is needed to strengthen malaria control.

articles.headline: Cryo-EM maps malaria transmission-blocking Pfs230–Pfs48/45 fertilization complex
articles.title_original: Cryo-EM structure of endogenous Plasmodium falciparum Pfs230 and Pfs48/45 fertilization complex.
articles.authors: Dietrich MH, Chmielewski J, Chan LJ, Tan LL, Adair A, Lyons FMT, Gabriela M, Lopaticki S, Dite TA, Dagley LF, Pazzagli L, Gupta P, Kamil M, Vaughan AM, Rojrung R, Abraham A, Mazhari R, Longley RJ, Zeglinski K, Gouil Q, Mueller I, Fabb SA, Shandre-Mugan R, Pouton CW, Glukhova A, Shakeel S, Tham WH
articles.details: Using cryo-electron microscopy, researchers solved the structure of the endogenous Plasmodium falciparum Pfs230–Pfs48/45 complex, proteins essential for male gamete fertilization in the mosquito midgut and parasite transmission. Pfs48/45 was found to bind Pfs230 domains 13 and 14; transgenic parasites lacking these domains had defective Pfs230 gamete localization and reduced oocyst formation. Nanobodies against domains 13 and 14 blocked complex formation and reduced transmission, and these domains were targets of naturally acquired immunity. Immune sera from mRNA lipid nanoparticle immunizations also blocked parasite transmission.

articles.headline: Imported malaria in New York concentrated in NYC, key counties
articles.title_original: Imported Malaria in New York: Geographic Patterns and Implications for Emergency Physicians.
articles.authors: Webb Z
articles.details: This surveillance study examined county- and city-level trends in imported malaria across New York State and New York City from 2018 to 2024. New York City accounted for 79.1% of reported cases, and within the rest of the state 11 of 57 counties contributed 88.3% of cases outside NYC. Cases declined markedly in 2020, coinciding with COVID-19 travel restrictions, then rebounded in 2021. The authors emphasize incorporating travel history and local county-level awareness when evaluating febrile patients in emergency departments, and the need for targeted prevention in identified hotspots.

articles.headline: Walnut leaf extract reduces oxidative stress in mouse cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Effect of walnut leaves on oxidative stress caused by murine cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Abdel-Gaber R, Alharbi A, Almohawis N, Al Quraishy S, Al-Shaebi E
articles.details: In a murine model of Plasmodium berghei cerebral malaria, methanol extract of Juglans regia (walnut) leaves reduced brain oxidative stress markers—nitric oxide, malondialdehyde, and hydrogen peroxide—and increased the antioxidant enzymes catalase, superoxide dismutase, reduced glutathione, and total antioxidant capacity. Thirty female C57BL/6 mice (20–25 g, 9–12 weeks) were divided into six groups (G1–G6), and brains were harvested on day 9 for analysis, with iNOS measured by ELISA and immunohistochemistry. Although the treatment significantly increased iNOS expression, subsequent administration of the walnut leaf extract (JRLE) effectively mitigated this increase. The authors conclude that the leaves are an alternative source of new antioxidants and antimalarial agents.

articles.headline: Review: targeting Plasmodium telomere maintenance as new antimalarial strategy
articles.title_original: Plasmodium telomere maintenance: uncovering the Achilles' heel for novel antimalarials.
articles.authors: Wakai TN, Anzaku DO, Afolabi IS
articles.details: This review examines disrupting telomere maintenance in Plasmodium as a novel antimalarial approach. The authors note that, unlike humans, Plasmodium maintains continuous telomerase activity and uses unique telomere-binding proteins across its lifecycle, driving virulence and antigenic variation. They highlight that parasite telomeres harbor G-quadruplex DNA structures that may be druggable targets, and that the parasite depends on its telomerase catalytic subunit PfTERT, whose inhibition has blocked telomere elongation and impaired replication. The authors suggest combining telomere-directed therapies with existing antimalarials to overcome resistance, while cautioning that current studies are limited and inconclusive and that off-target effects must be considered.

articles.headline: SARS-CoV-2 prevalence and malaria co-infection studied in Equatorial Guinea
articles.title_original: Circulation of SARS-CoV-2 and Co-Infection with Plasmodium falciparum in Equatorial Guinea.
articles.authors: López-Farfán D, Ncogo P, Oki C, Riloha M, Ondo V, Cano-Jiménez P, Martínez-Martínez FJ, Molina-de la Fuente I, Comas I, Irigoyen N, Berzosa P, Benito Llanes A, Gómez-Díaz E
articles.details: This study assessed SARS-CoV-2 prevalence, circulating variants, and malaria co-infection in 1556 volunteers at six health centres in Bioko and Bata from June to October 2021, using SARS-CoV-2 antigen tests, microscopy for malaria, and whole-genome sequencing of positive samples. SARS-CoV-2 prevalence was 3.03% and malaria prevalence 22.25%; SARS-CoV-2 occurred at similar frequency across age groups whereas malaria was most frequent in children and teenagers. Six malaria/SARS-CoV-2 co-infections were found (0.37% prevalence). Of 43 sequenced isolates, most belonged to the Delta lineage and were introduced from Europe on multiple occasions. The authors provide first-time real prevalence estimates relevant to pandemic preparedness.

articles.headline: Review: plant secondary metabolites as antimalarial drug leads
articles.title_original: Plant-derived secondary metabolites for malaria treatment: extraction, mechanisms, and therapeutic potential.
articles.authors: Wassie AT, Ahmed IN, Bachheti RK, Tadesse MG, Wotango AS, Bachheti A, Gonfa YH, Assen AH
articles.details: This review compiles antimalarial plant-derived secondary metabolites, drawing data from Web of Science, PubMed, Scopus, Google Scholar, and Science Direct, and identifying 34 medicinal plants along with extraction techniques and efficacy factors. Key compound classes include alkaloids, flavonoids, terpenoids, and derivatives (e.g., quinine, artemisinin, quercetin, chalcones). Several compounds showed potent activity against Plasmodium falciparum: joziknipholone A (IC50 = 0.17 μM), joziknipholone B (IC50 = 0.26 μM), ceramicine R (IC50 = 2.80 μM), cajachalcone (IC50 = 7.4 μM), and knipholone clooxanthrone (IC50 = 14.58 μM for D6, 9.42 μM for W2). The authors highlight these as templates for developing drugs against drug-sensitive and drug-resistant malaria.

articles.headline: Correction notice: malaria vector habitat and LLIN intervention study
articles.title_original: Correction: Ecological drivers of malaria vector habitat and transmission over 1 year of long-lasting insecticidal net intervention in Côte d'Ivoire.
articles.authors: Talbot B, Alou LPA, Koffi AA, Sih C, Dangbenon E, Zoh MG, Camara S, Assi SB, N'Guessan R, Messenger LA, Protopopoff N, Cook J, Kulkarni MA
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Metagenomic complexity of asymptomatic Plasmodium infections in Ghana
articles.title_original: Metagenomic complexity of high, seasonal transmission of Plasmodium spp. in asymptomatic carriers in Northern Sahelian Ghana.
articles.authors: Tan MH, Bangre O, Rios-Teran CA, Tiedje KE, Deed SL, Zhan Q, Rasyidi F, Pascual M, Ansah PO, Day KP
articles.details: This study applied a metagenomic approach to characterize mixed-species and mixed-strain Plasmodium infections in 188 afebrile residents of high, seasonal transmission areas in Northern Sahelian Ghana, estimating multiplicity of infection via antigen and microsatellite genotyping. Prevalence and inter-/intra-species complexity were significantly higher in larger sampled blood volumes. Infections showed high metagenomic complexity, including single-, double-, and triple-species infections involving P. falciparum, P. malariae, P. ovale curtisi, and P. ovale wallikeri, with some highly complex infections unexplained by age or location. The authors conclude that more sensitive measurement of within-host complexity in asymptomatic carriers is needed to optimize surveillance and control.

articles.headline: PfPP1 phosphatase regulates malaria parasite transmission to mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum PP1 phosphatase is a key regulator of malaria parasite transmission.
articles.authors: Royer L, Colard-Itté E, Tavella T, Lorthiois A, Goussin S, N'Dri M-E, Sabra R, Deiss A, Thiberge S, Lamarque MH, Lavazec C
articles.details: Using a conditional depletion strategy, this functional study examined the serine-threonine phosphatase PfPP1 during Plasmodium falciparum gametocyte development, gamete formation, and transmission to mosquitoes. PfPP1 was found to regulate the deformability and permeability of mature gametocyte-infected erythrocytes through dephosphorylation of protein kinase A (PKA) substrates, modulating host cell mechanical properties important for gametocyte persistence in the bloodstream. PfPP1 also controlled gametocyte activation via stimulation of the cyclic GMP (cGMP)/protein kinase G (PKG) pathway. The authors conclude PfPP1 is pivotal for transmission and represents a potential target for blocking malaria parasite transmission.

articles.headline: Melochia umbellata leaf extract cures malaria in infected rats
articles.title_original: Curative antimalarial efficacy and hepato-renal protective effects of Melochia umbellata leaf extract in rats infected with Plasmodium berghei.
articles.authors: Ngouana V, Tsouh Fokou PV, Tsakem Nangap MJ, Kemzeu R, Kamche Youbi A, Boyom FF
articles.details: This in vivo study evaluated the curative antimalarial and hepato-renal protective effects of Melochia umbellata aqueous leaf extract (MULAE) in Plasmodium berghei-infected rats, with phytochemical characterization confirming alkaloids, phenols, flavonoids, and saponins. In acute toxicity testing (OECD 423), single oral doses of 2000 and 5000 mg/kg caused no mortality, indicating LD50 greater than 5000 mg/kg. In the curative assay, rats were treated for five days with MULAE (100, 200, 400 mg/kg), chloroquine (10 mg/kg), or distilled water; MULAE suppressed parasitemia dose-dependently, with the 400 mg/kg dose achieving 86.20% chemosuppression and restoring hemoglobin to 14.9 g/dL. It also improved hematological, renal, hepatic, and oxidative markers. The authors conclude the findings validate its traditional use and support its potential as a source of novel antimalarial and organ-protective agents.

articles.headline: Drug-resistance markers near-universal in Vietnam's asymptomatic malaria carriers
articles.title_original: Molecular Profiles of Antimalarial Drug Resistance in Plasmodium Species from Asymptomatic Malaria Carriers in Gia Lai Province, Vietnam.
articles.authors: Lê HG, Võ TC, Kang JM, Van Khanh C, Trinh NTM, Hanh NTL, Cho M, Quang HH, Na BK
articles.details: A study of 2,171 individuals across three districts of Gia Lai province, Vietnam, used quantitative real-time PCR and sequencing to detect asymptomatic submicroscopic malaria and to characterize antimalarial drug-resistance genes (pfk13, pfcrt, pvmdr-1, pvcrt-o). The asymptomatic submicroscopic infection rate was 2.67%, with P. vivax more prevalent than P. falciparum and mixed P. falciparum/P. vivax infections also identified; adults and females were the most affected groups. Among carriers, resistance-associated mutations were present at high frequency, including pfk13 C580Y (100%), pfcrt M74I/N75E/K76T (100%), and pvmdr-1 F1076L (100%). The authors stress an urgent need for improved surveillance and targeted intervention to prevent the silent spread of malaria.

articles.headline: Review of public-private partnerships for climate-resilient malaria control in Africa
articles.title_original: Challenges and opportunities for greater public-private partnership for the implementation of the WHO operational framework for building climate resilient health systems to improve malaria control and elimination in Sub-Saharan Africa: a rapid review.
articles.authors: Lumumba S, Kamau S, Ntwiga I, Muchangi JM, Kiarie J, Kosgei S, Mwamburi M, Kimathi G
articles.details: This PRISMA- and CRD-guided systematic review examined public-private partnerships (PPPs) for implementing the WHO Operational Framework to build climate-resilient health systems for malaria control in Sub-Saharan Africa. From 173 papers identified, 11 articles were included. Identified challenges included ineffective legal and policy frameworks, bureaucracy, limited buy-in and guideline adherence by private partners, and a lack of systemic integration of climate risk assessments into health planning, while opportunities lay in the health workforce, essential medicines and technologies, and emergency preparedness and management. The authors conclude that PPPs remain a viable approach to adapting the WHO Operational Framework given the region's technical and financial constraints.

articles.headline: Malaria care-seeking delays linked to ethnicity, occupation in Myanmar and Thailand
articles.title_original: Malaria care-seeking among febrile patients in Myanmar and Thailand: an accelerated failure time analysis.
articles.authors: Htwe KZZ, Aung PL, Sripoorote P, Soe MT, Petchvijit P, Aung PP, Khamsiriwatchara A, Lawpoolsri S, Cui L, Parker DM, Kyaw MP, Sattabongkot J, Kaewkungwal J
articles.details: This longitudinal study analyzed time to care-seeking among febrile patients suspected of malaria attending community-based facilities in Myanmar (n=2,960) and Thailand (n=15,576), using log-logistic Accelerated Failure Time models to estimate Time Ratios (TRs). Mean fever duration at diagnosis was 1.8 days (SD 1.3) in Myanmar and 2.9 days (SD 1.8) in Thailand. Longer delays were associated with Shan ethnicity (TR 1.48), farming, uncertain malaria history, and diagnosis with P. falciparum (TR 1.10 Myanmar; 1.27 Thailand) or P. vivax (TR 1.23 Myanmar; 1.20 Thailand); bed net use and prior episodes shortened delays. The authors recommend targeted interventions to promote timely care-seeking.

articles.headline: Voltage-switching mass spectrometry improves malaria detection in clinical samples
articles.title_original: Voltage Switching on a Dual Nano-Electrospray/Atmospheric Pressure Chemical Ionization Platform Provides Higher Discrimination Power for Malaria Detection via Direct Infusion Mass Spectrometry.
articles.authors: Ferguson R, Kulyk DS, Badu-Tawiah AK
articles.details: This analytical study tested a dual nano-electrospray (nESI)/atmospheric pressure chemical ionization (APCI) mass spectrometry platform using step voltage switching to generate composite spectra richer in chemical information than constant-voltage nESI or APCI alone. Nine malaria-infected and nine uninfected samples (18 total) were analyzed, with partial least-squares discriminant analysis (PLS-DA) and volcano plots used for classification. Constant-voltage APCI misclassified four samples and nESI misclassified two, whereas the voltage-switching composite spectra correctly classified all 18 clinical samples, providing the highest discriminatory power. The authors conclude voltage switching enhances detection of downregulated species and improves malaria discrimination.

articles.headline: Recombinant P. falciparum proteasome platform enables selective antimalarial inhibitor design
articles.title_original: A recombinant expression system for the Plasmodium falciparum proteasome enables structural analysis of its assembly and the design of selective inhibitors.
articles.authors: Fajtova P, Zhang H, Urich L, da Silva EB, da Silva CH, Almaliti J, Al-Hindy M, Boura E, Kirkman LA, Lin G, Bogyo M, Fidock DA, Gerwick WH, Zhao J, O'Donoghue AJ
articles.details: Researchers developed a recombinant Plasmodium falciparum 20S proteasome (Pf20S) expression system by co-expressing all fourteen subunits plus the chaperone homolog Ump1 in insect cells, yielding both mature and immature complexes. Cryo-EM of immature complexes revealed how Ump1 and the β2 and β5 propeptides coordinate β-ring assembly, differing from human and yeast homologs, and β1, β2, and β5 were confirmed catalytically active. Clinical inhibitors bortezomib, carfilzomib, and marizomib were potent but non-selective, whereas the tripeptide-epoxyketone J-80 inhibited Pf20S β5 with an IC50 of 22.4 nM and 90-fold selectivity over human β5. The platform accelerates development of selective antimalarial therapeutics.

articles.headline: BinaxNOW malaria rapid test verified across hospitals in low-prevalence area
articles.title_original: BinaxNOW vs malaria: A diagnostic showdown: Design and implementation of a verification strategy across 4 academic hospitals in an area with low malaria prevalence.
articles.authors: Turbett SE, Bielawski D, Dighe AS, Kaseta N, Lewandrowski K, Mercer L, Patten C, Sta Cruz ML, Shenoy ES, Branda JA
articles.details: This study describes implementation and verification of the FDA-cleared BinaxNOW Malaria rapid antigen test across a health care network, using archived well-characterized blood samples to overcome scarce clinical specimens in a low-prevalence setting. In the initial evaluation, BinaxNOW was compared to blood smear in 294 whole-blood specimens; for site-based verification, each site tested 10 previously antigen-positive and 10 antigen-negative specimens. The test correctly identified Plasmodium in 100% of P. falciparum-positive specimens, with 96% identified to species level, and overall sensitivity and specificity of 100%. Site verification showed 100% positive and negative percent agreement, concordance, and reproducibility.

articles.headline: Anopheles maculatus wing shape varies by season in western Thailand
articles.title_original: Seasonal variation in wing geometry of the malaria vector Anopheles maculatus (Diptera: Culicidae) in Western Thailand.
articles.authors: Sumruayphol S, Chaiphongpachara T, Laojun S
articles.details: Using landmark- and outline-based geometric morphometrics on the same wing image set, researchers assessed seasonal variation in wing structure and contour of the malaria vector Anopheles maculatus from malaria hotspots in western Thailand across hot, wet, and dry seasons. Differences were tested by non-parametric ANOVA (1,000 replicates) with Bonferroni post hoc tests at p<0.05. Wing structure size differed significantly between hot and dry seasons, and wing structure shape differed between dry-versus-hot and dry-versus-wet populations; wing contour shape differed only between dry and wet populations. The authors suggest these findings reflect seasonal adaptation and could inform malaria surveillance.

articles.headline: How Pfcoronin mutations alter artemisinin susceptibility in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Cellular and molecular basis of Pf Coronin function in artemisinin resistance in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Ullah I, Farringer MA, Khushu M, Burkhard AY, Hathaway E, Willett BC, Shin SH, Sharma AI, Martin MC, Harrigan O, Shao KL, Dvorin JD, Hartl DL, Volkman SK, Bopp S, Absalon S, Wirth DF
articles.details: This laboratory study investigates how Pf Coronin, a non-essential actin regulator active during the ring stage of Plasmodium falciparum, contributes to artemisinin resistance, complementing the established role of Pfkelch13 mutations. The authors show that Pf Coronin facilitates efficient hemoglobin uptake, and that resistance-associated Pfcoronin mutations disrupt its interaction with Pf Actin and its ring-stage localization, impairing endocytosis and reducing hemoglobin acquisition. Pf Coronin and Pf Kelch13 act in distinct cellular regions but both converge to limit heme availability and artemisinin activation. The findings indicate that Pfcoronin mutations reduce endocytosis and modulate artemisinin susceptibility during the clinically relevant ring stage.

articles.headline: Fever boosts malaria parasite binding to blood vessel walls
articles.title_original: Febrile temperature enhances Plasmodium falciparum cytoadhesion by disrupting the endothelial glycocalyx.
articles.authors: Introini V, Long R, Oyerinde OR, Sender SS, Stein F, Hwang GM, Gutierrez BL, Seydel KB, Birbeck G, Bernabeu M
articles.details: Using a 3D 'fever-on-a-chip' model that recapitulates human brain and lung microvessels, this study examined how febrile temperatures affect endothelial cytoadhesion in malaria. Short febrile episodes at 40°C, common in treated cerebral malaria patients, rapidly enhanced infected red blood cell and immune cell binding under flow. Mechanistically, this was driven by endothelial glycocalyx shedding that exposed the endothelial receptors EPCR and ICAM-1, and preserving glycocalyx integrity with a broad MMP inhibitor prevented the temperature-induced rise in cytoadhesion. The authors identify fever as a host-specific amplifier of vascular pathology in malaria and point to endothelial-protective or antipyretic strategies to mitigate febrile microvascular damage.

articles.headline: Nanoparticle junctional vaccine protects against malaria alone or with R21
articles.title_original: Protective immunity against malaria by a nanoparticle CIS43-based junctional vaccine alone or in combination with R21.
articles.authors: Tripathi P, Koo JH, Chen X, Da Silva Pereira L, Dillon M, Zhang B, Lofgren M, Nguyen KT, Teng IT, Bonilla B, Kerscher S, Kong WP, Ransier A, Stephens T, Tsybovsky Y, Weldon SR, Douek DC, Pierson TC, Batista FD, Idris AH, Seder RA, Kwong PD, Zhou T
articles.details: This study developed a malaria vaccine displaying tandem repeats of the PfCSP junctional epitope (targeted by the protective CIS43 antibody) on a self-assembling nanoparticle, and compared it alone or combined with the benchmark R21 vaccine using B cell analysis and monoclonal antibody isolation. At a 1 μg dose, the junctional and R21 vaccines produced comparable reductions in liver burden; at 0.25 μg, the junctional vaccine gave a modest reduction compared with R21, and combining the two vaccines modestly enhanced protection over either alone. The R21 vaccine elicited antibodies primarily against the major repeats, whereas the junctional vaccine elicited antibodies against both junctional and major repeat regions. The authors conclude the nanoparticle junctional vaccine is a promising approach to broaden anti-malaria immunity.

articles.headline: Senegal study links COVID-19 vaccine attitudes to malaria vaccine acceptance
articles.title_original: Attitudes to malaria vaccination in children: what can be learned from their consistency with the temporal dynamics of COVID-19 vaccination intention? A prospective cohort study in rural Senegal (July 2020-November 2023).
articles.authors: Seror V, Cortaredona S, Ba EH, Maradan G, Sokhna C
articles.details: A six-wave telephone survey of 600 randomly selected Senegalese households (558 participated, 93.00%) used a latent class mixed model to track COVID-19 vaccine intention/uptake across the 2021-2023 campaigns and compare these patterns with attitudes to childhood malaria vaccination. Very strong positive attitudes to malaria vaccination (65.28% of participants) were associated with higher personal COVID-19 vaccine uptake (p<0.001). Three COVID-19 patterns emerged: continuously strong intention (34.88%), increasingly strong (33.40%), and increasingly less strong (31.72%). Households where both surveyed members had continuously strong COVID-19 intention were more likely to favor malaria vaccination for children (p=0.001), and the authors suggest targeting parents who need incentives to improve compliance.

articles.headline: Commentary: resurgence of malaria in Africa is an avoidable crisis
articles.title_original: The resurgence of malaria in Africa is an avoidable crisis-here's what we must do.
articles.authors: Kaseya J, Dereje N
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Plasmodium falciparum shows high genetic diversity in northern Côte d'Ivoire
articles.title_original: Genetic Diversity of Plasmodium falciparum in Korhogo Health District, Northern Côte d'Ivoire.
articles.authors: Benié EMA, Beuret C, Schnoz A, Cantoreggi SL, Ding XC, Silué KD, Nsanzabana C
articles.details: This study assessed Plasmodium falciparum genetic diversity using msp1, msp2, and glurp markers in dried blood spots collected in Korhogo health district, Northern Côte d'Ivoire, between 2019 and 2020. Of 179 randomly selected samples genotyped, amplification succeeded for 82% (msp1), 85% (msp2), and 75% (glurp). For msp1, the K1 family had 20 genotypes, MAD20 had 23, and RO33 had one; for msp2, 3D7 had 59 and FC27 had 33 genotypes; glurp had 45 genotypes. Expected heterozygosity was ≥0.9 for all three markers, indicating a highly diverse parasite population, providing baseline data for evaluating interventions.

articles.headline: Mini review: how malaria disrupts red cell production in bone marrow
articles.title_original: Malaria and dyserythropoiesis: a mini review.
articles.authors: Liu FF, Li K
articles.details: This synthesis of in vitro, ex vivo, clinical, and postmortem studies proposes a unified mechanistic framework for malaria-associated dyserythropoiesis involving four converging pathways. A cytokine storm (IL-6, TNF-α, IFN-γ, MIF) suppresses erythropoietin, upregulates hepcidin, and destabilizes GATA-1 to divert erythroid progenitors toward myeloid fate; hemozoin crystals catalyze Fenton chemistry and lipid peroxidation, generating 4-hydroxynonenal adducts that cripple GATA-1 and trigger mitochondrial apoptosis of erythroblasts; Plasmodium preferentially infects orthochromatic erythroblasts, prolonging a 10-day gametocyte maturation cycle beyond the host's 3-4-day enucleation window and releasing extracellular vesicles that arrest differentiation; and hemozoin-laden macrophages remodel erythroblastic islands, causing local iron restriction and sustained oxidative stress. The authors highlight single-cell and spatial-omics technologies and 3-D bone-marrow organoids as platforms for testing niche-targeted therapies.

articles.headline: Coinfection prevalence and liver markers in Hepatitis B patients, Ghana
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Helicobacter pylori, Salmonella typhi, Plasmodium falciparum, and Toxoplasma gondii infections and levels of liver function markers among Hepatitis B Virus infected Ghanaians: A cross-sectional study in the Greater Accra Region.
articles.authors: Nyarko EN, Amakye EK, Ofori EK, Appiah M, Anim M, Lartey NL, Ametepe S, Adu EA, Kumi J, Dodoo DND, Adom M, Owiredu E, Kwafo E, Obirikorang C
articles.details: This cross-sectional study of 120 HBsAg-positive HBV-infected persons in the Greater Accra Region of Ghana screened for Helicobacter pylori, Salmonella typhi, Plasmodium falciparum, and Toxoplasma gondii using lateral flow immunochromatographic assays (P. falciparum also confirmed by blood film microscopy) and measured liver function markers. Of participants, 62.5% were male and 51.7% were aged 21-30. Prevalences were 40.8% (H. pylori), 2.5% (S. typhi), 19.2% (T. gondii), and 2.5% (P. falciparum). ALT was higher in HBV-H. pylori coinfected versus HBV-only persons (33.5 vs 23.5 IU, p<0.001). Authors recommend broadening screening for HBV coinfections beyond malaria.

articles.headline: Free web tool automates virtual drug screening against P. falciparum
articles.title_original: PlasmoDocking: A User-Friendly Open-Source Web Tool for Virtual Screening Targeting Plasmodium falciparum Enzymes.
articles.authors: Guariero FL, Macedo EP, Laia EB, Evaristo JAM, Evaristo GPC, Zanchi FB
articles.details: This is a software/methods report describing PlasmoDocking, an open-source web platform that automates molecular docking-based virtual screening against Plasmodium falciparum targets. Users can submit up to 10 molecular structures (.sdf format) for automated AutodockGPU screening against 38 pre-configured parasite targets, comparing binding energies with co-crystallized ligands. Built with Python and Next.js, the tool aims to accelerate antimalarial drug discovery by enabling simultaneous multi-target docking; the code and web app are publicly available.

articles.headline: Antimalarial self-medication pooled at 55% across sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Issues associated with malaria self-medication in sub-Saharan Africa: A systematic literature review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Amaka JI, MacLeod E, Picozzi K, Fyfe J, Ezenyi IC, Ijaiya IS, Attah DD, Godwin BA, Obisike VU, Galamaji MM
articles.details: This PRISMA-based systematic review and meta-analysis examined malaria self-medication in sub-Saharan Africa, including 27 studies that met inclusion criteria. The pooled prevalence of malaria self-medication was 55.3%. Reported drivers included low income, cheap availability of non-prescription drugs, large family size, lack of health insurance, difficulty accessing formal healthcare, and previous satisfactory use of specific drugs. The authors conclude that health authorities should incorporate informal drug-market stakeholders into frameworks promoting more informed antimalarial use, given the practice's link to antimalarial drug resistance.

articles.headline: Spatial repellents cut malaria infections by half in trial meta-analysis
articles.title_original: Efficacy of Spatial Repellents in Malaria Prevention: A Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials.
articles.authors: Prager M, Bergmann F, Pracher L, Zeitlinger M, Jorda A
articles.details: This meta-analysis pooled four randomized controlled trials conducted in China, Indonesia, and Kenya involving 6,745 participants to assess spatial repellents for malaria prevention. Malaria infection risk was lower with spatial repellents (681 [20.0%] of 3,399) than controls (1,055 [31.0%] of 3,346), a 52% reduction (pooled risk ratio 0.48; 95% CI 0.33–0.70; P<0.001). No significant effects were seen on mosquito density (mean difference −10.0; 95% CI −29.6 to 9.7) or sporozoite rates (risk ratio 1.13; 95% CI 0.83–1.54). High heterogeneity limited certainty, but authors support spatial repellents as a complementary prevention tool.

articles.headline: Burkina Faso halts transgenic mosquito malaria-control plan
articles.title_original: Burkina Faso halts plan to fight malaria with transgenic mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Kupferschmidt K
articles.details: This news item reports that a misinformation campaign may have eroded trust and contributed to a 'brutal, humiliating' raid on a research center.

articles.headline: Correction notice: aspartic protease inhibitors for P. vivax drug development
articles.title_original: Correction: Computational identification of aspartic protease inhibitors for antimalarial drug development against Plasmodium Vivax.
articles.authors: Alruwaili M, Alhassan HH, Almutary H, Tahir Ul Qamar M
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: In-silico multi-epitope vaccine targets mosquito FREP1 to block malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Designing a multi-epitope vaccine against the midgut-specific fibrinogen-related protein 1(FREP1) of Anopheles stephensi to enhance protection against the malaria parasite: a step beyond traditional vaccine development approaches.
articles.authors: Yadav M, Dahiya N, Singh H, Kataria D, Janjoter S, Sehrawat N
articles.details: This immunoinformatics/computational study designed a multi-epitope transmission-blocking vaccine targeting the Anopheles stephensi midgut protein fibrinogen-related protein 1 (FREP1), which is involved in Plasmodium transmission within the mosquito. The construct was evaluated in silico for allergenicity, antigenicity, toxicity, immunogenicity, population coverage, conservancy, solubility, and secondary/tertiary structure. Molecular docking showed stable binding to the TLR4 receptor, and molecular dynamics simulation plus in-silico immunization indicated favorable binding energy and a strong predicted immune response. Codon optimization and in-silico cloning in Escherichia coli suggested efficient protein expression. The authors conclude the FREP1-based vaccine is a promising candidate for blocking parasite transmission in the vector; no experimental or clinical validation was reported.

articles.headline: Curative drug-interaction analysis of artesunate/pyronaridine combinations in a malaria mouse model
articles.title_original: Fractional curative killing of pyronaridine or artesunate combinations with tafenoquine, 4-aminoquinolines, or azithromycin in a murine malaria-luciferase model.
articles.authors: Lee G, Aneke JS, Sullivan DJ Jr
articles.details: Using a high-parasitemia Plasmodium berghei ANKA-luciferase mouse blood-stage model, researchers measured curative killing interactions of multiple-dose, short half-life artesunate or single-dose, long half-life pyronaridine combined with single-dose, long half-life quinolines (chloroquine, amodiaquine, tafenoquine) and with azithromycin. Parasite reduction rate correlated only nonspecifically with final curative interactions. Tafenoquine/artesunate and pyronaridine/amodiaquine had fractional combination curative doses of 0.83 and 0.93, with other interactions near neutral (0.9–1.1); all combinations fell in the additive range. Azithromycin was additive to artesunate or pyronaridine. The authors propose murine curative drug interactions as a benchmark for human studies.

articles.headline: Azithromycin analogs with chloroquinoline groups show enhanced fast malaria killing
articles.title_original: Characterizing the quick-killing mechanism of action of azithromycin analogs against malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Mao EY, Nguyen W, Jana GP, Maity BC, Pazicky S, Giannangelo C, Reader J, Famodimu MT, Birkholtz L-M, Delves MJ, Creek DJ, Bozdech Z, Laleu B, Burrows JN, Sleebs BE, Gancheva MR, Wilson DW
articles.details: This laboratory study characterized the quick-killing mechanism of five azithromycin analogs against malaria parasites, two containing differentially oriented chloroquinoline moieties. Non-chloroquinoline analogs improved quick-killing potency about 10- to 42-fold over azithromycin, and chloroquinoline-containing analogs added a further 2- to 17-fold improvement. Inhibition of hemoglobin digestion and heme polymerization linked the analogs to a chloroquine-like mechanism, though none lost efficacy against chloroquine-resistant asexual blood-stage parasites. Resistance selection attempts failed, and thermal shift proteomics showed analogs stabilized 34–155 proteins in trophozoites with minimal overlap with chloroquine. The most potent analog improved gametocytocidal activity over azithromycin. The authors conclude these analogs act via broad mechanisms and are promising fast, broad-acting candidates.

articles.headline: 7% asymptomatic Plasmodium prevalence, P. ovale dominant, in northeastern South Africa
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium species in asymptomatic individuals in North-Eastern South Africa: 2018 - 2019.
articles.authors: Munzhedzi M, Guler JL, Krivacsy S, Shifflett P, Operario DJ, Dillingham R, McQuade ETR, Bessong PO
articles.details: In a survey of 985 asymptomatic individuals using finger-prick dried blood spots, a P. falciparum-specific RDT and high-resolution melt analysis tested for P. falciparum, P. ovale, P. vivax, and P. malariae, with Pfk13 sequencing for artemisinin resistance. Plasmodium prevalence was 7.1% (70/985; 95% CI 0.054–0.087). The dominant species was P. ovale (57.14%; n=40), followed by P. falciparum (37.1%; n=26), P. malariae (1.43%), and P. vivax (1.43%); P. falciparum/P. ovale mixed infections were 2.9%, and infections differed significantly by village (p<0.01). Pfk13 was amplified from 5/30 samples; 57 SNPs were found (39/57, 70.1% non-synonymous), none previously linked to artemisinin resistance, though novel propeller-domain SNPs (H719Q, P701T, M472I, I526R, P443S) were detected. Authors note P. ovale may be missed by the RDT and that R21/RTS,S vaccines may not protect against it.

articles.headline: Armed conflict in eastern DR Congo and malaria outbreak risk in under-fives
articles.title_original: The impact of armed conflict in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo on the risk of malaria outbreaks in children under five years old.
articles.authors: Yokolo H, Tague C, Ekouo J, Makeda D, Akilimali A
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Tafenoquine after G6PD screening cost-effective for vivax malaria in Brazil
articles.title_original: The cost-effectiveness of tafenoquine following screening with STANDARD™ G6PD screening for the treatment of vivax malaria in the Brazilian Public Health System.
articles.authors: Peixoto HM, Bastos Gottin LL, Brito-Sousa JD, Sampaio V, Daumerie PG, Jambert E, Monteiro W, Lacerda MVG, Devine A
articles.details: A decision-tree modelling study from the Brazilian Public Health System perspective evaluated adults with vivax malaria over 12 months. Single-dose tafenoquine given after semi-quantitative G6PD screening (≥70% activity) was compared with current practice (7-day low-dose primaquine, 0.5 mg/kg/day, without screening) and a primaquine screening strategy (7-day primaquine for those with ≥30% G6PD activity). Tafenoquine cost US$2894 per DALY averted versus current practice, well below the US$7752 willingness-to-pay threshold, and dominated the primaquine screening strategy (averting 0.14 DALYs with US$13 savings). It had >98% likelihood of being cost-effective in both comparisons.

articles.headline: Preterm birth raises childhood malaria risk in multigravid women's offspring
articles.title_original: Preterm Birth and Malaria Susceptibility in Offspring of Uninfected Multigravid Women.
articles.authors: Barry A, Dang L, Sidibe Y, Issiaka D, Gaoussou S, Hu Z, Dicko Y, Mahamar A, Attaher O, Diarra BS, Keita S, Dicko A, Duffy PE, Fried M
articles.details: In this longitudinal cohort study of mother-child pairs in Ouélessébougou, Mali, an area of high seasonal malaria transmission, pregnancy malaria (PM) was associated with an increased hazard of first malaria infection and first clinical malaria in children of women of all gravidities. Among 1679 children included in adjusted models, followed for a mean of 25.8 months for up to 5 years, preterm delivery (PTD) increased the hazard of first malaria infection only in offspring of multigravid women (HR 1.76; 95% CI 1.05-2.95; P=.03). The hazard ratio of first parasitemia for preterm versus full-term offspring was 2.17 (95% CI 1.25-3.75) in uninfected secundigravida women and 3.63 (95% CI 1.90-5.93) in multigravida women, and the parasitemia incidence rate ratio for PTD was 2.74 (95% CI 1.80-4.18) in uninfected multigravida women. The association between PTD and malaria risk varied by maternal gravidity and infection history during pregnancy.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria in migrants linked to splenomegaly and anemia
articles.title_original: Clinical Findings in Migrants With Asymptomatic Plasmodium Infections.
articles.authors: Eliasson I, Wyss K, Tafesse Bogale R, Forsblom S, Lindquist E, Rönnberg C, Hansson M, Beshara S, Nordling I, Hertting O, Wångdahl A, Färnert A
articles.details: A retrospective observational study evaluated medical records of asymptomatic sub-Saharan African migrants identified through a screening study in Stockholm, Sweden. Clinical data from 65 Plasmodium PCR-positive individuals were compared with 54 PCR-negative individuals (assessed for schistosomiasis and/or strongyloidiasis). PCR-positive individuals had higher proportions of anemia (21.1% vs 6.1%, P=.048), elevated ESR (58.1% vs 25.0%, P=.008), raised IgM (30.5% vs 10.5%, P=.030), and splenomegaly (25.4% vs 2.5%, P=.002). Splenomegaly and laboratory parameters improved after antimalarial treatment. The authors conclude screening and treatment of subclinical malaria should be considered in both endemic and nonendemic settings.

articles.headline: Anopheles vectors confirmed in Colombian gold-mining malaria areas
articles.title_original: Anopheles species associated with malaria transmission in gold-mining areas of northwest Colombia.
articles.authors: Rojas MA, Ahumada ML, Velasco-Pareja MC, Chaparro P, Yasnot-Acosta MF, Orjuela LI
articles.details: A cross-sectional study in five gold-mining localities of El Bagre, Antioquia, Colombia, collected adult mosquitoes indoors and outdoors, confirmed species by COI sequencing, and tested for Plasmodium by ELISA and nested PCR. Eleven Anopheles species were identified. Six specimens were PCR-positive: Anopheles darlingi infected with P. falciparum, and An. nuneztovari s.s. and An. punctimacula infected with P. vivax. The entomological inoculation rate ranged from 1.5 to 6.6 infective bites/person/month across localities. Larval stages were found in natural and man-made gold-mining excavations, though physical and biological parameters showed no significant association with abundance. The authors suggest larval source management as a supplementary control measure.

articles.headline: MALDI-TOF accurately identifies secondary African malaria vectors
articles.title_original: MALDI-TOF MS for identification of Afro-tropical secondary malaria vectors.
articles.authors: Tuwei M, Karisa J, Wanjiku C, Kiuru C, Ondieki Z, Odongo T, Mure F, Otieno B, Meli P, Okoko M, Bartilol B, Gona R, Constantino L, Cole G, Anastácio T, Armazia R, Alves C, Rui P, Ramaita E, Rono M, Candrinho B, Mwangangi J, Mbogo C, Charlwood D, Saute F, Rabinovich R, Chaccour C, Maia MF
articles.details: This study evaluated MALDI-TOF MS for identifying secondary malaria vectors (Anophelines other than An. gambiae s.l. and An. funestus s.l.) using adult mosquitoes collected in Kenya and Mozambique. Spectra from individual cephalothoraxes were referenced against a library validated by Sanger sequencing of ITS2 and COI. Identification showed 91% overall accuracy and a kappa of 0.87 versus sequencing, with 100% sensitivity and specificity for most species except distinguishing An. cf. coustani 2 NFL-2015 from An. ziemanni. Identified species included An. cf. coustani 2 NFL-2015, An. pretoriensis, An. rufipes, An. ziemanni, An. coustani, and An. pharoensis. The authors conclude MALDI-TOF MS is as efficient as DNA sequencing for monitoring these vectors.

articles.headline: Low childhood immunization in southern Togo threatens malaria chemoprevention rollout
articles.title_original: Assessing immunization coverage in Southern togo: implications for perennial malaria chemoprevention delivery.
articles.authors: Arikawa S, Tchankoni MK, Gbeasor-Komlanvi F, Atekpe SP, Atcha-Oubou T, Figueroa-Romero A, Orne-Gliemann J, Menendez C, Gonzalez R, Briand V, Ekouevi D
articles.details: This study analyzed secondary data from a 2022 multi-stage household survey in Haho district, southern Togo, examining 583 children aged 12-23 months to assess Expanded Program on Immunization (EPI) coverage relevant to delivering perennial malaria chemoprevention (PMC). Only 60.7% were fully immunized and 15.8% were zero-dose (lacking the first diphtheria-tetanus-pertussis vaccine); uptake fell from 84% at birth to 35% for the second measles vaccine at 15 months. Zero-dose status was significantly associated with low caregiver education (aOR 5.02, 95% CI 1.85-13.60), negative attitudes toward vaccine safety (aOR 8.60, 95% CI 3.32-22.30), and lack of reliance on modern healthcare (aOR 3.06, 95% CI 1.26-7.48), while distance to facilities was not. Authors conclude community engagement must be strengthened for effective PMC delivery.

articles.headline: Combined CSP and Pfs48/45 nanoparticle vaccine blocks malaria infection and transmission
articles.title_original: A combined designed CSP and Pfs48/45 infection and transmission blocking vaccine for malaria.
articles.authors: Gupta R, Dickey TH, Salinas ND, Patel PN, Ma R, Shi D, Singleton M, Ouahes T, Pham TP, Miura K, Long CA, Lambert LE, Tolia NH
articles.details: Researchers engineered a self-assembled ferritin nanoparticle vaccine (CSPj5c-17-4-ferritin) co-presenting structure-based designed immunogens from the pre-erythrocytic circumsporozoite protein (CSPj5c) and the transmission-blocking Pfs48/45 antigen (17-4) to target two stages of the parasite life cycle. In mice, immunization conferred protection against challenge with transgenic sporozoites expressing Plasmodium falciparum CSP, and purified IgGs from immunized rabbits elicited potent transmission-reducing activity. Adding the engineered 17-4 antigen improved immune responses to CSPj5c and protection from sporozoite challenge. The authors conclude this is a promising multistage malaria vaccine candidate for malaria control.

articles.headline: Herbal five-flower remedy plus artesunate improves outcomes in mouse cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Antimalarial and neuroprotective effects of ethanolic extracts of the five-flower remedy in an experimental cerebral malaria model.
articles.authors: Plirat W, Chaniad P, Phuwajaroanpong A, Konyanee A, Lalert L, Septama AW, Punsawad C
articles.details: This experimental study tested the ethanolic extract of the five-flower remedy (FFR), a traditional herbal formulation, in a Plasmodium berghei ANKA-induced experimental cerebral malaria model in male C57BL/6 mice. FFR (600 mg/kg) was given alone and combined with artesunate for seven consecutive days starting at symptom onset, with assessment of parasitemia, clinical progression, behavior, and brain histopathology. FFR alone improved outcomes, while combination with artesunate significantly reduced parasitemia (80%), increased survival, reduced neurological deficits, and decreased brain hemorrhage, leukocyte infiltration, and neuronal apoptosis. The authors conclude artesunate plus FFR could be a valuable adjunct treatment for cerebral malaria, warranting further mechanistic study.

articles.headline: Cyclic-nucleotide-binding protein CBP-O drives Plasmodium ookinete invasion of mosquito
articles.title_original: A divergent cyclic nucleotide binding protein promotes Plasmodium ookinete infection of the mosquito.
articles.authors: Kwecka D, Wang Z, Eigminas E, Liu L, Regan JC, Kim C, Philip N
articles.details: This study characterized a previously cryptic cyclic nucleotide-binding protein, named CBP-O, in the malaria parasite's ookinete stage that colonizes mosquitoes. PbCBP-O localized markedly to the ookinete apex, and disrupting the protein severely compromised ookinete invasion of mosquitoes. Domain dissection showed the N- and C-termini have distinct functions, and PbCBP-O exhibited dual binding specificity to both cGMP and cAMP. The authors conclude the apical tip of the ookinete serves as a platform to transduce cyclic nucleotide signals essential for malaria parasite transmission.

articles.headline: Flavin-deficient red blood cells inhibit malaria parasite growth
articles.title_original: Flavin-deficient erythrocytes offer protection against malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Hemasa A, van Loon W, Fu J, Spry C, Jäger J, van Schalkwyk DA, Reverberi R, Ndoli J, Mockenhaupt FP, Contini C, Saliba KJ
articles.journal: Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A
articles.details: Testing a decades-old hypothesis, researchers used an erythrocyte FAD-dependent glutathione reductase activity assay to identify individuals with flavin-deficient erythrocytes (FDE) in Ferrara, Italy (23% of 150 screened) and Huye, Rwanda (13% of 169), none of whom had dietary riboflavin deficiency. FDE from Ferrara individuals, and erythrocytes depleted of flavins in vitro by riboflavin starvation, inhibited intraerythrocytic proliferation of Plasmodium falciparum, with evidence that these cells are susceptible to oxidative stress. Genetic analysis identified FAD synthase gene mutations in three FDE individuals from Huye. The authors conclude flavin-deficient erythrocytes offer protection against malaria parasites, consistent with a genetic basis.

articles.headline: Fc engineering boosts protection from malaria CSP antibodies in animal models
articles.title_original: Analysis of Fc-dependent effector functions of anti-malaria circumsporozoite protein antibodies.
articles.authors: Stefanutti E, Ramani R, Whitener B, Dang H, Bélanger S, Somasundaram L, Cortina K, De Marco A, Tam T, Chai Q, Cameroni E, Gupta R, Schmid MA, Miller JL, Zumsteg AB, Purcell LA, Drewry LL
articles.details: This laboratory study tested whether Fc-dependent immune effector functions contribute to protection by anti-circumsporozoite protein (CSP) monoclonal antibodies and whether Fc engineering can enhance them. Fc-dependent effector functions were required for maximal protection by prophylactic CSP mAb 317, and Fc engineering modulated activity across multiple in vitro effector assays. The mAbs L9 and CIS43 were stronger drivers of antibody-dependent phagocytosis, NK activation and killing, and complement deposition, whereas 317 (but not L9 or CIS43) drove enhanced activation of CSP-responsive T-cells after dendritic-cell antigen acquisition. The authors conclude effector function is an important anti-CSP mechanism enhanceable through Fc engineering.

articles.headline: Plant compound β-caryophyllene repels Thailand malaria-vector Anopheles mosquitoes
articles.title_original: COMPARATIVE BEHAVIORAL RESPONSES OF Β-CARYOPHYLLENE AGAINST ANOPHELES MOSQUITO SPECIES, POTENTIAL VECTORS OF MALARIA IN THAILAND.
articles.authors: Lhaosudto S, Sathantriphop S, Kongmee M, Karpkird T, Leepasert T, Ngoen-Klan R, Chareonviriyaphap T
articles.details: This excito-repellency assay found that β-caryophyllene, a nontoxic plant-derived compound, repelled four Anopheles malaria vector species, with An. minimus showing the highest escape rates (100%) at 2.5% (contact) and 5% (noncontact) concentrations. An. harrisoni also showed strong responses at 7.5% (94.34% contact, 72.73% noncontact), while An. epiroticus responded minimally (12-25.42%). Significant differences between contact and noncontact trials occurred only in An. minimus and An. harrisoni, and no knockdown or mortality occurred. Kaplan-Meier analysis showed faster escape under contact conditions and log-rank tests confirmed dose-dependent repellency, suggesting β-caryophyllene is a promising natural spatial repellent against malaria vectors.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence 21% in under-five febrile children, southern Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Malaria and Associated Factors among Under Five Febrile Children Attending Ener Amanuel Health Center, Southern Ethiopia: An Institution-based Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Tseha ST, Ebabu M, Aberar T
articles.details: This institution-based cross-sectional study enrolled 218 febrile children under five years attending Ener Amanuel Health Center in Enor-Ener Woreda, Southern Ethiopia, from December 2022 to April 2023, with malaria diagnosed by microscopy. Malaria prevalence was 21.1%, with P. falciparum predominant (60.9%). Factors significantly associated with Plasmodium infection were proximity to mosquito breeding sites (P=0.025), not sleeping under insecticide-treated nets (ITNs) (P=0.021), and having beds unsuitable for ITN hanging (P=0.002). The authors conclude malaria remains a significant public health concern and that strengthening ITN utilization and eliminating breeding sites are essential.

articles.headline: Stakeholder analysis of scaling up Malakit malaria intervention in Guiana Shield
articles.title_original: Assessing scalability factors of a malaria intervention using Malakit and target drug administration among hard-to-reach populations: An original approach focusing on key stakeholders - implementers and decision-makers - in the Guiana shield.
articles.authors: Carboni C, Sanna A, Lambert Y, Plessis L, Bardon T, Adenis A, Nacher M, Vreden S, Suárez-Mutis M, Douine M
articles.details: This pre-planning scalability assessment, conducted from June 2023 to March 2024, used a participatory approach to evaluate community-based malaria interventions—Malakit self-testing/self-treatment kits, Targeted Drug Administration against P. vivax, or their combination—among mobile, hard-to-reach goldmining populations in the Guiana Shield (Brazil, French Guiana, and Suriname). Methods included working sessions, semi-structured stakeholder interviews, and participant observation analyzed with the ExpandNet/WHO Framework, Mendelow's Matrix, and SWOT analysis. Stakeholders were categorized as implementers, decision-makers, beneficiaries, and partners. Strengths included Malakit's effectiveness; weaknesses included data gaps and regulatory challenges. The authors conclude financial and regulatory barriers can hinder scalability while political and epidemiological factors offer opportunities.

articles.headline: Malaria in hard-to-reach populations: challenges and solutions
articles.title_original: Malaria in hard-to-reach populations: Challenges and solutions.
articles.authors: Fouque F, Ravasi D, Masoudi GA, Rodriguez-Morales AJ
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Human diversity and mobility as key challenges for malaria control
articles.title_original: Biodiversity of malaria ecosystems, challenges for control: When human diversity is forgotten.
articles.authors: Fouque F, Boutsika K
articles.details: This perspective argues that the great biodiversity of malaria transmission contexts has shaped control efforts throughout human history. It notes that within the past 150 years malaria was eliminated from all temperate countries of the northern hemisphere, but a resurgence began about 50 years ago, affecting mostly sub-Saharan Africa, followed by a renewed decline in incidence and mortality after the 2000s. The authors conclude that access to health is now the main driver of the disease and that reaching hard-to-reach and mobile populations will be essential to sustain progress in malaria control.

articles.headline: Targeted interventions led to P. falciparum elimination in Suriname
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Suriname: how targeted interventions in marginalized populations led to elimination.
articles.authors: Hiwat-Van Laar H, Cairo H, Hardjopawiro L, Eersel M, Vreden S, Adhin M, Panchoe D, van Eer E, Jintie H, van Dijk M, Mesones Lapouble O
articles.details: This descriptive study analyzed national malaria surveillance data from 2000 to 2024, disaggregated by age, sex, and country of origin, to assess interventions contributing to Plasmodium falciparum (Pf) elimination in Suriname. Pf ceased to be the primary prevalent species in 2007, and the last indigenous Pf case was reported in 2018, as the disease shifted from indigenous and tribal communities in the Interior to mobile migrants in remote gold mining sites. The authors conclude that a targeted, inclusive strategy providing low-barrier access to care eliminated Pf, but continued importation from neighboring countries and reduced funding pose a re-introduction risk requiring regional Guiana Shield action.

articles.headline: Systematic review of village health workers in malaria elimination
articles.title_original: Role of village health worker in control activities for malaria elimination efforts: A systematic review.
articles.authors: Wangdi K, Wangchuk D, Alamnia T, Cao P, Kotepui M, Suwannatrai AT, Ugyel L, Vardoulakis S, Tsheten T
articles.details: This systematic review searched five life sciences databases plus Google from inception to October 2024 for studies on the role of village health workers (VHWs) in malaria elimination in hard-to-reach areas. Of 14,884 articles screened, 44 met inclusion criteria; 65.9% (29) were from Africa and the rest from Asia, and 37 were from hard-to-reach areas. VHWs were mostly involved in diagnosis and treatment of malaria (21/44), with smaller numbers addressing behaviour change communication, reactive case detection, insecticidal nets, seasonal malaria chemoprevention, and intermittent preventive treatment. The authors conclude VHWs were primarily engaged in routine control rather than elimination activities and should be integrated into elimination programs.

articles.headline: Vernonia amygdalina leaf extracts show in vitro activity against P. knowlesi
articles.title_original: Vernonia amygdalina (Delile) exhibits in vitro anti-plasmodial activities against Plasmodium knowlesi.
articles.authors: Zulkefli NZ, Cheong FW, Amir A, Lau YL
articles.details: This in vitro study tested crude dichloromethane (DCM), methanol (MeOH), and water (H2O) extracts of Vernonia amygdalina leaves against Plasmodium knowlesi using schizont maturation inhibition assays, with cytotoxicity assessed in Vero cells by MTT assay. IC50 values were 1.356 ± 0.23 μg/mL (DCM), 1.131 ± 0.16 μg/mL (MeOH), and 1.973 ± 0.30 μg/mL (H2O), while CC50 values were 48.61, 63.73, and 86.91 μg/mL respectively, indicating MeOH and H2O extracts were likely non-toxic to mammalian cells. All extracts showed Selectivity Index above 10, with MeOH highest, leading the authors to suggest the leaves are a potential candidate for antiplasmodial drug development pending isolation of active metabolites.

articles.headline: Costing pgSIT mosquito control facilities for West Africa
articles.title_original: Estimated cost and operational structure of pgSIT malaria vector control programs in selected West African countries.
articles.authors: Gendron WAC, Raban R, Mondal A, Sánchez C HM, Zilberman D, Ilboudo PGC, D'Alessandro U, Marshall JM, Akbari OS
articles.details: This modeling study optimized the facility size and cost of precision guided sterile insect technique (pgSIT) programs, which generate sterile male mosquitoes for mass release to suppress malaria. Building on prior estimates—shown to be highly cost-effective in a malaria endemic region of West Africa but based on only 15-31% capacity for sex sorting, the limiting and costliest production step—the authors calculated the cost per million Anopheles gambiae suppressed as facilities scaled up. They developed an optimized facility size per 9.2 million mosquitoes suppressed as a framework for scaling and improving cost effectiveness. The authors conclude the intervention could potentially interrupt malaria transmission while strengthening local public health institutions, manufacturing capacity, local jobs, and regional health security.

articles.headline: Severe falciparum malaria linked to low sodium, potassium in Sudanese children
articles.title_original: Exploring the Effect of Malaria on Biochemical Parameters Among Sudanese Patients with Complicated Falciparum Malaria.
articles.authors: Ali MO, Tamomh AG, Abdulrahman NA, Ibrahim NS, Eltayeb MA, Osman FI
articles.details: A case-control study of 400 participants (200 with severe falciparum malaria, 200 healthy controls) measured biochemical parameters using an automated analyzer. In infected children, plasma potassium and sodium were significantly decreased (p<0.05), while urea and creatinine were unchanged; in infected adults, plasma urea was significantly higher than controls (p<0.05) but creatinine, potassium, and sodium were unaffected. Urea correlated positively with sodium in children (r=0.322, p<0.001). Authors conclude malaria is a risk factor for hyponatremia and hypokalemia in children and azotemia in adults, with no age or sex effect.

articles.headline: Fatal falciparum malaria complicated by acute subdural hematoma
articles.title_original: A fatal case report of Plasmodium falciparum malaria complicated by acute subdural hematoma.
articles.authors: Xie N, Ye H, Jiang X
articles.details: This case report describes a male patient in his late 50s weighing 90 kg who developed fever, headache, and generalized myalgia 3 days after returning from roughly a month in Burkina Faso. Nine days elapsed between symptom onset and the diagnosis of severe falciparum malaria and start of artesunate treatment. Despite effective anti-P. falciparum therapy, the patient died of multiple organ failure complicated by an acute subdural hematoma.

articles.headline: Plant extract shows antimalarial, immune-modulating effects in infected mice
articles.title_original: Antiplasmodial and immunomodulatory activities of dichloromethane extract of Stachytarpheta jamaicensis in Plasmodium berghei infected mice.
articles.authors: Amaechi EC, Owolodun OA, Lambe KO
articles.details: This study tested dichloromethane leaf extract of Stachytarpheta jamaicensis in chloroquine-resistant Plasmodium berghei-infected mice across three doses (200, 400, 600 mg/kg) with distilled-water negative and dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine positive controls; parasitemia and survival were monitored for 30 days post-infection. Phytochemical screening detected only alkaloids. The extract showed significant antiplasmodial effect on day 11 (P<0.05), with the 200 mg/kg dose mediating parasite clearance and improved survival, associated with sustained TGF-β and modulation of pro- and anti-inflammatory cytokines (IFN-γ, TGF-β). Authors suggest it as a candidate for novel antimalarial and immunomodulatory development.

articles.headline: Crateva adansonii extracts kill Anopheles gambiae larvae and pupae
articles.title_original: Bioactive profiling, larvicidal and pupicidal activities of Crateva adansonii against two developmental stages of malaria vector, Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Ileke KD, Babatunde NA, Akeju AV
articles.details: This study evaluated ethanolic leaf, stem, and root extracts of Crateva adansonii against the malaria vector Anopheles gambiae, using GC-MS for bioactive profiling and bioassays at 1%, 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20% concentrations. Root extracts produced 100% larval mortality at 15% and 20% within 12–24 h, and 20% leaf extract gave 100% mortality in 6 h. The lowest root LC50 and LC90 against larvae were 0.04% and 8.07% at 24 h; leaf/bark extracts reached complete mortality at 20% in 6 h with lowest LC50 1.78 and LC90 14.75. Authors conclude the plant offers a strong eco-friendly insecticidal alternative.

articles.headline: Review of host immune response to P. falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Overview of the host immune response to P. falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Antwi-Baffour S, Mensah BT, Annison L
articles.details: This review summarizes the host immune response to malaria, describing innate defenses (macrophages, dendritic cells, natural killer cells, and inflammatory cytokines) and adaptive responses in which T cells destroy infected cells and B cells produce antibodies that neutralize the parasite, block its invasion of host cells, and aid clearance by phagocytes. It notes that protective immunity is complex and shaped by parasite diversity, host genetics, and prior exposure, with some individuals developing partial immunity while others remain susceptible to severe disease. The authors emphasize that understanding these mechanisms is essential for developing effective vaccines and therapies.

articles.headline: Most household members of SMC-covered children carry P. falciparum in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Patterns of patent and sub-patent Plasmodium falciparum infections in household members of children under seasonal malaria chemoprevention coverage in the health district of Nanoro, Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Kambou SAE, Sondo P, Kabore B, Bayala IMC, Kouevi AFC, Compaore EW, Millogo KS, Ismaïla B, Rouamba T, Kazienga A, Karim D, Rouamba E, Dahal P, Otienoburu SD, Raman J, Dhorda M, Bamba S, Guérin PJ, Tinto H
