articles.details: A cross-sectional survey nested in the SMC_RST study during the 2022 SMC campaign in Nanoro, Burkina Faso, assessed patent (varATS qPCR+/RDT+) and sub-patent (qPCR+/RDT-) Plasmodium falciparum infections in 745 household members living with children under seasonal malaria chemoprevention, with results available for 650 (87.2%). Overall infection prevalence was 68.6% (446/650), comprising 27.4% patent (178/650) and 41.2% sub-patent (268/650). Patent infections declined with age (37.7% in 5-14 years to 17.1% in ≥25 years), while sub-patent carriage predominated in those ≥15 years; no significant difference was seen across villages. The authors support extending SMC to school-aged children and testing/treating household members.

articles.headline: Indonesia's subnational progress toward 2030 malaria elimination, with Papua the key challenge
articles.title_original: Shrinking the malaria map in Indonesia: progress of subnational control, elimination, and future strategies.
articles.authors: Herdiana H, Prameswari HD, Puspadewi RT, Fajariyani SB, Diptyanusa A, Theodora M, Supriyanto D, Hawley WA
articles.details: This review analyzed published and unpublished reports and grey literature (1950-2023), largely from Indonesia's Ministry of Health, to describe the country's subnational malaria elimination verification process and progress toward nationwide elimination by 2030. Cases fell significantly by 2015 but rose during the COVID-19 pandemic; as of 2023, 389 of 514 districts and five of 38 provinces were verified free of local transmission, with fastest progress in western Indonesia. Cases are now concentrated in Papua, which reports over 90% of national cases, with lowland Papua incidence exceeding 400 per 1000 person-years. Zoonotic Plasmodium knowlesi infections are increasing, and the authors conclude new intervention combinations are needed, especially in high-endemic Papua.

articles.headline: Limited timely care-seeking and asymptomatic P. vivax in Myanmar migrants in Thailand
articles.title_original: Malaria care-seeking behaviours and infection prevalence among short-term Myanmar migrants in Thailand.
articles.authors: Aung PL, Sripoorote P, Inthitanon N, Wattanagoon Y, Hamers RL, Van Nuil JI, Cui L, Nguitragool W, Jones L, Sudewo AG, Sattabongkot J, Parker DM
articles.details: A community-based cross-sectional study in March-April 2025 surveyed 300 short-term Myanmar migrants across six malaria-endemic villages of Tha Song Yang District, Tak Province, western Thailand, using a structured questionnaire and PCR testing of dried blood spots. Over 60% recognized the need to seek care for fever or chills, but only 35% reported doing so within 24 hours, and although 98.0% preferred public facilities only 50.3% had ever visited one for suspected malaria. Higher care-seeking was associated with daily wage labor, infrequent return to Myanmar, prior malaria experience, and greater knowledge, while Karen ethnicity was negatively associated. Six (2%) afebrile P. vivax infections were detected; lower knowledge (OR=13.5) and care-seeking scores (OR=5.86) were linked to infection.

articles.headline: Health education boosted LLIN acceptance but not faster care-seeking in Brazil
articles.title_original: Contributions of health education and social mobilization in the implementation of a malaria control programme using long-lasting insecticidal nets.
articles.authors: da Costa FM, de Souza Rodrigues MM, Scarpassa VM
articles.details: From January 2010 to December 2012, intensive Health Education and Social Mobilization Strategies (HESMS), including mass distribution of 8,083 long-lasting insecticidal nets (LLINs), training, and monitoring, were implemented in 10 rural communities in Porto Velho, Rondônia, Brazil, near the Jirau Hydroelectric Power Plant. Local agent training yielded a 99.93% LLIN acceptance rate, and 92.13% of residents had sufficient nets, with 57.41% reporting consistent use. However, the intervention did not reduce delays in seeking malaria diagnosis within 24 hours of symptom onset; only Abunã and Imbaúba-Jirau villages reported consistent cases (pre-intervention monthly medians of 24 and 7), with 65% and 61% seeking treatment within 24 hours. The authors conclude HESMS aided LLIN acceptance but did not improve timely care-seeking.

articles.headline: Avian malaria parasite shifts gene expression from donor to recipient host
articles.title_original: Generalist Malaria Parasites and Host Imprinting: Unveiling Transcriptional Memory.
articles.authors: García-Longoria L, Palinauskas V, Aželytė J, Marzal A, Ovelleiro D, Hellgren O
articles.details: Using the avian malaria parasite Plasmodium homocircumflexum as a model, researchers experimentally inoculated it into different bird species (bypassing the vector) to test whether gene expression stays stable, resets to the recipient host, or reflects epigenetic inheritance. Expression differed significantly by host species and time post-infection, rejecting a universal profile; it was primarily donor-influenced at 8 days post-infection but more recipient-aligned at 16 days. Supporting epigenetic inheritance, 2,647 differentially expressed genes were associated with the donor at 8 dpi versus only 271 linked to the recipient at 16 dpi, while SNP analyses showed low genetic differentiation, rejecting genetic selection. The authors conclude the parasite shifts from donor-dependent to recipient-dependent expression via epigenetic regulation and transcriptional plasticity.

articles.headline: Congenital P. falciparum malaria in infant born to Angola-exposed mother
articles.title_original: Imported Malaria and Congenital Acquisition in Infant, Portugal, 2024.
articles.authors: Lopes I, Dias J, Das Neves E, Morais M, Santos-Reis A, Garcia AM, Varandas L, Lopes D
articles.details: Case report of a 3-month-old infant in Portugal diagnosed with Plasmodium falciparum infection by optical microscopy. The mother had had malaria in Angola 13 months earlier, before emigrating to Portugal, and remained asymptomatic throughout and after pregnancy. The authors confirmed an imported malaria case with congenital transmission using molecular techniques.

articles.headline: Author response on nosocomial P. falciparum malaria transmission in Spain
articles.title_original: Nosocomial Transmission of Plasmodium falciparum Malaria, Spain, 2024 (Response).
articles.authors: Romero MFL, de Pipaón MRP, Amuedo MDN, Rubio Muñoz JM, Jiménez-Hoyuela JM, Cisneros JM
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Nosocomial transmission of P. falciparum malaria reported in Spain
articles.title_original: Nosocomial Transmission of Plasmodium falciparum Malaria, Spain, 2024.
articles.authors: Gómez Perales JL, Mendoza AG, Amares MTG
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Review of diagnosing and managing imported malaria in children
articles.title_original: Diagnosis and Management of Imported Malaria in the Emergency Department.
articles.authors: McQueen A, Mannheim J
articles.details: A review summarizing the diagnosis and management of children with imported malaria presenting to nonendemic countries amid increasing global migration. It emphasizes key features of the travel history, diagnostic challenges, and management strategies in the acute setting. No quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: CYP2D6 poor metabolizers common among Venezuelan malaria patients on primaquine
articles.title_original: CYP2D6 Genotype and Primaquine Treatment in Patients with Malaria, Venezuela.
articles.authors: Pacheco C, Hernández-Acosta A, Pulido N, Ceballos Y, Saavedra D, Gómez C, Moreno N, Herrera F
articles.details: Study determining CYP2D6*4 and CYP2D6* genotypes and metabolizer phenotypes in 96 patients with suspected malaria in Venezuela. Intermediate or poor metabolizer phenotypes were found in approximately 25% of cases, and 9 of 44 malaria patients had Plasmodium vivax recurrence. The authors suggest public health authorities evaluate the benefits of increasing total primaquine treatment doses.

articles.headline: P. vivax microgametes pose diagnostic challenge in Colombian malaria case
articles.title_original: Plasmodium vivax microgametes in a Colombian malaria case: A diagnostic challenge.
articles.authors: Rodríguez Lugo DA, Morcillo Muñoz JA, Medina-Lozano LJ, Faccini-Martínez ÁA
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Low-cost eave screening cuts mosquito bites alongside bed nets in Cameroon
articles.title_original: A low-cost house eave screening technique complements insecticide-treated nets in reducing indoor human exposure to the bites of anopheles mosquitoes in a malaria endemic area of south forested Cameroon: results from a pilot study.
articles.authors: Metitsi Tesongang RD, Elanga-Ndille E, Cheteug Nguetsa G, Awono-Ambene HP, Fomena A, Tabue R, Ndo C
articles.details: This pilot study in a forested area of Cameroon tested screening open house eaves with wire insect mesh to reduce exposure to Anopheles mosquitoes, comparing improved houses with neighbouring unscreened control houses across pre-, intervention, and post-intervention phases. Baseline malaria prevalence was 38.85% and the entomological inoculation rate (EIR) was 73 infected bites per person per year; Anopheles gambiae s.l. was the main vector, susceptible to pirimiphos-methyl but resistant to bendiocarb and pyrethroids, and only pyrethroid+PBO ITNs were effective. ITN possession was 89.62% and use 79%. The intervention produced a 5.5-fold EIR reduction in improved houses (37.70 to 6.90 ib/p/y), cost $23.6 per house, and was well received, supporting integration with other vector control strategies.

articles.headline: Adding non-pyrethroid spraying or behaviour change to nets cuts malaria 22-23%
articles.title_original: Efficacy of non-pyrethroid indoor residual spraying or intensive behaviour change communication in combination with long-lasting insecticidal nets for malaria control in west Africa: a pragmatic, cluster-randomised, controlled trial.
articles.authors: Moiroux N, Zongo I, Assi SB, Some A, Tchiekoi NB, Zogo B, Soma DD, Hien DFDS, Taconet P, Ahoua Alou LP, Lefevre T, Cohuet A, Fournet F, Mouline K, Koffi AA, Dabiré RK, Pennetier C
articles.details: This pragmatic, parallel-group, cluster-randomised controlled trial in Burkina Faso and Côte d'Ivoire assigned 39 villages (10,750 inhabitants) to LLIN alone (16 villages, control), LLIN plus non-pyrethroid indoor residual spraying with pirimiphos-methyl (11 villages), or LLIN plus intensive behaviour change communication (BCC; 12 villages), with data collected 10 months before and 10 months after randomisation. Among 3612 malaria cases by passive case detection, the post-intervention period showed a 23% reduction in malaria incidence in the LLIN plus IRS group (rate ratio 0.77, 95% CI 0.64-0.93, p=0.0073) and a 22% reduction in the LLIN plus BCC group (0.78, 0.63-0.96, p=0.020) versus control. No IRS-related adverse effects occurred. Authors conclude adding non-pyrethroid IRS or intensive BCC to LLINs can effectively reduce malaria cases, with the BCC result promising but needing confirmation.

articles.headline: Statistical framework gauges malaria surveillance strength in five eliminating countries
articles.title_original: Evidence-based decision making for malaria elimination applying the Freedom From Infection statistical framework in five malaria eliminating countries: an observational study.
articles.authors: Stresman G, Nelli L, Wu L, Byrne I, Surendra H, Fernandez-Camacho B, Ruiz-Cabrejos J, Arana LB, Gonçalves AALM, Monteiro DDSR, Desir L, Ureña K, Tejada Beato MJ, Dumont E, Hill M, Grignard L, Elechosa S, Bunagan R, Xuan Thang N, Huong Binh NT, Hong Ngoc NT, Tetteh KKA, Noland GS, Hamre KES, da Veiga Leal S, DePina A, Thang N, Espino FE, Carrasco-Escobar G, Matthiopoulos J, Drakeley C
articles.details: This study extended the Freedom From Infection (FFI) framework to produce species-specific estimates of surveillance system sensitivity and probability of freedom from malaria, applied across five malaria-eliminating settings. Monthly routine data on Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax from 1515 facilities, plus data from 12 community health workers and 10,767 individuals from cross-sectional surveys (active case detection), were analysed. Strong systems featured access to testing and treatment supplies, diagnostic and case-management training within the previous 12 months, and shorter travel times; only about half of facilities (841 of 1515 for P. falciparum and 771 of 1455 for P. vivax) had sufficient sensitivity to achieve and maintain a high probability of freedom. The authors conclude the FFI framework supports species-specific decision making, and adding community case management and active case detection can improve estimates where routine data alone are insufficient.

articles.headline: Can behaviour change communication improve malaria control?
articles.title_original: Can behaviour change communication improve malaria control?
articles.authors: Sherrard-Smith E, Ngufor C
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Review argues robust surveillance is essential for malaria elimination
articles.title_original: The road to malaria elimination: why a robust surveillance system is imperative.
articles.authors: Reithinger R
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Computational screening finds new inhibitors of resistant P. falciparum DHFR
articles.title_original: Integrated computational strategies for the identification of Plasmodium falciparum dihydrofolate reductase inhibitors.
articles.authors: Gogoi D, Dutta PP, Gogoi B, Baruah VJ, Paul R, Sarmah B, Baruah A, Kakoti BB, Kalita B, Dutta P, Yadav RNS
articles.details: Researchers used pharmacophore modeling-assisted virtual screening (model r = 0.94, built from 17 compounds) plus molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulations, and in vitro testing to find novel inhibitors of mutant Plasmodium falciparum dihydrofolate reductase (pfDHFR), the target of antifolates such as pyrimethamine and cycloguanil. Screening with drug-likeness and ADME/Tox filters identified 28 compounds with strong binding to resistant pfDHFR variants. The top compound showed IC50 values of 30.822 μg/mL against the 3D7 strain and 126.077 μg/mL against the Dd2 strain, significantly outperforming other compounds (p < 0.05). The authors conclude that combining pharmacophore modeling with experimental methods can help discover antifolate-resistance-circumventing antimalarials.

articles.headline: Cationic lipid stearylamine kills chloroquine-sensitive and -resistant P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Cationic Lipid Stearylamine Displays Fast-Acting Antimalarial Action Against In Vitro Blood Stages of Chloroquine Susceptible and Resistant Plasmodium falciparum Strains.
articles.authors: Rajendran V, Gurukkalot K, Naveen NC
articles.details: This in vitro study evaluated the cationic lipid stearylamine (SA) against blood-stage Plasmodium falciparum using a SYBR-Green I assay in drug-sensitive (Pf3D7) and chloroquine-resistant (PfINDO) strains. SA was fast-acting and cytostatic, with first-cycle IC50 values of 2.17, 1.97, and 1.33 µg/mL (Pf3D7) and 3.50, 2.76, and 1.94 µg/mL (PfINDO) at 12, 24, and 48 h, and sustained activity at 72 h and 96 h; short exposures (1–6 h) gave an average IC50 of 1.68 µg/mL, indicating irreversible growth arrest. SA induced reactive oxygen species at IC50 concentrations with minimal hemolysis. The authors suggest incorporating SA into drug-delivery systems to reduce toxicity, enhance efficacy, and slow resistance.

articles.headline: Commentary marks 21 years of the BioMalPar malaria research program
articles.title_original: BioMalPar at 21 - a coming of age for malaria research and interventions.
articles.authors: Aniweh Y, Dziekan J, Jore MM, Kho S, Mancio-Silva L, Merrick CJ, Nahrendorf W, Ralph SA, Vaughan AM, Verhoef JMJ
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Naphthoquinone-triazole hybrids outperform atovaquone against resistant malaria
articles.title_original: A dual In vitro and In silico approach to evaluate 1,4-naphthoquinone-1,2,3-triazole hybrids against atovaquone-resistant malaria.
articles.authors: Agarwal P, N'Da DD, van der Westhuizen CJ, van Zyl RL
articles.details: This combined in vitro and in silico study evaluated ten 1,4-naphthoquinone-1,2,3-triazole hybrids against atovaquone-resistant (FCR3) Plasmodium falciparum, which resists atovaquone via cytochrome b mutation in the cytochrome bc1 complex. Molecular modelling used a Saccharomyces cerevisiae structure (PDB 3CX5) with a built mutant model simulating the Y279S mutation (equivalent to Y268S in P. falciparum), alongside predicted pharmacokinetics and drug-likeness. Hybrids D12 and D13 showed strong antiplasmodial activity, 61- and 52-fold greater than atovaquone respectively, with in silico binding correlating with in vitro results and predicted good oral bioavailability and high gastrointestinal absorption without significant severe toxicity. The authors identify D12 and D13 as lead candidates warranting further optimisation.

articles.headline: PCR misses mixed P. falciparum/P. vivax malaria in returning siblings
articles.title_original: Polymerase chain reaction fails to detect mixed malaria infections in siblings from Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Hall AD, Whitehurst DA, Mortensen JE, Prus K, Staat MA, Schlaudecker EP, Prasanphanich NS
articles.details: Case report of two pediatric siblings who had recently returned from Ethiopia, where P. falciparum and P. vivax are co-endemic. PCR initially failed to detect P. vivax; recurrent symptoms eventually led to diagnosis of P. falciparum/P. vivax co-infection confirmed by both PCR and blood smear. The authors conclude that current diagnostics may miss mixed infections, causing delayed or incomplete treatment, and urge clinicians to maintain high suspicion for malaria in patients returning from co-endemic regions even after prolonged periods without re-exposure.

articles.headline: Proposed two-tier strategy to protect Africa's malaria control gains
articles.title_original: Safeguarding malaria control gains in Africa through 'species sanitation' and structural resilience.
articles.authors: Okumu F, Odero JO, Charlwood JD, Knols BGJ, Monroe A
articles.details: This perspective proposes a layered strategy for African endemic countries to sustain malaria control beyond short-term commodity-based approaches. The authors recommend, first, an expanded 'species sanitation' that targets ecological vulnerabilities of the few highly efficient Anopheles vectors to suppress or eliminate them, and second, gradually building structural resilience through improved housing and environmental management. They argue future innovations such as gene-drive mosquitoes and longer-lasting vaccines could amplify impact, and that success depends on human-centred policies, increased domestic funding, cross-sector partnerships, resilient health systems, and planning beyond the usual 5-year cycles.

articles.headline: Travel blogs give inconsistent, often inappropriate malaria prevention advice
articles.title_original: Content analysis of malaria-related health information published in online travel blogs.
articles.authors: Din S, Bishay R, Asad U, Flaherty GT
articles.details: Content analysis of online travel blogs identified via Google search and analysed across 35 variables to characterise travellers' malaria information. Of 100 blogs retrieved, 92 contained malaria-related information; ten different drugs were mentioned, five of which were not appropriate for prevention of falciparum malaria. Pre-travel consultation was recommended by almost a third of blogs, and most blogs were supported by commercial sponsors linked to the travel industry or travel health products. The authors conclude the travel medicine community should produce reliable lay-traveller material to share with prominent bloggers.

articles.headline: Intermediate Amazon forest cover linked to higher malaria transmission risk
articles.title_original: Intermediate forest cover and malaria risk in an Amazon deforestation frontier.
articles.authors: Laporta GZ, Valle D, Prist PR, Ilacqua RC, Santos TC, Madeira FP, Silva GM, Brilhante AF, Nolan MS, Sperança MA
articles.details: A pilot natural experiment across 40 sites in a deforestation frontier in Acre state, Brazilian Amazon, examined how landscape structure influences malaria transmission, collecting mosquito vector abundance/infection data and human malaria infections. Sites with intermediate forest cover (often highly fragmented) showed higher Nyssorhynchus mosquito abundance and greater anopheline infection rates, increasing risk of P. vivax and P. falciparum infection. All detected human infections were asymptomatic (n=16), forming a persistent transmission reservoir. The authors suggest elimination strategies should combine radical cure treatment with alternative vector control and landscape-based approaches such as forest conservation/restoration.

articles.headline: Case series: spontaneous splenic rupture after malaria
articles.title_original: Case series of spontaneous splenic rupture in patients with malaria: A rare surgical emergency in non-traumatic cases.
articles.authors: Tola GG, Hemba YG, Hussen SK, Matiop MP
articles.details: Case series of four patients with spontaneous splenic rupture, all with a recent history of malaria treatment. One was a previously healthy 30-year-old male from a malaria-endemic region in Ethiopia who presented with acute left upper quadrant pain, dizziness, and massive hemoperitoneum requiring emergency splenectomy; the other three were hemodynamically stable and managed conservatively with monitoring, supportive care, and serial imaging, none requiring surgery during follow-up. The authors attribute rupture to vascular congestion, reticuloendothelial hyperplasia, and a fragile splenic capsule, stressing early recognition and imaging, with splenectomy reserved for uncontrolled bleeding.

articles.headline: Perspective: how drug resistance emerged across HIV, TB, and malaria
articles.title_original: A timeline of reckoning: Tracking the historical rise of antimicrobial resistance across HIV, TB, and malaria.
articles.authors: Williamson T, Adderley J, Quinn K, Clark TG, Lewin SR, Doerig C
articles.details: This perspective reviews the historical timelines between drug deployment and the emergence of antimicrobial resistance for HIV, tuberculosis, and malaria. The authors report that malaria develops resistance to monotherapies more slowly than HIV and TB. They note that combination therapies markedly reduce the risk of de novo resistance emergence, but pre-existing drug resistance remains a challenge, underscoring the need for continuous surveillance and diverse, innovative approaches to manage antimicrobial resistance.

articles.headline: Gene drive spreads protective FREP1 allele to block mosquito malaria infection
articles.title_original: Driving a protective allele of the mosquito FREP1 gene to combat malaria.
articles.authors: Li Z, Dong Y, You L, Corder RM, Arzobal J, Yeun A, Yang L, Marshall JM, Dimopoulos G, Bier E
articles.details: Researchers generated congenic strains of Anopheles stephensi mosquitoes edited to carry either the parasite-susceptible FREP1L224 allele or the putative-refractory FREP1Q224 allele. The FREP1Q224 allele conferred robust resistance to infection by both human and rodent malaria parasites with negligible fitness costs. Using a novel linked allelic-drive system that selectively replaces the L224 codon with the Q224 allele, the protective allele could be efficiently driven into mosquito populations, rendering them refractory to parasite infection. The authors present this as a new genetic approach to aid malaria elimination.

articles.headline: Dual-dye LAMP assay detects Plasmodium knowlesi with 100% sensitivity
articles.title_original: Establishment of a visual mixed-dye loop mediated isothermal amplification assay for detecting Plasmodium knowlesi.
articles.authors: Lai MY, Lau YL
articles.details: This study developed a visual dual-colour loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) assay for detecting Plasmodium knowlesi, using two dyes (phenol red and hydroxynaphthol blue) to produce a clearer, higher-contrast colour change than single-dye LAMP for naked-eye observation. The assay was tested on 55 clinical samples using nested PCR as the reference method. It showed 100% sensitivity (95% CI: 91.9-100%) and 100% specificity (95% CI: 78.2-100%). The authors conclude it is a rapid, cost-effective, user-friendly diagnostic tool suited to resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Computational screening finds MAA compounds as falcipain-2 inhibitors
articles.title_original: Screening mycosporine-like amino acids (MAAs) as potential inhibitors of a malaria-associated cysteine protease falcipain-2.
articles.authors: Singh VK, Sahu N, Kesheri M, Kanchan S, Kumar A, Sinha RP
articles.details: This in-silico study screened a library of MAAs as potential inhibitors of falcipain-2 (FP-2), a cysteine protease haemoglobinase of Plasmodium falciparum, using E-64 as a reference inhibitor. Molecular docking on 27 compounds, followed by ADME and oral toxicity evaluation, identified seven compounds with favorable properties and no toxicity, showing favorable binding and high stability (per RMSD, RMSF, and RoG plots). The top two hits by drug score, shinorine and mycosporine-hydroxyglutamicol, were selected for simulation studies. The authors conclude MAAs possess significant antimalarial activity by impeding the enzyme's active site.

articles.headline: RDT versus smear microscopy for malaria diagnosis, all cases P. vivax
articles.title_original: Comparative evaluation of rapid diagnostic test and peripheral smear microscopy for malaria diagnosis.
articles.authors: Bhise M, Anandani G, Goswami P, Padhi A, Agarwal A
articles.details: This retrospective study in India compared rapid diagnostic tests (RDT) and peripheral blood smear (PBS) microscopy in 150 suspected malaria cases. PBS confirmed 30 positives (20%) and RDT detected 20 positives (13.33%), all identified as Plasmodium vivax. PBS detected both P. vivax and P. falciparum, whereas RDT failed to identify P. falciparum but detected two P. vivax cases missed by PBS. The authors conclude RDT cannot fully replace PBS microscopy but serves as a useful complementary diagnostic tool.

articles.headline: Transmission-blocking bacterium Su_YN1 harmless to South American malaria vector
articles.title_original: Compatibility of Serratia ureylitica Su_YN1, Malaria Transmission-Blocking Bacterium, with the Anopheles aquasalis Vector.
articles.authors: Ferreira MADS, Martins ESDR, Katak RM, Campos KMM, Rocha EM, Roque RA, Lalwani PJ, Silva LA, Carmo EJD, de Andrade PP, Wang S, Moreira LA, Jacobs-Lorena M, Ríos-Velásquez CM
articles.details: This study evaluated whether Serratia ureilytica Su_YN1, a bacterium that strongly impairs P. falciparum and P. berghei development in Anopheles mosquitoes, is compatible with the South American vector Anopheles aquasalis. The researchers assessed effects on mosquito fitness and survival. They found the bacterium did not affect longevity, blood feeding, fecundity, or fertility of A. aquasalis. The authors conclude these results provide baseline support for potentially implementing Su_YN1 to control malaria transmission in South America.

articles.headline: Congenital malaria with P. falciparum and P. vivax coinfection in neonate
articles.title_original: Congenital malaria in a neonate born in a malaria-endemic area: a case report.
articles.authors: Andarge BD, Almaw K
articles.details: This case report describes congenital malaria in a 48-hour-old male neonate born to a 23-year-old Ethiopian primigravida who had been treated for uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria with artemether-lumefantrine (Coartem) one week before delivery. The neonate presented with respiratory distress and persistent fever, initially managed as early-onset neonatal sepsis. Blood film microscopy confirmed P. falciparum and P. vivax coinfection at 1120 parasites/μL, while the asymptomatic mother tested negative by RDT and microscopy. The neonate was successfully treated with intravenous artesunate followed by oral artemisinin-lumefantrine with complete recovery. The authors emphasize considering congenital malaria in neonates and strengthening antenatal care.

articles.headline: Pregnancy with falciparum malaria, stillbirth, and amoebic liver abscess managed
articles.title_original: Management of a Pregnant Woman With Malaria, Stillbirth, and a 12 × 11-cm Amoebic Liver Abscess in a Burundian Hospital: A Case Report.
articles.authors: Castaldi MA, Castaldi SG
articles.details: This case report describes a pregnant woman in a rural hospital in Burundi with falciparum malaria and a hepatic amoebic abscess, complicated by fetal death in utero at 22 weeks' gestation. The authors state this is the first reported coexistence of these two parasitic infections, which require completely different treatments, during pregnancy. Maternal mortality was averted, and the authors attribute successful management to staff skills, inventiveness, and advances in ultrasonographic technology.

articles.headline: Dolutegravir lowers lumefantrine levels in HIV-malaria coinfection, Ghana
articles.title_original: Drug-drug interaction between dolutegravir and artemether-lumefantrine in HIV and malaria mono- and co-infections: a pharmacogenetic analysis from Ghana.
articles.authors: Thomford NE, Kellermann T, Twum JA, Anyimadu J, Dixon C, Sappor D, Blackhurst D, Barnie PA, Ryabinina O, Nyarko SB, Biney RP, Ekor M, Kyei GB
articles.details: This pharmacogenetic study of 261 participants in the Central region of Ghana investigated drug-drug interactions between artemether-lumefantrine and dolutegravir (DTG) in HIV and malaria mono- and coinfections, with plasma drug concentrations measured by LC-MS/MS and genotyping of CYP2B6, CYP3A4, CYP3A5, and UGT1A. Median parasitaemia was 947.34 parasites/µL for malaria monoinfection and 5287.36 parasites/µL for HIV-malaria coinfection on day 1, falling to 0 by days 3 and 7. Day 7 lumefantrine was 741.5 ng/mL in monoinfection versus 426.0 ng/mL in coinfection, and DTG concentrations also decreased in coinfection. The authors warn that resulting sub-optimal levels could affect clinical outcomes.

articles.headline: Bibliometric analysis maps global HIV-malaria interaction research trends
articles.title_original: Unraveling the HIV-malaria interactions: a bibliometric analysis of global research trends and emerging insights.
articles.authors: Zhang H, Chen G, Ma P, Lai Y
articles.details: This bibliometric and knowledge-graph study analyzed 4,717 articles on HIV-malaria interactions published between 1995 and 2024 from the Web of Science Core Collection using CiteSpace software. Publications showed a fluctuating upward trend, with the United States, the University of London, and Grant Dorsey having the greatest publication counts among countries, institutions, and authors respectively. Identified research hotspots included epidemiology in African populations, co-infection pathogenic mechanisms, prevention and treatment strategies, antimalarial-antiretroviral drug interactions, and malaria vaccine responses in HIV-infected individuals. The authors call for future work on pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic drug interactions and malaria vaccines for HIV-positive populations.

articles.headline: Acute kidney injury common and deadly in severe malaria patients
articles.title_original: Prevalence, Associated Factors, and Prognosis of Acute Kidney Injury in Severe Malaria Among Sub-Saharan Africans.
articles.authors: Mbengue M, Cheikh Gaye A, Tshabayembi J, Fall S, Sall I, Diouf M, Keita N, Gaye I, Seydi M, Fary Ka EH, Cristiano F, Niang A
articles.details: This multicenter, retrospective study over a 5-year period in nephrology, infectious diseases, and intensive care departments of Dakar hospitals included 321 patients with severe malaria (defined by WHO criteria), 244 men and 77 women, mean age 36.25 years. Acute kidney injury (KDIGO criteria) was present in 158 patients, a prevalence of 49.2%; anuria occurred in 64.3% and oliguria in 26.8%, and 19 patients underwent hemodialysis. Death occurred in 41 patients (12.77%). In multivariate analysis, AKI was independently associated with death (OR = 3.584; 95% CI 1.467-8.754; p = 0.004), and associated factors included herbal medicine, diabetes, respiratory distress, and anemia. The authors conclude AKI is common and independently increases mortality risk.

articles.headline: Artemether mildly better than quinine for childhood cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Comparison of Artemether Versus Quinine for the Treatment of Cerebral Malaria in Children: A Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials.
articles.authors: Saeed H, Tahir D, Khan MM, Faheem MT, Malik AH, Saeed S, Azeem S, Syed M, Allami A
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 7 randomized controlled trials (databases searched to February 2023) compared artemether versus quinine for cerebral malaria, caused typically by P. falciparum, in children. It pooled 1,225 patients: 618 receiving artemether and 607 receiving quinine. Parasite clearance time was significantly shorter with artemether (mean difference -7.63; 95% CI -11.06 to -4.21; P < 0.0001), but fever clearance time, coma clearance time, and mortality showed no statistically significant differences. The authors conclude artemether had mildly superior efficacy; artemisinin-sensitive regions should continue artemisinin use, while artemisinin-resistant regions may consider quinine as an alternative.

articles.headline: P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein highly polymorphic in Congolese children
articles.title_original: Genetic polymorphism of circumsporozoite protein of Plasmodium falciparum isolates in children in Brazzaville, Republic of Congo.
articles.authors: Baina MT, Djontu JC, Ntabi JDM, Lissom A, Eouasse AGK, Elenga VA, Mapanguy CCM, Mouanga AM, Ntoumi F
articles.details: Sequencing the full-length Pfcsp gene from 202 children infected with P. falciparum in the Republic of Congo (a cross-sectional study from March to October 2021, using nested PCR and the Oxford Nanopore platform) revealed extensive polymorphism at sites relevant to the RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M vaccines. The N-terminal region contained 30 haplotypes (A98G most frequent at 25.6%; KLKQP most conserved), the central repeat region 50 haplotypes dominated by NANP, and the C-terminal region was highly polymorphic with 76 haplotypes among 174 sequenced samples. In the C-terminal Th2R region the K317E (87.4%) and N321K (83.9%) mutations predominated, and in the Th3R region E357Q (75.9%) was most common. The authors present these as baseline data on Pfcsp diversity at vaccine-targeted epitopes.

articles.headline: Scoping review maps climate change effects on African malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Impact of climate change on malaria transmission in Africa: A scoping review of literature.
articles.authors: Chapoterera B, Naidoo K, Marume A
articles.details: This systematic review of 90 studies published between January 2010 and December 2024 (searched across PubMed, Scopus, African Index Medicus, and Embase, with thematic analysis) found that climate change strongly shapes malaria transmission in Africa. Even slight temperature increases significantly accelerate mosquito vector and parasite development, while changes in rainfall—whether excessive or diminished—affect mosquito breeding sites and cause fluctuations in malaria incidence, with regional variation underscoring the need for localised interventions. The authors conclude that climate factors are crucial to malaria prevalence and distribution, that traditional interventions such as chemical spraying may become less effective, and that integrating ecological data and strengthening climate-responsive policies will aid control and elimination.

articles.headline: Garlic and neem extracts kill malaria mosquito larvae in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Insecticidal Activities of Plants Extract Against Malaria Vectors in Hadiya Zone, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Assemie A, Mulu D, Getahun A, Awoke N, Muluken W, Enyew T, Gebremariam A, Aynalem Y
articles.details: This study evaluated the insecticidal activity of ethanol extracts of Azadirachta indica (neem) and Allium sativum (garlic) against malaria vector larvae in Hadiya zone, Ethiopia. From 20 g of each plant, extracts were tested on fourth-instar larvae at 50, 100, 150, 200, and 250 ppm, with LC50 determined by probit analysis and significance by chi-square. Phytochemicals including alkaloids, saponin, tannin, phenol, anthraquinones, flavonoids, glycosides, steroids, and terpenes were detected. The highest mortality (90.66%) occurred with A. sativum at 250 ppm. A. sativum significantly affected Anopheles gambiae s.l. (X²=13.6, p=0.0087) and Anopheles pharoensis (X²=11.002, p=0.0266), while A. indica significantly affected only An. pharoensis (X²=14.26, p=0.0065). A. sativum had the lowest LC50 (39 ppm) and was more toxic than A. indica.

articles.headline: Chromatin state dynamics across P. falciparum blood-stage cycle
articles.title_original: Chromatin state dynamics during the Plasmodium falciparum intraerythrocytic development cycle.
articles.authors: Brown AS Jr, Llinás M, Mahony S
articles.details: This study mapped chromatin states across the 48-hour asexual intraerythrocytic developmental cycle (IDC) of Plasmodium falciparum, integrating genome-wide profiles of chromatin accessibility, histone modifications, and Heterochromatin Protein 1 (HP1) occupancy. Most genes are expressed periodically during the IDC alongside dynamic shifts in histone marks and accessibility. Some chromatin states stayed stable while others were dynamic and tied to gene activation or repression. By examining DNA-binding sites of selected transcription factors, the authors grouped TFs by chromatin preference, providing a global overview of how chromatin state dynamics coordinate asexual blood-stage development.

articles.headline: Bed nets plus IPTp sharply cut malaria in pregnant women
articles.title_original: Do women attending antenatal clinics who use only intermittent preventive treatment (IPTp) have higher Plasmodium falciparum prevalence compared to those who used both IPTp and slept under insecticide-treated nets?
articles.authors: Madukwe JC, Ahaneku EB, Onukafor OS, Edokpayi S, Madukwe PC, Enwereji NU, Santos EM
articles.details: A cross-sectional study of 143 pregnant women across three hospitals in southern and southeastern Nigeria compared Plasmodium falciparum infection (by rapid diagnostic test) between those using intermittent preventive treatment (IPTp) alone versus IPTp plus insecticide-treated nets (ITNs). Overall, P. falciparum was detected in 65 (45%) participants; prevalence was 61.2% among IPTp-only users versus 17.0% among those using both. After adjusting for gestational week, income, and education, IPTp alone gave no significant protection, while IPTp plus ITN was associated with 86% reduced odds of infection. The authors conclude the protection is largely driven by ITN use.

articles.headline: PfMYST epigenetics drives artemisinin ring-stage survival
articles.title_original: Epigenetically conferred ring-stage survival in Plasmodium falciparum against artemisinin treatment.
articles.authors: Yu X, He J, Wang C, Mu J, Chen X, Zhao Y, He X, Liu S, Sa JM, Platon L, Tang J, Si W, Tang R, Menard D, Wellems TE, Jiang C, Cao J, Zhang Q
articles.details: Researchers systematically evaluated all putative histone acetyltransferases (HATs) of Plasmodium falciparum in 30 culture-adapted and 43 subcloned field isolates to find epigenetic modulators of artemisinin (ART) ring-stage survival (RS), a feature of artemisinin partial resistance linked to PfK13 mutations. Only PfMYST showed full association with RS modulation, confirmed by knockdown experiments showing altered metabolic processes. Using single-cell RNA-seq, ChIP-seq, and CRISPR/Cas9, the authors identified and validated PfMYST-targeted RS-related genes and found significant interplay between PfMYST and PfK13. PfMYST epigenetic modulation also extended to amodiaquine, pyrimethamine, chloroquine, and pyronaridine.

articles.headline: New PI4K inhibitor kills all malaria stages, low resistance
articles.title_original: Novel Inhibitors of Plasmodium Phosphatidylinositol 4-kinase IIIβ with Low Propensity for Resistance: Life Cycle Stage Activity and In Vivo Efficacy in a Humanized Mouse Malaria Infection Model.
articles.authors: Dziwornu GA, Mmonwa MM, Coertzen D, Krugmann L, Salomane N, Leshabane M, Thomas J, da Rocha S, Reader J, Masike K, Njoroge M, Sevilleno N, Coyle R, Boonyalai N, Mayville E, Lee MCS, Fidock DA, Coulson LB, Woodland JG, Wicht KJ, Ghorpade SR, Birkholtz LM, Chibale K
articles.details: This study presents a novel antimalarial chemotype derived from the anticancer ATM kinase inhibitor AZD0156 that inhibits phosphatidylinositol 4-kinase IIIβ (PI4K) in Plasmodium. The compounds showed activity against all stages of the P. falciparum life cycle, with lower propensity for resistance and toxicity than previous Plasmodium PI4K inhibitors. The lead compound 18 was efficacious in a humanized NOD-scid IL-2Rγnull mouse model of P. falciparum malaria, achieving an ED90 of 4.6 mg/kg.

articles.headline: Plasmodium ARK1 kinase controls atypical mitosis, a drug target
articles.title_original: Plasmodium ARK1 regulates spindle formation during atypical mitosis and forms a divergent chromosomal passenger complex.
articles.authors: Nagar A, Yanase R, Zeeshan M, Ferguson DJP, Abel S, Pashley SL, Mishra A, Eze A, Rea E, Brady D, Bottrill AR, Vaughan S, Le Roch KG, Guttery DS, Holder AA, Tromer EC, Sharma P, Tewari R
articles.details: This study identifies Aurora-related kinase 1 (ARK1) as a key regulator of spindle formation and kinetochore dynamics during the atypical mitosis of Plasmodium, which lacks canonical regulators such as Polo and Bub1 kinases. Conditional ARK1 depletion disrupted spindle biogenesis, kinetochore segregation, karyokinesis, and cytokinesis in both schizogony and male gametogony, and impaired parasite transmission. Interactome analysis revealed ARK1 as the catalytic core of a divergent chromosomal passenger complex (CPC) containing two divergent INCENPs but lacking Survivin and Borealin. The authors propose the ARK1–INCENP interface as a potential multistage target for malaria therapy.

articles.headline: Salacia debilis compounds inhibit P. falciparum PfENR enzyme in silico
articles.title_original: Computational elucidation of Plasmodium falciparum enoyl-ACP reductase inhibition by Salacia debilis compounds: adaptive ligand binding drives antimalarial activity.
articles.authors: Manu P, Padiga Seidu M, Adomako A, Asamoah CK, Duodu O, Britwum A, Laryea MK, Darko G, Borquaye LS
articles.details: This computational study screened three Salacia debilis isolates—SD03 (benzyl 2-methoxybenzoate), SD04 (1,10-dihydroxy-6H-benzo[c]chromen-6-one), and SD05 (8-hydroxy-3,4-dimethoxydibenzo[b,d]furan-1-carboxylic acid)—using high-throughput screening and molecular docking. P. falciparum enoyl-ACP reductase (PfENR), part of the parasite's type II fatty acid biosynthesis pathway absent in humans, was identified as the primary target, with binding affinities of -8.50 to -8.90 kcal/mol. Extended 500 ns molecular dynamics simulations confirmed stable complexes engaging key residues Tyr111, Tyr267, and Leu315, mirroring the benchmark inhibitor triclosan. SD04 emerged as the lead candidate based on favorable pharmacokinetic predictions, validating PfENR as an antimalarial target.

articles.headline: Factors linked to HIV infection in young people, Mozambique 2015 survey
articles.title_original: Factors associated with HIV infection among adolescents and young people in Mozambique: sub-analysis of immunization, malaria and HIV/AIDS indicator survey in Mozambique-IMASIDA 2015.
articles.authors: Chicanequisso EM, Sabonete A, Sacarlal J, Rossetto ÉV, Cassimo MN, Baltazar CS
articles.details: This sub-analysis of the 2015 Immunization, Malaria and HIV/AIDS Indicators Survey assessed factors associated with HIV infection among 4,247 participants aged 15-24 years in Mozambique, using sex-stratified multiple logistic regression. Overall, 295/4,247 (6.9%) were HIV-infected (females, 237/2,427 [9.8%]). In males, being a widower was associated with infection (AOR=52.69, 95%CI 2.07-1340.33, p=0.02). In females, factors included being aged 20-24 (AOR=2.06), widowed (AOR=15.59), first cohabitation below 18 years (AOR=1.71), residing in Zambézia (AOR=3.52), and being wealthier (AOR=2.20); higher education was protective (AOR=0.07). Authors call for strategies targeting girls and young women.

articles.headline: Dietary L-DOPA boosts mosquito melanization, cutting lifespan and malaria burden
articles.title_original: Dietary L-3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine (L-DOPA) augments cuticular melanization in Anopheles mosquitos reducing their lifespan and malaria burden.
articles.authors: Camacho E, Dong Y, Chrissian C, Cordero RJB, Saraiva RG, Anglero-Rodriguez Y, Smith DFQ, Jacobs E, Hartshorn I, Patiño-Medina JA, DePasquale M, Dziedzic A, Jedlicka A, Smith B, Mlambo G, Tripathi A, Broderick NA, Stark RE, Dimopoulos G, Casadevall A
articles.details: This study found that dietary L-DOPA, a naturally occurring tyrosine derivative present in mosquito habitats, is incorporated into mosquito melanin via a non-canonical pathway and has a profound transcriptional effect on mosquito physiology and immunity. The changes were associated with enhanced immunity to Plasmodium falciparum and Cryptococcus neoformans infection, increased pigmentation, and reduced lifespan; greater melanization also raised the mosquitoes' capacity to absorb electromagnetic radiation, affecting their temperature. Bacteria in the mosquito microbiome acted as sources of dopamine, a substrate for melanization. The authors suggest L-DOPA could serve as an environmentally friendly vector control agent to reduce malaria transmission, warranting further research and field studies.

articles.headline: Anopheles distribution and resurgence risk in malaria-free Paraguay
articles.title_original: Unravelling Anopheles Dynamics in a Malaria-Free Paraguay: Species Distributions, Bioclimatic Niches, and Implications for Resurgence Risks.
articles.authors: Del Puerto F, Grissetti M, Ferreira L, Franco L, Herrera L
articles.details: This study re-evaluated Anopheles presence data and modeled environmentally suitable areas using DIVA-GIS/MaxEnt software for malaria surveillance in Paraguay, certified malaria-free by WHO in 2018. Earlier 2016-2017 surveys in Caaguazú and Alto Paraná found the most prevalent species—Anopheles albitarsis (59.4%), Anopheles strodei (21.5%), and others—all negative for Plasmodium. Bioclimatic suitability covered the Humid Chaco, Cerrado, Paraná Atlantic Forest, and Southern Cone Mesopotamian savanna ecoregions, with precipitation of the wettest month (80.4%) and driest month (18.4%) as most relevant variables. Anopheles darlingi, the main local vector, was not found, though species richness indices suggest possible cryptic species.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence and diagnostics in Mampikony District, northern Madagascar
articles.title_original: Epidemiology of Malaria in Northern Madagascar as Exemplified by the Mampikony District.
articles.authors: Kołodziej D, Wilczyńska W, Kasprowicz D, Marchelek-Myśliwiec M, Korzeniewski K
articles.details: This study assessed malaria prevalence and diagnostic effectiveness among 782 residents who reported to healthcare centres in Mampikony District, northern Madagascar, between 2023 and 2024, using rapid diagnostic tests (mRDTs) and RT-PCR. In 2023 (484 patients), prevalence was 4.5% by mRDT and 8.2% by RT-PCR; in 2024 (298 patients), it rose to 8.4% by mRDT and 12.4% by RT-PCR. Malaria was more prevalent in women and adults, though differences between groups were not statistically significant. Cases were predominantly P. falciparum, with some P. vivax and mixed infections. Authors recommend more sensitive diagnostics like RT-PCR and updated treatment strategies given growing asymptomatic carriage.

articles.headline: Simplified malaria control model preserves full model's transmission dynamics
articles.title_original: Model reduction and analysis: A case study of a malaria control model.
articles.authors: Korsah MA, Johnston ST, Tiedje K, Day K, Walker CR, Flegg JA
articles.details: This methods study applied a reduction technique to a comprehensive malaria control mathematical model to derive a simplified system retaining the essential dynamics. The authors validated the reduced model against the full model using Bayesian MCMC, alongside a simulation study, parameter identifiability analysis, and sensitivity analysis. They report the reduced system exhibits similar transmission characteristics to the full model while offering greater flexibility and computational efficiency, concluding it is a useful tool for evaluating and implementing malaria control strategies.

articles.headline: Malaria parasites evolve increased virulence in vaccinated hosts beyond target antigens
articles.title_original: Understanding the traits underlying vaccine-driven virulence evolution in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Jeong Y, Kamiya T, Mideo N
articles.details: Using a hierarchical Bayesian framework, researchers fitted a within-host malaria infection dynamics model to experimental time-series data from mice inoculated with rodent malaria parasites that had evolved in either vaccinated or sham-vaccinated control mice. Vaccine-evolved parasites elicited lower targeted immune killing and reduced anemia-driven erythropoiesis, at both treatment-mean and individual levels. Early-infection parasite multiplication rates did not differ across treatments, arguing against evolution of the vaccine target antigen AMA-1. The authors conclude that parasite traits beyond target antigens, including modulation of host responses, can evolve under vaccination and influence disease severity.

articles.headline: Neutrophil NETs drive cerebral malaria via TLR9/NF-κB dendritic cell activation
articles.title_original: TLR9/NF-κB-mediated dendritic cell activation by neutrophil extracellular traps drives pathogenesis in experimental cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Yao S, Zhao Y, Yao C, Li Q, Sun M, Wang Q, Zheng L, Cao Y
articles.details: In a Plasmodium berghei ANKA (PbA) murine cerebral malaria model, rapid neutrophil mobilization occurred after infection. Neutrophil depletion protected mice from neuropathology, reduced brain CD8+ T cell infiltration and activation, and attenuated splenic dendritic cell (DC) and parasite-specific T cell responses; among TLR4, TLR7, and TLR9, only DC TLR9 expression fell after depletion. Neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) activated bone marrow-derived DCs via the TLR9/NF-κB pathway through their DNA component (abolished by DNase I), promoting CD8+ T cell activation TLR9-dependently. Inhibiting NETs with Sivelestat impeded cerebral malaria onset and progression. The authors propose a NETs–TLR9/NF-κB–DC–CD8+ T cell axis in pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Yemen's malaria crisis and implications for GCC countries
articles.title_original: Yemen's Malaria Crisis and its Implications for the GCC (Gulf Cooperation Council) Countries.
articles.authors: Ebrahim SH, Al-Maslamani M, Al-Hosani F, Al-Abri S, Alqahtani M, Alahmad B, Bland S, Memish Z
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Broadly inhibitory anti-PfEMP1 antibodies from malaria-exposed child block EPCR binding
articles.title_original: Identification of broadly inhibitory anti-PfEMP1 antibodies by mass spectrometry sequencing of plasma IgG from a malaria-exposed child.
articles.authors: Turner L, Nunez de Villavicencio Diaz T, Raghavan SSR, Kana IH, Lyimo E, Reitzel C, Wang CW, Berube E, Jensen RW, Loeffler JR, Fernández-Quintero ML, Theander TG, Lusingu JPA, Le Bihan T, Han X, Minja DTR, Ward AB, Ma B, Lavstsen T
articles.journal: Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A
articles.details: Researchers combined mass spectrometry analysis of antigen-purified polyclonal plasma IgG with Ig transcript sequencing to identify human monoclonal antibodies targeting the CIDRα1 domains of P. falciparum PfEMP1, which bind endothelial protein C receptor (EPCR) and drive severe malaria. They identified a clonal antibody family from a 9-year-old child that potently and broadly inhibited EPCR binding across CIDRα1 domains and blocked infected-erythrocyte binding to EPCR. Structural analysis of one variant (110-3) showed an epitope overlapping the EPCR binding site and those of prior antibodies C7 and C74; however, 110-3 also contacts variable residues, reducing its breadth. The findings support that broadly inhibitory antibodies target similar conserved epitopes and arise commonly in malaria-exposed individuals.

articles.headline: Plasma microRNAs may flag cerebral malaria early in Indian patients
articles.title_original: Potential of microRNAs as diagnostic markers for distinguishing malaria severity in samples from an Indian cohort.
articles.authors: Gupta A, Arora K, Bhandari S, Sikka R, Wassmer SC, Bharti PK, Gupta H
articles.details: Biomarker study quantifying plasma levels of six microRNAs by TaqMan-RT-qPCR in patients with cerebral malaria (CM; n=43), severe non-CM (n=50), uncomplicated malaria (n=79), asymptomatic malaria (n=80), and non-malarial febrile illness (n=69). hsa-miR-21-5p, hsa-miR-150-5p, and hsa-miR-3158-3p correlated with severe malaria and CM (p<0.0005, study power >80%). A random forest model combining the three microRNA levels predicted CM on admission with 83% sensitivity, 100% specificity, and 92% balanced accuracy. Authors conclude these microRNAs may offer a non-invasive approach for early CM diagnosis.

articles.headline: Model finds multiclonal infections drive multidrug-resistant malaria recombination
articles.title_original: Effects of recombination on multi-drug resistance evolution in Plasmodium falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Tran KT, Nguyen TD, Weissman DB, Li EZ, Mok S, Small-Saunders JL, Bousema T, Zupko RJ, Tran TN, Boni MF
articles.details: Modeling study using an individual-based P. falciparum transmission model extended with 25 drug-resistance loci, individual mosquito bites, and interrupted feeds (mosquitoes feeding on multiple hosts) to study recombination. For prevalence >5%, 80% to 97% of multidrug-resistant (MDR) recombinant genotypes arose from single uninterrupted bites on hosts with multi-clonal infections rather than interrupted feeds. Comparing management strategies over 15 years, triple artemisinin-combination therapy (ACT) gave the largest reductions in treatment failures and the longest delay until artemisinin resistance reached a 1% threshold; multiple first-line therapies were second best and ACT cycling third.

articles.headline: Review of airport, seaport, and locally acquired malaria in Europe
articles.title_original: Airport/seaport and autochthonous malaria in Europe from 1969 to 2022: A systematic review.
articles.authors: Balzli D, Gültekin N, Stanga Z, Ülgür I, Hedrich N, Fehr J, Schlagenhauf P
articles.details: PRISMA-guided systematic review (PROSPERO CRD42023444243) of PubMed, Ovid MEDLINE, EMBASE, Scopus, and CINAHL on airport/seaport and autochthonous malaria in Europe. It included 68 papers describing 115 cases: 68 airport/seaport cases and 47 autochthonous cases. France, Germany, Italy, and Spain reported both types. Case fatality rates were 13.24% for airport/seaport malaria and 2.13% for autochthonous malaria. Authors attribute the high fatality to delayed or missed diagnosis and warn that rising temperatures, migration, and travel may facilitate competent Anopheles vectors and reemergence.

articles.headline: Nearly 60% of pregnant women at a Somali hospital had malaria
articles.title_original: The Prevalence of Malaria Among Pregnant Women at a Tertiary Care Hospital in Somalia: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Mohamud SM, Doğan S, Hussein AI, Mohamud RYH, Orhan Z, Doğaner A
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 398 pregnant women attending the obstetric clinic of a tertiary hospital in Mogadishu, screened by Giemsa-stained blood smear microscopy. 238 (59.8%) tested positive for malaria, of which 218 (91.6%) were P. falciparum. Being in the second trimester (OR 0.524, 95% CI 0.279-0.983; p=0.044), third trimester (OR 0.442, 95% CI 0.245-0.797; p=0.007), and indoor residual spraying (OR 0.192, 95% CI 0.108-0.342; p<0.001) were associated with decreased odds of malaria. Authors recommend targeted education, preventive measures, and routine antenatal malaria screening.

articles.headline: Live imaging maps vesicle release and membrane rupture in malaria egress
articles.title_original: Spatiotemporal dynamics of signal dependent exocytosis and parasitophorous vacuolar membrane rupture during Plasmodium falciparum egress.
articles.authors: Dedkhad W, Tran T, Fierro MA, Brooks C, Muralidharan V
articles.details: Cell biology study using a superecliptic pHluorin (SEP) exocytosis reporter and a parasitophorous vacuolar membrane (PVM) fluorescent reporter with time-lapse imaging of live P. falciparum-infected red blood cells to track signal-dependent exocytosis during egress. Exocytosis of egress-specific vesicles was triggered as early as 3 hours before merozoite egress; the PVM ruptured at a single site and rapidly expanded, followed by RBC membrane rupture and merozoite release. Using conditional mutants, knockdown of the endoplasmic reticulum calcium-binding protein PfERC inhibited exocytosis, showing PfERC is required for release of egress-specific vesicles.

articles.headline: Review of PEXEL and non-PEXEL protein export in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: An overview of the interconnectedness of the PEXEL and non-PEXEL mediated protein export in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Das S
articles.details: This review summarizes current understanding of how Plasmodium falciparum exports parasite-derived proteins into infected red blood cells. It describes the conserved pentameric PEXEL (Plasmodium Export Element) motif and the well-studied PEXEL-based export pathway, alongside PEXEL-negative exported proteins (PNEPs) that lack the motif yet follow a similar trafficking route. The authors discuss the structural diversity and functions of discovered PNEPs and outline remaining open questions about export mechanisms in P. falciparum and related apicomplexans.

articles.headline: Malaria–dengue coinfection in febrile children in western Kenya
articles.title_original: Correlation and epidemiologic distribution of emerging coinfections of Plasmodium falciparum and dengue virus among febrile children in malaria-endemic zones in western Kenya.
articles.authors: Ogony J, Mangeni J, Ayodo G, Menya D, Akinyi I, Oyugi B, Yongo A, Okumu F, Lwanga C, Oluoch F, Karanja S
articles.details: This prospective cohort study screened 1004 participants in Kisumu (urban) and Busia (peri-urban/rural) Counties, western Kenya, recruiting 380 febrile children under five who were seropositive for Plasmodium falciparum and/or dengue virus. The overall disease burden was 37.8% (380/1004), with prevalences of 21.4% (215/1004) for P. falciparum, 8.9% (90/1004) for dengue, and 7.5% (75/1004) for coinfection. Busia had the highest P. falciparum-only infections (31%, 118/380) and Kisumu the highest dengue-only (16.6%, 63/380), and disease distribution was associated with location (X²=19.45, P<0.001), with significant county differences for dengue (P=0.038) and malaria (P<0.001). Authors conclude dengue is actively spreading in this malaria-endemic region and urge clinician awareness of differential diagnoses.

articles.headline: Low malaria knowledge among rural Ethiopian households
articles.title_original: Malaria signs, symptoms, prevention knowledge and its associated factors among rural Ethiopians.
articles.authors: Kuti KA, Zuma SM
articles.journal: Afr J Prim Health Care Fam Med
articles.details: This community-based cross-sectional study surveyed 634 randomly selected adults (≥18 years, mostly household heads) across three malaria-endemic rural districts in the Bale Zone, Ethiopia, using a pre-tested structured questionnaire analyzed in SPSS version 28. Less than half (44.2%) showed good overall understanding of malaria signs, symptoms, and prevention; some wrongly attributed transmission to sun exposure, lack of rest, or alcohol. Commonly recognized symptoms included fever, headache, and uncoordinated speech, and known preventive measures included eliminating breeding sites, insecticide-treated nets, and indoor residual spraying. Education, religion, marital status, family size, and presence of children and pregnant women were associated with better knowledge. Authors conclude malaria-related knowledge is low and call for knowledge-transfer strategies.

articles.headline: Socioeconomic inequality in malaria prevention uptake in Ghana
articles.title_original: Between-and within-socioeconomic groups temporal inequality in the uptake of malaria prevention strategies among pregnant women and under-five children in Ghana (2003-2022).
articles.authors: Okova D, Lukwa AT, Edusei M, Bodzo P, Atta-Obeng C, Chiwire P, Hongoro C
articles.details: This study analyzed nationally representative Ghana Demographic and Health Survey data (2003, 2008, 2014, 2022) on insecticide-treated net (ITN) use among pregnant women and children under five and intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp) uptake, using the Erreygers normalized concentration index (ENCI) and Theil index decomposition. ITN use among under-fives rose modestly from 58.1% (2003) to 62.9% (2022), while pregnant women's use returned to 60% in 2022; IPTp coverage rose from 0.85% (2003) to 60% (2022). ITN use was consistently pro-poor (ENCI for pregnant women -0.04 in 2003 to -0.32 in 2022), whereas IPTp became pro-rich from 2008. Most inequality was within-group (85% of ITN inequality among pregnant women in 2022). Authors urge equity-focused, locally targeted interventions.

articles.headline: Diabetes and metabolic syndrome linked to severe malaria in adults
articles.title_original: Diabetes and metabolic syndrome in adults with malaria and associations with severe disease: results from two tertiary hospitals in Cameroon.
articles.authors: Awungafac G, Njonnou SRS, Noubom M, Libon LIAA, Gesu CN, Färnert A, Choukem SP, Wyss K
articles.details: This cross-sectional study with prospective inclusion enrolled adults (≥21 years) diagnosed with malaria at two tertiary public hospitals in Bafoussam and Dschang, West Region, Cameroon, assessing diabetes, obesity, and metabolic syndrome (Met-S) and using multivariable logistic regression for associations with severe malaria (WHO criteria). Among 289 adults with P. falciparum malaria, 120 (41.6%) met at least one severe-malaria criterion. Diabetes occurred in 26/120 (21.7%) severe versus 10/169 (6.0%) non-severe cases (p<0.001), and Met-S in 39/120 (32.5%) versus 27/166 (16.3%) (p=0.001); obesity was similar between groups. In multivariable analysis, diabetes (aOR=3.24; 95%CI 1.38–7.62) and Met-S (aOR=3.18; 95%CI 1.60–6.37) were independently associated with severe malaria. Authors recommend diabetes investigation in adults with severe malaria.

articles.headline: Anemia more common in HIV-malaria coinfected adults on HAART
articles.title_original: Hematological Profiles of Adults Coinfected With HIV and Malaria Receiving Highly Active Antiretroviral Therapy at Bonga Gebretsadik Shawo General Hospital, Southwest Ethiopia: A Comparative Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Demango F, Tadasa E, Kiya GT
articles.details: A hospital-based comparative cross-sectional study of 196 HIV-infected adults on highly active antiretroviral therapy (98 HIV-only and 98 HIV-malaria coinfected) compared hematological profiles. Red blood cells, hemoglobin, and hematocrit differed significantly between groups (p<0.05), and viral load correlated negatively with multiple blood parameters. Overall, anemia, leukopenia, and thrombocytopenia were present in 88 (44.9%), 77 (39.3%), and 50 (25.5%) participants. Among coinfected participants, prevalence was 60 (61.2%), 43 (43.88%), and 30 (30.6%), with anemia significantly higher in coinfected than HIV-only participants (p<0.001).

articles.headline: Phase-based holographic imaging improves malaria-infected red cell detection
articles.title_original: Enhanced detection of malaria infected red blood cells through phase driven classification.
articles.authors: Kyeremah C, Paul AS, Haehn D, Duraisingh MT, Yelleswarapu CS
articles.details: This study tested a multi-feature classification approach using lensless inline digital holographic microscopy (DHM) to detect Plasmodium falciparum-infected red blood cells. Phase-based features were more reliable than morphological features and outperformed single-feature methods. The parasitemia detection rate improved from 48% (feature-specific method) to 61% (phase-based features method) on the same sample set, with high specificity (98-100%). The method slightly underestimated parasitemia compared to Giemsa staining (90%) but offers a real-time, label-free tool for rapid automated malaria diagnosis.

articles.headline: LLIN monitoring shows low net use in Dominican Republic malaria foci
articles.title_original: Insights from LLIN post-distribution monitoring surveys in the malaria transmission foci of the Dominican Republic: implications for quantification and distribution strategies.
articles.authors: Ventura G, Félix MYS, Bueno NT, Ströfer NM, Raposo JLC, Solís Á, Parra RGB, Montoya LF
articles.details: A cross-sectional household survey monitored long-lasting insecticidal net (LLIN) coverage, access, use, and condition in two malaria foci of the Dominican Republic: Azua (4-6 months post-distribution) and San Juan (one year post-distribution). LLIN coverage of sleeping spaces was 64% (Azua) and 63% (San Juan), with reported retention of 88.4% and 80.9%. Access was 58.8% and 65.4%; overall LLIN use was only 32.3% (Azua) and 50.5% (San Juan). Most nets remained serviceable but were washed with aggressive products and dried in the sun. Authors call for behavioural change strategies and revised quantification methods.

articles.headline: Aspartyl protease inhibitor blocks P. falciparum blood stage and early gametocytes
articles.title_original: Aspartyl protease inhibition interferes with Plasmodium falciparum asexual blood-stage and early gametocyte development.
articles.authors: Niramolyanun G, Praikongkatham C, Jenwithisuk R, Roobsoong W, Sattabongkot J, Pankao V, Kangwanrangsan N
articles.details: This in vitro study used pepstatin A, a broad-spectrum aspartyl protease inhibitor, against different developmental stages of Plasmodium falciparum (asexual blood-stage, early gametocytes, late gametocytes, and gamete), counting intact parasites microscopically to calculate percent inhibition. At 100 µM, pepstatin A inhibited asexual-stage and early-stage gametocyte development by 47% and 73%, respectively, causing chromatin condensation, vacuolization, and haemozoin clumping. It did not affect late-stage gametocyte development or gamete formation. Authors conclude aspartyl protease inhibition moderately affects both stages and warrants further development as a dual-action drug target.

articles.headline: Irrigated urban farming drives malaria transmission in Accra, Ghana
articles.title_original: Urban malaria in Accra, Ghana: transmission risk and influence of irrigated vegetable farms.
articles.authors: Sabtiu ARM, Sraku IK, Owusu-Asenso CM, Akuamoah-Boateng Y, Doe RT, Boadu EN, Azumah JD, Eyeson NAS, Abdulai A, Amoateng G, Attah SK, Aboagye-Antwi F, Afrane YA
articles.details: This entomological study in Accra found that irrigated urban farming drives urban malaria transmission, with the highest sporozoite infectivity occurring indoors. Across ten sites grouped by irrigated urban farming (IUF), socioeconomic status, and peri-urban setting, 41,747 mosquitoes were collected by human landing catch and Prokopack aspiration with molecular detection; 95.49% were host-seeking, of which Anopheles made up 52.54%. IUF sites had the highest An. gambiae s.l. abundance (48.63%, 10,466/21,520), and indoor An. gambiae s.l. showed higher sporozoite infectivity (4.56%, 16/351) than outdoor (3.78%, 9/238; P=0.009), with the Tuba irrigated site highest (6.41%, 10/156). The authors recommend targeted indoor control and management of immature vector habitats in IUF areas.

articles.headline: Facilitators and barriers to community malaria case management in Kenya
articles.title_original: Facilitators and barriers of Community Case management of Malaria implementation in Homabay, Busia and Kakamega Counties, Kenya.
articles.authors: Lumumba S, Luciani A, Sifuma B, Kinyua D, Gikunda G, Ogutu N, Onyango J, Mukami D, Kiilu C, Kassim S, Opanga Y, Githuka G
articles.details: A cross-sectional mixed-methods study in Busia, Homabay, and Kakamega Counties assessed community case management of malaria (CCMm) delivered by community health volunteers (CHVs), using a household questionnaire of 528 participants, 4 focus group discussions, and 20 key informant interviews. 72% of households had received a malaria service (Busia 75%, Homabay 72%, Kakamega 71%), 62% considered CHVs a regular source of healthcare, and about 85% were satisfied with CHV services. Facilitators included availability of malaria commodities, a functional referral system, and support supervision; barriers included myths about mosquito nets, stockouts of mRDT kits and antimalarials, and inaccessible roads. The authors conclude continuous CHV training, sustained commodities, and incentives are essential.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence 14.6% in pregnant women in Dschang, Cameroon
articles.title_original: Epidemiology of malaria in pregnant women attending antenatal consultation in Dschang, West Cameroon.
articles.authors: Ebai CB, Ngoufo FN, Teh RN, Kodjo JTS, Muyang EJ, Kimbi HK
articles.details: A hospital-based cross-sectional study in two health facilities in Dschang, Western Cameroon examined malaria parasitaemia and associated factors among pregnant women using questionnaires and thick/thin blood smears. Of 314 participants, 46 (14.6%) were positive, all with Plasmodium falciparum. Bushes around houses increased infection risk (OR=2.40, p=0.03), while a ceiling (OR=0.20, p<0.01), IPTp uptake (OR=0.23, p<0.01), and window screens (OR=0.14, p=0.01) were protective. Geometric mean parasite density was highest in the second trimester (2190/µL, p=0.016), those with more than three gravidities (1022/µL, p=0.009), those with sweating (1946/µL, p=0.004), and, unexpectedly, those using insecticide-treated nets (1536/µL, p<0.001). Authors call for continuous sensitisation on prevention.

articles.headline: Indigofera oblongifolia eases Plasmodium chabaudi lung inflammation in mice
articles.title_original: Morphometric analysis and immunobiological investigation of Indigofera oblongifolia on the infected lung with Plasmodium chabaudi.
articles.authors: Murshed M, Al-Tamimi J, Ebaid H, Al-Quraishy S
articles.details: This mouse study evaluated Indigofera oblongifolia leaf extract (IOLE) with silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) versus chloroquine (CQ) 10 mg/kg for lung inflammation from Plasmodium chabaudi infection. Fifty female C57BL/6 mice were divided into five groups (control, IOLE-AgNPs, infected, infected+IOLE-AgNPs, infected+CQ), with lung histopathology and immunohistochemistry for TNF-α and IL-6. IOLE-AgNPs significantly restored normal lung tissue, reducing alveolar/septal congestion, edema, and necrosis and lowering cytokine levels, showing antimalarial and antioxidant effects. CQ gave only moderate histological improvement, with persistent congestion, edema, necrosis, and proinflammatory cytokine expression, which the authors attribute to drug resistance. They conclude I. oblongifolia is a promising alternative for malaria-related lung pathology.

articles.headline: Drone eDNA plus portable qPCR detects Plasmodium in Amazon canopy
articles.title_original: Real-time malaria detection in the Amazon rainforest via drone-collected eDNA and portable qPCR.
articles.authors: Ip YCA, Montemartini L, Chang JJM, Desiderato A, Franco-Sierra ND, Geckeler C, Herrera MAG, Gregorini M, Jucker M, Kirchgeorg S, Lüthi M, Mächler E, Thostrup FB, Murari G, Mura M, Pulido-Santacruz P, Sangermano F, Schindler T, Melvad C, Mintchev S, Deiner K
articles.details: This proof-of-concept study coupled drone-based environmental DNA (eDNA) canopy swabbing with portable qPCR to detect Plasmodium DNA during a 24-hour field exercise in the Amazon rainforest. Drone-lowered sterile swabs were extracted and run on a multiplex pan-Plasmodium assay targeting five human-infecting species (limit of detection 0.2 parasites/µL). Of 12 samples (10 canopy swabs, 2 field blanks; 13 total runs), one canopy swab amplified in duplicate (Ct=28.7 and 29.23), positive controls amplified (Ct=30.82 and 31.11), and all other samples and blanks were negative. Acoustics confirmed co-occurring howler monkeys (Alouatta spp.), a known reservoir, while no Anopheles were trapped. The workflow averaged 1.5 h per assay without cold chain, supporting landscape-level, One Health surveillance.

articles.headline: Night watchmen and outdoor activity raise malaria risk in Zanzibar
articles.title_original: Occupations at high risk for malaria in Zanzibar: a case-control study, may-august 2023.
articles.authors: Makenga G, Gallalee S, Mkali H, Khamis MI, Ramadhan A, Ali MH, Hassan WS, Hassan J, Makwaruzi S, Mgata S, Gulaka M, Govella NJ, Molteni F, Kitojo C, Reaves E, Ballard SB, Serbantez N, Ikonje A, Afenu M, Mkude S, Smith JL, Mohamed S, Shija SJ, Gueye CS, Gosling R
articles.details: A case-control study in Zanzibar identified malaria high-risk populations across two urban districts (Mjini, Magharibi B) and two rural districts (Kati, Micheweni), recruiting 197 symptomatic cases and 557 controls frequency-matched by age and sex, using logistic regression for locally acquired infection (no travel outside Zanzibar in the prior 3 weeks). In urban districts, night watchmen/police (OR 5.3, 95% CI 2.7–10.6, p<0.001), construction workers (OR 3.0, 95% CI 1.8–5.0, p=0.007), and farmers (OR 1.6, 95% CI 1.1–2.2, p=0.01) had higher odds, as did night-time activities, meals outside, and travel within Zanzibar. In rural districts, outdoor night-time activities (OR 3.8, p=0.006) and outdoor meals (OR 2.7, p=0.03) were risks; net use was protective only in rural districts (p=0.015). Authors recommend interventions tailored to occupational groups.

articles.headline: African P. falciparum found in invasive An. stephensi during outbreak
articles.title_original: Genetic surveillance of Plasmodium-Anopheles compatibility markers during Anopheles stephensi associated malaria outbreak.
articles.authors: Waymire E, Getachew D, Gunarathna I, Spear J, Lloyd G, Follis M, Kaye AA, Ali S, Yared S, Carter TE
articles.details: This genetic surveillance study analyzed Plasmodium P47 and mosquito P47Rec compatibility markers in Anopheles stephensi linked to a malaria outbreak in Dire Dawa, Ethiopia (2022), using PCR and Sanger sequencing. Of 160 mosquitoes screened, 6.21% were positive for P. falciparum DNA and 4.37% for P. vivax DNA. Pfs47 SNPs at positions 707 and 725 showed only the African haplotype, and minimum spanning network analysis connected Dire Dawa sequences to African strains, indicating African origin; Pv47 showed shared African and Asian haplotypes. The authors identified a single P47Rec amino acid change as a potential molecular marker for outbreak propensity and suggest An. stephensi contributed to the outbreak.

articles.headline: Temperature and nutrition stress alter An. stephensi fitness and malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Effects of temperature and nutrition stress on Anopheles stephensi, an Indian urban malaria vector.
articles.authors: Ghosh C, Kumar N, Mogaveerthi S, Lamba S, Ramanjini CK, Joshi SG, Chandran P, Kumar S, Swain S
articles.details: This transgenerational laboratory study exposed Anopheles stephensi to low temperature (4 °C eggs; 18 °C larvae–adults), high temperature (35.5 °C), and low nutrition (33 mg/100 larvae) over 8 generations, measuring life-history traits, vectorial competence with in vitro cultured P. falciparum, and ovarian polytene chromosomes. Fecundity, hatchability, developmental time, longevity, morphometrics, and pupal mass decreased in high-temperature and low-nutrition lines but increased in the low-temperature line; egg-to-adult development took ~7.5, ~11, and ~25 days in HT, NT, and LT lines. P. falciparum oocyst infection rates rose 1.46-fold (HT), 1.28-fold (LT), and 1.15-fold (NT) versus control, and paracentric inversions appeared at two 3L sites in the NT line. The authors conclude these stressors affect life cycle, vectorial competence, and adaptation.

articles.headline: DHIS2 malaria data poorly matches facility records in eastern Uganda
articles.title_original: Concordance of data on key malaria indicators between DHIS2 and source documents, and influencing factors at public primary health facilities in eastern Uganda: a mixed methods study.
articles.authors: Nuwasiima S, Mpimbaza A, Muteebwa L, Nagawa E, Arinaitwe E, Kiberu F, Ejalu DL, Mugerwa J, Batte C, Mukisa J, Agaba B, Mukunya D, Kalyango JN, Kamya MR, Nankabirwa JI
articles.details: This parallel convergent mixed-methods study assessed concordance between DHIS2 weekly reports and facility source documents on key malaria indicators at 12 public health facilities in Mayuge district, Uganda, reviewing 2021–2022 data with in-depth interviews and modified Poisson regression. Concordance was 36.7% (95% CI 25.2–49.9) for suspected malaria cases, 53.6% for cases tested, 55.3% for test-positive cases, and 56.8% for Test Positivity Rate—all below the WHO ≥80% standard. Presence of HMIS personnel (aPR 1.41), timely reporting (aPR 1.15), and RDT stockouts (aPR 0.55) were significantly associated with concordance. The authors conclude trained data staff and timely reporting improve data quality, and electronic data capture may reduce errors.

articles.headline: Temperature-dependent age-structured PDE malaria model with optimal control
articles.title_original: Age-structured malaria model with temperature-dependent dynamics and optimal control analysis within a partial differential equation framework.
articles.authors: Petros LD, Abdisa TE, Etefa DT, Menbiko DK, Gizaw AK, Simma EA, Yewhalaw D, Deressa CT
