articles.details: This modeling study develops a temperature-dependent, two-class age-structured malaria model using partial differential equations and optimal control strategies, analyzing equilibria stability via the basic reproduction number R0 and global stability through Lyapunov functionals. The authors cite an estimated 263 million malaria cases and 597,000 deaths globally in 2023, mostly in Africa. Empirical validation using six years of infection prevalence data from the Jimma zone yielded a fit with R² of 0.68 and adjusted R² of 0.63. The model showed superior predictive accuracy versus an existing age-structured model in capturing temperature-dependent trends, supporting improved prediction and control under varying environmental conditions.

articles.headline: Climate and environmental drivers of malaria incidence in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Modelling the impact of climatic and environmental variables on malaria incidence in Tanzania: Implications for achieving the WHO's 2030 Targets.
articles.authors: Lutambi AM, Emidi B, Mbuya FG, Ryoba M, Sagamiko TD, Hugo AK, Mbalawata IS
articles.details: This study assessed the impact of climatic and environmental variables on malaria incidence in Tanzania using DHS geo-covariate data, Moran's I spatial autocorrelation testing, and a geostatistical Bayesian model to predict incidence and percentage change over a ten-year interval. Mean malaria incidence fell from 0.347 (95% CI 0.336–0.357) in 2000 to 0.118 (95% CI 0.114–0.122) in 2020 as ITN coverage rose from 0.037 to 0.496. Spatial clustering was evident (Moran's I 0.93 in 2000, 0.87 in 2010, 0.74 in 2020), with higher burden in Lake, western, eastern, and southern zones. Higher temperature (OR 1.06; 1.13), enhanced vegetation index (ORs 5.28–6.22), and aridity (ORs 1.11; 1.07) increased incidence. The authors conclude fine-scale, region-specific interventions are needed to meet WHO 2030 targets.

articles.headline: Imported P. ovale malaria misdiagnosed as P. falciparum, cured with chloroquine-primaquine
articles.title_original: Imported plasmodium ovale malaria from Côte d'Ivoire: a case report.
articles.authors: Plata-Puyana JD, Vanegas Beltrán JK, Martínez-Ávila MC
articles.details: This case report describes a 29-year-old man who developed fever, chills, and myalgia after returning to Bogotá, Colombia, from a three-month stay in Côte d'Ivoire. Initial microscopy misidentified the parasite as P. falciparum, but PCR confirmed Plasmodium ovale. He was first treated empirically with artemether-lumefantrine, then switched to chloroquine followed by primaquine (15 mg base daily for 28 days) for radical cure. G6PD testing was unavailable, but he was monitored clinically and tolerated treatment without adverse effects. The authors stress molecular diagnostics and clinician awareness for imported malaria.

articles.headline: Qualitative study of malaria patients' post-discharge experiences in South Africa
articles.title_original: Post-discharge experiences of Malaria patients in a Vhembe district village, South Africa.
articles.authors: Kwinda W, Luhalima TR, Mudzweda AD
articles.details: This exploratory, descriptive, contextual qualitative study examined the post-discharge experiences of people diagnosed with malaria at Mhinga village, Vhembe district, Limpopo province, South Africa. Semi-structured interviews were audio-recorded with participants selected through purposive sampling, and data underwent thematic analysis. Two themes emerged: post-discharge experiences from Mhinga clinic and from the hospital. Participants reported complicated malaria symptoms persisting after discharge from both the clinic and hospital, including transfer to Malamulele hospital. The authors conclude that awareness of prevention, enhanced surveillance, and vector control could reduce admissions.

articles.headline: IRS and seasonal chemoprevention reduced P. malariae and P. ovale in Ghana
articles.title_original: Longitudinal analysis of the prevalence of minor Plasmodium spp. in the reservoir of asymptomatic infections through sequential interventions in Northern Sahelian Ghana.
articles.authors: Rios-Teran CA, Tiedje KE, Bangre O, Deed SL, Argyropoulos DC, Koram KA, Oduro AR, Ansah PO, Day KP
articles.details: This interrupted time-series study assessed how sequential indoor residual spraying (IRS) and seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) combined with insecticidal nets affected minor Plasmodium species in Bongo District, Ghana. Five age-stratified surveys of ~2,000 participants each were conducted at the end of wet seasons between 2012 and 2022, with infections detected by 18S rRNA species-specific PCR. After IRS, prevalence fell for P. malariae (13.7% to 1.4%) and P. ovale spp. (5.7% to 0.4%) by 2015. After IRS stopped and SMC began, by 2017 P. malariae (2.9%) and P. ovale spp. (4.0%) rebounded except in under-5s; by 2020-2022 P. malariae rose (7.4%, 5.8%) while P. ovale spp. declined (2.6%, 1.3%).

articles.headline: Review of obstacles to Ethiopia's 2030 malaria elimination goal
articles.title_original: Challenges to Malaria Elimination in Ethiopia by 2030: A Review.
articles.authors: Adugna T
articles.details: This review summarizes challenges to malaria elimination in Ethiopia, where malaria affects three-fourths of the land area and more than two-thirds of the population living below 2000 m altitude. Over 42 Anopheles species have been identified, with Anopheles arabiensis historically the only major vector, but the invasive Anopheles stephensi—resistant to all four insecticide classes and transmitting both P. falciparum and P. vivax—has emerged. Both parasite species have developed resistance to several antimalarial drugs. The authors note a recent long civil war has driven a spike in cases, with rapid spread in regions such as Amhara and Oromo, posing major impediments to the country's malaria elimination aim.

articles.headline: Resting and house-entry behaviour of Anopheles darlingi in Peruvian Amazon
articles.title_original: Experimental hut resting and entrance behaviour of Anopheles darlingi from Zungarococha, a malaria endemic community in Loreto, Northern Peruvian Amazon.
articles.authors: Castro-Llanos F, Stoops CA, Grieco JP, Escobedo-Vargas K, Lopez-Sifuentes V, Lenhart A, Stell FM, Larson RT, Achee NL, Vásquez GM
articles.details: This study used experimental huts in Zungarococha village, Loreto, Peru, to compare indoor resting and house-entry behaviours of wild-caught and colonized Anopheles darlingi, a primary malaria vector. Human Landing Collections ran from June 2014 to May 2015, with controlled indoor releases and interception-trap evaluations in 2015. An. darlingi showed a bimodal outdoor biting pattern peaking at 1800 h and 2200 h, with densities linked to Amazon River levels near Iquitos. Both colonized and wild mosquitoes rested mainly on the lower door (29.8% vs 32.7%), window, and roof. Wild entry peaked at 2200-2300 h, and interception-trap capture rates were lower than indoor HLC rates.

articles.headline: Meta-analysis: pfpm2/3 gene amplification higher in Asia than Africa
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum plasmepsin2/3 gene duplication in Africa and Asia: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Marwa KJ, Kilonzi M, Mnkugwe RH, Baraka V, Kapesa A, Mwaiswelo R, Zinga M, Mmbando BP, Lusingu JPA, Kamugisha E
articles.details: A systematic review and meta-analysis of 34 studies (4,005 P. falciparum isolates) examined amplification of the plasmepsin2/3 (pfpm2/3) gene, which reduces piperaquine susceptibility and drives dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine treatment failure. Using a random-effects model, the pooled proportion of pfpm2/3 amplification was 16.0% (95% CI 8.0-26.0%), with high heterogeneity (I2 > 95%). Amplification was greater in Asia (25.0%, 95% CI 9.0-45.0%) than Africa (8.0%, 95% CI 2.0-15.0%), and higher in 2016-2020 (19%) than before 2016 (11%). The authors call for monitoring partner-drug resistance to protect ACT efficacy.

articles.headline: Decade of malaria burden data in Sindh, Pakistan; floods doubled incidence
articles.title_original: Subnational malaria burden in Sindh, Pakistan: over a decade of evidence for tailored strategies.
articles.authors: Baig N, Ali Z, Ahmed M, Jamal WZ, Rehman SU, Ahmed Z, Rahoojo RH, Jagirani JA, Usmani BA, Fatmi Z
articles.details: A 13-year longitudinal time-series analysis (2012-2024) examined suspected malaria cases from 1,088 primary healthcare facilities across 23 districts of Sindh province, Pakistan. Suspected malaria incidence was 92 per 1,000 people per year, with pooled estimates of 16.7 million cases over the period (about 1.28 million annually) and a malaria positivity rate of 12.3%. Six districts carried over 53% of the burden, and cases peaked in the wet and post-monsoon seasons. Malaria incidence doubled after the 2022 floods and has persisted, prompting calls for tailored, subnational control strategies.

articles.headline: Molecular docking finds candidate inhibitors of P. falciparum invasion proteins
articles.title_original: Targeting invasion-associated proteins PfSUB2 and PfTRAMP in Plasmodium falciparum: identification of potential inhibitors via molecular docking.
articles.authors: Okafor EO, Bella-Omunagbe M, Elugbadebo T, Dokunmu TM, Adebiyi E
articles.details: This computational study predicted the 3D structures of P. falciparum subtilisin-like protease 2 (PfSUB2) and thrombospondin-related apical merozoite protein (PfTRAMP), proteins essential for asexual blood-stage growth and red blood cell invasion, then screened them as drug targets. Using AutoDock Vina in PyRx, 519 compounds were docked against both proteins. Compounds 139,974,934 and 154,414,021 showed better binding affinities than the standard inhibitor PMSF, and compound 155,204,487 showed dual-target potential with better affinity to both proteins. The authors recommend these three compounds as candidates for antimalarial drug development.

articles.headline: Fractional mathematical model explores malaria transmission dynamics
articles.title_original: Complex travelling wave solutions of fractional nonlinear coupled malaria model: bifurcation, chaos, and multistability.
articles.authors: Abdullah, Shakeel M, Muhammad S, Ahmadzai HG
articles.details: This mathematical modeling study analyzed malaria transmission using a fractional nonlinear coupled model with a beta derivative. Using the General Exponential Rational Function Method (GERFM), the fractional partial differential equations were transformed into ordinary differential equations, yielding complex traveling wave solutions including kink, anti-kink, and dark solitons. The analysis demonstrated bifurcation, quasi-periodic and chaotic patterns, multistability, and sensitivity, illustrated via 2D and 3D graphs. The authors conclude the work supports more accurate predictive models of disease spread and extends the use of fractional calculus in modeling complex biological systems.

articles.headline: High P. vivax prevalence and genetic diversity in remote Peruvian Amazon community
articles.title_original: High Prevalence, Genetic Diversity and Temporal Differentiation of Plasmodium vivax in a Remote Hard-to-Reach Community from the Peruvian Amazon Region.
articles.authors: Ramirez R, Torres K, Rodríguez P, Acosta C, Guzmán-Guzmán M, Llanos-Cuentas A, Vinetz JM, Escalante AA, Gamboa D
articles.details: A longitudinal surveillance study in Santa Emilia, a hard-to-reach community in Loreto, Peru, monitored Plasmodium vivax genetic diversity and population structure. Of 3,434 samples collected via active and passive case detection in 2015-2016, 221 were genotyped using 16 neutral microsatellites, with 139 previously genotyped 2013 samples included for comparison. P. vivax prevalence by qPCR was 56%, with 44% submicroscopic infections in 2015-2016. Genetic diversity and population differentiation were high across years; over 40% of infections were polyclonal in 2013 and 2015 versus 29% in 2016. Four populations were detected with increasing admixture in 2015-2016, and no recent bottleneck, indicating persistent diversity that challenges Peru's elimination plan.

articles.headline: Imported P. falciparum strains hamper Bioko Island malaria elimination
articles.title_original: Regular Plasmodium falciparum importation onto Bioko Island, Equatorial Guinea, hampers malaria elimination from the island.
articles.authors: Stabler TC, Dwivedi A, Guo B, Shrestha B, Joshi S, Rivas MR, Donfack OT, Guerra CA, García GA, Daubenberger C, Silva JC
articles.details: Researchers generated whole-genome sequences from P. falciparum field samples collected during the Bioko Island Malaria Elimination Project's 2019 Malaria Indicator Survey to assess local transmission and importation. Bioko parasites did not significantly differentiate from nearby continental populations, and within-host diversity and polyclonal infection levels resembled moderate-transmission areas. However, higher-than-expected genetic diversity, comparable to high-transmission areas, suggested imported strains contribute to local transmission, with parasite flow most pronounced with the Gabonese population. The authors conclude elimination requires interrupting local transmission while addressing importation through targeted traveler interventions and cross-border collaboration with Gabon.

articles.headline: Atypical Arp2/3 complex drives nuclear actin in malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Discovery of an Atypical Arp2/3 Complex in Malaria Parasites Sheds New Light on Nuclear Actin.
articles.authors: Hentzschel F, Frischknecht F, Marti M
articles.details: This perspective discusses the authors' recent discovery of an atypical Arp2/3 actin-nucleating complex in malaria parasites, previously thought absent in apicomplexans, consisting of five divergent subunits plus a putative kinetochore-associated factor. The complex ensures proper kinetochore-spindle attachment during male gametogenesis, likely by nucleating actin at the mitotic spindle. Disrupting Arp2/3 function or actin polymerization caused defective DNA segregation into gametes and developmental arrest in the mosquito. The findings reveal unexpected diversity in Arp2/3 composition and an essential role in malaria transmission, and the article outlines open mechanistic questions.

articles.headline: Benchmarking identity-by-descent detection tools in P. falciparum genomes
articles.title_original: Benchmarking and optimization of methods for the detection of identity-by-descent in high-recombining Plasmodium falciparum genomes.
articles.authors: Guo B, Takala-Harrison S, O'Connor TD
articles.details: Using population genetic simulations tailored for high recombination plus empirical MalariaGEN Pf7 data, the authors benchmarked identity-by-descent (IBD) segment callers—probabilistic (hmmIBD, isoRelate), identity-by-state-based (hap-IBD, phased IBD), and Refined IBD—for Plasmodium falciparum. Low marker density per genetic unit from high recombination significantly compromised IBD accuracy, with most callers showing high false-negative rates for shorter segments that parameter optimization partially mitigated. Optimized IBD better captured selection signals and population structure, while effective population size (Ne) inference was highly sensitive to detection errors. The authors recommend hmmIBD for Plasmodium, especially for quality-sensitive analyses like Ne estimation.

articles.headline: COVID-19 disruptions linked to malaria resurgence in Lambaréné, Gabon
articles.title_original: Malaria amidst the COVID-19 pandemic in Gabon: an application of autoregressive integrated moving average (ARIMA) models within an interrupted time series (ITS) framework to hospital-based data.
articles.authors: Roeder F, Adegnika OS, Honkpehedji YJ, Huth M, Lell B, Adegnika AA, Lopes-Rafegas I, Sicuri E
articles.details: Using observational data from two tertiary referral hospitals in Lambaréné, Gabon (2018 to early 2023), the authors applied ARIMA models within an interrupted time series framework to assess pandemic-related changes in the monthly malaria diagnosis rate per 1000 all-cause diagnoses, with a sub-analysis of maternal malaria. Following an initial drop (-47.32, P=0.031), the diagnosis rate concavely increased (linear term 7.32, P=0.001; squared term -0.19, P=0.001) to a peak above pre-pandemic levels, likely from disrupted control activities and waning tool efficacy. No meaningful increase in maternal malaria incidence was estimated. The authors stress maintaining general-population malaria control.

articles.headline: Plasmodium DNA detected in wild Peruvian Amazon coatis, not armadillos
articles.title_original: Molecular Screening of Plasmodium spp. in Free-Living Ring-Tailed Coatis (Nasua nasua) and Nine-Banded Armadillos (Dasypus novemcinctus) in the Peruvian Amazon.
articles.authors: Ulloa GM, Greenwood AD, Cornejo OE, Monteiro FOB, Santolalla ML, Mayor P
articles.details: Researchers screened free-ranging ring-tailed coatis (Nasua nasua, n=44) and nine-banded armadillos (Dasypus novemcinctus, n=66) from an Indigenous community in the Peruvian Amazon for Plasmodium using nested PCR targeting the mitochondrial cytb gene. Plasmodium spp. DNA was detected in two coatis (4.7%), with sequencing revealing one lineage identical to P. vivax/P. simium and another to P. malariae/P. brasilianum; cox3-based reanalysis in an independent laboratory confirmed P. malariae/P. brasilianum in one coati. No infections were found in armadillos. The authors conclude coatis may host diverse Plasmodiidae as likely incidental hosts and call for expanded surveillance of non-primate mammals.

articles.headline: Urban malaria pooled prevalence 23% across sub-Saharan Africa, travel a key risk
articles.title_original: Urban malaria and population mobility in sub-Saharan Africa: systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Merga H, Degefa T, Birhanu Z, Lee MC, Yan G, Yewhalaw D
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis pooled 39 articles on urban malaria epidemiology in sub-Saharan Africa, using a random-effects model. The overall pooled prevalence of urban malaria was 23.01% (95% CI 19.42–26.59) with very high heterogeneity (I²=99.8%, p<0.0001). A history of travel was associated with higher odds of infection (pooled OR=4.17, 95% CI 2.33–7.46; 2 studies), as was owning livestock in the house (pooled OR=4.1, 95% CI 1.62–10.39; 5 studies). The authors conclude effective urban malaria control needs an integrated approach addressing socio-economic, environmental, and behavioural drivers.

articles.headline: Invasive Anopheles stephensi in Ethiopia fully susceptible to four novel insecticides
articles.title_original: Susceptibility of the invasive malaria vector Anopheles stephensi in Ethiopia to novel chemical insecticides and insect growth regulator.
articles.authors: Abiy E, Degefa T, Balkew M, Merga H, Alemayehu E, Mitiku A, Lee MC, Yan G, Yewhalaw D
articles.details: Using WHO bottle bioassays, this study tested Ethiopian populations of the invasive vector Anopheles stephensi (reared from larval collections) against novel insecticides broflanilide (18 µg/bottle), clothianidin (10 µg/bottle), chlorfenapyr (100 µg/bottle), and the insect growth regulator pyriproxyfen (100 µg/bottle). Broflanilide and clothianidin produced 100% mortality (100/100) at 24 hours; chlorfenapyr mortality was 66% (66/100) at 24 hours rising to 100% at 48 hours. Pyriproxyfen mortality was 0%, 11%, and 15% at 24/48/72 hours but caused 100% oviposition inhibition. Authors conclude these agents are suitable for indoor residual spraying and larviciding against An. stephensi.

articles.headline: Rainfall and temperature linked to delayed child malaria mortality in Uganda
articles.title_original: Climate-driven malaria mortality among children in malaria-endemic areas of Uganda.
articles.authors: Kiragga A, Otieno F, Nabukeera B, Ng'etich M, Tozan Y, Kajungu D
articles.details: This time-series study used a distributed lag non-linear model on weekly malaria mortality data from a population-based cohort in the Iganga and Mayuge districts of Uganda to assess climatic effects across all ages, children under 5, and children aged 5–14. Increased mortality risk was found at a lag of 11–12 weeks following rainfall above 646 mm, and at a lag of 5–11 weeks when temperatures ranged 25.2–29.9 °C. Children's malaria mortality was more sensitive to temperature than rainfall, and male children aged 5–14 were more vulnerable to temperature-related mortality than females; rainfall showed no significant association in children.

articles.headline: Adding social vulnerability improves malaria risk maps in Senegal
articles.title_original: Integrating vulnerability and hazard in malaria risk mapping: the elimination context of Senegal.
articles.authors: Morlighem C, Nnanatu CC, Aheto JMK, Linard C
articles.details: This study presents an integrated malaria risk-mapping approach for Senegal, decomposing risk into 'hazard' (infected-vector potential, e.g. rainfall, temperature) and 'vulnerability' (population predisposition, e.g. health-care access, housing). Using Bayesian geostatistical models with 2017 DHS and 2020–21 MIS data plus remotely sensed covariates, the authors predicted malaria prevalence in children under five at 1 km resolution. Including vulnerability alongside hazard factors in the 2017 DHS model improved accuracy (median predictive R²=0.64), and vulnerability-only models outperformed hazard-only models. Models trained on 2020–21 MIS data performed poorly (best median R²=0.13), likely due to dry-season collection. Authors stress integrating both factor types.

articles.headline: MRI shows experimental cerebral malaria more severe in male mice
articles.title_original: Characterization of experimental cerebral malaria by volumetric MRI A comparative study across the sexes.
articles.authors: Comino Garcia Muñoz A, Marfouk O, Michel CP, Varlet I, Royer E, Perles-Barbacaru TA, Viola A
articles.details: This study used in vivo volumetric MRI to examine sex differences in experimental cerebral malaria in C57BL/6J mice infected with Plasmodium berghei ANKA. Volumetric analysis revealed sex-dependent differences in brain swelling and lesion distribution, particularly microhemorrhages, and a regionalization of brain edema: swelling-prone structures common to both sexes (cortex and pons) and sex-specific alterations (inferior and superior colliculi, midbrain). The authors conclude experimental cerebral malaria is more severe in male than in female C57BL/6J mice.

articles.headline: PBO nets show higher use and lower malaria than pyrethroid nets in Kenya
articles.title_original: Net ownership, utilization and malaria burden in the context of Piperonyl butoxide-LLINs intervention in western Kenya.
articles.authors: Oyweri J, Onyango PO, Machani MG, Bungei J, Cheruiyot S, Afrane YA, Lee MC, Zhong D, Zhou G, Githure J, Atieli H, Yan G
articles.details: A cross-sectional study one year after net distribution surveyed 380 households across 12 villages, split into a pyrethroid-only LLIN arm (181 households) and a piperonyl butoxide (PBO) LLIN arm (199 households), using blood smears and RT-PCR to detect Plasmodium. Net ownership was 89% (pyrethroid) versus 96% (PBO), usage 80.2% versus 87.6%, and attrition 17.9% versus 7.6%. Households with nets were 1.3 times more likely to use them (aOR=1.338, 95% CI 1.224-1.462, p<0.001). Malaria prevalence by qPCR was 34.7% (pyrethroid) versus 29.0% (PBO), prevalence ratio 0.84 (95% CI 0.65-1.03). Authors conclude PBO clusters had better ownership, usage, and lower prevalence, but bedbug infestations and insufficient sleeping beds limited effectiveness.

articles.headline: Review: mosquito biology insights to guide malaria vector control in Africa
articles.title_original: Know your enemy: understanding mosquito biology to advance malaria elimination in Africa.
articles.authors: Zembere K, Kalonde PK, Bickton FM
articles.details: This review article discusses the fundamental biology of Anopheles malaria vectors—including adult and juvenile life stages, the intrinsic incubation period, and blood-seeking behaviour—to inform vector control by national programmes and communities. It notes that vector control accounted for a 65% reduction in malaria cases between 2000 and 2015. The authors argue that understanding mosquito biology can guide more effective interventions and the development of innovative control tools.

articles.headline: Untreated falciparum malaria linked to kidney dysfunction and electrolyte imbalance
articles.title_original: Evaluation of serum electrolytes and kidney function among untreated malaria (Plasmodium falciparum) patients in health centers, Konso zone, South Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Basire B, Kibret B, Kifle K, Assefa F
articles.details: A case-control study enrolled 108 participants (54 with confirmed untreated P. falciparum malaria and 54 non-malaria controls) to assess kidney function and serum electrolytes via an automated chemistry analyzer. Serum creatinine and urea were significantly higher in cases (1.32 ± 0.29 mg/dL and 39.8 ± 8.34 mg/dL) than controls (0.92 ± 0.32 mg/dL and 25.78 ± 7.97 mg/dL), both p<0.05. Sodium and potassium were significantly lower in cases (132.15 ± 3.96 mmol/L and 3.44 ± 0.40 mmol/L) versus controls (137.11 ± 3.11 mmol/L and 3.94 ± 0.39 mmol/L). Authors conclude malaria significantly affects kidney function and electrolytes and recommend routine evaluation of these parameters.

articles.headline: CUT&Tag adapted to profile P. falciparum chromatin from low-input samples
articles.title_original: CUT&Tag and DiBioCUT&Tag enable investigation of the AT-rich epigenome of Plasmodium falciparum from low-input samples.
articles.authors: Gockel J, Ramón-Zamorano G, Kimmel J, Spielmann T, Bártfai R
articles.details: Researchers adapted cleavage under targets and tagmentation (CUT&Tag) to the AT-rich genome of Plasmodium falciparum to overcome drawbacks of ChIP-seq (GC-content artifacts, high material needs, labor-intensive protocols). The method produced reproducible heterochromatin profiles concordant with ChIP-seq using as little as 10,000 nuclei or crude parasite isolates. They also developed DiBioCUT&Tag, using dimerization-induced recruitment of biotin ligase for proximity labeling of chromatin components followed by anti-biotin CUT&Tag. The authors conclude these methods improve genome-wide profiling of chromatin-associated proteins from low-input malaria parasite samples.

articles.headline: Mosquito nutrition shifts malaria biting time, may drive residual transmission
articles.title_original: Biting time of day in malaria mosquitoes is modulated by nutritional status.
articles.authors: Oke CE, Rund SSC, Machani MG, Sabtiu ARM, Akuamoah-Boateng Y, Afrane YA, Reece SE
articles.details: In a semi-field experiment, female Anopheles gambiae s.l. received a blood meal on day 3 and one of three feeding treatments (0.5% sucrose; 10% sucrose; or 10% sucrose plus an extra blood meal on day 6), then were released on day 10 with human-mimic traps to assess host-seeking timing. Low-resourced mosquitoes were 2-3 fold more likely to host seek overall and 5-10 fold more likely to host seek at an earlier time of day than well-resourced mosquitoes, which sought hosts mainly in the second half of the night. Authors conclude nutritional condition drives plasticity in biting time and may be an underappreciated contributor to residual malaria transmission, suggesting sugar-baited traps could influence behaviour affecting ITN success.

articles.headline: Malaria RDT cassettes reused for PCR diagnosis at mass gathering
articles.title_original: [Use of genetic material extracted from rapid malaria diagnostic tests using PCR during the Grand Magal of Touba in Senegal].
articles.authors: Diouf C, Ouaddane I, Diatta G, Goumbala MLB, Sow A, Fam D, Faye M, Gautret P, Sokhna C
articles.details: This validation study collected 2,381 used malaria rapid diagnostic tests (Bioline Malaria Ag P.f/Pan and ParaHIT) from eight health facilities in the Mbacke department during the Grand Magal of Touba in 2022 and 2023, extracting DNA/RNA to confirm antigen readings by real-time qPCR (reference standard). By test reading 213 (8.9%) were positive (135 Bioline, 78 ParaHIT) versus 194 (8.1%) P. falciparum-positive by qPCR. Specificity was 97.3% (Bioline) and 92.5% (ParaHIT); positive predictive values 71.1% and 26.9%, and negative predictive values 95.3% and 98.7%. The authors conclude genetic material from RDT cassettes can be used for subsequent PCR diagnosis.

articles.headline: Used malaria RDTs detect tick-borne relapsing fever borreliosis
articles.title_original: [Identification of tick-borne borreliosis cases by qPCR from rapid malaria diagnostic tests during the Grand Magal of Touba in Senegal].
articles.authors: Diouf C, Ouaddane I, Diatta G, Goumbala MLB, Sow A, Fam D, Faye M, Gautret P, Sokhna C
articles.details: Researchers performed nucleic acid extraction and PCR for dengue virus, Borrelia spp., Bartonella spp., and Coxiella burnetii on 2,381 malaria rapid diagnostic tests collected from eight healthcare facilities in the Mbacke department during the Grand Magal of Touba in 2022 and 2023. Thirteen cases (0.5%) of tick-borne relapsing fever borreliosis were identified by qPCR, including two malaria-borreliosis co-infections; no samples were positive for dengue, Bartonella, or C. burnetii. The authors conclude malaria RDT antigens can diagnose non-malaria fevers such as borreliosis and serve as an accessible sample source for studying fevers of unknown origin.

articles.headline: Case report: diabetic ketoacidosis, pancreatitis, and malaria together
articles.title_original: Convergence of diabetic ketoacidosis, acute pancreatitis, and malaria: A case report.
articles.authors: Kouame KI, Mobio PMN, Bouh JK, Konan JK, Coulibaly TK, Toure CW, Diebi LAA, Kouakou JNH, Koffi BE, Yapo PY
articles.details: This case report describes a 20-year-old female admitted with abdominal/pelvic pain, fever, asthenia, polyuria, polydipsia, and deteriorating consciousness (Glasgow score 9), fever 38.5 °C, and hyperglycemia. Testing revealed severe diabetic ketoacidosis, hypertriglyceridemia, hyperamylasemia, hyperlipasemia, and malaria parasite density, with CT confirming acute stage E pancreatitis; the diagnosis was inaugural type 1 diabetes ketoacidosis unbalanced by pancreatitis and malaria. Treatment with insulin, rehydration, antimalarial, and analgesic therapy led to a favorable outcome after 10 days with normalized blood sugar. The authors emphasize rapid multidisciplinary management of this rare convergence.

articles.headline: Benzo-ring tweaks to antimalarial MMV008138 fail to improve efficacy
articles.title_original: Benzo-ring modification on Malaria Box hit MMV008138: effects on antimalarial potency and microsomal stability.
articles.authors: Ghavami M, Li H, Liu L, Butler JH, Ding S, Butschek GJ, Haney RS, Council-Troche RM, Grams RJ, Merino EF, Davis JM, Totrov M, Cassera MB, Carlier PR
articles.details: This medicinal chemistry study explored the structure-activity relationship of the benzo- (A-ring) of tetrahydro-β-carboline MMV008138, which controls asexual blood-stage Plasmodium falciparum by inhibiting IspD, an enzyme in the MEP pathway for synthesis of isopentenyl pyrophosphate (IPP). The work aimed to increase in vitro antimalarial potency and metabolic stability; 19 benzo-ring variants were synthesized and tested against multidrug-resistant Dd2 P. falciparum, but only three derivatives (20a, c, d) showed asexual blood-stage activity with EC50 values within 3-fold of the parent. One analog (20c) markedly improved microsomal stability, but this did not improve plasma exposure relative to the parent and did not produce oral efficacy in a mouse model of malaria.

articles.headline: Review: repurposing leukemia drug imatinib for malaria treatment
articles.title_original: The emerging role of Imatinib in malaria management: a review of evidence and future directions.
articles.authors: Ajekiigbe VO, Anthony CS, Ogieuhi IJ, Adeola JO, Bassey PU, Gbolagade-Jonathan P, Aremu SO, Omoleke AA, Bakare IS, Babalola AV
articles.details: This literature review synthesized studies from database inception to February 2025 (PubMed, Scopus, Google Scholar, Cochrane, Web of Science, Embase) on the tyrosine kinase inhibitor imatinib, used for chronic myeloid leukemia, as a potential antimalarial. Across nine studies with varying sample sizes, dosages, and follow-up, imatinib was associated with declines in parasite density and pyrexia, parasite growth inhibition, and synergism with antimalarials such as artesunate, with a favorable safety profile and no adverse drug-related events reported. The authors discuss pharmacokinetics and use in children and pregnant women, and call for randomized controlled trials with larger samples and combination therapies.

articles.headline: Artesunate curbs liver-damaging CD8+ T cells via JAK2-STAT3 in malaria mice
articles.title_original: Single-cell profiling reveals immunoregulation of artemisinin on CD8(+)GZMB(+) T cells via JAK2-STAT3 in malaria-infected mice.
articles.authors: Chen J, Gao P, He X, Hu Y, Zhou W, Liu Y, Wang J, Liu X, Bai Y, Chen L, Wang C, Cheng G, Zhang X, Wong YK, Liao F, Xu C, Ou J, Wu Y, Zhang W, Gao Y, Tu Y, Wang J
articles.details: Using single-cell RNA sequencing of 241,837 cells from liver, spleen, and peripheral blood of mice infected with Plasmodium berghei ANKA, researchers profiled immune changes after artesunate (ART) treatment. The liver showed the most pronounced transcriptomic shifts. CD8+GZMB+ T lymphocytes, marked by elevated cytokine and cytotoxic scores, were identified as drivers of hepatic injury. ART modulated this pathogenic subset through the JAK2-STAT3 pathway, reducing its frequency and inflammatory response, revealing a novel immunoregulatory mechanism of artemisinin beyond parasite killing.

articles.headline: Scoping review: community engagement key to larviciding malaria control in Africa
articles.title_original: Assessing the role of community involvement and capacity building in larviciding applications for malaria control in Africa: A scoping review.
articles.authors: Shirima G, Masserey T, Gervas H, Chitnis N, Kiware S, Mirau S
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: This PRISMA-ScR scoping review searched PubMed, Scopus, and Embase for literature published until December 2024 on community engagement and capacity building in larviciding for African malaria control. After applying inclusion and exclusion criteria, 32 papers spanning 13 African countries, primarily sub-Saharan, were included. Findings indicated larviciding significantly reduced mosquito density and, in some cases, malaria incidence. Community engagement strategies (workshops, participatory meetings) and capacity building were critical to successful implementation, though logistical barriers, lack of awareness, and financial constraints persisted.

articles.headline: Ten-year retrospective review of imported and non-travel malaria in Europe
articles.title_original: Imported and non-travel related cases of malaria in Europe - a ten-year analytic retrospective review.
articles.authors: Vanitha R, Abelein F, Stoyanova KP
articles.details: Malaria is a significant global health problem that predominantly affects tropical regions. The abstract provides no further details.

articles.headline: Mosquito metabolism and gut microbes shape P. vivax infection in Anopheles darlingi
articles.title_original: Metabolic reprogramming and gut microbiota ecology drive divergent Plasmodium vivax infection outcomes in Anopheles darlingi.
articles.authors: Carlos BC, Voges K, de Andrade Affonso PH, Jaye A, Tong Rios C, Tinoco-Nunes B, Alonso DP, MacCallum RM, Moreno M, Vlachou D, Souza-Neto JA, Christophides GK
articles.details: Researchers studied vector-parasite-microbiota interactions in Anopheles darlingi using experimental infections with field-derived Plasmodium vivax gametocytaemic blood, yielding two phenotypes: low and high oocyst burdens. Midgut transcriptomics showed low-infection mosquitoes mounted early, sustained responses (detoxification, redox regulation, aromatic amino acid catabolism, purine depletion) with reduced bacterial diversity and enrichment of Enterobacteriales and Pseudomonadales (linked to anti-Plasmodium activity). High-infection mosquitoes showed limited metabolic reprogramming and Flavobacteriales expansion. Low-infection outcomes consistently arose from bloodmeals with the lowest gametocyte densities, reframing vector competence as a dynamic metabolic-microbial outcome and highlighting transmission-blocking targets.

articles.headline: New qPCR assay measures P. falciparum growth, resistance, and recovery after artemisinin
articles.title_original: Measuring growth, resistance, and recovery after artemisinin treatment of Plasmodium falciparum in a single semi-high-throughput assay.
articles.authors: Sievert MAC, Qahash T, Singh PP, Shoue DA, Checkley LA, Brenneman KV, Cassady Z, Kumar S, Li X, Nosten FH, Anderson TJC, Vaughan AM, Romero-Severson J, Ferdig MT
articles.details: Researchers developed and validated the Growth, Resistance, and Recovery assay (GRRA), a straight-from-blood, SYBR Green-based qPCR protocol in 96-well format, to phenotype artemisinin partial resistance (ART-R) in Plasmodium falciparum, building on Pfkelch13 as the resistance marker. Using clinical isolates and progeny from two genetic crosses, the assay measured growth (6-96 h), resistance (120 h), and recovery (120-192 h). Resistance at 120 h was the most consistent ring-stage phenotype; growth versus resistance showed minor but significant correlation, while recovery correlated with neither. Dried blood spot samples matched liquid-sample viability, enabling ex vivo surveillance.

articles.headline: Seasonal and socioeconomic inequities in malaria infection in rural Guinea
articles.title_original: Inequity in the transmission of malaria infection among children and adolescents: a cohort study in rural Guinea.
articles.authors: Toure AA, Sidibe S, Magassouba AS, Beavogui AH, Conde M, Toure AF, Sidibe T, Keita KS, Delamou A, Kouanda S
articles.details: This cohort study in Mafèrinyah, Guinea, followed participants aged 1-19 years with monthly home visits over nine months, detecting malaria via blood smears and analyzing equity using the PROGRESS framework, concentration indices (CI), and mixed-effects logistic models. Malaria varied by season, age, and household characteristics: children in households with older heads (50-77 years) had higher odds in the dry season (OR=3.44), while adolescents in the rainy season were more vulnerable in middle-aged-headed (OR=15.78) and single-parent households (OR=4.52). Concentration indices showed modest pro-rich inequity among adolescents (CI=0.059-0.085). The authors conclude household head age, education, gender, and occupation are key seasonal equity drivers requiring targeted interventions.

articles.headline: Caregivers find seasonal malaria chemoprevention highly acceptable in South Sudan
articles.title_original: "We witnessed medicines help our children, then we became proud": experiences and acceptability of seasonal malaria chemoprevention in Aweil, South Sudan.
articles.authors: Okot F, Sans MS, Viganò E, Rom A, Mubiru D, Baker K, Khan J, Julla A
articles.details: This qualitative study assessed the feasibility and acceptability of seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) in Aweil South county, Northern Bahr el Ghazal, South Sudan, where five monthly cycles of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine plus amodiaquine were given to children aged 3-59 months. Data came from key informant interviews and focus group discussions with caregivers and Boma Health Workers. Among 97 participants, acceptability was high, driven by perceived effectiveness, door-to-door delivery, and use of trusted Boma Health Workers. Reported challenges included environmental barriers, inadequate health worker remuneration, and difficulty reaching all eligible children. The authors conclude the findings can refine community engagement strategies for SMC in fragile settings.

articles.headline: Review supports single low-dose primaquine for malaria control in Africa
articles.title_original: Single low-dose primaquine for malaria control in Africa: a systematic review of safety, efficacy and implementation barriers.
articles.authors: Ye T, Caspar E, Niyomwungere D, Ouedraogo A, Thiebaut L, Strubel PE, Raobela O, Nyandwi J, Rakotosaona R, Ratsimbasoa A, Sirima SB, Menard D
articles.details: This systematic review (PRISMA) examined the safety, efficacy, and implementation of single low-dose primaquine (SLDPQ, 0.25 mg/kg) co-administered with artemisinin-based combination therapies for uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria in Africa, searching 7 databases and screening 819 records. Data were analyzed from 41 studies, including 15 randomised controlled trials and 26 non-trial studies. SLDPQ was safe and well-tolerated, including in G6PD-deficient individuals and children under 5, and eight RCTs confirmed significant reductions in gametocyte carriage, though community-level impact evidence remains limited. Key barriers include misconceptions about toxicity, absent paediatric formulations, and operational challenges. The authors recommend prioritizing SLDPQ scale-up within malaria elimination strategies.

articles.headline: Household entomological inoculation rate weakly predicts individual malaria risk
articles.title_original: Relating household entomological measures to individual malaria risk.
articles.authors: McClure M, Arinaitwe E, Kamya MR, Rosenthal PJ, Nankabirwa J, Kilama M, Musiime A, Dorsey G, Greenhouse B, Rodriguez-Barraquer I
articles.details: This study used data from a cohort of 439 children aged 0.5-5 years in 239 households across three Ugandan districts (low-EIR Jinja, intermediate-EIR Kanungu, high-EIR Tororo) to assess whether household entomological inoculation rate (EIR) reflects individual infection risk. Clinical malaria was captured via passive and quarterly active surveillance, with vector densities and sporozoite rates estimated using CDC light traps and modeled with Poisson generalized additive mixed-effects models. Across sites, higher average EIR was associated with higher incidence, but within-site associations varied: positive in Kanungu (IRR 1.09, 95% CrI 1.04-1.14) but absent in Jinja (1.02, 0.774-1.26) and Tororo (1.02, 0.986-1.06). The authors conclude the EIR-incidence relationship depends on site-specific transmission dynamics.

articles.headline: Training audit improves physician adherence to Sudan malaria guidelines
articles.title_original: Assessing Physicians' Adherence to the 2023 Sudan Malaria Case Management Protocol.
articles.authors: Muhammed A, Mohammed AAY, Mohammed Ali MA, Elsmani I, Elsetiha AIMM, Salih MMA, Hadeed H, Mahmoud AEM, Mohamed MMM, Hamrawi NWA, Abdalla AYH, Elkabashi A, Abdalgadir Hamdnaalla MA, Elshafie M, Abdelraouf Ahmed HA, Osman Ahmed Osman M, Abdulla OAA, Fakher Aldeen Noman FAR, Alfaki A, Hussien Hamed MO
articles.details: This two-cycle clinical audit at Dongola Specialized Hospital assessed and aimed to improve physicians' adherence to Sudan's national malaria diagnosis and treatment guidelines, with a first cycle (N=42), targeted interventions (physician training sessions and dissemination of updated guidelines), and a second cycle (N=39). Baseline adherence was low for general protocol compliance (30.95%), correct intramuscular artesunate injection site (30.95%), and second-line treatment for uncomplicated malaria in pregnancy (23.81%), but high (90.48%) for first-line P. falciparum treatment. After intervention, protocol adherence rose to 89.74% (58.79% improvement), correct IM artesunate site to 79.49%, second-line treatment for uncomplicated malaria in pregnancy to 64.10%, and correct sulfadoxine/pyrimethamine intermittent preventive treatment to 92.31%; first-line artemether/lumefantrine was maintained at 100%. The authors conclude that continuous training and auditing improve the quality of malaria management.

articles.headline: Mass drug administration's malaria benefit mapped across Senegal communes
articles.title_original: Using transportability methods to map the local effectiveness of mass drug administration for malaria in Senegal.
articles.authors: Roh ME, Tong Y, Heitmann GB, Ba EKC, Ndiaye JL, Fogelson A, Milligan P, Seck A, Diallo A, Lo AC, Baiocchi M, Gosling R, Bennett A, Hsiang MS, Benjamin-Chung J
articles.details: Using transportability models with machine learning, researchers generalized results from a cluster-randomized trial—in which mass drug administration (MDA) reduced malaria incidence by 55% in areas already using seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC)—to 116 non-trial Communes where SMC is standard-of-care. Accounting for differences in weather, vegetation, and population density, they estimated significant incidence reductions of 36%–65% in 74 non-trial Communes, with larger reductions where precipitation and vegetation were higher and temperatures lower. MDA was not effective in the post-intervention year, supporting that its effects are short-lived.

articles.headline: Falciparum malaria mimicking TTP in a case report
articles.title_original: Severe Plasmodium Falciparum Malaria Mimicking Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP): A Case Report From China.
articles.authors: Wang C, Kong M, Huang M, Tang N, Mo H
articles.details: This case report describes a 51-year-old man who presented with fever progressing to disturbed consciousness, with decreased platelet count and elevated lactate and indirect bilirubin levels. His PLASMIC score was 7, suggesting high likelihood of thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP), and von Willebrand factor was markedly elevated, but normal ADAMTS13 activity excluded TTP. Peripheral blood smears revealed a large amount of P. falciparum, and the patient improved after intravenous artesunate. The authors stress rapid differential diagnosis between malaria and TTP, both involving thrombotic microangiopathy, to enable early lifesaving treatment.

articles.headline: Malaria-anemia comorbidity affects 39% of Sub-Saharan pregnant women
articles.title_original: Malaria-anemia comorbidity and its determinants among pregnant women in high- and moderate-malaria-risk countries in Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Zegeye AF, Wassie M, Tamir TT, Tekeba B, Mekonen EG, Zeleke GA, Gebrehana DA
articles.details: Using Malaria Indicator Surveys from 17 Sub-Saharan African countries (2016–2022) with 50,545 weighted samples, this multilevel logistic regression study assessed malaria-anemia comorbidity and its determinants among pregnant women. The pooled prevalence was 39.00% (95% CI 29.00–49.00). Significant determinants included no formal education (OR=1.43), untreated bed nets (OR=1.23), poor wealth index (OR=2.37), no indoor residual spraying (OR=2.15), rural residence (OR=2.73), and residing in West (OR=8.00), Central (OR=6.76), and South (OR=18.76) Sub-Saharan Africa. The authors call for targeted interventions for rural pregnant women and region-specific strategies.

articles.headline: Modeling malaria epidemic periods by district and group in Togo
articles.title_original: Identifying malaria epidemic periods in Togo by health district and target group: a generalised additive model approach.
articles.authors: Thomas A, Bakai TA, Atcha-Oubou T, Tchadjobo T, Ecochard R, Rabilloud M, Voirin N
articles.details: This study analyzed monthly confirmed malaria cases from 2013 to 2017, routinely collected by Togo's National Malaria Control Programme, aggregated by health district and target group (children <5 years, children ≥5 years and adults, and pregnant women). Generalised additive models estimated excess cases relative to January. A total of 5,522,650 confirmed cases were reported, with children <5, older children/adults, and pregnant women representing 36.6%, 58.5%, and 4.9% respectively. A time lag of at least one month followed precipitation onset; Savanes region districts had later, larger epidemic increases, while Maritime and Lomé-commune districts had short or undetectable periods. The authors recommend tailoring interventions, including SMC, to local transmission.

articles.headline: Community health workers in Zambia's sugar bait malaria trial
articles.title_original: Comparative analysis of the use of Community Health Workers while deploying the Attractive Targeted Sugar Bait (ATSB) for malaria control in Western Province, Zambia.
articles.authors: Ndalama F, Mulenga D, Arnzen A, Akalalambili S, Tobolo T, Maluma C, Chishya C, Saili K, Ashton RA, Miller J, Silumbe K, Chanda J, Hamainza B, Littrell M, Orange E
articles.details: This study examined the management of Community Health Workers (CHWs) supporting a phase III trial of Attractive Targeted Sugar Baits (ATSBs) in Western Zambia, comparing trial practices from 2021 to 2023 against best practices identified via a literature review and WHO and Zambia Ministry of Health guidelines. Five best practices and five common challenges were identified across recruitment, training, supervision, incentivization, and community support. Trial practices largely aligned with best practices, particularly in training and incentive structures, but challenges included gender imbalance (32% female CHWs in year 2), limited community involvement in CHW selection, and coordination between CHWs and research team members. The authors conclude that adequately funded, rigorously implemented field research facilitates adherence to CHW best practices.

articles.headline: Malaria preventive therapy doesn't improve school-children's nutrition in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Malaria-malnutrition interaction: prevalence, risk factors, and the impact of intermittent preventive therapy for malaria on nutritional status of school-age children in Muheza, Tanga, Tanzania - A cross-sectional survey and a randomized controlled open-label trial.
articles.authors: Hhera JJ, Makenga G, Baraka V, Francis F, Nakato S, Gesase S, Mtove G, Madebe R, Kyaruzi E, Minja DTR, Lusingu JPA, Van Geertruyden JP
articles.details: This study analyzed secondary data from a cross-sectional baseline survey and a randomized controlled open-label trial in children aged 5-15 years, who were assigned 1:1:1 to dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP), artesunate-amodiaquine (ASAQ), or control, with intermittent preventive treatment given at recruitment, 4 months, and 8 months. At baseline, malaria prevalence was 27%; 23% of children ≤10 years were underweight, 21% were stunted, and 28% were thin or severely thin. Children with malaria had 78% higher odds of stunting (AOR=1.78, 95%CI 1.36-2.33, P<0.001), and low socioeconomic status raised odds of being underweight (AOR=1.50, 95%CI 1.13-2.01, P=0.006). Changes in weight, height, and BMI did not differ significantly between DP or ASAQ groups and control. The authors conclude preventive antimalarial use alone may not improve nutritional status, and integrated malaria-nutrition interventions are needed.

articles.headline: Galectin-3 not linked to malaria-related insulin resistance in Ghanaian adults
articles.title_original: Galectin-3 did not associate with malaria-related insulin resistance in diabetic and non-diabetic respondents at a Ghanaian General Hospital.
articles.authors: Nortey E, Derkyi-Kwarteng L, Amoako-Sakyi D, Bockarie AS, Yeboah S, Acquah S
articles.details: At Tema General Hospital, galectin-3 could neither predict beta-cell function (HOMA-B) nor insulin resistance (HOMA-IR) in diabetic or non-diabetic adults with or without malaria, despite fasting measurement of anthropometrics, blood pressure, glucose, full blood count, lipids, insulin, and galectin-3. Participants with type 2 diabetes were older with higher systolic blood pressure and glucose but lower parasite levels, and regardless of diabetes status, total cholesterol, LDL, HDL, and galectin-3 were higher while triglycerides were lower in those with malaria. Insulin resistance instead associated with glucose (Exp(B)=1.83, CI 1.27-2.63, P=0.001) and insulin (Exp(B)=3.14, CI 1.87-5.23, P<0.001), the model explaining 67.7% to 91% of the variation. The authors conclude galectin-3's relationship with insulin resistance and beta-cell function is more complex than linear.

articles.headline: Expanded malaria community-health-worker role feasible and accepted in Myanmar
articles.title_original: Acceptability, feasibility and fidelity of an expanded role for community health workers for malaria elimination in Myanmar: A mixed-method study.
articles.authors: Oo WH, Khant KM, Htwe EP, Htike W, Aye Tun N, Oo MC, Thu KM, Zaw AK, Galau NH, O'Flaherty K, Agius PA, Fowkes FJI
articles.details: This mixed-method study nested in an effectiveness trial assessed the acceptability, feasibility, and fidelity of an expanded role for malaria community health workers (CHWs) providing limited primary health care. Data included in-depth interviews with community leaders (n=6) and health stakeholders (n=14), focus group discussions (n=36), supervision (n=69) and field observation visits (n=6), plus a cross-sectional survey of community members (n=643). The expanded model was acceptable to 97.4% (626/643) of community members because it addressed health-service demand (93.0%, 598/643), and was feasible given high CHW fidelity (97.1%, 67/69) and community reliance (98.3%, 632/643), though training, supervision, supply chains, referral, and data recording could improve. The authors conclude the model is feasible and acceptable and, with further investment, may support malaria elimination goals.

articles.headline: Capsicum frutescens fractions boost antioxidants in malaria-infected mice
articles.title_original: Evaluating the Oxidative Stress Reducing Potentials of Sub-Fractions of Capsicum Frutescens L. Aqueous Extract on Early Established Plasmodium in Mice.
articles.authors: Nwikwe DC, Ofeniforo BE, Asogwa NT, Nwonuma CO, Balogun EA
articles.details: This experimental study assessed the oxidative stress and antioxidant effects of sub-fractions of Capsicum frutescens L. fruit (CFL) aqueous extract in Plasmodium berghei (NK65)-parasitized mice. Two hundred four mice were randomized into seven groups (n=4): normal saline control, 10 mg/kg chloroquine, and groups receiving 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 4.0, and 8.0 mg/kg of each sub-fraction, with blood and liver sampled on Days 4 and 8 post-inoculation to measure malondialdehyde (MDA) and antioxidant enzymes. Treatment significantly reduced MDA levels (p<0.05) and dose-dependently increased antioxidant enzymes versus untreated mice, with sub-fraction B more pronounced than A and C. The authors conclude CFL sub-fractions, especially sub-fraction B, possess in vivo antiplasmodial and antioxidant potency.

articles.headline: Inhibitory and activating KIRs both recognize malaria RIFINs on NK cells
articles.title_original: Both inhibitory and activating KIRs recognize RIFINs: a dual-edged mechanism of NK cell control in malaria.
articles.authors: Cuapio A, Ljunggren HG
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Host sphingolipids enable Plasmodium liver-stage growth and invasion
articles.title_original: Host sphingolipids support Plasmodium berghei liver stage development.
articles.authors: Schroeder EA, Colón IC, Petruzziello PE, Derbyshire ER
articles.details: This laboratory study investigated how Plasmodium berghei liver-stage parasites acquire host lipids. Exogenous C16-ceramide enhanced parasite development and nuclear replication, while host-cell membrane sphingomyelin was essential for invasion. Live microscopy with NBD-labeled sphingolipids showed lipids are actively transported into the parasitophorous vacuole (PV) throughout the liver stage, supported by the host ceramide transporter CERT1, which was enriched at the PV; its genetic disruption significantly reduced P. berghei load and ceramide trafficking. Depleting host salvage-pathway proteins CERS3 and SPHK1 affected PV size and infection rate but not invasion. Authors conclude host sphingolipid metabolism may be a novel multistage antimalarial target.

articles.headline: 7-day high-dose primaquine cuts vivax relapse versus 14-day course
articles.title_original: Shortening the Journey to Cure: A Quasi-experimental Trial Comparing Seven-Day and 14-Day Primaquine Radical Therapy for Vivax Malaria.
articles.authors: Shahab K, Salam A, Salih N, Bilal M, Jan M
articles.details: This quasi-experimental trial in Peshawar, Pakistan enrolled 100 patients (ages 18–76) with confirmed Plasmodium vivax malaria, split into a 7-day high-dose primaquine group (1 mg/kg/day, n=50) and a standard 14-day low-dose group (0.5 mg/kg/day, n=50), with follow-up on days 14, 28, 60, and 90. Disease recurrence was substantially lower with the 7-day regimen (2% vs 26%, p=0.012), and adherence was higher (94% vs 76%, p=0.012). Both regimens were well tolerated, with slightly more very mild gastrointestinal symptoms in the 7-day group and no serious adverse events. Authors conclude the shorter high-dose course offers comparable efficacy with much-improved adherence.

articles.headline: Review: plant-based nanotechnology for Chagas disease and malaria
articles.title_original: Harnessing Phytonanotechnology to Tackle Neglected Parasitic Diseases: Focus on Chagas Disease and Malaria.
articles.authors: García M, Magi MS, García MC
articles.details: This review examines phytochemicals (products of natural origin, PNOs) with demonstrated activity against Trypanosoma cruzi and Plasmodium spp., the agents of Chagas disease and malaria. It notes current therapies are limited by toxicity, poor efficacy, drug resistance, and accessibility, while natural compounds face barriers including poor solubility, low bioavailability, and chemical instability. The authors analyze recent phytonanoformulation designs, emphasizing nanocarrier-based delivery systems incorporating PNOs to improve pharmacokinetic and therapeutic properties. They highlight the promise of phytonanotechnology to overcome current limitations and support more effective, accessible antiparasitic treatments.

articles.headline: High caregiver acceptance of malaria vaccine in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Community readiness and acceptance for the implementation of the malaria vaccine among caretakers of at-risk children in sub-Saharan Africa: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Kigongo E, Puleh SS, Kabunga A, Akech SI, Ocen F, Opollo MS, Ebong M
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis (PRISMA-guided) assessed malaria vaccine acceptance and willingness among caregivers of children under five in sub-Saharan Africa. From 1611 records, 34 studies met inclusion criteria and 25 studies (combined 25,867 participants) entered the meta-analysis. The pooled acceptance rate was 82% (95% CI: 73%–90%) and pooled willingness was 80% (95% CI: 70%–90%), with no statistically significant differences by COVID-19 period or region, though the lowest acceptance (53%) was reported in the DRC. Heterogeneity was very high (I²>99%) and publication bias was indicated for willingness (Egger's test, P=0.002). Authors conclude there is strong community readiness for vaccine rollout but a need for context-specific strategies.

articles.headline: Correction: Artemisia extracts against artemisinin-resistant P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Correction: Assessment of the in vitro activity and selectivity of Artemisia afra and Artemisia annua aqueous extracts against artemisinin-resistant Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Roesch C, Ashraf K, Vantaux A, Marin AA, Maher SP, Franetich JF, Kloeung N, Ke S, Vo HTM, Barale JC, Mazier D, Witkowski B
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Environment, not LLIN type, drives malaria mosquito habitat in Côte d'Ivoire
articles.title_original: Ecological drivers of malaria vector habitat and transmission over 1 year of long-lasting insecticidal net intervention in Côte d'Ivoire.
articles.authors: Talbot B, Alou LPA, Koffi AA, Sih C, Dangbenon E, Zoh MG, Camara S, Assi SB, N'Guessan R, Messenger LA, Protopopoff N, Cook J, Kulkarni MA
articles.details: Using data from a cluster randomized controlled trial in Tiébissou, Central Côte d'Ivoire across 33 clusters, researchers built ecological niche models for Anopheles coluzzii, An. gambiae s.s., and An. funestus s.s., and for Plasmodium falciparum-infected vectors, at baseline and 1 year after long-lasting insecticidal net (LLIN) intervention, combining mosquito density and infection data with satellite remote sensing of land cover, climate, topography, and population density. Suitable habitat declined by less than 1% to more than 60% across vector species and by more than 50% for P. falciparum-infected vectors. No effect of intervention arm on species occurrence was detected, while combinations of land cover, climate, topography, and population density showed strong significant effects. The authors conclude vector species composition should be considered when deploying control interventions.

articles.headline: Expanding community malaria care to all ages found acceptable in Madagascar
articles.title_original: An Anthropological Analysis of Acceptability and Feasibility of Expanding Community-Based Malaria Management to All Ages in Madagascar: Levers and Challenges for National Scale-Up.
articles.authors: Rabesandratra HF, Mattern C, Brazy-Nancy E, Harimanana A, Irinantenaina J, Razanadranaivo HL, Andrianambinintsoa PTD, Dentinger C, Steinhardt L, Garchitorena A
articles.details: Expanding malaria community case management (mCCM) from children under 5 to community members of all ages was acceptable and feasible to beneficiaries and community health workers (CHWs), based on qualitative surveys within a cluster randomized trial in one district of Madagascar from November 2020 to December 2021. Data came from 87 semistructured interviews and 12 focus groups across intervention and control arms, exploring malaria understanding, fever care-seeking, perceptions of CHW roles, and acceptability and feasibility. Stakeholders found age-expanded mCCM consistent with existing CHW roles and practices and reported greater recognition of adults' susceptibility to malaria, but structural and community-based challenges—including the cost of care—were not fully resolved. The authors conclude successful scale-up will require addressing structural challenges and sociodemographic inequalities.

articles.headline: Plasma proteomics reveals 11-protein signature linked to severe malaria
articles.title_original: Systems analysis of clinical malaria reveals proteomic perturbation and innate-adaptive crosstalk linked to disease severity.
articles.authors: Lautenbach MJ, Wyss K, Yman V, Foroogh F, Satarvandi D, Mousavian Z, Sondén K, Wang J, Álvez MB, Bergström S, Nilsson P, Edfors F, Brodin P, Uhlén M, Sundling C, Färnert A
articles.details: This systems-biology study analyzed 1,463 plasma proteins during and after acute Plasmodium falciparum malaria in adult travelers, integrating results with single-cell RNA sequencing data from a subset of donors to link protein responses to peripheral immune cells. The investigators identified perturbations in over 250 proteins from diverse origins, including hormones, circulating receptors, and intracellular or membrane-bound proteins not previously studied in malaria. Protein profiles clustered participants by disease severity and yielded a compressed 11-protein signature enriched in severe malaria. The authors conclude the work links systemic proteomic changes to immune cell communication and organ-specific responses, providing a resource for biomarker discovery and therapeutic target identification.

articles.headline: US military malaria risk tied to service member's endemic birthplace
articles.title_original: Malaria Incidence in US Military Families Is Related to Service Member's Birthplace.
articles.authors: Helfrich AM, Lu D, Grance M, Chu X, Hickey PW
articles.details: This retrospective cohort study examined all service members and family members enrolled in the US Military Health System (MHS) between October 2012 and September 2018, grouping participants by whether the service member was born in a malaria-endemic or nonendemic country. Malaria cases were identified by ICD codes and compared using incidence rate ratios (IRRs). Among 281 malaria cases identified, the malaria-endemic group (n=67) had over 5 times the risk of the nonendemic group (n=214) (IRR 5.2, 95% CI 2.4–10.4). Those connected to sub-Saharan Africa had an average annual incidence of 42 cases per 100,000 persons, accounting for 86.6% of endemic-group cases, and only 48% of all cases sought pretravel medical counseling. The authors conclude familial connection to endemic countries confers markedly higher travel-related malaria risk despite universal insurance and care access.

articles.headline: Colombia's 1950s–60s malaria eradication campaign cost $41.8 million, failed
articles.title_original: Costs and impact of the Malaria Eradication Campaign in Colombia, 1957-1969.
articles.authors: Padilla-Rodríguez JC, Olivera MJ
articles.details: This partial economic evaluation estimated the institutional costs of Colombia's Campaña de Erradicación de la Malaria using microdata from the Servicio de Erradicación de la Malaria, the División de Campañas Directas, and the Pan American Health Organization from 1958 to 1969, classifying costs by operational phase (attack, surveillance, maintenance) and category (personnel, benefits, equipment, other). The total cost was USD $41,788,924, with 74.1% from national sources; the attack phase accounted for 80.4% (USD $33,603,645), of which 40% went to spraying personnel and 19% to insecticide. The average cost per household spraying was USD $2.70, and the campaign prevented an estimated 568,439 malaria cases (about 41,965 per year). The authors conclude that despite this expenditure the campaign ultimately failed to achieve eradication.

articles.headline: Novel Kelch13 mutations spread clonally in Gabon P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Clonal Transmission of Emerging Novel Plasmodium falciparum Kelch13 Mutations and Increasing Complexity of Infection in Libreville, Gabon, 2021-2023.
articles.authors: Ndong Ngomo JM, Mawili-Mboumba DP, Mouity TN, Leonetti A, Mabika DM, Mihindou JC, Mbondoukwé NP, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Bouyou-Akotet MK, Niaré K
articles.details: Molecular surveillance of febrile P. falciparum-infected patients at the Melen peri-urban sentinel site in Libreville, Gabon (2021-2023) used molecular inversion probes to sequence drug-resistance genes and genome-wide SNPs in 468 genotyped samples. No validated or candidate K13 artemisinin-resistance mutations were found, but 21 carried K13 mutations of unknown significance: E433D (n=14), Q613H (n=5), V520I, and V637I. E433D prevalence rose from 0.7% (2022) to 5.5% (2023); E433D/Q613H parasites showed higher identity-by-descent than wild-type (P<0.001) with clonal transmission chains. MDR1 Y184F rose from 52.6% to 68.8%; CRT K76T stayed rare. Complexity of infection increased, suggesting intensifying transmission.

articles.headline: Mozambique genomic surveillance: no artemisinin resistance, high SP resistance
articles.title_original: Antimalarial drug resistance and population structure of Plasmodium falciparum in Mozambique using genomic surveillance at health facilities in 2021 and 2022.
articles.authors: Boene S, Rovira-Vallbona E, da Silva C, García-Ulloa M, Rafael B, Canana N, Aranda-Díaz A, Cisteró P, García-Fernández C, Tembisse D, Ndimande N, Chidimatembue A, Matambisso G, Palmer B, Chico AR, Dimene M, Saifodine A, Inácio J, da Silva M, Plucinski M, Bonnington C, Wate F, de Carvalho E, Mathe G, Pujol A, Arregui-Gallego B, Comiche K, Nhama A, Nhamussua L, Aide P, Saute F, Enosse S, Greenhouse B, Candrinho B, Mayor A
articles.details: Targeted amplicon sequencing of 1146 P. falciparum samples from Mozambique collected in 2021 (n=321) and 2022 (n=825 rainy season, n=155 dry season) detected no pfk13 markers of partial artemisinin resistance and no pfpm2 duplications, with pfpm2 copy number assessed by qPCR. Prevalence of pfdhfr/pfdhps quintuple mutants linked to sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP) resistance was high across all regions (>92.5% in 2021, >87.8% in 2022), but pfdhps-A581G was rare (1.6% in 2021, 0.8% in 2022). Prevalence of pfdhps-436 mutations (p<0.001) and the genetic complexity of infections both increased from South to North. The authors support continued use of artemisinin-based combination therapies and call for close monitoring of SP-based chemoprevention.

articles.headline: Fractional-order mathematical model of malaria treatment and prevention
articles.title_original: Fractional-order model of malaria incorporating treatment and prevention strategies.
articles.authors: Agbata BC, Kovaci S, Agbebaku DF, Dervishi R, Abah E, Mbah GCE, Emadifar H, Smerat A
articles.details: This modeling study developed a fractional-order mathematical model, extending integer-order nonlinear differential equations, to evaluate how treatment and prevention strategies affect malaria transmission. The authors established conditions for solution existence and uniqueness, assessed endemic equilibrium stability using a Lyapunov function, and performed sensitivity analysis of the basic reproduction number. Simulations using the fractional Adams-Bashforth-Moulton method, illustrated with surface and contour plots, showed that improving treatment and implementing preventive measures such as mosquito control and timely medication significantly reduced malaria cases, while increased mosquito contact and ineffective treatment worsened impact. No specific quantitative epidemiological outcomes were reported.

articles.headline: Host miR-92a-3p explored as sequence-based antimalarial therapy target
articles.title_original: Exploring the Potential of miRNA-92a-3p as Lead for Sequence-Based Therapies for Malaria.
articles.authors: Prabhu SR, Paul S, Umakanth S, Hande M, Saadi AV, Gupta H, Satyamoorthy K
articles.details: This study investigated host microRNA miR-92a-3p as a potential inhibitor of P. falciparum erythrocyte membrane protein (PfEMP) in clinical malaria. In a cross-sectional analysis, Duffy binding-like (DBL) domain sequences were amplified from 20 infected patient samples via PCR, cloning, and Sanger sequencing to assess variability and miR-92a target-site availability across uncomplicated and complicated cases, alongside a case-control study of host miR-92a gene polymorphisms. Despite sequence variability in the DBLα domain, a conserved miR-92a-binding motif was consistently observed. Mutation analysis found variants in two healthy controls and nine cases. Authors conclude miR-92a holds potential as an antisense therapeutic strategy.

articles.headline: Ferrocenyl quinoline-benzimidazole hybrids show multistage antiplasmodial activity
articles.title_original: Ferrocenyl Quinoline-Benzimidazole Hybrids: A Multistage Strategy to Combat Drug-Resistant Malaria.
articles.authors: Golding TM, Garnie LF, Rabie T, Reader J, Birkholtz LM, Wicht KJ, Smith GS
articles.details: This drug-development study designed and synthesized ferrocenyl-based quinoline-benzimidazole molecular hybrids, inspired by ferroquine, and tested in vitro blood-stage antiplasmodial activity against drug-sensitive NF54 and multidrug-resistant K1 P. falciparum strains. The hybrids showed potent submicromolar activity, and an N,N-dimethylaminomethyl side chain enhanced activity against both strains. One compound showed multistage activity targeting immature and mature gametocytes. Mechanistic studies implicated inhibition of hemozoin formation, supported by heme fractionation analysis, with fluorescence assays indicating reactive oxygen species generation under oxidative conditions. Authors highlight these hybrids as promising antiplasmodial drug candidates.

articles.headline: Anopheles vector seasonality and climate in malaria-free Sri Lanka
articles.title_original: Seasonal abundance and climatic determinants of Anopheles vectors in Sri Lanka: its implications in the malaria post-elimination era.
articles.authors: Hewavitharane M, Ranawaka G, Saparamadu MDJS, Premaratne R, Jayasooriya HTR
articles.details: A two-year entomological survey sampled female Anopheles mosquitoes monthly across five districts spanning Sri Lanka's dry, intermediate, and wet zones, using cattle-baited traps, indoor/outdoor human landing catches, and indoor hand collections, with rainfall, temperature, and humidity data. Fifteen Anopheles species were identified. Primary vectors were predominantly caught by human landing catch at all sites except the dry-zone coastal site, while secondary vectors dominated cattle-baited traps. An. culicifacies reached its highest mean adult density in the intermediate zone, peaking during the second inter-monsoon and southwest monsoon. Vector abundance was significantly influenced by climatic factors and collection site (p<0.05), with An. culicifacies density rising with humidity. Authors conclude the intermediate zone has the highest receptivity, warranting ongoing surveillance to mitigate reintroduction risk.

articles.headline: Plasmodium vivax Pv47 diversity shaped by mosquito-driven selection
articles.title_original: Worldwide genetic diversity of Plasmodium vivax Pv47 is consistent with natural selection by anopheline mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Molina-Cruz A, Gonzalez-Ceron L, Dwivedi A, Torres TZB, Raytselis N, Young M, Kamath N, McNinch C, Su X, Ford A, Ferreira MU, Arévalo-Herrera M, Herrera S, Lo E, Silva JC, Barillas-Mury C
articles.details: This study analyzed global genetic diversity of Pv47, the P. vivax ortholog of P. falciparum Pfs47, comparing it with Pfs47. Most common Pv47 polymorphisms were non-synonymous and clustered in regions similar to Pfs47, with domain 2 showing an excess of non-synonymous substitutions suggesting positive selection. The greatest haplotype diversity was found in East/Southeast Asia and Oceania, and Pv47 showed marked worldwide geographic population structure. A K27E polymorphism was associated with differing infectivity to Anopheles albimanus and Anopheles pseudopunctipennis, two phylogenetically distant vectors in Mexico. Authors conclude that adaptation to different Anopheline species drives Pv47 diversity by selecting compatible haplotypes.

articles.headline: Falciparum drug susceptibility shifts in Uganda, 2019–2024
articles.title_original: Changes in susceptibility of Plasmodium falciparum to antimalarial drugs in Uganda over time: 2019-2024.
articles.authors: Okitwi M, Orena S, Tumwebaze PK, Katairo T, Taremwa Y, Byaruhanga O, Tukwasibwe S, Nsobya SL, Legac J, Bailey JA, Cooper RA, Conrad MD, Rosenthal PJ
articles.details: Researchers measured ex vivo susceptibility to nine antimalarial drugs in P. falciparum isolates from uncomplicated malaria patients in eastern (2019-2024) and northern (2021-2024) Uganda, with deep sequencing of 80 genes to link susceptibility to resistance markers. Among 1114 isolates, median IC50s were low-nanomolar for chloroquine, monodesethylamodiaquine, piperaquine, pyronaridine, lumefantrine, mefloquine, and DHA, but higher for quinine and pyrimethamine. Over time, susceptibility improved for chloroquine but decreased for lumefantrine, mefloquine, and DHA, mirrored by changes in known marker prevalences. Decreased lumefantrine susceptibility was linked to wild-type PfCRT K76T or PfMDR1 N86Y, and mutant PfK13 C469Y/A675V, PfCARL D611N; decreased DHA susceptibility to PfK13 C469Y/A675V and PfMDR1 Y500N. Authors warn of potential loss of efficacy of leading regimens, though clinical consequences remain uncertain.

articles.headline: Ferroportin inhibitor VIT-2763 worsens Plasmodium infection in mice
articles.title_original: Pharmacologic Inhibition of Erythrocyte Ferroportin Expression Exacerbates Plasmodium Infection.
articles.authors: Zeydabadinejad S, Theis BF, Park JS, Gohara AF, Vijay-Kumar M, Yeoh BS, Saha P
articles.details: Using a P. yoelii mouse model, researchers examined the hepcidin-ferroportin iron-regulatory axis and the effect of the oral ferroportin inhibitor VIT-2763 (Vamifeport). Infection significantly downregulated hepatic hepcidin and increased hepatic ferroportin, while RBC-ferroportin protein was suppressed. Treatment with VIT-2763 increased parasitemia, along with elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines, erythropoietin, and liver injury markers; it suppressed hepcidin and increased ferroportin in hepatocytes while reducing ferroportin in RBCs. Authors conclude the findings reveal tissue-specific ferroportin regulation in hepatocytes and RBCs and underscore the therapeutic potential of modulating the hepcidin-ferroportin axis in malaria.

articles.headline: Amodiaquine-resistance markers accumulate in São Tomé and Príncipe
articles.title_original: Trajectory of Plasmodium falciparum Molecular Markers of Amodiaquine Resistance in São Tomé and Príncipe.
articles.authors: Té S, Das Neves E, Pellicer A, Broumou I, Zerebinski J, Santos-Reis A, Nóbrega de Sousa T, Färnert A, Santos A, Santos MJ, Gil JP, Lopes D
articles.details: This study evaluated recent dynamics in the prevalence of the P. falciparum amodiaquine-resistance-associated pfmdr1 86Y/Y184/1246Y haplotype, analyzing infections from 2020-2022 and comparing them with historical data, in a setting where artesunate-amodiaquine has been first-line treatment since 2005. Results suggest the introduction of artesunate-amodiaquine drove successive accretion of single nucleotide polymorphisms within a pre-existing environment of near-fixed pfcrt 76T, leading to emergence of a dominant 86Y/Y184/1246Y haplotype. Authors call for increased vigilance of artesunate-amodiaquine performance in São Tomé and Príncipe.

articles.headline: Lightweight hybrid capsule network detects malaria parasites in blood smears
articles.title_original: Hybrid Capsule Network for precise and interpretable detection of malaria parasites in blood smear images.
articles.authors: Alawfi B
articles.details: This methods study proposes Hybrid CapNet, a lightweight architecture combining CNN feature extraction with dynamic capsule routing for parasite identification and life-cycle stage classification, trained with a composite margin, focal, reconstruction, and regression loss. It was evaluated on four benchmark datasets (MP-IDB, MP-IDB2, IML-Malaria, MD-2019) for intra- and cross-dataset performance. The model used 1.35M parameters and 0.26 GFLOPs, reached up to 100% multiclass classification accuracy, and improved over baseline CNNs in cross-dataset tests; Grad-CAM confirmed focus on biologically relevant parasite regions. The authors conclude it is an interpretable, low-cost option suitable for mobile, resource-limited diagnosis.

articles.headline: High stated acceptance but lower actual uptake of ivermectin MDA
articles.title_original: Community acceptance of Ivermectin mass drug administration for malaria in Southern Thailand.
articles.authors: Aung PL, Sripoorote P, Rachaphaew N, Sattabongkot J, Parker DM, Nguitragool W, Phuanukoonnon S
