articles.details: This cross-sectional survey assessed acceptance of planned ivermectin mass drug administration (MDA) for malaria among household heads in three districts of Surat Thani Province, southern Thailand, using structured questionnaires and logistic regression. Of 391 participants, 96.4% expressed acceptance; forest-related workers (aOR 4.2, 95% CI 1.1–16.1) and those believing malaria could be eliminated locally (aOR 9.1, 95% CI 2.8–29.9) had higher acceptance. However, only 59.0% of 3,137 eligible individuals completed all three MDA rounds. The authors highlight a gap between stated acceptance and actual participation and stress engaging forest-related workers and strengthening community engagement.

articles.headline: Review of malaria burden, risk factors, and control across East Africa
articles.title_original: The burden of malaria in East Africa: prevalence, risk factors, and control strategies.
articles.authors: Bashir SG, Ahmed NI, Abdullahi YB, Abdi YH, Abdi MS, Musa MK
articles.details: This systematic review synthesized peer-reviewed literature, national reports, and WHO surveillance data from 2015–2024 across twelve East African countries (Burundi, Comoros, Djibouti, Eritrea, Ethiopia, Kenya, Madagascar, Rwanda, Somalia, South Sudan, Tanzania, Uganda) to analyze incidence, mortality, resistance, intervention coverage, and climate impacts. Uganda, South Sudan, and Burundi reported the highest incidence (250+ per 1000) and Eritrea and Comoros the lowest. Artemisinin partial resistance exceeded 20% in multiple areas, pyrethroid resistance is undermining vector control, and only 48% of required funding was secured. Integrated strategies (LLINs, indoor residual spraying, vaccination, community engagement) reduced severe malaria by up to 47%.

articles.headline: Malaria–schistosomiasis co-infection and malnutrition in Ondo schoolchildren
articles.title_original: Malaria, urogenital schistosomiasis and co-infection and nutritional status of school children in Ondo state.
articles.authors: Ola EM, Balogun TH, Isijola RO, Ajakaye OG
articles.details: Among 185 schoolchildren screened in two Ondo State communities (Ipogun and Oke Igbo) with the ParaHit malaria rapid test and urine microscopy, malaria, urogenital schistosomiasis, and co-infection were all more prevalent in Oke Igbo: malaria 36 (57.1%) versus 60 (49.2%) in Ipogun, schistosomiasis 18 (28.6%) versus 5 (4.1%), and co-infection 13 (20.6%) versus 4 (3.3%). Malnutrition rates were similar between the communities (60, 77.9% in Ipogun; 28, 75.5% in Oke Igbo), but children with either infection or co-infection showed a higher frequency of chronic malnutrition. Co-infection was significantly associated with locality (Oke Igbo 0.78 times less likely, P=0.00, CI 0.09–0.49) and male sex (2.19 times more likely, P=0.02, CI 1.13–4.28). The authors highlight the need for comprehensive health and nutritional interventions.

articles.headline: Soursop leaf extract boosts antimalarial drug efficacy in mice
articles.title_original: Enhanced Antimalarial Efficacy of Annona muricata Leaf Extract Combined With Artesunate, Chloroquine, and Pyrimethamine in Plasmodium berghei-Infected ICR Mice.
articles.authors: Sarakul O, Boonhok R, Somsak V
articles.details: This experimental study tested Annona muricata crude ethanolic leaf extract (AME) alone and combined with artesunate (ART), chloroquine (CQ), and pyrimethamine (PYR) in Plasmodium berghei ANKA-infected ICR mice, measuring parasitemia, survival, and synergistic interactions versus monotherapies. AME alone showed significant antimalarial activity, especially at higher doses. AME combined with ART and with PYR produced significant synergy, achieving over 90% inhibition of parasitemia and prolonging mean survival times up to 30 days, whereas AME with CQ showed no synergy. The authors conclude AME, particularly with ART or PYR, could enhance efficacy and help mitigate drug resistance, warranting further validation.

articles.headline: P. vivax genomes highly conserved in Duffy-negative Sudanese individuals
articles.title_original: Highly conserved Plasmodium vivax genomes in Duffy-negative individuals from Sudan.
articles.authors: Schroeder RE, Ahmed S, Ford A, Elfaki M, Hamad SO, Ahmed TME, Mohamed S, Manko E, Clark TG, Campino S, Hamid MMA, Lo E
articles.details: This genomic and epidemiological study compared Plasmodium vivax infections in Duffy-positive versus Duffy-negative individuals in Sudan, sequencing parasite genomes and collecting epidemiological data. Duffy-positive individuals had significantly higher parasitemia than Duffy-negatives (p=0.0000132), and Duffy-positive P. vivax genomes were much more genetically diverse across all 14 chromosomes and 44 erythrocyte binding gene candidates, with merozoite surface protein family genes accounting for much of the diversity. Many erythrocyte binding gene candidates showed positive and negative selection pressure, and some binding-implicated genes carried amino acid mutations to structurally different residues that may affect binding and antigenic conformation.

articles.headline: Two-step RDT plus dbPCR-NALFIA improves malaria diagnosis in febrile patients
articles.title_original: Refining malaria diagnosis in high-transmission areas: a dual-approach with rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) and dbPCR-NALFIA.
articles.authors: Some DY, Kiemde F, Sorgho H, Tahita MC, Bere AW, Ouedraogo A, Banao SV, Bagre K, Djiri G, Some G, Rouamba T, Hien YE, Savadogo A, Schallig HDFH, Tinto H
articles.details: This diagnostic evaluation in febrile patients at two rural health facilities in Burkina Faso assessed a sequential algorithm combining a two-step RDT detecting PfHRP2 and pLDH with confirmatory direct-on-blood mini PCR-nucleic acid lateral flow immunoassay (dbPCR-NALFIA) for undetermined (PfHRP2+/pLDH-) cases, using microscopy and qPCR as references. Of 438 samples, 87.2% (382/438) had conclusive sequential interpretation without needing dbPCR-NALFIA, with sensitivity 98.8% and specificity 95.3%. After confirming undetermined cases, the full algorithm reached 97.9% sensitivity, 94.8% specificity, 97.2% PPV, and 96.1% NPV, versus 95.2% sensitivity and 73.2% specificity for single HRP2-based RDT. Authors conclude the combined approach enhances malaria diagnosis.

articles.headline: High msp2 allelic diversity in asymptomatic P. falciparum carriers in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Genetic polymorphism of msp2 in Plasmodium falciparum isolates among asymptomatic malaria infections from two ecological settings in Cameroon.
articles.authors: Bouopda-Tuedom AG, Abate L, Feufack-Donfack BL, Ngou CM, Bayibeki AN, Moukoko CEE, Ayong LS, Berry A, Lefèvre T, Morlais I, Nsango SE
articles.details: This study characterized Plasmodium falciparum genetic diversity from dried blood spots of asymptomatic individuals in forest (Mfou) and humid savanna (Tibati) settings in Cameroon, using real-time PCR for infection status and nested PCR with capillary electrophoresis for msp2 allelic polymorphism and multiplicity of infection (MOI). Malaria prevalence was 59.2% (876/1480) in Mfou and 63.4% (808/1274) in Tibati, with 36 and 42 msp2 alleles detected respectively and no genetic differentiation between sites; the IC/3D7 family was most polymorphic and prevalent. Over 60% of isolates had multiclonal infections, higher in Mfou (68.9%, MOI=2.08) than Tibati (57.29%, MOI=1.80), with MOI negatively correlated with age and higher in males in Mfou. Authors report high transmission with distinct epidemiological patterns.

articles.headline: Night outdoor activity and gardens raise malaria risk in Mudzi, Zimbabwe
articles.title_original: Risk factors for contracting malaria in six wards of Mudzi District, Zimbabwe: A case control-study.
articles.authors: Fambirai T, Chimbari MJ, Ndarukwa P
articles.details: This unmatched case-control study in Mudzi District, Zimbabwe, recruited 94 cases (RDT-positive) and 91 controls (neighbours of cases without a positive RDT) to identify socio-economic, behavioural, and health system factors behind persistent malaria transmission despite indoor residual spraying. Independent risk factors were engaging in night outdoor social and religious activities (AOR=8.13; 95% CI 1.74-37.90) and having a garden (AOR=4.51; 95% CI 1.55-13.12), while wearing full body clothing at night (AOR=0.13; 95% CI 0.03-0.53) and sleeping in a sprayed room (AOR=0.04; 95% CI 0.01-0.31) were protective. The majority of cases (96.74%) and controls (92.22%) had good knowledge of malaria. The authors recommend sustaining IRS plus intensified, targeted community engagement and awareness.

articles.headline: Global study maps care-quality inequalities for NTDs and malaria, 1990-2021
articles.title_original: Inequalities in disease burden and care quality of neglected tropical diseases and malaria, 1990-2021: Findings from the 2021 Global Burden of Disease Study.
articles.authors: He R, Zhu W, Hui S, Yu M, Li H, Li Y, Huang P, Yu R
articles.details: Using Global Burden of Disease Study 2021 data, this analysis derived a quality of care index (QCI) via principal component analysis from six primary indicators (incidence, mortality, prevalence, DALYs, years of life lost, years lived with disability) to compare NTD and malaria care across regions, genders, and age groups. From 1990 to 2021 incidence increased while age-standardized mortality rates and DALYs declined. In 2021 QCI was highest in Bahrain (91.9), Qatar (91.5), and the United Arab Emirates (91.5), and lowest in Uganda (78.5), Niger (77.1), and Burkina Faso (75.2), correlating positively with economic development; care was poorest in those under 10 and over 95 and largely equal between genders. Authors urge improving care in low SDI areas and young children.

articles.headline: Malaria (correspondence reply)
articles.title_original: Malaria. Reply.
articles.authors: Daily JP, Parikh S
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Malaria (correspondence)
articles.title_original: Malaria.
articles.authors: Barkati S, Balerdi-Sarasola L, Yansouni CP
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Host time-of-day does not affect liver-stage malaria establishment in mice
articles.title_original: Time-of-day of infection: impact on liver stage malaria parasites in untreated and drug-treated hosts.
articles.authors: Schneider P, O'Donnell AJ, Herbert-Mainero A, Reece SE
articles.details: This experimental study used Plasmodium berghei-infected Anopheles stephensi mosquitoes to infect mice during their rest or active phase, in both untreated and pyrimethamine-treated animals, quantifying liver-stage parasite burdens by qPCR adjusted for sporozoite inocula. Across three experiments, pyrimethamine reduced liver parasite burdens, but host time-of-day (active vs rest phase) had no biologically significant effect on burdens in either treated or untreated hosts. The authors conclude that host circadian rhythms do not affect vector-to-host transmission, suggesting time-of-day effects on vectorial capacity components are more epidemiologically influential than host rhythms.

articles.headline: High P. vivax slide positivity and risk factors at Thailand-Myanmar border
articles.title_original: Slide positivity, trends, and risk factors of febrile Plasmodium vivax malaria along the Thailand-Myanmar border, 2018-2023.
articles.authors: Aung PL, Rachaphaew N, Sripoorote P, Htwe KZZ, Suk-Aum K, Kaewkungwal J, Lawpoolsri S, Cui L, Sattabongkot J
articles.details: This six-year community-based longitudinal passive surveillance study used microscopy and logistic regression to assess febrile Plasmodium vivax infection in a malaria-endemic district of western Thailand along the Myanmar border. Among 13,347 febrile malaria-suspected patients, the cumulative P. vivax slide positivity rate was 11.0%, with cases rising in April, November, and December. Significant risk factors included school-aged children 5-14 years (aOR 1.56), males (aOR 1.19), Myanmar nationals (aOR 2.37), other non-Thai nationals (aOR 5.50), prior malaria (aOR 1.59), recent travel (aOR 2.38), and livestock work (aOR 2.49); consistent bed-net use was protective (aOR 0.39). Authors call for strengthened surveillance targeting high-risk groups.

articles.headline: Climate change raises malaria transmission suitability in Canada
articles.title_original: Current and future temperature suitability for autochthonous transmission of malaria in Canada.
articles.authors: Siebels K, Ng V, Ogden N, Schofield S, Ludwig A
articles.details: This modeling study applied temperature and duration thresholds from historic Plasmodium transmission studies to gridded historical and projected climate data to estimate the frequency of conditions suitable for autochthonous Plasmodium vivax and P. falciparum transmission in Canada, where competent vectors Anopheles quadrimaculatus and A. freeborni are present. From 2000 to 2023, yearly frequencies of suitable conditions rose for both species, with surges reaching 34% of the Canadian population temporarily living under suitable conditions for P. falciparum and 56% for P. vivax. The authors conclude climate change increases the risk of autochthonous malaria transmission in Canada.

articles.headline: Anopheles diversity and malaria transmission across western Cameroon altitudes
articles.title_original: Diversity of anopheline species and malaria transmission dynamics in high-altitude areas of western Cameroon.
articles.authors: Kiam BC, Bouopda-Tuedom AG, Mbida Mbida JA, Ibrahima I, White SJ, Tchuenkam PVK, Popkin-Hall ZR, Mbouh M, Nanssong V CT, Abate L, Onguene CJ, Tumamo Fotso B, Sadler JM, Parr JB, Lin JT, Juliano JJ, Ali IM, Dinglasan RR, Nsango SE
articles.details: This entomological survey characterized malaria vectors at three localities along an altitudinal gradient (700 m, 1400 m, 1500 m) using human landing catches in 17 villages from May to June 2023, identifying species by morphology and PCR. Of 2835 Anopheles collected, An. gambiae s.l. dominated (82.88%), followed by An. funestus s.l. (15.92%). Human-biting rate was highest at Penka Michel (45.25 b/h/n) versus Santchou (3.1) and Dschang (0.41) (p<0.001), with an entomological inoculation rate 13 times higher at Penka Michel (1.11 vs 0.08 infective bites/human/night). P. falciparum dominated, with P. malariae and P. ovale also detected. Authors stress continued surveillance and added control tools like larviciding.

articles.headline: P. falciparum egress disrupts blood-brain barrier via JAK-STAT
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum egress disrupts endothelial junctions and activates JAK-STAT signaling in a microvascular 3D blood-brain barrier model.
articles.authors: Piatti L, Batzilla A, Nakaki F, Fleckenstein H, Korbmacher F, Long RKM, Schraivogel D, Hawkins JA, Romero-Uruñuela T, López-Gutiérrez B, Sanz Sender S, Schwab Y, Steinmetz LM, Sharpe J, Bernabeu M
articles.details: Researchers built a 3D blood-brain barrier model using primary brain endothelial cells, astrocytes, and pericytes to study cerebral malaria. Exposure to P. falciparum egress products increased microvascular permeability, downregulated junctional and vascular development genes, raised ferroptosis, antigen presentation, and type I interferon markers, and upregulated JAK-STAT signaling across all barrier cell types. Cytoadherent schizont-stage infected erythrocytes caused similar but localized changes with inter-endothelial gaps at egress sites. The JAK-STAT inhibitor Ruxolitinib prevented the permeability increase, suggesting a potential adjunctive therapy target.

articles.headline: Time-series forecasting of malaria in pregnancy across Nigeria
articles.title_original: Time series analysis of malaria in pregnancy, using wavelet and SARIMAX models.
articles.authors: Oniyelu DO, Folorunsho O, Adewole L, Bakare EA, Okoronkwo C, Eze N
articles.details: This study analyzed malaria in pregnancy (MIP) variability across Nigeria from January 2015 to January 2025 using wavelet coherence and comparing ARIMA versus SARIMAX models to forecast short-term incidence over two years. Significant regional variability emerged, with Cross River peaking in 2017 and 2019 and Enugu troughing in 2017; strong MIP-general malaria cross-correlations appeared in Kebbi, Niger, Yobe, and Ondo. SARIMAX captured trends better and was adopted for Cross River, Enugu, Ondo, and Yobe, while ARIMA suited Kebbi and Niger. Authors emphasize climate-sensitive forecasting and seasonal, targeted interventions to support elimination goals.

articles.headline: Modeling target product profiles for malaria gene-drive mosquitoes
articles.title_original: A model-informed target product profile for population modification gene drives for malaria control.
articles.authors: Mondal A, Sánchez C HM, Marshall JM
articles.details: Using an eco-epidemiological model, researchers identified parameter values for population-modification gene drives meeting two target outcomes: a 50% reduction in clinical malaria incidence sustained at least three years, and a time-to-impact under one year. They modeled two African settings (Burkina Faso and Kenya) at three transmission intensities each, varying homing rates, resistance allele generation, fitness costs, and effector efficacy. Meeting the window-of-protection criterion also satisfied time-to-impact. Outcomes were most influenced by gene-drive allele fitness cost and effector efficacy, with low functional-resistance generation preferred; existing Anopheles homing rates suffice. Key field-measurable parameters pose an assessment challenge.

articles.headline: Screen finds novel compounds active against malaria hypnozoites
articles.title_original: Identification of novel compounds with prophylactic activity against hypnozoites using a Plasmodium cynomolgi in vitro model.
articles.authors: Zeeman A-M, Vermaat L, Castellote Alvaro MI, Van der Werff NM, Glas-Nieuwenhuis IG, Harupa-Chung A, Calderón F, Gamo F-J, Fernandez Alvaro E, Kocken CH
articles.details: To find compounds with prophylactic activity against Plasmodium liver stages, particularly hypnozoites, 568 compounds from the GSK open-access TCAMS library were tested in vitro against Plasmodium cynomolgi liver stages. Twelve compounds showed IC50 values below 2 µM against hypnozoites, with the most active reaching nanomolar activity, and active compounds fell into six chemical clusters. Some were linked to known modes of action such as cytochrome bc1 or kinase inhibition, while others remain unknown. Authors attribute the high hit rate to preselecting compounds active against gametocytes and other Plasmodium liver stages, endorsing this preselection approach.

articles.headline: GABA receptor RDL controls hearing sensitivity in malaria mosquitoes
articles.title_original: The GABA(A) receptor RDL modulates the auditory sensitivity of malaria mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Ellis DA, Alampounti AC, Suppermpool A, Georgiades M, Freeman E, Bagi J, Tytheridge S, Terrazas-Duque D, Albert JT, Andrés M
articles.details: This laboratory study investigated how GABA signaling shapes auditory function in malaria mosquitoes, focusing on the GABAA receptor resistance to Dieldrin (RDL), an insecticide target, and its interaction with octopamine. Antagonizing RDL with picrotoxin altered the ear's anatomy and mechanical state, effects abolished in octopamine receptor AgOctβ2 knockouts, indicating octopamine dependence. Picrotoxin also increased spontaneous antennal nerve firing and enhanced mechanically evoked responses, effects only partially present in AgOctβ2 mutants. The authors conclude RDL controls auditory sensitivity and is implicated in mating of natural mosquito populations.

articles.headline: Novel filarial worms and Plasmodium found in Thai fighting chickens
articles.title_original: Discovery of potentially novel species of the Onchocercidae (Nematoda: Filarioidea) in Burmese fighting chickens (Gallus gallus): Genetic insights into avian filariasis and co-infection with Plasmodium juxtanucleare.
articles.authors: Narapakdeesakul D, Junsiri W, Kongtawee R, Lattisarapunt K, Taweethavonsawat P
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: Researchers used microscopy and cox1 molecular diagnostics to examine 12 Burmese fighting chickens (Gallus gallus) in Nakhon Ratchasima Province, eastern Thailand, for filarioids and haemoparasite co-infection. Two of 12 chickens tested positive for onchocercid filarioids, including one mixed infection requiring subcloning of two distinct isolates (CH07-S1 and CH07-S2); phylogenetic analysis suggested potentially novel onchocercid species or genera distinct from known avian filariae. Plasmodium juxtanucleare was identified in 6 of 12 chickens, including one co-infection with onchocercids. The authors highlight molecular tools for revealing filarial diversity and emerging avian filariasis hotspots.

articles.headline: Primaquine mass drug administration eliminates vivax malaria in Myanmar villages
articles.title_original: Efficacy of Focal Primaquine Mass Administration for Eliminating Plasmodium vivax Malaria in Northern Myanmar: A Cluster-Randomized Trial.
articles.authors: Aung PL, Soe MT, Soe TN, Zhao Y, Cao Y, Aung PP, Oo TL, Lawpoolsri S, Nguitragool W, Sattabongkot J, Kaewkungwal J, Kyaw MP, Parker DM, Cui L
articles.details: This cluster-randomized crossover trial tested primaquine mass drug administration (PQ-MDA), 0.25 mg/kg/day PQ base for 14 days as directly observed therapy, against Plasmodium vivax transmission in northern Myanmar, a subtropical area with perennial transmission. Two village groups alternated as intervention and control, switching after 9 months; PQ was administered to 541 participants in group 1 and 667 in group 2. Clinical P. vivax incidence sharply declined to zero within a month and remained vivax-free for almost a year. Subclinical P. vivax prevalence dropped significantly (round 1 adjusted OR 0.46, 95% CI 0.25-0.83; round 2 aOR 0.15, 95% CI 0.03-0.65), and the authors conclude PQ-MDA effectively reduced both clinical and subclinical infections.

articles.headline: New sequencing assay distinguishes vivax malaria relapse from reinfection
articles.title_original: Microhaplotype deep sequencing assays to capture Plasmodium vivax infection lineages.
articles.authors: Kleinecke M, Sutanto E, Rumaseb A, Hoon KS, Trimarsanto H, Osborne A, Manrique P, Peters T, Hawkes D, Benavente ED, Whitton G, Siegel SV, Pearson RD, Amato R, Rai A, Nhien NTT, Nguyen HC, Assefa A, Degaga TS, Abate DT, Rahim AG, Pasaribu AP, Sutanto I, Alam MS, Pava Z, Lopera-Mesa T, Echeverry D, William T, Anstey NM, Grigg MJ, Day NP, White NJ, Kwiatkowski DP, Taylor AR, Noviyanti R, Neafsey D, Price RN, Auburn S
articles.details: Researchers developed a high-throughput amplicon deep-sequencing assay of 93 multi-SNP microhaplotype markers to measure identity-by-descent (IBD) between Plasmodium vivax clinical isolates, aiming to distinguish recrudescence, reinfection, and relapse. The assay was evaluated in 745 global infections, including 128 infection pairs from a randomized controlled trial (NCT01680406). Simulations showed low pairwise IBD estimation error (RMSE < 0.12), and IBD networks clustered strongly by geography. IBD analysis showed fewer suspected relapses or recrudescence in patients treated with primaquine, with greater impact when paired with chloroquine than with artemether-lumefantrine. The authors conclude IBD from amplicon sequencing yields new insights into P. vivax treatment and transmission.

articles.headline: Tafenoquine kills blood-stage malaria parasites by blocking hemozoin formation
articles.title_original: Tafenoquine Disrupts Intraerythrocytic Stages of Malaria Parasites by Inhibiting Nonclassical Pathways of β-Hematin Nucleation and Growth.
articles.authors: Lee HJ, Massih S, Abdullah M, Rimer JD, Sullivan DJ, Vekilov PG
articles.details: This mechanistic study examined how the antimalarial tafenoquine (TQ) disrupts intraerythrocytic malaria parasites by interfering with heme detoxification. Using β-hematin, a synthetic analogue of hemozoin, the authors showed TQ blocks a fraction of crystal growth sites and kinks, mildly inhibits classical layer-based growth, and potently suppresses a nonclassical growth mode and crystal nucleation by impeding formation of mesoscopic hematin-rich clusters that serve as shared precursors. Parasite-killing assays showed a stage-specific effect, with greater ring-stage suppression when crystals nucleate than trophozoite suppression during crystal growth. The authors conclude TQ disrupts parasite survival by inhibiting hemozoin synthesis alongside mitochondrial disruption.

articles.headline: Computational screen identifies Penicillium compounds as PfLDH antimalarial leads
articles.title_original: Penicillium-Derived Inhibitors of Plasmodium falciparum Lactate Dehydrogenase (PfLDH): A Computational Approach for Novel Antimalarial Therapy Development.
articles.authors: Sama-Ae I, Muengthongon P, Tohlaeh A, Rukhachan W, Kiattikul P, Samaeng F, Mitklin A, Rahman MA, Tedasen A, Kwankaew P, Kotepui M, Kepan A
articles.details: This in silico study screened a curated library of Penicillium-derived secondary metabolites as inhibitors of Plasmodium falciparum lactate dehydrogenase (PfLDH), a key glycolytic enzyme. Drug-likeness and toxicity filtering yielded 42 candidates, and molecular docking identified three—Penicilactone B, Penicillimide, and Penicillisocoumarin A—with binding affinities exceeding the controls NADH and pyruvate. Penicilactone B showed the strongest binding (-8.71 kcal/mol) and lowest inhibitory constant (414.77 nM), with 200-ns molecular dynamics confirming stable binding-pocket interactions and off-target predictions suggesting minimal human LDH interaction. The authors conclude these compounds are promising antimalarial leads warranting experimental validation.

articles.headline: Multi-compartment immune monitoring in severe malaria-associated ARDS case
articles.title_original: Monitoring of deep lymphocyte phenotypes in the blood and bronchoalveolar lavage fluid of patients with severe malaria-associated acute respiratory distress syndrome.
articles.authors: Gomes AMC, Adão-Serrano M, Cunha MRD, Silva JS, Sousa AE, Ribeiro JM, Fernandes SM
articles.details: This longitudinal case report describes a patient with severe malaria-associated acute respiratory distress syndrome supported by venovenous extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO), using deep lymphocyte phenotyping of both blood and bronchoalveolar lavage fluid. Despite persistent lymphopenia, the authors observed dynamic shifts in T cells and rare innate lymphoid cell populations, with a predominant type 2 immune profile at the acute phase and decreasing exhausted T-cell populations indicating recovery. Blood and bronchoalveolar lavage fluid profiles differed significantly. The authors conclude multiple-compartment immune monitoring is feasible and valuable for guiding precise immune modulation.

articles.headline: Scoping review of social determinants driving malaria vulnerability in Southeast Asia
articles.title_original: Malaria and determinants of health: a scoping review of malaria vulnerabilities in Southeast Asia.
articles.authors: Md Hanif SA, Hassan MR, Safian N, Sutan R, Alabed AAA, Rafi'i MR, Md Asari SN, Naserrudin NA, Dapari R
articles.details: This scoping review, following PRISMA-ScR guidelines, examined how social determinants of health shape malaria vulnerability in Southeast Asian populations. A systematic search of PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science for open-access quantitative studies identified 22 eligible studies covering various endemic settings, including cross-border regions and migrant populations. Findings were synthesized into five thematic domains: biological influences, demographic and socioeconomic parameters, built and lived environments, behaviour and practices, and access to healthcare services and information. The authors conclude that malaria vulnerability is multifaceted and requires region-specific, multi-sectoral strategies and strengthened health systems.

articles.headline: P. malariae rRNA gene variant impairs nested PCR detection
articles.title_original: First report of a Plasmodium malariae SSU rRNA gene variant in Africa associated with reduced amplification by nested PCR.
articles.authors: Goto M, Yamamoto K, Komaki-Yasuda K, Kano S, Ohmagari N
articles.details: This case report describes a 56-year-old Japanese man who developed fever two months after a two-month stay in Sierra Leone, with quartan malaria suspected on blood smear and Plasmodium malariae confirmed by nested PCR. The species-specific secondary PCR band was fainter than the universal-primer primary band, a reversal of the typical pattern; sequencing revealed a four-base deletion in the SSU rRNA (18S rRNA) gene within the species-specific reverse primer binding site, suggesting impaired amplification. Cytochrome b sequencing showed 100% identity with P. malariae. The authors stress the need for continued molecular surveillance and sequence-based validation for accurate diagnosis.

articles.headline: Machine learning and spatial analysis map global malaria burden determinants
articles.title_original: Unraveling global malaria incidence and mortality using machine learning and artificial intelligence-driven spatial analysis.
articles.authors: Rahman MS, Shiddik MAB
articles.details: This study applied spatial analysis, the XGBoost machine learning classifier, and explainable and causal AI to data from 106 countries between 2000 and 2022, sourced from the WHO, World Bank, and UNICEF, to identify malaria high-risk areas and determinants. In 2022 malaria cases reached 251.75 million, with peak fatalities of 99,554 in 2020; Nigeria recorded the highest incidence, followed by the Democratic Republic of the Congo and India, while South Sudan, Zambia, and the Central African Republic had the highest mortality. The XGBoost model performed best (RMSE=0.63, r²=0.93, MAE=0.46), identifying basic sanitation access, electricity, population growth, and under-5 mortality as key determinants. The authors advocate integrating AI-driven spatial models into national surveillance.

articles.headline: Vitamin B5 shapes feeding and reproduction in Anopheles stephensi mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Pantothenate regulates feeding and reproduction in the malaria vector Anopheles stephensi, with patterns dependent on supplementation scheme and parental nutrition.
articles.authors: Dobson ME, Kaylor HL, Pruett SL, Brady J, Savoie-Penton K, Isoe J, Debebe Y, Riehle MA, Luckhart S
articles.details: This laboratory study tested how pantothenate (vitamin B5), the precursor of coenzyme A, affects the malaria vector Anopheles stephensi when supplemented via either water (slowly released from the crop into the midgut) or blood (delivered directly to the midgut). Researchers assessed blood-feeding behavior, reproduction, and offspring sex ratio across multiple generations, with and without offspring supplementation. Pantothenate regulated vector traits in concentration-, delivery-, and age-dependent ways, with the largest effects on reproduction; unsupplemented offspring of water-supplemented mothers showed increased fecundity (a transgenerational effect), whereas blood supplementation altered reproduction only in mothers and their supplemented offspring. The authors suggest targeting pantothenate–CoA biology could enable novel mosquito control strategies.

articles.headline: Dry season and polyclonal infection linked to higher Plasmodium gametocyte carriage
articles.title_original: Risk factor for gametocyte carriage and gametocytemia in Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Li M, Bian Y, Ruan S, Wu Z, Zhang D, Ma T, Wu Y, Liu X, Wang D, Lin J, Pan D, Cui W, Wang L, Wei H, Zhang X, Wang Q, Zeng W, Yang Z, Cao Y, Cui L, Parker DM, Zhao Y
articles.details: This study analyzed risk factors for gametocyte carriage and gametocytemia in P. vivax and P. falciparum using thick smears collected from 2011 to 2020 in Laiza Township, Kachin State, Myanmar, with clonality genotyped at Pvmsp3α/β and Pfmsp1/2. Among 8240 P. vivax infections, 7249 were gametocyte-positive; among 510 P. falciparum cases, 56 were positive. A significant P. vivax gametocyte cluster occurred May 2015–August 2017 (P=0.001). For P. vivax, dry season, prior malaria, fever, and parasite density were associated with carriage; gametocyte density rose with asexual density (P<0.001) but was lower in rainy season and with prior infection. Polyclonal P. vivax infections had higher gametocyte densities than monoclonal (P<0.0001). Authors highlight seasonal strategies for elimination.

articles.headline: Modeling vaccine properties needed for lasting childhood malaria protection
articles.title_original: Performance characteristics and potential public health impact of improved pre-erythrocytic malaria vaccines targeting childhood burden.
articles.authors: Malinga J, Braunack-Mayer L, Masserey T, Cavelan A, Chandramohan D, Dicko A, Ouédraogo JB, Kelly SL, Le Rutte EA, Nekkab N, Penny MA
articles.details: Using an individual-based malaria transmission model informed by trial data, researchers quantified how pre-erythrocytic vaccine (PEV) efficacy, duration of protection, dose spacing, endemicity, deployment, and coverage affect prevention of uncomplicated and severe childhood malaria. A vaccine with 90% initial efficacy against infection, a 6–12 month half-life of protection, co-administered with a blood-stage drug and followed by yearly boosters, produced 60–80% yearly incidence reduction, consistent with seasonal RTS,S and R21 trials. Halting vaccination after five years still sustained at least 35% incidence reduction in children under six over the next 12 months where PfPR2-10 <30%. Longer half-life (12–18 months) or higher coverage achieved equal impact at lower efficacy. The authors call for well-defined target product profiles linking vaccine properties to deployment use cases.

articles.headline: Correction notice for Panama asymptomatic malaria RT-MqPCR detection study
articles.title_original: Correction: Detection through the use of RT-MqPCR of asymptomatic reservoirs of malaria in samples of patients from the indigenous Comarca of Guna Yala, Panama: Essential method to achieve the elimination of malaria.
articles.authors: Cáceres Carrera L, Santamaría AM, Castillo AM, Romero L, Urriola E, Torres-Cosme R, Calzada JE
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Review proposes targeting Plasmodium glutathione S-transferases against drug resistance
articles.title_original: Targeting Plasmodium glutathione S-transferases to overcome antimalaria drug resistance in Southern Africa.
articles.authors: Otun OS, Achilonu I
articles.details: This review examines antimalarial drug resistance in Southern Africa and proposes Plasmodium glutathione S-transferases (GSTs)—multifunctional enzymes involved in detoxification and redox control—as molecular targets for new therapies. The authors note that while artemisinin resistance has not yet been observed in the region, molecular surveillance in sub-Saharan Africa has identified P. falciparum k13 mutations known to confer resistance. They discuss the role of GSTs in resistance development and present GST inhibition as a potential adjunct therapy and pre-emptive strategy to protect current treatment regimens and consolidate malaria control.

articles.headline: Hepatitis A causes liver failure, later autoimmune hepatitis, in young girl
articles.title_original: Hepatitis A-Induced Fulminant Hepatic Failure Linked to Autoimmune Hepatitis in a Child Amidst Diagnostic Challenges of Dengue and Malaria.
articles.authors: Bilal M, Ajmal MU, Ali A
articles.details: This case report describes a previously healthy five-year-old girl presenting with a four-day history of high-grade fever, abdominal pain, vomiting, and lethargy, with pallor, icterus, hepatosplenomegaly, and right-sided pleural effusion on examination. Initial thrombocytopenia, leukopenia, and ascites prompted a provisional diagnosis of dengue hemorrhagic fever, but dengue serology was negative; further workup revealed HAV IgM positivity and Plasmodium vivax parasitemia. Her condition worsened with bilateral pleural effusions and altered mental status consistent with mild hepatic encephalopathy, and she was managed conservatively without liver transplantation using intravenous antibiotics, oral antimalarials, lactulose, rifaximin, and transfusional support. Persistent transaminitis with ANA positivity (1:320, speckled) suggested evolving type 1 autoimmune hepatitis, which responded favourably to oral corticosteroids.

articles.headline: Rickettsiosis may trigger Plasmodium vivax relapse in returned traveler
articles.title_original: Acute Rickettsiosis Triggering Plasmodium vivax Relapse in a Returned Traveler: A Case Report and Clinical Review of Travel-Related Coinfections.
articles.authors: Bagga R, Fuller C, Shahzad K, Bado E, Joshi J, Fernando D, Hempel A, Boggild AK
articles.details: This case report and clinical review describes a febrile traveler returning to Canada from South America with confirmed intercurrent rickettsial and Plasmodium vivax infection, where the rickettsiosis potentially triggered a relapse of P. vivax. The authors note that confirming or excluding malaria without subsequent rickettsial testing—especially with P. vivax or P. ovale—may mask the possibility of relapse, and that low clinical suspicion of co-infections, limited knowledge of disease geographic distribution, and lack of point-of-care testing can cause missed or misdiagnosis. The article reviews the literature on tropical coinfections in returning travelers.

articles.headline: New PX1 locus lowers P. falciparum drug susceptibility, spreading in Uganda
articles.title_original: A novel locus associated with decreased susceptibility of Plasmodium falciparum to lumefantrine and dihydroartemisinin has emerged and spread in Uganda.
articles.authors: Niare K, Tafesse B, Treat M, Sadler J, Okitwi M, Orena S, Asua V, Kreutzfeld O, Legac J, Samuel NL, Yeka A, Rosenthal PJ, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Conrad MD
articles.details: Using whole-genome Plasmodium falciparum sequences from Uganda, researchers assessed signatures of selection to identify loci tied to decreased drug susceptibility amid emerging artemisinin partial resistance and declining lumefantrine susceptibility. Extended shared haplotypes appeared for Kelch13 C469Y and A675V mutations, but the strongest recent-selection signal centered on chromosome 7 at the phosphoinositide-binding protein gene px1 (PF3D7_0720700). A PIN haplotype (PX1 SNPs L1222P, M1701I, D1705N plus two indels), first seen in 2008, rose to >50% prevalence in northern Uganda by 2016 and eastern Uganda by 2023. PIN-carrying parasites showed significantly decreased ex vivo IC50 susceptibilities to lumefantrine, mefloquine, and dihydroartemisinin, with or without K13 mutations.

articles.headline: High IPTp uptake linked to low malaria prevalence in pregnant Nigerian women
articles.title_original: Intermittent preventive treatment, malaria, HIV, hepatitis and pregnancy outcomes in Nigerian women: a cross-sectional study in two healthcare facilities.
articles.authors: Chukwuanukwu RC, Eleje GU, Menkiti FE, Ikpeze GC, Mbachu CN, Obiegbu NP, Ogbomade CC, Anyaegbu HI, Ehiaghe AF, Ezeagwuna DA, Efobi CC, Egwuatu EC, Orjinta KK, James JE, Agbanu CM, Onubuogu CU, Ezenwa OB, Okafor CD, Onah KS, Chukwuanukwu TO
articles.details: This dual hospital-based cross-sectional study in two facilities in Anambra State, Nigeria, assessed intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp) use and the prevalence of malaria, HIV, and hepatitis, plus premature rupture of membranes (PROM), among 134 pregnant women who provided blood samples. Malaria was found in 8 (5.97%), HIV in 5 (3.73%), and hepatitis B in 6 (4.47%); preeclampsia occurred in 5 (3.73%), PROM in 3 (2.24%), and preterm deliveries in 5.2%. IPTp use was relatively high at 73.9%. The authors concluded malaria prevalence was lower than prior regional reports, likely due to high IPTp implementation, emphasizing its role in reducing malaria in pregnancy.

articles.headline: Survey detects avian Plasmodium and West Nile virus in Spanish wetland Culex
articles.title_original: Vector surveillance of avian Plasmodium and West Nile virus in Culex mosquitoes from Doñana, a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
articles.authors: Martínez-de la Puente J, Tamayo-Quintero J, Ruiz-López MJ, Veiga J, Ruiz S, Vázquez A, Herrero L, Soriguer RC, Figuerola J
articles.details: This vector surveillance study molecularly screened mosquitoes from a highly protected wetland in southern Spain for West Nile virus (WNV), avian Plasmodium, and filarioid nematodes. Across 95 pools comprising 1376 females of 4 Culex species (390 Cx. pipiens, 880 Cx. perexiguus, 102 Cx. modestus, 4 Cx. laticinctus), WNV was detected in 5 Cx. perexiguus pools, and avian Plasmodium in 28 pools (17 Cx. perexiguus, 11 Cx. pipiens). Three Plasmodium lineages were found—P. vaughani (SYAT05; n=22), P. matutinum (LINN1; n=4), and Plasmodium sp. (SGS2; n=1)—with prevalence significantly associated with mosquito species and sampling session. No pools were positive for Dirofilaria spp.

articles.headline: High pfhrp2/3 gene deletions threaten malaria RDTs in Amhara, Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Deletion of target gene (histidine-rich protein 2/3) for Plasmodium falciparum rapid diagnostic tests in Amhara region, Ethiopia: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Adamu A, Alemu G, Yimer M, Tegegne B, Mekasha S
articles.details: This health facility-based cross-sectional study across 28 facilities in three survey domains of the Amhara region, Ethiopia, enrolled 1232 microscopy-confirmed P. falciparum patients and used RDTs, microscopy, and PCR to assess pfhrp2/pfhrp3 gene deletions. PCR was performed on 179 samples; 158 (88.3%) carried one or both deletions, with 92 (51.4%) dual pfhrp2/3, 4 (2.2%) pfhrp2-only, and 62 (34.6%) pfhrp3 deletions. Among 109 false-negative RDT results, 102 (93.6%) were attributable to pfhrp2/3 deletions. The authors conclude prevalence exceeds the WHO 5% threshold and recommend continued use of LDH-based Biocredit RDTs.

articles.headline: Community-led Bti larviciding sharply cuts mosquito numbers in The Gambia
articles.title_original: Empowering communities for malaria control: Effectiveness of community-led biolarviciding using Bacillus thuringiensis israelensis in The Gambia.
articles.authors: Jassey B, Yudhastuti R, Manjang B, Touray I, Ridha MR, Diyanah KC, Indah Permatasari F
articles.details: A non-randomized controlled trial in malaria-endemic regions of The Gambia compared community-led versus expert-supervised application of Bacillus thuringiensis var. israelensis (Bti) larvicide against an untreated control, with biweekly entomological surveys analyzed by generalized linear mixed models and negative binomial regression. By round 10, community-led and expert-supervised arms achieved 96.8% and 98.6% reductions in larval density, 97.4% and 99.1% reductions in pupal emergence, and 96.2% and 98.8% reductions in adult mosquito populations, respectively (p < 0.001), with over 85% of participants reporting visible mosquito reduction. The authors conclude community-led biolarviciding is a scalable, sustainable vector control strategy.

articles.headline: Bayesian model maps emerging malaria hotspots across Cameroon health districts
articles.title_original: Identifying hotspots of malaria incidence and mortality for tailored interventions in Cameroon using routine data from 2011 to 2021: A Bayesian space-time variability modeling.
articles.authors: Arnold FF, Kirakoya-Samadoulougou F, Marcelin A, Jean F, Ye Y, Samadoulougou S
articles.details: This study applied a Bayesian spatiotemporal variability model to routine malaria data from 189 contiguous health districts in Cameroon to classify spatial trends into hotspots, coldspots, and neutral-spots for uncomplicated incidence, severe incidence, and deaths. Decreasing trends were seen in 42.9% (81), 44.9% (85), and 28.6% (54) of districts for uncomplicated, severe, and death outcomes, while increasing trends appeared in 44.9% (85), 37.6% (71), and 32.8% (62) of districts, respectively. Additionally, 24.3% (46), 23.3% (44), and 21.7% (41) of districts had a high likelihood of becoming hotspots. The authors conclude space-time modeling can support proactive, targeted interventions.

articles.headline: Dietary lycopene reduces malaria oxidative damage in mouse brain and lungs
articles.title_original: Lycopene Mitigates Malaria-Induced Reactive Oxygen and Nitrogen Species and Oxidative Damage in Mice Brain and Lungs.
articles.authors: Varela ELP, Gomes ARQ, da Silva Barbosa Dos Santos A, Dos Santos Guimarães M, de Carvalho EP, Ferreira OO, de Oliveira MS, Iriti M, de Aguiar Andrade EH, Percário S
articles.details: In this animal study, mice were infected with Plasmodium berghei via intraperitoneal injection of 10^6 parasitized red blood cells and treated orally with lycopene (3.11 mg/kg bw/day) or N-acetylcysteine (NAC, 62 mg/kg bw/day), with evaluations at 1, 4, 8, and 12 days post-infection. Brain and lung markers measured included TBARS, antioxidant capacity (ABTS and DPPH), uric acid, and nitric oxide. Infection elevated these markers and caused mortality, while lycopene significantly attenuated infection-induced increases in TBARS, uric acid, and nitric oxide versus the Pb and NAC+Pb groups (p < 0.0001), normalizing them toward Sham levels. The authors highlight lycopene's potential against malaria-related oxidative stress.

articles.headline: Mass azithromycin lowered child malaria parasitemia only before seasonal chemoprevention
articles.title_original: Biannual Mass Azithromycin Distributions for Preschool Children and Malaria Parasitemia: A Secondary Analysis of the MORDOR Cluster Randomized Trial.
articles.authors: Arzika AM, Abdou A, Maliki R, Lebas E, Cook C, Vanderschelden B, O'Brien KS, Cotter SY, Varnado NE, Callahan EK, Bailey RL, West SK, Rosenthal PJ, Porco TC, Lietman TM, Keenan JD
articles.details: This cluster randomized trial in Niger, conducted as an ancillary trial to a larger study of mass azithromycin and child mortality, compared biannual mass distribution of oral azithromycin (single 20 mg/kg dose) versus placebo in children aged 1-59 months across 30 communities (15 azithromycin, 1695 children; 15 placebo, 3031 children), with parasitemia assessed in a random sample of 40 children per community. Baseline parasitemia was 8.9% (azithromycin) and 6.7% (placebo). Through month 48, parasitemia was not significantly lower with azithromycin (-3.3 percentage points; 95% CI, -5.8 to -0.2; permutation P = .05), but in the period before seasonal malaria chemoprevention was introduced (after the month 36 visit) it was significantly lower (4.8 PP lower; 95% CI, -7.4 to -1.3; P = .02). The benefit was significant only for the first three years of the trial.

articles.headline: Fluctuating warm, humid conditions reprogram Anopheles stephensi immunity and development
articles.title_original: Fluctuating Warm and Humid Conditions Differentially Impact Immunity and Development in the Malaria Vector Anopheles stephensi.
articles.authors: Lemos-Silva T, De Neef E, Valgaerts Y, Simões ML
articles.details: Laboratory experiments reared Anopheles stephensi from egg to adult under diurnal fluctuations of +4°C and +10% relative humidity above baseline, then assessed innate immune gene expression, development, longevity, and response to bacterial infection. Elevated fluctuating conditions primed basal immune responses and accelerated pre-adult development without reducing adult lifespan; bacterial infection under these conditions caused complex immune reprogramming and enhanced larval and adult survival. The conditions also altered transcriptional activity of promoters used to express transgenes in genetically modified mosquitoes. Authors conclude immune priming could lower vectorial capacity initially, but post-challenge immune suppression could increase Plasmodium susceptibility, with implications for transmission.

articles.headline: Review of behavioral health interventions for malaria control
articles.title_original: Behavioral health interventions in malaria control: Efficacy and implementation.
articles.authors: Obeagu EI, Obeagu GU, Iduh MU
articles.details: This review examines the efficacy and implementation of behavioral health interventions in malaria control, focusing on community education, behavioral change communication, and community-based strategies. Community education improved awareness and uptake of insecticide-treated nets and early treatment-seeking; behavioral change communication used mass media, interpersonal communication, and social mobilization; community-based interventions fostered ownership, sustainability, and reductions in malaria prevalence. The authors note challenges including cultural barriers, resource limitations, and weak monitoring and evaluation, and recommend culturally tailored approaches, sustainable funding, capacity building, multi-sectoral collaboration, and innovative communication technologies to integrate behavioral strategies with existing medical measures. No quantitative effect sizes are reported.

articles.headline: Larval plastic exposure alters gut microbiota of Anopheles arabiensis
articles.title_original: The Effect of Larval Exposure to Plastic Pollution on the Gut Microbiota of the Major Malaria Vector Anopheles arabiensis Patton (Diptera: Culicidae).
articles.authors: Misser S, Chen CY, Ismail A, Oliver SV
articles.details: This study examined how larval exposure to degraded plastic, plastic additives (phthalic acid, Bisphenol-A), and latex beads affects the adult gut microbiota of Anopheles arabiensis, comparing insecticide-unselected (SENN) and insecticide-selected (SENN-DDT) strains. Larval exposure had minimal effect on alpha-diversity, but each plastic stressor altered beta-diversity in a non-strain-specific manner. Plastic-treated SENN showed more unique bacterial genera, while untreated SENN-DDT had the highest abundance of unique genera. Exposure increased bacteria associated with plastic degradation and pesticide metabolism. There was no significant change in Plasmodium-protective genera, but inflammation-associated genera increased in both strains, suggesting potential immune modulation.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence 8% in Tanzanian children 6-59 months
articles.title_original: Prevalence and determinants of malaria among children aged 6-59 months in Tanzania: a nationwide cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Adam J, Luoga P, Nyamhanga T, Makunenge C, Ayubu M
articles.details: This secondary analysis of 2022 Tanzanian Demographic and Health Survey and Malaria Indicator Survey data examined a weighted sample of 4,923 children aged 6-59 months who underwent malaria rapid diagnostic testing, with associations assessed via adjusted odds ratios. Malaria prevalence was 8% (95% CI 6.2-9.5), highest in Tabora (23%) and below 1% in Dodoma, Arusha, Kilimanjaro, Singida, Songwe, and Zanzibar. Significant determinants were age 24-59 months (aOR 1.71), rural residence (aOR 6.92), lower economic status (aOR 3.08), and never sleeping under an insecticide-treated net (aOR 2.73). The authors call for targeted prevention in rural and economically disadvantaged populations and improved access to ITNs.

articles.headline: Bayesian spatio-temporal model maps declining malaria risk in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Bayesian spatio-temporal modeling and prediction of malaria cases in Tanzania mainland (2016-2023): unveiling associations with climate and intervention factors.
articles.authors: Njotto LL, Senyoni W, Cronie O, Stensgaard AS
articles.details: This study analyzed aggregated monthly malaria counts from Tanzania mainland regions over 2016-2023 using Standardized Incidence Ratios and a Bayesian spatio-temporal model (INLA) with CAR spatial structure and RW2 temporal effects, stratified by age (<5 and ≥5 years). It recorded 23.4 million cases in those aged ≥5 years and 17.3 million in children under 5. Malaria risk decreased 11.0% in children under 5 and 10.0% in those ≥5 years; long-lasting insecticide nets reduced risk by 1.2% (under 5) and 7.0% (≥5). Minimum temperature, wind speed, and high NDVI were associated with increased risk, with various lagged climatic effects. Authors conclude these environmental factors and spatial maps are critical for targeted control.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria linked to poorer growth in young Malawian children
articles.title_original: Impact of asymptomatic malaria infection on children's growth in rural Malawi.
articles.authors: Sady H, Chaima D, Hallamaa L, Ashorn U, Banda J, Mangani C, Kamwendo J, Maleta K, Ashorn P, Fan YM
articles.details: In a longitudinal analysis nested in the LCNI-5 clinical trial, dried blood spots from 840 children aged 6-18 months (N=697 at baseline, sampled every 3 months for a year) in rural Malawi were tested for asymptomatic P. falciparum by real-time PCR and linked to growth Z-scores. Malaria-positive children had lower mean weight-for-age Z-score (-1.03 vs. -0.87, 95% CI -0.17 to -0.04) and weight-for-length Z-score (-0.03 vs. -0.13, 95% CI -0.22 to -0.06), while length-for-age showed no significant association. No significant effect was seen at individual time points except at 12 months. The association persisted after adjusting for confounders, suggesting asymptomatic malaria impairs child growth.

articles.headline: CYP2B6*6 genotype linked to slower artemether-lumefantrine parasite clearance in Uganda
articles.title_original: CYP2B6*6 single nucleotide polymorphism among patients with uncomplicated malaria in Adjumani district, Uganda: Implications on efficacy of artemether-lumefantrine.
articles.authors: Angwe MK, Mwebaza N, Nsobya SL, Kiguba R, Vudriko P, Dralabu S, Omali D, Tumwebaze MA, Ocan M
articles.details: This study genotyped 100 patients with microscopically confirmed uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria treated with artemether-lumefantrine at Adjumani District Hospital, West Nile region, Uganda, using PCR-RFLP on blood collected on days 0 and 3. Participants were 65% female and 35% male, mean age 17 ± 10 years. The CYP2B6*6 variant allele frequency was 0.37, with genotypes 43% GG, 17% TT, and 40% GT. Day 3 microscopy positivity was 14% (6/43) in GG, 37.5% (15/40) in GT, and 17.64% (3/17) in TT; corresponding PCR positivity was 58.1% (25/43), 72.5% (29/40), and 52.9% (9/17). Heterozygous (GT) patients had 10-fold slower parasite clearance than homozygous patients (U=323.0; Z=-2.3442; p=0.019), supporting incorporation of host metabolising-enzyme genotype into therapeutic efficacy studies.

articles.headline: Ruxolitinib rescues mice from lethal malaria hypoglycemia and hyperinflammation
articles.title_original: JAK/STAT inhibition protects glucocorticoid receptor knockout mice from lethal malaria-induced hypoglycemia and hyperinflammation.
articles.authors: Prenen F, Vandermosten L, Knoops S, Pollenus E, Possemiers H, Dagneau de Richecour P, Caratti G, Cawthorne C, Vettorazzi S, Cranshoff Y, Schols D, Claes S, Deroose CM, Himmelreich U, Tuckermann J, Van den Steen PE
articles.details: Using a mouse model of Plasmodium chabaudi AS infection, researchers showed that glucocorticoid receptor (GR)-mediated processes are essential for disease tolerance. GR deletion in infected mice caused lethal hypoglycemia and a cytokine storm, driven by liver and spleen metabolic dysfunction (increased glucose uptake, glycogen depletion, a glycolytic profile, and reduced gluconeogenic gene expression). This hypoglycemic state was strongly associated with overactivation of the JAK/STAT pathway and excessive cytokine expression. Treatment with the JAK1/2 inhibitor ruxolitinib significantly improved survival by preventing lethal hypoglycemia and suppressing hyperinflammation, highlighting it as a candidate adjuvant therapy for metabolic complications in severe malaria.

articles.headline: Review: apicoplast transporters as novel malaria drug targets
articles.title_original: The apicoplast transportome of the malaria parasite.
articles.authors: Akkerman LJ, Kooij TWA, de Vries LE
articles.details: This review discusses the transportome of the malaria parasite's apicoplast, a four-membrane organelle of red algal origin important for metabolic processes including isopentenyl pyrophosphate (IPP) and coenzyme A (CoA) biosynthesis. Because transporters supply substrates and export products for these pathways, they are proposed as excellent novel drug targets. The authors describe a Plasmodium apicoplast transportome comprising 11 confirmed and 17 putative transporters identified to date, and anticipate discovery of key players such as the IPP and CoA exporters, which could be exploited as drug targets.

articles.headline: Personal perspective: fighting malaria as a patient and scientist
articles.title_original: Fighting malaria as a patient and scientist.
articles.authors: Tadesse FG
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Better statistical models for measuring Plasmodium infection in mosquitoes
articles.title_original: When a mean can be meaningless: evaluating mosquito infections with Plasmodium parasites.
articles.authors: Ubiaru PC, Ranford-Cartwright LC
articles.details: This methods/opinion paper argues that generalized linear or mixed models are the most appropriate statistical tools for analyzing experimental or field data on Plasmodium infection levels in mosquitoes, where parasite distributions are non-normal and involve multiple variables. The authors propose specific approaches for datasets with groups showing zero or near-zero prevalence or many zero parasite counts, as expected from effective transmission-blocking interventions. They note these methods apply broadly to other parasitic infections with similar parasite-count patterns across hosts.

articles.headline: ART-heme adduct proposed as biomarker for artemisinin resistance in mice
articles.title_original: Pharmacokinetics and metabolism of artemisinin (ART) in Plasmodium yoelii: ART-heme adduct as a potential biomarker for its resistance.
articles.authors: Du S, Xu K, Liu Z, Xing J
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: Using a rodent model, researchers established an artemisinin (ART)-induced strain of Plasmodium yoelii (iPy) after prolonged ART pressure in infected mice and compared its metabolic and pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic profiles with the parent strain (Py). ART showed lower activity against iPy (ED90 2.9-fold higher), and iPy-infected mice survived at least 28 days. ART was detected in parasites as an ART-heme adduct, with Cmax reached at 3–5 h; plasma clearance rose 3–4-fold after multiple doses. After normalizing to infected RBC burden, ART-heme exposure (AUC0-t) in iPy parasites was 61.1% of that in Py. The authors conclude ART-heme adduct could be a biomarker for ART activity and resistance.

articles.headline: Hematology analyzer pilot detects malaria early in screening program
articles.title_original: [Pilot Project for Early Diagnosis of Malaria Infection Using a Hematology Analyzer].
articles.authors: Shin HE, Shin HI, Lee MR, Ju JW, Lee HI
articles.details: A pilot project in malaria-risk areas of the Republic of Korea evaluated whether routine hematological analysis could enable early malaria detection in asymptomatic or unsuspected individuals; 47 medical institutions participated. When infected red blood cells (iRBC) were identified, individuals were classified as suspected cases and referred for confirmatory testing. Between April and October 2024, among 7.27 million hematologic analyses, 1,359 iRBC cases were detected; of 499 cases referred for confirmation, 239 were confirmed malaria-positive. The authors conclude that proactive confirmatory testing of iRBC-flagged individuals can enhance early detection using existing medical systems and support elimination goals.

articles.headline: Plasmodium brasilianum detected in one wild Brazilian primate
articles.title_original: Molecular Surveillance of Plasmodium spp. Infection in Neotropical Primates from Bahia and Minas Gerais, Brazil.
articles.authors: Santos LKNSS, Aquino-Teixeira SM, Bernal-Valle S, Daltro BS, Noetzold M, Silva ARC, Alvarenga DAM, Silva LB, Oliveira RS, Oliveira CH, Celestino IA, Gonçalves-Dos-Santos ME, Teixeira TJ, Sevá AP, Campos FS, Ribeiro BM, Roehe PM, Simonini-Teixeira D, Abreu FVS, Brito CFA, Albuquerque GR
articles.details: This molecular surveillance study screened Neotropical primates for Plasmodium spp. across 47 municipalities in Bahia and Minas Gerais, Brazil, to assess zoonotic transmission risk. A total of 298 blood and tissue samples were collected from 10 primate species across five genera (e.g., 175 Callithrix penicillata, 31 Callithrix kuhlii, 28 Callithrix geoffroyi), using nested PCR targeting the 18S SSU rRNA gene followed by sequencing. Only one sample (0.3%), from Bahia, tested positive for Plasmodium brasilianum/P. malariae, representing the first detection in a free-living C. geoffroyi in Brazil. The authors stress the importance of continued surveillance of Plasmodium in non-human primates.

articles.headline: Glucose-targeted dihydroartemisinin nanoprodrug shows antimalarial efficacy in lab
articles.title_original: Glucose-functionalized redox-responsive dihydroartemisinin prodrug nanosystem for targeted malaria therapy.
articles.authors: Wang R, Yang J, Qiang J, Li Q, Wang G, Ping C, Liu K, Wang R, Zheng B, Ren G, Zhang S
articles.details: Researchers developed a glucose-functionalized, redox-responsive dihydroartemisinin (DHA) prodrug nanosystem (D@GLU-PMs-SS) to overcome artemisinins' poor solubility and short half-life and to enhance drug accumulation in Plasmodium. The system, built from a DHA-dithiodipropionic acid-octadecylamine prodrug and a TPGS-arbutin conjugate, was stable under storage and physiological conditions and was activated by glutathione (GSH) to release free DHA. In vitro, glucose functionalization enhanced erythrocyte uptake via GLUT-mediated transport, reduced GSH in Plasmodium, and effectively inhibited parasite growth while maintaining biosafety. The authors conclude the strategy improves nanoparticle targeting and warrants further clinical investigation.

articles.headline: Microfluidic sensor detects reduced red cell deformability in P. falciparum infection
articles.title_original: Measuring Asymmetric Ionic Current Waveform Through Micropores for Detecting Reduced Red Blood Cell Deformability Due to Plasmodium falciparum Infection.
articles.authors: Yokota K, Hirano K, Kajimoto K, Hashimoto M
articles.details: This study developed a microfluidic sensing device to evaluate decreased red blood cell (RBC) deformability at the single-cell level by measuring ionic current waveforms through micropores, using an in vitro P. falciparum culture system. When RBC deformability was reduced by infection, ionic current waveforms changed and became increasingly asymmetric as deformability decreased. Computer simulations suggested these waveform parameters are largely independent of RBC size and may be a reliable indicator of diminished deformability. The authors conclude the sensor enables detailed single-cell analysis of malaria-associated deformability reduction.

articles.headline: P. falciparum cross-stage antigen PfSDP elicits short-lived antibody response
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Subtilisin-like Domain-Containing Protein (PfSDP), a Cross-Stage Antigen, Elicits Short-Lived Antibody Response Following Natural Infection with Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Kengne-Ouafo JA, Morang'a CM, Nyakoe NK, Dosoo D, Tackie R, Mutungi JK, Bah SY, Amenga-Etego LN, Urban B, Awandare GA, Dinko B, Aniweh Y
articles.details: Through in silico screening, researchers identified Pf3D7_1105800, encoding a P. falciparum subtilisin-like domain-containing protein (PfSDP), expressed across all developmental stages at transcript and protein levels and relatively conserved across continents with signs of directional selection. PfSDP co-localized with PfMSP-1 (asexual) and partially with Pfs48/45 (sexual). Anti-PfSDP peptide-specific antibodies inhibited erythrocyte invasion by 20-60% in a dose-dependent manner. The protein was immunogenic in children from endemic areas, with antibody levels rising from acute infection to day 7 post-treatment, then gradually decaying. Authors suggest the limited invasion effect implies PfSDP may have other roles in parasite development.

articles.headline: Outcrossing gives fitness advantage in P. falciparum mosquito-stage genetic crosses
articles.title_original: Advantages of outcrossing in Plasmodium falciparum: insights from genetic crosses using fluorescent labelled parasites.
articles.authors: Li X, Jutzeler KS, Abatiyow BA, Rezakhani N, Haile MT, Leeb AS, Patel H, Kappe SHI, Nosten F, Cheeseman IH, Ferdig MT, Anderson TJC, Vaughan AM, Kumar S
articles.details: To investigate the fitness consequences of selfing versus outcrossing, researchers generated fluorescent-labelled clones of NF54 (mCherry, African) and NHP4026 (GFP, Thai), crossed them, and scored genotypes of 8540 oocysts from 435 mosquitoes sampled 7 to 14 days post infection. In two independently replicated crosses, the proportion of outcrossed oocysts decreased and inbreeding increased over the course of infection, consistent with faster maturation of sporozoites from outcrossed versus selfed oocysts. Selfed NF54 oocysts were larger than outcrossed or selfed NHP4026 oocysts. The authors conclude there is a substantial outcrossing advantage that maximizes recombination in transmitted parasite populations.

articles.headline: Cohort study protocol for Lassa fever incidence and malaria coinfection in West Africa
articles.title_original: Prospective cohort study to evaluate Lassa fever incidence, symptoms and coinfection with malaria in West Africa: the Enable Lassa Research Programme ('ENABLE 1.5') - study protocol.
articles.authors: Mandi H, Asogun D, Ayodeji O, Azuogu BN, Camacho A, Fischer W, Fulton R, Grant DS, Günther S, Ithete N, Illori E, Jan K, Kagia C, Martens N, Menon S, Ndiaye A, Ndifon M, Nsaibirni R, Ntiri M, Oloo P, Okogbenin S, Penfold S, Sankawulo-Ricks M, Sillah M, Suykerbuyk P, Tornieporth N, Vielle NJ, Williams M, Wohl D, Xie F, Yimer S, Breugelmans JG
articles.details: This is a study protocol (ENABLE 1.5) for a prospective cohort study assessing the incidence of symptomatic RT-PCR-confirmed Lassa fever disease, including malaria coinfection, across five study sites (one in Liberia, three in Nigeria, one in Sierra Leone). A total of 5000 participants will be recruited (minimum 1000 per site), equally stratified into ages 0-5, 6-10, 11-17, 18-50 and >50 years, and followed for 12 months with symptom assessments every 2 weeks and blood draws every 3 months for IgG serology. Suspected Lassa cases will undergo malaria rapid diagnostic testing and blood smear microscopy. No results are reported as this is a protocol.

articles.headline: Abbott Bioline RDT shows high sensitivity, low specificity for malaria in rural Cameroon
articles.title_original: Malaria infection prevalence and diagnostic performance of the Abbott Bioline Malaria Ag P.f/Pan rapid test in rural populations of Central Cameroon.
articles.authors: Missoup VO, Suh PF, Ndjebakal CN, Tagne D, Poumachu Y, Joko S, Kouamendjouo Djilla A, Njiokou F, Mbacham W, Wondji C, Ndo C
articles.details: In cross-sectional surveys in rural Central Cameroon (March 2022 and May 2024), Plasmodium infection was detected by RDT and microscopy, with real-time PCR validating discordant results, to assess the Abbott Bioline Malaria Ag P.f/Pan RDT. Infection prevalence was 64.9% by microscopy and 71.7% by RDT, with P. falciparum predominant (microscopy: 97.48%). Against microscopy, the RDT showed high sensitivity (93.37%; CI 91.15-95.77%), low specificity (68.50%; CI 62.40-74.17%), PPV 84.43%, NPV 85.71%, LR+ 2.96, LR- 0.09, and moderate agreement (k=0.652; P<0.001). The authors conclude the RDT is a reliable diagnostic tool in this highly endemic setting.

articles.headline: Genomic surveillance maps diverse Kelch13 resistance mutations in Zambia
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Genomic Surveillance Reveals a Diversity of Kelch 13 Mutations in Zambia.
articles.authors: Aranda-Díaz A, Mwanza S, Makhanthisa TI, Lauterbach SB, De Amaral F, Chisenga M, Mangena B, Routledge I, Letinić B, Kasonde B, Chongwe G, Mwenda MC, Miller JM, Hibwato T, Jacob C, Hamainza B, Bwalya S, Chiwaula J, Matoba J, Sikaala C, Chimumbwa J, Wesolowski A, Smith JL, Raman J, Hawela M
articles.details: This genomic surveillance study collected dried blood spots from confirmed Plasmodium falciparum cases at 61 health facilities across all 10 Zambian provinces (March–July 2023). Multiplexed amplicon sequencing genotyped 2,486 samples for mutations in 12 genes linked to resistance to seven antimalarials. Markers potentially linked to artemisinin partial resistance included the validated k13 P574L (0.66% adjusted national prevalence) and candidate k13 P441L (1.39%); distribution was heterogeneous, reaching up to 46% of infections at some facilities. The mdr1 N86 genotype (decreased lumefantrine susceptibility) was found in all samples, and sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine markers were very high (dhps K540E, 93.26%). Authors call for routine molecular surveillance.

articles.headline: Deep learning emulator speeds calibration of agent-based malaria model
articles.title_original: Multitask deep learning for the emulation and calibration of an agent-based malaria transmission model.
articles.authors: Mondal A, Anirudh R, Selvaraj P
articles.details: The authors present a multitask deep learning approach to emulate and calibrate a complex agent-based model of malaria transmission, which is computationally intensive to calibrate directly. The neural network emulator was trained on a large suite of simulations from the EMOD malaria model, capturing relationships between immunological parameters and epidemiological outcomes such as age-stratified incidence and prevalence across eight sub-Saharan African study sites. The trained emulator was then combined with parameter estimation techniques to calibrate the underlying model to reference data, demonstrating the potential of machine learning-guided emulator design for complex scientific processes.

articles.headline: Egypt's malaria elimination program analyzed as model for Africa
articles.title_original: Comparative analysis of Egypt's malaria elimination strategies and implementation science: Pathways to achieve malaria-free status for other African countries.
articles.authors: Elendu C, Amaechi DC, Elendu RC, Saggi SK, Elendu TC, Amaechi EC, Elendu ID, Dang K, Amosu OP, Idowu OF, Oseni TS
articles.details: This retrospective implementation science study analyzed Egypt's WHO-certified malaria elimination program (certified October 20, 2024) using a RE-AIM lens, evaluating interventions including insecticide-treated nets, indoor residual spraying, treatment protocols, and education campaigns targeting children under five and pregnant women. The program achieved a 92% recruitment rate, 78% adherence to preventive measures, a 96% reduction in malaria incidence over 15 years, and a 94% reduction in Anopheles density; 98% of cases were detected within 48 hours and treatment coverage reached 91%. Cost per DALY averted was $24, with an estimated $1.5 billion saved. Fidelity was 93% and acceptability averaged 87%.

articles.headline: mRNA-LNP PvCSP vaccine immunogenic and protective in mice
articles.title_original: mRNA-LNP vaccine encoding the Plasmodium vivax circumsporozoite protein is highly immunogenic and confers protection in mice.
articles.authors: Limsalakpetch A, Kum-Arb U, Yongvanitchit K, Im-Erbsin R, Ubalee R, Waters N, Vesely BA, Muramatsu H, Weissman D, Tam YK, Yoshida S, Adams J, Yadava A, Pardi N, Pichyangkul S
articles.details: This study tested nucleoside-modified mRNA-lipid nanoparticle (LNP) vaccines targeting the Plasmodium vivax circumsporozoite protein (PvCSP) in mice. Two constructs were compared; despite lower protein expression, the construct with the wild-type signal peptide and GPI anchor induced significantly higher antibody titers against PvCSP and its repeat region than the construct lacking GPI. Intradermal and intramuscular delivery gave dose-dependent antibody responses (intradermal superior at lower dose), while intravenous responses were very poor. A delayed third intramuscular dose 5 months after the second markedly increased anti-repeat antibodies and T cell responses and conferred strong protection against sporozoite challenge, with protection linked to anti-repeat antibody magnitude and avidity.

articles.headline: CD44 cross-linking promotes P. falciparum red cell invasion
articles.title_original: CD44 cross-linking promotes Plasmodium falciparum invasion.
articles.authors: Kongsomboonvech AK, Scally SW, Le Guen Y, Valissery P, Salinas ND, Cowman AF, Tolia NH, Egan ES
articles.details: Using genetically modified red blood cells from primary human hematopoietic stem cells, the authors investigated CD44 as a host factor for Plasmodium falciparum invasion. The anti-CD44 monoclonal antibody BRIC 222 significantly promoted invasion through CD44 cross-linking, which altered phosphorylation of RBC cytoskeletal proteins and increased accessibility of surface proteins including the essential invasion receptor Basigin. The parasite ligand EBA-175, which interacts with CD44, similarly enhanced invasion and induced membrane changes. Both BRIC 222 and EBA-175 increased binding of the PfRH5/PCRCR invasion complex to Basigin. The authors propose CD44 cross-linking coordinates downstream ligand-receptor interactions and cytoskeletal signaling, making RBCs more permissive to invasion.

articles.headline: Western Kenya P. falciparum carry chloroquine but not artemisinin resistance markers
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum parasites harbouring chloroquine-resistant but not artemisinin-resistant alleles in Busia County, Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Onchieku NM, Gesusu N, Caspar E, Karani L, Thiong'o K, Kamau L, Kiboi D, Thiebaut L, Ma L, Kimani F, Matoke-Muhia D, Ménard D
articles.details: Molecular surveillance using highly multiplexed deep amplicon sequencing of dried blood spots from malaria patients in Busia County, Western Kenya, analyzed drug-resistance SNPs in P. falciparum. Of 118 samples, Pfkelch13 mutations appeared in 6 (5.1%), all the A578S variant that does not confer artemisinin partial resistance. Mutant Pfcrt alleles were found in 11% (13 samples), 62% carrying the CVIET triple-mutant haplotype, while 98% had dhfr mutations (N51I, C59R, or S108N) and all carried dhps mutations (A437G at 84%). No Pfhrp2 deletions or Pfmdr1 codon 86/1246 mutations were detected. The authors conclude validated artemisinin-resistance markers are absent but sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine-sensitive parasites remain rare.

articles.headline: L9-epitope virus-like particle vaccines give sterilizing malaria immunity in mice
articles.title_original: Virus-like particle vaccines targeting a key epitope in circumsporozoite protein provide sterilizing immunity against malaria.
articles.authors: Nepal Y, Francian A, Flores-Garcia Y, Roberts BT, David SA, Zavala F, Chackerian B
articles.details: This mouse immunization study tested pre-erythrocytic malaria vaccines displaying the L9 epitope of P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein (CSP) on bacteriophage virus-like particles, comparing Qβ L9 VLPs, recombinant MS2 L9 VLPs of varying peptide lengths, and the licensed RTS,S/AS01E. MS2 L9 VLPs displaying longer peptides combined with a potent novel adjuvant elicited strong, durable antibody responses that lowered liver burden and prevented infection. Qβ L9 VLPs, MS2 L9 VLPs, and RTS,S/AS01E each gave significant protection from liver-stage infection. A combination of MS2 L9 and Qβ L9 VLPs, presenting the epitope in distinct structural contexts, produced sterilizing immunity in the highest percentage of mice.

articles.headline: EaveTubes cut indoor malaria mosquito density in Tanzania field trial
articles.title_original: The In2Care® EaveTubes improved protection against malaria vectors in a small-scale field study at Kagera Sugar Ltd, Missenyi, Tanzania.
articles.authors: Kweka EJ, Farenhorst M, Dijk TV, Zinga M, Osinga A, Mazigo H
articles.details: A small-scale field efficacy study of In2Care EaveTubes (ET) for malaria vector control was conducted at Kagera Sugar Ltd, Missenyi, Tanzania. In total 350 Eave Tubes were installed in 100 iron-sheet barrack rooms and 35 mud houses in one camp, with two untreated camps as controls, over a 9-month intervention period. EaveTubes significantly reduced indoor anopheline vector densities by 58% overall and 75% during peak mosquito season, with no degradation of insecticide content over 6 months of field use. Malaria cases declined in both treated and control camps versus baseline, with the largest incidence reduction (25.6%) in the ET intervention camp. Authors conclude ET are a durable, effective addition to the vector-control toolbox.

articles.headline: Lumefantrine detectable in hair weeks after malaria treatment
articles.title_original: Can hair concentrations of artemether-lumefantrine be used as a tool to retrospectively determine drug exposure during malaria treatment?
articles.authors: Schnyder JL, Vos-van der Meer M, van Hest RM, Mathot R, Schlagenhauf P, de Jong HK, Grobusch MP
articles.details: This proof-of-concept study assessed whether artemether-lumefantrine (AL) and their metabolites could be detected in hair as a retrospective marker of drug exposure in uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria. Hair samples from six patients at Amsterdam UMC were collected four weeks after treatment initiation and analyzed by LC-MS/MS. Lumefantrine and its metabolite desbutyl-lumefantrine were detected in all six samples, with quantifiable levels in five, while artemether and dihydroartemisinin were undetectable, likely due to their short half-lives. No participants developed recrudescence. The authors conclude hair analysis may serve as a retrospective tool to assess lumefantrine exposure, potentially distinguishing inadequate drug exposure from reduced parasite susceptibility, and call for a larger validation study.

articles.headline: Scoping review maps malaria vector control strategies across Pakistan
articles.title_original: Malaria vector control strategies in Pakistan: a scoping review.
articles.authors: Irshad HA, Jehanzeb H, Yaseen A, Saleem U, Javaid MD, Tareen HK, Mukhtar M, Silviera H, Beg MA
articles.details: This scoping review examined published literature on WHO-recommended malaria vector control strategies in Pakistan, searching PubMed, Scopus, CINAHL, Embase, and Google Scholar for articles in all languages published before September 2023, with four independent reviewers and analysis by province, vector, and method. A total of 46 articles were included.
