Reported strategies were insecticide-treated nets (ITN, 29.79%), indoor residual spraying (IRS, 29.79%), spatial spraying (12.77%), spatial/airborne repellents (4.26%), larval source management (4.26%), and house modifications (4.26%). Khyber Pakhtunkhwa relied mainly on ITN (55%) while Punjab used IRS (50%) as its primary method. The authors highlight an increasing use of ITNs over time and provincial differences, calling for improved research and precise reporting to guide policy.

articles.headline: Imperfect malaria test sensitivity cuts power of vaccine-effectiveness studies
articles.title_original: Power and sample size considerations for test-negative design with bias correction: a case study on the world first malaria vaccine.
articles.authors: Ko YK, Alfvén T, Yoneoka D
articles.details: A methods study used Monte Carlo simulations to assess how correcting for diagnostic misclassification affects statistical power and sample size in test-negative design (TND) studies of malaria vaccine effectiveness (VE), modeling test sensitivities of 60%, 80%, and 95% across six vaccination-status categories defined by time since the third or fourth dose. Assuming low vaccination coverage (<10%), VE below 50% against clinical malaria, and a 30% malaria positivity rate among unvaccinated symptomatic individuals, uncorrected VE was consistently underestimated, while bias-corrected estimates were approximately unbiased but had wider confidence intervals. At 80% sensitivity, only three groups reached 80% power with 10,000 participants, versus 6,000 needed under a perfect test. The authors conclude power calculations must account for outcome misclassification to avoid underpowered studies and misleading VE estimates.

articles.headline: First evidence that P. falciparum Grp170 acts as independent chaperone
articles.title_original: Biochemical and biophysical characterization of Plasmodium falciparum glucose regulated protein 170.
articles.authors: Muzenda FL, Stofberg ML, Mthembu W, Strauss E, Zininga T
articles.details: This laboratory study characterized the structure and function of Plasmodium falciparum PfGrp170 (PfHsp70-y), an endoplasmic reticulum Hsp110 homologue that is essential for parasite survival and thought to aid export of parasite proteins to the host cell cytosol. Recombinant full-length PfGrp170 was successfully overexpressed in E. coli under native conditions; secondary-structure analysis showed a predominantly α-helical protein with moderate thermal stability (Tm above 55 °C). ATP perturbed its structural stability, increasing sensitivity to heat and proteolysis, and the protein showed basal ATPase activity and suppressed heat-induced aggregation of malate dehydrogenase in a nucleotide-independent manner. Docking and molecular dynamics indicated selective nucleotide binding that was more responsive to ATP than ADP, providing the first direct evidence of independent chaperone function and suggesting a potential antimalarial drug target.

articles.headline: Deep-learning model classifies malaria-infected cells with 99.24% accuracy
articles.title_original: MalNet-DAF: Dual-Attentive Fusion Deep Learning Model for Malaria Parasite Classification.
articles.authors: Patro KK, Prakash AJ, Madarapu S, Ari S, Routray S, Mukherjee A, Mishra S
articles.details: This study introduces MalNet-DAF (Malaria Network with Dual-Attentive Fusion), a deep-learning model for classifying malaria-infected cells that combines stacked Convolutional Neural Networks for spatial feature extraction with Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory networks, plus Spatial and Temporal Attention Modules and dense layers with ReLU activation and dropout. Trained and validated on a publicly available malaria dataset from the National Institutes of Health (NIH), the model achieved a classification accuracy of 99.24%, outperforming traditional baseline models. The authors conclude that dynamic attention-driven deep learning can support real-time clinical decision-making in malaria diagnosis.

articles.headline: Bed-net coverage strong in Bangladesh but lags in Rohingya camps
articles.title_original: Assessing long-lasting insecticidal net coverage, access, and use: Insights from malaria-endemic regions and Rohingya camps in Bangladesh.
articles.authors: Hossain MS, Neogi AK, Phru CS, Ferdous NN, Hasan A, Islam S, Rahman MM, Rahaman MM, Islam MN, Das SK, Bhuiyan ATMRH, Islam MN, Islam MA, Alam MS
articles.details: A cross-sectional survey collected data from 1,575 households across five malaria-endemic districts in Bangladesh and 10 forcibly displaced Myanmar national (FDMN) camps in Cox's Bazar (May–October 2023) using structured interviews to assess long-lasting insecticidal net (LLIN) coverage, access, and use. Among Bangladeshi households, 97.6% owned at least one LLIN, 93.2% had sufficient coverage, and 96.4% slept under an LLIN the previous night, with use of 95.0% among pregnant women and 98.3% among children under 5. In FDMN households, 98.2% owned a net but only 44.3% had sufficient coverage and 65.7% slept under one, with inadequate supply a key barrier. The authors conclude Bangladesh surpasses the WHO 80% threshold but targeted interventions are needed for the FDMN population.

articles.headline: Fatal rapidly progressive P. falciparum malaria in young traveler
articles.title_original: Severe Malaria Due to Plasmodium falciparum in an Immunocompetent Young Adult: Rapid Progression to Multiorgan Failure.
articles.authors: Sanclemente-Cardoza V, Payán-Salcedo HA, Estela-Zape JL
articles.details: This case report describes a 21-year-old man with recent travel to an endemic area (Guapi, Colombia) who presented with fever, severe respiratory distress, and oxygen saturation below 75%, requiring intubation, during which he suffered pulseless electrical activity cardiac arrest with return of spontaneous circulation. Thick blood smear confirmed Plasmodium falciparum infection, and he progressed to multiorgan failure including ARDS with capillary leak pulmonary edema, refractory distributive shock, acute kidney injury with severe hyperkalemia, and consumptive thrombocytopenia. Despite mechanical ventilation, vasopressors, methylene blue, attempted hemodialysis, and platelet transfusions, he had a second cardiac arrest and died. The authors emphasize the rapid progression of severe malaria and the need for early diagnosis, intensive multidisciplinary management, and preventive strategies for travelers.

articles.headline: NR2F6 deletion protects mice from experimental cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: NR2F6 as a Disease Driver and Candidate Therapeutic Target in Experimental Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Stefan VE, Klepsch V, Thuille N, Steinlechner M, Peer S, Siegmund K, Lackner P, Schmutzhard E, Albrecht-Schgör K, Baier G
articles.details: This experimental study used a Plasmodium berghei ANKA (PbA) mouse model of cerebral malaria to test the role of NR2F6, an immune checkpoint, by comparing Nr2f6-knockout and wild-type C57BL6/N mice. Nr2f6 deficiency conferred a significant survival benefit, with less severe endotheliopathy, reduced blood-brain barrier damage, fewer sequestered parasites, and fewer cytotoxic T-lymphocytes in the brain. The authors conclude NR2F6 plays a causal, non-redundant role in disease progression and that pharmacological inhibitors of the NR2F6 pathway could protect blood-brain barrier integrity against cerebral malaria.

articles.headline: Anopheles mosquitoes incriminated as deer malaria vectors in Florida
articles.title_original: Incriminating vectors of deer malaria (Plasmodium odocoilei) at a Florida deer farm.
articles.authors: Rockwell M, Wisely SM, Mathias DK, Burkett-Cadena ND
articles.details: At a deer farm in Gadsden County, FL, with documented Plasmodium odocoilei transmission, mosquitoes were collected using CO2-baited light traps, aspirators, and resting shelters, then tested by PCR and Sanger sequencing for white-tailed deer (WTD) blood meals and infection. Among 38 mosquito species, four specialized in WTD feeding (≥75% of blood meals): Anopheles quadrimaculatus s.l. (88.9%), An. punctipennis (83.3%), An. crucians s.l. (81.4%), and Culex erraticus (87.7%). P. odocoilei infection rates were highest in An. quadrimaculatus s.l. (4.1%), An. punctipennis (3.1%), and An. crucians s.l. (0.47%). The authors conclude An. quadrimaculatus s.l. and An. punctipennis are the most likely natural vectors, pending vector competence studies.

articles.headline: Artisanal mining top risk factor for urban malaria, Zimbabwe
articles.title_original: Emergence of urban malaria and the associated risk factors: a case-control study in Mutare city, Zimbabwe.
articles.authors: Mharakurwa S, Mbwana N, Mharakurwa T, Karumbidza T, Mathe V, Fusire P, Tangwena A, Chauke W, Mukuzunga M, Dhliwayo P, Munyati SM, Gwanzura L, Bailey JA, Moss WJ
articles.details: This cross-sectional and case-control study examined risk factors for locally transmitted malaria across all eight primary health care facilities in Mutare city, Zimbabwe, from 2022 to 2023, enrolling RDT/microscopy-positive cases and test-negative symptomatic controls. In a multivariate logistic model, significant risk factors included residential locale (RR 3, 95%CI 1.1-5.8, p=0.029, N=7,222), household proximity to still surface water or unprotected wells (RR 16, 3.8-67.5, p<0.001), and recent travel (RR 9, 5.2-14.4, p<0.001). Artisanal mining was the strongest risk factor (RR 22, 10.7-44.1, p<0.001), and males had four-fold higher odds than females (RR 4, 2.0-6.5). The authors recommend environmental management and targeted intervention packages.

articles.headline: Operational lessons from RTS,S malaria vaccine pilots in three countries
articles.title_original: Subnational introduction of the RTS,S/AS01(E) malaria vaccine into routine immunization: experience and lessons from the three pilot countries.
articles.authors: Jalang'o R, Amponsa-Achiano K, Chisema M, Malm K, Khalayi L, Mhone B, Mohammed W, Asiedu-Bekoe F, Haji A, Njoroge J, Zimba B, Chirwa E, Tweneboah PO, Sillah J, Magafu MGMD, Bergstrom C, Goodman T, Walldorf J, Kelleher K, Pellaux-Furrer E, Hamel MJ, Adjei MR, Okine RNA
articles.details: This paper summarizes operational lessons from the subnational pilot introduction of the RTS,S/AS01E malaria vaccine into routine immunization in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi under the WHO Malaria Vaccine Implementation Programme. Ghana and Kenya administered doses at 6, 7, 9, and 24 months (Ghana later moving the fourth dose to 18 months), while Malawi used 5, 6, 7, and 22 months. Coverage reached about 80% for the first dose and around 75% for the third dose by 2023, but fourth-dose coverage was low (median 46% in 2023). Key lessons included clear stakeholder roles, effective launch and demand generation, flexible dose scheduling, and updated data collection tools.

articles.headline: Modeling shows larviciding cuts malaria but varies widely by village
articles.title_original: Heterogeneous impacts for malaria control from larviciding across villages and considerations for monitoring and evaluation.
articles.authors: Sherrard-Smith E, Fillinger U, Tia JB, Winskill P, Koudou BG, Tchicaya ESF, Sanou A, Okumu F, Opiyo M, Majambere S, Hamlet A, Charles G, Lambert B, Churcher TS
articles.details: An individual-based falciparum transmission model simulated epidemiological trials of larval source management (larvicide plus ITNs) in Western Kenya and Côte d'Ivoire. In Western Kenya, generalised linear models estimated 82% (90% CrI 64-92%) and 88% (79-94%) reductions in adult Anopheles funestus and An. gambiae complex respectively by CDC light traps; in Côte d'Ivoire, an 82% (56-93%) reduction of the dominant An. gambiae vector was estimated. The model predicted malaria prevalence reductions of 48-72% in children 6 months to 10 years in Kenya and 11-78% reductions in all-age clinical incidence across Côte d'Ivoire villages, broadly matching observed outcomes. High between-village heterogeneity means reductions cannot be simply extrapolated to other regions.

articles.headline: Plasmepsin II/III copy number drives piperaquine resistance in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: The plasmepsin-piperaquine paradox persists in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Walsh B, Summers RL, Rangel GW, Hagenah LM, Mok S, Llinás M, Fidock DA, Wirth DF, Bopp S
articles.details: Using CRISPR-Cas9, researchers generated single knockouts of plasmepsin II and plasmepsin III and a double knockout in two isogenic Cambodian P. falciparum lines with differing plasmepsin copy numbers, to probe reduced piperaquine (PPQ) sensitivity linked to dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine partial resistance. Deleting plasmepsin II and/or III increased PPQ sensitivity. Higher copy-number parasites showed no significant increase in hemoglobin digestion, peptide composition differences, or free heme production after PPQ; plasmepsin II deletion slowed hemoglobin digestion, and amino-acid-limited media had little effect. Modulating digestive vacuole pH altered PPQ response, implicating DV homeostasis in resistance.

articles.headline: Malaria co-infection eases cutaneous leishmaniasis in mice
articles.title_original: Impact of co-infection with Plasmodium berghei ANKA in Leishmania major-parasitized mice on immune modulation and cutaneous leishmaniasis.
articles.authors: Ornellas-Garcia U, Freire-Antunes L, Rangel-Ferreira M, de Sousa CHG, Ribeiro-Almeida ML, Daniel-Ribeiro CT, Cuervo P, Ribeiro-Gomes FL
articles.details: In C57BL/6 mice, models of cutaneous leishmaniasis (Leishmania major) and experimental cerebral malaria (Plasmodium berghei ANKA) were combined to study co-infection effects on immunity. Acute P. berghei ANKA infection reduced progression of ongoing cutaneous leishmaniasis, lowering lesion size and dermal parasite burden. Co-infection raised serum TNF compared with L. major alone and reduced recruitment of activated T cells and inflammatory monocytes to the infection site. The authors conclude co-infecting pathogens can cross-regulate immune responses, with implications for disease management in co-endemic areas.

articles.headline: Entomological study maps malaria transmission hotspot in Burkina Faso village
articles.title_original: The spatial heterogeneity of malaria transmission: An entomological investigation in a highly endemic setting of Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Perugini E, Poggi C, Micocci M, Pichler V, Nelli L, Torre AD, Guelbeogo WM, Pombi M
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: This entomological study examined spatial transmission heterogeneity in an ITN-covered village (Goden, Burkina Faso), using pyrethrum spray collections in 59 houses across three areas and GAMLSS models linking entomological indices to environmental factors. Anopheles coluzzii dominated, comprising 79% of An. gambiae (s.l.) collected (n=1492). Human blood index was low at 45% (n=687), yet sporozoite rate was high at 10% (n=930), and 43% of human blood meals contained erythrocytic parasite stages. Indices clustered in the north-west, indicating a hotspot; infective mosquitoes showed 1.8-fold higher anthropophily than non-infective ones, suggesting a mechanism sustaining local transmission.

articles.headline: Polycyclic compounds reverse memory loss in experimental cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Polycyclitol Derivatives Restore Long- Term Memory Via cdk5/p25 Activation of Tau Signaling in Experimental Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Simhadri PK, Rashid S, Karri S, Bhat BA, Mehta G, Babu PP
articles.details: In an experimental cerebral malaria model, two of four polycyclic derivatives tested as adjuncts to artemether — SR4-02 and SR4-04 — significantly improved learning and memory compared with artemether alone and with SR4-01/SR4-03. These groups showed reduced hippocampal and cortical phospho-tau (Ser396) and enhanced neuronal arborization in the CA1 and CA3 hippocampal subregions and the cortex, with SR4-04 also showing reduced Cdk5-p25-mediated tau phosphorylation. Behavioral testing used the Barnes maze, T-maze, and a novelty-based recognition task. The authors note that about 25% of children surviving cerebral malaria in Sub-Saharan Africa experience cognitive impairment and suggest SR4-02 and SR4-04 as promising adjunctive therapies.

articles.headline: Data-driven mathematical model of temperature- and altitude-dependent malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Analysis of a mathematical model for malaria using data-driven approach.
articles.authors: Rajnarayanan A, Kumar M, Tridane A
articles.details: This modeling study integrates temperature- and altitude-dependent transmission functions into a compartmental SIR-SI model of malaria, introducing a new transmission function capturing environmental dependencies. The authors perform steady-state analysis, establishing stability criteria for disease-free and endemic equilibria via linearization. For parameter estimation they compare ANNs, RNNs, and physics-informed neural networks (PINNs), with PINNs embedding epidemiological dynamics to enable physics-constrained inference and improved prediction over purely data-driven methods. They also apply Dynamic Mode Decomposition to derive an interpretable, data-driven transmission risk index from infection trajectories for real-time risk assessment.

articles.headline: Bivalent viral-vectored vaccine protects against P. falciparum and vivax, blocks transmission
articles.title_original: Malaria bivalent viral vectored vaccine protects against Plasmodium falciparum and vivax and blocks parasite transmission.
articles.authors: Yamamoto Y, Katayama T, Fabbri C, Niwa S, Okuhara D, Iyori M, Hasyim AA, Mizukami H, Shida H, Lopes S, Yoshida S
articles.details: Researchers developed a bivalent malaria vaccine using vaccinia virus LC16m8Δ (m8Δ) and adeno-associated virus type 1 (AAV1) carrying gene cassettes encoding Pfs25-PfCSP and Pvs25-PvCSP fusion proteins. In a heterologous m8Δ-prime/AAV1-boost regimen, immunization provided 70% protection against both PfCSP/Pb and PvCSP/Pb transgenic sporozoites. A membrane feeding assay using P. vivax isolates from infected patients in the Brazilian Amazon showed 90% transmission-blocking efficacy. The bivalent vaccine outperformed monovalent combinations and maintained immune responses for over 7 months, suggesting promise for malaria control and elimination.

articles.headline: Mathematical model shows asymptomatic infections drive ~30% of malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Impact of asymptomatic infections on malaria transmission dynamics.
articles.authors: Haringo AT, Obsu LL, Bushu FK
articles.details: This study developed a deterministic mathematical model to examine malaria spread with a focus on asymptomatic infections as reservoirs sustaining transmission. The model analyzed positivity, boundedness, and stability of equilibria, and calculated the basic reproduction number R0 using the next-generation matrix method, revealing backward bifurcation. The threshold analysis indicated that asymptomatic cases contribute approximately 30% to R0, while symptomatic cases account for about 70%. The authors conclude that integrated control strategies addressing both symptomatic and asymptomatic cases are needed to effectively reduce malaria transmission.

articles.headline: First molecular detection of zoonotic Plasmodium in Assamese macaques, northern Thailand
articles.title_original: First molecular detection of zoonotic Plasmodium knowlesi, Plasmodium cynomolgi, and Plasmodium inui in Assamese macaques from northern Thailand.
articles.authors: Wilaisri P, Kaewchot S, Rucksaken R, Jarudecha T, Hmaidee T, Wichainchot S, Thabthimsri C, Sricharern W
articles.details: This study assessed the prevalence and molecular characterization of zoonotic Plasmodium spp. in Assamese macaques (Macaca assamensis) from Chiang Rai Province, northern Thailand. In total, 133 blood samples collected at Tham Pla temple were examined using semi-nested PCR targeting the 18S rRNA gene. Plasmodium DNA was detected in 24.06% (32/133), with P. knowlesi in 13.53% (18/133), P. cynomolgi in 9.77% (13/133), and P. inui in 0.75% (1/133). The authors report this as the first molecular evidence of these zoonotic species in Assamese macaques in Thailand and highlight the macaques' potential role as natural reservoirs, underscoring the need for continued surveillance.

articles.headline: Tephrosia villosa root extract shows antimalarial activity in infected mice
articles.title_original: Antimalarial activity of root extract of Tephrosia villosa L. Pers. (Fabaceae) on Plasmodium berghei-infected mice.
articles.authors: Alemu MA, Moges TA, Ketsela Zeleke T, Abebe RB, Zewdu WS
articles.details: This study evaluated the antimalarial effectiveness of a dichloromethane/methanol (1:1) root extract of Tephrosia villosa against Plasmodium berghei infection in mice using 4-day suppressive, Rane's (curative), and prophylactic tests at doses of 100, 200, and 400 mg/kg. Acute oral toxicity testing showed no signs of toxicity at 2 g/kg over 24 hours and 14 days. At 400 mg/kg, the extract suppressed parasitemia by 24.78%, 52.16%, and 49.09% in the 4-day suppressive, curative, and prophylactic models respectively (p < 0.001), and reduced declines in packed cell volume, body weight, and temperature versus negative controls. The authors conclude the extract is safe and has antimalarial effects, supporting prior in vitro results.

articles.headline: XGBoost best among ML models for malaria detection on synthetic data
articles.title_original: Comparative analysis of machine learning models for malaria detection using validated synthetic data: a cost-sensitive approach with clinical domain knowledge integration.
articles.authors: Sekhar GVC, Alemu C
articles.details: Researchers compared five machine learning models—Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, and Enhanced Bayesian Logistic Regression—for malaria detection using a validated synthetic dataset representing Sub-Saharan African epidemiological conditions, which achieved 87% representativeness against published clinical benchmarks. Using cost-sensitive threshold optimization and bootstrap confidence intervals, XGBoost achieved the highest performance (0.956, 95% CI 0.952–0.961) with competitive clinical cost (5,496), a 2.8% improvement over Random Forest; Enhanced Bayesian Logistic Regression was comparable (0.954, 95% CI 0.950–0.959). McNemar's test showed significant differences between XGBoost and Random Forest. The authors conclude XGBoost offers optimal accuracy and cost-effectiveness but real-world clinical validation remains essential.

articles.headline: New platform to discover malaria transmission-blocking drugs in vitro and in vivo
articles.title_original: An all-in-one pipeline for the in vitro discovery and in vivo testing of Plasmodium falciparum malaria transmission blocking drugs.
articles.authors: Brancucci NMB, Gumpp C, van Gemert GJ, Yu X, Passecker A, Nardella F, Thommen BT, Chambon M, Turcatti G, Halby L, Blasco B, Duffey M, Arimondo PB, Bousema T, Scherf A, Leroy D, Kooij TWA, Rottmann M, Voss TS
articles.details: This methods study presents a drug discovery platform for compounds that kill Plasmodium falciparum mature stage V gametocytes, the only stage infective to mosquitoes. It uses transgenic NF54/iGP1_RE9Hulg8 parasites engineered to conditionally produce large numbers of stage V gametocytes expressing a red-shifted firefly luciferase viability reporter, enabling a robust in vitro gametocytocidal screening assay. The authors also establish an in vivo model by infecting humanized NODscidIL2Rγnull mice with pure stage V gametocytes, using whole-animal bioluminescence imaging to assess gametocyte killing and clearance kinetics of reference drugs and clinical candidates, and combine it with mosquito feeding assays.

articles.headline: Korea 2024 malaria vector surveillance shows higher mosquito density, lower infection rate
articles.title_original: [Malaria Vector Surveillance in 2024].
articles.authors: Han BG, Kim HW, Ju JW, Lee HI
articles.details: This vector surveillance program monitored Anopheles mosquito density at 76 sites from April to October 2024 in the Republic of Korea, where 94.1% of domestic malaria cases occurred in Seoul, Incheon, Gyeonggi, and Gangwon Provinces; mosquitoes were tested for Plasmodium vivax by molecular methods. Cumulative trap index reached 120.6, a 66.6% increase over 2023 and 59.3% above the 2020–2022 average, with a first peak (TI 18.2) at week 27 and malaria cases rising 5–6 weeks after mosquito increases. Malaria-positive mosquitoes were found in three pools (MIR=0.15) in Paju-si at weeks 31, 32, and 34; though the same number of positive pools as 2023 (MIR=0.21), MIR fell due to higher Anopheles density.

articles.headline: Expanded nanopore assay (DRAG2) for malaria parasite genetic surveillance
articles.title_original: An expanded method for malaria parasite genetic surveillance using targeted nanopore sequencing.
articles.authors: Harrott AJR, Morang'a CM, Pearson RD, Sakyi ML, Osumanu A, Amoako EK, Bara FD, Hosmillo M, Rowe K, Aniweh Y, Awandare GA, Zeukeng F, Goodfellow I, Ariani CV, Amenga-Etego LN, Hamilton WL
articles.details: This methods paper describes DRAG2, an updated targeted multiplex PCR plus nanopore sequencing assay for Plasmodium falciparum molecular surveillance, building on the earlier DRAG1 workflow. DRAG2 adds amplicon targets including more antimalarial drug resistance markers, the polymorphic surface antigen merozoite surface protein 2 (msp2), and the 18S ribosomal RNA gene for Plasmodium species detection. The authors report assay performance across a range of parasitaemias and sample types (venous blood and dried blood spots), with quality-control recommendations including economical synthetic plasmids carrying both 'test' SNPs (drug resistance markers) and non-natural 'control' SNPs that signal contamination, plus coverage thresholds and standard operating procedures. They note further testing in endemic settings and regulatory approval are still needed.

articles.headline: Tutorial on using learning curves to set sample size for malaria ML models
articles.title_original: How to use learning curves to evaluate the sample size for malaria prediction models developed using machine learning algorithms.
articles.authors: Zaloumis SG, Rajasekhar M, Simpson JA
articles.details: This methods tutorial demonstrates how to generate and interpret learning curves to determine the training sample size needed for malaria prediction models built with machine learning. Using data simulated from a published in vivo gene expression dataset of 1,043 P. falciparum isolates—29% (299/1,043) from slow-clearing infections (parasite clearance half-life >5 h)—the authors built a mock model to predict artemisinin resistance status from gene expression, comparing sparse PLS-DA plus SVMs and random forests with prediction error measured as balanced error rate. On a 208-sample test set, the balanced error rate fell from 50% (both methods) at the smallest training set (20 samples) to 14% for sPLSDA+SVMs and 22% for random forests at the largest (835 samples); curve shapes suggest exceeding 835 samples is unlikely to improve accuracy much. They conclude learning curves are a simple, application-specific tool for sizing future studies.

articles.headline: Commentary: ivermectin against malaria
articles.title_original: Ivermectin against Malaria - Good News in Bad Times.
articles.authors: Steketee RW
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Mass ivermectin cuts childhood malaria infection 26% in cluster trial
articles.title_original: Ivermectin to Control Malaria - A Cluster-Randomized Trial.
articles.authors: Chaccour C, Maia M, Kariuki M, Ruiz-Castillo P, Wanjiku C, Kasiwa L, Brazeal A, Casellas A, Ngama M, Onyango T, Elobolobo E, Kazungu K, Mael M, Wangari W, Nuru K, Otuko R, Sanz A, Ringera I, Matano A, Mitora S, Ribes M, Brew J, Gorski N, Nicolas P, Stanulovic S, Omondi I, Furnival-Adams J, Túnez L, Mbarak J, Vegove V, Yaa E, Mramba S, Kibet Y, Nyambura N, Rotich C, Wanjiru S, Vura M, Wanjiku F, Sam L, Collins L, Xia K, Hammann F, Saúte F, Rudd M, Rist C, Jones C, Mwangangi J, Rabinovich NR
articles.details: In a cluster-randomized trial in Kwale, coastal Kenya, where bed-net coverage and use are high, 84 clusters with 28,932 eligible participants were assigned 1:1 to mass administration of ivermectin (400 μg/kg) or albendazole (400 mg, active control) once monthly for 3 consecutive months at the start of the short rains. Children 5–15 years were tested monthly for 6 months. Malaria infection incidence was 2.20 per child-year with ivermectin versus 2.66 with albendazole (adjusted incidence rate ratio 0.74; 95% CI 0.58–0.95; P=0.02), a 26% reduction. Serious adverse events did not differ significantly, and no safety concerns were identified.

articles.headline: Case report: undiagnosed malaria complicates urgent cesarean section
articles.title_original: Emergent Cesarean Section Due to Malaria: A Perioperative Challenge.
articles.authors: Bandarupalli T, Zbeidy R
articles.details: This case report describes a woman in her early 30s at 39 weeks of gestation, with no prior health issues, who had developed malarial symptoms after returning from Haiti five months earlier. Severe fetal heart rate decelerations during labor necessitated an immediate cesarean section; given her febrile state, rapid induction with etomidate and succinylcholine was used to secure the airway and limit hemodynamic instability. Intraoperative monitoring addressed malaria-related effects such as hypotension and coagulation anomalies, and empirical antimalarial treatment was started before confirmatory diagnosis. The authors emphasize flexible, anticipatory anesthetic strategies for urgent obstetric procedures involving complex infections.

articles.headline: Poor antimalarial adherence in Rwanda despite high malaria knowledge
articles.title_original: Understanding Malaria Treatment Adherence in Rwanda: Implications for Artemisinin Resistance.
articles.authors: Gashema P, Jordan A, Saramba E, Wernsman Young N, Iradukunda PG, Karema C, Mazarati JB, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Banek K
articles.details: This cross-sectional knowledge, attitudes, and practices study enrolled 406 febrile patients or caregivers at six government health facilities in moderate-to-high transmission areas of Rwanda from December 2023 to February 2024; 56% (228/406) were female and 80% (324/406) rural. Malaria knowledge was high, with 81% (329/406) correctly identifying symptoms. However, of the 44.3% (180/406) treated in the prior 6 months, only 46% (83/180) completed the appropriate 3-day course, while 37% (66/180) stopped within 2 days. Among those taking antimalarials for fever, 54% (49/109) obtained medication from drug outlets/pharmacies. The authors conclude poor adherence heightens artemisinin resistance risk.

articles.headline: P. vivax infects Duffy-negative Cameroonians; RDTs miss cases
articles.title_original: Epidemiological Insights and Duffy Binding Protein Evolution of Plasmodium vivax in Duffy-Negative Cameroonians.
articles.authors: Dieng CC, Ning RT, Kipayko C, Schroeder R, Mdluli-Berndt N, Ayukenchengamba B, Nematchoua Z, Doris S, Reward AE, Ule ISN, Kimbi HK, Lo E
articles.details: This study assessed Plasmodium vivax prevalence and genetic diversity across three ecological zones in Cameroon. P. vivax was detected in approximately 11% of febrile patients and 5.5% of asymptomatic individuals, all Duffy-negative, with clinical infections showing higher parasitemia than community-detected infections. Standard PvLDH-based rapid diagnostic tests gave false-negative results even at high parasitemia, whereas qPCR showed superior sensitivity. Genetic analysis of PvDBP1 identified two prevalent mutations, I379L (73%) and E225K (61%), suggesting adaptive evolution; Cameroonian isolates clustered with Botswanan but were distinct from Ethiopian and Sudanese isolates. The authors stress enhanced surveillance given asymptomatic transmission potential.

articles.headline: Microglia become reactive, express iNOS in experimental cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Microglia exhibit a dynamic response, modulating inducible nitric oxide synthase expression and the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines during experimental cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Freire-Antunes L, Ornellas-Garcia U, Rangel-Ferreira M, Ribeiro-Almeida ML, Carvalho LJM, Daniel-Ribeiro CT, Ribeiro-Gomes FL
articles.details: Using the experimental cerebral malaria model (Plasmodium berghei ANKA-infected C57BL/6 mice), this study used flow cytometry and lineage markers to characterize resident brain cells and infiltrating leukocytes. After infection, the numbers of Ly6C+ T cells, inflammatory monocytes, resident macrophages, and microglia (CD45low CD11b+ TMEM119+ CD206-) increased. Ex vivo analysis showed more inflammatory monocytes, resident macrophages, and microglia expressing inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS), as well as cells producing interleukin-1β or TNF. The authors conclude microglia are pronouncedly reactive in experimental cerebral malaria, providing insight into brain immune responses and cell dynamics.

articles.headline: Western Kenya health facilities under-prepared for childhood malaria and dengue
articles.title_original: Public health facility vulnerabilities, preparedness, and health outcomes for Plasmodium falciparum and dengue virus-infected children under 5 years with acute febrile illnesses in Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Ogony J, Menya D, Mangeni J, Ayodo G, Karanja S
articles.details: A three-month prospective cohort study of public health facilities, following 378 participants, found that 17 facilities (81.0%) could diagnose and treat malaria but only 4 (18.0%) could diagnose and treat dengue. In Bunyala Sub-County, 6 of 10 facilities were located on or near floodplains or wetlands, compared with 5 (45.0%) in Kisumu. The longest hospitalizations (>5 days) occurred at the Kisumu site, while the highest recovery rate was in Bunyala (184; 96.8%) versus Kisumu (171; 91.0%). Data came from questionnaires to facility staff and guardians of children plus key informant interviews, and the authors conclude facilities are vulnerable and unprepared for climate-driven infectious disease, especially dengue.

articles.headline: Review finds malaria-and-gender research mostly biomedical, reducing gender to sex data
articles.title_original: Conceptualization of gender in published malaria and gender research: a systematic descriptive review.
articles.authors: Atobrah D, Kwansa BK, Okyere-Asante PG, Kyere A, Badasu DM, Kretchy IA
articles.details: This systematic descriptive review examined how gender has been conceptualized in published malaria and gender research, searching PubMed, Scopus, Science Direct, and Google Scholar for English articles from 1995 to 2024 using the keywords "malaria AND gender," following PRISMA and applying Morgan and colleagues' gender analysis matrix. A total of 57 articles met inclusion criteria. The majority were biomedical in nature, reducing gender analysis to only sex-disaggregated data, and most used quantitative, largely laboratory-based approaches focused on sub-Saharan Africa. The authors call for more qualitative, mixed-methods, and community-based social science research that addresses domains such as access to resources, division of labour, roles, norms, and decision-making power.

articles.headline: Most households in Ugandan district self-medicate for malaria, often underdosing
articles.title_original: Self-medication for malaria and associated factors in Kakumiro District, Uganda, August 2023: implications for malaria management and mortality prevention.
articles.authors: Zalwango MG, Migisha R, Agaba BB, Bulage L, Kwesiga B, Kadobera D, Ario AR
articles.details: A community-based cross-sectional survey in Kakumiro District, Uganda, in August 2023 used multistage sampling of 592 households to identify factors associated with self-medication for malaria, analyzed with a modified Poisson regression model. Of 592 participants, 368 (62%; 95% CI 58.2-65.9%) had self-medicated. Self-medication was associated with household heads aged ≥35 years (APR 1.77; 95% CI 1.04-3.01), distance ≥5 km to a facility (APR 3.05; 95% CI 2.09-4.47), and home antimalarial storage (APR 2.21; 95% CI 1.36-3.59); having ≥6 household malaria episodes in 6 months was protective (APR 0.39; 95% CI 0.23-0.65). The main reason was facility stockouts; 85% used the recommended drug but the dose was inappropriate in 66%, yet 85% recovered without hospitalization. Authors warn of resistance and mortality risks.

articles.headline: Only 31% of pregnant Tanzanian women get optimal IPTp-SP malaria doses
articles.title_original: Individual and community-level correlates of optimal doses of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine for intermittent preventive treatment of malaria during pregnancy in Tanzania: a multilevel analysis of the 2022 national survey.
articles.authors: Stephano EE, Yusheng T, John TW, Kibusi SM, Yamin L, Mtoro MJ
articles.details: This analytical cross-sectional study used 2022 Tanzania Demographic and Health Survey data from 4497 women who gave birth within two years before the survey, applying multilevel mixed-effects logistic regression to identify correlates of optimal (≥3 doses) sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp-SP). Overall optimal uptake was 31.2% (95% CI 29.1-33.3). Higher odds were seen for women aged 25-34 (AOR 1.52) and 35-49 (AOR 1.47), primary education (AOR 1.34), having one (AOR 1.65) or two children (AOR 1.37), more than three ANC visits (AOR 1.77), high-ANC communities (AOR 1.49), and Northern (AOR 1.61) and Lake (AOR 1.68) zones; Zanzibar had very low uptake (AOR 0.01) as IPTp-SP is no longer recommended there. Authors urge integration with reproductive health services.

articles.headline: Perceived effectiveness and education drive community engagement for under-five malaria vaccine
articles.title_original: Community engagement in promoting malaria vaccine uptake for children Under-Five.
articles.authors: Adoma PO, Acquah F, Yakubu A, Cudjoe L, Achiam WKK, Agorinya IA
articles.details: This descriptive and analytical cross-sectional study used structured questionnaires and systematic sampling to recruit 1,183 participants (from a sample size table for a 2,500 under-five population) to examine factors influencing community engagement in promoting malaria vaccine uptake among children under five. Using bivariate and random forest analysis, perception of vaccine effectiveness, educational level, and age were the most relevant factors, while vaccine safety and acceptability were moderate factors. People with higher education prioritized vaccine effectiveness and safety, whereas those with lower education prioritized vaccine cost and cultural considerations. Authors conclude that educational programs tailored to demographic differences in perceptions can leverage community engagement to boost vaccine uptake.

articles.headline: Review: transfusion-transmitted malaria risk and blood-screening gaps in India
articles.title_original: Transfusion-transmitted Plasmodium spp. infections and safety challenges for malaria in the Indian subcontinent: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Kojom Foko LP, Sharma S, Sharma A
articles.details: This review of 122 studies involving more than 6.5 million individuals examined transfusion-transmitted malaria (TTM) in India over the past five decades. Plasmodium parasitaemia in donors ranged from ∼0% to 0.87% by light microscopy and ∼0% to 2.3% by rapid diagnostic tests, while post-transfusion malaria cases ranged from 0.8% to 6.8% across studies. The authors note the risk is time- and diagnosis-method-dependent, involves the often-neglected P. malariae alongside P. vivax and P. falciparum, and is compounded by drug-resistant and pfhrp2 gene-deleted P. falciparum. They recommend point-of-care nucleic acid amplification screening for safer transfusion and to support malaria elimination.

articles.headline: Review warns of overreliance on 18S rRNA gene for malaria PCR
articles.title_original: Overreliance on Plasmodium 18S rRNA gene for malaria molecular diagnosis-inferences from systematic review.
articles.authors: Deora N, Khan MR, Singh P, Zehra N, Sircar S, Pande V, Mallick PK, Sinha A
articles.details: This systematic review examined PCR-based molecular targets used for detecting Plasmodium infections, searching PubMed and Google Scholar to retrieve the molecular targets and assay types. Almost all studies (93%) used the 18S rRNA gene as the molecular target, and nested PCR alone was the most common assay (68%); 85% of studies using 18S rRNA and nested PCR relied on an approach developed in 1993. The authors conclude that decades of overreliance on a single molecular target is a concern, drawing a parallel to false-negative PfHRP2-based RDTs, and call for validation of newer multi-copy targets for limit of detection, reproducibility, lower cost, and multiplexing.

articles.headline: EEG distinguishes retinopathy-negative cerebral malaria from nonmalarial coma
articles.title_original: Using Electroencephalography to Assess Coma Etiology in Children with Retinopathy-Negative Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Takle M, Andrews A, Riggle BA, Zelleke T, Harrar D, Zhang J, Zhang B, Wilson KJ, Beare NAV, Taylor TE, Seydel KB, Ray S, Postels DG
articles.details: This study compared qualitative and quantitative EEG findings from 30-minute recordings of children aged 3 months to 14 years hospitalized at Queen Elizabeth Central Hospital with retinopathy-negative cerebral malaria (CM), retinopathy-positive CM, and nonmalarial coma, to assess the role of acute malaria infection in retinopathy-negative CM. Neither qualitative nor quantitative EEG could distinguish retinopathy-positive from retinopathy-negative CM, but quantitative EEG differentiated retinopathy-negative CM from nonmalarial coma (AUROC 0.83), rising to 0.87 when combining methods. The authors conclude that EEGs of retinopathy-negative CM resemble retinopathy-positive CM and differ from nonmalarial coma, supporting acute malarial infection as pathophysiologically important in retinopathy-negative CM.

articles.headline: Chaperonin complex controls apicoplast proteostasis and is a drug target
articles.title_original: A chaperonin complex regulates organelle proteostasis in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Tissawak A, Rosin Y, Katz Galay S, Qasem A, Shahar M, Trabelsi N, Furman-Schueler O, Johnson SM, Florentin A
articles.details: Using genetic tools in Plasmodium parasites, researchers identified and characterized a conserved chaperonin (CPN) complex regulating proteostasis in the apicoplast, an organelle whose proteome is 90% nucleus-encoded. Ablation of the apicoplast CPN60 subunit caused parasite death from organellar damage within the first replication cycle, unlike the delayed-death phenotype of apicoplast translation inhibitors. CPN60 was not upregulated during heat shock; instead it physically bound both active and inactive forms of the Clp proteolytic complex and modulated its stability. Screening inhibitors of the bacterial CPN60 orthologue GroEL showed anti-Plasmodium activity with targets beyond the apicoplast, suggesting chaperonin inhibition as a potential antimalarial strategy.

articles.headline: Anti-phosphatidylserine antibodies and cytokines linked to vivax thrombocytopenia
articles.title_original: Thrombocytopenia in patients with Plasmodium vivax in Colombia is associated with anti-phosphatidylserine autoantibodies and IL-6, IFNγ, IL-10 and TGF-β.
articles.authors: Velasco-Pareja MC, Tovar-Acero C, Cantero Guevara ME, Rodriguez A, Rivera-Correa J, Yasnot-Acosta MF
articles.details: This study evaluated plasma cytokines/chemokines and anti-phosphatidylserine (anti-PS) autoantibodies in patients with P. vivax malaria and thrombocytopenia (MT), malaria without thrombocytopenia (M), and healthy controls in Córdoba, Colombia, to explore mechanisms of thrombocytopenia. IFNγ, IL-6, and IL-10 were increased in the MT group (P<0.05), whereas TGF-β1 was increased in the M group (P<0.0001). Anti-PS antibody levels were higher in the MT group and correlated negatively with platelet counts. The authors suggest thrombocytopenia in P. vivax malaria may arise from autoantibodies targeting phosphatidylserine on activated platelets, driven by a pro-inflammatory cytokine imbalance, with TGF-β1 possibly playing a protective role.

articles.headline: Scoping review: community knowledge shapes malaria prevention success
articles.title_original: A scoping review of community knowledge in malaria prevention and control programmes.
articles.authors: Abdul Rahim FA, Mahmud MAF, Abdul Mutalip MH, Yoep N, Aminuddin MAH, Mohd Ngesom AM
articles.details: This PRISMA-ScR scoping review mapped evidence on community knowledge in malaria prevention, control, and elimination, searching Scopus, PubMed, and Web of Science for English-language studies published 2000-2024. It included 63 studies from 27 countries, predominantly Africa (68.3%) and Asia (28.6%). Higher awareness was consistently linked with better preventive practices and earlier treatment-seeking, but knowledge gaps persisted around environmental risk factors and vector control. The authors conclude community knowledge is a pivotal determinant of prevention success and call for locally tailored, evidence-based education.

articles.headline: Malaria service readiness boosts IPTp-SP uptake more in urban Senegal
articles.title_original: Bridging urban-rural disparities in malaria care during pregnancy in Senegal: evidence from household and health facility surveys.
articles.authors: Jiang Y, Liang D, Zhao J, Prasad S, Ndiop M, Thiam SA, Diallo I, Sene D, Mpembeni R, Huang J
articles.details: This analysis linked seven annual rounds of Demographic and Health Surveys and Service Provision Assessments in Senegal (2012-2019) at the regional level, using a malaria service readiness index and a Heckman selection model to study IPTp-SP (sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine) uptake in pregnancy. The average doses received was 1.66 (95% CI 1.65-1.68), higher in urban (1.73) than rural (1.63) areas. Each one-point rise in service readiness increased doses by 0.251, but a significant interaction (Coef. -0.523, P<0.001) showed rural women gained fewer doses (0.089) than urban women (0.612) per unit. Authors urge differential urban-rural strategies.

articles.headline: Correction: CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing system for Plasmodium berghei
articles.title_original: Correction to 'A scalable CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing system facilitates CRISPR screens in the malaria parasite Plasmodium berghei'.
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: BG-Pro and Silver Bullet UV traps outperform CDC trap in Kenya
articles.title_original: Assessment of BG-Pro (Biogent AG) and Silver Bullet 2.1 (Lumin8) UV-Light Traps Efficiency for Surveillance of Malaria Vectors in Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Amugune BL, Tamre R, Mogaka D, Mbare O, Bukhari T, Fillinger U, Njoroge MM
articles.details: This study compared the efficiency of BG-Pro UV and Silver Bullet 2.1 UV light traps against the UV LED CDC trap for malaria vector surveillance in a rice irrigation area of western Kenya, testing indoors across eight houses over 64 nights. The BG-Pro UV outperformed the CDC trap for An. gambiae s.l. (RR 2.0, 95% CI 0.9-3.9) and An. funestus s.l. (RR 3.5, 95% CI 1.9-6.4), while the SB 2.1 UV was more effective for An. gambiae s.l. (RR 4.3, 95% CI 2.5-7.3), An. funestus s.l. (RR 7.1, 95% CI 3.9-13.1), and Culex spp. (RR 2.7, 95% CI 1.2-6.0). Both traps caught three to six times more than the CDC trap, making them promising surveillance tools in low-density settings.

articles.headline: Prior COVID-19 linked to severe malaria and dengue in southern India
articles.title_original: COVID-19 infection and its association with severe malaria & dengue: an epidemiological study from Southern India.
articles.authors: Ommen SJ, Mithra P, T R, Kumar N, Holla R, Kulal N, Rao M, Unnikrishnan B
articles.details: This case-control study of 293 participants positive for either malaria or dengue assessed whether prior COVID-19 infection was associated with severe disease, using chi-square tests and binary logistic regression. Of participants, 23.9% (70) had malaria and 76.1% (223) had dengue; 30% of malaria cases and 35.8% of dengue cases were severe. Among those with a history of COVID-19, 85.7% (12) developed severe malaria (aOR 263.7, 95% CI 34.9-1987.4, p<0.001) and 98.1% (52) developed severe dengue (aOR 55.93, 95% CI 7.17-435.92, p<0.001). Lower socioeconomic class was also associated with severe dengue. Authors conclude prior COVID-19 raises the chance of severe malaria and dengue.

articles.headline: Malaria rapid test accuracy varies by transmission level and demographics
articles.title_original: Varying Malaria Rapid Diagnostic Test Accuracy by Regional Transmission Level and Demographics in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Wiener D, Seth MD, Mandara CI, Madebe RA, Popkin-Hall ZR, Giesbrecht D, Bakari C, Lyimo B, Pereus D, Francis F, Mbwambo D, Aaron S, Lusasi A, Lazaro S, Sheahan TP, Parr JB, Bailey JA, Ishengoma DS, Juliano JJ
articles.details: A study compared three national-standard malaria rapid diagnostic tests (mRDTs) and one research mRDT against qPCR using samples from a 2021 cross-sectional study in Tanzania. Test agreement and performance differed by age, gender, and regional malaria transmission rate. The research test overall underperformed, showing poor sensitivity across all transmission strata, and odds ratios suggested transmission intensity may influence mRDT agreement and diagnostic performance. The authors conclude these findings inform test accuracy and help reduce outcome misclassification in malaria prevalence estimates. No specific numeric sensitivity values or odds ratios are reported in the abstract.

articles.headline: Computational tool nominates conserved T cell epitopes for malaria vaccine design
articles.title_original: Computational Prioritization of T Cell Epitopes to Overcome HLA Restriction and Antigenic Diversity in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Laurenson AJ, Pierce BG, Takala-Harrison S, Laurens MB
articles.details: Researchers developed TEpiNom, a computational tool integrating P. falciparum sequence diversity, predicted T cell epitope-HLA binding affinities, and sub-Saharan African HLA allele frequencies to identify conserved, broadly covering epitopes. They analyzed 42 candidate antigen proteins using consensus sequences from 18 African countries and HLA frequencies from 24 sub-Saharan populations, predicting CD8+ and CD4+ epitopes with NetMHCpan-4.1 and NetMHCIIpan-4.1 and filtering by >95% conservation, median binding rank <10%, and broad HLA coverage. The tool identified 2,265 MHC I and 1,992 MHC II conserved epitopes spanning pre-erythrocytic, erythrocytic, and sexual stage proteins; key pre-erythrocytic MHC I epitopes reached near 100% HLA-A/B/C coverage and MHC II epitopes gave 98.5%-100% coverage for a given parasite life stage. The authors present this as a strategy for broad, durable multi-epitope vaccine design.

articles.headline: Genome atlas maps positive selection in African malaria mosquito vectors
articles.title_original: An atlas of positive selection in the genomes of major malaria vectors.
articles.authors: Nagi SC, Harding NJ, Lawniczak MKN, Donnelly MJ, Miles A
articles.details: Investigators built an atlas of recent positive selection in the major African malaria vectors—the An. gambiae species complex and An. funestus—by analyzing whole-genome sequence data from 4,306 mosquitoes collected across twenty-one countries. Using a new approach to identify and localize selection signals, they found extensive parallel evolution between species, with all important An. funestus loci also under selection in An. gambiae s.l. They confirmed intense selection at known resistance loci (target-site Vgsc, Rdl, Ace1 and metabolic Cyp6p, Cyp9k1, Gste, Coeaexf) and described novel signals at a diacylglycerol kinase on the X chromosome and, in An. gambiae s.l., at the metabolic regulator Keap1. An open-source web resource with automated updates supports real-time monitoring of emerging resistance variants.

articles.headline: Gold mining and mobility drive localized malaria in Yanomami Territory
articles.title_original: Human mobility, garimpos and spatiotemporal malaria transmission in the Yanomami Indigenous Territory: a retrospective observational study.
articles.authors: Barbieri AF, Santos RO
articles.details: Using epidemiological surveillance data from 2007 to 2023, researchers built Malaria Mobility Networks (MMNs) to distinguish autochthonous from imported malaria cases within and near Brazil's Yanomami Indigenous Territory, where illegal gold mining (garimpo) has contributed to resurgence. Cases (86%) and population (91%) were spatially concentrated, while most grid cells had misattributed malaria (94%) and population (86%) risks; the analysis improved spatial identification of cases by 12% (70% in garimpos) and corrected 20% of inaccurate coordinates. Autochthonous malaria in indigenous localities reached 90% in 2023, and MMNs involving infection in garimpos rose since 2019, peaking around 27% in 2022 and being more associated with urban residence. The authors conclude transmission aligns with garimpo dynamics and garimpo-urban networks.

articles.headline: Low provider adherence to malaria treatment guidelines in Southwest Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Evaluation of anti-malarial treatment for elimination of malaria in South West Ethiopia: a concurrent triangulation design for prompt decision.
articles.authors: Gebremichael MA, Gebremedhin T, Niguse W, Mamo G, Alemayehu B, Negeri E, Temam I, Zewude H, Bogale T, Sahile E, Pawlos E
articles.details: A facility-based cross-sectional study triangulated with qualitative inquiry (focus group discussions and in-depth interviews) assessed healthcare provider adherence to malaria diagnosis and treatment guidelines in public facilities in Southwest Ethiopian Peoples Regional State, reviewing 1,684 patient cards (mean patient age 20.99 ± 15.61 years). Microscopy and/or multispecies RDT screened 1,616 patients (95.96%), and P. falciparum was the majority diagnosis at 982 (58.31%), followed by P. vivax and mixed infections. Overall provider adherence to updated treatment standards was low at 36.99% (95% CI 33.93-38.52%). The leading reported barrier was anti-malarial drug shortages, followed by lack of training, staff shortages, and lab material shortages. Authors recommend ensuring drug supply, qualified staff, continuous training, and supervision.

articles.headline: Serum endocan rises with malaria severity, highest in severe P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Preliminary assessment of serum endocan as a biomarker of disease severity in Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax malaria.
articles.authors: Nantavisai K, Puttikamonkul S, Viriyavejakul P
articles.details: This study measured serum endocan, a marker of endothelial dysfunction, in 99 participants: 20 healthy controls, 36 P. vivax, 30 uncomplicated P. falciparum, and 13 severe P. falciparum malaria, by ELISA on day 0 and day 7. All malaria groups had higher endocan than controls (p<0.0001), with levels highest in severe P. falciparum (median 4.67 ng/ml), then uncomplicated P. falciparum (3.27 ng/ml) and P. vivax (1.85 ng/ml). Endocan correlated positively with parasite density in P. vivax (rs=0.46, p=0.0066) and severe P. falciparum (rs=0.63, p=0.0251), and negatively with platelet count in P. vivax (rs=-0.55, p=0.001). Authors conclude endocan is a severity-dependent biomarker of endothelial injury.

articles.headline: Artemisinin partial-resistance mutations rising in Ethiopian P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Rising prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum Artemisinin partial resistance mutations in Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Brhane BG, Fola AA, Nigussie H, Leonetti A, Kassa M, Hailgiorgis H, Wuletaw Y, Abera A, Mohammed H, Sime H, G/Tsadik A, Assefa G, Solomon H, Tasew G, Tollera G, Hailu M, Juliano JJ, Assefa A, Parr JB, Bailey JA
articles.details: Researchers analyzed 1199 clinical P. falciparum infections from 12 sentinel sites across five regions in Ethiopia (2019–2023) using two molecular inversion probe panels targeting drug-resistance genes and genome-wide SNPs. The k13 R622I mutation appeared in 15.7% of samples with regional variation, and three validated artemisinin partial-resistance mutations (P441L, P574L, A675V) were detected for the first time, with A675V found only in a Gambella clinic serving Sudanese/South Sudanese refugees. Partner-drug resistance polymorphisms in crt, mdr1, dhps, and dhfr were nearly fixed; 87.2% of infections were monogenomic (COI=1), with high local relatedness suggesting clonal transmission. Authors urge enhanced ACT efficacy monitoring.

articles.headline: Malaria trials often underestimate cluster heterogeneity, reducing study power
articles.title_original: Characterisation of between-cluster heterogeneity in malaria cluster randomised trials to inform future sample size calculations.
articles.authors: Biggs J, Challenger JD, Dee D, Elobolobo E, Chaccour C, Saute F, Staedke SG, Vilakati S, Chung JB, Hsiang MS, Dabira ED, Erhart A, D'Alessandro U, Tripura R, Peto TJ, Von Seidlein L, Mukaka M, Mosha J, Protopopoff N, Accrombessi M, Hayes R, Churcher TS, Cook J
articles.details: This meta-analysis of cluster-summary data from 24 malaria cluster randomised trials calculated true prevalence and incidence values of the coefficient of variation (k), a measure of between-cluster heterogeneity, using methods-of-moments and regression modelling. Empirical k estimates often exceeded those used in original sample size calculations, which reduced study power and effect-size precision. Elevated k was associated with incidence outcomes (versus prevalence), lower endemicity settings, and uneven intervention coverage across clusters. Authors conclude their informed k estimates can improve the robustness of future malaria CRT sample size calculations when cluster-level data are unavailable.

articles.headline: Cost analysis of genetic sterile-mosquito malaria control in The Gambia
articles.title_original: Evaluating the cost of malaria elimination by Anopheles gambiae precision guided SIT in the Upper River region, The Gambia.
articles.authors: Gendron WAC, Raban R, Mondal A, M Sánchez C H, Smidler AL, Zilberman D, Ilboudo PGC, D'Alessandro U, Marshall JM, Akbari OS
articles.details: Researchers modelled the health outcomes and costs of precision guided sterile insect technique (pgSIT), which releases genetically sterile male mosquitoes, working alongside current interventions against the Anopheles gambiae vector in the Upper River region of The Gambia. The intervention is predicted to prevent approximately 230 deaths and about 48,000 sick days per year. Estimated costs were 11–94 USD per disability-adjusted life year saved, 10–86 USD per infection prevented, and 0.36–3.03 USD per person in the treated region annually. Authors conclude the cost per DALY is comparable to current interventions in the region.

articles.headline: Repurposed Chk1 inhibitor CHIR-124 kills malaria parasites via dual targets
articles.title_original: The Human Chk1 Inhibitor CHIR-124 Shows Multistage Activity Against Plasmodium falciparum via Dual Inhibition of PfArk1 and Hemozoin Formation.
articles.authors: Wicht KJ, Woodland JG, Garnie LF, Langeveld H, Taylor D, Godoy LC, Pasaje CFA, de Souza ML, Siqueira-Neto JL, Ghidelli-Disse S, Lafuente-Monasterio MJ, Gamo FJ, Coertzen D, Reader J, van der Watt M, Bridgford JL, Girling G, Coyle R, Scheurer C, Wittlin S, Lee MCS, Voss TS, Winzeler EA, Fidock DA, Niles JC, Birkholtz LM, Coulson LB, Chibale K
articles.details: This study repurposed the human checkpoint kinase 1 (Chk1) inhibitor CHIR-124 against P. falciparum. The compound was active in vitro against both drug-sensitive and drug-resistant asexual blood-stage parasites and competitively bound several Plasmodium kinases, including P. falciparum aurora-related kinase-1 (PfArk1), with moderate activity against liver and gametocyte stages. Conditional knockdown confirmed PfArk1 involvement in its activity. CHIR-124 also inhibited β-hematin (synthetic hemozoin) formation, causing a dose-dependent rise in free heme correlating with growth inhibition. Authors conclude its activity reflects polypharmacology through dual inhibition of PfArk1 and hemozoin formation, supporting design of dual inhibitors with reduced resistance risk.

articles.headline: Blood count parameters classify P. falciparum severity and prior exposure
articles.title_original: Comparative Analysis of Initial Full Blood Count Parameters in Adults Infected With Plasmodium falciparum for Classification of Disease Severity and Previous Exposure Across Endemic (Gabon) and Nonendemic (Germany) Settings.
articles.authors: von Wedel C, Matthies LC, Dierks C, Tober-Lau P, Bardtke L, Lingscheid T, Nordmann T, Jochum J, Huebl L, Tappe D, Zoller T, Pechmann K, Okwu DG, Malinga EG, Ralser M, Sander LE, Zoleko-Manego R, Ramharter M, Mombo-Ngoma G, Kurth F, Mischlinger J
articles.details: This analysis of 1392 adults infected with Plasmodium falciparum in Gabon and Germany evaluated full blood count parameters individually and via an ensemble machine learning classifier to predict disease severity from asymptomatic parasitemia to severe malaria. Greater severity was associated with significantly lower platelet counts (218 vs 150 ×10³/µL in Gabonese asymptomatic vs uncomplicated; 85 vs 40 ×10³/µL in German uncomplicated vs severe; both P < .0001) and higher neutrophil counts. The machine learning classifier outperformed single parameters (AUC 0.94 and 0.84). Lymphocyte and monocyte counts tracked prior exposure, being lower in naive than previously exposed patients.

articles.headline: Neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio accurately flags malaria inflammation
articles.title_original: Diagnostic value of full blood count derived systemic inflammatory biomarkers in malaria infection.
articles.authors: Boachie J, Ahiable D, Ajabuin LA, Amissah R, Asmah-Brown A, Apalebilah S, Owusu-Poku AG, Eshun H, Adu P, Nyarkoh JK
articles.details: In a case-control study of 45 malaria patients and 50 healthy individuals, full blood count–derived systemic inflammatory biomarkers were assessed for diagnostic value. Malaria patients had higher monocyte-to-lymphocyte ratio (MLR), neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR), platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio (PLR), and aggregate index of systemic inflammation (AISI). Parasite count correlated moderately with NLR (ρ = 0.5) and MLR (ρ = 0.4), and weakly negatively with PLR and AISI (ρ = −0.2 each). NLR had excellent diagnostic value (AUC = 0.937); at a cutoff of 2.12 or above it was 86.7% sensitive and 92.0% specific, offering a cost-effective inflammatory marker for resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Splenic macrophage subsets divide labor in mouse malaria immunity
articles.title_original: Red pulp macrophages clear parasites, while marginal metallophilic and marginal zone macrophages support CD4(+) T cell activation during Plasmodium yoelii infection.
articles.authors: Falkenstein J, Ninnemann A, Hose M, Elwy A, Lang KS, Matuschewski K, Buer J, Westendorf AM, Hansen W
articles.details: Using Plasmodium yoelii infection in mice, this study dissected the roles of splenic macrophage subsets in parasite clearance and T cell responses. In dendritic cell–deficient mice, marginal zone and marginal metallophilic macrophages increased with altered cytokine profiles. Diphtheria toxin depletion of these marginal-zone macrophages in CD169DTR mice reduced IFNγ production by CD4+ T cells but did not change parasitemia, whereas clodronate-liposome macrophage ablation partially reduced T cell activation and raised parasitemia. Specific red pulp macrophage depletion (SpiCflox/flox × vav1cre) elevated parasitemia. The authors conclude marginal-zone macrophages support CD4+ T cell activation while red pulp macrophages are indispensable for clearing infected red blood cells.

articles.headline: Review of P. falciparum pathogenesis and new therapeutic targets
articles.title_original: Emerging Molecular Mechanisms in Malaria Pathogenesis and Novel Therapeutic Approaches: A Focus on P. falciparum Malaria.
articles.authors: Sanyaolu A, Marinkovic A, Prakash S, Balendra V, Shazley O, Gardellini T, Jan A, Younis K, Okorie C, Izurieta R
articles.details: This review examines the molecular mechanisms of malaria pathogenesis, including how the parasite evades the immune system and develops resistance to current drugs, and surveys emerging therapeutic approaches. It highlights genetic and biochemical advances revealing new parasite vulnerabilities, and discusses the RTS,S and R21 vaccines alongside antibody-based therapies. The authors note that extending the duration of vaccine-induced immunity and ensuring effectiveness across diverse parasite strains remain major challenges, and emphasize that ending malaria also requires equitable access to treatment, strong health systems, and international collaboration.

articles.headline: Weather and ENSO events shape malaria transmission in Brazilian Amazon
articles.title_original: The impact of weather and extreme events on malaria transmission in the Brazilian Amazon: a case-crossover and population-based study.
articles.authors: Arisco NJ, Peterka C, Schwartz J, Castro MC
articles.details: This case-crossover study analyzed individual-level reported malaria cases from the Brazilian Amazon surveillance system to estimate the effects of weather and El Niño–Southern Oscillation (ENSO) events, using generalized additive quasi-Poisson models. Among 5,381,105 recorded malaria cases, temperatures between 25.64 and 30.85 °C and precipitation >4.46 cm in the prior week increased infection risk by up to 9% (95% CI: 8–10%) and 86% (95% CI: 35–155%), respectively, while two- and three-week lagged higher temperatures and diurnal variation reduced risk by up to 27% and 59%. El Niño and La Niña were associated with net reductions of 2179 and 37,258 cases, with marked spatiotemporal heterogeneity. The authors call for weather-based early warning systems to achieve malaria elimination.

articles.headline: Uganda 2022 malaria epidemic: 3.3 million excess cases across 64 districts
articles.title_original: Malaria epidemics and its drivers in Uganda in 2022.
articles.authors: Aregawi MW, Maiteki C, Rek JC, Agaba B, Katureebe C, Ranjbar M, Zhang C, Kiware S, Opigo J
articles.details: This study analysed District Health Information System (DHIS2) data from 2017–2022 in Uganda, using a 75th-percentile threshold (2017–2021) to define epidemics and area-under-the-curve calculations to quantify excess burden. Malaria cases rose 31% in 2022 versus the 2017–2021 third quartile, with 64 of 146 districts experiencing epidemics (4 year-round). The 2022 epidemic accounted for an estimated 3,379,309 excess outpatient cases, 3,018,920 excess confirmed cases, and 149,789 excess inpatient cases. Epidemics paradoxically occurred more in IRS and iCCM districts; rainfall lagged by two months significantly raised incidence (p<0.01), each millimetre linked to 13.4 additional cases. Authors conclude programmatic gaps, not climate, mainly drove the resurgence.

articles.headline: Malaria care cascade for children and pregnant women in Guinea and Sierra Leone
articles.title_original: The malaria care cascade and factors associated with receiving a malaria test in children under 5 years of age and pregnant women with fever: a cross-sectional household survey from three regions in Guinea and three districts in Sierra Leone in 2022.
articles.authors: Burger G, Röbl K, Fischer HT, Feddern L, Pozo-Martin F, Geurts B, Baldé H, Kaba I, Menelik-Obbarius S, Stein K, Merhi V, Weishaar H, Kallon MMM, Dumbuya K, Sellu J, Mbawah AK, Delamou A, El Bcheraoui C
articles.details: This cross-sectional household survey (May–August 2022) assessed the malaria care cascade and factors linked to receiving a malaria test during fever among children under 5 and pregnant women across three regions of Guinea and three districts of Sierra Leone. Caregivers of 1,377 children and 254 pregnant women with fever were surveyed. Reported testing rates ranged 42–96% in children and 62–91% in pregnant women by area; treatment within 2 days of fever onset reached 54–81% of children and 24–69% of pregnant women. Hospitalisation occurred in 6.5% and 4.3% of children and 9.5% and 12% of pregnant women in Guinea and Sierra Leone. Trust in the health system, informative messaging, and ANC attendance were associated with testing.

articles.headline: Placental malaria and preeclampsia association explored in Malawi
articles.title_original: The Relationship between Placental Malaria and Preeclampsia in Blantyre, Malawi.
articles.authors: Boudova S, Divala TH, Mungwira R, Mawindo P, Tomoka T, Laufer MK
articles.details: Using data from a randomized controlled trial of intermittent preventive therapy in pregnancy in Malawi, researchers examined the association between placental malaria (PM) and preeclampsia (PreE), including eclampsia, among singleton pregnancies with placental histopathology and PCR. Among 751 pregnancies, 125 (16.7%) had PM by histopathology or PCR, including 105 with hemozoin pigment indicating early-pregnancy infection. PreE without eclampsia occurred in 25 (3.3%), eclampsia in 6 (0.7%), and any hypertensive disorder in 98 (13.0%). Individuals with hemozoin had nearly twice the PreE rate (6.7% vs 3.7%, P=0.16), but this was not statistically significant; the study was underpowered, and further research is warranted.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccination to offset climate-driven control gaps in Madagascar
articles.title_original: Vaccination to mitigate climate-driven disruptions to malaria control in Madagascar.
articles.authors: Rice BL, Raobson E, Miharisoa S, Rebaliha M, Lewinski J, Raharinirina H, Golden CD, Vecchi GA, Wesolowski A, Grenfell B, Metcalf CJE
articles.details: This analysis examined how malaria vaccines could mitigate disruptions to malaria control after extreme weather events in Madagascar. Data on malaria infection following major tropical cyclones indicate infection rebounds rapidly during gaps in interventions after extreme events. Noting that vaccines offer a longer duration of protection than other control options, the authors quantified the reduction in symptomatic infections expected across a range of vaccination scenarios, framing vaccination as a way to address interruptions in prevention and treatment deployment.

articles.headline: Malaria and anemia in Ethiopian schoolchildren strongly linked
articles.title_original: Prevalence of malaria, anemia and associated factors among school children in Hawassa city, Sidama, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Gena A, Asnake S, Menjetta T
articles.details: A cross-sectional study (April–June 2024) in selected public primary schools in Hawassa City, Sidama, Ethiopia, recruited 329 children, using blood films, rapid diagnostic tests, and a digital hemoglobinometer with logistic regression. Malaria prevalence was 8.5% and anemia 9.4%, with 29% of anemic children also infected. Anemia odds were highest among children with malaria (AOR=4.983, 95% CI 1.067–23.265) and prior malaria history (AOR=9.121, 95% CI 1.686–49.336), and malaria-infected children were over 13 times more likely to be anemic (AOR=13.258, 95% CI 3.188–55.139). Insecticide-treated net use (AOR=0.024) and knowledge of malaria transmission (AOR=0.205) significantly reduced malaria risk, and the authors call for integrated malaria prevention and nutrition programs.

articles.headline: Blood group not linked to malaria risk in deployed soldiers on prophylaxis
articles.title_original: Impact of the ABO and Rh Blood Groups on Malaria Incidence, Recurrence, and Severity Among a Moroccan Military Contingent in the Central African Republic.
articles.authors: El Faria W, Loubadi Y, Moutmir Y, Ababou M, Hammani A, Mahtat EM, Jennane S, El Maaroufi H, Doghmi K
articles.details: This eight-month study (October 2024–May 2025) followed 749 Moroccan soldiers deployed in the Central African Republic, all taking weekly mefloquine chemoprophylaxis and using bed nets and repellents, to test whether ABO and Rh blood groups affected malaria incidence, recurrence, or severity. During the study, 156 soldiers (20.8%) had at least one malaria episode, 89% caused by P. falciparum, and 8 (1.1%) developed severe malaria. Statistical analysis found no significant association between ABO or Rh blood group and malaria incidence, recurrence, or severity, suggesting that rigorous prevention measures outweigh any blood-group effect.

articles.headline: How var2csa acts as a switching hub coordinating malaria antigenic variation
articles.title_original: The molecular basis of coordinated antigen switching enabling chronic infection by human malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Visone JE, Florini F, Hadjimichael E, Patel V, Deitsch KW
articles.details: This mechanistic study examines how P. falciparum coordinates switching between var genes, which encode the surface protein PfEMP1 underlying antigenic variation and chronic infection. The authors describe how noncoding RNAs, an upstream open reading frame, and the nonsense-mediated mRNA decay pathway interact to let the conserved gene var2csa function as a transiently activated 'switching hub.' This hub is proposed to unify the var gene family into a single coordinated network, thereby perpetuating chronic infection. No quantitative clinical results are reported.

articles.headline: Commentary urges malaria vaccination and action for children in Uganda
articles.title_original: Addressing the malaria crisis among children in Uganda: the urgent need for vaccination and strategic action.
articles.authors: Akilimali A, Tague C, Karegeya A, Mugenyi N, Taj J, Mufungizi I, Naaz F, Mugisha MJM, Cakirwa H, Yokolo H, Ekouo J, Makeda D, Hangi S, Mbabazi S
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: HRP2 RDTs miss P. malariae and P. ovale in young children
articles.title_original: Undetected Plasmodium malariae and P. ovale infections in HRP2 RDT-positive children with uncomplicated malaria in Nanoro, Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Kouevi AFC, Bayala IMC, Sondo P, Kaboré B, Millogo KS, Kambou SAE, Compaore EW, Nikiema M, Kazienga A, Rouamba T, Gnémé A, Tinto H
articles.details: This study in the Nanoro health district, Burkina Faso, assessed how often P. malariae and P. ovale infections go undetected by HRP2-based rapid diagnostic tests, which are specific to P. falciparum. From July 2021 to June 2022, 207 children under 5 with uncomplicated malaria who tested HRP2-positive were examined by microscopy and nested PCR. Microscopy detected 4 non-falciparum cases (3 P. malariae, 1 P. ovale), with additional sub-patent infections in 6 (P. ovale) and 5 (P. malariae) cases; mixed infections had lower parasite densities than P. falciparum mono-infections. Age and sex had no effect on mixed-infection positivity (X²=0.16, p=0.683). The authors conclude HRP2 RDTs underestimate non-falciparum burden and new diagnostic tools are needed.

articles.headline: P. knowlesi triggers IL-10 dominance in humans, IL-6 in macaques
articles.title_original: Comparative immunological study of Plasmodium knowlesi infections in humans and macaques: insights into cytokine dynamics.
articles.authors: Abd Rachman Isnadi MF, Yong YK, Grigg MJ, Sipangkui S, Lee PC, Nuin NA, Tan AF, Molius P, Tuuga A, Abd Sukor JR, Rajahram G, Daim S, Chua TH
articles.details: This study in Sabah, Malaysia, compared cytokine responses and DNA release in P. knowlesi infection between humans and macaques. Blood from 30 P. knowlesi-infected individuals and 30 healthy controls, plus serum from 35 wild macaques, was analyzed by PCR and immunological assays, with six cytokines (TNF, IFNγ, IL-1β, IL-4, IL-6, IL-10) measured. Parasitaemia correlated strongly with parasite genomic DNA; infected macaques showed significantly higher parasite gDNA and mtDNA than humans. Humans displayed IL-10 dominance positively associated with parasite gDNA, while macaques showed IL-6 dominance unrelated to gDNA; despite lower gDNA, patients had a higher IL-10/TNF ratio indicative of severity. The authors highlight differing immune responses in acute (human) versus presumed chronic (macaque) infection.

articles.headline: Protocol: high-dose short-course primaquine after G6PD testing for P. vivax
articles.title_original: High daily dose Short COurse PrimaquinE after G6PD testing for the radical cure of Plasmodium vivax malaria in Indonesia and Papua New Guinea: the SCOPE implementation study protocol.
articles.authors: SCOPE Study Group
articles.details: This is a staged, binational, multicentre before-and-after implementation study protocol evaluating the safety, feasibility, and cost-effectiveness of an enhanced P. vivax radical-cure package. Interventions include pre-treatment G6PD testing using a semi-quantitative point-of-care device, then high total-dose primaquine (7mg/kg) given over 7 days for G6PD-normal patients (≥70% activity), 14 days for intermediate patients (30–<70% activity), or weekly primaquine over 8 weeks for deficient patients (<30% activity), plus patient education, community-based review, and enhanced surveillance. Stage 1 will enrol 800 patients at 4 clinics across Indonesia and Papua New Guinea to assess safety; Stage 2 will run 12 months, expand to 10 clinics, and recruit approximately 11,410 patients to evaluate feasibility and cost-effectiveness. No results are reported.

articles.headline: Deep-learning ensemble detects malaria in blood smears at 96% accuracy
articles.title_original: Automated multi-model framework for malaria detection using deep learning and feature fusion.
articles.authors: Shahin OR, Alshammari HH, Alabdali RN, Salaheldin AM, Saleh N
articles.details: This study proposes an automated multi-model framework for malaria detection combining transfer learning (ResNet-50, VGG16, DenseNet-201) for feature extraction, feature fusion with principal component analysis for dimensionality reduction, and a hybrid support vector machine plus long short-term memory classifier, with majority voting aggregating model outputs. It was validated on a publicly available dataset of 27,558 microscopic thin blood smear images. The majority-voting ensemble achieved 96.47% accuracy, 96.03% sensitivity, 96.90% specificity, 96.88% precision, and an F1-score of 96.45%. The authors conclude the framework advances diagnostic reliability and computational efficiency over existing methods.

articles.headline: Congenital malaria prevalence 6.5% in Nigerian newborns; maternal parasitaemia key
articles.title_original: Prevalance and risk factors of congenital malaria in Osogbo, Southwestern Nigeria.
articles.authors: Odediji SA, Olabanji SA, Olawuyi AO, Amoo KO, Amoo-Adeboye BO, Adeleke MA
