articles.details: This cross-sectional study assessed the prevalence and risk factors of congenital malaria (malaria parasites in newborn peripheral smear within the first seven days of life) at the State Specialist Hospital, Osogbo, Southwestern Nigeria. Peripheral and cord blood samples were collected from 77 newborns within the first 24 hours of delivery and examined by light microscopy and malaria rapid diagnostic tests, with risk factors assessed via a structured proforma. Overall congenital malaria prevalence was 6.5%, with all affected infants also showing cord blood parasitaemia. Maternal malarial parasitaemia was a strong predictor (p<0.05), whereas maternal retroviral status, intermittent prophylactic therapy, therapeutic antimalarial use, and bednet usage were not significantly associated (p>0.05). The authors call for additional multicentre studies.

articles.headline: Nanoscale wing surface analysis distinguishes two Anopheles mosquito species
articles.title_original: Unveiling the Nanomorphological Architecture of Malaria Vectors' Wings via Minkowski and Multifractal Formalisms.
articles.authors: Ramos GQ, Pinto LCC, Matos RS, Costa CAR, Lopes SCP, Santana RAG, Justiniano SCB, Miranda KR, da Fonseca Filho HD
articles.details: This study quantitatively analysed the wing surface morphology of the mosquitoes Anopheles darlingi and Anopheles aquasalis, integrating high-resolution atomic force microscopy with Minkowski functionals and multifractal formalism. It revealed interspecific and dorso-ventral asymmetries at micro- and nanoscales, with An. aquasalis showing higher elevation variance and denser nanomorphological heterogeneity. Multifractal spectra (box-counting, q = -10 to +10) showed larger width (Δα ≈ 1.073) and stronger left-symmetry (H ≈ -0.852) in An. aquasalis versus narrower, more symmetric spectra in An. darlingi (Δα ≈ 1.009; H ≈ -0.532), with generalized fractal dimensions (D0 ≈ 2.00; D2 range 1.951–1.982) and singularity asymmetry (Δf up to -1.732) further differentiating species and wing sides. The authors conclude An. aquasalis, especially its ventral surface, has higher nanomorphological complexity.

articles.headline: Corrigendum to WIMANET wildlife malaria network workshop report
articles.title_original: Corrigendum to "The Wildlife Malaria Research network (WIMANET): Meeting report on the 1st WIMANET workshop" [Int. J. Parasitol: Parasites and Wildlife 25 (2024) 100989].
articles.authors: Gutiérrez-López R, Ferraguti M, Bodawatta KH, Chagas CRF, Chakarov N, Duc M, Emmenegger T, García-Longoria L, Lopes RJ, Martínez-de la Puente J, Renner SC, Santiago-Alarcon D, Sehgal RNM, Stankovic D, Marzal A, Dunn JC
articles.details: No abstract is available for this article on PubMed — open the full text for details.

articles.headline: Baseline malaria survey near new Côte d'Ivoire dam shows 43% prevalence
articles.title_original: Epidemiological Profile and Risk Factors for Malaria in Rural Communities Before the Operationalization of the Singrobo-Ahouaty Dam, Southern Côte d'Ivoire.
articles.authors: Avenié TJD, Silué KD, Guindo-Coulibaly N, Koné N, Touré S, Valian KL, Kouadio KS, Bédia AMJ, Déabo BF, Diabagaté K, Nsanzabana C, Coulibaly JT
articles.details: A cross-sectional household survey using thick/thin smears and rapid diagnostic tests established baseline malaria data during construction of the Singrobo-Ahouaty dam. Overall prevalence was 43.1% (394/915), with a mean parasite density of 405.7 parasites/µL. School-age children (6-13 years) had the highest prevalence at 74.3% (p<0.0001), while children 0-5 years had the highest density (1218.0 parasites/µL, p<0.0001); the highest density overall was in Ahérémou 1 (5663.9 parasites/µL). Risk factors included informal work (ORa=1.5), raw-material sector work (ORa=1.4), market gardening/rice farming (ORa=0.9, p=0.043), and frequent mosquito bites (OR=0.4, p=0.017); the authors call for stronger vector control and improved bed net distribution.

articles.headline: Review urges investment in traditional medicine research against malaria in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Investing in traditional medicine: leveraging evidence and innovative research to strengthen the fight against malaria in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Onukansi FO, Anokwuru CC, Eneh SC, Odey GO, Onah DO, Sokunbi T, Innocent DC, Dozie UW, Umoke PC, Ojo TO, Ezejindu CN
articles.details: This literature-based analysis explores traditional African medicine, particularly in Nigeria, as a resource against malaria, drawing on the example of Artemisia annua in modern antimalarial development. The authors argue that scientific validation remains a key barrier to wider acceptance and advocate for increased funding, rigorous efficacy investigations, enhanced regulation of herbal medicine, and pharmacological safety testing. They note Nigeria carries the highest global malaria burden and growing rural reliance on herbal remedies, concluding that integrating traditional medicine into national health systems could yield new strategies against malaria and other infectious diseases. No quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: 22 malaria cases signal possible local transmission in northern Iran, 2023
articles.title_original: The resurgence of malaria in Northern Iran in 2023: a wake-up call.
articles.authors: Koohsar F, Faridnia R, Jorjani O, Hojjati MT, Yadagiri G, Noshak G, Pirzaman HT, Kalani H
articles.details: This report describes a sudden rise in malaria in Golestan Province, northern Iran, analyzing 445 individuals with suspected symptoms referred for diagnosis, using P. falciparum/P. vivax antigen detection and thin/thick smears. Among them, 4.94% (22/445) were infected; all 22 were native to the province with no prior history. P. vivax accounted for 77.27% (17/22), P. falciparum 18.18% (4/22), and co-infection 4.54% (1/22). Mean parasite counts were 16,029±5,060/µL for P. vivax and 105,460±102,146/µL for P. falciparum. All patients responded positively to treatment. Given local vectors and observed gametocytes, the authors warn of potential local transmission.

articles.headline: Study protocol: kidney disease after severe malaria in Ugandan children
articles.title_original: Malaria associated pathogenesis of chronic kidney disease (MAP-CKD): a prospective study of children hospitalized with severe malaria.
articles.authors: Batte A, Nakulima V, Namazzi R, Aujo JC, Starr MC, Schwaderer AL, Lintner-Rivera M, Ahumwire A, Ogwal D, Tenywa E, Oyang W, Ssenkusu JM, Bangirana P, Idro R, Kalyesubula R, Dreyer G, McCulloch M, Bassat Q, John CC, Goldstein SL, Conroy AL
articles.details: This is a prospective multi-site cohort study protocol examining acute kidney injury (AKI) and progression to chronic kidney disease (CKD) following severe malaria in children. It will enrol 750 children with severe malaria and 375 community controls aged 3 months to under 16 years at three sites in Uganda, followed for two years with kidney assessments at 1, 2, 4, 12, and 24 months. The study will characterize host pathways of maladaptive kidney repair (cell stress, angiogenesis, tubulointerstitial injury), identify CKD risk factors, and use cognitive/behavioral assessments and brain MRI to evaluate AKI effects on brain injury. No results are reported (trial registration ISRCTN10885288).

articles.headline: Malaria infection rate varies widely between individuals in Malian cohort
articles.title_original: Marked heterogeneity in malaria infection rate in a Malian longitudinal cohort.
articles.authors: LaVerriere E, Johnson ZM, Shieh M, Nziza N, Alter G, Buckee CO, Crompton PD, Traore B, Tran TM, Neafsey DE
articles.details: Researchers genotypically profiled over 13,000 blood samples from a six-year longitudinal cohort in Mali, generating P. falciparum amplicon sequencing data from 464 participants (aged 3 months-25 years) across the 2011 transmission season and tracking 120 participants over five subsequent seasons. Infection rate, measured as molecular force of infection (molFOI), varied extensively among individuals (0-55 in 2011), was independent of age, and was consistent within individuals across seasons despite near-universal bednet use. The HbS allele was associated with lower molFOI, and CSP C-term and RH5 antibody signatures correlated with low-molFOI participants, suggesting immune correlates of protection. Authors attribute the variation largely to heterogeneous mosquito exposure.

articles.headline: Drug-resistance markers and RDT-evading HRP2/3 deletions in central Ethiopia P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Kelch13 Variants and Histidine-Rich Protein 2/3 Deletions in Central Ethiopia, 2023.
articles.authors: Tufa TB, Oliveira R, Schallenberg E, Hendry JA, Igiraneza C, Jara-Reznakova B, Eggers Y, Sill H, Kenea D, Luedde T, Feldt T, Ndoli JM, Mockenhaupt FP, van Loon W
articles.details: This molecular surveillance study assessed pfhrp2/3, pfk13, and pfmdr1 variants in 220 symptomatic malaria patients at health facilities in central Ethiopia in 2023. P. falciparum was found in 86.4%, P. vivax in 12.3%, and P. ovale in 3.6% of isolates. Among P. falciparum infections, the validated artemisinin partial-resistance marker pfk13 R622I occurred in 1.1% (2/174) and candidate marker P441L in 1.7% (3/174). pfhrp2, pfhrp3, and dual deletions were seen in 24.0% (36/150), 78.8% (118/150), and 22.7% (34/150) respectively, and pfmdr1 indicated reduced lumefantrine sensitivity. The authors conclude many infections are missed by HRP-based RDTs while pfk13 mutations remain rare.

articles.headline: Review: environmental drivers of Anopheles vectors of zoonotic malaria in Southeast Asia
articles.title_original: Impact of environmental factors on the bionomics of Anopheles mosquito vectors of zoonotic malaria: A narrative review.
articles.authors: Masse RS, Vythilingam I, Fornace K, Othman H, Liu X, Jaafar AJ, Shahar MK, Abdul Rahman NA, Khairul Azman AH, Jeyaprakasam NK
articles.details: This narrative review examines how environmental factors shape the bionomics of Anopheles mosquito vectors transmitting non-human primate (zoonotic) malaria in Southeast Asia, driven primarily by Plasmodium knowlesi with emerging P. cynomolgi, P. inui, and P. fieldi infections in humans, notably in Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam. It highlights key determinants—temperature, relative humidity, elevation, precipitation, seasonality, and land use—that influence vector abundance, survival, and transmission intensity. The authors advocate a One Health approach integrating vector surveillance, sustainable land management, and targeted public health interventions to inform evidence-based malaria control.

articles.headline: P. falciparum antigens linked to reduced malaria risk in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Exploring the P. falciparum antigens associated with reduced risk of malaria in pregnancy.
articles.authors: Mwai L, Musundi S, Waweru H, Nagaoka H, Limbua PG, Tsuboi T, Takashima E, Gitaka J, Kanoi BN
articles.details: This study assessed human antibody responses to 698 P. falciparum recombinant antigens among Kenyan primigravida and multigravida women to identify targets for placental malaria, where infected red blood cells adhere to the placenta and poor birth outcomes are highest in primigravidae lacking protective antibodies. The number of antigens recognized increased with gravidity. Three antigens—PF3D7_1301800 (SURFIN 13.1), PF3D7_0424400 (SURFIN 4.2), and PF3D7_1252100 (rhoptry neck antigen 3 domain)—were statistically significant in multigravida women. All five VAR2CSA domains were immunoreactive (seroprevalence 42–62%) and correlated with the selected antigens, suggesting co-acquisition, but none were significantly associated with gravidity. The authors suggest these antigens could be prioritized for vaccine development.

articles.headline: IDO1-AhR pathway drives regulatory T cells in P. vivax malaria
articles.title_original: IDO1-AhR axis increases T regulatory cells in Plasmodium vivax malaria infection.
articles.authors: Dos Santos RO, Carvalho NO, de Morais TBDN, Sobrinho WBS, da Cruz MGS, de Faria ES, de Araújo FF, Magalhães VP, Lopes SCP, Malheiros A, da Mota AJ, Santiago HDC, Nogueira PA, Teixeira de Carvalho A, de Lacerda MVG, Lalwani P
articles.details: This study investigated tryptophan catabolism and regulatory T cells (Tregs) during Plasmodium vivax infection using PBMCs stimulated in vitro with P. vivax-infected erythrocyte lysate, plus plasma measures in acute patients comparing first episodes versus prior infections. Stimulation increased IDO-1 expression in CD14+ cells, raised the kynurenine/tryptophan (KYN/TRP) ratio, and induced pro-inflammatory cytokines. Inhibiting IDO reduced the KYN/TRP ratio and Treg frequencies; MyD88 inhibition lowered IDO-1 expression and KYN/TRP ratio; IDO and AhR inhibition reduced Tregs and CD4+ T cell proliferation. Acute patients showed elevated KYN/TRP ratios and Tregs that positively correlated, while individuals with prior episodes had lower Tregs, IFN-γ, and KYN/TRP. The authors conclude IDO-mediated tryptophan catabolism promotes a tolerogenic phenotype during P. vivax infection.

articles.headline: Four artemisinin combinations remain highly effective for uncomplicated malaria in Mozambique
articles.title_original: Efficacy of artemether-lumefantrine, artesunate-amodiaquine, dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine and artesunate-pyronaridine for the treatment of uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Mozambique, 2022.
articles.authors: Nhama A, Chidimatembue A, Nhamussua L, Bassat Q, da Silva C, Nhacolo A, Arnaldo P, Salvador C, Cassy A, Candrinho B, Dimene M, Carvalho E, Saifodine A, Wate F, Mucavele H, Torres-Mendoza Y, Horton B, Plucinski M, Cistero P, Mayor A, Aide P
articles.details: This in vivo therapeutic efficacy study (WHO 2009 protocol) enrolled 828 patients aged 6 months to 11 years with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria across five sentinel sites in Mozambique, testing artemether-lumefantrine (AL, 462), artesunate-amodiaquine (AS-AQ, 183), dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP, 91), and artesunate-pyronaridine (AS-PY, 92), with PCR correction via msp1/msp2/poly-α genotyping. Of 60 recurrent infections, 55 were new and 5 recrudescences. Day 28 PCR-corrected AL efficacy ranged 97.63–100% across sites; AS-AQ was 100% in Dondo and Moatize; day 42 DP efficacy was 100% (Massinga) and 97.75% (Mopeia). All drugs exceeded the WHO 90% cut-off, were well tolerated with adverse events in under 2%. The authors conclude AL and AS-AQ remain effective and DP and AS-PY are viable alternatives (NCT05343312).

articles.headline: Disability prevalence 11% in Tanzania linked to age, sex, wealth
articles.title_original: Socioeconomic and geographical disparities in disability distribution among Tanzanian population: insights from the 2022 demographic and health survey and malaria indicator survey.
articles.authors: Luoga P, Mahiti GR, Kapinga O, Herzon B, Mwaisengela SM, Materu PA, German CJ, Kasangala AA, Mtulo GE, Kinyenje ES, Hokororo JC, Ngonyani TL, Kacholi G, Kapologwe NA, Kumalija CJ, Anasel MG, Eliakimu ES
articles.details: Secondary analysis of the 2022 Tanzania Demographic and Health Survey and Malaria Indicator Survey, with a weighted sample of 58,425 individuals aged 15-95 years, used modified Poisson regression to assess socioeconomic and geographical disparities in disability. Overall disability prevalence was 11%. Higher adjusted prevalence ratios were seen for those aged ≥50 (APR 3.85; 95% CI 3.33-4.52), females (APR 2.67), secondary education (APR 2.58), separated/widowed (APR 2.91), wealthiest households (APR 2.74), and those with health insurance (APR 2.83); the Northern zone exceeded the Lake zone. Authors call for targeted interventions for vulnerable populations.

articles.headline: Chloroquine, pyrimethamine resistance markers rising in Ecuadorian P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Tracking Plasmodium falciparum antimalarial resistance markers during a malaria pre-elimination period in the Pacific coast of South America.
articles.authors: Ñacata I, Early AM, Boboy J, Neafsey DE, Sáenz FE
articles.details: Molecular surveillance of antimalarial resistance genes in 67 Plasmodium falciparum samples from northwestern Ecuador (2019-2021), genotyping SNPs in Pfcrt, Pfdhps, Pfdhfr, Pfmdr-1, Pfk13 and Pfaat1 by Sanger and whole-genome sequencing, compared with 2013-2015 haplotype frequencies. Mutant haplotypes CVMET (Pfcrt) and CICNI (Pfdhfr) increased to 100% of infections in Esmeraldas; the Pfmdr-1 haplotype NEDFSDFY was detected for the first time, while wild-type SAKAA (Pfdhps) and MYRIC (Pfk13) remained at 100%. The A16V mutation in Pfdhfr (proguanil resistance) was reported for the first time in Ecuador. Authors conclude chloroquine- and pyrimethamine-resistant parasites rose, but sulfadoxine- and artemisinin-sensitive parasites prevail, so first-line artemether-lumefantrine plus primaquine remains useful.

articles.headline: Review of artificial intelligence applications against malaria
articles.title_original: An insight into the role of artificial intelligence in combating malaria: recent developments.
articles.authors: Sachdeva H, Sharma M
articles.details: This literature review examines uses of artificial intelligence in the fight against malaria, covering diagnosis, surveillance, treatment, drug discovery, and vector control. It highlights AI methods including machine learning, deep learning, and in silico drug repurposing for personalised medicine, faster drug discovery, improved diagnostics, and forecasting transmission dynamics, particularly in the context of drug-resistant Plasmodium parasites. The authors argue that sustained investment in AI-driven research is needed to advance malaria eradication goals. No quantitative results are reported.

articles.headline: Evaluating Cambodia's 1-3-7 malaria surveillance and response strategy
articles.title_original: Evaluating malaria reactive surveillance and response strategies in northeast Cambodia: a mixed-methods study.
articles.authors: Win Han Oo, Siv S, Kaungmyat Khant, Htike W, Molyta MM, Lynn T, Aye Tun N, Agius PA, Fowkes FJI
articles.details: A mixed-methods study in Ratanakiri and Stung Treng provinces, northeast Cambodia, evaluated the 1-3-7 reactive surveillance and response (RASR) strategy, combining secondary analysis of routine malaria data with a cross-sectional survey, in-depth interviews, and focus group discussions among programme stakeholders, frontline health workers, and mobile/migrant populations. In 2020 and 2022, 72% and 59% of cases were notified and investigated within one day of diagnosis, and reactive case detection timeliness was 89% and 45%, respectively. Despite barriers including limited community participation, poor phone coverage, heavy workloads, and insufficient budget, the strategy was deemed acceptable; authors recommend adequate funding, capacity building, and better community engagement.

articles.headline: LDH-based malaria RDTs evaluated against HRP2 in Senegal
articles.title_original: Performance and usability evaluation of three LDH-based malaria rapid diagnostic tests in Kédougou, Senegal.
articles.authors: Sambe BS, Zobrist S, Sheahan W, Soni D, Diagne A, Sarr I, Diatta AS, Diaw SOM, Golden A, Slater H, Jang IK, Roa N, Pal S, Sarr FD, Faye J, Vigan-Womas I, Dieye Y, Cisse M, Domingo GJ, Niang M
articles.details: A cross-sectional diagnostic accuracy and usability study in Kédougou, Senegal, tested the SD BIOLINE HRP2 RDT and three LDH-based BIOCREDIT RDTs (Pf pLDH; Pf pLDH/HRPII; Pf/Pv pLDH/pLDH) in febrile patients, using PCR as reference and a quantitative multiplex antigen assay. Of 220 participants, 154 (70%) were P. falciparum-positive by PCR. The Pf (pLDH/HRPII) test had the highest sensitivity at 78% (95% CI 70.9-84.5%) and specificity 89%; all RDTs outperformed microscopy (53% sensitivity). PfLDH lines alone (61-64%) were less sensitive than the SD BIOLINE HRP2 test (71%), and the improved sensitivity was driven by the HRP2 line. Authors conclude that in populations with low hrp2/hrp3 deletion rates, PfLDH alone cannot compensate for HRP2 performance, and analytical limits of detection can predict RDT performance.

articles.headline: Delayed treatment and high parasitaemia raise severe malaria hospitalization risk
articles.title_original: Factors associated with hospitalizations due to severe malaria in the non-endemic Brazilian region: a case-control study in the extra-Amazon Region from 2011 to 2019.
articles.authors: da Silva AC, Duarte EC, Marchesini PB, Viana GMR, Ramalho WM, Garcia KKS
articles.details: A case-control study using Brazilian Notifiable Diseases (Sinan) and SIH/SUS hospitalization records analysed factors associated with severe malaria hospitalizations in the extra-Amazon Region, with cases being hospitalized patients and controls being non-hospitalized notifications, assessed via multiple logistic regression. Risk factors included Black or mixed-race ethnicity (OR=1.22, 95% CI 1.04-1.43), low education (elementary OR=2.21; high school OR=1.72), infection acquired in the Amazon Region (OR=1.50) or abroad (OR=1.72), high parasite counts (>100,000/mm3 OR=3.15, 95% CI 2.20-4.50), and delayed treatment (8+ days OR=2.08, 95% CI 1.62-2.66). Active case detection was protective (OR=0.65). Authors conclude delayed treatment and healthcare access inequalities drive severe malaria.

articles.headline: Systematic review of traditional anti-malarial plants in Mayurbhanj, India
articles.title_original: A review on the ethnomedicinal plants used for the traditional treatment of malaria in the Mayurbhanj district of Odisha, India.
articles.authors: Behera SK, Subudhi L, Mohapatra S, Panda AK
articles.details: A PRISMA-based systematic review compiled ethnomedicinal evidence on anti-malarial medicinal plants used by traditional healers in Mayurbhanj, Odisha, India, drawing on 20 included literature sources/book chapters. The study identified 40 plant species across 24 families, predominantly Acanthaceae and Apocyanaceae, used for anti-malarial treatment. Formulations were mainly decoctions, pastes, juices, and powders given orally, with leaves and roots the most-used parts and no reported side effects. The authors highlight the rich diversity of these remedies and call for further study to support diagnosis and preserve tribal knowledge.

articles.headline: Cameroonian P. falciparum shows high msp1/2 genetic diversity
articles.title_original: Genetic Diversity and Phylogenetic Relatedness of Cameroonian Plasmodium falciparum Isolates and Comparative Analysis with Global Populations.
articles.authors: Kojom Foko LP, Hawadak J, Singh V
articles.details: This study analysed the genetic diversity and phylogeny of Plasmodium falciparum isolates from three regions of Cameroon (Littoral, North, Far North) by genotyping merozoite surface protein markers msp1 block 2 and msp2 block 3 using PCR and Sanger sequencing, comparing against ~2,500 global sequences. The K1 and MAD20 families showed high structural diversity from 3-amino-acid repeats; IC/3D7 sequences were dominated by GSGA (31.3%) and GASGSA (25%) repeats. K1 and RO33 sequences were phylogenetically closer to China/Myanmar isolates, while MAD20, IC/3D7, and FC27 were closer to India, Panama, and Papua New Guinea. Authors conclude the isolates display a complex, highly structured population.

articles.headline: New P. berghei reporter line screens transmission-blocking malaria compounds
articles.title_original: Ookluc: A Plasmodium berghei Line for Identifying Transmission-blocking Compounds.
articles.authors: Novais JT, Calit J, Morosini LG, da Silva VA, Marques RF, Gaitán XA, Bargieri DY
articles.details: This methods/video article describes Ookluc, a transgenic Plasmodium berghei line developed for high-throughput screening of transmission-blocking compounds targeting the parasite's sexual stages. The line expresses nanoluciferase exclusively after zygote formation, with peak luminescence during ookinete development, providing a quantitative luminescence readout of gamete-to-zygote and zygote-to-ookinete differentiation. The authors present the assay as a tool to accelerate transmission-blocking drug discovery, addressing the limitation that existing high-throughput screening for gametocytocidal compounds relies primarily on P. falciparum cultures.

articles.headline: Mathematical model: treating screened malaria cases beats deportation
articles.title_original: A meta-population model of malaria with asymptomatic cases, transmission blocking drugs, migration and screening.
articles.authors: Tchoumi SY, Banasiak J, Ouifki R
articles.details: This theoretical study presents a two-patch malaria model in which individuals move freely between sites, with one site having better resources including screening facilities and transmission-blocking drugs (TBDs) that confer full but waning immunity and non-infectivity; entrants are screened and may be refused, admitted and treated, or enter illegally. The authors provide qualitative analysis of endemic equilibria and backward bifurcations and analyse low-migration dynamics using regular perturbation theory. Numerical simulations indicate malaria is better controlled by admitting and treating detected cases in the better-resourced site than by deporting infected individuals who may then re-enter illegally.

articles.headline: P. vivax dbp and ebp2 gene amplification varies across Amazon regions
articles.title_original: The pattern of gene amplification of members of the Plasmodium vivax erythrocyte binding-like proteins family across the Amazon rainforest.
articles.authors: Guedes KS, Fernandes GM, Sanchez BAM, Ntumngia FB, Adams JH, Kano FS, Fontes CJF, Sousa TN, Carvalho LH
articles.details: This study analysed copy number variation (CNV) at the dbp and ebp2 erythrocyte binding-like loci in 192 Plasmodium vivax isolates from three malaria-endemic Amazon sites (eastern, western, southwestern), using qPCR with β-tubulin as reference and genotyping host DARC polymorphisms. In the western region, 25% of isolates showed dbp amplification (up to 8 copies) versus 2–9% in southwestern and eastern regions (2–3 copies); ebp2 amplification was detected in 15% of western isolates and 1–4% elsewhere. All individuals were DARC positive, and no association was observed between gene CNVs and DARC genotypes.

articles.headline: Early parasitemia rise predicts cerebral malaria onset in infected mice
articles.title_original: Temporal Parasitemia Trends Predict Risk and Timing of Experimental Cerebral Malaria in Mice Infected by Plasmodium berghei ANKA.
articles.authors: Murin PJ, Daniel-Ribeiro CT, Carvalho LJM, Martins YC
articles.details: Using machine learning on a cohort of 153 C57BL/6, 164 CBA, and 53 Swiss Webster mice infected with Plasmodium berghei ANKA, researchers built a logistic regression model to estimate experimental cerebral malaria (ECM) risk from day 1–4 parasitemia and a Random Forest Regressor to predict symptom onset day. Overall 64.5% developed ECM, with onset between 5 and 11 days. A parasitemia increase of ≥0.05 between day 1 and day 3 predicted ECM development with 97% sensitivity, and the Random Forest model predicted onset day with a mean absolute error of 0.43 and R² of 0.64. The authors conclude parasitemia dynamics can identify high-risk mice and improve experimental consistency.

articles.headline: Scoping review protocol on African views of gene drive mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Knowledge, Perspectives, and Risks Perceptions on Gene Drive and Genetically Modified Mosquitoes for Malaria Control among African Stakeholders: A Scoping Review Protocol.
articles.authors: Sogodogo A, Diarra S, Keita S, Diallo I, Hartley S, Telly N, Dolo H, Sanogo D, Kané F, Tangara CO, Diakité M, Sangho O, Kayentao K, Fritz H, Winch PJ, Doumbia S
articles.details: This is a scoping review protocol aimed at understanding the knowledge, perspectives, and risk perceptions of African stakeholders regarding gene drive and genetically modified mosquitoes for malaria control. The planned search covers PubMed, Embase, Science Direct, Cochrane, and Google Scholar, with screening of scientific articles and grey literature by two independent reviewers on Rayyan, followed by thematic analysis of extracted data. No results are reported; the protocol is registered on the Open Science Framework.

articles.headline: Plasma proteomics identifies candidate biomarkers of malaria in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Plasma protein biomarkers of Plasmodium falciparum infection in pregnant women: a high-throughput proteomics study.
articles.authors: Kanoi BN, Waweru H, Kobia FM, Mukala J, Kirira P, Mogere D, Gallini R, Åberg M, Vatish M, Gitaka J, Kamali-Moghaddam M
articles.details: This high-throughput proteomics study used the proximity extension assay (PEA) to screen plasma from malaria-exposed pregnant women for differentially expressed proteins as candidate biomarkers of Plasmodium falciparum infection during pregnancy, motivated by the lack of tools to detect placental infection in pregnancy-associated malaria (PAM). In malaria-positive pregnancies, IgG Fc receptor IIb (FCGR2B) and heme oxygenase-1 (HO-1) were elevated, while neurturin (NRTN) and IL-20 were downregulated. The authors highlight IL-20 as a top candidate biomarker warranting validation in larger cohorts with placental histopathology.

articles.headline: Diverse antigens can still work as P. vivax exposure markers
articles.title_original: Navigating parasite antigen genetic diversity in the design of Plasmodium vivax serological exposure markers for malaria.
articles.authors: Bareng P, Wu KW, Takashima E, Argyropoulos D, Smith L, Naung M, Mazhari R, Schoffer K, Kiernan-Walker N, Abraham A, Lamont M, Mehra S, Lim P, Sattabongkot J, Monteiro W, Lacerda M, Healer J, Chitnis CE, Tham WH, Tsuboi T, Mueller I, Barry AE, Longley RJ
articles.details: This study assessed how Plasmodium vivax genetic diversity affects antibody-based serological exposure markers (a proxy for hypnozoite carriage), analysing global genetic data and producing new antigen haplotypes reflecting global diversity versus the Sal-1 reference, then testing antibody responses against qPCR-detectable infection in cohorts from Brazil and Thailand to identify infection within the prior 9 months. Extensive diversity was found in DBPII and MSP5, with the percentage of haplotypes sharing similar sequence identity to Sal-1 ranging from 0.4% (MSP5) to 99% (S16); RBP2a and DBPII showed regional immunogenicity differences. For 5 of 8 proteins, diversity had little impact on accuracy, and where it did (e.g. RBP2a) it could be overcome by combining multiple antigens. The authors conclude even highly diverse antigens can be effective markers.

articles.headline: R21/Matrix-M vaccine elicits broad immune responses in malaria-exposed adults
articles.title_original: R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccine drives diverse immune responses in pre-exposed adults: insights from a phase IIb controlled human malaria infection trial.
articles.authors: Kibwana E, Bundi C, Kimani D, Nyamako L, Keter K, Mutiso A, Ogwang R, Bellamy D, Rapi K, Bajer A, Provstgaard-Morys S, Stockdale L, Munoz O, Datoo MS, Lawrie A, Ramos-Lopez F, Roberts R, Hamaluba M, Hill AVS, Bejon P, Ewer KJ, Kapulu M
articles.details: In a Phase IIb controlled human malaria infection trial, plasma and PBMCs from semi-immune healthy adult volunteers living in coastal Kenya were analysed after R21/Matrix-M vaccination (n=19) and challenge with Plasmodium falciparum (PfSPZ NF54) sporozoites (n=17). Vaccination raised IgA, IgM, and IgG titres and IgG1 and IgG3 subclasses (but not IgG2 or IgG4) against full-length R21, NANP, and the C-terminus, with complement-fixing capability. IgG+ and IgA+ antigen-specific memory B cells were boosted (short-lived for IgA), and CXCR5+/PD1+ circulating Tfh cells (predominantly Th2/Th17) increased. The authors highlight diverse responses potentially contributing to protection in adults with prior exposure.

articles.headline: Vivax malaria with severe thrombocytopenia reveals underlying chronic lymphocytic leukemia
articles.title_original: Underlying Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia in a Case of Vivax Malaria With Thrombocytopenia: A Case Report.
articles.authors: Saha A, Mukhopadhyay M, Talapatra A, Bandhopadhay T, Sardar S
articles.details: This case report describes a 64-year-old male presenting with high-grade fever, extensive petechial rashes, subconjunctival hemorrhage, and oral mucosal bleeds. Laboratory tests showed severe thrombocytopenia (platelet count 15,000 per cubic millimeter), leukocytosis (total leukocyte count 29,100 per microliter), and coagulopathy (INR 4.2), with negative cultures ruling out infection. A blood smear was positive for Plasmodium vivax, and flow cytometry diagnosed chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL). The authors conclude that leukocytosis with thrombocytopenia in malaria, absent sepsis, should raise suspicion of underlying CLL.

articles.headline: First detection of rodent malaria parasite in invasive black rats in Gabon
articles.title_original: Rodent malaria parasites detected in the invasive Rattus rattus in Gabon.
articles.authors: Mbou-Boutambe C, Boundenga L, Degrugillier F, Gauthier P, Arnathau C, Rivero A, Granjon L, Rougeron V, Prugnolle F
articles.details: This study reports the first molecular detection of Plasmodium yoelii in the invasive black rat (Rattus rattus) in Gabon, based on systematic molecular screening of 527 rodents captured in rural villages between 2021 and 2022. Two R. rattus individuals tested positive for P. yoelii, and phylogenetic analysis confirmed identity with strains previously isolated from native regional rodents. Despite a low infection prevalence (0.38%), the authors conclude this challenges the view that rodent malaria parasites are restricted to native hosts and raises questions about invasive rodents integrating into local Plasmodium transmission cycles.

articles.headline: Rapid multiplex LAMP assay diagnoses malaria using Chelex-100 extraction
articles.title_original: Rapid and Multiplex Diagnosis of Malaria Using Chelex-100 Extraction and LAMP-MS Assay.
articles.authors: Lim MS, Choe YL, Jang WS
articles.details: This protocol describes a rapid, low-cost malaria diagnostic platform combining simplified Chelex-100 resin-based nucleic acid extraction with a multiplex loop-mediated isothermal amplification microscanner (LAMP-MS) assay. The platform enables simultaneous detection of Plasmodium falciparum, Plasmodium vivax, pan-malaria, and an internal control within 40 minutes from DNA extraction to result interpretation, using visual endpoint detection via a microscope or microscanner. The authors report sensitivity and specificity comparable to traditional PCR-based diagnostics, positioning it as a practical, field-deployable, and scalable solution for resource-constrained settings.

articles.headline: Alveolin-alveolin interactions drive cytoskeletal protein localization in malaria parasite
articles.title_original: Malaria Cytoskeletal Proteins Require Alveolin-Alveolin Interactions for Differential Localization: Recruitment and Organization of Alveolin Proteins.
articles.authors: Cepeda Diaz AK, Back PS, Sreenivasamurthy SK, Dvorin JD
articles.details: This study characterizes four alveolins—intermediate filament-like cytoskeletal proteins—of Plasmodium falciparum to understand how alveolin domains mediate protein-protein interactions and recruitment to cytoskeletal substructures. The authors found variable, rather than uniform, dependence on alveolin domains: PfIMC1e requires PfIMC1f for sequential recruitment to the basal complex, whereas PfIMC1c and PfIMC1g localize to the inner membrane complex without interacting with each other. Alveolin domains were not interchangeable, containing unique signatures for specialized localization, and a region outside PfIMC1e's alveolin domain was important for basal complex recruitment. The work provides direct evidence that alveolin domains mediate both alveolin-alveolin interactions and compartment-specific localization.

articles.headline: Malaria hemozoin dampens dendritic cell interferon response to bacteria
articles.title_original: Malaria-derived hemozoin skews dendritic cell responses to bacterial infections by reducing interferon gene-transcription by SWI/SNF-NuRD.
articles.authors: Lasaviciute G, Tariq K, Sugathan A, Quin J, Polenkowski M, Bujila I, Skorokhod O, Troye-Blomberg M, Sverremark-Ekström E, Östlund Farrants AK
articles.details: This laboratory study examined how the malaria pigment hemozoin (HZ) affects monocyte-derived dendritic cells (moDC) co-exposed to bacterial lipopolysaccharide (LPS). HZ altered the early transcriptional response of a subset of IFNγ-controlled genes and reduced expression of HLA-DR and PD-L1, while having no effect on inflammatory genes strongly induced by LPS. The reduced HLA-DR/PD-L1 expression was linked to the NuRD chromatin remodeling complex, decreased binding of the NF-κB factor RELA, and replacement of the SWI/SNF variant PBAF by NuRD at specific promoters without changes in chromatin accessibility. The authors conclude HZ's immune-modulatory effect may alter responses to bacterial co-infections.

articles.headline: Malaria antibody affinity develops separately across antigens in infants
articles.title_original: Development of high affinity antibodies to Plasmodium falciparum merozoite and sporozoite antigens during infancy and adulthood.
articles.authors: Lugaajju A, Idro R, Kiwuwa S, Beeson JG, Drew DR, Mortazavi SE, Linse S, Persson KEM
articles.details: A longitudinal study in Uganda followed newborn infants and their mothers for nine months, measuring antibody affinity (dissociation rate constant, kd) against the merozoite antigens AMA1 and MSP2 and the sporozoite antigen CSP. Affinity against AMA1 and MSP2 decreased from birth to six months in infants then increased to nine months, without reaching maternal levels, whereas CSP affinity did not change; mothers showed no significant affinity changes for any antigen. Specific antibody affinities showed almost no correlation with total antibody levels, and significant correlations were observed between affinities and some atypical memory B cells. The authors conclude that high-affinity antibody development evolves separately across antigens, informing vaccine development.

articles.headline: ITN use linked to 34% lower child malaria risk in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Effects of vector control interventions on spatio-temporal changes of falciparum malaria risk in children aged 2-10 in sub-Saharan African regions during 2011-2020.
articles.authors: Chol CJ, Belay DB, Fenta HM, Chen DG
articles.details: This spatiotemporal analysis used Malaria Atlas Project data on P. falciparum prevalence and vector control across 634 administrative areas in 45 sub-Saharan African countries from 2011 to 2020, applying Bayesian Markov-chain Monte Carlo regression models. Average annual prevalence among children aged 2–10 fell from 21.32% in 2011 to 16.75% in 2016, with a slight resurgence in 2017. Each unit increase in annual insecticide-treated net (ITN) use correlated with a 34.07% reduction in infection risk. The auto-regressive process showed highly significant temporal correlation and modest spatial correlation, with highest risk in West-central, Central, and certain Eastern regions, which the authors say should be prioritized for intervention.

articles.headline: How malaria was translated and reconceptualized in Qing and Republican China
articles.title_original: Malaria in China: a discourse-historical perspective.
articles.authors: Miao P
articles.details: This historical-linguistic article analyzes English-Chinese dictionaries (1830s–1900s) and vernacular newspapers and periodicals to trace how "ague" and "malaria" entered Chinese as "nueji" and "zhangqi." It identifies three phases of conceptual transfer: early missionary dictionaries (1822–1860s) prioritized symptom-based translations; the 1870s–1920s saw terminological competition between nueji and zhangqi reflecting clashes between traditional Chinese etiology and western theories; and by the 1930s–1940s nueji became dominant through institutional standardization while western parasitological frameworks were selectively assimilated. The authors argue disease introduction transcended lexical substitution, acting as a contested battlefield among competing medical discourses during China's medical modernization.

articles.headline: Rare P. vivax malaria causing pancytopenia from hemophagocytic syndrome in pregnancy
articles.title_original: A Rare Presentation of Plasmodium vivax Malaria: Pancytopenia due to Hemophagocytic Syndrome in a Pregnant Woman.
articles.authors: Lema GD, Dagnaw AB, Gebeyaw ED
articles.details: This case report describes a 22-year-old gravida one woman, nine weeks pregnant, from Debre Berhan, Ethiopia, who presented with one week of high-grade fever, headache, nausea, vomiting, joint pain, and fatigue after recent travel to a malaria-endemic region. She was hypotensive and febrile with pale conjunctiva, hepatosplenomegaly, pancytopenia, and elevated triglycerides and serum ferritin; blood smear showed trophozoite and gametocyte stages of P. vivax, and she was diagnosed with hemophagocytic syndrome (HPS) by clinical criteria. The patient fully recovered after antimalarial and supportive treatment. The authors highlight this as a rare but serious presentation, emphasizing timely diagnosis for maternal and fetal outcomes.

articles.headline: Recurrent malaria in Riau, Indonesia mostly P. vivax after 28 days
articles.title_original: Postmalaria elimination: Overview of recurrent malaria cases in Riau Province, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Lesmana SD, Afandi D, Rosdiana D, Murtono H, Azzuhra MN, Panjaitan GR
articles.details: This descriptive retrospective study reviewed malaria surveillance data from Riau Province, covering 2019-2022, drawn from the Provincial Health Office. Of 4622 total malaria cases, 232 were recurrent, with 100% recurring after 28 days; the study could not distinguish relapse from reinfection. Among recurrent cases, 220 (94.83%) were Plasmodium vivax, 11 (4.74%) Plasmodium falciparum, and one was a mixed infection. Rapid diagnostic tests were used in 69% of diagnoses, most cases were in males aged 19-59, and 73.45% did not receive a complete after-treatment evaluation; the authors conclude that relapse is a significant challenge to elimination efforts.

articles.headline: Malaria linked to raised inflammation, anemia, malnutrition in Iraqi children
articles.title_original: Analysis of the relationship between malaria and inflammatory biomarker levels in children in rural Iraq: Impact of nutritional deficiencies, anemia, and hematological disorders.
articles.authors: Al-Sultany AK, Al-Mamoori ZZ, Kareem MN, Mohsein OA
articles.details: This case-control study compared 150 children with malaria and 50 controls aged 5–8 years in rural Iraq, measuring CRP, IL-6, and TNF-α by ELISA, hemoglobin by complete blood count, and iron, zinc, vitamin D, and ferritin via Cobas e411. Malaria patients had higher CRP (18.4 vs 5.2 mg/L), IL-6 (10.2 vs 3.1 pg/mL), and TNF-α (12.5 vs 4.0 pg/mL) (all P<0.001), with lower hemoglobin (9.8 vs 12.5 g/dL) and iron (45.3 vs 90.2 μg/dL) but higher ferritin (30.0 vs 100.0 ng/mL). Malnutrition (65% vs 15%), anemia (70% vs 20%), and blood disorders (40% vs 10%) were more prevalent (all P<0.001). The authors call for integrated nutritional and anti-inflammatory care.

articles.headline: Plasmodium found in cattle but not humans, Indonesia zoonotic study
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium spp. in humans and cattle: Implications for zoonotic malaria transmission in Indonesia.
articles.authors: Hasyim H, Jassey B, Yudhastuti R, Misnaniarti M, Liberty IA, Sunarsih E, Priyanto L, Dalilah D, Yusri Y, Taufiq FH, Maharani FE, Hakim L, Herlinda S
articles.details: This cross-sectional study in a malaria-endemic region of Indonesia sampled 41 human participants and 43 cattle, using endpoint PCR targeting Plasmodium genus-specific DNA. All human samples were negative (0%, 95% CI 0.0%–8.5%), while one cattle sample was positive (2.33%, 95% CI 0.06%–12.0%), confirmed by a distinct 240 base pair band on agarose gel electrophoresis. The authors conclude that, although no zoonotic transmission to humans was observed, detection of Plasmodium DNA in cattle supports integrating animal health surveillance into malaria elimination under a One Health framework, recommending species-specific diagnostics and broader geographic studies.

articles.headline: Two imported P. falciparum cases recrudesce after artemether-lumefantrine
articles.title_original: [Successful Treatment of Two Cases of Plasmodium falciparum Malaria that Developed Recrudescence after Artemether-Lumefantrine Therapy].
articles.authors: Arslan M, Berberoğlu U
articles.details: This case report describes two middle-aged men with Plasmodium falciparum malaria imported from Gabon and Uganda, who developed symptoms in Türkiye and were treated with six doses of artemether-lumefantrine (AL). After discharge with improvement, both re-presented with high fever and chills showing late clinical failure. One patient was cleared with six doses of AL, and the other only after non-ACT treatment with quinine and doxycycline, with no subsequent recrudescence. The authors caution that even after apparent parasite clearance on blood smear following three days of appropriate AL treatment, treatment failure can occur, including in African P. falciparum malaria.

articles.headline: Review of cerebral malaria pathogenesis, treatment, and survivor care gaps
articles.title_original: Cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Postels DG, Katangwe-Chirwa T
articles.details: This review covers cerebral malaria, the most lethal form of Plasmodium falciparum infection, defined clinically as otherwise unexplained coma in someone with malaria parasitemia. The authors note that one in six children with cerebral malaria dies and many survivors have neurologic, cognitive, or behavioral sequelae, with acute seizures common and increasing seizure numbers associated with worse outcomes. They report that optimal treatment pathways are standardized but an effective adjunctive therapy to reduce mortality or morbidity remains elusive, and that imaging, EEG, and transcranial doppler findings correlate with outcomes. The review summarizes epidemiology, clinical features, diagnosis, care pathways, sequelae, and care gaps.

articles.headline: Green-synthesized silver nanoparticles kill malaria, filariasis, dengue mosquito larvae
articles.title_original: Eco-Friendly Synthesis of Silver Nanoparticles and Its Larvicidal Property Against Malaria, Filariasis, and Dengue Vectors.
articles.authors: Raguvaran K, Kalpana M, Devapriya P, Kalaivani S, Angelina M, Krismastuti FSH, Rasool A, Maheswaran R
articles.details: This study green-synthesized silver nanoparticles (Ag-NPs) using Aerva lanata flower extract and tested larvicidal and pupicidal efficacy against the mosquito vectors Culex quinquefasciatus, Anopheles stephensi, and Aedes aegypti. The Ag-NPs, characterized by UV-Vis, FT-IR, XRD, SEM-EDX, TEM, zeta potential, and DLS, showed LC50 values of 6.501, 20.875, and 22.649 ppm respectively, with histopathological damage and modulation of antioxidant (SOD, GPx) and detoxification (GST, AChE) enzymes indicating oxidative stress as the mode of action. Biosafety tests showed minimal toxicity to Artemia salina. The authors conclude the plant-based nanobiopesticide has strong potential for sustainable mosquito control amid insecticide resistance.

articles.headline: In silico multi-epitope blood-stage malaria vaccine designed against P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Development of a multi-epitope vaccine candidate targeting blood-stage of malaria through immunoinformatics approach.
articles.authors: Mandal S, Khushi, Chanu WP, Srivastava R, Patgiri SJ, Natarajaseenivasan K
articles.details: This in-silico immunoinformatics study designed a multi-epitope vaccine candidate targeting four conserved blood-stage Plasmodium falciparum 3D7 proteins: PfPHB1, PfPHB2, PfHSP70, and PfGARP. B cell, helper T lymphocyte, and cytotoxic T lymphocyte epitopes were identified for antigenicity, non-toxicity, and non-allergenicity, then assembled into a construct using the FliC protein of Salmonella typhimurium as adjuvant; structures were predicted with PSIPRED and AlphaFold2. Molecular docking showed strong interactions with TLR5, codon optimization and in silico cloning in E. coli K12 indicated efficient expression, and C-ImmSim immune simulation predicted a robust immune response. The authors conclude this is a step toward a malaria vaccine requiring experimental validation.

articles.headline: Genomics shows P. simium drives zoonotic malaria in Southeast Brazil
articles.title_original: Plasmodium simium: birth and evolution of a zoonotic malaria parasite species.
articles.authors: de Albuquerque NRM, Ladeia WA, Scalsky RJ, Dwivedi A, Stabler TC, Rodrigues PT, de Oliveira TC, Silva JC, Ferreira MU
articles.details: This population genomics study analyzed whole-genome sequence data from 31 Plasmodium simium isolates alongside a global dataset of 1,086 P. vivax isolates to investigate the origin and host adaptation of P. simium, a monkey parasite causing human outbreaks. Analyses revealed P. simium is a discrete lineage most genetically similar to Latin American P. vivax (especially the Amazon Basin of Brazil) and to ancient European P. vivax, with half the nucleotide diversity of Latin American P. vivax, consistent with a recent Brazilian origin. Sympatric monkey and human isolates were as closely related as meiotic half-siblings, revealing ongoing zoonotic transmission. The authors conclude P. simium causes most, and possibly all, infections previously attributed to P. vivax along the Serra do Mar Mountain Range.

articles.headline: Pf8 open dataset releases P. falciparum genomes from 33,325 samples
articles.title_original: Pf8: an open dataset of Plasmodium falciparum genome variation in 33,325 worldwide samples.
articles.authors: Abdel Hamid MM, Abdelraheem MH, Acheampong DO, Adam I, Aide P, Ajibaye O, Ali M, Almagro-Garcia J, Amambua-Ngwa A, Amenga-Etego L, Aniebo I, Aninagyei E, Ansah F, Apinjoh TO, Ariani CV, Auburn S, Awandare GA, Balmer A, Bejon P, Boene S, Bwire G, Candrinho B, Chidimatembue A, Chindavongsa K, Comiche K, Conway D, Dara A, Diakite M, Djimde A, Dondorp A, Doumbia S, Drury E, Fanello CA, Ferdig M, Figueroa K, Gamboa D, Golassa L, Gonçalves S, Guindo MDA, Hamaluba M, Hanboonkunupakarn B, Howe K, Hussien M, Imwong M, Ishengoma D, Jeans J, Kabaghe A, Kamuhabwa A, Kindermans JM, Konate DS, Kwiatkowski DP, Lee C, Lee SK, Lee SJ, Ley B, Llanos-Cuentas A, Marfurt J, Matambisso G, Maude RR, Maude RJ, Mayor A, Mayxay M, Maïga-Ascofaré O, McCann RS, Miles A, Miotto O, Mohamed AO, Morang'a CM, Murie K, Ngasala BE, Nguyen TN, Nolasco O, Nosten F, Noviyanti R, O'Connor Í, Oboh M, Ochola-Oyier LI, Olufunke Falade C, Olukosi A, Olumide A, Olusola FI, Onyamboko MA, Oriero EC, Oyibo WA, Pannebaker D, Pearson RD, Phiri K, van der Pluijm RW, Price RN, Quang HH, Rajkumar Devaraju V, Randrianarivelojosia M, Ranford-Cartwright L, Rayner JC, Rovira-Vallbona E, Rowlands K, Ruano-Rubio V, Sanchez JF, Saúte F, Shettima S, da Silva C, Simpson VJ, Suddaby S, Takken W, Thu AM, Toure M, Unlu E, Valdivia HO, van Vugt M, Waithira N, Wellems T, Wendler J, White N, Wuendrich Ogidan R
articles.details: This data resource describes Pf8, the latest MalariaGEN release of curated Plasmodium falciparum genome variation data from over 33,000 samples across 99 partner studies and 122 locations spanning more than 50 years, with open access to raw sequencing data and genotypes at over 12 million genomic positions. For the first time it includes copy-number variation calls in the drug-resistance genes gch1 and crt, alongside calls for mdr1, plasmepsin2/3, and hrp2/hrp3 deletions linked to rapid diagnostic test failures. It also offers derived datasets, interactive web applications, an updated Python package, and analysis notebooks, with example analyses showing the decline of chloroquine resistance-associated mutations in Africa and copy-number variation across 10 distinct sub-populations. The authors describe it as the largest open dataset of genome variation in any eukaryotic species.

articles.headline: Historical tribute to malaria pioneer Giovanni Battista Grassi
articles.title_original: Giovanni Battista Grassi (1854-1925): a forgotten Italian scholar and his fundamental studies on malaria.
articles.authors: Martini M, Orsini D
articles.details: This historical article commemorates Giovanni Battista Grassi (1854-1925), an Italian doctor, zoologist, botanist, and entomologist and a founder of the Italian school of malariology, on the centenary of his death. It retraces the evolution of malaria research, noting Louis Pasteur's germ theory, Alphonse Laveran's 1880 discovery and his 1889 demonstration that malaria is caused by a Plasmodium protozoan attacking red blood cells, and the recognition of four main human malaria species. The authors highlight Grassi's pivotal study begun in 1898, emphasizing his identification of the vector of human malaria as a turning point in understanding and treating the disease.

articles.headline: Human Plasmodium cynomolgi co-infection misdiagnosed as P. vivax
articles.title_original: Molecular and Microscopic Challenges in Detecting Plasmodium cynomolgi Co-Infections with Plasmodium vivax: A Case Report.
articles.authors: Yaacob MA, Radin Hisam RS, Parina Ismail N, Md Yusuf NA, Rubio Muñoz JM, Hashim S, Zhueng TJ
articles.details: This case report describes a 32-year-old male with no prior malaria history, initially diagnosed by microscopy and qPCR as Plasmodium vivax. Cross-examination revealed a mixed-species infection, and real-time PCR with sequencing confirmed co-infection of P. vivax and the zoonotic non-human primate parasite P. cynomolgi. The authors highlight that P. cynomolgi shares high genetic homology and morphological similarity with P. vivax, causing diagnostic cross-reactivity, and stress the urgent need for improved molecular diagnostics as P. cynomolgi emerges as a zoonotic threat in Southeast Asia.

articles.headline: Brucea javanica extracts inhibit P. falciparum, add to antimalarials
articles.title_original: Growth Inhibition and Additive Effect to Antimalarial Drugs of Brucea javanica Extracts on Asexual Blood-Stage Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Kangwanrangsan N, Niramolyanun G, Praikongkatham C, Chantree P, Martviset P, Pankao V
articles.details: This in vitro study tested Brucea javanica root and fruit extracts against asexual blood-stage Plasmodium falciparum, alone and co-incubated with artesunate and chloroquine, assessing parasite development by microscopy. Effective doses caused no red blood cell hemolysis. The root extract and fruit extract inhibited growth with IC50 values of 0.41 ± 1.14 µg/mL and 0.26 ± 1.15 µg/mL, respectively, disrupting development at the ring stage and causing nuclear clumping and pyknotic death. Both extracts showed additive effects with artesunate and chloroquine, significantly reducing IC90 levels, supporting further study as novel antimalarial formulations.

articles.headline: Meta-analysis: chlorfenapyr nets most effective against malaria in Africa
articles.title_original: Effectiveness and Efficacy of Long-Lasting Insecticidal Nets for Malaria Control in Africa: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials.
articles.authors: Demissie DB, Fetensa G, Desta T, Tiyare FT
articles.journal: Int J Environ Res Public Health
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 11 randomized controlled trials, involving 21,916 households, assessed long-lasting insecticidal nets for malaria control in Africa amid pyrethroid resistance. The chlorfenapyr group had a 10% reduction in malaria infection risk, with pooled prevalence of 25.96 per 100 children versus 32.38 in the piperonyl butoxide group and 41.60 in the pyrethroid-only control. Entomologically, vector density and inoculation rates fell 23% with chlorfenapyr, 12% with piperonyl butoxide, and 7% with pyrethroid-only. Authors conclude chlorfenapyr LLINs are highly effective at reducing malaria infection, case incidence, and anemia in children.

articles.headline: Immunoinformatics designs multiepitope P. falciparum malaria vaccine candidate
articles.title_original: Search continues: Exploring immunoinformatics platforms for designing an effective multiepitope malaria vaccine candidate.
articles.authors: Osuji C, Etuk-Udo G
articles.details: This computational study used a structural bioinformatics and reverse vaccinology pipeline to design a multiepitope subunit vaccine against Plasmodium falciparum. Epitopes were mined from variant surface antigens expressed across the parasite life cycle. From an initial pool of 133 epitopes, 30 vaccine-fit epitopes were selected, producing a 570-amino-acid construct containing 12 linear B-cell, 11 cytotoxic T-lymphocyte, and 7 helper T-lymphocyte epitopes conjugated with adjuvants and linkers. The construct showed favorable structural, antigenic, and physicochemical properties, 95.04% global population coverage, robust molecular binding, and simulated immune responses, illustrating immunoinformatics' potential to accelerate vaccine development.

articles.headline: Most P. vivax Pvfam 'a' binding proteins target Basigin
articles.title_original: Majority of the erythrocyte binding proteins of the Pvfam "a" family of Plasmodium vivax interact with Basigin to assist parasite entry into the host cell.
articles.authors: Tripathi M, Santoshi M, Sharma YD, Rathore S
articles.details: This study investigated red cell invasion by Plasmodium vivax using solid-phase binding assays and surface plasmon resonance. The majority—7 of 10—of the Pvfam 'a' family erythrocyte binding proteins (PvTRAg, PvTRAg33.5, PvTRAg35.2, PvTRAg34, PvTRAg36, PvTRAg38, and PvTRAg69.4) were shown to interact with the erythrocyte receptor Basigin. Each protein interfered with parasite growth in a heterologous culture system, and combinations produced higher growth inhibition, suggesting multiple parasite ligands interacting with the same receptor are important during invasion. The findings aid understanding of P. vivax biology and development of therapeutics for vivax malaria.

articles.headline: CD8+ T cells drive tubular kidney damage in malaria
articles.title_original: CD8(+) T cells promote tubule-interstitial damage in malaria-induced acute kidney injury.
articles.authors: Teixeira DE, Alves SADS, Pinheiro AS, Silva LS, Silva-Aguiar RP, Peruchetti DB, Pádua TA, Souza MC, Caruso-Neves C, Pinheiro AAS
articles.details: In a mouse model of malaria-induced acute kidney injury (MAKI), researchers used adoptive T-cell transfer, antibody-mediated CD8+ T-cell depletion, and the immunomodulator FTY720 to probe pathogenesis. Transfer of T cells from infected donors into naive mice reproduced tubule-interstitial damage (without affecting glomerular function) and increased CD8+ T-cell accumulation in kidneys, while selective CD8+ depletion protected against tubular injury but not glomerular alterations. FTY720 prevented proteinuria, lowered the urine-to-creatinine ratio, and reduced urinary γ-glutamyl transferase (a tubular injury marker) without changing plasma urea or creatinine. The authors conclude CD8+ T cells specifically mediate tubule-interstitial injury in MAKI.

articles.headline: Machine-learning tool counts and measures malaria oocysts automatically
articles.title_original: OocystMeter, a machine-learning algorithm to count and measure Plasmodium oocysts, reveals clustering patterns in the Anopheles midgut.
articles.authors: Peng D, Marrogi E, Nelson E, Liu Q, Rinvee TA, de Vries LE, Thornburg K, Singh N, Shaw WR, Catteruccia F
articles.details: The authors developed OocystMeter, a Mask R-CNN-based software to automatically segment Plasmodium oocysts from mercurochrome-stained mosquito midgut images, training it on a curated dataset of 11,178 P. falciparum oocysts in Anopheles gambiae midguts annotated by expert parasitologists. The tool achieved accuracy comparable to human parasitologists, with Spearman's correlation of 0.998 for oocyst counts and 0.978 for size measurements. Combined with spatial analysis, it revealed a non-random, clustered distribution of oocysts independent of midgut anatomical regions or geometric axes, particularly in infections with fewer than 75 oocysts/midgut. The tool is freely available online.

articles.headline: mRNA blood-stage vaccine fully protects mice against lethal malaria
articles.title_original: An mRNA Vaccine Expressing Blood-Stage Malaria Antigens Induces Complete Protection Against Lethal Plasmodium yoelii.
articles.authors: Ott AC, Loll PJ, Burns JM Jr
articles.details: In a proof-of-concept study using the P. yoelii mouse model, researchers tested two mRNA vaccines encoding PyMSP119 fused to PyMSP8 (PyMSP1/8), one designed for secretion (PyMSP1/8-sec) and one for membrane-bound antigen (PyMSP1/8-mem). After three immunizations and lethal P. yoelii 17XL challenge, the secreted mRNA vaccine was more immunogenic than the recombinant rPyMSP1/8 formulation, and all PyMSP1/8-sec-immunized animals survived, with 50% showing no detectable parasitized RBCs (<0.01%) and significantly lower mean peak parasitemia. Both mRNA vaccines were protective, but the membrane-bound version provided significantly higher protection, highlighting that antigen localization matters for mRNA vaccine design.

articles.headline: Mathematical model evaluates vaccination plus control measures for malaria
articles.title_original: Vaccination and combined optimal control measures for malaria prevention and spread mitigation.
articles.authors: Eme KA, Kamrujjaman M, Alam M, Ali MA
articles.details: This study presents a deterministic mathematical model of malaria transmission to assess the effectiveness of prevention strategies, establishing positivity and boundedness of solutions and local stability of equilibria. A sensitivity analysis identified key parameters influencing the basic reproduction number (R0) for evaluating interventions. The findings indicate that although current vaccines are not 100% effective, vaccination could significantly contribute to malaria control alongside measures such as mosquito nets and insecticide spraying. The authors conclude that a comprehensive approach combining multiple strategies is needed to effectively reduce transmission in endemic regions.

articles.headline: Modeling forecasts ivermectin mass drug administration impact on African malaria
articles.title_original: Wonder drugs and where to use them: a forecast of ivermectin's impact on malaria in Africa.
articles.authors: Greenhalgh S, Roman A, Provencher A
articles.details: Building on a Burkina Faso study showing ivermectin inhibits malaria transmission by killing parasites and mosquitoes, the authors built a mathematical model using transmission data from 41 sub-Saharan African countries to evaluate mass drug administration (MDA) of ivermectin over five years. Annual MDA reaching 12.5%, 25%, or 50% of the population averted 248.7, 261.4, and 288.7 malaria incidences per thousand people and saved 5.4, 5.7, and 6.3 disability-adjusted life years, respectively. MDA was cost-effective in 41, 18, and 6 countries (and very cost-effective in 22, 6, and 3) for the three scenarios. The authors conclude ivermectin MDA should be strongly considered as a complementary malaria intervention.

articles.headline: Tandem repeats in P. vivax putative CSP show recent diversifying selection in India
articles.title_original: Evolution of tandem repeats in putative circumsporozoite protein to enhance its function: A recent and exclusive event in Plasmodium vivax in India.
articles.authors: Dash M, Pande V, Das A, Sinha A
articles.details: Researchers sequenced the putative circumsporozoite protein (PvpuCSP) in 71 Plasmodium vivax isolates from endemic regions of India to assess polymorphism and natural selection. The N- and C-terminal regions were well conserved (about 80% of the gene), while the C-terminal repeat region (1.8–2.2 kb) was highly variable, yielding 37 different alleles among the 71 samples. The authors conclude that recent, exclusive accumulation of repeats produces high length polymorphism, making PvpuCSP a potential marker for diversity and infection complexity, with episodic diversifying selection (supported by significant p-values and likelihood ratios) driving amino acid diversity.

articles.headline: Acridone antimalarial T111 active against blood, liver, and mosquito stages
articles.title_original: Potent Acridone Antimalarial against All Three Life Stages of Plasmodium.
articles.authors: Kancharla P, Dodean RA, Li Y, Zhang X, Kelly S, Charlton J, Binauhan A, Garcia L, Caridha D, Madejczyk MS, Jin X, Dennis WE, Kudyba K, McEnearney S, Lee PJ, Blount C, DeLuca J, Vuong C, Pannone K, Dinh HT, Mdaki K, Leed S, Martin ML, Pybus BS, Chimong A, Cui L, Vega-Rodriguez J, Okitwi M, Kreutzfeld O, Docan C, Green C, Liu Y, Agulay A, Mirsalis J, Farrell SN, McFadden GI, Goodman CD, Probst AS, Nilsen A, Catteruccia F, Rosenthal PJ, Riscoe M, Cooper R, Roth A, Kelly J
articles.details: This study presents an acridone antimalarial chemotype, lead candidate T111, active against all three major Plasmodium life cycle stages. T111 showed potent in vitro activity against cultured parasites, ex vivo activity against clinical isolates, oral single-dose cure in an asexual blood-stage rodent model, inhibition of sexual blood-stage parasites, activity against relapsing parasites in non-human primate liver cells, prevention of parasite development in mosquitoes, and synergy with tafenoquine against blood and liver stages. Resistance-selection analysis suggested inhibition of the mitochondrial electron transport chain via a mechanism distinct from existing antimalarials, and rat toxicology showed a favorable therapeutic index.

articles.headline: Generative deep learning infers evolutionary history of African malaria mosquitoes
articles.title_original: On the use of generative models for evolutionary inference of malaria vectors from genomic data.
articles.authors: Eneli AA, Siu PC, Perez MF, Burt A, Fumagalli M, Mathieson S
articles.details: Researchers applied unsupervised machine learning to quantify genetic structure of Anopheles mosquito populations in sub-Saharan Africa, then adapted a generative deep learning algorithm to infer the joint evolutionary history of populations sampled in Guinea and Burkina Faso. Using a novel model selection approach, they found that an evolutionary model with post-split migration fit the population pair better than one without migration. For the migration model, the method outperformed earlier summary-statistic-based approaches, particularly in capturing population genetic differentiation. The authors conclude that machine learning and generative models are a valuable direction for understanding malaria vector evolution, including joint inference of demography and natural selection.

articles.headline: Intensive control reshapes P. falciparum genomics near elimination in Myanmar
articles.title_original: Impact of intensive control on malaria population genomics in Eastern Myanmar.
articles.authors: Li X, Arya G, Thu A, Landier J, Parker D, Delmas G, Reyes A, Lwin K, Sriprawat K, Nosten F, Anderson T
articles.details: This study examined the genomic impact of an elimination program (malaria posts plus mass drug administration) in Kayin State, Myanmar, which reduced transmission by 97%, using 2270 genome-sequenced Plasmodium falciparum infections from 283 malaria posts sampled over 58 months (2015–2020). Parasites were genetically depauperate: 1726 single-genotype infections comprised 166 unique genomes (≥90% IBD), and nine families (≥45% IBD) accounted for 62.5% of parasites. Effective population size declined, with minimal change in artemisinin resistance alleles until 2020, when one predominant kelch13-R561H genotype remained. Relatedness correlated over ≤20 km, and MDA produced founder effects, giving genomic evidence of control efficacy.

articles.headline: Severe malaria in pregnancy causes massive subamniotic hemorrhage: case report
articles.title_original: A rare presentation of massive subamniotic hemorrhage in a pregnant woman with severe malaria: A case report.
articles.authors: Tsegaye MA, Abchale AB, Elssa MW, Mekuria KN, Biza NK, Gemechu FA, Tesfay RH
articles.details: This case report describes a 28-year-old pregnant woman who developed massive subamniotic hemorrhage due to severe malaria, leading to preterm delivery. Severe Plasmodium falciparum infection can cause placental hematomas (retroplacental, subchorionic, or subamniotic), and subamniotic hemorrhages, though rare, may contribute to preterm delivery and fetal growth restriction. The mother recovered with minimal bleeding after delivery, but the neonate died in the neonatal ICU from complications of prematurity; the placenta contained approximately 800 mL of lysed blood. The authors emphasize early detection and management of placental hematomas in pregnant women with malaria to reduce maternal and fetal risk.

articles.headline: Malaria depletes amino acids and micronutrients in Nigerian patients
articles.title_original: Malaria-induced dysregulation of selected micronutrients (Ca, Mg, Na, K, Zn, Fe, Co) and plasma free amino acids; implications for pathogenesis and host immunity in a Nigerian population.
articles.authors: Ekeanyanwu CR, Chikezie PC, Ekeanyanwu CL
articles.details: A cross-sectional study of 200 participants (malaria-infected versus healthy controls) measured plasma amino acids by HPLC and selected micronutrients (Ca, Mg, Na, K, Zn, Fe, Co) by ICP-MS. With increasing malaria severity, amino acid profiles were significantly altered (p<0.01) except for histidine and proline; branched-chain amino acids leucine and valine were depleted, and arginine, tyrosine, tryptophan, glutamine, methionine, and cysteine were significantly reduced (p<0.01). Infected individuals also showed significant deficiencies in calcium, sodium, zinc, and iron (p<0.01). Authors conclude malaria induces nutrient imbalances that may contribute to disease severity and warrant study of targeted nutritional interventions.

articles.headline: Glycosylation of P. falciparum TRAP needed for mosquito infection
articles.title_original: Glycosylation of Plasmodium falciparum TRAP is essential for infection of mosquito salivary glands.
articles.authors: Gupta P, Rezakhani N, Pazzagli L, Patel H, Zanghi G, Kamil M, Watson A, Vigdorovich V, Camargo N, Knutson E, Sather DN, Vaughan AM, Swearingen KE
articles.details: Using CRISPR/Cas9 site-directed mutagenesis, researchers generated two glyco-null Plasmodium falciparum TRAP mutants (TRAP_T256A removing O-fucosylation at Thr256 and TRAP_W250F removing C-mannosylation at Trp250) and compared their fitness across the life cycle to NF54 wild type and a TRAP knockout. Both glyco-null mutants showed major fitness defects comparable to knockout: sporozoites failed to productively colonize mosquito salivary glands, were highly impaired in gliding motility, and could not invade cultured human hepatocytes. TRAP abundance was significantly reduced despite normal transcript levels, indicating that TSR glycosylation is critical for TRAP expression and function in the mosquito stage.

articles.headline: Simulated graphene SPR biosensor for stage-specific malaria detection
articles.title_original: Design and simulation of a graphene-integrated SPR biosensor for malaria detection.
articles.authors: Tene T, Arias Arias F, Paredes-Páliz KI, González García JC, Bonilla García N, Vacacela Gomez C
articles.details: This theoretical study designed and optimized a surface plasmon resonance (SPR) biosensor incorporating graphene, silicon nitride, and a thiol-tethered ssDNA layer for malaria detection and stage differentiation. Two configurations (Sys3 and Sys4) were simulated using the transfer matrix method and evaluated against three malaria stages—ring, trophozoite, and schizont—based on refractive index variations. Sys3 achieved sensitivities of 353.14, 291.14, and 263.26°/RIU, while Sys4 reached 315.71, 294.81, and 268.65°/RIU. Sys3 showed a higher quality factor and lower detection limit, whereas Sys4 offered improved biomolecular recognition. Although limited to simulation, the authors suggest potential for label-free, stage-specific point-of-care diagnostics.

articles.headline: Novel Kelch13 A675T and high SP/chloroquine resistance in Burundi
articles.title_original: A Novel Plasmodium falciparum Kelch13 A675T Mutation and High Levels of Chloroquine and Sulfadoxine-Pyrimethamine Resistance in Burundi.
articles.authors: Kayode T, Niyukuri D, Holzschuh A, Da Silva G, Huwe T, Lerch A, Nyandwi J, Koepfli C
articles.details: P. falciparum isolates from Cibitoke Province, Burundi were sequenced for ten resistance markers (Illumina/Nanopore), with plasmepsin-2 and mdr1 copy number typed by digital PCR. Among 157 isolates, no validated pfkelch13 ART-R mutations were found, but a novel pfkelch13 A675T mutation was identified (A675V was previously validated in neighboring Rwanda). Background mutations were frequent (pffd D193Y 45.9%, pfarps10 V127M 11.5%). SP resistance was widespread with quintuple haplotypes in 82.3% and sextuple in 12.3% of mono-infections; pfcrt triple mutant (94.9%) and pfmdr1 N-F-D (57.9%) indicated sustained chloroquine resistance. No pfpm2 duplications and rare pfmdr1 amplifications (1.8%). Authors warn of threats to IPTp/SMC and the need for stronger surveillance.

articles.headline: Artemisinin-resistant K13 R622I and RDT negativity rising in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Artemisinin resistant kelch13 R622I and RDT negativity approaching predominance in northern Ethiopia and emerging C580Y of African origin threaten falciparum malaria control.
articles.authors: Zeleke AJ, Fola AA, Tollefson GA, Niaré K, Leonetti A, Taropawala O, Marglous J, Crudale R, Brhane BG, Assefa A, Kiyuka P, Parr JB, Hailu A, Aemero M, Bailey JA
articles.details: Using molecular inversion probe sequencing, researchers sampled two districts (Gondar Zuria and Tach Armachiho) in northwest Ethiopia over a year. Among 903 people with P. falciparum malaria, the validated artemisinin partial resistance mutation k13 R622I had markedly higher prevalence (44.3%) than earlier reports, significantly higher in Gondar Zuria (52%) than Tach Armachiho (40%; p<0.001). HRP2-based RDT negativity was higher in R622I mutants than wild types (48.3% vs 30.7%; p<0.001), and 98.2% of R622I samples carried the mdr1 NFD haplotype linked to reduced lumefantrine susceptibility. The k13 C580Y mutation was detected in two patients (0.4%) in Gondar Zuria—the first in the Horn of Africa—with clonal (IBD=1) parasites whose flanking haplotypes suggest local de novo emergence rather than Southeast Asian origin.

articles.headline: DNA repair pathways differ between core and subtelomeric malaria genome regions
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum DNA repair dynamics reveal unique roles for TLS polymerases and PfRad51 in genome diversification.
articles.authors: Vishwanatha A, Zhang X, Liu YJ, Leung A, Herring M, Chan A, Calhoun S, Deitsch K, Kirkman L
articles.details: This laboratory study of Plasmodium falciparum created knockout parasite lines of the DNA repair enzymes PfRad51 and the translesion synthesis (TLS) polymerases PfPolζ and PfRev1 to probe how the parasite maintains its conserved core genome versus diverse subtelomeric regions. Irradiation hypersensitivity varied across the cell cycle for TLS-knockout parasites but was uniform for PfRad51-knockout parasites. PfRad51 was essential for homologous recombination-mediated repair of double strand breaks in the core genome, whereas a Rad51-independent homology-directed repair operated in the subtelomeric region in all three knockout lines. The authors propose this location-dependent damage response maintains chromosomal diversification.

articles.headline: No artemisinin-resistance mutations found in Brazilian P. falciparum samples
articles.title_original: Tracking Drug Resistance in Plasmodium falciparum: Genetic Diversity of Key Resistance Markers in Brazilian Malaria Hotspots.
articles.authors: de Abreu-Fernandes R, de Queiroz LT, Almeida-de-Oliveira NK, de Lavigne Mello AR, de Aguiar Barros J, Pratt-Riccio LR, Melo GC, Brasil P, Daniel-Ribeiro CT, Menard D, Ferreira-da-Cruz MF
articles.details: This study analyzed P. falciparum from 141 patients in Brazil (2013-2023) using PCR and DNA sequencing to identify single-nucleotide polymorphisms in the pfcrt, pfmdr1, and pfk13 genes. Half the samples carried the SVMNTMCGI haplotype in pfcrt and none showed C350R mutations; in pfmdr1 the NYCDY haplotype dominated (70%), with low N86Y (4%) and no Y184F. No pfk13 mutations linked to artemisinin partial resistance were detected, and only one Amazonas sample was wild-type across all genes. Significant linkage disequilibrium occurred within pfcrt and pfmdr1 but not between them. The authors conclude these findings support continued ACT efficacy while underscoring the need for ongoing genetic surveillance.

articles.headline: Mathematical model optimizes mass drug administration to limit malaria resistance
articles.title_original: Optimizing MDA and antimalarial treatment in the presence of drug resistance for effective malaria control.
articles.authors: Nimpa MM, Teytsa HN, Mbang J, Wondji CS, Djidjou-Demasse R
articles.details: This modeling study developed a compartmental mathematical model incorporating asymptomatic, paucisymptomatic, and clinical infection states to evaluate strategies combining mass drug administration (MDA) with antimalarial treatment under drug resistance. Global sensitivity analysis identified MDA coverage, treatment access, the mutation probability from treated sensitive to treated resistant infections, and initial resistance prevalence as critical factors. Using optimal control analysis, the authors found that at low mutation rates the best strategy combines antimalarials and MDA with a gradual reduction in antimalarial use, whereas at higher mutation rates it prioritizes broader MDA while substantially reducing antimalarial reliance to minimize resistance.

articles.headline: Reanalysis reveals ungulate Plasmodium forms a distinct evolutionary group
articles.title_original: Bringing to light unnoticed data on the genetic and host diversity of ungulate Plasmodium.
articles.authors: Makanga BK, Boundenga L, Bitome-Essono PY, Arnathau C, Rougeron V, Prugnolle F
articles.details: This study reanalyzed previously overlooked xenosurveillance data to clarify the evolutionary relationships of Plasmodium parasites infecting ungulates, including new host species in the genera Tragelaphus, Syncerus, and Cephalophus identified in African forest and savanna ungulates. Phylogenetic analyses confirmed that ungulate Plasmodium form a monophyletic group distinct from parasites infecting primates, rodents, and bats. Within this group two main clades were identified: Cephalophus parasites clustered with previously described sequences, while those from Tragelaphus and Syncerus formed distinct lineages likely representing novel species.
