The authors stress the importance of expanding study in under-explored regions and hosts.

articles.headline: Liver-targeted CD81 siRNA blocks malaria infection in mice
articles.title_original: Protection from malaria infection using liver-targeted siRNA.
articles.authors: Steel RWJ, Schepis A, Nguyen T, Milstein S, Yucius K, Tu HC, Fishilevich E, Haslett P, Kappe SHI
articles.details: This study tested N-acetylgalactosamine (GalNAc)-conjugated small interfering RNA (siRNA) targeting CD81, the hepatocyte entry receptor for P. falciparum sporozoites, as a host-based malaria prevention strategy. The siRNA efficiently silenced both mouse and human hepatic CD81 in vivo. In a mouse malaria model, CD81 GalNAc-siRNA blocked parasite liver infection in a dose-dependent manner and prevented the onset of blood-stage infection, and it also reduced P. falciparum infection in human liver-chimeric mice. The authors conclude that siRNA represents a promising host-based approach to prevent malaria infection.

articles.headline: Modelling shows indoor residual spraying cut malaria transmission 10% in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Modelling the Effect of Indoor Residual Spraying on Malaria Transmission in Nassarawa State, North-central Nigeria.
articles.authors: Suleiman AG, Kareem L, Obi C
articles.details: This modelling study used a simple SIRS compartmental model (ordinary differential equations solved via Euler's method) fitted to routine malaria surveillance data from a Local Government Area where indoor residual spraying (IRS) had been piloted, comparing it with a selected control LGA. The estimated malaria transmission rate was marginally lower in the intervention LGA than the control (0.95 versus 1.05), corresponding to a 10% reduction in transmission over two years. The authors attributed the modest effect to how IRS was implemented and to uncertainties in routine surveillance data, recommending attention to spray frequency and more robust data collection.

articles.headline: Ultra-sensitive RDT outperforms conventional RDT in asymptomatic pregnant women
articles.title_original: Comparison of diagnostic accuracy of ultra-sensitive and conventional rapid diagnostic tests for malaria in asymptomatic pregnant women at a tertiary hospital in North-Central Nigeria.
articles.authors: Anenga UM, Swende TZ, Hembah-Hilekaan SK
articles.details: This cross-sectional comparative study of 107 asymptomatic pregnant women at an antenatal clinic compared a conventional rapid diagnostic test (co-RDT) and an ultra-sensitive RDT (us-RDT) against microscopy as the reference standard for detecting Plasmodium falciparum. P. falciparum prevalence was 14% by microscopy, 12.1% by us-RDT, and 2.8% by co-RDT. The us-RDT had markedly higher sensitivity (66.7% vs 13.3%), positive predictive value (83.3% vs 50.0%), negative predictive value (94.7% vs 87.4%), and accuracy (93.5% vs 86.0%), with similar specificity (97.8%); the difference in performance was significant (p=0.002). The authors conclude ultra-sensitive RDTs are more effective for detecting P. falciparum in this population.

articles.headline: Imported malaria in Chengdu mostly P. falciparum from Africa, 2016–2023
articles.title_original: [Epidemiological characteristics of imported malaria cases in Chengdu City from 2016 to 2023].
articles.authors: Feng L, Wang L, Jiang X, Tan K, Li P
articles.journal: Zhongguo Xue Xi Chong Bing Fang Zhi Za Zhi
articles.details: This retrospective epidemiological analysis of surveillance data examined 463 imported malaria cases reported in Chengdu City from 2016 to 2023. Plasmodium falciparum was predominant (71.27%, 330/463), and most cases originated from Africa (94.17%, 436/463), followed by Asia (5.61%) and South America (0.22%), with a male-to-female ratio of 21.05:1.00 and most cases in migrant laborers aged 20–50 years. At initial diagnosis, 74.78% (341/456) were correctly diagnosed; correct diagnosis was high at county-level (91.43%) but very low at township centers (11.11%), village clinics (0/4), and individual doctors (0/28). The authors call for targeted health education for laborers traveling to Africa and improved diagnostic capacity at grassroots institutions.

articles.headline: Two-thirds of Nigerian secondary school adolescents willing to get malaria vaccine
articles.title_original: Willingness of secondary school adolescents to get vaccinated against Malaria: a cross-sectional study in Enugu, South-East Nigeria.
articles.authors: Nduagubam OC, Ossai EN, Chinawa AT, Onukwuli VO, Uwaezuoke NA, Okafor CN, Chinawa JM
articles.details: This cross-sectional observational study surveyed 496 secondary school adolescents across six schools in Enugu (June–September 2023) to assess willingness to accept a malaria vaccine and associated factors. Overall, 68.1% were willing to receive the vaccine, while only 32.1% had good knowledge of malaria vaccination and 59.3% reported that it could protect against seasonal influenza. Willingness was higher among those under 15 years (AOR=2.2, 95%CI 1.1–4.7), lower among males (AOR=0.6, 95%CI 0.4–0.9), and higher among those with good vaccine knowledge (AOR=2.2, 95%CI 1.4–3.4). The authors conclude willingness is influenced by gender, knowledge of the malaria vaccine, and age.

articles.headline: Propolis compounds tectochrysin and galangin inhibit P. falciparum in lab tests
articles.title_original: In vitro and in silico investigations of Propolis-derived phytochemicals as potential inhibitors of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Khan DA, Hasan MN, Boonhok R, Sungkanu S, Singhaboot Y, Shorna AA, Hasan A, Chotivanich K, Wilairatana P, Siyadatpanah A, Norouzi R, Sama-Ae I, Mitsuwan W, Paul AK, de Lourdes Pereira M, Sundar SS, Mahboob T, Wiart C, Labana RV, Chimplee S, Nissapatorn V
articles.details: This in vitro and in silico study evaluated ethanol-extracted Propolis from various Iranian regions against Plasmodium falciparum NF54 using a SYBR Green I fluorescence assay, with GC-MS identification of active phytochemicals and molecular docking plus 100-ns molecular dynamics simulations against P. falciparum lactate dehydrogenase (PfLDH). Propolis from Kermanshah showed the highest activity (IC50 = 6.69 ± 1.44 μg/mL), with tectochrysin and galangin as major constituents. Docking gave strong binding affinities for tectochrysin (−7.8 kcal/mol) and galangin (−7.5 kcal/mol) to PfLDH, exceeding chloroquine and quinine, and MD simulations confirmed stable binding. The authors conclude these compounds are promising PfLDH inhibitors warranting further in vivo and clinical validation.

articles.headline: Parasite genomics tracks falciparum decline during Myanmar elimination program
articles.title_original: Malaria parasite population genomics during an elimination program in Eastern Myanmar.
articles.authors: Li X, Arya GA, Thu AM, Landier J, Parker DM, Delmas G, Reyes A, Lwin KM, Sriprawat K, Nosten F, Anderson TJC
articles.details: Genomic surveillance study in Kayin State, eastern Myanmar, using 5014 dried blood spots from P. falciparum-infected patients at 413 malaria posts over 58 months (November 2015–August 2020), with 2270 parasite genomes sequenced and analyzed by identity-by-descent. The population was genetically depauperate: 1726 single-genotype infections comprised 166 unique genomes, and nine families accounted for 62.5% of parasites; relatedness was correlated up to ~20 km. Kelch13 diversity was stable from 2016–2019, but only the kelch13-R561H clonal genotype predominated in 2020, and mass drug administration produced parasite founder effects. The authors conclude parasite population size fell and genetic drift may outweigh selection in low-transmission elimination settings.

articles.headline: Plasmodium infection alters mouse skin odors, increasing mosquito attraction
articles.title_original: [Changes in murine skin odors following Plasmodium infections and their impact on mosquito attraction].
articles.authors: Li J, Li M, He B, Liu T, Zhu F, Zhang J, Xu W
articles.journal: Zhongguo Xue Xi Chong Bing Fang Zhi Za Zhi
articles.details: Controlled animal experiment with twenty 6-week-old female C57BL/6 mice split into infection (n=10, injected with 1×10^6 P. yoelii via tail vein) and PBS control (n=10) groups, using a triple-cage olfactometer with Anopheles stephensi, GC-MS of skin volatiles, and 16S rRNA sequencing of skin microbiota. Parasitemia rose to 51.00±3.61% by day 6 post-infection (P<0.001), and mosquito attraction increased over days (peaking at 66.84%, χ2=9.54, P<0.05). Infected mice had higher p-cresol, ethylbenzene, and nonanal and lower toluene, benzaldehyde, and indole, plus higher Streptococcus and Rothia and lower Lactococcus abundance. The authors link Plasmodium infection to skin odor and microbiota changes affecting vector attraction.

articles.headline: Faculty training program in applied malaria modeling: lessons learned
articles.title_original: Building contextually-relevant training programs for scientific development: process and lessons learned in implementing two iterations of a faculty enrichment program in applied malaria modeling.
articles.authors: Onyango L, Ouédraogo-Ametchie G, Stahlfeld A, Holden TM, Richter R, Runge M, Toh KB, Agorinya I, Mensah B, Gerardin J
articles.details: Qualitative evaluation of a 4-month faculty enrichment program in applied malaria modeling run at Northwestern University, USA, in 2022 and 2023, covering technical skills, communication skills, and malaria epidemiology. Two cohorts of participants and instructors were interviewed at baseline, midline, and endline about expectations, experiences, and challenges. Participants valued growth in technical and research skills and knowledge-transfer opportunities but reported challenges with cross-disciplinary learning and balancing program components. The authors conclude that technical training programs must be informed by participant needs and use continuous feedback and multi-pronged approaches.

articles.headline: RAA plus nanopore sequencing accurately identifies four Plasmodium species
articles.title_original: [Establishment and preliminary evaluation of recombinase-aided isothermal nucleic acid amplification combined with nanopore sequencing for identification of Plasmodium species].
articles.authors: Lin W, Chen L, Zhang C, Wei H, Tang C, Wang R, Lin L, Lin M
articles.journal: Zhongguo Xue Xi Chong Bing Fang Zhi Za Zhi
articles.details: Diagnostic development and evaluation study using dried blood spots from 89 malaria patients to identify P. vivax, P. ovale, P. malariae, and P. falciparum via recombinase-aided isothermal amplification (RAA) of the 18S rRNA gene combined with nanopore sequencing, compared against RT-qPCR and nested PCR (nPCR, the gold standard). Of 49 sequenced samples, nPCR found 22 P. falciparum, 6 P. malariae, 6 P. vivax, 14 P. ovale, and one mixed P. falciparum-P. malariae infection. Using nPCR as reference, RAA-nanopore sequencing achieved 92.00% sensitivity, 97.33% specificity, and 96.00% accuracy, exceeding RT-qPCR (88.24%, 97.32%, 95.00%). The authors conclude it is a sensitive, specific supplementary method for species identification.

articles.headline: Review: malaria and malnutrition cycle in India's tribal areas
articles.title_original: Malaria and malnutrition in tribal areas of India: implications for paediatric health.
articles.authors: Verma AK, Shrivastava S, Saxena N
articles.details: Mini-review discussing the interplay of malaria and undernutrition affecting paediatric health among tribal populations in India. It describes how undernutrition impairs immune development while malaria causes anemia, fever, and potentially death, and how repeated malaria and intestinal infections alter gut flora to worsen malabsorption and malnutrition. The authors argue that inadequate diet, poor sanitation, water quality, and limited healthcare access trap tribal communities in a cycle of hunger and infection, and call for an integrated approach addressing both nutritional and malaria-specific needs to eliminate malaria from these areas.

articles.headline: Review of how malaria parasites evade the host immune system
articles.title_original: [Progress of researches on mechanisms underlying immune escape of Plasmodium].
articles.authors: Wu Y, He J, Cao X, Li J, Shen S, Du Y, Tan C
articles.journal: Zhongguo Xue Xi Chong Bing Fang Zhi Za Zhi
articles.details: This review summarizes mechanisms by which Plasmodium parasites escape host immune recognition and clearance. It covers antigenic variation, epigenetic regulation, antagonism against IgM antibody, activation of the cGAS-STING signaling pathway, suppression of splenic immune functions, and molecular camouflage. The authors frame these immune escape strategies as a challenge for malaria control and aim to inform development of malaria vaccines and antimalarial agents.

articles.headline: Seasonal malaria chemoprevention linked to fewer non-malarial pediatric infections
articles.title_original: Indirect Benefits of Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention for Non-Malarial Pediatric Infections and Routine Antibiotic Use in Real-World Programmatic Settings: A Pre-Post Study Using Positive and Negative Controls.
articles.authors: Gebreegziabher EA, Ouattara M, Bountogo M, Coulibaly B, Boudo V, Ouedraogo T, Lebas E, Hu H, O'Brien KS, Hsiang MS, Glidden DV, Arnold BF, Lietman TM, Sié A, Oldenburg CE
articles.details: This pre-post study in rural Burkina Faso compared non-malarial diagnoses (pneumonia, diarrhea, acute malnutrition) and antibiotic prescriptions during seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC, monthly sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine plus amodiaquine) administration weeks versus a three-week post-intervention period, using data from 51 health facilities and Poisson regression with positive and negative controls. Modest declines per 1,000 person-weeks were seen post-SMC for pneumonia (0.7; 95% CI 0.3-1.0), diarrhea (0.2; 0.1-0.4), acute malnutrition (0.05; 0.001-0.09), and antibiotics (0.90; 0.4-1.4). Malaria diagnoses and antimalarial prescriptions fell by 3.7 and 3.6, while injury (negative control) stayed stable (0.02). Authors conclude SMC may have benefits beyond malaria prevention.

articles.headline: Plasmodium vivax causes cerebral malaria and organ failure in adult
articles.title_original: Cerebral Malaria and Multi-Organ Dysfunction Caused by Plasmodium vivax in an Adult: A case from Oman with a literature review.
articles.authors: Al Kindi SM, Chhetri S, Al Aamri ZZ, Dhuri SD, Al Balushi ZY, Khamis F
articles.details: This case report describes a previously healthy 38-year-old Pakistani male who presented to a tertiary hospital emergency department with cerebral malaria and multi-organ dysfunction syndrome caused by Plasmodium vivax, confirmed by peripheral blood smear and PCR. He was treated with intravenous artesunate and supportive intensive care and achieved full neurological recovery. The authors note this as the first documented such case in Oman and emphasize considering P. vivax as a cause of severe malaria even in non-endemic regions.

articles.headline: Review backs malaria vaccines for eradication in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Mitigation of Malaria in Sub-Saharan Africa through Vaccination: A Budding Road Map for Global Malaria Eradication.
articles.authors: Alum EU, Ainebyoona C, Egwu CO, Onohuean H, Ugwu OP, Uti DE, Alum BN, Echegu DA
articles.details: This narrative review of peer-reviewed literature from 2014 to 2024 evaluated malaria mitigation strategies in sub-Saharan Africa, emphasizing vaccination toward eradication. The RTS,S/AS01 vaccine showed moderate efficacy (30-55%) in preventing malaria in children under five in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi, while R21/Matrix-M showed up to 77% efficacy in trials. Implementation challenges include limited funding, vaccine hesitancy, poor healthcare infrastructure, and community misconceptions. The authors conclude vaccination is a promising, cost-effective, scalable strategy and call for stronger health systems, acceptance campaigns, and research funding.

articles.headline: Rheumatoid factor in 12.5% of asymptomatic chronic hepatitis B carriers
articles.title_original: Prevalence of rheumatoid factor in asymptomatic chronic hepatitis B surface carriers in the context of endemic malaria: cross-sectional study at University Hospital of Cocody, Abidjan, Côte d'Ivoire.
articles.authors: Goran-Kouacou APV, Yéboah OR, Assi AUA, Séri YJ, Adou AH, N Guessan K, Dassé SR
articles.details: This prospective cross-sectional study enrolled 120 asymptomatic chronic carriers of hepatitis B surface antigen without autoimmune diseases or active malaria at the University Hospital of Cocody in Abidjan, Côte d'Ivoire, measuring rheumatoid factor by immunoturbidimetry (positivity threshold >14 IU/mL). Rheumatoid factor positivity prevalence was 12.5% (95% CI 6.54-18.46); mean age was 36.4 ± 11.2 years and 60% were male. Advanced age (≥30 years) was independently associated with positivity (aOR 3.32; 95% CI 1.06-10.39; p=0.04), with no significant association for sex. The authors suggest monitoring rheumatoid factor in older carriers.

articles.headline: Review of health education's role in China's malaria elimination
articles.title_original: [Application of health education during the progress towards malaria elimination in China: a review].
articles.authors: Zhou M, Lin K
articles.journal: Zhongguo Xue Xi Chong Bing Fang Zhi Za Zhi
articles.details: This review describes how health education contributed to malaria elimination in China, which was certified malaria-free by WHO in 2021 after decades of integrated control. It covers community-based and school-based education, activities targeting entry-exit personnel and healthcare workers, and diversified media outreach. The authors argue health education raised public awareness and prevention capacity and is important for reducing the risk of re-establishment from imported malaria, offering insights for control strategy in the post-elimination stage. No quantitative outcomes are reported.

articles.headline: Four rapid tests compared for detecting P. vivax malaria
articles.title_original: [Comparison of Rapid Diagnostic Tests for the Detection of Plasmodium vivax Malaria].
articles.authors: Ku B, Jung H, Shin HI, Lee MR, Ju JW, Lee HI
articles.details: This study evaluated four Pf/Pan rapid diagnostic test (RDT) kits, all able to detect the five human malaria species, using positive samples confirmed by nested PCR, with parasitemia rate based on real-time PCR cycle threshold values. Across kits, sensitivity ranged from 70.9% to 96.5%, and in low-parasitemia samples sensitivity ranged from 43.8% to 93.8%, while specificity was 100.0% for all kits. The authors conclude the findings inform RDT product selection by revealing differences in effectiveness for presumptive malaria diagnosis.

articles.headline: Two reference genomes assembled for malaria mosquito Anopheles darlingi
articles.title_original: Two chromosomal reference genome sequences for the malaria mosquito, Anopheles ( Nyssorhynchus) darlingi, Root, 1926 from French Guiana and Peru.
articles.authors: Gendrin M, Heu K, Moreno M, Gamboa D, Vinetz JM, Tong C, Conn JE, Johnson HF, Heaton H, Wagah MG, Collins JC, Krasheninnikova K, Pelan SE, Pointon DB, Torrance JW, Tracey A, Uliano-Silva M, Wood JMD, von Wyschetzki K, McCarthy SA, Lawniczak MKN, Neafsey DE, Makunin A
articles.details: The authors present two genome assemblies, each from an individual female Anopheles (Nyssorhynchus) darlingi mosquito from wild populations in French Guiana and Peru. Each assembly spans approximately 180 megabases, with most scaffolded into three chromosomal pseudomolecules including the assembled X sex chromosome, and complete mitochondrial genomes of 15.4 kilobases. The two assemblies differ by two inversions in chromosome arm 2R.

articles.headline: Saffron-chloroquine chitosan nanocomposites boost antimalarial activity in vitro
articles.title_original: In Vitro Antimalarial Activity of Chloroquine-Crocus Sativus Conjugated to Chitosan Nanocomposits against 3D7 and K1 Strains of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Khezri A, Nateghpour M, Motevali Haghi A, Elmi T, Rahimi Foroushani A, Shafii Ardestani M, Hanifian H
articles.details: This in vitro study conjugated saffron (Crocus sativus) extract and chloroquine separately to chitosan nanocomposites and tested them against chloroquine-sensitive and chloroquine-resistant Plasmodium falciparum, calculating IC50 values. Conjugation efficiency was 64.4% for chloroquine and 42.9% for saffron. IC50s (μg/ml) for Chloroquine, Nanoparticle-Chloroquine, Saffron, and Nanoparticle-Saffron were 0.3, 0.8, 42.5, and 6.24 for the sensitive strain and 5, 1, 12.5, and 3.12 for the resistant strain. Combination therapy using a fixed-ratio method showed synergistic effects, and the synthesized nanocomposites inhibited parasite growth better than non-nano forms, with significant differences observed in some cases (P<0.05).

articles.headline: Severe falciparum malaria with kidney injury recovers after artesunate
articles.title_original: Managing Acute Kidney Injury in Severe Falciparum Malaria: Insights from a Challenging Case.
articles.authors: Putra DE, Kasman I, Boro AMB, Purnama A, Zulfikar E
articles.details: This case report describes a 22-year-old male with a seven-day history of intermittent fever after travel to malaria-endemic areas, presenting somnolent with icteric sclera, hepatomegaly, and dark urine. Labs showed serum creatinine 3.52 mg/dL (311 μmol/L), elevated transaminases, hyperbilirubinemia, thrombocytopenia, and 9.7% P. falciparum parasitemia; imaging showed pulmonary edema, enlarged kidneys, ascites, and pleural effusion. Diagnosed with severe falciparum malaria complicated by acute kidney injury, he received six days of IV artesunate, then oral dihydroartemisinin/piperaquine and single-dose primaquine, plus two hemodialysis sessions, and recovered without sequelae.

articles.headline: Avian malaria parasites alter metabolic rate and immune response in siskins
articles.title_original: Dynamics of resting metabolic rate and innate immune response in malaria-infected Eurasian siskins.
articles.authors: Erokhina M, Bushuev A, Platonova E, Khaitov V, Davydov A, Mukhin A
articles.details: An experimental study infected juvenile siskins with two avian malaria parasites—Plasmodium relictum (SGS1 lineage, common in the Northern Palearctic) and P. ashfordi (GRW2 lineage, from Central and Southern Africa)—and tracked resting metabolic rate (RMR) and interleukin-6 (IL-6) during the acute phase. SGS1 infection was marked by reduced RMR and IL-6 levels, while GRW2 birds showed a similar initial IL-6 drop that was not sustained, plus distinct RMR dynamics from SGS1 birds. The study did not conclusively show that tropical (GRW2) malaria causes more severe effects, underscoring the complexity of host–parasite interactions.

articles.headline: Undiagnosed asymptomatic malaria in pregnancy harms mothers and infants
articles.title_original: Detrimental infant and maternal outcomes of undiagnosed asymptomatic malaria in pregnancy.
articles.authors: Beeson JG, Opi DH
articles.details: This commentary notes that in low malaria transmission areas most infections during pregnancy go undetected by standard diagnostic tests. It reports that these missed asymptomatic infections have substantial negative impacts for both the mother and the developing fetus. No study design, sample size, or quantitative results are provided in the abstract.

articles.headline: P. falciparum prevalence varies widely in Bamako schoolchildren
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum infection in school-aged children in urban and peri-urban schools of Bamako, Mali.
articles.authors: Toure M, Kane F, Sanogo D, Keita S, Keita B, Aro AZ, Keita M, Konate D, Diawara SI, Yaro AS, Kone A, Magassa M, Kante S, Tangara CO, Tangara K, Coulibaly H, Cisse B, Thiam SM, Traore AS, Shaffer JG, Sogoba N, Diakite M, Doumbia S
articles.details: A school-based survey in Bamako, Mali, divided the city into four ecological strata (urban center, high altitude, riverside, and peripheral neighborhoods) and randomly selected 200–230 children aged 6–13 per school (three schools per stratum), testing for Plasmodium falciparum by malaria rapid diagnostic tests and microscopy. Prevalence ranged from 0% to 15.5% across sites, and travel history and fever were not associated with infection (p > 0.05). Anemia raised the odds of infection (OR = 2.38, 95% CI 1.57–3.61), as did living at an urban site along the Niger River (OR = 3.37, 95% CI 2.05–7.45). The authors conclude that school-aged children are a major parasite reservoir in urban Bamako and a target for control.

articles.headline: Korea's 1979 malaria elimination status was misattributed, review finds
articles.title_original: Misattribution of Korea's malaria elimination status in 1979.
articles.authors: Seol R, Kim Y
articles.details: A source-based historical review examined WHO Weekly Epidemiological Record, WHO Western Pacific Regional Office reports, US Department of Defense health reports, and academic and web citations to trace the belief that WHO declared the Republic of Korea (ROK) malaria-free in 1979. WHO records actually identified the Democratic People's Republic of Korea (DPRK) as having eliminated malaria by 1979, while ROK had not eliminated it as of 1980. The authors conclude the error arose from a misreading in US DoD documents and has persisted through repeated unverified citations, underscoring the need for citation accuracy in global health reporting.

articles.headline: Plant extract with silver nanoparticles reduces malarial spleen damage in mice
articles.title_original: Histopathologic effect and immunomodulatory of Indigofera oblongifolia with silver nanoparticles against splenic injury by Plasmodium chabaudi infection in mice.
articles.authors: Murshed M, Al-Tamimi J, Alzaylaee H, Ahmeda H, Al-Quraishy S
articles.details: This experimental study tested Indigofera oblongifolia leaf extract combined with silver nanoparticles (IOLE AgNPs) against Plasmodium chabaudi-induced splenic damage in female C57BL/6 mice. Fifty infected mice were treated with IOLE AgNPs or chloroquine phosphate. Histopathological and immunohistochemical analyses showed that IOLE AgNPs restored splenic architecture, reduced inflammatory markers, and improved immune responses compared with control and chloroquine-treated groups. The authors suggest IOLE AgNPs may offer a novel approach to mitigate malaria-associated splenic dysfunction.

articles.headline: Plasmodium falciparum η-class carbonic anhydrase as antimalarial drug target
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum η-carbonic anhydrase.
articles.authors: Supuran CT, Capasso C
articles.details: This review covers Plasmodium falciparum carbonic anhydrase (PfCA), an enzyme involved in pyrimidine biosynthesis and considered crucial for the parasite's intraerythrocytic life cycle, with much higher CA levels in infected versus uninfected red blood cells. Initially classed as α-CA from 2004, it was shown in 2014 to belong to a new genetic class, η-CA, with zinc coordinated by two histidines and a glutamine. Truncated and longer cloned forms efficiently catalyze CO2 hydration but have not been crystallized. Many low-nanomolar in vitro inhibitors (anions, sulfonamides, phenols, coumarins) exist, but only acetazolamide and an ureido-substituted benzenesulfonamide showed potent growth inhibition of P. falciparum in infected red blood cells. The authors call η-CAs innovative but preliminary drug targets needing further structural study.

articles.headline: Antifolate resistance gene mutations in zoonotic Plasmodium cynomolgi
articles.title_original: Molecular analysis of DHFR and DHPS gene mutations in Plasmodium cynomolgi from humans and macaques in Southeast Asia.
articles.authors: Sangsri R, Jongkon N, Choowongkomon K, Malaivijitnond S, Day NPJ, Dondorp AM, Imwong M
articles.details: This molecular study sequenced DHFR and DHPS genes of the zoonotic parasite Plasmodium cynomolgi from 9 asymptomatic human blood samples (Battambang, Cambodia; eight monoinfections, one mixed with P. vivax) and 29 macaque samples (Saraburi, Thailand; all monoinfections). Human samples were 100% identical to the P. cynomolgi RO strain, with no DHFR mutations and one DHPS mutation (V451I). Macaque samples carried PcyDHFR mutations N44T and C49S, with two haplotypes based on I7 variation: haplotype 1 (72.41%, wild-type I7) and haplotype 2 (27.59%, I7 mutation). Mutations occurred near catalytic sites but, by molecular docking with eight antifolate drugs, did not significantly affect binding affinity. The authors conclude current antifolates remain potentially effective and stress monitoring drug resistance in zoonotic malaria.

articles.headline: AI matches frontline workers on malaria RDT reading, beats them on faint lines
articles.title_original: Malaria RDT (mRDT) interpretation accuracy by frontline health workers compared to AI in Kano state, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Frade S, Cooper S, Smedinghoff S, Hattery D, Ruan Y, Isabelli P, Ravi N, McLaughlin M, Metz L, Finette B
articles.details: This comparative study in Kano State, Nigeria evaluated whether AI-based computer vision could enhance malaria rapid diagnostic test (mRDT) interpretation by frontline healthcare workers (FHWs), benchmarking AI, FHW, and expert Panel Reader interpretations using weighted F1 scores. Against Panel Reads, AI matched 96.38% of positives and 97.12% of negatives, while FHWs matched 96.82% of positives and 94.31% of negatives. FHW and AI interpretations agreed 97.52% on positives and 93.38% on negatives. Overall accuracy was higher for AI (weighted F1 96.4) than FHWs (95.3). On 163 faint positive mRDTs, AI correctly identified 90.2% versus 76.1% by FHWs. The authors conclude AI performs comparably to FHWs and exceeds them on faint positives, with potential to improve malaria diagnosis in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Review of emerging biosensor and nanotechnology tools for malaria detection
articles.title_original: Advances in malaria detection.
articles.authors: Ike BW, Kathiresan V, Miya L, Karpoormath R
articles.details: This review examines current and emerging malaria diagnostic tools, noting that Egypt attained malaria-free status in 2024 while countries including Eritrea, Ethiopia, Ghana, Kenya, Nigeria, Somalia, Sri Lanka, Sudan, and Yemen remain "High Burden High Impact" zones, with malaria causing over 435,000 deaths annually. It argues that treatment resistance, atypical symptoms, and limited sensitivity and specificity of conventional methods make diagnosis challenging, and that a non-invasive tool with multiplex capability and better analytical performance is needed. The authors highlight novel biosensor technologies using advanced nanotechnology and biochemical approaches as rapid, sensitive, cost-effective, point-of-care alternatives requiring minimal technical expertise, and evaluate their potential to advance malaria control and eradication.

articles.headline: Review proposes harnessing IgA mucosal immunity against malaria
articles.title_original: Targeting mucosal immunity in malaria control: the underexplored role of IgA.
articles.authors: Muwonge H, Ssewanyana I, Yeka A, Byakika-Kibwika P
articles.details: This review explores the underexplored role of Immunoglobulin A (IgA) in malaria immunity, noting an estimated 263 million cases and 597,000 deaths in 2023 and stalled progress from insecticide-treated nets, ACTs, and the RTS,S vaccine. While IgG and IgM have dominated research, emerging evidence suggests IgA can block parasite entry, activate complement, and modulate inflammation, though its protective efficacy is unestablished. The authors synthesize literature on sporozoite- and merozoite-specific IgA responses, how IgA arises in a non-mucosal infection, and oral, nasal, or prime-boost vaccine platforms that might harness IgA alongside IgG. They identify gaps in correlating IgA with clinical immunity and call for specialized animal models and longitudinal human cohorts to support IgA-based transmission-reducing vaccines.

articles.headline: Global burden of neglected tropical diseases and malaria declined since 1990
articles.title_original: Global, regional, and national burden of neglected tropical diseases and malaria, 1990-2021.
articles.authors: Tuergan T, Abulaiti A, Tulahong A, Zhang R, Shao Y, Aji T
articles.details: Using the Global Burden of Disease 2021 database, this descriptive and modeling study analyzed temporal trends in neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) and malaria from 1990 to 2021, applying linear regression, four forecasting models, decomposition, and frontier analysis. Both case numbers and age-standardized rates showed a recent downward trend, and projections indicate continued decline through 2046. Estimated annual percentage changes correlated positively with age-standardized rates and Human Development Index scores. The authors conclude that despite overall improvement, substantial disparities persist across regions and countries, warranting targeted interventions and sustained investment.

articles.headline: Global mapping of P. vivax circumsporozoite variants and mosquito susceptibility
articles.title_original: Updated global distribution of Plasmodium vivax circumsporozoite protein variants and their correlation with vector susceptibility: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Sampaio MP, Cerilo-Filho M, Amaral YAGD, Almeida MNG, Correa RI, Fernando DJV, Reis NF, Miceli LA, Silva JRS, Storti-Melo LM, Baptista ARS, Machado RLD
articles.details: This PRISMA systematic review and meta-analysis selected 94 studies (from 26,888 screened) to assess the global distribution of Plasmodium vivax circumsporozoite protein (PvCSP) variants—VK210, VK247, and P. vivax-like—in humans (65 studies) and Anopheles vectors (29 studies). VK210 was the most prevalent variant globally in both humans and vectors. Of 94 Anopheles species, 22 (23.4%) were susceptible to PvCSP-variant infections: all 22 (100%) to VK210 mono-infections, 14 (63.6%) to VK247, and six (27.3%) to mixed infections. The rare P. vivax-like variant was found in humans from Brazil and Guiana and in Anopheles oswaldoi and Anopheles daeneorum only in Brazil. The authors highlight implications for vector competence, control, and vaccine development.

articles.headline: Qualitative study of priority-setting in Ghana's HIV, TB, malaria programmes
articles.title_original: What are the priority-setting approaches for HIV/AIDS, TB and malaria programmes in Ghana? A qualitative perspective from key informants.
articles.authors: Aryeetey GC, Koduah A, Affram AA, Owusu R, Ruiz F, Vassall A, Nonvignon J
articles.details: This qualitative study explored priority-setting approaches in Global Fund-supported HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis, and malaria programmes in Ghana through in-depth interviews with ten key informants from the three disease programmes, the Ministry of Health, and global health partners, analyzed inductively and deductively. Four main approaches emerged: identification of health needs, stakeholder participation, transparency, and contextual factors, with priorities set via national strategies, partners, and surveillance data. Public involvement was limited, and contextual barriers included inadequate funding and aligning priorities with funders. The authors conclude that explicit priority-setting approaches were used only to a limited extent in these programmes.

articles.headline: New expansion microscopy method images malaria parasites inside mosquito tissues
articles.title_original: Mosquito Tissue Ultrastructure-Expansion Microscopy (MoTissU-ExM) enables ultrastructural and anatomical analysis of malaria parasites and their mosquito.
articles.authors: Liffner B, Silva TLAE, Vega-Rodriguez J, Absalon S
articles.details: This methods paper develops Mosquito Tissue Ultrastructure-Expansion Microscopy (MoTissU-ExM), adapting U-ExM (which physically expands samples ~4.5-fold) to dissected mosquito salivary glands and midguts. The technique preserves both host and parasite ultrastructure, enabling in situ visualization of oocysts and sporozoites, and the authors provide a step-by-step protocol. They validate expected expansion by directly comparing the same tissues before and after treatment and highlight key host and parasite structures made visible. The paper discusses use cases, drawbacks, troubleshooting, and future adaptations for studying malaria parasite biology.

articles.headline: Ellagic acid disrupts hemoglobin digestion in P. falciparum in vitro
articles.title_original: Effects of Ellagic Acid on Hemoglobin Ingestion, Transport, and Digestion in the Digestive Vacuole of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Thuduhena AC, Abu-Bakar N, Hamzah SN
articles.details: This in vitro study examined ellagic acid's antimalarial mechanism against chloroquine-sensitive (3D7) and chloroquine-resistant (W2) Plasmodium falciparum strains, using synchronized ring-stage parasites and transmission electron microscopy to assess its effects on hemoglobin ingestion, transport, and digestion. Ellagic acid significantly inhibited both strains (P < 0.001), with IC50 values of 1.0 ± 0.7 nM (3D7) and 1.3 ± 0.1 nM (W2), surpassing artemisinin (IC50 2.2 ± 0.2 nM for 3D7; 4.3 ± 0.3 nM for W2). Electron microscopy showed disruption of hemoglobin metabolism at the early trophozoite stage. The authors conclude ellagic acid is a promising antimalarial targeting hemoglobin processes in both strains.

articles.headline: False-positive malaria rapid test traced to African tick bite fever
articles.title_original: False-Positive Malaria Rapid Diagnostic Test Likely Due to African Tick Bite Fever: A Case Report.
articles.authors: Zewude RT, Ahmad SZ, Joseph T, Boggild AK
articles.details: This single case report describes a febrile traveler returning from South Africa who had a false-positive malaria rapid diagnostic test (RDT). The patient responded quickly to oral atovaquone-proguanil, but the ultimate diagnosis was African tick bite fever; repeat RDT and thick- and thin-film microscopy did not confirm malaria, and only rickettsial serology was contributory among microbiological tests. The authors use the case to highlight diagnostic test performance characteristics and the risk of premature diagnostic closure.

articles.headline: Chromosome-level reference genome for malaria mosquito Anopheles nili
articles.title_original: A chromosomal reference genome sequence for the malaria mosquito, Anopheles nili, Theobald, 1904.
articles.authors: Nsango SE, Ayala D, Agbor JP, Johnson HF, Heaton H, Wagah MG, Collins JC, Krasheninnikova K, Pelan SE, Pointon DB, Sims Y, Torrance JW, Tracey A, Uliano-Silva M, Wood JMD, von Wyschetzki K, McCarthy SA, Neafsey DE, Makunin A, Lawniczak MKN
articles.details: The authors present a genome assembly from an individual male Anopheles nili, a malaria vector mosquito, taken from a wild population in Cameroon. The genome spans 195 megabases, with most of the assembly scaffolded into three chromosomal pseudomolecules including an assembled X sex chromosome. The complete mitochondrial genome was also assembled and is 15.4 kilobases in length.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence varies by space and time in Western Kenya highlands
articles.title_original: Spatio-temporal characterization of malaria prevalence in a peri-urban resource limited setting of Western Kenya Highlands.
articles.authors: Muzame BA, Omukunda E, Mulama D, Okoth P
articles.details: This longitudinal study in Mbale town and surrounding Vihiga County, Western Kenya highlands, recruited 768 microscopy-confirmed malaria patients presenting at a health center from December 2019 to November 2020, using questionnaires and microscopy with linear regression analysis. P. falciparum accounted for 98.7% of infections, while P. malariae, P. vivax, and P. ovale together made up 1.3%. Age, gender, and location showed an effect on prevalence (R2 = 0.7, F(3,764) = 1.854, p < 0.136), and prevalence varied by month with rainfall and temperature. The authors conclude that spatio-temporal factors and mosquito breeding grounds influenced prevalence and that control strategies should be strengthened.

articles.headline: Acute kidney injury in severe malaria patients at a Rwandan hospital
articles.title_original: Factors associated with acute kidney injury and outcomes in patients with malaria in a district hospital in Rwanda.
articles.authors: Umuhire L, Dushimiyimana V, Nkuranyabahizi M, Ngendahayo F, Shyaka JC, Ngerageze I, Rajeswaran L, Chironda G
articles.details: This retrospective clinical audit reviewed 122 files of patients admitted with severe malaria at a district hospital in Rwanda in 2016-2017, using census sampling and descriptive and inferential statistics. Among confirmed severe malaria cases, 16.3% had acute kidney injury (AKI); significant associated clinical factors were dehydration (p=.01), high-grade fever (p=.002), profuse sweating (p=.034), vomiting (p=.043), and diarrhea (p=.025). Of the 20 patients who developed AKI, 55% completely recovered, 15% died, and 30% were transferred to higher facilities for hemodialysis. The authors call for educating district-hospital staff on diagnosing and managing AKI in complicated malaria.

articles.headline: Gender and cultural beliefs about malaria-to-typhoid progression in Central Kenya
articles.title_original: The relationship between gender and cultural beliefs of malaria into typhoid progression among rural rice farmers in Central Kenya.
articles.authors: Woldu D
articles.details: This cross-sectional study (N=250) used a structured questionnaire among Kikuyu rice farmers in the Mwea division of central Kenya to examine cultural beliefs that malaria progresses into typhoid and their association with gender. More than 62% of women and 47% of men adhered to the malaria-into-typhoid progression belief system, and multivariable logistic regression showed women were more than twice as likely as men to hold this belief (aOR 2.15; 95% CI 1.21–3.79, p < 0.009). The authors conclude these cultural factors underlie gender disparities in knowledge that could affect women's health outcomes.
